## Acknowledgments

Most statisticians have had occasion to rely on asymptotic or large sample arguments. This author is no exception. However, it happened that for a number of years, he also had to teach the subject. It appeared then that the theory was a bewildering maze without any logical structure. Out of pedagogical necessity an attempt was made to organize at least part of the subject. It turned out that one could indeed organize a part, and perhaps a sizable part, around very few essential ideas and elements.

This book is the outcome of such an organizational effort. The ideas and techniques used reflect first and foremost the influence of Abraham Wald's writings. Another very direct influence was that of Jerzy Neyman, who asked a variety of questions but who also promoted my academic career. Some other easily discernible influences are those of Jaroslav Hájek and Charles Stein. Not so visible, but indispensable, were the teachings of Etienne Halphen, who attempted (without success) to convert us to the Bayesian creed long before it became fashionable, but who also taught us a great deal about the interplay between theory and practice.

My first notes on "asymptotics" were written around 1955-56 with the help of Thomas S. Ferguson. They were "classical" in character, and it quickly became evident that we could not provide a comprehensive and logical account of the theory without some additional thinking. This took a number of years. As a result, the work went through many revisions, subjecting many secretaries to thankless work. Among the people who deserve high praise for turning my indecipherable manuscripts into elegant typescript, I must particularly thank Mrs. Julia Rubalcava, Miss Gail Coe, Mrs. Patricia Hardy, and Madame Micheline Marano. I also wish to extend belated thanks to Mrs. Ginette Henkin who, at one time, typed for me a treatise in French on Statistical Decision Theory. The work was never published; a few traces of it are left in Chapter 2 of this work.

The present version was prepared by Ms. "Bionic Fingers" Ruth Suzuki, whose patience and skill are most extraordinary indeed.

Finally, I wish to thank all the students who suffered through my lectures on the subject.

One of them, Mr. Yu-Lin Chang, took the trouble to read most of the manuscript with considerable care, thus saving me from the embarrassment of very many errors. There are certainly some left, but this is not his fault. Another student, Dr. Yannis Yatracos, compiled the references and the start of an index. Some other students contributed easily identifiable results. If these are mentioned here, I tried to give credit. In some other cases, it is more difficult to give credit because the influence is more spread out. For instance, I owe a special debt to Grace Yang, who in 1966-67 made me realize that even in the locally asymptotically normal case it can be advantageous to treat the problem as if it was what is now called "the random information case." I should have known that, but a glance at the Appendices of my 1960 paper shows that I missed the boat. Grace's suggestion turned out to be most valuable.

Some of the results reported here were, at appropriate times, the subject
of research efforts supported by various agencies. We are particularly indebted to the Alfred P. Sloan Foundation, the U.S. National Science Foundation, the U.S. Army Research Office, the Office of Naval Research, and to a lesser but non-negligible extent to the National Institutes of Health.

For a different kind of support, I am most indebted to my wife, Louise, who rarely complained about my idiosyncracies or about the considerable amount of family time I spent on the present subject.

Finally, I must thank the editorial and production departments at SpringerVerlag for their patience, their help, and a superb final product.

## To the Reader

## 1. Introduction

This book grew out of lectures delivered at the University of California, Berkeley, over many years. The subject is a part of asymptotics in statistics, organized around a few central ideas. The presentation proceeds from the general to the particular since this seemed the best way to emphasize the basic concepts. The reader is expected to have been exposed to statistical thinking and methodology, as expounded for instance in the book by H. Cramér [1946] or the more recent text by P. Bickel and K. Doksum [1977]. Another possibility, closer to the present in spirit, is Ferguson [1967]. Otherwise the reader is expected to possess some mathematical maturity, but not really a great deal of detailed mathematical knowledge. Very few mathematical objects are used; their assumed properties are simple; the results are almost always immediate consequences of the definitions. Some objects, such as vector lattices, may not have been included in the standard background of a student of statistics. For
these we have provided a summary of relevant facts in the Appendix.
The basic structures in the whole affair are systems that Blackwell called "experiments" and "transitions" between them. An "experiment" is a mathematical abstraction intended to describe the basic features of an observational process if that process is contemplated in advance of its implementation. Typically, an experiment consists of a set $\Theta$ of theories about what may happen in the observational process. Each theory $\theta \in \Theta$ specifies a probabilistic model for the behavior of the things under observation. This is summarized by a probability measure $P_{\theta}$ on a certain $\sigma$-field $\mathscr{A}$. The $\sigma$-field is independent of $\theta$. It is the collection of events for which the available instruments permit a determination of whether or not the event occurs. For technical reasons we have used a slightly different set-up, but this does not change anything of practical relevance. It was just more convenient to rewrite the definitions in such a way that the desired theorems are always true, instead of imposing restrictive conditions on a more standard system.

We have taken a restricted view of the aims of the statistician who contemplates performing an experiment. His goals are described in the framework of Wald's decision theory, with loss functions, risk functions, and related objects.

The main underlying theme can then be described as follows: The statistician faces a certain experiment $\mathscr{E}=\left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$. It may be a rather complex affair. He proceeds by searching for another experiment $\mathscr{F}=\left\{Q_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ that is (1) known and mathematically tractable, and (2) close enough to $\mathscr{E}$ so that techniques selected for $\mathscr{F}$ can be transformed into techniques for $\mathscr{E}$ without dire consequences.

There are very few known and tractable experiments. The one-dimensional Gaussian shift family may qualify. The same cannot be said of Gaussian shift families in higher dimensions. In spite of this, the large amount of space devoted here to Gaussian shift families is mostly a consequence of other facts. They impose themselves on one from many directions, such as the use of Taylor expansions, invariance properties, observational processes where each individual observation gives only a tiny bit of information, etc. Some of these reasons are detailed in Chapters 8, 9, and 10.

We have mentioned "approximating" an experiment $\mathscr{E}$ by another experiment $\mathscr{F}$. To make sense of this, a distance (or more precisely, a pseudo distance) between experiments has been introduced. This could be done in several manners. One possibility was to use only loss functions that are bounded by unity and insist that $\mathscr{E}$ is close to $\mathscr{F}$ if any risk function achievable on $\mathscr{E}$ is uniformly close to a risk function of $\mathscr{F}$, and conversely. Another possibility was to say that $\mathscr{E}$ and $\mathscr{F}$ are close to each other if each of the two can be very closely mimicked by a randomization of the other. It turns out that the two distances so defined are not only related, but exactly equal.

From time to time results are stated as limit theorems obtainable as something called $n$ "tends to infinity." This is especially so in Chapter 7 where the results are just limit theorems. Otherwise we have made a special effort to state the results in such a way that they could eventually be transformed into approximation results. Indeed, limit theorems "as $n$ tends to infinity" are log-
ically devoid of content about what happens at any particular $n$. All they can do is suggest certain approaches whose performance must then be checked on the case at hand. Unfortunately the approximation bounds we could get were too often too crude and cumbersome to be of any practical use. Thus we have let $n$ tend to infinity, but we would urge the reader to think of the material in approximation terms, especially in subjects such as the ones described in Chapter 11.

The main body of the theory makes no reference to independence of observations or any such structural aspects of the experiments under consideration. It is therefore applicable to many domains involving stochastic processes and other similar affairs. However, a great deal of the argumentation
is local, with approximations valid only in small neighborhoods. To piece these together (as in Chapter 11) requires the use of good auxiliary estimates. We have demonstrated the existence of such estimates only in situations that involve independent observations. One could do it in other contexts, for instance for Markov processes. However, recent results of L. Birgé convinced me that it could be premature to describe such situations at this time.

The passage from purely local results to global ones is described in Chapter 11 for the Gaussian approximations. One can do something similar for other exponential families. Some indications are given in Chapter 14, but a full theory is not developed there.

Chapters 16 and 17 treat, in some detail, experiments in which one observes independent or independent identically distributed random variables. This is mostly to illustrate the general theory and show that it encompasses a sizeable portion of standard asymptotics.

Even in the standard independent identically distributed case no attempt has been made to cover the entire field. A compendium of the results available for that case would require many volumes. The reader will find supplementary material in the book by Serfling [1980]; it hardly overlaps with this volume. Further material can be found in the book by I.A. Ibragimov and R.Z. Has'minskii [1979], whose spirit is closer to ours. At one time we had intended to give an account of the results on "second order efficiency" in line with the concepts developed here. This would take much space. For these results the reader is referred to the works of J. Pfanzagl [1980] and of M. Akahira and K. Takeuchi [1981].

## 2. Summary of Contents and Historical Notes

This is a brief description of the contents of this volume, chapter by chapter, with a few historical remarks. To mention "history" is to imply attributions of priority. We have not intentionally failed to attribute priorities, nor did we intentionally misattribute them. If that has happened by chance, we shall take refuge behind Steve Stigler's "law of eponymy" [1979] which states that "No scientific discovery is named after its original discoverer."

Chapter 1 gives the basic definitions involving experiments, decision spaces,
and transitions. The framework is not far removed from that used by A. Wald [1950], except that we remain in the nonsequential case. Also, we elected to work with vector lattices and positive linear operators instead of families of measures and Markov kernels. The main reason for this choice is that it allows one to work essentially as if all the objects used were finite sets. The basic compactness results (Theorem 2, Chapter 1) and the minimax theorem (Theorem 1, Chapter 2) are always true, instead of being encumbered by unappetizing ad hoc assumptions.

The necessary elements of the theory of vector lattices can be found in the Appendix. They were drawn from the papers of S. Kakutani [1941a] [1941b], J. Dieudonné [1944], and F. Riesz [1940]. A short account occurs in Bourbaki's Integration, Chapter 2. There are now extensive book-length accounts of the subject. Two excellent references are H.H. Schaefer [1974] and D. Fremlin [1974].

An important technical result of Chapter 1 is Theorem 1. It says that every transition can be approximated by very simple Markov kernels. The result is a strengthened version of a theorem in Le Cam [1964]. The strengthening was performed to accommodate a request by W. Moussatat.

For readers who prefer to work with measurable spaces and Markov kernels we have provided a translation in the form of representation theorems. For further information regarding the representation of positive operators by Markov kernels, see Sazonov [1962] and L. Schwartz [1973a] [1973b].

The name "experiment" for a family of probability measures seems to have been coined by D. Blackwell [1951]. The concept itself is much older; it was familiar to Wald [1939]. One could perhaps give part of the credit for its elaboration to Neyman and Pearson who repeatedly, and against R. A. Fisher's blandishments (see Fisher [1955], page 70), emphasized the necessity of considering all reasonable alternatives.

Chapter 2 gives some basic results from the theory of statistical decision functions (Wald [1950]), from the theory of comparison of experiments, and from the theory of approximation of experiments (Le Cam [1964]). Theorem 1 is a form of the minimax theorem akin to that given in Le Cam [1955]. It is followed by the attendant results on completeness of classes of procedures directly related to Bayes procedures. The initial minimax theorem is that of von Neumann [1928]. Many authors, including J. Ville [1938], Wald [1945], Karlin [1950], and Kneser [1952] gave extensions and modifications of the result. There are better minimax theorems due to M. Sion [1958]. See also C. Berge and Ghouila-Houri [1962].

Comparison of experiments was investigated by Bohnenblust, Shapley, and Sherman [1950] following an idea of von Neumann. The statistical wording with the relation to "sufficiency" is due to Blackwell [1951] [1953] and Stein [1951]; see also C. Boll [1955]. The idea of "deficiency" and distances is taken from Le Cam [1964]. The basic Theorem 2 says that several definitions of "deficiency" are all equivalent. It is taken from Le Cam [1964]. The elaboration in terms of conical measures (Choquet [1969]) was influenced by the work of Torgersen [1970]. The chapter also contains some results on the minimal forms of experiments. These are directly related to the idea of sufficiency.



Chapter 3 revolves around distributions of likelihood ratios and their connections with deficiencies or distances between experiments. It uses Blackwell's "canonical measures." Theorem 1 shows that, for finite parameter sets, the distance between experiments is bounded by the dual Lipschitz distance between their respective canonical measures. This has many implications. One of them is a compactness result, given in Theorem 1 and Theorem 2. The compactness result for finite parameter sets occurs in Le Cam [1969].

The general case, for the weak topology, was stated in Le Cam [1972]. That the "proof" given there is wrong was pointed out to me by W. Moussatat. I then gave a more elaborate proof using representations of conical measures along the lines of the last part of Chapter 2. The proof given here was obtained independently of the work of E. Siebert [1979], but, except for matters of notation and terminology, it appears analogous to that of Siebert.

Proposition 1, relating deficiencies and testing deficiencies, is taken from Torgersen [1970]. It implies the characterization of equivalence of experiments by isometries of their linear spans (Proposition 2). This characterization is given in Le Cam [1964] and in Torgersen [1970]. Theorem 3, characterizing boundedly complete experiments as extreme points, is imitated from Torgersen [1977]. Proposition 4 involves direct products of experiments and the fact that making a direct product amounts to convolute conical measures. The convolution operation admits a representation by pointwise products of the associated Hellinger transforms. Theorem 4 is a form of the Blackwell-Sherman-Stein theorem. For other related results, see V. Strassen [1965].

Chapter 4 gives a variety of inequalities. The inequalities between Hellinger distances and $L_{1}$-norms occur in C. Kraft [1955]. The relations with the chisquare like distance (called $k(P, Q)$ ) are easy, but we did not find them in the literature. The distance called $k(P, Q)$ occurs in a paper of Sanghvi [1953]. Proposition 2 and some of the results that follow it are essential in that they give relations between proximity of measures, as computed by $L_{1}$-norms, and proximity of likelihood ratios. The inequalities called ( $a$ ) or ( $d$ ) imply what is called Scheffé's theorem (Scheffé [1947]). The inequalities between conditional expectations (Proposition 3 and 4) will be used in connection with studies of "insufficiency" (Chapter 5) and convergence of posterior distributions (Chapter 12). Proposition 5 is about what happens if one uses for a pair ( $P_{0}, P_{1}$ ) tests that were designed to be optimal for a pair ( $G_{0}, G_{1}$ ) with small dual Lipschitz distances $\left\|P_{i}-G_{i}\right\|_{D}$.

Chapter 5 concerns sufficiency and approximate sufficiency. For a dominated experiment $\mathscr{E}=\left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ on a $\sigma$-field $\mathscr{B}$, a $\operatorname{sub} \sigma$-field $\mathscr{A} \subset \mathscr{B}$ is sufficient if and only if the experiment obtained by restricting the $P_{\theta}$ to $\mathscr{A}$ is equivalent to $\mathscr{E}$ itself. This was proved by Bahadur [1955] but had escaped my attention when I wrote Le Cam [1964]. It is not a direct consequence of the Blackwell-Sherman-Stein theorem. Here we list eight different properties and show that they are all equivalent to sufficiency. To avoid some technicalities and paradoxes (Burkholder [1961]) we work with positive projections in vector lattices instead of conditional expectation operations. The literature on such projections is large. See, for instance, Neveu [1972] and the references given therein.

If a $\sigma$-field $\mathscr{A} \subset \mathscr{B}$ is not sufficient there are several ways of measuring how far it is from being sufficient. One of them is the deficiency of Chapter 2. One can also see how much the $P_{\theta}$ should be modified to make $\mathscr{A}$ sufficient. This gives a different number called "insufficiency" here. Actually there are several variations on that theme and we were not able to show that they are all identical. At any rate they give numbers larger than the deficiencies. Examples are given of this, as well as theorems that allow bounding insufficiencies in terms of deficiencies in special cases. See, for instance, Proposition 5. Since deficiencies or insufficiencies can often be evaluated only on very small parts of the parameter spaces, a machine to put these local evaluations together is needed. Propositions 6 and 7 give some results in that direction. Proposition 8 corrects a wonderfully wrong inequality of Le Cam [1974].

Chapter 6 is about compactness and contiguity. It lists a theorem of Dunford, Pettis, and Grothendieck [1953] [1955]. (The proof is in the Appendix.) It also proves the equivalence of five definitions of contiguity and some useful corollaries of the definitions. Propositions 5 and 6 , dealing with joint limiting distributions, are easy. Special cases have appeared in the literature. (See Le Cam [1960], Hall and Loynes [1977] for instance.) The special case of limiting Gaussian distributions is briefly mentioned in Proposition 7. Other results of this type, for infinitely divisible limit distributions, are given in Chapter 16 as well as in Le Cam [1960]. A particular case occurs in Behnen and Neuhaus [1975]. The chapter ends with some results of Lindae [1972]. Theorem 1 of Section 4 is particularly important. It is one of very few links between the weak and strong convergences of experiments.

As explained earlier in this Introduction, we attempted to state results as much as possible so that they could be read as approximation theorems instead of limit theorems. Chapter 7 is an exception. It deals exclusively with limit theorems. Proposition 1 is about a relation between limit experiments and experiments obtained from taking limits in the sense of distributions. The latter are always weaker than the former. The situation where the two limits are equivalent deserves special mention. It is described here in terms of "stable" and "distinguished" sequences of statistics. It is shown that a sequence is distinguished only if one can approximately recover from it the likelihood ratios, and that in a nearly continuous manner. (See Theorem 1, and Proposition 2, Section 3). Theorem 2, Section 3 says that a sequence $\left\{T_{n}\right\}$ of statistics is "stable" if and only if any other sequence $\left\{T_{n}^{\prime}\right\}$, such that $T_{n}-T_{n}^{\prime}$ tends to zero in probability, is also asymptotically equivalent to $T_{n}$ for all statistical purposes. The results are improvements on statements given in Le Cam [1972].

Section 4 deals with a form of what is sometimes called the Hájek-Le Cam asymptotic minimax theorem. The name comes from the works of Le Cam [1953], Hájek [1972], and Le Cam [1972]. Hájek's paper of 1972 contains other results on local asymptotic admissibility. A fairly general form of the results is given in Section 5, Theorem 1. This is adapted from Le Cam [1979].

Chapter 8 deals with some properties related to invariance of distributions or experiments under the operation of transformation groups. We have used a form of the Markov-Kakutani fixed point theorem obtainable from the arguments of

F . Eberlein [1949]. For other results on almost invariant means or amenable groups, see Greenleaf [1969].

The Markov-Kakutani result yields easily the existence of what could be called "almost invariant Markov kernels." To make them actually invariant, we rely on a massive sledgehammer: locally compact groups admit liftings that commute with the group shifts (A. and C. Ionescu Tulcea [1967]). From this one easily obtains a form of the celebrated Hájek convolution theorem, from Hájek [1970], and some results of Torgersen [1972].

Section 4 of Chapter 8 deals with a peculiar phenomenon: In many situations statisticians pass to the limit for parameter spaces or statistics that are "renormalized" by multiplying by sequences of numbers (or matrices) that tend to infinity. Think, for instance, of the familiar $\sqrt{n}\left(\hat{\theta}_{n}-\theta\right)$. The peculiar phenomenon is that such renormalizations automatically imply certain invariance properties for the limits. We do this here for experiments and for limiting distributions. For a different account see Jeganathan [1982].

Section 5, Chapter 8 deals with a particular matter that arises from the above remark: If an experiment is invariant under shift and if it is an exponential family, what can it be? A partial characterization is given. It covers in particular the so-called mixed normal experiments, but includes certain other exponential families already described by E. Dynkin [1951].

Section 6 returns to invariance in general and gives a brief account of the Hunt-Stein theorem and of the Hall-Ghosh-Wijsman-Stein theorem [Hall-GhoshWijsman [1965]), together with a variation by Torgersen [1976].

Chapter 9 is about infinitely divisible experiments. They occur readily as approximations, or limits, for experiments involving many independent observations. The two basic types are the Gaussian shift experiments and the experiments in which one observes Poisson processes. Every infinitely divisible experiment can be obtained from direct products of Gaussian and Poisson experiments. The Poisson experiments are characterized in Section 4, Lemmas 3 and 4 . Their limits may involve Gaussian factors. They are described in Proposition 1 . Section 5 gives a very simple central limit theorem showing that, for a finite parameter set, direct products of experiments that are individually not too informative can be approximated by Poisson experiments. This is extended in Proposition 4 to certain cases where the parameter space is infinite. Examples show that the result is not valid without restrictions.

Chapter 10 is about asymptotically Gaussian experiments. It gives a variety of necessary and sufficient conditions for convergence of a sequence of experiments to a Gaussian one. The natural parameterization for Gaussian experiments is a Hilbert space. Most of the results are written in that framework. The results were written many years ago. Many even preceded W. Moussatat's thesis [1976]. Since that time many authors, e.g., Millar [1979] and Beran [1980], have found the Hilbert space approach convenient and useful in nonparametric situations.

Theorem 1 is a basic asymptotic sufficiency result. Section 3 describes a fairly general framework that I met in many applications. A particular feature
of it includes the possibility of replacing a parameter space by a space that is "tangent" to it. I used this since the mid-1950s without any references to the literature. That was so because, after a valiant effort, we did not find any.

However, W. Moussatat pointed out that the same idea occurs in a paper of H. Chernoff [1954]. It is hard to conceive that I had not heard Chernoff talk about it, but I certainly did not recollect it. Section 3 ends with some results on what has to be added to convergence of distributions to make experiments converge to Gaussian ones. It partially reproduces results of Le Cam [1977]. The gist of it is that if one has asymptotically normally distributed random vectors that behave as distinguished statistics, then the random part of the logarithms of likelihood ratios must be closely approximated by linear functions of these vectors.

Section 4 has to do with convergence in distribution to the standard Gaussian cylinder measure of a Hilbert space. Lemma 6 and Proposition 3 relate that to approximations of experiments indexed by the full Hilbert space by other experiments with a finite dimensional parameter space.

Section 5 is a digression intended mostly to show that for many stochastic processes one can obtain limit Gaussian experiments. It uses certain martingale limit theorems as suggested in Billingsley [1961] and Le Cam [1974]. For more recent results see P. Jeganathan [1981], Hall and C. Heyde [1980], and Greenwood and Shiryayev [1985].

Section 6 returns to asymptotically Gaussian experiments indexed by a Hilbert space. It gives a variety of asymptotic admissibility (or minimaxity) results derivable from a basic result of T.W. Anderson [1955]. Most authors prefer to Radonify the standard Gaussian cylinder measure of the Hilbert space by plunging it into a suitable Banach space completion. This is the same kind of thing as replacing white noise by a Wiener process. One can show that this does not change at all the standard Gaussian shift experiment of the Hilbert space. To obtain results about subconvex loss functions $l$ with finite minimum risk, we have used instead an extension of the domain of definition of the Gaussian cylinder measure. An extension of this kind seems to have been used by Skohorod [1974]. One of the statements (Proposition 5 and its Corollary) is that sets of the type $B=\{w: l(w) \leq \alpha\}$ must have polars that are G.B. sets in the sense of Dudley [1967].

The arguments of Chapter 10 yield a number of results applicable in familiar cases to asymptotics in shrinking neighborhoods of a fixed parameter value. They are of little practical importance in cases where one desires to estimate the value of a parameter. For that, one needs results of a more global character. A particular set of results of that nature is given in Chapter 11. It relies on the fact that, given an already well behaved auxiliary estimate, one can work in a neighborhood of the estimated value and obtain more refined estimates that are asymptotically sufficient.

Any statistician who has at some time tried to get an approximation to a maximum likelihood estimate through Newton's method will understand immediately the spirit of that chapter. The technique used is the basis of the assertions in Le Cam [1956] and more generally in Le Cam [1960]. Unfortu-
nately every time I wrote the necessary theory in what was supposed to be a definitive form, someone came along with a problem in which my conditions were not quite satisfied. Thus it appeared necessary to write down a rather general version of the theory. The version to be found here covers every case in which finite dimensional Gaussian shift approximations exist. It can be specialized to cases where appropriate Euclidean parameterizations are assumed. It also covers parameterizations through differentiable manifolds.

A previous version used tangent spaces, as in Chapter 10, Section 3. One of my friends complained that "Tu 1'as écrit exprès pour qu'on ne puisse pas le lire." That was not true. However, that version was unable to cope with a very simple problem on logistic models brought to me by Clare Mahan [1979]. The new version is hopefully not subject to such difficulties.

The basic construction involves certain quadratic expressions that could be used as approximations for the logarithms of likelihood ratios of Gaussian experiments. In applications, the matrices used in these quadratics are often random, thus we have allowed that. There are cases in which the random character of the matrices is very essential; the construction still works.

Thus, the so-called "locally asymptotically mixed normal" families come under the scope of the method. Even though the chapter starts with that degree of generality, what it aims for is only the locally asymptotically Gaussian case.

Section 2 gives a few supplementary explanations on the why and how of the framework used here. Section 3 describes the items to be used in the construction and the construction itself. The basic construction assumes that certain quadratic forms are available. An alternate form uses the parallelogram identities to construct the necessary quadratic forms.

Section 4 gives a series of definitions relating to approximations of logarithms of likelihood ratios by quadratic expressions. The point is that the construction of Section 3 fits quadratics to these logarithms on a small set. One wants the approximations to hold on larger sets. Section 5 records such an approximability result in the form of Theorem 1 and a variant, Theorem 2, using quadratics obtained by the mid-point method. One of the conditions placed on the items used in the construction is a cardinality restriction on the range of the auxiliary estimates. It is shown that if one has estimates that converge at the right rate one can discretize them to satisfy the required restriction.

Section 6 specializes the results of Section 5 to the asymptotically Gaussian situation. Besides the basic approximability requirement (G1) of Definition 1, it uses various conditions that bear on the items used in the construction, as in Section 5. It is shown later on (Lemma 4 and Proposition 5) that, under (G1) and a few other restrictions, the basic sets and quadratics of the construction can themselves be constructed. Thus the only very essential assumption to be made otherwise is the existence of good auxiliary estimates. Even though the asymptotically finite dimensional case is the only one for which entirely satisfactory results exist, we have indicated some possibilities for precompact infinite dimensional situations. In the asymptotically Gaussian case one needs to work with random quadratic forms and with nonrandom ones. There are many possibilities depending on what needs to be achieved. We have indicated
the possibility of constructing several families of quadratic forms, with different properties. Theorem 1 gives a basic approximation property. Proposition 1 records some of the usual implications for convergence of distributions. Theorem 2 is an aside that may be useful to deal with nuisance parameters. Theorem 3 is a basic result on asymptotic sufficiency, imitated from Le Cam [1956] and Le Cam [1960]. These theorems do not depend very much on the local structure of the underlying parameter spaces. The assumptions most often used in the literature imply that, locally, the structure of the parameter space is very much like that of an entire Euclidean space. This leads to Definition 8 and some of its consequences.

Section 7 specializes the results of Section 6 to those cases commonly referred to as LAN in the literature. They assume a Euclidean structure for the parameter space and special properties of the quadratic forms and norms used.

Section 8 is aimed at showing that one can effectively reduce the approximately Gaussian case to the heteroschedastic Gaussian one by small distortions. Results of this nature occur in Wald [1943] and Le Cam [1956]. Michel and Pfanzagl [1970] show that one can approximate the experiments by others where the likelihood ratios have exactly the Gaussian form, except that they are multiplied by functions of $\theta$ that tend to unity. Section 9 records optimality properties for the standard tests used in the Gaussian case. Then it shows how to translate them for the asymptotic framework. It is imitated from Wald [1943]. It also discusses very briefly the locally asymptotically mixed normal situation.

Section 10 is a digression on chi-square, minimum chi-square estimates, and similar topics. It is taken mostly from my class notes of 1959 and before. However, we have added some techniques of Rao and Robson [1974]. Some additional information can be found in Dudley [1976] and in Le Cam, Mahan, and Singh [1983].

Chapter 12 is about convergence properties for posterior distributions. Throughout, the attitude is to look at joint distributions and not at what happens at particular values of the parameter $\theta$. Section 2 deals with some simple inequalities relating integrated $L_{1}$-distances between posterior distributions to distances between joint distributions. It also contains some results about the possibility of "localization" of the arguments. It does not cover the results of G. Steck [1957] that rely on equicontinuity assumptions. Section 3 is a rewrite of the first part of Le Cam [1958]. It tries to say that Bayes estimates will converge at the maximum possible rate. Section 4 deals with situations where the posterior distributions are asymptotically Gaussian. A result of the same general occurs in Laplace [1820].

Several of the results collected here were taken from an unpublished paper of Le Cam [1968]. Proposition 5 occurs in Le Cam [1974] but the proof given there is not correct. For further results see Jeganathan [1980], and Basawa and B.L.S. Prakasa Rao [1980].

Theorems 2 and 3 given here can be viewed as integrated versions of what is often called a Bernstein-von Mises theorem, from the works of Bernstein [1917] and von Mises [1931]. Actually, Bernstein's theorem deals with a different matter, namely the (vague) convergence of posterior distributions given the
average of the observations in a shift situation.
Chapter 13 is a rewrite of Le Cam [1979]. It is concerned with a curious property of sequential experiments. No assumptions about independence of the successive observations are made, but one assumes that for each fixed nonrandom stopping rule the experiments are approximable by exponential families. It then turns out that the sequential experiments can be approximated by stopping times used on processes with independent increments. If the approximating exponential families are Gaussian, the relevant process is standard Brownian motion, with the necessary dimension.

Chapter 14 is an attempt to do with more general exponential families what was done in Chapters 10 and 11 for Gaussian approximations. The reason for such an attempt is clear: Exponential families offer a much wider range of possibilities than the Gaussian ones. The main results say essentially (and under some restrictions) that an experiment $\mathscr{E}$ can be approximated by an exponential family if and only if the logarithms of likelihood ratios in $\mathscr{E}$ satisfy approximately the linearity restrictions that are exactly satisfied by the exponential family. Such results apply in particular to the so-called LAMN families mentioned in Chapter 11. It is suggested that one can carry out for exponential families a program similar to that of Chapter 11 and that even without knowing very precisely which exponential family would fit. In general, the dimension of the sufficient statistics may be larger than that of the parameter space, as in the LAMN case.

Chapter 15 is an aside on central limit theorems and related approximation theorems. Some of the results, but relatively few of them, will be used in Chapter 16. We have given a detailed treatment to emphasize that one should as much as possible think in terms of approximations instead of limits. The method used is based on works of P. Lévy [1937] and Kolmogorov [1956] [1963] using concentration functions and the bounds of Esseen [1966] and Kanter [1976]. The main approximation result is Theorem 1, Section 4. As an example of use of the concentration inequalities, we have included a special result, Proposition 4 of Section 2. It is nice in that it involves no recentering of the variables, but the main reason for including it here is that we do not know any simple proof for it. There are recent papers of Arak and Zaitsev which give much better results.

Section 3 is about shift-compactness and related topics. Section 5 is more classical in that it deals with limit theorems. However, we have emphasized the choice of centering constants since that is very essential for the results of Chapter 16. For another treatment, without emphasis on centering constants, the reader is referred to Araujo and Giné [1980].

All the preceding chapters, except Chapter 15 which is an aside, deal with statistical problems in general, without any assumptions of independence or whatever on the observations. The classical results of asymptotic statistical theory were obtained first assuming that one observes independent identically distributed variables and that the number of such observations tends to infinity. The purpose of Chapters 16 and 17 is to show that the present general theory does apply in such cases and that it allows one to recover the results usually associated with the maximum likelihood method.

Chapter 16 deals with independent observations that are not necessarily identically distributed. At many places it is assumed that no single observation gives much information. Otherwise the distributions can vary fairly arbitrarily. Section 2 is concerned mostly with binary experiments and limiting distributions for likelihood ratios. It is mostly a rewrite of Le Cam [1966] and [1969]. It contains necessary and sufficient conditions for contiguity in forms applicable to likelihood ratios for individual observations or their square roots. Conditions of this type were also given by Oosterhoff and van Zwet [1979] without any negligibility assumptions. A somewhat more general result occurs in Le Cam and Traxler [1978]. Proposition 5 is imitated from Le Cam [1960]. Section 3 deals with asymptotic normality of logarithms of likelihood ratios.

In Section 4, the parameter space is metrized by a Hilbertian distance $H$ obtained from sums of the square Hellinger distances on components. It gives some results on probability of errors for balls for the distance $H$. For the case of independent identically distributed observations, the results are easy. They are imitated from Le Cam [1973]. The general independent case is more complex. Theorem 1 reproduces a result of L. Birgé [1980]. Lemma 12 and its consequences are adapted from letters of L. Birgé [1981]. One of the remarkable facts is that even though the $H$-balls do not satisfy the capacity requirements of Huber and Strassen [1973], the likelihood ratio tests between their closest points have very good minimax-like properties. However, Lemma 12 gives a bound on the probabilities of error for arbitrary product measures.

Section 5 uses multiple testing procedures to produce estimates. The first procedure described is that of L . Birgé [1980]. It is a modification of a procedure proposed in Le Cam [1973], also described in this same section. In both cases, the performance of the estimates are related to the Kolmogorov dimension (Kolmogorov $[1954])$ of the space $(\Theta, H)$. It has been shown by Birge [1980] that the rates of convergence obtainable in this manner are the best possible in several situations, including estimation of densities restricted to lie in Sobolev balls. See also Bretagnolle and Huber [1976]. One knows that in some particular cases the Kolmogorov dimension is not the right thing to use, but no alternative general concept has been proposed.

Section 6 deals with the risk of Bayes estimates under the same kind of dimensionality restrictions. For the Bayes estimates, or for the WeissWolfowitz [1966] maximum probability estimates, we need conditions that are stronger than those used for the methods of Section 5. The same is true for Grenander's [1981] method of "sieves." Section 6 owes a lot to L . Schwartz [1965].

One of the most favored procedures to construct estimates is in fact a class of methods called "minimum distance methods." See Neyman [1949] and Wolfowitz [1957]. Here we look at empirical measures and distances, or norms obtained by taking suprema over certain families of sets. For the usual KolmogorovSmirnov distance, appropriate references are DvoretzkyKiefer and Wolfowitz [1956], Kiefer and Wolfowitz [1958], and Kiefer [1961], [1972].

For the case of nonidentically distributed independent observations this author observed long ago (see Le Cam [1961], Le Cam [1969]) that a Poissonization technique can be used. However, better techniques arose in connection with the
work of D. Pollard [1981]. For the i.i.d. case and VapnikCrervonenkis classes of sets, an essential paper is Dudley [1978]. The version given here allows the classes to change as the number of observations changes. More precisely it gives bounds applicable to any particular system of product measures. For other bounds see Bretagnolle [1980] and K. Alexander [1982]. For a better treatment of measurability problems, and many other results, see Dudley and Phillip [1983]. There are easy ways to pass from sets to functions (see Le Cam [1982]), but we have not included the work of Pollard [1981], since his book, Pollard [1984], is most readable.

Chapter 17 deals with independent identically distributed observations in the most classical sense. There, the Hilbert spaces of Chapter 11 can be realized in many ways. Differentiability of maps from Euclidean spaces to these Hilbert spaces is a weaker condition than the classical Cramér conditions (Cramér [1946]) but it does imply many things. We give a theory in many ways analogous to that of Cramér [1946]. We also point out the relation between differentiability and the usual $\sqrt{n}$ convergence rate (see Le Cam [1970]). Lemmas 5 and 6 of Section 2 improve on a badly messed up proof and result of Le Cam [1970], according to a verbal remark of J. Hájek. See also Hájek and Sidak [1967]. In Section 3 we have included some remarks about the very definition of "Fisher information" and a number of results taken from Saks [1937] and Gelfand [1938] about existence of derivatives. Section 4 is concerned with the possibility of approximation by linearly indexed Gaussian experiments with rates of convergence other than $\sqrt{n}$. There are examples, some of which are treated in detail by Woodroofe [1972]. I did not ignore them in Le Cam [1969], but the rates must be of the type $(\sqrt{n}) \phi(n)$ with $\phi(n)$ slowly varying.

Section 5 is mostly a rewrite of a paper by Le Cam and Schwartz [1960]. It gives necessary and sufficient conditions for the existence of consistent estimates. It also recalls a result of Vapnik and Cervonenkis [1971] on the convergence of empirical measures. Section 6 deals with a variety of results and methods that yield $\sqrt{n}$-consistent estimates. It gives a partial account of some results of R . Beran [1981] on the construction of "robust" estimates. Section 7 is about Bayes estimates. It shows, according to Doob [1949], that they are almost everywhere (in the measure sense) consistent, and according to Freedman [1963], [1965], that they can be inconsistent "nearly everywhere," in a topological sense.

This same section gives a form of the theorem called the Bernstein-von
Mises theorem. Section 8 is about maximum likelihood. The method is praised almost everywhere in the literature (see Efron [1978]). For some remarkable results, see Pfanzagl and Wefelmeyer [1978]. Because of this we just give counterexamples, taken from Neyman and Scott [1948], Kiefer and Wolfowitz [1956], and Bahadur [1958]. Section 9 shows that much of the theory remains applicable to cases where the number of observations is determined at random by the observations themselves.

The Appendix forms a short course in analysis, with emphasis on subjects in which we often found our students not entirely well prepared.

## CHAPTER 1

## Experiments-Decision Spaces

## 1. Introduction

The basic structure which underlies most of the present notes, as well as a large part of what is called decision theory, is a mathematical abstraction intended to represent an "experiment".

The structure traditionally used to this effect consists of a family $\left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ of probability measures on a $\sigma$-field $\mathscr{A}$ carried by a set $\mathscr{X}$. Since we shall use throughout a slightly different definition, some explanation may be helpful.

In common parlance an experiment may be any sort of activity in which one makes observations. The role of the various mathematical objects mentioned above is best understood if one contemplates the situation in advance of the actual activation of the experiment. In this optique, a possible interpretation is the following.
(i) The class $\mathscr{A}$ is a list of events which might conceivably occur during the experiment. To belong to the list an event should preferably satisfy two conditions. First, the experimenter must have it in mind. Second, there should be some available machinery to decide whether or not the event occurs.
(ii) Each element of the set $\Theta$ represents a particular "theory" about the physical phenomena involved in the experiment. Each such theory is translated by assignment of probabilities $P_{\theta}(A)$ to the events of the class $\mathscr{A}$.
(iii) The set $\mathscr{X}$ which carries $\mathscr{A}$ is presumably the set of all possible results of the experiment. Giving the "result" $x$ is deemed to be equivalent to listing all the events which have occurred.

If all the sets involved in the preceding description are finite, this representation carries some force of persuasion, especially for those who have been trained to accept it by the usual books and courses. It would be very naive to take for granted that, even in the finite case, the relations implied by the above structure cannot be challenged. For instance, the usual identification of " $A$ implies $B$ " with the set relation $A \subset B$ entails that $\mathscr{A}$ is a lattice, and even worse, a distributive lattice. Without resorting to quantum theories, one can easily doubt that events of the real world are defined and measurable to such accuracy as to permit such an assertion. One can also doubt that the class $\mathscr{A}$ need not depend on the "theory" $\theta$. One can also wonder whether such a simplified description is adequate for experiments such as psychological measurements in which the simple fact of carrying out a measurement modifies what was measured.

We shall not dwell here on this type of difficulty. However, we need to point out that, when $\mathscr{A}$ is not finite, the traditional description makes use of certain mathematical constructs which have been introduced for convenience in other contexts. Since there the matter of mathematical convenience is the relevant one, one may feel free to vary the constructs as long as their relations to the



real world are not essentially affected.
We have chosen here to dispense with the set $\mathscr{X}$ representing the "results." One can always reintroduce it, or more exactly, several forms of it, by using as "points" complete listings of systems of events which could occur in an experiment. The sets so created will vary according to whether one assumes that any family of events whose finite subfamilies are "compatible" is also compatible or, on the contrary, introduce additional compatibility requirements for infinite families. The relations between infinite lattice operations (such as inf or sup) and the corresponding set intersections and unions are affected by such choices, and so is the possible "countable additivity" of the "probabilities" $P_{\theta}$.

Because the condition that $\mathscr{A}$ is a $\sigma$-field, that is a Boolean algebra closed under countable intersections, is also affected by the choice of the set $\mathscr{X}$, we have dispensed with it and replaced it by a completeness relation understandable in terms of the use to be made of the "probabilities" $P_{\theta}$. This is, of course, very essential to mathematical derivations but is just as legal a procedure as introducing "real" numbers as limit of rational numbers and then pretending that, since we are used to them, the real numbers exist.

We do not want to imply that the Boolean algebra $\mathscr{A}$ is of no significance. On the contrary, the specification of $\mathscr{A}$ is one mathematical procedure by which one attempts to specify what the experimenter can and cannot do. This is acutely visible in the description of sequentially conducted experiments. We do mean to convey the idea that in many infinite models "points" are objects which are remote from direct experience and that the way of handling infinite operations on the Boolean algebra is best left to considerations which need not necessarily follow traditions established for entirely different purposes. Finally, challenging the idea that $\mathscr{A}$ needs to be a Boolean algebra is far from idiotic, even though we have elected to ignore this aspect here.

Before introducing the definition to be used in the sequel, let us consider another possible description of "experiments." Let us retain the idea that there is a set $\Theta$ of available "theories," but agree that there are certain objects called "random variables" which have a life of their own in the physical world but have the property that if "measured" in an experiment they produce a real number. (One can argue, as above, that real numbers do not really exist, but one can also argue with Archimedes that "measurable magnitudes" are representable by numbers.) If two "random variables" can be "measured" in the same experiment, one obtains two numbers which can be added, multiplied, etc. One can also take the minimum or maximum of the two, multiply them by other real numbers, and so forth.

This leads to the possibility of representing an "experiment" by a family of "random variables" which are quite undefined but form a vector space $V$. Retaining only those variables which are bounded, one can express the relations induced by the theory $\theta$ by giving a linear functional $P_{\theta}$ on the space $V$. For $v \in V$, the value $v P_{\theta}$ is the "expectation" of the variable $v$. A more complete description of what this leads to is excellently given by I. Segal.

For the present purposes, we shall ignore the difficulties created by noncommutativity, and insist that an experiment is given by a vector lattice $V$ and a
family of positive linear functionals $\left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ on the lattice $V$. Assuming that $V$ contains that random variable $I$ whose value is identically unity, we shall also require that $I P_{\theta}=1$, always.

Such a definition is very close to the definition to be retained in the sequel except for one circumstance. The lattice $V$ need not be complete in any reasonable sense. Thus, limiting arguments may become painful. To avoid this, we shall complete $V$ appropriately. This will give a very usable definition. Note, however, that $V$ represents in principle the family of bounded random variables which can be observed. When appropriate concepts of computability can be introduced, the difference between $V$ and its completion will need to be investigated. Taking into account these preliminaries, we can now introduce a few standard pieces of notation which will finally lead to a precise definition.

## 2. Vector Lattices- $L_{1}$-Spaces-Transitions

By a vector space, without any further qualification, will be meant a vector space over the field of real numbers. A lattice is an ordered set in which any two elements $x, y$, have a minimum $x \wedge y$ and a maximum $x \vee y$. An ordered vector space $\mathscr{F}$ is a vector space with an order structure which is compatible with the vector structure in the sense that $x \leq y$ implies $x+z \leq y+z$ and $\lambda x \leq \lambda y$ for all $z \in \mathscr{F}$ and all real numbers $\lambda \geq 0$. An ordered vector space which is a lattice is called a vector lattice.

In a vector lattice, we shall write $x^{+}=x \vee 0$ and $x^{-}=(-x) \vee 0$ and also $|x|=x^{+}+x^{-}$. Two elements $x$ and $y$ of $\mathscr{F}$ are called disjoint if $|x| \wedge|y|=0$. The interval $[x, y]$ determined by the pair $(x, y)$ is the set $[x, y]=\{z \in \mathscr{F}$; $x \leq z \leq y\}$. A norm $\|\cdot\|$ on the vector lattice $\mathscr{F}$ is compatible with the order if $|x| \leq|y|$ implies $\|x\| \leq\|y\|$.

A Banach lattice $\mathscr{F}$ is a vector lattice with a compatible norm for which $\mathscr{F}$ is complete. The Banach lattice $\mathscr{F}$ is called an $L$-space if its norm is such that $x \geq 0$ and $y \geq 0$ implies $\|x+y\|=\|x\|+\|y\|$. It is called an $M$-space if $x \geq 0$, $y \geq 0$ implies $\|x \vee y\|=(\|x\|) \vee(\|y\|)$.

A vector lattice $\mathscr{F}$ is called order complete (or simply "complete") if every order bounded subset $A \subset \mathscr{F}$ has a supremum $\sup A$ in $\mathscr{F}$. A band $\mathscr{F}_{1}$ in a complete vector lattice $\mathscr{F}$ is a vector space $\mathscr{F}_{1} \subset \mathscr{F}$ such that
(i) $y \in \mathscr{F}_{1},|x| \leq y$ implies $x \in \mathscr{F}_{1}$.
(ii) For every set $A \subset \mathscr{F}_{1}$ which is bounded in $\mathscr{F}$ the supremum $\sup A$ (in $\mathscr{F}$ ) belongs to $\mathscr{F}_{1}$.

Let $\mathscr{F}$ be an $L$-space and let $\mathscr{S}$ be a subset of the positive cone $\mathscr{F}^{+}= \left\{x^{+} ; x \in \mathscr{F}\right\}$ of $\mathscr{F}$. The smallest band containing $\mathscr{S}$ is also an $L$-space. It is the smallest vector subspace $\mathscr{F}_{1}$ of $\mathscr{F}$ which is complete for the norm and is such that if $\mu \in \mathscr{F}$ satisfies $0 \leq \mu \leq \sum s_{j}$ for some finite sum of elements of $\mathscr{S}$ then $\mu \in \mathscr{F}_{1}$.

The dual of an $L$-space $L$, that is, the space of continuous linear functionals on the Banach space $L$, coincides with the space of linear functionals which are order bounded in the sense that they transform intervals $[x, y]$ of $L$ into bounded
intervals of the line.
Let $M$ be the dual of $L$. Call an element $u \in M$ positive if $\left\langle u, \phi^{+}\right\rangle \geq 0$ for every $\phi \in L$. For this order, $M$ is a vector lattice. It has a "unit" $I$ defined by $I \phi=\left\|\phi^{+}\right\|-\left\|\phi^{-}\right\|$. The conjugate norm $\|u\|=\sup \{|\langle u, \phi\rangle| ;\|\phi\| \leq 1\}$ makes $M$ into an $M$-space. The norm can also be defined by $\|u\|=\inf \{\alpha ;|u| \leq \alpha I\}$. Let $D$ be a set and let $\Gamma$ be a set of bounded real valued functions defined on D . Such a set will be called a uniform lattice if it satisfies the following conditions:
(i) $\Gamma$ is a vector space and a vector lattice for the operations carried out pointwise;
(ii) the function $I$, identically equal to unity on $D$ is an element of $\Gamma$;
(iii) for the norm defined by $\|\gamma\|=\inf \{\alpha ;|\gamma| \leq \alpha I\}$ the space $\Gamma$ is complete.

For this norm the space $\Gamma$ has a dual $\Gamma^{*}$ which is an $L$-space for the order and the norm induced by $\Gamma$. One of the most important objects we shall deal with can be defined as follows.

Let $L^{\prime}$ and $L^{\prime \prime}$ be two $L$-spaces. A transition $T$ from $L^{\prime}$ to $L^{\prime \prime}$ is a positive linear map of $L^{\prime}$ into $L^{\prime \prime}$ such that $\left\|T \mu^{+}\right\|=\left\|\mu^{+}\right\|$for every $\mu \in L^{\prime}$. The adjoint of a transition is a positive linear map of the dual $M^{\prime \prime}$ of $L^{\prime \prime}$ into the dual $M^{\prime}$ of $L^{\prime}$ for which the unit of $M^{\prime}$ is the image of the unit of $M^{\prime \prime}$. Let us pass now to the statistical objects.

## 3. Experiments-Decision Procedures

Definition. An experiment $\mathscr{E}$ indexed by the set $\Theta$ is a map $\theta \mapsto P_{\theta}$ from $\Theta$ to an $L$-space. This map is subject to the restrictions that each $P_{\theta}$ is positive and that $\left\|P_{\theta}\right\|=1$.

Here $\Theta$ represents a set of "theories" about the natural mechanism of the experiment and each $P_{\theta}$ represents the operator which yields the mathematical expectation of the bounded observable variables.

The band generated by $\left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ is also called the $L$-space of $\mathscr{E}$ or the $L$-space generated by $\mathscr{E}$. It will be denoted $L(\mathscr{E})$. It has for its dual an $M$-space, to be denoted $M(\mathscr{E})$. This $M$-space will be identified here to the (completion of the) space of bounded observable random variables.

Let $\Gamma$ be a uniform lattice on a set $D$. A transition $T$ from the $L$-space $L(\mathscr{E})$ of an experiment to the dual $\Gamma^{*}$ of $\Gamma$ will also be called a decision procedure or statistic, or an estimate based or available on $\mathscr{E}$ and taking values in $D$. This is somewhat abusive, but corresponds to the idea that such transitions represent randomized estimates or slight generalizations of what is usually meant by this term.

For a pair ( $L, \Gamma$ ) as above, we shall use a notation imitated from the usual matrix notation as follow. If $T$ is a transition from $L$ to $\Gamma^{*}$, we shall denote its transpose by the same letter and write $\gamma T \mu$ for the number which is (i) the value $\langle\gamma, T \mu\rangle$ of $T \mu \in \Gamma^{*}$ at $\gamma \in \Gamma$; (ii) the value $\langle\gamma T, \mu\rangle$ of $\gamma T \in M$ at $\mu \in \mathrm{L}$; (iii) the value of $T$, considered as a bilinear map from $\Gamma \times L$ to the line, at the pair $(\gamma, \mu) \in \Gamma \times L$. This notation is also a remembrance of the form

$$
\gamma T \mu=\int\left[\int \gamma(s) T(d s, x)\right] \mu(d x)
$$

obtainable in the representation by measures and Markov kernels.
Consider an experiment $\mathscr{E}$ indexed by a set $\Theta$. To describe a decision problem in the sense of Wald, one needs not only the experiment $\mathscr{E}$, but two additional objects: (i) a set $D$ of "possible decisions"; (ii) a loss function $W$ which maps $\Theta \times D$ into the set $(-\infty,+\infty]$. It will always be assumed that for each $\theta \in \Theta$ one has

$$
\inf _{t \in D} W_{\theta}(t)>-\infty
$$

In addition we shall consider only decision problems which are regular in the sense that one is given a certain uniform lattice $\Gamma$ on the set $D$. The lattice $\Gamma$ will be used to define decision procedures. Relations between $\Gamma$ and $W$ need not be assumed except for possible representation of risks by ordinary integrals. For an experiment $\mathscr{E}$ and a regular decision space ( $D, W, \Gamma$ ), one poses the following definition.

Definition. A decision procedure $\rho$ is a transition from the $L$-space $L(\mathscr{E})$ of $\mathscr{E}$ to the dual $\Gamma^{*}$ of $\Gamma$. The risk at $\theta$ of the procedure $\rho$ is the value defined by

$$
R(\theta, \rho)=W_{\theta} \rho P_{\theta}=\sup _{\gamma}\left\{\gamma \rho P_{\theta} ; \gamma \in \Gamma, \gamma \leq W_{\theta}\right\}
$$

Each decision procedure $\rho$ gives a risk function $\theta \mapsto R(\theta, \rho)$ defined on $\Theta$. For an experiment $\mathscr{E}$ and a regular decision space ( $D, W, \Gamma$ ) there is a certain space $\mathscr{B}(\Gamma, L)$ of available decision procedure. This is the set of all transitions from $L$ to $\Gamma^{*}$, or equivalenty the set of all positive normalized bilinear forms on $\Gamma \times L$. To this space corresponds a space $R(\mathscr{E}, D, W, \Gamma)$ of available risk functions, namely, the space of all functions $f$ from $\Theta$ to $(-\infty,+\infty]$ which are such that for some $\rho \in \mathscr{B}(\Gamma, L)$ one has $W_{\theta} \rho P_{\theta} \leq f(\theta)$ for all $\theta \in \Theta$.

As far as computation of risk functions goes, the structure of the set $D$ is rather immaterial since the only relevant feature is that $D$ specifies which functions $\theta \mapsto W_{\theta}$ can be used. However, the above formulation uses the uniform lattice $\Gamma$ as a crutch in the definition of procedures and risk. Different lattices $\Gamma$ may well lead to different sets $\mathscr{B}(\Gamma, L)$. We have kept this formulation to make it possible to return to the more traditional definitions. The definitions given above have been selected to insure that the decision procedures form a compact set on which the risk functions are lower semicontinuous. The consequences of this will appear in Chapter 2.

Some readers would probably be more at ease within the traditional definitions where an experiment is given by probability measures and where decision procedures are Markov kernels. For the definitions given here one can always construct representations of this more traditional nature. However, there are many possible representations, each with merits and defects. It is more convenient to ignore them and work in the vector lattice framework. Indications on the representations are given at the end of this chapter.

## 4. A Basic Density Theorem

We shall now state a theorem which is important on two separate counts. First, it suggests that the present definitions of decision procedures and similar objects have not been stretched beyond the pale. The class $\mathscr{B}(\Gamma, L)$ is essentially the smallest class which is closed under reasonable limiting operations and contains the simplest of all decision procedures. Second, the theorem is a powerful technical tool in various arguments.

Let $L$ be an $L$-space with dual $M$. Let $H$ be a linear subspace of $M$ which is a sublattice in the sense that $u \in H$ implies $|u| \in H$. Let $\Gamma$ be a uniform lattice on a set $D$. A transition $T$ from $L$ to $\Gamma^{*}$ will be called $(\Gamma, H)$ continuous if for every $\gamma \in \Gamma$ the image $\gamma T$ belongs to $H$.

A transition $T$ from $L$ to $\Gamma^{*}$ will be called finitely supported if there is a fixed finite subset $F$ of $D$ such that for each $\mu \in L$ the image $T \mu$ has its support in $F$. (An element $v$ of $\Gamma^{*}$ has its support in the finite set $F$ if there are numbers $\alpha_{s}$ such that

$$
\langle\gamma, v\rangle=\sum_{s \in \mathscr{F}} \alpha_{s} \gamma(s)
$$

for all $\gamma \in \Gamma$.)
With spaces $L$ and $\Gamma$ as just described, let $\mathscr{K}$ be the family of subsets of $\Gamma \times L$ which are finite unions of rectangles $A \times B, A \subset \Gamma, B \subset L$, where $A$ is $\sigma\left(\Gamma, \Gamma^{*}\right)$ compact and $B$ is $\sigma(L, M)$ compact.

Theorem 1. Let $H$ be a linear sublattice of $M$ such that $I \in H$. Assume that the unit ball $\{h ; h \in H,|h| \leq I\}$ of $H$ is $\sigma(M, L)$ dense in the unit ball $\{u ; u \in M,|u| \leq I\}$ of $M$.

Let $\mathscr{B}_{0}$ be the set of all transitions from $L$ to $\Gamma^{*}$ which are finitely supported and $(\Gamma, H)$ continuous.

Then $\mathscr{B}_{0}$ is dense in the set $\mathscr{B}(\Gamma, L)$ of all transitions from $L$ to $\Gamma^{*}$ for the topology of uniform convergence on the elements of $\mathscr{K}$.

An example may help to understand the intent of the theorem. (The proof follows the statement of Theorem 2.)

Suppose that $\mathscr{E}$ is an experiment given in the traditional fashion by probability measures $P_{\theta}$ on a $\sigma$-field $\mathscr{A}$ carried by $\mathscr{X}$. Take for $\mathscr{X}$ the interval $[0,1]$ and for $\mathscr{A}$ the Borel sets. Take for $D$ a compact space and for $\Gamma$ the space $C(D)$ of continuous functions on $D$.

For the lattice $H$ take the linear functionals which correspond to the continuous functions on $[0,1]$ so that $h P_{\theta}$ is identifiable to the integral $\int h(x) P_{\theta}(d x)$ of the continuous function $h$. The set $\mathscr{B}_{0}$ is then the set of procedures obtained as follows. Take a finite set $\left\{s_{1}, s_{2}, \ldots, s_{h}\right\}$ in $D$. To each $s_{j}$ assign a probability $\phi_{j}(x)$ depending on $x \in \mathscr{X}$ in such a way that $0 \leq \phi_{j}(x) \leq 1, \sum_{j} \phi_{j}(x)=1$, and in such a way that $x \mapsto \phi_{j}(x)$ is continuous in $x$.

Suppose now that $\Theta$ is also a compact space. Take a loss function $W$ on $\Theta \times D$. For each $\varepsilon>0$ and each $\rho \in \mathscr{B}(\Gamma, L)$ there will be a $\sigma \in \mathscr{B}_{0}$ such that $\sup _{\theta}\left|W_{\theta} \rho P_{\theta}-W_{\theta} \sigma P_{\theta}\right|<\varepsilon$ if the function $W$ is separately continuous on
$\Theta \times D$ and if all the $P_{\theta}$ admit with respect to a fixed finite measure $\mu$ a bounded density. This example uses a space of continuous functions as an instance of the sublattice $H$.

More generally, it will happen quite often that a lattice such as $H$ is provided by the description of the statistical problem itself. Returning to the intuitive description given at the beginning of this Chapter, suppose that the vector lattice $V$ of the "truly observable" bounded random variables is given. Suppose that one takes in it a unit $I$ which is the random variable always equal to unity. Define a norm on $V$ by the relation $\|v\|=\inf \{\alpha ;|v| \leq \alpha I\}$. For this norm $V$ would be an $M$-space, except that $V$ might not be complete.

Take a band $L$ in the dual of $V$. For simplicity assume that $L$ separates the elements of $V$. (Otherwise one would replace $V$ by the appropriate quotient.)

Then we have a triplet ( $V, L, M$ ) where $M$ is the dual of $L$ and where $V$ may be identified to a sublattice $H$ of $M$. The system ( $H, L, M$ ) always satisfies the requirements of Theorem 1 since in fact the unit ball of $H$ is order dense in the unit ball of $M$. (See Appendix, Nakano's theorem.)

When $H$ is represented as a uniform lattice of functions on a certain set $\mathscr{X}$, the transitions which belong to the set $\mathscr{B}_{0}$ admit representations by Markov kernels of a very simple nature. In fact, suppose that $T \in \mathscr{B}_{0}$ is supported by the finite set $F \subset D$. Then there are positive elements $u_{s} \in H$ such that $T$ may be written in the form

$$
T=\sum_{s \in F} \delta_{s} u_{s}
$$

where $\delta_{s}$ is the measure of unit mass sitting at the point $s$ and $u_{s}(x)$ is the probability assigned to $s$ if $x$ is observed. Another simple but important theorem is the following.

Theorem 2. For any space $L$ and any uniform lattice $\Gamma$, the set $\mathscr{B}(\Gamma, L)$ of transitions from $L$ to $\Gamma^{*}$ is a compact Hausdorff space for the topology of pointwise convergence on $\Gamma \times L$.

This topology of pointwise convergence coincides also with the topology of uniform convergence on rectangles $A \times B$ with $A \subset \Gamma, B \subset L$ both strongly compact. To prove Theorem 2, consider an ultrafilter $\mathscr{U}$ on the space $\mathscr{B}(\Gamma, L)$. For a fixed pair $(\gamma, \lambda) \in \Gamma \times L$, the numbers $\gamma \rho \lambda$ remain bounded. Thus they have along $\mathscr{U}$ a certain limit, say $\alpha(\gamma, \lambda)$. Now note that algebraic and order properties are preserved by passages to the limit, so that $\alpha$ is a positive bilinear form defined on $\Gamma \times L$. In addition, if $\lambda \geq 0$ one has $1 \rho \lambda=\|\lambda\|$ for all $\rho \in \mathscr{B}$. Thus at the limit, $\alpha(1, \lambda)=\|\lambda\|$. Since $\alpha$ is a positive normalized bilinear form on $\Gamma \times L$, it is given by some transition; hence the compactness statement. The Hausdorff property is quite obvious; hence the theorem.

The proof of Theorem 1 is somewhat more complex. The argument given below can be separated into two parts. In the first one obtains the approximation result for set $A \times B$, where $A$ is a finite subset of $\Gamma$ and $B$ is an interval of $L$. This is not difficult. The second part uses a Hahn Banach argument to pass to the general sets $A \times B$. The reader may wish to skip this second part since
arbitrary weakly compact pairs ( $A, B$ ) will not be used much in the remainder of this volume.

Proof of Theorem 1. Consider first one element $\gamma \in \Gamma$ such that $0 \leq \gamma \leq 1$. We claim that given $\varepsilon>0$ there is a partition of unity $\left\{u_{j} ; j=0,1, \ldots, n\right\}, u_{j} \in \Gamma, u_{j} \geq 0, \sum u_{j}=1$ such that on the support of each $u_{j}$ the function $\gamma$ oscillates by no more than $\varepsilon$. This is a well known fact which may be proved as follows.

Take an integer $n$ such that $n \varepsilon \geq 2$. Divide the interval $[0,1]$ by the values $(j / n), j=0, \ldots, n$. Let $f_{1}=\gamma \wedge(1 / n)$, and, recursively let $f_{j}=\gamma \wedge(j / n)-\gamma \wedge ((j-1) / n)$. Define $u_{0}=1-n f_{1}$ and for $j=1, \ldots, n-1$ let $u_{j}=n\left(f_{j}-f_{j+1}\right)$ These $u_{j}$ have the desired properties.

Now consider a finite subset $A=\left\{\gamma_{i} ; i=1, \ldots, m\right\} \subset \Gamma$. Multiplying partitions obtainable for each $\gamma_{i}$ one sees that there is some partition of unity $\left\{u_{j} ; j=0, \ldots, n\right\}$ such that on the support of each $u_{j}$ every $\gamma_{i}$ oscillates by no more than $\varepsilon$. Some of the $u_{j}$ may be identically zero. If so, ignore them and for each one of the rest select a point $t_{j} \in D$ such that $u_{j}\left(t_{j}\right)>0$. To each $\gamma \in \Gamma$ one can associate an image $\gamma S \in \Gamma$ whose value at $t$ is simply $\sum_{j} \gamma\left(t_{j}\right) u_{j}(t)$. The map $S$ so obtained is positive, linear, and such that if $\gamma$ oscillates by no more than $\varepsilon$ on the support of the $u_{j}$, then $\|\gamma S-\gamma\| \leq \varepsilon$.

This being acquired, take a transition $T$ and a particular element $\mu \geq 0$ of $L$. By assumption, the unit ball $B_{1}$ of $H$ is $\sigma(M, L)$ dense in the unit ball $\bar{B}_{1}$ of $M$. Thus $B_{1}$ is also dense in $\bar{B}_{1}$ for the topology of uniform convergence on the intervals of $L$, which are $\sigma(L, M)$ compact.

Therefore, given $\varepsilon^{\prime}>0$, there are elements $v_{j} \in H, 0 \leq v_{j} \leq 1$ such that

$$
\sum_{j}\langle | u_{j} T-v_{j}|, \mu\rangle<\varepsilon^{\prime} /(n+2) .
$$

We can now convert these $v_{j}$ into a partition of unity $\left\{w_{j} ; j=0, \ldots, n+1\right\}$ in $H$ as follows.

Take $w_{0}=v_{0}, w_{1}=\left(v_{1}+w_{0}\right) \wedge 1-w_{0}$, and proceeding recursively, let $s_{k}=\sum_{i \leq k} w_{i}$ and for $k<n$

$$
w_{k}=\left[\left(v_{k}+s_{k-1}\right) \wedge 1\right]-s_{k-1}
$$

For $k=n$, let $w_{n}=1-s_{n-1}$.
Since $v_{k}+s_{k-1} \leq \sum_{j \leq k} v_{j}$ for all $k<n$, one can write

$$
0 \leq v_{k}-w_{k} \leq\left(\sum_{j \leq k} v_{j}\right)-\left(\sum_{j \leq k} v_{j}\right) \wedge 1
$$

It follows that

$$
\sum_{k<n}\left\langle v_{k}-w_{k}, \mu\right\rangle \leq \varepsilon^{\prime}
$$

Similarly, $\langle | v_{n}-w_{n}|, \mu\rangle \leq \varepsilon^{\prime}$. This gives $\sum_{j \leq n}\langle | u_{j} T-w_{j}|, \mu\rangle<3 \varepsilon^{\prime}$.
With this partition $\left\{w_{j}\right\}$ and the previously selected points $t_{j}$, one can construct a transition $T_{0}$ by the prescription

$$
\gamma T_{0} \lambda=\sum_{j} \gamma\left(t_{j}\right)\left\langle w_{j}, \lambda\right\rangle .
$$

For the elements $\gamma$ of the finite set $A$ used to construct the partition $u_{j}$, this gives

$$
\gamma T_{0} \mu=\langle\gamma S T, \mu\rangle+\sum \gamma\left(t_{j}\right)\left\langle\left(w_{j}-u_{j} T\right), \mu\right\rangle .
$$

Hence

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left|\gamma T_{0} \mu-\gamma T \mu\right| & \leq\|\gamma-\gamma S\|\|\mu\|+3 \varepsilon^{\prime}\|\gamma\| \\
& \leq \varepsilon\|\mu\|+3 \varepsilon^{\prime}\|\gamma\|
\end{aligned}
$$

Thus the statement of the theorem holds for pairs ( $A, B$ ) where $A$ is finite and $B$ is an interval $[0, \mu]$ of $L$. To complete the proof, we must now pass from the special pairs ( $A, B$ ) used above to general pairs ( $A, B$ ) of weakly compact sets.

For this purpose note first the following important fact. Take $A$ to be $\sigma\left(\Gamma, \Gamma^{\prime}\right)$ compact and $B$ to be $\sigma(L, M)$ compact. Give the product $A \times B$ the corresponding product of the weak topologies.

If $T$ is a transition from $L$ to $\Gamma^{\prime}$ then the function $(\gamma, \lambda) \mapsto \gamma T \lambda$ is continuous on $A \times B$.

Indeed, consider $B^{\prime}=T B$. This is a $\sigma\left(\Gamma^{\prime}, \Gamma^{\prime \prime}\right)$ compact subset of $\Gamma^{\prime}$. Hence (see Appendix), $B^{\prime}$ is also compact for the Mackey topology $\tau\left(\Gamma^{\prime}, \Gamma\right)$. Therefore if $m_{v} \in B^{\prime}$ converges to $m_{0}$ for $\sigma\left(\Gamma^{\prime}, \Gamma\right)$ it converges for $\tau\left(\Gamma^{\prime}, \Gamma\right)$, that is uniformly on the $\sigma\left(\Gamma, \Gamma^{\prime}\right)$ compacts of $\Gamma$. The desired assertion follows.

To go further, let $C$ be the space of continuous functions on $A \times B$ weak. We already know that the set $K$ of restrictions to $A \times B$ of the elements of $\mathscr{B}$ is a compact set for pointwise convergence on $A \times B$ (see Theorem 2 above). It follows that $K$ is a compact subset of $C$ for the topology $\sigma\left(C, C^{\prime}\right)$ (see Appendix).

The set $K_{0}$ of restrictions of the elements of $\mathscr{B}_{0}$ is a convex set which is dense in $K$ for pointwise convergence on $A \times B$, hence also for $\sigma\left(C, C^{\prime}\right)$. Thus $K_{0}$ is also dense in $K$ for the uniform convergence on $A \times B$ since in the Banach space $C$ weak and strong closures of convex sets are the same. This concludes the proof of Theorem 1.

## 5. Building Experiments from Other Ones

Before entering into the problem of representations let us mention a few constructions often used to build experiments.
(i) Let $\mathscr{E}: \theta \mapsto P_{\theta}$ and $\mathscr{F}: \theta \mapsto Q_{\theta}$ be two experiments indexed by the same set $\Theta$. The experiment which corresponds to taking independent observations from $P_{\theta}$ and $Q_{\theta}$ is the direct product experiment $\mathscr{E} \otimes \mathscr{F}$ defined by the map $\theta \mapsto P_{\theta} \otimes Q_{\theta}$ to the tensor product $L(\mathscr{E}) \otimes L(\mathscr{F})$.
(ii) Let $T$ be a set carrying a $\sigma$-field $\mathscr{B}$ and a probability measure $\pi$. Let $L$ be
a given $L$-space. For each $t \in T$ let $\mathscr{E}_{t}: \theta \mapsto P_{\theta, t} \in L$ be an experiment indexed by $\Theta$. Assume that the maps $t \mapsto P_{\theta, t}$ are scalarly measurable for each fixed $\theta$. For each $u$ in the dual $M$ of $L$ and each $B \in \mathscr{B}$ define $\int_{B}\left\langle u, P_{\theta, t}\right\rangle \pi(d t)$. This extends to the vector lattice generated by the pairs ( $u, I_{B}$ ) giving a map $\theta \mapsto Q_{\theta}$ to a certain space $L^{\prime}$. It is called the mixture of the $\mathscr{E}_{t}$ for the measure $\pi$.
(iii) Let $\mathscr{E}: \theta \mapsto P_{\theta}$ be an experiment with $L$-space $L(\mathscr{E})=L_{1}$. Let $T$ be a transition to some other $L$-space $L_{2}$. One can inflate $\mathscr{E}$ by $T$ in the following way. Represent the $P_{\theta}$ by probability measures on a set $\mathscr{X}$ and the elements of $L_{2}$ by measures on a set $\mathscr{Y}$. Represent $T$ by a Markov kernel $x \mapsto K_{x}$ mapping $\mathscr{X}$ into the measures on $\mathscr{Y}$. The inflated experi-
ment $\theta \mapsto T \otimes P_{\theta}$ consists of selecting a pair ( $x, y$ ) by first choosing $x$ according to $P_{\theta}$ and then $y$ according to $K_{x}$.

A representation-free definition can be given as follows. Let $M_{i}$ be the dual of $L_{i}$. The transpose of $T$ is a map from $M_{2}$ to $M_{1}$. For a pair ( $\left.u, v\right)$, $u \in M_{1}, v \in M_{2}$ one defines a value $\left\langle(v T) u, P_{\theta}\right\rangle$ and then extends by linearity and completion.
(iv) Let $\mathscr{E}: \theta \mapsto P_{\theta}$ be an experiment with spaces $L$ and $M$. Let $H$ be a sublattice of $M$ which is $\sigma(M, L)$ closed and contains the identity $I$. Identify two elements of $L$ if they agree on $H$. This gives a map $\mu \mapsto \dot{\mu}$ to another $L$ space $\dot{L}$, quotient of $L$ by the identification described. In particular, $P_{\theta}$ gives $\dot{P}_{\theta}$ and this gives a quotient experiment $\dot{\mathscr{E}}: \theta \mapsto \dot{P}_{\theta}$. (In the traditional notation this operation is obtained by restricting the measures $P_{\theta}$ to a sub- $\sigma$-field of the original one. However, the condition that $H$ is $\sigma(M, L)$ closed is somewhat stronger than the " $\sigma$ " part of the $\sigma$-field property.)

## 6. Representations-Markov Kernels

To end this chapter, we shall describe briefly the relations between the above framework and the more usual one in which the experiments are families of measures and the role of our transitions is played by Markov kernels.

One simple, but essential, piece of information needed to pass to representations is the following result of Kakutani and Krein.

Consider a vector lattice $V$ and a particular positive element $I$ of $V$. Define the function $\|v\|=\inf _{\alpha}\{\alpha:|v| \leq \alpha I\}$. Assume that this is a norm on $V$. Then $V$ with this norm behaves like an $M$-space, except that perhaps it is not complete. For simplicity, let us assume that $V$ is indeed complete for the norm defined by the unit $I$.
