The positive part of the unit ball of the dual $V^{\prime}$ of $V$ has a set $Z$ of nonzero extreme points which can be characterized in many equivalent ways. They are lattice homomorphisms of $V$ into $R$. Alternatively, $\phi$ is extreme if and only if $0 \leq \psi \leq \phi, \psi \in V^{\prime}$ implies that $\psi=a \phi$ for some number $a$.

One can also note that there exists on $V \times V$ one and only one bilinear map $\left(v_{1}, v_{2}\right) \mapsto v_{1} v_{2}$ of $V \times V$ into $V$ such that $v_{1} v_{2} \geq 0$ if $v_{i} \geq 0, i=1,2$ and such that $I v=v I=v$. The multiplication so defined is commutative. For this multiplication the elements of $Z$ are those functionals which are multiplicative.

That is, $\phi \in Z$ if and only if $\phi\left(v_{1} v_{2}\right)=\phi\left(v_{1}\right) \phi\left(v_{2}\right)$. In other words, $Z$ is the space of characters of the algebra $V$. For all these assertions see Schaefer [1974] or the Appendix to this volume.

The set $Z$ can be given the topology induced by $\sigma\left(V^{\prime}, V\right)$. For this topology $Z$ is a compact Hausdorff space. Each element $v \in V$ gives an element $\hat{v} \in C(Z)$ of the space of continuous functions on $Z$ by the natural evaluation map. A famous theorem of Kakutani asserts that the map $v \mapsto \hat{v}$ is an isomorphism of $V$ onto $C(Z)$ for the Banach space structure, the vector lattice structure, and the algebraic structure.

In other words, an $M$-space (with unit) is always representable as the space $C(Z)$ of continuous functions on a suitable compact $Z$.

In this section we have several such $M$-spaces. One of them is the uniform lattice $\Gamma$ on the space $D$ of possible decisions. In this case the compact $\hat{D}$ which gives the representation $\Gamma \sim C(\hat{D})$ is simply the completion of $D$ for the smallest uniform structure which makes the elements of $\Gamma$ uniformly continuous.

Similarly, if $M$ is the $M$-space of some experiment $\mathscr{E}$, then $M$ can be represented as the space $C(Z)$ of a suitable compact $Z$. The fact that this particular compact may be extremely large need not concern us at this point.

Now consider $\mathscr{E}=\left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ with its $L$-space $L$ and its $M$-space $M$ represented as $C(Z)$. Then the elements of $L$ are bounded linear functionals on $C(Z)$. Thus they are representable as Radon measures on $Z$. Similarly, the elements of the dual $\Gamma^{*}$ of $\Gamma$ are represented by Radon measures on the compact $\hat{D}$.

Finally, let $T$ be a transition from $L$ to $\Gamma^{*}$. Its adjoint is a map $u \mapsto u T$ from $\Gamma$ to $M$ or equivalently from $C(\widehat{D})$ to $C(Z)$. Thus we can "evaluate" $u T$ at each $z \in Z$. Call the value so obtained $u T_{z}$. The map $u \mapsto u T_{z}$ from $C(\hat{D})$ to the line is clearly a positive linear map such that $I T_{z}=1$. It can also be represented as an integral $u T_{z}=\int_{\hat{D}} u(t) K(d t, z)$, which represents $T$ by the Markov kernel $K$.

Thus, as asserted previously, we have not introduced in the abstract framework any objects which could not be introduced as well in the traditional structure. This assertion is also valid for the definition of the risks $W_{\theta} \rho P_{\theta}$ at least whenever the lower semicontinuity assumption

$$
W_{\theta}=\sup \left\{\gamma ; \gamma \in \Gamma, \gamma \leq W_{\theta}\right\}
$$

is satisfied.
Indeed, in this case, the function $W_{\theta}$ admits a largest lower semicontinuous extension $\hat{W}_{\theta}$ to the whole of the compact $\hat{D}$. This is defined as before by $\hat{W}_{\theta}=\sup \left\{\gamma \in C(\hat{D}), \gamma \leq W_{\theta}\right.$ on $\left.D\right\}$. If $\rho$ is represented by the Markov kernel $K(d t, z)$ as above, then, since we are dealing here only with Radon measures, we can write

$$
W_{\theta} \rho P_{\theta}=\iint \hat{W}_{\theta}(t) K(d t, z) P_{\theta}(d z)
$$

and the double integral can be taken as an iterated integral if one so wishes. Thus one could ask "why then use the abstract framework?" The point is that



the above representation is very special. It is only one of a multitude of possibilities, and the usual setup where $\mathscr{E}=\left\{P_{\theta}, \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ is given by probability measures $P_{\theta}$ on a $\sigma$-field $\mathscr{A}$ carried by a set $\mathscr{X}$ does not insure that the $\sigma$-field $\mathscr{A}$ or the set $\mathscr{X}$ are selected well enough to be able to proceed without trouble. The abstract framework avoids the troubles caused by $\mathscr{X}$ or similar sets by ignoring them. However, since many readers may be much more familiar with the traditional setup, we shall now indicate that it may be used provided suitable assumptions are duly satisfied.

Let us start with $\mathscr{E}=\left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ with its $L$-space $L$ and its $M$-space $M$. If the $P_{\theta}$ are measures on $\{\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{A}\}$, then there is a certain space $\mathscr{M}$ of bounded $\mathscr{A}$-measurable numerical functions defined on $\mathscr{X}$. Clearly each $f \in \mathscr{M}$ defines a linear functional $\dot{f}$ on $L$. In other words, the space $\dot{\mathscr{M}}$ of these equivalence classes is a subspace of $M$. However, unless the system $\left\{\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{A}, P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ has special properties, it does not follow that $\dot{\mathscr{M}}$ is the entire space $M$.

There are a variety of conditions which are equivalent to the property $\dot{\mathscr{M}}= M$ (see Appendix). Perhaps the simplest is that the unit ball of $\dot{\mathscr{M}}$ is complete for the structure of convergence in measure for all $P_{\theta}$. (This is defined by $u_{v} \rightarrow 0$ if $\int\left|u_{v}\right| d P_{\theta} \rightarrow 0$ for all $\theta$.)

When the unit ball of $\dot{\mathscr{M}}$ is not so complete, one could find nets of tests $\phi_{\nu} \in \mathscr{M}$ which would like to converge in probability, but their limit is outside $\mathscr{M}$. However, even this completeness condition is not sufficient to proceed happily. It would be most convenient if not only tests, but also decision procedures in general could be obtained from $\mathscr{M}$.

Now consider the following requirement. Suppose that for every compact $Z$ all transitions from $L$ to the dual $C^{\prime}(Z)$ of $C(Z)$ are representable by Markov kernels $K(d t, x)$ with $\int \gamma(t) K(d t, x)$ an $\mathscr{A}$-measurable function of $x$ for $\gamma \in C(Z)$. Then one must at least have $\dot{\mathscr{M}}=M$. However, much more is true. In fact there must be a lifting $l$ which is a linear multiplicative operation defined on $\dot{\mathscr{M}}$ and assigning to each $\dot{f} \in \mathscr{M}$ an element $l(\dot{f})$ of $\mathscr{M}$ whose class is that same $\dot{f}$.

This can be seen as follows. Take $L$ and $M$ as above and let $Z$ be the compact space of characters of $M$. Then the identity map $\gamma \mapsto \hat{\gamma}$, which to each $\gamma \in C(Z)$ assigns the corresponding element of $\mathscr{M}$, is indeed the adjoint of a transition $T$ from $L$ to $C^{\prime}(Z)$. Suppose that there is a Markov kernel $K(d z, x)$ such that $\hat{\gamma}$ is equivalent to $\int \gamma(z) K(d z, x)$. Then, the kernel $K$ is obviously a linear lifting of $\mathscr{M}$ into $\mathscr{M}$. By the argument of A . and C . Ionescu Tulcea, it follows that there is a lifting of $\mathscr{M}$ into $\mathscr{M}$. (In fact, $K$ "almost" provides it.)

Now it is easy to prove that the existence of such a lifting is equivalent to a property, called $\sum$-finiteness which may be described thus. The system $\left\{\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{A}, P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ is $\sum$-finite if there is a partition $\left\{A_{j} ; j \in J\right\}$ where $A_{j} \in \mathscr{A}$ and where $J$ may have arbitrary cardinality such that:
(i) If $f$ is a bounded function defined on $\mathscr{X}$ such that each $f I_{A_{j}}$ is $\mathscr{A}$ measurable, then $f$ is already $\mathscr{A}$-measurable.
(ii) For each $\lambda \in L$ one has $\int f d \lambda=\sum \int_{A_{j}} f d \lambda$.
(iii) On each set $A_{j}$ the family $\left\{P_{\theta}\right\}$ is dominated by a finite measure
(It is asserted in Theorem 5 of Le Cam [1964] that this is a consequence of the equality $\dot{\mathscr{M}}=M$. However, the "proof" contains an idiotic mistake. The assertion is incorrect as shown by examples of D . Fremlin. It was shown by J.M.G. Fell [1956] that the equality $\dot{\mathscr{M}}=M$ implies $\sum$-finiteness when $\left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ has a cardinality which does not exceed the continuum.)

In other words, to proceed happily in the traditional structure one should assume that $\left\{\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{A}, P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ is $\sum$-finite. However, it is possible to carry out an argument showing that if one passes to the space of likelihood ratios one can doctor up the $\sigma$-field on this space to insure $\sum$-finiteness. This is, of course, also automatically so on the Kakutani space $Z$ of $M$. Thus, proceeding "happily" in the traditional framework would have us introduce assumptions which are unnecessary if one looks at the "right" space $\{\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{A}\}$.

Now let us pass to the decision space $D$ with the loss function $W$ defined on $\Theta \times D$. As already mentioned, as far as computation of risks are concerned, the only role of the space $D$ is that each element $d \in D$ specifies a certain loss function $\theta \mapsto W_{\theta}(d)$ which belongs to the space $\mathscr{F}\{\Theta, \bar{R}\}$ of all extended numerical functions defined on $\Theta$. One of the most commonly used assumptions concerning these elements of $\mathscr{F}\{\Theta, \bar{R}\}$ is the following.

Assumption (The Subcompactness Assumption). Let $f$ be an element of $\mathscr{F}\{\Theta, \bar{R}\}$. If for each finite set $F \subset \Theta$ there is a $d \in D$ such that $W_{\theta}(d) \leq f(\theta)$ for $\theta \in F$, then there is a $d_{0} \in D$ such that $W_{\theta}\left(d_{0}\right) \leq f(\theta)$ for all $\theta$. Furthermore $\inf _{d} W_{\theta}(d)>-\infty$ for each $\theta$.

A number of variants of this assumption are in use. Often it is stated by saying that $D$ is a topological space and that for each $m \in R$ and each $\theta$ the sets $\left\{d: W_{\theta}(d) \leq m\right\}$ are compact. Another variant has it that the above sets form a compact class of subsets of $D$. That is, if the intersection of a family of them is empty, there is already a finite subfamily with empty intersection.

Suppose then that the above subcompactness assumption is satisfied. Consider the set $\bar{D}$ of all functions $f$ in $\mathscr{F}\{\Theta,(-\infty,+\infty]\}$ such that $W_{\theta}(d) \leq f(\theta)$, all $\theta$, for some $d \in D$. Give $\mathscr{F}\{\Theta, \bar{R}\} \supset \mathscr{F}\{\Theta,(-\infty,+\infty]\}$ the topology of pointwise convergence. The subcompactness assumption is equivalent to the statement that $\bar{D}$ is a compact subset of $\mathscr{F}\{\Theta,(-\infty,+\infty]\}$.

Since, under that assumption, replacing $D$ by $\bar{D}$ just means introducing extra possible decisions, which are not any better than the elements of $D$, we could replace $D$ by $\bar{D}$. Then, on the compact $\bar{D}$ one can take the uniform lattice $\Gamma$ of continuous numerical functions. It is clear that the evaluation functions, at each $\theta$, are continuous functions from $\bar{D}$ to $\bar{R}$, hence at least lower semicontinuous on $\bar{D}$. In particular, in this setting, each $W_{\theta}$ is the pointwise supremum of a countable set of those elements of $\Gamma$ which are below it.

Finally, assuming that one has a representation $\left\{\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{A}, P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ which is $\sum$-finite, a transition $\rho$ from $L$ to $\Gamma^{*}=C^{\prime}(\bar{D})$ will be representable by a Markov kernel, $K(d t, x)$, which is a Radon measure for each $x$. One can then choose such a representation so that the risk $W_{\theta} \rho P_{\theta}$ is again the integral

$$
\iint W_{\theta}(t) K(d t, x) P_{\theta}(d x)
$$

Returning to the why and how of the "abstract" setting given previously, the reader will easily convince himself that the abstract setting is just meant to ignore the representation problems described here. The main "statistical" statements elaborated in this book do not depend on such representations. To close, let us say a few words on the system used here as far as category theory is concerned.

In the present setup, for a given set $\Theta$, one may consider that the class of experiments indexed by $\Theta$ is a category whose objects are the experiments themselves and whose morphisms are the transitions.

Several authors have used categories where the objects are experiments $\left\{\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{A}, P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ in the traditional sense, and whose morphisms are Markov kernels (see Čenšov [1972]; Morse and Sacksfeder [1966]). This is, of course, a possibility. However, suppose that $\left\{\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{A}, P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ is given and that $\mathscr{A}_{0} \subset \mathscr{A}$ is a sufficient sub- $\sigma$-field of $\mathscr{A}$. Then, the present system allows passages from the $\mathscr{A}_{0}$ experiment to the $\mathscr{A}$ experiment by a transition. This, however, need not be a Markov kernel, unless ( $\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{A}$ ) is specially chosen.

Therefore, for reasons of simplicity, we shall proceed under the general "abstract" definitions given at the beginning of this chapter.

## CHAPTER 2

## Some Results from Decision Theory: Deficiencies

## 1. Introduction

This chapter contains a very brief summary of some essential results from Wald's theory of statistical decision functions. This is followed by a basic theorem which allows us to define "deficiencies" of distances between experiments. We also give a representation of Bayesian problems, optimal gains in terms of conical measures.

## 2. Characterization of the Spaces of Risk Functions: Minimax Theorem

Let $\mathscr{E}: \theta \mapsto P_{\theta}$ be an experiment with $L$-space $L=L(\mathscr{E})$. Let $(D, \Gamma, W)$ be a regular decision space with loss function $W$. (See Definitions in Chapter 1.) Let us recall that we allow as space $\mathscr{B}(\Gamma, L)$ of decision functions all the transitions $\rho$ from $L$ to $\Gamma^{*}$ and that the risk $W_{\theta} \rho P_{\theta}$ is defined as $\sup _{\gamma}\left\{\gamma \rho P_{\theta} ; \gamma \in \Gamma, \gamma \leq W_{\theta}\right\}$.

Let $\mathscr{R}$ be the space of all functions from $\Theta$ to $(-\infty,+\infty]$ which are such that $f \geq g$ for some $g$ of the form $\theta \mapsto W_{\theta} \rho P_{\theta}$. Let $\widetilde{\Theta}$ be the space of probability measures which have finite support on $\Theta$ and let $K(\Theta)$ be the cone of all positive measures which have finite support on $\Theta$. For each $\mu \in K(\Theta)$ let $\chi(\mu)= \inf \left\{\int f d \mu ; f \in \mathscr{R}\right\}$. The relevance of the characteristic envelope $\chi$ is as follows.

Theorem 1. Let ( $\mathscr{E}, D, W, \Gamma$ ) be a regular decision problem. Then for each $\theta \in \Theta$ the map $\rho \mapsto W_{\theta} \rho P_{\theta}$ is linear and lower semicontinuous on the compact $\mathscr{B}(\Gamma, L)$. A function $f$ belongs to the set $\mathscr{R}$ if and only if $\chi(\mu) \leq \int f d \mu$ for all $\mu \in K(\Theta)$.
(In particular,

$$
\left.\sup _{\mu \in \tilde{\Theta}} \chi(\mu)=\inf _{f \in \mathscr{R}} \sup _{\mu} \int f d \mu\right)
$$

In the set $\mathscr{R}$, certain elements have names as follows.
(i) An $f \in \mathscr{R}$ is admissible if $g \leq f, g \in \mathscr{R}$ implies $g=f$.
(ii) An $f \in \mathscr{R}$ is called a Bayes point for $\mu \in \widetilde{\Theta}$ if $\int f d \mu=\chi(\mu)$.
(iii) Let $a$ be a positive function which is not identically zero on $\Theta$. An $f \in \mathscr{R}$ is minimal in the direction $a$ if there is no $\varepsilon>0$ and $g \in \mathscr{R}$ such that $g+\varepsilon a \leq f$ with $(f-g) I(a>0)$ not identically zero.
(iv) Let $K_{a}$ be the set of all elements of $K(\Theta)$ such that $\int a d \mu=1$. An element $f \in \mathscr{R}$ is called a Wald point in the direction $a$ if

$$
\inf \int f d \mu-\chi(\mu)=0
$$

for an infimum taken over all $\mu \in K_{a}$ such that $\chi(\mu)<\infty$.
Finally a subset $\mathscr{R}_{1}$ of $\mathscr{R}$ is called complete if for each $f \in \mathscr{R}$ there is a $g \in \mathscr{R}_{1}$ such that $g \leq f$.

Let $(\mathscr{E}, D, \Gamma, W)$ be a regular decision problem such that $\chi(\mu)$ is not identically infinity. Then
(a) the admissible risk functions form a complete class.
(b) A point $f$ such that $f(\theta)<\infty$ for some $\theta$ with $a(\theta) \neq 0$ is minimal in the direction $a$ if and only if it is a Wald point in that direction. The admissible points are those which are minimal in all directions.
(c) In the space of functions from $\Theta$ to $[-\infty,+\infty]$ with pointwise convergence, each admissible point is a limit of Bayes points.
(d) Suppose that $f \in \mathscr{R}$ is admissible. Let $\theta_{0}$ be a particular element of $\Theta$ and let $b$ denote a number $b<f\left(\theta_{0}\right)$. There is a finite set $S \subset \Theta$ and an $\varepsilon>0$ depending only on $\theta_{0}, f$, and $b$ such that $g \in \mathscr{R}, g\left(\theta_{0}\right) \leq b$ implies $g(\theta)>\varepsilon+f(\theta)$ for some $\theta \in S$.

In certain situations it is more pleasant to use measures which do not have finite support. For instance, suppose that $\Theta$ is a compact Hausdorff space and that all the maps $\theta \mapsto \gamma \rho P_{\theta}$ for $\gamma \in \Gamma$ and $\rho \in \mathscr{B}(\Gamma, L)$ are continuous. Then one can use instead of $\widetilde{\Theta}$ the space $\mathscr{M}^{+}(\Theta)$ of non-null positive Radon measures on $\Theta$, provided that $\mathscr{R}$ be replaced by the space of lower semicontinuous functions larger than some $\theta \mapsto W_{\theta} \rho P_{\theta}$.

Let us also mention a result which is valid in this case, with the further assumption that the maps $\theta \mapsto W_{\theta} \rho P_{\theta}$ themselves be continuous. Then, take for $\mathscr{R}$ the set of continuous functions larger than some $\theta \mapsto W_{\theta} \rho P_{\theta}$. The set $\mathscr{R}$ is closed in $C(\Theta)$. If $f \in \mathscr{R}$ is not a Bayes point for some $\mu \in \mathscr{M}^{+}(\Theta)$, there
is an $\varepsilon>0$ and a $g \in \mathscr{R}$ and such that $g \leq f-\varepsilon$. These statements are well known and easily proven as follows.

The lower semicontinuity assertion of Theorem 1 is here a consequence of the fact that the risks $W_{\theta} \rho P_{\theta}$ have been defined as suprema of expressions $\gamma \rho P_{\theta}$ which are continuous. Consider the space $\mathscr{F}$ of all functions from $\Theta$ to $[-\infty,+\infty]$ and topologize it by the topology of pointwise convergence. We claim that the set $\mathscr{R}$ is a subset of $\mathscr{F}$ having the following properties:
(1) If $f_{i} \in \mathscr{R}, i=1,2$ and $\alpha \in[0,1]$, there is an $f_{3} \in \mathscr{R}$ such that $f_{3} \leq \alpha f_{1}+(1-\alpha) f_{2}$.
(2) $\mathscr{R}$ is a compact subset of $\mathscr{F}$ contained in the space of functions from $\Theta$ to $(-\infty,+\infty]$.

The convexity assertion is obvious. To prove the compactness, let $\left\{f_{v}\right\}$ be an ultrafilter on $\mathscr{R}$ and let $f_{0}$ be its limit in $\mathscr{F}$. For each $\nu$ there is a $\rho_{\nu} \in \mathscr{B}(\Gamma, L)$ such that $W_{\theta} \rho_{\nu} P_{\theta} \leq f_{\nu}(\theta)$ for all $\theta$. In the compact $\mathscr{B}(\Gamma, L)$ the ultrafilter $\left\{\rho_{\nu}\right\}$ converges to some limit $\rho_{0}$. By semicontinuity, $W_{\theta} \rho_{0} P_{\theta} \leq f_{0}(\theta)$. Statements (a) and (d) are immediate consequences of the compactness of $\mathscr{R}$.

To prove the second assertion of Theorem 1 and statement (c) we may proceed as follows. Take an $f \in \mathscr{F}$ such that $\chi(\mu) \leq \int f d \mu$ for all $\mu$. To show that $f \in \mathscr{R}$ it is enough to consider what happens on the set $S=\{\theta ; f(\theta)<\infty\}$. Let $A$ be a finite subset of $S$ and let $\varepsilon$ be a number $\varepsilon \in(0,1)$. Let $A_{\varepsilon}$ be the set of probability measures $\mu$ defined on $A$ and such that $\mu[\{\theta\}] \geq \varepsilon$ for all $\theta \in A$. This is nonempty as soon as $\varepsilon \operatorname{card} A<1$.

For each $g \in \mathscr{R}$ let $g^{\prime}$ be the function defined on $A_{\varepsilon}$ by $g^{\prime}(\mu)=\int g d \mu$. Note that $g^{\prime}$ is either everywhere finite on $A_{\varepsilon}$ or everywhere equal to infinity. The finite functions $g^{\prime}$ obtainable in this manner form a certain convex set $C(A, \varepsilon)$. It is a closed set. The inequality $\chi(\mu) \leq \int f d \mu$ says that $f^{\prime}$ is not separated from $C(A, \varepsilon)$ and therefore that $f^{\prime} \in C(A, \varepsilon)$.

The compactness of $\mathscr{R}$ implies also that there is some $g_{A} \in \mathscr{R}$ such that $g_{A}^{\prime} \leq f^{\prime}$ but such that $g_{A}^{\prime}-\alpha \notin C(A, \varepsilon)$ for all $\alpha>0$. This implies that

$$
\inf _{\mu}\left\{g_{A}^{\prime}(\mu)-\chi(\mu) ; \mu \in A_{\varepsilon}\right\}=0
$$

by the same separation argument. However, $A_{\varepsilon}$ is compact and the lower semicontinuous function $g_{A}^{\prime}-\chi$ must reach its minimum on that compact. Thus $g_{A}$ is a Bayes point such that $g_{A}^{\prime} \leq f^{\prime}$ on $A_{\varepsilon}$.

Now take limits as $\varepsilon \rightarrow 0$ and then as $A$ increases through the direction of all finite subsets of $\Theta$. Any cluster point $g$ of the $g_{A}$ in $\mathscr{R}$ will be such that $g \leq f$, concluding the proof of the theorem as well as of statement (c). To proceed further we shall introduce additional definitions as follows.

## 3. Deficiencies; Distances

Let $\Theta$ be a given set. Let $\mathscr{E}: \theta \mapsto P_{\theta}$ and $\mathscr{F}: \theta \mapsto Q_{\theta}$ be two experiments indexed by $\Theta$.

Definition 1. The deficiency $\delta(\mathscr{E}, \mathscr{F})$ of $\mathscr{E}$ with respect to $\mathscr{F}$ is the number

$$
\delta(\mathscr{E}, \mathscr{F})=\inf _{T} \sup _{\theta} \frac{1}{2}\left\|Q_{\theta}-T P_{\theta}\right\|
$$

for an infimum taken over all transitions $T$ from the $L$-space $L(\mathscr{E})$ to the $L$-space $L(\mathscr{F})$.

If $\mathscr{F}$ was originally defined as a map from $\Theta$ to some arbitrary $L$-space $L$ in which $L(\mathscr{F})$ is a band, one does not change the above number by allowing transitions to $L$ instead of $L(\mathscr{F})$.

We shall call "distance" of $\mathscr{E}$ to $\mathscr{F}$ the number

$$
\Delta(\mathscr{E}, \mathscr{F})=\max \{\delta(\mathscr{E}, \mathscr{F}), \delta(\mathscr{F}, \mathscr{E})\} .
$$

This is a pseudo metric. If $\Delta(\mathscr{E}, \mathscr{F})=0$, one says that the two experiments are equivalent. The equivalence class of an experiment $\mathscr{E}$ will also be called the type of $\mathscr{E}$ and denoted by $\dot{\mathscr{E}}$. For a given set $\Theta$ the experiment types indexed by $\Theta$ form a set $E(\Theta)$ which shall be shown below to be complete for the metric $\Delta$.

The relation $\delta(\mathscr{E}, \mathscr{F})=0$ is an order relation on $E(\Theta)$. It is read either " $\mathscr{E}$ is better than $\mathscr{F}$ " or " $\mathscr{F}$ is weaker than $\mathscr{E}$ " or " $\mathscr{E}$ is stronger than $\mathscr{F}$."

The set $E(\Theta)$ always has a weakest element. This is the trivial experiment where all the $P_{\theta}$ are the same. The set $E(\Theta)$ also has a strongest element called the perfect experiment. It can be obtained by assigning to $\theta$ the Dirac measure $\delta_{\theta}$ entirely concentrated at $\theta$.

It is often convenient to use subsets of $\Theta$ instead of $\Theta$ itself. If $S \subset \Theta$ and $\mathscr{E}=\theta \mapsto P_{\theta}$ the map $\theta \mapsto P_{\theta}$ restricted to $\theta \in S$ defines an experiment indexed by $S$. It will be denoted $\mathscr{E}_{S}$. For two such experiments $\mathscr{E}$ and $\mathscr{F}$ the deficiency $\delta\left(\mathscr{E}_{S}, \mathscr{F}_{S}\right)$ will also be called the deficiency of $\mathscr{E}$ with respect to $\mathscr{F}$ on $S$.

There is a very important relation between these concepts and the risk performance to be expected in decision problems. This relation is the subject of Theorem 2 below. To state it we shall assume given a particular $\Theta$ and two experiments, $\mathscr{E}=\left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ and $\mathscr{F}=\left\{Q_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$. Furthermore, it is convenient to introduce a particular class $\mathscr{C}$ of decision spaces as follows. The class $\mathscr{C}$ consists of those decision spaces $(D, \Gamma, W)$ where
(i) $D$ is a finite set;
(ii) $\Gamma$ is $C(D)$;
(iii) the loss function $W$ is such that $0 \leq W_{\theta}(s) \leq 1$ for all $\theta \in \Theta$ and $s \in D$.

Noting as in Chapter 1, that computation of risks does not actually involve the set $D$ but only the elements $\theta \mapsto W_{\theta}(s)$ of the space $\mathscr{F}(\Theta, \bar{R})$, one could just say that the class $\mathscr{C}$ consists of problems where the decision space is a finite subset of the space $\mathscr{S}=\mathscr{F}\{\Theta,[0,1]\}$ of functions from $\Theta$ to the interval $[0,1]$. The value of the loss at a point $\theta$ is the evaluation at $\theta$ of the function $s \in \mathscr{S}$ chosen.

Theorem 2. Let $\mathscr{E}: \theta \mapsto P_{\theta}$ and $\mathscr{F}: \theta \mapsto Q_{\theta}$ be two experiments indexed by the set $\Theta$. Let $\alpha \in[0,1]$ be a fixed number. Then the following statements are all equivalent.
(i) There is a transition $T$ from $L(\mathscr{E})$ to $L(\mathscr{F})$ such that

$$
\sup _{\theta} \frac{1}{2}\left\|Q_{\theta}-T P_{\theta}\right\| \leq \alpha
$$

(ii) $\delta(\mathscr{E}, \mathscr{F}) \leq \alpha$.
(iii) For every finite set $S \subset \Theta$ one has $\delta\left(\mathscr{E}_{S}, \mathscr{F}_{S}\right) \leq \alpha$.
(iv) Let $\chi(\mu, \mathscr{E}, W)$ and $\chi(\mu, \mathscr{F}, W)$ be the characteristic envelope defined at the beginning of Section 2. Then for each probability measure $\mu$ which has finite support on $\Theta$ and each decision space ( $D, \Gamma, W$ ) of the class $\mathscr{C}$, one has

$$
\chi(\mu, \mathscr{E}, W) \leq \chi(\mu, \mathscr{F}, W)+\alpha
$$

(v) Let $H$ be a sublattice of the dual $M(\mathscr{F})$ of $L(\mathscr{F})$. Assume that $H$ satisfies the conditions of Theorem 1, Chapter 1. For each probability measure $\mu$ with finite support on $\Theta$, each space $(D, \Gamma, W) \in \mathscr{C}$, each $(\Gamma, H)$ continuous transition $\sigma$ from $L(\mathscr{F})$ to $\Gamma^{\prime}$, each $\alpha^{\prime}>\alpha$, there is a transition $\rho$ from $L(\mathscr{E})$ to $\Gamma^{\prime}$ such that

$$
\int\left(W_{\theta} \rho P_{\theta}\right) \mu(d \theta) \leq \int\left(W_{\theta} \sigma Q_{\theta}\right) \mu(d \theta)+\alpha^{\prime}
$$

Remark. The above statements are given in decreasing order of strength, each implying the next. To prove the theorem we shall proceed stepwise using several lemmas. The first is as follows.

Lemma 1. If condition (v) holds for a lattice $H \subset M(\mathscr{F})$, it holds for $H=M(\mathscr{F})$ itself.

Proof. According to Theorem 1, Chapter 1, for each $\varepsilon>0$ and each transition $\sigma_{0}$ from $L(\mathscr{F})$ to $\Gamma^{\prime}$ there is a $(\Gamma, H)$ continuous transition $\sigma$ such that

$$
W_{\theta} \sigma Q_{\theta}<W_{\theta} \sigma_{0} Q_{\theta}+\varepsilon
$$

for all the $\theta$ belonging to the finite support of $\mu$.
Thus we can proceed as if $H=M(\mathscr{F})$ and shall do so below. Our next lemma is easier to understand and prove if one assumes that $H=M(\mathscr{F})$ has been represented by the set of continuous functions $F=C(\mathscr{Y})$ on the compact space $\mathscr{Y}$ of the Kakutani representation. Thus we shall use this representation here.

Consider a decision space ( $K, C(K), W$ ) where $K$ is a compact convex subset of the space $\mathscr{S}=\mathscr{F}\{\Theta,[0,1]\}$ and $W_{\theta}(s)$ is the evaluation at $\theta$ of the element $s$ of $\mathscr{S}$. Retain only those $K$ whose linear span is finite dimensional, so that they are in fact compact convex subsets of some Euclidean space. Let $\sigma$ be a transition from $L(\mathscr{F})$ to the dual $C^{\prime}(K)$ of $C(K)$. This can be represented by a Markov kernel $y \mapsto \sigma_{y}$ which to each $y$ assigns a probability measure $\sigma_{y}$ on $K$. Let $\bar{\sigma}(y)$ be the center of gravity $\int s \sigma_{y}(d s)=\bar{\sigma}(y)$.

Lemma 2. The map $y \mapsto \bar{\sigma}_{y}$ is a nonrandomized procedure such that

$$
W_{\theta} \sigma \equiv W_{\theta} \bar{\sigma}
$$

This is obvious.
We are now ready for the next lemma, for which we assume given a $\mu$ with finite
support on $\Theta$, a particular $\left(D_{0}, \Gamma_{0}, W_{0}\right) \in \mathscr{C}$ and a particular $\sigma_{0}$ available on $\mathscr{F}$ for this problem.

Lemma 3. Let condition (v) of Theorem 2 be satisfied and let $\beta$ be a number $\beta>\alpha^{\prime}>\alpha$. Then there is a finitely supported transition $T$ from $L(\mathscr{E})$ to the dual $H^{\prime}$ of $H=C(\mathscr{Y})=M(\mathscr{F})$ such that

$$
\int\left(W_{\theta} \sigma_{0}\right) T P_{\theta} \mu(d \theta)<\int W_{\theta} \sigma_{0} Q_{\theta} \mu(d \theta)+\beta
$$

Proof. Let $\bar{\sigma}$ be the nonrandomized procedure associated with $\sigma_{0}$ by taking centers of gravity. Let $K_{0}$ be the convex hull of $D_{0}$ in $\mathscr{S}$. Then $\bar{\sigma}$ is a continuous map from $\mathscr{Y}$ to $K_{0}$. It has a strict range $K \subset K_{0}$.

Since $\mu$ has finite support, given an $\varepsilon, 0<\varepsilon<\beta-\alpha^{\prime}$, Theorem 1, Chapter 1 insures the existence of a finitely supported transition $\sigma_{1}$ from $L(\mathscr{F})$ to $C^{\prime}(K)$ such that $W_{\theta} \sigma_{1} Q_{\theta} \leq W_{\theta} \bar{\sigma} Q_{\theta}+\varepsilon$ for all $\theta$ in the finite support of $\mu$. Let $D_{1}= \left\{s_{1}, s_{2}, \ldots, s_{k}\right\}$ be the finite set $D_{1} \subset K$ which supports $\sigma_{1}$. Since condition (v) holds for all $D$ and all $\sigma$, it holds in particular for $D_{1}$ and $\sigma_{1}$. Thus there is a transition $\rho$ from $L(\mathscr{E})$ to the dual of $C\left(D_{1}\right)$ such that

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int W_{\theta} \rho P_{\theta} \mu(d \theta) & <\int W_{\theta} \sigma_{1} Q_{\theta} \mu(d \theta)+\alpha^{\prime} \\
& <\int W_{\theta} \sigma_{0} Q_{\theta} \mu(d \theta)+\beta
\end{aligned}
$$

The transition $\rho$ may be written in the form $\sum_{j} \delta\left(s_{j}\right) u_{j}$, where $\delta\left(s_{j}\right)$ is the mass unity at $s_{j}$ and $u_{j} \in M(\mathscr{E}), u_{j} \geq 0, \sum u_{j}=1$.

Since each $s_{j}$ belongs to the range $K$ of $\bar{\sigma}$, one can write $s_{j}=\bar{\sigma}\left(y_{i}\right)$ for some $y_{i} \in \mathscr{Y}$ and let $T$ be the transition from $L(\mathscr{E})$ to $H^{\prime}$ described by $\sum \delta\left(y_{j}\right) u_{j}$. Clearly $\rho$ is obtainable by the composition $\rho=\bar{\sigma} T$. But $\bar{\sigma} T$ and $\sigma_{0} T$ have exactly the same risk functions, hence the result.

Lemma 4. Let condition (v) of Theorem 2 hold. Then there is a transition $T$ from $L(\mathscr{E})$ to the dual $H^{\prime}$ of $M(\mathscr{F})$ such that

$$
\frac{1}{2}\left\|Q_{\theta}-T P_{\theta}\right\| \leq \alpha
$$

for all $\theta \in \Theta$.
Proof. Let $\mathscr{B}$ denote the space of transitions from $L=L(\mathscr{E})$ to $H^{\prime}$ with the weak topology of pointwise convergence on $H \times L$. (The reader is reminded that we assume $H=M(\mathscr{F})$.) Let $\Sigma$ be the space of finitely supported transitions from $L(\mathscr{F})$ to the dual of the space $C(\mathscr{S})$ of continuous functions on the compact space $\mathscr{S}=\mathscr{F}\{\Theta,[0,1]\}$. Finally, let $\tilde{\Theta}$ be the space of probability measures with finite support on $\Theta$.

Let $f$ be the function defined on $\mathscr{B} \times \Sigma \times \widetilde{\Theta}$ by

$$
f(T, \sigma, \mu)=\int W_{\theta} \sigma T P_{\theta} \mu(d \theta)-\int W_{\theta} \sigma Q_{\theta} \mu(d \theta)
$$

Let

$$
\begin{aligned}
\beta(\sigma, \mu) & =\inf _{T}\{f(T, \sigma, \mu) ; T \in \beta\} \\
\beta(\mu) & =\sup _{\sigma} \beta(\sigma, \mu)
\end{aligned}
$$

According to Lemma 3, if $\beta>\alpha^{\prime}>\alpha$ one has $\beta(\sigma, \mu)<\beta$.
Fix an element $\mu$ of $\widetilde{\Theta}$. Then, on $\mathscr{B} \times \Sigma$ the function $f$ is bilinear. Furthermore, for each $\sigma \in \Sigma$, this function is continuous on the compact $\mathscr{B}$. Thus, according to the minimax theorem (Theorem 1) if $\beta(\mu)=\sup _{\sigma} \beta(\sigma, \mu)$ there is some $T \in \beta$ such that

$$
f(T, \sigma, \mu) \leq \beta(\mu)
$$

for all $\sigma \in \Sigma$ simultaneously.
This $T$ may depend on $\mu$ here. Keeping $\mu$ fixed, again take the corresponding $T$ and a number $\delta \in(0,1)$. For each $\theta \in \Theta$ there is some element $\phi_{\theta}, 0 \leq \phi_{\theta} \leq 1$, of $H$ such that

$$
\phi_{\theta}\left[T P_{\theta}-Q_{\theta}\right] \geq \frac{1}{2}(1-\delta)\left\|T P_{\theta}-Q_{\theta}\right\|
$$

One may suppose, in addition, that each $\phi_{\theta}$ is a finite linear combination $\phi_{\theta}=\sum c_{\theta, j} v_{\theta, j}$ where the $v_{\theta, j}$ are idempotents $(=$ indicators $)$ in $H$.

Now $\mu$ has a finite support $\Theta_{0} \subset \Theta$. Consider the map $y \mapsto \sigma_{y}$ which to each $y \in \mathscr{Y}$ assigns the element of $\mathscr{S}$ whose value is zero if $\theta \notin \Theta_{0}$ and whose value at $\theta \in \Theta_{0}$ is precisely $\phi_{\theta}(y)$. This is a transition of the class $\Sigma$ whose risk is given by $W_{\theta} \sigma_{y} \equiv \phi_{\theta}(y)$. Therefore

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{2}(1-\delta) \int\left\|Q_{\theta}-T P_{\theta}\right\| \mu(d \theta) & \leq \int \phi_{\theta}\left[T P_{\theta}-Q_{\theta}\right] \mu(d \theta) \\
& =f(T, \sigma, \mu) \leq \beta(\mu)
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $\delta$ is arbitrary, one concludes that $\frac{1}{2} \int\left\|Q_{\theta}-T P_{\theta}\right\| \mu(d \theta) \leq \beta(\mu)$.
To go further, for each $T \in \mathscr{B}$ let $r_{T}$ be the function defined on $\Theta$ by $r_{T}(\theta)= \left\|T P_{\theta}-Q_{\theta}\right\|$ and let $\mathscr{R}$ be the set $\mathscr{R}=\left\{r_{T} ; T \in \mathscr{B}\right\}$ obtainable in this fashion. By convexity of the norm, the class $\mathscr{R}$ is subconvex. In addition, for each $\theta \in \Theta$ the function

$$
T \mapsto\left\|T P_{\theta}-Q_{\theta}\right\|=\sup \left\{\left|\phi\left(T P_{\theta}-Q_{\theta}\right)\right| ; \phi \in H,|\phi| \leq 1\right\}
$$

is lower semicontinuous on the compact $\mathscr{B}$. Therefore, according to the minimax theorem again

$$
\inf _{T} \sup _{\mu} \int r_{T}(\theta) \mu(d \theta)=\sup _{\mu} \inf _{T} \int r_{T}(\theta) \mu(d \theta)
$$

and the infimum on $T$ is attained.
Thus there is a $T$ such that

$$
\frac{1}{2}\left\|Q_{\theta}-T P_{\theta}\right\| \leq \sup _{\mu} \beta(\mu)
$$

However $\beta(\mu) \leq \beta$ for every $\beta>\alpha^{\prime}>\alpha$. Hence, $\sup _{\mu} \beta(\mu) \leq \alpha$. This concludes the proof of the Lemma.

We can now complete the proof of Theorem 2 very rapidly.
End of Proof of Theorem 2. Taking $H=M(\mathscr{F})$ and taking for $H^{\prime}$ the dual of $H$, we have established the existence of a transition $T$ from $L(\mathscr{E})$ such that

$$
\frac{1}{2}\left\|Q_{\theta}-T P_{\theta}\right\| \leq \alpha
$$

for all $\theta \in \Theta$. The images $T P_{\theta}$ are in $H^{\prime}$, dual of $M(\mathscr{F})$. However, $L(\mathscr{F})$ itself is a band in $H^{\prime}$ (see Appendix). Thus there is a positive linear projection $\Pi$ of $H^{\prime}$ such that $H^{\prime}$ is the direct sum of $\Pi H^{\prime}=L(\mathscr{F})$ and $(I-\Pi) H^{\prime}$.

Let $m$ be an arbitrary positive element of $L(\mathscr{F})$ such that $\|m\|=1$. For every $\lambda \geq 0, \lambda \in L(\mathscr{E})$ let

$$
T_{1} \lambda=\Pi T \lambda+[\|\lambda\|-\|\Pi T \lambda\|] m
$$

The map so obtained extends linearly to the space $L(\mathscr{E})$. It is a transition from $L(\mathscr{E})$ to $L(\mathscr{F})$. Finally

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left\|T_{1} P_{\theta}-Q_{\theta}\right\| & \leq\left\|\Pi T P_{\theta}-Q_{\theta}\right\|+\left[\left\|P_{\theta}\right\|-\left\|\Pi T P_{\theta}\right\|\right] \\
& =\left\|T P_{\theta}-Q_{\theta}\right\|
\end{aligned}
$$

This concludes the proof of the Theorem.

## 4. The Form of Bayes Risks-Choquet Lattices

A particular corollary of Theorem 2 is that two experiments $\mathscr{E}$ and $\mathscr{F}$ are equivalent if and only if for all decision spaces of the class $\mathscr{C}$ the envelopes $\chi(\mu, \mathscr{E}, W)$ and $\chi(\mu, \mathscr{F}, W)$ are the same.

Since these are the Bayes risks for particular simple decision problems, it is convenient to have some description of the values of these risks. However, it turns out to be easier to work with gains instead of risks as follows.

Given a decision space ( $D, \Gamma, W$ ) of the class $\mathscr{C}$, instead of minimizing the average risks $\int\left(W_{\theta} \rho P_{\theta}\right) \mu(d \theta)$ one may equivalently maximize the average gain $\int\left(V_{\theta} \rho P_{\theta}\right) \mu(d \theta)$ for the gain function $V_{\theta}=1-W_{\theta}$. Since $\mu$ has finite support, we can introduce numbers $\pi_{\theta}=\mu(\{\theta\})$ and rewrite the integrals as finite sums of the type $\int\left(V_{\theta} \rho P_{\theta}\right) \mu(d \theta)=\sum_{\theta} \pi_{\theta} V_{\theta} \rho P_{\theta}$.

Finally, for each $s \in D$ let $a_{s, \theta}=\pi_{\theta} V_{\theta}(s)$ so that $0 \leq a_{s, \theta} \leq \pi_{\theta}$. We can then consider the element $m_{s}$ of $L(\mathscr{E})$ given by the sums

$$
m_{s}=\sum_{\theta} a_{s, \theta} P_{\theta}
$$



This leads to the following result.
Proposition 1. For the maximizing problem just described, the maximum average gain

$$
\sup _{\rho} \int\left(V_{\theta} \rho P_{\theta}\right) \mu(d \theta)=1-\chi(\mu, \mathscr{E}, W)
$$

is given by the norm

$$
\left\|\sup _{s} \sum_{\theta} a_{s, \theta} P_{\theta}\right\|
$$

of the maximum of the elements $m_{s}=\sum a_{s, \theta} P_{\theta}$ in $L(\mathscr{E})$.
Proof. With $m_{s}=\sum a_{s, \theta} P_{\theta}$ the norm of the supremum $\sup _{s} m_{s}$ can be written in the form

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left\|\sup _{s} m_{s}\right\| & =\left\langle 1, \sup _{s} m_{s}\right\rangle \\
& =\sup _{u} \sum u_{s} m_{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $u=\left\{u_{s}, s \in D\right\}$ is a partition of unity, $u_{s} \in M(\mathscr{E}), u_{s} \geq 0, \sum_{s} u_{s}=1$. Now $\sum u_{s} m_{s}=\sum_{\theta} \sum_{s} a_{s, \theta}\left\langle u_{s}, P_{\theta}\right\rangle$ is exactly the gain attached to the procedure $\rho$ which assigns to the point $s \in D$ the probability defined by $u_{s}$. Since all the procedures $\rho$ available here are representable in this form, the result follows.

In the above Proposition we have encountered finite sets of finite linear combinations of the type $\sum a_{s, \theta} P_{\theta}$. These sets will play an important role in the future. For this reason it is convenient to have a more tractable notation. An improvement can be obtained by introducing a certain space $\mathscr{H}$ which will be used again in Chapter 3. It is defined as follows.

Let $R^{\Theta}$ be the vector space of all real valued functions defined on $\Theta$. Each $f \in R^{\Theta}$ may be evaluated at $\theta$. This evaluation is a positive linear functional $l_{\theta}$ defined on $R^{\oplus}$. In other words, $l_{\theta}(f)=f(\theta)$.

Now consider not the whole of $R^{\Theta}$ but the cone $R_{+}^{\Theta}$ of functions from $\Theta$ to $[0, \infty)$. Let $K(\Theta)$ be the cone of positive finite measures carried by finite subsets of $\Theta$. An element $a \in K(\Theta)$ can be specified by a function $a=\left\{a_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ with $a_{\theta} \geq 0$ and $a_{\theta}=0$ except on a finite subset of $\Theta$.

To each $a \in K(\Theta)$ associate the function $\phi_{a}$ defined on $R_{+}^{\Theta}$ by $\phi_{a}=\sum a_{\theta} l_{\theta}$. If $S$ is a finite family of elements of $K(\Theta)$, let

$$
\phi=\sup _{a \in S} \sum a_{\theta} l_{\theta}
$$

be the function assigned to that finite set. The functions so obtainable form a certain set $\mathscr{H}_{c}$ of functions defined on $R_{+}^{\Theta}$. It is easily checked that $\mathscr{H}_{c}$ is a convex cone. Also, by construction, if $\phi_{1}$ and $\phi_{2}$ are two elements of $\mathscr{H}_{c}$ then $\phi_{1} \vee \phi_{2} \in \mathscr{H}_{c}$.

We shall denote $\mathscr{H}$ the space of differences of the type $\phi_{1}-\phi_{2}, \phi_{i} \in \mathscr{H}_{c}$, and call that $\mathscr{H}$ the Choquet lattice of $R_{+}^{\Theta}$. It is easily checked that $\mathscr{H}$ is indeed a vector lattice, by using the relation

$$
\left(\phi_{1}-\phi_{2}\right)^{+}=\left(\phi_{1} \vee \phi_{2}\right)-\phi_{2}
$$

Now, each $\phi \in \mathscr{H}_{c}$ can be obtained as a finite supremum of the type

$$
\phi=\sup _{s \in S} \sum a_{s, \theta} l_{\theta}, \quad a_{s, \theta} \geq 0
$$

The numbers $a_{s, \theta}$ involved there can be used to introduce a decision problem with a gain function $V_{\theta}(s)$ and a prior probability $\pi_{\theta}$. It is enough for the purpose to take

$$
\pi_{\theta}=\frac{\sup _{s} a_{s, \theta}}{\sum_{\eta} \sup a_{s \eta}}
$$

and write $\pi_{\theta} V_{\theta}(s)=a_{s, \theta}$.
In particular, the problems of the class $\mathscr{C}$ described previously, in which $0 \leq V \leq 1$ would arise from elements $\phi$ of the subset $\mathscr{H}_{1}$ of $\mathscr{H}_{c}$ formed by those combinations $\left\{a_{s, \theta}\right\}, \theta \in \Theta, s \in S$ where

$$
\sum_{\theta} \sup _{s} a_{s, \theta} \leq 1
$$

We shall say that these sets are bounded by a finitely supported probability. It is clear that the same $\phi \in \mathscr{H}_{c}$ can be obtained in many different ways. That is, there may be different sets, say $\left\{a_{s, \theta}\right\}$ and $\left\{b_{t, \theta}\right\}, t \in T$ such that

$$
\phi=\sup _{s} \sum a_{s, \theta} l_{\theta}=\sup _{t} \sum_{\theta} b_{t, \theta} l_{\theta}
$$

However, this turns out to be immaterial. To state the appropriate result in a simple effective manner, we shall introduce two new spaces $\operatorname{Lin}(\mathscr{E})$ and Lat ( $\mathscr{E}$ ) as follows.

Definition. If $\mathscr{E}=\left\{P_{\theta}, \theta \in \Theta\right\}$, the space $\operatorname{Lin}(\mathscr{E})$ is the smallest LINEAR subspace of $L(\mathscr{E})$ which contains all the $P_{\theta}, \theta \in \Theta$.

The space $\operatorname{Lat}(\mathscr{E})$ is the smallest vector SUBLATTICE of $L(\mathscr{E})$ which contains all the $P_{\theta}, \theta \in \Theta$.

This allows us to define a correspondence $\left\{\left(\phi, M_{\phi}\right), \phi \in \mathscr{H}\right\}$ as follows.
If $\phi=\sum_{\theta} a_{s, \theta} l_{\theta}$ then $M_{\phi}=\sum_{\theta} a_{s, \theta} P_{\theta}$ is associated with $\phi$.
If $\phi=\sup _{s} \sum_{\theta} a_{s, \theta} l_{\theta}, a_{s, \theta} \geq 0$ for a finite supremum of finite linear combinations, then

$$
M_{\phi}=\sup _{s} \sum_{\theta} a_{s, \theta} P_{\theta}
$$

is associated with $\phi$.
Finally, if $\phi=\phi_{1}-\phi_{2}, \phi_{i} \in \mathscr{H}_{c}$, then $M_{\phi}=M_{\phi_{1}}-M_{\phi_{2}}$ is associated with $\phi$.

With these definitions we can state the following simple result.
Proposition 2. The correspondence ( $\phi, M_{\phi}$ ) defined above is a mapping from $\mathscr{H}$ onto the minimal lattice $\operatorname{Lat}(\mathscr{E})$. Furthermore, it is an homomorphism of the vector lattice structures and maps the positive cone of $\mathscr{H}$ onto the positive cone of $\operatorname{Lat}(\mathscr{E})$.

Proof. Take a finite system $\left\{a_{s, \theta}\right\}, s \in S$, as described. Let $\phi_{s}=\sum_{\theta} a_{s, \theta} l_{\theta}$ and $\phi=\sup _{s} \phi_{s}$. For each $s$, the coefficients $a_{s, \theta}$ are well determined by $\phi_{s}$, since the linear functionals $\left\{l_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ are linearly independent. Thus if $\phi_{s}= \sum a_{s, \theta} l_{\theta}, M_{\phi_{s}}$ is uniquely determined.

To go further, take a particular element $j \in S$ and let $\psi_{j}$ be the supremum

$$
\psi_{j}=\sup _{s}\left\{\phi_{s} ; s \in S_{j}\right\}
$$

where $S_{j}=\{s \in S, s \neq j\}$. If $\psi_{j}(f) \geq \phi(f)$ for all $f \in R_{+}^{\Theta}$, or equivalently, if $\psi_{j} \geq \phi_{j}$, there is some convex combination $\left\{\pi_{s} ; s \in S_{j}\right\}$ such that $\pi_{s} \geq 0$, $\sum \pi_{s}=1$, and $\sum_{s} \pi_{s} \phi_{s} \geq \phi_{j}$.

Take a $v \geq 0, v \in M(\mathscr{E})$ and let $v=\sum v_{s} ; s \in S_{j}, 0 \leq v_{s} \leq v$. The value $\left\langle v, \sup \left\{M_{\phi_{s}}, s \in S_{j}\right\}\right\rangle$ is the supremum of the sums $\sum_{s} v_{s} M_{\phi_{s}}$ over all such partitions of $v$. However, the set $\left\{v_{s} ; s \in S_{j}\right\}$, with $v_{s}=\pi_{s} v$ is such a partition. It follows readily that

$$
\sup _{s}\left\{M_{\phi_{s}} ; s \in S_{j}\right\} \geq M_{\phi_{j}}
$$

and that, consequently $\phi_{j}$ could be eliminated from the computation.
Eliminating all such redundant elements, one may assume that for each $j \in S$ there is at least one $f$ such that $\phi(f)>\psi_{j}(f)$. In this case the inequality holds on an open subset of $R_{+}^{\Theta}$ and the corresponding coefficients $a_{j, \theta}$ are thereby uniquely determined. This argument carried out shows also that if $\phi^{\prime}=\sum_{\theta} a_{t, \theta}^{\prime} l_{\theta}, t \in S^{\prime}$ is such that $\phi^{\prime} \leq \phi$ then $\sum a_{t, \theta}^{\prime} P_{\theta} \leq M_{\phi}$.

Thus $M_{\phi}$ is well defined for each $\phi \in \mathscr{H}_{c}$ and the map $\phi \mapsto M_{\phi}$ is an increasing map on $\mathscr{H}_{c}$. It is very easily checked that the map is also additive and positively homogeneous. Thus its extension to the whole of $\mathscr{H}$ is a positive linear map.

Finally, the construction itself shows that if $\phi=\phi_{1} \vee \phi_{2}, \phi_{i} \in \mathscr{H}_{c}$, then $M_{\phi}=M_{\phi_{1}} \vee M_{\phi_{2}}$. Also, if $\phi_{i} \in \mathscr{H}_{c}$, again $\left[\phi_{1}-\phi_{2}\right]^{+}=\phi_{1} \vee \phi_{2}-\phi_{2}$ and $\left[M_{\phi_{1}}-M_{\phi_{2}}\right]^{+}=\left(M_{\phi_{1}} \vee M_{\phi_{2}}\right)-M_{\phi_{2}}$.

This completes the argument, except for the assertion that the positive cone of $\mathscr{H}$ maps onto that of $\operatorname{Lat}(\mathscr{E})$. However, this is an obvious consequence of the relations just written, hence the result.

Remark 1. The space $\mathscr{H}$ will occur again in Chapter 3 for the description of what is called the distribution of likelihood ratios.

Remark 2. Since the map $\phi \mapsto M_{\phi}$ is positive linear, so is the evaluation map $\phi \mapsto I M_{\phi}$ at the unit of $M(\mathscr{E})$. This particular map will often be noted $\phi \mapsto\langle m, \phi\rangle$ and be called the conical measure of the experiment $\mathscr{E}$.

Remark 3. The conical measure just defined characterizes the optimal Bayes gains since, for $\phi \in \mathscr{H}_{c}$, one has $\langle m, \phi\rangle=\left\|M_{\phi}\right\|$ which is the Bayes gain by Proposition 1.

Remark 4. In the proof of Proposition 2 we have shown that $\phi_{i} \in \mathscr{H}_{c}, \phi_{1} \leq \phi_{2}$ implies $M_{\phi_{1}} \leq M_{\phi_{2}}$ and therefore $\left\langle m, \phi_{1}\right\rangle \leq\left\langle m, \phi_{2}\right\rangle$. This is equivalent to the positivity of the map $\phi \mapsto\langle m, \phi\rangle$ and should not be confused with the assertion that $\langle m, \phi\rangle \geq 0$ for $\phi \in \mathscr{H}_{c}$. The latter relation is of a different nature entirely. It will occur later in Theorem 4.

The preceding Proposition 2 has an important consequence. To describe it, note that we now have many spaces, $\operatorname{Lin}(\mathscr{E}), \operatorname{Lat}(\mathscr{E}), L(\mathscr{E})$, and the original $L$-space $L$ used to define the experiment $\mathscr{E}=\left\{P_{\theta}, \theta \in \Theta\right\}$. Obviously

$$
\operatorname{Lin}(\mathscr{E}) \subset \operatorname{Lat}(\mathscr{E}) \subset L(\mathscr{E}) \subset L
$$

It is also convenient to introduce the closure $L_{m}(\mathscr{E})$ of $\operatorname{Lat}(\mathscr{E})$ in $L(\mathscr{E})$. This is an $L$-space in its own right. One could consider the map $\theta \mapsto P_{\theta}$ as a map from $\Theta$ to $L_{m}(\mathscr{E})$. This is an experiment; we shall call it the minimal form of the experiment $\mathscr{E}$.

Now consider two experiments, say $\mathscr{E}=\left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ and $\mathscr{E}^{\prime}=\left\{P_{\theta}^{\prime} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$. One can induce a correspondence between $\operatorname{Lat}(\mathscr{E})$ and $\operatorname{Lat}\left(\mathscr{E}^{\prime}\right)$ using as an auxiliary space the Choquet space $\mathscr{H}$. An element $\phi$ of $\mathscr{H}$ yields the element $M_{\phi}$ of $\operatorname{Lat}(\mathscr{E})$ and the element $M_{\phi}^{\prime}$ of $\operatorname{Lat}\left(\mathscr{E}^{\prime}\right)$. The correspondence so induced leads to the following assertion.

Theorem 3. Let $\mathscr{E}=\left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ be an experiment indexed by $\Theta$. Then $\mathscr{E}$ and its minimal form $\mathscr{E}_{m}$ are equivalent. Furthermore, if $\mathscr{E}^{\prime}=\left\{P_{\theta}^{\prime} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ is any other experiment equivalent to $\mathscr{E}$, every transition $T$ from $L_{m}(\mathscr{E})$ to $L_{m}\left(\mathscr{E}^{\prime}\right)$ such that $T P_{\theta}=P_{\theta}^{\prime}$, is automatically an isomorphism of the Banach structure of $L_{m}(\mathscr{E})$ onto that of $L_{m}\left(\mathscr{E}^{\prime}\right)$.

Proof. According to Propositions 1 and 2, the type of $\mathscr{E}$ is entirely determined by the values $\left\|M_{\phi}\right\|, \phi \in \mathscr{H}_{c}$. In this respect, $\mathscr{E}$ and its minimal form $\mathscr{E}_{m}$ do not differ. For the second assertion, consider the maps $\phi \mapsto M_{\phi}$ to $\operatorname{Lat}(\mathscr{E})$ and the corresponding map $\phi \mapsto M_{\phi}^{\prime}$ to Lat ( $\mathscr{E}^{\prime}$ ).

If $\phi$ has the form $\phi=\sum a_{\theta} l_{\theta}$, then the relation $T P_{\theta}=P_{\theta}^{\prime}$ implies $T M_{\phi}=M_{\phi}^{\prime}$ by linearity of $T$. If $\phi=\sup _{s} \phi_{s}$ with $\phi_{s}=\sum a_{s, \theta} l_{\theta}$, then, since $T$ is positive we have

$$
T\left(\sup _{s} M_{\phi_{s}}\right) \geq \sup _{s} M_{\phi_{s}}^{\prime} .
$$

However, $T$ preserves the norm of positive elements. Thus, $\mathscr{E}$ and $\mathscr{E}^{\prime}$ being equivalent, we have $\left\|M_{\phi}\right\|=\left\|M_{\phi}^{\prime}\right\|$ and therefore $\left\|\mathrm{TM}_{\phi}\right\|=\left\|M_{\phi}^{\prime}\right\|$ for all $\phi \in \mathscr{H}_{c}$. It follows that

$$
T\left(\sup _{s} M_{\phi_{s}}\right)=\sup _{s} M_{\phi_{s}}^{\prime} .
$$

Thus $T M_{\phi}=M_{\phi}^{\prime}$ for $\phi \in \mathscr{H}_{c}$ and therefore also for $\phi \in \mathscr{H}$. Since $T$ is an isometric map of $\operatorname{Lat}(\mathscr{E})$ onto $\operatorname{Lat}\left(\mathscr{E}^{\prime}\right)$, its extension to the whole of $L_{m}(\mathscr{E})$ is an isometry onto $L_{m}\left(\mathscr{E}^{\prime}\right)$. The result follows.

Remark. The above theorem says in particular that $\mathscr{E}$ and $\mathscr{E}^{\prime}$ are equivalent if and only if the correspondence $P_{\theta} \leftrightarrow P_{\theta}^{\prime}$ extends to a positive linear isomorphism of the spaces $L_{m}(\mathscr{E})$ and $L_{m}\left(\mathscr{E}^{\prime}\right)$.

Returning to the statistical situation and the Bayes risks, we have seen that the Bayes risks are obtainable by the positive linear map $\phi \mapsto\langle m, \phi\rangle$, with $\langle m, \phi\rangle=\left\|M_{\phi}\right\|$ for $\phi \in \mathscr{H}_{c}$. Those problems where the gain function $V_{\theta}$ satisfies the inequality $0 \leq V_{\theta} \leq 1$ correspond to elements $\phi \in \mathscr{H}_{c}$ such that

$$
\phi=\sup _{s} \sum a_{s, \theta} l_{\theta}, \quad \sum_{\theta} \sup _{s} a_{s, \theta} \leq 1 .
$$

The set of these elements $\phi$ is denoted $\mathscr{H}_{1}$. This yields the following.
Theorem 4. Let $\mathscr{E}=\left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ and $\mathscr{E}^{\prime}=\left\{P_{\theta}^{\prime} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ be two experiments indexed by $\Theta$. Let $m$ and $m^{\prime}$ be their respective conical measures. Then
(i) $\delta\left(\mathscr{E}, \mathscr{E}^{\prime}\right)=\sup _{\phi \in \mathscr{H}_{1}}\left\{\left\langle m^{\prime}, \phi\right\rangle-\langle m, \phi\rangle\right\}$
(ii) $\Delta\left(\mathscr{E}, \mathscr{E}^{\prime}\right)=\sup _{\phi \in \mathscr{H}_{1}}\left|\left\langle m^{\prime}, \phi\right\rangle-\langle m, \phi\rangle\right|$.

In particular, $\mathscr{E}$ is better than $\mathscr{E}^{\prime}$ if and only if

$$
\langle m, \phi\rangle \geq\left\langle m^{\prime}, \phi\right\rangle
$$

for all $\phi \in \mathscr{H}_{c}$.
The statement follows immediately from the assertions of Propositions 1 and 2 and an application of Theorem 2.

## CHAPTER 3

## Likelihood Ratios and Conical Measures

## 1. Introduction

Many of the usual statistical arguments depend strongly on the introduction of likelihood ratios and on the study of the distributions of those ratios. We have not used these concepts so far, even though they may be considered implicit in some of the derivations of Chapter 2.

In this chapter, we extract some of the essential features of the likelihood ratio distributions and relate them to the objects used in Chapter 2. This approach, as well as the previous one, leads rapidly to the introduction of certain spaces of homogeneous functions. The basic one is a space called $\mathscr{H}$ here. It was used by Choquet who named "conical measures" the positive linear functionals on $\mathscr{H}$. We have shown in Chapter 2 that any experiment $\mathscr{E}$ defines a conical measure and that the type of $\mathscr{E}$ is entirely determined by that conical measure.

When the parameter space $\Theta$ is finite a particular "localization" of the conical measure is what Blackwell called the "canonical measure" of the experiment.

It will be shown here that the conical measure is just exactly the operator which gives the expectation of homogeneous functions of likelihood ratios.

One derives from these considerations an isomorphism between conical measures of resultant unity and experiment types. It is shown that convolution of conical measures corresponds to direct products of experiments. A Laplace transform of this convolution operation is given by the Hellinger transform. Mixture of experiments corresponds to convex combinations of conical measures.

We have also included two interesting results of Torgersen. One of them says that the type of an experiment is already determined by norms of the form $\left\|\sum a_{\theta} P_{\theta}\right\|$ for finite linear combinations. The other says that experiments which are extreme points in the convex set of experiments indexed by $\Theta$ are precisely those which are "boundedly complete" in their minimal form. Finally, a particular expression of the comparison criterion yields what is called the Blackwell-Sherman-Stein theorem.

## 2. Homogeneous Functions of Measures

Let us start with likelihood ratios. Those are traditionally defined in a framework where an experiment $\mathscr{E}=\left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ is given by probability measures $P_{\theta}$ on a $\sigma$-field $\mathscr{A}$ carried by a set $\mathscr{X}$. Thus we shall use such a system.

Assuming this, let $\alpha$ be another positive finite measure on $(\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{A})$. For each $\theta$, the measure $P_{\theta}$ has a part $P_{\theta}^{\prime}$ dominated by $\alpha$. One can take the RadonNikodym density $f_{\theta}=d P_{\theta}^{\prime} / d \alpha$. This will be called the likelihood ratio of $P_{\theta}$ to $\alpha$.

Let $R_{+}=[0, \infty)$ and let $R_{+}^{\Theta}$ be the set of all functions from $\Theta$ to $R_{+}$. Assuming that one has selected specific versions of the densities $f_{\theta}$, one can assign to each $x \in \mathscr{X}$ a particular element $\theta \mapsto f_{\theta}(x)$ of $R_{+}^{\Theta}$. This element will be denoted $f(x)$ so that $f(x)$ is the likelihood vector or function $f(x)= \left\{f_{\theta}(x) ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ relative to $\alpha$.

Take on $R_{+}^{\Theta}$ the usual product $\sigma$-field. Then the map $x \mapsto f(x)$ is measurable. The image $\alpha^{*}$ of the measure $\alpha$ is a certain finite measure on $R_{+}^{\Theta}$. It is called the distribution of likelihood ratios relative to $\alpha$. Note that both the densities $f_{\theta}$ and the image measure $\alpha^{*}$ are based on $\alpha$.

Of course, $\alpha^{*}$ depends on $\alpha$. For instance, if $\Theta$ is a finite set of cardinality, $\operatorname{card} \Theta=n$, one can take for $\alpha$ the average $\alpha=(1 / n) \sum_{\theta} P_{\theta}$ of the measures $P_{\theta}$. The resulting image $\alpha^{*}$ is then a probability measure $\beta$ carried by the simplex of $R_{+}^{\Theta}$ formed by the vectors $\left\{v_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ such that $v_{\theta} \geq 0$ and $\sum_{\theta} v_{\theta}=n$.

The measure $\beta$ induced in this manner is Blackwell's canonical measure of the experiment $\mathscr{E}$. If instead of the ordinary average one would take another average $\sum c_{\theta} P_{\theta}, c_{\theta}>0, \sum c_{\theta}=1$ one would obtain a measure carried by the set of vectors $v$ such that $v_{\theta} \geq 0$ and $\sum c_{\theta} v_{\theta}=1$.

However, there is a large class of functions $\phi$ on $R_{+}^{\theta}$ whose integrals $\int \phi(f) \alpha^{*}(d f)$ are "well determined" in the sense that they are not affected if one replaces $\alpha$ by an equivalent measure. These functions are the "integrable" functions $\phi$ which
are positively homogeneous in the sense that $\phi(t f)=t \phi(f)$ for all $f \in R_{+}^{\Theta}$ and all $t \in[0, \infty)$.

To proceed, consider a positively homogeneous $\phi$ which is measurable on $R_{+}^{\Theta}$. The measurability implies the existence of countable sets $D \subset \Theta$ such that $\phi$ depends only on the coordinates $\theta \in D$ in the sense that if $f^{(1)}$ and $f^{(2)}$ agree on $D$ then $\phi\left(f^{(1)}\right)=\phi\left(f^{(2)}\right)$. Fix such a countable set and consider two finite positive measures, say $\alpha$ and $\beta$ on $(\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{A})$. Denote $f_{\theta}$ the likelihood ratios $d P_{\theta} / d \alpha$ and let $g_{\theta}$ be the ratio $d P_{\theta} / d \beta$.

Lemma 1. Let $\alpha$ and $\beta$ be two measures as described. Assume either that $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are equivalent, or that $\beta$ dominates $\alpha$ and $\alpha$ dominates every $P_{\theta}, \theta \in D$, where $D$ is a countable set determining $\phi$.

Then, for every set $A \in \mathscr{A}$ if the integral $\int_{A} \phi[f(x)] \alpha(d x)$ exists, so does the integral $\int_{A} \phi[g(x)] \beta(d x)$ and the two expressions have the same value.

To prove this, let $\tau$ be the density $\tau=d \alpha / d \beta$. Note that $g_{\theta}(x)=\tau(x) f_{\theta}(x)$ for all $\theta \in D$ and all $x \in \mathscr{X}$ except perhaps on a set which has $\alpha$ measure zero. Also, if $\alpha$ dominates all the $P_{\theta}, \theta \in D$, then the $g_{\theta}$ vanish almost everywhere on the set $S=\{x: \tau(x)=0\}$. Thus the integral $\int_{S} \phi[g(x)] \beta(d x)$ is always zero. The desired result follows by substitution.

Taking this into account let us say that a function $\phi$ belongs to $\mathscr{H}(\mathscr{E})$ if (i) it is defined and measurable on $R_{+}^{\Theta}$;
(ii) it is positively homogeneous;
(iii) if $\phi$ depends only on the coordinates $\theta \in D$, there is some finite positive $\alpha$ dominating all $P_{\theta}, \theta \in D$ such that $\int|\phi(f(x))| \alpha(d x)<\infty$.

According to the above, to each $\phi \in \mathscr{H}(\mathscr{E})$ one can attach a number

$$
\langle m, \phi\rangle=\int \phi[f(x)] \alpha(d x)
$$

One can also assign to $\phi$ a measure on $\mathscr{A}$, say $M_{\phi}$, by the formula

$$
M_{\phi}(A)=\int_{A} \phi[f(x)] \alpha(d x)
$$

The reader will note that we have used here the same notation as in Proposition 2 of Chapter 2. The reason for this is very simple: they are indeed essentially the same objects. This is easily seen if one defines the above integrals by Hellinger's method without any reference to dominating measures, and is a consequence of the following lemma.

We recall that for any $\Theta$, the space $R_{+}^{\Theta}$ is the space of functions from $\Theta$ to $[0, \infty)$. The evaluation at $\theta$ yield a linear functional $l_{\theta}$ on $R_{+}^{\Theta}$. The space $\mathscr{H}_{c}$ is the space of finite suprema of finite sums of the type $\sup _{s} \sum a_{s, \theta} l_{\theta}$ and $\mathscr{H}$ is the space of differences $\phi_{1}-\phi_{2}, \phi_{i} \in \mathscr{H}_{c}$. (The subscript $c$ is used to indicate that the elements of $\mathscr{H}_{c}$ are the positive convex elements of $\mathscr{H}$. )

Consider, then, an experiment $\mathscr{E}=\left\{P_{\theta}, \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ given by measures on $(\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{A})$. Take finite partitions $\pi=\left\{A_{j} ; j=1,2, \ldots, k\right\}$ by elements of $\mathscr{A}$. For each $A \in \mathscr{A}$ and each $\theta \in \Theta$ compute the number $P_{\theta}\left(A \cap A_{j}\right)$. This yields functions $\gamma_{\pi, j}(A)$ which are elements of $R_{+}^{\Theta}$.

Lemma 2. Assume that $\Theta$ is finite. Let $\phi$ be positively homogeneous, convex, continuous, and non-negative on $R_{+}^{\Theta}$. Let $\alpha$ be any finite measure which dominates all the $P_{\theta}, \theta \in \Theta$. Then the integral

$$
M_{\phi}(A)=\int_{A} \phi[f(x)] \alpha(d x)
$$

is precisely equal to the supremum

$$
\sup _{\pi} \sum_{j} \phi\left[\gamma_{\pi, j}(A)\right]
$$

taken over all finite partitions $\pi$ of $\mathscr{X}$.
Proof. Let $J(\pi)=\sum_{j} \phi\left[\gamma_{\pi, j}(A)\right]$. Then, by convexity, if $\pi_{2}$ is finer than $\pi_{1}$ one has $J\left(\pi_{1}\right) \leq J\left(\pi_{2}\right)$. Thus, as one refines the partitions the $J(\pi)$ increase to a limit, say $J$. To relate this to the integral, one may as well assume that $\alpha$ is the probability measure average of the $P_{\theta}$. As usual, let $f=\left\{f_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$, $f_{\theta}=d P_{\theta} / d \alpha$.

For a partition $\pi=\left\{A_{j} ; j=1, \ldots, k\right\}$, let $g_{\pi}$ be the function whose value on $A_{j}$ is an element of $R_{+}^{\Theta}$ such that $\alpha\left(A_{j}\right) g_{\pi}=\gamma_{\pi, j}(A)$. One recognizes that $g_{\pi}$ is the conditional expectation $I_{A} f$ for the measure $\alpha$ and for the $\sigma$-field generated by the partition $\pi$. It follows then from the martingale theorems that, as $\pi$ is refined indefinitely, $\int\left|g_{\pi}-I_{A} f\right| d \alpha$ tends to zero. By continuity and homogeneity,

$$
J(\pi)=\sum_{j} \phi\left[\gamma_{\pi, j}(A)\right]=\int \phi\left(g_{\pi}\right) d \alpha
$$

converges to $J=\int_{A} \phi(f) d \alpha$. Hence the result.
Corollary. For arbitrary $\Theta$, the map $\phi \mapsto M_{\phi}$ defined here coincides on $\mathscr{H}$ with the map defined in Chapter 2, Proposition 2.

The Choquet space $\mathscr{H}$ is a linear sublattice of the bigger space $\mathscr{H}(\mathscr{E})$. Except for this difference in domains of definition the object obtained here is the same as that of Chapter 2. However, even the difference in domains can be remedied, according to the following.

Consider an arbitrary experiment $\mathscr{E}=\left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ where $P_{\theta} \in L(\mathscr{E})$ is not necessarily taken as represented by a measure. Then, we can define the map $\phi \mapsto M_{\phi}$ from $\mathscr{H}$ to the minimal $L$-space of $\mathscr{E}$ denoted $L_{m}(\mathscr{E})$ in Chapter 2. It can be shown that this map is always $\sigma$-smooth, in the sense that if $\phi_{n} \in \mathscr{H}$ is a sequence decreasing pointwise to zero on $R_{+}^{\Theta}$, then $\left\|M_{\phi_{n}}\right\| \rightarrow 0$.

This is rather obvious if one uses representations of the $P_{\theta}$ by measures on $\{\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{A}\}$, and can be shown directly without much difficulty. Such $\sigma$-smooth maps can be extended by the procedure used by Daniell for the definition of integrals: One takes increasing sequences $\left\{\phi_{n}\right\}, \phi_{n} \in \mathscr{H}$ and assigns to $\varphi= \sup _{n} \phi_{n}$ the image map $\sup M_{\phi_{n}}$. Then one squeezes as usual. This leads to the following assertion.

The map $\phi \mapsto M_{\phi}$ defined here for $\phi \in \mathscr{H}(\mathscr{E})$ is the Daniell extension of the same map restricted to the Choquet lattice $\mathscr{H}$.

In Chapter 2 we have defined the conical measure $m$ of the experiment $\mathscr{E}$ as that positive linear functional defined on $\mathscr{H}$ which takes the value $\langle m, \phi\rangle= \left\|M_{\phi}\right\|$ for $\phi \in \mathscr{H}_{c}$.

It follows from the above argument that $\langle m, \phi\rangle$ is also the integral

$$
\langle m, \phi\rangle=\int \phi[f(x)] \alpha(d x)
$$

for any finite positive measure $\alpha$ which dominates all the $P_{\theta}$ involved in the definition of $\phi \in \mathscr{H}$.

In particular, not only are these integrals independent of the choice of the dominating measure, they are also independent of the particular representation by measures used for the $P_{\theta}$ and are in fact totally determined by the type of the experiment $\mathscr{E}$. What is more, the function $\phi \mapsto\langle m, \phi\rangle$ determines the type of $\mathscr{E}$. But one may ask whether they also determine the commonly used "distributions of likelihood ratios."

The answer is a qualified "yes." It is qualified by the necessity of specifying in some way which "localization" of the conical measure is to be considered. When $\Theta$ is finite or countable, a localization of the conical measure $m$ is a finite Radon measure $\mu$ on the product space $R_{+}^{\Theta}$ such that $\langle m, \phi\rangle=\int \phi d \mu$ for all $\varphi \in \mathscr{H}$. If $\Theta$ is uncountable, one may also want to look at localizations which are $\sigma$-smooth measures but not necessarily Radon measures. For simplicity we shall first describe the situation under the assumption that $\Theta$ is finite.

In this case $R_{+}^{\Theta}$ has a unit simplex $U$ formed of all those vectors $u= \left\{u_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ such that $u_{\theta} \geq 0$ and $\sum u_{\theta}=1$. One may metrize $U$ by the maximum coordinate norm

$$
\left|u^{\prime}-u^{\prime \prime}\right|=\sup _{\theta}\left|u_{\theta}^{\prime}-u_{\theta}^{\prime \prime}\right|
$$

The space of Radon measures on the compact $U$ can then be metrized by the corresponding dual Lipschitz norm

$$
\left\|\mu^{\prime}-\mu^{\prime \prime}\right\|_{D}=\sup _{f \in \Lambda}\left|\int f d \mu^{\prime}-\int f d \mu^{\prime \prime}\right|
$$

where $\Lambda$ is the set of functions $f$ defined on $U$ and satisfying the inequalities $|f| \leq 1$ and

$$
\left|f\left(u^{\prime}\right)-f\left(u^{\prime \prime}\right)\right| \leq\left|u^{\prime}-u^{\prime \prime}\right|
$$

If $\mathscr{E}=\left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ is given by measures $P_{\theta}$ on ( $\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{A}$ ), one can take for dominating measure the sum $S=\sum P_{\theta}$. The likelihood ratio vector $f= \left\{f_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}, f_{\theta}=d P_{\theta} / d S$ gives then an image of $S$ which is concentrated on $U$. It is a localization on $U$ of the conical measure $m_{\mathscr{E}}$ of the experiment $\mathscr{E}$. We shall occasionally call it the Blackwell measure of $\mathscr{E}$, even though Blackwell considered instead the image of the average of the $P_{\theta}$ on the simplex $\sum u_{\theta}=\operatorname{card} \Theta$.

Theorem 1. Assume that $\Theta$ is finite and let $\mathbb{E}(\Theta)$ be the space of experiment types indexed by $\Theta$. For each $\mathscr{E} \in \mathbb{E}(\Theta)$ the corresponding concial measure $m_{\mathscr{E}}$ admits a uniquely defined localization $\mu_{\mathscr{E}}$ on the simplex $U$.

The distance $\Delta$ between experiments satisfies the inequality

$$
\Delta(\mathscr{E}, \mathscr{F}) \leq\left\|\mu_{\mathscr{E}}-\mu_{\mathscr{F}}\right\|_{D} .
$$

Every positive Radon measure $\mu$ carried by $U$ and such that $\int u_{\theta} d \mu=1$ for all $\theta \in \Theta$, arises from some $\mathscr{E} \in \mathbb{E}(\Theta)$. The set of such measures is a set on which the distance $\Delta$ and the dual Lipschitz norm define the same compact topology.

Proof. Let $\Gamma$ be the set of restrictions to $U$ of the elements $\phi$ of the Choquet lattice $\mathscr{H}$. Clearly $\Gamma$ is a vector lattice of bounded functions. It contains the constants and separates the points of $U$. Thus, it is uniformly dense in the space $C(U)$ of continuous functions on $U$.

Note also that each $\phi \in \mathscr{H}$ is uniquely determined by its restriction $\phi_{U}$ to $U$. From this one verifies easily that the relation

$$
\left\langle\mu_{\mathscr{E}}, \phi_{U}\right\rangle=\left\langle m_{\mathscr{E}}, \phi\right\rangle
$$

defines a positive linear functional on $C(U)$. This is the desired localization. The evaluation linear functional $l_{\theta}$ satisfy on $U$ the requirement $\left|l_{\theta}\right| \leq 1$ and the Lipschitz condition $\left|l_{\theta}\left(u^{\prime}\right)-l_{\theta}\left(u^{\prime \prime}\right)\right| \leq\left|u^{\prime}-u^{\prime \prime}\right|$. Thus the same is true of all the $\phi$ which belong to the set $\mathscr{H}_{1}$ used in Chapter 2 to determine deficiencies. This gives the inequality

$$
\Delta(\mathscr{E}, \mathscr{F}) \leq\left\|\mu_{\mathscr{E}}-\mu_{\mathscr{F}}\right\|_{D} .
$$

The remaining statements are now obvious consequences of the compactness of $U$.

## 3. Deficiencies for Binary Experiments: Isometries

When the set $\Theta$ has only two elements, say $\Theta=\{0,1\}$, the canonical representation on the unit simplex appears awkward. One may simplify it as follows. The simplex

$$
U=\left\{\left(u_{0}, u_{1}\right) ; u_{i} \geq 0, u_{0}+u_{1}=1\right\}
$$

can be parameterized by one of the coordinates, say $x=u_{1}$. This sends it isomorphically on the interval $[0,1]$. The corresponding image on $[0,1]$ of the canonical measure is the sum of distributions

$$
\mu=\mathscr{L}\left[f \mid P_{0}\right]+\mathscr{L}\left[f \mid P_{1}\right]
$$

for the density $f=d P_{1} / d S, S=P_{0}+P_{1}$.
The elements of the set $\mathscr{H}_{1}$ which gives the deficiencies are representable accordingly. An element $\phi \in \mathscr{H}_{1}$ becomes a function obtainable as follows. One



takes two numbers, $a$ and $b, a \geq 0, b \geq 0, a+b=1$, and a finite family of pairs $\left(a_{j}, b_{j}\right) ; j \in J$ such that $0 \leq a_{j} \leq a$ and $0 \leq b_{j} \leq b$. Then what corresponds to $\phi$ is defined by the supremum

$$
\phi(x)=\sup _{j} \phi_{j}(x)
$$

with

$$
\phi_{j}(x)=a_{j} x+b_{j}(1-x)
$$

Even though this is a parameterization of the simplex $U$, we shall keep the same notations.

In this system, a binary experiment, that is a pair ( $P_{0}, P_{1}$ ), is therefore represented by a positive measure $\mu$ on $[0,1]$. The measure $\mu$ is such that

$$
\int x \mu(d x)=\int(1-x) \mu(d x)=1
$$

To $P_{1}$ corresponds the measure which has density $x$ with respect to $\mu$. Similarly $P_{0}$ becomes the measure having density $1-x$.

Returning to the general case, the set $\mathscr{H}_{1}$ was the set of all $\phi \in \mathscr{H}$ obtainable from Bayes decision problems with finite sets of possible decisions and gains bounded by zero and unity. One may also consider a subset of $\mathscr{H}_{1}$ consisting of all those $\phi \in \mathscr{H}_{1}$ which arise from Bayesian testing problems.

Let that set be called $\Phi$. It is easily verifiable that $\phi \in \mathscr{H}$ belongs to $\Phi$ if and only if it has the form $\phi=\phi_{1} \vee \phi_{2}$ where $\phi_{i}, i=1,2$ are finite linear combinations, say

$$
\phi_{i}=\sum a_{i, \theta} l_{\theta}
$$

with $a_{i, \theta} \geq 0$ and $\sum_{\theta} \sup _{i} a_{i, \theta} \leq 1$.
If $\mathscr{E}=\left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ and $\mathscr{F}=\left\{Q_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ are two experiments with respective conical measures $m_{\mathscr{E}}$ and $m_{\mathscr{F}}$ we can then, with Torgersen, define a testing deficiency

$$
\delta^{\prime}(\mathscr{E}, \mathscr{F})=\sup _{\phi \in \Phi}\left\{\left\langle m_{\mathscr{F}}, \phi\right\rangle-\left\langle m_{\mathscr{E}}, \phi\right\rangle\right\}
$$

This leads us to an important equality of Torgersen.
Consider two binary experiments $\mathscr{E}=\left\{P_{0}, P_{1}\right\}$ and $\mathscr{F}=\left\{Q_{0}, Q_{1}\right\}$. Represent them on the interval $[0,1]$ obtaining measures $\mu$ and $v$. We recall that, for instance, $\mu=\mathscr{L}\left(f \mid P_{0}\right)+\mathscr{L}\left(f \mid P_{1}\right)$ for $f=d P_{1} / d\left(P_{0}+P_{1}\right)$.

Proposition 1. Let $\mathscr{E}=\left\{P_{0}, P_{1}\right\}$ and $\mathscr{F}=\left\{Q_{0}, Q_{1}\right\}$ be two binary experiments represented on $[0,1]$ respectively, by measures $\mu$ and $v$. Let $\lambda=v-\mu$. Then the testing deficiency $\delta^{\prime}(\mathscr{E}, \mathscr{F})$ coincides with the deficiency $\delta(\mathscr{E}, \mathscr{F})$. It is given by the expression

$$
\delta(\mathscr{E}, \mathscr{F})=\sup _{z \in[0,1]} \int[z(1-x)] \vee[(1-z) x] d \lambda
$$

Proof. The deficiency $\delta(\mathscr{E}, \mathscr{F})$ is the supremum $\sup \left\{\int \phi d \lambda ; \phi \in \mathscr{H}_{1}\right\}$. Each $\phi \in \mathscr{H}_{1}$ is obtainable from a finite supremum $\phi=\sup _{j \in J} \phi_{j}$ where

$$
\phi_{j}(x)=a_{j} x+b_{j}(1-x)
$$

with $0 \leq a_{j} \leq a, 0 \leq b_{j} \leq b, a+b=1$. Thus $\phi$ is a convex function which is piecewise linear with changes of slopes occurring at points $z_{j} \in[0,1]$. One can assume that the $z_{j}$ have been ordered increasingly and that the slope at $z_{j}$ increases by an amount $s_{j}$.

From the construction of $\phi_{j}$, its maximum possible slope is obtained for $a_{j}=a$ and $b_{j}=0$. Similarly the minimum slope is obtained for $\left(a_{j}, b_{j}\right)=(0, b)$. Thus the total change in slope $\sum_{j} s_{j}$ of $\phi$ is at most equal to unity. One can also write $\phi$ in the form $\phi(x)=l+\sum_{j} s_{j}\left[x-z_{j}\right]^{+}$for a suitable linear affine function $l$. It follows that

$$
\int \phi d \lambda=\sum_{j} s_{j} \int\left[x-z_{j}\right]^{+} d \lambda
$$

cannot exceed $\sup _{z \in[0,1]} \int[x-z]^{+} d \lambda$.
Now consider the function $\phi_{z}$ given by

$$
\phi_{z}(x)=[z(1-x)] \vee[(1-z) x]
$$

for a $z \in[0,1]$. This is a function of the class $\Phi$ obtainable from testing problems. It changes slope by exactly one at the point $z$ and $\int \phi_{z} d \lambda=\int[x- z]^{+} d \lambda$. This concludes the proof of the Proposition.

Now let us return to an arbitrary experiment $\mathscr{E}=\left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$. For a particular testing problem the gain is given by the norm $\left\|m_{1} \vee m_{2}\right\|$ of the maximum of finite sums $m_{i}=\sum a_{i, \theta} P_{\theta}$ with $0 \leq a_{i, \theta} \leq \pi_{\theta}$ where $\sum \pi_{\theta}=1$. One can also rewrite

$$
m_{1} \vee m_{2}=\frac{1}{2}\left[m_{1}+m_{2}\right]+\frac{1}{2}\left|m_{1}-m_{2}\right|
$$

Hence

$$
\left\|m_{1} \vee m_{2}\right\|=\frac{1}{2} A+\frac{1}{2}\left\|m_{1}-m_{2}\right\|
$$

with $A=\sum \sum a_{i, \theta}$. Noting that $m_{1}-m_{2}=\sum_{\theta}\left[a_{1, \theta}-a_{2, \theta}\right] P_{\theta}$ with | $a_{1, \theta}-a_{2, \theta} \mid \leq \pi_{\theta}$, this gives immediately the following result of Torgersen.

Lemma 3. Let $\mathscr{E}=\left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ and $\mathscr{F}=\left\{Q_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ be two experiments indexed by $\Theta$. Then the testing deficiency $\delta^{\prime}(\mathscr{E}, \mathscr{F})$ is the supremum

$$
\delta^{\prime}(\mathscr{E}, \mathscr{F})=\frac{1}{2} \sup \left\{\left\|\sum c_{\theta} Q_{\theta}\right\|-\left\|\sum c_{\theta} P_{\theta}\right\|\right\}
$$

taken over all finite linear combinations such that $\sum\left|c_{\theta}\right|=1$.
This has a remarkable consequence as follows.
Proposition 2. Let $\mathscr{E}=\left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ and $\mathscr{F}=\left\{Q_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ be two experiments
indexed by $\Theta$. Let $E=\operatorname{Lin}(\mathscr{E})$ be the linear space of $\left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ in $L(\mathscr{E})$ and let $F=\operatorname{Lin}(\mathscr{F})$ be the corresponding linear space for $\mathscr{F}$. The two experiments $\mathscr{E}$
and $\mathscr{F}$ are equivalent if and only if the correspondence $\sum c_{\theta} P_{\theta} \leftrightarrow \sum c_{\theta} Q_{\theta}$ between $E$ and $F$ is isometric.

Proof. If $\mathscr{E}$ and $\mathscr{F}$ are equivalent they are equivalent for testing problems. Thus, by the above lemma the correspondence is an isometry.

Conversely, suppose that it is an isometry. Take a finite set $S$ of cardinality $n$ in $\Theta$ and let $\bar{P}_{0}$ be the average $(1 / n) \sum_{\theta \in S} P_{\theta}$. Let $\bar{P}_{1}$ be another average of the type

$$
\bar{P}_{1}=\sum_{\theta \in S} b_{\theta} P_{\theta}
$$

with $b_{\theta} \geq 0, \sum b_{\theta}=1$. Take a $z \in[0,1]$ and form the combination

$$
\sum a_{\theta} P_{\theta}=z \bar{P}_{0}-(1-z) \bar{P}_{1}
$$

Proceed similarly for $\mathscr{F}$ obtaining

$$
\sum a_{\theta} Q_{\theta}=z \bar{Q}_{0}-(1-z) \bar{Q}_{1}
$$

Then $\left\|z \bar{P}_{0}-(1-z) \bar{P}_{1}\right\|=\left\|z \bar{Q}_{0}-(1-z) \bar{Q}_{1}\right\|$. Thus by Proposition 1 , the two binary experiments $\left\{\bar{P}_{0}, \bar{P}_{1}\right\}$ and $\left\{\bar{Q}_{0}, \bar{Q}_{1}\right\}$ must be equivalent. This implies that the distributions

$$
\mathscr{L}\left[f \mid \bar{P}_{0}\right] \text { and } \mathscr{L}\left[g \mid \bar{Q}_{0}\right]
$$

for $f=d \bar{P}_{1} / d \bar{P}_{0}$ and $g=d \bar{Q}_{1} / d \bar{Q}_{0}$ must be the same. However, $f$ can be written as a sum

$$
f=\sum b_{\theta} X_{\theta}
$$
