with $X_{\theta}=d P_{\theta} / d \bar{P}_{0}$. Since the coefficients $b_{\theta}$ are arbitrary except for the restrictions $b_{\theta} \geq 0, \sum b_{\theta}=1$, it follows that the two vectors

$$
\left\{X_{\theta} ; \theta \in S\right\} \text { and }\left\{Y_{\theta} ; \theta \in S\right\}, \quad Y_{\theta}=d Q_{\theta} / d \bar{Q}_{0}
$$

have the same distributions. Thus the Blackwell canonical measures of

$$
\mathscr{E}_{S}=\left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in S\right\} \text { and } \mathscr{F}_{S}=\left\{Q_{\theta} ; \theta \in S\right\}
$$

are the same. The result follows.

## 4. Weak Convergence of Experiments

To elaborate some consequences of the results of Section 3, let us consider a particular set $\Theta$ and the set $\mathbb{E}(\Theta)$ of all experiment types indexed by $\Theta$. So far we have mostly considered $\mathbb{E}(\Theta)$ as a metric space for the distance $\Delta$. One can also topologize it by a weak topology as follows.

Let $A$ be the set of finite subsets of $\Theta$. For each $\alpha \in A$ and $\mathscr{E} \in \mathbb{E}(\Theta)$ let $\mathscr{E}_{\alpha}$ be the restriction $\mathscr{E}_{\alpha}=\left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \alpha\right\}$ of $\mathscr{E}=\left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ to the set $\alpha$. Metrize $\mathbb{E}(\alpha)$ by the distance $\Delta$.

Definition. The weak topology of $\mathbb{E}(\Theta)$ is the weakest topology for which all the maps $\mathscr{E} \mapsto \mathscr{E}_{\alpha}, \alpha \in A$ are continuous.

It is a convenient fact that for this topology $\mathbb{E}(\Theta)$ is always a compact Hausdorff space. This will be shown below. Before we proceed to prove it, let us observe that for a general $\Theta$ we have three sets of objects which introduce themselves naturally.

The first objects are the elements of $\mathbb{E}(\Theta)$ itself. Each $\mathscr{E} \in \mathbb{E}(\Theta)$ induces a conical measure $m_{\mathscr{E}}$ of resultant identically unity on $R_{+}^{\Theta}$. Thus our second set of objects is the set $\mathscr{M}_{1}$ of all conical measures of resultant one on $R_{+}^{\Theta}$. The third set of objects is the set obtained as follows.

Taking for $A$ the class of finite subsets of $\Theta$, consider families $\left\{\mathscr{E}_{\alpha} ; \alpha \in A\right\}$ where $\mathscr{E}_{\alpha} \in \mathbb{E}(\alpha)$. Such a family will be called coherent if whenever $\alpha \subset \beta$ the type $\mathscr{E}_{\alpha}$ is the restriction to $\alpha$ of the type $\mathscr{E}_{\beta}$. The third set of objects is the class, say $\mathscr{K}$, of coherent families $\left\{\mathscr{E}_{\alpha} ; \alpha \in A\right\}$.

Note that each $\mathscr{E} \in \mathbb{E}(\Theta)$ yields a coherent family $\left\{\mathscr{E}_{\alpha} ; \alpha \in A\right\}$. Note also that any conical measure $m \in \mathscr{M}_{1}$ yields a coherent family $\left\{\mathscr{E}_{\alpha} ; \alpha \in A\right\}$. Indeed, for each $\alpha$ one can obtain from $m$ a Blackwell measure which defines the desired $\mathscr{E}_{\alpha}$. The family is obviously coherent.

Thus we have natural maps, say $i$ and $j$, such that $i$ injects $\mathbb{E}(\Theta)$ into $\mathscr{M}_{1}$ and $j$ injects $\mathscr{M}_{1}$ into $\mathscr{K}$. By definition $\mathscr{M}_{1}$ is a subspace of the space of positive linear functionals on the lattice $\mathscr{H}$ used to define the conical measures. Thus $\mathscr{M}_{1}$ can be given the topology of pointwise convergence on $\mathscr{H}$. Similarly $\mathscr{K}$ can be topologized by convergence of the coordinate experiments $\mathscr{E}_{\alpha}$. With these definitions we can state the following result.

Theorem 2. The natural injections $i$ and $j$ of the diagram

$$
\mathbb{E}(\Theta) \xrightarrow{i} \mathscr{M}_{1} \xrightarrow{j} \mathscr{K}
$$

are surjective and continuous. All three spaces $\mathbb{E}(\Theta), \mathscr{M}_{1}$, and $\mathscr{K}$ are compact Hausdorff spaces.

Proof. The continuity of the injections $i$ and $j$ is quite obvious. It is also obvious that $j$ is surjective, and that $\mathscr{M}_{1}$ is compact. Thus to prove the theorem it is sufficient to show that any family $\left\{\mathscr{E}_{\alpha} ; \alpha \in A\right\}$ which is coherent arises from some $\mathscr{E} \in \mathbb{E}(\Theta)$.

To prove this, take such a family $\left\{\mathscr{E}_{\alpha} ; \alpha \in A\right\}$. For each $\mathscr{E}_{\alpha}$ one can find some experiment $\mathscr{E}_{\alpha}=\left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \alpha\right\}$ which has an $L$-space $L\left(\mathscr{E}_{\alpha}\right)$ and a minimal space $L_{m}\left(\mathscr{E}_{\alpha}\right)$ which will be noted here $L_{\alpha}$.

One can select such a minimal space $L_{\alpha}$ for each $\alpha \in A$. For the purpose of the following argument it is convenient to assume that if $\alpha \neq \beta$, then $L_{\alpha}$ and $L_{\beta}$ are disjoint. This can be achieved by taking suitable copies. One can then form the sum

$$
F=\bigcup_{\alpha} L_{\alpha}
$$

Now, according to Theorem 3, Chapter 2, each $L_{\alpha}$ is well determined up to isomorphism of its entire Banach lattice structure. Also, since $\alpha \subset \beta$ implies that $\mathscr{E}_{\alpha}$ is a restriction of $\mathscr{E}_{\beta}$, the space $L_{\alpha}$ is isomorphic to a well determined sublattice of $L_{\beta}$. Specifically, if $\mathscr{E}_{\beta}$ is taken in the form

$$
\mathscr{E}_{\beta}=\left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \beta\right\},
$$

then $L_{\alpha}$ is isomorphic to the closed sublattice spanned by $\left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \alpha\right\}$.
Thus we have an isomorphic imbedding $T_{\beta, \alpha}$ injecting $L_{\alpha}$ into $L_{\beta}$. It is clear that if $\alpha \subset \beta \subset \gamma$ then $T_{\gamma, \alpha}=T_{\gamma, \beta} T_{\beta, \alpha}$ by construction. Thus we have an inductive system and we may form the inductive limit of the system $\left\{L_{\alpha} ; \alpha \in A\right\}$ for the maps $T_{\beta, \alpha}$. This is the quotient $G$ of $F$ by the equivalence relation which identifies $x \in L_{\alpha}$ and $y \in L_{\beta}$ if there is a $\gamma \supset \alpha \cup \beta$ and a $z \in L_{\gamma}$ such that $T_{\gamma, \alpha} x=T_{\gamma, \beta} y=z$. Let $S$ be the identification map of $F$ onto $G$.

One can transport the norm, the linear structure, and the order structure of the $L_{\alpha}$ to $G$ in an obvious fashion. For instance, if $\lambda \in G$ then $\lambda$ comes from some $x$ in some $L_{\alpha}$. One gives $\lambda$ the norm $\|\lambda\|=\|x\|$. Similarly one writes $\lambda \geq 0$ if $x \geq 0$. Finally, if $\lambda$ and $\mu$ are in $G$ then there is some $L_{\alpha}$ with a pair $(x, y)$ such that $\lambda=S x$ and $\mu=S y$. One writes $\lambda+\mu=S(x+y)$.

It is easily verifiable that the results obtained this way do not depend on which $L_{\alpha}$ has been selected for the purpose. One can just as easily check that $G$ is indeed a normed vector lattice and that the property $\|\lambda+\mu\|=\|\lambda\|+\|\mu\|$ for $\lambda \geq 0, \mu \geq 0$ has been preserved. Let then $L$ be the completion of $G$ for the norm. This is an abstract $L$-space in which the coherent family $\left\{\mathscr{E}_{\alpha}\right\}$ has been represented by a unique family $\mathscr{E}=\left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$, which is an experiment. This concludes the proof of the Theorem.

As a corollary let us note the following.
Proposition 3. Let $\Theta$ be a set with its space $\mathbb{E}(\Theta)$ of experiment types. Let $K(\Theta)$ be the space of finite signed measures with finite support on $\Theta$. Let $\mathscr{W}$ be a filter on $\mathbb{E}(\Theta)$. For each $\mathscr{E}=\left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\} \in \mathbb{E}(\Theta)$ and $a \in K(\Theta)$ compute $\left\|\int P_{\theta} a(d \theta)\right\|$. Assume that for each $a \in K(\Theta)$ the numbers $\left\|\int P_{\theta} a(d \theta)\right\|$ converge along $\mathscr{W}$ to some limit, say $f(a)$. Then there is an $\mathscr{F}=\left\{Q_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\} \in \mathbb{E}(\Theta)$ such that $f(a)=\int Q_{\theta} a(d \theta), a \in K(\Theta)$ and such that $\mathscr{W}$ converges to $\mathscr{F}$ for the weak topology of $\mathbb{E}(\Theta)$.

Proof. According to the compactness assertion of Theorem 2 it is sufficient to show that convergence of the numbers $\left\|\int P_{\theta} a(d \theta)\right\|$ induces a separated topology on $\mathbb{E}(\Theta)$. (It is clearly not stronger than the weak topology.)

Now note according to Proposition 2 and Theorem 1 that each $\mathbb{E}(\alpha), \alpha \subset \Theta$, $\alpha$ finite could also be metrized by the distance $\Delta^{\prime}(\mathscr{E}, \mathscr{F})=\max \left\{\delta^{\prime}(\mathscr{E}, \mathscr{F})\right.$,
$\left.\delta^{\prime}(\mathscr{F}, \mathscr{E})\right\}$, which is equivalent to $\Delta$ itself on $\mathbb{E}(\alpha)$. This concludes the proof.
Regarding the structure of the set $\mathbb{E}(\Theta)$ on an arbitrary set $\Theta$ one can add the following remarks. The set $\left\{m_{\mathscr{E}}, \mathscr{E} \in \mathbb{E}(\Theta)\right\}$ of conical measures corresponding to elements of $\mathbb{E}(\Theta)$ is precisely the set $\mathscr{M}_{1}$ of conical measures having resultant identically unity. As such it is obviously a compact convex subset of the Riesz dual $\mathscr{H}^{*}$ of the Choquet lattice $\mathscr{H}$. In particular, if $m_{i} \in \mathscr{M}_{1}, i=1,2$ then so is $m=\alpha m_{1}+(1-\alpha) m_{2}$ for $\alpha \in[0,1]$. Also, according to the KreinMilman theorem, $\mathscr{M}_{1}$ is the closed convex hull of its set of extreme points. Thus it is interesting to understand what statistical concepts correspond to the convex combinations of conical measures and what features characterize the extreme points.

For the first question, consider two experiments $\mathscr{E}_{i} ; i=1,2$, with $\mathscr{E}_{i}=\left\{P_{\theta, i}\right.$; $\theta \in \Theta\}$. Let $m_{i}$ be the conical measure of $\mathscr{E}_{i}$. One can form another experiment $\mathscr{E}$ as follows. Take a random variable $\xi$ which can take only the values 1 and 2 and takes value 1 with probability $\alpha$. Draw $\xi$. If $\xi=i$ perform $\mathscr{E}_{i}$.

When the $P_{\theta, i}$ are measures on a space $\left(\mathscr{X}_{i}, \mathscr{A}_{i}\right)$, this corresponds to taking the direct $\operatorname{sum}\left(\mathscr{X}_{1}, \mathscr{A}_{1}\right) \oplus\left(\mathscr{X}_{2}, \mathscr{A}_{2}\right)$ and making measures $P_{\theta}=\alpha P_{\theta, 1}+(1- \alpha) P_{\theta, 2}$. (Note that this is valid only for a direct sum, since our variable $\xi$ is observed.) It is easily checked that the conical measure $m=\alpha m_{1}+(1-\alpha) m_{2}$ is exactly the conical measure of the experiment $\mathscr{E}$ so constructed using the variable $\xi$.

One could proceed more generally, mixing through arbitrary variables and forming measures of the type $P_{\theta}(A \times B)=\int_{B} P_{\theta, \xi}(A) F(d \xi)$. However, this will not be necessary below except perhaps for interpretations of Choquet's representation theorem.

## 5. Boundedly Complete Experiments

To characterize extreme points, we shall first prove an auxiliary lemma which gives a usable, but not very impressive piece of information.

Consider $\Theta$ and an experiment $\mathscr{E}=\left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$. To that experiment is associated the minimal lattice $\operatorname{Lat}(\mathscr{E}) \subset L_{m}(\mathscr{E}) \subset L(\mathscr{E})$ and the conical measure $m=m_{\mathscr{E}}$. We have also associated to $\mathscr{E}$ a map $T: \phi \mapsto M_{\phi}$ from $\mathscr{H}$ onto $\operatorname{Lat}(\mathscr{E})$. This is a positive linear map which is a lattice homomorphism sending the positive cone of $\mathscr{H}$ onto that of $\operatorname{Lat}(\mathscr{E})$ (see Proposition 2, Chapter 2).

Introducing the Riesz dual $\mathscr{H}^{*}$ of $\mathscr{H}$ and the Riesz, or Banach, dual $L^{*}$ of Lat ( $\mathscr{E}$ ), we get a transpose map ${ }^{t} T$ which sends $L^{*}$ into $\mathscr{H}^{*}$. This can be diagrammed as follows

$$
\begin{aligned}
\operatorname{Lat}(\mathscr{E}) & \stackrel{T}{\leftarrow} \mathscr{H} \\
L^{*} & \xrightarrow{t} \mathscr{H}^{*}
\end{aligned}
$$

Of course $L^{*}$ is also the dual of the minimal $L$-space $L_{m}(\mathscr{E})$. The image by ${ }^{t} T$ of the unit 1 of $L^{*}$ is the conical measure $m$ of the experiment $\mathscr{E}$.

Since the positive cone of $\mathscr{H}$ maps onto that of $\operatorname{Lat}(\mathscr{E})$, the transpose map ${ }^{t} T$ is also a lattice homomorphism. The following lemma provides some information about its range.

Lemma 4. Let $J$ be the interval $J=\{u ; 0 \leq u \leq 1\}$ in $L^{*}$. Then the image ${ }^{t} T J$ of $J$ in $\mathscr{H}^{*}$ is the interval $\left\{m^{\prime}: 0 \leq m^{\prime} \leq m\right\}$ of $\mathscr{H}^{*}$.

Proof. The set ${ }^{t} T J$ is a compact convex subset of $\mathscr{H}^{*}$ for the weak topology $\sigma\left(\mathscr{H}^{*}, \mathscr{H}\right)$. Thus, if $v \in \mathscr{H}^{*}$ does not belong to ${ }^{t} T J$, it can be separated from it by some $\phi \in \mathscr{H}$. Since this $\phi$ depends only on a finite number of coordinates $\theta \in \Theta$, the general statement will be true if and only if it is true when $\Theta$ is finite.

Thus, suppose $\Theta$ is finite. According to Theorem 3, Chapter 2, one may select any minimal version of the experiment $\mathscr{E}$. One possibility is as follows. Since $\Theta$ is finite, let $\mu$ be the localization of the conical measure $m$ on the simplex $U$ of $R_{+}^{\Theta}$. This is a Radon measure which has a support $K \subset U$. One may take for $\mathscr{E}$ the experiment given by the measures $P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta$, such that $P_{\theta}$ has density $u_{\theta}$ with respect to $\mu$ on $K$.

In this representation, the measure $M_{\phi}$ assigned to a $\phi \in \mathscr{H}$ is simply the measure defined by $d M_{\phi}=\phi d \mu$. Thus the map $T$ is the multiplication map $\phi \mapsto \phi d \mu$. The dual space $L^{*}$ is the space of equivalence classes of bounded measurable functions on $K$. The transpose map ${ }^{t} T$ takes such a bounded measurable function $v$ and assigns to it the measure $v d \mu$ on $K$. Now, if $m^{\prime}$ is a conical measure such that $m^{\prime} \leq m$, it is also localizable by a Radon measure $\mu^{\prime}$ on $K$. Of course, $0 \leq \mu^{\prime} \leq \mu$. Hence $\mu^{\prime}$ has with respect to $\mu$ a bounded density. In other words, $d \mu^{\prime}=v d \mu$ for some $v, 0 \leq v \leq 1$. This completes the proof of the Lemma.

Returning to the linear space $\operatorname{Lin}(\mathscr{E})$ spanned by $\mathscr{E}$, let $\overline{\operatorname{Lin}(\mathscr{E})}$ be the Banach space which is its closure in $L(\mathscr{E})$. Recall that the closure of $\operatorname{Lat}(\mathscr{E})$ is the minimal $L$-space $L_{m}(\mathscr{E})$.

Definition. An experiment $\mathscr{E}$ is called boundedly complete if the Banach space $\operatorname{Lin}(\mathscr{E})$ coincides with $L(\mathscr{E})$.

Note that if $\mathscr{E}$ is boundedly complete, the identity $\overline{\operatorname{Lin}(\mathscr{E})}=L(\mathscr{E})$ implies at least that $L_{m}(\mathscr{E})=L(\mathscr{E})$. Thus, $\mathscr{E}$ must already be in its minimal form. With these definitions, we have the following theorem, due to Torgersen.

Theorem 3. The following conditions are equivalent:
(i) The experiment type of $\mathscr{E}$ is an extreme point of $\mathbb{E}(\Theta)$.
(ii) The experiment $\mathscr{E}_{m}$, minimal form of $\mathscr{E}$, is boundedly complete.

Proof. Suppose first that $\mathscr{E}$ is not extreme. Then, its conical measure $m$ can be written $m=\alpha m_{1}+(1-\alpha) m_{2}$ for some $\alpha \in(0,1)$ and two different conical measures $m_{i} \in \mathscr{M}_{1}$. In fact, one may take $\alpha=\frac{1}{2}$. Consider the minimal space $\operatorname{Lat}(\mathscr{E})$ and its dual $L_{m}^{*}$ as in Lemma 5. Let $T$ be the homomorphism $\phi \mapsto M_{\phi}$. Since $\alpha m_{1} \leq m$, there is some $v \in L_{m}^{*}$ such that ${ }^{t} T v=\alpha m_{1}$ and $0 \leq v \leq 1$.

Let $F$ be the space of all finite linear combinations $\sum a_{\theta} l_{\theta}$ of coordinate evaluations. To say that $\overline{\operatorname{Lin}(\mathscr{E})}$ coincides with $\overline{\operatorname{Lat}(\mathscr{E})}$ is to say that the images $T \phi, \phi \in F$ are dense in $\operatorname{Lat}(\mathscr{E})$. However, all the elements of $\mathscr{M}_{1}$ have resultant unity, thus they coincide on $F$. It follows that $\left\langle\phi, \alpha m_{1}\right\rangle=\langle T \phi, v\rangle$ is identically equal to $\alpha\langle\phi, m\rangle$ on $F$ and therefore on $\operatorname{Lat}(\mathscr{E})$ whenever $\mathscr{E}$ is boundedly com-
plete. This gives $v \equiv \alpha$ and $m_{1}=m$. Therefore, if the minimal version $\mathscr{E}_{m}$ of $\mathscr{E}$ is boundedly complete, $\mathscr{E}$ is an extreme point.

Conversely, if $\mathscr{E}_{m}$ is not boundedly complete, there is some $v \in L_{m}^{*}$ such that $|v| \leq 1$ and such that $v \neq 0$ but $\left\langle v, P_{\theta}\right\rangle=0$ for all $\theta$. Then $w=\frac{1}{2}(1+v)$ is such that $0 \leq w \leq 1$ and $\left\langle w, P_{\theta}\right\rangle=\frac{1}{2}$ for all $\theta$. The image $m_{1}={ }^{t} T(2 w)$ is some conical measure $m_{1}$ of resultant unity. Obviously $m=\frac{1}{2}\left(m_{1}+m_{2}\right)$, with $m_{2}={ }^{t} T[2(1-w)]$. Thus $\mathscr{E}$ is not extreme.

## 6. Convolutions: Hellinger Transforms

We have just seen that performing a "mixture" of experiments translates itself into an averaging operation on the conical measures. Some other operations on experiments have similarly recognizable translations in terms of conical measures. A particularly simple illustration is offered by the formation of direct products. For this purpose note that the space $R_{+}^{\Theta}$ is a semi-group for coordinatewise multiplication. Thus, one can define the corresponding convolution operation for measures or conical measures.

Let then $\mathscr{E}=\left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ and $\mathscr{F}=\left\{Q_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ be two experiments indexed by the same set $\Theta$. Let $m_{\mathscr{E}}$ and $m_{\mathscr{F}}$ be the corresponding conical measures.

Proposition 4. Let $\mathscr{E} \otimes \mathscr{F}$ be the direct product $\theta \mapsto P_{\theta} \otimes Q_{\theta}$ of the two experiments $\mathscr{E}$ and $\mathscr{F}$. Then the conical measure associated with $\mathscr{E} \otimes \mathscr{F}$ is the convolution

$$
m_{\mathscr{E} \otimes \mathscr{F}}=m_{\mathscr{E}} * m_{\mathscr{F}}
$$

of the conical measures $m_{\mathscr{E}}$ and $m_{\mathscr{F}}$.
Proof. This can be seen very readily by looking at finite subsets of $\Theta$ and writing the value of $\left\langle m_{\mathscr{E} \otimes \mathscr{F}}, \phi\right\rangle$ as an integral.

It is often convenient, and certainly very common, to represent convolution algebras by algebras of functions in which convolution becomes pointwise multiplication. A well-known transform, the Hellinger transform does precisely this for the present case.

To define the transform, let $\widetilde{\Theta}$ be the set of probability measures with finite support on $\Theta$. Each $\pi \in \widetilde{\Theta}$ has a representation by families of numbers $\left\{\pi_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ with $\pi_{\theta} \geq 0, \sum_{\theta} \pi_{\theta}=1$, and with $\left\{\theta ; \pi_{\theta}>0\right\}$ finite.

For each a $\pi$, form the Hellinger product $\prod_{\theta}\left(d P_{\theta}\right)^{\pi_{\theta}}$. The Hellinger transform is the function defined on $\widetilde{\Theta}$ by

$$
\pi \mapsto \Phi_{\mathscr{E}}(\pi)=\left\|\prod_{\theta}\left(d P_{\theta}\right)^{\pi_{\theta}}\right\|
$$

To put it differently, if $m_{\mathscr{E}}$ is the conical measure of $\mathscr{E}$ defined on $\mathscr{H}$, then the function $\phi=\prod_{\theta} l_{\theta}^{\pi_{\theta}}$ does not belong to $\mathscr{H}$ but it always belongs to the domain of the Daniell extension of $m_{\mathscr{E}}$. For that extension $\Phi_{\mathscr{E}}(\pi)=\left\langle m_{\mathscr{E}}, \phi\right\rangle$.

Proposition 5. The correspondence $\mathscr{E} \mapsto \Phi_{\mathscr{E}}$ between the set $\mathbb{E}(\Theta)$ of experiment types and the set of their Hellinger transforms is bijective.

The Hellinger transform of a direct product $\mathscr{E} \otimes \mathscr{F}$ is the pointwise product $\Phi_{\delta} \Phi_{\mathscr{F}}$ of the corresponding Hellinger transforms. Weak convergence of experiment types coincides with pointwise convergence of their Hellinger transforms.

Proof. That direct product of experiments, or convolution of the corresponding conical measures, translates itself by pointwise multiplication of the transforms follows from the multiplicative properties of the functions $\prod_{\theta} l_{\theta}^{\pi_{\theta}}$.

To prove the other statements it is sufficient to consider the case where $\Theta$ is a finite set. In this case one can localize the conical measures in Blackwell's canonical form. On the unit simplex of $R_{+}^{\Theta}$, the Hellinger transform can be regarded as a Laplace transform. The result follows from the uniqueness of Laplace transforms and the compactness assertion of Theorem 1.

## 7. The Blackwell-Sherman-Stein Theorem

The convolution described above is a particular case of a more general operation called dilation. To dilate is to expand without modifying centers of gravity and can be described as follows.

Suppose first that $\Theta$ is finite. For each $f \in R_{+}^{\Theta}$, let $K_{f}$ be a conical measure of resultant identically unity. If $\phi \in \mathscr{H}$ one may then define an expression which, in integral notation, takes the form $\int \phi(f g) K_{f}(d g)$. Assuming appropriate measurability, one can then define the dilation through $K$ of the conical measure $m$ by

$$
\left\langle m_{1}, \phi\right\rangle=\int\left[\int \phi(f g) K_{f}(d g)\right] m(d f)
$$

For an arbitrary set $\Theta$ one can say that $m_{1}$ is a dilation of $m$ if the above described relation holds for every finite subset of $\Theta$. This leads to the following statement which is a form of the celebrated Blackwell-Sherman-Stein theorem.

Theorem 4. Let $\mathscr{E}=\left\{P_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ and $\mathscr{F}=\left\{Q_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ be two experiments in-
dexed by $\Theta$. Let $m_{\mathscr{E}}$ and $m_{\mathscr{F}}$ be the corresponding conical measures. Then the following statements are equivalent.
(i) $\delta(\mathscr{E}, \mathscr{F})=0$;
(ii) for every $\phi \in \mathscr{H}_{c}$ one has $\left\langle m_{\mathscr{E}}, \phi\right\rangle \geq\left\langle m_{\mathscr{F}}, \phi\right\rangle$;
(iii) $m_{\mathscr{E}}$ is a dilation of $m_{\mathscr{F}}$.

Proof. We already know that (i) and (ii) are equivalent. Also, they are equivalent to the existence of a transition $T$ such that $T P_{\theta} \equiv Q_{\theta}$. Represent both $\mathscr{E}$ and $\mathscr{F}$ by measures on the Kakutani representation spaces $\mathscr{X}$ and $\mathscr{Y}$, respectively. Then $T$ is represented by a Markov kernel, say $K(x, d y)$. One can then "inflate" the experiment $\mathscr{E}$ to an experiment $\mathscr{E}^{*}=\left\{P_{\theta}^{*}, \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ which consists of selecting a pair $(x, y) \in \mathscr{X} \times \mathscr{Y}$, choosing $x$ first according to $P_{\theta}$, and then $y$ according to $K(x, d y)$. This $\mathscr{E}^{*}$ is, of course, equivalent to $\mathscr{E}$.

Taking a finite set $A \subset \Theta$, let $\mu$ be a probability measure which dominates all the $P_{\theta}^{*}, \theta \in A$. The product space $\mathscr{X} \times \mathscr{Y}$ carries its own Borel field. One can also consider on $\mathscr{X} \times \mathscr{Y}$ the $\sigma$-field, say $\mathscr{B}$, of cylinder sets with measurable bases
in $\mathscr{Y}$. The restriction of $P_{\theta}^{*}$ to $\mathscr{B}$ is equivalent to the measure $Q_{\theta}$, projection of $P_{\theta}^{*}$ on $\mathscr{Y}$. For each $\theta \in A$ let $w_{\theta}$ be the density $w_{\theta}=d P_{\theta}^{*} / d \mu$ on ( $\mathscr{X} \times \mathscr{Y}$ ). Let $u_{\theta}$ be the density $d Q_{\theta} / d \mu$ on $\mathscr{B}$. Finally, let $\mathscr{B}_{1}$ be the $\sigma$-field induced by the map $(x, y) \rightarrow u(y)=\left\{u_{\theta}(y) ; \theta \in A\right\}$. Note that one may write $w_{\theta}=u_{\theta} v_{\theta}$ for some $v_{\theta} \geq 0$. Also, taking expectations for the dominating measure $\mu$ one has $E\left[w_{\theta} \mid \mathscr{B}\right]=E\left[w_{\theta} \mid \mathscr{B}_{1}\right]=u_{\theta}$. The conditional distribution of the vector $v=\left\{v_{\theta} ; \theta \in A\right\}$ given $\mathscr{B}_{1}$ is obtainable through some Markov kernel, say $K(u, d v)$.

Take a $\phi$ in the Choquet space of $R_{+}^{A}$. Then, one can write

$$
\left\langle m_{\mathscr{E}}, \phi\right\rangle=E \phi(w)=E\left\{E\left[\phi(w) \mid \mathscr{B}_{1}\right]\right\}=E\left\{\int \phi(u v) K(u, d v)\right\}
$$

Also, $\left\langle m_{\mathscr{F}}, \phi\right\rangle=E \phi(u)$. Thus $m_{\mathscr{E}}$ appears as a dilation of $m_{\mathscr{F}}$.
The implication (iii) ⇒ (ii) is an obvious consequence of the convexity of the elements $\phi$ of the space $\mathscr{H}_{c}$. Therefore the three statements are indeed equivalent.

Remark 1. The above result will not be of major importance in our arguments. However, statement (iii) often affords as easy way of checking whether or $\operatorname{not} \delta(\mathscr{E}, \mathscr{F})=0$, as will be seen in examples later on.

Remark 2. The construction used in the proof of the theorem gives on $\mathscr{X} \times \mathscr{Y}$ an experiment $\left\{P_{\theta}^{*} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ for which the coordinate $x$ acts as a sufficient statistic. Such sufficiency relations will be studied in detail in Chapter 5.

The "sufficiency" relation is often described by sub- $\sigma$-fields, taking an experiment $\mathscr{F}=\left\{Q_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ given by measures on a $\sigma$-field $\mathscr{A}_{2}$ and another experiment $\mathscr{E}=\left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ obtained by restricting the $Q_{\theta}$ to a sub-field $\mathscr{A}_{1} \subset \mathscr{A}_{2}$. It should be pointed out that Theorem 4, as such, does not say that if $\delta(\mathscr{E}, \mathscr{F})=0$ then $\mathscr{A}_{1}$ is a sufficient sub-field. The arguments appropriate for this situation will be given in Chapter 5.

To terminate, we shall mention briefly a construction which can be used to replace an arbitrary experiment $\mathscr{E}$ by an equivalent one which is $\Sigma$-finite. The construction also yields an alternate proof of Theorem 2.

Take an arbitrary $\Theta$ and a conical measure $m$ of resultant identically unity on $\Theta$. If $A$ is a subset of $\Theta$ the cone $R_{+}^{\Theta}$ contains the cone, say $C_{0}(A)$, of functions $f$ which vanish identically on $A$. The conical measure $m$ can then be written as a sum $m=m^{\prime}+m^{\prime \prime}$ of two components such that $m^{\prime}$ is "carried" by $C_{0}(A)$ and $m$ " is "outside" of $C_{0}(A)$. Going further, consider the set $A_{1}=\left\{\theta_{1}\right\} \cup A$ obtained by adjoining a point $\theta_{1} \notin A$ to $A$. Then $m^{\prime}$ can be split further into a part $m$ " "carried" by $C_{0}\left(A_{1}\right)$ and a part $m_{\theta_{1}}$ carried by $C_{0}(A)$ but situated "outside" $C_{0}\left(A_{1}\right)$.

Now suppose that $\Theta$ has been well ordered and that it is in fact a segment $\Theta=\{\theta: \theta<\kappa\}$ of the ordinal numbers. For each $\theta \in \Theta$ introduce the cones $C_{0}\left(A_{\theta}\right), A_{\theta}=\{\alpha ; \alpha<\theta\}$ and $C_{0}\left(A_{\theta, 1}\right), A_{\theta, 1}=\{\alpha ; \alpha \leq \theta\}$. Then $m$ has a component, say $m_{\theta}$ carried by $C_{0}\left(A_{\theta}\right)$ and outside $C\left(A_{\theta, 1}\right)$.

One can verify that $m$ is the sum $m=\sum_{\theta \in \Theta} m_{\theta}$. Also, each $m_{\theta}$ has a localization $\mu_{\theta}$ on the set $F_{\theta}$ of $f \in R_{+}^{\Theta}$ which vanish on $A_{\theta}$ but are equal to



unity at $\theta$ itself. If $l_{\theta}$ denotes, as before, evaluation at $\theta$ of the elements of $R_{+}^{\Theta}$, the sum

$$
Q_{\theta}(d f)=\sum_{\alpha \in \Theta} l_{\theta}(f) \mu_{\alpha}(d f)
$$

defines a probability measure on $R_{+}^{\Theta}$.
Instead of $R_{+}^{\Theta}$ one may take the compact $[0, \infty]^{\Theta}$ and the corresponding compacts $\bar{F}_{\theta}$. Let $S$ then be the locally compact space direct sum of disjoint copies of the $\bar{F}_{\theta}$. One can transport $\mu_{\alpha}$ to $\bar{F}_{\alpha} \subset S$, getting a Radon measure $\bar{\mu}_{\alpha}$. Similarly the sum $Q_{\theta}$ becomes a probability measure $P_{\theta}$ on $S$. It can be readily verified that the experiment $\left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ so obtained is a $\Sigma$-finite experiment with conical measure $m$.

## CHAPTER 4

## Some Basic Inequalities

## 1. Introduction

The usual approach to asymptotic problems relies on a large list of inequalities, and so does the present approach. This Chapter is not intended to begin with a selected list of such inequalities, but only to point out a few which are directly relevant to relations between properties of likelihood ratios and properties of the underlying measures. Of course, we shall freely use other classical inequalities such as Hölder's inequality which says that logarithm of the Hellinger transform (Chapter 3) is a convex function.

## 2. Hellinger Distances: $L_{1}$-Norm

Let $P$ and $Q$ be two probability measures on a $\sigma$-field $\mathscr{F}$. Some interesting quantities are the $L_{1}$-norm

$$
\|P-Q\|=\sup _{|f| \leq 1}\left|\int f d P-\int f d Q\right|
$$

and the half norm $D(P, Q)=\frac{1}{2}\|P-Q\|$.
One can write $D(P, Q)=1-\|P \wedge Q\|$. Also $\|P \wedge Q\|$ is precisely equal to the sum of probabilities of errors for that test between $P$ and $Q$ which minimizes the said sum. Finally, if $S$ is a joint distribution whose marginals are $P$ and $Q$, the number $\|P \wedge Q\|$ is the maximum probability which can be placed on the diagonal.

Even though $D(P, Q)$ has a particularly clear statistical significance, it is not
too easily manageable; e.g., in direct product situations, Another distance which is often useful is the Hellinger distance $H(P, Q)$ defined as follows.

Suppose that $P$ and $Q$ are arbitrary finite positive measures. Then

$$
H^{2}(P, Q)=\frac{1}{2} \int(\sqrt{d P}-\sqrt{d Q})^{2}
$$

It is immediate that $H^{2}$ has for minimum zero and for maximum $\frac{1}{2}\|P+Q\|$. Also

$$
H^{2}(P, Q) \leq D(P, Q) \leq H(P, Q)\left[\|P+Q\|-H^{2}(P, Q)\right]^{1 / 2}
$$

Thus $H$ and $D$ are equivalent in the sense that they define the same uniform structures.

One can also write $H^{2}$ in the form

$$
H^{2}(P, Q)=\frac{1}{2}\|P+Q\|-\int \sqrt{d P d Q}
$$

The integral $\int \sqrt{d P d Q}$ is the "affinity" between $P$ and $Q$. (Sometimes this name is reserved for the coefficient $\rho(P, Q)=[\|P\|\|Q\|]^{-1 / 2} \int \sqrt{d P d Q}$.) Since $[x(1-x)]^{1 / 2} \geq \min (x, 1-x)$ for $x \in[0,1]$ one has always $\|P \wedge Q\| \leq \int \sqrt{d P d Q}$.

Note that for a pair $\mathscr{E}=(P, Q)$ of probability measures the values $H(P, Q)$, $\rho(P, Q), D(P, Q)$, and $\|P \wedge Q\|$ depend only on the type of the experiment. Also, one may recover $\|P \wedge Q\|$ from the Hellinger transform $\int(d P)^{1-\alpha}(d Q)^{\alpha}$, $\alpha \in[0,1]$ by the Laplace transform inversion formula.

Let $P$ and $Q$ be two finite positive measures on a $\sigma$-field $\mathscr{F}$. One can define a likelihood ratio $X=(d Q / d P)$ by the usual procedure allowing infinity as a possible value so that $X$ is well defined up to a null set for $\mu=P+Q$.

Proposition 1. Write $E$ for integrals taken under $P$. Assume $\|P\|=\|Q\|=1$ and let $Z=\sqrt{X}$. Then
(i) $E Z^{2}=E X=$ mass of the part of $Q$ dominated by $P$.
(ii) $E Z=\int \sqrt{d P d Q}=1-H^{2}(P, Q)$.
(iii) Variance $Z=E Z^{2}-(E Z)^{2}=H^{2}(P, Q)\left[2-H^{2}(P, Q)\right]-\beta(Q, P)$ where $\beta(Q, P)$ is the mass of $Q$ that is $P$-singular.

For positive measures, there is still another distance which arises naturally. It is closely related to the Hellinger distance described above and was in fact used by Hellinger himself. If multiplied by a suitable constant, it becomes a close relative of Karl Pearson's $\chi^{2}$. The distance in question is given by $k(P, Q)$ with

$$
k^{2}(P, Q)=\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{(d P-d Q)^{2}}{d(P+Q)}
$$

One can verify that, on the space of positive finite measures, $k$ is indeed a distance. The only point which is perhaps not entirely obvious is the triangular inequality. However if $a, b$, and $c$ are numbers such that $0 \leq a \leq b \leq c$, one can check that

$$
\frac{c-a}{\sqrt{c+a}} \leq \frac{b-a}{\sqrt{b+a}}+\frac{c-b}{\sqrt{c+b}}
$$

Indeed the right side may be written

$$
(c-a)\left\{\alpha \frac{1}{\sqrt{b+a}}+(1-\alpha) \frac{1}{\sqrt{c+b}}\right\}
$$

with $\alpha=(b-a)(c-a)^{-1}$. Also,

$$
\alpha(b+a)+(1-\alpha)(c+b)=c+a
$$

Therefore the desired inequality follows from the convexity of the function $x \mapsto 1 / \sqrt{x}$. From this, it is easily argued that $k$ satisfies the triangular inequality

We have said that $k$ is closely related to the Hellinger distance $H$. In fact,

$$
k^{2}(P, Q)=\frac{1}{2} \int(\sqrt{d P}-\sqrt{d Q})^{2} \frac{(\sqrt{d P}+\sqrt{d Q})^{2}}{d(P+Q)}
$$

and since $P+Q \leq(\sqrt{d P}+\sqrt{d Q})^{2} \leq 2(P+Q)$ one has

$$
H^{2} \leq k^{2} \leq 2 H^{2}
$$

and $k^{2} \leq \frac{1}{2}\|P-Q\|$. One could also note the equality

$$
k^{2}(P, Q)=2 H^{2}(P, Q)-\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{(\sqrt{d P}-\sqrt{d Q})^{4}}{d(P+Q)}
$$

Pearson's $\chi^{2}$ is usually defined for measures on a $\sigma$-field generated by a finite partition. It would take the form

$$
\chi^{2}=\int \frac{(d Q-d P)^{2}}{d P}
$$

The relation with $k^{2}$ is clear.
The distance called $k$ arises in a simple estimation problem as follows. Consider an experiment $\mathscr{E}=\left(P_{0}, P_{1}\right)$ given by a pair of measures $P_{i} ; i=0,1$. Take for a set of possible decisions the whole interval $[0,1]$ and for loss function $W_{i}(t)=(t-i)^{2}$. If $\rho$ is a decision procedure let $S(\rho)$ be the sum of the risks $S(\rho)=W_{0} \rho P_{0}+W_{1} \rho P_{1}$.

Let $f$ be the Radon Nikodym density

$$
f=\frac{d P_{1}}{d\left(P_{0}+P_{1}\right)}
$$

This is a map to $[0,1]$ and is therefore one of the possible decision procedures for our problem.

Lemma 1. The estimate which minimizes the risk sum $S(\rho)$ is given by the RadonNikodym density $f$. The value of the minimum risk sum is

$$
\inf _{\rho} S(\rho)=\int \frac{d P_{0} d P_{1}}{d\left(P_{0}+P_{1}\right)}=\left\{\frac{1}{2}\left\|P_{0}+P_{1}\right\|-k^{2}\left(P_{0}, P_{1}\right)\right\} \frac{1}{2}
$$

For any other decision procedure $\rho$, one has (writing $\rho$ as a Markov kernel $\rho(x, d t))$

$$
S(\rho)-\int \frac{d P_{0} d P_{1}}{d\left(P_{0}+P_{1}\right)}=\iint[t-f(x)]^{2} \rho(x, d t)\left[P_{0}(d x)+P_{1}(d x)\right]
$$

Proof. This results from simple algebraic manipulations with quadratic expressions.

## 3. Approximation Properties for Likelihood Ratios

Passing to an inequality of a different nature, consider two pairs of measures ( $P_{1}, P_{2}$ ) and ( $Q_{1}, Q_{2}$ ). The following proposition says that if $\left\|P_{i}-Q_{i}\right\|, i=1,2$ are small, so is the difference between the densities $d P_{2} / d P_{1}$ and $\left(d Q_{2} / d Q_{1}\right)$.

Proposition 2. Let ( $P_{1}, P_{2}$ ) and ( $Q_{1}, Q_{2}$ ) be two pairs of positive measures on the same space ( $\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{A}$ ). Then

$$
\begin{array}{r}
\int\left\{1 \wedge\left|\frac{d P_{2}}{d P_{1}}-\frac{d Q_{2}}{d Q_{1}}\right|\right\} d\left(P_{1}+Q_{1}\right) \leq \\
\\
\left\{2\left\|P_{1}-Q_{1}\right\|+2\left\|P_{2}-Q_{2}\right\|+\right. \\
\left\{2\left\|\left\|P_{2}+Q_{2}\right\|\right\}^{1 / 2}\right.
\end{array}
$$

Proof. Let us introduce measures $\mu=P_{1}+Q_{1}, v=P_{1} \wedge Q_{1}, S=\frac{1}{2}\left(P_{2}+Q_{2}\right)$, and $D=\frac{1}{2}\left(P_{2}-Q_{2}\right)$. The quantity to bound is

$$
R=\int\left\{1 \wedge\left|\frac{d P_{2}}{d P_{1}}-\frac{d Q_{2}}{d Q_{1}}\right|\right\} d \mu
$$

Replacing $\mu$ by the smaller measure $v$ and noting that $\mu-2 v=\left|P_{1}-Q_{1}\right|$, one can write that

$$
R \leq 2 R_{1}+\left\|P_{1}-Q_{1}\right\|
$$

with

$$
R_{1}=\int\left\{1 \wedge\left|\frac{d P_{2}}{d P_{1}}-\frac{d Q_{2}}{d Q_{1}}\right|\right\} d v
$$

Expressing this in terms of the average $S$ and the difference $D$ we obtain

$$
R_{1} \leq \int\left[1 \wedge\left|\frac{d S}{d P_{1}}-\frac{d S}{d Q_{1}}\right|\right] d v+\int\left[1 \wedge\left|\frac{d D}{d P_{1}}+\frac{d D}{d Q_{1}}\right|\right] d v
$$

Since $v=P_{1} \wedge Q_{1}$, the last integral is at most equal to $2\|D\|$. For the first integral on the right hand side note that for any number $f$ one has $1 \wedge|f| \leq \sqrt{|f|}$. Thus by Schwarz' inequality

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int 1 \wedge\left|\frac{d S}{d P_{1}}-\frac{d S}{d Q_{1}}\right| d v & \leq \int\left|\frac{d S}{d P_{1}}-\frac{d S}{d Q_{1}}\right|^{1 / 2} d v \\
& \leq\left\{\int \frac{d S}{d v} d v \int\left|\frac{d v}{d P_{1}}-\frac{d v}{d Q_{1}}\right| d v\right\}^{1 / 2}
\end{aligned}
$$

Under the square root sign the first integral is bounded by $\|S\|$. For the second, write $P_{1}=v+\phi, Q_{1}=v+\psi$ with $\phi \wedge \psi=0$. On a set where $\psi$ vanishes and $v$ and $\phi$ do not, the integral becomes

$$
\int\left|\frac{d v}{d(v+\phi)}-1\right| d v=\int\left|\frac{d \phi}{d(v+\phi)}\right| d v \leq \int|d \phi|=\|\phi\|
$$

Proceeding similarly for $\psi$ one obtains

$$
R_{1} \leq 2\|D\|+\left\{\|S\|\left\|P_{1}-Q_{1}\right\|\right\}^{1 / 2}
$$

Hence, adding all the terms, the inequality as stated.
Another inequality of this type, involving only a pair ( $P, Q$ ) of finite positive measures, is as follows. Let $\mu=P+Q, X=d Q / d P$. Then, for $\varepsilon \in(0,1)$

$$
\varepsilon \mu\left\{X \notin\left[\frac{1-\varepsilon}{1+\varepsilon}, \frac{1+\varepsilon}{1-\varepsilon}\right]\right\} \leq\|P-Q\| .
$$

Since logarithms of likelihood ratios are often involved in asymptotic arguments, it would be convenient to have an analogue of Proposition 2 for them. However, the situation is more complicated. Using the notation of Proposition 2, let $P_{1}=Q_{1}$ be the Lebesgue measure on $[0,1]$. Let $P_{2}$ be the Lebesgue measure on $\left[0, n^{2}\right]$ normalized so that $\left\|P_{2}\right\|=1$. Take

$$
\frac{d Q_{2}}{d P_{2}}=0
$$

on the interval $[0,1]$ and

$$
\frac{d Q_{2}}{d P_{2}}=n^{2}\left[n^{2}-1\right]^{-1}
$$

for the interval $\left(1, n^{2}\right]$. Then $\left\|P_{1}-Q_{1}\right\|=0$ and $\left\|P_{2}-Q_{2}\right\|=\left(2 / n^{2}\right)$. However, $Q_{1}\{|\log X-\log Y|=\infty\}=1$, for $X=d P_{2} / d P_{1}$ and $y=d Q_{2} / d Q_{1}$.

In spite of this, use the notations of Proposition 2 and write $\Lambda=\log X$ and $\Lambda^{\prime}=\log Y$. Then

$$
Q_{1}\left\{\left|\Lambda-\Lambda^{\prime}\right|>\varepsilon\right\} \leq Q_{1}\left[\Lambda^{\prime}<\log 2 \varepsilon\right]+\varepsilon^{-2} \int r(X-Y) d Q_{1}
$$

with $r(x)=\min [1,|x|]$.

In the opposite direction, two measures $P$ and $Q$, whose likelihood ratio $X=d Q / d P$ is often close to unity, must be very close to each other. For instance, for $\varepsilon \in(0,1)$ one has

$$
\|Q-P\|=2 \int[1-(X \wedge 1)] d P+\|Q\|-\|P\|
$$

(b) $\|Q-P\| \leq 2 \varepsilon P[1-\varepsilon \leq X<1]+2 P[X<1-\varepsilon]+\|Q\|-\|P\|$; (c) $\|Q-P\| \leq \frac{2 \varepsilon}{1+\varepsilon} Q[1<X \leq 1+\varepsilon]+2 Q[X>1+\varepsilon]+\|P\|-\|Q\|$.

In particular, if $\|P\|=\|Q\|=1$, then

$$
\|Q-P\| \leq 2 \varepsilon+2 \min \{P[X<1-\varepsilon], Q[X>1+\varepsilon]\}
$$

When approximating a likelihood ratio by some other function, the above inequalities can be used as follows.

Let $P$ be probability measure and let $\mu$ be a finite measure which dominates $P$. Let $X=d P / d \mu$ and let $Y \geq 0$ be the presumed approximation to $X$. If $\int Y d \mu \geq 1$, there is some $c \in[0, \infty]$ such that $\int(Y \wedge c) d \mu=1$. Consider the measure $Q$ given by $d Q=(Y \wedge c) d \mu$. Then

$$
\|Q-P\|=2 \int[X-[Y \wedge c]]^{+} d \mu
$$

If, on the contrary, $0<\int Y d \mu<1$, define $Q_{1}$ by $d Q_{1}=Y d \mu$ and then let $Q=Q_{1}\left\|Q_{1}\right\|^{-1}$. Then, again

$$
\|Q-P\| \leq 2 \int[X-Y]^{+} d \mu
$$

To prove this last statement note that

$$
\left\|Q_{1}-P\right\|=2 \int[X-Y]^{+} d \mu+\left\|Q_{1}\right\|-1
$$

and $Q-Q_{1}=\left\{\left\|Q_{1}\right\|^{-1}-1\right\} Q_{1}$ has mass $1-\left\|Q_{1}\right\|$.
We shall encounter many problems where one is led to approximate a logarithm of likelihood ratio $\Lambda=\log d P / d \mu$ by a "simpler" expression $L$. In such a case a variation on the above inequalities becomes useful.

Assume either that $\mu$ dominates $P$ or allow suitable infinite values. Both $P$ and $\mu$ being defined on a $\sigma$-field $\mathscr{F}$, assume that $L$ is $\mathscr{F}$-measurable and takes values in $(-\infty,+\infty)$. Let $\psi_{1}$ be the indicator of the set $\{|L-\Lambda| \leq \log 2\}$. Let $\psi_{2}$ be the indicator of $\{L+\log 2<\Lambda\}$ and let $\psi_{3}$ be the indicator of $\{L>\Lambda+\log 2\}$. Define $d P_{i}=\psi_{i} d P$ and $d Q_{i}=\psi_{i} e^{L} d \mu$. If possible, define $d Q=e^{L} d \mu$. Then, (g)

$$
\begin{gathered}
\text { one has } \frac{1}{2} P_{1} \leq Q_{1} \leq 2 P_{1} \text { and }\left\|Q_{1}-P_{1}\right\| \leq \eta \text { with } \\
\eta=\inf _{0 \leq \varepsilon \leq \log 2}\left\{\left(e^{\varepsilon}-1\right) P_{1}[|L-\Lambda|<\varepsilon]+P_{1}[|L-\Lambda|>\varepsilon]\right\} .
\end{gathered}
$$

(h) One has $Q_{2} \leq \frac{1}{2} P_{2}$ and

$$
\left\|P_{2}\right\|-\left\|Q_{2}\right\|=\left\|P_{2}-\dot{Q}_{2}\right\| \leq P[L \leq \Lambda-\log 2]
$$

One has $Q_{3} \geq 2 P_{3}$ and $\left\|Q_{3}-P_{3}\right\|=\left\|Q_{3}\right\|-\left\|P_{3}\right\|$.
Thus, if $\int r(L-\Lambda) d P$ is small, $\|Q-P\|$ will be small if and only if the same is true of $|\|Q\|-\|P\||$.

It is not sufficient that $|L-\Lambda|$ be small in $P$-measure since $\left\|Q_{3}\right\|-\left\|P_{3}\right\|$ may well be very large. In those inequalities, the measure $\mu$ enters only through the combination $e^{L} d \mu$. Replacing $\mu$ by an equivalent measure introduces the same multiplier on both measures and does not change the terms used in the inequalities.

## 4. Inequalities for Conditional Distributions

Another important inequality is relative to the difference between two conditional expectations.

Suppose that $\mathscr{X}$ is an analytic set in some Polish space and let $\mathscr{B}$ denote its $\sigma$-field of Borel sets. Let $\mathscr{A}$ be a sub- $\sigma$-field of $\mathscr{B}$ and let $P_{i} ; i=0,1$ be two finite positive measures on $\mathscr{B}$. Let $P_{i}^{\prime}$ be the restriction of $P_{i}$ to $\mathscr{A}$. In this case there are Markov kernels $x \mapsto F_{i, x}$ from ( $\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{A}$ ) to ( $\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{B}$ ) such that

$$
\int\left[\int u(y) F_{i, x}(d y)\right] P_{i}(d x)=\int u(x) P_{i}(d x)
$$

for all bounded $\mathscr{B}$-measurable functions $u$.
The kernels $F_{i}$ are well defined almost everywhere with respect to $P_{0}^{\prime} \wedge P_{1}^{\prime}$. However, if for instance $P_{1}^{\prime}$ has a part which is $P_{0}^{\prime}$ singular, then $F_{0, x}$ is not well defined for that part. In spite of this it will be convenient to have bounds for the $L_{1}$-norm $\left\|F_{1, x}-F_{0, x}\right\|$ of the difference of the kernels, or at least for integrals of that norm.

For this purpose let $S=P_{0}+P_{1}$ and $S^{\prime}=P_{0}^{\prime}+P_{1}^{\prime}$. Here, as in all cases where $\mathscr{B}$ is countably generated, the equivalence class for $S^{\prime}$ of the function $x \mapsto \left\|F_{1, x}-F_{0, x}\right\|$ is the supremum over $u,|u| \leq 1$ of the classes of the functions $x \mapsto \int u(y)\left[F_{1, x}(d y)-F_{0, x}(d y)\right]$. Without any Polish-type assumptions on $(\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{B})$ this need not be the case. One can always manage to insure it, without any assumptions on $\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{A}$, or $\mathscr{B}$, if the kernels $F_{i}$ are obtained by a lifting in the manner shown by A . and C . Ionescu Tulcea. Thus we shall seek a general bound which reduces to a bound on $\int\left\|F_{1, x}-F_{0, x}\right\| S(d x)$ in the Polish situation described above.

To do this, take an arbitrary ( $\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{B}$ ) with an arbitrary $\mathscr{A} \subset \mathscr{B}$ and a pair ( $P_{0}, P_{1}$ ) just as before. Let $M$ be the space of equivalence classes, for $S$, of the bounded $\mathscr{B}$ measurable functions. Similarly, let $M^{\prime}$ be the space of equivalence classes, for $S^{\prime}$, of the bounded $\mathscr{A}$ measurable functions. Call conditional expectation operator adapted to $M^{\prime}$ and $P_{i}$ any positive linear map $E_{i}$ of $M$ into $M^{\prime}$ which is such that
(i) $1 E_{i} \leq 1$;
(ii) for every $u \in M^{\prime}$ and $v \in M$ one has $\left\langle u\left(v E_{i}\right), P_{i}\right\rangle=\left\langle u v, P_{i}\right\rangle$ (this is Kolmogorov's identity).

The existence of such operators follows, as usual, from the Radon-Nikodym theorem. Note that $E_{i}$ is not necessarily uniquely defined. However, there is a smallest idempotent $u_{i} \in M^{\prime}$ such that $\left\langle u_{i}, P_{i}^{\prime}\right\rangle=\left\langle 1, P_{i}^{\prime}\right\rangle$. One can easily check that the operator $\underline{E}_{i}$ defined for $v \in M$ by $v \underline{E}_{i}=u_{i}\left(v E_{i}\right)$ is well defined. It is also a conditional expectation operator for $P_{i}$.

With this notation let $D$ be the operator $D=E_{1}-E_{0}, E_{i}$ being adapted to $P_{i}$. Let $|D|$ be the supremum

$$
|D|=\frac{1}{2} \sup _{v}\{|v D|, v \in M,|v| \leq 1\}
$$

taken in the complete lattice $M^{\prime}$ of equivalence classes. Then $2|D|$ will coincide with the equivalence class of $x \mapsto\left\|F_{1, x}-F_{0, x}\right\|$ in the regular cases mentioned previously. The element $|D|$ will be called the modular bound of $D$.

Let $\Pi$ be the conditional expectation operator adapted to $M, M^{\prime}$, and the sum $S=P_{0}+P_{1}$. Let $f_{i}=d P_{i} / d S$ and let $f_{i}^{\prime}$ be the corresponding density $f_{i}^{\prime}=d P_{i}^{\prime} / d S^{\prime}$ on the smaller $\sigma$-field $\mathscr{A}$. One may assume that $f_{i} \geq 0, f_{i}^{\prime} \geq 0$, and $f_{0}+f_{1}=f_{0}^{\prime}+f_{1}^{\prime}=1$.

Proposition 3. With the notations just described for all $v \in M$, one can write $\left(v E_{i}\right) f_{i}^{\prime}=\left(v f_{i}\right) \Pi$. Also

$$
\langle | E_{1}-E_{0}|, S\rangle \leq\left\|P_{1}-P_{0}\right\|+\left\|P_{1}^{\prime}-P_{0}^{\prime}\right\| \leq 2\left\|P_{1}-P_{0}\right\|
$$

Proof. The first statement is a consequence of the Kolmogorov identity used to define conditional expectations. For the second, note that we may write

$$
\left[v\left(f_{1}-f_{0}\right)\right] \Pi=\frac{1}{2}\left[v E_{1}-v E_{0}\right]+\frac{1}{2}\left[v E_{1}+v E_{0}\right]\left[f_{1}^{\prime}-f_{0}^{\prime}\right]
$$

Thus the modular bound $\left|E_{1}-E_{0}\right|=\frac{1}{2} \sup \left\{\left|v\left(E_{1}-E_{0}\right)\right|,|v| \leq 1\right\}$ is bounded by

$$
\left|f_{1}-f_{0}\right| \Pi+\left|f_{1}^{\prime}-f_{0}^{\prime}\right|
$$

The desired inequality follows.
Note. On the set where $f_{0}^{\prime} f_{1}^{\prime}>0$ one could write

$$
\left|E_{1}-E_{0}\right|=\frac{1}{2}\left|\frac{f_{1}}{f_{1}^{\prime}}-\frac{f_{0}}{f_{0}^{\prime}}\right| \Pi
$$

as results from the first statement of Proposition 3. We have not used this directly for the proof of the Proposition because of the arbitrariness of, say, $v E_{0}$, on the set where $P_{1}$ is $P_{0}$ singular.

Of course, the bound would not be valid without the inequality $1 E_{i} \leq 1$ used in the definition of the conditional operators. There is another bound on the integrals of $\left|E_{1}-E_{0}\right|$ which looks rather different from the one given in

Proposition 3. It will be used on several occasions, and in particular in the study of sufficiency and insufficiency. It is as follows.

Proposition 4. Let $P_{i}, i=0,1$ be as above and let $C$ be a number $C \geq 1$. Then

$$
\langle | E_{1}-E_{0}\left|, P_{1}\right\rangle \leq \beta+\frac{1}{2}(1+C)\left\|P_{0}+P_{1}\right\|^{1 / 2}\left\{k^{2}\left(P_{0}, P_{1}\right)-k^{2}\left(P_{0}^{\prime}, P_{1}^{\prime}\right)\right\}^{1 / 2}
$$

with $\beta=\left\|P_{1}^{\prime}-P_{1}^{\prime} \wedge C P_{0}^{\prime}\right\|$.
Proof. Consider the modular bound $\left|E_{0}-E_{1}\right|$ of the difference $D=E_{0}-E_{1}$. This is always bounded by unity. Thus,

$$
\langle | E_{1}-E_{0}\left|, P_{1}\right\rangle \leq \beta+\langle | E_{1}-E_{0}\left|, P_{1}^{\prime} \wedge C P_{0}^{\prime}\right\rangle
$$

and it will be enough to bound the last term on the right. Since $P_{1}^{\prime} \wedge C P_{0}^{\prime}$ is carried by the set $f_{0}^{\prime} f_{1}^{\prime}>0$, we may use the expression given in the Note which follows Proposition 3.

Still working only on the set $f_{0}^{\prime} f_{1}^{\prime}>0$ we may write

$$
\left(\frac{f_{1}}{f_{1}^{\prime}}-\frac{f_{0}}{f_{0}^{\prime}}\right)\left(f_{0}^{\prime} \wedge C f_{1}^{\prime}\right)=\phi \psi
$$

with

$$
\begin{aligned}
\phi & =\frac{f_{1} f_{0}^{\prime}-f_{0} f_{1}^{\prime}}{\left(f_{0}^{\prime}+f_{1}^{\prime}\right) \sqrt{f_{0}+f_{1}}}=f_{1} f_{0}^{\prime}-f_{0} f_{1}^{\prime} \\
\psi & =\frac{f_{0}^{\prime}+f_{1}^{\prime}}{f_{0}^{\prime} f_{1}^{\prime}}\left(f_{0}^{\prime} \wedge C f_{1}^{\prime}\right) \sqrt{f_{0}+f_{1}}=\frac{v \wedge[(1-v) C]}{v(1-v)}
\end{aligned}
$$

with $v=f_{0}^{\prime}=1-f_{1}^{\prime}$. Note that the maximum value of $\psi$ can only be attained if $v$ takes the value $v=C[1+C]^{-1}$, the maximum being then equal to $1+C$. Thus $|\psi| \leq 1+C$.

Since, on the set in question, $\left|E_{1}-E_{0}\right|$ is given by the conditional expectation for the sum $S$ of the absolute value

$$
\frac{1}{2}\left|\frac{f_{1}}{f_{1}^{\prime}}-\frac{f_{0}}{f_{0}^{\prime}}\right|,
$$

we may write

$$
2\langle | E_{1}-E_{0}\left|, P_{1}^{\prime} \wedge C P_{0}^{\prime}\right\rangle=\langle | \phi| | \psi|, S\rangle \leq\langle | \phi|, S\rangle(1+C)
$$

By Schwarz' inequality, we have

$$
\left.|\langle | \phi|, S\rangle\left.\right|^{2} \leq\left.\langle | \phi\right|^{2}, S\right\rangle\|S\| .
$$

However, since $f_{0}+f_{1}=f_{0}^{\prime}+f_{1}^{\prime}=1$ one has also

$$
\phi=\frac{f_{1}}{f_{0}+f_{1}}-\frac{f_{1}^{\prime}}{f_{0}^{\prime}+f_{1}^{\prime}}
$$

Thus, according to Lemma 1, the value $\left.\left.\langle | \phi\right|^{2}, S\right\rangle$ is precisely the difference $\frac{1}{2}\left\{k^{2}\left(P_{0}, P_{1}\right)-k^{2}\left(P_{0}^{\prime}, P_{1}^{\prime}\right)\right\}$. The desired inequality follows.

Instead of $k^{2}$ we could have used here the minimum risk sum

$$
\alpha\left(P_{0}, P_{1}\right)=\int \frac{d P_{0} d P_{1}}{d\left(P_{0}+P_{1}\right)}
$$

since $2 \alpha\left(P_{0}, P_{1}\right)=\frac{1}{2}\left\|P_{0}+P_{1}\right\|-k^{2}\left(P_{0}, P_{1}\right)$. One can note that the risk sum is related to the sum of error probabilities in testing problems in a simple manner. In fact, $\alpha\left(P_{0}, P_{1}\right)=\int_{0}^{1} R(z) d z$, with a sum of error probabilities $R(z)$ corresponding to the prior distribution which assigns mass $z$ to $P_{1}$ and $1-z$ to $P_{0}$. That is,

$$
R(z)=\left\|\left[(1-z) P_{0}\right] \wedge\left(z P_{1}\right)\right\|
$$

Another interesting application of Lemma 1 occurs as follows. Let $\mathscr{E}=$ ( $P_{0}, P_{1}$ ) be a given experiment and let $T$ be a "statistic" available on $\mathscr{E}$ with value in some Euclidean or Polish space, say $\mathscr{Y}$, with metric $\rho$. Then $T$ has for $P_{i}$ a distribution $\mathscr{L}\left[T \mid P_{i}\right]=T P_{i}$.

It often happens that one also has certain approximations, say $G_{i}$ of the distributions $T P_{i}$. It is then tempting to use the $G_{i}$ instead of the $T P_{i}$. In fact it is a very common practice to use the best tests of $G_{0}$ against $G_{1}$, compute error probabilities for this problem, and hope that (1) for the test used, the error probabilities corresponding to the $T P_{i}$ are about the same as for the approximations $G_{i}$, and (2) one could not do very much better using the measures $P_{i}$ themselves.

These hopes are unlikely to be fulfilled unless the statistic $T$ and the approximations $G_{i}$ are suitably selected. The following proposition gives some indications on the possibilities, assuming, as is often the case in practice, that the density $g=d G_{1} / d v, v=G_{0}+G_{1}$ is "smooth".

To state the result, consider the sum $v=G_{0}+G_{1}$ and the sum $\mu=T P_{0}+ T P_{1}$. Let $\|\mu-v\|_{D}$ be the dual Lipschitz norm of the difference $\mu-v$. Also, since only two measures $P_{i}$ are involved, and since the range space ( $\mathscr{Y}, \rho$ ) is Polish, any transition $T$ can be represented by a Markov kernel $T(x, d y)$. With all of this, one can state the following.

Proposition 5. Assume that there is a function $\phi$ satisfying the Lipschitz condition

$$
\left|\phi\left(y_{1}\right)-\phi\left(y_{2}\right)\right| \leq K \rho\left(y_{1}, y_{2}\right)
$$

such that

$$
\int|\phi(y)-g(y)| v(d y)<\varepsilon
$$

Then, writing $\phi_{1}=(\phi \wedge 1)^{+}$and $f_{1}=d P_{1} / d\left(P_{0}+P_{1}\right)$,



$$
\begin{aligned}
& \iint\left|f_{1}(x)-\phi_{1}(y)\right|^{2} T(x, d y)\left(P_{0}+P_{1}\right)(d x) \\
& \quad \leq 2 \varepsilon+2 K\left\{\left\|G_{0}-T P_{0}\right\|_{D}+\left\|G_{1}-T P_{1}\right\|_{D}\right\}+\alpha\left(G_{0}, G_{1}\right)-\alpha\left(P_{0}, P_{1}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

Proof. According to Lemma 1 the sum

$$
\int \sum_{i}(g-i)^{2} d G_{i}=\alpha\left(G_{0}, G_{1}\right)
$$

is the minimum risk sum for the loss function $W_{i}(t)=(t-i)^{2}$. Since $\phi_{1}$ satisfies the same Lipschitz condition as $\phi$, this yields

$$
\int \sum_{i}\left(\phi_{1}-i\right)^{2} d T P_{i} \leq 2 \sum_{i} K\left\|T P_{i}-G_{i}\right\|_{D}+\alpha\left(G_{0}, G_{1}\right)+\varepsilon
$$

The result is then an immediate consequence of the second part of Lemma 1.

The above result will be used in the chapters on asymptotic normality to show that if statistics which are asymptotically normal in the usual sense can be treated 'as if' they were normal, then the underlying experiments must already converge to Gaussian experiments. Several results related to this phenomenon, and to what has become known as "robustness" will be given in the chapter on limit theorems.

## CHAPTER 5

## Sufficiency and Insufficiency

## 1. Introduction

Theoretical statistics makes abundant use of a certain concept named "sufficiency." It is traditionally introduced as follows.

One takes an experiment $\mathscr{E}=\left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ where the objects $P_{\theta}$ are measures on a $\sigma$-field $\mathscr{A}$. One considers a sub- $\sigma$-field $\mathscr{A}^{\prime} \subset \mathscr{A}$ and says that $\mathscr{A}^{\prime}$ is sufficient for $\mathscr{E}$ if for every bounded $\mathscr{A}$-measurable function $u$ there is an $\mathscr{A}^{\prime}$ measurable function $u^{\prime}$ such that

$$
\int u v d P_{\theta}=\int u^{\prime} v d P_{\theta}
$$

for all $\theta \in \Theta$ and all bounded $\mathscr{A}^{\prime}$-measurable functions $v$.
In this chapter we shall use a definition which is very close to the traditional one but differs from it on a technical detail. As is easily verifiable, the correspondence $u \mapsto u^{\prime}$, if it exists, induces a projection from the space of equivalence
classes of bounded $\mathscr{A}$-measurable functions onto the subspace of equivalence classes of $\mathscr{A}^{\prime}$-measurable functions.

These spaces are sequentially closed but not necessarily closed for tractable weak topologies. As a result, the traditional definition exhibits some features which may appear counter intuitive. For instance, it has been shown by Burkholder that one may have triplets of $\sigma$-fields, $\mathscr{A}^{\prime} \subset \mathscr{A}^{\prime \prime} \subset \mathscr{A}$ such that $\mathscr{A}^{\prime}$ is sufficient, but $\mathscr{A}^{\prime \prime}$ is not.

To avoid the technical problems, we shall use a vector lattice framework instead of the usual measure theoretic setting. As already explained in Chapter 1 , this can be interpreted as meaning that we reserve the right to modify the underlying measurable spaces $(\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{A})$ or $\left(\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{A}^{\prime}\right)$ to make them behave themselves.

Section 2 gives a brief summary of some properties of projections in $M$ spaces, emphasizing the connection with conditional expectation operators. Section 3 formulates a long series of possible definitions of "sufficiency" and gives a general theorem which asserts their equivalence.

Section 4 proceeds to definitions of "insufficiency" and gives some relations between these new numbers and the deficiencies of Chapter 2.

Section 5 gives two quilt patch theorems which allow using local arguments in certain cases where good estimates permit reduction of the problems to vicinities of the estimated values.

## 2. Projections and Conditional Expectations

Let $\mathscr{E}=\left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ be an experiment given by objects $P_{\theta}$ which are elements of a certain abstract $L$-space, say $L$. Let $M$ be the dual of $L$. The band $L(\mathscr{E})$ generated in $L$ by $\mathscr{E}$ need not be the entire space $L$. Nothing essential would be lost by assuming $L=L(\mathscr{E})$, but the more general setting used here will allow us to keep a consistent terminology throughout the discussion. We shall use subsets of $M$ according to the following definition.

Definition 1. Let $H$ be a subset of $M$. It will be called a uniform sublattice of $M$ if:
(i) it is a vector subspace and a sublattice of $M$;
(ii) the unit element 1 of $M$ belongs to $H$;
(iii) it is closed in $M$ for the uniform norm $\|u\|=\inf \{\alpha ;|u| \leq \alpha 1\}$.

Definition 2. A uniform sublattice $H \subset M$ will be called complete if it is closed in $M$ for the weak topology $\sigma(M, L)$.

The term "complete" is explainable as follows. Instead of the weak topology, one could use the topology $\beta(M, L)$ of uniform convergence on the intervals of $L$. Since the dual of $M$ for $\beta$ is still the space $L$ itself, the weak and $\beta$ closures of linear subspaces such as $\mathscr{H}$ coincide. Thus "complete" is intended to recall that the intervals of $H$ are $\beta(M, L)$ complete, or equivalently that they are Dedekind complete for the order convergence induced by $L$.

Using the fact that $\sigma(M, L)$ and $\beta(M, L)$ closures coincide, one can immediately verify that if $H$ is a uniform sublattice its $\sigma(M, L)$ closure in $M$ is a
complete sublattice. Indeed, the lattice operations are $\beta(M, L)$ continuous. In the sequel we shall be interested in a uniform sublattice $H$ and its closure $M^{\prime}$. We shall also be interested in positive linear projections $\Pi$ which map $M$ onto $H$ or $M^{\prime}$. The notational system used will be as follows.

A linear map from $M$ to $M$ or $M^{\prime}$ will act on its left, the image of $u$ by $\Pi$ being noted $u \Pi$. If $u$ and $v$ belong to $M$, their product for the unique multiplication of $M$ will be noted $u v$. If $P$ is some element of $L$ the evaluation of $P$
at $u \in M$ will be noted either $u P$, or more specifically $\langle u, P\rangle$, to allow for suitable groupings of the entities in the bilinear forms.

It will also be convenient to note $u \cdot P$ the element of $L$ which has density $u$ with respect to $P$, that is, the element of $L$ defined by $\langle v, u \cdot P\rangle=\langle v u, P\rangle$, $v \in M$. The following lemmas are classical.

Lemma 1. Let $\Pi$ be a positive linear projection of $M$ onto a linear subspace $H$. Then:
(i) If $\Pi$ is $\sigma(M, L)$ continuous, its range $H$ is necessarily $\sigma(M, L)$ closed.
(ii) If $a \in M, a \geq 0$ and $a \Pi=0$ implies $a=0$, then $H$ is a sublattice of $M$.

Proof. For the first assertion let $\left\{u_{v}\right\}, u_{v} \in H$ be a filter which converges to some $u \in M$. Then $u_{v}=u_{v} \Pi$ converges to $u$ and also to $u \Pi$ so that $u=u \Pi$. For the second statement take a $u \in H$. Then $u^{+} \geq u$ implies $u^{+} \Pi \geq u \Pi=u$ and therefore $u^{+} \Pi \geq(u \Pi)^{+}=u^{+}$. This may be written $u^{+} \Pi=u^{+}+a$ with $a \in M, a \geq 0$. It follows that $u^{+} \Pi=\left(u^{+} \Pi\right) \Pi=u^{+} \Pi+a \Pi$. This gives $a \Pi=0$ and therefore $a=0$ by assumption. Thus $u^{+} \Pi=u^{+}$, and the desired result is acquired.

Lemma 2. Let $H$ be a complete sublattice of $M$. Let $\Pi$ be a positive linear projection of $M$ onto $H$. Then

$$
(u v) \Pi=u(v \Pi)
$$

for every $u \in H$ and $v \in M$.
Proof. Take a $v \in M$ such that $0 \leq v \leq 1$ and let $u$ be an idempotent of $H$. The positivity of $\Pi$ implies the inequalities

$$
\begin{gathered}
(u v) \Pi \leq u \Pi=u \\
{[(1-u) v] \Pi \leq(1-u) \Pi=1-u}
\end{gathered}
$$

Multiplying by $u+(1-u)$ before and after application of $\Pi$ one obtains also

$$
\begin{aligned}
v \Pi & =u[v \Pi]+(1-u)[v \Pi] \\
& =(u v) \Pi+[(1-u) v] \Pi
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $u$ and $1-u$ are disjoint, this identity and the previous inequalities imply that $(u v) \Pi=\mathrm{u}[v \Pi]$. Thus the desired result holds for the idempotents $u \in H$ and therefore also for finite linear combinations of idempotents. The latter combinations are norm dense in $H$, hence the result since $\Pi$ is norm continuous.

Consider now a uniform sublattice $H \subset M$ and its $\sigma(M, L)$ closure $M^{\prime}$ in $M$. Take a $\mu \geq 0, \mu \in L$. Then there is in $M^{\prime}$ a smallest idempotent $J_{\mu}$ such that $\left\langle J_{\mu}, \mu\right\rangle=\langle 1, \mu\rangle=\|\mu\|$. If $\mu$ and $v$ are two positive elements of $L$ which are disjoint when restricted to $M^{\prime}$, one verifies easily that $J_{\mu}$ and $J_{\nu}$ are likewise disjoint. Let $L^{\prime}$ be the quotient of $L$ obtained by restricting the elements of $L$ to $M^{\prime}$.

In $L^{\prime}$ the restriction $\mu^{\prime}$ of $\mu$ generated a band in $L^{\prime}$. It follows from the above that for any $P^{\prime} \in L^{\prime}$ the element $J_{\mu} \cdot P^{\prime}$ defined by $\left\langle v, J_{\mu} \cdot P^{\prime}\right\rangle=\left\langle v J_{\mu}, P^{\prime}\right\rangle$ for $v \in M^{\prime}$ is precisely the component of $P^{\prime}$ in the band $L_{\mu}^{\prime}$ generated by $\mu^{\prime}$ in $L^{\prime}$.

One can also introduce the lattice $M_{\mu}^{\prime}=\left\{v J_{\mu} ; v \in M^{\prime}\right\}$. If $\Pi$ is any positive linear projection of $M$ onto $M^{\prime}$, the map $u \mapsto(u \Pi) J_{\mu}$ is a positive linear projection of $M$ onto $M_{\mu}^{\prime}$ such that $1 \Pi J_{\mu}=J_{\mu}$.

Lemma 3. Let $M^{\prime}$ be a complete sublattice of $M$. Let $\mu$ be an element of $L^{+}$. Then there is one and only one positive linear projection $\Pi$ of $M$ onto $M_{\mu}^{\prime}$ such that
(i) $1 \Pi=J_{\mu}$;
(ii) $\langle u \Pi, \mu\rangle=\langle u, \mu\rangle$ for all $u \in M$.

The projection so defined is $\sigma(M, L)$ continuous.
Proof. Take a particular $u \in M$ such that $u \geq 0$. Let $\phi$ be the linear functional defined on $M_{\mu}^{\prime}$ by the formula $\langle v, \phi\rangle=\langle u v, \mu\rangle, v \in M_{\mu}^{\prime}$.

This is a positive linear functional satisfying the inequality $\langle v, \phi\rangle \leq\|u\|\langle v, \mu\rangle$ for all $v \geq 0, v \in M_{\mu}^{\prime}$. Thus, according to the Radon-Nikodym theorem, $\phi$ possesses a density $u^{\prime}, 0 \leq u^{\prime} \leq\|u\|, u^{\prime} \in M_{\mu}^{\prime}$ such that $\langle u v, \mu\rangle=\langle v, \phi\rangle= \left\langle u^{\prime} v, \mu\right\rangle$ for all $v \in M_{\mu}^{\prime}$.

One verifies easily that such a $u^{\prime}$ is uniquely determined. Thus there is a well determined map $\Pi$ of $M$ into $M_{\mu}^{\prime}$. This map is a projection such that $1 \Pi=J_{\mu}$ and $u \Pi=u$ if $u \in M_{\mu}^{\prime}$. Thus, to complete the proof it will be enough to prove that $\Pi$ is weakly continuous.

However, if $P^{\prime} \in L^{\prime}$ then $P^{\prime}=J_{\mu} \cdot P^{\prime}+\left(1-J_{\mu}\right) \cdot P^{\prime}$ and therefore $\left\langle u \Pi, P^{\prime}\right\rangle= \left\langle(u \Pi) J_{\mu}, P^{\prime}\right\rangle$ for all $u \in M$. Thus, by transposition, one may write $\Pi P^{\prime}= \Pi\left(J_{\mu} \cdot P^{\prime}\right)$. As noted before, $J_{\mu} \cdot P^{\prime}$ is the component of $P^{\prime}$ in the band generated by $\mu^{\prime}$ in $L^{\prime}$. In addition, $\Pi$ is certainly continuous for the norm. By construction $\Pi \mu^{\prime}=\mu$, hence by positivity $\Pi v^{\prime} \in L$ for any $v^{\prime} \leq c \mu^{\prime}$. By continuity for the norm, $\Pi v^{\prime} \in L$ for any $v^{\prime}$ in the band of $\mu^{\prime}$ in $L^{\prime}$. The desired result follows.

Definition 3. Let $M^{\prime}$ be a complete sublattice of $M$ and let $\mu$ be a positive element of $L$. The unique projection $\Pi$ whose existence is asserted by Lemma 3 will be called the MINIMAL conditional expectation operator associated with $\mu$ and $M^{\prime}$.

By contrast we shall call conditional expectation operator associated with $\mu$ and $M^{\prime}$ any positive linear projection $\Pi$ of $M$ onto $M^{\prime}$ such that $\langle u \Pi, \mu\rangle=\langle u, \mu\rangle$ for all $u \in M$ or even any positive linear map $A$ of $M$ into $M^{\prime}$ such that $1 A \leq 1$ and $\langle v(u A), \mu\rangle=\langle v u, \mu\rangle$ for all $v \in M^{\prime}$.

A pleasant corollary of the above Lemma 3 is as follows.
Lemma 4. Let $M^{\prime}$ be a complete sublattice of $M$. Then there exist $\sigma(M, L)$ continuous positive linear projections of $M$ onto $M^{\prime}$.

Proof. Let $L^{\prime}$ be the space obtained by restricting the elements of $L$ to
$M^{\prime}$. In $L^{\prime}$, let $\left\{\lambda_{\alpha}^{\prime} ; \alpha \in A\right\}$ be a family of positive elements $\lambda_{\alpha}^{\prime} \geq 0,\left\|\lambda_{\alpha}^{\prime}\right\|=1$ such that (i) the $\lambda_{\alpha}^{\prime}$ are two by two disjoint, and (ii) the band generated by $\left\{\lambda_{\alpha}^{\prime} ; \alpha \in A\right\}$ is $L^{\prime}$ itself. The existence of such families follows readily from Zorn's lemma. Note also that, according to M. Riesz' theorem, each $\lambda_{\alpha}^{\prime}$ has an extension $\lambda_{\alpha}, \lambda_{\alpha} \geq 0,\left\|\lambda_{\alpha}\right\|=1$ which belongs to $L$.

For each $\alpha$ there is an idempotent $u_{\alpha} \in M^{\prime}$ which is the smallest such that $\left\langle u_{\alpha}, \lambda_{\alpha}^{\prime}\right\rangle=1$. Also, there is a minimal conditional expectation operator $\Pi_{\alpha}$ associated with the extension $\lambda_{\alpha}$. One verifies easily that $1 \Pi_{\alpha}=u_{\alpha}$ and that $\Pi=\sum_{\alpha} \Pi_{\alpha}$ is a positive linear projection. This $\Pi$ is also $\sigma(M, L)$ continuous since $\Pi \lambda_{\alpha}^{\prime}=\lambda_{\alpha} \in L$ and since the band generated by the $\lambda_{\alpha}^{\prime}$ is $L^{\prime}$ itself.

In the sequel we shall have occasion to investigate a complete sublattice $M^{\prime} \subset M$ and several projections of $M$ onto $M^{\prime}$. Take, for instance, two such positive linear projections $\Pi_{i}, i=1,2$, and let $D$ be the difference $D=\Pi_{1}-\Pi_{2}$. Since $M^{\prime}$ is order complete one may define a modulus by the relation

$$
v|D|=\sup \{u D ; u \in M,|u| \leq v\}
$$

for all $v \geq 0, v \in M$.
In Chapter 4 we have called the image $\frac{1}{2}[1|D|]=|D|$ the modular bound of $D$. The following lemma records a usable property of this modulus.

Lemma 5. For every $\mu \in L, \mu \geq 0$ one has the equality

$$
\frac{1}{2} \sup _{|u| \leq 1}\langle u D, \mu\rangle=\langle | D|, \mu\rangle
$$

Proof. Consider two elements $u_{i}, i=1,2$ of $M$ taken such that $\left|u_{i}\right| \leq 1$. Then $u_{i} D \in M^{\prime}$ and so does the element $v=\operatorname{sign}\left[u_{1} D-u_{2} D\right]$. Note that $\left|v\left(u_{1}-u_{2}\right)\right| \leq\left|u_{1}-u_{2}\right|$. Thus, if $u_{3}=\frac{1}{2}\left(u_{1}+u_{2}\right)+\frac{1}{2} v\left(u_{1}-u_{2}\right)$ one can write

$$
u_{1} \wedge u_{2} \leq u_{3} \leq u_{1} \vee u_{2}
$$

In particular $\left|u_{3}\right| \leq 1$. Now, by application of Lemma 2,

$$
\begin{aligned}
u_{3} D & =\frac{1}{2}\left[u_{1} D+u_{2} D\right]+\frac{1}{2} v\left[\left(u_{1}-u_{2}\right) D\right] \\
& =\left(u_{1} D\right) \vee\left(u_{2} D\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

It follows that

$$
\sup _{|u| \leq 1}\langle u D, \mu\rangle=\sup _{|u| \leq 1}\langle | u D|, \mu\rangle
$$

Furthermore the images $|u D|,|u| \leq 1$ form a directed set so that

$$
\sup _{|u| \leq 1}\langle | u D|, \mu\rangle=\left\langle\left[\sup _{|u| \leq 1}|u D|\right], \mu\right\rangle .
$$

This gives the desired result.

## 3. Equivalent Definitions for Sufficiency

In this section we shall consider an experiment $\mathscr{E}=\left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ and spaces $L$ and $M$ as in Section 2. The letter $H$ will denote a uniform sublattice of $M$ and $M^{\prime}$ will be the complete sublattice closure of $H$ in $M$ for $\sigma(M, L)$.

The quotient space obtained by restricting the elements of $L$ to $M^{\prime}$ will be noted $L^{\prime}$. Note that $L^{\prime}$ is also isomorphic to the space obtained by restricting to $H$ itself. However, we shall occasionally emphasize restrictions to $H$ instead of $M^{\prime}$. Thus, if $P \in L$, its restriction to $M^{\prime}$ will be called $P^{\prime}$ and its restriction to $H$ will be called $P^{H}$.

Recall that in Lemma 3 (Section 2) we introduced the minimal conditional operators attached to a pair ( $\mu, M^{\prime}$ ). If $P_{\theta}$ is an element of the experiment $\mathscr{E}$, the minimal conditional operator attached to $P_{\theta}$ and $M^{\prime}$ will be noted $\Pi_{\theta}$.

Finally, recall that in Chapter 4, we introduced not only the Hellinger distance $H(P, Q)$ defined by $2 H^{2}(P, Q)=\int(\sqrt{d P}-\sqrt{d Q})^{2}$, but also a "chisquare" distance $k$ defined by

$$
k^{2}(P, Q)=\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{(d P-d Q)^{2}}{d(P+Q)}
$$

It should be clear from the definitions themselves that $\left\|P^{H}-Q^{H}\right\|=\left\|P^{\prime}-Q^{\prime}\right\|$ and similarly $k\left(P^{\prime}, Q^{\prime}\right)=k\left(P^{H}, Q^{H}\right)$ for any pairs ( $P, Q$ ) of positive elements of $L$. With these notations, we now give a list of properties which may be enjoyed by $H$ or $M^{\prime}$.

Property (A). There is a positive linear projection $\Pi$ of $M$ onto $M^{\prime}$ such that

$$
\left\langle u \Pi, P_{\theta}\right\rangle=\left\langle u, P_{\theta}\right\rangle
$$

for all $u \in M$ and all $\theta \in \Theta$.
Property (B). For each $u \in M$, there is a $u^{\prime} \in M^{\prime}$ such that

$$
\left\langle u^{\prime} v, P_{\theta}\right\rangle=\left\langle u v, P_{\theta}\right\rangle
$$

for all $v \in M^{\prime}$ and all $\theta \in \Theta$.
To state the next property, let $L^{H}$ be the space obtained by restricting the elements of $L$ to $H$ and let $\mathscr{E}^{H}$ be the corresponding experiment $\mathscr{E}^{H}=\left\{P_{\theta}^{H} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$.

Property (C). The deficiency $\delta\left(\mathscr{E}^{H}, \mathscr{E}\right)$ is equal to zero.
Property (D). For every pair ( $s, t$ ) of elements of $\Theta$ and every $u \in M, 0 \leq u \leq 1$, there is a $v \in M^{\prime}, 0 \leq v \leq 1$ such that $\left\langle u, P_{s}\right\rangle=\left\langle v, P_{s}\right\rangle$ and $\left\langle u, P_{t}\right\rangle=\left\langle v, P_{t}\right\rangle$.

Property (E). For every pair ( $s, t$ ) of elements of $\Theta$ the minimal operators $\Pi_{s}, \Pi_{t}$ satisfy the relation $\Pi_{s} J_{t}=\Pi_{t} J_{s}$ ( $J_{s}$ being the idempotent of $M^{\prime}$ associated with $P_{s}$ ).

Property (F). For every pair ( $s, t$ ) of element of $\Theta$ one has $k\left(P_{s}, P_{t}\right)= k\left(P_{s}^{H}, P_{t}^{H}\right)$.
Property (G). For every pair ( $s, t$ ) of elements of $\Theta$ one has $H\left(P_{s}, P_{t}\right)= H\left(P_{s}^{H}, P_{t}^{H}\right)$.

Property (H). For every pair ( $s, t$ ) of elements of $\Theta$ let $L_{s, t}$ be the twodimensional subspace of $L$ spanned by $\left(P_{s}, P_{t}\right)$. Let $L_{s, t}^{H}$ be the corresponding span of ( $P_{s}^{H}, P_{t}^{H}$ ). Then the correspondence $0 \leftrightarrow 0, P_{s} \leftrightarrow P_{s}^{H}, P_{t} \leftrightarrow P_{t}^{H}$ extends to an isometry between $L_{s, t}$ an $L_{s, t}^{H}$, for the $L$-norms.

There is a variant of Property ( C ) which is also interesting and sometimes easy to use. It is as follows.

Property (CI). Let $\mu=\sum a_{\theta} P_{\theta}$ be afinite linear combination of elements of $\mathscr{E}$. Then there is a $v \in M^{\prime}, 0 \leq v \leq 1$ such that $v \cdot \mu=\mu^{+}$.

It is easily checked that Property (CI) is an assertion equivalent to the assertion that the linear spans $\operatorname{Lin}\left(\mathscr{E}^{\prime}\right)$ and $\operatorname{Lin}(\mathscr{E})$ are isometric and that, according to Chapter 3, it must be equivalent to Property (C) itself. Note that Properties (D) to (H) involve only pairs ( $s, t$ ) of elements of $\Theta$. One could restate Property ( C ) in a similar manner.

Properties (F), (G), and (H) assert isometries of the correspondences $P_{\theta} \leftrightarrow P_{\theta}^{H}$. However, such an isometry is not enough for Property (H) which uses the $L$-norms. Of course, isometry of the linear spans $\operatorname{Lin}(\mathscr{E})$ and $\operatorname{Lin}\left(\mathscr{E}^{H}\right)$ has already been shown to be equivalent to Property (C) in Chapter 3, Proposition 2.

Let us first show that the isometry properties are equivalent and equivalent to the pairwise version of Property (C).

Proposition 1. Let $(s, t)$ be a pair of elements of $\Theta$. For such a pair, Properties $(F),(G)$, and $(H)$ are equivalent and they are equivalent to the property that the binary experiment ( $P_{s}^{H}, P_{t}^{H}$ ) is not weaker than ( $P_{s}, P_{t}$ ).

Proof. The operation which restricts the $P_{\theta}$ to $H$ is obviously a transition. Thus $\mathscr{E}_{s, t}^{H}=\left(P_{s}^{H}, P_{t}^{H}\right)$ is always weaker than $\mathscr{E}_{s, t}=\left(P_{s}, P_{t}\right)$. Thus, if $\delta\left[\left(P_{s}^{H}, P_{t}^{H}\right),\left(P_{s}, P_{t}\right)\right]=0$ the two binary experiments are equivalent. Since the Hellinger distance, the distance $k$, and the $L$-norms are entirely determined by the type
of the experiment, the equivalence in question certainly implies Properties (F), $(G)$, and (H).

Conversely suppose that Property (F) holds. The expression of the square distance $k^{2}$ as an average of the gains in testing problems shows immediately that, since $\mathscr{E}_{s, t}^{H}$ is weaker than $\mathscr{E}_{s, t}$, the equality $k^{2}\left(P_{s}^{H}, P_{t}^{H}\right)=k^{2}\left(P_{s}, P_{t}\right)$ will not hold unless $\delta\left(\mathscr{E}_{s, t}^{H}, \mathscr{E}_{s, t}\right)=0$.

A similar argument can be carried out for the Hellinger distances, or equivalently for the affinities $\rho\left(P_{s}, P_{t}\right)$ and $\rho\left(P_{s}^{H}, P_{t}^{H}\right)$. Indeed, according to the Blackwell-Sherman-Stein theorem the canonical measure $m$ of $\mathscr{E}_{s, t}$ is a dilation of the canonical measure $m^{H}$ of $\mathscr{E}_{s, t}^{H}$. However, since for each $\alpha \in(0,1)$ the functions $x^{1-\alpha}(1-x)^{\alpha}$ are strictly concave, the relation $\rho\left(P_{s}, P_{t}\right)=\rho\left(P_{s}^{H}, P_{t}^{H}\right)$ implies that the dilation leaves $m^{H}$ invariant.

Finally, assume the existence of the isometry mentioned in Property (H). This is some bijective correspondence between the two spaces $L_{s, t}$ and $L_{s, t}^{H}$. By assumption the origins of the two spaces are in the correspondence. Then an argument of Mazur shows that the correspondence must be linear. By Chapter 3, Proposition 2, this implies that $\mathscr{E}_{s, t}$ and $\mathscr{E}_{s, t}^{H}$ are equivalent. This being
acquired, let us pass to the property of conditional expectations expressed by (E).

Proposition 2. Let ( $s, t$ ) be a pair of elements of $\Theta$ such that $k\left(P_{s}, P_{t}\right)= k\left(P_{s}^{H}, P_{t}^{H}\right)$. Then Property ( $E$ ) holds for this pair.

Proof. We shall sketch two proofs. First let us return to the initial step in the proof of Proposition 4, Chapter 4. This can easily be modified to yield the inequality

$$
\langle | \Pi_{s}-\Pi_{t}\left|J_{s}, P_{t}\right\rangle \leq \beta_{1}+\beta_{2},
$$

where $\beta_{1}=\left\|Q-Q^{\prime} \wedge C P_{s}^{\prime}\right\|, Q^{\prime}$ being the part of $P_{t}^{\prime}$ which is dominated by $P_{s}^{\prime}$ and where $\beta_{2}$ is the number

$$
\beta_{2}=(1+C)\left[k^{2}\left(P_{s}, P_{t}\right)-k^{2}\left(P_{s}^{\prime}, P_{t}^{\prime}\right)\right]^{1 / 2}
$$

Here, $\beta_{2}=0$ for all choices of $C$. Thus since $C$ may be taken arbitrarily large, we must have $\langle | \Pi_{s}-\Pi_{t}\left|J_{s}, P_{t}\right\rangle=0$. Similarly $\langle | \Pi_{s}-\Pi_{t}\left|J_{t}, P_{s}\right\rangle=0$. This yields $\Pi_{s} J_{s}=\Pi_{s} J_{t} J_{s}=\Pi_{t} J_{s} J_{t}=\Pi_{t} J_{s}$ as needed for Property (E).

Another argument could be carried out as follows. By Proposition 1 above, the experiments $\mathscr{E}_{s, t}=\left(P_{s}, P_{t}\right)$ and $\mathscr{E}_{s, t}^{\prime}=\left(P_{s}^{\prime}, P_{t}^{\prime}\right)$ are equivalent. Let $\mu= \frac{1}{2}\left(P_{s}+P_{t}\right)$. Then $\left(P_{s}, \mu\right)$ is still equivalent to $\left(P_{s}^{\prime}, \mu^{\prime}\right)$. Here $P_{s}^{\prime} \leq 2 \mu$. This applying Proposition 4, Chapter 4 with $C \geq 2$ and letting $\Pi$ be any conditional operator adapted to $\mu$, one obtains $\langle | \Pi_{s}-\Pi\left|, P_{s}\right\rangle=0$. Similarly $\langle | \Pi_{t}-\Pi\left|, P_{t}\right\rangle=$ 0 . The result follows.

We are now in a position to prove the main theorem of this section, which is as follows.

Theorem 1. Let $\mathscr{E}=\left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ be an experiment with $P_{\theta} \in L$ as described. Let $H$ be a uniform sublattice of $M$ and let $M^{\prime}$ be the closure of $H$ in $M$ for $\sigma(M, L)$. Then all the properties, ( $A$ ) to ( $H$ ), are equivalent.

Proof. According to Lemma 2, Section 2, a projection $\Pi$ which satisfies Property (A) is automatically a conditional operator adapted to all the $P_{\theta}$. Thus $(\mathrm{A}) \Rightarrow(\mathrm{B})$. It is also obvious that $(\mathrm{A}) \Rightarrow(\mathrm{C})$, that $(\mathrm{B}) \Rightarrow(\mathrm{D})$, and that (C) ⇒ (D).
