Note also that Property (D) is an assertion of equivalence of binary experiments for testing problems, and, therefore, by Torgersen's result an assertion of identity of experiment types for binary experiments

Since we have already seen that Properties (F), (G), and (H) are all equivalent to the identity of the experiment types for binary experiments and that they imply Property (E), it will be sufficient to show that (E) implies (A).

For this purpose consider a finite set $S=\left\{s_{j} ; j=1, \ldots, k\right\} \subset \Theta$ and let $\mu=\sum_{s \in S} P_{s}$. Let $J_{\mu}$ be the smallest idempotent in $M^{\prime}$ for which $\langle 1, \mu\rangle= \left\langle J_{\mu}, \mu\right\rangle$. Construct a finite partition $\left\{\gamma_{j}\right\}$ of $J_{\mu}$ taking $\gamma_{1}=J_{s_{1}}$ and $\gamma_{j}= J_{s_{j}}\left[1-\sum_{i<j} \gamma_{i}\right]$.

Define an operator $A_{j}$ by $u A_{j}=u \gamma_{j} \Pi_{s_{i}}$. This is a positive linear projection onto the set $\left\{v \gamma_{j} ; v \in M^{\prime}\right\}$. For any pair ( $i, j$ ) one can write

$$
\begin{aligned}
\gamma_{j} \Pi_{s_{j}} J_{s_{i}} & =\gamma_{j} \Pi_{s_{j}} J_{s_{j}} J_{s_{i}}=\gamma_{j} \Pi_{s_{i}} J_{s_{i}} J_{s_{j}} \\
& =\gamma_{j} \Pi_{s_{i}} J_{s_{i}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Thus, letting $A_{s}=\sum_{j} A_{j}$ one has

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left\langle u A_{s}, P_{i}\right\rangle & =\left\langle u\left(\sum \gamma_{j}\right) \Pi_{s_{i}} J_{s_{i}}, P_{i}\right\rangle \\
& =\left\langle u \Pi_{s_{i}}, P_{i}\right\rangle=\left\langle u, P_{i}\right\rangle
\end{aligned}
$$

Each finite set $S$ provides such an operator $A_{s}$. It is well defined and is a positive linear projection such that $1 A_{s} \leq 1$.

Let $\Pi=\sup _{s} A_{s}$, the supremum being taken over all finite subsets of $\Theta$. This $\Pi$ is also a positive linear projection such that $1 \Pi \leq 1$. It is easily checked that the image $1 \Pi$ of the unit of $M$ is the smallest idempotent $J$ in $M^{\prime}$ such that $\left\langle J, P_{\theta}\right\rangle=\left\|P_{\theta}\right\|$ for all $\theta$.

It remains to "lift" the projection $\Pi$ so that it maps $M$ onto $M^{\prime}$. One may proceed in the same manner as for Lemma 4 as follows. Let $L(\mathscr{E})$ be the band defined by $\mathscr{E}$ in $L$. Let $L^{\perp}$ be the band formed by elements disjoint from $L(\mathscr{E})$. In $L^{\perp}$ one may find a family $\left\{\mu_{\alpha}\right\}, \mu_{\alpha} \geq 0, \mu_{\alpha} \in L^{\perp}$ such that (i) the $\mu_{\alpha}$ are two by two disjoint, and (ii) the band generated by $\left\{\mu_{\alpha}\right\}$ is $L^{\perp}$ itself. Consider then the family $\left\{v_{\alpha}\right\}, v_{\alpha}=(1-J) \cdot \mu_{\alpha}$. Each $v_{\alpha}$ has a minimal conditional operator $\Pi_{\alpha}$. These operators certainly satisfy the relations which would be written $\Pi_{\alpha} J_{\beta}=\Pi_{\beta} J_{\beta}$ since they are disjoint. They are also disjoint of the $\Pi_{\theta}, \theta \in \Theta$. Thus we may "lift" $\Pi$, replacing it by

$$
\Pi_{1}=\Pi+\sup _{\alpha} \Pi_{\alpha}
$$

Now the image $1 \Pi_{1}$ is the unit of $M$ itself. Finally, if $v \in M^{\prime}$, one may decompose it into the pieces $J_{\theta} v$ and $J_{\alpha} v$. It follows readily that $v \Pi=v$, and the proof is complete.

Remark. Note that the assertion is existence of a positive linear projection which maps $M$ onto $M^{\prime}$, not into. Thus if $M^{\prime \prime}$ is another complete sublattice such that $M^{\prime} \subset M^{\prime \prime}$ and if $M^{\prime}$ satisfies Property (A), so does $M^{\prime \prime}$.

Definition 4. Let $H$ be a uniform sublattice of $M$ and let $M^{\prime}$ be the weak closure of $H$ in $M$. If the equivalent Properties (A) to (H) are satisfied by $H$, one will say that the complete lattice $M^{\prime}$ is SUFFICIENT for the experiment $\mathscr{E}$. The lattice $H$ will then be called completion sufficient.

Theorem 1 asserts, for instance, that if the isometry Property (F) holds then there is a positive linear projection $\Pi$ satisfying (A). It does not say that $\Pi$ is $\sigma(M, L)$ continuous. However, this happens to be true and easily verifiable: the transpose of $\Pi$ maps $L^{\prime}$ into the dual $M^{*}$ of $M$. However, $L$ is a band in $M^{*}$ and the images of the elements $P_{\theta}^{\prime}$ (and $v_{\alpha}^{\prime}$ ) belong to $L$. Thus the band $L^{\prime}$ generated by the $P_{\theta}^{\prime}$ and $v_{\alpha}^{\prime}$ is entirely mapped into $L$.

To conclude, let us mention a result which is of some aesthetic interest. Proposition 3. Let $\mathscr{E}=\left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ be an experiment, with its $L$-space $L=$
$L(\mathscr{E})$. Let $M=M(\mathscr{E})$ be the dual of $L$. Then the set of complete sublattices $M^{\prime} \subset M$ which are sufficient for $\mathscr{E}$ possesses a smallest element.

Proof. Let $\mathscr{S}$ be the set of sufficient complete sublattices. Consider a totally ordered subset $\left\{M_{\alpha}^{\prime}\right\} \subset \mathscr{S}$ and let $M^{\prime}=\bigcap_{\alpha} M_{\alpha}^{\prime}$. Let $\Pi_{\alpha}$ be the projection adapted to $M_{\alpha}^{\prime}$. Topologize the set of positive linear projections by the topology of pointwise convergence on $M \times L$.

The tails $\left\{\Pi_{\alpha}: M_{\alpha}^{\prime} \subset M_{\alpha}\right\}$ form the base of a filter which has at least one cluster point, say $\Pi$, for pointwise convergence on $M \times L$. Passing to the limit in the equalities $\left\langle u \Pi_{\alpha}, P_{\theta}\right\rangle=\left\langle u, P_{\theta}\right\rangle$ one sees that $\Pi$ is still a positive linear projection adapted to all the $P_{\theta}$. As such, it is necessarily weakly continuous and maps $M$ onto a closed sublattice of the intersection $M^{\prime}$ (see Lemma 1 ). An application of Zorn's lemma shows then that the set $\mathscr{S}$ must possess minimal elements.

Let $M_{1}^{\prime}$ and $M_{2}^{\prime}$ be two such minimal elements and let $\Pi_{1}$ and $\Pi_{2}$ be the corresponding projections. Consider the operator $T=\Pi_{1} \Pi_{2}$. This is still the transpose of a transition from $L$ to itself since $\left\langle u \Pi_{1} \Pi_{2}, P_{\theta}\right\rangle=\left\langle u \Pi_{1}, P_{\theta}\right\rangle= \left\langle u, P_{\theta}\right\rangle$. Let $\Pi_{0}$ be any cluster point of the sequence $(1 /(n+1)) \sum_{j=0}^{n} T^{j}$. This is a projection such that $T \Pi_{0}=\Pi_{0} T=\Pi_{0}$. One verifies easily that $\Pi_{0}$ is adapted to each $P_{\theta}, \theta \in \Theta$. The range $M \Pi_{0}$ of $\Pi_{0}$ is a closed sublattice of $M \Pi_{2}=M_{2}^{\prime}$. Thus, since $M_{2}^{\prime}$ was minimal $\Pi_{0}=\Pi_{2}$, and consequently $T \Pi_{0}=\Pi_{1} \Pi_{2}=\Pi_{2}$. Similarly $\Pi_{2} \Pi_{1}=\Pi_{1}$. Suppose now that $v \in M_{2}^{\prime}$ does not belong to $M_{1}^{\prime}$. Then $v-v \Pi_{1} \neq 0$. Also, since our projections automatically satisfy Kolmogorov's relation,

$$
\left\langle\left(v \Pi_{1}\right)\left(v-v \Pi_{1}\right), P_{\theta}\right\rangle=0
$$

for all $\theta \in \Theta$. This yields

$$
\left\langle v^{2}, P_{\theta}\right\rangle=\left\langle\left(v \Pi_{1}\right)^{2}, P_{\theta}\right\rangle+\left\langle\left(v-v \Pi_{1}\right)^{2}, P_{\theta}\right\rangle
$$

Thus, there would be at least one $P_{\theta}$ for which $\left.\left\langle v^{2}, P_{\theta}\right\rangle\right\rangle\left\langle\left(v \Pi_{1}\right)^{2}, P_{\theta}\right\rangle$. Let $w=v \Pi_{1}$. Then by the same argument $\left\langle\left(w \Pi_{2}\right)^{2}, P_{\theta}\right\rangle \leq\left\langle w^{2}, P_{\theta}\right\rangle= \left\langle\left(v \Pi_{1}\right)^{2}, P_{\theta}\right\rangle<\left\langle v^{2}, P_{\theta}\right\rangle$. Thus

$$
\left\langle\left(v \Pi_{1} \Pi_{2}\right)^{2}, P_{\theta}\right\rangle<\left\langle v^{2}, P_{\theta}\right\rangle
$$

However, $v \Pi_{1} \Pi_{2}=v \Pi_{2}=v$. Therefore the assumption that $v \in M_{2}^{\prime}$ does not belong to $M_{1}^{\prime}$ leads to a contradiction. The desired assertion follows.

Remark. Proposition 3 can also be proved in a different manner, according to an argument of D.W. Müller. For this purpose, consider the minimal $L$-space $L_{m}(\mathscr{E})$ of $\mathscr{E}$. Let $\mathscr{E}^{\prime \prime}$ be the minimal form of $\mathscr{E}$ corresponding to $L_{m}(\mathscr{E})$. By assumption, if $M^{\prime}$ is sufficient then $\mathscr{E}^{\prime \prime}, \mathscr{E}^{\prime}$, and $\mathscr{E}$ are equivalent. Thus, there are transitions $T_{1}$ and $T_{2}$ according to the following scheme

$$
L\left(\mathscr{E}^{\prime \prime}\right) \stackrel{T_{2}^{\prime}}{\leftarrow} L\left(\mathscr{E}^{\prime}\right) \stackrel{T_{1}^{\prime}}{\leftarrow} L(\mathscr{E}),
$$



with transpose

$$
M\left(\mathscr{E}^{\prime \prime}\right) \xrightarrow{T_{2}} M\left(\mathscr{E}^{\prime}\right)=M^{\prime} \xrightarrow{T_{1}} M(\mathscr{E}) .
$$

Here $T_{1}$ is a quotient for the corresponding $L$-space and an imbedding for the $M$-spaces.

By the argument used in Section 2, Lemma 3, one verifies that $T_{2}$ is a lattice imbedding of $M\left(\mathscr{E}^{\prime \prime}\right)$ into $M\left(\mathscr{E}^{\prime}\right)$. Thus, one can say that the canonical imbedding of $M\left(\mathscr{E}^{\prime \prime}\right)$ into $M$ provides the smallest complete sublattice of $M$ which is sufficient.

## 4. Insufficiency

The preceding sections investigate a situation where restricting an experiment $\mathscr{E}=\left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ to a uniform sublattice $H$ does not change the statistical status of $\mathscr{E}$. For asymptotic arguments, one also needs an approximate version of this sort of relation. Its study is the object of this section.

We shall assume as given, an experiment $\mathscr{E}=\left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ where $P_{\theta}$ is an element of the space $L$. The band $L(\mathscr{E})$ of $\mathscr{E}$ in $L$ is not necessarily assumed to be all of $L$. The dual of $L$ is a space $M$, and $H$ is a uniform sublattice of $M$ with $\sigma(M, L)$ closure $M^{\prime}$ in $M$. If $\Pi$ is a positive linear projection of $M$ onto $M^{\prime}$, its transpose is a positive linear map of $L$ into the dual $M^{*}$ of $M$.

Restricting the elements $P_{\theta}$ of $\mathscr{E}$ to the sublattice $H$, or to $M^{\prime}$, yields another experiment $\mathscr{E}^{\prime}=\left\{P_{\theta}^{\prime} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$. This $\mathscr{E}^{\prime}$ is weaker than $\mathscr{E}$ and the "loss of information" incurred in the restriction could be measured by the deficiency $\delta\left(\mathscr{E}^{\prime}, \mathscr{E}\right)$.

In this section we shall propose another measure of loss of information and call it "insufficiency" since it measures how far $M^{\prime}$ is from being sufficient. As we shall explain below, there are in fact several plausible candidates for the title of insufficiency. We have not been able to ascertain their relations in full detail, but will give inequalities involving them.

Definition 5. Let $H$ be a sublattice of $M$ and let $M^{\prime}$ be the $\sigma(M, L)$ closure of $H$ in $M$. The insufficiency of $H$ or $M^{\prime}$ for the experiment $\mathscr{E}=\left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ is the number

$$
\eta(H, \mathscr{E})=\eta\left(M^{\prime}, \mathscr{E}\right)=\inf _{\pi} \sup _{\theta} \frac{1}{2}\left\|P_{\theta}-\Pi P_{\theta}\right\|
$$

obtained by allowing $\Pi$ to range over the set of all positive linear projections of $M$ onto $M^{\prime}$.

Note that here $\Pi$ is not necessarily $\sigma(M, L)$ continuous, so that even though $P_{\theta} \in L$, the image $\Pi P_{\theta}$ may be in $M^{*}$. One of the technical reasons for the choice of this definition is the following simple lemma.

Lemma 6. Assuming $\left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\} \subset L$, there exists a positive linear projection $\Pi$ of $M$ onto $M^{\prime}$ such that $\eta(H, \mathscr{E})=\sup _{\theta} \frac{1}{2}\left\|P_{\theta}-\Pi P_{\theta}\right\|$. Furthermore, the number $\eta(H, \mathscr{E})$ is the supremum

$$
\eta(H, \mathscr{E})=\sup _{S} \eta\left(H, \mathscr{E}_{S}\right)
$$

where $\mathscr{E}_{S}=\left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in S\right\}$ and $S$ ranges through the family of finite subsets of $\Theta$.
Proof. Let $\mathscr{P}$ denote the space of positive linear projections of $M$ onto $M^{\prime}$ with the topology of pointwise convergence on $M \times L$. Then $\mathscr{P}$ is a compact space and the norms

$$
\left\|P_{\theta}-\Pi P_{\theta}\right\|=\sup _{|u| \leq 1}\left|\left\langle u \Pi, P_{\theta}\right\rangle-\left\langle u, P_{\theta}\right\rangle\right|
$$

are lower semicontinuous on $\mathscr{P}$. This gives the first assertion. Similarly assume that $\Pi_{S}$ achieves the infimum $\eta\left(H, \mathscr{E}_{S}\right)=\sup _{\theta \in S} \frac{1}{2}\left\|P_{\theta}-\Pi_{S} P_{\theta}\right\|$. Let $\Pi$ be any cluster point of the system $\left\{\Pi_{s}\right\}$ as $S$ increases along the direction of finite subsets of $\Theta$. Then

$$
\sup _{\theta \in \Theta} \frac{1}{2}\left\|P_{\theta}-\Pi P_{\theta}\right\| \leq \lim _{S} \eta\left(H, \mathscr{E}_{S}\right) \leq \eta(H, \mathscr{E})
$$

This concludes the proof of the lemma.
As mentioned previously, there are other plausible candidates for the concept of insufficiency. To introduce one of them, note that in Definition 5 the family $\left\{\Pi P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ represents an experiment, with $\Pi P_{\theta} \in M^{*}$, but for which $M^{\prime}$ is still sufficient in a reasonable sense.

More precisely, let $\bar{M}$ be the dual of $M^{*}$. One can identify $M$ with a $\sigma\left(\bar{M}, M^{*}\right)$ dense subset of $\bar{M}$. Similarly $M^{\prime}$ can be identified with a subset of $\bar{M}$. If $\mathscr{F}=\left\{Q_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ is any experiment with $Q_{\theta} \in M^{*}$ it makes sense to inquire whether $M^{\prime}$ is completion sufficient or, equivalently, whether the closure $\bar{M}^{\prime}$ is sufficient.

Thus, let $\mathscr{S}\left(M^{\prime}\right)$ be the class of experiments $\mathscr{F}=\left\{Q_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}, Q_{\theta} \in M^{*}$ for which $M^{\prime}$ is completion sufficient. One can then define another number $\eta_{1}\left(M^{\prime}, \mathscr{E}\right)$ by the formula

$$
\eta_{1}\left(M^{\prime}, \mathscr{E}\right)=\inf _{\mathscr{F}} \sup _{\theta} \frac{1}{2}\left\|P_{\theta}-Q_{\theta}\right\|,
$$

where $\mathscr{F}$ ranges through $\mathscr{S}\left(M^{\prime}\right)$.
Finally, it might be considered more pleasant to restrict the projections involved in Definition 5, allowing only $\sigma(M, L)$ continuous projections. This would give another number:

$$
\eta_{2}\left(M^{\prime}, \mathscr{E}\right)=\inf _{\Pi} \sup _{\theta} \frac{1}{2}\left\|P_{\theta}-\Pi P_{\theta}\right\|,
$$

in which $\Pi$ is restricted to be $\sigma(M, L)$ continuous. It would be nice to know when all three numbers $\eta_{1}, \eta$, and $\eta_{2}$ are the same. Failing this, we shall provide some simple inequalities.

Proposition 4. Let $\mathscr{F}=\left\{Q_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}, Q_{\theta} \in M^{*}$ be an experiment for which $M^{\prime}$ is completion sufficient. Then there exists another experiment $\mathscr{F}_{1}= \left\{Q_{\theta}^{1} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$, with the same sufficiency property such that (i) each $Q_{\theta}^{1}$ belongs to the band $L(\mathscr{E})$ of $\mathscr{E}$, and (ii) $\left\|P_{\theta}-Q_{\theta}^{1}\right\| \leq\left\|P_{\theta}-Q_{\theta}\right\|$ for all $\theta$. Furthermore, there is a $\sigma(M, L)$ continuous positive linear projection $\Pi$ of $M$ onto $M^{\prime}$ such that

$$
\left\|P_{\theta}-\Pi P_{\theta}\right\| \leq 2\left\|P_{\theta}-Q_{\theta}^{1}\right\|
$$

for all $\theta$.
Corollary. One has always the inequalities

$$
\eta_{1}\left(M^{\prime}, \mathscr{E}\right) \leq \eta\left(M^{\prime}, \mathscr{E}\right) \leq \eta_{2}\left(M^{\prime}, \mathscr{E}\right) \leq 2 \eta_{1}\left(M^{\prime}, \mathscr{E}\right)
$$

Proof. Since $L(\mathscr{E})$ is a band in $M^{*}$ there is a smallest idempotent; say $u$, of $\bar{M}$ such that $\langle u, \lambda\rangle=\|\lambda\|$ for every $\lambda \geq 0, \lambda \in L(\mathscr{E})$. One may write each $Q_{\theta}$ as a $\operatorname{sum} Q_{\theta}=u \cdot Q_{\theta}+(1-u) \cdot Q_{\theta}$ of two disjoint parts. If $\left\|P_{\theta}-Q_{\theta}\right\| \equiv 2$, that is, $u \cdot Q_{\theta} \equiv 0$, there is nothing to prove. Otherwise, take an arbitrary $\xi \in \Theta$ for which $\left\|u \cdot Q_{\xi}\right\|=1 / \alpha>0$. Define elements $Q_{\theta}^{1}$ of $L(\mathscr{E})$ by the formula

$$
Q_{\theta}^{1}=u \cdot Q_{\theta}+\alpha\left\langle(1-u), Q_{\theta}\right\rangle\left[u \cdot Q_{\xi}\right] .
$$

Clearly, $\left\|P_{\theta}-Q_{\theta}^{1}\right\| \leq\left\|P_{\theta}-Q_{\theta}\right\|$.
To show that $M^{\prime}$ is still sufficient for $\mathscr{F}_{1}=\left\{Q_{\theta}^{1} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$, take two numbers $a$ and $b$ and two elements $(s, t)$ of $\Theta$. Let $\lambda$ be the sum

$$
\lambda=a Q_{s}^{1}+b Q_{t}^{1}=u \cdot \mu
$$

with

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mu & =a Q_{s}+b Q_{t}+c Q_{\xi} \\
c & =\alpha a\left\langle 1-u, Q_{s}\right\rangle+\alpha b\left\langle 1-u, Q_{t}\right\rangle
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $\bar{M}^{\prime}$ is sufficient for $\mathscr{F}$, the alternate version (CI) of Property (C) of Section 3 yields the existence of a $v \in \bar{M}^{\prime}$ such that $0 \leq v \leq 1$ and $v \cdot \mu=\mu^{+}$. However, $v \cdot \mu$ is the sum of the two disjoint parts $v \cdot \mu=(v u) \cdot \mu+[v(1-u)] \cdot \mu$, from which it follows that $(v u) \cdot \mu=v \lambda=\lambda^{+}$. Thus $\mathscr{F}_{1}=\left\{Q_{\theta}^{1}\right\}$ satisfies Property (H) of Section 3 and $M^{\prime}$ itself is therefore sufficient for $\mathscr{F}_{1}$.

Thus, nothing is gained by allowing $Q_{\theta}$ to belong to $M^{*}$ instead of $L$. To alleviate notation for the remainder of the proof, we shall assume that $\mathscr{F}$ has already been replaced by $\mathscr{F}_{1}$ and write $\mathscr{F}=\mathscr{F}_{1}=\left\{Q_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ with $Q_{\theta} \in L$.

Since $M^{\prime}$ is sufficient for $\mathscr{F}$, there is a positive linear projection $\Pi_{1}$ of $M$ onto $M^{\prime}$ such that, if $Q_{\theta}^{\prime}$ is the restriction of $Q_{\theta}$, to $M^{\prime}$ one has $Q_{\theta}=\Pi_{1} Q_{\theta}^{\prime}$ for all $\theta$.

There is also a smallest idempotent $u_{1}$ of $M^{\prime}$ such that $\left\langle u_{1}, Q_{\theta}^{\prime}\right\rangle=1$ for all $Q_{\theta}^{\prime}$. Let $u_{2}=1-u_{1}$ and consider the subsets $M_{i}=\left\{u_{i} v ; v \in M\right\}$ and $M_{i}^{\prime}=\left\{u_{i} v ; v \in M^{\prime}\right\}$. One may replace the projection $\Pi_{1}$ by the projection $u_{1} \Pi_{1}$. Also, using Lemma 4 (Section 2) one can find a $\sigma(M, L)$ continuous
positive linear projection $\Pi_{2}$ of $M_{2}$ onto $M_{2}^{\prime}$. Let then $\Pi=u_{1} \Pi_{1}+u_{2} \Pi_{2}$. This projects $M$ onto $M^{\prime}$. Also it is $\sigma(M, L)$ continuous since $\Pi_{1}$ automatically sends $L^{\prime}(\mathscr{F})$ into $L(\mathscr{F})$.

For $\Pi$ one may write

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left\langle v-v \Pi, P_{\theta}\right\rangle & =\left\langle v-v \Pi, Q_{\theta}\right\rangle+\left\langle v(I-\Pi),\left(P_{\theta}-Q_{\theta}\right)\right\rangle \\
& =\left\langle v(I-\Pi),\left(P_{\theta}-Q_{\theta}\right)\right\rangle
\end{aligned}
$$

Thus, if $|v| \leq 1$ we have

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left|\left\langle v-v \Pi, P_{\theta}\right\rangle\right| & \leq\left\|P_{\theta}-Q_{\theta}\right\|+\left\|P_{\theta}^{\prime}-Q_{\theta}^{\prime}\right\| \\
& \leq 2\left\|P_{\theta}-Q_{\theta}\right\|
\end{aligned}
$$

This concludes the proof of the proposition.
Remark 1. The number $\eta\left(M^{\prime}, \mathscr{E}\right)$ can also be described as the infimum

$$
\eta\left(M^{\prime}, \mathscr{E}\right)=\inf _{\mathscr{F}} \sup _{\theta} \frac{1}{2}\left\|P_{\theta}-Q_{\theta}\right\|
$$

taken over all experiments $\mathscr{F}=\left\{Q_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}, Q_{\theta} \in M^{*}$ such that
(i) $\bar{M}^{\prime}$ is sufficient for $\mathscr{F}$;
(ii) the restriction $Q_{\theta}^{\prime}$ of $Q_{\theta}$ to $M^{\prime}$ coincides with the restriction $P_{\theta}^{\prime}$ of $P_{\theta}$.

The number $\eta_{2}\left(M^{\prime}, \mathscr{E}\right)$ is obtained in exactly the same manner, but for experiments $\mathscr{F}=\left\{Q_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ such that $Q_{\theta} \in L$. This suggests that $\eta$ and $\eta_{2}$ are perhaps just the same thing. However, we do not have a proof that this is indeed the case.

Remark 2. The number $\eta$ may be described in still another fashion. Let $\Pi_{\theta}$ be a conditional expectation operator adapted to $P_{\theta}$ and $M^{\prime}$, for instance, the minimal such operator.

For each positive linear projection $\Pi$ of $M$ onto $M^{\prime}$ one may obtain the modular bound $\left|\Pi_{\theta}-\Pi\right|$. Then

$$
\eta\left(M^{\prime}, \mathscr{E}\right)=\inf _{\Pi} \sup _{\theta}\langle | \Pi_{\theta}-\Pi\left|, P_{\theta}\right\rangle .
$$

This could be interpreted as follows. Suppose that the $\Pi_{\theta}$ are to be estimated using estimates obtainable on $\mathscr{E}^{\prime}=\left\{P_{\theta}^{\prime} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$. Use the loss function $\left|\Pi_{\theta}-\Pi\right|$. (This is not a loss function in the sense of Chapter 2 since it depends on the observed values. However it is certainly a reasonable loss function otherwise.) Then $\eta\left(M^{\prime}, \mathscr{E}\right)$ appears as the minimum risk for this estimation problem.

More generally and except perhaps for the occurrence of factors equal to 2 or $\frac{1}{2}$, the number $\eta$ represents what happens to deficiency if one allows loss functions which are $M^{\prime}$-measurable. However, the estimation of $\Pi_{\theta}$ described above will allow us to get various bounds on insufficiencies, in particular for cases in which $M$ is obtained by observing two independent variables, say $X$ and $Y$, and $M^{\prime}$ is obtained by observing $X$ only. In such a case the projection $\Pi_{\theta}$ is the operation of taking expectations over the $Y$ variables, and the problem amounts to estimation of $\mathscr{L}(Y)$ from the observation of $X$. Some specifics will be given further on.

Remark 3. In the present setup we have two numbers, $\delta\left(\mathscr{E}^{\prime}, \mathscr{E}^{\circ}\right)$ and $\eta\left(M^{\prime}, \mathscr{E}\right)$, which are both intended to measure "loss of information." The definitions are very much alike since in both cases one takes an infimum of the type

$$
\inf _{T} \sup _{\theta} \frac{1}{2}\left\|P_{\theta}-T P_{\theta}^{\prime}\right\|
$$

However, for deficiency, $T$ can be any transition, while for insufficiency $\dot{T}$ is restricted to be a transition which is a projection.

Thus, one has always $\delta\left(\mathscr{E}^{\prime}, \mathscr{E}\right) \leq \eta\left(M^{\prime}, \mathscr{E}\right)$. The description of insufficiency as a risk in an estimation problem for conditional distribution operators shows that, in that problem insufficiency behaves like the minimum risk. On the contrary, deficiency measures the norm of the bias. Thus one could guess that the insufficiency may be large even when the deficiency is small.

It will be shown now that (i) under suitable assumptions insufficiency is bounded by functions of the deficiencies, and (ii) if all supplementary assumptions are removed, no such majoration is possible.

For the first assertion, consider an experiment $\mathscr{E}=\left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}, P_{\theta} \in L$, as usual with a complete sublattice $M^{\prime} \subset M$. Let $\mu$ be a particular element of $L$ having the form

$$
\mu=\sum_{\theta} a_{\theta} P_{\theta}, a_{\theta} \geq 0, \sum a_{\theta}=1
$$

For each $\theta \in \Theta$ let $\mathscr{E}_{\theta}$ be the pair $\mathscr{E}_{\theta}=\left(\mu, P_{\theta}\right)$ and let $\mathscr{E}_{\theta}^{\prime}=\left(\mu^{\prime}, P_{\theta}^{\prime}\right)$ be formed by the restrictions to $M^{\prime}$.

Proposition 5. Let $C$ be a numer $C>0$. Then $\eta\left(M^{\prime}, \mathscr{E}\right) \leq \beta+(1+C)(2 \delta)^{1 / 2}$ with (i) $\beta=\sup _{\theta}\left\|P_{\theta}^{\prime}-P_{\theta}^{\prime} \wedge C \mu^{\prime}\right\|$, and (ii) $\delta=\sup _{\theta} \delta\left(\mathscr{E}_{\theta}^{\prime}, \mathscr{E}_{\theta}\right) \leq \delta\left(\mathscr{E}^{\prime}, \mathscr{E}\right)$.

Proof. Let $\Pi$ be any conditional expectation operator adapted to $\mu$ and let $\Pi_{t}$ be similarly adapted to $P_{t}$. Then according to Chapter 4, Proposition 4, and writing $\beta_{t}=\left\|P_{t}^{\prime}-P_{t}^{\prime} \wedge C \mu\right\|$, we have

$$
\langle | \Pi_{t}-\Pi\left|, P_{t}^{\prime}\right\rangle \leq \beta_{t}+(1+C)\left\{k^{2}\left(\mu, P_{t}\right)-k^{2}\left(\mu^{\prime}, P_{t}^{\prime}\right)\right\}^{1 / 2}
$$

Now, the modular bound $\left\|\Pi_{t}-\Pi\right\|$ satisfies the relation

$$
\langle | \Pi_{t}-\Pi\left|, P_{t}^{\prime}\right\rangle=\frac{1}{2}\left\|\Pi_{t} P_{t}^{\prime}-\Pi P_{t}^{\prime}\right\|=\frac{1}{2}\left\|P_{t}-\Pi P_{t}^{\prime}\right\|
$$

We have also seen that

$$
k^{2}\left(\mu, P_{t}\right)-k^{2}\left(\mu^{\prime}, P_{t}^{\prime}\right) \leq 2 \delta\left(\mathscr{E}_{\theta}^{\prime}, \mathscr{E}_{\theta}\right)
$$

Thus the entire statement will be proved if we show that $\delta\left(\mathscr{E}_{\theta}^{\prime}, \mathscr{E}_{\theta}\right) \leq \delta\left(\mathscr{E}^{\prime}, \mathscr{E}^{\circ}\right)$. However, an inequality $\left\|P_{\theta}-T P_{\theta}^{\prime}\right\| \leq \alpha$ for a transition $T$ obviously implies $\left\|\left(\sum a_{\theta} P_{\theta}\right)-T\left(\sum a_{\theta} P_{\theta}^{\prime}\right)\right\| \leq \alpha \sum\left|a_{\theta}\right|$. Hence the result.

Corollary. If $\Theta$ is finite, of cardinality $k$, one has

$$
\eta\left(M^{\prime}, \mathscr{E}\right) \leq(1+k) \sqrt{2 \delta\left(\mathscr{E}^{\prime}, \mathscr{E}\right)} .
$$

Note. The inequality of Proposition 5 involves the deficiencies $\delta$ in the form of a square root. There are reasons to believe that this is the best possible power of $\delta$, but we have no proof. Whether one can remove the boundedness implied by the use of $P_{t}^{\prime} \wedge C \mu^{\prime}$ appears doubtful. Of course, if $\Theta$ can be partitioned in pieces $\Theta_{i}$ such that for $i \neq j$ every $P_{s}^{\prime}, s \in \Theta_{i}$ is disjoint from every $P_{t}^{\prime}, t \in \Theta_{j}$, the pieces can be treated separately from each other. However, this is an improvement of minor importance.

To give examples which show that $\eta\left(M^{\prime}, \mathscr{E}\right)$ may remain large although $\delta\left(\mathscr{E}^{\prime}, \mathscr{E}\right)$ becomes arbitrarily small, we shall have recourse to results which will be proved only in later chapters. Thus, even though two examples will be listed here, the required computations and arguments will be given only later on.

Example 1. A first example can be obtained from ordinary Gaussian shift experiments. For the purpose, take for $\Theta$ an $r$-dimensional Euclidean space. Let $p_{\theta}$ be the Gaussian $\mathscr{N}(\theta, I)$ measure on a copy $\mathscr{X}$ of $\Theta$. Let $\mathscr{E}=\mathscr{E}^{n+1}$ be the experiment obtained by taking $n+1$ independent identically distributed observations from $p_{\theta}$. Let $\mathscr{E}^{\prime}=\mathscr{E}^{n}$ be the subexperiment in which only the first $n$ observations are taken into account. Then Torgersen has shown that $\delta\left(\mathscr{E}^{\prime}, \mathscr{E}\right)=\frac{1}{2}\left\|G_{n}-G_{n+1}\right\|$, where $G_{n}$ is the $\mathscr{N}\left(0, n^{-1} I\right)$ measure in $\mathscr{X}$. One can also show that the insufficiency $\eta$ satisfies the inequality $\eta \leq w \leq 2 \eta$, where $w$ has the form

$$
w=\operatorname{Prob}\left\{|t| \leq \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{r}{n}\right)^{1 / 2}\right\}
$$

for a $t$-statistic with $r$ degrees of freedom. It follows that for $n$ large $\delta\left(\mathscr{E}^{\prime}, \mathscr{E}\right)$ is roughly proportional to $\sqrt{r} / n$ but the insufficiency $\eta$ is roughly proportional to $\left(\frac{r}{n}\right)^{1 / 2}$. The actual evaluation depends on invariance considerations which will be expounded in Chapter 8.

Another example can be constructed using Poisson experiments which will be described more fully in Chapter 9. One example is as follows.

Example 2. Let $\mathscr{X}$ be the interval $[0,1]$ with the Lebesgue measure $\lambda$. Each point $x \in \mathscr{X}$ has a binary expansion $x=\sum_{j \geq 1} \xi_{j}(x) 2^{-j}$. Take for parameter space the set of integers $\Theta=\{1,2, \ldots\}$. For an integer $\theta$ let $p_{\theta}$ be the measure which has density $2 \xi_{\theta}$ with respect to $\lambda$. For an integer $n$ let $\mathscr{E}^{\prime}$ be the experiment obtainable by first selecting according to a Poisson distribution, with expectation $n$, an integer $v$, and then taking $v$ independent observations from a measure $p_{\theta}$. Let $\mathscr{E}$ be the experiment obtainable by carrying out $\mathscr{E}^{\prime}$ and then taking again $v_{1}$ independent observations, the variable $v_{1}$ having a Poisson distribution with $E v_{1}=1$.

Then, one may show that $\delta\left(\mathscr{E}^{\prime}, \mathscr{E}\right) \leq(1 / 2 \sqrt{n})$ but $\eta \geq \frac{1}{2}$.

## 5. Estimating Conditional Distributions

In this section we shall use again the structure described in the previous sections, with an experiment $\mathscr{E}=\left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}, P_{\theta} \in L$ and with a lattice $M^{\prime}$ which is a
complete sublattice of the dual $M$ of $L$. The experiment $\mathscr{E}^{\prime}$ is formed by the restrictions $P_{\theta}^{\prime}$ of the $P_{\theta}$ to $M^{\prime}$.

The problem to be considered can be described roughly as follows. Suppose that "locally" the lattice $M^{\prime}$ has small insufficiency. Suppose also that the experiment $\mathscr{E}^{\prime}$ already provides very good estimates of $\theta$. Then one might wish to infer that "globally" the insufficiency of $M^{\prime}$ is small.

Two theorems to this general effect will be given below. Unfortunately we do not know what the situation is in general and have been able to prove results only under additional restrictions. The rough description given above can be made more precise as follows.

Let us assume that the parameter set $\Theta$ is metrized by a distance noted $d$ and that we are provided with two real numbers, $a$ and $b$, such that $0 \leq 2 a \leq b$. The main assumptions relating to possibility of localization would then be as follows.
(I) There are estimates $\hat{\theta}$, available on $\mathscr{E}^{\prime}$, such that $P_{\theta}^{\prime}\{d(\hat{\theta}, \theta)>a\} \leq \varepsilon_{1}$.
(II) If $S \subset \Theta$ is a subset of diameter inferior to $b$ then the insufficiency of $M^{\prime}$ for the experiment $\mathscr{E}_{S}=\left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in S\right\}$ does not exceed $\varepsilon_{2}$.
According to (I), one could truncate the $P_{\theta}^{\prime}$ to the set $\{d(\hat{\theta}, \theta) \leq a\}$ and reduce the problem to a situation where $d(s, t)>a$ implies that $P_{s}^{\prime}$ and $P_{t}^{\prime}$ are in fact disjoint. Then it looks very plausible that $\eta\left(M^{\prime}, \mathscr{E}\right)$ would be small since, having carried out $\mathscr{E}^{\prime}$, one can eliminate from consideration all the points $\theta$, except those which are in a certain set of diameter $2 a$. It is tempting to conclude that if good decision procedures are available for sets of diameter $b \geq 4 a$, then good decision procedures can be constructed for the whole of $\Theta$.

In fact, the situation is more complex. One can easily construct an example where $\Theta$ consists of successive integers $1,2, \ldots, 6$ and where the following properties hold.
(i) If $s$ and $t$ are not consecutive integers then $P_{s}^{\prime}$ and $P_{t}^{\prime}$ are disjoint.
(ii) For any subset $S \subset \Theta$ consisting of at most 5 points, the insufficiency is not more than a given $\varepsilon \in\left(0, \frac{1}{2}\right)$.
(iii) For the entire $\Theta$ the insufficiency is exactly $2 \varepsilon$.

One can also complicate this example to show that the construction described in Proposition 6 below cannot work, irrespective of the dimension of $\Theta$ unless the local projections are adequately selected. However, at the time of this writing we do not have a complete understanding of the possibilities. One first result uses smoothness assumptions as follows.

Proposition 6. Let $\Pi_{\theta}$ be a conditional expectation operator adapted to $P_{\theta}$ and $M^{\prime}$. Assume that there is a symmetric numerical function $W \geq 0$ defined on $\Theta \times \Theta$ and an element $f$ of $M^{\prime}$ such that for each pair $(s, t) \in \Theta \times \Theta$ the modular bounds satisfy the relation

$$
\left|\Pi_{s}-\Pi_{t}\right| \leq W(s, t) f
$$

Let $\beta_{1}$ be $\beta_{1}=\sup _{\theta}\left\langle f^{2}, P_{\theta}^{\prime}\right\rangle$ and let $\beta_{2}$ be the minimax risk for the experiment $\mathscr{E}^{\prime}$ and the problem of estimating $\theta$ with a loss function $W^{2}$. Then $\eta\left(M^{\prime}, \mathscr{E}\right) \leq \sqrt{\beta_{1} \beta_{2}}$.

Proof. According to general principles one may assume that $\Theta$ is a finite set. Then, by Theorem 1, Chapter 1, any estimate $\hat{\theta}$ based on $\mathscr{E}^{\prime}$ can be approximated by finite sums

$$
\rho=\sum_{t, j} \delta_{t} \alpha_{t, j} u_{j}
$$

where $u_{j} \geq 0, \sum_{j} u_{j}=1, \alpha_{t, j} \geq 0, \sum_{t} \alpha_{t, j}=1$, and where the $u_{j}$ are indicators in $M^{\prime}$ and the $\delta_{t}$ are Dirac masses at $t$.

Define an operator $A_{j}$ by the sum

$$
A_{j}=u_{j} \sum_{t} \alpha_{t, j} \Pi_{t}
$$

This is a projection of $M$ onto the set $\left\{u_{j} v ; v \in M^{\prime}\right\} \subset M^{\prime}$. Clearly

$$
u_{j}\left|\Pi_{j}-\Pi_{\theta}\right| \leq u_{j} \sum_{t} \alpha_{t, j}\left|\Pi_{t}-\Pi_{\theta}\right| \leq u_{j} f \sum_{t} \alpha_{t, j} W(t, \theta) .
$$

Thus, if $\Pi$ is the projection $\Pi=\sum_{j} A_{j}$ one can write

$$
\left|\Pi-\Pi_{\theta}\right| \leq f \sum_{t, j} u_{j} \alpha_{t, j} W(t, \theta)
$$

Let $g=\sum_{j} \sum_{t} u_{j} \alpha_{t, j} W(t, \theta)$.
Then, by Schwarz' inequality

$$
\left.\left|\langle | \Pi-\Pi_{\theta}\right|, P_{\theta}^{\prime}\right\rangle\left.\right|^{2} \leq\left\langle f^{2}, P_{\theta}^{\prime}\right\rangle\left\langle g^{2}, P_{\theta}^{\prime}\right\rangle .
$$

Now

$$
g^{2}=\sum_{j} u_{j}\left|\sum_{t} \alpha_{t, j} W(t, \theta)\right|^{2} \leq \sum_{j} u_{j} \sum_{t} \alpha_{t, j} W^{2}(t, \theta) .
$$

Thus $\left\langle g^{2}, P_{\theta}^{\prime}\right\rangle$ is at most equal to the risk $W_{\theta}^{2} \rho P_{\theta}$ of the procedure $\rho$. Since $\inf _{f} \sup _{\theta} W_{\theta}^{2} \rho P_{\theta}=\beta_{2}$, the desired result follows.

As an indication of circumstances where Proposition 6 may be applicable consider the following situation.

One starts with an experiment $\mathscr{E}^{\prime}$ given by measures $P_{\theta}^{\prime}$ on a space ( $\Omega, \mathscr{A}^{\prime}$ ). There is also an integer valued $\mathscr{A}^{\prime}$-measurable function $v$ defined on $\Omega$ and a certain space ( $\mathscr{Y}, \mathscr{B}$ ) with probability measures $q_{\theta}$ on $\mathscr{B}$.

The experiment $\mathscr{E}$ consists of the following. First one carries out $\mathscr{E}^{\prime}$ getting a point $\omega \in \Omega$ and a value $v(\omega)=m$. Then one takes $m$ independent observations in $\mathscr{Y}$ according to the measure $q_{\theta}$.

Here the space underlying $\mathscr{E}$ is a direct sum $\bigcup_{m} A_{m} \times \mathscr{Y}^{m}$ where $A_{m}$ is the set $A_{m}=\{\omega ; v(\omega)=m\}$. On $A_{m}$ the conditional expectation operators are simply the product measures $q_{\theta}^{m}$ on $\mathscr{Y}^{m}$. Thus on $A_{m}$ one has $\left|\Pi_{t}-\Pi_{s}\right|_{1}= \frac{1}{2}\left\|q_{t}^{m}-q_{s}^{m}\right\|$. The standard inequalities involving Hellinger distances show that

$$
\frac{1}{2}\left\|q_{t}^{m}-q_{s}^{m}\right\| \leq \sqrt{2 m} H\left(q_{t}, q_{s}\right)
$$

The above argument yields the inequality

$$
\eta\left(M^{\prime}, \mathscr{E}\right) \leq \sqrt{2 \beta v_{0}}
$$

with $v_{0}=\sup _{\theta} E_{\theta} v$ and with $\beta$ equal to the minimax risk

$$
\beta=\inf _{\hat{\theta}} \sup _{\theta} E_{\theta} H^{2}(\hat{\theta}, \theta)
$$

for the experiment $\mathscr{E}^{\prime}$. We shall use this formulation later to compare experiments with their Poissonized versions.

To formulate another result which yields global properties we need a suitable definition of dimension. An appropriate one can be introduced using a slight variation on Kolmogorov's definition of entropy. This will be used later. For the present we shall use a temporary definition, as follows.

Definition. Let $\Theta$ be metrized by the distance $d$. Let $a$ and $b$ be two nonnegative numbers. A partition $\left\{B_{j} ; j \in J\right\}$ of $\Theta$ will be called a partition of type $(C, a, b)$ if
(i) the diameter diam $B_{j}$ of each set $B_{j}$ of the partition is at most $b$;
(ii) each ball of radius $a$ intersects at most $C$ elements of the partition $\left\{B_{j} ; j \in J\right\}$.

The existence of partitions of type ( $C, a, b$ ) is obviously related to some sort of dimensionality concept. Note, however, that here $a$ and $b$ are fixed and not arbitrarily small as is usual for metric definitions of dimension.

Proposition 7. Let $\mathscr{E}=\left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ be indexed by the metric space ( $\Theta, d$ ). Let $M^{\prime}$ be a complete sublattice of $M$. Assume that the following conditions are satisfied for finite subsets $S$ of $\Theta$ and the given pair $(a, b)$.
(i) Each finite $S \subset \Theta$ can be covered by some partition of type ( $C, a, b$ ).
(ii) There is a nonrandomized estimate $\hat{\theta}$ available on $\mathscr{E}_{S}^{\prime}=\left\{P_{\theta}^{\prime} ; \theta \in S\right\}$ such that

$$
\sup _{\theta \in S} P_{\theta}^{\prime}\{d(\hat{\theta}, \theta)>a\} \leq \varepsilon_{1}
$$

(iii) If $S$ is a finite set of diameter at most $b+2 a$, then $\eta\left(M^{\prime}, \mathscr{E}_{S}\right) \leq \varepsilon_{2}$. Then the insufficiency $\eta\left(M^{\prime}, \mathscr{E}\right)$ satisfies the inequality

$$
\eta\left(M^{\prime}, \mathscr{E}\right) \leq \varepsilon_{1}+\varepsilon_{2} C
$$

Proof. According to Lemma 6 one may assume without loss of generality that $\Theta$ is a finite set. This will be done below. Then $\Theta$ admits some partition $\left\{B_{j} ; j \in J\right\}$ of type ( $C, a, b$ ). For each $B_{j}$ let $\widehat{B}_{j}$ be the set of points $\theta$ such that $\operatorname{dist}\left(\theta, B_{j}\right) \leq a$.

Then the diameter of $\widehat{B}_{j}$ is at most $b+2 a$. Thus, by assumption, there is a projection $A_{j}$ of $M$ onto $M^{\prime}$ such that $\left\|A_{j} P_{\theta}^{\prime}-P_{\theta}\right\| \leq 2 \varepsilon_{2}$ for all $\theta \in \widehat{B}_{j}$.

Let $u_{j}$ be the indicator of the event $\hat{\theta} \in B_{j}$. These $u_{j}$ form a partition of unity. Define a map $\Pi$ by the relation

$$
v \Pi=\sum_{j}\left(v u_{j}\right) A_{j}
$$

Note that each $A_{j}$ automatically satisfies the relation $(v u) A_{j}=\left(v A_{j}\right) u$ for $v \in M$ and $u \in M^{\prime}$. Thus $v \Pi$ can also be written $\sum_{j} u_{j}\left(v A_{j}\right)$. From this one can readily check that $\Pi$ is a projection of $M$ onto $M^{\prime}$.

Now consider a particular point $\theta \in \Theta$ and let $V$ be the ball $V=\{t$; $d(t, \theta) \leq a\}$. Let $K$ be the set of indices $j$ such that $V \cap B_{j}$ is not empty. By assumption, if $w=\left\{\sum u_{j} ; j \in J \backslash K\right\}$ then $\left\langle w, P_{\theta}^{\prime}\right\rangle \leq \varepsilon_{1}$. This gives

$$
\left\|\Pi P_{\theta}^{\prime}-P_{\theta}\right\| \leq 2 \varepsilon_{1}+\sum_{j \in K}\left\|\mu_{j}\right\|,
$$

where $\mu_{j}$ is the element which has density $u_{j}$ with respect to $\Pi P_{\theta}^{\prime}-P_{\theta}$. However, $\mu_{j}$ is also $A_{j} P_{\theta}^{\prime}-P_{\theta}$ restricted to $u_{j}$, so that since $j \in K$ implies $\theta \in \widehat{B}_{j}$ we have $\left\|\mu_{j}\right\| \leq 2 \varepsilon_{2}$. It follows that

$$
\frac{1}{2}\left\|P_{\theta}-\Pi P_{\theta}^{\prime}\right\| \leq \varepsilon_{1}+C \varepsilon_{2},
$$

where $C$ is the bound on the cardinality of $K$. This concludes the proof.
Remark 1. A limit theorem using construction similar to the above will be given in Chapter 11. The modifications made in the proof will allow for lack of uniformity of the convergence.

Remark 2. The construction used in the proof of Proposition 7 is very brutal. It is possible to construct examples showing that it cannot yield a better inequality than the one stated here. However we do not know whether it is possible to improve the result by suitable selection of the local operators $A_{j}$. Improvement would be useful, especially for limit theorems in nonparametric situations.

In the above propositions we have worked with insufficiencies. One might wonder whether the general intuitive feeling expressed at the beginning of this section can also admit some formalization in terms of deficiencies, instead of insufficiencies.

Unfortunately, for deficiencies the situation seems even more complicated. However we can still obtain a result related to the statement of Proposition 7. For this purpose we need the following lemma.

Lemma 7. Let $\Theta$ be a finite set metrized by the distance $d$. Let $b$ denote a given positive number. Let $\left\{B_{j} ; j \in J\right\}$ be a partition of $\Theta$ which is of type $(C, b, b)$. Then there is a continuous partition of unity $\left\{u_{j} ; j \in J\right\}, u_{j} \geq 0, \sum_{j} u_{j}=1$ with the following properties.
(i) The $u_{j}$ satisfy the Lipschitz condition

$$
\sum_{j}\left|u_{j}(s)-u_{j}(t)\right| \leq \frac{8 C}{b} d(s, t)
$$



(ii) For each ball $B$ of radius $b$ the number of indices such that $\sup _{s}\left\{u_{j}(s) ; s \in B\right\}>$ 0 is at most $C$.
(iii) The support of each $u_{j}$ has a diameter at most $2 b$.

Remark. A lemma of this nature appears in a paper published by the present author but without the coefficient $C$ in property (i). This is obviously totally incorrect, even if the summation sign is also omitted.

Proof. For each $j$ let $V_{j}$ be the set of points whose distance to $B_{j}$ is at most $b / 2$. Then diam $V_{j} \leq 2 b$. If $S$ is a subset of $\Theta$ write $d(t, S)=\inf _{s}\{d(t, s) ; s \in S\}$ and let $v_{j}$ be the function

$$
v_{j}(s)=\frac{d\left(t, V_{j}^{c}\right)}{d\left(t, V_{j}^{c}\right)+d\left(t, B_{j}\right)}
$$

This is unity on $B_{j}$ and zero on $V_{j}^{c}$. Let $w$ be the sum $w=\sum v_{j}$ and let $u_{j}=v_{j} / w$.
Then, for any two points $s$ and $t$ of $\Theta$ one can write

$$
\begin{aligned}
u_{j}(s)-u_{j}(t) & =\frac{1}{W^{2}}\left[v_{j}(s) \sum_{k} v_{k}(t)-v_{j}(t) \sum_{k} v_{k}(s)\right] \\
& =\frac{1}{W^{2}} \sum_{k}\left[v_{j}(s) v_{k}(t)-v_{j}(t) v_{k}(s)\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

Now

$$
v_{j}(s) v_{k}(t)-v_{j}(t) v_{k}(s)=v_{j}(s)\left[v_{k}(t)-v_{k}(s)\right]-\left[v_{j}(s)-v_{j}(t)\right] v_{k}(s)
$$

Thus, taking absolute values and summing over both $j$ and $k$ one obtains

$$
\sum\left|u_{j}(s)-u_{j}(t)\right| \leq \frac{2}{W} \sum_{k}\left|v_{k}(t)-v_{k}(s)\right|
$$

In this expression the denominator $W$ may be taken equal to $w(t)=\sum_{j} v_{j}(t) \geq$ 1. The sum on the right hand side can be bounded as follows.

First, note that for any given pair ( $s, t$ ) the number of indices $k$ for which $v_{k}(t)+v_{k}(s)>0$ is at most $2 C$. Second, note that each $v_{k}$ satisfies the Lipschitz condition $\left|v_{k}(t)-v_{k}(s)\right| \leq(2 / b) d(s, t)$.

Taking sums we obtain

$$
\sum_{j}\left|u_{j}(s)-u_{j}(t)\right| \leq \frac{8 C}{b} d(t, s)
$$

as claimed. This leads to the following assertion.
Proposition 8. Let $\mathscr{E}=\left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ and $\mathscr{F}=\left\{Q_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ be two experiments indexed by the same set $\Theta$. Assume that $\Theta$ is metrized by a distance $d$ and that all finite subsets $S$ of $\Theta$ enjoy the following properties.
(i) Finite sets $S$ admit partitions of type ( $C, b, b$ ).
(ii) There is an $a \leq b / 2$ and there are estimates $\hat{\theta}$ available on $\mathscr{E}_{S}=\left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in S\right\}$ such that

$$
\sup _{\theta \in S} P_{\theta}\{d(\hat{\theta}, \theta)>a\}<\varepsilon_{1}
$$

(iii) If $S$ is a finite set of diameter at most $4 b$, then $\delta\left(\mathscr{E}_{S}, \mathscr{F}_{S}\right) \leq \varepsilon_{2}$. Then

$$
\delta(\mathscr{E}, \mathscr{F}) \leq \varepsilon_{1}+\varepsilon_{2}+8 C \frac{a}{b}
$$

Proof. As usual, it is enough to prove the result under the supplementary assumption that $\Theta$ itself is finite. Furthermore one can readily expand the experiment $\mathscr{E}$ to replace $\hat{\theta}$ by a nonrandomized estimate. Thus we shall assume $\Theta$ finite and $\hat{\theta}$ nonrandomized below.

Let $\left\{B_{j} ; j \in J\right\}$ be some partition of type $(C, b, b)$. Let $\left\{u_{j} ; j \in J\right\}$ be the partition of unity provided by Lemma 7 . For each $j \in J$ select a transition $T_{j}$ such that

$$
\frac{1}{2}\left\|Q_{\theta}-T_{j} P_{\theta}\right\| \leq \varepsilon_{2}
$$

for all $\theta$ which are a distance at most $3 b$ from $B_{j}$. Let $f_{j}$ be the element of $M(\mathscr{E})$ defined by $f_{j}=u_{j}(\hat{\theta})$. These form a partition of unity in $M(\mathscr{E})$. If $\lambda \in L(\mathscr{E})$, let $f_{j} \cdot \lambda$ be the element of $L(\mathscr{E})$ which has density $f_{j}$ with respect to $\lambda$. Define a transformation $T$ by the relation $T \lambda=\sum_{j} T_{j}\left(f_{j} \cdot \lambda\right)$. The operation so defined is a transition from $L(\mathscr{E})$ to $L(\mathscr{F})$.

Now consider a particular point $\theta \in \Theta$. Let $R_{\theta}$ be the restriction of the measure $P_{\theta}$ to the set $V=\{\hat{\theta} \in B\}, B=\{t ; d(t, \theta) \leq a\}$. Then, by assumption, $\left\|P_{\theta}-R_{\theta}\right\| \leq \varepsilon_{1}$. Let $J(\theta)$ be the set of indices $j \in J$ such that $u_{j}(s)>0$ for some $s \in B$. The inequality $u_{j}(s)>0, s \in B$ implies that the distance from $\theta$ to $B_{j}$ is at most $b$, hence that $\left\|T_{j} P_{\theta}-Q_{\theta}\right\| \leq 2 \varepsilon_{2}$.

For $j \in J(\theta)$ let $\alpha_{j}$ be defined by $\alpha_{j}\left\|R_{\theta}\right\|=\left\langle f_{j}, R_{\theta}\right\rangle$. Let $v$ be the indicator of the set $V=\{\hat{\theta} \in B\}$. Since $f_{j}$ satisfies a Lipschitz condition, one sees that

$$
\sum_{j} v\left|f_{j}-\alpha_{j}\right| \leq 2 \times \frac{8 C}{b} a
$$

This yields

$$
\left|\sum_{j} T_{j} f_{j} \cdot R_{\theta}-\sum \alpha_{j} T_{j} R_{\theta}\right|=\left|\sum_{j} T_{j}\left(f_{j}-\alpha_{j}\right) R_{\theta}\right| \leq \sum_{j} T_{j}\left|f_{j}-\alpha_{j}\right| \cdot R_{\theta} .
$$

Now each $\left|f_{j}-\alpha_{j}\right| \cdot R_{\theta}$ has a certain norm, say $\beta_{j}$, and since $\sum_{j} v\left|f_{j}-\alpha_{j}\right| \leq 16 C a / b$, one has also $\sum \beta_{j} \leq 16 C a / b$. Using the fact that each $T_{j}$ preserves the norms of positive elements we obtain

$$
\left\|\sum_{j} T_{j} f_{j} \cdot R_{\theta}-\sum_{j} \alpha_{j} T_{j} R_{\theta}\right\| \leq \frac{16 C a}{b}
$$

Define $T_{1}=\sum_{j} \alpha_{j} T_{j}, j \in J(\theta)$, and let $T_{2}$ be given by

$$
T_{2} \lambda=T(1-v) \cdot \lambda+T_{1} v \cdot \lambda
$$

We have just seen that

$$
\left\|T_{2} P_{\theta}-T P_{\theta}\right\| \leq 16 \frac{C a}{b}
$$

Note also that $\left\|T(1-v) \cdot P_{\theta}\right\|=\left\|P_{\theta}\right\|-\left\|R_{\theta}\right\|$ and similarly $\left\|T_{1}(1-v) \cdot P_{\theta}\right\|= \left\|P_{\theta}\right\|-\left\|R_{\theta}\right\|$. Finally,

$$
\left\|Q_{\theta}-T_{1} P_{\theta}\right\| \leq \sum_{j \in J(\theta)} \alpha_{j}\left\|Q_{\theta}-T_{j} P_{\theta}\right\| \leq 2 \varepsilon_{2}
$$

Thus, adding all terms, we obtain

$$
\left\|Q_{\theta}-T_{2} P_{\theta}\right\| \leq 2\left[\left\|P_{\theta}\right\|-\left\|R_{\theta}\right\|\right]+2 \varepsilon_{2}+16 \frac{C a}{b}
$$

giving the result as stated.
The reader will note that the inequality of Proposition 8 is useless unless the ratio $(a / b)$ is so small that $8 C a \leq b$. In the applications, the coefficient $C$ will be bounded by a number of the type $2^{D}$, where $D$ is a certain dimension or entropy number. One can then, for a give $a$, insure that $C a / b$ is small by taking $b$ sufficiently large. However, the number called $\varepsilon_{2}$ also depends on the choice of $b$ and may then become too large.

The argument used here could also be used in the situation where $\mathscr{E}$ is given by measures $P_{\theta}$ which are restrictions of the $Q_{\theta}$ to a complete sublattice $M^{\prime}= M(\mathscr{E})$ of $M(\mathscr{F})$ as in Proposition 7, and where the assumption $\delta\left(\mathscr{E}_{S}, \mathscr{F}_{S}\right) \leq \varepsilon_{2}$ is replaced by $\eta\left(M^{\prime}, \mathscr{F}\right) \leq \varepsilon_{2}$.

In this situation the $T_{j}$ used in the above proof would be selected as projections and one could write

$$
\sum_{j}\left[T_{j} f_{j} \cdot R_{\theta}-\sum \alpha_{j} T_{j} R_{\theta}\right]=\sum_{j}\left(T_{j}-\Pi\right)\left(f_{j}-\alpha_{j}\right) \cdot R_{\theta}
$$

for any other projection $\Pi$ of $M(\mathscr{F})$ onto $M(\mathscr{F})$, and in particular for $\Pi$ equal to the projection $\Pi_{\theta}$ which is adapted to $Q_{\theta}$ itself. Then application of Schwarz' inequality would yield results of the type

$$
\frac{1}{2}\left\|T_{2} P_{\theta}-Q_{\theta}\right\| \leq \varepsilon_{1}+\varepsilon_{2}\left[1+8 \frac{C a}{b}\right]
$$

for the transition constructed as in the proof of Proposition 8.
Such inequalities may perhaps be of some use for cases in which the dimension
of $\Theta$ is large but insufficiencies are small on sets which are enormous compared to the diameter of confidence sets available on the weaker experiment.

## CHAPTER 6

## Domination, Compactness, Contiguity

## 1. Introduction

In this chapter we discuss briefly certain conditions which often facilitate asymptotic arguments. These conditions are satisfied by a sufficient variety of situations to make their study interesting. Perhaps the most useful is the property called "contiguity" for sequences or nets $\left\{\mathscr{E}_{v}\right\}, \mathscr{E}_{v}=\left\{P_{0, v}, P_{1, v}\right\}$ of binary experiments. The one-sided version of this is obviously some sort of "asymptotic domination."
"Domination" itself is closely related to the stronger property of being weakly relatively compact, and weak relative compactness is of course weaker than strong relative compactness, or equivalently strong precompactness. Hence the title of the chapter.

After recalling the relevant definitions, we show, in Section 2, that the properties in question are not affected if one replaces an experiment $\mathscr{E}$ by an equivalent experiment $\mathscr{F}$, and that they are affected little if one replaces $\mathscr{E}$ by a neighboring experiment $\mathscr{F}$.

Section 3 describes contiguity and some of its implications. Section 4 gives a result of D . Lindae for sequences of experiments whose limit is strongly precompact.

## 2. Definitions and Elementary Relations

Let $L_{1}$ be an abstract $L$-space and let $\lambda$ and $\mu$ be two positive elements of $L_{1}$. One says that $\lambda$ dominates $\mu$ if $\mu$ belongs to the band generated by $\lambda$, or equivalently, if

$$
\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty}\|\mu-(\mu \wedge n \lambda)\|=0
$$

Let $\mathscr{E}=\left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ be an experiment with values $P_{\theta} \in L_{1}$ and let $L=L(\mathscr{E})$ be the $L$-space of $\mathscr{E}$.

Definition 1. Let $\lambda$ be a positive element of $L_{1}$. One says that $\mathscr{E}$ is dominated by $\lambda$ if each $P_{\theta}, \theta \in \Theta$ is dominated by $\lambda$. One says that $\mathscr{E}$ is homogeneous if each $P_{\theta}, \theta \in \Theta$ dominates $\mathscr{E}$.

It is visible from the definition that if $\lambda$ dominates $\mathscr{E}$ so does the component of $\lambda$ in the band $L(\mathscr{E})$. Another simple fact is as follows.

Lemma 1. If $\mathscr{E}=\left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ is dominated by $\lambda$ then there is a $\mu$ of the type $\mu=\sum\left(1 / 2^{n}\right) P_{\theta_{n}}$ which dominates $\mathscr{E}$.

Proof. Assume that $\Theta$ is well ordered and that in fact it is a segment $\Theta=\{\theta<x\}$ of the ordinals. For each $\theta$ let $L_{\theta}$ be the subset of $L$ formed by elements which are (i) in the band of $P_{\theta}$, and (ii) disjoint from all the $P_{\alpha}, \alpha<\theta$. Then $L_{\theta}$ is a band in which $\lambda$ has a certain component, say $\lambda_{\theta}$. Since $\|\lambda\|= \sum_{\theta}\left\|\lambda_{\theta}\right\|$ all the $\lambda_{\theta}$ must be zero, except for those of a countable subset, say $\left\{\theta_{n} ; n=1,2, \ldots\right\}$. Then $\lambda$ is dominated by $\sum\left(1 / 2^{n}\right) P_{\theta_{n}}$ and the result follows.

To introduce a property stronger than domination we shall use the following concept. Consider an $L$-space $L$, a positive element $\lambda \in L,\|\lambda\|=1$ and a subset $S \subset L$. Let $\alpha$ and $c$ denote two numbers of the interval $[0, \infty)$.

Definition 2. The subset $S$ will be called ( $\alpha, c \lambda$ ) limited if $\||\mu|-(|\mu| \wedge c \lambda)| | \leq \alpha$ for every $\mu \in S$.

The term "limited" used here arises from Köthe's description of a property which is equivalent to weak compactness. The following theorem (see Appendix) is the basic result relating various properties to weak compactness. Let $M$ be the dual of $L$ and let $\pi(M, L)$ be on $M$ the topology of uniform convergence on the intervals of $L$.

Theorem 1 (Dunford-Pettis-Grothendieck). For any subset $S$ of $L$ the following conditions are all equivalent:
(i) $S$ is relatively $w(L, M)$ countably compact in $L$.
(ii) $S$ is relatively $w(L, M)$ sequentially compact in $L$.
(iii) $S$ is relatively $w(L, M)$ compact in $L$.
(iv) $S$ is $\pi$-equicontinuous on the unit ball of $M$.
(v) If $u_{n} \in M$ is a sequence which converges to zero for $\pi(M, L)$ then

$$
\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} \sup _{\phi}\left\{\left|\left\langle u_{n}, \phi\right\rangle\right| ; \phi \in S\right\}=0
$$

(vi) For each $\varepsilon>0$ there is a lattice interval $J$ of $L$ such that $\inf _{\phi}\{\|\mu-\phi\|$; $\phi \in J\}<\varepsilon$ for all $\mu \in S$.
(vii) There is an element $\lambda \in L$ such that $\lambda \geq 0,\|\lambda\|=1$, and

$$
\lim _{m \rightarrow \infty} \sup _{\mu}\{\||\mu|-|\mu| \wedge m \lambda\| ; \mu \in S\}=0
$$

Köthe called a set $S$ having property (v) above "limited." According to (vii) and Definition 2, a set $S$ is limited if there is some $\lambda$ such that for each $\alpha>0$ one can find a $c$ such that $S$ is $(\alpha, c \lambda)$ limited.

Proposition 1. Let $\mathscr{E}=\left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ and $\mathscr{F}=\left\{Q_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ be two experiments indexed by $\Theta$. Let $T$ be a transition from $L(\mathscr{E})$ to $L(\mathscr{F})$. Assume that
(i) $\left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ is $(\alpha, c \lambda)$ limited.
(ii) $\left\|Q_{\theta}-T P_{\theta}\right\| \leq \varepsilon$ for every $\theta$.

Then, for $\mu=T \lambda$ the set $\mathscr{F}=\left\{Q_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ is $(\alpha+\varepsilon, c \mu)$ limited.
Proof. First note that

$$
T\left(P_{\theta} \wedge c \lambda\right) \leq\left(T P_{\theta}\right) \wedge(T c \lambda)=\left(T P_{\theta}\right) \wedge(c \mu)
$$

Thus $\left\|T P_{\theta}-\left[T P_{\theta} \wedge(c \mu)\right]\right\| \leq \alpha$. The result follows.
Corollary. If $\delta(\mathscr{E}, \mathscr{F})=0$ and if $\mathscr{E}$ is weakly relatively compact, so is $\mathscr{F}$. Similarly if $\delta(\mathscr{E}, \mathscr{F})=0$ and if $\mathscr{E}$ is dominated, so is $\mathscr{F}$.

In particular, the property of being ( $\alpha, c \lambda$ ) limited for some $\lambda$, the property of being limited, or being dominated are properties of the type of the experiment $\mathscr{E}$. As such, these properties must be expressible in terms of the conical measure of the experiment $\mathscr{E}$. Since the translation is easy, we shall not dwell on it here.

In the foregoing we have mentioned weak compactness properties for sets $S \subset L$ or experiments $\mathscr{E}=\left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$. One can also consider strong compactness properties. Since the space $L$ is complete for its norm, a subset $S \subset L$ is strongly relatively compact in $L$ if and only if it is precompact for the norm, that is, if for each $\varepsilon>0$ there is a finite subset $\left\{s_{j} ; j=1,2, \ldots, m\right\}$, such that

$$
\inf _{j}\left\|s-s_{j}\right\|<\varepsilon \quad \text { for every } s \in S
$$

This property is enormously stronger than relative compactness for $w(L, M)$. In turn, this latter is stronger than domination. By contrast, precompactness for the uniform structure of $w(L, M)$ amounts to very little since all norm bounded subsets of $L$ are $w(L, M)$ precompact. For the purpose of taking limits we shall often use nets or filters of experiments.

Keeping $\Theta$ fixed, a net $\left\{\mathscr{E}_{v} ; v \in N\right\}$ of experiments $\mathscr{E}_{v}=\left\{P_{\theta, v} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ is given by a directed set $N$ and, by experiments $\mathscr{E}_{v}$ depending on $v$. For many purposes, sequences are sufficient. They are obtainable by taking for $N$ the set of integers $1,2, \ldots$ in its natural order.

However, very many problems relative to stochastic processes introduce naturally nets which are not sequences. For instance, $N$ could be the class of finite subsets of $[0,1]$ ordered by inclusion.

For such nets or sequences the above definitions are not adequate to proceed conveniently. One needs analogues valid "as $v \in N$ gets large." We shall mention here some possible analogues, which will be met again below in connection with "contiguity". Fix $\Theta$ and consider a net $\mathscr{E}_{v} ; v \in N$ of experiments indexed by $\Theta$. For each $v$ let $\lambda_{v} \in L\left(\mathscr{E}_{v}\right)$ be an element such that $\lambda_{v} \geq 0,\left\|\lambda_{\nu}\right\|=1$.

Definition 3. The net $\left\{\mathscr{E}_{v}, \lambda_{v} ; v \in N\right\}$ possesses the uniform asymptotic domination property (UAD) if for each $\varepsilon>0$ there is a $c=c(\varepsilon) \in[0, \infty)$ and a $v(\varepsilon) \in N$ such that if $v \geq v(\varepsilon)$ then $\mathscr{E}_{v}$ is $\left(\varepsilon, c \lambda_{v}\right)$ limited.

The following is easily verifiable.
Proposition 2. Let $\left\{\mathscr{E}_{v} ; v \in N\right\}$ be a net of experiments indexed by $\Theta$. Let $\mathscr{E}$ be another such experiment. Assume that $\Delta\left(\mathscr{E}, \mathscr{E}_{v}\right) \rightarrow 0$. Then $\mathscr{E}$ is limited if and only if there are $\lambda_{\nu} \in L\left(\mathscr{E}_{\nu}\right)$ such that $\left(\mathscr{E}_{\nu}, \lambda_{\nu}\right)$ has the uniform asymptotic domination property.

For the proof of existence of the elements $\lambda_{\nu}$ one can repeat the argument of Proposition 1.

The UAD property admits many variations. For instance, one could assume that $c(\varepsilon)$ depends only on $\varepsilon$ but that what was called $v(\varepsilon) \in N$ depends on $\theta$ as well as on $\varepsilon$. In this case, for sequences $\mathscr{E}_{v} ; v \in N$ which converge weakly to $\mathscr{E} \in \mathbb{E}(\Theta)$, one obtains a property equivalent to the fact that the limit $\mathscr{E}$ is limited.

If one makes both $v(\varepsilon, \theta)$ and $c(\varepsilon, \theta)$ depend on $\theta$ one obtains, for sequences again, a property equivalent to the fact that the weak limit $\mathscr{E}$ is dominated. The case where $\Theta$ has only two elements is the simplest. For it, all the asymptotic properties just listed coincide and they give rise to the concept of contiguity described below.

## 3. Contiguity

In this section we shall consider a given directed set $N$ and associated nets $\left\{\mathscr{E}_{v} ; v \in N\right\}$ where $\mathscr{E}_{v}=\left(P_{v}, Q_{v}\right)$ is an experiment given by a pair ( $P_{v}, Q_{v}$ ) and called a binary experiment.

For a binary experiment $\mathscr{E}=(P, Q)$ one can define densities by the relations $d P=u d(P+Q)$ and $d Q=v d(P+Q)$. The map $(u, v)$ sends the sum $P+Q$ into a Radon measure on the simplex $\{(u, v) ; u \geq 0, v \geq 0, u+v=1\}$ of $R^{2}$.

This is the localization of the conical measure of $\mathscr{E}$ called the Blackwell measure of $\mathscr{E}$.

Equivalently one can send the sum $P+Q$ on $[0,1]$ by the map $v$. Also the ratio $w=v / u=v(1-v)^{-1}$ is called the likelihood ratio of $Q$ with respect to $P$. This map sends $P$ into a measure $F$ on $[0, \infty)$. It sends $Q$ into a measure $G$ on ( $0, \infty$ ]. (See Chapter 3 for additional details.)

Consider then a net $\left\{\mathscr{E}_{v} ; v \in N\right\}$ of binary experiments $\mathscr{E}_{v}=\left(P_{v}, Q_{v}\right)$. Let $W_{v}$ be the likelihood ratio ( $d Q_{v} / d P_{v}$ ) and let $F_{v}=\mathscr{L}\left(W_{v} \mid P_{v}\right)$ and $G_{v}= \mathscr{L}\left(W_{v} \mid Q_{v}\right)$. Both $F_{v}$ and $G_{v}$ will be considered as elements of the dual $C^{*}[[0, \infty]]$ of the space $C[[0, \infty]]$ of continuous functions on the compact interval $[0, \infty]$. The following proposition is easy but fundamental.

Proposition 3. For a net $\left\{\mathscr{E}_{v} ; v \in N\right\}$ as described, the following statements are all equivalent.
(i) Let $G_{v}$ be the distribution under $Q_{v}$ of the likelihood ratio $W_{v}$. Then for each $\varepsilon>0$ there is a $b=b(\varepsilon)$ and a $v(\varepsilon)$ such that $v \geq v(\varepsilon)$ implies $G_{v}\{[b, \infty]\}<\varepsilon$ (or, equivalently $G_{\nu}\{[0, b]\} \geq 1-\varepsilon$ ).
(ii) For each $\varepsilon>0$ there is a $c=c(\varepsilon)$ and a $v(\varepsilon)$ such that $v \geq v(\varepsilon)$ implies

$$
\left\|Q_{v}-Q_{v} \wedge\left(c P_{v}\right)\right\|<\varepsilon
$$

(iii) Let $\mathscr{E}=(P, Q)$ be a cluster point of the net $\left\{\mathscr{E}_{v}\right\}$ in the space $\mathbb{E}(\Theta), \Theta= \{0,1\}$. Then $P$ dominates $Q$.
(iv) For cluster points $\mathscr{E}=(P, Q)$ as in (iii), the conical measure of $\mathscr{E}$ has no mass on the line $\{(u, v) ; u=0\}$ of $R^{2}$.
(v) Let $F_{v}$ be the distribution under $P_{v}$ of the likelihood ratio $W_{v}$. If $F$ is a cluster point of $\left\{F_{v}\right\}, v \in N$ in the space $C^{*}[[0, \infty]]$, then $\int w F(d w)=1$.

Proof. The pair ( $F_{v}, G_{v}$ ) is obtained from the representation used on [ 0,1 ] in Chapter 3, Section 2 by the map $w=v(1-v)^{-1}$. Thus $\left(F_{v}, G_{v}\right)$ is obviously an experiment equivalent to the experiment $\mathscr{E}_{v}=\left(P_{v}, Q_{v}\right)$ itself. According to Proposition 1 the statements $\left\|G_{v}-G_{v} \wedge c F_{v}\right\|<\varepsilon$ and $\left\|Q_{v}-Q_{v} \wedge c P_{v}\right\|<\varepsilon$ are equivalent. With this replacement all the properties involve only the pair ( $\left.F_{v}, G_{v}\right)$.

Let $S_{v}=F_{v}+G_{v}$. This is the image of $P_{v}+Q_{v}$ by the map $W_{v}$. Convergence of $S_{v}$ in $C^{*}[[0, \infty]]$ is equivalent to convergence of the experiment $\mathscr{E}_{v}$. Also, with the usual conventions for infinite values, one may write

$$
\begin{aligned}
& F_{v}(d w)=(1+w)^{-1} S_{v}(d w) \\
& G_{v}(d w)=w(1+w)^{-1} S_{v}(d w)
\end{aligned}
$$

This shows, among other things, that $G_{v}$ consists of a point mass at the point $+\infty$ and of a part $G_{v}^{\prime}$ carried by $[0, \infty)$ which may be written $G_{v}^{\prime}(d w)= w F_{v}(d w)$, so that

$$
\int w F_{v}(d w)=G_{v}\{[0, \infty)\} \leq 1
$$

This being noted, let us first show that (i) and (ii) are equivalent. Clearly, for $c>1$.

$$
G_{v}\{[c, \infty]\}=c F_{v}\{[c, \infty]\}+\left\|G_{v}-G_{v} \wedge c F_{v}\right\|
$$

Thus (i) implies (ii). Conversely assume that (ii) holds. Take an $\varepsilon>0$, a $c>1$ such that $\left\|G_{v}-G_{v} \wedge c F_{v}\right\|<\varepsilon / 2$, and let $b=2 c / \varepsilon$. Then according to Markov's inequality $c F_{v}\{[b, \infty]\} \leq \varepsilon / 2$. This yields

$$
G_{v}\{[b, \infty]\} \leq c F_{v}\{[b, \infty]\}+\left\|G_{v}-G_{v} \wedge c F_{v}\right\|<\varepsilon
$$

The other statements in the Proposition involve cluster points. This means taking limits along "subsets" or "finer filters." Note that since both $\mathbb{E}[\{0,1\}]$ and $[0, \infty]$ are compact sets one can always find convergent subsets or convergent finer filters. We shall proceed by showing that if (i) is violated, one can find cluster points $G$ of the net $G_{v}$ with $G\{+\infty\}>0$, and then argue that (iii), (iv), and (v) are variations of the same statement.

Suppose that (i) does not hold. Then there is an $\varepsilon>0$ such that for each integer $m$ and each $v \in N$ there is some $\mu \in N, \mu \geq v$ for which $G_{\mu}\{[m, \infty]\} \geq \varepsilon$. Let $B(v, m)$ be the set of sums $S_{\mu}=F_{\mu}+G_{\mu}$ for those $\mu \geq v$ which satisfy the stated inequality. The sets $B(v, m)$ form the base of a filter, say $\mathscr{F}$.

Taking an ultrafilter $\mathscr{U}$ finer than $\mathscr{F}$, one obtains a subnet of $\left\{\mathscr{E}_{v} ; v \in N\right\}$ such that (i) along $\mathscr{U}$ the experiments $\mathscr{E}_{v}$ and the measures $S_{v}$ converge, and (ii) for each $m$, the inequality $G_{\nu}\{[m, \infty]\} \geq \varepsilon$ eventually holds. Since convergence of $S_{v}$ entails convergence of both $F_{v}$ and $G_{v}$, it follows readily that along $\mathscr{U}$ the pairs $\left(F_{\nu}, G_{\nu}\right)$ converge to a limit, say $(F, G)$ with $G\{[m, \infty]\} \geq \varepsilon$ for all $m$ and therefore $G\{(+\infty)\} \geq \varepsilon$.

This would provide a limit experiment ( $F, G$ ) which obviously violates (iii). Thus (iii) implies (i). The converse implication is quite trivial. Therefore, it will be sufficient to show that (iii), (iv), and (v) are always equivalent. For this purpose it is clearly enough to proceed assuming that the sums $S_{v}$ converge to a limit $S$. However, in this case the terms $F_{v}$ also converge to some limit, say $F$, carried by $[0, \infty]$ and such that $F(d w)=(1+w)^{-1} S(d w)$. Similarly $G_{v}$ converges to a limit $G$ with $G(d w)=w(1+w)^{-1} S(d w)$.

Now the statement that $F$ dominates $G$ is clearly equivalent to the assertion that $G$ has no mass at infinity. The integral

$$
\int w F(d w)=\int_{[0, \infty)} \frac{w}{1+w} S(d w)
$$

is precisely equal to $G\{[0, \infty)\}$. From this it follows that (iii), (iv), and (v) are equivalent, and this completes the proof of the Proposition.

These statements are easily seen to be particular forms of the statement that $\left\{\mathscr{E}_{v}, P_{v}\right\}$ has the UAD property of Section 2. When $N$ is the set of integers in the natural order, one can give another equivalent statement as follows.

Proposition 4. Let $\left\{\mathscr{E}_{v} ; v \in N\right\}$ be a sequence of experiments $\mathscr{E}_{v}=\left(P_{v}, Q_{v}\right)$. This sequence satisfies the equivalent statements of Proposition 3 if and only if every sequence $T_{\nu}$ of real valued statistics (with $T_{\nu}$ defined on $\mathscr{E}_{\nu}$ ) which converges in probability to zero for $\left\{P_{v}\right\}$ does the same for $\left\{Q_{v}\right\}$.

The "if" part of this proposition can be extended to general nets by introducing subnets. However, without the introduction of subnets, it fails. To see this, take a norm dense countable subset $\left\{\mu_{n}\right\}$ of the set of probability measures which are dominated by the Lebesgue measure $\lambda$ on $[0,1]$. Let $N$ be the set of ordinals smaller than the first uncountable ordinal. Index the $\mu_{n}$ by points $v \in N$ so that each $\mu_{n}$ occurs in all intervals $v \geq \omega, \omega \in N$. If $\left|T_{v}\right| \leq 1$ and $\int\left|T_{\nu}\right| d \lambda \rightarrow 0$ then $\int\left|T_{\nu}\right| d \lambda$ is eventually zero. However, condition (ii) of Proposition 3 cannot hold.

The asymptotic domination property described by the equivalent conditions of Proposition 3 is often referred to under the name of "contiguity". In fact there are two concepts which have come to bear this name. Initially the term was used to describe a symmetric relation as follows.

Definition 4. Let $\left\{\mathscr{E}_{v}\right\}, \mathscr{E}_{v}=\left(P_{v}, Q_{v}\right), v \in N$ be a net of binary experiments. The nets $\left\{P_{v}\right\}$ and $\left\{Q_{v}\right\}$ are called contiguous if all the cluster points of the net $\left\{\mathscr{E}_{v}\right\}$ are homogeneous experiments.

Current usage relies also on the following terminology.
Definition 5. The net $\left\{Q_{v}\right\}$ is called contiguous to the net $\left\{P_{v}\right\}$ if in all cluster points $\mathscr{E}=(P, Q)$ of the net $\left\{\mathscr{E}_{v}\right\}, \mathscr{E}_{v}=\left(P_{v}, Q_{v}\right)$, the term $Q$ is dominated by $P$.

Before passing to a discussion of some of the implications of contiguity let us mention two easy results which are very often useful.

Lemma 2. Let $\left\{\mathscr{E}_{v}\right\}, \mathscr{E}_{v}=\left(P_{v}, Q_{v}\right)$ and $\left\{\mathscr{E}_{v}^{\prime}\right\}, \mathscr{E}_{v}^{\prime}=\left(P_{v}^{\prime}, Q_{v}^{\prime}\right)$ be two nets of binary experiments indexed by the same directed set N. Assume that
(i) The net $\left\{Q_{v}\right\}$ is contiguous to the net $\left\{P_{v}\right\}$.
(ii) The deficiency $\delta\left(\mathscr{E}_{v}, \mathscr{E}_{v}^{\prime}\right)$ tends to zero.

Then $\left\{Q_{\nu}^{\prime}\right\}$ is also contiguous to $\left\{P_{\nu}^{\prime}\right\}$.
Proof. It is clearly sufficient to prove the result under the supplementary assumption that $\left\{\mathscr{E}_{v}\right\}$ has a limit $\mathscr{E}=(P, Q)$ and $\left\{\mathscr{E}_{v}^{\prime}\right\}$ has a limit $\mathscr{E}^{\prime}=\left(P^{\prime}, Q^{\prime}\right)$. Then, according to (ii), one has $\delta\left(\mathscr{E}, \mathscr{E}^{\prime}\right)=0$ and there is a transition $T$ such that $T P=P^{\prime}, T Q=Q^{\prime}$. The result follows by Proposition 1 .

Since contiguity involves only the types of the experiments $\mathscr{E}_{v}$, one can check whether it holds in many different ways. One possibility is to use Hellinger transforms. We shall have occasion to use the following.

Lemma 3. Let $\left\{\mathscr{E}_{v}\right\}, \mathscr{E}_{v}=\left(P_{v}, Q_{v}\right)$ be a net of binary experiments and let $\phi_{v}$ be the Hellinger transform $\phi_{\nu}(\alpha)=\int\left(d P_{\nu}\right)^{1-\alpha}\left(d Q_{\nu}\right)^{\alpha}$ for $\alpha \in[0,1]$. Then $\left\{Q_{\nu}\right\}$ is contiguous to $\left\{P_{v}\right\}$ if and only if

$$
\lim _{\substack{\alpha \rightarrow 1 \\ \alpha<1}} \liminf _{v} \phi_{v}(\alpha)=1
$$

Proof. Suppose that $\mathscr{E}_{v} \rightarrow \mathscr{E}=(P, Q)$ with Hellinger transform $\phi$. Then

$$
\lim _{\substack{\alpha \rightarrow 1 \\ \alpha<1}} \phi(\alpha)
$$

is simply the mass of $Q$ which is dominated by $P$. The result follows easily.
One of the main reasons for the introduction of the contiguity relations is that they permit assertions about limiting distributions under $\left\{Q_{v}\right\}$ when one has enough information about limiting distributions under $\left\{P_{v}\right\}$.

To obtain a fairly general statement consider the following situation. One is given a net $\left\{\mathscr{E}_{v}\right\} ; \mathscr{E}_{v}=\left(P_{v}, Q_{v}\right), v \in N$ of binary experiments and a space $Z$ with a uniform structure and a corresponding space of bounded uniformly continuous real functions $\Gamma(Z)$. For each $v$ let $T_{v}$ be "a statistic with values in $Z$ available on $\mathscr{E}_{v}$." That is, $T_{v}$ is a transition from $L\left(\mathscr{E}_{v}\right)$ to the dual of $\Gamma(Z)$. Let $W_{v}$ be the likelihood ratio $W_{v}=d Q_{v} / d P_{v}$. Let $Z_{1}$ be the product of $Z$ by the interval $[0, \infty]$. Then $Z_{1}$ with its product uniformity has a space $\Gamma\left(Z_{1}\right)$ of bounded uniformly continuous real functions. The pair ( $T_{v}, W_{v}$ ) defines in a standard manner a transition from $L\left(\mathscr{E}_{v}\right)$ to the dual of $\Gamma\left(Z_{1}\right)$.

Thus there is no difficulty in defining the joint distribution $F_{v}=\mathscr{L}\left(T_{v}, W_{v} \mid P_{v}\right)$ of the pair ( $T_{v}, W_{v}$ ) under $P_{v}$. This is of course the image of $P_{v}$ by the transition ( $T_{v}, W_{v}$ ). Since the expectation of $W_{v}$ under $P_{v}$ is not larger than unity it is also easy to define an element $G_{v}^{\prime}$ of the dual of $\Gamma\left(Z_{1}\right)$ which the element whose density with respect to $\mathscr{L}\left(T_{v}, W_{v} \mid P_{v}\right)$ is given by the identity map $w$ of [ $0, \infty$ ) into itself.

In symbols,

$$
G_{v}^{\prime}(d t, d w)=w F_{v}(d t, d w)
$$

This $G_{v}^{\prime}$ can also be obtained in a different manner. If $Q_{v}^{\prime}$ is the part of $Q_{v}$ which is dominated by $P_{v}$, then $G_{v}^{\prime}$ is the image $\mathscr{L}\left(T_{v}, W_{v} \mid Q_{v}^{\prime}\right)$ of $Q_{v}^{\prime}$ by the transition $\left(T_{v}, W_{v}\right)$. (Note that here we have worked on the product $Z \times[0, \infty]$ but one could as well work on the product $Z \times[0, \infty)$. No essential difference will ensue.)

In the dual $\Gamma^{*}$ of $\Gamma=\Gamma\left(Z_{1}\right)$ several different topologies are usable. The most common one is the vague topology. A net $\left\{\mu_{\nu}\right\}$ converges vaguely to $\mu_{0}$ if for each $\gamma \in \Gamma\left(Z_{1}\right)$ the values $\left\langle\gamma, \mu_{\nu}\right\rangle$ converge to $\left\langle\gamma, \mu_{0}\right\rangle$. The following result is almost obvious.



Proposition 5. Let $\left\{\mathscr{E}_{v}\right\}, \mathscr{E}_{v}=\left(P_{v}, Q_{v}\right), v \in N$ be a net of binary experiments. For each $v$ let $W_{v}$ be the likelihood ratio ( $d Q_{v} / d P_{v}$ ) and let $T_{v}$ be a statistic with values in $Z$ available on $\mathscr{E}_{v}$. Assume that the following conditions hold.
(i) The net $\left\{Q_{v}\right\}$ is contiguous to the net $\left\{P_{v}\right\}$.
(ii) The joint distributions $F_{v}=\mathscr{L}\left(T_{v}, W_{v} \mid P_{v}\right)$ converge vaguely to a limit $F$.

Then the joint distributions $G_{v}=\mathscr{L}\left(T_{v}, W_{v} \mid Q_{v}\right)$ converge vaguely to a limit $G$ which has a density with respect to $F$, according to the formula $G(d t, d w)=w F(d t, d w)$.

Proof. Let $\gamma$ be an element of $\Gamma\left(Z_{1}\right)$ and let $a=\sup \left\{|\gamma(z)| ; z \in Z_{1}\right\}$. According to the form (ii) in Proposition 3 of the contiguity relation, given $\varepsilon>0$ there is some number $c$ and some $v_{0} \in N$ such that $v \geq v_{0}$ implies $\left\|Q_{v}-Q_{v} \wedge c P_{v}\right\| \leq \varepsilon / a$. Passing to the images $F_{v}=\mathscr{L}\left(T_{v}, W_{v} \mid P_{v}\right)$ and $G_{v}=\mathscr{L}\left(T_{v}, W_{v} \mid Q_{v}\right)$ one can also say that $\left\|G_{v}-H_{v, c}\right\| \leq \varepsilon / a$, for $H_{v, c}=G_{v} \wedge c F_{v}$.

Let $w_{c}$ be the element $w_{c}=w \wedge c$ of $\Gamma\left(Z_{1}\right)$. Then one can easily verify that $H_{v, c}$ is the image of $Q_{v} \wedge c P_{v}$ by the transition ( $T_{v}, W_{v}$ ). Also $H_{v, c}$ has a density $w_{c}$ with respect to $F_{v}$. Since $F_{v} \rightarrow F$ it follows that $H_{v, c}$ converges to $H_{c}$ which has density $w_{c}$ with respect to $F$. Thus, for the given $\gamma$ one has eventually

$$
\left|\left\langle\gamma, G_{v}\right\rangle-\left\langle\gamma, H_{v, c}\right\rangle\right| \leq \varepsilon
$$

Also

$$
\lim _{v}\left|\left\langle\gamma, H_{v, c}\right\rangle-\left\langle\gamma w_{c}, F\right\rangle\right|=0
$$

and

$$
\left|\langle\gamma w, F\rangle-\left\langle\gamma w_{c}, F\right\rangle\right| \leq \varepsilon
$$

This gives

$$
\limsup \left|\left\langle\gamma, G_{v}\right\rangle-\langle\gamma w, F\rangle\right| \leq 2 \varepsilon
$$

and yields the desired result since $\varepsilon$ can be taken arbitrarily small.
Note that the limit $G$ is always in the band generated by $F$ so that if $F$ satisfies any sort of tightness, $\tau$-smoothness, or $\sigma$-smoothness condition, so does $G$. For various purposes the vague topology is too weak. Most of the topologies used in practice can be obtained by a procedure describable as follows.

Let $\mathscr{B}$ denote a family of bounded subsets of $\Gamma\left(Z_{1}\right)$ subject to the following restrictions:
(a) $\mathscr{B}$ is increasingly directed. That is, if $B_{i} \in \mathscr{B}, i=1,2$ there is a $B_{3} \in \mathscr{B}$ such that $B_{1} \cup B_{2} \subset B_{3}$.
(b) Let $w_{c}=w \wedge c$. Then if $B \in \mathscr{B}$ the set $\left\{\gamma w_{c} ; \gamma \in B\right\}$ is contained in some
element of $\mathscr{B}$.
(c) The union $\cup\{B: B \in \mathscr{B}\}$ is the entire space $\Gamma\left(Z_{1}\right)$. A net $\left\{\mu_{\nu}\right\}, \mu_{\nu} \in \Gamma^{*}$ is said to converge to a limit $\mu_{0}$ for the $\mathscr{B}$-topology if for each $B \in \mathscr{B}$ the numbers $\sup \left\{\left|\left\langle\gamma, \mu_{\nu}-\mu_{0}\right\rangle\right|, \gamma \in B\right\}$ converge to zero.

The condition called (c) above was imposed here to insure that the $\mathscr{B}$ topology be stronger than the vague topology, It could be replaced by any one of a number of other conditions which imply the same thing at least on the positive part of $\Gamma^{*}$. This replacement is often useful. For instance, if $Z$ is the line, one may want to work only with functions which satisfy Lipschitz conditions.

As an example of a very strong form of a $\mathscr{B}$-topology one may take sets $B$ of the form $\mathbf{B}=\left\{\gamma_{1}(t) \gamma_{2}(w)\right\}$ where $\gamma_{2}$ is a fixed continuous function on $[0, \infty]$ but $\gamma_{1}$ is allowed to run through the entire unit ball of $\Gamma(Z)$. In this case the $\mathscr{B}$ convergence of $\mathscr{L}\left(T_{\nu}, W_{\nu} \mid P_{\nu}\right)$ implies the norm convergence of $\mathscr{L}\left(T_{\nu} \mid P_{\nu}\right)$. With these notations one can state an analogue of Proposition 5 as follows.

Proposition 6. With the same notation as in Proposition 5 assume that the net $\left\{Q_{v}\right\}$ is contiguous to $\left\{P_{v}\right\}$ and that $F_{v}=\mathscr{L}\left(T_{v}, W_{v} \mid P_{v}\right)$ converges for the $\mathscr{B}$ topology to a limit $F$.
