Then $G_{v}=\mathscr{L}\left(T_{v}, W_{v} \mid Q_{v}\right)$ converges for the same $\mathscr{B}$ topology to the limit $G=w F$.

The proof is the same as that of Proposition 5.
As is well known, the statistical literature is replete with examples where the logarithm of likelihood ratios $\Lambda_{v}=\log W_{v}=\log \left(d Q_{v} / d P_{v}\right)$ have limiting Gaussian distributions. Because of the prevalence of this type of situation we shall give below a few results relative to the asymptotically Gaussian case.

Consider a net $\left\{\mathscr{E}_{v}\right\} ; \mathscr{E}_{v}=\left(P_{v}, Q_{v}\right), v \in N$ of binary experiments. Let $\Lambda_{v}=\log \left(d Q_{v} / d P_{v}\right)$ (considered as a random variable in an extended sense, that is with values in $[-\infty,+\infty])$. Let us say that distributions $\mathscr{L}\left(\Lambda_{v} \mid P_{v}\right)$ are asymptotically Gaussian if there are numbers $a_{v}, \sigma_{v}^{2}, \sigma_{v}^{2} \geq 0$ such that the Lévy distance between $\mathscr{L}\left(\Lambda_{v} \mid P_{v}\right)$ and the Gaussian distribution $\mathscr{N}\left(a_{v}, \sigma_{v}^{2}\right)$ tends to zero.

Proposition 7. Assume that for the net $\left\{\mathscr{E}_{v}\right\}$ the distributions $\mathscr{L}\left\{\Lambda_{v} \mid P_{v}\right\}$ are asymptotically Gaussian $\mathscr{N}\left(a_{\nu}, \sigma_{\nu}^{2}\right)$. Then $\left\{Q_{\nu}\right\}$ is contiguous to $\left\{P_{\nu}\right\}$ if and only if
(i) $\limsup _{v} \sigma_{v}^{2}<\infty$;
(ii) $\lim a_{v}+\frac{1}{2} \sigma_{v}^{2}=0$.

If so, the two nets $\left\{P_{v}\right\},\left\{Q_{v}\right\}$ are contiguous and $\mathscr{L}\left(\Lambda_{v} \mid Q_{v}\right)$ is asymptotically the Gaussian distribution $\mathscr{N}\left(a_{v}+\sigma_{v}^{2}, \sigma_{v}^{2}\right)$.

Proof. Let $\xi$ be a Gaussian variable with expectation zero and variance unity. For values $\alpha \in(0,1)$ the quantity

$$
\phi_{v}(\alpha)=E \exp \alpha\left(a_{v}+\sigma_{v} \xi\right)=\exp \left\{\alpha\left(a_{v}+\frac{\alpha}{2} \sigma_{v}^{2}\right)\right\}
$$

is an approximation to the Hellinger transform $\int\left(d P_{\nu}\right)^{1-\alpha}\left(d Q_{\nu}\right)^{\alpha}$. Contiguity of
$\left\{Q_{v}\right\}$ to $\left\{P_{v}\right\}$ implies that for each $\alpha \in(0,1)$ one must have

$$
0<\liminf _{v} \phi_{v}(\alpha) \leq \lim _{v} \sup _{v} \phi_{v}(\alpha) \leq 1
$$

This implies in particular that $\liminf _{v} a_{v}+\frac{1}{4} \sigma_{v}^{2}>-\infty$. If so, and if $\sigma_{v}^{2}$ becomes arbitrarily large, then $a_{v}+(\alpha / 2) \sigma_{v}^{2}$ would become arbitrarily large for all $\alpha>\frac{1}{2}$. This is impossible. Thus $\sigma_{v}^{2}$ and therefore $\left|a_{v}\right|$ must eventually remain bounded.

For contiguity it is also necessary that, for given $\varepsilon>0$, there is some $\alpha_{0} \in (0,1)$ such that $\alpha \geq \alpha_{0}$ implies $\liminf \phi_{v}(\alpha) \geq 1-\varepsilon$. Equivalently, for $\alpha$ close to unity one must have at the same time
(a) $\limsup _{v} a_{v}+(\alpha / 2) \sigma_{v}^{2} \leq 0$
(b) $\liminf _{\nu} a_{\nu}+(\alpha / 2) \sigma_{\nu}^{2} \geq-\varepsilon$

From this it follows easily that condition (ii) of the proposition is necessary. Conversely, if the conditions are satisfied, one may assume that $a_{v} \rightarrow a$ and $\sigma_{v}^{2} \rightarrow \sigma^{2}$, with $a+\frac{1}{2} \sigma^{2}=0$. An application of either Proposition 3, part (v) or Lemma 3 yields immediately that $\left\{Q_{v}\right\}$ is contiguous to $\left\{P_{v}\right\}$. However, since the limiting distributions of $\exp \left(a_{v}+\sigma_{v} \xi\right)$ are then entirely on the open interval $(0, \infty)$, this also implies the contiguity of $\left\{P_{v}\right\}$ to $\left\{Q_{v}\right\}$. The final statement of the proposition is an immediate consequence of Proposition 6.

This gives the following corollary. Let $T_{v}$ be a real valued statistic defined on $\mathscr{E}_{v}$. Suppose that the conditions of Proposition 7, including contiguity, are satisfied and that the distribution $\mathscr{L}\left[\Lambda_{\nu}, T_{\nu} \mid P_{\nu}\right]$ of ( $\Lambda_{\nu}, T_{\nu}$ ) under $P_{\nu}$ converges to a Gaussian limit $G$. Then $\mathscr{L}\left[\Lambda_{v}, T_{v} \mid Q_{v}\right]$ converges to a Gaussian limit $G^{*}$. Furthermore, $G$ and $G^{*}$ have the same covariance structure. If the expectation of $G$ is $(\alpha, \beta)$, the expectation of $G^{*}$ is $\left(\alpha^{*}, \beta^{*}\right)$ with $\alpha^{*}=\frac{1}{2} \sigma^{2}=\alpha+\sigma^{2}$ and $\beta^{*}=\beta+C$, where $C$ is the covariance of ( $\Lambda, T$ ) in the limiting distribution $G$.

A similar phenomenon occurs if instead of assuming that $G$ is Gaussian one assumes only that $G$ is infinitely divisible, with the first coordinate $\Lambda$ Gaussian $\mathscr{N}\left(a, \sigma^{2}\right), a=-\frac{1}{2} \sigma^{2}$. Then $G^{*}$ is similarly infinitely divisible and the only difference between $G$ and $G^{*}$ is the difference in centering constants. This is exactly the same as above, with $C$ equal to the covariance of ( $\Lambda, T$ ) in the Gaussian component of $G$. See Chapter 16 for further elaboration.

Instead of binary experiments, consider an arbitrary set $S$ and experiments $\mathscr{E}_{v}=\left\{P_{s, v} ; s \in S\right\}$. Take a particular $\theta \in S$ and the logarithms

$$
\Lambda_{v}(s, \theta)=\log \frac{d P_{s, v}}{d P_{\theta, v}}
$$

Suppose that for each $s$ the nets $\left\{P_{s, v}\right\}$ and $\left\{P_{\theta, v}\right\}$ are contiguous. For the distributions induced by $P_{\theta, v}$ the $\Lambda_{v}$ form a certain stochastic process. Assume that the finite dimensional distributions of $\Lambda_{\nu}$ converge to those of a Gaussian process $Z$ which has covariance $\operatorname{Cov}[Z(s), Z(t)]=K(s, t)$.

Then $Z$ must have expectations given by $E_{\theta} Z(s)=-\frac{1}{2} K(s, s)$. Furthermore, if one induces the distributions of $\Lambda_{s}$ by $P_{t, n}$ instead of $P_{\theta, n}$ the processes $\Lambda_{v}$ will still have Gaussian limits with the same covariance kernel $K$ but with expectations

$$
E_{t} Z(s)=K(s, t)-\frac{1}{2} K(s, s)
$$

One encounters occasionally pairs $\left(T_{v}, \Lambda_{v}\right), \Lambda_{v}=\log \left(d Q_{v} / d P_{v}\right)$, where $\mathscr{L}\left(T_{v} \mid P_{v}\right)$ is asymptotically normal but $\mathscr{L}\left(\Lambda_{v} \mid P_{v}\right)$ is not. Even so it is sometimes possible to conclude that $\mathscr{L}\left(T_{v} \mid Q_{v}\right)$ is asymptotically normal.

An example is as follows. Suppose that the nets $\left\{P_{v}\right\},\left\{Q_{v}\right\}$ are contiguous and suppose that $\mathscr{L}\left(T_{\nu}, \Lambda_{\nu} \mid P_{\nu}\right)$ converges to some infinitely divisible distribution in the plane $R^{2}$. Then, if $\mathscr{L}\left[T_{v} \mid P_{v}\right]$ is asymptotically normal, so is $\mathscr{L}\left[T_{v} \mid Q_{v}\right]$ and the passage from the cluster points of $\mathscr{L}\left(T_{v} \mid P_{v}\right)$ to the corresponding cluster points of $\mathscr{L}\left(T_{\nu} \mid Q_{\nu}\right)$ is again effected by keeping the variances unchanged and adding to the expectations the covariances of the Gaussian parts of the approximating infinitely divisible distributions.

## 4. Strong Compactness and a Result of D. Lindae

In this section we shall consider nets $\left\{\mathscr{E}_{v}\right\}, v \in N$ of experiments $\mathscr{E}_{v}=\left\{P_{\theta, v} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ indexed by a given set $\Theta$. In this situation, there are very few procedures available for proving that the $\mathscr{E}_{v}$ converge, in the sense that $\Delta\left(\mathscr{E}_{v}, \mathscr{E}\right) \rightarrow 0$, to a limit $\mathscr{E}$. One such procedure is contained in the modification of a theorem of D. Lindae given below.

The result does not appear remarkably strong. However, it turns out that it is very often usable and therefore interesting. Recall that each $\mathscr{E}_{v}$ is given by a map $\theta \mapsto P_{\theta, \nu}$ with $P_{\theta, \nu}$ in a certain abstract $L$-space $L\left(\mathscr{E}_{\nu}\right)$. These spaces will be metrized by their norm.

Theorem 2. Assume that the net $\left\{\mathscr{E}_{v}\right\}, v \in N$ converges weakly to a limit $\mathscr{E}=\left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ such that the set $\left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ be strongly precompact in $L(\mathscr{E})$. Suppose also that for each $\varepsilon>0$ there is a $v(\varepsilon) \in N$ and a number $\alpha(\varepsilon)>0$ such that if $v \geq v(\varepsilon)$ and $\left\|P_{s}-P_{t}\right\| \leq \alpha(\varepsilon)$, then $\left\|P_{s, v}-P_{t, v}\right\| \leq \varepsilon$. Then $\Delta\left(\mathscr{E}_{v}, \mathscr{E}\right) \rightarrow 0$.

Proof. One may assume $\alpha(\varepsilon) \leq \varepsilon$. Since $\mathscr{E}$ is precompact there is a finite set $S \subset \Theta$ such that $\inf _{s \in S}\left\|P_{\theta}-P_{s}\right\|<\alpha(\varepsilon)$ for each $\theta \in \Theta$. By assumption this implies

$$
\inf _{s \in S}\left\|P_{\theta, v}-P_{s, v}\right\| \leq \varepsilon
$$

for all $v$ sufficiently large.
Also, by assumption there are transitions $T_{1, \nu}$ and $T_{2, \nu}$, from $L(\mathscr{E})$ to $L\left(\mathscr{E}_{\nu}\right)$ and from $L\left(\mathscr{E}_{\nu}\right)$ to $L(\mathscr{E})$, respectively, such that both

$$
\sup _{s \in S}\left\|T_{1, v} P_{s}-P_{s, v}\right\|=\varepsilon_{1, v}
$$

and

$$
\sup _{s \in S}\left\|T_{2, v} P_{s, v}-P_{s}\right\|=\varepsilon_{2, v}
$$

tend to zero. However, if $\left\|P_{\theta}-P_{s}\right\| \leq \varepsilon$ and $\left\|P_{\theta, v}-P_{s, v}\right\| \leq \varepsilon$ for some $s \in S$ this implies also

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left\|T_{1, v} P_{\theta}-P_{\theta, v}\right\| & \leq \varepsilon_{1, v}+2 \varepsilon \\
\left\|T_{2, v} P_{\theta, v}-P_{\theta}\right\| & \leq \varepsilon_{2, v}+2 \varepsilon
\end{aligned}
$$

The result follows.
Remark 1. The preceding theorem is stated using the norm of the abstract $L$-spaces $L\left(\mathscr{E}_{v}\right)$. One could just as well use some other distances, such as, for instance, the Hellinger distance.

Remark 2. We shall encounter later many examples where $\Theta$ is a topological space, for instance, a Euclidean space or a Hilbert space, and where the map $\theta \mapsto P_{\theta}$ from $\Theta$ to $L(\mathscr{E})$ is continuous. In this situation, for each compact set $K \subset \Theta$, the foregoing Theorem 1 will allow us to prove that $\Delta_{k}\left(\mathscr{E}_{v}, \mathscr{E}^{\circ}\right)$ tends to zero if we can show that $\mathscr{E}_{v} \rightarrow \mathscr{E}$ weakly and that pairs $\left(P_{s, v}, P_{t, v}\right),(s, t) \in K \times K$ behave properly.

To conclude let us mention briefly some other results of Lindae which are valid when $\mathscr{E}=\left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ is precompact. Suppose, for instance, that $\Theta$ itself is a compact metric space and that the limit map $\theta \mapsto P_{\theta}$ is continuous.

Fix a particular $\theta_{0} \in \Theta$. For each $v$ let $X_{v}(\theta)$ be the likelihood ratio

$$
X_{v}(\theta)=\frac{d P_{\theta, v}}{d P_{\theta_{0}, v}}
$$

considered as a random variable for the distribution induced by $P_{\theta_{0}, v}$. Define $X(\theta)$ similarly. Then there is, of course, no reason to expect that the processes $X_{v}(\theta)$ or even $X(\theta)$ would have continuous paths.

For instance, if one writes $Y(\theta)=\sqrt{X(\theta)}$ the continuity of the map $\theta \mapsto P_{\theta}$ implies little more than continuity in quadratic mean of the process $Y$. Even if $X$ and all the $X_{v}$ have continuous paths and if $\Delta\left(\mathscr{E}_{v}, \mathscr{E}\right) \rightarrow 0$, one cannot conclude that the processes $X_{v}$ have distribution converging to those of $X$ in the sense of Prohorov. This implication happens to be true only when $\Theta$ is finite. The following argument is intended to shed a glimmer of light on the situation.

Suppose that $N$ is the set of integers in their natural order. Suppose also that $\Theta$ is compact metrizable. Let $C(\Theta)$ denote the set of bounded continuous functions on $\Theta$. The set $C(\Theta)$ will be metrized by its uniform norm. Measures on $C(\Theta)$ will be given the corresponding dual Lipschitz norm noted $\|\mu\|_{D}$.

Proposition 8. Assume that $\mathscr{E}=\left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ is such that $\mathscr{E}$ is dominated by $P_{\theta_{0}}$ and such that the set $\left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ is precompact in $L(\mathscr{E})$. Let $\left\{\mathscr{E}_{n}\right\} ; n \in N$, $\mathscr{E}_{n}=\left\{P_{\theta, n} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ be a sequence of experiments indexed by $\Theta$ and such that $\Delta\left(\mathscr{E}_{n}, \mathscr{E}\right) \rightarrow 0$. Then there are experiments $\mathscr{F}_{n}=\left\{Q_{\theta, n} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ and $\mathscr{G}_{n}= \left\{R_{\theta, n} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ with the following properties:
(i) $Q_{\theta, n} \in L\left(\mathscr{E}_{n}\right)$ and $\sup _{\theta}\left\|Q_{\theta, n}-P_{\theta, n}\right\| \rightarrow 0$;
(ii) $R_{\theta, n} \in L(\mathscr{E})$ and $\sup _{\theta}\left\|R_{\theta, n}-P_{\theta}\right\| \rightarrow 0$;
(iii) Let

$$
X_{n}(\theta)=\frac{d Q_{\theta, n}}{d Q_{\theta_{0}, n}}
$$

and let

$$
Y_{n}(\theta)=\frac{d R_{\theta, n}}{d R_{\theta_{0}, n}}
$$

both considered as random variables for the denominator measure. Let $F_{n}$ be the distribution of the process $X_{n}(\theta)$, and let $G_{n}$ be the corresponding distribution for $Y_{n}$. Then both $F_{n}$ and $G_{n}$ are on $C(\Theta)$ and

$$
\left\|F_{n}-G_{n}\right\|_{D} \rightarrow 0
$$

Proof. Let $\pi=\left\{u_{j}, j=0,1,2, \ldots, m\right\}, u_{j} \geq 0, \sum u_{j}=1$ be a finite partition of unity by elements $u_{j}$ of $C(\Theta)$. For each $u_{j}$ one can select a $t_{j} \in \Theta$ such that $u_{j}\left(t_{j}\right)>0$. We can also assume that $u_{0}\left(\theta_{0}\right)=1$ and that $t_{0}$ has been taken equal to $\theta_{0}$. For such a partition $\pi$ one can define an experiment $\mathscr{G}_{\pi}= \left\{R_{\theta, \pi} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ according to the interpolation formulas

$$
R_{\theta, \pi}=\sum_{j} u_{j}(\theta) P_{t_{j}}
$$

Take also a transition $T_{n}$ from $L(\mathscr{E})$ to $L\left(\mathscr{E}_{n}\right)$ such that $\varepsilon_{n}=\sup _{\theta}\left\|T_{n} P_{\theta}-P_{\theta, n}\right\|$ be as small as possible and let

$$
Q_{\theta, \pi, n}=\sum_{j} u_{j}(\theta) T_{n} P_{t_{j}}
$$

Considering the likelihood ratios

$$
X_{\pi, n}(\theta)=\frac{d Q_{\theta, \pi, n}}{d Q_{\theta_{0}, \pi, n}}
$$

one sees that natural versions of these have their paths in $C(\Theta)$. Since $\Delta\left(\mathscr{E}_{n}, \mathscr{E}\right) \rightarrow 0$, the corresponding distributions $F_{\pi, n}$ on $C(\Theta)$ are such that $\left\|F_{\pi, n}-G_{\pi}\right\|_{D} \rightarrow 0$ for the limiting distribution $G_{\pi}$ similarly associated with the $R_{\theta, \pi}$.

Now take an integer $m>1$. One can certainly select the partition $\pi$ so that

$$
\sup _{\theta}\left\|R_{\theta, \pi}-P_{\theta}\right\|<\frac{1}{m}
$$

This implies

$$
\sup _{\theta}\left\|Q_{\theta, \pi, n}-T_{n} P_{\theta}\right\|<\frac{1}{m}
$$

and therefore

$$
\sup _{\theta}\left\|Q_{\theta, \pi, n}-P_{\theta, n}\right\|<\frac{1}{m}+\varepsilon_{n}
$$

An easy diagonal argument completes the proof.

Remark. It is to be noted that we have not asserted above that the distributions $G_{n}$ obtained from approximating $R_{\theta, n}$ converge to the distribution of the process $X(\theta)=\left(d P_{\theta} / d P_{\theta_{0}}\right)$. This is simply impossible when the latter cannot be selected to have continuous paths and is, on the contrary, automatic if $X$ does have continuous paths.

## CHAPTER 7

## Some Limit Theorems

## 1. Introduction

The purpose of this chapter is to describe some of the relations that occur when a net $\left\{\mathscr{E}_{v}\right\}$ of experiments converges to a limit in the weak sense of Chapter 2. Several of the results obtained in the previous chapters indicate that even the convergence $\Delta\left(\mathscr{E}_{v}, \mathscr{E}\right) \rightarrow 0$ is not a very strong relation. It is certainly much weaker than many of the stochastic process convergences often used as assumptions in the statistical literature. However, as we shall show below, even weak convergence is already rich in recognizable statistical implications.

Throughout this chapter we retain the general notations of Chapters 1 and 2. Convenience in proving the appropriate results depends materially on the fact that we define statistical procedures as transitions and define risks by suprema as described in Chapter 2. However, since this may make certain concepts such as convergence in distribution or convergence in measure look somewhat unfamiliar, the relevant relations are described in Section 2 below.

Section 3 starts by a simple result which states that experiments obtained by taking limits of distributions are weaker than the limits of the experiments themselves. Those sequences or nets of statistics for which the limiting distributions form an experiment as good as the limit of the experiments possess very special and remarkable properties. We have called them "distinguished" and will show that from them one can recover likelihood ratios in a nearly continuous manner. In addition if one modifies their values a little, their efficiency is not modified much.

Section 4 is devoted to a different kind of lower semicontinuity. The basic result is that when a function is not a possible risk function for an experiment $\mathscr{E}$, it is also not possible for the experiments which are close enough to $\mathscr{E}$. This implies certain asymptotic minimax and asymptotic admissibility results often found in the literature only in very special cases.

In Section 5 we consider a net $\left\{\mathscr{E}_{v}\right\}$ converging weakly to $\mathscr{E}$ and a procedure $\sigma$, which is available on $\mathscr{E}$ but is the unique one attaining a certain risk. It is then shown that when $\sigma$ is nonrandomized, two nets $\left\{\rho_{v, i}\right\}, i=1,2, \rho_{v, i}$ which are available on $\mathscr{E}_{\nu}$, and which both have risks tending to that of $\sigma$, must be such that $\rho_{v, 1}-\rho_{v, 2}$ tends to zero in probability.

## 2. Convergence in Distribution or in Probability

Let $\mathscr{E}=\left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ be an experiment given by elements $P_{\theta} \in L=L(\mathscr{E})$. Let $Z$ be a given set with a uniform lattice $\Gamma$. Let $\mathscr{B}(\mathscr{E})$ denote the space of all transitions from $L(\mathscr{E})$ to the dual $\Gamma^{*}$ of $\Gamma$. Equivalently $\mathscr{B}(\mathscr{E})$ is the space of positive normalized bilinear forms on $\Gamma \times L$.

In Chapter 2 we relied heavily on the fact that $\mathscr{B}(\mathscr{E})$ is compact for the weak topology of pointwise convergence on $\Gamma \times L$. Also, the interpretation of an element $\rho \in \mathscr{B}(\mathscr{E})$ is that it is a "randomized statistic" with values in $Z$.

For "statistics" one often talks about convergence in distribution or convergence in probability, and this can be done even when $\mathscr{E}$ is not kept fixed. Since these forms of convergence will appear in the following sections, we shall presently recall how they may be described by suitable pseudo-metrics in the present context. For this purpose consider a bounded subset $A \subset \Gamma$ and a subset $B \subset \Theta$. To such a pair ( $A, B$ ) one can associate several pseudo-metrics as follows:
(i) $d_{A, B}\left(\rho_{1}, \rho_{2}\right)=\sup _{\theta, \gamma}\left\{\left|\gamma \rho_{1} P_{\theta}-\gamma \rho_{2} P_{\theta}\right| ; \gamma \in A, \theta \in B\right\}$;
(ii) $\overline{d_{A, B}^{\prime}}\left(\rho_{1}, \rho_{2}\right)=\sup _{\theta, \gamma}\left\{\langle | \gamma \rho_{1}-\gamma \rho_{2}\left|, P_{\theta}\right\rangle ; \gamma \in A, \theta \in B\right\}$;
(iii) $\bar{d}_{A, B}\left(\rho_{1}, \rho_{2}\right)=\sup _{\theta}\left\{\left\langle\left(\sup _{\gamma \in A}\left|\gamma \rho_{1}-\gamma \rho_{2}\right|\right), P_{\theta}\right\rangle ; \theta \in B\right\}$.

In this latter $\bar{d}$, the supremum inside the linear pairing bracket is taken in the complete vector lattice $M(\mathscr{E})$ dual of $L(\mathscr{E})$.

It is clear that one has always

$$
d_{A, B} \leq \bar{d}_{A, B}^{\prime} \leq \bar{d}_{A, B}
$$

If one lets $A$ vary in a given class $\mathscr{C}_{1}$ and $B$ vary in a class $\mathscr{C}_{2}$ the corresponding families of pseudo-metrics define various uniform structures on $\mathscr{B}(\mathscr{E})$. For two such uniformities we shall use names as follows.

Definition 1. Let $\mathscr{C}_{1}$ be the class of finite subsets of $\Gamma$ and let $\mathscr{C}_{2}$ be the class of finite subsets of $\Theta$. Then the family $d_{A, B}, A \in \mathscr{C}_{1}, B \in \mathscr{C}_{2}$ defines the structure of vague convergence in distribution on $\mathscr{B}(\mathscr{E})$. The family $\bar{d}_{A, B}, A \in \mathscr{C}_{1}, B \in \mathscr{C}_{2}$ defines the structure of convergence in probability on $\mathscr{B}(\mathscr{E})$.

These names arise as follows. First note that, in all the pseudo-metrics considered, the introduction of the set $B$ is only intended to permit assertion of uniform convergence on such sets. The major differences between $d_{A, B}, \bar{d}_{A, B}^{\prime}$, and $\bar{d}_{A, B}$ occur in the order in which the operations are performed with respect to the operation of taking a supremum on $\gamma \in A$.

If, for instance, $B$ is reduced to the one element $\{\theta\}$, then $\mu_{i}=\rho_{i} P_{\theta}$ is an element of the dual $\Gamma^{*}$ of $\Gamma$ and $d_{A, B}\left(\rho_{1}, \rho_{2}\right)=\sup \left\{\left|\gamma \mu_{1}-\gamma \mu_{2}\right| ; \gamma \in A\right\}$. Thus, $\mathscr{C}_{1}$ and $\mathscr{C}_{2}$ being as in Definition 1, the pseudo-metrics $d_{A, B}$ do correspond to the usual vague convergence of the $\mu_{i}$. When $\rho_{i}$ is considered as an estimate with values in $Z$, the image $\rho_{i} P_{\theta}$ is exactly the distribution of $\rho_{i}$ given $\theta$, often written $\mathscr{L}\left(\rho_{i} \mid P_{\theta}\right)$. Hence the name.

Before giving an interpretation of the pseudo-metrics of the type $\bar{d}_{A, B}$, it is useful to show that the uniform structures of Definition 1 would not be changed
if one would replace the class $\mathscr{C}_{1}$ of finite sets by the class of strongly precompact subsets of $\Gamma$. In fact, the following stronger result holds.

Lemma 1. Let $A$ be a precompact subset of $\Gamma$ and let $B$ denote an arbitrary subset of $\Theta$. Then, for every $\varepsilon>0$ there is a finite set $S \subset \Gamma$ and an $\varepsilon_{1}>0$ such that $\bar{d}_{S, B}^{\prime}\left(\rho_{1}, \rho_{2}\right)<\varepsilon_{1}$ implies $\bar{d}_{A, B}\left(\rho_{1}, \rho_{2}\right)<\varepsilon$.

Proof. Since $A$ is precompact one can find a finite set $S=\left\{\gamma_{j} ; j=1,2\right.$, $\ldots, m\} \subset A$ such that for each $\gamma \in A$ one has $\left|\gamma-\gamma_{j}\right|<\varepsilon / 4$ for at least one index $j$. This gives

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sup _{\gamma \in A}\left|\gamma \rho_{1}-\gamma \rho_{2}\right| & <\frac{\varepsilon}{2}+\sup _{j}\left|\gamma_{j} \rho_{1}-\gamma_{j} \rho_{2}\right| \\
& <\frac{\varepsilon}{2}+\sum_{j}\left|\gamma_{j} \rho_{1}-\gamma_{j} \rho_{2}\right|
\end{aligned}
$$

Thus $\bar{d}_{A, B}\left(\rho_{1}, \rho_{2}\right)$ will be smaller than $\varepsilon$ as soon as

$$
\sup _{\theta \in B} \sup _{\gamma \in S}\langle | \gamma \rho_{1}-\gamma \rho_{2}\left|, P_{\theta}\right\rangle<\frac{\varepsilon}{2 m}=\varepsilon_{1}
$$

This concludes the proof of the lemma.
Now, to return to the interpretation as convergence in probability, take a finite set $S$ and define on $Z$ itself a pseudo-metric $r\left(z_{1}, z_{2}\right)=\sup \left\{\left|\gamma\left(z_{1}\right)-\gamma\left(z_{2}\right)\right| ; \gamma \in S\right\}$. Let $A$ be the set of real functions defined on $Z$ and satisfying the relations $|\gamma| \leq 1$ and $\left|\gamma\left(z_{1}\right)-\gamma\left(z_{2}\right)\right| \leq r\left(z_{1}, z_{2}\right)$. One verifies immediately that this is a precompact set in $\Gamma$.

If the $\rho_{i}$ are represented by Markov kernels, say $\rho_{i} \sim \rho_{i}(d z, x)$, and if $B$ is reduced to the one element $B=\{\theta\}$, then $\bar{d}_{A, B}$ could be written

$$
\bar{d}_{A, B}\left(\rho_{1}, \rho_{2}\right)=\int\left\{\sup _{\gamma \in A}\left|\int \gamma(z) \phi(d z, x)\right|\right\} P_{\theta}(d x)
$$

with $\phi(d z, x)=\rho_{1}(d z, x)-\rho_{2}(d z, x)$.
The term $\sup _{\gamma \in A}\left|\int \gamma(z) \phi(d z, x)\right|$ appears then as the dual Lipschitz norm of the element $\phi(d z, x), x$ fixed, on the space $Z$ pseudo-metrized by $r$. In particular, if $\rho_{i}(d z, x)$ is concentrated at a particular point, say $\xi_{i}(x)$, the term in question is $r\left[\xi_{1}(x), \xi_{2}(x)\right]$ and $\bar{d}_{A, B}\left(\rho_{1}, \rho_{2}\right)$ has the usual form $\bar{d}_{A, B}\left(\rho_{1}, \rho_{2}\right)= \int r\left[\xi_{1}(x), \xi_{2}(x)\right] P_{\theta}(d x)$.

Up to this point we have considered that the experiment $\mathscr{E}=\left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ was fixed. However, if one keeps ( $Z, \Gamma$ ) and $\Theta$ fixed, the pseudo-metrics $d_{A, B}$, $\bar{d}_{A, B}^{\prime}$, and $\bar{d}_{A, B}$ can be defined simultaneously on all the spaces $\mathscr{B}(\mathscr{E})$ for all the experiments $\mathscr{E}$ indexed by $\Theta$. Thus if one considers a net $\left\{\mathscr{E}_{v} ; v \in N\right\}$ of such experiments, and, for each $\mathscr{E}_{\nu}$, a pair $\rho_{i, \nu}, i=1,2$ of elements of $\mathscr{B}_{\left(\mathscr{E}_{\nu}\right) \text {, it }}$ makes sense to say that $\rho_{1, \nu}-\rho_{2, \nu}$ tends to zero vaguely in distribution or in probability. In such cases it should be remembered that the pseudo-metrics $\bar{d}^{\prime}$ and $\bar{d}$ are defined on the experiments themselves. They are not calculable on the type of the experiment only.

In fact, suppose that $\mathscr{E}=\left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ and $\mathscr{F}=\left\{Q_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ are two experiments indexed by $\Theta$. Let $L(\mathscr{E})$ and $L(\mathscr{F})$ be their respective $L$-spaces. Let
$\operatorname{Lin}(\mathscr{E})$ and Lat ( $\mathscr{E}$ ) be, respectively, the linear span of the set $\left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ and the smallest sublattice of $L(\mathscr{E})$ generated by $\operatorname{Lin}(\mathscr{E})$. If $\mathscr{E}$ and $\mathscr{F}$ are equivalent, there is some transition $T$ from $L(\mathscr{E})$ to $L(\mathscr{F})$ such that $T P_{\theta}=Q_{\theta}$. This $T$ is an isometry of $\operatorname{Lat}(\mathscr{E})$ onto $\operatorname{Lat}(\mathscr{F})$ and is uniquely defined on $\operatorname{Lat}(\mathscr{E})$. However, it definitely need not be uniquely defined on the whole of $L(\mathscr{E})$.

The correspondence which associates $\sigma T \in \mathscr{B}(\mathscr{E})$ to $\sigma \in \mathscr{B}(\mathscr{F})$ is an isomorphism for the structures of vague convergence in distribution. It is not necessarily an isomorphism for convergence in probability. However, one can always assert that $\left|\gamma \sigma_{1} T-\gamma \sigma_{2} T\right| \leq\left|\gamma \sigma_{1}-\gamma \sigma_{2}\right| T$ so that the map in question is uniformly continuous.

The terminology vague convergence in distribution is rather awkward, so we shall often say simply convergence in distribution or vague convergence instead. However, note that even in the pseudo-metrics $d_{A, B}$ one could take for $A$ very large sets (the unit ball of $\Gamma$ for instance). The structure so defined would still be a "convergence in distribution" but the distributions then converge in the sense of the norm.

## 3. Distinguished Sequences of Statistics

Let $\Theta$ be a given set and let $\mathbb{E}=\mathbb{E}(\Theta)$ be the set of experiment types indexed by $\Theta$. Let $Z$ be another set carrying a uniform lattice $\Gamma$ with dual $\Gamma^{*}$. Experiments indexed by $\Theta$ with values in $\Gamma^{*}$ are families $D=\left\{D_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}, D_{\theta} \in \Gamma^{*}$. The space $\mathbb{D}$ of such families can be topologized by the vague convergence which says that $D_{v}=\left\{D_{\theta, v} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ converges to $D_{0}$ if $\gamma D_{\theta, v}$ converges to $\gamma D_{\theta, 0}$ for all $\theta \in \Theta$ and all $\gamma \in \Gamma$.

For purposes of notational convenience we shall denote by $t p$ the map from DD to $\mathbb{E}$ which associates with each experiment $D \in \mathbb{D}$ its type $D=t p D$ in $\mathbb{E}$.

Note that $\mathbb{D}$ with its vague topology is a compact Hausdorff space. So is $\mathbb{E}$ with its weak topology. It turns out that for most spaces ( $Z, \Gamma$ ) the canonical map tp from $\mathbb{D}$ to $\mathbb{E}$ is not continuous. In fact, assuming that $\Gamma$ separates the points of $Z$, one can show that the map tp is continuous if and only if $Z$ is a finite set. However, the map $t p$ does possess a kind of lower semicontinuity property as follows.

Proposition 1. Let $\left\{D_{v}\right\} ; v \in N$ be a net of experiments in D. Assume that $\left\{D_{v}\right\}$ converges vaguely in $\mathbb{D}$ to a limit $D_{0}$ and that the experiment types tp $D_{v}$ converge weakly in $\mathbb{E}$ to a limit $E$. Then $D_{0}$ is weaker than $E$, that is, $\delta\left(E, D_{0}\right)=0$.

Proof. Since the deficiency $\delta\left(E, D_{0}\right)$ is the supremum of the deficiencies taken on finite subsets of $\Theta$, it is enough to prove the result for the case where $\Theta$ itself is finite. Assuming this, consider a decision problem with a set $K$ of possible decisions and a loss function $W$ defined on $K \times \Theta$. Take $K$ finite and assume $0 \leq W \leq 1$. On $K$ take the lattice $C(K)$ of bounded functions. Now let $\sigma$ be a particular decision procedure, transition from $L\left(D_{0}\right)$ to the dual of $C(K)$. It has a risk given by the expressions $W_{\theta} \sigma D_{\theta, 0}$.

According to Theorem 1, Chapter 1, for a given $\varepsilon>0$ there is some other



procedure $\rho$ which (i) is continuous in the sense that $u \rho \in \Gamma$ for each $u \in C(K)$, and (ii) is also such that

$$
\sup _{\theta}\left|W_{\theta} \sigma D_{\theta, 0}-W_{\theta} \rho D_{\theta, 0}\right|<\varepsilon
$$

Since $\rho$ maps $C(K)$ into $\Gamma$ it may be used for any one of the experiments in DD . Its risk at $D_{\theta, v} \in D_{v}$ is given by $W_{\theta} \rho D_{\theta, v}$. Since $D_{\theta, v}$ converges vaguely to $D_{\theta, 0}$, there is some $v(\varepsilon) \in N$ such that $v \geq v(\varepsilon)$ implies $\left|W_{\theta} \rho D_{\theta, v}-W_{\theta} \rho D_{\theta, 0}\right|< \varepsilon$ for all $\theta \in \Theta$. This yields

$$
W_{\theta} \rho D_{\theta, v} \leq W_{\theta} \sigma D_{\theta, 0}+2 \varepsilon
$$

One can also assume that $v(\varepsilon)$ has been chosen such that $v \geq v(\varepsilon)$ implies $\Delta\left(D_{v}, E\right)<\varepsilon$. If so, there is some procedure $\tau$ available on $E=\left\{E_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ such that $W_{\theta} \tau E_{\theta} \leq W_{\theta} \rho D_{\theta, v}+\varepsilon$. Finally this gives $W_{\theta} \tau E_{\theta} \leq W_{\theta} \sigma D_{\theta, 0}+3 \varepsilon$. This is true for every finite $K$, every $W$, and every $\sigma$. Thus, by Theorem 2, Chapter 2, one has $\delta\left(E, D_{0}\right) \leq 3 \varepsilon$ and the result follows since $\varepsilon$ is arbitrary.

In view of the foregoing proposition, we shall introduce a definition as follows. Let $\left\{D_{v}\right\} ; v \in N$ be a net in $\mathbb{D}$ and let $\Phi=\left\{\left(D_{v}, t p D_{v}\right)\right\}, v \in N$ be the associated net in $\mathbb{D} \times \mathbb{E}$. Since $\mathbb{D} \times \mathbb{E}$ is compact the net $\Phi$ possesses in $\mathbb{D} \times \mathbb{E}$ a nonempty compact set of cluster points.

Definition 2. The net $\left\{D_{v}\right\} ; v \in N$ will be called stable if every cluster point $(D, E)$ of the associated net $\Phi$ is such that $\delta(D, E)=0$.

In other words, $\left\{D_{v}\right\}$ is stable if its set of cluster points is contained in the graph of the function tp.

To give equivalent characterizations of stable nets we shall henceforth assume that $\Theta$ is finite. If so, each $D \in \mathbb{D}$ defines an element $\mu_{D}=\sum_{\theta} D_{\theta}$ of $\Gamma^{*}$ and a vector $V$ of likelihood ratios

$$
\begin{aligned}
& V=\left\{V_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\} \\
& V_{\theta}=\frac{d D_{\theta}}{d \mu_{D}}
\end{aligned}
$$

The image of $\mu_{D}$ by the map $V$ is the Blackwell canonical measure of $D$ on the unit simplex $U$ of $R^{\Theta}$. Of course the map $V$ depends on the experiment $D$ under consideration. It should be noted $V_{D}$. However, to avoid excess of indices we shall often condense the notation and write, for instance, $V_{v}$ for the map associated with an experiment $D_{v}$.

Let $C=C(U)$ be the space of bounded continuous functions on $U$ and let $C^{*}$ be the dual of $C$. Recall that a transition from $\Gamma^{*}$ to $C^{*}$ is called "continuous" if its transpose maps $C$ into $\Gamma$.

Finally, if $S$ is a subset of $\Theta$ and $D_{v}$ is indexed by $\Theta$, the experiment $\left\{D_{v, \theta} ; \theta \in S\right\}$ will be noted $D_{v}(S)$. With these preliminaries one can state the following.

Theorem 1. Assume that $\Theta$ is finite. Let $\left\{D_{\nu}\right\} ; v \in N$ be a net or filter in $\mathbb{D}$. Then the following statements are all equivalent.
(i) The net $\left\{D_{v}\right\}$ is stable.
(ii) For every set $S \subset \Theta$ containing two elements, the net $\left\{D_{v}(S)\right\}$ is stable.
(iii) For each $\varepsilon>0$, there is a finite set $J$ and continuous transitions $T_{\varepsilon, j}$ from $\Gamma^{*}$ to $C^{*}$ such that

$$
\limsup _{v} \inf _{j \in J} \sup _{\theta}\left\|V_{v} D_{\theta, v}-T_{\varepsilon, j} D_{\theta, v}\right\|_{D}<\varepsilon
$$

(the norm being the dual Lipschitz norm of $C^{*}$ ).
(iv) For each $\varepsilon>0$ there is a finite set $J$ and elements $\gamma_{\theta, \varepsilon, j} \in \Gamma, 0 \leq \gamma_{\theta, \varepsilon, j} \leq 1$ such that

$$
\limsup _{v} \inf _{j \in J} \sup _{\theta}\langle | V_{\theta, v}-\gamma_{\theta, \varepsilon, j}\left|, \mu_{D_{v}}\right\rangle<\varepsilon,
$$

where $V_{\theta, v}$ is the density

$$
V_{\theta, v}=\frac{d D_{\theta, v}}{d \mu_{D_{v}}}
$$

and

$$
\mu_{D_{v}}=\sum_{\theta} D_{\theta, v}
$$

Proof. That (i) implies (ii) is clear. To prove the converse, consider a particular cluster point $\left(D_{0}, E\right)$ of the net $\left\{\left(D_{v}, t p D_{v}\right)\right\}$ in $\mathbb{D} \times \mathbb{E}$. By Proposition 1 the experiment $D_{0}$ is weaker than $E$. However, if (ii) holds, $D_{0}$ and $E$ are pairwise
equivalent. This entails $\delta\left(D_{0}, E\right)=0$. Indeed, since $D_{0}$ is weaker than $E$, there is a transition $K$ such that $D_{\theta, 0}=K E_{\theta}$. One can then form another experiment $F$, equivalent to $E$ with "marginals" $E$ and $D_{0}$, respectively. In measuretheoretic notation this experiment would be written

$$
F_{\theta}(d x, d y)=E_{\theta}(d x) K(x, d y)
$$

Pairwise equivalence of $D_{0}$ and $E$ becomes pairwise sufficiency of the second coordinate, hence sufficiency (Chapter 5, Section 3, Theorem 1).

To prove that (iii) implies (i), one may as well assume that $J$ has only one element, that $D_{\nu}$ converges in $\mathbb{D}$ to a limit $D_{0}$ and that $t p D_{\nu}$ converges in $\mathbb{E}$ to a limit $E$. Then the Blackwell representatives $V_{v} D_{\theta, v}$ converge in $C^{*}$ to limits $B_{\theta}$, and the images $T_{\varepsilon} D_{\theta, v}$ converge to $T_{\varepsilon} D_{\theta, 0}$. Thus the inequality in (iii) may be replaced by the equivalent simpler inequality $\sup _{\theta}\left\|B_{\theta}-T_{\varepsilon} D_{\theta, 0}\right\|_{D} \leq \varepsilon$. Let $\mathscr{F}_{\varepsilon}$ be the experiment $\mathscr{F}_{\varepsilon}=\left\{T_{\varepsilon} D_{\theta, 0} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$. Then $\mathscr{F}_{\varepsilon}$ is weaker than the experiment $D_{0}=\left\{D_{\theta, 0} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$. Let $\varepsilon$ tend to zero; $\left\{\mathscr{F}_{\varepsilon}\right\}$ has cluster points. Let $\mathscr{F}$ be one of them. Then since each $\mathscr{F}_{\varepsilon}$ is weaker than $D_{0}$, so is the cluster point $\mathscr{F}$. Still, as $\varepsilon \rightarrow 0$, the $T_{\varepsilon} D_{\theta, 0}$ have for limits the $B_{\theta}$ of the Blackwell representation of $E$. Thus, by Proposition 1, the cluster point $\mathscr{F}$ is at least as strong as $E$. In summary, $E$ is weaker than $\mathscr{F}$ and $\mathscr{F}$ is weaker than $D_{0}$ which implies that $E$ is weaker than $D_{0}$ and therefore equivalent to $D_{0}$.

Suppose now that (iv) holds with $J$ reduced to one element. For each $v$, let $\beta(\theta, v)$ be constants selected so that $\left\langle\beta(\theta, v) \gamma_{\theta, \varepsilon}, \mu_{D_{v}}\right\rangle=1$. It is certainly possible to find such constants for sufficiently large $v$, and in fact one will have $|\beta(\theta, v)-1|<2 \varepsilon$ for all sufficiently large $v$. The family $\left\{\beta(\theta, v) \gamma_{\theta, \varepsilon} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ is a partition of unity on $Z$. It defines a continuous transition $T_{\varepsilon, \nu}$ from $\Gamma^{*}$ to $C^{*}$. For all large $v$ one will have $\langle | V_{\theta, v}-\beta(\theta, v) \gamma_{\theta, \varepsilon}\left|, \mu_{D_{v}}\right\rangle<2 \varepsilon$ and therefore $\left\|V_{v} D_{\theta, v}-T_{\varepsilon, v} D_{\theta, v}\right\|_{D}<2 \varepsilon$. However, since the various $T_{\varepsilon, v}$ differ by at most $2 \varepsilon k$ for $k=\operatorname{card} \Theta$, one will have $\left\|V_{v} D_{\theta, v}-T_{\varepsilon, v_{0}} D_{\theta, v}\right\|_{D}<4 \varepsilon k$ as soon as both $v_{0}$ and $v$ are sufficiently large. Thus (iii) holds and (i) follows.

To complete the proof it remains to show that when statement (i) holds so does (iv). We shall first prove this under the supplementary assumption that the net $\left\{\left(D_{v}, t p D_{v}\right)\right\}, v \in N$ converges in $\mathbb{D} \times \mathbb{E}$ to a limit $(D, E)$.

If $D=\left\{D_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ is fixed, for any $\varepsilon>0$, one can find elements $u_{0} \in \Gamma$ such that $0 \leq u_{\theta} \leq 1, \sum u_{\theta}=1$ and $\sum_{\theta}\langle | V_{\theta}-u_{\theta}|, \mu\rangle<\varepsilon$. Here $\mu=\sum_{\theta} D_{\theta}$ and $V_{\theta}$ is the density $V_{\theta}=d D_{\theta} / d \mu$.

To go further we shall proceed as in Lemma 1 and Proposition 5 of Chapter 4. To this effect consider a decision problem where the set of possible decisions is the simplex $U$ of $R^{\Theta}$. Let $e_{\theta}$ denote the element of $U$ whose $\theta$-th coordinate is unity, the others being then automatically zero.

Take for loss function $W_{\theta}(t)$ the square Euclidean distance $\left\|e_{\theta}-t\right\|^{2}$. If $T$ is a transition from $\Gamma^{*}$ to $C^{*}$, let $S(T, D)$ be the sum of the risks $W_{\theta} T D_{\theta}$ for the experiment $D$. Then, easy algebra shows that the transition which minimizes the risk sum is the likelihood ratio vector $V$ itself. The minimum risk sum is $M(D)=S(V, D)$ equal to a certain sum $\sum_{s \neq t} K(s, t)$ for $(s, t) \in \Theta \times \Theta$ and $K(s, t)=\left\langle V_{s} V_{t}, \mu\right\rangle$. Finally, if $T$ is any transition the differences $S(T, D)- M(D)$ could be written, using measure-theoretic notations, in the form

$$
S(T, D)-S(V, D)=\int\|V(x)-u\|^{2} T(d u, x) \mu(d x)
$$

If $t p D_{v}$ converges to $t p D$ then the minimum risk sums $M\left(D_{v}\right)$ converge to $M(D)$. Now let $T$ be the transition formed by the partition $u=\left\{u_{\theta}\right\}$ selected above. For this $T$ we have

$$
S(T, D)-M(D)=\left\langle\|V-u\|^{2}, \mu\right\rangle \leq \sum_{\theta}\langle | V_{\theta}-u_{\theta}|, \mu\rangle<\varepsilon / 2
$$

Thus, for all sufficiently large $\nu$ one will have $\left|M\left(D_{\nu}\right)-M(D)\right|<\varepsilon / 2$ and, since $D_{v} \rightarrow D$ and since $T$ is continuous,

$$
S\left(T, D_{v}\right)-M\left(D_{v}\right)<2 \varepsilon
$$

This implies $\left\langle\left\|V_{v}-u\right\|^{2}, \mu_{v}\right\rangle<2 \varepsilon$ and therefore $\langle | V_{v}-u\left|, \mu_{v}\right\rangle \leq \sqrt{2 \varepsilon}$. Since $\varepsilon$ can be chosen arbitrarily, the result is proved for the case where ( $D_{v}, t p D_{v}$ ) converges to $(D, t p D)$.

For the general case, let $K$ be the set of all cluster points of the net $\left\{\left(D_{\nu}, t p D_{\nu}\right)\right\}, v \in N$ in the space $\mathbb{D} \times \mathbb{E}$. When (i) holds, $K$ is a compact subset of the graph of
the map $t p$. Therefore, $t p$ is continuous on the projection $K_{1}$ of $K$ in $\mathbb{D}$. If $\left\{D_{\alpha}\right\}, \alpha \in A$ is some net with $D_{\alpha} \in K_{1}$ which converges to a $D \in K_{1}$, then the preceding argument becomes applicable.

Using the compactness of $K_{1}$, one concludes as above that there is some finite set $J$ and partitions of unity $u_{j}=\left\{u_{\theta, j} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}, j \in J$ with $u_{\theta, j} \in \Gamma$ such that if $D \in K_{1}$ and $\mu_{D}=\sum_{\theta} D_{\theta}$ then

$$
\sum_{\theta}\langle | V_{\theta}-u_{\theta, j}\left|, \mu_{D}\right\rangle<\varepsilon
$$

for at least one $j \in J$. The final result follows as before, concluding the proof of the theorem.

Remark 1. What the theorem says is that a net is stable if and only if one can approximately recover the distributions of likelihood ratios from the $D_{\theta, v}$ in a continuous manner, or according to (iv), if and only if one can approximately recover the likelihood ratio themselves in a continuous manner.

Remark 2. One can prove directly that (i) implies (iii) without going through the argument that (iv) implies (iii). When the net $\left\{\left(D_{v}, t p D_{v}\right)\right\}$ converges in $\mathbb{D} \times \mathbb{E}$ to a limit $(D, E), E=t p D$, this is easily done as follows. Let $B= \left\{B_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ be the Blackwell representation of $E$. If $D$ and $E$ are equivalent then there is some transition $T_{1}$ such that $B_{\theta}=T_{1} D_{\theta}$ for all $\theta \in \Theta$. Let $\Lambda$ be the set of Lipschitz functions $f$ defined on $U$ and such that $|f| \leq 1, \mid f(u)-f\left(u^{\prime}\right)\left|\leq\left|u-u^{\prime}\right|\right.$. This is a compact set. Therefore Theorem 1, Chapter 1, implies the existence of a continuous transition $T$ such that $\left|f T D_{\theta}-f T_{1} D_{\theta}\right|<\varepsilon$ for all $f \in \Lambda$ and all $\theta$ in the finite set $\Theta$. This inequality may be written $\left\|T D_{\theta}-B_{\theta}\right\|_{D}<\varepsilon$. The result follows easily.

This being as is, let us pass to another related concept which involves nets $\left\{\mathscr{E}_{v}\right\}, v \in N$ of experiments which have the form $\mathscr{E}_{v}=\left\{P_{\theta, v} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ with arbitrary spaces $L\left(\mathscr{E}_{v}\right)$.

Suppose that ( $Z, \Gamma$ ) is kept fixed but that for each $v$ one has a transition $S_{v}$ from $L\left(\mathscr{E}_{v}\right)$ to $\Gamma^{*}$. As mentioned before, $S_{v}$ represents a randomized estimate or statistic with values in $Z$. It has distributions $D_{\theta, v}=S_{v} P_{\theta, v}=\mathscr{L}\left(S_{v} \mid P_{\theta, v}\right)$. The experiment $D_{v}=\left\{S_{v} P_{\theta, v} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ is, of course, weaker than $\mathscr{E}_{v}$ itself. However, it may happen that not much information is lost in passing from $\mathscr{E}_{v}$ to $D_{v}$. For instance, it may happen that $\Delta\left(D_{v}, \mathscr{E}_{v}\right) \rightarrow 0$. This is some form of "asymptotic sufficiency" or more exactly, "asymptotic efficiency" of the $S_{v}$. However, it does not imply in any way that, if $\mathscr{E}_{v} \rightarrow \mathscr{E}$ and if $D_{v} \rightarrow D$ in $\mathbb{D}$, the type of $D$ is the same as that of $\mathscr{E}$. Of course, according to Proposition 1 one would always have $\delta(\mathscr{E}, D)=0$. We shall use definition as follows.

Definition. Let $\left\{\mathscr{E}_{v}\right\}, v \in N, \mathscr{E}_{v}=\left\{P_{\theta, v} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ be a net of experiments indexed by $\Theta$. For each $v$ let $S_{v}$ be a transition from $L\left(\mathscr{E}_{v}\right)$ to $\Gamma^{*}$. Let $D_{v}= \left\{D_{\theta, v} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ with $D_{\theta, v}=S_{v} P_{\theta, v}$. The net $\left\{S_{v}\right\}$ is called distinguished if for every cluster point $(D, E)$ of the net $\left\{\left(D_{\nu}, \mathscr{E}_{\nu}\right)\right\}$ in $\mathbb{D} \times \mathbb{E}$ the deficiency $\delta(D, E)$ is zero.

Thus $\left\{S_{v}\right\}$ is distinguished if and only if (i) the net $\left\{D_{v}\right\}$ is stable and (ii) for every finite set $A \subset \Theta$ the deficiencies $\delta_{A}\left(D_{v}, \mathscr{E}_{v}\right)$ converge to zero. An easy consequence of Theorem 1 is as follows.

Proposition 2. Let $\left\{\mathscr{E}_{v}\right\}, v \in N$ be a net of experiments as described above. For each $v$ let $S_{v}$ and $S_{v}^{\prime}$ be two transitions from $L\left(\mathscr{E}_{v}\right)$ to $\Gamma^{*}$. Suppose that for each $\theta \in \Theta$ and each $\gamma \in \Gamma$ one has

$$
\lim _{v}\left\langle\gamma, S_{v}^{\prime} P_{\theta, v}\right\rangle-\left\langle\gamma, S_{v} P_{\theta, v}\right\rangle=0
$$

Then if $\left\{S_{\nu}\right\}$ is distinguished so is $\left\{S_{\nu}^{\prime}\right\}$.
Proof. This is an immediate consequence of the property (iii) in Theorem 1. Proposition 2 above uses a weak form of convergence, namely convergence in distribution. To state a converse in a stronger form, we shall use a rather fine kind of convergence in probability. Unfortunately the result cannot be stated without making use of filters or nets and subnets. For the convenience of the reader we recall that subnets are obtained through a process which involves two separate directed sets, say $N$ and $\Xi$. If one has a net indexed by $N$, one forms a subnet indexed by $\Xi$ by using a map $\xi \mapsto \nu(\xi)$ of $\Xi$ into $N$ subject to the condition that for each $v_{0} \in N$ there is some $\xi_{0} \in \Xi$ such that $\xi \geq \xi_{0}$ implies $v(\xi) \geq v_{0}$. We shall call the pair consisting of $\Xi$ and of the map $\xi \mapsto v(\xi)$ a subdirection of $N$.

Consider then a net $\left\{\mathscr{E}_{v}\right\}, v \in N$ of experiments $\mathscr{E}_{v}=\left\{P_{\theta, v} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ indexed by a given set $\Theta$. For each $v \in N$ let $T_{v}$ be a transition from $L\left(\mathscr{E}_{v}\right)$ to $\Gamma^{*}$. Furthermore let $[\Xi, \xi \mapsto v(\xi)]$ be a subdirection of $N$. For each $\xi \in \Xi$, let $T_{\xi}^{\prime}$ be another transition from $L\left[\mathscr{E}_{v(\xi)}\right]$ to $\Gamma^{*}$. This yields two nets $\left\{D_{v(\xi)}\right\}$ and $\left\{D_{\xi}^{\prime}\right\}, \xi \in \Xi$ of experiments given by

$$
D_{v(\xi)}=\left\{T_{v(\xi)} P_{\theta, v(\xi)} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}
$$

and

$$
D_{\xi}^{\prime}=\left\{T_{\xi}^{\prime} P_{\theta, v(\xi)} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}
$$

By analogy with Definition 2 one can introduce a definition as follows.
Definition 3. Consider a net $\left\{\mathscr{E}_{v}, T_{v}\right\}, v \in N$ as above. The net $\left\{T_{v}\right\}$ will be called stochastically stable if for every subdirection $[\Xi, \xi \mapsto v(\xi)]$ with associated transitions $T_{\xi}^{\prime}$ the relations

$$
\lim _{\xi} \sup _{\theta}\langle | \gamma T_{\xi}^{\prime}-\gamma T_{\nu(\xi)}\left|, P_{\theta, \nu(\xi)}\right\rangle=0
$$

for all $\gamma \in \Gamma$ imply that for every two point set $B \subset \Theta$ the distances $\Delta_{B}\left[D_{\xi}^{\prime}\right.$, $D_{\nu(\xi)}$ ] converge to zero.

Theorem 2. Let $\left\{\mathscr{E}_{v}\right\}, v \in N$ be a net of experiments $\mathscr{E}_{v}=\left\{P_{\theta, v} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$, For each $v$ let $T_{v}$ be a transition from $L\left(\mathscr{E}_{v}\right)$ to $\Gamma^{*}$. Assume that $\left\{T_{v}\right\}$ is stochastically stable. Then the net $\left\{D_{v}\right\}$ is stable. If, in addition, $\Delta_{B}\left(\mathscr{E}_{v}, D_{v}\right) \rightarrow 0$ for every two point set $B \subset \Theta$, then the net $\left\{T_{\nu}\right\}$ is distinguished.

Proof. Let $\Pi$ be the set of pointed finite partitions of unity $\pi=\left\{\left(\gamma_{j}, z_{j}\right) ; j \in J\right\}$ with $\gamma_{j} \in \Gamma, \gamma_{j} \geq 0, \sum_{j} \gamma_{j}=1$, and with $z_{j} \in Z$ such that $\gamma_{j}\left(z_{j}\right)>0$. This
set may be directed, a partition $\pi^{\prime}=\left\{\left(\gamma_{k}^{\prime}, z_{k}^{\prime}\right)\right\}$ being finer than $\pi$ if for each $k$ there is a $j$ such that $\gamma_{k}^{\prime}(z)>0$ implies $\gamma_{j}(z)>0$. This will be written $\pi \leq \pi^{\prime}$. Let $\Xi=N \times \Pi$ be the set of pairs $\xi=(v, \pi)$ with $v \in N$ and $\pi \in \Pi$, Direct $\Xi$ by the relation $(v, \pi) \leq\left(v^{\prime}, \pi^{\prime}\right)$ if $v \leq v^{\prime}$ and $\pi \leq \pi^{\prime}$. If $\pi$ is the partition $\pi=\left\{\left(\gamma_{j}, z_{j}\right) ; j \in J\right\}$ let $K_{\pi}$ be the map of $\Gamma$ into itself defined by $\gamma K_{\pi}=\sum_{j} \gamma\left(z_{j}\right) \gamma_{j}$. The transpose of $K_{\pi}$ is a transition from $\Gamma^{*}$ to $\Gamma^{*}$. For every pair $\xi=(v, \pi)$, let $T_{(v, \pi)}^{\prime}$ be the transition $T_{(v, \pi)}^{\prime}=K_{\pi} T_{v}$.

If $\gamma$ is any element of $\Gamma$, the norm

$$
\left\|\gamma-\gamma K_{\pi}\right\|=\sup _{z}\left|\sum_{j}\left[\gamma(z)-\gamma\left(z_{j}\right)\right] \gamma_{j}(z)\right|
$$

is at most equal to the oscillation of $\gamma$ on the supports of the $\gamma_{j}$. Thus, for each $\gamma$ one has $\lim _{\xi}\left\|\gamma-\gamma K_{\pi}\right\|=0$ and therefore

$$
\lim _{\xi} \sup _{\theta}\langle | \gamma T_{\xi}^{\prime}-\gamma T_{v(\xi)}\left|, P_{\theta, v(\xi)}\right\rangle=0
$$

where if $\xi=(v, \pi)$ one writes $v(\xi)=v$ and $\pi(\xi)=\pi$.
Letting $D_{v(\xi)}$ and $D_{\xi}^{\prime}$ be the corresponding experiments, the assumed stochastic stability of $T_{v}$ insures that $\Delta_{B}\left[D_{v(\xi)}, D_{\xi}^{\prime}\right] \rightarrow 0$ for all two point sets $B \subset \Theta$. Since $D_{\xi}^{\prime}$ is obtained from $D_{v(\xi)}$ by the transition $K_{\pi(\xi)}$, the experiment $D_{\xi}^{\prime}$ is weaker than $D_{v(\xi)}$. Thus if $\left(E, E^{\prime}\right)$ is a weak cluster point, in $\mathbb{E} \times \mathbb{E}$, of the net of types $\left(t p D_{v(\xi)}, t p D_{\xi}^{\prime}\right)$, one concludes that $E^{\prime}$ is weaker than $E$ but equivalent to $E$ pairwise. Thus $E$ and $E^{\prime}$ are equivalent.

At this point it is visible that to prove the theorem it is enough to consider the case where $\Theta$ is finite (or even a two point set) and where the net $\left(D_{v}, t p D_{v}\right)$ converges in $\mathbb{D} \times \mathbb{E}$ to a limit $(D, E)$. One can also assume that the $\mathscr{E}_{v}$ themselves have types which converge in $\mathbb{E}$ to a limit, say $E^{+}$.

If $D_{\nu}$ converges in $\mathbb{D}$ to the limit $D=\left\{D_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$, then the net $\left\{D_{\xi}^{\prime}\right\}$ converges in $\mathbb{D}$ to the same limit $D$, as follows readily from the fact that $\lim _{\pi}\left\|\gamma-\gamma K_{\pi}\right\|=0$. Since, as shown above, $t p D_{v(\xi)}$ and $t p D_{\xi}^{\prime}$ have the same limit $E$ in $\mathbb{E}$, it follows that $\left(D_{\xi}^{\prime}, t p D_{\xi}^{\prime}\right)$ converges to $(D, E)$ in $\mathbb{D} \times \mathbb{E}$. Take an $\varepsilon>0$ and let $G=\{F \in \mathbb{E}, \Delta(E, F)<\varepsilon\}$ be a neighborhood of $E$ in $\mathbb{E}$. Then there is some pair $\xi_{0}=\left(v_{0}, \pi_{0}\right)$ such that $\xi=(v, \pi), \xi \geq \xi_{0}$ implies that $t p D_{\xi}^{\prime} \in G$.

Fix a particular $\pi \geq \pi_{0}$ and let it be of the form $\pi=\left\{\left(\gamma_{j}, z_{j}\right) ; j \in J\right\}$. Let $D_{v, \pi}^{\prime}$ be the experiment $D_{v, \pi}^{\prime}=\left\{K_{\pi} D_{\theta, v} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ with $D_{\theta, v}=T_{v} P_{\theta, v}$. For fixed $\pi$ the net $\left\{K_{\pi} D_{\theta, v}\right\}, v \in N$ tends vaguely to the limit $F_{\theta, \pi}=K_{\pi} D_{\theta}$. However, all the $K_{\pi} D_{\theta, v}$ are carried by the fixed finite set $\left\{z_{j} ; j \in J\right\}$. Thus, for them, vague convergence and norm convergence are equivalent. In other words, there is a $v_{1} \geq v_{0}$ such that $v \geq v_{1}$ implies $\left\|K_{\pi} D_{\theta, v}-K_{\pi} D_{\theta}\right\|<\varepsilon$ and therefore $\Delta\left(D_{\nu, \pi}^{\prime}, F_{\pi}\right)<\varepsilon$ with $F_{\pi}=\left\{K_{\pi} D_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$. This particular $F_{\pi}$ is weaker than $D$ itself. Consequently the above inequality implies also $\delta\left(D, D_{\nu, \pi}^{\prime}\right)<\varepsilon$. Since $\Delta\left(D_{v, \pi}^{\prime}, E\right)<\varepsilon$, one concludes that $\delta(D, E)<2 \varepsilon$. Therefore, since
$\varepsilon$ is arbitrary one can assert that $\delta(D, E)=0$ and, by Proposition 1, that $\Delta(D, E)=0$.

This proves the stability of the net $\left\{D_{v}\right\}$. To obtain the conclusion that $\left\{T_{v}\right\}$ is distinguished, just note that, under the supplementary assumption made for the purpose, the limits $E$ of $t p D_{v}$ and $E^{+}$of $t p \mathscr{E}_{v}$ are pairwise equivalent. They are also such that $\delta\left(E^{+}, E\right)=0$ and therefore equivalent. This concludes the proof of the theorem.

Remark 1. When $\left\{\mathscr{E}_{v}\right\}, v \in N$ is a sequence, that is, when $N$ is the set of positive integers in their natural order, and $\Theta$ is finite or countable, one can proceed with subsequences without using the full force of Definition 3. It is sufficient to consider sequences $\left\{T_{\nu}^{\prime}\right\}$ satisfying the relation

$$
\lim _{v} \sup _{\theta}\langle | \gamma T_{v}^{\prime}-\gamma T_{v}\left|, P_{\theta, v}\right\rangle=0
$$

To see this, note that one can find a countably generated sublattice $\Gamma_{0} \subset \Gamma$ which is completion sufficient for all the experiments $D_{v}$. In this case, one needs to consider only sequences of partitions $\pi$. Application of the usual diagonal arguments will then give the results.

Remark 2. In the foregoing arguments we have used two different but related concepts, respectively, associated with the words "stable" and "distinguished." It should be clear that the technical parts of the argument revolve almost entirely around "stability." However, the nice property for many statistical purposes is that of being distinguished. We shall encounter it again in the chapter devoted to invariance and use it to prove a general version of what is called Hajek's convolution theorem. The result in question is a refinement of the following remark.

Suppose that $\left\{T_{v}\right\}$ is distinguished for the net $\left\{\mathscr{E}_{v}\right\}, \mathscr{E}_{v}=\left\{P_{\theta, v} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$. Suppose also that the $T_{\nu} P_{\theta, \nu}$ converge to limits $D_{\theta}$. Consider some other space $Z^{\prime}$ with a uniform lattice $\Gamma^{\prime}$ and, for each $v$, a transition $T_{\nu}^{\prime}$ from $L\left(\mathscr{E}_{\nu}\right)$ to the dual of $\Gamma^{\prime}$. Suppose that the distributions $T_{v}^{\prime} P_{\theta, v}$ converge to limits $D_{\theta}^{\prime}$. Then there is a transition $K$ from $\Gamma^{*}$ to the dual of $\Gamma^{\prime}$ such that $D_{\theta}^{\prime}=K D_{\theta}$. Indeed, by Proposition 1 the experiment $D^{\prime}=\left\{D_{\theta}^{\prime} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ is weaker than the limit of the experiments $\mathscr{E}_{v}$, which, since $\left\{T_{v}\right\}$ is distinguished, is no other than $D=\left\{D_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$.

To conclude this section, let us note that the property of being distinguished is almost essential for the full validity of certain arguments often carried out in practice. For this purpose consider a net $\left\{\mathscr{E}_{v}\right\}, v \in N$ where each $\mathscr{E}_{v}$ is a binary experiment $\mathscr{E}_{v}=\left\{P_{0, v}, P_{1, v}\right\}$. Let $T_{v}$ be a statistic available on $\mathscr{E}_{v}$. Explicitly, $T_{v}$ is a transition from $L\left(\mathscr{E}_{v}\right)$ to $\Gamma^{*}$. Let us assume that $T_{v} P_{i, v}$ converges to a limit $D_{i}$, for $i=0,1$.

Consider test problems in which one wishes to distinguish between $i=0$ and $i=1$ and suppose that for the limit $D=\left(D_{0}, D_{1}\right)$ one knows the "best," that is Neyman-Pearson, test, say $\psi$ defined on $Z$.

It is then very tempting to use the test $\psi T_{v}$ for the pair $D_{v}=\left(D_{0, v}, D_{1, v}\right)$ itself. In fact many people will not hesitate to do so, at least if they can convince themselves that the level $\psi T_{v} D_{0, v}$ and the power $\psi T_{v} D_{1, v}$ both converge to the
limits achievable for $D$, that is, $\alpha=\psi D_{0}$ and $\beta=\psi D_{1}$ respectively. Suppose then that the statistician does so and claims in addition that the tests obtained that way are asymptotically optimal.

This may be a valid claim, especially if it is done only for a particular pair $(\alpha, \beta)$, or a small interval of values of $\alpha$. However, not too many claims of this nature can be made without implying that the net $\left\{T_{v}\right\}$ is distinguished.

Because of the lack of continuity of the correspondence between levels $\alpha$ and corresponding powers we shall describe the situation in a Bayesian context, allowing prior probabilities ( $p, q$ ), $p+q=1$ which may vary in the interval $[0,1)$.

Suppose then that it is desired to test $i=0$ against $i=1$ with a gain equal to unity if the correct decision is made, and with gain zero if the wrong decision is made. According to Chapter 3, for prior probabilities ( $p, q$ ) and for the experiment $D_{v}$, the optimal average gain in this problem is the value $g_{v, p}=\left\|p D_{0, v} \vee q D_{1, v}\right\|$.

Passing to the representation on the interval $[0,1]$, with $f_{v}$ equal to the density of $D_{1, v}$ with respect to $D_{0, v}+D_{1, v}$ and with $m_{v}$ equal to the image of $D_{0, v}+D_{1, v}$ by $f_{v}$, the value $g_{v, p}$ may also be written

$$
g_{v, p}=\int\{p(1-f) \vee(1-p) f\} m_{v}(d f)
$$

If $p$ is allowed to vary in some interval $J$ the functions in the curly brackets are convex, but no combination of them is strictly convex outside $J$.

Consider then the corresponding gain $g_{p}$ for the experiment $D=\left(D_{0}, D_{1}\right)$ formed by the limiting distributions. Let $m$ be the measure which represents $D$ on the interval $[0,1]$. Let $M$ be any cluster point of the net $\left\{m_{v}\right\}$. According to Proposition 1, $m$ is weaker than $M$ so that $M$ is obtainable from $m$ by dilation and $\liminf _{\nu} g_{\nu, p} \geq g_{p}$ for all $p$.

The hopeful procedure described above would have it that for some set of values of $p$ the two limits are in fact the same. This cannot be unless the dilation which give $M$ from $m$ never moves any masses across one of the "corner" points $p$ of the functions $p(1-f) \vee(1-p) f$. However, it may well occur if the dilation moves masses only on each side of such corner points. In other words, the performance of test statistics are not affected if one modifies them monotonically away from their appointed cutoff values.

However, if one lets $p$ range over some dense subset of $[0,1]$, or if one adds to the above testing problems any decision problem which corresponds to a strictly convex element $\phi$ of the extended Choquet space, convergence of the gains will necessarily entail that $m$ and $M$ are the same. In such a case the net $\left\{T_{\nu}\right\}$ has to be distinguished.

For instance, if one takes $p$ at random from some probability measure $\pi$ which has $[0,1]$ for support, convergence of the average gains $\int g_{v, p} \pi(d p)$ to $\int g_{p}(d p)$ cannot hold unless $\left\{T_{\nu}\right\}$ is distinguished.

## 4. Lower-Semicontinuity for Spaces of Risk Functions

In this section it will be assumed that one is given a set $\Theta$, a set $Z$ with a uniform lattice $\Gamma$, and a particular loss function $W$ defined on $\Theta \times Z$. Further, we assume that a particular experiment $\mathscr{F}=\left\{Q_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ has been singled out for special attention. As usual it is assumed that $W$ satisfies the conditions $\inf _{z} W_{\theta}(z)>-\infty$ for all $\theta \in \Theta$ and that the risks are defined as explained in Chapter 2.

The main intent of this section is to show that if a function $f$ is not a possible risk function for the pair ( $\mathscr{F}, W$ ) it is not possible either for pairs ( $\mathscr{E}, V$ ) which are sufficiently close to $(\mathscr{F}, W)$. Another assertion says that if $\rho$, available on $\mathscr{E}$, yields risks $V_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} \rho P_{\boldsymbol{\theta}} \leq f(\theta)$, then there is a $\sigma$ available on $\mathscr{F}$ which has a distribution close to that of $\rho$.

In order to state the results in a brief form we shall call special those loss functions $V$ defined on $\Theta \times Z$ which are such that $z \mapsto V_{\theta}(z)$ belongs to $\Gamma$ for each $\theta$. Thus, the risk $W_{\theta} \sigma Q_{\theta}$ of a procedure $\sigma$ available on $\mathscr{F}$ is the supremum

$$
W_{\theta} \sigma Q_{\theta}=\sup V_{\theta} \sigma Q_{\theta}
$$

taken over all special loss functions $V$ such that $V \leq W$ (that is $V_{\theta}(z) \leq W_{\theta}(z)$ for all $\left.(\theta, z)\right)$. It will be convenient to use a directed set $\mathscr{S}$ of triplets as follows.

Definition 1. The set $\mathscr{S}$ consists of triplets ( $S, V, \alpha$ ) where (i) $S$ is a finite subset of $\Theta$, (ii) $V$ is a special loss function, $V \leq W$, and (iii) $\alpha$ is a number $\alpha>0$. The set is directed by the relation $(S, V, \alpha) \leq\left(S^{\prime}, V^{\prime}, \alpha^{\prime}\right)$ if $S \subset S^{\prime}, V \leq V^{\prime}$, and $\alpha^{\prime} \leq \alpha$.

Let $f$ be any function from $\Theta$ to $(-\infty,+\infty]$. Let $\mathscr{B}(\mathscr{F})$ be the set of transitions from $L(\mathscr{F})$ to the dual of $\Gamma$. The set of decision procedures $\sigma \in \mathscr{B}(\mathscr{F})$ which satisfy the inequality $W_{\theta} \sigma Q_{\theta} \leq f(\theta)$ for all $\theta \in \Theta$ will be noted $D(f, \mathscr{F})$. With this notation a first result is as follows.

Proposition 1. Let $\mathscr{F}, f$, and $W$ be fixed. Let $U \subset \mathscr{B}(\mathscr{F})$ be a weak neighborhood of the set $D(f, \mathscr{F})$. Then there is a triplet $(S, V, \alpha) \in \mathscr{S}$ such that the set

$$
D(S, V, \alpha)=\left\{\sigma \in \mathscr{B}(\mathscr{F}) ; V_{\theta} \sigma Q_{\theta} \leq f(\theta)+\alpha \text { for all } \theta \in S\right\}
$$

is contained in $U$.
Proof. The sets $D(S, V, \alpha)$ are closed, hence compact, subsets of the space $\mathscr{B}(\mathscr{F})$. Any $\sigma$ contained in the intersection

$$
\cap\{D(S, V, \alpha) ;(S, V, \alpha) \in \mathscr{S}\}
$$

is such that $V_{\theta} \sigma Q_{\theta} \leq f(\theta)+\alpha$ for all $\theta \in \Theta$, all special $V \leq W$ and all $\alpha>0$. Thus, such a $\sigma$ must belong to $D(f, \mathscr{F})$. Suppose then that $U$ is open and let $C$ be the complement of $U$. This $C$ is compact and the sets $C \cap D(S, V, \alpha)$
are decreasingly directed. Since their intersection must be empty, one of the $C \cap D(S, V, \alpha)$ is empty. This yields the desired conclusion.

For the next statement, recall that, for a loss function $W$ an experiment $\mathscr{E}=\left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ has a set of available risk functions $\mathscr{R}(\mathscr{E}, W)$. This is the set of functions $f$ from $\Theta$ to $(-\infty,+\infty]$ such that $W_{\theta} \sigma P_{\theta} \leq f(\theta)$, all $\theta$, for some $\sigma \in \mathscr{B}(\mathscr{E})$. If $m$ is a probability measure with finite support on $\Theta$, one will write

$$
\chi(\mathscr{E}, W, m)=\inf _{f}\left\{\int f d m ; f \in \mathscr{R}(\mathscr{E}, W)\right\}
$$

Theorem 1. Let $\mathscr{F}=\left\{Q_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ be given and let $f$ be a function from $\Theta$ to $(-\infty,+\infty]$. Assume that $f$ does not belong to $\mathscr{R}(\mathscr{F}, W)$. Then there is a number $\varepsilon>0$, a triplet $(S, V, \alpha) \in \mathscr{S}$, and a probability measure $m$ with finite support on $S$ such that the inequality $\delta_{S}(\mathscr{F}, \mathscr{E})<\varepsilon$ implies

$$
\int(f+\alpha) d m<\chi(\mathscr{E}, V, m)
$$

Proof. According to Proposition 1, there is a triplet $(S, V, \alpha) \in \mathscr{S}$ such that the
set $\left\{\sigma \in \mathscr{B}(\mathscr{F}) ; V_{\theta} \sigma Q_{\theta} \leq f(\theta)+2 \alpha\right.$, all $\left.\theta \in S\right\}$ is empty. This set is not modified if in its expression we replace $f$ by a function $g$ equal to $f$ on $S$ and equal to $+\infty$ on the complement of $S$. Thus, according to the minimax theorem of Chapter 2 there is probability measure $m$ carried by $S$ and such that $\int(f+2 \alpha) d m<\chi(\mathscr{F}, V, m)$.

Let $b=\sup \left\{\left|V_{\theta}(z)\right| ; \theta \in S, z \in Z\right\}$. This is a finite number since $S$ is finite and since $V$ is a special loss function. Take $2 \varepsilon=\alpha / b$ and consider an experiment $\mathscr{E}=\left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ such that $\delta_{S}(\mathscr{F}, \mathscr{E}) \leq \varepsilon$. Then, there is a transition $T$ from $L(\mathscr{F})$ to $L(\mathscr{E})$ such that

$$
\left\|P_{\theta}-T Q_{\theta}\right\| \leq 2 \varepsilon \quad \text { for all } \theta \in S .
$$

If $\rho \in \mathscr{B}(\mathscr{E})$, then $\sigma=\rho T \in \mathscr{B}(\mathscr{F})$ satisfies the inequalities

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\theta} \sigma Q_{\theta}=V_{\theta} \rho T Q_{\theta} & \leq V_{\theta} \rho P_{\theta}+b\left\|P_{\theta}-T Q_{\theta}\right\| \\
& \leq V_{\theta} \rho P_{\theta}+\alpha
\end{aligned}
$$

for all $\theta \in S$. It follows that

$$
\chi(\mathscr{F}, V, m) \leq \chi(\mathscr{E}, V, m)+\alpha
$$

This yields

$$
\int(f+2 \alpha) d m<\chi(\mathscr{F}, V, m) \leq \chi(\mathscr{E}, V, m)+\alpha
$$

and therefore

$$
\int(f+\alpha) d m<\chi(\mathscr{E}, V, m)
$$



This completes the proof of the theorem.
The theorem says in particular that the function $g$ equal to $f+\alpha$ on $S$ and to $+\infty$ outside of $S$ cannot belong to $\mathscr{R}(\mathscr{E}, V)$. Historically this type of result has been used first when the function $f$ is a constant, say $f(\theta)=a$. In this case, to say that $f \notin \mathscr{R}(\mathscr{F}, W)$ is to say that $a$ is strictly smaller than the minimax risk $\inf _{g} \sup _{\theta}\{g(\theta) ; \theta \in \Theta, g \in \mathscr{R}(\mathscr{F}, W)\}$.

The theorem says then that $a$ is also strictly smaller than the minimax risk

$$
\inf _{g} \sup _{\theta}\{g(\theta) ; \theta \in S, g \in \mathscr{R}(\mathscr{E}, V)\}
$$

as soon as (i) $\mathscr{E}$ is close enough to $\mathscr{F}$ in the weak topology of $\mathbb{E}(\Theta)$, (ii) $V \leq W$ is big enough, and (iii) $S$ is a sufficiently large finite set.

The reader may note that the refinement obtained by introduction of the special loss function $V$ complicates the statement to a certain extent. However, this replacement of $W$ by $V$ happens to be needed in many limiting arguments where one wants to deal, for instance, with variances of limiting distributions instead of limits of variances.

The preceding Theorem 1 could also be stated informally as follows: Suppose that for sufficiently large triplets $(S, V, \alpha)$ and for experiments $\mathscr{E}$ close to $\mathscr{F}$ there is a procedure $\rho$ for which $V_{\theta} \rho P_{\theta} \leq f(\theta)+\alpha, \theta \in S$. Then there is for $\mathscr{F}$ a procedure $\sigma$ such that $W_{\theta} \sigma Q_{\theta} \leq f(\theta)$, all $\theta \in \Theta$.

We shall now refine this result by showing that not only such $\sigma$ exist but that one can even select them so that the distributions $\rho P_{\theta}$ and $\sigma Q_{\theta}$ are close to each other. To state the result we shall need still another directed set of triplets as follows.

Definition. The set $\mathscr{T}$ is the set of triplets $(A, B, \varepsilon)$ such that
(i) $A$ is a strongly precompact subset of $\Gamma$;
(ii) $B$ is a subset of $\Theta$ such that the set $\left\{Q_{\theta} ; \theta \in B\right\}$ is strongly precompact;
(iii) $\varepsilon$ is a number $\varepsilon>0$.

This set $\mathscr{T}$ will be directed as before by the relation $(A, B, \varepsilon) \leq\left(A^{\prime}, B^{\prime}, \varepsilon^{\prime}\right)$ if $A \subset A^{\prime}, B \subset B^{\prime}$, and $\varepsilon^{\prime} \leq \varepsilon$. Note that the set $\mathscr{T}$ depends on the presumably fixed experiment $\mathscr{F}=\left\{Q_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ and that $\mathscr{T}$ is a family one could use to define the convergences in distribution or in probability of Section 2.

Theorem 2. Fix $\mathscr{F}, f$, and $W$. Then, for each triplet $(A, B, \varepsilon) \in \mathscr{T}$ there is a triplet $(S, V, \varepsilon) \in \mathscr{S}$ and a number $\delta>0$ with the following properties. Consider an experiment $\mathscr{E}=\left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ such that $\delta_{B \cup S}(\mathscr{F}, \mathscr{E})<\delta$ and a $\rho \in \mathscr{B}(\mathscr{E})$ such that $V_{\theta} \rho P_{\theta} \leq f(\theta)+\alpha$ for all $\theta \in S$.

Then there is a $\sigma \in \mathscr{B}(\mathscr{F})$ for which
(i) $W_{\theta} \sigma Q_{\theta} \leq f(\theta)$ for all $\theta \in \Theta$,
(ii) $\sup \left\{\left|\gamma \rho P_{\theta}-\gamma \sigma Q_{\theta}\right| ;(\gamma, \theta) \in A \times B\right\}<\varepsilon$.

Proof. Let $U$ be the set of procedures $\tau \in \mathscr{B}(\mathscr{F})$ such that there is a $\sigma \in D(f, \mathscr{F})$ for which

$$
\sup \left\{\left|\gamma \tau Q_{\theta}-\gamma \sigma Q_{\theta}\right| ;(\gamma, \theta) \in A \times B\right\}<\varepsilon .
$$

This set $U$ is a weak neighborhood of $D(f, \mathscr{F})$ in $\mathscr{B}(\mathscr{F})$. Therefore, by Proposition 1 , there is a triplet $(S, V, \alpha) \in \mathscr{S}$ such that the set

$$
D=\left\{\sigma \in \mathscr{B}(\mathscr{F}) ; V_{\theta} \sigma Q_{\theta} \leq f(\theta)+2 \alpha, \quad \text { all } \theta \in S\right\}
$$

is contained in $U$.
Let $a=\sup \{\|\gamma\|, \gamma \in A\}$ and let $b=\sup \left\{\left|V_{\theta}(z)\right| ; \theta \in S, z \in Z\right\}$. Take for $\delta$ any number $\delta>0$ such that $4 a \delta<\varepsilon$ and $4 b \delta<\alpha$. Suppose that $\mathscr{E}=\left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ is such that $\delta_{B \cup S}(\mathscr{F}, \mathscr{E}) \leq \delta$ and let $T$ be some transition such that $\left\|P_{\theta}-T Q_{\theta}\right\| \leq 2 \delta$ for all $\theta \in B \cup S$. Any element $\rho$ of $\mathscr{B}(\mathscr{E})$ yields an element $\tau=\rho T$ of $\mathscr{B}(\mathscr{F})$ which satisfies the relation

$$
V_{\theta} \tau Q_{\theta} \leq V_{\theta} \rho P_{\theta}+2 b \delta
$$

for all $\theta \in S$. Thus, if $\rho$ is in addition such that $V_{\theta} \rho P_{\theta} \leq f(\theta)+\alpha$ for all $\theta \in S$, its image $\tau=\rho T$ will be an element of the set $D \subset U$. By definition of $U$, this means that there is a $\sigma \in D(f, \mathscr{F})$ for which $\left|\gamma \tau Q_{\theta}-\gamma \sigma Q_{\theta}\right|<\varepsilon / 2$ for all $(\gamma, \theta) \in A \times B$. This yields

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left|\gamma \rho P_{\theta}-\gamma \sigma Q_{\theta}\right| & \leq\left|\gamma \rho P_{\theta}-\gamma \rho T Q_{\theta}\right|+\left|\gamma \rho T Q_{\theta}-\gamma \sigma Q_{\theta}\right| \\
& <2\|\gamma\| \delta+\frac{\varepsilon}{2}<\varepsilon
\end{aligned}
$$

for all pairs $(\gamma, \theta) \in A \times B$. This concludes the proof of the theorem.

## 5. A Result on Asymptotic Admissibility

In this section we shall use the same notation as in Section 4, with $\Theta, \mathscr{F}, Z$, $\Gamma, f$, and $W$ fixed and with the same sets $\mathscr{S}$ and $\mathscr{T}$ of triplets.

Our aim is to show that if $\sigma$ is a uniquely admissible procedure, available on $\mathscr{F}$, and nonrandomized, the procedures $\rho$ available on $\mathscr{E}$, close to $\mathscr{F}$, which mimic the risk of $\sigma$ must be every close to each other.

Specifically we shall consider three properties, called A, B, and C below and show that they are equivalent. The properties are properties of the pair ( $f, \mathscr{F}$ ) as follows.

Property (A). For every experiment $\mathscr{F}^{\prime}=\left\{Q_{\theta}^{\prime} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ which is equivalent to $\mathscr{F}$, the set $D\left(f, \mathscr{F}^{\prime}\right)$ of procedures $\sigma^{\prime}$ available on $\mathscr{F}^{\prime}$ such that $W_{\theta} \sigma^{\prime} Q_{\theta}^{\prime} \leq f(\theta)$, all $\theta$, consists of one and only one element.

Property (B). There is one and only one element $\sigma$ in the set $D(f, \mathscr{F})$ and this $\sigma$ is nonrandomized.

Note that the above Property B is stated in a way which appears to depend on the experiment $\mathscr{F}$ itself and not only on its type. Indeed, the property can be split into two pieces: " $\sigma$ is unique" and " $\sigma$ is nonrandomized," which are not separately preserved if one replaces $\mathscr{F}$ by an equivalent experiment. The last property, Property C is the approximation property described at the beginning of this section. It is as follows.

Property (C). The pair ( $f, \mathscr{F}$ ) enjoys property $C$ if $D(f, \mathscr{F})$ is nonempty and if for every triplet $(A, B, \varepsilon) \in \mathscr{T}$ there are numbers $\delta>0$ and triplets $(S, V, \alpha) \in \mathscr{S}$ satisfying the following requirements:

Let $\mathscr{E}=\left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ be such that $\delta_{B \cup S}(\mathscr{F}, \mathscr{E}) \leq \delta$. Then two elements $\rho_{i} i=$ 1,2 of $\mathscr{B}(\mathscr{E})$ which both satisfy the inequalities $V_{\theta} \rho_{i} P_{\theta} \leq f(\theta)+\alpha, \theta \in S$, must also satisfy the relation

$$
\sup _{\theta \in B}\left\langle\left[\sup _{\gamma \in A}\left|\gamma \rho_{1}-\gamma \rho_{2}\right|\right], P_{\theta}\right\rangle<\varepsilon
$$

It should be clear that Property ( C ) is at least as strong as (A). In fact, suppose that $(f, \mathscr{F})$ enjoys $(\mathrm{C})$ and let $\mathscr{E}$ be any experiment such that $\delta(\mathscr{F}, \mathscr{E})=$ 0 . Suppose that $\rho_{i}, i=1,2$ are elements of $\mathscr{B}(\mathscr{E})$ such that $W_{\theta} \rho_{i} P_{\theta} \leq f(\theta)$ for all $\theta \in \Theta$. Then, if Property ( C ) holds, one has $\langle | \gamma \rho_{1}-\gamma \rho_{2}\left|, \boldsymbol{P}_{\theta}\right\rangle=0$ for all $\gamma$ and all $\theta$. Thus $\rho_{1}$ and $\rho_{2}$ must be equal. In other words, if $\delta(\mathscr{F}, \mathscr{E})=0$ and $D(f, \mathscr{E})$ is not empty, then $D(f, \mathscr{E})$ consists of a single element of $\mathscr{B}(\mathscr{E})$.

To proceed, we need a few elementary properties characterizing nonrandomized procedures. For this purpose we shall consider $\mathscr{B}(\mathscr{F})$ as a set of positive normalized maps from $\Gamma$ to the dual $M$ of $L=L(\mathscr{F})$. If $\mathscr{B}(\mathscr{F})$ is given the weak topology of pointwise convergence on $\Gamma \times L$ then $\mathscr{B}(\mathscr{F})$ is a compact convex set. Thus, according to the Krein-Milman theorem, $\mathscr{B}(\mathscr{F})$ is the closed convex hull of its set of extreme points. Note that if $\sigma \in \mathscr{B}(\mathscr{F})$ and $\gamma \in \Gamma$ one has always $\gamma^{2} \sigma-(\gamma \sigma)^{2} \geq 0$. This follows from the usual argument which gives Schwarz' inequality.

It is easy to see that the extreme points of $\mathscr{B}(\mathscr{F})$ are precisely the elements $\sigma$ which are multiplicative $\left(\gamma_{1} \gamma_{2} \sigma=\left(\gamma_{1} \sigma\right)\left(\gamma_{2} \sigma\right)\right)$ or equivalently such that $\gamma^{2} \sigma- (\gamma \sigma)^{2}=0$ for all $\gamma \in \Gamma$. In one direction, suppose that $\sigma=\frac{1}{2}\left(\sigma_{1}+\sigma_{2}\right)$. Then, simple algebra yields

$$
\gamma^{2} \sigma-(\gamma \sigma)^{2} \geq \frac{1}{4}\left[\gamma\left(\sigma_{1}-\sigma_{2}\right)\right]^{2}
$$

Thus multiplicativity of $\sigma$ does imply $\sigma_{1}=\sigma_{2}$. Conversely, let $\sigma$ be an extreme point of $\mathscr{B}(\mathscr{F})$. Take an element $v \in \Gamma, 0 \leq v \leq 1$ and let $w=1-v$. Then, for every $\gamma \in \Gamma$ one may write $\gamma=\gamma v+\gamma w$. Define maps $\sigma_{1}$ and $\sigma_{2}$ by the formulas

$$
\begin{aligned}
\gamma \sigma_{1} & =[(\gamma v) \sigma][1-w \sigma]+[(\gamma w) \sigma][1+v \sigma] \\
\gamma \sigma_{2} & =[(\gamma v) \sigma][1+w \sigma]+[(\gamma w) \sigma][1-v \sigma] .
\end{aligned}
$$

One verifies immediately that both $\sigma_{i}$ so defined are positive linear maps of $\Gamma$ into $M$. Evaluated at $\gamma=1$ they give $1 \sigma_{i}=1$. Thus they are elements of $\mathscr{B}(\mathscr{F})$ such that $\sigma=\frac{1}{2}\left(\sigma_{1}+\sigma_{2}\right)$. Since $\sigma$ is assumed to be extreme, this must imply $\sigma_{1}=\sigma_{2}$. Simple algebra shows that this is equivalent to the equality $(\gamma v) \sigma=(\gamma \sigma)(v \sigma)$. Thus this must hold for all choices of $\gamma \in \Gamma$ and all choices of $v, 0 \leq v \leq 1, v \in \Gamma$ it follows that $\sigma$ is multiplicative.

Because of these equivalences we shall take as characterization of the property of being nonrandomized the identity $\gamma^{2} \sigma=(\gamma \sigma)^{2}$. With this notation we can assert the following.

Proposition 1. Assume that ( $f, \mathscr{F}$ ) enjoys Property ( $A$ ). Then the unique element $\sigma$ of $D(f, \mathscr{F})$ is nonrandomized.

Proof. Construct another experiment $\mathscr{E}=\left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ as follows. The tensor product $\Gamma \otimes M$ of $\Gamma$ by $M$ is obtained by taking pairs $\left(\gamma_{j}, u_{j}\right) \in \Gamma \times M$, forming finite linear combinations $\sum a_{j}\left[\gamma_{j} \otimes u_{j}\right]$, and identifying to zero the combinations which cancel all continuous bilinear forms on $\Gamma \times M$.

The procedure $\sigma$ gives a map $\Phi$ of $\Gamma \otimes M$ into $M$ by linear extension of the $\operatorname{map}(\gamma \otimes u) \mapsto[\gamma \otimes u] \Phi=(\gamma \sigma) u$ of $\Gamma \times M$ into $M$.
