This linear map $\Phi$ is a positive map of $\Gamma \otimes M$ into $M$ which preserves the constants. One can define an element $P_{\theta}$ of the dual $(\Gamma \otimes M)^{*}$ of $\Gamma \otimes M$ by

$$
\left\langle\gamma \otimes u, P_{\theta}\right\rangle=\left\langle(\gamma \sigma) u, Q_{\theta}\right\rangle .
$$

This may also be written $P_{\theta}=\Phi Q_{\theta}$, where $\Phi$ operating on its right is a transition. Thus $\mathscr{E}$ is weaker than $\mathscr{F}$. However, $\mathscr{E}$ is very clearly at least as strong as $\mathscr{F}$, so that $\Delta(\mathscr{E}, \mathscr{F})=0$.

According to Property (A) this implies that the set $D(f, \mathscr{E})$ contains a single element, say $\rho$. To construct this $\rho$ consider an element $v \in(\Gamma \otimes M)^{*}$ and let $\rho v$ be its projection defined by $\langle\gamma, \rho v\rangle=\langle\gamma \otimes 1, v\rangle$. It is clear that $\rho$ is a transition from $(\Gamma \otimes M)^{*}$ to $\Gamma^{*}$ such that $\rho P_{\theta}=\sigma Q_{\theta}$ for all $\theta$.

Note also that the transpose of $\rho$ is the multiplicative map $\gamma \mapsto \gamma \otimes 1$ from $\Gamma$ to $\Gamma \otimes M$. Now consider any transition $T$ from $L(\mathscr{E})$ to $L(\mathscr{F})$ such that $T P_{\theta}=Q_{\theta}$ for all $\theta$. By Property (A) one must have $\rho=\sigma T$. One can then write

$$
\begin{aligned}
{\left[\gamma^{2} \sigma-(\gamma \sigma)^{2}\right] T } & \leq\left[\gamma^{2} \sigma T-(\gamma \sigma)^{2} T\right]+\left[(\gamma \sigma)^{2} T-(\gamma \sigma T)^{2}\right] \\
& =\gamma^{2} \sigma T-(\gamma \sigma T)^{2} \\
& =\gamma^{2} \rho-(\gamma \rho)^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

This yields

$$
\begin{aligned}
{\left[\gamma^{2} \sigma-(\gamma \sigma)^{2}\right] Q_{\theta} } & =\left[\gamma^{2} \sigma-(\gamma \sigma)^{2}\right] T P_{\theta} \\
& \leq\left[\gamma^{2} \rho-(\gamma \rho)^{2}\right] P_{\theta}=0
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore $\sigma$ itself is nonrandomized, concluding the proof of the proposition.
It follows from the above that (A) implies (B). Thus to show that all three properties are equivalent it will be enough to show that (B) implies (C). For this purpose we need a lemma which states in a particular form that for nonrandomized procedures the neighborhoods for convergence in probability are the same as the weak neighborhoods.

Lemma 1. Let $\sigma$ be a nonrandomized element of $\mathscr{B}(\mathscr{F})$. Let $(A, B, \varepsilon)$ be one of the triplets of $\mathscr{T}$. Then, the set of elements $\tau \in \mathscr{B}(\mathscr{F})$ such that

$$
\left\langle\left[\gamma^{2} \tau-(\gamma \tau)^{2}\right]+[\gamma \tau-\gamma \sigma]^{2}, Q_{\theta}\right\rangle<\varepsilon
$$

for all $\gamma \in A$ and all $\theta \in B$ is a weak neighborhood of $\sigma$ in $\mathscr{B}(\mathscr{F})$. Proof. Since $\gamma^{2} \sigma=(\gamma \sigma)^{2}$ one can write

$$
\gamma^{2} \tau-(\gamma \tau)^{2}+[\gamma \tau-\gamma \sigma]^{2}=\gamma^{2} \tau-\gamma^{2} \sigma-2[\gamma \tau-\gamma \sigma] \gamma \sigma .
$$

Let $\lambda_{t, \gamma}$ be the element of $L(\mathscr{F})$ which has density ( $\gamma \sigma$ ) with respect to $Q_{t}$. Note that the set $K=\left\{\lambda_{t, \gamma} ; t \in B, \gamma \in A\right\}$ is a strongly precompact subset of $L(\mathscr{F})$.

Thus the system of inequalities

$$
\left(\gamma^{2} \tau-\gamma^{2} \sigma\right) Q_{t}-2(\gamma \tau-\gamma \sigma) \lambda<\varepsilon
$$

with $t \in B, \gamma \in A$, and $\lambda \in K$ defines a weakly open subset of $\mathscr{B}(\mathscr{F})$. This is the desired result.

Note. In the above lemma, the neighborhood of $\sigma$ is defined by requiring that both $\left\langle\left[\gamma^{2} \tau-(\gamma \tau)^{2}\right], Q_{\theta}\right\rangle$ and $\left\langle[\gamma \tau-\gamma \sigma]^{2}, Q_{\theta}\right\rangle$ be small. It is clear that the second term is related to the neighborhoods of convergence in probability. The inequality $\left\langle\left[\gamma^{2} \tau-(\gamma \tau)^{2}\right], Q_{\theta}\right\rangle<\varepsilon$ which does not even involve $\sigma$, says essentially that $\tau$ is in a neighborhood of the nonrandomized procedures. This is the only part of the lemma which will be used below.

Theorem 1. Assume that $\Theta, Z, \Gamma$, and $W$ are fixed. Then, for a pair $(f, \mathscr{F})$ all three Properties $(A),(B)$, and $(C)$ are equivalent.

Proof. As already noted, it will be sufficient to show that Property (B) implies (C). As stated, (C) involves the quantity $\sup _{\theta \in B}\left\langle\left[\sup _{\gamma \in A}\left|\gamma \rho_{1}-\gamma \rho_{2}\right|\right], P_{\theta}\right\rangle$. However, according to Lemma 1, Section 2, one could as well substitute for the desired inequality the weaker form

$$
\sup _{\theta \in B} \sup _{\gamma \in A}\langle | \gamma \rho_{1}-\gamma \rho_{2}\left|, P_{\theta}\right\rangle<\varepsilon
$$

If so, take two elements $\rho_{i}, i=1,2$ of $\mathscr{B}(\mathscr{E})$ and introduce their average $\rho=\frac{1}{2}\left(\rho_{1}+\rho_{2}\right)$ and the difference $\phi=\frac{1}{2}\left(\rho_{1}-\rho_{2}\right)$. Then

$$
(\gamma \phi)^{2}+(\gamma \rho)^{2}=\frac{1}{2}\left[\left(\gamma \rho_{1}\right)^{2}+\left(\gamma \rho_{2}\right)^{2}\right] .
$$

Also

$$
\begin{aligned}
\gamma^{2} \rho-(\gamma \rho)^{2} & =\frac{1}{2}\left[\gamma^{2} \rho_{1}-\left(\gamma \rho_{1}\right)^{2}\right]+\frac{1}{2}\left[\gamma^{2} \rho_{2}-\left(\gamma \rho_{2}\right)^{2}\right]+(\gamma \phi)^{2} \\
& \geq(\gamma \phi)^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

Thus, to prove the desired inequality it will be enough to show that one can select the triplets $(S, V, \alpha)$ so that $V_{\theta} \rho_{i} P_{\theta} \leq f(\theta)+\alpha, \theta \in S$ implies

$$
\left\langle\gamma^{2} \rho-(\gamma \rho)^{2}, P_{\theta}\right\rangle<\varepsilon_{1}=\frac{1}{4} \varepsilon^{2}
$$

for all $\theta \in B$ and $\gamma \in A$. For this purpose, let $U$ be the set of elements $\tau \in \mathscr{B}(\mathscr{F})$ which satisfy the inequality $\sup _{\gamma \in A} \sup _{\theta \in B}\left\langle\gamma^{2} \tau-(\gamma \tau)^{2}, Q_{\theta}\right\rangle<\left(\varepsilon_{1} / 2\right)$.

According to Lemma 1, this is a weak neighborhood of $\sigma$ in $\mathscr{B}(\mathscr{F})$. Thus, by Proposition 1 of Section 4, there is a triplet $(S, V, \alpha) \in \mathscr{S}$ such that $V_{\theta} \tau Q_{\theta} \leq f(\theta)+2 \alpha, \theta \in S$, implies $\tau \in U$. Let $a=\sup \{\|\gamma\| ; \gamma \in A\}$ and $b=\sup \left\{\left|V_{\theta}(z)\right| ; \theta \in\right. S, z \in Z\}$. Take a number $\delta>0$ such that $a^{2} \delta<\varepsilon_{1}$ and $4 b \delta<\alpha$. Consider experiments $\mathscr{E}=\left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ for which $\delta_{B \cup S}(\mathscr{F}, \mathscr{E}) \leq \delta$. For such an $\mathscr{E}$ let $T$ be a transition such that $\left\|P_{\theta}-T Q_{\theta}\right\| \leq 2 \delta$ for all $\theta \in B \cup S$.

If $\rho_{i}, i=1,2$ are two elements of $\mathscr{B}(\mathscr{E})$ which satisfy the inequalities $V_{\theta} \rho_{i} P_{\theta} \leq f(\theta)+\alpha$ for all $\theta \in S$ their average $\rho$ satisfies the same inequalities. For the element $\tau=\rho T$ of $\mathscr{B}(\mathscr{F})$ this gives

$$
V_{\theta}(\rho T) Q_{\theta} \leq f(\theta)+\alpha+2 b \delta \leq f(\theta)+2 \alpha
$$

Thus, $\rho T$ belongs to the neighborhood $U$ so that

$$
\left\langle\left[\gamma^{2} \rho T-(\gamma \rho T)^{2}\right], Q_{\theta}\right\rangle<\frac{\varepsilon_{1}}{2}
$$

for $\gamma \in A, \theta \in B$. However, one can also write (see the proof of Proposition 1)

$$
\left[\gamma^{2} \rho-(\gamma \rho)^{2}\right] T \leq \gamma^{2} \rho T-(\gamma \rho T)^{2} .
$$

It follows that

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left\langle\left[\gamma^{2} \rho-(\gamma \rho)^{2}\right], P_{\theta}\right\rangle & \leq \frac{\varepsilon_{1}}{2}+\frac{1}{2}\|\gamma\|^{2}\left\|P_{\theta}-T Q_{\theta}\right\| \\
& \leq \frac{\varepsilon_{1}}{2}+a^{2} \delta<\varepsilon_{1}
\end{aligned}
$$

This concludes the proof of the theorem.
Remark 1. The preceding Theorem 1, just as Theorem 2 of Section 4, has a general formal structure of the type
"…given ( $Z, \Gamma, W, \mathscr{F}, f, A, B, \varepsilon$ ) there exists ( $\delta, S, V, \alpha$ ) such that $\ldots$ "
In particular the system ( $\delta, S, V, \alpha$ ) is entirely determined by properties which do not involve at all the experiments $\mathscr{E}$ but only the limit experiment $\mathscr{F}$. This remark will allow us to describe the misbehavior of so-called "super-efficient estimates" in a stronger form than is usual in the literature.

Remark 2. The "uniqueness" property described by Property (A) or the stronger Property (C) may appear fairly restrictive. It does require a particular structure for the pair ( $f, \mathscr{F}$ ), but it happens to be satisfied in a large variety of situations. Two particular cases can be described as follows.

Example 1. Assume that the experiment $\mathscr{F}=\left\{Q_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ is "boundedly complete," that is, according to Chapter 2, the linear space $\operatorname{Lin}(\mathscr{F})$ spanned by $\mathscr{F}$ is dense in $L(\mathscr{F})$. Consider a testing problem in which $Z$ is the two element set $Z=\{0,1\}$ and in which the loss function $W$ is defined by a partition $\Theta=\Theta_{0} \cup \Theta_{1}$ of $\Theta$ so that $W_{\theta}(z)=0$ if $z=i$ and $\theta \in \Theta_{i}$, and furthermore $W_{\theta}(z)=1$ when $z=i$ and $\theta \in \Theta_{j}, i \neq j$. Let $\sigma$ be an admissible test which is nonrandomized. It has a risk function $f(\theta)=W_{\theta} \sigma Q_{\theta}$ and the pair ( $f, \mathscr{F}$ ) certainly satisfies Property (B).

Example 2. Suppose that $Z$ is a finite dimensional vector space and let $\Gamma= C^{b}(Z)$. Consider a loss function $W$ such that for each $\theta \in \Theta$ the map $z \mapsto W_{\theta}(z)$ is strictly convex. This means that $W_{\theta}\left[\frac{1}{2}\left(z_{1}+z_{2}\right)\right]<\frac{1}{2}\left[W_{\theta}\left(z_{1}\right)+W_{\theta}\left(z_{2}\right)\right]$ for all pairs $\left(z_{1}, z_{2}\right)$ with $z_{1} \neq z_{2}$. Assume also for simplicity that $\lim _{z} W_{\theta}(z)=\infty$ if $|z| \rightarrow \infty$.

Take a version of $\mathscr{F}=\left\{Q_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ where the $Q_{\theta}$ are measures on a suitable space, so that all the admissible procedures $\sigma \in \mathscr{B}(\mathscr{F})$ are representable by Markov kernels. This is possible, according to Chapter 1. On such a version it is clear that all admissible procedures with finite risk will necessarily be nonrandomized. Thus if $\sigma$ is admissible, if its risk $f(\theta)=W_{\theta} \sigma Q_{\theta}$ is finite for each $\theta$, then the pair ( $f, \mathscr{F}$ ) enjoys Property (B) and therefore Property (C). Here the set $\left\{Q_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ need not be "boundedly complete."

It could happen, of course, that there be admissible estimates $\sigma$ with risk $f(\theta)=W_{\theta} \sigma Q_{\theta}$ which is finite on a part $\Theta_{0}$ of $\Theta$ but infinite on the complement $\Theta_{1}$ of $\Theta_{0}$. The same conclusion would still hold for such $\sigma$ if it happened also that band generated by the set $\left\{Q_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta_{0}\right\}$ is the whole of $L(\mathscr{F})$.

## CHAPTER 8

## Invariance Properties

## 1. Introduction

In this chapter we describe briefly certain properties that arise when a group of transformations acts on the index set $\Theta$ of an experiment $\mathscr{E}=\left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$. This situation occurs in several different contexts. One of them leads to a form of Hájek's convolution theorem. Another reason for the study of invariance properties comes from the very common practice of taking limiting distributions of estimates, say $X_{n}$, by rescaling and investigating expressions such as $\delta_{n}^{-1}\left(X_{n}-\theta\right)$ with a sequence $\left\{\delta_{n}\right\}, \delta_{n}>0$, which tends to zero. The chapter is divided into sections as follows.

Section 2 recalls a form of the Markov-Kakutani fixed point theorem and gives an application to pairs of experiments $\mathscr{E}=\left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ and $\mathscr{E}^{\prime}=\left\{P_{\theta}^{\prime}\right.$; $\theta \in \Theta\}$ which remain appropriately related under a group of permutations of the index set $\Theta$. Section 3 is mostly of a technical nature. It often happens that one can readily prove properties which might be called quasi invariance. Passing from this to true invariance can sometimes be achieved in various ways. Here we shall rely on a powerful theorem of A. and C. Ionescu Tulcea which asserts the existence of a lifting which commutes with the group operations. Although the theorem is stated in greater generality we only sketch a proof for the Euclidean shift groups.

The theorem is applied to show that under certain circumstances transitions which commute with the group operations are convolutions. One deduces from there a form of Hájek's convolution theorem and a result of Torgersen.

Section 4 gives two related results arising from the use of rescaling factors $\delta_{n}, \delta_{n} \rightarrow 0$ in Euclidean situations. One of them concerns limits of "differential" experiments of the type $\mathscr{F}_{\theta, n}=\left\{P_{\theta+\delta_{n}, n} ; \xi \in R^{k}\right\}$. The other concerns limits of distributions of the type $\mathscr{L}\left\{\delta_{n}^{-1}\left(X_{n}-\theta-\delta_{n} \xi\right) \mid \theta+\delta_{n} \xi\right\}$.

Section 5 begins a study of shift invariant exponential families and shows in particular that the only shift invariant linearly indexed exponential families are the Gaussian shift experiments. This suggests that the prevalence of Gaussian limits is due to the mode of expression used to formulate these passages to the limit more than to independence and central limit theorem considerations.

The possibilities of reduction of the class of decision procedures by invariance considerations linked to theorems of the Hunt-Stein type are briefly noted in Section 6.

## 2. The Markov-Kakutani Fixed Point Theorem

The Markov-Kakutani theorem is a simple but effective tool often used to demonstrate the existence of fixed points. It arises in the following context.

Let $\mathscr{V}$ be a separated topological vector space on the reals. Let $\mathscr{S}$ be a set of linear maps of $\mathscr{V}$ into itself. Consider a nonempty subset $K$ of $\mathscr{V}$ subject to the following conditions:
(i) $K$ is a compact convex subset of $\mathscr{V}$.
(ii) If $S \in \mathscr{S}$ then its restriction to $K$ is continuous.
(iii) The set $K$ is mapped into itself by each $S$ in $\mathscr{S}$; that is, $S K \subset K$ for each $S \in \mathscr{S}$.

A point $\xi \in K$ is called invariant if $S \xi=\xi$ for each $S \in \mathscr{S}$. Let $\mathscr{L}$ be the space of all linear maps from $\mathscr{V}$ to $\mathscr{V}$ whose restrictions to $K$ are continuous. One says that $\mathscr{S}$ admits on $K$ left almost invariant means if for every $\xi \in K$, every finite subset $F \subset \mathscr{S}$ and every neighborhood $V$ of the origin of $\mathscr{V}$ there is an $A \in \mathscr{L}$ such that $A \xi \in K$ and $S A \xi-A \xi \in V$ for every $S \in F$.

Before proceeding any further let us note the following simple fact: Under the conditions (i), (ii), (iii) expressed above, the set $J$ of invariant points of $K$ is always a convex compact subset of $K$ (which, however, may be empty).

Indeed let $J_{S}$ be the set of elements of $K$ which are invariant by $S$. This set $J_{S}$ is the set of first coordinates of the intersection $G_{S} \cap G_{I}$ where $G_{S}$ is the graph $G_{S}=\{(\xi, S \xi) ; \xi \in K\}$ and $G_{I}$ is the diagonal graph $G_{I}=\{(\xi, \xi) ; \xi \in K\}$.

This implies in particular that in order that the set $J=\cap\left\{J_{S} ; S \in \mathscr{S}\right\}$ be nonempty it is necessary and sufficient that all finite intersections of the type $\cap\left\{J_{S} ; S \in F\right\}, F \subset \mathscr{S}$, with $F$ finite be nonempty. Keeping these remarks in mind, the basic Markov-Kakutani theorem can be stated as follows.

Theorem 1. Suppose that the pair ( $K, \mathscr{S}$ ) satisfies conditions (i), (ii), and (iii) and assume in addition that $\mathscr{S}$ admits left almost invariant means. Then there are elements $\xi \in K$ such that $S \xi=\xi$ for all $S \in \mathscr{S}$. This conclusion is true in particular if the elements of $\mathscr{S}$ commute on $K$ or if $\mathscr{S}$ acting on $K$ is a solvable group.

Proof. Let $F$ be a finite subset of the set $\mathscr{S}$ and let $\mathscr{L}$ be the set of linear maps whose restrictions to $K$ are continuous. For each neighborhood $V$ of the origin
of $\mathscr{V}$ let $W(V)$ be the set of pairs $(\xi, A), \xi \in K, A \in \mathscr{L}$ such that $A \xi \in K$ and $S A \xi-A \xi \in V$ for every $S \in F$. By assumption these sets $W(V)$ are nonempty. They decrease as $V$ decreases. Thus, they form the base of a filter, say $\mathscr{B}$. Let $\mathscr{U}_{0}$ be an ultrafilter finer than $\mathscr{B}$ and let $\mathscr{U}_{1}$ be its image on $K$ by the map $(\xi, A) \mapsto A \xi$. The ultrafilter $\mathscr{U}_{1}$ converges to a limit, say $\xi_{0} \in K$. Since each $S \in F$ is continuous, the images $S(A \xi)$ converge to $S \xi_{0}$. However, by construction, $\lim _{\mathscr{U}_{1}} S(A \xi)-(A \xi)=0=S \xi_{0}-\xi_{0}$. This gives the desired result.

For the additional statement of the theorem one may proceed as follows. The solvable group case reduces immediately to the abelian case by the following argument. Let $G$ be a distinguished subgroup of $\mathscr{S}$ containing the commutators $S^{-1} T^{-1} S T$ of $\mathscr{S}$. Let $\Gamma$ be the set of $\xi \in K$ such that $g \xi=\xi$ for all $g \in G$. This $\Gamma$ is compact and convex. If $S$ and $T$ belong to $\mathscr{S}$ and $\xi \in \Gamma$, then $S^{-1} T^{-1} S T \xi=\xi$ and therefore $S T \xi=T S \xi$. Furthermore $S \xi \in \Gamma$ since this may be written $S^{-1} g S \xi=\xi$. Thus $\mathscr{S}$ acts in a commutative manner on $\Gamma$ and one is reduced to the abelian case by induction on the composition chain of $\mathscr{S}$.

If the elements of $\mathscr{S}$ commute on $K$, then one can readily form almost invariant means by taking averages of the type

$$
S_{n}=\frac{1}{n} \sum_{j=0}^{n-1} S^{j}
$$

Indeed, let $A$ be a finite product of averages of that type, including the average $S_{n}$ as described. Note that $S S_{n}-S_{n}=(1 / n)\left(S^{n}-I\right)$. Thus $(S A- A)(K)$ is contained in the image $(1 / n)\left(S^{n}-I\right)(K) \in(1 / n)(K-K)$. Since $K$ is compact, $K-K$ is absorbed by all neighborhoods $V$ of the origin and the result follows.

Remark. Another case where $\mathscr{S}$ admits almost invariant means can be described as follows. Let $\Omega$ be a compact group. If $u$ is a bounded measurable function defined on $\Omega$ and $\gamma \in \Omega$ let $u_{\gamma}$ be defined by $u_{\gamma}(\omega)=u(\gamma \omega)$. Let $\mu$ be a probability measure on $\Omega$ which is a left invariant Haar measure, that is such that $\int u_{\gamma} d \mu=\int u d \mu$ for all bounded measurable $u$. Let $\omega \mapsto S_{\omega}$ be a representation of $\Omega$ by linear transformations satisfying (i), (ii), (iii). If this map is appropriately measurable, the integral $A=\int S_{\omega} \mu(d \omega)$ is an invariant mean for the family $\mathscr{S}=\left\{S_{\omega} ; \omega \in \Omega\right\}$.

On particular application of the Markov-Kakutani theorem is as follows. Let $\Theta$ be a set which indexes two experiments $\mathscr{E}=\left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ and $\mathscr{E}^{\prime}= \left\{P_{\theta}^{\prime} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$. Let ( $S, S^{\prime}$ ) be a pair where $S$ is a transition from $L(\mathscr{E})$ to $L(\mathscr{E})$ and $S^{\prime}$ is a transition from $L\left(\mathscr{E}^{\prime}\right)$ to $L\left(\mathscr{E}^{\prime}\right)$.

Definition 1. The pair ( $S, S^{\prime}$ ) leaves the pair ( $\mathscr{E}^{\circ}, \mathscr{E}^{\prime}$ ) invariant if
(1) $S$ restricted to the set $\mathscr{E}$ is a permutation;
(2) $S^{\prime}$ restricted to the set $\mathscr{E}^{\prime}$ is a permutation;
(3) If $S P_{\theta_{1}}=P_{\theta_{2}}$ then $S^{\prime} P_{\theta_{2}}^{\prime}=P_{\theta_{1}}^{\prime}$ (so that $S^{\prime}$ acts as "inverse" of $S$ on the indices).

Transitions from $L(\mathscr{E})$ to $L\left(\mathscr{E}^{\prime}\right)$ form a convex set which need not be compact for the usual topologies. Because of this, it is convenient to introduce the second dual, say $M^{\prime}$, of the space $L\left(\mathscr{E}^{\prime}\right)$ and consider the set $K$ of all transitions from $L(\mathscr{E})$ to $M^{\prime}$. Each transition $S^{\prime}$ from $L\left(\mathscr{E}^{\prime}\right)$ to $L\left(\mathscr{E}^{\prime}\right)$ has a unique extension which is a transition from $M^{\prime}$ to $M^{\prime}$ and which will be noted by the same letter $S^{\prime}$. There is also a positive linear projection $\Pi$ of $M^{\prime}$ onto the band $L\left(\mathscr{E}^{\prime}\right)$ in $M^{\prime}$. We have considered transitions $S^{\prime}$ which send $L\left(\mathscr{E}^{\prime}\right)$ into $L\left(\mathscr{E}^{\prime}\right)$. However, such an $S^{\prime}$ need not send the complementary band $(I-\Pi) M^{\prime}$ into itself.

A particular $S^{\prime}$ will do so only if it commutes with the projection $\Pi$. The following lemma gives a condition under which this commutativity property holds.

Lemma 1. Let $S^{\prime}$ be a transition which is an isometry of $L\left(\mathscr{E}^{\prime}\right)$ onto $L\left(\mathscr{E}^{\prime}\right)$. Then the extension of $S^{\prime}$ to $M^{\prime}$ commutes with the projection $\Pi$.

Proof. Passing to the second dual, the isometry $S^{\prime}$ becomes a positive isometry of $M^{\prime}$ onto $M^{\prime}$. Thus it is a lattice isomorphism. In particular if $\mu \in M^{\prime}$ and $\lambda \in L\left(\mathscr{E}^{\prime}\right) \subset M^{\prime}, \mu \geq 0, \lambda \geq 0$, then $S^{\prime}(\mu \wedge \lambda)=\left(S^{\prime} \mu\right) \wedge\left(S^{\prime} \lambda\right)$. This implies the desired result immediately.

In the present context, for pairs ( $S, S^{\prime}$ ) which leave a pair of experiments ( $\mathscr{E}, \mathscr{E}^{\prime}$ ) invariant, the isometry requirement of Lemma 1 is not automatic. However, it is always satisfied when the family $\left\{S^{\prime} ;\left(S, S^{\prime}\right) \in \mathscr{S}\right\}$ is a group of bijective transformations of $L\left(\mathscr{E}^{\prime}\right)$ onto itself.

When $L\left(\mathscr{E}^{\prime}\right)$ is appropriately respresented by measures on a measurable space, positive isometries of $L\left(\mathscr{E}^{\prime}\right)$ correspond to bijective point transformations. This is the case most commonly studied in the statistical literature.

This leads to the following situation. Consider the set $K$ of all transitions from $L(\mathscr{E})$ to the second dual $M^{\prime}$ of $L\left(\mathscr{E}^{\prime}\right)$. Topologize $K$ by the topology of pointwise convergence on $M\left(\mathscr{E}^{\prime}\right) \times L(\mathscr{E})$. Each pair of transitions ( $S, S^{\prime}$ ), where $S$ maps $L(\mathscr{E})$ into $L(\mathscr{E})$ and $S^{\prime}$ maps $L\left(\mathscr{E}^{\prime}\right)$ into $L\left(\mathscr{E}^{\prime}\right)$, induces a map $T \mapsto S^{\prime} T S$ of $K$ into itself.

Theorem 2. Let $\left(\mathscr{E}, \mathscr{E}^{\prime}\right), \mathscr{E}=\left\{P_{\theta}: \theta \in \Theta\right\}, \mathscr{E}^{\prime}=\left\{P_{\theta}^{\prime} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ be a pair of experiments indexed by $\Theta$. Let $\mathscr{S}$ be a family of pairs ( $S, S^{\prime}$ ) of transitions that leave the pair ( $\mathscr{E}, \mathscr{E}^{\prime}$ ) invariant. Let $\mathscr{S}_{1}$ be the induced family of transformations on $K$. Assume that $\mathscr{S}_{1}$ admit left almost invariant means. For each $\varepsilon \in[0,1]$, let $K(\varepsilon)$ be the subset of $K$ formed by transitions from $L(\mathscr{E})$ to $M^{\prime}\left(\mathscr{E}^{\prime}\right)$ such that $\sup _{\theta} \frac{1}{2}\left\|P_{\theta}^{\prime}-T P_{\theta}\right\| \leq \varepsilon$. Then, if $K(\varepsilon)$ is not empty, it contains a transition $T$ such that $S^{\prime} T S=T$ for all pairs $\left(S, S^{\prime}\right) \in \mathscr{S}$. The invariant transition $T$ can be taken to be a transition from $L(\mathscr{E})$ to $L\left(\mathscr{E}^{\prime}\right)$ in each of the following three cases:

Case 1. $\varepsilon=0$.
Case 2. For $\left(S, S^{\prime}\right) \in \mathscr{S}$, the transition $S$ is an isometry of $L(\mathscr{E})$ and $S^{\prime}$ is an isometry of $L\left(\mathscr{E}^{\prime}\right)$. Furthermore there exist a transition of $T_{0}$ from $L(\mathscr{E})$ to $L\left(\mathscr{E}^{\prime}\right)$ satisfying $S^{\prime} T_{0} S=T_{0}$ for $\left(S, S^{\prime}\right) \in \mathscr{S}$.

Case 3. The pairs ( $S, S^{\prime}$ ) are isometries. In addition $\varepsilon<1$ and the family $\left\{S ;\left(S, S^{\prime}\right) \in \mathscr{S}\right\}$ does not leave invariant any nondegenerate band of $L(\mathscr{E})$ except $L(\mathscr{E})$ itself.

Proof. The map induced by a pair ( $S, S^{\prime}$ ) maps any set $K(\varepsilon)$ into itself



since one can write $P_{\theta_{1}}^{\prime}-S^{\prime} T S P_{\theta_{1}}=S^{\prime} P_{\theta_{2}}^{\prime}-S^{\prime} T P_{\theta_{2}}=S^{\prime}\left(P_{\theta_{2}}^{\prime}-T P_{\theta_{2}}\right)$ with $S P_{\theta_{1}}=P_{\theta_{2}}$. Also $K(\varepsilon)$ is compact for the topology of pointwise convergence on $M\left(\mathscr{E}^{\mathscr{E}}\right) \times L(\mathscr{E})$. Therefore the Markov-Kakutani theorem implies the existence of an invariant $T \in K(\varepsilon)$ whenever $K(\varepsilon)$ is not empty.

For the other assertions, note that when $\varepsilon=0$, the set $K(0)$ is the set of transitions $T$ such that $P_{\theta}^{\prime}=T P_{\theta}$ for all $\theta$. These transitions are automatically transitions from $L(\mathscr{E})$ to $L\left(\mathscr{E}^{\prime}\right)$. This takes care of Case 1.

For Cases 2 and 3 it is convenient to represent $M(\mathscr{E})$ as the space of continuous functions $C(X)$ on the spectrum $X$ in the Gelfand-Kakutani representation. Positive isometries of $L(\mathscr{E})$ yield positive isomtries of $M(\mathscr{E})$ which can in turn be represented by continuous permutations of the spectrum $X$.

Consider the image $1 \Pi T$ of the unit of $M\left(\mathscr{E}^{\prime}\right)$ by the map $\Pi T$ acting on its left. It is an element of $M(\mathscr{E})$ and therefore it can be represented by an element $\gamma \in C(X)$ with $0 \leq \gamma \leq 1$. If the $S^{\prime}$ commute with the projection $\Pi$ and if $T$ is invariant, then $1 \Pi T S \lambda=1 S^{\prime} \Pi T S \lambda=1 \Pi S^{\prime} T S \lambda=1 \Pi T \lambda$. Thus $\gamma$ is invariant under the permutations induced on $X$ by $\left\{S ;\left(S, S^{\prime}\right) \in \mathscr{S}\right\}$.

Now take an integer $k$ and let $J_{k, i}=\left\{x \in X ; i-1<2^{k} \gamma(x) \leq i\right\}$, for $i=1$, $\ldots, 2^{k}$. Let $\Pi_{k, i}$ be the projection of $L(\mathscr{E})$ into itself that replaces a $\lambda$ by its restriction to the set $J_{k, i}$. Consider the transformation $T_{k}$ defined by $T_{k} \lambda= \Pi T \sum_{i}\left(2^{k} / i\right) \Pi_{k, i} \lambda$. If $\lambda \geq 0$, one will have

$$
1 \Pi T \Pi_{k, i} \lambda=\left\langle\gamma, \Pi_{k, i} \lambda\right\rangle \leq \frac{i}{2^{k}}\left\|\Pi_{k, i} \lambda\right\|
$$

Therefore $\left\|T_{k} \lambda\right\| \leq\|\lambda\|$. As $k$ increases, the maps $T_{k}$ increase, tending to a limit $T_{\infty}$. Also, since the projections $\Pi_{k, i}$ commute with the $S$ of the pairs ( $S, S^{\prime}$ ), one has $S^{\prime} \Pi T \Pi_{k, i} S \lambda=\Pi T \Pi_{k, i} \lambda$. Thus $S^{\prime} T_{k} S=T_{k}$ and similarly $S^{\prime} T_{\infty} S=T_{\infty}$. Note also that $\left\|P_{\theta}^{\prime}-T_{k} P_{\theta}\right\| \leq\left\|P_{\theta}^{\prime}-T P_{\theta}\right\|$ since $T_{k} P_{\theta}$ is obtained by replacing a part of $(1-\Pi) T P_{\theta}$ by an element of $L\left(\mathscr{E}^{\prime}\right)$. A positive $\lambda \in L(\mathscr{E})$ can be represented as $\lambda=\Pi_{\infty} \lambda+\left(1-\Pi_{\infty}\right) \lambda$, where $\Pi_{\infty}$ is the projection that restricts $\lambda$ to the set $\{u ; \gamma(u)<0\}$.

In Case 2 of the theorem, let $V$ be defined by $V \lambda=T_{\infty} \Pi_{\infty} \lambda+T_{0}\left(1-\Pi_{\infty}\right) \lambda$. This $V$ is a transition from $L(\mathscr{E})$ to $L\left(\mathscr{E}^{\prime}\right)$. It is invariant and such that $\| P_{\theta}^{\prime}- V P_{\theta}\|\leq\| P_{\theta}^{\prime}-T P_{\theta} \|$. This gives the desired result. For Case 3 the situation is simpler: the function $\gamma$ must be constant on the compact $X$. Otherwise, restricting measures to some closed set $\{x \in X ; \gamma(x) \in F\}$ would yield a proper band of $L(\mathscr{E})$ that remains invariant under the action of $\left\{S:\left(S, S^{\prime}\right) \in \mathscr{S}\right\}$. If $\gamma$ is constant the $\gamma_{\infty}=1 T_{\infty}$ must be either identically unity or identically zero. It cannot be identically zero since $\varepsilon<1$. Hence the result.

Remark. Let the elements of the pairs ( $S, S^{\prime}$ ) be isometries and assume the
existence of almost invariant means. Let $K_{1}$ be the subset of $K$ formed by invariant transitions from $L(\mathscr{E})$ to $M^{\prime}\left(\mathscr{E}^{\prime}\right)$. Let $K_{2}$ be the subset formed by those $T \in K_{1}$ such that the element $1 \Pi T$ of $C(X)$ takes only values zero and unity. Each $T \in K_{2}$ yields a projection $\Pi_{T}$ of $L(\mathscr{E})$ into $L(\mathscr{E})$ such that $S \Pi_{T}= \Pi_{T} S$ for all $S$. It also yields a "subtransition" $\Pi T=T \Pi_{T}$ of $L(\mathscr{E})$ to $L\left(\mathscr{E}^{\prime}\right)$. If
$T_{i} ; i=1,2$ are two elements of $K_{2}$ one can form another "subtransition" $T_{3}$ by taking

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{3} & =\Pi T_{1}+\Pi T_{2}\left(1-\Pi_{T_{1}}\right)=\Pi T_{1}+T_{2} \Pi_{T_{2}}\left(1-\Pi_{T_{1}}\right) \\
& =\Pi T_{1}+\Pi T_{2}-T_{2} \Pi_{T_{2}} \Pi_{T_{1}} .
\end{aligned}
$$

This is larger than $\Pi T_{1}$. Also $1 T_{3} \geq \max \left(1 \Pi T_{1}, 1 \Pi T_{2}\right)$. Thus, along a maximal totally ordered set, these transitions have a limit, say $T^{*}$, a subtransition from $L\left(\mathscr{E}^{\mathscr{E}}\right)$ to $L\left(\mathscr{E}^{\prime}\right)$ such that $S^{\prime} T^{*} S=T^{*}$. This $T^{*}$ itself yields an element $\gamma^{*}=1 T^{*}$ of $C(X)$ and $\gamma^{*}$ takes only values zero and unity. It corresponds to a projection $\Pi^{*}$. The space $L(\mathscr{E})$ is the direct sum of two bands $L_{1}=\Pi^{*} L(\mathscr{E})$ and $L_{0}=\left(1-\Pi^{*}\right) L(\mathscr{E})$ such that $\left\|T^{*} \lambda\right\|=\|\lambda\|$ for any $\lambda>0, \lambda \in L_{1}$ while $T^{*} \lambda=0$ for any $\lambda \in L_{0}$. In other words, there is an invariant transition $T^{*}$ from $L_{1}$ to $L\left(\mathscr{E}^{\prime}\right)$. For $\lambda$ in the complementary band $L_{0}$ one has $\Pi T \lambda=0$ for all elements $T \in K_{1}$. Thus the assumption of existence of the invariant $T_{0}$ in Case 2 could be replaced by apparently weaker assumption that for each $\lambda \in L(\mathscr{E}), \lambda>0$, there is some invariant $T_{\lambda} \in K_{1}$ such that $\left\|\Pi T_{\lambda} \lambda\right\| \neq 0$.

Torgersen has used theorems analogous to Theorem 2 to obtain effective computations of deficiencies in certain special cases. A step in that direction is the following result.

Let us say that $\mathscr{S}$ acts transitively on $\mathscr{E}$ if for every pair of indices $\left(\theta_{1}, \theta_{2}\right)$ there is a pair $\left(S, S^{\prime}\right) \in \mathscr{S}$ such that $S P_{\theta_{1}}=P_{\theta_{2}}$. Take a particular $\theta_{0} \in \Theta$ and let ( $P, P^{\prime}$ ) be the pair ( $P_{\theta_{0}}, P_{\theta_{0}}^{\prime}$ ). Let $C$ be the positive part of the unit ball of $M\left(\mathscr{E}^{\prime \prime}\right)$.

Corollary. Assume that $\mathscr{S}$ acts transitively on $\mathscr{E}$ and that the $S^{\prime}$ are isometries. Assume also that, are in Theorem 2, the deficiency $\delta\left(\mathscr{E}, \mathscr{E}^{\prime}\right)= \inf _{T} \sup _{\theta} \frac{1}{2}\left\|P_{\theta}^{\prime}-T P_{\theta}\right\|$ for an infimum taken over the set $K_{0}$ of all invariant transitions from $L(\mathscr{E})$ to $L\left(\mathscr{E}^{\prime}\right)$. Then

$$
\delta\left(\mathscr{E}, \mathscr{E}^{\prime}\right)=\sup _{f \in C} \inf _{T \in K_{0}}\left[f P^{\prime}-f T P\right]
$$

Proof. For each $\theta$ there is a pair ( $S, S^{\prime}$ ) with $S P=P_{\theta}$ and therefore $S^{\prime} P_{\theta}^{\prime}=P^{\prime}$. Thus, for every invariant transition $T$ one has

$$
\left\|P_{\theta}^{\prime}-T P_{\theta}\right\|=\left\|S^{\prime}\left(P_{\theta}^{\prime}-T P_{\theta}\right)\right\|=\left\|P^{\prime}-S^{\prime} T S P\right\|=\left\|P^{\prime}-T P\right\| .
$$

Note that $C$ is $w\left(M\left(\mathscr{E}^{\prime}\right), L\left(\mathscr{E}^{\prime}\right)\right)$ compact. Thus, by the minimax theorem,

$$
\sup _{f} \inf _{T}\left\{f P^{\prime}-f T P ; f \in C, T \in K_{0}\right\}=\inf _{T} \sup _{f}\left\{f P^{\prime}-f T P ; f \in C, T \in K_{0}\right\} .
$$

However, $\sup _{f} f\left(P^{\prime}-T P\right)=\frac{1}{2}\left\|P^{\prime}-T P\right\|$. This yields the desired result.
Further reductions may be obtained under stronger transitivity requirements as shall be shown below and in Section 3. Before passing to this, note that the statistical literature contains other forms of invariance requirements expressible on an individual experiment $\mathscr{E}$ instead of a pair ( $\mathscr{E}, \mathscr{E}^{\prime}$ ).

We shall use in Section 4 the following concept. Let $G$ be a group. For each $\alpha \in G$ let $s_{\alpha}$ be a permutation of the set $\Theta$. Assume that the map $s: \alpha \mapsto s_{\alpha}$ is a representation of $G$ in the sense that $s_{\alpha \beta}$ is the composition $s_{\alpha \beta}=s_{\alpha} \circ s_{\beta}$.

Definition 2. The experiment $\mathscr{E}=\left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ is invariant under the action of $G$ through $s$ if for every $\alpha \in G$ the experiment $\left\{P_{s_{\alpha} \theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ is equivalent to $\mathscr{E}$ itself.

If $\mathscr{E}$ is invariant according to this definition there are transitions $S_{\alpha}$ of $L(\mathscr{E})$ into itself such that $S_{\alpha} P_{\theta} \equiv P_{s_{\theta} \theta}$. These become candidates for forming systems to which Theorem 2 might be applicable.

The above Definition 2 may seem remote from the situation most often encountered in the literature. However, it is not very remote, according to the following lemma.

Lemma 2. Let $\mathscr{E}=\left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ be invariant according to Definition 2. Then there is a measurable space ( $\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{A}$ ), measures $F_{\theta}$ on ( $\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{A}$ ) and measurable permutations $t_{\alpha}$ of $(\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{A})$ such that
(i) $\mathscr{F}=\left\{F_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ is equivalent to $\mathscr{E}$,
(ii) the map $\alpha \mapsto t_{\alpha}$ is a representation of $G$;
(iii) for all $\alpha$ and $\theta$ one has $F_{s_{\alpha} \theta}=t_{\alpha} F_{\theta}$.

Proof. Let $L_{m}$ be the minimal $L$-space of the experiment $\mathscr{E}$. The invariance property yields existence of transitions $T_{\alpha}$ from $L_{m}$ to $L_{m}$ such that $T_{\alpha} P_{\theta} \equiv P_{s_{\alpha} \theta}$. However in this case the $T_{\alpha}$ must be isometries of $L_{m}$ (see Chapter 2, Theorem 3). By construction $T_{\alpha} T_{\beta} P_{\theta}=P_{s_{\alpha} s_{\beta} \theta}$ so that, according again to Theorem 3 of Chapter 2, one must have $T_{\alpha} T_{\beta}=T_{\alpha \beta}$.

Positive isometries of $L_{m}$ have for transpose positive isometries of the dual $M$ of $L_{m}$. Represent this dual $M$ as the space $C(\mathscr{X})$ of continuous real functions on the Gelfand-Kakutani representation space $\mathscr{X}$. The positive isometries of $C(\mathscr{X})$ are obtainable in the form $f T=f \circ t$, where $t$ is a continuous permutation of $\mathscr{X}$. Thus $T_{\alpha}$ yields a permutation $t_{\alpha}$ of $\mathscr{X}$ and the desired result follows.

Note. In many cases it is not necessary to have recourse to the GelfandKakutani representation. Another construction will be used in Section 4 for a situation where there is a particular $P_{\theta_{0}}$ which dominates all the other $P_{\theta}$.

Returning to the group $G$ and the representation $s$, let us suppose that both $\mathscr{E}=\left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ and $\mathscr{E}^{\prime}=\left\{P_{\theta}^{\prime} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ are invariant under the action of $G$ through $s$. Take both $\mathscr{E}$ and $\mathscr{E}^{\prime}$ in their minimal equivalent form. Then for each $\alpha$ there is a transition $S_{\alpha}$ such that $P_{s_{\gamma} \theta} \equiv S_{\alpha} P_{\theta}$. There is also a transition $T_{\alpha}$ such that $P_{s_{\alpha} \theta}^{\prime} \equiv T_{\alpha} P_{\theta}^{\prime}$. Let $S_{\alpha}^{\prime}$ be the inverse of the isometry $T_{\alpha}$, or equivalently let $S_{\alpha}^{\prime}=T_{\beta}$ with $\beta=\alpha^{-1}$. Then the pairs ( $S_{\alpha}, S_{\alpha}^{\prime}$ ) are candidates for the application of Theorem 2. In fact, if $G$ admits almost invariant means, all the conclusions of Theorem 2 become available.

## 3. A Lifting Theorem and Some Applications

As mentioned in the introduction, application of the Markov-Kakutani theorem often yields existence of suitably invariant equivalence classes, while the desired conclusion would be existence of invariant functions. There is a powerful lifting
theorem due to A . and C . Ionescu Tulcea, which gives a simple method of passing from classes to functions. It can be stated as follows.

Let $G$ be a locally compact group with a Haar measure noted $\lambda$. Let $\mathscr{M}$ be the space of all bounded real valued measurable functions defined on $G$. Here, a function $f$ is called "measurable" in the Lusin-Bourbaki sense that for each compact $K \subset G$ and each $\varepsilon>0$ there is another compact $K_{\varepsilon} \subset K$ such that $\lambda\left(K \backslash K_{\varepsilon}\right)<\varepsilon$ and such that the restriction of $f$ to $K_{\varepsilon}$ is continuous. Two elements $f$ and $g$ of $\mathscr{M}$ are called locally equivalent if $\int|f-g| u d \lambda=0$ for every continuous function $u$ with compact support on $G$.

Let $\mathscr{M}$ be the space of equivalence classes of $\mathscr{M}$ for this local equivalence relation. The operations such as addition, multiplication, etc., pass over from $\mathscr{M}$ to $\mathscr{M}$. For any $\alpha \in G$ one can also define a shift operation $S^{\alpha}$ on $\mathscr{M}$ by the relation $\left(f S^{\alpha}\right)(x)=f(\alpha x)$. This will be called the left shift by $\alpha$.

The reader should note that the definition used here does not conform with the usual. It is intended to be used with an analogous shift operation on measures so that $\left\langle f S^{\alpha}, \mu\right\rangle=\left\langle f, S^{\alpha} \mu\right\rangle$. The standard definitions would involve a shift operations $S(\alpha)$ such that $(S(\alpha) f)(x)=f\left(\alpha^{-1} x\right)$. Then one defines the shift operations $S(\alpha)$ on measures by $\langle f, S(\alpha) \mu\rangle=\left\langle S\left(\alpha^{-1}\right) f, \mu\right\rangle$. This yields $\langle f, S(\alpha) \mu\rangle=\int f(\alpha x) \mu(d x)$ which is equal to the value $\left\langle f S^{\alpha}, \mu\right\rangle=\left\langle f, S^{\alpha} \mu\right\rangle$ of our definition.

The shift operation $S^{\alpha}$ defined above can also be carried over from $\mathscr{M}$ to $\dot{\mathscr{M}}$. (It does not matter whether $\lambda$ is left or right invariant since left and right Haar measures are equivalent.)

Note that the operation $S^{\alpha}$ acts on the function situated on its left. This is in keeping with the notation used throughout the present work, where transitions operate on measures situated on their right and on the left on functions or equivalence classes of functions, or on elements of a dual $M$ of an $L$-space. The same convention will be used below for liftings.

A lifting of $\dot{\mathscr{M}}$ is a positive linear map, say $A$, from $\dot{\mathscr{M}}$ to $\mathscr{M}$, which transforms the algebraic and lattice operations of $\mathscr{M}$ into the corresponding pointwise operations of $\mathscr{M}$ and is such that $u A \in u$ for each class $u \in \mathscr{M}$. One says that the lifting commutes with the left shifts if $u S^{\alpha} A=u A S^{\alpha}$ for all $u \in \mathscr{M}$ and $\alpha \in G$.

Theorem 3. For every locally compact group $G$, there is a lifting of $\dot{\mathscr{M}}$ which commutes with the left shifts.

Proof. We shall only sketch a proof for the case where $G$ is the ordinary $k$ dimensional Euclidean space, considered as an additive group. The general case requires more complicated arguments for which we can only refer the interested reader to the original papers.

For the finite dimensional space $R^{k}$ one may proceed as follows. Give $R^{k}$ its maximum coordinate norm. For each integer $n$, let $B(n)$ be the ball of diameter $(1 / n)$ centered at the origin.

If $u \in \mathscr{M}$, define averages $\left(u A_{n}\right)(x)$ by the formula

$$
\left(u A_{n}\right)(x)=\frac{1}{\lambda[B(n)]} \int_{B(n)} u^{\prime}(x+y) \lambda(d y)
$$

where $u^{\prime}$ is any function $u^{\prime} \in u$. According to the Lebesgue differentiation theorems, for almost all $x \in R^{k}$ the integral

$$
\frac{1}{\lambda[B(n)]} \int_{B(n)}\left|u^{\prime}(x+y)-u^{\prime}(x)\right| \lambda(d y)
$$

converge to zero as $n \rightarrow \infty$. Consider then an ultrafilter $\mathscr{F}$ finer than the filter of tails of the sequence $\{n\}$.

Along $\mathscr{F}$ the function $x \mapsto\left(u A_{n}\right)(x)$ converge pointwise to a limit, say, $u A$. The operation $A$ so defined is clearly a positive linear map of $\mathscr{M}$ into $\mathscr{M}$. It is such that $u A \in u$. Thus $A$ is what is called a linear lifting. Also for every $u$ which is the class of a continuous function, the image $u A$ is precisely that continuous function. Finally $A$ commutes with the shifts since all the $A_{n}$ do.

The operation $A$ so obtained is a linear lifting which does not necessarily preserve the lattice, or algebraic properties. An extreme point argument exactly analogous to the one carried out for the general lifting theorem (see Appendix) yields the existence of the desired lifting.

Briefly, for each idempotent $v \in \mathscr{M}$ one defines lower and upper bounds $\underline{v}=\lim _{n}(v A)^{n}$ and $\bar{v}=\lim (v A)^{1 / n}$. Let then $\mathscr{B}$ denote the set of all linear liftings $B$ such that (1) they commute with shifts and (2) they are such that $\underline{v} \leq v B \leq \bar{v}$ for all idempotents $v \in \mathscr{M}$. This set is compact convex and has at least one extreme point which is one of the desired liftings. We shall now give a few applications of Theorem 3. One of them is as follows.

Consider a locally compact group $Z$. If $\mu$ is a finite Radon measure on $Z$ and $\alpha \in Z$, let $S^{\alpha} \mu$ be the measure defined by

$$
\int f(z)\left(S^{\alpha} \mu\right)(d z)=\int f(\alpha z) \mu(d z)
$$

Let $\mathscr{F}=\left\{F_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ be an experiment defined by Radon measures on the locally compact group $Z$. Let $L(\mathscr{F})$ be the $L$-space of $\mathscr{F}$.

Proposition 1. Assume that for every $\alpha \in Z$ and $\theta \in \Theta$ one has $S^{\alpha} F_{\theta} \in L(\mathscr{F})$. Let $K$ be a transition from $L(\mathscr{F})$ to the space of Radon measures on $Z$. Assume that $S^{\alpha} K \mu=K S^{\alpha} \mu$ for all $\mu \in L(\mathscr{F})$. Then there is a probability measure $Q$ such that $K$ is representable as a convolution $K \mu=\mu * Q$ if and only if the transpose of $K$ transforms the space $C^{b}(Z)$ of bounded continuous functions into itself. This happens in particular if all the $F_{\theta}$ are discrete or if all of them are absolutely continuous with respect to the Haar measure of $Z$.

Proof. Suppose that all the $F_{\theta}$ are absolutely continuous with respect to one of the Haar measures. Let $\lambda$ be the right Haar measure and let $f \geq 0$ be a bounded function with compact support. By Fubini's theorem, for all $\mu \in L(\mathscr{F})$ one may write

$$
\int\left\{\int f(\alpha z) \mu(d z)\right\} \lambda(d \alpha)=\int \mu(d z) \int f(\alpha z) \lambda(d \alpha)=\|\mu\| \int f(\alpha) \lambda(d \alpha)
$$

Thus, since the shifts $S^{\alpha}$ map $L(\mathscr{F})$ into itself, a bounded function $f$ is equivalent to zero on $L(\mathscr{F})$ if and only if it is locally equivalent to zero for
$\lambda$. This means that the dual $M$ of $L(\mathscr{F})$ can be identified with the space of equivalence classes of the bounded $\lambda$-measurable functions, two functions being declared equivalent if their difference is locally negligible. Indeed, $M$ is the dual of the space $L_{1}$ all bounded Radon measures dominated by $\lambda$ on $Z$ and $L(\mathscr{F})$ is a subspace of $L_{1}$ which separates $M$.

Returning to the transition $K$, consider its transpose $f \mapsto f K$ which sends the dual of the space of all bounded Radon measures into $M$. The space $C_{0}$ of bounded continuous functions which vanish at infinity is in the domain of $K$. Note also that according to our definitions the shift operation $S^{\alpha}$ operates so that $\left\langle f S^{\alpha}, \mu\right\rangle=\left\langle f, S^{\alpha} \mu\right\rangle$.

By transposition the relation $K S^{\alpha}=S^{\alpha} K$ gives the relation $g K S^{\alpha}=g S^{\alpha} K$ for all $g \in C_{0}$. Let $A$ be a lifting of $M$ which commutes with the shifts. Applying it to the above commutation relation we obtain $g K S^{\alpha} A=g S^{\alpha} K A$, pointwise, for all $g \in C_{0}$. Since $S^{\alpha} A=A S^{\alpha}$ this can also be written

$$
g T S^{\alpha}=g S^{\alpha} T \quad \text { for } T=K A
$$

Consider then the map $g \mapsto(g T)(0)$ where 0 is the identity element of the group. This is a positive linear functional defined on $C_{0}$. Thus, there is a positive finite Radon measure, say $Q$, such that $(g T)(0)=\int g(z) Q(d z)$. Since $g T S^{\alpha}=g S^{\alpha} T$, one concludes that $(g T)(\alpha)=\left(g S^{\alpha}\right) T(0)=\int g(\alpha z) Q(d z)$ and therefore

$$
\langle g, K \mu\rangle=\langle g T, \mu\rangle=\iint g(\alpha z) \mu(d \alpha) Q(d z)
$$

for all $g \in C_{0}$. This completes the proof of the Proposition in the absolutely continuous case.

When the $F_{\theta}$ are all discrete, the dual $M(\mathscr{F})$ of $L(\mathscr{F})$ is the space of bounded functions on $Z$. Equality in this dual is pointwise equality. Thus, in this case the result is immediate. Finally, if $K$ maps $C_{0}$ into $C^{b}(Z)$, the equality $g K S^{\alpha}= g S^{\alpha} K$ in $M(\mathscr{F})$ implies pointwise equality for all $g \in C_{0}$. Thus the result holds in all cases mentioned and since convolutions certainly map $C^{b}(Z)$ into $C^{b}(Z)$, the entire Proposition is proved.

The above arguments lead almost immediately to a nice result of J. Hájek. A form of it is as follows. Consider the case where $\Theta$ and $Z$ are one and the same locally compact group $G$. For each $n$ let $\mathscr{F}_{n}=\left\{P_{\theta, n} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ be an experiment indexed by $\Theta$. Consider also two statistics, say $X_{n}$ and $Y_{n}$, available on $\mathscr{F}_{n}$ and taking values in $G=Z=\Theta$.

Proposition 2. Assume that the following conditions are satisfied:
(i) The distributions $F_{\theta, n}=\mathscr{L}\left(X_{n} \mid P_{\theta, n}\right)$ and $G_{\theta, n}=\mathscr{L}\left(Y_{\eta} \mid P_{\theta, n}\right)$ converge, respectively, to limits $F_{\theta}$ and $G_{\theta}$.
(ii) The limits $F_{\theta}$ and $G_{\theta}$ are such that $F_{\alpha \theta}=S^{\alpha} F_{\theta}$ and $G_{\alpha \theta}=S^{\alpha} G_{\theta}$.
(iii) For the sequence of experiments $\left\{\mathscr{F}_{n}\right\}$ the $X_{n}$ are a distinguished sequence.
(iv) The $F_{\theta}$ are absolutely continuous with respect to the Haar measure of $G$.

Furthermore, $G$ admits almost invariant means.

Then there is a probability measure $Q$ such that $G_{\theta}=F_{\theta} * Q$ for all $\theta$. Proof. Condition (iii) says that the experiment $\mathscr{F}=\left\{F_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ is the limit of the experiments $\mathscr{F}_{n}$. Thus $\mathscr{F}$ is at least as strong as the experiment $\left\{G_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$, according to Chapter 7, Proposition 1. This implies the existence of a transition $K$ such that $G_{\theta}=K F_{\theta}$. However, according to Theorem 2, one may select for $K$ a transition such that $S^{\alpha} K=K S^{\alpha}$ for all $\alpha \in \Theta$. According to Proposition 1 such a transition is obtainable by convolution, so that $G_{\theta}=K F_{\theta}=F_{\theta} * Q$. This completes the proof of the proposition.

Note. It can be shown that when requirement (ii) of the proposition holds, absolute continuity with respect to the Haar measure is already implied by absolute continuity with respect to any arbitrary Radon measure $\mu$ on $G$. Indeed, one may assume that for every positive continuous function $u$ with compact support on $G$ the relation $\int u d \mu=0$ is equivalent to $\int u d F_{\theta}=0$ for all $\theta$. (That is, one may assume that $\mu$ is locally in the band generated by the $F_{\theta}$.) If so, $S^{\alpha} \mu$ is also locally equivalent to $\mu$. Therefore $\mu$ is locally equivalent to the Haar measure.

Another application is a result of Torgersen as follows.
Let $G$ be a locally compact group and let $P$ and $P^{\prime}$ be two probability measures which are Radon measures on $G$. Let $\mathscr{E}$ be the experiment $E=\left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in G\right\}$ with $P_{\theta}=S^{\theta} P$. Similarly, define $\mathscr{E}^{\prime}$ by $P_{\theta}^{\prime}=S^{\theta} P^{\prime}$. For any $\alpha \in G$ define the right shifts $D^{\alpha}$ by $\left\langle f, D^{\alpha} \mu\right\rangle=\int f(x \alpha) \mu(d x)$.

Proposition 3. Assume that $G$ admits almost invariant means and that $\mathscr{E}$ is dominated by the Haar measure of $G$. Then the deficiency $\delta\left(\mathscr{E}^{\circ}, \mathscr{E}^{\prime}\right)$ is given by the formula

$$
\delta\left(\mathscr{E}, \mathscr{E}^{\prime}\right)=\inf _{\mu} \frac{1}{2}\left\|P^{\prime}-\int D^{\alpha} P \mu(d \alpha)\right\|
$$

where $\mu$ ranges over all probability measures which are Radon measures on $G$.
This same deficiency may also be written as

$$
\delta\left(\mathscr{E}, \mathscr{E}^{\prime}\right)=\sup _{f \in C_{1}} \inf _{\alpha}\left[f P^{\prime}-f D^{\alpha} P\right]
$$

for a set $C_{1}$ which is the set of continuous functions $f$ which have compact support and are such that $0 \leq f \leq 1$.

Proof. Let $T$ be any transition which is left invariant. Then according to Proposition 1 one may write $T P_{\theta}=P_{\theta} * \mu$ for some probability measure $\mu$. This may also be written in the form $F_{\theta} * \mu=\int D^{\alpha} P_{\theta} \mu(d \alpha)$. Thus

$$
\left\|P_{\theta}^{\prime}-T P_{\theta}\right\|=\left\|P_{\theta}^{\prime}-\int D^{\alpha} P_{\theta} \mu(d \alpha)\right\|=\left\|P^{\prime}-\int D^{\alpha} P \mu(d \alpha)\right\|
$$

For the second expression note that, according to the corollary of Theorem 2, Section 2 one may write

$$
\delta\left(\mathscr{E}, \mathscr{E}^{\prime}\right)=\sup _{f \in C_{1}} \inf _{T}\left[f P^{\prime}-f T P\right]
$$

Here $f T P$ may be written
$f T P=\int\left[\int f(x \alpha) P(d x)\right] \mu(d \alpha) \leq \sup _{\alpha} \int f(x \alpha) P(d x)=\sup \left\langle f, D^{\alpha} P\right\rangle$
The result follows.
These formulas yield explicit determinations of deficiencies in many cases. One first application of interest is as follows.

Consider a linear model $X=A^{\prime} \theta+\varepsilon$ where $\theta$ ranges through some finite dimensional vector space $\Theta$, the object $A^{\prime}$ is a linear map to a Euclidean vector space and $\varepsilon$ is a Gaussian random vector which has for covariance matrix the identity matrix of the Euclidean space. (This is a standard linear model in which the covariances are known.) Such a linear model defines an experiment $\mathscr{E}=\left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$. Consider also another such linear model $Y=B^{\prime} \theta+\eta$ where $\eta$ is a Gaussian variable with covariance matrix the identity in some Euclidean space, possibly different from the one attached to $\mathscr{E}$. Let $\mathscr{F}=\left\{Q_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ be the corresponding experiment.

It is then easy to show that $\mathscr{F}$ is better than $\mathscr{E}$ if and only if the matrix $B B^{\prime}-A A^{\prime}$ is positive semidefinite. Explicit computation of distances gives the following result.

Corollary 1 (Torgersen and Hansen). For the linear models described above, assume that $B B^{\prime}-A A^{\prime}$ is positive semidefinite and that $A A^{\prime}$ is nonsingular. Then

$$
\delta(\mathscr{E}, \mathscr{F})=\frac{1}{2} E\left\{\left|\frac{\left(\operatorname{det} B B^{\prime}\right)^{1 / 2}}{\left(\operatorname{det} A A^{\prime}\right)^{1 / 2}} \exp \left[-\frac{1}{2} Z^{\prime} \Gamma Z\right]-1\right|\right\}
$$

where $\Gamma=B B^{\prime}-A A^{\prime}$ and where $Z$ is a Gaussian variable with mean zero and covariance matrix $\left(A A^{\prime}\right)^{-1}$.

Proof. Let $\hat{\theta}$ be the least-squares estimate of $\theta$ in the experiment $\mathscr{E}$. This is a sufficient statistic with a Gaussian distribution $F_{\theta}$ which has expectation $\theta$ and a covariance matrix equal to the inverse of $A A^{\prime}$. Similarly the least-squares estimate for $\mathscr{F}$ has a Gaussian distribution $G_{\theta}$ with inverse covariance matrix $B B^{\prime}$.

Consider the problem of testing $G=G_{0}$ against the family $\left\{F_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$. For such a problem there is a least favorable prior distribution on $\Theta$. It is obviously the measure concentrated at $\theta=0$. Thus, according to Proposition 3 one has

$$
\delta(\mathscr{E}, \mathscr{F})=\frac{1}{2}\|G-F\|,
$$

with $F=F_{0}$. The result follows by writing out the densities for $G$ and $F$. A particular case of the above is the situation where $B B^{\prime}$ is a multiple of $A A^{\prime}$, say $B B^{\prime}=c A A^{\prime}$, with $c>1$. In such a case, let $k$ be the dimension of $\Theta$ and let $\chi^{2}$ be a random variable which has a $\chi^{2}$ distribution with $k$ degrees of freedom. Then $\delta(\mathscr{E}, \mathscr{F})$ may be written as

$$
\delta(\mathscr{E}, \mathscr{F})=\operatorname{Prob}\left\{\frac{\log c}{c-1} \leq \frac{\chi^{2}}{k} \leq \frac{c \log c}{c-1}\right\}
$$

This situation occurs, for instance, when the experiment $\mathscr{E}$ is a direct product of $m$ independent replicates of a given experiment $\mathscr{E}_{1}$ and $\mathscr{F}$ is a similar product of $n=c m$ replicates of $\mathscr{E}_{1}$.

In such a case $\mathscr{F}$ may be written as a direct product $\mathscr{F}=\mathscr{E} \times \mathscr{K}$ where $\mathscr{K}$ consists of $r=n-m$ replicates of $\mathscr{E}_{1}$. In such a direct product $\mathscr{E}$ appears as a subexperiment of $\mathscr{F}$. Thus, according to Chapter 5 one can also measure the information lost by ignoring $\mathscr{K}$ by the number called "insufficiency" of $\mathscr{E}$ in $\mathscr{E} \times \mathscr{K}$.

In the present case the conditional expectation operator associated to $\theta$ and the $\sigma$-field of $\mathscr{E}$ is exactly the distribution $K_{\theta}$ of the variables observable in $\mathscr{K}=\left\{K_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$. Thus the insufficiency may be written

$$
\eta=\inf _{H} \sup _{\theta} \frac{1}{2} \int\left\|K_{\theta}-H_{x}\right\| P_{\theta}(d x)
$$

for an infimum taken over all Markov kernels $H: x \mapsto H_{x}$. For any such kernel $K$ and for $\varepsilon>0$ one could define measurable functions $x \mapsto t(x) \in \Theta$ such that $\left\|K_{t(x)}-K_{x}\right\| \leq \varepsilon+\inf _{\theta}\left\|K_{\theta}-K_{x}\right\|$, giving $\left\|K_{\theta}-K_{t(x)}\right\| \leq \varepsilon+ 2\left\|K_{\theta}-K_{x}\right\|$.

Consider then the problem of estimating $\theta$ with estimates available on $\mathscr{E}$ and a loss function $W(\theta, t)=\frac{1}{2}\left\|K_{\theta}-K_{t}\right\|$. This problem admits a minimax risk with a certain value, say $\beta$. It follows then that $\frac{1}{2} \beta \leq \eta \leq \beta$.

To evaluate $\beta$ one may replace the experiment $\mathscr{E}=\left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ by the experiment $\left\{F_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ given by the distributions $F_{\theta}$ of the least-squares estimates of $\mathscr{E}$. Similarly one may assume that $K_{\theta}$ is the distribution of the least-squares estimates of $\mathscr{K}$. By assumption $F_{\theta}$ has mean $\theta$ and inverse covariance matrix $M=A A^{\prime}$. With $r=n-m$, the distribution $K_{\theta}$ has mean $\theta$ and inverse covariance matrix $\Gamma=(r / m) M$. It follows that

$$
W(\theta, t)=\frac{1}{2}\left\|K_{\theta}-K_{t}\right\|=\operatorname{Pr}[|u| \leq s]
$$

for a Gaussian $\mathscr{N}(0,1)$ variable $u$ and a number $s>0$ such that $s^{2}= \frac{1}{4}(r / m)(\theta-t) M(\theta-t)^{\prime}$.

The usual computations, using a Bayesian approach with Gaussian prior distributions which have mean zero and inverse covariances proportional to $M$, show that the minimax risk $\beta$ is precisely equal to the probability

$$
\operatorname{Pr}\left\{|t| \leq \frac{1}{2} \sqrt{k \frac{r}{m}}\right\}
$$

for a $t$-statistic $t$ which has a number of degrees of freedom $k$ equal to the dimension of $\Theta$.

A comparison between the deficiency $\delta(\mathscr{E}, \mathscr{F})$ and the insufficiency $\eta$ is interesting in this case. Letting $\alpha$ be the difference $\alpha=c-1=(r / m)$, the deficiency is equal to

$$
\delta=\operatorname{Pr}\left\{\frac{\log (1+\alpha)}{\alpha} \leq \frac{\chi^{2}}{k} \leq \frac{(1+\alpha)}{\alpha} \log (1+\alpha)\right\}
$$

while the insufficiency is bounded above by $\beta=\operatorname{Pr}\left\{|t| \leq \frac{1}{2} \sqrt{k \alpha}\right\}$. It is also bounded below by $\frac{1}{2} \beta$. Thus, for $k$ fixed and for $\alpha$ small the deficiency is roughly proportional to $\alpha \sqrt{k}$.

On the contrary, the insufficiency is roughly proportional to $\sqrt{\alpha} \sqrt{k}$. This is in general agreement with the bounds obtained in Chapter 5. In fact, Chapter 5, Section 4, Proposition 5 gives bounds of the type $\eta \leq \operatorname{cst} \delta^{1 / 2}$. Here for fixed $k$ and small $\alpha$ we obtain a reverse inequality of the type $\eta \geq \operatorname{cst} \delta^{1 / 2}$. This suggests that the bounds of Chapter 5 cannot readily be improved.

As a further application of the results of Proposition 3, consider two experiments, $\mathscr{E}=\left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ and $\mathscr{F}=\left\{Q_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$, indexed by a Euclidean space $\Theta$. Assume that $P_{\theta}=\mathscr{L}[X+\theta]$ for a certain variable $X$. Assume also that $Q_{\theta}=\mathscr{L}[Y+\theta]$ for some other variable $Y$.

Corollary. In the shift situation just described, assume that the covariances of $X$ and $Y$ exist and are identical. Assume also that $\mathscr{L}(X)$ is not a translate of $\mathscr{L}(Y)$. Then the experiments $\mathscr{E}$ and $\mathscr{F}$ cannot be comparable.

Indeed, if, for instance, $\delta(\mathscr{E}, \mathscr{F})=0$, one could obtain $\mathscr{L}(Y)$ as a convolution $\mathscr{L}(Y)=\mathscr{L}(X+Z)$ for $X$ and $Z$ independent. However, if $Z$ is not degenerate the covariances of $X$ and $Y$ cannot be equal.

## 4. Automatic Invariance of Limits

Throughout this section we shall consider a sequence $\left\{\mathscr{E}_{n}\right\}, \mathscr{E}_{n}=\left\{P_{\theta, n} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ of experiments indexed by a set $\Theta$ which is a Euclidean space $R^{k}$. It is also assumed that for each $n$ one has a statistic $X_{n}$ available on $\mathscr{E}_{n}$, taking its values in $R^{k}$, and a sequence of numbers $\delta_{n}>0, \delta_{n} \rightarrow 0$. (One could take for $\Theta$ a measurable subset of $R^{k}$, as will be explained further on. However, this complicates the notation.) It will be convenient to use a copy of $R^{k}$, called $V$, and to introduce the following notation.

For each $n$ and $\theta$, let $F_{n}(\theta, \xi)$ be the element

$$
F_{n}(\theta, \xi)=P_{\theta+\delta_{n} \xi, n}
$$

of the experiment $\mathscr{E}_{n}$. Similarly $G_{n}(\theta, \xi)$ will be the distribution

$$
G_{n}(\theta, \xi)=\mathscr{L}\left\{\delta_{n}^{-1}\left(X_{n}-\theta\right) \mid \theta+\delta_{n} \xi\right\}
$$

of $\delta_{n}^{-1}\left(X_{n}-\theta\right)$ for the measure $P_{\theta+\delta_{n} \xi, n}$.
The family $\mathscr{F}_{\theta, n}=\left\{F_{n}(\theta, \xi) ; \xi \in V\right\}$ will be called the differential experiment



attached to $\mathscr{E}_{n}$ at the point $\theta$. The function $\theta \mapsto \mathscr{F}_{\theta, n}$ will be called an atlas of $\mathscr{E}_{n}$.

The function of two variables $(\theta, \xi) \mapsto F_{n}(\theta, \xi)$ can be subjected to shift operations on either one of its two arguments $\theta$ and $\xi$. We shall call $S_{i}^{\alpha}$ the shift by an amount $\alpha$ operating on the $i$ th argument, $i=1,2$, so that for instance,

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \left(F_{n} S_{1}^{\alpha}\right)(\theta, \xi)=F_{n}(\theta+\alpha, \xi) \\
& \left(F_{n} S_{2}^{\alpha}\right)(\theta, \xi)=F_{n}(\theta, \xi+\alpha)
\end{aligned}
$$

For the function $G_{n}$ a similar notation will be used. However, $G_{n}(\theta, \xi)$ being a measure on $R^{k}$ can also be shifted in that capacity, so that $S^{\beta} G_{n}(\theta, \xi)$ is the distribution of $\beta+\delta_{n}^{-1}\left(X_{n}-\theta\right)$. Note also the identity

$$
\mathscr{L}\left\{\delta_{n}^{-1}\left(X_{n}-\theta-\delta_{n} \alpha\right) \mid P_{\theta+\delta_{n}(\alpha+\xi), n}\right\}=G_{n}\left[\theta+\delta_{n} \alpha, \xi\right]=S^{-\alpha} G_{n}[\theta, \alpha+\xi]
$$

Similarly, for $F_{n}$ one has the identity

$$
F_{n}\left(\theta+\delta_{n} \alpha, \xi\right)=F_{n}[\theta, \alpha+\xi]
$$

so that the experiment $\mathscr{F}_{\theta+\delta_{n} \alpha, n}$ attached to $\mathscr{E}_{n}$ at the point $\theta+\delta_{n} \alpha$ is the same as the experiment $\left\{F_{n}(\theta, \alpha+\xi) ; \xi \in V\right\}$ where the index $\xi$ has been moved over by the vector $\alpha$.

Using the shift symbolism introduced above, the identities become

$$
G_{n} S_{1}^{\delta_{n} \alpha}=S^{-\alpha} G_{n} S_{2}^{\alpha}
$$

and

$$
F_{n} S_{1}^{\delta_{n} \alpha}=F_{n} S_{2}^{\alpha}
$$

We shall refer to those identities by the name of cartographers identities, to recall that they are introduced by the process of making differential atlases of the correspondences $\theta \mapsto P_{\theta, n}$ and $\theta \mapsto \mathscr{L}\left\{\delta_{n}^{-1}\left(X_{n}-\theta\right) \mid \theta\right\}$.

The introduction of the extra variable $\xi \in V$ may appear to be just another unwarranted complication of the formulation. However, the reader will note that it is a common practice in asymptotic arguments where one fixes a particular point $\theta$ and looks only at the behaviour of statistics, estimates, or experiments in shrinking vicinities of the point $\theta$.

Of course, there is no deep reason why the vicinities in question should be vicinities which shrink at a rate $\delta_{n}$. It is just a very common practice to look at things such as $\left\{P_{t, n} ;|t-\theta| \leq b \delta_{n}\right\}$ for a fixed $\theta$ and then rescale the differences $t-\theta$ to stabilize them.

At first glance such a procedure seems to be harmless and devoid of consequences. However, as we shall see, it does have consequences which reflect themselves in rather strong restrictions on the possible limits of the differential experiments $\mathscr{F}_{\theta, n}$ or the possible limits of the distributions $G_{n}(\theta, \xi)$.

The reason is easily visible; for instance, on the cartographer indentity $G_{n} S_{1}^{\delta_{n} \alpha}=S^{-\alpha} G_{n} S_{2}^{\alpha}$. On the right side the coefficient $\delta_{n}$ does not appear explicitly. On the left side the shift operation operates only by an amount $\delta_{n} \alpha$ which tends to zero. This, passing to the limit one should expect that, with a modicum of continuity, one will obtain an invariance relation such as $G=S^{-\alpha} G S_{2}^{\alpha}$. This is precisely what happens, but it is perhaps surprising, or even frustrating that the amount of continuity needed to obtain such a relation is very little indeed. We shall proceed to show this, first assuming only a measurability restriction on the experiment $\mathscr{E}_{n}$, but passing to the limit in an averaged sort of a way and then applying the result to certain cases where further regularity restrictions are supposed to be satisfied. In order to take limits we need to introduce some topologies.

For the distributions $G_{n}(\theta, \xi)$ proceed as follows. Let $C_{0}$ be the space of all continuous functions which are defined on $V=R^{k}$ and tend to zero at infinity. Let $K$ be the set of all positive linear functionals of norm at most unity on $C_{0}$. Then $G_{n}(\theta, \xi)$ are elements of $K$. This set $K$ is compact and metrizable for the topology of pointwise convergence on $C_{0}$. This is the topology which will be used here. However, instead of looking at functions such as $(\theta, \xi) \mapsto G_{n}(\theta, \xi)$ with values in $K$, we shall first take averages. For this purpose one needs appropriate measurability conditions. The following restriction will be used.

Definition 3. The experiments $\mathscr{E}_{n}=\left\{P_{\theta, n} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ are measurable if for every $u$ in the dual $M\left(\mathscr{E}_{n}\right)$ of the space $L\left(\mathscr{E}_{n}\right)$ the functions $\theta \mapsto\left\langle u, P_{\theta, n}\right\rangle$ are Lebesgue measurable on $\Theta=R^{k}$.

If this measurability condition is satisfied, the maps $(\theta, \xi) \mapsto G_{n}(\theta, \xi)$ and the
maps $(\theta, \xi) \mapsto\left\langle u, F_{n}(\theta, \xi)\right\rangle$ are automatically jointly measurable in $(\theta, \xi)$. Let then $\mathscr{W}^{\prime}$ be the set of all probability measures which are absolutely continuous with respect to the Lebesgue measure $\lambda$ of $R^{k}$ and let $L_{1}$ be the space of all bounded measures absolutely continuous with respect to $\lambda$.

Assuming measurability, one can define the integrals

$$
\bar{G}_{n}(\mu, v)=\iint G_{n}(\theta, \xi) \mu(d \theta) v(d \xi)
$$

for all pairs $(\mu, v) \in L_{1} \times L_{1}$. For the experiments $\mathscr{F}_{\theta, n}$ one can proceed in a related but different fashion as follows.

First, for $v \in \mathscr{W}$ one can define the Gelfand integral

$$
F_{n}(\theta) v=\int F_{n}(\theta, \xi) v(d \xi)
$$

as an element of the second dual of $L\left(\mathscr{E}_{n}\right)$. This gives an averaged experiment

$$
\overline{\overline{\mathscr{F}}}_{\theta, n}=\left\{F_{n}(\theta) v ; v \in \mathscr{W}\right\} .
$$

(We have used the notation $F_{n}(\theta) v$ to indicate that the object represented by $F_{n}(\theta)$ is a transition from bounded absolutely continuous measures on $V$ to the second dual of $L\left(\mathscr{E}_{n}\right)$.)

Let $\mathscr{H}$ be the Choquet space of the product $R^{\#}$. Each averaged experiment $\overline{\mathscr{F}_{\boldsymbol{\theta}}, \boldsymbol{n}}$ corresponds to a certain conical measure, say $m_{\boldsymbol{\theta}, \boldsymbol{n}}$, which is positive linear functional on $\mathscr{H}$. For each $h \in \mathscr{H}$ the maps $\theta \mapsto\left\langle h, m_{\theta, n}\right\rangle$ are measurable and one can again take averages $\bar{m}_{n}(\mu)=\int m_{\theta, n} \mu(d \theta)$ for $\mu \in \mathscr{W}$. Each such $\bar{m}_{n}(\mu)$ is still a conical measure on $R^{\mathscr{W}}$.

Note that $\bar{m}_{n}(\mu)$ is not the conical measure of the experiments $\left\{\int\left[F_{n}(\theta) v\right] \mu(d \theta)\right.$; $v \in \mathscr{W}\}$. It is the conical measure of the mixture where one first observes a $\theta$ selected according to $\mu$ and then performs an experiment $\overline{\mathscr{F}}_{\theta, n}$.

In fact it will be more convenient to allow more freedom to the measures $\mu$ used above and use the space $L_{1}$ of all bounded measures which are absolutely continuous with respect to the Lebesgue measure $\lambda$ on $R^{k}$. Thus we shall identify the maps $\mu \mapsto \bar{m}_{n}(\mu)$ defined on $\mathscr{W}$ with the restriction to $\mathscr{W}$ of the corresponding linear maps from $L_{1}$ to the Riesz dual $\mathscr{H}^{*}$ of $\mathscr{H}$. The set of maps $\mu \mapsto \bar{m}(\mu)$ obtainable by the averaging process described above can be circumscribed as follows.

Note first that $\mathscr{W}$ itself defines an experiment by the identity map of $\mathscr{W}$ into $L_{1}$. Then, the averaged experiments $\overline{\mathscr{F}}_{\theta, n}=\left\{F_{n}(\theta) v ; v \in \mathscr{W}\right\}$ are seen to be obtained by applying the transition $F_{n}(\theta)$ from $L_{1}$ to the second dual of $L\left(\mathscr{E}_{n}\right)$. In particular, $\overline{\mathscr{F}}_{\theta, n}$ is always weaker than the experiment $\mathscr{W}$ itself. Let $w$ be the conical measure of $\mathscr{W}$.

The set $C$ of all conical measures which correspond to experiments weaker than $\mathscr{W}$ itself is easily characterizable. Indeed, let $\mathscr{H}_{c}$ be the space of positive convex elements of $\mathscr{H}$ used in Chapter 3 to compare conical measures. A conical measure $m$ belongs to $C$ if and only if it has resultant identically unity and is such that $\langle h, m\rangle \leq\langle h, w\rangle$ for all $h \in \mathscr{H}_{c}$. Thus the maps $\mu \mapsto \bar{m}_{n}(\mu)$ defined above are all elements of the set $\bar{\Gamma}$ described below.

Definition 4. The space $\bar{\Gamma}$ is the space of positive linear maps $\mu \mapsto \bar{m}(\mu)$ from $L_{1}$ to $\mathscr{H}^{*}$ such that if $\mu \in \mathscr{W}$ then $\bar{m}(\mu)$ has resultant unity and is such that $\langle h, \bar{m}\rangle \leq\langle h, w\rangle$ for all $h \in \mathscr{H}_{c}$. It will be given the topology of pointwise convergence on $\mathscr{H} \times L_{1}$.

For distributions of estimates one may proceed similarly, using a product $L_{1} \times L_{1}$ where the first space $L_{1}=L_{1}(\Theta)$ is the space of bounded Radon measures absolutely continuous with respect to the Lebesgue measure $\lambda$ on $\Theta$ and where the second $L_{1}$ is the space $L_{1}(V)$ defined similarly on $V$.

Definition 5. The space $B$ is the space of positive bilinear maps $\bar{G}$ from $L_{1} \times L_{1}=L_{1}(\Theta) \times L_{1}(V)$ to the dual $C_{0}^{*}$ of $C_{0}$ subject to the restriction that $\|\bar{G}(\mu, v)\| \leq\|\mu\|\|v\|$ for all pairs $(\mu, v) \in L_{1} \times L_{1}$. It is topologized by the topology of pointwise convergence on $C_{0} \times L_{1} \times L_{1}$.

One of the reasons for the introduction of the spaces $B$ and $\bar{\Gamma}$ is the following remark.

Lemma 3. The space $B \times \bar{\Gamma}$ is a compact metrizable space.
Proof. Both $B$ and $\bar{\Gamma}$ are defined by algebraic relations and inequalities which are preserved by pointwise passages to the limit. This implies the desired compactness. For the metrizability note that both $C_{0}$ and $L_{1}$ are separable Banach spaces. Convergence on a countable dense subset of $C_{0} \times L_{1} \times L_{1}$ implies convergence on all of $C_{0} \times L_{1} \times L_{1}$. Therefore $B$ is metrizable. For $\bar{\Gamma}$, let us first prove
that $C$ itself is metrizable. For this, note that if $F$ is any transition defined on $L_{1}$ one has $\left\|F \mu_{1}-F \mu_{2}\right\| \leq\left\|\mu_{1}-\mu_{2}\right\|$. Let then $D_{0}$ be a countable dense subset of $\mathscr{W} \subset L_{1}$. Let $\mathscr{H}\left(D_{0}\right)$ be the subspace of $\mathscr{H}$ which is the smallest vector sublattice of $\mathscr{H}$ containing all the evaluation maps for coordinates in $D_{0} \subset \mathscr{W}$. Because of the Lipschitz condition recalled above, convergence of elements of $C$ on $\mathscr{H}\left(D_{0}\right)$ already implies convergence on all of $\mathscr{H}$. Thus $C$ is metrizable and since $L_{1}$ is separable, metrizability of $\bar{\Gamma}$ follows as above. This concludes the proof of the Lemma.

Now let us return to the measurable experiments $\mathscr{E}_{n}=\left\{P_{\theta, n} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ and the associated maps $\bar{G}_{n}$ and $\bar{m}_{n}$. Recall that we have noted $\bar{m}_{n}(\mu)$ the integral $\bar{m}_{n}(\mu)=\int m_{\theta, n} \mu(d \theta)$ where $m_{\theta, n}$ is the conical measure of $\overline{\mathscr{F}}_{\theta, n}= \left\{F_{n}(\theta) v ; v \in \mathscr{W}\right\}$. Let us consider also the conical measures $m_{\theta, n}^{\alpha}$ of the experiments $\overline{\mathscr{F}}_{\theta, n}^{\alpha}=\left\{F_{n}(\theta)\left(S^{\alpha} v\right) ; v \in \mathscr{W}\right\}$ and their integrals $\bar{m}_{n}^{\alpha}(\mu)=\int m_{\theta, n}^{\alpha} \mu(d \theta)$.

Lemma 4. Assume that the experiments $\mathscr{E}_{n}=\left\{P_{\theta, n} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ are measurable and let $\bar{G}_{n}, \bar{m}_{n}$, and $\bar{m}_{n}^{\alpha}$ be the associated maps, $\bar{G}_{n} \in B, \bar{m}_{n} \in \bar{\Gamma}, \bar{m}_{n}^{\alpha} \in \bar{\Gamma}$. Then, for every
$\alpha \in$ Vone has
(i) $S^{\alpha} \bar{G}_{n}\left[S^{\delta_{n} \alpha} \mu, v\right]=\bar{G}_{n}\left[\mu, S^{\alpha} v\right]$;
(ii) $\bar{m}_{n}\left[S^{\delta_{n}^{\alpha}} \mu\right]=\bar{m}_{n}^{\alpha}(\mu)$;
(iii) $\left\|\bar{G}_{n}\left[S^{\alpha} \mu, v\right]-\bar{G}_{n}[\mu, v]\right\| \leq\|v\|\left\|S^{\alpha} \mu-\mu\right\|$; and, if $\mu \in \mathscr{W}$,
(iv) $\Delta\left[\bar{m}_{n}\left(S^{\alpha} \mu\right), \bar{m}_{n}(\mu)\right] \leq\left\|S^{\alpha} \mu-\mu\right\|$ for the distance $\Delta$ between the experiment types which correspond to the indicated conical measures.

Proof. The relations (i) and (ii) follow by integration from the cartographers relation on the maps $(\theta, \xi) \mapsto G_{n}(\theta, \xi)$ and $\theta \mapsto m_{\theta, n}$. Relation (iii) is a consequence of the definitions. As to the assertion (iv), if $h \in \mathscr{H}$ one may write

$$
\left|\int\left\langle h, m_{\theta, n}\right\rangle\left[\left(S^{\alpha} \mu\right)(d \theta)-\mu(d \theta)\right]\right| \leq\|h\|\left\|S^{\alpha} \mu-\mu\right\|
$$

However, the functions $h \in \mathscr{H}$ which are used to define the distance $\Delta$ are all such that $0 \leq h \leq 1$. The result follows.

The equicontinuity relations (iii) and (iv) written above have a consequence which may be stated as follows.

Proposition 4. Let ( $\bar{G}, \bar{m}$ ) be any cluster point in $B \times \bar{\Gamma}$ of the sequence $\left\{\left(\bar{G}_{n}, \bar{m}_{n}\right)\right\}$. Then for all ( $\alpha, \mu, v$ ) one has
(i) $S^{\alpha} \bar{G}(\mu, v)=\bar{G}\left(\mu, S^{\alpha} v\right)$
(ii) $\bar{m}(\mu)=\bar{m}^{\alpha}(\mu)$.

Proof. The measures which enter in the expressions on the left sides of (i) and (ii) are of the form $S^{\delta_{n} \alpha} \mu$. However, for $\mu \in L_{1}$, the norms $\left\|S^{\delta_{n} \alpha} \mu-\mu\right\|$ tend to zero as $n \rightarrow \infty$. The result follows.

As can be seen, Proposition 4 is a very simple result which yields some sort of invariance of limits without any restrictions whatsoever, except the measurability of the map $\theta \mapsto\left\langle u, P_{\theta, n}\right\rangle$. However, as stated, it applies to the elements of $B \times \bar{\Gamma}$ and not directly to the maps $(\theta, \xi) \mapsto G_{n}(\theta, \xi)$ and $\theta \mapsto \mathscr{F}_{\theta, n}=\left\{F_{n}(\theta, \xi) ; \xi \in V\right\}$ which gave rise to $\bar{G}_{n}$ and $\bar{m}_{n}$.

To return from the elements of $B$ and $\bar{\Gamma}$ to maps respectively defined on $\Theta \times V$ and $\Theta$, we shall first select representatives of the experiments which correspond to the conical measures $m \in C$ and then apply twice the lifting operation $A$ which was obtained in the proof of Theorem 3, Section 3.

To represent conical measures $m \in C$, consider the Choquet space $\mathscr{H}$ of $R^{\mathscr{W}}$. If $\omega \in R^{\mathscr{W}}$ let $l_{v}(\omega)$ be its value at $v \in \mathscr{W}$. Select a $v_{0} \in \mathscr{W}$ which is equivalent to the Lebesgue measure; for instance, a standard Gaussian measure of $R^{k}$. Let $\mathscr{H}_{\nu_{0}}$ be the subspace of $\mathscr{H}$ formed by those $\phi \in \mathscr{H}$ such that $l_{\nu_{0}}(\omega)=0$ implies $\phi(\omega)=0$. Let $\Phi$ be the space of restrictions of the elements $\phi \in \mathscr{H}_{\nu_{0}}$ to the set $Z=\left\{\omega ; \omega \in R^{\mathscr{W}}, l_{\nu_{0}}(\omega)=1\right\}$. Then $m$ restricted to $\mathscr{H}_{\nu_{0}}$ can also be identified with the restriction to $\Phi$ of a certain probability measure $Q_{v_{0}}$ on the space $Z$. For any experiment, say $\left\{P_{v} ; v \in \mathscr{W}\right\}$ which has conical measure $m$, the measure $Q_{\nu_{0}}$ is simply the distribution for $P_{\nu_{0}}$ of the process

$$
\left\{v \mapsto \frac{d P_{v}}{d P_{v_{0}}}\right\}
$$

We shall take as a representative version of such experiments the experiment $\left\{Q_{v} ; v \in \mathscr{W}\right\}$, with $d Q_{v}=\left(l_{v} / l_{v_{0}}\right) d Q_{v_{0}}$ on $Z$.

Note that if $S$ is a permutation of the set $\mathscr{W}$, the experiment $\left\{v \mapsto Q_{S v} ; v \in \mathscr{W}\right\}$ is then represented by the family $\left\{T Q_{S v} ; v \in \mathscr{W}\right\}$, where $T$ is the point transformation of $Z$ into itself such that

$$
l_{v}[T(\omega)]=l_{S v}(\omega)\left[l_{S v_{0}}(\omega)\right]^{-1}
$$

In particular, if $S^{\alpha}$ is the usual shift operation on measures, there corresponds to it a point transformation $T_{\alpha}$. One verifies readily that $T_{\alpha+\beta}=T_{\alpha} T_{\beta}$.

Now consider a specific lifting $A$ obtained by the particular method used in the proof of Theorem 3, Section 3. To pass from the maps $\bar{G} \in B$ and $\bar{m} \in \Gamma$ to maps defined pointwise, we shall apply $A$ twice as follows.

First consider a $\bar{G} \in B$. For a particular triplet $\gamma \in C_{0},(\mu, v) \in L_{1} \times L_{1}= L_{1}(\Theta) \times L_{1}(V)$, the evaluation $\gamma \bar{G}(\mu, v)$ is a certain number which does not exceed $\|\gamma\|\|\mu\|\|v\|$.
