Fixing a $\gamma \in C_{0}, \gamma \geq 0$ and a $v \in L_{1}(V), v \geq 0$, the map $\gamma \bar{G}(\cdot, v)$ defined by $\mu \mapsto \gamma \bar{G}(\mu, v)$ is a positive linear map from $L_{1}(\Theta)$ to the line. As such it is also identifiable with a certain element of the space $\mathscr{M}(\Theta)$ of equivalence classes of bounded $\lambda$-measurable functions on $\Theta$. One may apply to it the lifting operation $A$ yielding an element of $\mathscr{M}(\Theta)$ which will be noted $\gamma \bar{G} A_{1}(v)$, with value at $\theta$ equal to $\gamma \bar{G} A_{1}(\theta, v)$. The subscript on $A$ indicates that the lifting operation is applied to the first argument of $\bar{G}$.

For each $\theta$, and for a fixed $\gamma \in C_{0}, \gamma \geq 0$, the map $\gamma \mapsto \gamma \bar{G} A_{1}(\theta, v)$ is again a bounded positive linear functional on $L_{1}(V)$. Applying the operation $A$ again, one obtains for each $\gamma$ an element of the space $\mathscr{M}(V)$ of bounded $\lambda$-measurable functions on $V$. Its value at $\xi \in V$ will be noted $\gamma \bar{G} A_{1} A_{2}(\theta, \xi)$.

This yields a positive linear functional $\gamma \mapsto \gamma \bar{G} A_{1} A_{2}(\theta, \xi)$ defined on $C_{0}$ and such that $\left|\gamma \bar{G} A_{1} A_{2}(\theta, \xi)\right| \leq\|\gamma\|$. Thus this map is also representable as the evaluation at $\gamma$ of a certain Radon measure $G(\theta, \xi)$ which belongs from space $K$. By construction $\bar{G}(\mu, v)$ is the iterated integral

$$
\bar{G}(\mu, v)=\int\left[\int G(\theta, \xi) v(d \xi)\right] \mu(d \theta)
$$

For the maps $\bar{m} \in \bar{\Gamma}$ one may proceed similarly as follows. For a fixed $h \in \mathscr{H}$, $h \geq 0$, consider the map $\mu \mapsto\langle h, \bar{m}(\mu)\rangle$. This is a positive linear map defined on $L_{1}(\Theta)$. Also, if $\mu \geq 0,\|\mu\|=1$, that is $\mu \in \mathscr{W}$, one has $0 \leq\langle h, \bar{m}(\mu)\rangle \leq\langle h, w\rangle$ for all $h \in \mathscr{H}_{c}$. Consider this map as an element of $\mathscr{M}(\Theta)$ and apply the lifting $A$ to it, yielding an element $\left\langle h, \bar{m} A_{1}\right\rangle$ of $\mathscr{M}(\Theta)$. Evaluating this at a particular $\theta$ gives a conical measure $m_{\theta}=\left(\bar{m} A_{1}\right)(\theta)$ such that $0 \leq\left\langle h, m_{\theta}\right\rangle \leq\langle h, w\rangle$ for all $h \in \mathscr{H}_{c}$. Thus $m_{\theta}$ is an element of $C$ and by construction $\bar{m}(\mu)=\int m_{\theta} \mu(d \theta)$.

At this point each $m_{\theta}$ is an element of $C$ which can be assigned an experiment, say $\overline{\mathscr{F}}_{\theta}=\left\{Q_{\theta, v} ; v \in \mathscr{W}\right\}$, where the $Q_{\theta, v}$ are probability measures on the subset $Z=\left\{\omega, l_{\nu_{0}}(\omega)=1\right\}$ as described previously.

Since $\overline{\mathscr{F}}_{\theta}$ is weaker than $\mathscr{W}$, there are transitions, say $F(\theta)$, from $L_{1}(V)$ to the space of measures on $Z$ such that $Q_{\theta, v}=F(\theta) v$. Applying the operation $A$ once more one obtains positive linear maps, say $\gamma \mapsto \gamma F(\theta) A_{2}$ from $C^{b}(Z)$ to $\mathscr{M}(V)$. For simplicity, we shall write $F(\theta, \xi)$ instead of $\left[F(\theta) A_{2}\right](\xi)$. The measure $F(\theta) v$ is then the integral

$$
F(\theta) v=\int F(\theta, \xi) v(d \xi)
$$

by construction.
Theorem 4. Let $\left\{\mathscr{E}_{n}\right\}$ be a sequence of measurable experiments, $\mathscr{E}_{n}=\left\{\underline{P}_{\theta, n}\right.$; $\theta \in \Theta\}, \Theta=R^{k}$. Let $\left(\bar{G}_{n}, \bar{m}_{n}\right)$ be the associated maps $\bar{G}_{n} \in B, \bar{m}_{n} \in \bar{\Gamma}$, and let $(\bar{G}, \bar{m})$ be a cluster point of the sequence $\left\{\left(\bar{G}_{n}, \bar{m}_{n}\right)\right\}$. Lift these maps $(\bar{G}, \bar{m})$ as explained above. Then
(a) For all pairs ( $\mu, v$ ) one has

$$
\bar{G}(\mu, v)=\iint G(\theta, \xi) \mu(d \theta) v(d \xi)
$$

where $G$ is a jointly Lebesgue measurable function defined on $\Theta \times V$ such that

$$
S^{\alpha} G(\theta, \xi)=G(\theta, \xi) S_{2}^{\alpha}=G(\theta, \xi+\alpha)
$$

for all $(\theta, \xi)$ and all $\alpha \in V$.
(b) For all $\theta \in \Theta$ the transitions $F(\theta)$ satisfy the relations $T_{\alpha} F(\theta) \mathrm{S}^{\alpha}=F(\theta)$ for all $\alpha \in V$ and the function $(\theta, \xi) \mapsto F(\theta, \xi)=\left[F(\theta) A_{2}\right](\xi)$ obtained from $F(\theta)$ satisfies the relation

$$
T_{\alpha} F(\theta, \xi+\alpha)=F(\theta, \xi)
$$

for all triplets $(\theta, \xi, \alpha)$.
(c) There are transitions $D_{\theta}$ such that (i) $G(\theta, \xi)=D_{\theta} F(\theta, \xi)$ for all $(\theta, \xi)$; (ii) $S^{\alpha} D_{\theta}=D_{\theta} T_{\alpha}$ for all $\alpha \in V$.

Proof. Statement (a) results from the construction itself, except that here we have asserted joint measurability of the function $G$. This can be checked by returning to the proof of Theorem 3, Section 3 as follows. The operation $A_{1}$ would be obtained by taking limits of maps of the type $\theta \mapsto \bar{G}\left[\mu_{\theta, r}, v\right]$ where $\mu_{\theta, r}$ is the normalized Lebesgue measure on a cube of diameter $1 / r$ centered at $\theta \in \Theta$. Taking similar operations in $V$ one sees that the object $\bar{G} A_{1} A_{2}$ is obtained by forming maps $(\theta, \xi) \mapsto \bar{G}\left(\mu_{\theta, r}, v_{\theta, s}\right)$ and passing to the limit first on $r$ and then on $s$. One could also take the limit by letting $r=s$ and letting $r$ tend to infinity. This would yield the lifting of Theorem 3, Section 3 on $\Theta \times V$ at once. Call this lifting $\hat{A}$. Then all we need to note is that $\bar{G} A_{1} A_{2}$ and $\bar{G} \hat{A}$ are almost everywhere equal, as follows from the Lebesgue differentiation theorems.

To prove statement (b) note first that the conical measures $m_{\theta}$ obtained by lifting the map $\bar{m}$ are all automatically shift invariant. Thus the experiments $\overline{\mathscr{F}}_{\theta}=\left\{Q_{\theta, v} ; v \in \mathscr{W}\right\}$ constructed on the set $Z=\left\{\omega \in R^{\mathscr{W}}, l_{\nu_{0}}(\omega)=1\right\}$ are also shift invariant. According to the remarks made about this construction process, the measure assigned to $v$ in the representation of the experiment $\left\{Q_{\theta, S^{\alpha} v}\right.$; $v \in \mathscr{W}\}$ is the measure $T_{\alpha} Q_{\theta, S^{\alpha} \nu}$, image of $Q_{\theta, S^{\alpha} \nu}$ by the point transformation $T_{\alpha}$ on $Z$. Thus if $F(\theta)$ is a transition such that $Q_{\theta, v}=F(\theta) v$, one must have $T_{\alpha} F(\theta) S^{\alpha} v=F(\theta) v$ for all $v \in \mathscr{W}$. Lifting this by the operation $A_{2}$ gives statement (b).

It remains to prove statement (c). For this purpose, let us return to the maps $\bar{G}_{n}$ and $\bar{m}_{n}$, and, for each $\mu \in \mathscr{W}$ consider the experiments

$$
\hat{\mathscr{G}}_{n}(\mu)=\left\{\bar{G}_{n}(\mu, v) ; v \in \mathscr{W}\right\}
$$

and

$$
\hat{\mathscr{F}}_{n}(\mu)=\left\{\hat{F}_{n}(\mu, v) ; v \in \mathscr{W}\right\},
$$

where, if $F_{n}(\theta) v=\int F_{n}(\theta, \xi) v(d \xi)$, one obtains $\hat{F}_{n}(\mu, v)$ by first observing $\theta$ according to the measure $\mu$ and then observing a variable which has distribution $F_{n}(\theta) v$. The experiment $\widehat{\mathscr{F}_{n}}(\mu)$ has a conical measure $\bar{m}_{n}(\mu)$ on the Choquet space $\mathscr{H}$ of $R^{\mathscr{W}}$.

The experiment $\hat{\mathscr{F}}_{n}(\mu)$, in which $\theta$ is observed, is stronger than the experiment in which the measure corresponding to $v$ is the average $\iint F_{n}(\theta, \xi) \mu(d \theta) v(d \xi)$. This latter experiment is in turn stronger than $\hat{\mathscr{G}}_{n}(\mu)$. Passing to the limit gives experiments $\hat{\mathscr{G}}(\mu)$ and $\hat{\mathscr{F}}(\mu)$ such that $\hat{\mathscr{F}}(\mu)$ is stronger than $\hat{\mathscr{G}}(\mu)$.

Now, according to Chapter 7, Proposition 1, the limit experiment $\hat{\mathscr{G}}_{(\mu)}$ is stronger than the experiment obtained by taking limits of the distributions $\bar{G}_{n}(\mu, v) ; v \in \mathscr{W}$. Here we have taken limits on $C_{0}$ so that the limit element $\bar{G}(\mu, v)$ is not necessarily a probability measure. If so, one can easily complement it by adding masses at the infinity of $R^{k}$. Doing so, we conclude that $\hat{\mathscr{F}}(\mu)$ is stronger than the experiment $\overline{\mathscr{G}}(\mu)=\{\bar{G}(\mu, v) ; v \in \mathscr{W}\}$.

Now take a partition of unity, $\left\{\gamma_{j} ; j=1,2, \ldots, r\right\}, \gamma_{j} \geq 0, \sum \gamma_{j}=1, \gamma_{j} \in C^{b}\left(R^{k}\right)$, and a finite set, say $J \subset \mathscr{W}$. Let $\left\{a_{i, j} ; i=1,2, \ldots, r ; j \in J\right\}$ be a finite set of numbers $a_{i, j} \geq 0$. Construct the sum

$$
R(\mu, v)=\sum_{i, j} a_{i, j}\left\langle\gamma_{i}, \bar{G}(\mu, j)\right\rangle
$$

According to Chapter 2, the numbers $a_{i, j}$ represent a Bayesian statistical decision problem where the possible decisions are the numbers $i=1,2, \ldots, r$. The sum $R(\mu, v)$ is the gain obtainable in this problem if, when $x$ is observed, one uses the decision procedure which selects $i$ with probability $\gamma_{i}(x)$.

Since $\hat{\mathscr{F}}(\mu)$ is stronger than $\hat{\mathscr{G}}(\mu)$ one can write

$$
R(\mu, v) \leq\langle\phi, \bar{m}(\mu)\rangle
$$

where $\phi$ is the element $\phi=\sup _{i} \sum_{j} a_{i, j} l_{j}$ of the Choquet space $\mathscr{H}$. Applying the lifting $A_{1}$ to the first coordinates yields the pointwise inequality

$$
\sum_{i, j} a_{i, j}\left\langle\gamma_{i}, \bar{G} A_{1}(\theta, j)\right\rangle \leq\left\langle\phi, m_{\theta}\right\rangle
$$

for all $\theta \in \Theta$.
Thus, by Proposition 1, Chapter 2, one can conclude that, for each $\theta \in \Theta$, the experiment $\left\{\bar{G} A_{1}(\theta, v) ; v \in \mathscr{W}\right\}$ is weaker than the experiment $\{F(\theta) v ; v \in \mathscr{W}\}$. This implies the existence of a transition, say $D_{\theta}$, such that $\bar{G} A_{1}(\theta, v)= D_{\theta} F(\theta) v$ for all $v \in \mathscr{W}$. At this point we may apply the lifting operation $A_{2}$ to the second argument and obtain the pointwise identity

$$
\bar{G} A_{1} A_{2}(\theta, \xi)=D_{\theta} F(\theta) A_{2}(\xi)=D_{\theta} F(\theta, \xi)
$$

which is valid for all pairs $(\theta, \xi) \in \Theta \times V$. The second part of statement (c) is then a consequence of Theorem 2, Section 2. This completes the proof of the Theorem.

Remark 1. The second part of property (c) in the Theorem says that the distributions $G(\theta, \xi)$ are those of an estimate which is "invariant" in the sense usually attached to this term in the literature. Consequences of this will be described further on.

Remark 2. The topology used here is very weak indeed. Some remarks on what happens for cases where convergence occurs in a stronger sense will be found below. Before passing on to this let us note that the passage to the limit with rescaling factors $\delta_{n}, \delta_{n} \rightarrow 0$ has induced a structural restriction on the limit experiments. One can show that the invariance in question is the only structural property so induced. This is the object of the following lemma, which uses a stronger topology.

Let $C$ be the space of conical measures which represent experiments weaker than $\mathscr{W}$. Let $\theta \mapsto m_{\theta, i}, i=1,2$, be two measurable maps from $\Theta$ to $C$. For each element $h$ of the Choquet space $\mathscr{H}$ and each $\mu \in \mathscr{W}$ one can define a pseudometric by

$$
\int\left|\left\langle h, m_{\theta, 1}-m_{\theta, 2}\right\rangle\right| \mu(d \theta) .
$$

The structure defined by all these pseudo-metrics, for $h \in \mathscr{H}$ and $\mu \in \mathscr{W}$, will be called the structure of convergence in measure. (It differs from the structure previously used on $\bar{\Gamma}$ in that here one takes the absolute value of the evaluations $\left\langle h, m_{\theta, 1}-m_{\theta, 2}\right\rangle$ before integrating.) We shall note $\Gamma$ the space of measurable maps so topologized.

Lemma 5. Let $\theta \mapsto \mathscr{F}_{\theta}$ be a measurable map from $\Theta$ to the set $C$ of experiments weaker than $\mathscr{W}$. If all the $\mathscr{F}_{\theta}$ are shift invariant there is a sequence $\left\{\mathscr{E}_{n}\right\}$,
$\mathscr{E}_{n}=\left\{P_{\theta, n} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ of experiments whose average atlases $\theta \mapsto \overline{\mathscr{F}}_{\theta, n}$ converge locally in measure to the map $\theta \mapsto \mathscr{F}_{\boldsymbol{\theta}}$.

Proof. Pave the set $\Theta$ with cubes of diameter $\varepsilon>0$ and retain only a finite number of the cubes, say $\left\{B_{j} ; j=1,2, \ldots, m\right\}$. Let $B_{0}$ be the complement of the union $\bigcup_{j} B_{j} ; j=1, \ldots, m$. Call a map $\theta \mapsto \mathscr{F}_{\theta}$ simple if it is constant on each $B_{j} ; j=0,1,2, \ldots, m$.

The set of simple maps obtained by varying the number $\varepsilon$ and the cubes of the paving is dense in $\Gamma$. Since the topology of $\Gamma$ is metrizable, it will be sufficient to show that every simple map $\theta \mapsto m_{\theta}$ where the $m_{\theta}$ are invariant is a limit of atlases as stated in the Lemma.

Consider then such a simple map $\theta \mapsto m_{\theta}$ defined by cubes $B_{j} ; j=1, \ldots, m$. Let $\theta_{j}$ be the center of $B_{j}$. Select a number $\alpha \in(0,1)$ and let $A_{j}$ be the open cube which is centered at $\theta_{j}$ and has a diameter $\alpha$ times that of $B_{j}$, so that $A_{j}$ is the interior of $\theta_{j}+\alpha\left(B_{j}-\theta_{j}\right)$. Remove the annular sets $B_{j}-A_{j}$ and replace the set $B_{0}$ by its interior. For any number $\varepsilon^{\prime}>0$ one can select the value of $\alpha$ so that the pieces so removed have a Lebesgue measure which does not exceed $\varepsilon^{\prime}$.

Let $\Omega=\bigcup_{j}\left\{A_{j} ; j=0,1,2, \ldots, m\right\}$ and let $\theta_{0}$ be some point in the open set $A_{0}$. The simple map $\theta \mapsto m_{\theta}$ which was defined on all of $\Theta$ can be restricted to $\Omega$. It is clearly sufficient to show that the restricted map can be approximated by maps obtained from atlases of experiments. For each $\theta \in \Omega$ one may form an experiment $\xi \mapsto F(\theta, \xi)$ as explained before the statement of Theorem 4, in such a manner that $m_{\theta}$ is the conical measure of the experiment $v \mapsto F(\theta) v= \int F(\theta, \xi) v(d \xi), v \in \mathscr{W}$. If $\theta \in A_{j}$ the measures $F(\theta, \xi)$ will be placed on a set $Z_{j}$, the $Z_{j}$ being disjoint copies of the set $Z$ used previously.

If $\theta=\theta_{j}+\delta_{n} \xi$ belongs to the set $B_{j}$, let $P_{\theta, n}$ be the measure $F\left(\theta_{j}, \xi\right)$. This gives a certain experiment $\mathscr{E}_{n}=\left\{P_{\theta, n} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ with average atlases $\overline{\mathscr{F}}_{\theta, n}= \left\{\int P_{\theta+\delta_{n} \xi, n} v(d \xi), v \in \mathscr{W}\right\}$.

Now, if $\theta \in A_{j}$ there is a certain subset, say $\mathscr{W}(\theta, n)$ of $\mathscr{W}$ formed by measures $v$ which are entirely carried by $B_{j}$. For these $v \in \mathscr{W}(\theta, n)$ the mesure $\int P_{\theta+\delta_{n} \xi, n} v(d \xi)$ coincides with the measure $F(\theta) v=\int F(\theta, \xi) v(d \xi)$. Since $\delta_{n} \rightarrow$ 0 , the $\mathscr{W}(\theta, n)$ expand to cover all of $\mathscr{W}$ and the atlases $\overline{\mathscr{F}}_{\theta, n}$ tend to the desired one on $\Omega=\bigcup_{j} A_{j}$. This gives the result for simple maps restricted to $\Omega$ and therefore for all measurable maps and concludes the proof.

As already mentioned, one often encounters situations where convergence takes place in a much stronger topology than the one used on $B \times \bar{\Gamma}$ in Theorem 4. For instance, one often considers sequences $\left\{X_{n}\right\}$ of statistics whose
distributions $G_{n}(\theta, 0)==\mathscr{L}\left\{\delta_{n}^{-1}\left(X_{n}-\theta\right) \mid P_{\theta, n}\right\}$ converge for all or almost all $\theta \in \Theta$.

This leads to the following remark in which a sequence of maps $\xi \mapsto G_{n}(\theta, \xi)$ is said to converge locally in measure to a limit $\xi \mapsto G^{\prime}(\theta, \xi)$ if for all $v \in \mathscr{W}$ one has

$$
\lim _{n} \int\left|\left\langle\gamma, G_{n}(\theta, \xi)\right\rangle-\left\langle\gamma G^{\prime}(\theta, \xi)\right\rangle\right| v(d \xi)=0
$$

for all $\gamma \in C_{0}$.
Lemma 6. Assume that the experiments $\mathscr{E}_{n}$ are measurable and that for each $\theta \in \Theta$ the sequence $G_{n}(\theta, 0)$ converge to a limit. Then, for all $\theta$ except those of set which has measure zero, the maps $\xi \mapsto G_{n}(\theta, \xi)$ converge locally in measure.

Proof. According to Egorov's theorem there is a sequence $K_{i} ; i=1,2, \ldots$ of compact subsets of $\Theta$ such that (i) the complement of $\cup K_{i}$ has Lebesgue measure zero, and (ii) on each $K_{i}$ the restriction of the maps $\theta \mapsto \mathscr{L}\left\{\delta_{n}^{-1}\left(X_{n}-\theta\right) \mid P_{\theta, n}\right\}$ are equicontinuous and converge uniformly.

Consider then one particular $K_{i}$ and a particular $\theta$ which is a point of density unity of $K_{i}$. At such a point the maps $\xi \mapsto G_{n}(\theta, \xi)$ converge locally in measure. Hence the assertion. (Here the fact that we are dealing with a sequence and not a general net or filter is important. It was irrelevant in Theorem 4 except for the fact that the relations $\delta_{n}>0, \delta_{n} \rightarrow 0$ must be possible relations.)

Another situation which occurs often may be described as follows. Let us say that the sequence $\mathscr{E}_{n}=\left\{P_{\theta, n} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ is $\delta_{n}$-regular at a particular $\theta$ if for all sequences $\left\{\left(s_{n}, t_{n}\right)\right\}$ the relations
(i) there is a $b<\infty$ such that $\delta_{n}^{-1}\left[\left|s_{n}-\theta\right|+\left|t_{n}-\theta\right|\right] \leq b$
(ii) $\lim _{n} \delta_{n}^{-1}\left|s_{n}-t_{n}\right|=0$
imply that

$$
\lim _{n}\left\|P_{s_{n}, n}-P_{t_{n}, n}\right\|=0
$$

If $\left\{\mathscr{E}_{n}\right\}$ is $\delta_{n}$-regular at all $\theta \in \Theta$, we shall simply say that it is $\delta_{n}$-regular. To describe an implication of this type of regularity consider the space $\mathscr{D}$ of all maps $\xi \mapsto D(\xi)$ from $V$ to the dual $C_{0}^{*}$ of $C_{0}$. Metrize $C_{0}^{*}$ by its dual Lipschitz norm. One can then metrize the space $\mathscr{D}$ by the topology of uniform convergence on the compacts of $V$.

Similarly, if $\mathbb{E}(V)$ is the space of types of experiments indexed by $V$ and if $C(V)$ is the corresponding set of conical measures, one can metrize both $\mathbb{E}(V)$ and $C(V)$ by the topology of uniform convergence on the compacts of $V$.

Lemma 7. Assume that $\left\{\mathscr{E}_{n}\right\}$ is $\delta_{n}$-regular at $\theta$. Then the maps $\xi \mapsto G_{n}(\theta, \xi)$ form a relatively compact sequence in $\mathscr{D}$ and the experiments $\mathscr{F}_{\theta, n}= \left\{F_{n}(\theta, \xi) ; \xi \in V\right\}$ form a relatively compact sequence in $\mathbb{E}(V)$ metrized by the uniform convergence on compacts.

Proof. This is an immediate consequence of Lindae's theorem for the space $\mathbb{E}(V)$. For the maps $\xi \mapsto G_{n}(\theta, \xi)$ it is a consequence of the inequality $\left\|G_{n}\left(\theta, \xi_{1}\right)-G_{n}\left(\theta, \xi_{2}\right)\right\| \leq \left\|F_{n}\left(\theta, \xi_{1}\right)-F_{n}\left(\theta, \xi_{2}\right)\right\|$ valid for the $L$-norms.



Now we could topologize the maps $\theta \mapsto G_{n}(\theta, \cdot) \in \mathscr{D}$ by the convergence of integrals of the type $\int G_{n}(\theta, \cdot) \mu(d \theta)$ for all $\mu \in \mathscr{W}$. Similarly if $m_{\theta, n}$ is the conical measure of $\mathscr{F}_{\theta, n}$ one could topologize the maps $\theta \mapsto m_{\theta, n}$ by the convergence
in $C(V)$ of the integrals $\int m_{\theta, n} \mu(d \theta)$. A relation between this topology and the topology of $B \times \bar{\Gamma}$ used for Theorem 4 can be expressed as follows.

Lemma 8. Assume that the experiments $\mathscr{E}_{n}$ are measurable and that they form a $\delta_{n}$-regular sequence. Let $\{r\} \subset\{n\}$ be a subsequence which is such that the pairs $\left(\bar{G}_{r}, \bar{m}_{r}\right)$ converge to a limit $(\bar{G}, \bar{m})$ in $B \times \bar{\Gamma}$. Then for all $\mu \in \mathscr{W}$ the integrals $\int G_{r}(\theta, \cdot) \mu(d \theta)$ and $\int m_{\theta, r} \mu(d \theta)$ converge in $\mathscr{D}$ and $C(V)$, respectively. Furthermore, for almost all $\theta \in \Theta$ the maps $\xi \mapsto G(\theta, \xi)$ and $\xi \mapsto F(\theta, \xi)$ selected in Theorem 4 are continuous maps for the $L$-norms.

Proof. The first statement is immediate. The last one follows from the remark that the lifting $A_{2}$ applied to an $\dot{f} \in \mathscr{\mathscr { M }}$ always selects an $f=\dot{f} A_{2}$ which is continuous if $\dot{f}$ is the class of a continuous function.

A corollary of this situation is that for almost all $\theta$ the limits $G(\theta, \xi)$ must be absolutely continuous with respect to the Lebesgue measure. Another corollary is that in the $\delta_{n}$-regular case one could dispense altogether with the averaging process which leads to $\int G_{n}(\theta, \xi) v(d \xi)$ and $\int F_{n}(\theta, \xi) v(d \xi), v \in \mathscr{W}$.

Finally, let us note that in the above lemma the measurability assumption is almost implied by the $\delta_{n}$-regularity. In fact if $\left\{\mathscr{E}_{n}\right\}$ is $\delta_{n}$-regular, there are continuous maps $\theta \mapsto P_{\theta, n}^{\prime}$ such that, for each $t \in \Theta$ one has

$$
\lim \sup _{|\theta-t| \leq \delta_{n} c}\left\|P_{\theta, n}-P_{\theta, n}^{\prime}\right\|=0
$$

To form the $P_{\theta, n}^{\prime}$ consider a locally finite cover of $\Theta$ by open cubes of diameter $\delta_{n}^{2}$. Let $\left\{u_{j} ; j=1,2, \ldots\right\}$ be a continuous partition of unity subordinated to this cover and let $P_{\theta, n}^{\prime}=\sum_{j} u_{j}(\theta) P_{\theta_{j}, n}$, where $\theta_{j}$ is a point such that $u_{j}\left(\theta_{j}\right)>0$.

To extend the applicability of the preceding results, consider the same situation as for Theorem 4 but for a set $\Theta$ which is not the entire $R^{k}$ but a measurable subset of it. Then the statement of Theorem 4 can still be applied to almost every $\theta \in \Theta$. Indeed one can always extend the maps $\theta \mapsto P_{\theta, n}$ defined on $\Theta$ only to measurable maps defined on the entire space $R^{k}$. This will leave the limits unaffected at all points $\theta \in \Theta$ which are points of Lebesgue density unity of $\Theta$.

When the sequence $\mathscr{E}_{n}=\left\{P_{\theta, n} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ is defined on the measurable set $\Theta$ and $\delta_{n}$-regular at each $\theta \in \Theta$ the situation is even simpler. One may construct a family $\left\{P_{\theta, n}^{\prime} ; \theta \in R^{k}\right\}$ by writing

$$
P_{\theta, n}^{\prime}=\sum_{j} u_{j}(\theta) P_{\theta_{J}, n}
$$

as described above for a partition of unity $\left\{u_{j}\right\}$ except that one takes for $\theta_{j}$ some point of $\Theta$ which is within $\delta_{n}^{2}$ of being closest to the support of $u_{j}$.

To conclude, let us note that, even if $\mathscr{E}_{n}$ is $\delta_{n}$-regular and if both sequences $\left\{G_{n}(\theta, \cdot)\right\}$ and $\mathscr{F}_{\theta, n}$ converge in $\mathscr{D}$ and $C(V)$, respectively, the limits need not
be the maps selected by the liftings in Theorem 4. They must be so for almost all $\theta$ but there may remain exceptional points.

An example can be constructed as follows. Let $\mathscr{E}_{n}$ be the experiment which consists in taking $n$ independent observations from a Gaussian distribution $\mathscr{N}(0, I)$ on the space $R^{k}$. Let $Y_{n}$ be the average of the $n$ observations, and assuming $k \geq 3$, let $X_{n}$ be the James-Stein estimate

$$
X_{n}=Y_{n}\left[1-\frac{k-2}{1+\left\|Y_{n}\right\|^{2}}\right]
$$

Finally, let $\delta_{n}=(1 / \sqrt{n})$.
Then the experiments $\mathscr{F}_{\theta, n}$ are all the same, independent of $\theta$ and $n$ and equivalent to the experiment $\mathscr{G}=\{\mathscr{N}(\xi, I) ; \xi \in V\}$. The selected representative $G(\theta, \xi)$ of Theorem 4 is for all $\theta$ the Gaussian distribution $\mathscr{N}(\xi, I)$. However, the sequence $\xi \mapsto G_{n}(0, \xi)$ converges in $\mathscr{D}$ to the distributions of the JamesStein estimates for $\mathscr{N}(\xi, I)$.

Letting $Y$ be a variable with an $\mathscr{N}(\xi, I)$ distribution; the measures $G(\theta, \xi)$ of Theorem 4 are distributions of estimates, say $T$, such that $T(Y+\alpha)=\alpha+T(Y)$ for all $\alpha \in R^{k}$. This is true for the pointwise limits of the $G_{n}(\theta, \xi)$ for almost all $\theta$ but fails here at the point $\theta=0$.

## 5. Invariant Exponential Families

In the preceding section it was shown that a commonly used rescaling procedure leads to invariance properties both for limits of experiments and for limiting distributions.

In many cases this rescaling procedure finds itself combined with the use of a few terms in a Taylor expansion of logarithms of likelihood ratios. The purpose of this section is to show that such a combination imposes extremely strong restrictions on the possible shapes of the limiting experiments. In fact, it will be shown that the only linearly indexed exponential families which are invariant are the Gaussian experiments.

We shall also indicate some of the possible forms when the indexing is not linear, but we do not have a complete characterization. Exponential families, also called Koopman-Darmois families, can be described by means of the following objects:
(1) A positive measure $\mu$ on a $\sigma$-field $\mathscr{A}$ carried by a set $\mathscr{X}$;
(2) a measurable map $x \mapsto T(x)$ from ( $\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{A}$ ) to a finite dimensional linear space $F^{\prime}$;
(3) the subset $\Xi$ of the dual $F$ of $F^{\prime}$ where the Laplace transform

$$
e^{A(\xi)}=\int \exp \{\xi T(x)\} \mu(d x)
$$

is finite,
(4) a map $\theta \mapsto \xi(\theta)$ from the parameter set $\Theta$ into $\Xi$.

The corresponding exponential family is then the experiment $\mathscr{E}=\left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$, with

$$
P_{\theta}(d x)=\exp \{\xi(\theta) T(x)-A[\xi(\theta)]\} \mu(d x)
$$

The set $\Xi$ is always convex, but the range $\xi(\Theta)$ of the map $\xi$ need not be convex. However, one can always assume without loss of generality that there is a $\theta_{0} \in \Theta$ such that $\xi\left(\theta_{0}\right)$ is the origin of $F$. Similarly one may assume that $F$ is the linear span of $\xi(\Theta)$. Assuming this, we shall note $\Xi_{0}$ the convex hull of $\xi(\Theta)$ in $F$.

Note that, here, it has been assumed that $F$ is finite dimensional. If this restriction is removed, any homogeneous experiment could be represented in the manner just described. When $\Theta$ itself is a subset of a linear space, and when the map $\theta \mapsto \xi(\theta)$ is linear we shall say that the exponential family $\mathscr{E}$ is linearly indexed.

The type of the experiment $\mathscr{E}$ can be characterized by the Hellinger transform of $\mathscr{E}$. For this purpose, let $\left\{\alpha_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ be a real valued function defined on $\Theta$, such that $\alpha_{\theta} \geq 0, \sum_{\theta} \alpha_{\theta}=1$ and such that $\alpha_{\theta}=0$ except on a finite subset of $\Theta$. One may then write

$$
\log \left\|\prod_{\theta}\left(d P_{\theta}\right)^{\alpha_{\theta}}\right\|=A\left[\sum_{\theta} \alpha_{\theta} \xi(\theta)\right]-\sum_{\theta} \alpha_{\theta} A[\xi(\theta)]
$$

It follows that the pair consisting of the map $\xi$ and of the function $A$ (defined on $\Xi_{0}$ ) entirely determines the type of $\mathscr{E}$. Conversely, let $\mathscr{E}_{i}=\left\{P_{\theta, i} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$, $i=1,2$ be two exponential families arising from the same map $\xi$. For $\mathscr{E}_{i}$ let $A_{i}$ be the logarithm of Laplace transforms which occur in the definition of the exponential family. Then if $\mathscr{E}_{1}$ and $\mathscr{E}_{2}$ have the same type the difference, $A_{1}-A_{2}$ is a linear function of $\xi \in \Xi_{0}$.

Among exponential families, the Gaussian shift experiments are characterized by the fact that the function $A$ is a quadratic. (Supplementary information on this feature will be found in Chapters 9 and 10.) This leads to the following result.

Proposition 5. Let $\mathscr{G}=\left\{G_{\xi} ; \xi \in B\right\}$ be an experiment indexed by an open subset $B$ of the finite dimensional space $R^{k}$. Assume that the origin of $R^{k}$ belongs to $B$ and that $G_{\xi}$ has the form

$$
G_{\xi}(d x)=\exp \{\xi T(x)-A(\xi)\} G_{0}(d x)
$$

Assume also that for each $u \in B$ there is some neighborhood $W$ of the origin of $R^{k}$ such that the experiments $\xi \mapsto G_{\xi}, \xi \in W$ and $\xi \mapsto G_{\xi+u}, \xi \in W$ have the same type. Then $\mathscr{G}$ is a Gaussian shift experiment restricted to $B$.

Proof. Let $\rho(s, t)$ be the affinity $\rho(s, t)=\int \sqrt{d G_{s} d G_{t}}$. This has the form described by

$$
-\log \rho(s, t)=\frac{1}{2}[A(s)+A(t)]-A\left[\frac{s+t}{2}\right] .
$$

The shift invariance property implies that for $u \in B$ and for sufficiently small values of $s$ and $t$ one has $\rho(s, t)=\rho(s+u, t+u)$ and therefore

$$
A(s+u)+A(t+u)-2 A\left[\frac{s+t}{2}+u\right]=A(s)+A(t)-2 A\left(\frac{s+t}{2}\right)
$$

In other words, the second differences of the function $A$ do not depend on $u$. Thus $A$ (which is infinitely differentiable on $B$ ) must be a quadratic. This completes the proof of the Proposition.

The result applies also to linearly indexed families, as one can easily see. For families which are not linearly indexed the situation is more complicated. For different purposes, and under various sets of assumptions, characterizations have been given by Dynkin, Ferguson, and Lindley. There is also a deeper result of Pfanzagl applicable to the one-dimensional case. We shall describe only a first step towards characterization as follows.

Consider an exponential family

$$
P_{\xi}(d x)=\exp \{\xi x-A(\xi)\} P_{0}(d x)
$$

where $\xi$ and $x$ are $m$-dimensional vectors. (Note that here we have dispensed with the map $x \mapsto T(x)$ used before. This does not entail any loss of generality.) Let $\Theta$ be the $k$-dimensional space $R^{k}$ and let $\theta \mapsto \xi(\theta)$ be a map from $\Theta$ to the domain $\Xi$ of existence of $A$. As explained before, we may assume that $\xi(0)=0$ and that the range space $R^{m}$ of $\xi$ is linearly spanned by the convex hull $\Xi_{0}$ of $\xi(\Theta)$.

To stabilize the situation further, one may assume that $\xi(0)=0$ is an interior point of $\Xi_{0}$. Finally, we shall assume that the given exponential family does not admit equivalent representation by a family of smaller rank. Specifically, it will be assumed that the only linear expressions $\xi x$ which are almost surely constant are those for which $\xi=0$.

If all these conditions are satisfied, we shall say that the exponential family is in standard form. A first remark is as follows.

Lemma 9. Let $\mathscr{E}=\left\{Q_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ be an exponential family with $Q_{\theta}(d x)= \exp \{\xi(\theta) x-A[\xi(\theta)]\} P_{0}(d x)$. Then $\mathscr{E}$ is shift invariant if and only if for every finite set $\left\{\left(\alpha_{j}, t_{j}\right) ; j \in J\right\}$ with $t_{j} \in \Theta, \alpha_{j} \geq 0, \sum \alpha_{j}=1$ the relation

$$
A\left[\sum \alpha_{j} \xi\left(t_{j}+\tau\right)\right]-\sum \alpha_{j} A\left[\xi\left(t_{j}+\tau\right)\right]=A\left[\sum \alpha_{j} \xi\left(t_{j}\right)\right]-\sum \alpha_{j} A\left[\xi\left(t_{j}\right)\right]
$$

holds identically in $\tau$.
This is an immediate consequence of the form of the Hellinger transforms. To separate $A$ from $\xi$ in the above relation we shall use a result as follows.

Lemma 10. Let $\mathscr{E}$ be as in Lemma 9. Assume in addition that $\mathscr{E}$ is in standard form. Consider finite sets $\left\{\left(\beta_{j}, t_{j}\right) ; j \in J\right\}$ with $t_{j} \in \Theta$ and $\sum \beta_{j}=0$. If $\sum \beta_{j} \xi\left(t_{j}\right)=0$, then $\sum \beta_{j} \xi\left(t_{j}+\tau\right)=0$ for all $\tau \in \Theta$.

Proof. Consider a finite set $\left\{\left(\alpha_{j}, t_{j}\right) ; j \in J\right\}$ with $\alpha_{j}>0$ and $\sum \alpha_{j}=1$. Let $\varepsilon$ be a positive number such that $\varepsilon \sup _{j}\left|\beta_{j}\right|<\inf _{j} \alpha_{j}$. Then the numbers $\alpha_{j}(\varepsilon)=\alpha_{j}+\varepsilon \beta_{j}$ are also positive numbers which add to unity.

Adding one element to the set $\left\{t_{j} ; j \in J\right\}$, if necessary, one may assume that the value $\xi\left(t_{j}\right)$ are not all equal. If so, the values $\xi\left(t_{j}+\tau\right)$ are likewise not identical, since, if they were, the experiment $\left\{Q_{t_{j}+\tau} ; j \in J\right\}$ would be trivial although $\left\{Q_{t}, j \in J\right\}$ is not. In the nontrivial case the $\xi\left(t_{j}+\tau\right), j \in J$ span a certain affine subspace of $R^{m}$. In this space the averages $\sum \alpha_{j} \xi\left(t_{j}+\tau\right)$ are interior to the convex hull of the $\xi\left(t_{j}+\tau\right) ; j \in J$. In a neighborhood of the average, the function $A$ is infinitely differentiable. Thus, substituting $\alpha_{j}(\varepsilon)= \alpha_{j}+\varepsilon \beta_{j}$ for $\alpha_{j}$ in the invariance identity of Lemma 9, one can differentiate twice with respect to $\varepsilon$ and then set $\varepsilon=0$. This yields an expression equal to the variance of the random variable $\left[\sum \beta_{j} \xi\left(t_{j}+\tau\right)\right] X$ for a vector $X$ distributed according to $P_{\xi}, \xi=\sum \alpha_{j} \xi\left(t_{j}+\tau\right)$.

Since the family was taken in standard form, such a variance cannot vanish unless $\sum \beta_{j} \xi\left(t_{j}+\tau\right)=0$. Comparing the relations obtained in this manner to those obtained for $\tau=0$ one concludes that $\sum \beta_{j} \xi\left(t_{j}\right)=0$ implies $\sum \beta_{j} \xi\left(t_{j}+\tau\right)=0$ for all $\tau$, as claimed.

The foregoing lemma can be stated in a different form, introducing appropriate spaces of functions and measures.

Take a particular experiment $\mathscr{E}$ written in standard form, with a particular map $\xi$ from $\Theta=R^{k}$ to $R^{m}$. If $e_{j} ; j=1,2, \ldots, m$ is a basis of $R^{m}$, one may write $\xi(t)=\sum_{j} \zeta_{j}(t) e_{j}$ for suitable elements $\zeta_{j}$ of the space $\mathscr{F}$ of all real valued functions defined on $\Theta$. The space $\mathscr{F}_{0}(\xi)$ spanned in $\mathscr{F}$ by these $\zeta_{j}$ has exactly dimension $m$. If not, the linear span of the set $\left\{\xi(t) ; t \in R^{k}\right\}$ could not be all of $R^{m}$.

Consider then the space $\mathscr{F}(\xi)$ spanned by the constant functions and $\mathscr{F}_{0}(\xi)$. In the space $\mathscr{M}$ of measures with finite support on $\Theta$, the set $\mathscr{F}(\xi)$ has a certain polar $\mathscr{M}_{0}(\xi)$ which is precisely the space of measures representable as linear combinations $\sum \beta_{j} \delta_{t_{j}}$ with $\sum \beta_{j}=0$ and $\sum \beta_{j} \xi\left(t_{j}\right)=0$. Lemma 10 says that this polar $\mathscr{M}_{0}(\xi)$ is shift invariant. That is, if $\mu \in \mathscr{M}_{0}(\xi)$, so does the measure $S^{\tau} \mu$ for all $\tau \in R^{k}$. Since $\mathscr{F}(\xi)$ is the polar of $\mathscr{M}_{0}(\xi)$ in $\mathscr{F}$ it follows that $\mathscr{F}(\xi)$ is likewise shift invariant.

In other words the shift operations $S^{\tau}, \tau \in R^{k}$ form a commutative group of endomorphisms of the linear space $\mathscr{F}(\xi)$ onto itself. (It is onto because of the preservation of dimension.)

One simple consequence of this is as follows. Suppose that the maps $\tau \mapsto f S^{\tau}$ are measurable, in the sense that for each $\mu \in \mathscr{M}$ the real map $\tau \mapsto\left\langle f S^{\tau}, \mu\right\rangle$ is measurable. Then according to Phillips theorem on semi-groups the maps $\tau \mapsto f S^{\tau}$ are already continuous. Equivalently the maps $t \mapsto f(t)$ are continuous.

A further consequence of this situation is that measurability implies differentiability. To see this, select a particular $t \in R^{k}, t \neq 0$ and consider the group $S^{z t}, z$ real acting on $\mathscr{F}(\xi)$. For simplicity of notation write $S^{z t}=T_{z}$. Then, for real numbers $x$ and $y$ one has $T_{x+y}=T_{x} T_{y}$ and also

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left(T_{z}-I\right) \int_{0}^{y} T_{x} d x & =\int_{z}^{z+y} T_{x} d x-\int_{0}^{y} T_{x} d x \\
& =\int_{y}^{z+y} T_{x} d x-\int_{0}^{z} T_{x} d x \\
& =\left(T_{y}-I\right) \int_{0}^{z} T_{x} d x
\end{aligned}
$$

For small values of $y$ the integral $\int_{0}^{y} T_{x} d x$ is close to the identity $I$. Thus it is invertible. Letting $D(t)=\left(T_{y}-I\right)\left[\int_{0}^{y} T_{x} d x\right]^{-1}$ one can write $\left(T_{z}-I\right)= D(t) \int_{0}^{z} T_{x} d x$.

It follows that $D(t)$ is the differentiation operation

$$
D(t)=\lim _{z} \frac{1}{z}\left[S^{z t}-I\right]
$$

and that, by integration, one may write $T_{z}$ in the form $T_{z}=\exp \{z D(t)\}$. This implies, in particular, that all the elements of $\mathscr{F}(\xi)$ are infinitely differentiable. It leads to the following result, where complex linear functionals $\gamma$ on $R^{k}$ are of the form $\gamma=u+i v$, for ordinary real functions $u$ and $v$.

Proposition 6. Let $\mathscr{E}=\left\{Q_{\theta} ; \theta \in R^{k}\right\}$ be an exponential family given by the expression

$$
Q_{\theta}(d x)=\exp \{\xi(\theta) x-A[\xi(\theta)]\} P_{0}(d x)
$$

for a measurable map $\theta \mapsto \xi(\theta)$ from $R^{k}$ to $R^{m}$. Assume that $\mathscr{E}$ is shift invariant and that it is in standard form.

Then there are (complex) linear functionals $\gamma_{j} ; j=0,1, \ldots, n$, with $n \leq m$ and with $\gamma_{0}=0$ for which one can write

$$
\xi(t)=\sum_{j} \phi_{j}(t) \exp \left\langle\gamma_{j}, t\right\rangle
$$

with functions $\phi_{j}$ of the type

$$
\phi_{j}(t)=\sum_{i} Z_{i, j} \pi_{i, j}(t)
$$

where the $Z_{i}$ are elements of $R^{m}$ and the $\pi_{i}$ are polynomials in $t$.
Proof. Let $\mathscr{F}^{*}$ be the complex extension $\mathscr{F}^{*}=\mathscr{F}(\xi)+i \mathscr{F}(\xi)$ of $\mathscr{F}(\xi)$. Consider one of the endomorphisms $D(t)$. Acting on $\mathscr{F}^{*}$ it possesses $m+1$ eigenvalues, counting multiplicities. One of these is equal to zero since $1 S^{\tau}=1$ for all values of $\tau$.

Take a particular $t_{0}$, for which $D\left(t_{0}\right)$ has the maximum possible number of distinct eigenvalues. Let them be $\lambda_{0}=1, \lambda_{1}, \ldots, \lambda_{n}$ so that $\lambda_{j}$ has multiplicity $r_{j}$. Note that if any one of the $\lambda_{j}$ is complex, its complex conjugate $\bar{\lambda}_{j}$ must also appear in the preceding list. For each $\left(\lambda_{j}, r_{j}\right)$ let $V_{i}$ be the complex linear subspace of $\mathscr{F}^{*}$ formed by elements $f$ such that $f\left[D\left(t_{0}\right)-\lambda_{j}\right]^{r_{j}}=0$. Standard
arguments of linear algebra show that $\mathscr{F}^{*}$ is the direct sum of the $V_{j}$ and that each $V_{j}$ is mapped into (and therefore onto) itself by $D\left(t_{0}\right)$.

Since all the $S^{\tau}$ commute and since all the $D(t)$ commute with the $S^{\tau}$, the space $V_{j}$ is also such that $V_{j} S^{\tau}=V_{j}$ and $V_{j} D(\tau) \subset V_{j}$ for all values of $\tau$. Furthermore, when restricted to a space $V_{j}$ each $D(\tau)$ admits only one eigenvalue, say $\gamma_{j}(\tau)$.

It follows then from the additivity relation $D(t+\tau)=D(t)+D(\tau)$ that $\gamma_{j}(t+\tau)=\gamma_{j}(t)+\gamma_{j}(\tau)$. Therefore $t \mapsto \gamma_{j}(t)$ is a complex linear functional defined on $R^{k}$. To indicate this fact we shall rewrite it in the form $\gamma_{j}(t)= \left\langle\gamma_{j}, t\right\rangle$. It follows that on $V_{j}$ the endomorphism $S^{t}$ has for eigenvalue the complex number $\exp \left\{\left\langle\gamma_{j}, t\right\rangle\right\}$.

Call $u_{j}$ the function defined by $u_{j}(t)=\exp \left\{\left\langle\gamma_{j}, t\right\rangle\right\}$. Let $W_{j}$ be the space of functions of the type $w=u_{j}^{-1} v$ for some $v \in V_{j}$. Then, for every $t$ and $\tau$ one may write

$$
\left(w S^{\tau}\right)(t)=\left[u_{j}^{-1}(\tau)\right] u_{j}^{-1}(t)\left(v S^{\tau}\right)(t)
$$

This shows that for every $\tau$ one has $W_{j} S^{\tau} \subset W_{j}$. Also on $W_{j}$ the only possible eigenvalue of $S^{\tau}$ is unity. Equivalently the only possible eigenvalue of $D(\tau)$ is zero.

Now to prove the result claimed in the Proposition it is clearly enough to show that the elements of $W_{j}$ are polynomials. On $W_{j}$ the operators $D(\tau)$ have only zero for eigenvalue. They are nilpotent, or more precisely there is some integer $n \leq \operatorname{dim} W_{j}$ such that $D^{n}(\tau)=0$ on $W_{j}$. This means that $w D^{n}(\tau)=0$ identically in $\tau$ for all $w \in W_{j}$. However, the only functions for which such a relation holds are polynomials. This concludes the proof of the Proposition.

To complete the characterization of invariant exponential families it would be necessary to find out which real valued functions $A$ can be inserted in the invariance relation of Lemma 9. We have not succeeded in obtaining a simple characterization. Thus, to terminate, we will just mention two very special cases which involves well known distributions.

Consider for $\alpha>0, \beta$ real, $\beta \neq 0$, the expression

$$
L[\theta, y ; \alpha, \beta]=-\frac{\alpha^{2}}{\beta^{2}}\{\exp \{\beta(y-\theta)\}-1-\beta(y-\theta)\}
$$

For $\beta=0$ take the limit value

$$
L[\theta, y ; \alpha, 0]=-\frac{\alpha^{2}}{2}(y-\theta)^{2}
$$

Proposition 7. Let $\Theta=R^{k}=R^{m}$ be the real line. Let $\mathscr{E}=\left\{Q_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ be an exponential family given by a map $\theta \mapsto \xi(\theta)$ from $R$ to $R$ and by measures whose

Radon-Nikodym densities are given by

$$
Q_{\theta}(d x)=\exp \{\xi(\theta) x-A[\xi(\theta)]\} P_{0}(d x)
$$

Assume that the exponential family $\mathscr{E}$ is shift invariant and that the map $\theta \mapsto \xi(\theta)$ is measurable. Then there are numbers $\alpha>0$ and $\beta \in(-\infty,+\infty)$ such that, taking $y=\log x$ instead of $x$ as a variable, one may write

$$
Q_{\theta}(d y)=\exp \{L[\theta, y ; \alpha, \beta]\} Q_{0}(d y)
$$

Note. In the degenerate case $\beta=0$ this yields a Gaussian shift experiment. In the nondegenerate case $\beta \neq 0$, passage back to variables $x=\exp \{\beta y\}$ shows that the variable $x$ has a Gamma distribution with fixed shape exponent and variable scale parameter.

Proof. Consider the space $\mathscr{F}(\xi)$ used for Proposition 6. In the present case $\mathscr{F}(\xi)$ has dimension exactly 2 . The corresponding eigenvalues $\gamma_{j}$ can either be both equal to zero, leading to the Gaussian distributions, or be such that $\gamma_{0}=0$ and $\gamma_{1} \neq 0$ is real. In this latter case the map $\theta \mapsto \xi(\theta)$ has the form $\xi(\theta)=e^{\gamma \theta}-1$ for some real number $\gamma$. The range of $\xi(\theta)$ is the interval $(-1,+\infty)$. Consider the invariance relation of Lemma 9 and take two derivatives exactly as in Lemma 10. The resulting relations says that if the second derivative $A^{\prime \prime}$ is evaluated at any convex combination $\sum \alpha_{j} \xi\left(t_{j}+\tau\right)$ then $A^{\prime \prime}\left[\sum \beta_{j} \xi\left(t_{j}+\tau\right)\right]$ remains constant in $\tau$. Taking for $\left\{t_{j}\right\}$ a two point set and letting one of the $\alpha_{j}$ tend to zero gives the relation

$$
A^{\prime \prime}\left(e^{\gamma \tau}-1\right) \times e^{2 \gamma \tau}=c
$$

In a different form, with $z=e^{\gamma \tau}-1$, this may be written $A^{\prime \prime}(z)=c(z+1)^{-2}$. It follows that $A(z)=c_{0}+c_{1} z-c_{2} \log (z+1)$. Since $A(0)=0$ one can let $c_{0}=0$. The coefficient $c_{2}$ is non-negative because $A$ is a convex function. Thus

$$
\int \exp \{z x\} Q_{0}(d x)=e^{c_{1} z}(1+z)^{-c_{2}}
$$

Equivalently $c_{1}-x$ has for the measure $Q_{0}$ a Gamma distribution with exponent $c_{2}$. The result, as stated, follows upon suitable rearrangement.

Another special case is the case where $\xi$ is a quadratic expression and where, on the range of $\xi$, the function $A$ vanishes. For $\theta \in \mathbb{R}^{k}$ the densities of such a family $\left\{Q_{\theta}\right\}$ with respect to $Q_{0}$ may be written $\exp \left\{\theta^{\prime} X-\frac{1}{2} \theta^{\prime} \Gamma \theta\right\}$ for certain random vectors $X$ and random matrices $\Gamma$. That $A \equiv 0$ on the range of $\xi$ means that $E \exp \left\{\theta^{\prime} X-\frac{1}{2} \theta^{\prime} \Gamma \theta\right\} \equiv 1$. The invariance condition of Lemma 9 reads $A\left[\sum \alpha_{j} \xi\left(t_{j}+\tau\right)\right] \equiv A\left[\sum \alpha_{j} \xi\left(t_{j}\right)\right]$. One can deduce from it that $E\left\{\exp \left\{\theta^{\prime} X\right\} \mid \Gamma\right\}=\exp \left\{\frac{1}{2} \theta^{\prime} \Gamma \theta\right\}$ almost everywhere. Here, however, a direct proof is simpler.

## 6. The Hunt-Stein Theorem and Related Results

Consider the problem of estimating the average lifetime $t$ of a radioactive atom from independent observations $x_{1}, x_{2}, \ldots, x_{n}$, each distributed according to an exponential density $(1 / t) \exp \{-x / t\}$. Suppose that for an estimated value $z$ the loss incurred is $W[(z-t) / t]$. If physicist $A$ works with the hour as a
unit and physicist $B$ works with seconds, they should report the same answer. That is, if $A$ uses an estimate $T_{A}$ and $B$ uses $T_{B}$, then $s T_{A}\left(x_{1}, \ldots, x_{n}\right)$ should equal $T_{B}\left[s x_{1}, \ldots, s x_{n}\right]$ for $s=3600$. This does not impose any particular structure on the function $T_{A}$. It need not be covariant in the sense that $T\left[s x_{1}, \ldots, s x_{n}\right]=s T\left(x_{1}, \ldots, x_{n}\right)$. Instead of "covariant" statisticians usually say "invariant." The claim that for any invariant statistical problem one should use "invariant" procedures is a misunderstanding. However, reduction by invariance is often a powerful tool to reduce difficult problems to tractable ones. There is also one case where reduction by invariance makes perfect sense, namely the case of tests. If, for instance, it is desired to test that the average life of atom 1 is larger than that of atom 2 on the basis of observations $x_{1}, \ldots, x_{m}, y_{1}, y_{2}, \ldots, y_{n}$ then it makes sense to require that the test function $\phi$ satisfy $\phi\left[s x_{1}, \ldots, s x_{m}\right.$; $\left.s y_{1}, \ldots, s y_{n}\right]=\phi\left[x_{1}, \ldots, x_{m} ; y_{1}, \ldots, y_{n}\right]$ so that physicist $A$ gets the same answer as physicist $B$.

Because of this we shall now describe two theorems relative to reduction by invariance. The first is a form of the Hunt-Stein theorem. The second is an extension by Hall, Ghosh, and Wijsman of a previous result of Stein. It says that "reduction by invariance" and "reduction by sufficiency" commute. Also included is a modification by Torgersen of this commutativity property.

We shall only deal with what could be called "almost invariance." In the case of transitions representable by Markov kernels one can also look at true invariance. Here our transitions are equivalence classes of Markov kernels. One can often pass from almost invariance to true invariance either by using liftings or by suitable use of Fubini's theorem.

Consider an experiment $\mathscr{E}=\left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ and a decision space $Z$ with a loss function $(\theta, z) \mapsto W_{\theta}(z)$ that is bounded below for each $\theta$. Take for uniform lattice $\Gamma$ on $Z$ the lattice generated by functions of the form $z \mapsto W_{\theta}(z) \wedge \alpha$, $\alpha \in \mathbb{R}$.

Let $\mathscr{S}$ be a set of pairs ( $S, S^{\prime}$ ) where $S$ is a transition from $L(\mathscr{E})$ to $L(\mathscr{E})$ and where $S^{\prime}$ is a transition from the space $L^{\prime}$ of finite measures on $Z$ to itself. Let us call the statistical problem ( $\mathscr{E}, W$ ) invariant under $\mathscr{S}$ if the following conditions hold for every pair $\left(S, S^{\prime}\right) \in \mathscr{S}$.
(1) $S$ restricted to $\left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ is a permutation.
(2) $S^{\prime}$ is the transpose of a map from $\Gamma$ to $\Gamma$. It is a permutation of $L^{\prime}$.
(3) If $S P_{\theta_{1}}=P_{\theta_{2}}$ then $\left[W_{\theta_{1}} \wedge \alpha\right] S^{\prime}=W_{\theta_{2}} \wedge \alpha$ for $\alpha \in \mathbb{R}$.

A risk function $r$ defined on $\Theta$ is called invariant if $r\left(\theta_{1}\right)=r\left(\theta_{2}\right)$ whenever there is a pair $\left(S, S^{\prime}\right) \in \mathscr{S}$ such that $S P_{\theta_{1}}=P_{\theta_{2}}$.

Theorem 1 (Hunt-Stein). Let the problem ( $\mathscr{E}, W$ ) be invariant under $\mathscr{S}$. Let $T_{0}$ be a decision procedure whose risk function $\theta \mapsto r\left(\theta, T_{0}\right)$ is invariant under $\mathscr{S}$. Then if $\mathscr{S}$ admits left almost invariant means, there is a decision procedure $T$ such that $r(\theta, T) \leq r\left(\theta, T_{0}\right)$ for all $\theta$ and such that $S^{\prime} T S=T$ for all $\left(S, S^{\prime}\right) \in \mathscr{S}$.

Proof. Let $K$ be the set of procedures $T$ such that $r(\theta, T) \leq r\left(\theta, T_{0}\right)$ for all $\theta$. This is a convex compact set. If $T$ belongs to $K$ so does $S^{\prime} T S$ since $\left(W_{\theta_{1}} S^{\prime}\right) T\left(S P_{\theta_{1}}\right)=W_{\theta_{2}} T P_{\theta_{2}}$. Thus we are under conditions where the MarkovKakutani theorem applies, and the result follows.

Remark. The condition that $\mathscr{S}$ admit almost invariant means cannot be entirely removed. It can also happen that invariant procedures are not admissible. Thus the argument according to which one should limit oneself to invariant procedures needs to be taken with caution.

Even though reduction by invariant needs to be viewed with caution, one may want to use it and at the same time use a "reduction by sufficiency." The latter is firmly grounded in decision theory, provided one believes that the model is adequate.

Let $\mathscr{E}=\left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ be an experiment with its $L$-space $L=L(\mathscr{E})$. Let $\mathscr{S}$ be a set of transitions from $L$ to $L$. According to the usual transposition formula $\langle u S, P\rangle=\langle u, S P\rangle$, an $S \in \mathscr{S}$ is also a map from the dual $M$ of $L$ to $M$.

Theorem 2. Assume that each $S \in \mathscr{S}$ induces a permutation on the set $\left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$. Let $M_{1}$ be a closed sublattice of $M$. Assume further that
(i) $M_{1}=M_{1} S$ for all $S=\mathscr{S}$;
(ii) $M_{1}$ is sufficient for $(M, \mathscr{E})$;
(iii) each $S \in \mathscr{S}$ is multiplicative on $M$.

Let $H$ be the set of $u \in M$ such that $u S=u$ identically for $S \in \mathscr{S}$. Then $H \cap M_{1}$ is sufficient for the experiment formed by the restrictions of the $P_{\theta}$ to $H$.

Note. Recall that $S$ is multiplicative if it is an isometry of $L$ onto $L$. Equivalently, in representation by measures on a set, $S$ arises from a point transformation.

Proof. The common conditional expectation given $M_{1}$ for the $P_{\theta}$ is a projection $\Pi$ of $M$ onto $M_{1}$. By definition, if $v \in M_{1}$ and if $u \in M$ one has $\left\langle v(u \Pi), P_{\theta}\right\rangle=\left\langle v u, P_{\theta}\right\rangle$. It follows that $\left\langle v(u \Pi) S, P_{\theta}\right\rangle=\left\langle(u v) S, P_{\theta}\right\rangle$. If $S$ is multiplicative, this can also be written $\left\langle(v S)(u \Pi) S, P_{\theta}\right\rangle=\left\langle(v S)(u S), P_{\theta}\right\rangle$. Letting $v S=w$, one sees that $\left\langle w(u \Pi) S, P_{\theta}\right\rangle=\left\langle w(u S), P_{\theta}\right\rangle$ for all $w \in M_{1}$. If in addition $u S=u$, this shows that $\left\langle w(u \Pi) S, P_{\theta}\right\rangle \equiv\left\langle w u, P_{\theta}\right\rangle$ and consequently $(u \Pi) S=u \Pi$. In other words, if $u \in H$ then $u \Pi \in H \cap M_{1}$. This yields the desired assertion.

Note that here there was no need to assume that $\mathscr{S}$ admit almost invariant means. For the following result, due to Torgersen, we shall again use such a restriction.

Let us consider the situation described by Theorem 2 of Section 2, with two experiments $\mathscr{E}$ and $\mathscr{E}^{\prime}$. Assume that the pair is invariant under a family $\mathscr{S}$ of pairs ( $S, S^{\prime}$ ) as in Section 2. Let $H$ be the subset of $M(\mathscr{E})$ formed by elements $u$ such that $u S=u$ for all $\left(S, S^{\prime}\right) \in \mathscr{S}$. Let $H^{\prime}$ be the corresponding subset of $M\left(\mathscr{E}^{\prime}\right)$. Let $\mathscr{F}$ be the experiment obtained by restricting the $P_{\theta}$ to $H$. Let $\mathscr{F}^{\prime}$ be obtained similarly by restriction to $H^{\prime}$.

Theorem 3 (Torgersen). Assume that $\mathscr{S}$ admits left almost invariant means. Then

$$
\delta\left(\mathscr{F}, \mathscr{F}^{\prime}\right) \leq \delta\left(\mathscr{E}, \mathscr{E}^{\prime}\right)
$$

Proof. Let $T_{0}$ be such that $\left\|P_{\theta}^{\prime}-T_{0} P_{\theta}\right\| \leq 2 \delta\left(\mathscr{E}, \mathscr{E}^{\prime}\right)$. Then, according to Theorem 2, Section 2 there is a $T_{1}$ such that $S^{\prime} T_{1} S \equiv T_{1}$ with the same property.



If $v \in H^{\prime}$, then $v T_{1}=v S^{\prime} T_{1} S=\left(v T_{1}\right) S$. Thus $v T_{1} \in H$. In other words, $T_{1}$ is also a transition for the pair $\left(\mathscr{F}, \mathscr{F}^{\prime}\right)$. The result follows.

## CHAPTER 9

## Infinitely Divisible, Gaussian, and Poisson Experiments

## 1. Introduction

Experiments which arise as limits "when the number of observations gets large" are often infinitely divisible in a sense which will be made clear in Section 2. Examples of infinitely divisible experiments are provided by the Gaussian shift families described in Section 3. More typical are the Poisson experiments described in Section 4. A full characterization of infinitely divisible experiments is given in Section 5 after we prove a very elementary sort of "central limit theorem." Further results in this direction will also be found in the chapter on independent observations.

## 2. Infinite Divisibility

Let $\Theta$ be an arbitrary set and let $\mathscr{E}=\left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ be an experiment indexed by $\Theta$. One could say that $\mathscr{E}$ admits an $n$th root, say $\mathscr{E}_{n}$, if $\mathscr{E}$ can be written as the direct product $\mathscr{E}=\mathscr{E}_{n, 1} \times \mathscr{E}_{n, 2} \times \cdots \times \mathscr{E}_{n, n}$ of $n$ copies of $\mathscr{E}_{n}$. This, however, would impose various restrictions on the structure of the spaces which carry the experiments $\mathscr{E}$ and $\mathscr{E}_{n}$. Instead, we shall consider divisibility for experiment types and use the following definition.

Definition 1. An experiment $\mathscr{E}$ indexed by $\Theta$ is said to possess an nth root $\mathscr{E}_{n}$ if $\mathscr{E}$ is equivalent to the direct product of $n$ copies of $\mathscr{E}_{n}$. The experiment $\mathscr{E}$ is called infinitely divisible if it admits nth roots for arbitrarily large values of $n$.

Other equivalent definitions are obtainable as follows. Let $R_{+}^{\Theta}$ be the space of functions from $\Theta$ to $[0, \infty)$. Let $\mathscr{H}$ be the Choquet lattice generated by the coordinate evaluations and let $\mathscr{C}(\Theta)$ be the space of all conical measures on $\mathscr{H}$. It has been observed in Chapter 3 that $R_{+}^{\Theta}$ is a semi-group for coordinatewise multiplication, and that making a direct product of two experiments corresponds to convoluting their conical measures for the semi-group operation. If two conical measures, say $\mu$ and $v$, are convoluted, the resultant of the convolution $\mu \nu$ is the product of the individual resultants.

Thus, if $\mu \nu$ is the conical measure of an experiment, the resultants of $\mu$ and $v$ must be invertible elements of $R_{+}^{\Theta}$. Now, if $x$ is the resultant of $\mu$ and if $\delta$ is the conical measure representable by the Dirac mass at $x^{-1}$, the convolution $\delta \mu$ has resultant identically unity. Thus it is the conical measure of an experiment.

In other words, if the conical measure $m$ of an experiment factorizes in the form $m=\mu v$ it also factorizes in the form $m=\mu_{1} v_{1}$, where both $\mu_{1}$ and $v_{1}$ are conical measures of experiments. Therefore, instead of saying that an experiment is infinitely divisible one can say equivalently that its conical measure admits $n$th roots, $n$ arbitrarily large, in the convolution ring of conical measures.

Since convolution is represented by pointwise multiplication of Hellinger transforms, one could also say that the Hellinger transform $\phi$ of $\mathscr{E}$ has the form $\phi=\phi_{n}^{n}$ for suitable Hellinger transforms $\phi_{n}$.

As should be expected from the theory of sums of independent random variables, Poisson and Gaussian experiments will play a special role in the theory of divisibility. In fact we shall show, in Section 5, that all the infinitely divisible experiments can be obtained by suitable arrangements of convolutions of Poisson and Gaussian experiments. For the present let us note the following simple fact.

Lemma 1. Let $\mathbb{E}(\Theta)$ be the space of experiment types indexed by $\Theta$. Give $\mathbb{E}(\Theta)$ its weak topology. Let $\mathbb{E}_{D}(\Theta)$ be the subset of $\mathbb{E}(\Theta)$ formed by infinitely divisible experiments. Then $\mathbb{E}_{D}(\Theta)$ is a compact subset of $\mathbb{E}(\Theta)$.

Proof. It was shown in Chapter 3 that $\mathbb{E}(\Theta)$ is compact. Suppose now that $\mathscr{E} \in \mathbb{E}(\Theta)$ is a weak limit of experiments $\mathscr{E}_{v}$ each of which admits an $n$th root, say $\mathscr{E}_{v, n}$. Passing to a finer filter if necessary, one may assume that $\mathscr{E}_{v, n}$ converges to some limit, say $\mathscr{E}_{n}^{\prime}$. Then the direct product of the $\mathscr{E}_{v, n}$ also converges to the direct product of the $\mathscr{E}_{n}^{\prime}$. This gives the desired result.

## 3. Gaussian Experiments

An interesting class of infinitely divisible experiments is the class of homoschedastic Gaussian experiments, also called Gaussian shift experiments or, abusively, Gaussian experiments below.

Finite dimensional examples of these may be described as follows. Let $\lambda$ be the Lebesgue measure on a $k$-dimensional space $R^{k}$. Let $\Gamma$ be a nonsingular positive definite matrix and let $\beta$ be a map from some set $\Theta$ to $R^{k}$. Let $G_{\theta}$ be the measure whose density with respect to $\lambda$ is given by the expression

$$
\frac{(\operatorname{det} \Gamma)^{1 / 2}}{(2 \pi)^{k / 2}} \exp \left\{-\frac{1}{2}\left(x-\beta_{\theta}\right) \Gamma\left(x-\beta_{\theta}\right)^{\prime}\right\}
$$

The family $\left\{G_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ is a family of Gaussian distributions with a fixed covariance matrix and with expectations which vary as $\theta$ runs through $\Theta$.

One could, of course, consider a more general situation where $\Gamma$ is also a function of $\theta$. In fact we shall encounter just such families in Chapter 11. However, for the present, we shall consider only the homoschedastic case; that is, the case where $\Gamma$ is fixed.

The reader will note that in such a case the logarithms of the type $\log \left(d G_{t} / d G_{s}\right)$ have Gaussian distributions. This is the property that will be used here for a general definition. Let $\Theta$ be an arbitrary set and let $\mathscr{G}=\left\{G_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ be an experiment indexed by $\Theta$. Take as origin a particular element, say $s$, of $\Theta$ and
consider the function $X(\theta)=\log \left(d G_{\theta} / d G_{s}\right)$. Give to these the distributions induced by the measure $G_{s}$. This yields a stochastic process $\theta \mapsto X(\theta)$.

Definition 2. The experiment $\mathscr{G}=\left\{G_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ is called a Gaussian shift experiment, or an homoschedastic Gaussian experiment if (i) it is homogeneous, and (ii) the process $\theta \mapsto X(\theta)$ is a Gaussian process.

This definition seems to depend on the choice of a special point $s \in \Theta$. However, it is readily verifiable that if the process $X$ is Gaussian for one value $s_{0}$ of $s$ it is also Gaussian for any other choice of the point $s$.

In fact, if one takes the same measurable functions $X(\theta)$ as above, but induces the distributions of the process by a measure $G_{t}, t \in \Theta$, instead of $G_{s}$, the process so obtained will be a Gaussian process which has the same covariance structure as the previous one but has expectations shifted by an amount $K(\theta, t)$ equal to the covariance for $G_{s}$ of the pair $(X(\theta), X(t))$.

The type of a Gaussian shift experiment is entirely characterized by its Hellinger transform which is easily computable as follows. Since the experiment is homogeneous one may write $X(t)=Y(t)-\frac{1}{2} \sigma^{2}(t)$, where $Y(t)$ is a Gaussian variable which has mean zero and variance $\sigma^{2}(t)$. The Hellinger transform

$$
\phi(\alpha)=\left\|\prod_{j}\left(d G_{t_{j}}\right)^{\alpha_{j}}\right\|=E_{s} \exp \left\{\sum \alpha_{j} X\left(t_{j}\right)\right\}
$$

takes the form

$$
\phi(\alpha)=\exp \left\{\frac{1}{2} \sum_{j} \sum_{k} \alpha_{j} \alpha_{k} K\left(t_{j}, t_{k}\right)-\frac{1}{2} \sum \alpha_{j} \sigma^{2}\left(t_{j}\right)\right\},
$$

where $K\left(t_{j}, t_{k}\right)$ is the covariance $K\left(t_{j}, t_{k}\right)=E_{s} Y\left(t_{j}\right) Y\left(t_{k}\right)$. Of course, $\sigma^{2}(t)=K(t, t)$.

The logarithm of $\phi(\alpha)$ consists of a term which is linear in $\alpha$ and of a term which is quadratic. Every Hellinger transform having this structure is the Hellinger transform of a Gaussian experiment. Gaussian shift experiments have a natural habitat which may be described as follows.

Let $V$ be any real vector space with a hilbertian norm denoted $\|v\|$. On such a space there is a standard Gaussian process $v \mapsto\langle Z, v\rangle$ such that $E\langle Z, v\rangle=0$ and $E|\langle Z, v\rangle|^{2}=\|v\|^{2}$. To construct an experiment take for $G_{0}$ the distribution of this process and take for $G_{v}$ the measure whose density with respect to $G_{0}$ is given by $\exp \left\{\langle Z, v\rangle-\frac{1}{2}\|v\|^{2}\right\}$. This gives an experiment $\mathscr{G}=\left\{G_{v} ; v \in V\right\}$ indexed by $V$. It clearly satisfies the conditions of Definition 2 and will be called the standard Gaussian experiment of the Hilbert space $(V,\| \|)$.

Any experiment which satisfies the conditions of Definition 2 can be obtained from the standard Gaussian experiment of a Hilbert space. To see this let $t \mapsto Y_{0}(t)$ be any real Gaussian process defined for $t \in \Theta$ and such that $E Y_{0}(t) \equiv 0$. Take a particular point $s \in \Theta$ and let $Y(t)=Y_{0}(t)-Y_{0}(s)$.

Let $V_{0}$ be the space of signed measures with finite support on $\Theta$. For each $v \in V_{0}$ define $\langle Z, v\rangle=\int Y(t) v(d t)$ and $\|v\|^{2}=E|\langle Z, v\rangle|^{2}$. For the norm so
defined, $V_{0}$ is prehilbertian and may be completed to give a Hilbert space $V$. The process $v \mapsto\langle Z, v\rangle$ extends from $V_{0}$ to $V$ by continuity in quadratic mean. The set $\Theta$ may be identified with a suitable subset of $V_{0} \subset V$. The special point $s$ selected above becomes the origin of $V$. This being done, one can consider the experiment $\left\{G_{v} ; v \in V\right\}$ which is the standard Gaussian experiment of $V$.

If the process $Y$ used in the construction is the process obtained by taking the logarithms $X(t)=\log \left(d G_{t} / d G_{s}\right)$ of Definition 2 and then letting $Y(t)= X(t)-E_{s} X(t)$, the Gaussian experiment $\left\{G_{v} ; v \in V\right\}$ is obtained from $\left\{G_{\theta}\right.$; $\theta \in \Theta\}$ by extension of the set of indices.

In the foregoing discussion we have referred to $\left\{G_{v} ; v \in V\right\}$ as the standard Gaussian experiment of the Hilbert space $V$. This is abusive since the experiment in question is obviously not unique. However, its equivalence class is uniquely defined. Hence the abuse of language. It will be convenient, for further use, to have an alternative description of standard Gaussian experiments. It is as follows.

Lemma 2. Let $V$ be a real vector space with a hilbertian norm noted $\|v\|$. Let $\mathscr{G}=\left\{G_{v} ; v \in \Theta\right\}$ be an homogeneous experiment indexed by a convex subset $\Theta$ of $V$ such that $0 \in \Theta$. For each $v$ let

$$
L(v)=\log \frac{d G_{v}}{d G_{0}}+\frac{1}{2}\|v\|^{2}
$$

Consider this as a random variable for distributions induced by $G_{0}$. Then $\mathscr{G}$ is the restriction to $\Theta$ of the standard Gaussian experiment of the Hilbert space $(V,\|\cdot\|)$ if and only if $L((s+t) / 2)=\frac{1}{2}[L(s)+L(t)]$ almost surely for each pair ( $s, t$ ) of elements of $\Theta$.

Proof. The linearity relation, as stated, is obviously necessary. To prove the converse, first take one $v \in \Theta$. Note that the additivity relation implies that $L(\alpha s)=\alpha L(s)$ for every positive binary number $\alpha$ such that both $\alpha s$ and $s$ belong to $\Theta$.

Similarly, if $\left\{v_{j} ; j=1,2, \ldots, k\right\}$ is any $k$-tuple of elements of $\Theta$, then $L\left[\sum \alpha_{j} v_{j}\right]= \sum \alpha_{j} L\left(v_{j}\right)$ almost surely for positive binary combinations which stay in $\Theta$. Now let $\Lambda(v)=\log \left(d G_{v} / d G_{0}\right)$. Since $\mathscr{G}$ is homogeneous one has $E \exp \left\{\Lambda\left(\sum \alpha_{j} v_{j}\right)\right\}=$ 1 or equivalently

$$
E \exp \left\{\sum \alpha_{j} L\left(v_{j}\right)\right\}=\exp \left\{\frac{1}{2}\left\|\sum \alpha_{j} v_{j}\right\|^{2}\right\} .
$$

This holds for positive binary combinations such that $\sum \alpha_{j} \leq 1$ and therefore also for combinations with real coefficients.

It follows that the process $v \mapsto L(v)$ is a linear process indexed by $\Theta$. The distribution of $L(v)$ is a Gaussian distribution which has expectation zero and variance $E|L(v)|^{2}=\|v\|^{2}$. Thus $v \mapsto L(v)$ is the restriction to $\Theta$ of a standard Gaussian linear process of $V$. The result follows.

It is immediate from the above that the Gaussian shift experiments of Definition 2 are infinitely divisible.

For further uses, let us mention in passing that if $\left\{G_{v} ; v \in V\right\}$ is the standard Gaussian experiment of a Hilbert space $V$, then the map $v \mapsto G_{v}$ is continuous for the norm of $V$ and the $L_{1}$-norm of measures.

Indeed, the formula for the Hellinger transform given previously shows that the affinity between $G_{s}$ and $G_{t},(s, t) \in V \times V$ is given by

$$
\int \sqrt{d G_{s} d G_{t}}=\exp \left\{-\frac{1}{8}\|s-t\|^{2}\right\}
$$

This implies that the square Hellinger distance $H^{2}\left(G_{s}, G_{t}\right)$ does not exceed $\frac{1}{8}\|s-t\|^{2}$. Therefore

$$
\frac{1}{2}\left\|G_{s}-G_{t}\right\| \leq \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\|s-t\|
$$

In particular, the restriction $\left\{G_{v} ; v \in K\right\}$ to a precompact subset $K$ of $V$ is a precompact experiment.

It will be shown in Chapter 10 that the Gaussian experiments arise very readily as limits, even in cases where the observations are not independent, provided that "each observation brings a "uniformly" negligible amount of information." Some Gaussian shift experiments were already encountered in a totally different context as part of the implications of invariance studied in Chapter 8. Nevertheless, in the present context, Gaussian shift experiments are just a degenerative form of the Poisson experiments described below.

## 4. Poisson Experiments

Let $\mathscr{A}$ be a ring of subsets of a set $\mathscr{X}$. Let $\mu$ be a positive additive set function from $\mathscr{A}$ to $[0, \infty)$. A Poisson process of base $\mu$ is a map $A \mapsto X(A)$ which to each $A \in \mathscr{A}$ associates a random variable $X(A)$ in such a way that
(1) If $A_{1} \cap A_{2}=\varnothing$ then $X\left(A_{1} \cup A_{2}\right)=X\left(A_{1}\right)+X\left(A_{2}\right)$.
(2) Each $X(A)$ has a Poisson distribution with $E X(A)=\mu(A)$.
(3) If $A_{j} ; j=1,2, \ldots, k$ are pairwise disjoint elements of $\mathscr{A}$ then the variables $X\left(A_{j}\right) ; j=1,2, \ldots, k$ are independent.

The existence of such processes is not subject to doubt. The fact that $\mu$ is only assumed to be finitely additive is rather irrelevant. One can always make it countably additive at the cost of completing $\mathscr{X}$. By distribution of the Poisson process $A \mapsto X(A)$ will be understood the usual probability measure on the Kolmogorov $\sigma$-field of $R^{\mathscr{A}}$.

Definition 3. Let $\Theta$ be a set. For each $\theta \in \Theta$ let $\mu_{\theta}$ be a finitely additive set function on $\mathscr{A}$ as described above. Let $P_{\theta}$ be the distribution of the Poisson process of base $\mu_{\theta}$. By the Poisson experiment $\mathscr{P}$ based on $\left\{\mu_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ will be meant the experiment $\theta \mapsto P_{\theta}$.

Such a Poisson experiment is always infinitely divisible since, for each integer $n$, one can construct an experiment $\mathscr{P}_{n}$ based on $\left\{(1 / n) \mu_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ and since $\mathscr{P}=\mathscr{P}_{n}^{n}$.

There is another aspect to the divisibility of $\mathscr{P}$. Let $\mathscr{P}_{A}$ be the experiment obtained by restricting all the $\mu_{\theta}$ to an element $A$ of $\mathscr{A}$. Then $\mathscr{P}$ is the weak limit of $\mathscr{P}_{A}$ as $A$ grows along the directed set $\mathscr{A}$. Also if $A_{j} ; j=1,2, \ldots, k$ are pairwise disjoint and such that $A=\bigcup A_{j}$, then $\mathscr{P}_{A}$ is the direct product of the $\mathscr{P}_{A_{j}}$.

The type of the Poisson experiment $\mathscr{P}$ based on $\left\{\mu_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ can be characterized by its Hellinger transform obtainable as follows. In the product space $R_{+}^{\Theta}$ let $U(\Theta)$ be the unit simplex formed by functions $\theta \mapsto u(\theta)$ such that $u(\theta) \geq 0, \sum_{\theta} u(\theta)=1$ and $u(\theta)=0$ except for a finite set of values of $\theta$. On the product $U(\Theta) \times \mathscr{R}_{+}^{\Theta}$ define a function $\psi_{u}(v)$ by

$$
\psi_{u}(v)=\sum_{\theta} u(\theta) v(\theta)-\prod_{\theta}[v(\theta)]^{u(\theta)} .
$$

For fixed $u$, this is a non-negative, convex homogeneous function on $\mathscr{R}_{+}^{\Theta}$. It does not belong to $\mathscr{H}$ itself but is a limit of elements of $\mathscr{H}$.

It is easily seen that the Hellinger integral - $\Psi_{\mathscr{P}}(u)=\int\left\{\sum u(\theta) d \mu_{\theta}-\prod_{\theta}\left(d \mu_{\theta}\right)^{u(\theta)}\right\}$ is always well defined even though its value may be $+\infty$. In particular, if $u(s)=u(t)=\frac{1}{2}$, this Hellinger integral takes the form $\frac{1}{2} \int\left(\sqrt{d \mu_{s}}-\sqrt{d \mu_{t}}\right)^{2}$. Direct computation for bounded $\mu_{\theta}$ on a finitely generated field and passages to the limit yield the following result.

Lemma 3. Let $\mathscr{P}:\left\{\theta \mapsto P_{\theta}\right\}$ be the Poisson experiment based on $\left\{\mu_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$. The Hellinger transform of $\mathscr{P}$ is given by the formula

$$
\int \Pi\left(d P_{\theta}\right)^{\alpha_{\theta}}=\exp \left\{\Psi_{\mathscr{P}}(\alpha)\right\}
$$

with

$$
\Psi_{\mathscr{P}}(\alpha)=\int\left\{\prod_{\theta}\left(d \mu_{\theta}\right)^{\alpha_{\theta}}-\sum \alpha_{\theta} d \mu_{\theta}\right\}
$$

In particular, $\int \sqrt{d P_{s} d P_{t}}=\exp \left\{-\frac{1}{2} \int\left(\sqrt{d \mu_{s}}-\sqrt{d \mu_{t}}\right)^{2}\right\}$. This particular form of the affinity has a consequence which leads to a useful simplification. Let $\mathscr{E}: \theta \mapsto Q_{\theta}$ be an arbitrary experiment. Call it perfect if the $Q_{\theta}$ are pairwise disjoint and call it imperfect otherwise. It follows from above that if $\mathscr{P}: \theta \mapsto P_{\theta}$ is any Poisson experiment then $\Theta$ can be partitioned in sets $\Theta_{i}$ such that:
(i) If $s$ and $t$ belong to the same $\Theta_{i}$ then $\int \sqrt{d P_{s} d P_{t}}>0$. In other words, $\theta \mapsto P_{\theta}, \theta \in \Theta_{i}$ is pairwise imperfect.
(ii) If $s \in \Theta_{i}$ and $t \in \Theta_{j}$ with $i \neq j$, then $P_{s}$ and $P_{t}$ are disjoint.

To go further it will be convenient to use a particular representation of Poisson experiments in the situation where $\Theta$ is a finite set of cardinality, card $\Theta=k$. For this purpose assume that the set functions $\mu_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta$ are not only finite on the ring $\mathscr{A}$ but also $\sigma$-additive on this ring. Let $F=(1 / k) \sum_{\theta} \mu_{\theta}$. Then one can find Radon-Nikodym densities $f_{\theta}, f_{\theta} \geq 0,(1 / k) \sum f_{\theta}=1$ such that $d \mu_{\theta}=f_{\theta} d F$.

Let $A$ be an element of $\mathscr{A}$ and let $S$ be the simplex $S=\left\{x ; x_{\theta} \geq 0, \sum x_{\theta}=k\right\}$ in $R_{+}^{\Theta}$. For each $A$ the image of the measure $I_{A} d F$ by the map $f=\left\{f_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$
is a finite measure $M_{A}$ carried by $S$. Remove from $S$ the unit element 1 whose coordinate are all equal to unity. Let $S_{0}$ be the set so obtained and restrict $M_{A}$ to $S_{0}$ obtaining a measure $M_{A}^{\prime}$ on $S_{0}$. If $A$ increases in $\mathscr{A}$, then $M_{A}^{\prime}$ increases. If it tends to a limit $M$ on the locally compact set $S_{0}$ we shall call $M$ the characteristic measure of the Poisson experiment $\mathscr{P}$ based on $\left\{\mu_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$. The fact that the $M_{A}^{\prime}$ may tend to a limit is a consequence of the following lemma.

Lemma 4. Let $\Theta$ be a finite set of cardinality $k$. Let $\mathscr{P}: \theta \mapsto P_{\theta}$ be a Poisson experiment based on bounded $\sigma$-additive measures $\mu_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta$. Assume that for every pair ( $s, t$ ) of elements of $\Theta$ one has

$$
\int \sqrt{d P_{s} d P_{t}}=\exp \left\{-\frac{1}{2} \int\left(\sqrt{d \mu_{s}}-\sqrt{d \mu_{t}}\right)^{2}\right\} \geq \exp \left\{-\frac{1}{2} b\right\}
$$

Let $M$ be the characteristic measure of $\mathscr{P}$. Then

$$
\sum_{\theta}\left|x_{\theta}-1\right|^{2} d M \leq 4 k(k+1) b
$$

Conversely, let $M$ be a positive measure carried by $S_{0}$ and such that $\sum_{\theta}\left|x_{\theta}-1\right|^{2} d M \leq b$. Then $M$ is the characteristic measure of a Poisson experiment $\theta \mapsto P_{\theta}$ such that if $\alpha_{\theta} \geq 0, \sum \alpha_{\theta}=1$ one has

$$
\int \Pi\left(d P_{\theta}\right)^{\alpha_{\theta}} \geq \exp \{-(17) b\}
$$

Proof. Let $F=(1 / k) \sum_{\theta} \mu_{\theta}, d \mu_{\theta}=f_{\theta} d F$ as before, and let $y_{\theta}=\sqrt{f_{\theta}}$. Then

$$
\int\left(\sqrt{d \mu_{s}}-\sqrt{d \mu_{t}}\right)^{2}=\int\left(y_{s}-y_{t}\right)^{2} d F
$$

Also, if $g=(1 / k) \sum y_{\theta}$ then (since $\left.(1 / k) \sum y_{\theta}^{2}=1\right)$ one has $0 \leq g \leq 1$ and $1-g^{2}=(1 / k) \sum\left(y_{\theta}-g\right)^{2}$. This gives

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{k} \sum\left(y_{\theta}-1\right)^{2} & =\frac{1}{k} \sum\left(y_{\theta}-g\right)^{2}+(1-g)^{2} \\
& =2(1-g) \leq 2\left(1-g^{2}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

Note that $\left(y_{\theta}-g\right)^{2} \leq(1 / k) \sum\left(y_{s}-y_{\theta}\right)^{2}$ and therefore

$$
1-g^{2} \leq \frac{1}{k^{2}} \sum \sum\left(y_{s}-y_{t}\right)^{2}
$$

Integrating with respect to $F$ gives

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum \int\left(y_{\theta}-1\right)^{2} d F & \leq 2 k \int(1-g) d F \leq 2 k \int\left(1-g^{2}\right) d F \\
& \leq 2 k \sup _{s, t} \int\left(\sqrt{d \mu_{s}}-\sqrt{d \mu_{t}}\right)^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

Writing $y_{\theta}-1=v_{\theta}$ this gives $f_{\theta}=\left(1+v_{\theta}\right)^{2}$ and therefore $f_{\theta}-1=2 v_{\theta}[1+ \left.\left(v_{\theta} / 2\right)\right]$. Since $-1 \leq v_{\theta} \leq \sqrt{k}-1$, this yields $\left|v_{\theta}\right| \leq\left|f_{\theta}-1\right| \leq(1+\sqrt{k})\left|v_{\theta}\right|$ and finally

$$
\sum \int\left|x_{\theta}-1\right|^{2} d M \leq 2 k(1+\sqrt{k})^{2} b
$$

under the conditions of the proposition.
Conversely, note that if $M$ is any bounded measure on $S$ it yields a Poisson experiment $\mathscr{P}_{M}$. The Hellinger transform of $\mathscr{P}_{M}$ has the form $\int \prod_{\theta}\left(d P_{\theta}\right)^{\alpha_{\theta}}= \exp \phi(\alpha)$ with

$$
\phi(\alpha)=\int\left(\prod_{\theta} x_{\theta}^{\alpha_{\theta}}-\sum \alpha_{\theta} x_{\theta}\right) d M
$$

Clearly $-k\|M\| \leq \phi(\alpha) \leq 0$.
Consider then the neighborhood $V=\left\{x:\left|x_{\theta}-1\right|<\frac{1}{4}\right\}$ in $S$. If $M$ is a measure on $S_{0}$ such that $\sum_{\theta} \int\left|x_{\theta}-1\right|^{2} d M<b$, then $M\left(V^{c}\right) \leq 16 b$. Thus it will be enough to investigate the part $Q$ of $M$ carried by $V$. Let $v=\sum \alpha_{\theta} x_{\theta}$. Then $|v-1| \leq \frac{1}{4}$ on $V$. Write $x_{\theta} / v=1+z_{\theta}$ so that $-\frac{2}{5} \leq z_{\theta} \leq \frac{2}{3}$ and

$$
\begin{aligned}
\psi(\alpha) & =\int\left(\prod_{\theta} x_{\theta}^{\alpha_{\theta}}-\sum \alpha_{\theta} x_{\theta}\right) d Q \\
& =\int\left\{\prod_{\theta}\left(1+z_{\theta}\right)^{\alpha_{\theta}}-1\right\} v d Q
\end{aligned}
$$

For each $\xi \in[0,1]$ let $\omega(\xi)$ be the function $\omega(\xi)=\prod_{\theta}\left(1+\xi z_{\theta}\right)^{\alpha_{\theta}}$. Note that $0<\omega(\xi) \leq 1$ and that

$$
\begin{aligned}
\omega^{\prime}(\xi) & =\sum_{\theta} \alpha_{\theta} z_{\theta} \frac{1}{1+\xi z_{\theta}} \omega(\xi) \\
& =-\sum_{\theta} \alpha_{\theta} z_{\theta}^{2} \frac{1}{1+\xi z_{\theta}} \xi \omega(\xi)
\end{aligned}
$$

since $\sum \alpha_{\theta} z_{\theta}=0$. For values which arise in $V$ this gives

$$
0 \leq-\omega^{\prime}(\xi) \leq \frac{3}{2} \xi \sum_{\theta} \alpha_{\theta} z_{\theta}^{2}
$$

Hence, since $\psi(\alpha)$ is the integral of $\omega(1)-\omega(0)$,

$$
\begin{aligned}
0 \leq-\psi(\alpha) & \leq \frac{3}{4} \int\left(\sum_{\theta} \alpha_{\theta} z_{\theta}^{2}\right) v d Q \\
& \leq \int\left(\sum \alpha_{\theta} z_{\theta}^{2}\right) v^{2} d Q
\end{aligned}
$$

Note that since $\sum \alpha_{\theta} x_{\theta}=v$ we have

$$
