\sum \alpha_{\theta}\left(x_{\theta}-1\right)^{2}=\sum \alpha_{\theta}\left(x_{\theta}-v\right)^{2}+(1-v)^{2}=v^{2} \sum \alpha_{\theta} z_{\theta}^{2}+(1-v)^{2}
$$

Hence $0 \leq-\psi(\alpha) \leq \sum \alpha_{\theta}\left|x_{\theta}-1\right|^{2} d Q$.
It follows that $M$ is the characteristic measure of the Poisson experiment $\mathscr{E}= \left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ obtained by writing $d P_{\theta}=x_{\theta} d M$ on the space $S_{0}$ and that this experiment satisfies the inequality desired for its Hellinger transform. This concludes the proof of the lemma.

To state another analogue of the usual theorems on infinitely divisible distributions consider Poisson experiments $\mathscr{E}_{n}$ with characteristic measures $M_{n}$. Writing $r^{2}(x)=\sum_{\theta}\left|x_{\theta}-1\right|^{2}$ for simplicity, assume that $\int r^{2} d M_{n}<\infty$ for all $n$. Recall that an experiment $\mathscr{E}=\left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ is called "pairwise imperfect" if $\int \sqrt{d P_{s} d P_{t}}>0$ for all pairs $(s, t)$ of elements of $\Theta$.

Let $\mathscr{K}\left(S_{0}\right)$ be the space of continuous functions with compact support on the locally compact space $S_{0}$. Say that $M_{n}$ converges vaguely to $M$ if $\int \gamma d M_{n} \rightarrow \int \gamma d M$ for all $\gamma \in \mathscr{K}\left(S_{0}\right)$. Finally, for $\varepsilon>0$, let $V(\varepsilon)$ be the neighborhood $V(\varepsilon)=\left\{x: r^{2}(x)<\varepsilon\right\}$.

Proposition 1. Let $\left\{\mathscr{E}_{n}, n=1,2, \ldots\right\}$ be a sequence of Poisson experiments with characteristic measures $M_{n}$ subject to the restriction $\int r^{2} d M_{n}<\infty$. In order that
the sequence $\left\{\mathscr{E}_{n}\right\}$ converge to a pairwise imperfect experiment $\mathscr{E}$ it is necessary and sufficient that the following three conditions hold:
(i) $\limsup \sup _{n} \int r d M_{n}<\infty$.
(ii) The measures $M_{n}$ converge vaguely to a limit $M$.
(iii) For every pair ( $s, t$ ) of elements of $\Theta$ the iterated limits

$$
\lim _{\varepsilon \rightarrow 0} \lim _{n} \int_{V(\varepsilon)}\left(x_{s}-1\right)\left(x_{t}-1\right) d M_{n}=K(s, t)
$$

exist.
If so, the limit experiment $\mathscr{E}$ is the direct product of a Poisson experiment (with characteristic measure M) by a Gaussian shift experiment (with covariance kernel $K$ ).

Proof. Fix a vector $\alpha=\left\{\alpha_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ and let $f_{\alpha}(x)=\prod_{\theta} x_{\theta}^{\alpha_{\theta}}-\sum_{\theta} \alpha_{\theta} x_{\theta}$ be the function whose integral gives the logarithm of the Hellinger transform for Poisson experiments. This is a continuous function which vanishes at the "infinity" of the locally compact space $S_{0}$, namely at the point of $S$ whose coordinates are identically unity. Consider also the functions

$$
g_{s, t}(x)=\frac{1}{r^{2}(x)}\left(x_{s}-1\right)\left(x_{t}-1\right)
$$

and let $\mathscr{U}$ be the smallest uniform structure which makes all the $g_{s, t}$ and all the elements of $\mathscr{K}\left(S_{0}\right)$ uniformly continuous.

One can complete $S_{0}$ for this uniform structure, yielding a compact space, say $\bar{S}$. The condition $\sup _{n} \int r^{2} d M_{n}<\infty$ implies that the "Khintchin measures,"
$K_{n}$ defined by $d K_{n}=r^{2} d M_{n}$ have bounded norms, and are therefore a relatively compact set if considered as elements of the dual of $C(\bar{S})$ for the weak topology induced by $C(\bar{S})$. Conditions (ii) and (iii) are equivalent to the statement that the Khintchin measures $K_{n}$ converge for this weak topology.

A Taylor expansion of $f_{\alpha}(x)$ around the point of $S$ whose coordinates are all unity shows that the first derivative of $f_{\alpha}$ at that point vanishes and that the second derivative has the form

$$
\sum_{j} \sum_{k} \alpha_{j} \alpha_{k} z_{j} z_{k}-\sum \alpha_{\theta} z_{\theta}^{2}
$$

with $z_{j}=x_{j}-1$.
Thus, if (i), (ii), and (iii) hold one can conclude that the expression

$$
\int f_{\alpha} d M_{n}=\int \frac{f_{\alpha}}{r^{2}} d K_{n}
$$

converges to the limit

$$
\psi(\alpha)=\frac{1}{2}\left[\sum_{s} \sum_{t} \alpha_{s} \alpha_{t} K(s, t)-\sum_{\theta} \alpha_{\theta} K(\theta, \theta)\right]+\int f_{\alpha} d M
$$

This gives the form of $\mathscr{E}$ described in the final statement of the Proposition. Conversely, suppose that the experiment $\mathscr{E}_{n}$ converges to a pairwise imperfect $\mathscr{E}$. Then, according to Lemma 4 , the Khintchin measures $K_{n}$ form a relatively compact sequence for the topology induced by $C\left(\bar{S}_{0}\right)$. Extracting a convergent subsequence one obtains a formula of the type written for $\psi(\alpha)$ above. Thus to show the necessity of the conditions it is sufficient to show that such an expression $\psi(\alpha)$, given for all vectors $\alpha$, determines both the limit measure $M$ and the covariance kernel $K$.

The uniqueness can be obtained by an argument entirely similar to that of Proposition 5, Chapter 3. Briefly, let $\left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ be the limit experiment. Take a particular $t \in \Theta$ and let $Y_{\theta}=\log \left(d P_{\theta} / d P_{t}\right)$. Consider only vectors $\alpha$ such that $\alpha_{\theta}>0$ for all $\theta \in \Theta$. Write the sum $\sum \alpha_{\theta} Y_{\theta}$ in the form $u Z$ where $u=\sum_{\theta \neq t} \alpha_{\theta}$ and $Z=u^{-1} \sum \alpha_{\theta} Y_{\theta}$. Then, taking expectations for the measure $P_{t}$ one has $\exp \psi(\alpha)=E \exp \{u Z\}$.

Also, for $\beta_{\theta}=u^{-1} \alpha_{\theta}$ if $\theta \neq t$ and for $\beta_{t}=0$ one may write

$$
\psi(\alpha)=\int\left\{\exp \left[u \sum \beta_{\theta} y_{\theta}\right]-1-u \sum \beta_{\theta}\left(e^{y_{\theta}}-1\right)\right\} d M+Q(u)
$$

where $Q$ is a combination of a linear and a quadratic expression.
It follows that the image of $M$ by the map $\left\{x_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\} \mapsto\left\{y_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta, \theta \neq t\right\}$, $y_{\theta}=\log x_{\theta}$, is the Lévy measure of the joint distribution of the $Y_{\theta}, \theta \neq t$. This is well determined if $\psi(\alpha)$ is given. If $P_{t}$ dominates all the other $P_{\theta}$, this already shows that $M$ is entirely determined. If not, one can repeat the argument for each $t \in \Theta$ and determine $M$ uniquely also. Returning to the function $\psi$ one



can now subtract the Lévy term and determine the coefficients of the quadratic expression. This concludes the proof of the Proposition.
(Note that the distribution of the vector $Y_{\theta} ; \theta \neq t$, is infinitely divisible and that the above argument allows one to read off its characteristic function from $\psi$ itself.)

Remark 1. Consider the affinities $\rho(s, t)=\int \sqrt{d P_{s} d P_{t}}$ and let $d^{2}(s, t)= -\log \rho(s, t)$. We have noted before that for Poisson experiments the function $d \geq 0$ so obtained satisfies the triangular inequality on $\Theta$. This remains true for the limits obtained in the preceding Proposition 1. Thus if one removes the condition that the limit experiment be pairwise imperfect, there will be a partition $\Theta_{i}, i=1, \ldots, m$ of $\Theta$ such that (i) on each $\Theta_{i}$ Proposition 1 remains applicable, and (ii) if $s \in \Theta_{i}$ and $t \in \Theta_{j}$ are in different sets of the partition then $P_{s}$ and $P_{t}$ are disjoint.

Remark 2. For Gaussian experiments, as defined here, the corresponding measure are always mutually absolutely continuous. This does not remain true for Poisson experiments. In fact, conditions for absolute continuity are easy to obtain. Consider only two points, say $s$ and $t$. One may then write the Hellinger transform

$$
\phi(\alpha)=\int\left(d P_{s}\right)^{1-\alpha}\left(d P_{t}\right)^{\alpha}
$$

in the form

$$
\phi(\alpha)=\exp \{-\psi(\alpha)\}
$$

with

$$
\psi(\alpha)=\int\left\{\left[(1-\alpha) d \mu_{s}+\alpha d \mu_{t}\right]-\left(d \mu_{s}\right)^{1-\alpha}\left(d \mu_{t}\right)^{\alpha}\right\}
$$

where the measures $\mu_{s}$ and $\mu_{t}$ are the base measures for the corresponding Poisson processes.

Since the limit of $\phi(\alpha)$ as $\alpha$ grows to 1 in ( 0,1 ) is the mass of $P_{t}$ which is dominated by $P_{s}$, a necessary and sufficient condition for absolute continuity of $P_{t}$ with respect to $P_{s}$ is that $\psi(\alpha)$ tend to zero as $\alpha$ grows to unity.

Let $v=\mu_{s}+\mu_{t}$ and take the Radon-Nikodym densities of $\mu_{s}$ and $\mu_{t}$ with respect to $v$. Let these be $f$ and $g$ respectively. These densities are functions on the space $\mathscr{X}$ which carries $\nu$, if $\nu$ is $\sigma$-additive and $\sigma$-finite. Otherwise they must be considered elements of the dual of the $L$-space generated by the restrictions of $v$ to sets $A$ such that $v(A)<\infty$. In any event one may write

$$
\psi(\alpha)=\int\left\{[(1-\alpha) f+\alpha g]-f^{1-\alpha} g^{\alpha}\right\} d v
$$

with $f+g=1$. As function of $\alpha \in[0,1]$ the expression under the integral sign is a positive concave function vanishing at $\alpha=0$ and $\alpha=1$. Thus it is bounded by the maximum of $2 \alpha b$ and $2(1-\alpha) b$, where $b=\frac{1}{2}(\sqrt{f}-\sqrt{g})^{2}$ is the value it takes at $\alpha=\frac{1}{2}$.

If $P_{t}$ is not entirely disjoint from $P_{s}$, the value $\psi(1 / 2)$ is finite. Therefore $\max \{\alpha b,(1-\alpha) b\}$ is $v$ integrable. Passing to the limit as $\alpha \rightarrow 1$ one sees that the limit of $\psi(\alpha)$ is the integral of $g I\{f=0\}$. Thus a necessary and sufficient condition for absolute continuity of $P_{t}$ with respect to $P_{s}$ is that $\mu_{t}$ be dominated by $\mu_{s}$ and that $\int\left(\sqrt{d \mu_{s}}-\sqrt{d \mu_{t}}\right)^{2}$ be finite.

## 5. A Central Limit Theorem

To complete the characterization of infinitely divisible experiments we need some sort of "central limit theorem." Here we shall use only a very weak form of the central limit theorem. It would be useful to have a better result giving appropriate bounds for the distance between a product of experiments and the accompanying Poisson experiment. However, we do not have reasonable bounds, except perhaps in the finite dimensional cases which will be discussed in the chapter on independent observations. For the present we shall use only a very simple inequality.

Let $\Theta$ be an arbitrary set. For each integer $j=1,2, \ldots, n$ let $\mathscr{E}_{j}$ be some experiment indexed by $\Theta$. Let $\mathscr{F}_{j}$ be the experiment which consists in first selecting a Poisson variable $N$ such that $E N=1$ and then carrying out observations according to the direct product $\mathscr{E}_{j}^{N}$ of $N$ copies of $\mathscr{E}_{j}$. The direct product $\mathscr{F}$ of the $\mathscr{F}_{j}$ will be called the Poisson experiment which accompanies the direct product $\mathscr{E}$ of the $\mathscr{E}_{j}$.

If $\mathscr{E}_{j}$ is given by measures $\gamma_{\theta, j}$ on a set $\left(\mathscr{X}_{j}, \mathscr{A}_{j}\right)$ then $\mathscr{F}_{j}$ is equivalent to the Poisson experiment based on the measures $\gamma_{\theta, j}, \theta \in \Theta$. The product $\mathscr{F}$ is equivalent to a Poisson experiment obtainable by taking the direct sum of the spaces $\left(\mathscr{X}_{j}, \mathscr{A}_{j}\right)$ and placing on it the measures $\mu_{\theta}$ which are the direct sums of the $\gamma_{\theta, j}$. One verifies readily that if $\phi_{j}$ is the Hellinger transform of $\mathscr{E}_{j}$ then the Hellinger transform of $\mathscr{E}$ is the product $\Pi \phi_{j}$ and the Hellinger transform of $\mathscr{F}$ is $\psi=\exp \left\{\sum\left(\phi_{j}-1\right)\right\}$.

In the above description of the construction of the Poisson experiment $\mathscr{F}$ we have taken the direct sum of the spaces $\left(\mathscr{X}_{j}, \mathscr{A}_{j}, \gamma_{\theta, j}\right)$. One can often proceed otherwise. For instance, if the spaces $\left(\mathscr{X}_{j}, \mathscr{A}_{j}\right)$ are copies of a certain $(\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{A})$ and if the $\gamma_{\theta, j}$ are also copies of a certain $\gamma_{\theta}$ one may take for $\mathscr{F}$ the Poisson experiment based on the family of measures $\sum_{j} \gamma_{\theta, j}$. This remains true even when the $\gamma_{\theta, j}$ differ whenever the space ( $\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{A}$ ) is such that the densities $d \gamma_{\theta, j} / d \gamma_{s, j}$ can be taken independent of $j$.

To show this, suppose that $\Theta$ is finite and take the sum $m_{j}=\sum_{\theta} \gamma_{\theta, j}$. Then the conical measure of $\mathscr{E}_{j}$ is the image $M_{j}$ of $m_{j}$ by the map

$$
f_{j}=\left\{\frac{d \gamma_{\theta, j}}{d m_{j}} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}
$$

to the simplex $U(\Theta)$ of $R_{+}^{\Theta}$. If this map is independent of $j$ the image $M$ of $\sum_{j} m_{j}$ is the sum of the individual images $M_{j}$. Since the conical measure of $\mathscr{F}$ is the convolution $\operatorname{exponential} \exp \{M-\|M\|\}$ it is also the convolution
$\operatorname{exponential~} \exp \left\{\sum_{j}\left[M_{j}-\left\|M_{j}\right\|\right]\right\}$. Hence the result. (This is precisely the representation used in Section 4 with characteristic measures.)

The reader will note that passage to the space of likelihood ratios $R_{+}^{\Theta}$ automatically insures that the densities $d \gamma_{\theta, j} / d \gamma_{s, j}$ are independent of $j$. This gives a simple representation of $\mathscr{F}$ whenever the families $\gamma_{\theta, j}$ are dominated. When they are not dominated one can still proceed using the direct sum decomposition briefly described at the end of Chapter 3.

To proceed further we shall concentrate on the case where $\Theta$ is a finite set and let $U(\Theta)$ be the unit simplex of $R_{+}^{\Theta}$, so that the Hellinger transforms are functions on $U(\Theta)$. Let $\mathscr{E}_{j} ; j=1,2, \ldots, n$ be $n$ experiments indexed by $\Theta$. Let $\phi_{j}$ be the Hellinger transform of $\mathscr{E}_{j}$. Introduce the average $\bar{\phi}=(1 / n) \sum_{j} \phi_{j}$ and the numbers

$$
\begin{aligned}
\gamma_{1}(\alpha) & =\frac{1}{2} \sup _{j}\left[1-\phi_{j}(\alpha)\right] \\
\gamma_{2}(\alpha) & =\frac{1}{n} \sum_{j}\left[\phi_{j}(\alpha)-\bar{\phi}(\alpha)\right]^{2}[1-\bar{\phi}(\alpha)]^{-2} \\
\gamma(\alpha) & =\min \left\{\gamma_{1}(\alpha), \frac{1}{n}\left[1+\gamma_{2}(\alpha)\right]\right\}
\end{aligned}
$$

Lemma 5. Let $\phi=\Pi \phi_{j}$ and let $\psi$ be the exponential $\psi=\exp \left\{\sum\left(\phi_{j}-1\right)\right\}$. Then the inequalities

$$
0 \leq \psi-\phi \leq e \gamma
$$

hold for every $\alpha \in U(\Theta)$.
Proof. Let $\psi_{j}=\exp \left(\phi_{j}-1\right)$. Replacing the $\psi_{j}$ successively by $\phi_{j}$ one obtains the inequality $0 \leq \psi-\phi=\sum_{j} R_{j}\left(\psi_{j}-\phi_{j}\right)$ with a coefficient

$$
R_{j}=\left(\prod_{k<j} \psi_{k}\right)\left(\prod_{i>j} \phi_{i}\right) \leq \frac{\psi}{\psi_{j}}
$$

Since $0 \leq \phi_{j} \leq 1$, one has $\psi_{j} \geq e^{-1}$. Hence $0 \leq \psi-\phi \leq e \psi \sum_{j}\left(\psi_{j}-\phi_{j}\right)$. This yields the inequality

$$
0 \leq \psi-\phi \leq \frac{e}{2} \sum_{j}\left(1-\phi_{j}\right)^{2} \psi
$$

One can also write

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum\left(1-\phi_{j}\right)^{2} & =\sum\left(\bar{\phi}-\phi_{j}\right)^{2}+\sum(1-\bar{\phi})^{2} \\
& =n(1-\bar{\phi})^{2}\left(1+\gamma_{2}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

Thus, letting $\omega=\sum\left(1-\phi_{j}\right)=n(1-\bar{\phi})$ one obtains

$$
0 \leq \psi-\phi \leq \frac{e}{2}\left(1+\gamma_{2}\right) \frac{1}{n} \omega^{2} e^{-\omega}
$$

The maximum value of $\omega^{2} e^{-\omega}$ is 2 , yielding one of the desired assertions. The inequality involving $\gamma_{1}$ is obtainable by replacing the sum $\sum_{j}\left(1-\phi_{j}\right)^{2}$ by $\left[\max _{j}\left(1-\phi_{j}\right)\right] \sum_{j}\left(1-\phi_{j}\right)$. The result follows.

For the comparison of the direct product $\mathscr{E}$ with the accompanying Poisson experiment $\mathscr{F}$ this gives an immediate corollary as follows.

Corollary. There is a function $\eta$ defined on $[0,1]$ depending only on the cardinality of $\Theta$ and tending to zero at zero such that

$$
\Delta(\mathscr{E}, \mathscr{F}) \leq \eta\left[\sup _{\alpha} \gamma(\alpha)\right]
$$

In particular if all the $\mathscr{E}_{j}$ have the same type

$$
\Delta(\mathscr{E}, \mathscr{F}) \leq \eta\left(\frac{1}{n}\right)
$$

Proof. If $\sup _{\alpha} \gamma(\alpha)$ converges to zero, the difference $\psi-\phi$ between the Hellinger transforms also converges to zero. The existence of the function $\eta$ follows, then, immediately from the compactness of the space $\mathbb{E}(\Theta)$ of experiment types and the uniqueness of the Hellinger transforms.

To state another corollary of Lemma 5 consider a sequence $\left\{\mathscr{E}_{\nu}\right\}$ of experiments. Assume that each $\mathscr{E}_{v}$ is a direct product of experiments $\mathscr{E}_{v, j} ; j=1,2$, $\ldots, n_{\nu}$. Let $\mathscr{F}_{\nu}$ be the accompanying Poisson experiment.

Proposition 2. Assume that $\Theta$ is finite, that $n_{\nu} \rightarrow \infty$, and that there are other experiments, say $\mathscr{E}_{v}^{\prime}$ such that $\sup _{j} \Delta\left(\mathscr{E}_{v, j}, \mathscr{E}_{v}^{\prime}\right) \rightarrow 0$. Then

$$
\Delta\left(\mathscr{E}_{v}, \mathscr{F}_{v}\right) \rightarrow 0
$$

Proof. Consider only vectors $\alpha$ which are interior to $U(\Theta)$. For such an $\alpha$ let $\bar{\phi}_{v}(\alpha)=\left(1 / n_{v}\right) \sum_{j} \phi_{v, j}(\alpha)$ and let $V_{v}(\alpha)$ be the variance term

$$
V_{v}(\alpha)=\frac{1}{n_{v}} \sum_{j}\left[\phi_{v, j}(\alpha)-\bar{\phi}_{v}(\alpha)\right]^{2}
$$

According to the assumptions made, there is some other Hellinger transform, say $\phi_{\nu}^{\prime}$, such that $\sup _{j}\left|\phi_{\nu, j}(\alpha)-\phi_{\nu}^{\prime}(\alpha)\right| \rightarrow 0$. Therefore $V_{\nu}(\alpha) \rightarrow 0$. Now let $\gamma_{1, v}(\alpha)=\sup _{j}\left[1-\phi_{\nu, j}(\alpha)\right]$ and $\gamma_{2, v}(\alpha)=V_{\nu}(\alpha)\left[1-\bar{\phi}_{\nu}(\alpha)\right]^{-2}$. The number $\gamma_{\nu}(\alpha)$ which enters in the formula of Lemma 5 is the minimum of $\underline{\gamma}_{1, v}(\alpha)$ and $\left(1 / n_{\nu}\right)\left[1+\gamma_{2, \nu}(\alpha)\right]$. In the present case $\gamma_{1, \nu}(\alpha)$ differs from $1-\bar{\phi}_{\nu}(\alpha)$ by a quantity which tends to zero as $v \rightarrow \infty$. The minimum of $\left[1-\bar{\phi}_{v}(\alpha)\right]$ and

$$
\frac{1}{n_{v}} \frac{1}{\left[1-\bar{\phi}_{v}(\alpha)\right]^{2}}
$$

always tend to zero. Thus $\gamma_{\nu}(\alpha)$ tends to zero. The conclusion follows by application of Proposition 5, Chapter 3.

A consequence of the above argument is a characterization of infinitely divisible experiments which can be stated as follows.

Proposition 3. Let $\Theta$ be an arbitrary set and let $\mathscr{E}=\left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ be an experiment indexed by $\Theta$. Then $\mathscr{E}$ is infinitely divisible if and only if it is a weak limit of Poisson experiments. Equivalently, $\mathscr{E}$ is infinitely divisible if and only if there exists a partition $\Theta=\bigcup_{k} \Theta_{k}$ into sets $\Theta_{k}$ such that
(i) If $s$ and $t$ belong to different sets of the partition, then $P_{s}$ and $P_{t}$ are disjoint.
(ii) For a given $\Theta_{k}$, the experiment $\mathscr{E}_{k}=\left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta_{k}\right\}$ is a direct product of a Gaussian experiment by a Poisson experiment and its Hellinger transform never vanishes.

Proof. Proposition 2 may be applied to each finite subset of $\Theta$. It then provides for weak convergence of the appropriate experiments. The first result follows, then, from Proposition 2 and the remark that limits of infinitely divisible experiments are themselves infinitely divisible.

For the second statement one may use Proposition 1 and the Remarks which follow it. This gives the existence of the partition $\left\{\Theta_{k}\right\}$ and the desired representation.

One could inquire whether Proposition 2 could be modified to allow infinite sets of indices $\Theta$ and still retain convergence in the sense of distance between experiments. A result of this nature, for sets $\Theta$ which satisfy suitable dimensionality restrictions, will be given in the chapter on independent observations, as a by-product of a theorem on the existence of estimates which converge at the maximum possible speed. Another, simpler, result can be obtained by using Lindae's theorem (Chapter 6, Section 4, Theorem 1). It is as follows.

Proposition 4. Let $\Theta$ be an arbitrary set and let $\left\{\mathscr{E}_{v}\right\}$ be a sequence of experiments indexed by $\Theta$. Assume that $\mathscr{E}_{\nu}$ is a direct product of experiments $\mathscr{E}_{\nu, j} ; j=1,2, \ldots, n_{\nu}$. Let $\mathscr{F}_{\nu}$ be the accompanying Poisson experiment. Assume that $n_{\nu} \rightarrow \infty$ and that there are other experiments, say $\mathscr{E}_{v}^{\prime}$, such that $\sup _{j} \Delta\left(\mathscr{E}_{v, j}, \mathscr{E}_{v}^{\prime}\right) \rightarrow 0$. Assume in addition that either $\mathscr{E}_{v}$ tends weakly to a limit or that $\mathscr{F}_{v}$ does and that this limit is precompact. Then

$$
\Delta\left(\mathscr{E}_{v}, \mathscr{F}_{v}\right) \rightarrow 0
$$

Proof. By Proposition 2, the sequences $\mathscr{E}_{v}$ and $\mathscr{F}_{v}$ have the same weak limit. Consider also pairs ( $s_{\nu}, t_{\nu}$ ) of elements of $\Theta$. Let $\widehat{\mathscr{E}}_{\nu}$ be $\mathscr{E}_{\nu}$ with a set of indices restricted to $\left(s_{\nu}, t_{\nu}\right)$. Define $\widehat{\mathscr{F}_{\nu}}$ similarly. By Proposition 2 again $\Delta\left(\widehat{\mathscr{E}}_{\nu}, \widehat{\mathscr{F}}{ }_{\nu}\right) \rightarrow 0$. Thus, for a precompact limit, Lindae's theorem becomes applicable and the desired result follows.

Precompactness is a strong requirement which can probably be replaced by weaker assumptions. However it cannot be deleted entirely, as will now be shown by an example.

Take for $\Theta$ the set of integers $\{1,2, \ldots\}$. Let $\mathscr{X}$ be the interval $[0,1]$ with its Borel sets and the Lebesgue measure $\lambda$. Each point $x \in[0,1]$ admits a binary expansion $x=\sum_{j \geq 1} \xi_{j}(x) 2^{-j}$ with $\xi_{j}$ equal to zero or unity. If $x$ has two binary expansions, take, for instance, the terminating one. For $\theta \geq 1$ let $\gamma_{\theta}$ be the measure which has density $2 \xi_{\theta}$ with respect to $\lambda$. Let $\mathscr{E}^{n}$ be the experiment which consists in taking $n$ independent observations, identically
distributed according to one of the $\gamma_{\theta}$. This is the direct product of $n$ copies of $\mathscr{E}^{1}$.

The accompanying Poisson experiment $\mathscr{F}_{n}$ consists in first observing a Poisson variable $N$ with $E N=n$ and then performing $\mathscr{E}^{N}$. Let us first show that the deficiency $\delta\left(\mathscr{E}^{n-1}, \mathscr{E}^{n}\right)$ is at least $\frac{1}{2}$.

For this purpose take a large integer $k$ and let $m=k^{3} 2^{n}$. Consider the set $\Theta_{m}=\{1,2, \ldots, m+1\}$ and the following decision problem. The set $D$ of possible decisions is the set of subsets of $\Theta_{m}$ which have cardinality at most $k^{3}+k^{2}$. The loss function $W(\theta, S)$ is always zero except if $\theta \in \Theta_{m}$ and $\theta \notin S$. In this case $W(\theta, S)$ is unity.

For a given $\theta \in \Theta_{m}$ and a given number, say $v$, of observations let $X$ be the number of indices $j \neq \theta$ such that $\xi_{j}\left(x_{i}\right)=1$ for all $i=1,2, \ldots, v$. This variable $X$ is a binomial variable which corresponds to $m$ trials and a probability of success $2^{-\nu}$. Therefore $E X=k^{3} 2^{n-\nu}$ and var $X \leq k^{3} 2^{n-\nu}$.

In particular, for $v=n$ one has $E X=k^{3}$ and for $v=n-1$ one has $E X=2 k^{3}$. Therefore, if $k$ becomes large and $v=n$, the probability that $X<k^{3}+k^{2}$ tends to unity. On the contrary, if $v=n-1$ the probability that $X>2 k^{3}-k^{2}$ will tend to unity. For $X<k^{3}+k^{2}$ the selection of the set $S$ is easy, since one can take precisely that set of indices $j$ such that $\xi_{j}\left(x_{i}\right)$ is identically unity.

On the contrary, for $v=n-1$ and $X>2 k^{3}-k^{2}$ the distribution given $X$ of the sets of indices $j \neq \theta$ for which $\prod_{i} \xi_{j}\left(x_{i}\right)=1$ is the same as if these sets were taken at random from all sets of cardinality $X$. As long as $\theta \in \Theta_{m}$, the distribution of the $\xi_{j}$ for $j \notin \Theta_{m}$ do not depend on $\theta$.

Thus, the experiment $\mathscr{E}^{n-1}$, with $\theta$ restricted to $\Theta_{m}$ is equivalent to the experiment where one selects a binomial variable $X$ with expectation and variance independent of $\theta$ and then selects a set $T \subset \Theta_{m}$ which contains $\theta$, has cardinality $X+1$ with the part contained in $\Theta_{m} \backslash\{\theta\}$ taken at random over sets of cardinality $X$.

Now put on $\Theta_{m}$ a uniform prior. The posterior distribution will be uniform on the set $T$ itself. Thus, whenever $X>k^{3}+k^{2}$ the posterior expected risk is equal to

$$
1-\frac{k^{3}+k^{2}}{X+1}
$$

This implies that the minimax risk is at least

$$
\left(1-\frac{k^{3}+k^{2}}{2 k^{3}-k^{2}}\right) P\left(X>2 k^{3}-k^{2}\right) .
$$

For $k$ large this tends to the value $1 / 2$. Therefore $\delta\left(\mathscr{E}^{n-1}, \mathscr{E}^{n}\right) \geq \frac{1}{2}$.
Now consider the Poisson experiment $\mathscr{F}_{n}$ for $n$ large. The probability that the Poisson variable $N$, with $E N=n$, satisfies the inequality $N>n$ is then close to $1 / 2$. Similarly the probability that $N<n$ is close to $1 / 2$. Taking the same estimation problem as above, one concludes that for $\mathscr{F}_{n}$ the minimax risk is close to $1 / 4$ at least. Thus $\delta\left(\mathscr{F}_{n}, \mathscr{E}^{n}\right) \geq \frac{1}{4}-\varepsilon_{n}$ for $n$ large, $\varepsilon_{n} \rightarrow 0$.

One could also consider a similar estimation problem, but for an integer $m=k^{3} 2^{n+1}$ and still restricting the selection of sets to sets of cardinality $k^{3}+k^{2}$. Then the minimax risk for $\mathscr{E}^{n}$ is approximately $1 / 2$, at least, while the risk for $\mathscr{F}_{n}$ is approximately $1 / 4$. Thus $\delta\left(\mathscr{E}^{n}, \mathscr{F}_{n}\right) \geq \frac{1}{4}-\varepsilon_{n}$ for some $\varepsilon_{n}$ which tends to zero as $n \rightarrow \infty$.

Remark. The above argument shows that $\mathscr{F}_{n}$ and $\mathscr{E}_{n}$ are not comparable, even though the Hellinger transform of $\mathscr{F}_{n}$ is always larger than that of $\mathscr{E}_{n}$. This is not necessarily due to the infinite character of $\Theta$. For a two point set $\Theta$ it is easy to construct a binary experiment $\mathscr{E}$ and its Poissonized version $\mathscr{F}$ so that $\delta(\mathscr{E}, \mathscr{F})$ and $\delta(\mathscr{F}, \mathscr{E})$ are both positive. Part of the result on the comparison of $\mathscr{E}_{n}$ and $\mathscr{F}_{n}$ could also be obtained in a different manner, observing that here $\delta\left(\mathscr{E}^{n}, \mathscr{E}^{n+1}\right) \geq \frac{1}{2}$ although the deficiency $\delta\left(\mathscr{F}_{n}, \mathscr{F}_{n+r}\right)$ for $r$ small compared to $\sqrt{n}$ is small. A simple result is as follows.

Lemma 6. Let $\Theta$ be an arbitrary set and let $\left\{\gamma_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ be a family of probability measures indexed by $\Theta$. For every number $n>0$ let $\mathscr{F}_{n}$ be the Poisson experiment based on the measures $n \gamma_{\theta}$. Then, for $r \geq 0$ one has

$$
\delta\left(\mathscr{F}_{n}, \mathscr{F}_{n+r}\right) \leq \frac{r}{\sqrt{2 n}}
$$

Proof. If $\mu$ and $v$ are two measures let $H(\mu . v)$ be their Hellinger distance, so that $H^{2}(\mu, \mathrm{v})=\frac{1}{2} \int(\sqrt{d \mu}-\sqrt{d v})^{2}$.

Let $P_{\theta}$ be the distribution of the Poisson process which has base $n \gamma_{\theta}$. Let $Q_{\theta}$ be the distribution corresponding to $(n+r) \gamma_{\theta}$. Then

$$
\begin{aligned}
H^{2}\left(P_{\theta}, Q_{\theta}\right) & =1-\exp \left\{-H^{2}\left[n \gamma_{\theta},(n+r) \gamma_{\theta}\right]\right\} \\
& \leq H^{2}\left(n \gamma_{\theta},(n+r) \gamma_{\theta}\right) \\
& =\frac{1}{2}[\sqrt{(n+r)}-\sqrt{n}]^{2} .
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $\frac{1}{2}\left\|P_{\theta}-Q_{\theta}\right\| \leq 2 H\left(P_{\theta}, Q_{\theta}\right)$ one can conclude that

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{2}\left\|P_{\theta}-Q_{\theta}\right\| & \leq \sqrt{2}(\sqrt{n+r}-\sqrt{n}) \\
& =\sqrt{2 n}(\sqrt{1+r / n}-1) \\
& \leq \frac{r}{\sqrt{2 n}}
\end{aligned}
$$

This implies the desired result.
This lemma shows that it is impossible to have $\delta\left(\mathscr{E}^{n}, \mathscr{E}^{n+1}\right) \geq 1 / 2$ and have at the same time $\Delta\left(\mathscr{E}^{n}, \mathscr{F}_{n}\right) \rightarrow 0$. It shows further that if $\mathscr{E}_{n}^{n}$ is a direct product of $n$ copies of another experiment $\mathscr{E}_{n}$ (which may vary with $n$ ) and if $\mathscr{F}_{n}$ is the accompanying Poisson experiment, then a necessary condition for the convergence of $\Delta\left(\mathscr{E}_{n}^{n}, \mathscr{F}_{n}\right)$ to zero is that $\Delta\left[\mathscr{E}_{n}^{n}, \mathscr{E}_{n}^{m(n)}\right] \rightarrow 0$ for all sequences $m(n)$ such that $[m(n)-n] / \sqrt{n} \rightarrow 0$.

## CHAPTER 10

## Asymptotically Gaussian Experiments: Local Theory

## 1. Introduction

In this chapter we study sequences, or nets, of experiments $\mathscr{E}_{v}$ which are asymptotically Gaussian in the sense that, as $v$ grows, they are more and more closely approximable by Gaussian experiments. The adjective "local" is meant to contrast this chapter with Chapter 11 where a "global" theory, more directly applicable to practical statistical problems, is given in a moderately general context.

If one refers to the origin of the theory presented here, the term "local" is not entirely correct. A better term would be "differential." Indeed, the subject of study arises in a variety of situations where one has a parameter set $\Theta$, subset of a Euclidean space, but where one fixes a particular $\theta_{0}$ and investigates only what happens for parameter values of the type $\theta=\theta_{0}+\delta_{n} t_{n}$, with $\delta_{n} \rightarrow 0$ and $\left\|t_{n}\right\|$ bounded. Here $\delta_{n}$ yields a sort of differentiation rate. In the most classical cases, $\delta_{n}=(1 / \sqrt{n})$.

Here we do not explicitly refer to this differential situation, but it should be kept in mind as an assist to intuition. Also the parameters will be given names such as $t, v$, etc., reserving the letter $\theta$ for the point in the underlying entire space which will reappear in Chapter 11.

Section 2 gives some description of various modes of convergence to Gaussian shift for experiments which are usually indexed by Euclidean sets. However, as explained in Chapter 9, the natural domain of parameterization for Gaussian shift experiments is Hilbert space. The fact that the Hilbert space may be infinite dimensional introduces occasional complications. If so, the finite and infinite dimensional cases are treated separately.

Section 3 deals with a situation where the original parameterization is not the natural one for the Gaussian approximation. Most of the complexity is notational in character. It is unavoidable in the sense that many statistical problems occur in that form. For instance, in multinomial situations, one may consider probability vectors $\left\{p_{j}(\theta) ; j=1,2, \ldots, k\right\}$ where $\theta$ runs through a fairly nondescript set of indices. Then to study the properties of estimation techniques such as minimum $\chi^{2}$ or maximum likelihood, it may be preferable to treat the probability vectors as natural parameters, returning to $\theta$ itself only after the main results have been derived.

This situation occurs often when the statistician investigates vectors $Z_{n}$ which are "asymptotically normal with approximate means $\xi_{n}(\theta)$." This possibility is considered in some detail. It is shown that treating the $Z_{n}$ "as if" they were Gaussian is not fully justifiable unless the logarithms of likelihood ratios are approximated by quadratic expressions in $Z_{n}-\xi_{n}(\theta)$. The section ends by giving supplementary information on the approximate sufficiency results already studied in Section 2.

Section 4 gives a few results concerning the convergence in the Prohorov sense of the linear processes introduced by the likelihood ratio approximations. Briefly, one can modify them to make them converge on Gaussian continuity sets.

Section 5 gives a special result on approximability by Gaussian experiments, using McLeish's form of the central limit theorem for martingales. It will be seen later that the result in question is very close to what is achievable in the case of independent observations.

Section 6 gives a variety of results about asymptotic admissibility, minimaxity. It is mostly an application of the results of Chapter 7 with an admixture of use of Hájek's convolution theorem and/or T.W. Anderson's theorem on the measure of convex sets. No attempt was made to cover all the possibilities of application, since this makes up a rather sizable part of the literature on asymptotic theory.

## 2. Convergence to a Gaussian Shift Experiment

In this section we consider a particular set $A$ and a Gaussian shift experiment $\mathscr{G}=\left\{G_{v} ; v \in A\right\}$ indexed by $A$. Recall that $\mathscr{G}$ is homogeneous and that it is such that the processes $v \mapsto \log \left(d G_{v} / d G_{0}\right)$ have Gaussian distributions (Chapter 9, Section 3). Here, as in the sequel, 0 will denote a particular point of $A$, taken to be the origin when $A$ is a vector space.

Let then $\left\{\mathscr{E}_{n}\right\}$ be a sequence (or a net), of experiment $\mathscr{E}_{n}=\left\{P_{v, n} ; v \in A\right\}$ all indexed by $A$. The following is immediate.

Lemma 1. The experiments $\mathscr{E}_{n}$ converge weakly to the Gaussian shift experiment $\mathscr{G}$ if and only if
(i) for every $v$ the sequences $\left\{P_{v, n}\right\}$ and $\left\{P_{0, n}\right\}$ are contiguous;
(ii) for every finite set $S \subset A$ the joint distributions, under $P_{0, n}$, of the variables $\log \left(d P_{v, n} / d P_{0, n}\right), v \in S$ converge to Gaussian distributions; and
(iii) these limiting Gaussian distributions have the same covariance structure as the variables $\log \left(d G_{v} / d G_{0}\right)$, under $G_{0}$.

Proof. This is immediate. The fact that $\mathscr{G}$ is homogeneous is equivalent to the contiguity assertion. Experiments converge weakly if and only if the distributions of likelihood ratios converge. Here, contiguity allows passage to the logarithms. Statement (iii) insures that the Gaussian limit is the specified Gaussian experiment $\mathscr{G}$.

An additional remark is as follows. Call a subset $S$ of $A$ precompact if the family $\left\{G_{v} ; v \in S\right\}$ is precompact. Let us say that $\mathscr{E}_{n}$ converges to $\mathscr{G}$ uniformly on the precompact subsets of $A$ if for each such precompact set $S$ the distance $\Delta\left(\mathscr{E}_{n, S}, \mathscr{G}_{S}\right)$ of the experiments restricted to $S$ converges to zero.

Lemma 2. The experiments $\mathscr{E}_{n}$ converge to $\mathscr{G}$ uniformly on the precompact subsets of $A$ if and only if
(i) $\mathscr{E}_{n}$ converge weakly to $\mathscr{G}$, and
(ii) if $\left\{u_{n}\right\}$ and $\left\{v_{n}\right\}$ are Cauchy sequences such that $\left\|G_{u_{n}}-G_{v_{n}}\right\| \rightarrow 0$, then $\left\|P_{u_{n}, n}-P_{v_{n}, n}\right\|$ tends to zero.

This is a consequence of Lindae's theorem (Chapter 6, Section 4, Theorem 1).

When the set $A$ has a vector space structure, one can rephrase the above lemmas to parallel the statement of Lemma 2, Chapter 9, Section 3. Explicitly assume that $V$ is a Hilbert space, with norm noted $\|v\|$. Assume also that $A$ is a subset of $V$, that the origin of $V$ belongs to $A$, and that $\mathscr{G}=\left\{G_{v} ; v \in A\right\}$ is the restriction to $A$ of the standard Gaussian shift experiment of $V$. Under these conditions let

$$
L_{n}(v)=\log \frac{d P_{v, n}}{d P_{0, n}}+\frac{1}{2}\|v\|^{2}
$$

Lemma 3. Under the conditions just described, assume that $A$ is a convex subset of $V$. Then, in order that the $\mathscr{E}_{n}$ converge weakly to the restriction to $A$ of the standard Gaussian experiment of $V$, it is necessary and sufficient that
(i) for every $v \in A$ the sequences $\left\{P_{v, n}\right\}$ and $\left\{P_{0, n}\right\}$ are contiguous,
(ii) for every pair ( $u, v$ ) of elements of $A$, the difference $2 L_{n}[(u+v) / 2]\left[L_{n}(u)+L_{n}(v)\right]$ converges to zero in probability.

Proof. Let $\mathbb{E}(A)$ be the space of experiment types indexed by $A$. For the weak topology this is a compact Hausdorff space (Chapter 3, Theorem 2). Let then $\mathscr{E} \in \mathbb{E}(A)$ be a cluster point of the sequence $\left\{\mathscr{E}_{n}\right\}$. Condition (i) implies that $\mathscr{E}$ is homogeneous. Further, writing $\mathscr{E}=\left\{F_{v} ; v \in A\right\}$ and $L^{\prime}(v)=\log \left(d F_{v} / d F_{0}\right)+ \frac{1}{2}\|v\|^{2}$, one sees that condition (ii) implies $2 L^{\prime}[(u+v) / 2]=L^{\prime}(u)+L^{\prime}(v)$ almost surely for $u, v$, and $u+v$ elements of $A$.

Thus, according to Chapter 9, Section 3, Lemma 2, the cluster point $\mathscr{E}$ is the restriction to $A$ of the standard Gaussian experiment of $V$. As such, it is uniquely determined on $A$ and the result follows.

Remark. One can replace the fixed pairs ( $u, v$ ) of Lemma 3 by arbitrary Cauchy sequences $\left\{u_{n}\right\}$ and $\left\{v_{n}\right\}$. In this case the conclusion is that the convergence is uniform on the precompact subsets of $A$.

The preceding Lemma 3 suggests that the expressions called $L_{n}$ could be approximated by linear processes. To be specific, consider the case where $A$ is a convex set containing the origin. For a given experiment $\mathscr{E}_{n}$, consider the Hilbert space $L_{2}\left(P_{0, n}\right)$ formed by the real valued functions which are $P_{0, n}$ square integrable.

A linear process indexed by $A$ and taking values in $L_{2}\left(P_{0, n}\right)$ is a map $v \mapsto \left\langle v, Z_{n}\right\rangle$ such that $\left\langle\alpha_{1} v_{1}+\alpha_{2} v_{2}, Z_{n}\right\rangle=\alpha_{1}\left\langle v_{1}, Z_{n}\right\rangle+\alpha_{2}\left\langle v_{2}, Z_{n}\right\rangle$ whenever the arguments belong to $A$. One may then inquire whether the maps $v \mapsto L_{n}(v)$ can be approximated by linear processes $v \mapsto\left\langle v, Z_{n}\right\rangle$. This seems to be true fairly generally, but we have proofs only for special cases. The finite dimensional case is easy and an immediate consequence of our next lemma. To state it, consider a $v \in A$ and define a variable $X_{n}(v)$ as $\left[-c_{n}(v)\right] \vee L_{n}(v)$ for a coefficient $c_{n}(v) \geq 0$ which is the supremum of all numbers $c>0$ for which $E \exp \left\{c \wedge\left[-L_{n}(v)\right]\right\} \leq \exp \left\{\frac{1}{2}\|v\|^{2}\right\}$.

Lemma 4. Assume that $A$ is a convex subset of $V$ such that $0 \in A$ and that $\mathscr{E}_{n}$ converges weakly to the standard Gaussian experiment restricted to A. Let



$\left\{v_{j} ; j=1,2, \ldots, k\right\}$ be any finite subset of $A$ and let $S$ be the linear span of this finite set. For $v \in S, v=\sum \alpha_{j} v_{j}$, let $\left\langle v, Z_{n}\right\rangle=\sum \alpha_{j} X_{n}\left(v_{j}\right)$. Then,
(i) for all $v \in S \cap A$ the difference $L_{n}(v)-\left\langle v, Z_{n}\right\rangle$ tends to zero in probability;
(ii) for all pairs $(u, v)$ of elements of $S \cap A$ the expression $E\left|\left\langle u, Z_{n}\right\rangle-\left\langle v, Z_{n}\right\rangle\right|^{2}$ converges to $\|u-v\|^{2}$.

Proof. Since the $L_{n}(v)$ are obtained from the logarithms of likelihood ratios, one has $E \exp \left\{L_{n}(v)\right\} \leq \exp \left\{\frac{1}{2}\|v\|^{2}\right\}$ for all $v \in A$ and all $n$. Thus $E\left|X_{n}(v)\right|^{2} \leq 4 \exp \left\{\frac{1}{2}\|v\|^{2}\right\}$. In addition, for any $\varepsilon>0$ there is a number $b$ depending on $v$ and $\varepsilon$ but independent of $n$ such that $E\left\{\left|X_{n}(v)\right|^{2}-b \wedge X_{n}^{2}(v)\right\}< \varepsilon$.

To show that the difference $L_{n}(v)-X_{n}(v)$ tends to zero in probability it is enough to show that the numbers $c_{n}(v)$ tend to $+\infty$. However this follows from the fact that the limiting distribution of $L_{n}(v)$ is a Gaussian measure with mean zero and variance $\|v\|^{2}$, so that $E \exp \left\{c \wedge\left[-L_{n}(v)\right]\right\}$ converges to $E \exp \left\{c \wedge[\leftarrow L(v)\}\right.$ which is strictly inferior to $\exp \left\{\frac{1}{2}\|v\|^{2}\right\}$ for finite values of c.

The first assertion is then a consequence of the linearity of the limit process $v \mapsto L(v)$ associated with the Gaussian experiment. The second assertion is a consequence of the uniform integrability of expressions of the type $\left|\sum \alpha_{j} X_{n}\left(v_{j}\right)\right|^{2}$. Hence the result.

When $A$ is finite dimensional, the above lemma yields immediately the existence of the desired linear processes $Z_{n}$. In fact, if $\left\{v_{j} ; j=1, \ldots, k\right\}, v_{j} \in A$, is a basis for the linear space spanned by $A$, one may define $\left\langle v, Z_{n}\right\rangle=\sum \alpha_{j} X_{n}\left(v_{j}\right)$, for $\sum \alpha_{j} v_{j}=v$. If one does not care about the square integrability of $Z_{n}$ but only about the linearity, one may take $\left\langle v, Z_{n}\right\rangle=\sum \alpha_{j} L_{n}\left(v_{j}\right)$. The infinite dimensional case seems to present additional difficulties. Thus we shall state only a special result as follows.

Assume that $V$ is a separable Hilbert space and take an orthonormal basis $v_{j} ; j=1,2, \ldots$, in $V$. Compute $X_{n}\left(v_{j}\right)$ as for Lemma 4. Let $Y_{n, 1}=X_{n}\left(v_{1}\right)$. If $Y_{n, j}$ has been defined for $j<k$, let $Y_{n, k}$ be the component of $X_{n}\left(v_{k}\right)$ which is orthogonal to the space spanned by $Y_{n, j} ; j<k$. Let $Z_{n}$ be the linear process defined by

$$
\left\langle v, Z_{n}\right\rangle=\sum \alpha_{j} Y_{n, j} \quad \text { if } v=\sum \alpha_{j} v_{j}
$$

Proposition 1. Assume that $V$ is a separable Hilbert space and assume that $\mathscr{E}_{n}$ converges weakly to the standard Gaussian experiment of $V$. Define a linear process $Z_{n}$ as just explained. Then $L_{n}(v)-\left\langle v, Z_{n}\right\rangle$ tends to zero in probability for all $v \in V$.

Proof. First note that $Z_{n}$ is well defined since $E\left|\left\langle v, Z_{n}\right\rangle\right|^{2} \leq(4 \sqrt{e}) \sum \alpha_{j}^{2}$ whenever $v=\sum \alpha_{j} v_{j}$. Consider a particular $v=\sum \alpha_{j} v_{j}$ and an $\varepsilon>0$. Then, there is some integer $k$ such that $\sum_{j>k} \alpha_{j}^{2}<\varepsilon$ and therefore $E\left|\left\langle v-u, Z_{n}\right\rangle\right|^{2} \leq 4 \sqrt{e} \varepsilon$ for $u=\sum_{j \leq k} \alpha_{j} v_{j}$. Consider also the difference $L_{n}(v)-L_{n}(u)$. It has a limiting Gaussian distribution with mean zero and variance $\|u-v\|^{2}<\varepsilon$. Thus, to show that $L_{n}(v)-\left\langle v, Z_{n}\right\rangle$ tends to zero it will be sufficient to show
that $L_{n}(u)-\left\langle u, Z_{n}\right\rangle \rightarrow 0$, or equivalently that for all $j \leq k$ the differences $X_{n}\left(v_{j}\right)-Y_{n, j}$ tend to zero. However, the joint limiting distribution of the $X_{n}\left(v_{j}\right)$ is the distribution of the corresponding vector $\left\{L\left(v_{j}\right) ; j=1, \ldots, k\right\}$ for the limiting Gaussian experiment. The variables $L\left(v_{j}\right)$ are independent. Thus the projection of $L\left(v_{i}\right)$ on the space orthogonal to $\left\{L\left(v_{j}\right) ; j<i\right\}$ is $L\left(v_{i}\right)$ itself.

According to statement (ii) of Lemma 4, the covariance structure of the $X_{n}\left(v_{j}\right)$ tends to that of the $L\left(v_{j}\right)$. This yields the desired result.

Note. One could replace the $Y_{n, j}$ by combinations $a_{n, j} Y_{n, j}+b_{n, j}$ which have mean zero and variance unity. Then $Z_{n}$ would be an isometry.

In the preceding proposition, as in Lemma 4, we have assumed only weak convergence of $\mathscr{E}_{n}$ to the Gaussian experiment $\mathscr{G}$. If one assumes that $\mathscr{E}_{n}$ converges to $\mathscr{G}$ uniformly on the precompact subsets of $A$, the conclusion can be strengthened to the assertion that $E\left\{1 \wedge\left|L_{n}(v)-\left\langle v, Z_{n}\right\rangle\right|\right\}$ converges to zero uniformly on the precompact subsets of $A$. This can be seen by obvious modifications of the proofs given above.

For the statement of our next result we shall use such a mode of convergence. There is, of course, an analogous result which uses only weak convergence. For the next statement we consider a sequence $\left\{\mathscr{E}_{n}\right\}$ of experiments $\mathscr{E}_{n}= \left\{P_{v, n} ; v \in A\right\}$ and denote $L_{n}(v)$ the expression

$$
L_{n}(v)=\log \frac{d P_{v, n}}{d P_{0, n}}+\frac{1}{2}\|v\|^{2}
$$

as before. Furthermore, we shall proceed as if the $P_{v, n}$ were measures on a space $\left(\mathscr{X}_{n}, \mathscr{A}_{n}\right)$. This is just a matter of language.

Theorem 1. Let $A$ be a subset of a Hilbert space such that $0 \in A$. Let $\left\{\mathscr{E}_{n}\right\}$, $\mathscr{E}_{n}=\left\{P_{v, n} ; v \in A\right\}$ be a sequence of experiments indexed by A. Assume that $\mathscr{E}_{n}$ converges uniformly on the precompact subsets of $A$ to the restriction to $A$ of the standard Gaussian experiment of the Hilbert space.

Let $\left\{Z_{n}\right\}$ be a sequence of linear processes such that $E_{0}\left\{1 \wedge\left|\left\langle v, Z_{n}\right\rangle-L_{n}(v)\right|\right\}$ converges to zero uniformly on the precompact subsets of $A$. Let $\mathscr{B}_{n}$ be the smallest $\sigma$-field for which the evaluations $\left\langle v, Z_{n}\right\rangle$ are measurable. Then there are probability measures $Q_{v, n} ; v \in A$ defined on $\mathscr{A}_{n}$ and such that
(i) for the family $\left\{Q_{v, n} ; v \in A\right\}$, the $\sigma$-field $\mathscr{B}_{n}$ is sufficient;
(ii) for every precompact $S \subset A$ one has $\lim _{n} \sup _{v \in S}\left\|Q_{v, n}-P_{v, n}\right\|=0$;
(iii) on the $\sigma$-field $\mathscr{B}_{n}$ the measures $Q_{v, n}$ and $P_{v, n}$ coincide.

Proof. It has been shown in Chapter 5 that statement (iii) can be obtained if (i) and (ii) already hold. Thus we shall produce only a family which satisfies (i) and (ii).

Consider the random variables $g(n, v)=\exp \left\{\left\langle v, Z_{n}\right\rangle-\frac{1}{2}\|v\|^{2}\right\}$. Define numbers $c_{n}(v)$ and variables $f(n, v)=c_{n}(v) \wedge g(n, v)$ as follows. Taking expectations for $P_{0, n}$ let $c_{n}(v)=\infty$ if $\operatorname{Eg}(n, v) \leq 1$. If $\operatorname{Eg}(n, v)>1$, let $c_{n}(v)$ be the supremum of the numbers $c>0$ such that $E\{c \wedge g(n, v)\} \leq 1$. By continuity one has $E f(n, v)=1$ whenever $c_{n}(v)<\infty$.

Let $M_{v, n}$ be the measure whose density with respect to $P_{0, n}$ is $f(n, v)$. Then $0<\left\|M_{v, n}\right\| \leq 1$ with $\left\|M_{v, n}\right\|=1$ for $c_{n}(v)<\infty$. Let $\left\{v_{n}\right\}$ be a Cauchy sequence
in $A$ and, for simplicity of notation let $P_{n}=P_{v_{n}, n}, M_{n}=M_{v_{n}, n}$, and similarly $f_{n}=f\left(n, v_{n}\right), g_{n}=g\left(n, v_{n}\right)$, etc. Let $h_{n}$ be the density $h_{n}=\left(d M_{n} / d P_{n}\right)$.

According to the inequalities of Chapter 4, and taking into account the fact that $\left\|M_{n}\right\|-\left\|P_{n}\right\|$ is either zero or negative, one may write, for $\varepsilon>0$,

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left\|P_{n}-M_{n}\right\| & \leq 2 \int\left(1-1 \wedge h_{n}\right) d P_{n} \\
& \leq 2 \varepsilon P_{n}\left[1-\varepsilon \leq h_{n} \leq 1\right]+2 P_{n}\left[h_{n}<1-\varepsilon\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

Since the sequences $\left\{P_{n}\right\}$ and $\left\{P_{0, n}\right\}$ are contiguous, the density $h_{n}$ is asymptotically equivalent to the ratio

$$
\frac{d M_{n}}{d P_{0, n}} \frac{d P_{0, n}}{d P_{n}}=f_{n}\left(\frac{d P_{n}}{d P_{0, n}}\right)^{-1}
$$

By assumption one knows that $g_{n}\left(d P_{n} / d P_{0, n}\right)^{-1}$ tends to unity in probability.

Thus to show that $h_{n}$ tends in probability to unity it will be enough to show that the coefficients $c_{n}=c_{n}\left(v_{n}\right)$ tend to infinity. This follows, as in Lemma 4, from the fact that the variables $\left\langle v_{n}, Z_{n}\right\rangle$ are asymptotically Gaussian with mean zero and variances $\left\|v_{n}\right\|^{2}$, so that if $c_{n}<c<\infty$ the limits of $E c_{n} \wedge g\left(n, v_{n}\right)$ are strictly inferior to unity.

This shows that $\left\|P_{n}-M_{n}\right\|$ tends to zero. To obtain the measures $Q_{v, n}$ of the theorem let $Q_{v, n}=\left\|M_{v, n}\right\|^{-1} M_{v, n}$. If $\left\|M_{n}\right\|<1$, then for the corresponding $Q_{n}$ one has $P_{n}-Q_{n}=\left[P_{n}-M_{n}\right]-\left(\left\|M_{n}\right\|^{-1}-1\right) M_{n}$ and therefore

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left\|P_{n}-Q_{n}\right\| & \leq\left\|P_{n}-M_{n}\right\|+1-\left\|M_{n}\right\| \\
& \leq 2\left\|P_{n}-M_{n}\right\|
\end{aligned}
$$

Thus along our Cauchy sequence $\left\{v_{n}\right\}$ the differences $\left\|P_{n}-Q_{n}\right\|$ always converge to zero. This implies the desired result.

Remark. The reader may note that the linearity of the process $Z_{n}$ is irrelevant in the above arguments.

One could also define $\sigma$-fields, such as the $\mathscr{B}_{n}$ used here, in different ways. For instance, let $V$ be a Hilbert space with an orthonormal basis $\left\{v_{j} ; j \in J\right\}$ of arbitrary cardinality. For each $j \in J$ let $X_{n, j}$ be some random variable such that $X_{n, j}-L_{n}\left(v_{j}\right)$ tends to zero in probability. Let $\mathscr{B}_{n}$ be the $\sigma$-field defined by the $X_{n, j}, j \in J$. If $\mathscr{E}_{n}$ converges to the Gaussian experiment $\mathscr{G}$ uniformly on the precompact subsets of $V$, then the conclusion of Theorem 1 is still valid for the present $\mathscr{B}_{n}$. Indeed it is enough to check it for Cauchy sequences $\left\{v_{n}\right\}$ where the $v_{n}$ are finite sums $v_{n}=\sum \alpha_{n, j} v_{j} ; j \in S_{n}$.

Using the precompactness of $\left\{v_{n}\right\}$ one reduces the problem further to a situation where the finite sets $S_{n}$ are independent of $n$. The result is then immediate.

Similar constructions can be carried out in very many different ways even if $A$ is a fairly arbitrary subset of a Hilbert space.

The foregoing theorem says that one can approximate the $\left\{P_{v, n}\right\}$ by a family $\left\{Q_{v, n}\right\}$ for which $\mathscr{B}_{n}$ is sufficient. However, the family $\left\{Q_{v, n}\right\}$ itself is rather complex. In certain cases one can replace the $Q_{v, n}$ by an exponential family. This does not actually give much more information than Theorem 1 itself but it allows one to argue more directly on the optimality of certain procedures, since some of these are known in the exponential situation. We shall now give a rather special result of that nature.

Call a subset $A$ of a Hilbert space polyhedral if it is either the convex hull of a finite set or, if a cone, convex hull of a finite number of rays.

Proposition 2. Let $A$ be a polyhedral subset of a Hilbert space. Assume that $0 \in A$ and that, when $A$ is cone, the origin is the vertex of $A$.

Assume that the experiments $\mathscr{E}_{n}$ and the linear processes $Z_{n}$ satisfy the conditions of Theorem 1. Then one can truncate the $Z_{n}$, obtaining processes $Z_{n}^{*}$ with the following property.

Let $M_{v, n}$ be the measure whose density with respect to $P_{0, n}$ is $\exp \left\{\left\langle v, Z_{n}^{*}\right\rangle-\frac{1}{2}\|v\|^{2}\right\}$. Then $\left\|P_{v, n}-M_{v, n}\right\| \rightarrow 0$ uniformly on the precompact subsets of $A$.

Proof. The case where $A$ is the convex hull of a finite set being much easier, we shall give the proof only for the case where $A$ is a polyhedral cone.

On each of the rays which generate $A$, select a particular element of norm unity. This gives a finite set, say $\left\{v_{j} ; j \in J\right\}$. If $z$ is an element of the dual space of the linear span of $A$, let $\rho(z)=\sup _{j}\left\langle v_{j}, z\right\rangle$. Consider the vector $Z_{n}$ and two numbers $m$ and $b$. Let $Z_{n}(b)=Z_{n}$ if $\rho\left(Z_{n}\right) \leq b$. Otherwise let $Z_{n}(b)=b \rho^{-1}\left(Z_{n}\right) Z_{n}$.

Now let $b(n, m)$ be the supremum of the numbers $b$ such that

$$
E \exp \left\{\left\langle s v_{j}, Z_{n}(b)\right\rangle-\frac{1}{2}\left\|s v_{j}\right\|^{2}\right\} \leq 1
$$

for all $j \in J$ and $0<s \leq m$.
The argument carried out in the proof of Theorem 1 shows that for each fixed integer $m$ the numbers $b(n, m)$ tend to infinity as $n$ does. Thus, we can find a sequence $m(n)$ such that both $m(n)$ and $b[n, m(n)]$ tend to infinity.

Let $Z_{n}^{*}=Z_{n}[b[n, m(n)]]$ for such a sequence. Clearly $Z_{n}^{*}-Z_{n}$ tends to zero in probability. Also, according to Hölder's inequality, if $v=\sum \alpha_{j} v_{j}, \alpha_{j} \geq 0$, $s=\sum \alpha_{j} \leq m(n)$ then

$$
E \exp \left\{\left\langle v, Z_{n}^{*}\right\rangle\right\} \leq \exp \frac{s}{2} \sum \alpha_{j}\left\|v_{j}\right\|^{2}
$$

According to the assumptions made $Z_{n}$, and therefore $\left\langle v, Z_{n}^{*}\right\rangle$ has a limiting Gaussian distribution with mean zero and variance $\|v\|^{2}$. It follows then from the above inequalities that $E \exp \left\{\left\langle v, Z_{n}^{*}\right\rangle-\frac{1}{2}\|v\|^{2}\right\}$ tends to unity uniformly on the compact subsets of $A$.

To show that the differences $\left\|M_{v, n}-P_{v, n}\right\|$ tend to zero uniformly on the compacts of $A$, one can then repeat the argumentation carried out in Theorem 1. Hence the result.

## 3. A Framework which Arises in Many Applications

In the preceding section, experiments $\mathscr{E}_{n}$ were indexed by a certain fixed set $A$ which could be treated as a natural parameterization of all the $\mathscr{E}_{n}$. In this section we describe a situation which is of the same nature, but is complicated by the fact that the given parameter spaces are not necessarily the natural ones.

Besides introducing definitions intended for use in such a case, we also make a bridge between the descriptions in terms of convergence of experiments and the more classical ones, involving convergence of distributions.

Consider sets $S_{n}$ and experiments $\mathscr{E}_{n}=\left\{P_{s, n} ; s \in S_{n}\right\}$ indexed by $S_{n}$. Assume also that one has a fixed vector space $V$, and, for each $n$, a map $\xi_{n}$ from $S_{n}$ to $V$.

Definition 1. The sequence $\left\{\xi_{n}\right\}$ will be called adequate if $\left\|P_{s_{n}, n}-P_{t_{n}, n}\right\| \rightarrow$ 0 for all pairs of sequences $\left\{s_{n}\right\},\left\{t_{n}\right\}$, such that $\xi_{n}\left(s_{n}\right)=\xi_{n}\left(t_{n}\right)$ for all sufficiently large values of $n$.

To describe the intentions behind this definition we need further concepts as follows.

Assume that $V$ is a Hilbert space. For each $n$ let $A_{n}$ be a subset of $V$ and let $\mathscr{F}_{n}^{i}, i=1,2$ be two experiments indexed by $A_{n}$.

Definition 2. The two sequences $\left\{\mathscr{F}_{n}^{1}\right\}$ and $\left\{\mathscr{F}_{n}^{2}\right\}$ will be called asymptotically equivalent on precompact sets if for every precompact subset $K \subset V$ the distance between the $\mathscr{F}_{n}^{i}, i=1,2$ restricted to $K \cap A_{n}$ tends to zero as $n \rightarrow \infty$.

Now consider the experiment $\mathscr{E}_{n}=\left\{P_{s, n} ; s \in S_{n}\right\}$. For each $v$ in the image $\xi_{n}\left(S_{n}\right)$, select a particular $s_{n}(v)$ such that $\xi_{n}\left(s_{n}\right)=v$. Define $F_{v, n}^{1}=P_{s_{n}, n}$. This gives an experiment $\mathscr{F}_{n}^{1}=\left\{F_{v, n}^{1} ; v \in \xi_{n}\left(S_{n}\right)\right\}$. One could also select alternates $t_{n}(v)$ such that $\xi_{n}\left(t_{n}\right)=v$ and obtain a second experiment $\mathscr{F}_{n}^{2}$ with elements $F_{v, n}^{2}=P_{t_{n}(v), n}$.

When the sequence $\left\{\xi_{n}\right\}$ is adequate, two sequences obtained in this manner are always asymptotically equivalent on precompact sets. One can say somewhat more. For instance let $\mathscr{F}_{n}{ }^{1}$ be given by measures on a space $\left(\mathscr{X}_{n}, \mathscr{A}_{n}\right)$. Let $\mathscr{B}_{n}$ be a sub- $\sigma$-field of $\mathscr{A}_{n}$. If $\mathscr{B}_{n}$ is asymptotically sufficient on precompact sets for $\mathscr{F}_{n}{ }^{1}$, then it has the same property for $\mathscr{F}_{n}{ }^{2}$. To put it differently, adequacy of the sequence $\left\{\xi_{n}\right\}$ allows replacement of the initial sets $S_{n}$ by subsets of $V$.

Here the subsets of $V$ which occur in the formulation are the ranges $A_{n}= \xi_{n}\left(S_{n}\right)$ of the maps $\xi_{n}$. It is often convenient to replace them by other sets $B_{n}$ which are more tractable. For this purpose, we may use the following definition, in which $V$ is a Hilbert space, $a_{n}$ is some element of $V$, and $U\left(a_{n}, b\right)$ is the ball of radius $b$ centered at $a_{n}$.

Definition 3. The two sequences $\left\{A_{n}\right\}$ and $\left\{B_{n}\right\}$ of subsets of $V$ are asymptotically tangent at $\left\{a_{n}\right\}$ if for every number $b$ the Hausdorff distance between $A_{n} \cap U\left(a_{n}, b\right)$ and $B_{n} \cap U\left(a_{n}, b\right)$ tends to zero as $n \rightarrow \infty$.

Note. This definition may appear unrelated to ordinary definitions of tangency for sets. It takes a more recognizable form if one assumes that the space $V$ has been obtained through a rescaling operation, so that the balls $U\left(a_{n}, b\right)$
are balls of diameter $\varepsilon_{n}, \varepsilon_{n} \rightarrow 0$ in the original distances. Then the assertion of tangency is that the distance between $A_{n} \cap U\left(a_{n}, b\right)$ and $B_{n} \cap U\left(a_{n}, b\right)$ is of smaller order than $\varepsilon_{n}$.

Returning to the sequence $\left\{\mathscr{E}_{n}\right\}, \mathscr{E}_{n}=\left\{P_{s, n} ; s \in S_{n}\right\}$, let $A_{n}=\xi_{n}\left(S_{n}\right)$. Let $a_{n} \in V$ and $B_{n} \subset V$ be such that $\left\{A_{n}\right\}$ and $\left\{B_{n}\right\}$ are asymptotically tangent at $\left\{a_{n}\right\}$. Assume in addition that $\left\{a_{n}\right\}$ is bounded. Form experiments $\mathscr{F}_{n}=\left\{F_{v, n} ; v \in A_{n}\right\}$ by taking some $s_{n}(v)$ such that $\xi_{n}\left[s_{n}(v)\right]=v$ and writing $F_{v, n}=P_{s_{n}(v), n}$.

To obtain an experiment indexed by $B_{n}$, select for each $v \in B_{n}$ some point $u(v)$ of $A_{n}$ such that $\|u(v)-v\| \leq(1 / n)+\inf _{u}\left\{\|u-v\|, u \in A_{n}\right\}$. Then, let $\mathscr{F}_{n}^{*}=\left\{F_{v, n}^{*} ; v \in B_{n}\right\}$ be defined by $F_{v, n}^{*}=F_{u(v), n}$.

We shall use such a construction repeatedly in Chapter 11, passing back and forth between a set $A_{n}$ and a "tangent set" $B_{n}$. To describe one property of the construction we need still further concepts. One of them is an asymptotic equicontinuity property as follows.

Definition 4. Let $\left\{\mathscr{F}_{n}\right\}, \mathscr{F}_{n}=\left\{F_{v, n} ; v \in A_{n}\right\}$ be a sequence of experiments indexed by subsets $A_{n}$ of the Hilbert space $V$. The sequence $\left\{\mathscr{F}_{n}\right\}$ will be called regular if for every pair $\left\{s_{n}\right\},\left\{t_{n}\right\}$ of sequences such that
(i) $s_{n} \in A_{n}, t_{n} \in A_{n}$,
(ii) $\left\|s_{n}-t_{n}\right\| \rightarrow 0$, and
(iii) $\left\{s_{n}\right\}$ and $\left\{t_{n}\right\}$ are in a precompact set $K$, the distance $\left\|F_{s_{n}, n}-F_{t_{n}, n}\right\|$ tends to zero.

For an instance, let $\mathscr{G}=\left\{G_{v}, v \in V\right\}$ be the standard Gaussian shift experiment of $V$. Assume that for every precompact set $K$ the distance between $\mathscr{F}_{n}$ restricted to $K \cap A_{n}$ and $\mathscr{G}$ similarly restricted to $K \cap A_{n}$ tends to zero. Then the sequence $\left\{\mathscr{F}_{n}\right\}$ must be regular.

Now, for each $n$, let $\left\{\gamma_{n, j} ; j \in J_{n}\right\}$ be a family of continuous functions defined on all of $V$ and such that $\gamma_{n, j} \geq 0, \sum_{j} \gamma_{n, j}=1$. Assume that each $\gamma_{n, j}$ of this partition of unity has a support whose diameter is inferior to some number $\varepsilon_{n}, \varepsilon_{n} \rightarrow 0$. There always exist such partitions since $V$ is paracompact.

Assume that for each $\gamma_{n, j}$ one is given an element $u_{n, j} \in V$ such that $\gamma_{n, j}\left(u_{n, j}\right)>0$. For each $(n, j)$ let $v_{n, j}$ be some element of $A_{n}$ such that

$$
\left\|u_{n, j}-v_{n, j}\right\| \leq \frac{1}{n}+\inf _{v}\left\{\left\|u_{n, j}-v\right\|, v \in A_{n}\right\}
$$

Let $\overline{\mathscr{F}_{n}}$ be the experiment $\overline{\mathscr{F}_{n}}=\left\{\bar{F}_{v, n} ; v \in V\right\}$ defined on the entire space $V$ by the relation

$$
\bar{F}_{v, n}=\sum_{j} \gamma_{n, j}(v) F_{v_{n, j}, n}
$$

With these constructions one may state the following.
Lemma 5. Assume that $\left\{\mathscr{F}_{n}\right\}$ is regular, construct an interpolated experiment $\overline{\mathscr{F}}_{n}$ as above. For sets $B_{n}$ asymptotically tangent to $A_{n}$ at zero, form the experiments $\mathscr{F}_{n}{ }^{*}$ as described, and the corresponding interpolated experiments $\overline{\mathscr{F}}_{n}{ }^{*}$. Then, for every precompact subset $K$ of $V$,
(i) $\sup _{v}\left\{\left\|F_{v, n}-\bar{F}_{v, n}\right\| ; v \in K \cap A_{n}\right\} \rightarrow 0$;
(ii) $\sup _{v}\left\{\left\|\bar{F}_{v, n}-\bar{F}_{v, n}^{*}\right\| ; v \in K\right\} \rightarrow 0$.

The proof is trivial.
For regular sequences $\left\{\mathscr{F}_{n}\right\}$ this will allow us to replace sets $A_{n}$ by their closure, or by tangent sets $B_{n}$ whenever it appears convenient to do so. This being the case, let us pass to the classical structure which often arises and introduces the framework described above.

Consider experiments $\mathscr{E}_{n}=\left\{P_{s, n} ; s \in S_{n}\right\}$ indexed by sets $S_{n}$ and the Hilbert space $V$. For each $n$ let $\xi_{n}$ be a map from $S_{n}$ to $V$ and, assuming that the $P_{s, n}$ are measures on a space $\left(\mathscr{X}_{n}, \mathscr{A}_{n}\right)$, let $Z_{n}$ be a measurable map from $\left(\mathscr{X}_{n}, \mathscr{A}_{n}\right)$ to $V$.

When $V$ is finite dimensional, $Z_{n}$ is also called a random vector. We shall use this terminology whether $Z_{n}$ is weakly or strongly measurable and whether $V$ is finite dimensional or not. Let $\mathscr{W}$ be the dual of $V$ and consider the following conditions. It will be assumed that there is a particular sequence, say $\left\{a_{n}\right\}, a_{n} \in S_{n}$, such that $\xi_{n}\left(a_{n}\right)=0$.

Condition (1). Let $\left\{s_{n}\right\}$ be a sequence such that the values $\xi_{n}\left(s_{n}\right)$ remain in a precompact subset of $V$. Then, for every bounded sequence $\left\{w_{n}\right\}, w_{n} \in \mathscr{W}$, the Dudley distance between the Gaussian distribution $\mathscr{N}\left[0,\left\|w_{n}\right\|^{2}\right]$ and the distribution

$$
\mathscr{L}\left\{\left\langle w_{n}, Z_{n}-\xi_{n}\left(s_{n}\right)\right\rangle \mid P_{s_{n}, n}\right\}
$$

tends to zero as $n \rightarrow \infty$.
In the finite dimensional case this can be stated by saying that $\mathscr{L}\left\{Z_{n}-\xi_{n}\left(s_{n}\right) \mid P_{s_{n}, n}\right\}$ converges to the standard Gaussian distribution $\mathscr{N}(0, I)$.

Let $G_{0}$ be the standard Gaussian distribution of $V$. In the finite dimensional case, this is usually noted $G_{0}=\mathscr{N}(0, I)$. In the infinite dimensional case one can describe it as the distribution of a linear process $w \mapsto\langle w, Z\rangle$ indexed by $\mathscr{W}$ and such that $\langle w, Z\rangle$ is Gaussian with mean zero and variance $\|w\|^{2}$.

Let $G_{v}$ be $G_{0}$ translated by $v$, that is, $G_{v}$ is the distribution of $v+Z$. Then $\left\{G_{v} ; v \in V\right\}$ is the standard Gaussian experiment of $V$. If $s \in S_{n}$, let $Q_{s, n}$ be the distribution of $\xi_{n}(s)+Z$. For each pair ( $s, t$ ) of elements of $S_{n}$ one can compute $\chi^{2}$-type distances as in Chapter 4. For instance

$$
k^{2}\left(P_{s, n}, P_{t, n}\right)=\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{\left(d P_{s, n}-d P_{t, n}\right)^{2}}{d\left(P_{s, n}+P_{t, n}\right)}
$$

According to the inequalities of Chapter 4, the following condition may be construed to mean that for pairs ( $s, t$ ) of elements of $S_{n}$ a suitably averaged power of tests based on $\mathscr{E}_{n}$ is not much larger than the corresponding average for the pair $\left(Q_{s, n}, Q_{t, n}\right)$.

Condition (2). For every sequence $\left\{s_{n}\right\}, s_{n} \in S_{n}$ such that $\left\{\xi_{n}\left(s_{n}\right)\right\}$ is precompact, one has

$$
\limsup _{n}\left\{k^{2}\left(P_{s_{n}, n}, P_{a_{n}, n}\right)-k^{2}\left(Q_{s_{n}, n}, Q_{a_{n}, n}\right)\right\} \leq 0
$$

Let $\Lambda_{n}(s)$ be the logarithm of the density of $P_{s, n}$ with respect to $P_{a_{n}, n}$. If $v \in V$ and $z \in V$ let $\langle v, z\rangle$ be the inner product defined as usual so that $4\langle v, z\rangle=\|v+z\|^{2}-\|v-z\|^{2}$. This identifies $v$ with an element of the dual $\mathscr{W}$ so that $\left\langle v, Z_{n}\right\rangle$ is a well defined random variable.

Theorem 2. For the situation described above, assume that Conditions 1 and 2 are both satisfied. Then,
(i) for every sequence $\left\{s_{n}\right\}$ such that $\left\{\xi_{n}\left(s_{n}\right)\right\}$ is precompact the difference

$$
\Lambda_{n}\left(s_{n}\right)+\frac{1}{2}\left\|\xi_{n}\left(s_{n}\right)\right\|^{2}-\left\langle\xi_{n}\left(s_{n}\right), Z_{n}\right\rangle
$$

tends to zero in probability;
(ii) let $C_{n} \subset S_{n}$ be such that the union of the sets $\xi_{n}\left(C_{n}\right)$ is precompact. Then the distance between the experiments $\left\{P_{s, n} ; s \in C_{n}\right\}$ and $\left\{Q_{s, n} ; s \in C_{n}\right\}$ tends to zero as $n \rightarrow \infty$;
(iii) the maps $\left\{\xi_{n}\right\}$ are adequate.

Proof. Consider a particular sequence $\left\{s_{n}\right\}$ for which $\left\{\xi_{n}\left(s_{n}\right)\right\}$ is a precompact set. Let $Y_{n}$ be the random variable $\left\langle\xi_{n}\left(s_{n}\right), Z_{n}\right\rangle$. Let $F_{n}^{0}$ be the distribution $F_{n}^{0}=\mathscr{L}\left(Y_{n} \mid P_{a_{n}, n}\right)$, and let $F_{n}^{1}=\mathscr{L}\left(Y_{n} \mid P_{s_{n}, n}\right)$. Similarly, let $\sigma_{n}^{2}=\left\|\xi_{n}\left(s_{n}\right)\right\|^{2}$ and let $G_{n}^{0}$ be the distribution $\mathscr{N}\left(0, \sigma_{n}^{2}\right)$ on the line. Let $G_{n}^{1}=\mathscr{N}\left(\sigma_{n}^{2}, \sigma_{n}^{2}\right)$.

Let $g_{n}$ be the density of $G_{n}^{1}$ with respect to $G_{n}^{0}+G_{n}^{1}$. This density satisfies a Lipschitz condition $\left|g_{n}\left(y_{1}\right)-g_{n}\left(y_{2}\right)\right| \leq b\left|y_{1}-y_{2}\right|$ with a coefficient $b \leq 1$. Let $f_{n}$ be the density of $P_{s_{n}, n}$ with respect to the sum $P_{a_{n}, n}+P_{s_{n}, n}=\mu_{n}$. According to Proposition 5, Chapter 4, one may write

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int\left|f_{n}-g_{n}\left(Y_{n}\right)\right|^{2} d \mu_{n} \leq & \frac{1}{2}\left\{k^{2}\left(P_{s_{n}, n}, P_{a_{n}, n}\right)-k^{2}\left(G_{n}^{1}, G_{n}^{0}\right)\right\} \\
& +2 b\left\{\left\|F_{n}^{0}-G_{n}^{0}\right\|_{D}+\left\|F_{n}^{1}-G_{n}^{1}\right\|_{D}\right\} .
\end{aligned}
$$

According to Condition (2) the difference between the square distances $k^{2}$ tends to something at most equal to zero. According to Condition (1), the Dudley distances tend to zero. Thus $f_{n}-g_{n}\left(Y_{n}\right)$ tends to zero in probability. This implies in particular that the cluster points of the distributions $\mathscr{L}\left(f_{n} \mid \mu_{n}\right)$ are the same as those of $\mathscr{L}\left(g_{n}\left(Y_{n}\right) \mid \mu_{n}\right)$. These latter are entirely carried by the open interval $(0,1)$. Therefore the sequences $\left\{P_{a_{n}, n}\right\}$ and $\left\{P_{s_{n}, n}\right\}$ are contiguous. Statement (i) of the Theorem follows then by looking at $\log \left[f_{n}\left(1-f_{n}\right)^{-1}\right]$.

To prove statement (ii) consider first the case where $C_{n}$ consists of a bounded number of elements, say $s_{n, j} ; j=1, \ldots, m$. One can then form the vector $\left\{Y_{n, j} ; j=1, \ldots, m\right\}$ with $Y_{n, j}=\left\langle\xi_{n}\left(s_{n, j}\right), Z_{n}\right\rangle$.

Since the expressions $\Lambda_{n}\left(s_{n, j}\right)$ are almost computable from the $Y_{n, j}$, these vectors $\left\{Y_{n, j} ; j=1, \ldots, m\right\}$ behave as distinguished sequences of statistics (Chapter 7, Section 3). Thus the corresponding limits of experiments and experiments formed by limits of distributions are the same. The result for sets such that $\bigcup_{n} \xi_{n}\left(C_{n}\right)$ is precompact follows then by application of Lindae's theorem.

The third statement is a consequence of the stronger observation that if $\xi_{n}\left(s_{n}\right)-\xi_{n}\left(t_{n}\right) \rightarrow 0$ then $\left\|P_{s_{n}, n}-P_{t_{n}, n}\right\| \rightarrow 0$. This completes the proof of the theorem.

Remark 1. Under the conditions of the theorem one may construct experiments $\left\{F_{v, n} ; v \in A_{n}\right\}$ with $A_{n}=\xi_{n}\left(S_{n}\right)$ as explained in this section. The argument of the theorem shows that they are regular.

Remark 2. Suppose that the $P_{s, n}$ are measures on $\left(\mathscr{X}_{n}, \mathscr{A}_{n}\right)$ and let $\mathscr{B}_{n}$ be the $\sigma$-field generated by $Z_{n}$. Then, according to the argument of Theorem 1 , the $\mathscr{B}_{n}$ are asymptotically sufficient in the sense that there are measures $P_{s, n}^{\prime}, s \in S_{n}$, such that (i) for this family the $\sigma$-field $\mathscr{B}_{n}$ is sufficient, and (ii) if $\bigcup_{n} \xi_{n}\left(C_{n}\right)$ is precompact then $\sup \left\{\left\|P_{s, n}^{\prime}-P_{s, n}\right\|, s \in C_{n}\right\} \rightarrow 0$.

Remark 3. Assuming that $V$ is finite dimensional the above Theorem 2 shows that the classical arguments using assumptions of the type

$$
\mathscr{L}\left[Z_{n}-\xi_{n}\left(s_{n}\right) \mid P_{s_{n}, n}\right] \rightarrow \mathscr{N}(0, I)
$$

will correspond very exactly to the arguments one can carry out with distances between experiments, but only in those cases where Condition (2) is also satisfied.

The asymptotic normality of the sequences of distributions is not by itself an insurance that tests based on $Z_{n}$, or estimates based on $Z_{n}$ will perform as well as those that could be obtained on the family $\left\{P_{s, n} ; s \in S_{n}\right\}$ itself.

If that is the case, Condition (2) is satisfied and the $Z_{n}$ are "distinguished" asymptotically sufficient. Otherwise the $Z_{n}$ might be asymptotically sufficient and not distinguished, or neither. When they are not asymptotically sufficient one may still want to look at procedures which depend only on the $Z_{n}$. In such a case on would, in Theorem 2, replace the distributions $P_{s, n}$ by the distributions $\mathscr{L}\left(Z_{n} \mid P_{s, n}\right)$.

## 4. Weak Convergence of Distributions

This section deals with a special topic. It may be omitted at first or second reading. The subject is as follows. In Section 2 it has been shown that weak convergence of experiments $\mathscr{E}_{n}$ to a Gaussian limit implies, at least in some cases, the existence of linear processes $Z_{n}$ which approximate the modified $\log$ likelihood functions $L_{n}$. In the finite dimensional case, these random vectors $Z_{n}$ have distributions which converge (for $P_{0, n}$ ) to the Gaussian distribution $\mathscr{N}(0, I)$. When the index set $A$ of the experiments $\mathscr{E}_{n}$ is infinite dimensional, the processes $Z_{n}$, if they exist, are also such that $\mathscr{L}\left\{\left\langle v, Z_{n}\right\rangle \mid P_{0, n}\right\}$ converges to $\mathscr{N}\left[0,\|v\|^{2}\right]$ for each $v$. For many test functions, say $\phi$, one would like to be able to assert that $\phi\left(Z_{n}\right)$ has an expectation which converges to what would be obtained if one replaced the processes $Z_{n}$ by the standard Gaussian linear process $Z$ of the Hilbert space $V$. For such purposes, the pointwise convergence of distributions described above is often too weak. Thus one would like to use stronger forms of convergence.

In this section we shall consider only the case where $V$ is a separable Hilbert space and investigate a mode of convergence describable as follows.

Let $K$ be a compact convex symmetric subset of $V$. Let $\mathscr{W}$ be the dual of $V$. The restrictions to $K$ of the elements of $\mathscr{W}$ form a linear subspace of the space $C(K)$ of continuous real valued functions in $K$. This space $C(K)$ will be topologized by its uniform norm. The embedding of $\mathscr{W}$ in $C(K)$ is a linear map which will be called $T_{K}$. The transpose of $T_{K}$ maps the dual $C^{*}(K)$ of $C(K)$ into $V$ according to the diagram

$$
\begin{gathered}
\mathscr{W} \xrightarrow{T_{K}} C(K) \\
V \stackrel{T_{K}}{\leftarrow} C^{*}(K) .
\end{gathered}
$$

Thus, the linear processes $v \mapsto\left\langle v, Z_{n}\right\rangle$ can be transported to $C^{*}(K)$ by writing $\left\langle\mu, T_{K} Z_{n}\right\rangle=\left\langle\mu T_{K}, Z_{n}\right\rangle$ for each $\mu \in C^{*}(K)$. If $F_{n}$ is the cylinder measure carried by $\mathscr{W}$ which describes the distribution of $Z_{n}$, then its images $T_{K} F_{n}$ by $T_{K}$ describes the distribution of $T_{K} Z_{n}$ :

Given such a compact $K$, or a countable family of such compacts we shall seek to modify $Z_{n}$ so that the distributions $\mathscr{L}\left(T_{K} Z_{n}\right)$ converge. However, the method of modification used here will not allow construction of modifications for arbitrary compacts $K$. In fact, the only compacts it can handle are those which were called Gaussian Continuity sets (G.C. for short) by Dudley. The method may be more valuable in that it replaces the infinite dimensional space $V$ by finite dimensional approximations without introducing too gross a disturbance on the experiments $\mathscr{E}_{n}$.

To proceed, let $\left\{v_{j} ; j=1,2, \ldots\right\}$ be an orthonormal basis for the separable Hilbert space $V$. Let $\Pi_{m}$ be the orthogonal projection of $V$ onto the subspace $V_{m}$ spanned by the vectors $v_{j}, j \leq m$.

If $\mathscr{E}_{n}=\left\{P_{v, n} ; v \in V\right\}$ is an experiment indexed by $V$, let $\mathscr{E}_{n}^{m}$ be the experiment $\mathscr{E}_{n}^{m}=\left\{P_{v, n}^{m} ; v \in V\right\}$ where $P_{v, n}^{m}$ is the measure assigned to $\Pi_{m} v$ in the experiment $\mathscr{E}_{n}$. If $K$ is any subset of $V$ and $\mathscr{E}$ and $\mathscr{E}^{\prime}$ are experiments indexed by $V$, let $\Delta_{K}\left(\mathscr{E}, \mathscr{E}^{\prime}\right)$ be the distance between their restrictions to $K$.

Lemma 6. Assume that $\mathscr{E}_{n}$ converges to $\mathscr{G}$ uniformly on the precompact subsets of $V$. Then as $m$ and $n$ both tend to infinity in any arbitrary manner the distances $\Delta_{K}\left(\mathscr{E}_{n}^{m}, \mathscr{G}\right), \Delta_{K}\left(\mathscr{G}^{m}, \mathscr{G}\right), \Delta_{K}\left(\mathscr{E}_{n}^{m}, \mathscr{E}_{n}\right)$ all tend to zero for every precompact subset $K$ of $V$.

Proof. Consider first the pair ( $\mathscr{G}^{m}, \mathscr{G}$ ). If $K$ is a compact subset of $V$ then $\sup _{v}\left\{\left\|\Pi_{m} v-v\right\|, v \in K\right\}$ tends to zero as $m \rightarrow \infty$. However, for any pair ( $u, v )$ of elements of $V$ one has $\left\|G_{u}-G_{v}\right\| \leq 2\|u-v\|$. Thus $\Delta_{K}\left(\mathscr{G}^{m}, \mathscr{G}\right)$ will tend to
zero for every compact set $K$. Note also that for any $K$ one has $\Delta_{K}\left(\mathscr{E}_{n}^{m}, \mathscr{G}^{m}\right) \leq \Delta_{K}\left(\mathscr{E}_{n}, \mathscr{G}\right)$, so that all the other assertions are obtainable by application of the triangular inequality.

To go further, let $Z_{n}$ be the linear process constructed for Proposition 1, Section 2. Let $\mu_{n}^{m}$ be the distribution of $\Pi_{m} Z_{n}$ for the measure $P_{0 . n}$. Here $\Pi_{m} Z_{n}$ is defined by the transposition $\left\langle v, \Pi_{m} Z_{n}\right\rangle=\left\langle\Pi_{m} v, Z_{n}\right\rangle$, but it may be



considered a random vector with values in $W_{m}=\Pi_{m} \mathscr{W}$, or by identification, in $V_{m}$ itself.

Metrize the space of distributions on $W_{m}$ by the dual Lipschitz norm $\|\mu-v\|_{D}$ induced by the Hilbert norm of $\mathscr{W}$. Letting $Z$ be the standard Gaussian linear process of $V$, let $v^{m}$ be the distribution of $\Pi_{m} Z$.

For each integer $m$ the distances $\left\|\mu_{n}^{m}-v^{m}\right\|_{D}$ tend to zero. Therefore there is a sequence $m(n)$, such that $m(n) \rightarrow \infty$ and such that $\left\|\mu_{n}^{m(n)}-v^{m(n)}\right\|_{D}$ also tend to zero as $n \rightarrow \infty$.

To proceed we shall assume that such a sequence $m(n)$ has been selected and let $\Pi^{n}=\Pi_{m(n)}, \mu^{n}=\mu_{n}^{m(n)}, v_{n}=v^{m(n)}, Z^{n}=\Pi^{n} Z_{n}$, and $\mathscr{F}_{n}=\mathscr{E}_{n}^{m(n)}$. Recall that

$$
L_{n}(v)=\log \frac{d P_{v, n}}{d P_{0, n}}+\frac{1}{2}\|v\|^{2}
$$

Recall also that a subset $K$ of $V$ is called a Gaussian continuity set (or a G.C.) if the standard Gaussian process $Z$ of $V$ admits a version whose restriction to $K$ has continuous paths. It is a theorem of Dudley that bounded convex G.C. sets are precompact. This leads to the following result.

Proposition 3. Assume that $\mathscr{E}_{n} \rightarrow \mathscr{G}$ uniformly on compact sets. Let $\mathscr{K}$ be the family of compact convex symmetric G.C. sets of V. For each $K \in \mathscr{K}$ let $T_{K}$ be the natural embedding of $\mathscr{W}$ into $C(K)$. Give each $C(K)$ its uniform norm. Then for the processes $Z^{n}$ constructed above,
(i) $L_{n}(v)-\left\langle v, Z^{n}\right\rangle \rightarrow 0$ in probability uniformly for $v$ in any precompact subset of $V$;
(ii) $\Delta_{K}\left(\mathscr{F}_{n}, \mathscr{E}_{n}\right) \rightarrow 0$ for every precompact subset $K \subset V$;
(iii) $\mathscr{L}\left[T_{K} Z^{n} \mid P_{0, n}\right]$ converges on $C(K)$ to $\mathscr{L}\left(T_{K} Z\right)$ for every $K \in \mathscr{K}$.

Proof. The first two assertions have already been proven. For the third assertion, let $K$ be an element of $\mathscr{K}$. There is some number $b$ such that $\|\boldsymbol{v}\| \leq b$ for all $v \in K$. Thus the semi-norm $\rho_{K}(w)=\sup \{|\langle v, w\rangle| ; v \in K\}$ is smaller than $b\|w\|$. It follows that the dual Lipschitz distance

$$
\left\|\mathscr{L}\left[T_{K} Z^{n} \mid P_{0, n}\right]-\mathscr{L}\left(T_{K} \Pi^{n} Z\right)\right\|_{D}
$$
