is smaller than a certain fixed multiple of the distance $\left\|\mu^{n}-v_{n}\right\|_{D}$ computed for the Hilbert norm. Thus, to prove the assertion that it will be sufficient to show that $\mathscr{L}\left[T_{K} \Pi^{n} Z\right]$ converges to $\mathscr{L}\left(T_{K} Z\right)$.

Now one can write $Z=\Pi^{n} Z+\left[\left(I-\Pi^{n}\right) Z\right]$. On the right side the two terms of the sum are independent Gaussian linear processes. Thus $T_{K} Z= \left(T_{K} \Pi^{n} Z\right)+T_{K}\left[\left(1-\Pi^{n}\right) Z\right]$ is also a sum of two independent Gaussian variables with values in $C(K)$. It follows that the family of distributions $\mathscr{L}\left[T_{K} \Pi^{n} Z\right]$; $n=1,2, \ldots$ is shift compact and therefore tight, since they are symmetric distributions. Convergence of the finite dimensional distributions is already acquired. Thus $\mathscr{L}\left(T_{K} \Pi^{n} Z\right)$ tends to $\mathscr{L}\left(T_{K} Z\right)$ for the distance induced by the uniform norm of $C(K)$. This completes the proof of the proposition.

Passage to spaces such as the $C(K)$ spaces used here is a common procedure in statistical problems. For instance, on the line one often uses the fact that the normalized difference between an empirical cumulative and the true cumulative distribution converges to the Brownian bridge. The operation corresponding to our $T_{K}$ is then the operation of taking an indefinite integral.

In this case the linear process $Z_{n}$ occurs in a natural manner. The intent of Propositions 1 and 3 is that, if convenient, one may look for such processes, even if they are not given beforehand.

However, note that the technique used for the construction of the processes $Z^{n}$ of Proposition 3 has severe limitations. As can be seen from the proof itself, it cannot yield convergence except for the sets $K$ which are G.C. sets.

For instance, if all the $\mathscr{E}_{n}$ were exactly equivalent to the Gaussian experiment $\mathscr{G}$, there would be processes $Z_{n}$ such that $\mathscr{L}\left(Z_{n}\right)=\mathscr{L}(Z)$, but the modification $Z^{n}$ would have distributions which converge only on G.C. sets. At the present time no other construction techniques are available.

## 5. An Application of a Martingale Limit Theorem

The occurrence of asymptotic normality is often linked to "Central Limit Theorems" for sums of independent or loosely dependent random variables. In this section we give an example of a theorem of this nature, based on martingale theory. Several of the lemmas given here will also be used repeatedly in Chapters 16 and 17 which consider the case of independent or independent identically distributed variables, possibly with random stopping times.

The central limit theorem for martingales, in its basic form, is contained in the 1935 work of Paul Lévy. The treatment given below takes advantage of the special nature of likelihood ratios as random variables. It was also influenced by the work of McLeish (1975).

Consider a sequence $\left\{\mathscr{E}_{n}\right\}$ of binary experiments $\mathscr{E}_{n}=\left(P_{0, n}, P_{1, n}\right)$ where the $P_{i, n}$ are measures on certain spaces $\left(\mathscr{X}_{n}, \mathscr{A}_{n}\right)$. It frequently happens that in addition to $\mathscr{A}_{n}$ one is provided with a sequence, say $\mathscr{A}_{n, k}, k=1,2, \ldots, k_{n}$ of sub- $\sigma$-fields of $\mathscr{A}_{n}$ such that $\mathscr{A}_{n, k} \subset \mathscr{A}_{n, k+1}$ and such that $\mathscr{A}_{n}$ is the last $\mathscr{A}_{n, k}$ with $k=k_{n}$. We shall take for $\mathscr{A}_{n, 0}$ the trivial $\sigma$-field consisting of $\varnothing$ and $\mathscr{X}_{n}$.

This situation occurs naturally if observations are taken sequentially in time, but it can also occur in other contexts.

Let $P_{i, n, k}$ be the restriction of $P_{i, n}$ to the $\sigma$-field $\mathscr{A}_{n, k}$ and let $\gamma_{n, k}$ be the Radon-Nikodym density, taken on $\mathscr{A}_{n, k}$, of the part of $P_{1, n, k}$ that is dominated by $P_{0, n, k}$. Denote conditional expectations for $P_{0, n}$ given the $\sigma$-field $\mathscr{A}_{n, k}$ by $E_{k}$. Then the $\gamma_{n, k}$ are such that $\gamma_{n, k} \geq 0$ and $E_{k} \gamma_{n, k+1} \leq \gamma_{n, k}$. We shall denote $f_{n, k}$ the ratio $f_{n, k}=\gamma_{n, k} / \gamma_{n, k-1}$ taking $\gamma_{n, k-1}=1$ if $k-1=0$. The logarithm of likelihood ratio

$$
\Lambda_{n}=\log \frac{d P_{1, n}}{d P_{0, n}}
$$

taken on $\mathscr{A}_{n}$ is then $\Lambda_{n}=\sum_{k} \log f_{n, k}$.

The basic conditions to be imposed here on the $f_{n, k}$ are as follows.
(A) As $n \rightarrow \infty$, the variables $\max _{k}\left|f_{n, k}-1\right|$ tend to zero in $P_{0, n}$ probability.
(B) The variables $\sum_{k}\left|f_{n, k}-1\right|^{2}$ remain bounded in $P_{0, n}$ probability.

Further conditions will be imposed later on. Also, we shall consider conditions ( $\mathrm{A}_{1}$ ), ( $\mathrm{B}_{1}$ ), similar to (A) and (B) but where $P_{0, n}$ is replaced by $P_{1, n}$, so that for instance ( $\mathrm{A}_{1}$ ) says
$\left(\mathrm{A}_{1}\right)$ As $n \rightarrow \infty$, the variables $\max _{k}\left|f_{n, k}-1\right|$ tend to zero in $P_{1, n}$ probability. These conditions are imitated from the analogous conditions used in the study of independent observations. Our first lemma allows the replacement of the sums $\sum \log f_{n, k}$ by the sums $\sum\left(f_{n, k}-1\right)$. The argument will be encountered many times in later chapters. For this reason it is given in a fairly general form.

Definition 1. Let $\phi$ be a real function defined on the real line. It will be called a function of class $P D(0,2)$ if
(i) $\phi$ is measurable,
(ii) $\phi(0)=0$, and
(iii) there are numbers $a$ and $b$ such that

$$
\lim _{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{1}{x^{2}}\left[\phi(x)-a x-\frac{b}{2} x^{2}\right]=0
$$

(The notation $P D(0,2)$ is a summary of Peano differentiable of order 2 at the point zero, with $\phi(0)=0$.)

Lemma 1. Let $\left\{Y_{n, k}\right\}$ be an arbitrary double array of random variables. Assume that
(i) $\max _{k}\left|Y_{n, k}\right| \rightarrow 0$ in probability,
(ii) $W_{n}=\sum_{k} Y_{n, k}^{2}$ is bounded in probability.

Let $V_{n}=\sum Y_{n, k}$ and let $\phi$ be a function of class $P D(0,2)$ for first and second Peano derivatives $a$ and $b$. Then $\sum_{k} \phi\left(Y_{n, k}\right)-\left[a V_{n}+(b / 2) W_{n}\right]$ tends to zero in probability.

Proof. Write $\phi(y)=a y+(b / 2) y^{2}+y^{2} R(y)$ for a remainder $R(y)$ which tends to zero if $y \rightarrow 0$. Then, the difference in the conclusion is the sum $\left|\sum_{k} Y_{n, k}^{2} R\left(Y_{n, k}\right)\right| \leq W_{n} \max _{k}\left|R\left(Y_{n, k}\right)\right|$. This tends to zero by (i) and (ii). Hence the result.

The next lemma uses standard martingale arguments to obtain information on the behavior of $\Lambda_{n}$, or equivalently $V_{n}-\frac{1}{2} W_{n}^{2}$ with $V_{n}=\sum_{j}\left(f_{n, j}-1\right)$ and $W_{n}^{2}=\sum_{j}\left(f_{n, j}-1\right)^{2}$. Let $Z_{n, k}=\left(f_{n, k}-1\right) I\left[f_{n, k}<2\right]$. Note that, under $P_{0, n}, E_{k-1} Z_{n, k}$ is negative. Thus we shall let $a_{n, k}=-E_{k-1} Z_{n, k}$ and $s_{n, k}^{2}= E_{k-1} X_{n, k}^{2}$, with $X_{n, k}=Z_{n, k}+a_{n, k}$.

Lemma 2. Assume that (A) and (B) hold. Then $\sum_{k} X_{n, k}$ remains bounded in $P_{0, n}$-probability and $\sum_{j}\left(f_{n, j}-1\right)$ remains bounded in $P_{0, n}$-probability if and only if $\sum_{k} a_{n, k}$ does.

Proof. According to (A) the differences $\sum\left(f_{n, j}-1\right)-\sum Z_{n, j}$ and $\sum\left(f_{n, j}-1\right)^{2}- \sum Z_{n, j}^{2}$ tend to zero in probability. Thus $\sum_{j} Z_{n, j}^{2}$ is bounded in probability. Take a number $b \in(1, \infty)$ and let $v$ be the first integer $k$ such that $\sum_{j \leq k} Z_{n, j}^{2} \geq b$. Then $\sum_{j \leq \nu} Z_{n, j}^{2} \leq b+1$ since $\left|Z_{n, j}\right| \leq 1$ for all $j$. The variables
$X_{n, j}=Z_{n, j}+a_{n, j}$ are martingale differences. Let $X_{n, j}(v)=X_{n, j} I(v \geq j)$. Taking into account that $\{v \geq j\}$ belongs to $\mathscr{A}_{j-1}$, one sees that the $X_{n, j}(v)$ are also martingale differences. One may write

$$
\begin{aligned}
X_{n, j}^{2}(v) & =\left(Y_{n, j}(v)-Y_{n, j-1}(v)\right)^{2} \\
& =Y_{n, j}^{2}(v)-Y_{n, j-1}^{2}(v)-2 Y_{n, j-1}(v) X_{n, j}(v)
\end{aligned}
$$

for $Y_{n, j}(v)=\sum_{k \leq j} X_{n, k}(v)$. This gives

$$
\begin{aligned}
E\left|\sum X_{n, j}(v)\right|^{2} & =\sum_{j} E X_{n, j}^{2}(v) \\
& =E \sum_{j} E_{j-1} X_{n, j}^{2}(v) \\
& \leq E \sum_{j} E_{j-1} Z_{n, j}^{2} I(v \geq j) \\
& \leq b+1
\end{aligned}
$$

It follows that

$$
P_{0, n}\left\{\left|\sum_{k} X_{n, k}\right|^{2} \geq \varepsilon^{-1}(b+1)\right\} \leq \varepsilon+P_{0, n}\left\{\sum_{k} Z_{n, k}^{2} \geq b\right\}
$$

Therefore, under (A) and (B), the sum $\sum_{k} X_{n, k}$ remains bounded in $P_{0, n}$ probability. The sum $\sum_{k} Z_{n, k}$ will do the same if and only if $\sum_{k} a_{n, k}$ remains bounded in $P_{0, n}$ probability.

Remark. The same argument shows that if any one of the variables $\sup _{k}\left|\sum_{j \leq k} X_{n, j}\right|, \sum_{j} X_{n, j}^{2}$ or $\sum_{j} s_{n, j}^{2}$ stays bounded in probability so do the other two.

Corollary. If (A) and (B) hold, then the sequence $\left\{P_{0, n}\right\}$ is contiguous to $\left\{P_{1, n}\right\}$ if and only if $\sum_{k} a_{n, k}$ stays bounded in $P_{0, n}$ probability.

Indeed, for $b<0$,

$$
P_{1, n}\left\{\Lambda_{n} \leq b\right\} \leq e^{b} P_{0, n}\left[\Lambda_{n} \leq b\right] \leq P_{0, n}\left[\Lambda_{n} \leq b\right]
$$

A similar argument can be carried out if (A) and (B) are replaced by ( $\mathrm{A}_{1}$ ) and ( $B_{1}$ ). It gives the following assertion.

Lemma 3. Let ( $\mathrm{A}_{1}$ ) and ( $\mathrm{B}_{1}$ ) be satisfied; then $\Lambda_{n}$ remains bounded in $P_{1, n}$ probability if and only if $\sum_{j} a_{n, j}$ does.

Proof. Let $E_{k-1}^{1}$ represent a conditional expectation given $\mathscr{A}_{k-1}$ for the measure $P_{1, n}$. Let $b_{n, k}=E_{k-1}^{1} Z_{n, k}$. Direct computation shows that $b_{n, k}= s_{n, k}^{2}+a_{n, k}^{2}-a_{n, k}$.

Let $v$ be the first integer $k$ such that $Z_{n, k} \leq-1 / 2$. According to the argument carried out for Lemma 2, the sums $\sum_{k \leq v}\left(Z_{n, k}-b_{n, k}\right)$ and $\sum_{k \leq v} E_{k-1}^{1}\left(Z_{n, k}-b_{n, k}\right)^{2}$ remain bounded in probability. However,

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{k-1}^{1}\left(Z_{n, k}-b_{n, k}\right)^{2} & =E_{k-1}\left(Z_{n, k}-b_{n, k}\right)^{2}\left(Z_{n, k}+1\right) \\
& \geq \frac{1}{2} E_{k-1}\left(Z_{n, k}-b_{n, k}\right)^{2} \\
& =\frac{1}{2}\left[s_{n, k}^{2}+\left(s_{n, k}^{2}+a_{n, k}^{2}\right)^{-2}\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

for all $k<v$. It follows that $\sum s_{n, k}^{2}$ and $\sum a_{n, k}^{4}$ are bounded in $P_{1, n}$ probability. Thus $\sum Z_{n, k}$ is bounded in $P_{1, n}$ probability only if $\sum a_{n, k}\left(1-a_{n, k}\right)$ or equivalently $\sum a_{n, k}$ remains bounded in $P_{1, n}$ probability.

Note that the expectation $a_{n, k}=-E_{k-1}\left(f_{n, k}-1\right) I\left(f_{n, k}<2\right)$ can also be written

$$
a_{n, k}=E_{k-1}\left(f_{n, k}-1\right) I\left(f_{n, k} \geq 2\right)+E_{k-1}\left(1-f_{n, k}\right)
$$

Let $f_{n, k}=\infty$ on the part of the space where the conditional distributions given $\mathscr{A}_{k-1}$ for $P_{1, n}$ are singular with respect to those relative to $P_{0, n}$. Then the sum

$$
C_{n, k}=E_{k-1} f_{n, k} I\left(f_{n, k} \geq 2\right)+E_{k-1}\left(1-f_{n, k}\right)
$$

represents the conditional probability for $P_{1, n}$ that $f_{n, k} \geq 2$. It is possible to show that if (A) and (B) hold and if $\left\{P_{0, n}\right\}$ and $\left\{P_{1, n}\right\}$ are contiguous then $\sum_{k} C_{n, k}$ must tend to zero in $P_{0, n}$ and $P_{1, n}$ probability.

Indeed, let $U_{n, k}=I\left[f_{n, k} \geq 2\right]$. Take a $b \in(1, \infty)$ and let $v$ be the first $k$ such that $\sum_{j \leq k} U_{n, j} \geq b$. Then, according to (A), $E \sum_{j \leq \nu} U_{n, j}$ tends to zero in $P_{0, n}$ probability, hence also in $P_{1, n}$ probability. It follows that $\sum_{j \leq \nu} C_{n, j}$ tends to zero in probability. However, according to (B), given $\varepsilon>0$ one can choose $b$ so that $P_{1, n}\left[\sum_{k} C_{n, k} \neq \sum_{j \leq \nu} C_{n, j}\right]<\varepsilon$.

In view of this we shall consider below the following two additional condition:
(C) $\sum_{k} E_{k-1}\left(1-f_{n, k}\right)$ tends to zero in $P_{0, n}$ probability.
(D) $\sum_{k} E_{k-1}\left(f_{n, k}-1\right) I\left(f_{n, k} \geq 2\right)$ tends to zero in $P_{0, n}$ probability.

Remark. The combination (A)-(D) does not imply contiguity of the sequences $\left\{P_{0, n}\right\},\left\{P_{1, n}\right\}$. An example can be constructed using a birth and death process that is critical or slightly subcritical for $P_{0, n}$ but slightly supercritical for $P_{1, n}$. However, it will be a consequence of the arguments given below that (A)-(D) implies contiguity of $\left\{P_{0, n}\right\}$ and $\left\{P_{1, n}\right\}$ if either $\sum_{j}\left(f_{n, j}-1\right)^{2} \leq b$ for some fixed constant $b<\infty$, or if $\sum_{j}\left(f_{n, j}-1\right)^{2}$ converges in $P_{0, n}$ probability to a constant $\sigma^{2}<\infty$.

To proceed let $Z_{n, k}=\left(f_{n, k}-1\right) I\left(f_{n, k}<2\right)$ as before and let $a_{n, k}=-E_{k-1} Z_{n, k}$ and $X_{n, k}=Z_{n, k}+a_{n, k}$.

Lemma 4. Let (A), (B), (C), and (D) be satisfied. Then the sums $\sum a_{n, k}, \sum a_{n, k}^{2}$, $\sum\left[\left(f_{n, k}-1\right)-X_{n, k}\right], \sum_{k}\left[\left(f_{n, k}-1\right)^{2}-X_{n, k}^{2}\right]$, and $\sum\left[X_{n, k}^{2}-s_{n, k}^{2}\right]$ all tend to zero in $P_{0, n}$ probability.

Proof. Since $a_{n, k} \geq 0$, the convergence of $\sum a_{n, k}$ to zero implies that $\sum a_{n, k}^{2} \rightarrow 0$. That $\sum\left(f_{n, j}-1\right)-\sum X_{n, k} \rightarrow 0$ follows from (A) and $\sum a_{n, k} \rightarrow 0$.

Consider the sums $\sum\left[Z_{n, k}^{2}-X_{n, k}^{2}\right]$ and write $Z_{n, k}^{2}-X_{n, k}^{2}=-2 a_{n, k} Z_{n, k}-a_{n, k}^{2}$. Schwarz inequality shows that $\sum\left[Z_{n, k}^{2}-X_{n, k}^{2}\right]$ and therefore $\sum_{k}\left[\left(f_{n, k}-1\right)^{2}-X_{n, k}^{2}\right]$ tends to zero. For the last sum, assume first that $\sum X_{n, k}^{2} \leq b<\infty$. Then $E\left\{\sum\left[X_{n, k}^{2}-s_{n, k}^{2}\right]\right\}^{2}=E \sum_{k}\left[X_{n, k}^{2}-s_{n, k}^{2}\right]^{2}$. However $\sum_{k}\left[X_{n, k}^{2}-s_{n, k}^{2}\right]^{2} \leq \left\{\sum_{k}\left|X_{n, k}^{2}-s_{n, k}^{2}\right|\right\} \max _{j}\left|X_{n, j}^{2}-s_{n, j}^{2}\right|$ so that $E\left|\sum\left[X_{n, k}^{2}-s_{n, k}^{2}\right]\right|^{2}$ tends to zero. The general result follows by using a stopping time as in Lemma 3.

To obtain further information it is convenient to introduce experiments $\mathscr{F}_{n}= \left\{Q_{t, n} ; t \in[0,1]\right\}$ such that the density of $Q_{t, n}$ with respect to $P_{0, n}$ is $q_{t, n}= \prod_{k}\left[1+t X_{n, k}\right]$.

Lemma 5. Let (A), (B), (C), and (D) hold. Then the distance $\Delta\left[\left(P_{0, n}, P_{1, n}\right)\right.$, $\left.\left(Q_{0, n}, Q_{1, n}\right)\right]$ tends to zero. The sequences $\left\{P_{0, n}\right\}$ and $\left\{P_{1, n}\right\}$ are contiguous if and only if $\sum_{j} X_{n, j}^{2}$ remains bounded in $Q_{1, n}$ probability.

Proof. According to Lemma 1 and Lemma 4, the differences

$$
\log q_{t, n}-t \sum_{k} X_{n, k}+\frac{t^{2}}{2} \sum X_{n, k}^{2}
$$

and

$$
\Lambda_{n}-t \sum X_{n, k}+\frac{t^{2}}{2} \sum X_{n, k}^{2}
$$

tend to zero in $P_{0, n}$ probability. This implies the first assertion. We already know, by Lemma 2, that $\left\{P_{0, n}\right\}=\left\{Q_{0, n}\right\}$ is contiguous to $\left\{Q_{1, n}\right\}$, hence to $\left\{P_{1, n}\right\}$ by the first assertion. In the other direction take any $s \geq 0$ and let $y \mapsto \int_{0}^{s}(s-\xi) e^{\xi y} d \xi=y^{-2}\left(e^{s y}-1-s y\right)$. This is an increasing function of $y$. In our case $X_{n, k} \leq 2$. Therefore

$$
e^{s X_{n . k}}-1-s X_{n, k} \leq X_{n, k}^{2} \frac{1}{4}\left(e^{2 s}-1-2 s\right)
$$

Taking expectations, one sees that

$$
E_{k-1} e^{s X_{n, k}} \leq \gamma(s) s_{n, k}^{2} \leq e^{\gamma(s) s_{n, k}^{2}}
$$

where $\gamma(s)=\frac{1}{4}\left[e^{2 s}-1-2 s\right]$. It follows that $\exp \left\{s \sum_{j \leq k} X_{n, j}-\gamma(s) \sum_{j \leq k} s_{n, j}^{2}\right\}$ is a supermartingale and that

$$
E \exp \left\{s \sum_{k} X_{n, k}-\gamma(s) \sum_{k} s_{n, k}^{2}\right\} \leq 1
$$

If $\sum_{k} X_{n, k}^{2} \leq b<\infty$, this means that $E \exp \left\{s \sum_{k} X_{n, k}\right\} \leq \exp \{\gamma(s) b\}$. Note also that for $t \in[0,1]$ one has $q_{t, n} \leq \exp \left\{t \sum_{k} X_{n, k}\right\}$. Thus, the $q_{t, n}$ are uniformly integrable for $P_{0, n}$ and $\left\{Q_{t, n}\right\}$ is contiguous to $P_{0, n}$. If it is only known that $\sum_{k} X_{n, k}^{2}$ is bounded in $Q_{1, n}$ probability, take an $\varepsilon>0$ and a $b$ such that
$Q_{1, n}\left\{\sum_{k} X_{n, k}^{2} \geq b\right\}<\varepsilon$. Let $v$ be the first $k$ such that $\sum_{j \leq k} X_{n, j}^{2} \geq b$, and let $Q_{1, n}^{\varepsilon}$ be the measure whose density with respect to $P_{0, n}$ is $\prod_{j \leq \nu}\left(1+X_{n, j}\right)$. Then $\left\|Q_{1, n}^{\varepsilon}-Q_{1, n}\right\|<2 \varepsilon$, and the preceding argument is applicable to $Q_{1, n}^{\varepsilon}$ and the result follows.

Remark. When $\left\{P_{0, n}\right\}$ and $\left\{P_{1, n}\right\}$ are contiguous the convergence of $\Delta\left[\left(P_{0, n}, P_{1, n}\right),\left(Q_{0, n}, Q_{1, n}\right)\right]$ to zero implies that $\left\|P_{1, n}-Q_{1, n}\right\| \rightarrow 0$. This conclusion need not remain true if the contiguity condition is violated.

We shall consider presently a special case where, under the combination (A)-(D), a more precise result is obtainable.

Theorem 1. Let conditions (A)-(D) be satisfied. Assume in addition that $\sum\left(f_{n, j}-1\right)^{2}$ converges in $P_{0, n}$ probability to a constant $\sigma^{2}$. Then the experiments $\left\{P_{0, n}, P_{1, n}\right\}$ are asymptotically Gaussian, with $\mathscr{L}\left\{\sum\left(f_{n, j}-1\right) \mid P_{0, n}\right\}$ tending to $\mathscr{N}\left(-\frac{1}{2} \sigma^{2}, \sigma^{2}\right)$.

Proof. Let $v$ be the first $k$ if any such that $\sum_{j \leq k} X_{n, j}^{2} \geq \sigma^{2}+1$. Otherwise, let
$v$ be the upper bound $k_{n}$ of the range of $j$. Then $V_{v, n}=\sum_{j \leq v} X_{n, j}$ and $V_{n}=\sum_{k} X_{n, k}$ differ only in cases whose $P_{0, n}$ probability tends to zero. Let $W_{v, n}^{2}=\sum_{j \leq v} X_{n, j}^{2}$. Then according to Lemma 5, if along some subsequence $\mathscr{L}\left(V_{v, n}, W_{v, n}^{2}\right)$ tends to a limit $\mathscr{L}\left(V, W^{2}\right)$, one has $E \exp \left[t V-\frac{1}{2} t^{2} W^{2}\right]=1$. Hence the result.

The foregoing argument relies heavily on the fact that if

$$
E\left\{\exp \left[t V-\frac{t^{2}}{2} W^{2}\right]\right\}=1
$$

for a nonrandom $W$ then

$$
E \exp \{t V\}=\exp \left\{\frac{t^{2}}{2} W^{2}\right\}
$$

and $V$ is therefore $\mathscr{N}\left(0, W^{2}\right)$. One may carry out the argument somewhat further and obtain additional information.

Lemma 6. Assume that (A), (B), (C), and (D) hold and assume in addition that $W_{n}^{2}=\sum X_{n, j}^{2} \leq b<\infty$. Then for any fixed $k$ and any sequence $\left\{g_{n}\right\}$ of $\mathscr{A}_{n, k}$ - measurable functions such that $\left|g_{n}\right| \leq 1$, the difference

$$
E g_{n} \exp \left\{t V_{n}-\frac{t^{2}}{2} W_{n}^{2}\right\}-E g_{n}
$$

tends to zero.
Proof. Let $E_{k}^{t}$ be a conditional expectation given $\mathscr{A}_{n, k}$ for the measures $Q_{t, n}$. Then $E^{t} g_{n}=E_{k}^{t} g_{n}=E g_{n} \prod_{j \leq k}\left(1+t X_{n, j}\right)$. Since $E\left|\prod_{j \leq k}\left(1+t X_{n, j}\right)-1\right| \rightarrow$ 0 , the difference $E^{t} g_{n}-E g_{n}$ tends to zero. The difference $q_{t, n}-\exp \left\{t V_{n}-\frac{1}{2} t^{2} W_{n}^{2}\right\}$ tends to zero in $P_{0, n}$ probability. According to Lemma 5, $E \exp \left\{s V_{n}\right\} \leq \exp \{\gamma(s) b\}$ for all $s \geq 0$. Thus $E\left|q_{t, n}-\exp \left\{t V_{n}-\frac{1}{2} t^{2} W_{n}^{2}\right\}\right| \rightarrow 0$ and so does $E q_{t, n} g_{n}-E g_{n} \exp \left\{t V_{n}-\frac{1}{2} t^{2} W_{n}^{2}\right\}$. Hence the result.

Definition. Let $\mathscr{G}=\left\{G_{t} ; t \in T\right\}$ be an experiment indexed by a set $T \subset \mathbb{R}$ with $0 \in T$. Such a $\mathscr{G}$ is called mixed normal if

$$
d G_{t}=\exp \left\{t \xi W-\frac{t^{2}}{2} W^{2}\right\} d G_{0}
$$

where $\xi$ is $\mathscr{N}(0,1)$ independent of $W$.
If in Lemma 6 one could take $g_{n}=\exp \left\{\frac{1}{2} t^{2} W_{n}^{2}\right\}$ it would follow that the joint distribution $\mathscr{L}\left(V_{n}, W_{n}^{2}\right)$ would be approximable by $\mathscr{L}\left(\xi W_{n}, W_{n}^{2}\right)$ and that $\left\{Q_{t, n} ; t \in[0,1]\right\}$ would be "asymptotically mixed normal." However, in this Lemma, $g_{n}$ is supposed to be $\mathscr{A}_{n, k}$-measurable for a fixed $k$. One can get the same asymptotic mixed normality if the $W_{n}^{2}$ can be approximated arbitrarily closely by $\mathscr{A}_{n, k}$ measurable functions. A case where this is possible occurs in the study of stochastic processes as follows.

Condition (S). The system $\left\{\mathscr{A}_{n, k} ; k=1, \ldots, k_{n}, P_{0, n}\right\}$ satisfies Condition ( $S$ ) if there are spaces ( $\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{A}$ ) independent of $n, \sigma$-fields $\mathscr{B}_{k}$ independent of $n$ such that $\mathscr{A}_{n, k}=\mathscr{B}_{k}$ for $n$ sufficiently large such that $P_{0, n}$ is the restriction to $\mathscr{A}_{n}=\mathscr{A}_{n, k_{n}} \subset \mathscr{A}$ of a fixed measure $P_{0}$ on $\mathscr{A}$.

Theorem 2. Let the conditions (A), (B), (C), (D), and (S) be satisfied. Assume in addition that the sums $W_{n}^{2}=\sum_{j} X_{n, j}^{2}$ converge in $P_{0}$-probability to a limit $W^{2}$. Then the experiments ( $P_{0, n}, P_{1, n}$ ) and $\left\{Q_{t, n} ; t \in[0,1]\right\}$ are asymptotically mixed normal. The distributions $\mathscr{L}\left(V_{n}, W_{n}^{2}\right)$ converge to $\mathscr{L}\left(\xi W, W^{2}\right)$ where $\xi$ is $\mathscr{N}(0,1)$ independent of $W$.

Proof. Consider first the case where $W_{n}^{2} \leq b<\infty$. Then according to Lemma 6 and according to the assumption that $W_{n} \rightarrow W$ in probability, the differences

$$
E g \exp \left\{t V_{n}-\frac{t^{2}}{2} W^{2}\right\}-E g
$$

tend to zero for any fixed $k$ and any bounded $\mathscr{B}_{k}$-measurable function. However, for any $\varepsilon>0$ one can find a $k$ and a $\mathscr{B}_{k}$-measurable $g$ such that $E\left|g-\exp \left(t^{2} / 2\right) W^{2}\right|<\varepsilon$. Thus

$$
E \exp \left\{t V_{n}\right\}-E \exp \left\{\frac{t^{2}}{2} W^{2}\right\} \rightarrow 0
$$

Since $\exp \left(t^{2} / 2\right) W^{2}=E \exp \{t \xi W\}$, the result follows.
For the general case, select an $\varepsilon>0$ and a $b$ such that $P_{0, n}\left\{W^{2}>b^{2}\right\}<\varepsilon$. Let $v$ be the first index such that $W_{\varepsilon, n}^{2}=\sum_{j \leq v} X_{n, j}^{2} \geq b^{2}$. Then $W_{\varepsilon, n}^{2} \rightarrow W^{2} \wedge b^{2}$ and the preceding result applies. Thus if $V_{\varepsilon, n}=\sum_{j \leq \nu} X_{n, j}$, the difference between $\mathscr{L}\left(V_{\varepsilon, n}, W_{\varepsilon, n}^{2}\right)$ and $\mathscr{L}\left\{\xi(W \wedge b),\left(W^{2} \wedge b^{2}\right)\right\}$ tends to zero. However $\mathscr{L}\left(V_{\varepsilon, n}, W_{\varepsilon, n}^{2}\right)$ and $\mathscr{L}\left(V_{n}, W_{n}^{2}\right)$ differ by at most $2 \varepsilon$ for $n$ sufficiently large. The result follows.

In this theorem, the convergence in probability of $W_{n}$ to a limit $W$ necessitates the use of a fixed underlying probability space, while convergence of experiments does not rely on any such device. However it is clear that the
result will hold whenever the variables $V_{\varepsilon, n} W_{\varepsilon, n}^{-1}$ and $W_{\varepsilon, n}^{2}$ are asymptotically independent. We shall also encounter cases where the underlying spaces are fixed in the study of stochastic processes and in Chapter 17 for independent identically distributed observations. Let us note also that asymptotically mixed normal experiments occur naturally from the invariance conditions of Chapter 8.

Theorem 1 was stated for experiments ( $P_{0, n}, P_{1, n}$ ) which are only binary experiments. If one has experiments $\mathscr{E}_{n}=\left\{P_{v, n} ; v \in V\right\}$ indexed by a vector space $V$, Theorem 1 may be applied to all pairs ( $P_{0, n}, P_{v, n}$ ). However, alternate approaches may be feasible. To mention one of them, let $f_{n, k}(v)$ be the density computed for the pair ( $P_{0, n}, P_{v, n}$ ) as $f_{n, k}$ was for the previous pairs ( $P_{0, n}, P_{1, n}$ ), and for a given sequence $\left\{\mathscr{A}_{n, k}\right\}$ of $\sigma$-fields.

Proposition 4. Let $V$ be a Hilbert space with a norm noted $\|v\|$. Assume that for each $v \in V$ the following conditions hold:
(i) $\left\{P_{0, n}\right\}$ and $\left\{P_{v, n}\right\}$ are contiguous,
(ii) $\max _{k}\left|f_{n, k}(v)-1\right| \rightarrow 0$ in probability,
(iii) $\sum_{k}\left|f_{n, k}(v)-1\right|^{2} \rightarrow\|v\|^{2}$.

Then, in order that the experiments $\mathscr{E}_{n}=\left\{P_{v, n} ; v \in V\right\}$ converge weakly to the standard Gaussian shift experiment $\mathscr{G}$ of $(V,\|\cdot\|)$, it is necessary and sufficient that for all pairs $(u, v)$ of elements of $V$ the sums

$$
\sum_{k}\left\{\left[f_{n, k}(u+v)-1\right]-\left[f_{n, k}(u)+f_{n, k}(v)-2\right]\right\}
$$

converge in probability to zero.
Proof. According to Lemma 1 , the sum $\sum_{k} \log f_{n, k}(v)$ is asymptotically equivalent to $\sum_{k}\left[f_{n, k}(v)-1\right]-\frac{1}{2}\|v\|^{2}$. Thus the sum $\sum_{k}\left[f_{n, k}(v)-1\right]$ is equivalent to the variable called $L_{n}(v)$ in Section 2. The result follows.

In the last condition one could also replace the expressions of the type $f_{n, k}(v)-1$ by the corresponding expressions $\left(\sqrt{f_{n, k}(v)}-1\right)+\frac{1}{8}\|v\|^{2}$.

## 6. Asymptotic Admissibility and Minimaxity

In this section, $V$ will be a separable Hilbert space with a norm noted $\|v\|$. The sequence $\left\{\mathscr{E}_{n}\right\}, \mathscr{E}_{n}=\left\{P_{v, n} ; v \in V\right\}$ is a sequence of experiments indexed by $V$. It is assumed that the $\mathscr{E}_{n}$ converge weakly to the standard Gaussian experiment $\mathscr{G}=\left\{G_{v} ; v \in V\right\}$ of $V$.

If $V$ is finite dimensional, problems of estimation or test for the experiment $\mathscr{G}$ have given rise to an extensive literature, which cannot be summarized here. Thus, the entire section deals only with some of the features of the infinite dimensional situation which may not be so well-known.

The section is not an exercise in futility, since it does lead to a variety of assertions concerning Kolmogorov-Smirnov or Cramér-von Mises tests or similar procedures, as we shall see later.

To $\mathscr{G}$ corresponds a linear process $v \mapsto\langle v, Z\rangle$ which is a standard Gaussian linear process of $V$. It induces a cylindrical measure on the dual $\mathscr{W}$ of the

Hilbert space $V$. One can also represent this measure as a $\sigma$-additive measure on the algebraic dual $V^{*}$ of $V$.

In either situation this measure will be identified with the element $G_{0}$ of the family $\left\{G_{v} ; v \in V\right\}$. The type of the experiment is unaffected by the mode of representation used. For many purposes the two spaces $V$ and $\mathscr{W}$ may be identified. In fact there is a well defined linear isometry $J$ of $\mathscr{W}$ onto $V$ such that $\left\langle J w_{1}, w_{2}\right\rangle=\left\langle J w_{2}, w_{1}\right\rangle$ for all pairs of elements of $\mathscr{W}$. We shall often use this isometry, but as much as possible we shall keep the roles of $V$ and $\mathscr{W}$ separate.

Here, we shall begin by a brief investigation of some properties of the experiment $\mathscr{G}$ itself. Passage to properties of the sequence $\left\{\mathscr{E}_{n}\right\}$ will then be carried out through the use of the results of Chapter 7, Sections 4 and 5.

It is often convenient to replace the cylindrical measures $G_{v}$ carried by $V$, by other cylindrical measures, say $F_{v}$, carried by some other space, say $\mathscr{X}$.

One possibility is as follows. Let $\mathscr{X}$ be a locally convex topological space with dual $\mathscr{Y}$. Let $T$ be a continuous map from the Hilbert space $\mathscr{W}$ to $\mathscr{X}$. The duality relation $\langle y, T Z\rangle=\langle y T, Z\rangle$ for $y \in \mathscr{Y}, y T \in V$, transforms the linear process $Z$ into a Gaussian linear process defined on $\mathscr{Y}$.

If $v \in V$, the logarithm of likelihood ratio $\Lambda(v)=\log \left(d G_{v} / d G_{0}\right)$ has the form

$$
\Lambda(v)=\langle v, Z\rangle-\frac{1}{2}\|v\|^{2}
$$

By transfer to $\mathscr{X}$ and $\mathscr{Y}$, this means that if $v=y T$ then $\Lambda(v)$ can also be written

$$
\Lambda(v)=\langle y, T Z\rangle-\frac{1}{2}\|y T\|^{2}
$$

Thus if $v=y T$ and if $F_{y}$ is the image $F_{y}=T G_{v}$ of $G_{v}$, the logarithm of likelihood ratio $\log \left(d F_{y} / d F_{0}\right)$ is equal to the above expression.

In this manner, we have obtained a Gaussian shift experiment $\left\{F_{y} ; y \in \mathscr{Y}\right\}$ indexed by the space $\mathscr{Y}$. If $T$ is injective, the experiment $T \mathscr{G}=\left\{T G_{v} ; v \in V\right\}$ is equivalent to $\mathscr{G}$ itself. The range $\mathscr{Y} T$ of $T$ in $V$ is then a dense subset of $V$. In most cases it is not the entire space $V$, but one may "recover" $V$ by a suitable completion of $\mathscr{Y}$, sometimes associated with the concept of reproducing kernel Hilbert spaces.

Among maps $T$ which are of interest are the continuous linear maps which radonify $G_{0}$. These are the maps $T$ such that $T G_{0}$ extends to a Radon measure on the topological space $\mathscr{X}$. If $T G_{0}$ is radonified so is $T G_{v}$ for each $v \in V$, since $G_{v}$ and $G_{0}$ dominate each other.

It is easily checked that there are very many pairs ( $T, \mathscr{X}$ ) such that $T$ radonifies $G_{0}$ and such that $T$ is also injective. When $\mathscr{X}$ is a Banach space with unit ball $B$, to say that $T$ is radonifying is to say that the image by $T$ applied to $\mathscr{Y}$ of the unit ball $B^{0}$ of $\mathscr{Y}$ is a Gaussian continuity set of $V$. If so, $B^{0} T$ must be strongly compact in $V$ and the image by $T$ of the unit ball of $\mathscr{W}$ is similarly a strongly compact subset of $\mathscr{X}$.



For the case where $\mathscr{X}$ is a Banach space we shall also encounter below, maps which do not radonify $G_{0}$ on $\mathscr{X}$ itself, but radonify $G_{0}$ in the second dual, say $\mathscr{X}^{\prime \prime}$ of $\mathscr{X}$ for the weak topology $w\left(\mathscr{X}^{\prime \prime}, \mathscr{X}\right)$. For such a map $T$, the image $B^{0} T$ is a Gaussian bounded (G.B.) set in the sense of Dudley. It is also a compact subset of $V$.

To proceed to some statistical statements, here is a lemma which is a particular case of results of A . Birnbaum. Let $C$ be a closed convex proper subset of $\mathscr{X}$. Let $C^{0}$ be its polar in $\mathscr{Y}$, defined as the set of all $y$ such that $\langle y, x\rangle \leq 1$ for all $x \in C$. Let $B$ be a subset of $\mathscr{Y}$.

Lemma 1. Assume that $T$ radonifies $G_{0}$. Let $\phi, 0 \leq \phi \leq 1$, be a measurable function defined on $\mathfrak{X}$. Assume that for each $y \in C^{0}$ there are arbitrarily large positive numbers $t$ such that ty $\in B$. Then $\int \phi d F_{y} \leq \int I_{C} d F_{y}$ for all $y \in B$ only if $\phi$ and $\phi I_{C}$ are equivalent.

Proof. Consider the ratio

$$
r(y)=\left(\int \phi d F_{y}\right)\left(\int I_{C} d F_{y}\right)^{-1}
$$

It may be written in the form

$$
r(y)=\left\{\int \phi \exp [\langle y, x\rangle-1] d F_{0}\right\}\left\{\int I_{C} \exp [\langle y, x\rangle-1] d F_{o}\right\}^{-1}
$$

Now let $y=m y_{0}$ where $m$ is large positive and $y_{0}$ is in the polar $C^{0}$. If $\phi$ is not negligible on the half space $\left\langle y_{0}, x\right\rangle>1$ then $r\left(m y_{0}\right)$ tends to infinity as $m \rightarrow \infty$. Thus $\phi$ is equivalent to $\phi \psi\left(y_{0}\right)$ where $\psi\left(y_{0}\right)$ is the indicator of the closed half-space $\left\{x:\left\langle y_{0}, x\right\rangle \leq 1\right\}$. Approximating $\phi$ by an uppersemicontinuous function and taking the intersection of all the closed half spaces in question, the result follows.

An immediate corollary is that for the experiment $\left\{F_{y} ; y \in \mathscr{Y}\right\}$ any closed convex proper subset of $\mathscr{X}$ provides an admissible test. Indeed let $B_{1}$ be the linear space spanned by the polar $C^{0}$. Let $B=B_{1} \backslash C^{0}$. Consider the problem of testing the hypothesis $y \in B$ against the alternative $y=0$. The function $I_{C}$ provides such a test. If $\phi$ is another test such that

$$
\int \phi d F_{0} \geq \int I_{C} d F_{0} \text { and } \int \phi d F_{y} \leq \int I_{C} d F_{y}
$$

for all $y \in B$, then $\phi$ and $I_{C}$ must be equivalent for all $F_{y}$. If so, then $\phi$ and $I_{C}$ are also equivalent for all the images $T G_{v}, v \in V$. Therefore $I_{C}$ is also admissible for the experiment $\mathscr{G}$ itself.

Instead of radonifying the cylinder measures $G_{v}$, as was done in the preceding argument, it is sometimes preferable to enlarge the domains of the $G_{v}$ by a "regularity" argument. We shall describe one such argument now and apply the resulting extension to statements about behavior of estimates.

Let $\Gamma_{1}$ be the class of functions defined on $\mathscr{W}$ and obtainable as follows. Let $T$ be a continuous linear map from $\mathscr{W}$ onto a finite dimensional space $F$.

Let $C^{b}(F)$ be the space of bounded continuous real valued functions on $F . A$ function $\gamma$ defined on $\mathscr{W}$ belongs to $\Gamma_{1}$ if there is a $T$, a finite dimensional $F$, and an element $u$ of $C^{b}(F)$ such that $\gamma$ is the composition $\gamma=u \circ T$.

It is obvious that $\Gamma_{1}$ is a vector lattice. Since $G_{v}$ is a cylinder measure on $\mathscr{W}$, the integrals $\left\langle\gamma, G_{v}\right\rangle$ are well determined. In turn, as $\gamma$ runs through $\Gamma_{1}$, they determine $G_{v}$.

Among the elements of $\Gamma_{1}$ are functions which we shall call polyhedral hats. To define one of them take a finite subset $\left\{v_{j} ; j=1, \ldots, m\right\} \subset V$ and a number $\varepsilon>0$. Let $\gamma_{j}$ be the function which is such that (i) $\gamma_{j}(w)=1$ if $\left\langle v_{j}, w\right\rangle \leq 1$, (ii) $\gamma_{j}(w)=0$ if $\left\langle v_{j}, w\right\rangle \geq 1+\varepsilon$, and (iii) the intermediate values are obtained by linear interpolation. Then $\gamma=\inf _{j} \gamma_{j}$ is a polyhedral hat. It is a symmetric polyhedral hat if $\gamma(w)=\gamma(-w)$ identically. One particular lemma is as follows.

Lemma 2. Let $C$ be a closed convex symmetric subset of $\mathscr{W}$. Let $\mu_{1}(C)$ be the infimum

$$
\mu_{1}(C)=\inf \left\{\left\langle\gamma, G_{0}\right\rangle ; \gamma \in \Gamma_{1}, \gamma \geq I_{C}\right\}
$$

and let

$$
\mu_{2}(C)=\inf \left\{\left\langle\gamma, G_{0}\right\rangle ; \gamma \geq I_{C}, \gamma \in \mathrm{SPH}\right\}
$$

where SPH is the space of symmetric polyhedral hats. Then $\mu_{1}(C)=\mu_{2}(C)$. Proof. Clearly $\mu_{1}(C) \leq \mu_{2}(C)$. To prove the reverse inequality, assume that $\gamma \in \Gamma_{1}$ is of the form $\gamma=u \circ T$ where $T$ maps $\mathscr{W}$ onto $F$ and $u \in C^{b}(F)$, for a finite dimensional $F$.

Let $C_{1}=T C$. Then, the inequality $\gamma \geq I_{C}$ is equivalent to the inequality $u \geq I_{C_{1}}$. The set $C_{1}$ itself is a measurable convex subset of $F$. The image $v=T G_{0}$ of $G_{0}$ on $F$ is a certain centered Gaussian measure. Since $T \mathscr{W}=F$, this measure $v$ is equivalent to the Lebesgue measure of $F$.

Thus the boundary of $C_{1}$ has $v$ measure zero and $v\left(C_{1}\right)$ is equal to the measure $v\left(\bar{C}_{1}\right)$ of the closure of $C_{1}$. For any $\gamma=u \circ T, u \in C^{b}(F), u \geq I_{C_{1}}$, one has automatically

$$
\int u d v \geq \int I_{C_{1}} d v=\int I_{\bar{C}_{1}} d v
$$

Now $\bar{C}_{1}$ is the intersection of the half spaces which contain it. Using the symmetry assumption one sees that $I_{\bar{C}_{1}}$ is the infimum of the symmetric polyhedral hats which cover it. Since $F$ is finite dimensional and since $v$ is a Radon measure on $F$, the result follows.

This lemma, and a famous result of T.W. Anderson, will allow us to extend the domain of $G_{0}$ to all the indicators $I_{C}$ of closed (or Borel) convex symmetric subsets of $\mathscr{W}$. Anderson's theorem is as follows. Let $F$ be a finite dimensional vector space. Let $f$ be a probability density with respect to the Lebesgue measure $\lambda$ of $F$.

Theorem 1 (Anderson). Assume that for each real $\alpha$ the set $\{x ; x \in F, f(x) \geq \alpha\}$ is a convex symmetric subset of $F$. Let $C$ be a convex symmetric subset of $F$. Then $\int I_{C}(x) f(x+y) \lambda(d x) \leq \int I_{C}(x) f(x) \lambda(d x)$ for every $y \in F$.

Proof. We shall not give a complete proof. Anderson first notes that one can reduce the general assertion to the following simpler one: If $C$ and $C_{1}$ are both convex symmetric subsets of $F$ then

$$
\lambda\left[C \cap\left(y+C_{1}\right)\right] \leq \lambda\left(C \cap C_{1}\right) .
$$

The general result follows by integration. For the special result, let $K_{1}= C \cap\left(y+C_{1}\right)$ and $K_{-1}=C \cap\left[(-y)+C_{1}\right]$. Finally let $K$ be the sum $K= \frac{1}{2}\left(K_{1}+K_{-1}\right)$. It is clear that $K \subset C \cap C_{1}$. Also $\lambda\left(K_{1}\right)=\lambda\left(K_{-1}\right)$ by symmetry. To prove the result it is then enough to show that

$$
\lambda(K) \geq \frac{1}{2}\left[\lambda\left(K_{1}\right)+\lambda\left(K_{-1}\right)\right]
$$

This may be obtained by application of the Brunn-Minkowski inequality which says among other things that, if $k$ is the dimension of $F$,

$$
[\lambda(K)]^{1 / k} \geq \frac{1}{2}\left\{\left[\lambda\left(K_{1}\right)\right]^{1 / k}+\left[\lambda\left(K_{-1}\right)\right]^{1 / k}\right\} .
$$

This theorem will be applied several times below. A first application is as follows.

Lemma 3. Let $C$ be a closed convex symmetric subset of $\mathscr{W}$ and let $\mu_{1}(C)$ be the number

$$
\mu_{1}(C)=\inf \left\{\left\langle\gamma, G_{0}\right\rangle ; \gamma \in \Gamma_{1}, \gamma \geq I_{C}\right\}
$$

Let $\left\{\Pi_{m}\right\}$ be an increasing sequence of orthogonal projections of $\mathscr{W}$ itself such that (i) each $\Pi_{m} \mathscr{W}$ is finite dimensional, and (ii) $\Pi_{m} w \rightarrow w$ for each $w$. Then

$$
\lim _{m} G_{0}\left[\Pi_{m} Z \in C\right]=\mu_{1}(C) .
$$

(Note. The result also holds if $M$ is a directed set and $\left\{\Pi_{m}, m \in M\right\}$ is such that $\Pi_{m} \mathscr{W}$ increases as $m$ does.)

Proof. As $m$ increases the concentrations $\sup _{y} G_{0}\left[\Pi_{m} Z \in y+C\right]$ decrease, since $m_{1} \leq m_{2}$ implies that $\Pi_{m_{2}} Z$ is a sum of two independent vectors $\Pi_{m_{1}} Z+ \left(\Pi_{m_{2}}-\Pi_{m_{1}}\right) Z$. Thus, by Anderson's theorem, $G_{0}\left[\Pi_{m} Z \in C\right]$ decreases and therefore converges to a limit, say $\phi(C)$.

According to the same argument, if $\gamma$ is a symmetric polyhedral hat of $C$ then the value $\left\langle\gamma, G_{0}\right\rangle$ is at most equal to the expectation $E\left\{\gamma\left(\Pi_{m} Z\right)\right\}$ and $\lim _{m} E\left\{\gamma\left(\Pi_{m} Z\right)\right\}=\left\langle\gamma, G_{0}\right\rangle$. Therefore

$$
\phi(C)=\lim _{m} G_{0}\left[\Pi_{m} Z \in C\right] \leq \lim _{m} E\left[\gamma\left(\Pi_{m} Z\right)\right]=\left\langle\gamma, G_{0}\right\rangle .
$$

Taking an infimum on $\gamma$, Lemma 2 implies that $\phi(C) \leq \mu_{1}(C)$.
Conversely, suppose that $\phi(C)<\mu_{1}(C)$. Then there is some $m_{0}$ such that $m \geq m_{0}$ implies $G_{0}\left[\Pi_{m} Z \in C\right]<\mu_{1}(C)$. Thus there is a symmetric polyhedral hat $\gamma, \gamma \geq I_{C}$ such that

$$
E \gamma\left(\Pi_{m} Z\right) \leq E \gamma\left(\Pi_{m_{0}} Z\right)<\mu_{1}(C)
$$

for all $m \geq m_{0}$. This yields $\left\langle\gamma, G_{0}\right\rangle=\lim E \gamma\left(\Pi_{m} Z\right)<\mu_{1}(C)$. This being impossible, we must have $\phi(C)=\mu_{1}(C)$.

Now let $H$ be the set of all bounded functions $\gamma$ defined on $\mathscr{W}$ such that if $\left\{\Pi_{m}\right\}$ is any increasing sequence of finite dimensional orthogonal projections tending to the identity, then $E \gamma\left(\Pi_{m} Z\right)$ converges to a limit $\langle\gamma, \Phi\rangle$ independent of the sequence $\Pi_{m}$.

It is clear that $H$ is a linear space. Also, if $C$ is convex symmetric closed then $\left\langle I_{C}, \Phi\right\rangle=\phi(C)=\mu_{1}(C)$ is the limit already obtained. Note that if $\gamma \in H, \gamma \geq 0$, then $\langle\gamma, \Phi\rangle \geq 0$ so that $\Phi$ is a positive linear extension of $G_{0}$ to the entire space $H$. Performing the same sort of extension for $G_{v}, v \in V$ one sees also that $H$ is shift invariant. For the next remark we need an additional definition.

Definition 1. Let $\gamma, \gamma \geq 0$ be a numerical function defined on $\mathscr{W}$. It will be called subconvex if for every real $\alpha$ the set $\{w ; \gamma(w) \leq \alpha\}$ is a closed convex symmetric subset of $\mathscr{W}$.

Lemma 4. Let $l$ be a bounded subconvex function defined on $\mathscr{W}$. Let $\Pi_{m}$ be an increasing sequence of orthogonal projections such that $\Pi_{m} \rightarrow I$. Then $l \in H$ and $E l\left(\Pi_{m} Z\right)$ increases to $\langle l, \Phi\rangle=\sup \left\{\gamma ;\left\langle\gamma, G_{0}\right\rangle, \gamma \in \Gamma_{1}, \gamma \leq l\right\}$.

Proof. That $E l\left(\Pi_{m} Z\right)$ increases follows from Anderson's theorem again. For the other statements let $b=\sup \{l(w), w \in \mathscr{W}\}$. Divide the interval $[0, b]$ by numbers $0=\beta_{0}<\beta_{1}<\cdots<\beta_{n}=b$ such that $\beta_{k+1}-\beta_{k}<\varepsilon$. Let $C_{j}=\left\{w ; l(w) \leq \beta_{j}\right\}$ and let $u_{j}$ be the indicator of $C_{j}$. Consider the function $f$ defined by $f=\sum_{j=1}^{n}\left(\beta_{j}-\beta_{j-1}\right) u_{j}$ and the function $g=\sum_{j=0}^{n-1}\left(\beta_{j+1}-\beta_{j}\right) u_{j}$. Then $f \leq l \leq g$ and $g-f \leq \varepsilon$. Clearly $f$ and $g$ belong to $H$, by Lemma 3. It follows that $l \in H$. Finally, if $0 \leq \gamma \leq f, \gamma \in \Gamma_{1}$, then $\left\langle\gamma, G_{0}\right\rangle=\langle\gamma, \Phi\rangle \leq\langle f, \Phi\rangle$ since $\Phi$ is positive linear. But these are $\gamma_{j} \in \Gamma_{1}$ such that $0 \leq \gamma_{j} \leq u_{j}$ and $\left\langle u_{j}-\gamma_{j}, \Phi\right\rangle \leq \varepsilon / n b$ so that if $\gamma=\sum_{j=1}^{n}\left(\beta_{j}-\beta_{j-1}\right) \gamma_{j}$ then $\langle f-\gamma, \Phi\rangle \leq \varepsilon$.

Using the space $H$ one can define another experiment, say $\overline{\mathscr{G}}=\left\{\Phi_{v}, v \in V\right\}$ by letting $\Phi_{0}=\Phi$ and taking for $\Phi_{v}$ that linear functional defined on $H$ such that $d \Phi_{v}=\exp \left\{\langle v, Z\rangle-\frac{1}{2}\|v\|^{2}\right\} d \Phi_{0}$.

It is immediate that $\mathscr{G}$ and $\overline{\mathscr{G}}$ are equivalent. Also if $K$ is the identification map $v \mapsto K v$ of $V$ into $\mathscr{W}$ such that $\left\langle v_{1}, K v_{2}\right\rangle=\left\langle v_{2}, K v_{1}\right\rangle$ and $\langle v, K v\rangle=\|v\|^{2}$, the functional $\Phi_{v}$ is simply $\Phi_{0}$ shifted by $K v$.

To obtain some corollaries, consider the problem of estimating the value of the parameter $v$. Here $V$ is the space of possible decisions. To further define the possibilities, let $\Gamma$ be the vector lattice of functions defined on $V$ exactly as $\Gamma_{1}$ was defined on $\mathscr{W}$, so that using the identification map $K$ above one has $\Gamma=\Gamma_{1} \circ K$.

This lattice $\Gamma$ may be used to define risk functions as in Chapter 1. Explicitly, if $l(v, t)$ is a loss function such that $\inf _{t} l(v, t)>-\infty$, and $T$ is a transition from $L(\mathscr{G})$ to the dual of $\Gamma$, one defines the risk at $v$ by

$$
R(v, T)=\sup _{\gamma}\left\{\gamma T G_{v} ; \gamma \in \Gamma, \gamma \leq l(v, t)\right\}
$$

Proposition 1. Consider the problem of estimating $v \in V$ with a loss equal to $l(t-v)$ if $v$ is the true value and the decision taken is $t$. Assume that $l$, defined on $V$, is subconvex. For the experiment $\left\{G_{v} ; v \in V\right\}$ the minimax risk is always equal to $\left\langle l, \Phi_{0}\right\rangle=E l(Z)$ if $l$ is bounded or to $\sup _{n}\left\langle n \wedge l, \Phi_{0}\right\rangle$ if $l$ is unbounded.

Proof. Let $\Pi_{m}$ be an orthogonal projection of $\mathscr{W}$ onto a finite dimensional subspace of $\mathscr{W}$. Denote its transpose by the same letter, so that $\left\langle v \Pi_{m}, w\right\rangle= \left\langle v, \Pi_{m} w\right\rangle$.

Consider first the problem of estimating $v$ assuming that it is restricted to the finite dimensional space $V \Pi_{m}$, range of $\Pi_{m}$ in $V$. Then for this restricted problem $\Pi_{m} Z$ is a sufficient statistic. Also, for the resulting finite dimensional Gaussian problem the minimax risk is well known to be equal to $E l\left(\Pi_{m} Z\right)$. This may be checked, for instance, using centered Gaussian distributions as prior distributions and letting the covariance of the prior distribution tend to "infinity." Alternatively, one may use the Hunt-Stein theorem which says that there is a shift invariant estimate which achieves the minimax risk. Thus the minimum risk for the more difficult problem of estimating $v \in V$ is at least equal to $E l\left(\Pi_{m} Z\right)$.

Assuming that $l$ is bounded, Lemma 4 shows that $E l\left(\Pi_{m} Z\right)$ increases to $\left\langle l, \Phi_{0}\right\rangle$. On the other hand, one may estimate $v$ by sending $Z$ into $V$ through the identification map $J$. The estimate $J Z$ so obtained does not actually lie in $V$. However, it is allowable under the general definition of transitions of Chapter 1. Since the distribution of $J Z$ for $G_{v}$ is the distribution $J G_{0}$ shifted by $v$, the risk of $J Z$ is exactly equal to $\left\langle l, \Phi_{0}\right\rangle$.

Finally, if $l$ is unbounded, the expression given in the Proposition is obtainable by applying the above to the loss functions $n \wedge l$ and then using the definition of the risk as a supremum. Hence the assertion.

Corollary. Assume that the minimax risk $R$ is strictly inferior to the supremum $b$ of $l$. Then there is an $\alpha<b$ and a set $B=\{w ; l(w) \leq \alpha\}$ such that on the polar $B^{0}$ of $B$, the process $Z$ admits a version which has bounded paths. Furthermore, $B$ is absorbing.

Proof. If $R<b$, there is some $\alpha<b$ and a $\beta>0$ such that if $B=\{w ; l(w) \leq \alpha\}$ then $\left\langle I_{B}, \Phi_{0}\right\rangle \geq \beta$. It is easily seen that then $B$ must be an absorbing set. Furthermore, letting $B^{0}$ be the polar of $B$, the inequality $\left\langle I_{B}, \Phi_{0}\right\rangle \geq \beta$ implies that for every finite set $F=\left\{v_{j} ; j=1, \ldots, n\right\}, F \subset B^{0}$, one must have

$$
G_{0}\left\{\sup \left|\left\langle v_{j} ; Z\right\rangle\right|>1\right\} \leq 1-\beta
$$

By the usual identification map, send the space $\mathscr{W}$ into a subset of the space $C\left(B^{0}\right)$ of continuous functions on $B^{0}$. Let $C^{\prime}$ be the dual of $C\left(B^{0}\right)$ and let $C^{\prime \prime}$ be the second dual. The above inequality shows that the image of $G_{0}$ in $C^{\prime \prime}$ is larger than a certain Radon measure which gives mass at least $\beta$ to the unit ball of $C^{\prime \prime}$. Thus, according to the zero-one laws available for Gaussian processes, the image of $G_{0}$ must itself be a Radon measure on $C^{\prime \prime}$ for $w\left(C^{\prime \prime}, C^{\prime}\right)$. (See, for instance, the book of A. Badrikian and S. Chevet). This gives the result.

The above could be paraphrased by saying that $B^{0}$ is a G.B. set in the sense
of Dudley. It need not be a G.C. set. In fact if $l$ is a semi-norm such that the polar of $B=\{w ; l(w) \leq 1\}$ is a G.B. set, then the image of $G_{0}$ by the canonical identification map is a Radon measure on $C^{\prime \prime}$ for $w\left(C^{\prime \prime}, C^{\prime}\right)$. It follows then from the results of Landau and Shepp or Fernique that there are numbers $a>0$ for which the expectation $E \exp \left\{c l^{2}(Z)\right\}$ is finite.

The above Proposition 1 says that the estimate $J Z$ image of $Z$ in $V$ is a minimax estimate of $v$ for all the subconvex loss functions. The reader should note however that $J Z$ is often thoroughly inadmissible. For instance, if $l(v)= 1 \wedge\|v\|^{2}$ the estimate $J Z$ has a risk which is always equal to unity. The trivial estimate which always estimates that $v=0$, has a risk equal to $l(v)$. On the contrary, Bayes estimates are often admissible. For instance, one can assert the following

Lemma 5. Let $l$ be a bounded function which is continuous for the strong topology of $V$. Let $\mu$ be a prior distribution which is a Radon measure on $V$ and has for support the space $V$ itself. Then the Bayes estimate for the loss $l(t-v)$ and the prior $\mu$ is admissible.

Proof. Let $T_{0}$ be the Bayes estimate and let $T$ be some other estimate such that $R(T, v) \leq R\left(T_{0}, v\right)$ for all $v$. Then $R(T, v)=R\left(T_{0}, v\right)$ almost everywhere. Under the assumptions made, the risks are continuous in $v$. Hence the assertion.

There are some priors $\mu$ for which the Bayes estimates are exactly obtainable. These are the Gaussian priors.

Consider, for instance, a measure $\mu$ which is a Gaussian Radon measure centered at the origin of $V$. Let $\xi$ be a variable which has distribution $\mu$. Then there is a symmetric linear map $M$ of $\mathscr{W}$ into $V$ such that $E|\langle\xi, w\rangle|^{2}=\langle M w, w\rangle$.

Consider each $G_{v}$ as a $\sigma$-additive measure on the algebraic dual $V^{*}$ of $V$. Define a joint distribution of the pair $(\xi, X)$ by first selecting $\xi$ according to $\mu$ and then selecting $X$ in $V^{*}$ according to $G_{\xi}$.

The pair so obtained has jointly a certain Gaussian distribution. Taking $Z$ distributed according to $G_{0}$ one can also write the covariance of $\left\langle v_{1}, Z\right\rangle$ and $\left\langle v_{2}, Z\right\rangle$ in the form $\left\langle v_{1}, K v_{2}\right\rangle=\left\langle v_{2}, K v_{1}\right\rangle$ where $K$ is the canonical map of $V$ onto $\mathscr{W}$. It follows that the marginal distribution, say $m$, of $X$ is also Gaussian, centered at zero, with a covariance which may be written $E|\langle v, X\rangle|^{2}=\langle v,(K+ K M K) v\rangle$.

The joint distribution of ( $\xi, X$ ) can also be obtained by first selecting an $x \in V^{*}$ according to this marginal $m$ and then selecting $\xi$ according to a suitable Gaussian measure $\mu_{x}$. Identification of covariances leads to the conclusion that $\mu_{x}$ has expectation $A x=(K+K M K)^{-1} K M x$ and a variance given by the linear operator $M_{1}=M-M K(K+K M K)^{-1} K M$.

Here the map $M$ is originally only a map from $\mathscr{W}$ to $V$. For the above to make sense it should be extended to $V^{*}$. A possibility is to use the symmetry and write $\langle M x, w\rangle=\langle M w, x\rangle$ for all $x \in V^{*}$. This being done, note that $M$ radonifies all the $\mu_{x}$ as well as the marginal distribution of $X$. Thus $A X$ may be considered as a Gaussian random vector with values in $V$ itself, and the above make perfectly good sense.

The reader may also want to verify that the joint distribution obtained from $m$ and the $\mu_{x}$ is indeed the same as that of the original $(\xi, X)$. This can be
achieved easily, taking in $\mathscr{W}$ an orthonormal basis $\left\{w_{j}\right\}$ such that $\left\langle M w_{i}, w_{j}\right\rangle=$ 0 if $i \neq j$. Then the conditional distribution of $\left\langle\xi, w_{j}\right\rangle$ given $X$ can be taken equal to the conditional distribution of $\left\langle\xi, w_{j}\right\rangle$ given only $\left\langle J w_{j}, X\right\rangle$. This leads to the following assertion.

Proposition 2. Let $\mu$ be a centered Gaussian Radon measure on $V$, as described. Then for every subconvex loss function $l$ a Bayes estimate of $v$ for the prior $\mu$ and the loss $l(t-v)$ is given by the formula $A X=(K+K M K)^{-1} K M X$. The Bayes risk is given by $\operatorname{El}(Y)$ where $Y$ is a random vector whose distribution is a Gaussian Radon measure on $V$ with expectation zero and covariance operator $M-M K(K+K M K)^{-1} K M$.

This follows immediately from Anderson's theorem applied to the posterior distributions $\mu_{x}$. The reader will note that, upon identification of $V$ and $\mathscr{W}$ the map noted $A$ above is of trace class and such that $\|A x\|<\|x\|$ for every $x \neq 0$.

The foregoing assertions refer only to the performance of tests or estimates for the experiment $\mathscr{G}$ itself. The results of Chapter 7 can be used to translate those assertions into statements applicable to the sequence $\left\{\mathscr{E}_{n}\right\}, \mathscr{E}_{n}= \left\{P_{v, n} ; v \in V\right\}$.

Here are two sample assertions which are direct applications of Theorem 1, Section 4, Chapter 7. Let $l$ be a subconvex loss function. Consider the problem of estimating $v \in V$ with losses equal to $l(t-v)$.

Proposition 3. Let $a$ be the number $a=E l(Z)=\left\langle l, \Phi_{0}\right\rangle$. Let $b$ denote a fixed number $b<a$. Assume that $\mathscr{E}_{n}$ converge weakly to $\mathscr{G}$ and let $R_{n}\left(T_{n}, v\right)$ be the risk for an estimate $T_{n}$ available on $\mathscr{E}_{n}$. There is an integer $N$ and a finite set $S \subset V$ such that $n \geq N$ implies $\sup _{v \in S} R_{n}\left(T_{n}, v\right)>b$ for all estimates $T_{n}$.

For another assertion, let $B$ denote a subset of $V$ and consider the problem of testing $v=0$ against the alternative that $v \in B$. Assume that if $v \neq 0$ there are elements $m v$ such that $m v \in B$ for arbitrarily large values of $m$.

Let $T$ be a linear map from $\mathscr{W}$ to a locally convex space $\mathscr{X}$. Let $F_{v}=T G_{v}$ and assume that $F_{0}$ extends to a Radon measure. Let $C$ be a closed proper convex subset of $\mathscr{X}$ and let $R(v)$ be the risk $R(v)=F_{v}(C)$ if $v \neq 0$. For $v=0$, let $R(0)=1-F_{0}(C)$. If $\phi_{n}$ is a test defined for $\mathscr{E}_{n}$, let $R_{n}\left(\phi_{n}, v\right)=\left\langle\phi_{n}, P_{v, n}\right\rangle$ if $v \neq 0$ and let $R_{n}\left(\phi_{n}, 0\right)=1-\left\langle\phi_{n}, P_{0, n}\right\rangle$.

Proposition 4. Assume that $\mathscr{E}_{n}$ tends weakly to $\mathscr{G}$. Let $v_{1}$ be a particular point of $V$ and let a be a number $a<R\left(v_{1}\right)$. Then there is an $\alpha>0$, a finite set $S \subset V$, and an integer $N$ such that, if $n \geq N$, any test $\phi_{n}$ available on $\mathscr{E}_{n}$ and such that $R_{n}\left(\phi_{n}, v_{1}\right) \leq a$ is also such that $R_{n}\left(\phi_{n}, v\right) \geq R(v)+\alpha$ for at least one $v \in S$.

The following assertion is a consequence of Theorem 1, Section 5, Chapter 7.

Proposition 5. Let $\mathscr{X}, T$, and $C$ and other notation be as above. Let $\phi_{n, 1}$ and $\phi_{n, 2}$ be two tests available on $\mathscr{E}_{n}$. Assume that for each $v \in V$ one has $\limsup _{n} R_{n}\left(\phi_{n, i}, v\right) \leq R(v)$. Then, for each $v$ one has $\lim _{n}\langle | \phi_{n, 2}-\phi_{n, 1}\left|, P_{v, n}\right\rangle=$ 0.

Proof. This follows immediately from the fact that $I_{C}$ is a nonrandomized admissible test (this section, Lemma 1) which is also unique, since the $F_{v}$ form an exponential family. Thus Theorem 1, Section 5, Chapter 7 is applicable.

The two preceding propositions do not assert anything about the existence of tests $\phi_{n}$ which may have risks $R_{n}\left(\phi_{n}, v\right)$ which converge to $R(v)$. Here is a remark concerning this.

Using the above notation, let $Z_{n}$ be any linear process which can be used to approximate the logarithm of the likelihood ratios, in the sense that $L_{n}(v)- \left\langle v, Z_{n}\right\rangle \rightarrow 0$ in probability for each $v$. Let $\phi_{n}$ be the test defined by $\phi_{n}= I_{C}\left(T Z_{n}\right)$. Then $\limsup _{n} R_{n}\left(\phi_{n}, v\right) \leq R(v)$ for all $v$ if and only if

$$
\lim _{n} P_{0, n}\left\{T Z_{n} \notin C\right\} \rightarrow R(0)
$$

To prove this note first that the value $G_{v}[T Z \in C]$ is the infimum of the numbers $\left\langle\gamma, G_{v}\right\rangle$ for $\gamma$ in the space $\Gamma_{1}$ of Lemma 14, with $\gamma \geq I_{C}(T Z)$. Thus, as usual

$$
\limsup _{n}\left\langle I_{C}\left(T Z_{n}\right), P_{v, n}\right\rangle \leq\left\langle I_{C}(T Z), G_{v}\right\rangle
$$

for all $v$. This gives the desired inequality automatically for all $v \neq 0$. At zero we require the reverse inequality. Hence the assertion.

To give further examples of possible assertions, assume now that $\mathscr{E}_{n} \rightarrow \mathscr{G}$ uniformly on compacts and let $Z^{n}$ be the particular linear process constructed for the sake of Proposition 3, Section 4.

Let $\rho$ be a continuous semi-norm on $V$. Let $\bar{V}$ be the Banach space completion of $V$ for the norm $\rho$. One can map $G_{0}$ to a cylinder measure of $\bar{V}$ by the identification map $J$ of $\mathscr{W}$ onto $V$ and then the imbedding into $\bar{V}$. Call $\rho G_{0}$-measurable if the image of $G_{0}$ by this operation extends to a Radon measure on $\bar{V}$.

Proposition 6. Let $b$ be a number, $b \in(0, \infty)$. Let $l(v)=b \wedge \rho(v)$. Consider the problem of estimating $v$ with losses $l(t-v)$. Assume that $\mathscr{E}_{n} \rightarrow \mathscr{G}$ uniformly on compacts. Let $T_{n}$ be the estimate $J Z^{n}$ of $v$ for the experiment $\mathscr{E}_{n}$. Then, for all $G_{0}$-measurable semi-norms $\rho$ the expressions $E\left\{l\left(v-T_{n}\right) \mid P_{v, n}\right\}$ converge to $E l(J Z)$. In particular $\left\{T_{n}\right\}$ is asymptotically minimax.

Proof. The statement that a continuous semi-norm $\rho$ is $G_{0}$-measurable is equivalent to the statement that the polar of $\{w ; \rho(w) \leq 1\}$ is a G.C. set. Thus, the assertion is an immediate consequence of Proposition 3, Section 4, and Proposition 3, this section.

To terminate, let us mention a result which is related to a theorem of $\mathbf{C}$. Stein about elimination of nuisance parameters.

Let $\Pi$ be an orthogonal projection of $V$ into itself. Let $X=\Pi V$ and $Y= (I-\Pi) V$. Identifying $V$ and its dual $\mathscr{W}$, one may also consider that $\Pi$ is a projection of $\mathscr{W}$ into itself. The spaces corresponding to $X$ and $Y$ will be noted $\mathscr{X}$ and $\mathscr{Y}$, respectively.

If $v \in V$ has the form $\xi+\eta$ for $\xi \in X$ and $\eta \in Y$, then the measure $G_{v}$ is the direct product $G_{v}=F_{\xi} \otimes H_{\eta}$ where $F_{\xi}=\Pi G_{v}$ and $H_{\eta}=(I-\Pi) G_{v}$. Let $D$ be any decision space, with a vector lattice of numerical functions $\Gamma$ and a loss function $l$ defined on $D \times V$.

Call a transition $T$ from $L(\mathscr{G})$ to $\Gamma^{*}$ invariant under the shifts $y \in Y$ if $T G_{\xi+\eta}=T G_{\xi}$ for every pair $(\xi, \eta) \in X \times Y$. Let $\mathscr{F}$ be the experiment $\mathscr{F}= \left\{F_{\xi} ; \xi \in X\right\}$. It is easily seen that an invariant transition $T$ defines uniquely a certain transition, say $S$, from $L(\mathscr{F})$ to $\Gamma^{*}$ such that $T$ is equivalent to $S \Pi$.

Indeed, consider the vector lattice $\operatorname{Lat}(\mathscr{F})$ generated by $\mathscr{F}$. It is enough to define $S$ on $\operatorname{Lat}(\mathscr{F})$. (Here, because of the completeness, it would be enough to define $S$ on $\operatorname{Lin}(\mathscr{F})$.) If for instance $m=\sum a_{j} F_{\xi}$, one can define $S m$ by $T\left(\sum a_{j} G_{\xi_{j}}\right)$ and similarly $S|m|$ by $T\left|\sum a_{j} G_{\xi_{j}}\right|$. It is enough to verify that the operation so defined is linear and positive. This follows easily from the remark that a sum $\sum a_{j} G_{\xi_{j}}$ can also be written $\left(\sum a_{j} F_{\xi_{j}}\right) \times H_{0}$.

Now suppose that the loss function $l$ defined on $D \times V$ is also invariant under the shifts of $Y$, so that, for each $a \in D$ one has $l(a, \xi+\eta)=l(a, \xi)$, for $\xi \in X$ and $\eta \in Y$.

Let $T_{1}$ be any transition from $L(\mathscr{G})$ to $\Gamma^{*}$ and let $R\left(T_{1}, v\right)$ be its risk function. If $R\left(T_{1}, v\right)$ is invariant under the shifts $\eta \in Y$, then according to the MarkovKakutani theorem, there is another transition $T$ such that $R(T, v) \leq R\left(T_{1}, v\right)$ for all $v$ and such that $T$ is invariant under the shifts of $Y$. Thus there is a transition $S$ from $L(\mathscr{F})$ to $\Gamma^{*}$ such that $T$ is equivalent to $S \Pi$.

In summary, if one desires to have risk functions which are invariant under the shifts of $Y$, one may totally ignore the space $\mathscr{Y}$ and work with the reduced experiment $\mathscr{F}$.

## CHAPTER 11

## Asymptotic Normality—Global

## 1. Introduction

This chapter elaborates properties of a certain widely applicable method of construction of estimates. The general idea is that one provides oneself with a well behaved auxiliary estimate of the parameter and that, in the vicinity of the estimated value, one refines the estimate using techniques adapted to the local structure of the experiment.

The properties of the construction are described here under assumptions to the effect that, locally, the logarithms of likelihood ratios admit approximations of a quadratic nature. Such approximation can often be written in the form

$$
\Lambda(t, \theta) \sim-\frac{1}{2}\{K[Z-\xi(t)]-K[Z-\xi(\theta)]\},
$$

where $\xi$ is a map from the parameter space to some linear or affine space, $Z$ is a random element taking values in that same affine space, and $K$ is a quadratic form which may itself be random. The intent of the method is to use the auxiliary estimate and variables obtainable otherwise from local expansions to construct the variables $Z$ and $K$. These can then be used for purposes of inference.

We have already encountered local quadratic approximations for the asymptotically Gaussian experiments of Chapter 10 . These will recur in this chapter. They correspond to a situation where the forms $K$ could be taken nonrandom. However, even in this case, the construction naturally leads to the use of random quadratic forms. There is also a wide class of cases, dubbed "locally asymptotically mixed normal experiments," where the use of random quadratic forms is unavoidable. Thus we have described the method and some of its properties assuming that random quadratic forms are used.

All the way through we consider a net or sequence, $\left\{\mathscr{E}_{v}\right\}$, of experiments $\mathscr{E}_{v}=\left\{P_{\theta, v} ; \theta \in \Theta_{v}\right\}$, where all the items listed depend on a certain index $v$ whose function is to "tend to infinity" along a certain directed set. In principle, $v$ occurs everywhere. Thus we have suppressed it from the notation, singling out those items which do not depend on $v$ by saying that they are fixed, or using other circumlocutions. Thus we shall refer to experiments $\mathscr{E}=\left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ without explicit mention of $v$, which, however, is very much there.

It turns out that most of the constructions and properties of the resulting estimates are conveniently described by using the canonical imbedding of the space $\Theta$ into the vector space $\mathscr{M}(\Theta)$ of finite signed measures with finite support on $\Theta$. Thus we shall describe properties based on the possibility of local approximations of the type

$$
\Lambda(t, \theta) \sim \frac{1}{2}\left\{K\left(Z-\delta_{t}\right)-K\left(Z-\delta_{\theta}\right)\right\}
$$

where $\delta_{t}$ is the Dirac mass at $t \in \Theta$, where $Z$ is a random element with values in $\mathscr{M}(\Theta)$, and where $K$ is a random quadratic form defined on suitable subspaces of $\mathscr{M}(\Theta)$.

For practical applications, it is often more convenient to work in linear spaces other than $\mathscr{M}(\theta)$, especially when the structure of the experiments $\mathscr{E}=\left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ already provide another suitable space. The reasons for the use of $\mathscr{M}(\Theta)$, and indications on relations with more traditional assumptions, are given in Sections 2 and 7 below.

Otherwise the general plan of this chapter is as follows. Section 3 gives a list of the objects necessary for the construction and the description of the construction itself. At this point no claims are made on the behavior of the resulting pairs ( $Z, K$ ) because no assumptions on the behavior of the experiment $\mathscr{E}$ have yet been introduced.

Section 4 introduces a variety of assumptions and definitions concerning local approximations by quadratic expressions. Using these, Section 5 gives the main approximation theorems for the general case. Section 6 gives particular results available in the locally asymptotically Gaussian situation, including asymptotic sufficiency properties of the centering statistics $Z$.

Section 7 discusses some special cases. Section 8 gives an extension of a result of Wald according to which one can pass from the actual distributions of the centering statistics to exactly Gaussian ones, and conversely, by small perturbations of the statistics.



Section 9 gives some applications to standard test problems, yielding asymptotic optimality assertions similar to those described by Wald in 1943. It also indicates some links between the theory expounded here and Neyman's theory of $C(\alpha)$ tests.

Section 10 is a digression on minimum $\chi^{2}$ methods and related subjects. As mentioned above, we shall consider nets $\left\{\mathscr{E}_{v}\right\}, v \in N$, where $N$ is a certain directed set and where $\mathscr{E}_{v}=\left\{P_{\theta, v} ; \theta \in \Theta_{v}\right\}$. However, $v$ will be suppressed from the notation.

No special assumptions need to be made on the directed set $N$, except that it is often convenient to assume that $N$ is nonpathological in the sense that there do exist functions $\varepsilon_{\nu}, \nu \in N$ such that $\varepsilon_{\nu}$ is real, $\varepsilon_{\nu}>0$ with $\varepsilon_{\nu}$ tending to zero along $N$. This is true for sequences and for most nets used in practice. It affords the possibility of some leeway in some of the constructions, but is not a very essential affair.

It is essential, however, to note that since $\Theta$ itself depends on $v$ it will be generally impossible to argue around a fixed value of $\theta$, independent of $v$. The pointwise convergence results so common in the statistical literature would not make good sense here. They are, however, weaker than the corresponding results given here. Occasionally we have pointed them out.

There are very good reasons why one should let the entire structure $\mathscr{E}=\left\{P_{\theta}\right.$; $\theta \in \Theta\}$ vary with $v$. One of them is that it is common practice. Statisticians tend to use more elaborate models when they have more information. This may mean, for instance, but not exclusively, increasing the number of parameters in the model when more data is available. Another reason is that limit theorems are not easily interpretable in practice. What one would like to have are approximation theorems, applicable to a fixed experiment $\mathscr{E}$. Such theorems might be derivable from the limit theorems given further on. However, this author's attempts to formulate them have not led to readable statements. Letting the entire structure depend on $v$ and passing to the limit is a compromise. The net $\{\nu\}$ is really not involved much and the statements almost read "if so and so tends to zero, so does such and such." Approximation results would be of the type "if so and so is not more than $\delta$, then such and such is less than $\varepsilon(\delta)$." The trouble with formulating them is that many $\delta$ 's are involved, with complex relations between the starting point and the end product.

## 2. Preliminary Explanations

Let $\mathscr{E}=\left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ be an experiment indexed by a set $\Theta$. Let $\Lambda(t, s)= \log \left(d P_{t} / d P_{s}\right)$ be a choice of the corresponding likelihood ratios. When $\Theta$ is a Euclidean space, or an open subset of a Euclidean space, one frequently uses approximations to $\Lambda(t, s)$ obtainable through Taylor expansions or related methods. Typically, one selects a particular point $\theta$ and, for $s$ close to $\theta$, one writes that $\Lambda(s, \theta)$ is approximated by

$$
(s-\theta)^{\prime} X_{\theta}-\frac{1}{2}(s-\theta)^{\prime} M_{\theta}(s-\theta),
$$

where $X_{\theta}$ is a vector of first derivatives at $\theta$ and $-M_{\theta}$ is a matrix of second derivatives. Sometimes the second derivatives are replaced by their expectation so that $M_{\theta}$ becomes the so-called Fisher information matrix.

This is an apparently simple, and certainly very common procedure. However, the simplicity is more apparent than real. Also, such a method suffers from various difficulties which makes it impossible to use as a general method. As a consequence we shall use throughout subsequent sections of this chapter a more abstract, but in fact, simpler formulation. This section will explain in part how the abstract framework is related to the more commonly used one. However, let us start with some examples intended to illustrate some of the possibilities.

Even if quadratic approximations do exist, a Taylor expansion need not be the procedure to obtain them. Here are some examples.
(1) Take $n$ independent identically distributed observations from the double exponential distribution whose Lebesgue density on the line is $\frac{1}{2} \exp \{-|x-\theta|\}$. Then first derivatives, taken where they exist, are usable but a second order Taylor expansion does not work. We shall see later, in Chapter 17, that quadratic approximations do exist. A more complex but analogous problem occurs if one takes independent observations from a distribution on $\mathbb{R}^{3}$ with a Lebesgue density proportional to $|x-\theta|^{-1 / 2} \exp \left\{-|x-\theta|^{2}\right\}$.
(2) Take $n$ independent observations from the distribution whose Lebesgue density on the line is $[1-|x-\theta|]^{+}$. This is a very regular case in which quadratic approximations exist, but Taylor expansions do not work.

Sometimes an apparently innocuous replacement of a parameter by a function of it can lead to various difficulties. A notorious example is as follows.
(3) Consider again $n$ independent identically distributed observations each obtained from a measure on the integers such that

$$
P(X=k)=E e^{-\lambda Y} \frac{(\lambda Y)^{k}}{k!},
$$

where $Y$ is a certain "mixing variable." The literature contains several papers where one assumes that $Y$ has the form $Y=\exp \left\{U \xi_{n}\right\}$ where $U$ has a distribution independent of $n$ and where $\xi_{n}$ is a parameter of the form $\xi_{n}=\mu n^{-1 / 4}$. The experiment is then indexed by $\theta=(\lambda, \mu)$, with $\mu \geq 0$. To have the same rate of convergence, $(\sqrt{n})$, for both parameters it was found convenient to write $\xi_{n}$ in an alternate form. One takes another real parameter, say $\alpha \geq 0$, and writes that $Y=\exp (U \sqrt{\alpha})$ and considers values of $\alpha$ proportional to $n^{-1 / 2}$. Such a modification is obviously of no importance whatsoever for any of the interesting statistical properties. However it has been observed by P.A.P. Moran that it makes the usual Taylor expansion argument invalid.
(4) Even if $\Theta$ is an open subset of a Euclidean space, there are many cases where natural quadratic approximations involve not $\theta$ itself (or more precisely the vector $s$ of the formula written above) but functions of $\theta$. Numerous examples have occurred in the study of multinomial situations, or contingency tables where one uses a vector $p(\theta)$ of all probabilities. Here a natural parameterization involves the probabilities themselves instead of $\theta$. Then the "natural" parameter space
is no longer an open subset of a Euclidean space, but a "surface" in it. Thus one is led directly to use parameterization sets $\Theta$ which are differentiable manifolds, sometimes defined by implicit equations. This means that it becomes convenient to work with local coordinate systems, depending on the particular points $\theta$ under consideration.
(5) The Taylor expansion calls for still another remark. It is entirely evident and so obvious that it tends to be forgotten. To write it, one has to specify the Euclidean structure at least to the extent of providing the bases in which the transpose and matrix operations are carried out. One can avoid some of the specifications by giving only the norm used on the Euclidean space. However, just as for the case of manifolds, this has to be done at a particular $\theta$ close to the "true value" of the parameter. This "true value" is, by definition, unknown. The natural norms to be used in the arguments may vary with the value of $\theta$. To replace them by norms independent of $\theta$ may complicate matters and lead to extraneous assumptions.
(6) A similar statement can be made about choices of bases. To preserve sufficiency properties we shall allow our centering variables to take values outside of $\Theta$. However, the items involved in the construction need to be taken from $\Theta$ itself. In cases where $\Theta$ is a well behaved subset of a Euclidean space, but not an open one, appropriate selection of local coordinate systems becomes a necessity. Suppose, for instance, that $\Theta$ is a subset of the ordinary Euclidean plane, formed by pairs $(x, y)$ such that $y \geq|x|$. In the interior of $\Theta$ choices of bases may be relatively unimportant. This is no longer the case at the apex $x=y=0$ or on other points of the boundary of $\Theta$.

All these considerations lead to a possible tentative definition of what should be meant by a local quadratic approximation to the log likelihood. It is definitely tentative and will be replaced by more abstract but more work able and more precise definitions in Section 4. The suggested possibility is as follows, using experiment indexed by a directed set suppressed from the notation.

Definition 1. Let $S$ be a subset of $\Theta$ and let $\xi$ be a map from $S$ to a Euclidean space $F$. Let $x^{\prime} y$ denote the inner product of a pair ( $x, y$ ) in $F$. The map $\xi$ provides a quadratic approximation related to the experiment $\left\{P_{s} ; s \in S\right\}$ if there exist random variables $X$ with values in $F$ and symmetric random matrices $M$ on $F$ such that all the following requirements are satisfied.
(i) For some $\theta \in S$ and all $s \in S$ the differences

$$
\Lambda(s, \theta)-\left\{[\xi(s)-\xi(\theta)]^{\prime} X-\frac{1}{2}[\xi(s)-\xi(\theta)]^{\prime} M[\xi(s)-\xi(\theta)]\right\}
$$

tend to zero in $P_{\theta}+P_{s}$ probability.
(ii) For all pairs $(s, t) \in S \times S$, the nets $\left\{P_{s}\right\}$ and $\left\{P_{t}\right\}$ are contiguous.
(iii) There are random variables $Y$ with values in $F$ such that $X=M[Y \quad \xi(\theta)]$.

In this definition the roles of conditions (i) and (ii) should be fairly clear. However, by themselves, they allow a large class of possibilities which would not normally be considered quadratic approximations because the presence of the quadratic term is not genuine.

In fact, (i) and (ii) just say that the measures involved satisfy a contiguity requirement and allow approximations of the log likelihood by finite dimensional expressions. According to our notational conventions, suppressing the index $v$ which tends to infinity, the maps $\xi$ and the spaces $F$ depend on $v$. The dimension of the finite dimensional approximation is allowed to vary with $v$ and tend to infinity. Thus all Koopman-Darmois families would satisfy (i). They also satisfy (ii) if one limits the range of the parameter to maintain contiguity. More details on this point will be found in Chapter 14. This same Chapter 14 contains an example where $S$ is the line, $F$ is the two-dimensional space $\mathbb{R}^{2}$, and $\Lambda(s, 0)$ is exactly given by an inner product $\Lambda(s, 0)=-\left(e^{-s}-1, s\right)\left(X_{1}, X_{2}\right)^{\prime}$ in which $X_{1}$ is random nondegenerate and $X_{2}$ is identically unity.

This last example does not satisfy our condition (iii), which enforces a certain presence for the matrices $M$. This same (iii) also means that the approximation can be written in the form

$$
-\frac{1}{2}\left\{[Y-\xi(s)]^{\prime} M[Y-\xi(s)]-[Y-\xi(\theta)]^{\prime} M[Y-\xi(\theta)]\right\}
$$

In many applications, one can assert that the matrices $M$ are positive semidefinite and such that $u^{\prime} M u=0$ implies $u^{\prime} X=0$. In such a case the condition (iii) is certainly satisfied, and the approximation formula takes the form written just above. It does seem to contain centering variables $Y$ which may be used in statistical arguments. However, the situation is not that simple.

As was the case for the Taylor expansion, the variables $X$ are usually defined by local considerations and so will be, in the manifold case, the spaces $F$ and the maps $\xi$. For instance, $F$ may be a tangent space to a manifold $\Theta$ at a particular $\theta$, since this $\theta$ will play the role of unknown value. The variables $X$ and $Y$ are not "statistics" usable in the inference processes. To transform them appropriately one would need to use the relations between various points provided by the global structure of $\Theta$. This is feasible, under suitable assumptions, but leads to a rather complicated theory. To avoid the complexities involved we shall use a framework in which nothing is assumed about the algebraic structure of $\Theta$, the relevant relations taking place in the space $\mathscr{M}(\Theta)$ of finite signed measures with finite support on $\Theta$.

To introduce these spaces, consider a set $S$, as in Definition 1. Let $\mathscr{M}(S)$ be the space of finite signed measures with finite support on $S$. Let $\mathscr{M}_{0}(S)$ be the linear subspace of $\mathscr{M}(S)$ formed by those $\mu \in \mathscr{M}(S)$ such that $\mu(S)=0$, and let $\mathscr{M}_{1}(S)$ be the affine subspace of $\mathscr{M}(S)$ formed by $\mu$ such that $\mu(S)=1$. Let $\delta_{s}$ be the probability measure giving mass unity to the point $s$. One can imbed $S$ into $\mathscr{M}_{1}(S)$ by the map $s \mapsto \delta_{s}$.
