Assume now that $S$ and $\xi$ satisfy the requirements of Definition 1 above. For $\lambda \in \mathscr{M}(S)$ let $\xi(\lambda)=\int \xi(s) \lambda(d s)$. This is an element of $F$. Therefore $K(\lambda)=[\xi(\lambda)]^{\prime} M[\xi(\lambda)]$ is well defined. Restricted to $\mathscr{M}_{0}(S)$ the expression $K(\lambda)$ is a quadratic form on the linear space $\mathscr{M}_{0}(S)$.

In all the arguments, one may as well assume that the space F of Definition 1 is spanned by the set $\{\xi(s)-\xi(\theta) ; s \in S\}$, or, alternatively, one may assume that the variables $Y-\xi(\theta)$ of condition (iii) Definition 1, remain in that span.

If so, the variable $Y$ itself takes values which may be written in the form $\sum c(s) \xi(s)$. Another way of saying this is that $Y=\int \xi(s) Z(d s)$, where $Z$ is a certain random element with values in $\mathscr{M}_{1}(S)$.

Upon translation in this framework, the approximation postulated in Definition 1 says that the difference

$$
\Lambda(s, \theta)+\frac{1}{2}\left\{K\left(Z-\delta_{s}\right)-K\left(Z-\delta_{\theta}\right)\right\}
$$

tends to zero in probability.
Now $\mathscr{M}_{1}(S)$ is a subspace of $\mathscr{M}_{1}(\Theta)$ and $Z$ may be viewed as a random element with values in $\mathscr{M}_{1}(\Theta)$. Thus if different sets $S$ with different maps $\xi$ are used, the space $\mathscr{M}_{1}(\Theta)$ provides a convenient vehicle to link them together.

Note that in the process of passing from $F$ to $\mathscr{M}_{1}(\Theta)$ the map $\xi$ has disappeared. The matrix $M$ has been changed into a quadratic form defined on $\mathscr{M}_{0}(S)$, but perhaps not on the entire space $\mathscr{M}_{0}(\Theta)$. However, we are passing here from a space $F$ of finite dimension to spaces $\mathscr{M}_{1}(S)$ or $\mathscr{M}_{1}(\Theta)$ which are usually infinite dimensional. This may be embarrassing to the practitioner, but he needs to work only on a finite dimensional subspace of $\mathscr{M}_{1}(S)$ or $\mathscr{M}_{0}(S)$ and will encounter there just about the same problems as if working in the spaces $F$. There are, however, other features introduced by this embedding. For instance, if $F$ can be kept independent of $\theta$ and $S$, statements about asymptotic sufficiency of centerings with values in $F$ appear more palatable than similar statements for centerings in $\mathscr{M}_{1}(\Theta)$. The difference needs to be kept in mind in the formulation of several of the results given later on in this chapter.

In Definition 1 we have allowed only finite dimensional spaces $F$ for the range of the maps $\xi$. However, the definition does not prevent the dimension of $F$ to tend to infinity as the (suppressed) index $v$ does. This implies in particular that random variables such as $[Y-\xi(\theta)]^{\prime} M[Y-\xi(\theta)]=K\left(Z-\delta_{\theta}\right)$ need not remain bounded in probability. An example can be constructed as follows. Let $\Theta$ be an infinite dimensional Hilbert space and let $\left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ be the canonical Gaussian experiment of $\Theta$, as defined in Chapter 10. Take for $S$ the intersection of the unit ball of $\Theta$ by a finite dimensional subspace of $\Theta$. Let the dimension of the subspace tend to infinity as $v$ does.

Unfortunately, as we shall see later, many of the arguments yielding properties of centering variables or estimates do not work unless the dimension of the relevant spaces remains bounded.

One could also consider the possibilities of quadratic approximations of the same kind as those described by Definition 1, but using instead of the Euclidean spaces $F$, suitable infinite dimensional spaces. In such a case the argument carried above, with an embedding in $\mathscr{M}(S)$, does not necessarily hold. One needs to complete $\mathscr{M}(S)$ to obtain the same kind of assertion.

Under such circumstances, the symbols occurring in Definition 1 need to be reinterpreted. One possibility is as follows. Instead of writing inner products, consider that $F$ is an arbitrary linear space with a dual $F^{\prime}$. Let $M$ be a symmetric linear map from $F$ to $F^{\prime}$. Write $\langle x, y\rangle$ for the evaluation at $x \in F$
of the element $y \in F^{\prime}$. Taking into account condition (iii) of Definition 1, the approximation to $\Lambda(s, \theta)$ takes the form

$$
\langle\xi(s)-\xi(\theta), M[Y-\xi(\theta)]\rangle-\frac{1}{2}\langle\xi(s)-\xi(\theta), M[\xi(s)-\xi(\theta)]\rangle
$$

Such an approximation formula can be translated as before, using the space $\mathscr{M}(S)$ and related spaces. Formally, one would consider $\mathscr{M}(S)$, its dual $\mathscr{F}(S)$ formed by all real valued functions on $S$ and the algebraic dual $\mathscr{M}^{*}(S)$ of $\mathscr{F}(S)$. The space $\mathscr{F}(S)$ contains the function 1, identically unity. Thus $\mathscr{M}^{*}(S)$ has a linear subspace $\mathscr{M}_{0}^{*}(S)$ formed by those elements $\mu$ such that $\langle\mu, 1\rangle=0$. There is also the corresponding affine space $\mathscr{M}_{1}^{*}(S)$ formed by elements such that $\langle\mu, 1\rangle=1$.

Just as before, the quadratic $\langle\xi(s)-\xi(\theta), M[\xi(s)-\xi(\theta)]\rangle$ induces a quadratic form $K$ on a subset of $\mathscr{M}_{0}^{*}(S)$. On that same subspace, $K$ defines an inner product by the usual formula

$$
\langle\lambda, \mu\rangle_{K}=\frac{1}{2}[K(\lambda+\mu)-K(\lambda)-K(\mu)]
$$

Extending the definition of the inner product if necessary, the approximation formula would take the form

$$
\Lambda(s, \theta) \sim\left\langle\delta_{s}-\delta_{\theta}, Z-\delta_{\theta}\right\rangle_{K}-\frac{1}{2} K\left(\delta_{s}-\delta_{\theta}\right),
$$

where $Z$ is a random element taking values in $\mathscr{M}_{1}^{*}(S)$.
Such approximations may indeed be worth consideration. In fact we shall encounter them in the discussion of the asymptotically Gaussian cases. However, note that here it will generally not be feasible to rewrite the approximation formula in the form

$$
\Lambda(s, \theta) \sim-\frac{1}{2}\left\{K\left(Z-\delta_{s}\right)-K\left(Z-\delta_{\theta}\right)\right\}
$$

because, in all cases which are analogous to the Gaussian case, the values of $K\left(Z-\delta_{\theta}\right)$ will be almost surely infinite. This does not prevent the possibility that the formula is usable for all the indices $v$ of the underlying net or sequence, with values of $K\left(Z-\delta_{\theta}\right)$ tending in probability to infinity.

The reader will note that in the above we have taken for granted the possibility of using random variables with values in an infinite dimensional space $\mathscr{M}^{*}(S)$. In fact, such uses raise a variety of problems of measurability. They are not exceedingly serious, but are not entirely trivial. However, this is not the place to discuss them.

## 3. Construction of Centering Variables

In this section we consider an experiment $\mathscr{E}=\left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ and describe the construction of certain centering variables called $\hat{Z}$. The construction requires
the availability of several objects listed below. However, in this section itself no sufficiently restrictive assumptions are made on the object in question and therefore no remarkable conclusions on the properties of the centerings can be derived. The study of such properties will be taken up in the next sections, under further restrictions.

Note also that the construction involves only one experiment $\mathscr{E}$. There is no need here to consider a net $\left\{\mathscr{E}_{v}\right\}$ and, in fact, this is a very necessary feature from a practical standpoint, since in practice there will be no $v$ tending to infinity, but only hopefully reasonable approximations.

Let $\mathscr{M}(\Theta)$ be the space of all finite signed measures with finite support on $\Theta$ Let

$$
\mathscr{M}_{0}(\Theta)=\{\mu ; \mu \in \mathscr{M}(\Theta), \mu(\Theta)=0\}
$$

and

$$
\mathscr{M}_{1}(\Theta)=\{\mu ; \mu \in \mathscr{M}(\Theta), \mu(\Theta)=1\} .
$$

The spaces $\mathscr{M}(\Theta)$ and $\mathscr{M}_{0}(\Theta)$ are linear spaces. The space $\mathscr{M}_{1}(\Theta)$ is an affine space in which one can imbed $\Theta$, representing $\theta$ by the Dirac mass $\delta_{\theta}$.

Let $\Lambda(s, t)$ be the logarithm of likelihood ratio $\log d P_{t} / d P_{s}$. The aim of the construction is to approximate $\Lambda(s, t)$ by a quadratic expression

$$
-\frac{1}{2}\left\{\hat{K}\left(\hat{Z}-\delta_{s}\right)-\hat{K}\left(\hat{Z}-\delta_{t}\right)\right\}
$$

where $\hat{Z}$ is a "centering variable" and where $\hat{K}$ is a quadratic form on $\mathscr{M}_{0}(\Theta)$ or on suitable linear subspaces of $\mathscr{M}_{0}(\Theta)$. Of course, such approximations are not generally feasible. However, the construction itself can always be carried out as long as the required pieces are available, with very few assumptions needed.

After listing the necessary objects, we describe the construction and a modification of it using other objects. After these descriptions we give some explanations on the intended role of the various items.

If $S \subset \Theta$, let $\mathscr{M}_{i}(S)$ be the subspace of $\mathscr{M}_{i}(\Theta)$ formed by the measures whose support lies in the set $S$. The basic items involved are as follows.

## (A) Nonrandom items

(1) A subset $\Omega$ of $\Theta$.
(2) For each $\omega \in \Omega$, a finite subset $S_{\omega} \subset \Theta$ such that $\omega \in S_{\omega}$.

## (B) Random items, defined on the experiment $\mathscr{E}$

(1) For each $\omega \in \Omega$, a random function $\Lambda_{\omega}^{\prime}$ defined on $S_{\omega} \times S_{\omega}$ and taking values in $(-\infty,+\infty)$. One assumes $\Lambda_{\omega}^{\prime}(s, s) \equiv 0$.
(2) For each $\omega \in \Omega$, a quadratic form $K_{\omega}$ defined on the space $\mathscr{M}_{0}\left(S_{\omega}\right)$.
(3) An estimate $\hat{\theta}$ with values in $\Omega$.

In many cases, the quadratic forms $K_{\omega}$ are not directly available. They need to be constructed from other information. This will lead us to the modifications of the lists $(\mathrm{A})$ and $(\mathrm{B})$ described further on.

For the time being, assume that the items listed in (A) and (B) are available and note that, if $K$ is a quadratic form on a linear subspace of $\mathscr{M}(\Theta)$, it defines on that subspace a bilinear inner product by the relations

$$
\begin{aligned}
\langle\lambda, \mu\rangle_{K} & =\frac{1}{2}[K(\lambda+\mu)-K(\lambda)-K(\mu)] \\
& =-\frac{1}{2}[K(\lambda-\mu)-K(\lambda)-K(\mu)]
\end{aligned}
$$

The construction of the centerings $\hat{Z}$ is carried out as follows. Let $\omega$ be the value taken by the estimate $\hat{\theta}$. Solve the system of linear equations

$$
\left\langle Z_{\omega}-\delta_{\omega}, \delta_{s}-\delta_{\omega}\right\rangle_{K_{\omega}}=\Lambda_{\omega}^{\prime}(s, \omega)+\frac{1}{2} K_{\omega}\left(\delta_{s}-\delta_{\omega}\right)
$$

for $s \in S_{\omega}$ and for a solution $Z_{\omega} \in \mathscr{M}_{1}\left(S_{\omega}\right)$. If such a solution $Z_{\omega}$ exists it is the desired centering $\hat{Z}$. Finally let $\hat{K}=K_{\hat{\theta}}$.
(When no solutions exists, one can take "approximate" solutions, if that can be given a meaning).

According to the definition of the inner products, solving the linear equations is exactly the same thing as finding a $Z_{\omega} \in \mathscr{M}_{1}\left(S_{\omega}\right)$ such that

$$
\Lambda_{\omega}^{\prime}(s, \omega)=-\frac{1}{2}\left[K_{\omega}\left(Z_{\omega}-\delta_{s}\right)-K_{\omega}\left(Z_{\omega}-\delta_{\theta}\right)\right]
$$

for all $s \in S_{\omega}$. Therefore the construction does attempt to provide an approximation of the kind described earlier. However, it does it only on the set $S_{\omega}$ and for the function $\Lambda_{\omega}^{\prime}$ instead of the logarithms of likelihood ratio $\Lambda$ itself. It will be the purpose of Sections 4 and 5 to extend the approximation beyond the set $S_{\omega}$.

Concerning the existence of the solutions, one can assert the following. Consider triplets ( $S, K, \theta$ ) which consist of a set $S \subset \Theta$, a quadratic form $K$ on $\mathscr{M}_{0}(S)$, and a point $\theta \in S$. Let $W(s)=\Lambda^{\prime}(s, \theta)+\frac{1}{2} K\left(\delta_{s}-\delta_{\theta}\right)$ for a function $\Lambda^{\prime}$ such that $\Lambda^{\prime}(\theta, \theta)=0$.

Lemma 1. Let ( $S, K, \theta$ ) be as just described. Assume that $S$ is a finite set and that $K(\lambda) \geq 0$ for all $\lambda \in \mathscr{M}_{0}(S)$. Then there exists a $Z \in \mathscr{M}_{1}(S)$ such that

$$
\left\langle Z-\delta_{\theta}, \delta_{s}-\delta_{\theta}\right\rangle_{K}=W(s)
$$

for all $s \in S$ if and only if for $\lambda \in \mathscr{M}_{0}(S)$ the relation $K(\lambda)=0$ implies $\int W(s) \lambda(d s)=0$. If so, one has

$$
K\left(Z-\delta_{\theta}\right)=\sup \left\{\left|\int W(s) \lambda(d s)\right|^{2} K^{-1}(\lambda) ; \lambda \in \mathscr{M}_{0}(S), K(\lambda) \neq 0\right\}
$$

Proof. Let $V=Z-\delta_{\theta}$ and let $f(\lambda)=\int W(s) \lambda(d s)$. Using the fact that $W(\theta)=0$, the equations can also be written in the form

$$
\langle V, \lambda\rangle_{K}=f(\lambda)
$$

for $\lambda \in \mathscr{M}_{0}(S)$. The function $\lambda \mapsto f(\lambda)$ is a linear function on the space $\mathscr{M}_{0}(S)$.
When $K$ is positive on $\mathscr{M}_{0}(S)$ the function $\lambda \mapsto \sqrt{K(\lambda)}$ is a Hilbertian seminorm on $\mathscr{M}_{0}(S)$. Since $\mathscr{M}_{0}(S)$ is finite dimensional, it is complete for all semi-norms. A linear function $f$ defined on $\mathscr{M}_{0}(S)$ is continuous for the seminorm $\sqrt{K}$ if and only if $K(\lambda)=0$ implies $f(\lambda)=0$. If $f$ is continuous, it admits a representation $f(\lambda)=\langle V, \lambda\rangle_{K}$ for some $V \in \mathscr{M}_{0}(S)$. The norm of the element $V$ is given by the usual relation

$$
K(V)=\sup \left\{|\langle V, \lambda\rangle|_{K}^{2} K^{-1}(\lambda) ; \lambda \in \mathscr{M}_{0}(S), K(\lambda) \neq 0\right\}
$$

Hence the result as stated.
Note. The assertion in Lemma 1 does not remain valid if the positivity of $K$ is removed from the assumptions. However in any event, and for $S$ finite, a necessary and sufficient condition for the existence of the solutions of the equations $\langle V, \lambda\rangle_{K}=f(\lambda), \lambda \in \mathscr{M}_{0}(S)$ can be stated as follows. Call two elements $\lambda$ and $\mu$ of $\mathscr{M}_{0}(S)$ "orthogonal" if $\langle\lambda, \mu\rangle_{K}=0$. Then the equations have a solution if and only if $f(\lambda)=0$ for every $\lambda$ orthogonal to all $\mu \in \mathscr{M}_{0}(S)$.

When the quadratic forms $K_{\omega}$ of list (B) are not initially available it may be possible to reconstruct them from other objects. A particularly frequent occurrence leads to the following construction. Assume given the following items.

## ( $\mathbf{A}^{\prime}$ ) Nonrandom items

(1) $A$ subset $\Omega$ of $\Theta$.
(2) For each $\omega \in \Omega$, a finite subset $S_{\omega}$ of $\Theta$, such that $\omega \in S_{\omega}$.
(3) A function $m$ from $S_{\omega} \times S_{\omega}$ to $\Theta$, with $m(s, s)=s$ identically for $s \in S_{\omega}$.

## (B') Random items, defined by the experiment $\mathscr{E}$

Let $\widetilde{S}_{\omega}$ be the set $\left\{m(s, t) ;(s, t) \in S_{\omega} \times S_{\omega}\right\}$.
(1) For each $\omega \in \Omega$, a random function $\Lambda_{\omega}^{\prime}$ from $\widetilde{S}_{\omega} \times \widetilde{S}_{\omega}$ to ( $-\infty,+\infty$ ) with $\Lambda_{\omega}^{\prime}(s, s)=0$ identically.
(2) An estimate $\hat{\theta}$ with values in $\Omega$.

The points $m(s, t)$ in the list (A'3) will be treated as if they were "midpoints" between $s$ and $t$. We shall refer to them as "presumed mid-points." With the items in the lists ( $\mathrm{A}^{\prime}$ ) and ( $\mathrm{B}^{\prime}$ ), one first constructs a quadratic form $K_{\omega}$ on $\mathscr{M}_{0}\left(S_{\omega}\right)$ as follows.

For each pair ( $s, t$ ) in $S_{\omega} \times S_{\omega}$ let

$$
D(s, t)=-4\left\{\Lambda_{\omega}^{\prime}[s, m(s, t)]+\Lambda_{\omega}^{\prime}[t, m(s, t)]\right\}
$$

Then, for $\lambda \in \mathscr{M}_{0}\left(S_{\omega}\right)$ let

$$
K_{\omega}(\lambda)=-\frac{1}{2} \iint D(s, t) \lambda(d s) \lambda(d t)
$$

At this point, one may proceed as before, since now we have the quadratic forms required by (B2). However, it is usually more pleasant to work with positive quadratic forms, that is, with forms such that $K(\lambda) \geq 0$, for $\lambda$ in the domain of $K$. Thus one may wish to replace the $K_{\omega}$ just obtained by positive forms. For this, one may need to select a small $\varepsilon>0$ and proceed as follows.

Let $\|\lambda\|$ be the $L_{1}$-norm of the measure $\lambda$ and let $\alpha=\inf _{K} \sup _{\lambda} \mid K(\lambda)- K_{\omega}(\lambda) \mid$, where $\lambda$ ranges in the set $\left\{\lambda ; \lambda \in \mathscr{M}_{0}\left(S_{\omega}\right),\|\lambda\| \leq 1\right\}$ and $K$ ranges over the set of all positive quadratic forms on $\mathscr{M}_{0}\left(S_{\omega}\right)$. Then, replace $K_{\omega}$ by a $K_{\omega}^{\prime}$ which is positive and such that

$$
\sup _{\lambda}\left\{\left|K_{\omega}^{\prime}(\lambda)-K_{\omega}(\lambda)\right|: \lambda \in \mathscr{M}_{0}\left(S_{\omega}\right),\|\lambda\| \leq 1\right\} \leq \alpha+\varepsilon
$$

It should perhaps be mentioned that in practice one rarely performs the construction described here in the spaces $\mathscr{M}_{i}\left(S_{\omega}\right)$ themselves. Very often one has spaces $F$, maps $\xi$, and experiments for which $\xi$ provides a quadratic approximation in the sense of Definition 1, Section 2. One then works in the corresponding spaces $F$, returning to the space $\mathscr{M}_{1}(\Theta)$ only at the end of the operation. This being said, let us pass to the intended roles of the various items in the lists (A) and $(\mathrm{B})$ or the modified lists $\left(\mathrm{A}^{\prime}\right)$ and $\left(\mathrm{B}^{\prime}\right)$.

It should be clear that the functions $\Lambda_{\omega}^{\prime}$ are intended to be approximations to the logarithms of likelihood ratios $\Lambda$ defined by

$$
\Lambda(s, t)=\log \frac{d P_{t}}{d P_{s}}
$$

on the experiment $\mathscr{E}$ itself. However it is most convenient to allow the $\Lambda_{\mathscr{\omega}}^{\prime}$ to differ from the $\Lambda$ as long as the difference is not too large. If our solution $\hat{Z}$ exists, one has

$$
\Lambda_{\omega}^{\prime}(s, \omega)=-\frac{1}{2}\left[\hat{K}\left(\hat{Z}-\delta_{s}\right)-\hat{K}\left(\hat{Z}-\delta_{\omega}\right)\right]
$$

exactly for $s \in S_{\omega}, \omega=\hat{\theta}$. Thus $\Lambda(s, \omega)$ will also be approximated by the same quadratic expression. Thus ( $\hat{Z}, \hat{K}$ ) provides a quadratic approximation for $\Lambda(s, \omega), \omega=\hat{\theta}, s \in S_{\omega}$. Note that for theoretical purposes this will dispense us from discussing the case where the equations of the construction are solvable or solved only approximately. For practical purposes this feature allows the use of approximate solutions.

It should be noted that one needs a bit more than the representation of $\Lambda_{\omega}^{\prime}(s, \omega)$ described above. One would like to have at least



$$
\Lambda_{\omega}^{\prime}(s, t)=-\frac{1}{2}\left[\hat{K}\left(\hat{Z}-\delta_{s}\right)-\hat{K}\left(\hat{Z}-\delta_{t}\right)\right]
$$

for all pairs $(s, t)$ of elements of $S_{\omega}$. It is clear that this cannot be achieved unless $\Lambda_{\omega}^{\prime}$ satisfies an additivity relation. This leads to the following definition.

Definition 1. The function $\Lambda_{\omega}^{\prime}$ is additive on its domain $S_{\omega} \times S_{\omega}$ if for all triplets ( $s, t, u$ ) formed by elements of $S_{\omega}$ one has almost surely

$$
\Lambda_{\omega}^{\prime}(s, u)=\Lambda_{\omega}^{\prime}(s, t)+\Lambda_{\omega}^{\prime}(t, u)
$$

We shall almost always assume that this additivity relation is satisfied. It is automatic if one takes for $\Lambda^{\prime}$ the logarithms of likelihood ratios for an homogeneous experiment $\left\{P_{s}^{\prime} ; s \in S_{\omega}\right\}$.

This should explain the role of $\Lambda_{\omega}^{\prime}$. The role of the auxiliary estimate $\hat{\theta}$ is also clear: The sets $S_{\omega}$ are used for local computations and $\hat{\theta}$ indicates which local computation should be used. However, the role of the set $\Omega$ is more difficult to explain, because it is there entirely for technical reasons.

Roughly, an auxiliary estimate $\hat{\theta}$ may well pick out particular points at which the logarithms of likelihood behave in a peculiar manner. (Think, for instance, of a case where $\hat{\theta}$ is a maximum likelihood estimate, with $\Lambda=\Lambda_{\omega}^{\prime}$ and with $\Lambda(s, \hat{\theta})=-\infty$ for all $s$. This can happen even if the experiment $\mathscr{E}$ allows excellent approximations by quadratic expressions.)

The points $\omega \in \Omega$ and the points in $S_{\omega}$ ought to be selected to avoid misbehavior. In the next sections we shall enforce a sort of acceptable behavior by making $\Omega$ discrete and such that, for any $P_{\theta}$, the number of possible values of $\hat{\theta}$ to be taken into consideration is limited. There are, of course, other possibilities, such as enforcing smoothness of the $\Lambda_{\omega}^{\prime}$, or adding to $\hat{\theta}$ some randomization, etc.

Now let us pass to the role of the "presumed mid-points" of the list ( $\mathrm{A}^{\prime}$ ). Consider a set $S$ and a quadratic form $K$ on the space $\mathscr{M}_{0}(S)$. Note that, $\theta$ being a specified point of $S$, the space $\mathscr{M}_{0}(S)$ is linearly spanned by the differences $\delta_{s}-\delta_{\theta}, s \in S$. For any $\lambda \in \mathscr{M}_{0}(S)$, the value of $K$ can also be written

$$
K(\lambda)=\iint\left\langle\delta_{s}-\delta_{\theta}, \delta_{t}-\delta_{\theta}\right\rangle_{K} \lambda(d s) \lambda(d t)
$$

Now use the formula

$$
\left\langle\delta_{s}-\delta_{\theta}, \delta_{t}-\delta_{\theta}\right\rangle_{K}=-\frac{1}{2}\left\{K\left(\delta_{s}-\delta_{t}\right)-K\left(\delta_{s}-\delta_{\theta}\right)-K\left(\delta_{t}-\delta_{\theta}\right)\right\},
$$

and note that if $\lambda \in \mathscr{M}_{0}(S)$, integrals of the type $\int K\left(\delta_{t}-\delta_{\theta}\right) \lambda(d s)= K\left(\delta_{t}-\delta_{\theta}\right) \lambda(S)$ are equal to zero. It follows that for $\lambda \in \mathscr{M}_{0}(S)$ one has

$$
K(\lambda)=-\frac{1}{2} \iint K\left(\delta_{s}-\delta_{t}\right) \lambda(d s) \lambda(d t)
$$

To obtain $K$ it is therefore sufficient to compute the values $K\left(\delta_{s}-\delta_{t}\right) ;(s, t) \in S \times S$.

Going further, suppose that $S$ contains a triplet $T=(s, t, m)$ where $m$ is a "presumed mid-point" between $s$ and $t$. Interpret this mid-point property as meaning that $K\left[\delta_{m}-\frac{1}{2}\left(\delta_{s}+\delta_{t}\right)\right] \leq \alpha^{2}$ for some small $\alpha \geq 0$. Suppose also that

$$
\Lambda^{\prime}(u, v)=-\frac{1}{2}\left[K\left(Z-\delta_{u}\right)-K\left(Z-\delta_{v}\right)\right]
$$

for some $Z \in \mathscr{M}_{1}(S)$ and all pairs $(u, v) \in S \times S$. Then

$$
\begin{aligned}
4\left[\Lambda^{\prime}(s, m)+\Lambda^{\prime}(t, m)\right]+K\left(\delta_{s}-\delta_{t}\right)= & 8\left\langle Z-\delta_{m}, \delta_{m}-\frac{1}{2}\left(\delta_{s}+\delta_{t}\right)\right\rangle_{K} \\
& -4 K\left[\delta_{m}-\frac{1}{2}\left(\delta_{s}+\delta_{t}\right)\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

In this section we have taken $D(s, t)=-4\left[\Lambda^{\prime}(s, m)+\Lambda^{\prime}(t, m)\right]$. Therefore, if $K$ is positive on $\mathscr{M}_{0}(S)$, this will yield

$$
\left|D(s, t)-K\left(\delta_{s}-\delta_{t}\right)\right| \leq 4 \alpha^{2}+8 \alpha\left[K\left(Z-\delta_{m}\right)\right]^{1 / 2}
$$

If $\alpha=0$, that is, if $m$ is an exact mid-point between $s$ and $t$, one would have exactly $D(s, t)=K\left(\delta_{s}-\delta_{t}\right)$. When a small deviation $\alpha$ is allowed, whether $D(s, t)$ will be a good approximation for $K\left(\delta_{s}-\delta_{t}\right)$ may depend on the size of $K\left(Z-\delta_{m}\right)$. As we shall see in Section 5 the situation is not hopeless.

The foregoing argument may appear circular, since, to find a mid-point $m$ between $s$ and $t$, one seems to need to look for points $m$ for which $K\left[\delta_{m}-\frac{1}{2}\left(\delta_{s}+\delta_{t}\right)\right]$ is small. However, when $K$ is positive, smallness of $K\left[\delta_{m}-\frac{1}{2}\left(\delta_{s}+\delta_{t}\right)\right]$ is implied by smallness of $\phi\left[\delta_{m}-\frac{1}{2}\left(\delta_{s}+\delta_{t}\right)\right]$ where $\phi$ is any seminorm equivalent to the semi-norm $\sqrt{K}$ induced by $K$. One may have good information on the "size" of $K$ without knowing $K$ itself.

In many practical situations, one knows or hopes that locally the log likelihoods admit quadratic approximations in the sense of Definition 1, Section 2. This definition involves a map $\xi$ to some Euclidean space $F$. A presumed midpoint between $s$ and $t$ can then be obtained by searching for points $m \in \Theta$ such that $\xi(m)-\frac{1}{2}[\xi(s)+\xi(t)]$ is zero or sufficiently small.

Finally, note that we have often argued as if $K$ was positive. This will be the case in most practical situations. However, an estimate of $K$ need not be positive. In this connection the following remark is of interest. Take a set $S$, a function $D$ from $S \times S$ to the line and let

$$
K(\lambda)=-\frac{1}{2} \iint D(s, t) \lambda(d s) \lambda(d t)
$$

for $\lambda \in \mathscr{M}_{0}(S)$.
If $S$ consists of two points $s$ and $t$, the positivity requirement for $K$ means that $D(s, t) \geq 0$. If $S$ is a triplet ( $s, t, u$ ) the positivity requirement for $K$ means that $\sqrt{D}$ is a pseudo-metric on $S$. That is, $D$ satisfies the inequality

$$
\sqrt{D(s, u)} \leq \sqrt{D(s, t)}+\sqrt{D(t, u)}
$$

and the other inequalities obtained from this by permutation of the elements $s, t, u$. Even when not striving to enforce the positivity of $K$, it is often reasonable to insist that the function $D^{1 / 2}$ be a pseudo-metric.

## 4. Definitions Relative to Quadratic Approximations

In this section we consider a net $\left\{\mathscr{E}_{v}\right\}$ formed by experiments $\mathscr{E}_{v}=\left\{P_{\theta, v} ; \theta \in \Theta_{v}\right\}$. In principle all the objects involved in the arguments depend on the index $v$ whose function is to tend to infinity along a certain direction, net, or sequence. Thus we shall suppress $v$ from the notation as much as possible, calling "fixed" or "independent of $v$ " those particular objects that do not depend on the index $v$ 。

The section contains little in the way of assertions on the performance of the objects listed. It is mostly a collection of definitions used in subsequent sections to investigate the properties of the centerings $\hat{Z}$ defined in Section 3.

To express some of the requirements to be imposed, we shall often say that certain random variables are "bounded in probability" or eventually bounded in probability. By this will be meant the following.

For each $v$, let $\left\{\mathscr{X}_{v}, \mathscr{A}_{v}, P_{v}\right\}$ be a probability space and let $X_{v}$ be a function from $\mathscr{X}_{v}$ to $[-\infty,+\infty]$. It is not assumed that $X_{v}$ is measurable. One says that $X_{v}$ is (eventually) bounded in probability if, for each fixed $\varepsilon>0$, there is a fixed $b(\varepsilon) \in(0, \infty)$ and a $v(\varepsilon)$ such that $v \geq v(\varepsilon)$ implies $P_{v}^{*}\left\{\left|X_{v}\right| \geq b(\varepsilon)\right\}<\varepsilon$. The star on $P_{v}$ means that outer probabilities are intended. Similarly, we shall often say that certain probabilities tend to zero when what is actually meant is that outer probabilities tend to zero.

Considering the experiments $\mathscr{E}$ (with the index $v$ dropped, as agreed), let $S$ be a subset of $\Theta$ and let $K$ be a quadratic form defined on the space $\mathscr{M}_{0}(S)$. Recall that a quadratic form is a function from $\mathscr{M}_{0}(S)$ to the line such that $K(\alpha \mu)=\alpha^{2} K(\mu)$ and such that the corresponding inner product $\langle\lambda, \mu\rangle_{K}= \frac{1}{2}\{K(\lambda+\mu)-K(\lambda)-K(\mu)\}$ is bilinear. Such a $K$ will be called eventually positive for $P_{\theta}$ if the $P_{\theta}$ probability that $\inf \left\{K(\lambda) ; \lambda \in \mathscr{M}_{0}(S)\right\}=-\infty$ tends to zero.

Let $\phi_{1}$ and $\phi_{2}$ be two (possibly random) semi-norms on the space $\mathscr{M}_{0}(S)$. Call them related under $P_{\theta}$ if the variables

$$
\sup \left\{\left|\log \frac{\phi_{1}(\lambda)}{\phi_{2}(\lambda)}\right| ; \lambda \in \mathscr{M}_{0}(S), \lambda \neq 0\right\}
$$

are eventually bounded in probability, for $P_{\theta}$.
If two semi-norms $\phi_{1}$ and $\phi_{2}$ are related, they will be called interchangeable if in addition

$$
\sup _{\lambda}\left\{\left|\phi_{1}(\lambda)-\phi_{2}(\lambda)\right| ; \phi_{1}(\lambda) \leq 1\right\}
$$

tends to zero in probability.
For quadratic forms $K_{i}$ on $\mathscr{M}_{0}(S)$ which are eventually positive for $P_{\theta}$, the functions $\lambda \mapsto \sqrt{K_{i}(\lambda)}$ have large probability of being semi-norms. The labels related and interchangeable will then be applied to the $K_{i}$ if they apply to the induced semi-norms. By a further abuse of language, we shall also say that quadratics $K$ and random semi-norms are related if $K$ is eventually positive and if the semi-norms $\phi$ and $\sqrt{K}$ are related. Similar abuses of language will be made for interchangeability. The properties described by the following definition will be encountered in several arguments.

Definition 1. Let $S$ be a subset of $\Theta$ and let $K$ be a quadratic form on $\mathscr{M}_{0}(S)$. The quadratic $K$ is called non-dispersive if there exist nonrandom semi-norms $\phi$ which are related to $K$ for all $P_{\theta} ; \theta \in S$.

If the nonrandom semi-norm $\phi$ can be taken equal to the $L_{1}$-norm $\phi(\mu)=\|\mu\|$ on $\mathscr{M}_{0}(S)$, the pairs ( $S, K$ ) will be called free.

Now consider two pairs ( $S_{0}, K_{0}$ ) and ( $S_{1}, K$ ).
Definition 2. Let $K$ be a quadratic form defined on the space $\mathscr{M}_{0}(S), S= S_{0} \cup S_{1}$. One says that ( $S_{0}, K_{0}$ ) spans ( $S_{1}, K$ ) if the following three conditions are satisfied.
(i) There is a nonrandom map $s \mapsto \mu_{s}$ from $S_{1}$ to $\mathscr{M}_{1}\left(S_{0}\right)$ such that $K\left(\mu_{s}-\delta_{s}\right)$ tends to zero in probability for all $s \in S_{1}$ and for all $P_{\theta} ; \theta \in S$.
(ii) This map $s \mapsto \mu_{s}$ is such that $\sup \left\{\left\|\mu_{s}\right\| ; s \in S_{1}\right\} \leq b<\infty$ for some fixed number $b$.
(iii) On $\mathscr{M}_{0}\left(S_{0}\right)$ the two quadratic forms $K$ and $K_{0}$ are interchangeable for all $P_{\theta}, \theta \in S$.

It can be shown that when $K$ is non-dispersive the existence of random maps satisfying (i) and (ii) implies the existence of nonrandom maps with the same properties.

We shall call subsets $S \subset \Theta$ contiguity classes if for all pairs $(s, t) \in S \times S$ the nets $\left\{P_{s}\right\}$ and $\left\{P_{t}\right\}$ are contiguous. For such classes the expression "for all $P_{\theta}, \theta \in S$ " which occurs in the preceding definitions can be replaced by "for some $P_{\theta}, \theta \in S^{\prime \prime}$. It will then often be omitted to alleviate the text.

The properties described so far do not involve the fine structure of the experiments $\mathscr{E}=\left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$. The next one does. To express it, assume that $\mathscr{E}_{S}=\left\{P_{S} ; s \in S\right\}$ is defined by measures $P_{s}$ on a certain $\sigma$-field $\mathscr{A}$. Let $\mathscr{E}_{S}^{\prime}=\left\{P_{s}^{\prime} ; s \in S\right\}$ be another experiment constituted by measures $P_{s}^{\prime}$ defined on the same $\sigma$-field $\mathscr{A}$.

Call the two experiments $\mathscr{E}_{S}^{\prime}$ and $\mathscr{E}_{S} L_{1}$-equivalent if $\sup \left\{\left\|P_{s}^{\prime}-P_{s}\right\| ; s \in S\right\}$ tends to zero. Now consider a pair ( $S, K$ ) formed by a set $S \subset \Theta$ and a quadratic form $K$ on $\mathscr{M}_{0}(S)$.

Definition 3. The experiments $\mathscr{E}_{S}=\left\{P_{s} ; s \in S\right\}$ are called $K$-controlled if there exists a choice of experiments $\mathscr{E}_{S}^{\prime}$, corresponding logarithms of likelihood ratios $\Lambda^{\prime}$, and points $\theta \in S$ such that
(i) $\mathscr{E}_{S}$ and $\mathscr{E}_{S}^{\prime}$ are $L_{1}$-equivalent,
(ii) the $\Lambda^{\prime}$ are finite valued (almost surely).
(iii) Let $W(s, \theta)=\Lambda^{\prime}(s, \theta)+\frac{1}{2} K\left(\delta_{s}-\delta_{\theta}\right)$. Then if measures $\mu \in \mathscr{M}_{0}(S)$ are selected nonrandomly so that $\|\mu\| \leq 1$ and so that $K(\mu)$ tends to zero in $P_{t}$ probability ( $t \in S$ ), the expressions $\int W(s, \theta) \mu(d s)$ also tend to zero in $P_{t}$ probability. Furthermore this remains true for subnets of the original net indexing the experiments $\mathscr{E}$.
(When the experiments are indexed by the integers in their natural order, the extra clause on subnets may be deleted.)

This definition calls for some comments. First note that the $\mathscr{E}_{S}$ themselves are called $K$-controlled. The experiments $\mathscr{E}_{S}^{\prime}$ will occur in proofs, but not in the applications.

Second, it would be most pleasant to consider a stronger property in which the restriction that $\|\mu\|$ remains bounded would be removed. Unfortunately, it becomes then difficult to deduce this stronger property from other assumptions, even in very simple situations with sets $S$ of bounded cardinality. The restriction to nonrandom measures $\mu$ proceeds from similar considerations.

As stated, and for $K$ positive, the property of being $K$-controlled represents some sort of equicontinuity relation for the linear functions $\mu \mapsto f(\mu)= \int W(s, \theta) \mu(d s)$ defined on $\mathscr{M}_{0}(S)$, normed by $\sqrt{K}$. Note, however, that $K$ occurs there twice, since it is already involved in the definition of $W(s, \theta)$.

Thus an experiment $\mathscr{E}_{S}$ which is $K$-controlled is not necessarily $K_{1}$-controlled for another quadratic form $K_{1}$ related to $K$. In fact, the discussion given at the end of Section 3, using presumed midpoints, suggests that when $S$ is sufficiently rich, the quadratics $K$ which provide $K$-control are fairly well determined.

Suppose, for instance, that a pair ( $s, t$ ) of points of $S$ admits a mid-point $m \in S$ such that $K\left[\delta_{m}-\frac{1}{2}\left(\delta_{s}+\delta_{t}\right)\right]=0$. Then, if $\mathscr{E}_{S}$ is $K$-controlled, one has $\int W(s, \theta) \mu(d s)=0$ for the measure $\mu=\delta_{m}-\frac{1}{2}\left(\delta_{s}+\delta_{t}\right)$. Writing this out in terms of $\Lambda^{\prime}$ and using the median identity for $K$ yields

$$
\Lambda^{\prime}(m, \theta)-\frac{1}{2}\left[\Lambda^{\prime}(s, \theta)+\Lambda^{\prime}(t, \theta)\right]=\frac{1}{8} K\left(\delta_{s}-\delta_{t}\right)
$$

Here we have assumed that $\Lambda^{\prime}$ is finite. Therefore it satisfies the additivity relations of Section 3 almost surely for any particular triplet of points of $S$ (for the probabilities $P_{s}^{\prime}, s \in S$ ). The relation written above could also be written

$$
\Lambda^{\prime}(s, m)+\Lambda^{\prime}(t, m)=-\frac{1}{4} K\left(\delta_{s}-\delta_{t}\right)
$$

It determines $K$ in function of the $\Lambda^{\prime}$.
The definition also involves appropriate selection of a particular point $\theta \in S$. This is not too serious. Indeed, according to the additivity relations just recalled, one will have almost surely for the measures $P_{s}^{\prime}$ and for each nonrandom $\mu \in \mathscr{M}_{0}(S)$ the relations

$$
\int W(s, \omega) \mu(d s)=\int W(s, \theta) \mu(d s)+\left\langle\mu, \delta_{\theta}-\delta_{\omega}\right\rangle_{K}
$$

Therefore

$$
\left|\int W(s, \omega) \mu(d s)\right|^{2} \leq 2\left|\int W(s, \theta) \mu(d s)\right|^{2}+2 K\left(\delta_{\theta}-\delta_{\omega}\right) K(\mu)
$$

and the $K\left(\delta_{\theta}-\delta_{\omega}\right)$ will usually be assumed to be bounded in probability. The relation of Definition 3 is obviously intended to imply that logarithms of likelihood ratios admit quadratic approximations, in the spirit of the discussion of Section 2, and with quadratic terms which have enough "presence" to control the situation. However, it also contains some hidden implications.

Suppose, for instance, that $\mathscr{E}_{S}$ is $K$-controlled and that the variables $K\left(\delta_{s}-\delta_{\theta}\right)$ are bounded in probability, for $P_{s}$ and $P_{\theta}$. Then the nets $\left\{P_{s}\right\},\left\{P_{\theta}\right\}$ are contiguous. To show this, consider the measure $\lambda=\delta_{s}-\delta_{\theta}$ so that $\int W(s, \theta) \lambda(d s)= \Lambda^{\prime}(s, \theta)+\frac{1}{2} K\left(\delta_{s}-\delta_{\theta}\right)$. Let $\mu=\alpha \lambda$ for numbers $\alpha$ which tend to zero. Then $K(\mu)=\alpha K\left(\delta_{s}-\delta_{\theta}\right)$ tends to zero in probability. The $K$-control says then that $\alpha \Lambda^{\prime}(s, \theta)$ also tends to zero in probability for $P_{s}$ and $P_{\theta}$. If $\left\{P_{s}\right\}$ and $\left\{P_{\theta}\right\}$ were not contiguous, one could find a subnet and numbers $\alpha$ tending to zero along it such that $\left(P_{s}+P_{\theta}\right)\left\{\left|\Lambda^{\prime}(s, \theta)\right| \geq \alpha^{-1}\right\}$ does not tend to zero.

The following lemma indicates that Definition 3 is not empty. It will be used repeatedly in the discussions of Section 5 and 6 below.

Lemma 1. Let $S$ be a contiguity class and let the cardinality of $S$ be bounded (independently of $v)$. Assume that $(S, K)$ is free. Then $\mathscr{E}_{S}$ is $K$-controlled. There are variables $Z$ with values in $\mathscr{M}_{1}(S)$ such that
(i) $K\left(Z-\delta_{\theta}\right)$ is bounded in probability;
(ii) $\sup _{s, \theta}\left\{\left|\Lambda(s, \theta)+\frac{1}{2}\left[K\left(Z-\delta_{s}\right)-K\left(Z-\delta_{\theta}\right)\right]\right| ;(s, \theta) \in S \times S\right\}$ tends to zero in probability.

Proof. If the sets $S$ have bounded cardinality, the contiguity assumption implies that, for arbitrary choices of the Radon-Nikodym densities, the quantities $\sup \{|\Lambda(t, s)| ;(s, t) \in S \times S\}$ are bounded in probability. Since ( $S, K$ ) is free, the variables sup $\left\{\left|K\left(\delta_{s}-\delta_{t}\right)\right| ;(s, t) \in S \times S\right\}$ are also bounded in probability. Thus

$$
\left|\int\left[\Lambda(s, \theta)+\frac{1}{2} K\left(\delta_{s}-\delta_{\theta}\right)\right] \mu(d s)\right| \leq \xi\|\mu\|
$$

for a certain random variable $\xi$ which is bounded in probability. Replacing $\|\mu\|$ by a bound $\|\mu\| \leq b \sqrt{K(\mu)}$ with $b$ bounded in probability, one sees that $\mathscr{E}_{S}$ is $K$-controlled. The other assertions are easy consequences of Lemma 1, Section 3.

If $S$ consists of two points only, say $s$ and $\theta$, in order that ( $S, K$ ) be free it is necessary and sufficient that $K\left(\delta_{s}-\delta_{\theta}\right)$ be eventually positive with $\log K\left(\delta_{s}-\delta_{\theta}\right)$ bounded in probability. Thus if $(S, K)$ is free and if $\mathscr{E}_{S}$ is $K$ controlled, $S$ must be a contiguity class. If $S$ does not have bounded cardinality, but ( $S, K$ ) is free, one can still obtain results analogous to those of Lemma 1. However, some supplementary assumptions seem necessary. To indicate one possibility, consider a case where ( $S, K$ ) is free and where

$$
\sup \left\{K\left(\delta_{s}-\delta_{t}\right) ;(s, t) \in S \times S\right\} \leq b
$$

for some fixed finite number $b$. Then to bound integrals of the type $\int\left[\Lambda^{\prime}(s, \theta)+\frac{1}{2} K\left(\delta_{s}-\delta_{\theta}\right)\right] \mu(d s)$ by multiples of $\|\mu\|$ or $\sqrt{K(\mu)}$ it will be sufficient to obtain bounds on the integrals $\int \Lambda^{\prime}(s, \theta) \mu(d s)$.

If $\Lambda^{\prime}(s, \theta)$ is a logarithm of likelihood ratio for some experiment $\mathscr{E}_{S}^{\prime}= \left\{P_{s}^{\prime} ; s \in S\right\}$, then $E_{\theta} \exp \left\{\Lambda^{\prime}(s, \theta)\right\} \leq 1$. Therefore, according to Hölder's inequality $E_{\theta} \exp \left\{\int \Lambda^{\prime}(s, \theta) \mu(d s)\right\} \leq 1$ for every probability measure $\mu \in \mathscr{M}_{1}(S)$. Thus, for such probability measures, $E_{\theta}\left[\int \Lambda^{\prime}(s, \theta) \mu(d s)\right]^{+} \leq 1$. The values of $\Lambda^{\prime}$ which may cause trouble are the large values of $\left[\Lambda^{\prime}(s, \theta)\right]^{-}$.

Now take an arbitrary positive number $x \geq 1$. Let $M_{s}=P_{s} \vee\left(e^{-x} P_{\theta}\right)$ and let $Q_{s, x}=\left\|M_{s}\right\|^{-1} M_{s}$. One has $M_{s} \geq P_{s}$ and $\left\|M_{s}-P_{s}\right\| \leq e^{-x} P_{\theta}\{\Lambda(s, \theta)< -x\}$. Therefore $\left\|P_{s}-Q_{s, x}\right\| \leq 2 e^{-x} P_{\theta}\{\Lambda(s, \theta)<-x\}$. The measures $Q_{s, x}$ form an experiment $\mathscr{F}=\left\{Q_{s, x} ; s \in S\right\}$ which may be made as close to $\mathscr{E}_{S}$ as desired by selecting $x$ sufficiently large. For the experiment $\mathscr{F}$, the logarithms of likelihood ratios $\Lambda^{\prime}$ may be selected so that

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Lambda^{\prime}(s, \theta) & \geq-x-\log \left\{1+e^{-x} P_{\theta}[\Lambda(s, \theta)<-x]\right\} \\
& \geq-(x+1)
\end{aligned}
$$

If so, one has $\int \Lambda^{\prime}(s, \theta) \mu(d s) \geq-(x+1)$ for every probability measure $\mu \in \mathscr{M}_{1}(S)$, and therefore $E_{\theta}\left|\int \Lambda^{\prime}(s, \theta) \mu(d s)\right| \leq x+2$ for all such measures. It follows that if $\mu$ is an arbitrary element of $\mathscr{M}_{0}(S)$ then $E_{\theta}\left|\int \Lambda^{\prime}(s, \theta) \mu(d s)\right| \leq (x+2)\|\mu\|$.

One could also argue similarly that

$$
P_{\theta}\left\{\left|\int \Lambda^{\prime}(s, \theta) \mu(d s)\right| \geq(x+1)\|\mu\|\right\} \leq 2 e^{-x}
$$

This suggests that when ( $S, K$ ) is free and when $S$ is a contiguity class, one may be able to let $x$ tend to infinity as $v$ increases and find $L_{1}$-equivalent experiments $\mathscr{F}=\left\{Q_{s, x} ; s \in S\right\}$ such that $\int \Lambda^{\prime}(s, \theta) \mu(d s)$ tends to zero in probability whenever $\|\mu\|$ (or $K(\mu)$ ) does. However we do not know under what conditions this can actually be done.

The Definitions 2 and 3 above will be used repeatedly in Sections 5 and 6 below. However, here is a lemma which exhibits at least part of their intended role.

Lemma 2. Let ( $S, K$ ) be such that $\mathscr{E}_{S}$ is $K$-controlled. Let $S_{0}$ be a subset of $S$ and let $\theta$ be a particular point of $S_{0}$. Assume that the following requirements are satisfied:
(i) For all $s \in S$ the variables $K\left(\delta_{s}-\delta_{\theta}\right)$ are bounded in $P_{s}$ and $P_{\theta}$ probability.
(ii) There are nonrandom $\mu_{s} \in \mathscr{M}_{1}\left(S_{0}\right)$ such that $K\left(\mu_{s}-\delta_{s}\right)$ tends to zero in probability, and such that $\|s\|$ stays bounded.
(iii) For some experiment $\mathscr{E}_{S}^{\prime}=\left\{P_{s}^{\prime} ; s \in S\right\}$ which satisfies the requirements of Definition 3 for a particular choice $\Lambda^{\prime}$ of logarithms of likelihood ratios, the experiments $\mathscr{E}_{S_{0}}^{\prime}=\left\{P_{s}^{\prime} ; s \in S_{0}\right\}$ admit centerings $Z \in \mathscr{M}_{1}\left(S_{0}\right)$ such that

$$
\Lambda^{\prime}(s, \theta)=-\frac{1}{2}\left[K\left(Z-\delta_{s}\right)-K\left(Z-\delta_{\theta}\right)\right]
$$

for all $s \in S_{0}$.
Then, for arbitrary choices of the logarithms $\Lambda(s, \theta)=\log \left(d P_{s} / d P_{\theta}\right)$ and for all $s \in S$, the differences

$$
\Lambda(s, \theta)+\frac{1}{2}\left[K\left(Z-\mu_{s}\right)-K\left(Z-\delta_{\theta}\right)\right]
$$

tend to zero in probability ( for all $P_{t} ; t \in S$ ).
Proof. Note first that the assertion refers to $\Lambda(s, \theta)$ itself, not to the $\Lambda^{\prime}$ selected for the experiments $\mathscr{E}_{S}^{\prime}$. However this is immaterial because Condition (i) and the $K$-control imply that $S$ is a contiguity class. Therefore $\Lambda^{\prime}(s, \theta)- \Lambda(s, \theta)$ tends to zero in probability whenever $\left\|P_{s}^{\prime}-P_{s}\right\|$ tends to zero. Now let $W(s, \theta)=\Lambda^{\prime}(s, \theta)+\frac{1}{2} K\left(\delta_{s}-\delta_{\theta}\right)$. By the control assumption, any norm bounded nonrandom choice of elements $\lambda \in \mathscr{M}_{1}(S)$ such that $K(\lambda) \rightarrow 0$ is also such that $\int W(s, \theta) \lambda(d s)$ tends to zero in probability. This applies in particular to $\lambda=\mu_{s}-\delta_{s}$. Rewriting this in terms of $\Lambda^{\prime}$, it follows that

$$
\Lambda^{\prime}(s, \theta)+\frac{1}{2} K\left(\delta_{s}-\delta_{\theta}\right)-\int W(t, \theta) \mu_{s}(d t)
$$

tends to zero in probability. Taking into account that $W(\theta, \theta)=0$ and that $\mu_{s} \in \mathscr{M}_{1}\left(S_{0}\right)$, one can rewrite the integral term in the form

$$
\int W(t, \theta) \mu_{s}(d t)=\left\langle Z-\delta_{\theta}, \mu_{s}-\delta_{\theta}\right\rangle_{K}
$$

for the centering $Z$ taken on ( $S_{0}, K$ ). Thus, one may write
$\Lambda^{\prime}(s, \theta)=-\frac{1}{2}\left[K\left(Z-\mu_{s}\right)-K\left(Z-\delta_{\theta}\right)\right]+\frac{1}{2}\left[K\left(\mu_{s}-\delta_{\theta}\right)-K\left(\delta_{s}-\delta_{\theta}\right)\right]+\varepsilon(s)$, where $\varepsilon(s)$ tends to zero in probability. Since $K\left(\delta_{s}-\delta_{\theta}\right)$ stays bounded and since $K\left(\mu_{s}-\delta_{s}\right)$ tends to zero, the difference $K\left(\mu_{s}-\delta_{\theta}\right)-K\left(\delta_{s}-\delta_{\theta}\right)$ tends to zero in probability. Hence the result as stated.
(The statement of the lemma assumes that $K\left(Z-\delta_{\theta}\right)$ is finite. If not one could still say that

$$
\Lambda^{\prime}(s, \theta)-\left\{\left\langle Z-\delta_{\theta}, \mu_{s}-\delta_{\theta}\right\rangle_{K}-\frac{1}{2} K\left(\mu_{s}-\delta_{\theta}\right)\right\}
$$

tends to zero in probability.)
As can be seen, the result refers to a centering $Z$ obtained on $S_{0}$ but for a quadratic form obtained by restricting the $K$ defined on all of $\mathscr{M}_{0}(S)$ to the subspace $\mathscr{M}_{0}\left(S_{0}\right)$. Also, the $\Lambda^{\prime}$ used to compute the centerings are those selected from the experiment $\mathscr{E}_{S}^{\prime}$ used to approximate $\mathscr{E}_{S}$ on the entire set $S$. To obtain applicable results it will be necessary to modify this.

We shall show, in Section 5, that one may proceed analogously with centerings defined on a pair ( $S_{0}, K_{0}$ ) where $K_{0}$ is defined only on $\mathscr{M}_{0}\left(S_{0}\right)$ and for experiments $\mathscr{E}_{S_{0}}^{\prime \prime}=\left\{P_{s}^{\prime \prime} ; s \in S_{0}\right\}$ which need not coincide with the restrictions of the $\mathscr{E}_{S}^{\prime}$ used here. Finally, it will be necessary to replace the measures $\mu_{s}$ by $\delta_{s}$ itself. This will necessitate a few additional assumptions.

## 5. Asymptotic Properties of the Centerings $\hat{Z}$

In this section we consider a net $\left\{\mathscr{E}_{v}\right\}$ of experiments $\mathscr{E}_{v}=\left\{P_{\theta, v} ; \theta \in \Theta_{v}\right\}$ and suppress the index $v$ from the notation whenever possible.

We assume also, throughout, that each $\mathscr{E}_{v}$ comes equipped with all the items listed in Section 3, Lists (A) and (B), or alternatively that the items of Section 3, Lists ( $\mathrm{A}^{\prime}$ ) and ( $\mathrm{B}^{\prime}$ ) have been used to reconstruct the objects of the Lists (A) and (B). The purpose of this section is to show that, under suitable restrictions, the logarithms of likelihood ratios of the $\mathscr{E}_{v}$ can be approximated by the resulting quadratic differences so that $\Lambda(s, \theta)$ is approximately $-\frac{1}{2}\left\{\hat{K}\left(\hat{Z}-\delta_{s}\right)-\hat{K}\left(\hat{Z}-\delta_{\theta}\right)\right\}$.

It would be pleasant if such an assertion could be made broadside for all pairs $(s, \theta) \in \Theta_{v} \times \Theta_{v}$. However, this will not be the case for most applications. Thus, the assertions will involve certain nets $\left\{\theta_{v}\right\}$ with $\theta_{v} \in \Theta_{v}$ and points $s_{v}$ belonging to suitable sets $S_{\nu}$ in the vicinity of $\theta_{\nu}$. Note, however, that to simplify the notations, we shall suppress the index $v$. Even when $\Theta$ itself does not depend on $v$, a statement of the type "if $\theta$ satisfies conditions $C, D, E$, then $\ldots$ " is still a statement about a net $\left\{\theta_{v}\right\}, \theta_{v} \in \Theta$. Therefore the assertions made here will retain certain uniformity features but usually not on the entire sets $\Theta_{v}$.

To state assumptions and properties, consider $\Theta$ as a metric space for the Hellinger distance $h$ defined by $h^{2}(s, t)=1-\int \sqrt{d P_{s} d P_{t}}$. Let $q \geq 0$ be the function defined on $\Theta \times \Theta$ by

$$
q^{2}(s, t)=-8 \log \int \sqrt{d P_{s} d P_{t}}
$$

and keep in mind that, unless the contrary is explicitly stated, everything in sight depends on the suppressed index $v$.

About the sets $S_{\omega}$ and the functions $\Lambda_{\omega}^{\prime}$ used in the construction, we shall make the following blanket assumption.
(B) (i) For each $\omega \in \Omega$, the q-diameter $\sup \left\{q(s, t) ;(s, t) \in S_{\omega} \times S_{\omega}\right\}$ of $S_{\omega}$ remains bounded.
(ii) For each $\omega \in \Omega$, the functions $\Lambda_{\omega}^{\prime}$ are logarithms of likelihood ratios for an experiment $\mathscr{E}_{\omega}^{\prime}=\left\{P_{s}^{\prime} ; s \in S_{\omega}\right\}$ such that $\sup _{s}\left\{\left\|P_{s}^{\prime}-P_{s}\right\| ; s \in S_{\omega}\right\}$ tends to zero. Furthermore $\mathscr{E}_{\omega}^{\prime}$ is homogeneous.
(iii) If presumed midpoints are used, then the foregoing (ii) is valid with $S_{\omega}$ replaced by the set $\widetilde{S}_{\omega}$ obtained by adjoining its mid-points to $S_{\omega}$.

This will be used all the way through Sections 5 and 6, and if it happens to be forgotten in some statements, it is supposed to be there anyway. Of course this is mostly a requirement placed on the construction itself, not on the experiments themselves. However, with the homogeneity required in (ii), it may imply a slight restriction on the net $\{v\}$. Indeed, some of the $\left\{P_{s} ; s \in S_{\omega}\right\}$ may not be homogeneous and we require them to be approximable by homogeneous ones. Since the sets $S_{\omega}$ are finite, this is always possible if the net $\{v\}$ is not pathological, that is, if there are numbers $\varepsilon_{v}, \varepsilon_{v}>0$ which tend to zero.

Otherwise (B) just records the intention of the construction. Now to pass to some other conditions.

Definition 1. Points $\theta \in \Theta$ are of type ( C ) if, for each fixed $b<\infty$, the relation $q(\theta, s) \leq b$ implies that the nets $\left\{P_{s}\right\}$ and $\left\{P_{\theta}\right\}$ are contiguous.

In other words, $\theta$ is of type ( C ) if for each fixed $\alpha>0$ the balls $\Theta(\theta, b)= \{s ; s \in \Theta, h(s, \theta) \leq 1-\alpha\}$ are contiguity classes in the sense of Section 4.

Definition 2. Points $\theta \in \Theta$ are of type (D) if
(i) for every fixed $b>\infty$ the cardinality of the sets $\Omega(\theta, b)=\{s ; s \in \Omega, q(s, \theta)< b$ \} remains bounded;
(ii) for $\omega \in \Omega$ such that $q(\theta, \omega)$ remains bounded, the cardinality card $S_{\omega}$ also remains bounded.

Definition 3. Points $\theta \in \Theta$ satisfy Condition (E) if the auxiliary estimate $\hat{\theta}$ is $q$-convergent in the sense that $q(\hat{\theta}, \theta)$ is bounded in $P_{\theta}$-probability

List (B) of Section 3 involves also the random quadratic forms $K_{\omega}$ and the random processes $\Lambda_{\omega}^{\prime}$. For their combination, let us say that the centering $Z_{\omega}$ exist if they satisfy the relations

$$
\Lambda_{\omega}^{\prime}(s, \omega)=-\frac{1}{2}\left\{K_{\omega}\left(Z_{\omega}-\delta_{s}\right)-K_{\omega}\left(Z_{\omega}-\delta_{\omega}\right)\right\}
$$

for all $s \in S_{\omega}$.
Definition 4. The systems ( $S_{\omega}, K_{\omega}, \Lambda_{\omega}^{\prime}$ ) satisfy Condition (F) at $\theta$ if for all $\omega \in \Omega$ such that $q(\theta, \omega)$ remains bounded, the following relations hold.
(i) The forms $K_{\omega}$ are eventually positive on $\mathscr{M}_{0}\left(S_{\omega}\right)$.
(ii) The centerings $Z_{\omega}$ exist.
(iii) They are strong in the sense that $K_{\omega}\left(Z_{\omega}-\delta_{\omega}\right)$ is bounded in $P_{\theta}$ probability.

With all these definitions, it will be possible to state an approximation theorem relative to the centerings $\hat{Z}$ of Section 3, at least for certain sets $S$. Before proceeding, let us note that the function $q$ used here need not be a distance or pseudo-distance. That is, it need not satisfy the triangular inequality. However, the Condition (C) of Definition 1 makes it possible to use it somewhat in the same manner as a distance according to the following lemma.

Lemma 1. Let $\theta$ be of type (C) and let $s$ and $t$ be elements of $\Theta$ such that both $q(\theta, s)$ and $q(s, t)$ remain bounded. Then so does $q(\theta, t)$ and the three nets $\left\{P_{\theta}\right\},\left\{P_{s}\right\}$, and $\left\{P_{t}\right\}$ are contiguous.

Proof. Let $X=\left(d P_{s} / d P_{\theta}\right)$ and let $Y=\left(d P_{t} / d P_{s}\right)$. By assumption, there is some fixed $b$ such that $q(s, t) \leq b$. Equivalently, there is a fixed $\varepsilon_{1}>0$ such that $\int \sqrt{d P_{s} d P_{t}}=\int \sqrt{Y} d P_{s} \geq \varepsilon_{1}$. Thus, by the assumed contiguity of $\left\{P_{s}\right\}$ and $\left\{P_{\theta}\right\}$, there is some fixed $\varepsilon_{2}>0$ such that $P_{\theta}\left\{\sqrt{Y} \geq \varepsilon_{2}\right\} \geq 2 \varepsilon_{2}$. Similarly, for any fixed $\varepsilon_{3}>0$ there is a fixed $b$ such that $\left(P_{\theta}+P_{s}\right)\{|\log X|>b\}<\varepsilon_{3}$. Taking $\varepsilon_{3}<\varepsilon_{2}$ one sees that $P_{\theta}\left\{\sqrt{X Y} \geq e^{-b} \varepsilon_{2}\right\} \geq \varepsilon_{2}$. However $\int \sqrt{X Y} d P_{\theta} \leq \int \sqrt{d P_{t} d P_{\theta}}$. Therefore $q(\theta, t)$ remains bounded and the desired result follows.

To proceed to an approximation result, consider a point $\theta \in \Theta$ and a subset $S \subset \Theta$ such that $\theta \in S$. Let $K$ be a random quadratic form defined on certain subspaces of $\mathscr{M}_{0}(\Theta)$.



Theorem 1. Assume that at $\theta$ the conditions (B), (C), (D), (E), and (F) are all satisfied. Assume also that if $\omega \in \Omega$ is such that $q(\theta, \omega)$ remains bounded, then all the following requirements are satisfied.
(i) $K$ is eventually defined on $\mathscr{M}_{0}\left(S \cup S_{\omega}\right)$.
(ii) For $s \in S \cup S_{\omega}$, the variables $K\left(\delta_{s}-\delta_{\theta}\right)$ are bounded in $P_{\theta}$ and $P_{s}$ probability.
(iii) The experiments $\left\{P_{s} ; s \in S \cup S_{\omega}\right\}$ are $K$-controlled in the sense of Section 4, Definition 3.
(iv) The pairs ( $S_{\omega}, K_{\omega}$ ) span the pairs ( $S, K$ ) in the sense of Section 4, Definition 2.

Then, for all $s \in S$, the difference

$$
\left.\Lambda(s, \theta)+\frac{1}{2}\left[K\left(\hat{Z}-\delta_{s}\right)-K \hat{Z}-\delta_{\theta}\right)\right]
$$

tends to zero in probability. Furthermore the variables $K\left(\hat{Z}-\delta_{s}\right)$ are bounded in $P_{s}$ probability.

Proof. It has already been noted in Section 4 that the combination of the conditions (ii) and (iii) above implies that if $s \in S$ then $\left\{P_{s}\right\}$ and $\left\{P_{\theta}\right\}$ are contiguous. Thus $\sup \{q(s, \theta) ; s \in S\}$ will remain bounded. The equivalence of $K$ and $K_{\omega}$ on the space $\mathscr{M}_{0}\left(S_{\omega}\right)$ implies also that for $(s, t) \in S_{\omega} \times S_{\omega}$ the variables $K_{\omega}\left(\delta_{s}-\delta_{t}\right)$ are bounded in probability.

This being noted, let us first show that if $\omega$ is any particular (nonrandom) element of $\Omega$ such that $q(\theta, \omega)$ remain bounded, then

$$
\Lambda(s, \omega)+\frac{1}{2}\left[K\left(Z_{\omega}-\delta_{s}\right)-K\left(Z_{\omega}-\delta_{\omega}\right)\right]
$$

tends to zero in probability, $Z_{\omega}$ being the centering associated with the functions $K_{\omega}, \Lambda_{\omega}^{\prime}$, and the set $S_{\omega}$. To do this we shall use the assumed $K$ control of the experiments $\mathscr{E}_{S \cup S_{\omega}}=\left\{P_{s} ; s \in S \cup S_{\omega}\right\}$. By definition this means that there are other experiments, say $\mathscr{E}_{\omega}^{\prime \prime}=\left\{P_{s}^{\prime \prime} ; s \in S \cup S_{\omega}\right\}$, with the following properties. First $\sup _{s}\left\{\left\|P_{s}^{\prime \prime}-P_{s}\right\| ; s \in S \cup S_{\omega}\right\}$ tends to zero. Second, one can choose logarithms of likelihood ratios $\Lambda^{\prime \prime}$ so that, writing $W^{\prime \prime}(s, \omega)=\Lambda^{\prime \prime}(s, \omega)+ \frac{1}{2} K\left(\delta_{s}-\delta_{\omega}\right)$, whenever $\lambda$ is selected nonrandomly and $K(\lambda)$ tends to zero in probability, so does $\int W^{\prime \prime}(s, \omega) \lambda(d s)$.

The spanning property assumed in (iv) of the Theorem yields a function $s \mapsto \mu_{s}$ from $S$ to $\mathscr{M}_{1}\left(S_{\omega}\right)$ such that $K\left(\mu_{s}-\delta_{s}\right)$ tends to zero in probability. Proceeding as in Lemma 2, Section 4, one concludes that

$$
\Lambda^{\prime \prime}(s, \omega)+\frac{1}{2} K\left(\delta_{s}-\delta_{\omega}\right)-\int W^{\prime \prime}(t, \omega) \mu_{s}(d t)
$$

tends to zero in probability. In this expression one can also replace $\mu_{s}$ by $\mu_{s}-\delta_{\omega}$.

Now let us consider the centerings $Z_{\omega}$ of Section 3. They are obtained by solving the equations

$$
\left\langle Z_{\omega}-\delta_{\omega}, \lambda\right\rangle_{K_{\omega}}=\int W^{\prime}(t, \omega) \lambda(d t)
$$

for $\lambda \in \mathscr{M}_{0}\left(S_{\omega}\right)$ and for a certain function

$$
W^{\prime}(t, \omega)=\Lambda_{\omega}^{\prime}(t, \omega)+\frac{1}{2} K_{\omega}\left(\delta_{t}-\delta_{\omega}\right) .
$$

According to Condition (B), $\Lambda_{\omega}^{\prime}$ is a logarithm of likelihood ratio for an experiment $\left\{P_{s}^{\prime} ; s \in S_{\omega}\right\}$ such that $\sup \left\{\left\|P_{s}^{\prime}-P_{s}\right\| ; s \in S_{\omega}\right\}$ tends to zero. Because of the contiguity requirements, this implies that, for $t \in S_{\omega}$, the differences $\Lambda_{\omega}^{\prime}(t, \omega)-\Lambda^{\prime \prime}(t, \omega)$ tend to zero in probability. Since the $\mu_{s}$ of the spanning property have bounded norms and since the cardinality of $S_{\omega}$ is bounded, one can assert that $\int\left[\Lambda_{\omega}^{\prime}(t, \omega)-\Lambda^{\prime \prime}(t, \omega)\right] \mu_{s}(d t)$ tends to zero in probability. Similarly, the assumed interchangeability of $K$ and $K_{\omega}$ on $\mathscr{M}_{0}\left(S_{\omega}\right)$ implies that $\int\left[K_{\omega}\left(\delta_{t}-\delta_{\omega}\right)-K\left(\delta_{t}-\delta_{\omega}\right)\right] \mu_{s}(d t)$ tends to zero in probability. Thus

$$
\Lambda^{\prime \prime}(s, \omega)+\frac{1}{2} K\left(\delta_{s}-\delta_{\omega}\right)-\left\langle Z_{\omega}-\delta_{\omega}, \mu_{s}-\delta_{\omega}\right\rangle_{K_{\omega}}
$$

tends to zero in probability.
Consider the difference

$$
\left\langle Z_{\omega}-\delta_{\omega}, \mu_{s}-\delta_{\omega}\right\rangle_{K_{\omega}}-\left\langle Z_{\omega}-\delta_{\omega}, \mu_{s}-\delta_{\omega}\right\rangle_{K}
$$

Since $K$ and $K_{\omega}$ are interchangeable on $\mathscr{M}_{0}\left(S_{\omega}\right)$ and since $K_{\omega}\left(Z_{\omega}-\delta_{\omega}\right)$ is bounded in probability, this difference tends to zero in probability. Finally

$$
\left\langle Z_{\omega}-\delta_{\omega}, \mu_{s}-\delta_{\omega}\right\rangle_{K}-\left\langle Z_{\omega}-\delta_{\omega}, \delta_{s}-\delta_{\omega}\right\rangle_{K}=\left\langle Z_{\omega}-\delta_{\omega}, \mu_{s}-\delta_{s}\right\rangle_{K}
$$

tends to zero in probability, since $K\left(\mu_{s}-\delta_{s}\right)$ does. Replacing $\Lambda^{\prime \prime}$ by $\Lambda$ itself, one concludes that

$$
\beta_{s}(\omega)=\Lambda(s, \omega)-\left\langle Z_{\omega}-\delta_{\omega}, \delta_{s}-\delta_{\omega}\right\rangle_{K}+\frac{1}{2} K\left(\delta_{s}-\delta_{\omega}\right)
$$

tends to zero in probability.
Now consider a fixed $b<\infty$ and those $\omega \in \Omega$ such that $q(\theta, \omega) \leq b$. According to Condition (D) the cardinality of this set remains bounded. Thus $\sup _{\omega}\left\{\beta_{s}(\omega) ; \omega \in \Omega, q(\theta, \omega) \leq b\right\}$ tends to zero in probability. Since $\hat{\theta}$ is $q$ convergent, the same is true of $\beta_{s}(\hat{\theta})$. Applying this to a particular $s$ and to the particular value $s=\theta$, one concludes that

$$
\Lambda(s, \theta)+\frac{1}{2}\left[K\left(\hat{Z}-\delta_{s}\right)-K\left(\hat{Z}-\delta_{\theta}\right)\right]
$$

tends to zero in probability if $\hat{Z}=\hat{Z}_{\omega}$ for $\omega=\hat{\theta}$. This concludes the proof of the theorem, except for the boundedness assertion on $K\left(\hat{Z}-\delta_{\theta}\right)$. It follows
similarly from the fact that $K\left(Z_{\omega}-\delta_{\omega}\right)$ is bounded in probability for all $\omega \in \Omega$ such that $q(\theta, \omega)$ remains bounded.

It will be shown later that Theorem 1 has a wide domain of applicability. However, it would be very convenient to extend this domain by weakening two of the assumptions made here, namely the assumption that the sets $S_{\omega}$ have bounded cardinality and the assumption that the centerings $Z_{\omega}$ are strong. These two hypotheses are related since it will be rare that $K_{\omega}\left(Z_{\omega}-\delta_{\omega}\right)$ stays bounded in probability on sets $S_{\omega}$ whose cardinality tends to infinity. The two assumptions are used in the proof at three main places.

One occurrence involves the fact that if $\sup _{s \in S_{\omega}}\left\|P_{s}^{\prime}-P_{s}^{\prime \prime}\right\|$ tends to zero, so does $\int\left[\Lambda_{\omega}^{\prime}(s, \omega)-\Lambda^{\prime \prime}(s, \omega)\right] \mu(d s)$ for $\mu \in \mathscr{M}\left(S_{\omega}\right)$, with $\|\mu\|$ bounded. Of course, if the likelihood ratios used to construct the centerings are the same as those used for the quadratic approximations on $S \cup S_{\omega}$, there is no problem. However, one cannot proceed in this manner in the applications. The situation is not entirely hopeless, however. The difficulty is that convergence in probability does not agree with locally convex topologies. If one could ensure that $E_{\omega}\left|\Lambda_{\omega}^{\prime}(s, \omega)-\Lambda^{\prime \prime}(s, \omega)\right|$ converge to zero for all $s \in S$, then $\int\left[\Lambda_{\omega}^{\prime}(s, \omega)-\Lambda^{\prime \prime}(s, \omega)\right] \mu(d s)$ would also tend to zero.

Of course, the assumption that $\sup \left\{\left\|P_{s}^{\prime}-P_{s}^{\prime \prime}\right\| ; s \in S_{\omega}\right\}$ tends to zero always implies that $\int\left\{\left[\Lambda_{\omega}^{\prime}(s, \omega)\right]^{+}-\left[\Lambda^{\prime \prime}(s, \omega)\right]^{+}\right\} \mu(d s)$ tends to zero for $\|\mu\|$ bounded. However, the large negative values of the $\Lambda^{\prime}$ or $\Lambda^{\prime \prime}$ may lead to difficulties.

The assumption that $Z_{\omega}$ is a strong centering was used when showing that if $K\left(\mu_{s}-\delta_{s}\right)$ tends to zero in probability, so does $\left\langle Z_{\omega}-\delta_{\omega}, \mu_{s}-\delta_{s}\right\rangle_{K}$. Here the situation is complex, but weakening the assumptions may be feasible. If, for instance, the experiments used were exactly Gaussian experiments, with covariances of logarithms of likelihood ratios given by $K$, one would have $E_{\omega}\left|\left\langle Z_{\omega}-\delta_{\omega}, \mu_{s}-\delta_{s}\right\rangle_{K}\right|^{2}= K\left(\mu_{s}-\delta_{s}\right)$. Thus, there will be cases where the convergence to zero of $K\left(\mu_{s}-\delta_{s}\right)$ implies that of $\left\langle Z_{\omega}-\delta_{\omega}, \mu_{s}-\delta_{s}\right\rangle_{K}$ but where $K\left(Z_{\omega}-\delta_{\omega}\right)$ does not remain bounded in probability.

Finally, the boundedness of $K\left(Z_{\omega}-\delta_{\omega}\right)$ is also involved in the replacement of $\left\langle Z_{\omega}-\delta_{\omega}, \mu_{s}-\delta_{\omega}\right\rangle_{K_{\omega}}$ by $\left\langle Z_{\omega}-\delta_{\omega}, \mu_{s}-\delta_{\omega}\right\rangle_{K}$. Such a replacement may be possible even if $K\left(Z_{\omega}-\delta_{\omega}\right)$ does not remain bounded in probability. What it requires is that $K$ and $K_{\omega}$ be close enough for the purpose. To require that $K=K_{\omega}$ on $\mathscr{M}_{0}\left(S_{\omega}\right)$ would bring us back essentially to the statement of Lemma 2, Section 4 and would be too strong an assumption. Indeed, in many cases either $K_{\omega}$ or $K$ are not directly available and must be approximated by constructions carried on other items. (We shall return to this point below.) Also, such an equality $K=K_{\omega}$ would put a severe stress on the practical possibilities of application as can be seen by taking two points $\theta_{1}$ and $\theta_{2}$ such that the balls $\Theta\left(\theta_{i}, b\right)=\left\{s ; q\left(\theta_{i}, s\right)<b\right\}$ overlap. If an $S_{\omega}$ is contained in the overlap, this might force the corresponding $K_{\omega}$ to agree both with a $K_{1}$ usable on $\Theta\left(\theta_{1}, b\right)$ and with a $K_{2}$ usable on $\Theta\left(\theta_{2}, b\right)$, and the usual applications will often involve quadratic forms which vary, albeit slowly, with the balls $\Theta(\theta, b)$ under consideration.

We have just mentioned above that in many cases the forms $K_{\omega}$ are not
available but must be constructed from other objects, as was done for instance in Section 3. This needs to be done for the purpose of obtaining the centerings $\hat{Z}$ themselves. Theorem 1 involves, in addition, another quadratic form $K$. There are many situations where one does not actually need to find $K$. The fact that it exists is sufficient to proceed. For instance, if $K$ is known to exist and be nonrandom, one can derive asymptotic sufficiency properties for the centerings $\hat{Z}$ from the approximation formula of Theorem 1 without actually computing $K$.

Similarly if it is known that there exists a $K$, perhaps random, for which the approximation formula holds, one can often conclude to asymptotic normality of posterior distributions, as will be shown in Chapter 12. Also, using T.W. Anderson's lemma, one can conclude that the centerings $\hat{Z}$ are asymptotic Bayes solutions for a variety of bowl shape loss functions.

However, there are cases where it may be necessary to obtain the quadratic form $K$. This brings us back to the use of the presumed midpoints of Section 3. Here is a result involving them and some semi-norm $\phi$. To start with, consider a set $T \subset \Theta$ and a map $m$ from $T \times T$ to $\Theta$. Assume that $m(t, t)=t$ identically and let $\tilde{T}$ be the range of $m$. In the applications below, $T$ will be a set such as $S_{\omega}$ or $S \cup S_{\omega}$.

Lemma 2. Let $K$ be a quadratic form on $\mathscr{M}_{0}(\tilde{T})$. Assume that the experiments $\left\{P_{t} ; t \in \tilde{T}\right\}$ are $K$-controlled and that $K$ is related to the semi-norm $\phi$. Assume that the quantities $\phi\left(\delta_{s}-\delta_{t}\right) ;(s, t) \in \widetilde{T} \times \widetilde{T}$ are bounded in probability and that the quantities $\phi\left[\delta_{m(s, t)}-\frac{1}{2}\left(\delta_{s}+\delta_{t}\right)\right]$ tend to zero in probability. Let

$$
D(s, t)=-4\{\Lambda[s, m(s, t)]+\Lambda[t, m(s, t)]\}
$$

and

$$
\bar{K}(\lambda)=-\frac{1}{2} \iint D(s, t) \lambda(d s) \lambda(d t)
$$

for $\lambda \in \mathscr{M}_{0}(T)$.
Then, for every pair $(s, t) \in T \times T$ the difference $D(s, t)-K(s, t)$ tends to zero in probability. Furthermore if $T_{0} \subset T$ is a subset of bounded cardinality such that ( $T_{0}, K$ ) is free, then the quadratics $K$ and $\bar{K}$ are interchangeable on the space $\mathscr{M}_{0}\left(T_{0}\right)$.

Remark. In the definition of $D(s, t)$ one can replace the $\Lambda$ by $\Lambda^{\prime}$ for any experiment $\mathscr{E}_{T}^{\prime}=\left\{P_{t}^{\prime} ; t \in T\right\}$ which is $L_{1}$-equivalent to $\mathscr{E}_{T}$.

Proof. According to the $K$-control condition, there exist experiments $\mathscr{E}^{\prime}= \left\{P_{t}^{\prime} ; t \in \tilde{T}\right\}$ and corresponding choices of likelihood ratios $\Lambda^{\prime}$ such that: (i) $\sup \left\{\left\|P_{t}-P_{t}^{\prime}\right\| ; t \in \tilde{T}\right\}$ tends to zero; (ii) for a suitable $\theta \in T$ and nonrandom measures $\mu \in \mathscr{M}_{0}(\tilde{T})$

$$
\int\left[\Lambda^{\prime}(v, \theta)+\frac{1}{2} K\left(\delta_{v}-\delta_{\theta}\right)\right] \mu(d v)
$$

tends to zero in probability whenever $K(\mu) \rightarrow 0$ with $\|\mu\|$ bounded. We have also remarked in Section 4 that such a relation implies that $\tilde{T}$ is a contiguity class. In such a case one may proceed as if the additivity relations $\Lambda^{\prime}(x, y)+ \Lambda^{\prime}(y, z)=\Lambda^{\prime}(x, z)$ were satisfied for triplets $(x, y, z)$ of elements of $\tilde{T}$. Given a pair ( $s, t$ ) and the assigned midpoint $m=m(s, t)$, we can replace $\theta$ by $m$ in the above relations, and apply them to the measure $\mu=\frac{1}{2}\left(\delta_{s}+\delta_{t}\right)-\delta_{m}$. One obtains that

$$
\Lambda^{\prime}(s, m)+\Lambda^{\prime}(t, m)+\frac{1}{2}\left[K\left(\delta_{s}-\delta_{m}\right)+K\left(\delta_{t}-\delta_{m}\right)\right]
$$

tends to zero in probability.
The median identity for $K$ yields

$$
\frac{1}{2}\left[K\left(\delta_{s}-\delta_{m}\right)+K\left(\delta_{t}-\delta_{m}\right)\right]=\frac{1}{4} K\left(\delta_{s}-\delta_{t}\right)+K\left[\delta_{m}-\frac{1}{2}\left(\delta_{s}+\delta_{t}\right)\right] .
$$

Therefore $D(s, t)-K\left(\delta_{s}-\delta_{t}\right)$ tends to zero in probability.
To obtain the second result, note that if $T_{0}$ has bounded cardinality, the numbers of values $D(s, t),(s, t) \in T_{0} \times T_{0}$ to be handled remains bounded. Thus

$$
\xi_{1}=\sup \left\{\left|D(s, t)-K\left(\delta_{s}-\delta_{t}\right)\right| ;(s, t) \in T_{0} \times T_{0}\right\}
$$

tends in probability to zero. This yields $|\bar{K}(\lambda)-K(\lambda)| \leq \xi_{1}\|\lambda\|^{2}$ with $\xi_{1}$ tending to zero in probability. The asserted interchangeability follows, since, on a free pair ( $T_{0}, K$ ), there are positive random variables $\xi$ which are bounded in probability and such that $\xi^{-1} K(\lambda) \leq\|\lambda\|^{2} \leq \xi K(\lambda)$.

Note that the semi-norm $\phi$ intervenes here only in that one needs to check that $\phi\left[\delta_{m}-\frac{1}{2}\left(\delta_{s}+\delta_{t}\right)\right]$ tends to zero in probability, the corresponding value of $K$ being unknown. We have put it here because of the situations described in Section 7.

Now note that the quadratic forms $\bar{K}$ obtained in this lemma may or may not be positive on their domain of definition. One may then wonder whether they can be used for the role of $K_{\omega}$ for the construction of centerings $Z_{\omega}$. This is clearly true if the pairs ( $S_{\omega}, K_{\omega}$ ) are free and if $S_{\omega}$ is contained in a set $T$ satisfying the requirements of Lemma 2 .

One can also wonder whether the form $K$ of Theorem 1 can be replaced by the form $\bar{K}$. To describe a possibility, let us return to the sets $S_{\omega}$ defined for each $\omega \in \Omega$. These were involved in the Conditions (E), but the $K_{\omega}$ occur only in Condition (F) of Definition 4. We shall replace Condition (F) by a Condition (M) as follows.

Condition (M). For each $\omega \in \Omega$ and each number $b$ let $\mathscr{B}(\omega, b)=\{\theta ; \theta \in \Theta$, $q(\omega, \theta)<b\}$. Let $\phi_{\omega}$ be a semi-norm and let $m_{\omega}$ be a function with values in $\Theta$. Assume that the following relations hold:
(i) For each fixed $b$, the function $m_{\omega}$ is eventually defined on $\mathscr{B}(\omega, b) \times \mathscr{B}(\omega, b)$. It is nonrandom. It satisfies $m_{\omega}(s, s)=s$ identically and if $s$ and $t$ remain in $\mathscr{B}(\omega, b)$, the values $q\left[\omega, m_{\omega}(s, t)\right]$ remain bounded.
(ii) For any set $S$ let $\widetilde{S}_{\omega}=\left\{m_{\omega}(s, t) ;(s, t) \in S \times S\right\}$. For each fixed $b$ the seminorms $\phi_{\omega}$ are eventually defined on $\mathscr{M}_{0}\left(\widetilde{S}_{\omega}\right), S=\mathscr{B}(\omega, b)$ and the quantities $\phi_{\omega}\left[\delta_{m(s, t)}-\frac{1}{2}\left(\delta_{s}+\delta_{t}\right)\right]$ tend to zero in probability.

Assuming this, define quantities $D_{\omega}(s, t)$ as follows. For each pair $(s, t)$ such that $q(\omega, s)+q(\omega, t)$ remains bounded, select measures $P_{v}^{\prime \prime} ; v \in\left\{s, t, m_{\omega}(s, t)\right\}$ so that $\sup _{v}\left\|P_{v}^{\prime \prime}-P_{v}\right\|$ tends to zero. Let $\Lambda^{\prime \prime}$ be a corresponding choice of likelihood ratios and let

$$
D_{\omega}(s, t)=-4\left\{\Lambda^{\prime \prime}\left[s, m_{\omega}(s, t)\right]+\Lambda^{\prime \prime}\left[t, m_{\omega}(s, t)\right]\right\}
$$

If $D_{\omega}$ is defined on a set $T \times T$ and $\lambda \in \mathscr{M}_{0}(T)$, let

$$
\bar{K}_{\omega}(\lambda)=-\frac{1}{2} \iint D_{\omega}(s, t) \lambda(d s) \lambda(d t)
$$

Finally, let $\hat{K}=\bar{K}_{\omega}$ for the value $\omega$ taken by the auxiliary estimate $\hat{\theta}$.
In the following theorem we assume that centerings $\widehat{Z}$ are constructed using the systems $S_{\omega}, \bar{K}_{\omega}$, and $\Lambda_{\omega}^{\prime}$ and setting $\omega=\hat{\theta}$ as before. We shall consider particular sets $S$ and let $T_{\omega}(S)$ be the range $\left\{m_{\omega}(s, t) ; s, t \in S \cup S_{\omega}\right\}$.

Theorem 2. Let $(\hat{Z}, \hat{K})$ be constructed as described above. Let $\theta$ be an element of $\Theta$ and let $S \subset \Theta$, with $\theta \in S$. Assume that the conditions (B), (C), (D), (E), and (M) are satisfied at $\theta$. Assume also that, if $\omega \in \Omega$ is such that $q(\theta, \omega)$ remains bounded, there exist quadratic forms $K$ with the following properties.
(i) $K$ is eventually defined on $\mathscr{M}_{0}\left(T_{\omega}\right)$, with $T_{\omega}=T_{\omega}(S)$.
(ii) The experiments $\left\{P_{s} ; s \in T_{\omega}\right\}$ are $K$-controlled.
(iii) The pairs ( $S_{\omega}, K$ ) are free and they span ( $S, K$ ).
(iv) If restricted to $\mathscr{M}_{0}\left(T_{\omega}\right)$, the semi-norms $\phi_{\omega}$ and the quadratic form $K$ are related.
(v) For $(s, t) \in T_{\omega} \times T_{\omega}$ the values $\phi_{\omega}\left(\delta_{s}-\delta_{t}\right)$ are bounded in probability.

Then, for all $s \in S$, the differences

$$
\Lambda(s, \theta)+\frac{1}{2}\left[\hat{K}\left(\hat{Z}-\delta_{s}\right)-\hat{K}\left(\hat{Z}-\delta_{s}\right)\right]
$$

tend to zero in probability. Furthermore $\phi_{\hat{\theta}}\left(\hat{Z}-\delta_{s}\right)$ is bounded in probability.
Proof. Proceed as in the proof of Theorem 1, using first particular $\omega \in \Omega$ such that $q(\theta, \omega)$ remains bounded. In the present case it has been assumed that the pairs ( $S_{\omega}, K$ ) are free. Thus, by Lemma 2 above, so are the pairs ( $S_{\omega}, \bar{K}_{\omega}$ ). As remarked in Section 4, Lemma 1, this eventually implies the existence of centerings $Z_{\omega}$ such that

$$
\Lambda_{\omega}^{\prime}(s, \omega)=-\frac{1}{2}\left[\bar{K}_{\omega}\left(Z_{\omega}-\delta_{s}\right)-\bar{K}_{\omega}\left(Z_{\omega}-\delta_{\omega}\right)\right]
$$

for all $s \in S_{\omega}$.
Following the same steps as in the proof of Theorem 1 one obtains an assertion to the effect that

$$
\Lambda(s, \omega)+\frac{1}{2}\left[K\left(Z_{\omega}-\delta_{s}\right)-K\left(Z_{\omega}-\delta_{\omega}\right)\right]
$$

tends to zero in probability and that $K\left(Z_{\omega}-\delta_{s}\right)$ is bounded in probability, and this for $s \in S \cup S_{\omega}$. On $\mathscr{M}_{0}\left(S_{\omega}\right)$, the norms induced by $K$ are equivalent to
the $L_{1}$-norm. Thus $\left\|Z_{\omega}-\delta_{\omega}\right\|$ is also bounded in probability. Thus, by Lemma $2, K\left(Z_{\omega}-\delta_{\omega}\right)-\bar{K}_{\omega}\left(Z_{\omega}-\delta_{\omega}\right)$ tends to zero in probability. Also, $\left\|Z_{\omega}-\delta_{s}\right\|$ is bounded in probability and $K(\mu)-\bar{K}_{\omega}(\mu)$ tends to zero in probability if $\|\mu\|$ remains bounded and if $\mu$ is in the space $\mathscr{M}_{0}\left[S_{\omega} \cup\{s\}\right]$. Thus $K\left(Z_{\omega}-\delta_{s}\right)- \bar{K}_{\omega}\left(Z_{\omega}-\delta_{s}\right)$ tends to zero in probability, for any $s \in S$. The desired result follows as in Theorem 1, using the fact that $\hat{\theta}$ takes only a limited number of values in the vicinity of $\theta$.

Remark 1. It should perhaps be noted that here the forms $\hat{K}$ are random in two separately important ways. First, their domains of definition are random. Second, on these domains, the values taken are random. This is so even if the experiments are $K$-controlled for nonrandom forms $K$.

Remark 2. In many cases Theorems 1 and 2 will be applied to situations where the sets $S$ are balls of the type $\Theta(\theta, b)=\{s ; s \in \Theta, q(s, \theta)<b\}$ with $b$ fixed and finite. If so, the assertion of Theorem 2 implies a certain character of asymptotic sufficiency for the pairs $(\hat{Z}, \hat{K})$. A proof to this effect can readily be carried out. One will be carried out in detail for the asymptotically Gaussian case in Section 6. However, note that Theorem 2 asserts a lot more than asymptotic sufficiency in the sense that the pairs ( $\widehat{Z}, \widehat{K}$ ) are also "distinguished" according to Chapter 7, Section 3.

This means that small modifications of them will retain the approximate sufficiency properties. This is very important in the present framework. To see it, note that giving a centering $\hat{Z}$ is to give, among other things, the support of the measure $\hat{Z}$. It is the support of some $Z_{\omega}$ and is contained in a certain $S_{\omega}$. The information on the set $S_{\omega}$ so obtained is not particularly useful. It is readily lost by maps into Euclidean spaces such as those which occur in Section 2, Definition 1 or in Section 7.
