If, in particular, these maps all end in the same finite dimensional Euclidean space $F$, as happens in the most frequently discussed examples in the literature, one wants to retain as statistic the image of $\hat{Z}$ in $F$ without reference to the random bases which permitted its construction. This point will be taken up again in Section 6.

Remark 3. There are some interesting situations where the sets $S$ of Theorems 1 and 2 are not the entire balls $\Theta(\theta, b)=\{s ; s \in \Theta, q(\theta, s) \leq b\}$. One case arises from the occurrence of nuisance parameters, that is, parameters which do occur in the specification of the experiment but not in the specification of the loss functions of interest to the statistician.

To consider such a case, suppose that $\Theta$ is a product set $\Theta=\Xi \times H$ and write $\theta=(\xi(\theta), \eta(\theta))$ with $\xi(\theta) \in \Xi$ and $\eta(\theta)$ in $H$. Suppose that $\eta(\theta)$ is a nuisance parameter. Then, one can take sets $S_{\omega}$ of the form $S_{\omega}=\left(V_{\omega}, \eta(\omega)\right)$ where $V_{\omega}$ is a finite subset of $\Xi$ and $\eta(\omega)$ is a specified second coordinate for
$\omega \in S_{\omega}$. One can also look at other sets $S$ which have the same structure, with $S=(V, \eta(\theta)), V \subset \Xi$.

To avoid having to refer to midpoints, let us place ourselves in the situation covered by Theorem 1. One sees that the only logarithms of likelihood occurring in the computations of the centerings are of the form

$$
\Lambda\{[\xi(s), \eta(\omega)] ;[\xi(\omega), \eta(\omega)]\},
$$

with $s \in S_{\omega}$ and therefore $\xi(s) \in V_{\omega}$. The logarithms which occur in the statement of the approximation result in Theorem 1 are of the form

$$
\Lambda\{[\xi(s), \eta(\theta)] ;[\xi(\theta), \eta(\theta)]\} .
$$

Consider, then, two points $\theta$ and $t$ whose second coordinate is the same, equal to the true unknown value of the nuisance parameter. Let $u$ have the same first coordinate $\xi(\theta)$ as $\theta$, but a second coordinate equal to $y(\omega)$ for an $\omega$ involved in the construction. Similarly let $v$ be such that $\xi(v)=\xi(t)$ and $y(v)=y(\omega)$.

The construction will yield an approximation for $\Lambda(v, u)$ while one would want to approximate $\Lambda(t, \theta)$. Assume that the $\Lambda$ do possess a quadratic approximation of the type $\Lambda(s, r) \sim-\frac{1}{2}\left[\Gamma\left(X-\delta_{s}\right)-\Gamma\left(X-\delta_{r}\right)\right]$ for a quadratic form $\Gamma$. Then the difference $\Lambda(t, \theta)-\Lambda(v, u)$ is approximately

$$
\Delta=\left\langle X-\delta_{\theta}, \lambda\right\rangle_{\Gamma}-\left\langle\delta_{t}-\delta_{v}, \delta_{v}-\delta_{u}\right\rangle_{\Gamma}-\frac{1}{2} \Gamma(\lambda),
$$

with $\lambda=\left(\delta_{t}-\delta_{\theta}\right)-\left(\delta_{v}-\delta_{u}\right)$. This will be small if (a) $\Gamma(\lambda)$ is small, and (b) the inner product $\left\langle\delta_{t}-\delta_{v}, \delta_{v}-\delta_{u}\right\rangle_{\Gamma}$ is small.

The first condition expresses a linearity property of the transformation that changes only second coordinates. This is often satisfied. For instance, it holds automatically under the LAN conditions of Section 7 or their LAMN variants. The second restriction is more drastic, imposing an orthogonality restriction on the coordinate system used to split the nuisance parameters from the parameters of interest.

Here $t$ and $\theta$ differ only in their first coordinates and $u$ and $\theta$ differ only in their second coordinates. There are cases where this implies the $\Gamma$-orthogonality of $\delta_{t}-\delta_{\theta}$ and $\delta_{u}-\delta_{\theta}$. If so, $\Delta$ becomes negligible and all one needs to approximate is the expression $\Lambda\{[\xi(s), \eta(\omega)] ;[\xi(\theta), \eta(\omega)]\}$. For this latter expression, Theorem 1 becomes applicable. Under its conditions, the centerings obtained will provide the required approximation.

In this manner, using an auxiliary estimate $\hat{\theta}$, one obtains approximations of the likelihood ratios for experiments $\left\{P_{t} ; t \in T_{\theta}\right\}$ where all the elements of $T_{\theta}$ have the same second coordinate, equal to the value $\eta(\theta)$ for the true value of $\theta$. Thus decision theoretic procedures can be carried out for loss functions depending only on the first coordinate, in ignorance of what the second might be.

This phenomenon was observed by C . Stein who described it in a finite dimensional framework, suggesting possibly wider applicability. In the present
context some dimensionality restriction (in the Kolmogorov sense) remains on the second coordinate space, because of the restriction of Condition (D) on the set $\Omega$. However, as shown by C . Stone in a particular case, one can occasionally obtain some statement without this restriction.

The argument given here depends on the fact that the two points $\omega$ and $\theta$ are such that $q(\theta, \omega)$ remains bounded. In the case considered by C . Stone, the difference we have called $\Delta$ above tends to zero in probability even though the second coordinates differ enough to prevent $q(\theta, \omega)$ from remaining bounded. A brief description of the situation will be given in Chapter 17.

Now let us return to the auxiliary estimates $\hat{\theta}$. Their convergence properties, requiring the boundedness in probability of $q(\theta, \hat{\theta})$, are of course very essential. On the other hand, restricting their values to a set $\Omega$ which is locally of bounded cardinality is only a technical device. One can dispense with it under suitable equicontinuity restrictions, as we shall see later in this section. This may be important in practice, since obtaining sets $\Omega$ which satisfy the cardinality restrictions of Condition (D) may be an unnecessary bother. Assuming that one wishes to use the cardinality restriction (D), the choice of the set $\Omega$ becomes necessary. Here we shall introduce still another definition.

Definition 5. Let $a_{1}$ and $a_{2}$ be fixed numbers such that $0<a_{1} \leq a_{2}<\infty$. Subsets $\Omega \subset \Theta$ will be called $a_{i}$-skeletons if
(i) $(s, t) \in \Omega \times \Omega, s \neq t$, implies $q(s, t) \geq a_{1}$;
(ii) for each $\theta \in \Theta$ there is an $\omega \in \Omega$ such that $q(\theta, \omega) \leq a_{2}$.

If $\Omega$ is an $a_{i}$-skeleton the cardinality of a set $\Omega(\theta, b)=\{\omega ; \omega \in \Omega, q(\theta, \omega) \leq b\}$ for a fixed number $b$ may still be infinite, or if finite for each $v$, unbounded as $v \rightarrow \infty$. To require a condition such as (D) is to require a sort of dimensionality restriction in the sense of Kolmogorov on the sets $\Omega(\theta, b)$, but with a fixed nonzero lower bound on the size of the coverings involved. Since, as we shall see, the possibility of good quadratic approximations implies much more drastic dimensionality requirements, this (D) (i) is not too serious an assumption. However, even if $\Omega$ has been selected, the natural estimates available need not take their values in $\Omega$ itself. Thus, the question arises of constructing estimates $\hat{\theta}$ with values in $\Omega$ from other estimates, say $\theta^{*}$, without destroying their convergence properties.

When $\Theta$ is a sizable subset of a Euclidean space, discretization will ordinarily pose no real problems. One just computes the coordinates of $\theta^{*}$ to a limited number of decimals and this is $\hat{\theta}$. For sets $\Theta$ which do not have such an agreeable structure the situation is somewhat more complicated, but not all that different.

First note that, in principle, the estimates $\theta^{*}$ or $\hat{\theta}$ are measurable maps from the spaces $(\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{A})$ underlying $\mathscr{E}$ to the space $\Theta$. For this to make sense $\Theta$ itself needs to be provided with a $\sigma$-field. However, it is not absolutely necessary that $\theta^{*}$ or $\hat{\theta}$ be measurable. Indeed, all the arguments carried out in this section involve inequalities which make sense whether or not the functions involved are measurable. Instead of saying that certain probabilities tend to zero, one makes the same statements about outer probabilities and nothing needs to be changed otherwise.

Taking this into account, proceed as follows. Suppose given the $a_{i}$-skeleton
$\Omega$. Well order $\Omega$ in an arbitrary manner. Let $U(\omega)$ be the open ball of radius $a_{2}+1$ centered at $\omega$. That is, $U(\omega)=\left\{s ; s \in \Theta, q(\omega, s)<a_{2}+1\right\}$. Let $V(\omega)= U(\omega) \backslash \bigcup_{\omega^{\prime}}\left[U\left(\omega^{\prime}\right) ; \omega^{\prime}<\omega\right]$. Each set $V(\omega)$ is the intersection of an open set with a closed set. For a given value $\theta^{*} \in \Theta$ let $\hat{\theta}$ be that $\omega \in \Omega$ such that $\theta^{*} \in V(\omega)$.

Lemma 3. Let $\hat{\theta}$ and $\theta^{*}$ be as just explained. Then if $\theta$ is of type ( C ) and if $\theta^{*}$ is $q$-convergent at $\theta$, the function $\hat{\theta}$ is also $q$-convergent at $\theta$. If $\theta^{*}$ is measurable for the $\sigma$-field of Borel sets of $\Theta$, then so is $\hat{\theta}$.

Suppose only that $\theta^{*}$ is measurable for the $\sigma$-field of $\Theta$ generated by the balls of $\Theta$. Let $\theta$ be a point satisfying both Conditions (C) and (D). Assume also that at $\theta$ the estimate $\theta^{*}$ is $q$-convergent. Then for every subset $B$ of $\Omega$ the difference $P^{*}[\hat{\theta} \in B \mid \theta]-P_{*}[\hat{\theta} \in B \mid \theta]$ between outer and inner probabilities tends to zero.

Proof. By assumption, for each $t \in \Theta$, there is some $s \in \Omega$ such that $q(s, t) \leq a_{2}$. Thus one will always have $q\left(\hat{\theta}, \theta^{*}\right)<a_{2}+1$. For a point of type (C) at which $\theta^{*}$ is $q$-convergent the $q$-convergence of $\hat{\theta}$ follows from Lemma 1 . If $B$ is a subset of $\Omega$, the set $B^{\prime}$ of points $s \in \Theta$ such that $\hat{\theta}(s) \in B$ is the set $\bigcup\{V(\omega)$; $\omega \in B\}$. Let $h$ be the Hellinger distance on $\Theta$. For each integer $n$, let $F(\omega, n)= \left\{t ; h\left[t, U^{c}(\omega)\right] \geq 1 / n\right\}$ and $V_{n}(\omega)=V(\omega) \cap F(\omega, n)$. Since $U(\omega)$ is open one has $\bigcup_{n} V_{n}(\omega)=V(\omega)$, thus $\bigcup\{V(\omega) ; \omega \in B\}=\bigcup_{n} S_{n}$ with $S_{n}=\bigcup\left\{V_{n}(\omega) ; \omega \in B\right\}$. Thus it will be sufficient to show that $S_{n}$ is measurable. However, $S_{n}$ is in fact closed. Indeed, for each $n$, one has $\left\{\overline{U(\omega)} \backslash \bigcup\left[U\left(\omega^{\prime}\right) ; \omega^{\prime}<\omega\right]\right\} \cap F(\omega, n)= V_{n}(\omega)$. Thus each $V_{n}(\omega)$ is closed. Also if $\omega^{\prime}<\omega$ then $V_{n}(\omega) \subset U\left(\omega^{\prime}\right)$ so that $V_{n}(\omega)$ and $V_{n}\left(\omega^{\prime}\right)$ are at Hellinger distance at least $1 / n$ from each other. Thus $S_{n}$ is closed and $\bigcup\{V(\omega) ; \omega \in B\}$ is an $F_{\sigma}$ set.

It follows that $\hat{\theta}$ is Borel measurable whenever $\theta^{*}$ is. If $\theta^{*}$ is only measurable for the $\sigma$-field induced by the balls of $\Theta$, the situation is more complex. However, let $\Theta(\theta, b)=\{s ; s \in \Theta, q(s, \theta) \leq b\}$ and let $\theta$ satisfy the assumptions of the lemma.

Then for each fixed $\varepsilon>0$ one can find a fixed $b$ such that $P_{\theta}\left\{\theta^{*} \notin \Theta(\theta, b)\right\}< \varepsilon$. In addition, by Lemma 1 again, the set of points $s \in \Theta$ for which there exists a $t \in \Theta(\theta, b)$ such that $q(s, t) \leq a_{2}+1$ is eventually contained in some ball $\Theta\left(\theta, b_{1}\right)$ for some fixed $b_{1}$. According to Condition (D), the set $\Omega \cap \Theta\left(\theta, b_{1}\right)$ is a finite set. Thus the set of points $\omega \in \Omega$ such that $U(\omega) \cap \Theta(\theta, b)$ is nonempty is a finite set. It follows that for these points $\omega$, the set $V(\omega)$ is in the field generated by the balls. Thus $\bigcup\{V(\omega) \cap \Theta(\theta, b) ; \omega \in B\}$ is also in this same field. The result follows immediately.

The reader will note that, although the general situation may be very complex under the assumptions made here, practical applications are unlikely to necessitate elaborate manipulations. In practice, given the skeleton $\Omega$, one simply replaces $\theta^{*}$ by a point of $\Omega$ closest to it. If there is more than one such point, one makes an arbitrary choice among them.

Now let us return to equicontinuity conditions which would allow $\hat{\theta}$ to take arbitrary values in $\Theta$.



Suppose that for each $t \in \Theta$ one has a pair ( $S_{t}, K_{t}$ ) consisting of a set $S_{t} \subset \Theta$ and a quadratic form $K_{t}$ on $\mathscr{M}_{0}\left(S_{t}\right)$. Suppose that one has selected functions $\Lambda_{t}^{\prime}\left(s, s^{\prime}\right)$ for $\left(s, s^{\prime}\right) \in S_{t} \times S_{t}$.

Take also an $a_{i}$-skeleton $\Omega \subset \Theta$ and reconsider Theorem 1 with centerings $Z^{*}=Z_{\theta^{*}}$ obtained from the unmodified estimate $\theta^{*}$. Assume that for $q(\theta, t) \leq b, b$ fixed, the cardinalities of the $S_{t}$ remain bounded and that the pairs ( $S_{t}, K_{t}$ ) are free. Then the $Z^{*}$ so obtained have no reason to yield acceptable results. They will, however, whenever the functions $K_{t}$ and $\Lambda_{t}^{\prime}$ used in the construction are close enough to the $K_{\omega}$ and $\Lambda_{\omega}^{\prime}$ one would have used after discretization. This means, for instance, the following. For each $t \in \Theta$, take an $\omega(t) \in \Omega$ such that $q(t, \omega(t)) \leq a_{2}$. Consider a fixed $b \in(0, \infty)$ and the variables

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \beta_{1}(t, b)=\sup _{x, y}\left|K_{t}\left(\delta_{x}-\delta_{y}\right)-K_{\omega(t)}\left(\delta_{x}-\delta_{y}\right)\right| \\
& \beta_{2}(t, b)=\sup _{x, y}\left|\Lambda_{t}^{\prime}(x, y)-\Lambda_{\omega(t)}^{\prime}(x, y)\right|
\end{aligned}
$$

where the supremum is taken over pairs $(x, y) \in \Theta$ such that $q(t, x)$ and $q(t, y)$ do not exceed $b$. Let $\beta(t, b)=\beta_{1}(t, b)+\beta_{2}(t, b)$.

If $\sup _{t}\{\beta(t, b) ; q(\theta, t) \leq b\}$ tends to zero in $P_{\theta}$ probability for each fixed $b$ and if the assumptions of Theorem 1 are otherwise satisfied, then the conclusion of the Theorem will hold for the centerings $Z^{*}$.

It is, however, important to note that enforcing such equicontinuity conditions may be more difficult than discretizing $\theta^{*}$ to obtain $\hat{\theta}$, unless the likelihood ratios $\Lambda(s, t)$ themselves are already very smooth.

## 6. The Asymptotically Gaussian Case

In these section we consider, as before, a net $\left\{\mathscr{E}_{v}\right\}$ of experiments $\mathscr{E}_{v}=\left\{P_{\theta, v}\right.$; $\left.\theta \in \Theta_{v}\right\}$ and suppress the index $v$. The purpose of the section is to investigate what happens to the centerings $\hat{Z}$ of Section 3 when the experiments $\mathscr{E}$ can be approximated locally by Gaussian experiments. We start by a short review of some properties of Gaussian experiments. At this stage one could reproduce the arguments of Section 5 and obtain results similar to Theorem 1 of Section 5. However, in the present case, there are many possibilities for the choice of qudratic forms to be inserted in the theorem. Thus, we spend some effort constructing a particular set of quadratic forms. These will be used in the approximation of likelihood ratios. They will also be used in the construction of confidence balls and other similar objects.

This being done, we state a Theorem 1, analogous to Theorem 1 of Section 5 , and a modified version of it intended for use when there are "nuisance parameters." It follows from this that the centerings $\hat{Z}$ possess certain local asymptotic sufficiency properties. Using a patchwork argument similar to that of Chapter 5, Section 4, Proposition 7, one obtains a global version of the sufficiency properties.

The entire development relies on the availability of sets $S_{\omega}, \omega \in \Omega$ with suitable properties. We give a description of a possible method of construction for these sets. Finally, we include some remarks on situations where the finite dimensionality restrictions are not satisfied, but where the experiments are subjected to local precompactness requirements.

Recall that a Gaussian experiment $\mathscr{G}=\left\{G_{s} ; s \in S\right\}$ has been defined (Chapter 9, Section 3) as an homogeneous experiment such that the logarithms $\Lambda_{G}(t, s)= \log \left(d G_{t} / d G_{s}\right)$ form a Gaussian process in $t \in S$ when the distributions are induced by $G_{s}$. Such a Gaussian experiment is entirely determined up to equivalence by its affinity function $\rho_{G}(s, t)=\int \sqrt{d G_{s} d G_{t}}$. In fact one can argue as follows. Take any $\theta \in S$ and consider the distributions induced by $G_{\theta}$. Then, one may write $\Lambda_{G}(t, \theta)=X_{t}-\frac{1}{2} \beta^{2}(t)$, where $E_{\theta} X_{t}=0$ and $\beta^{2}(t)=E_{\theta} X_{t}^{2}$. Similarly $\Lambda_{G}(s, \theta)=X_{s}-\frac{1}{2} \beta^{2}(s)$. The affinity is

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho_{G}(s, t) & =E_{\theta} \exp \left\{\frac{1}{2}\left(X_{s}+X_{t}\right)\right\} \exp \left\{-\frac{1}{4}\left[\beta^{2}(s)+\beta^{2}(t)\right]\right\} \\
& =\exp \left\{\frac{1}{4} C(s, t)-\frac{1}{8}\left[\beta^{2}(s)+\beta^{2}(t)\right]\right\}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $C(s, t)$ is the covariance $E_{\theta} X_{s} X_{t}$.
Writing $q_{G}^{2}(s, t)=-8 \log \rho_{G}(s, t)$, one obtains

$$
C(s, t)=-\frac{1}{2}\left[q_{G}^{2}(s, t)-q_{G}^{2}(s, \theta)-q_{G}^{2}(t, \theta)\right]
$$

For any measure $\mu \in \mathscr{M}_{0}(S)$ and any $\theta_{1} \in S$ one has

$$
\int \Lambda_{G}(s, \theta) \mu(d s)=\int \Lambda_{G}\left(s, \theta_{1}\right) \mu(d s)
$$

The variance of this integral under $G_{\theta}$ can be written

$$
\begin{aligned}
K(\mu) & =\iint C(s, t) \mu(d s) \mu(d t) \\
& =-\frac{1}{2} \iint q_{G}^{2}(s, t) \mu(d s) \mu(d t)
\end{aligned}
$$

It is independent of the point $\theta$ used to induce the distributions.
Returning to the experiments $\mathscr{E}=\left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$, this suggests the introduction of the function $q^{2}(s, t)=-8 \log \rho(s, t)$ for $\rho(s, t)=\int \sqrt{d P_{s} d P_{t}}$. It also suggests defining a quadratic form $\Gamma$ on $\mathscr{M}_{0}(\Theta)$ by

$$
\Gamma(\lambda)=-\frac{1}{2} \iint q^{2}(s, t) \lambda(d s) \lambda(d t)
$$

This last definition may not be possible if $q$ can take infinite values. For this reason we shall assume throughout this section that $q^{2}(s, t)<\infty$ for all pairs $(s, t) \in \Theta \times \Theta$.

This restriction appears to rule out a number of very important cases. However, it is almost always possible to insure it, using instead of $\mathscr{E}$ a small modification. For instance, if the net $\{v\}$ is not pathological one can take numbers $\alpha>0$ which tend to zero along $\{\nu\}$, a particular $\theta_{0} \in \Theta$ and replace $P_{\theta}$ by $(1-\alpha) P_{\theta}+\alpha P_{\theta_{0}}$. The experiment $\mathscr{E}^{\prime}$ obtained in this way will be such that $\rho^{\prime}(s, t)>0$ for all pairs $(s, t)$. If $\{v\}$ is pathological, such a modification may not be feasible. However, taking a fixed $\alpha$ of the order of $\alpha_{0}=\exp \{-\exp [\exp (137)]\}$, one sees that although mathematical assertions may be affected, practical ones are essentially unperturbed.

The assumption that $q^{2}$ is finite will be used later on in several arguments in which one covers the space $\Theta$ by balls of the type $\Theta(\theta, b)=\{t ; t \in \Theta, q(t, \theta) \leq b\}$. It is technically convenient, but not very essential.

It is regrettable that even if $q$ is finite the corresponding form $\Gamma$ need not be positive on $\mathscr{M}_{0}(\Theta)$. In fact, in the asymptotically Gaussian situation, one can expect $\Gamma$ to give good local information on the structure of the experiments. However, there is no reason at all to expect it to mean much for measures $\mu \in \mathscr{M}_{0}(\Theta)$ whose support has a large Hellinger distance diameter. Consider, for instance, a set $\Theta$ which consists of three points $\theta, s, t$. Let $P$ and $Q$ be two disjoint measures and let $\alpha$ and $\beta$ be small positive numbers. Let $P_{\theta}=P, P_{s}= (1-\alpha) Q+\alpha P$, and $P_{t}=(1-\beta) Q+\beta P$. Then $q^{2}(\theta, s)=-8 \log \sqrt{\alpha}, q^{2}(\theta, t)= -8 \log \sqrt{\beta}$, and $q^{2}(s, t)=-8 \log [\sqrt{(1-\alpha)(1-\beta)}+\sqrt{\alpha \beta}]$.

Let $\mu \in \mathscr{M}_{1}(\Theta)$ be the measure which gives mass ( +2 ) to $s$ and mass ( -1 ) to $t$. Then

$$
\Gamma\left(\mu-\delta_{\theta}\right)=4 \log \left(\beta \alpha^{-2}\right)-16 \log [\sqrt{(1-\alpha)(1-\beta)}+\sqrt{\alpha \beta}] .
$$

Making $\alpha$ and $\beta$ tend to zero, one sees that $q(s, t) \rightarrow 0, q(\theta, s)$, and $q(\theta, t)$ tend to infinity, but $\Gamma\left(\mu-\delta_{\theta}\right)$ can tend to anything one pleases, including $(-\infty)$.

The lack of positivity of $\Gamma$ is not too serious for statements similar to that of Section 5, Theorem 1. However it effectively prevents the use of $\Gamma$ for construction of confidence balls or test functions. Thus we shall use substitutes instead.

In some cases well behaved substitutes are readily available. For instance, assume that $\mathscr{E}$ is a direct product of experiments $\mathscr{E}_{j}=\left\{p_{\theta, j} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$. Let $h_{j}^{2}(s, t)= \frac{1}{2} \int\left(\sqrt{d p_{s, j}}-\sqrt{d p_{t, j}}\right)^{2}$. Assume that whenever $q(s, t)$ remains bounded, the quantities $\sup _{j} h_{j}^{2}(s, t)$ tend to zero. Then it is possible and convenient to replace $q^{2}$ by the square distance $d^{2}(s, t)=8 \sum_{j} h_{j}^{2}(s, t)$. This will be done routinely in Chapter 16.

If $\Theta$ is imbedded in a Euclidean space, various other possibilities may present themselves. One of them is described in Section 8. However, in the abstract, no adequate substitutes may be readily available. We shall proceed below to a construction which defines certain quadratic forms on $\mathscr{M}_{0}(\Theta)$. These will be used as local approximations for $\Gamma$.

Consider subsets of $\Theta$ subject to the restriction that for any pair $(s, t)$ of distinct points of the subset one has $q(s, t) \geq 1$. Let $J$ be a maximal subset of $\Theta$ with this property.

For each $j \in J$ and each integer $m \geq 0$, let $\Theta(j, m)=\{\theta ; \theta \in \Theta, q(j, \theta) \leq m\}$. Let $\mathscr{E}_{j, m}=\left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta(j, m)\right\}$ and let $\delta(j, m)=\inf \Delta\left(\mathscr{E}_{j, m}, \mathscr{G}\right)$, where $\mathscr{G}$ runs through the class of all Gaussian experiments indexed by $\Theta(j, m)$. If for some $j$ one has $\delta(j, m)=0$ for all $m$, then (since we assume that $q$ is finite) $\mathscr{E}$ is exactly Gaussian. Leaving this case aside as trivial, we shall proceed assuming that $\sup _{m} \delta(j, m)>0$ for all $j \in J$.

Consider also the auxiliary estimate $\hat{\theta}$ and let

$$
\varepsilon(j, m)=\max \left\{\exp \{-m\}, P_{j}[q(\hat{\theta}, j)>m]\right\}
$$

If there is a last finite integer $m$ such that $\delta(j, m) \leq \varepsilon(j, m)$, let it be called $m(j)$. Otherwise take $m(j)$ equal to the integer part of $\left[\sup _{m} \delta(j, m)\right]^{-1}$. For this value $m(j)$ let $\mathscr{E}_{j}=\mathscr{E}_{j, m(j)}$ and let $\mathscr{G}_{j}^{\prime}$ be a Gaussian experiment indexed by $\Theta[j, m(j)]$ such that $\Delta\left(\mathscr{E}_{j}, \mathscr{G}_{j}^{\prime}\right) \leq 2 \delta[j, m(j)]$. There always exists such an experiment, even if $\delta[j, m(j)]=0$. The experiment $\mathscr{G}_{j}^{\prime}$ yields a quadratic form $K_{j}^{\prime}$ on $\mathscr{M}_{0}\{\Theta[j, m(j)]\}$.

One can extend the domain of $\mathscr{G}_{j}^{\prime}$ to the entire space $\Theta$ in many different ways. One procedure which will serve our purpose is as follows. Let $L^{\prime}(t, j)$ be the logarithm of ( $d G_{t}^{\prime} / d G_{j}^{\prime}$ ) for the elements $G_{t}^{\prime}$ of $\mathscr{G}_{j}^{\prime}$. Consider this as a process, all distributions being induced by $G_{j}^{\prime}$. Take another Gaussian $\mathscr{N}[0$, $\left.16 m^{2}(j)\right]$ variable $Y$ independent of the process $L^{\prime}$. If $\theta \notin \Theta[j, m(j)]$ let $G_{\theta}$ have density exp $\left\{Y-\frac{1}{2} E Y^{2}\right\}$ with respect to $G_{j}^{\prime}$. This gives a Gaussian experiment $\mathscr{G}_{j}=\left\{G_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ which extends $\mathscr{G}_{j}^{\prime}$. It defines a quadratic form $K_{j}$ on all of $\mathscr{M}_{0}(\Theta)$.

Now assume that $J$ has been well-ordered. Let $V^{\prime}(j)$ be the open ball $V^{\prime}(j)= \{\theta ; q(\theta, j)<2\}$ and let $V(j)=V^{\prime}(j) \backslash \bigcup\left\{V^{\prime}(i) ; i<j\right\}$. The family $\{V(j) ; j \in J\}$ is a covering of the entire space $\Theta$.

If $\theta \in \Theta$ is an element of $V(j)$, let $\Gamma_{\theta}$ be the quadratic form $K_{j}$ of $\mathscr{G}_{j}$. By construction, this is a positive quadratic form on $\mathscr{M}_{0}(\Theta)$. One can also extend the definition to obtain a function $\mu \mapsto \Gamma_{\mu}$ which yields a positive quadratic form $\Gamma_{\mu}$ for each $\mu \in \mathscr{M}_{1}(\Theta)$. To do this, let $J(\mu)$ be the set of indices $j \in J$ such that $V(j)$ intersects the support of $\mu$ and let $\Gamma_{\mu}$ be the average of the $K_{j}, j \in J(\mu)$. Finally, substituting the centering $\hat{Z}$ in $\Gamma_{\mu}$, one obtains a form $\tilde{\Gamma}=\Gamma_{\hat{z}}$. Substituting the auxiliary estimate $\hat{\theta}$, one obtains a form $\hat{\Gamma}=\Gamma_{\hat{\theta}}$.

As mentioned earlier, the use of the discrete set $J$ is not absolutely mandatory, but it will allow us to obtain measurability properties for the random forms $\hat{\Gamma}$ and $\tilde{\Gamma}$. The use of the numbers $\varepsilon(j, m)$ in the construction is just a technical device. Instead of the balls $\Theta[j, m(j)]$ one could use other balls. For instance, if the net $\{v\}$ is not pathological, one could use numbers $\alpha_{v}>0$ which tend to zero along $\{v\}$ and replace $m(j)$ by the largest $m$ such that $\delta(j, m) \leq \alpha_{v}$. For practical applications, one would just take a fixed $\alpha_{0}$, very small and take the largest $m$ such that $\delta(j, m) \leq \alpha_{0}$. This will not endanger any practical assertion if $\alpha_{0}$ is, say, $\exp \{-\exp [\exp (137)]\}$, but damages the limit theorems. The use of the numbers $\varepsilon(j, m)$ makes the construction self-contained for each experiment $\mathscr{E}$. It also permits assertions in the form of limit theorems. To state
such theorems and to simplify the statement of other results, let us introduce other definitions.

Let us recall that the experiments $\mathscr{E}$ are indexed by the suppressed label $v$ and that, since $\Theta$ varies with $v$, points $\theta \in \Theta$ cannot be fixed. The points singled out for further study here are those around which the experiments $\mathscr{E}$ admit Gaussian approximations according to the following definition.

Definition 1. Sets $S \subset \Theta$ have property (G1) if
(i) $\sup \{q(s, t),(s, t) \in S \times S\}$ remains bounded;
(ii) there are Gaussian experiments $\mathscr{G}_{S}=\left\{G_{s} ; s \in S\right\}$ such that the distances $\Delta\left(\mathscr{G}_{S}, \mathscr{E}_{S}\right)$ between $\mathscr{G}_{S}$ and $\mathscr{E}_{S}=\left\{P_{s} ; s \in S\right\}$ tends to zero.

We shall say that a point $\theta \in \Theta$ satisfies (G1) if for every fixed $b<\infty$ the balls $\Theta(\theta, b)=\{t ; t \in \Theta, q(t, \theta) \leq b\}$ satisfy (G1).

There is a requirement ( G 0 ), weaker than ( G 1 ), that would be satisfactory for practically all the results of this section. We shall say that sets $S$ satisfy (G0) if whenever $S^{\prime} \subset S$ has bounded cardinality, then $S^{\prime}$ satisfies (G1).

The property (G0) is obviously equivalent to the conjunction of the following requirements.
(i) For all pairs $(s, t) \in S \times S$, the nets $\left\{P_{s}\right\}$ and $\left\{P_{t}\right\}$ are contiguous.
(ii) For some $\theta \in S$ and for subsets $T \subset S$ which have a bounded cardinality (independent of $v$ ) the joint distributions $\mathscr{L}\left\{\Lambda(s, \theta) ; s \in T \mid P_{\theta}\right\}$ can be approximated in Prohorov distance by Gaussian distributions.

Condition (G0) is easier to verify than (G1). It is weaker, but under the other conditions used in this section, (G0) will often imply (G1). A statement to this effect follows.

For any $\varepsilon>0$, consider subsets $T \subset S$ such that distinct elements of $T$ are at Hellinger distance at least $\varepsilon$. The Hellinger distance used here is that of $\mathscr{E}$, namely $h^{2}(s, t)=1-\int \sqrt{d P_{s} d P_{t}}$. Let $C(S, \varepsilon)$ be the cardinality of a maximal $T$ with the property just described.

Definition 2. The experiments $\mathscr{E}_{S}$ are called equi-precompact if there is a finite valued function $c$ independent of $v$ such that $C(S, \varepsilon) \leq c(\varepsilon)$ for all $\varepsilon$. The experiments $\mathscr{E}_{S}$ are called tail-precompact if there are experiments $\mathscr{E}_{S}^{\prime}$ which are equi-precompact and such that $\Delta\left(\mathscr{E}_{S}, \mathscr{E}_{S}^{\prime}\right)$ tends to zero.

The following lemma is an immediate consequence of Lindae's argument (see Chapter 6, Section 4).

Lemma 1. Let the $\mathscr{E}_{S}$ be tail-precompact. Then they satisfy (G1) if and only if they satisfy (G0).

In order to proceed, we shall also need assumptions on the pairs ( $S_{\omega}, K_{\omega}$ ) used in the construction of the centering variables. These will be the same as in Section 5 with modifications and supplementations described below.

Recall that Condition (B) of Section 5 imposes a boundedness restriction on the diameter of the sets $S_{\omega}$. It also specifies that the surrogate log-likelihood $\Lambda_{w}^{\prime}$ used for construction must be approximations of the true ones. Condition (D) says that, around $\theta$, the sets $\{s ; s \in \Omega, q(s, \theta) \leq b\}$ and the sets $S_{\omega}$ have bounded cardinality. Condition (E) says that the auxiliary estimate $\hat{\theta}$ converges at the appropriate rate. Instead of Condition (F) of Section 5 we shall use the
following stronger condition.
Definition 3. The systems ( $S_{\omega}, K_{\omega}$ ) satisfy Condition (FF) at $\theta$ if, whenever $\omega \in \Omega$ is such that $q(\theta, \omega)$ remains bounded,
(i) the pairs $\left(S_{\omega}, K_{\omega}\right)$ are free;
(ii) if $\mu \in \mathscr{M}_{0}\left(S_{\omega}\right)$ is such that $\|\mu\|$ remain bounded, then $K_{\omega}(\mu)-\Gamma(\mu)$ tends to zero.

Many statements involve combinations of several conditions. To shorten them we shall use the following definition.

Definition 4. Points $\theta \in \Theta$ will be called favorable if they satisfy (B), (D), (E), (FF), and (G1).

The assumption that $\theta$ is favorable involves the local structure of the experiments $\mathscr{E}$ through (G1) and (E) only. All the other assumptions refer to the construction pairs ( $S_{\omega}, K_{\omega}$ ) and the surrogate $\log$-likelihoods $\Lambda_{\omega}^{\prime}$.

It will be shown later in this section (Proposition 5) that (G1) and (E) are nearly enough to allow the choice of pairs ( $S_{\omega}, K_{\omega}$ ) that satisfy the other requirements.

This is true in spite of the very stringent finite dimensionality restrictions imposed by the combination (D) and (FF), because nothing in the assumptions says that the entire local structure of $\Theta$ has to be taken into account. The part of $\Theta$ involved in the $S_{\omega}$ can be a very thin subset of $\Theta$. A first assertion relative to the quadratic forms constructed above on $\mathscr{M}_{0}(\Theta)$ is as follows.

Lemma 2. Let the forms $\Gamma_{\theta}, \hat{\Gamma}$, and $\tilde{\Gamma}$ be constructed by the procedure described above. Let $\theta$ be a point of $\Theta$ and let $b$ denote a fixed number. Assume that Condition (G1) is satisfied at $\theta$ and let $K$ be the quadratic form induced by a Gaussian experiment whose distance to $\left\{P_{t} ; t \in \Theta(\theta, b)\right\}$ tends to zero. Then, if (D) and (E) are satisfied at $\theta$, the quantities

$$
\sup \left\{|\widehat{\Gamma}(\mu)-K(\mu)| ; \mu \in \mathscr{M}_{0}[\Theta(\theta, b)],\|\mu\| \leq b\right\}
$$

and

$$
\sup \left\{\left|\Gamma_{\theta}(\mu)-K(\mu)\right| ; \mu \in \mathscr{M}_{0}[\Theta(\theta, b)],\|\mu\| \leq b\right\}
$$

tend to zero in probability. If $\theta$ is favorable one can also replace $\hat{\Gamma}$ by $\tilde{\Gamma}$ in these expressions.

Proof. Consider the set $J$ used to construct the forms $\Gamma_{\theta}$. Take a $j \in J$ such that $q(\theta, j) \leq b$. The conditions (E) and (G1) imply that the corresponding integers $m(j)$ will tend to infinity. Therefore, according to Section 5, Lemma 1, one will eventually have $\Theta(\theta, b) \subset \Theta[j, m(j)]$. The forms $\Gamma_{\theta}$ and $K$ are induced by Gaussian experiments whose distances to $\left\{P_{t} ; t \in \Theta(\theta, b)\right\}$ tend to zero. If $\mathscr{G}=\left\{G_{t} ; t \in \Theta(\theta, b)\right\}$ is one of them, then

$$
\sup \left\{\left|\int \sqrt{d P_{s} d P_{t}}-\int \sqrt{d G_{s} d G_{t}}\right| ; s, t \in \Theta(\theta, b)\right\}
$$

tends to zero. For $s$ and $t$ in $\Theta(\theta, b)$ one has $q(\theta, s) \leq b$ and $q(\theta, t) \leq b$ and the convergence of the affinities imply that of their logarithms. Thus, if $K$ is the form attached to $\mathscr{G}$,

$$
\sup \left\{\left|K\left(\delta_{s}-\delta_{t}\right)-\Gamma\left(\delta_{s}-\delta_{t}\right)\right| ; s, t \in \Theta(\theta, b)\right\}
$$

will tend to zero. By integration one concludes that

$$
\sup \left\{|K(\mu)-\Gamma(\mu)| ; \mu \in \mathscr{M}_{0}[\Theta(\theta, b)],\|\mu\| \leq b\right\}
$$

tends to zero. This implies the result relative to $\Gamma_{\theta}$. For the random forms $\hat{\Gamma}$, take any fixed $\varepsilon>0$ and a fixed $b_{1}$ such that $P_{\theta}\left[q(\hat{\theta}, \theta) \geq b_{1}\right]< \varepsilon$. The preceding argument applies to any one of the possible values of $\hat{\theta}$ in $\Theta\left(\theta, b_{1}\right)$ and would yield the statement concerning $\hat{\Gamma}$ even without taking into account the fact that the number of values of $\hat{\theta}$ in $\Theta\left(\theta, b_{1}\right)$ remains bounded.

For the other forms $\widetilde{\Gamma}$, one can apply the same argument provided that one can show that $\Gamma_{\theta}\left(\hat{Z}-\delta_{\theta}\right)$ and $\left\|\hat{Z}-\delta_{\theta}\right\|$ remain bounded in probability. For a favorable $\theta$ this follows from Section 5, Theorem 1 and will be shown more specifically in the course of the proof of Theorem 1 below.

The reader will note that the forms $\widehat{\Gamma}$ could be used in the role of the forms $K_{\omega}$ for the pairs ( $S_{\omega}, K_{\omega}$ ) of the construction. However, to obtain the $\Gamma_{\theta}$ or $\widehat{\Gamma}$ may be a very difficult task while construction of suitable $K_{\omega}$ may be very simple, for instance by the midpoint method of Section 3. In the present case suitable midpoints can easily be characterized.

Lemma 3. Let $\theta$ satisfy (G1) and let $s$ and $t$ be such that $q(\theta, s)+q(\theta, t)$ remains bounded. Then $m \in \Theta$ will be an appropriate midpoint between $s$ and $t$ if and only if

$$
\frac{1}{2} q^{2}(s, t)-\left[q^{2}(s, m)+q^{2}(t, m)\right]
$$

tends to zero.
We shall see in Sections 7 and 8 cases in which $\Theta$ is imbedded in a Euclidean space with "universal reference norms." In certain of these cases, forms $K_{\omega}$ constructed by the midpoint method can also be used instead of the $\hat{\Gamma}$ described here.

The reader will note that, even at favorable points, assertions about the behavior of logarithms $\Lambda(s, \theta)$ are not feasible unless the points $s$ are somehow related to the sets $S_{\omega}$ used in the construction. To indicate such a relation, we shall use the "accessibility" requirement of Definition 5 below. Further on we shall use a weaker relation intended for the cases which involve nuisance parameters (see Section 5, Theorem 2, Remark 3).

Definition 5. Points $s \in \Theta$ are called accessible (in the construction system $\left.\left\{S_{\omega}, \omega \in \Omega\right\}\right)$, if for every $\omega \in \Omega$ such that $q(s, \omega)$ remains bounded there are $\mu_{s} \in \mathscr{M}_{1}\left(S_{\omega}\right)$ such that (i) $\left\|\mu_{s}\right\|$ remains bounded, and (ii) $\Gamma_{s}\left(\mu_{s}-\delta_{s}\right)$ tends to zero.

A set $S$ will be called accessible if its elements are accessible. If there are favorable points $\theta$ such that $q(\theta, s)$ remains bounded, one can replace $\Gamma_{s}$ by $\Gamma$ or $\Gamma_{\theta}$ in the preceding definition.

Theorem 1. Let the centerings $\hat{Z}$ be constructed by the method of Section 3. Let $\theta$ be a favorable point of $\Theta$ and let $(s, t)$ be an accessible pair such that $q(\theta, s)+q(\theta, t)$ remains bounded.

Then the difference

$$
\Lambda(s, t)+\frac{1}{2}\left[\Gamma\left(\hat{Z}-\delta_{s}\right)-\Gamma\left(\hat{Z}-\delta_{t}\right)\right]
$$

tends to zero in $P_{s}$ and $P_{t}$ probability and the variables $\Gamma\left(\hat{Z}-\delta_{\theta}\right)$ and $\left\|\hat{Z}-\delta_{\theta}\right\|$ are bounded in probability. The same result holds if $\Gamma$ is everywhere replaced by either one of $\Gamma_{\theta}, \widehat{\Gamma}$, or $\widetilde{\Gamma}$.

Proof. Consider some nonrandom $\omega \in \Omega$ such that $q(\theta, \omega)$ remains bounded and let $T$ be the set $S_{\omega} \cup\{s\} \cup\{t\}$. Let $\mathscr{G}_{T}$ be a Gaussian experiment such that $\Delta\left(\mathscr{E}_{T}, \mathscr{G}_{T}\right)$ tends to zero, and let $K$ be the corresponding quadratic form on $\mathscr{M}_{0}(T)$. The convergence of $\Delta\left(\mathscr{E}_{T}, \mathscr{G}_{T}\right)$ to zero implies that the experiment $\mathscr{E}_{T}$ is $K$-controlled.

Letting $W(u, \omega)=\Lambda(u, \omega)+\frac{1}{2} K\left(\delta_{u}-\delta_{\omega}\right)$ for $u \in T$, the $K$-control means that $\int W(u, \omega) \mu(d u) \rightarrow 0$ in probability if $\|\mu\|$ stays bounded and $K(\mu) \rightarrow 0$. The accessibility condition yields the existence of measures $\mu_{s} \in \mathscr{M}_{1}\left(S_{\omega}\right)$ such that $\left\|\mu_{s}\right\|$ remains bounded and such that $K\left(\mu_{s}-\delta_{s}\right) \rightarrow 0$. Arguing as in the proof of Theorem 1, Section 5, one sees that the difference

$$
\Lambda(s, \omega)+\frac{1}{2} K\left(\delta_{s}-\delta_{\omega}\right)-\left\langle Z_{\omega}-\delta_{\omega}, \mu_{s}-\delta_{\omega}\right\rangle_{K_{\omega}}
$$

tends to zero in probability.
Since the pairs ( $S_{\omega}, K_{\omega}$ ) are free, Lemma 1 of Section 4 implies that both $\left\|Z_{\omega}-\delta_{\omega}\right\|$ and $K_{\omega}\left(Z_{\omega}-\delta_{\omega}\right)$ are bounded in probability. It follows from this that, in the preceding expressions, one may replace $K_{\omega}$ by the form $K$. Thus

$$
\Lambda(s, \omega)+\frac{1}{2} K\left(\delta_{s}-\delta_{\omega}\right)-\left\langle Z_{\omega}-\delta_{\omega}, \delta_{s}-\delta_{\omega}\right\rangle_{K}
$$

tends to zero in probability. According to Lemma 3, the same statement holds if $K$ is replaced everywhere by either one $\Gamma, \Gamma_{\theta}, \widehat{\Gamma}$ or $\widetilde{\Gamma}$.

The argument applies also to the point $t$. Taking differences, one concludes that expressions such as

$$
\Lambda(s, t)+\frac{1}{2}\left[K\left(Z_{\omega}-\delta_{s}\right)-K\left(Z_{\omega}-\delta_{t}\right)\right]
$$

tend to zero in probability. This is true for every $\omega$ such that $q(\theta, \omega)$ remains bounded. To conclude the proof one uses the terminal argument of Theorem 1 , Section 5 , using the finiteness of the sets $\Omega(\theta, b), b$ fixed.

To describe another interesting property of the centerings $\hat{Z}$ consider a favorable point $\theta$ and say that the construction sets $S_{\omega}, \omega \in \Omega$ are coherent at that point if whenever $q(\omega, \theta)$ remains bounded the sets $S_{\omega}$ are accessible. This means that each pair ( $S_{\omega_{1}}, \Gamma_{\theta}$ ) spans a pair ( $S_{\omega_{2}}, \Gamma_{\theta}$ ) as long as $q\left(\omega_{1}, \theta\right)+q\left(\omega_{2}, \theta\right)$ remains bounded. It follows that, eventually, $\operatorname{dim} \mathscr{M}_{0}\left(S_{\omega_{1}}\right)=\operatorname{dim} \mathscr{M}_{0}\left(S_{\omega_{2}}\right)$, since
the pairs $\left(S_{\omega_{1}}, \Gamma_{\theta}\right)$ are free. This means that there are integers $k$ (depending on $v)$ since that if $q(\omega, \theta)$ remains bounded, then, eventually, $\operatorname{dim} \mathscr{M}_{0}\left(S_{\omega}\right)-k=0$. This number $k$ will be called the rank of the pairs ( $S_{\omega}, \Gamma_{\theta}$ ) at $\theta$. If all points $s$ such that $q(s, \theta)$ remains bounded are accessible, the numbers $k$ will also be called the rank of $\mathscr{E}$ at $\theta$.

Take a particular point $\omega_{1} \in \Omega$ among those closest (for $q$ ) to the point $\theta$ and let $\pi$ be the projection of $\mathscr{M}_{1}(\Theta)$ onto $\mathscr{M}_{1}(S), S=S_{\omega_{1}}$ orthogonally for $\Gamma_{\theta}$.

Proposition 1. Let $\theta$ be a favorable point at which the system $\left\{S_{\omega} ; \omega \in \Omega\right\}$ is coherent. Let $s \in \Theta$ be accessible and such that $q(s, \theta)$ remains bounded. Fix a number $b$ and let $S(b)=\bigcup\left\{S_{\omega} ; q(\omega, \theta) \leq b\right\}$. Then
(i) if $\lambda \in \mathscr{M}_{0}[S(b)]$ is such that $\|\lambda\|$ stays bounded, the dual Lipschitz distance between $\mathscr{L}\left\{\left\langle\hat{Z}-\delta_{s}, \lambda\right\rangle_{\Gamma_{\theta}} \mid P_{s}\right\}$ and the Gaussian distribution $\mathscr{N}\left[0, \Gamma_{\theta}(\lambda)\right]$ tends to zero.
(ii) Under the same conditions on $\lambda$ the distance between $\mathscr{L}\left\{\left\langle\hat{Z}-\pi \delta_{\theta}, \lambda\right\rangle_{\Gamma_{\theta}} \mid P_{\theta}\right\}$ and $\mathscr{N}\left[0, \Gamma_{\theta}(\lambda)\right]$ tends to zero.
(iii) If $q(t, \theta)$ remains bounded, the dual Lipschitz distance between $\mathscr{L}\{\tilde{\Gamma}(\hat{Z}- \left.\left.\delta_{t}\right) \mid P_{\theta}\right\}$ and a distribution $\mathscr{L}\left\{\eta_{1}+\chi^{2}\left(\eta_{2}\right)\right\}$ tends to zero, for a $\chi^{2}$ with $k$ degrees of freedom and noncentrality parameter $\eta_{2}=\Gamma_{\theta}\left(\pi \delta_{\theta}-\pi \delta_{t}\right)$ and for $\eta_{1}=\Gamma_{\theta}\left(\delta_{t}-\pi \delta_{t}\right)$.
(iv) If $q(t, \theta)$ tends to infinity, then $\widetilde{\Gamma}\left(\hat{Z}-\delta_{t}\right)$ tends to infinity in $P_{\theta}$ probability.

Proof. Apply the statement of Theorem 1 to two accessible values $s$ and $\tau$ such that $q(\theta, s)+q(\theta, \tau)$ remains bounded. The assertion of Theorem 1implies that the difference between $\left\langle\hat{Z}-\delta_{s}, \delta_{\tau}-\delta_{s}\right\rangle_{\Gamma_{\theta}}$ and $\Lambda(\tau, s)+\frac{1}{2} \Gamma_{\theta}\left(\delta_{\tau}-\delta_{s}\right. )$ tends to zero in $P_{s}$ and $P_{\theta}$ probability. Taking a $\lambda \in \mathscr{M}_{0}[S(b)]$, with $\|\lambda\|$ bounded, the coherence property of the $S_{\omega}$ implies that the difference between $\left\langle\hat{Z}-\delta_{s}, \lambda\right\rangle_{\Gamma_{\theta}}$ and $\int\left[\Lambda(\tau, s)+\frac{1}{2} \Gamma_{\theta}\left(\delta_{\tau}-\delta_{s}\right)\right] \lambda(d \tau)$ tends to zero in probability.

Let $\mathscr{G}$ be the Gaussian experiment corresponding to the form $\Gamma_{\theta}$. The assumption (G1) implies that if $\Lambda_{G}$ is the likelihood function associated with $\mathscr{G}$, then the distance between the distributions of $\int \Lambda(\tau, s) \lambda(d \tau)$ and $\int \Lambda_{G}(\tau, s) \lambda(d \tau)$ tends to zero. This yields the first assertion of the proposition. For the second, consider the same integrals $X=\int\left[\Lambda(\tau, s)+\frac{1}{2} \Gamma_{\theta}\left(\delta_{\tau}-\delta_{s}\right)\right] \lambda(d \tau)$ and the logarithm $\Lambda(\theta, s)$. According to the contiguity assumption implicit in (G1), the distribution under $P_{\theta}$ of $X$ is approximable by $\mathscr{N}[C, \Gamma(\lambda)]$ where $C$ is the covariance between $\Lambda_{G}(\theta, s)$ and $\int \Lambda_{G}(\tau, s) \lambda(d \tau)$ in the approximating Gaussian experiment. This covariance is $\left\langle\delta_{\theta}-\delta_{s}, \lambda\right\rangle_{\Gamma_{\theta}}$. Also $\Gamma_{\theta}(\hat{Z}-\pi \hat{Z})$ tends to zero in probability. This implies part (ii) of the proposition.

Now consider assertion (iii) and replace $\hat{Z}$ by $Y=\pi \hat{Z}$. According to Lemma 3 one can replace $\tilde{\Gamma}$ by $\Gamma_{\theta}$. Thus it is enough to look at the behavior of $\Gamma_{\theta}\left(Y-\delta_{t}\right)$. Now

$$
Y-\delta_{t}=Y-\pi \delta_{\theta}+\left(\pi \delta_{\theta}-\pi \delta_{t}\right)-\left(\delta_{t}-\pi \delta_{t}\right)
$$

and

$$
\Gamma_{\theta}\left(Y-\delta_{t}\right)=\Gamma_{\theta}\left[\left(Y-\pi \delta_{\theta}\right)+\left(\pi \delta_{\theta}-\pi \delta_{t}\right)\right]+\Gamma_{\theta}\left(\delta_{t}-\pi \delta_{t}\right) .
$$

The vector $Y-\pi \delta_{\theta}$ has, under $P_{\theta}$, a distribution approximable by a centered Gaussian distribution. Thus, $\Gamma_{\theta}\left\{\left(Y-\pi \delta_{\theta}\right)+\left(\pi \delta_{\theta}-\pi \delta_{t}\right)\right\}$ is asymptotically $\chi_{k}^{2}\left(\eta_{1}\right)$ with a noncentrality parameter $\eta_{1}=\Gamma_{\theta}\left(\pi \delta_{\theta}-\pi \delta_{t}\right)$. This yields statement (iii).

For the last statement, note that the forms $\widetilde{\Gamma}$ are positive on the entire space $\mathscr{M}_{0}(\Theta)$. Therefore

$$
\left[\tilde{\Gamma}\left(\hat{Z}-\delta_{t}\right)\right]^{1 / 2} \geq\left[\tilde{\Gamma}\left(\delta_{\theta}-\delta_{t}\right)\right]^{1 / 2}-\left[\tilde{\Gamma}\left(\hat{Z}-\delta_{\theta}\right)\right]^{1 / 2}
$$

According to statement (iii) of the proposition, the variables $\tilde{\Gamma}\left(\hat{Z}-\delta_{\theta}\right)$ are bounded in probability. Thus to obtain (iv) it is enough to show that $\tilde{\Gamma}\left(\delta_{t}-\delta_{\theta}\right) \rightarrow \infty$. However, if $\tilde{\Gamma}$ assumes a value $K_{j}, j \in J$, if both $\theta$ and $t$ belong to the ball $\Theta[j, m(j)]$, and if $K_{j}\left(\delta_{\theta}-\delta_{t}\right)$ remains bounded then $K_{j}\left(\delta_{\theta}-\delta_{t}\right)- q^{2}(\theta, t) \rightarrow 0$. If on the contrary $\theta \in \Theta[j, m(j)]$ and $t \notin \Theta[j, m(j)]$, then $K_{j}\left(\delta_{\theta}-\delta_{t}\right) \geq m(j)$. Since $m(j)$ tends to infinity, one see that $\tilde{\Gamma}\left(\delta_{\theta}-\delta_{t}\right)$ cannot stay bounded in probability unless $q(t, \theta)$ does. The result follows.

To describe certain consequences of Proposition 1, and for further use, let us introduce a supplementary assumption.

Definition 6. Condition (A) is satisfied if for every favorable $\theta$ every $s \in \Theta$ such that $q(\theta, s)$ remains bounded is accessible.

When (A) holds, the coherence condition of Proposition 1 is automatically satisfied. The assertion (iii) of the proposition means then that $\mathscr{L}\{\tilde{\Gamma}(\hat{Z}- \left.\left.\delta_{t}\right) \mid P_{\theta}\right\}$ differs little from $\mathscr{L}\left[\chi^{2}(\eta)\right]$ for a $\chi^{2}$ with $k$ degrees of freedom and noncentrality parameter $\eta=\Gamma_{\theta}\left(\delta_{t}-\delta_{\theta}\right)$. This suggests the use of balls $\{t: \left.\tilde{\Gamma}\left(\hat{Z}-\delta_{t}\right) \leq c\right\}$ as confidence balls for the unknown parameter $\theta$.

Instead of these balls one could use the sets $\left\{t: \Gamma_{t}\left(\hat{Z}-\delta_{t}\right) \leq c\right\}$. These are no longer "balls," but this is of little importance, perhaps. However, a difficulty may arise from the fact that for certain $t \in \Theta$ the quadratic forms $\Gamma_{t}$ may become extremely small. Thus, it may happen that such values of $t$ get included in the set $\left\{t ; \Gamma_{t}\left(\mathcal{Z}-\delta_{t}\right) \leq c\right\}$ even though the true value of $\theta$ is such that $q(t, \theta)$ is very large.

For this reason it may be preferable to use $\tilde{\Gamma}$ instead of $\Gamma_{t}$. When the forms $K_{\omega}$ used in the construction are obtained by a midpoint computation, one may be tempted to use them instead of the $\tilde{\Gamma}$ of Proposition 1. This is feasible, of course, only if the $K_{\omega}$ are defined either on the entire set $\mathscr{M}_{0}(\Theta)$ or at least on a very large subspace of it. We shall see, in Section 8, cases in which a midpoint procedure defines the $K_{\omega}$ on the entire $\mathscr{M}_{0}(\Theta)$ and where one can prevent degeneracy of the estimated $\hat{K}=K_{\hat{\theta}}$. Then, an analogue of Proposition 1 holds for these $\hat{K}$.

When the midpoint method yields estimated forms $\hat{K}$ which are positive and defined on $\mathscr{M}_{0}(\Theta)$, there is some advantage in using $\hat{K}\left(\hat{Z}-\delta_{t}\right)$ instead of



$\tilde{\Gamma}\left(\hat{Z}-\delta_{t}\right)$ to obtain confidence balls. Indeed it may happen that the experiment $\mathscr{E}$ is $K$-controlled in the sense of Sections 4 and 5 for random forms, but that it is not approximately Gaussian. In such a case the use of confidence balls $\left\{t ; \hat{K}\left(\hat{Z}-\delta_{t}\right) \leq c\right\}$ can still be defended, while the sets $\left\{t: \tilde{\Gamma}\left(\hat{Z}-\delta_{t}\right) \leq c\right\}$ do not enjoy any particularly remarkable properties.

The assertions of Theorem 1 are limited to accessible elements of $\Theta$. This is in the nature of things. However, when dealing with nuisance parameters, it is convenient to use somewhat more complex relations. Here we shall assume that the "nuisance parameters" are given locally by specifying for each $\theta \in \Theta$ a linear subspace $L_{\theta}$ of $\mathscr{M}_{0}(\Theta)$. The "parameters of interest" are then defined locally by using Charles Stein's orthogonality relations.

We shall say that the differences in the construction sets $S_{\omega}$ are orthogonal to the nuisance directions if whenever $q(\theta, \omega)$ remains bounded the inner products $\left\langle\mu, \delta_{s}-\delta_{\omega}\right\rangle_{\Gamma_{\theta}}$ tend to zero for all $s \in S_{\omega}$ and all $\mu \in L_{\theta}$ such that $\|\mu\| \leq 1$.

A point $s \in \Theta$ will be called accessible modulo $L_{\theta}$ if whenever $\omega \in \Omega$ is such that $q(\theta, \omega)$ remains bounded, there are elements $\mu_{s}$ and $\lambda_{s}$ with the following properties.
(a) $\left\|\mu_{s}\right\|+\left\|\lambda_{s}\right\|$ remains bounded;
(b) $\mu_{s} \in \mathscr{M}_{1}\left(S_{\omega}\right)$ and $\lambda_{s} \in L_{\theta}$;
(c) $\Gamma_{\theta}\left[\delta_{s}-\left(\mu_{s}+\lambda_{s}\right)\right]$ tends to zero;
(d) if $\Sigma_{s}$ is the support of $\lambda_{s}$, then $\sup _{\sigma}\left\{q(\theta, \sigma) ; \sigma \in \Sigma_{s}\right\}$ remains bounded;
(e) the experiments $\left\{P_{\sigma} ; \sigma \in \Sigma_{s}\right\}$ are tail precompact.

The intent of this definition may be difficult to visualize. It can be readily understood by drawing a two-dimensional plot where $\theta$ has two coordinates, say $\xi$ and $\eta$. Take $\xi$ as the parameter of interest and take $\eta$ as nuisance. Take for $\Omega$ the lattice of points with integer coordinates. For each $\omega \in \Omega$ let $S_{\omega}$ consist of the point $\omega$ itself and of a point $\omega^{\prime}$ such that $\eta\left(\omega^{\prime}\right)=\eta(\omega)$ and $\xi\left(\omega^{\prime}\right)= \xi(\omega)+1$. Let $\Gamma_{\theta}$ be the form associated with the identity matrix of $\mathbb{R}^{2}$. Then, for any given $\omega$, the points $s$ in the span of $S_{\omega}$ are those situated on the line $\{s: \eta(s)=\eta(\omega)\}$. Two points $s$ and $t$ with different ordinates but the same abscissa $\xi(s)=\xi(t)$ differ by an element $\lambda$ of the nuisance space $L_{\theta}$.

In the $\mathbb{R}^{2}$ case used as an example here, and in the general case, it is clear that the only log-likelihood values $\Lambda(t, s)$ of any relevance are those where $s$ and $t$ do not differ in their nuisance coordinates. This is reflected in the next result.

Theorem 2. Let $\theta$ be favorable and let $s$ and $t$ be two elements of $\Theta$ such that $q(\theta, s)+q(\theta, t)$ remains bounded. Assume that the differences in the construction sets are orthogonal to the nuisance directions. Assume also that if $\lambda \in L_{\theta}$ and $\|\lambda\| \leq 1$ then $\left\langle\lambda, \delta_{s}-\delta_{t}\right\rangle_{\Gamma_{\theta}}$ tends to zero. Then the quantity

$$
\Lambda(s, t)+\frac{1}{2}\left[\Gamma_{\theta}\left(\hat{Z}-\delta_{s}\right)-\Gamma_{\theta}\left(\hat{Z}-\delta_{t}\right)\right]
$$

tends to zero in $P_{\theta}$ probability.
Proof. We shall prove the result under the supplementary assumption that the supports $\Sigma_{s}$ and $\Sigma_{t}$ of the relevant elements $\lambda_{s}$ and $\lambda_{t}$ of the accessibility module $L_{\theta}$ definition have a bounded cardinality. The general tail-precompact case is
proved in the same manner, but its proof depends on the $K$-control assertion included in Proposition 3, later in this section.

Consider an $\omega \in \Omega$ such that $q(\theta, \omega)$ remains bounded. Let $T_{s}=S_{\omega} \cup \Sigma_{s} \cup\{s\}$ and let $T=T_{s} \cup T_{t}$. Let $K$ be the quadratic form defined on $\mathscr{M}_{0}(T)$ by a Gaussian experiment $\mathscr{G}_{T}$ such that $\Delta\left(\mathscr{G}_{T}, \mathscr{E}_{T}\right)$ tends to zero. The orthogonality relations given in terms of $\Gamma_{\theta}$ imply similar relations for $K$. Thus $K\left(\delta_{s}-\mu_{s}-\lambda_{s}\right)$ and $K\left(\delta_{t}-\mu_{t}-\lambda_{t}\right)$ tend to zero. It follows that $K\left[\left(\delta_{s}-\delta_{t}\right)-\left(\mu_{s}-\mu_{t}\right)-\left(\lambda_{s}-\lambda_{t}\right)\right]$ tends to zero. However the $\lambda_{s}-\lambda_{t}$ are asymptotically orthogonal to $\delta_{s}-\delta_{t}$ and to $\mu_{s}-\mu_{t}$. Therefore both $K\left(\lambda_{s}-\lambda_{t}\right)$ and $K\left[\left(\delta_{s}-\delta_{t}\right)-\left(\mu_{1}-\mu_{t}\right)\right]$ tend to zero. Since the experiments $\mathscr{E}_{T}$ are $K$-controlled one can find $L_{1}$-equivalent experiments $\mathscr{E}_{T}^{\prime \prime}=\left\{P_{\tau}^{\prime \prime} ; \tau \in T\right\}$ and selections of log-likelihoods $\Lambda^{\prime \prime}$ such that if $W^{\prime \prime}(\tau, \omega)=\Lambda^{\prime \prime}(\tau, \omega)+\frac{1}{2} K\left(\delta_{\tau}-\delta_{\omega}\right)$ then $\int W^{\prime \prime}(\tau, \omega) \mu(d \omega)$ tends to zero whenever $\|\mu\|$ remains bounded and $K(\mu)$ tends to zero. Applying this to $\left(\delta_{s}-\delta_{t}\right)-\left(\mu_{s}-\mu_{t}\right)$ one sees that

$$
W^{\prime \prime}(s, \omega)-W^{\prime \prime}(t, \omega)-\int W^{\prime \prime}(\tau, \omega)\left(\mu_{s}-\mu_{t}\right)(d \tau)
$$

tends to zero in probability.
From this point on, the proof proceeds exactly along the same lines as the proof of Theorem 1. Briefly, one replaces $\int W^{\prime \prime}(\tau, \omega)\left(\mu_{s}-\delta_{\omega}\right)$ by the inner products $\left\langle Z_{\omega}-\delta_{\omega}, \mu_{s}-\delta_{\omega}\right\rangle_{K_{\omega}}$ and then by $\left\langle Z_{\omega}-\delta_{\omega}, \mu_{s}-\delta_{\omega}\right\rangle_{K}$, and then again by $\left\langle Z_{\omega}-\delta_{\omega}, \delta_{s}-\delta_{\omega}\right\rangle$. Finally one replaces $\omega$ by its estimated value $\hat{\theta}$ using the same argument as in Theorem 1, Section 5. The result follows.

Remark 1. As in the other results previously derived in this section, we have assumed that the sets $S_{\omega}$ are available. It may happen that the nuisance spaces $L_{\theta}$ are specified for each $\theta \in \Theta$ but that the sets $S_{\omega}$ are not yet prescribed. Then, to make use of Theorem 2, one would endeavor to select sets $S_{\omega}$ with differences orthogonal to the appropriate $L_{\theta}$. This is not feasible unless the $L_{\theta}$ satisfy suitable continuity restrictions.

Assuming that $\Omega$ has been chosen, what is needed is that whenever $q(\theta, \omega)$ remains bounded the relations " $\mu \in L_{\omega},\|\mu\| \leq 1$ implies $\left\langle\mu, \delta_{s}-\delta_{\omega}\right\rangle_{\Gamma_{\theta}} \rightarrow 0$ ", entail that if $\lambda \in L_{\theta},\|\lambda\| \leq 1$ then $\left\langle\lambda, \delta_{s}-\delta_{\omega}\right\rangle_{\Gamma_{\theta}} \rightarrow 0$. In such a case, for each $\omega \in \Omega$ one would select the points $s \in S_{\omega}$ in such a way that $\delta_{s}-\delta_{\omega}$ be orthogonal to $L_{\omega}$ (for $\Gamma_{\omega}$ ). The assertions of Theorem 2 are then available.

Remark 2. Assuming that the nuisance spaces $L_{\theta}$ satisfy the continuity restriction just described, one can modify the accessibility assumption (A) to require only that if $\theta$ is favorable and if $q(s, \theta)$ remains bounded, then $s$ is accessible modulo $L_{\theta}$. In such a case the differences $\hat{Z}-\delta_{t}$ still possess asymptotic normality properties analogous to those of Proposition 2. Indeed Theorem 2 says that $\Lambda(s, t)$ is approximated by

$$
\left\langle\hat{Z}-\delta_{t}, \delta_{s}-\delta_{t}\right\rangle_{\Gamma_{\theta}}-\frac{1}{2} \Gamma_{\theta}\left(\delta_{t}-\delta_{s}\right)
$$

Since $\Lambda(s, t)$ is asymptotically normally distributed, the same will be true of $\left\langle\mu, \hat{Z}-\delta_{t}\right\rangle_{\Gamma_{\theta}}$ for $\mu \in \mathscr{M}_{0}(\Theta),\|\mu\| \leq 1$, as in Proposition 2, provided that $\mu$ be
asymptotically orthogonal to $L_{\theta}$.
Remark 3. The assertions of Theorems 1 and 2 have been proved under the restriction that $\theta$ be "favorable." This implies a boundedness restriction on the cardinality of the sets $S_{\omega}$ and means that, locally, the "parameters of interest" are restricted to lie very close to a linear space of bounded dimensionality.

For the nuisance parameters, the situation is somewhat different. The restriction imposed by Condition (D) is a dimensionality restriction in the Kolmogorov sense. It does not prevent the associated Hilbert spaces from becoming infinite dimensional.

Remark 4. As explained in Section 5, the $\Lambda(s, t)$ of Theorem 2 can be considered as logarithms of likelihood ratios in an unknown experiment which however happens to be the one at hand.

Now let us consider more specifically the case where the accessibility condition (A) of Definition 6 is satisfied at all favorable points. In such a case let $\Theta(\theta, b)=\{s: s \in \Theta, q(\theta, s) \leq b\}$ for a fixed value of $b$. Then, for every favorable $\theta$, the experiments $\mathscr{E}(\theta, b)=\left\{P_{s} ; s \in \Theta(\theta, b)\right\}$ are tail precompact and approximable by Gaussian experiments of bounded rank. The centerings $\hat{Z}$, if they are measurable, are asymptotically sufficient and distinguished for these experiments $\mathscr{E}(\theta, b)$.

This suggests that the centerings $\hat{Z}$ may have usable asymptotic sufficiency properties for the experiments $\mathscr{E}=\left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ themselves. However, consider two points $\theta_{1}$ and $\theta_{2}$ at which all the conditions (A), (D), (E), (FF), and (G1) are duly satisfied and let $S=\Theta\left(\theta_{1}, b\right) \cup \Theta\left(\theta_{2}, b\right)$. It does not follow that if $\hat{Z}$ is asymptotically sufficient for $\mathscr{E}\left(\theta_{1}, b\right)$ and $\mathscr{E}\left(\theta_{2}, b\right)$ it will also be asymptotically sufficient for $\mathscr{E}_{S}=\left\{P_{s} ; s \in S\right\}$.

Indeed, consider the following example. For each integer $m$ let $J_{m}$ be the interval $J_{m}=\left(m+\frac{1}{4}, m+\frac{3}{4}\right)$ of the line. For each integer $v$ and $\theta \in \Theta= \bigcup_{m} J_{m}$, let $P_{\theta, v}^{1}$ be the distribution of a Gaussian variable $X_{v}$ with expectation $\theta$ and variance $v^{-1}$. Let $Y_{v}$ be the fractional part $Y_{v}=X_{v}-\left[X_{v}\right]$ of $X_{v}$ and let $P_{\theta, v}^{2}$ be the distribution of $Y_{v}$. If $\theta \in J_{m}$, let $Q_{\theta, v}$ be the distribution of $Y_{v}+m$. Then $\left\|Q_{\theta, v}-P_{\theta, v}^{1}\right\| \leq 32 v^{-1}$. Therefore, for each given $m$, the insufficiency of $Y_{v}$ with respect to $X_{v}$ is at most $32 v^{-1}$. However, if $\mathscr{E}_{v}^{i}=\left\{P_{\theta, v}^{i} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$, the deficiency of $\mathscr{E}_{v}^{2}$ with respect to $\mathscr{E}_{v}^{1}$ tends to unity as $v \rightarrow \infty$ since $Y_{v}$ is unable to sort out which set $J_{m}$ contains $\theta$.

In this case the logarithms of likelihood ratio $\Lambda(s, \theta)$ do possess approximations of the type

$$
\Lambda(s, \theta) \sim-\frac{v}{2}\left\{\left|Y_{v}-\phi(s)\right|^{2}-\left|Y_{v}-\phi(\theta)\right|^{2}\right\}
$$

for all pairs $(s, \theta)$ which stay in the same $J_{m}$. The condition (G1) is certainly satisfied. Thus there seems to be something to prove as far as sufficiency of the centerings $\hat{Z}$ is concerned.

On the other hand, the $\hat{Z}$ retain certain pieces of information which seem rather irrelevant. For instance, take the precaution of making all the $S_{\omega}$ two
by two disjoint (this is possible in standard cases). To give $\hat{Z}$ is to give its support and therefore also the value of the auxiliary estimate $\hat{\theta}$ used in the construction. Also, if $\Theta$ is actually a Euclidean space and if one has mapped $\mathscr{M}(\Theta)$ linearly into $\Theta$, as in Section 2 the information about the support of $\hat{Z}$ is lost in the process, although the approximation results for likelihood ratios remain. Thus, we shall describe sufficiency properties not for the $Z$ themselves but for functions $f$ of them.

It would be very pleasant, of course, to be able to assert that for the experiments $\mathscr{E}$ themselves the insufficiency of $\hat{Z}$ or $f(\hat{Z})$ tends to zero. This is unfortunately not possible to prove unless one assumes that the conditions used here, such as (A), (D), (E), (FF) and (G1) hold uniformly on $\Theta$. It is also unfortunate that such conditions rarely hold uniformly in practical examples. (Consider for instance $\theta \in(0,1)$ with $P_{\theta}$ binomial with probability of success $\theta$ and number of observations v.) Thus it will be necessary to introduce some other possibilities.

For the functions $f$ we shall use the following requirement in which, as usual, everything may depend on the suppressed index $v$.

Definition 7. Let $\mathscr{X}$ be a space with a metric $r$. Let $f$ be a function from $\mathscr{M}_{1}(\Theta)$ to $\mathscr{X}$. The function $f$ will be called boundedly separating for $\mathscr{E}$ if the following requirements are satisfied.
(i) If $(s, t) \in \Theta \times \Theta$ and if $r\left[f\left(\delta_{s}\right), f\left(\delta_{t}\right)\right]$ remains bounded, so does $q(s, t)$.
(ii) Let $\theta \in \Theta$ satisfy condition (G0) and let $\lambda$ and $\mu$ belong to $\mathscr{M}_{1}[\Theta(\theta, b)]$. Assume $\|\lambda\|+\|\mu\| \leq b$.

Then, for every fixed $b, r[f(\lambda), f(\mu)]$ remains bounded if and only if $\Gamma_{\theta}(\lambda-\mu)$ does. If in addition $r[f(\lambda), f(\mu)]$ tends to zero, so does $\Gamma_{\theta}(\lambda-\mu)$.

Up to this point we have worked with inequalities for approximations of likelihood ratios by functions of $\hat{Z}$. Measurability problems could easily be ignored. However, lack of measurability would make our next result awkward to state. Thus we shall entertain some measurability considerations. For this it will be convenient to assume that each set $S_{\omega}$ of the construction has been given a total order and that the map from $\omega$ to the set so ordered is a Borel map for the Hellinger distance.

Let $\mathscr{B}_{1}$ be the Borel $\sigma$-field of $\mathscr{M}_{1}(\Theta)$ for the $L_{1}$-norm. Let $\mathscr{B}_{0}$ be the $\sigma$-field for which the maps $\mu \mapsto \int \gamma d \mu$ are measurable if $\gamma$ is a Borel function with finite support. The centerings $Z_{\omega}$ are obtainable through solutions of finite systems of linear equations in a space $\mathscr{M}_{1}\left(S_{\omega}\right)$ of finite dimension. Thus if the $Z_{\omega}$ are well determined, they are $\mathscr{B}_{0}$-measurable and therefore $\mathscr{B}_{1}$-measurable.

If the solutions are not unique, one can certainly select solutions in a measurable manner. Thus it is no great problem to ensure the measurability of the $Z_{\omega}$, even for $\mathscr{B}_{1}$. If $\Omega$ is a skeleton, as described in Section 5, and if the "estimates" $\hat{\theta}$ are measurable, then for each $P_{\theta}$ the number of possible values of $\hat{\theta}$ will be countable. Thus the $\hat{Z}$ will be measurable, even for $\mathscr{B}_{1}$. Even in the construction used to pass from another auxiliary estimate to $\hat{\theta}$ itself in Section 5, the $\hat{\theta}$ is nearly measurable for all $P_{\theta}$ for which anything is asserted. Also, under nearly all practically conceivable situations one can readily insure the measur-
ability of $\hat{\theta}$. Thus, strong measurability of the centerings $\hat{Z}$ is no real problem. For the function $f$ from $\mathscr{M}_{1}(\Theta)$ to a metric space $\mathscr{X}$, call $f$ "measurable" if it is measurable for the strong $\sigma$-field $\mathscr{B}_{1}$ of $\mathscr{M}_{1}(\Theta)$ and the Borel field of $\mathscr{X}$. Then, for any Borel set $B \subset \mathscr{X}$, the inverse image $A$ of $B$ by the map $f \circ \hat{Z}$ is an element of the $\sigma$-field $\mathscr{A}$ on which the $P_{\theta}$ are defined. It would actually be sufficient for our purposes to assume that for such an $A$ and for each $\theta \in \Theta$, there is an $A_{\theta} \in \mathscr{A}$ such that $A_{\theta}$ is $P_{\theta}$ equivalent to $A$.

Theorem 3. Let $\mathscr{E}=\left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ be given by measures $P_{\theta}$ on a $\sigma$-field A . Let $\hat{Z}$ be measurable and let $f$ be measurable and boundedly separating. Let $\mathscr{B} \subset \mathscr{A}$ be the $\sigma$-field induced by the map $f \circ \hat{Z}$.

Then there are other families of measures $\left\{Q_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ defined on the $\sigma$-field $\mathscr{A}$ for which the following relations hold:
(i) For $\left\{Q_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ the $\sigma$-field $\mathscr{B}$ is sufficient.
(ii) For $B \in \mathscr{B}$ one has $Q_{\theta}(B)=P_{\theta}(B)$ identically in $\theta$.
(iii) For every fixed $b$ and for every $\theta$ at which the conditions (A), (B), (D), (E), (FF), and (G0) are satisfied, the quantity

$$
\sup _{s}\left\{\left\|P_{s}-Q_{s}\right\| ; q(s, \theta) \leq b\right\}
$$

tends to zero as $v$ tends to infinity.
(In particular if (A), (B), (D), (E), (FF), and (G0) hold uniformly on a set $S$, then $\sup _{s}\left\{\left\|P_{s}-Q_{s}\right\| ; s \in S\right\}$ tends to zero. This is about as strong a statement as could be expected.)

Proof. Consider a $\theta$ at which all the conditions (A), (B), (D), (E), (FF), and (G0) are satisfied. Let

$$
S(\theta, b)=\left\{s, s \in \Theta, r\left[f\left(\delta_{s}\right), f\left(\delta_{\theta}\right)\right] \leq b\right\}
$$

and let $\bar{\eta}(\theta, b)$ be the insufficiency of $\mathscr{B}$ with respect to $\mathscr{A}$ for the experiment $\left\{P_{s}\right\}$ with $s \in S(\theta, b)$.

Let us first show that $\bar{\eta}(\theta, b)$ tends to zero. To do this, note that since $r\left[f\left(\delta_{s}\right), f\left(\delta_{\theta}\right)\right]$ remains bounded so does $q(s, \theta)$. Thus $q(s, \theta) \leq c$ for some fixed $c$ and all $s \in S(\theta, b)$. According to Chapter 5, Section 4, Proposition 5, and according to the contiguity condition included in (G0), it will be sufficient to show that for each pair $(s, \theta), s \in S(\theta, b)$ the deficiency of $\mathscr{B}$ with respect to $\mathscr{A}$ for the binary experiment $\left\{P_{s}, P_{\theta}\right\}$ tends to zero. Now consider $f(\hat{Z})$ and let $Z^{*}$ be any other point of $\mathscr{M}_{1}(\Theta)$ such that $r\left[f(\hat{Z}), f\left(Z^{*}\right)\right]$ tends to zero, with $Z^{*}$ bounded. Then $\Gamma_{\theta}\left(Z^{*}-\hat{Z}\right)$ tends to zero and $\Lambda(s, \theta)$ can be approximated by $-\frac{1}{2}\left\{\Gamma_{\theta}\left(Z^{*}-\delta_{s}\right)-\Gamma_{\theta}\left(Z^{*}-\delta_{\theta}\right)\right\}$.

In other words, if $f(\hat{Z})$ varies little, then $\Lambda(s, \theta)$ varies little. Thus not only is $f(\hat{Z})$ asymptotically sufficient for the experiment $\left\{P_{s} ; s \in S(\theta, b)\right\}$, but it is also distinguished in the sense of Chapter 7, Section 3.

Now take a fixed positive number $a>0$. Let $J$ be a subset of $\Theta$ that is maximal among all subsets of $\Theta$ in which two distinct points $s$ and $t$ satisfy the inequality $r\left[f\left(\delta_{s}\right), f\left(\delta_{t}\right)\right] \geq a$. For each element $j \in J$, let $C_{j}=\{x: x \in \mathscr{X}$,
$r(x, j) \leq a\}$ and let $A_{j}=\left\{s ; f\left(\delta_{s}\right) \in C_{j}\right\}$. Also, for each integer $m=0,1,2, \cdots$, let $A_{j}(m)$ be the set of points $s \in \Theta$ such that $r\left[f\left(\delta_{s}\right), f\left(\delta_{j}\right)\right] \leq m$. Let $\eta(j, m)$ be the insufficiency of $\mathscr{B}$ with respect to $\mathscr{A}$ for the experiment $\mathscr{E}_{j}(m)=\left\{P_{s}\right.$; $\left.s \in A_{j}(m)\right\}$.

Let $\eta_{j}=\sup _{m} \eta(j, m)$. If $\eta_{j}=0$ for some $j$, then the insufficiency of $\mathscr{B}$ for the entire experiment $\mathscr{E}=\left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ is equal to zero. If so, the conclusion of the theorem is part of the results exposed in Chapter 5. Thus we shall proceed below, assuming that $\eta_{j}>0$ for all $j \in J$.

Let $\gamma(j, m)$ be the number

$$
\gamma(j, m)=\exp \{-m\}+\sup _{t \in A_{J}} P_{t}\left\{r\left[f(\widehat{Z}), f\left(\delta_{t}\right)\right]>m\right\}
$$

By construction $\gamma(j, m)$ decreases as $m$ increases. Similarly, $\eta(j, m)$ increases as $m$ increases. Also $\gamma(j, 0) \geq 1$ and $\eta(j, m) \leq 1$. Thus, for certain indices $j \in J$, there will be some finite integer $m(j)$ which is the largest $m$ satisfying $\eta(j, m) \leq \gamma(j, m)$. If on the contrary $\eta(j, m) \leq \gamma(j, m)$ for all values of $m$, let $m(j)$ be equal
to the integer part of $\eta_{j}^{-1}$. Then $m(j)$ is always defined and finite. We shall let $A(j)=A_{j}[m(j)], \gamma(j)=\gamma[j, m(j)]$, and $\eta(j)=\eta[j, m(j)]$.

One first remark about the numbers $m(j)$ is that if $A_{j}$ contains some $\theta$ at which all the conditions (A), (B), (D), (E), (FF), and (G0) are satisfied, then $m(j)$ will tend to infinity. Indeed, for each fixed $m$, the insufficiency $\eta(j, m)$ tends to zero and $\gamma(j, m)$ is always at least $\exp \{-m\}$. This was the reason for the inclusion of the term $\exp \{-m\}$ in the definition of $\gamma(j, m)$.

Let $\mathscr{E}_{j}$ be the experiment $\mathscr{E}_{j}=\left\{P_{s} ; s \in A(j)\right\}$. Consider the associated spaces $L\left(\mathscr{E}_{j}\right)$ with duals $M\left(\mathscr{E}_{j}\right)$. Let $M^{\prime}\left(\mathscr{E}_{j}\right)$ be the subspace of $M\left(\mathscr{E}_{j}\right)$ induced by restricting the $P_{s}$ of $\mathscr{E}_{j}$ to $\mathscr{B}$. That is, $M^{\prime}\left(\mathscr{E}_{j}\right)$ is the closure in $M\left(\mathscr{E}_{j}\right)$ of the space of equivalence classes of bounded $\mathscr{B}$-measurable functions. By definition of the insufficiency, there is a positive linear projection $\Pi_{j}^{*}$ of $M\left(\mathscr{E}_{j}\right)$ onto $M^{\prime}\left(\mathscr{E}_{j}\right)$ such that $\left\|\Pi_{j}^{*} P_{s}-P_{s}\right\| \leq 2 \eta(j)$ for all $s \in A(j)$.

Returning to $\mathscr{E}$ itself, consider the corresponding spaces $M(\mathscr{E})$ and $M^{\prime}(\mathscr{E})$. Then $\Pi_{j}^{*}$ can be extended to the entire space $M(\mathscr{E})$ yielding a positive linear projection $\Pi_{j}$ of $M(\mathscr{E})$ onto $M^{\prime}(\mathscr{E})$ such that $\left\|\Pi_{j} P_{s}-P_{s}\right\| \leq 2 \eta(j)$ for all $s \in A(j)$. (See Chapter 5, Section 3, Proof of Theorem 1.) Note that, by construction, if there is in $A_{j}$ a $\theta$ which satisfies (A), (B), (D), (E), (FF), and (G0), then $\eta(j)$ tends to zero. Indeed $\eta(j) \leq \gamma(j)$ and $\gamma(j) \rightarrow 0$ since $m(j) \rightarrow \infty$.

Now well-order $J$ and proceed as in Section 5, Lemma 2, letting $U(j)$ be the open ball $\{x ; x \in \mathscr{X}, r(x, j)<a+1\}$ and letting $V(j)=U(j) \backslash \bigcup\left[U\left(j^{\prime}\right) ; j^{\prime}<j\right]$. Let $w(j)$ be the indicator in $M^{\prime}(\mathscr{E})$ of the set where $f(\hat{Z}) \in V(j)$. Define a map $\left[w(j) \Pi_{j}\right]$ from $M(\mathscr{E})$ to $M^{\prime}(\mathscr{E})$ by the equality $g\left[w(j) \Pi_{j}\right]=[g w(j)] \Pi_{j}$, where $g\left[w(j) \Pi_{j}\right]$ is the image of $g$ by the desired map and $[g w(j)] \Pi_{j}$ is the image of the product $[g w(j)]$ by $\Pi_{j}$.

Let $\Pi=\sum_{j} w(j) \Pi_{j}$. It has been shown (Chapter 5, Section 2, Lemma 2) that the image $[g w(j)] \Pi_{j}$ is equal to the product of $w(j)$ by $g \Pi_{j}$. From this and from the disjointness of the $w(j)$, one concludes immediately that $\Pi$ is a positive linear projection of $M(\mathscr{E})$ onto $M^{\prime}(\mathscr{E})$.

The corresponding image $Q_{\theta}=\Pi P_{\theta}$ belong to the second dual $L^{\prime \prime}$ of $L(\mathscr{E})$. They form an experiment for which $M^{\prime}(\mathscr{E})$ is sufficient. One can, if desired, replace the $Q_{\theta}$ by elements of $L(\mathscr{E})$, as done in Chapter 5, Section 4, Proposition 4. Either way one obtains an experiment for which $\mathscr{B}$ is completion sufficient. Thus it will be sufficient to show that for $b$ fixed and for a favorable $\theta$ satisfying (A), the quantity $\sup \left\{\left\|P_{s}-Q_{s}\right\| ; s \in \Theta(\theta, b)\right\}$ tends to zero.

Now take such a point $\theta$ and a fixed $b$. Then $\theta$ belongs to some $A_{i}$ for some $i \in J$. One can assume that $b$ has been taken so large that $A_{i} \subset \Theta(\theta, b)$. Also let $J(\theta)$ be the set $J(\theta)=\{j \in J: r[f(s), f(j)] \leq a+1$ for some $s \in \Theta(\theta, b)\}$. Then the corresponding numbers $\eta(j)$ tend to zero and the numbers $m(j)$ tend to infinity. Thus $\Theta(\theta, b)$ will eventually be contained in the corresponding sets $A(j)$.

To proceed, take a fixed $\varepsilon>0$ and an integer $m$ such that $\exp \{-m\}<\varepsilon$. Let $W$ be the set of points $z \in \mathscr{M}_{1}(\Theta)$ such that
(i) $z \in \mathscr{M}_{1}[\Theta(\theta, m)]$;
(ii) $\left|\Gamma_{\theta}\left(z-\delta_{\theta}\right)\right|+\left\|z-\delta_{\theta}\right\|+r\left[f(z), f\left(\delta_{\theta}\right)\right]<m$.

According to the conclusion of Theorem 1 and Proposition 1, there is some fixed $m$ such that $P_{\theta}\left\{\left|\Gamma_{\theta}\left(\hat{Z}-\delta_{\theta}\right)\right|+\left\|\hat{Z}-\delta_{\theta}\right\|>m\right\}<\varepsilon$. By the contiguity condition included in (G0), one can even find an integer $m$ such that

$$
P_{s}\left\{\left|\Gamma_{\theta}\left(\hat{Z}-\delta_{\theta}\right)\right|+\left\|\hat{Z}-\delta_{\theta}\right\|>m\right\}<\varepsilon \quad \text { for all } s \in \Theta(\theta, b)
$$

A similar inequality may be written for the event $\hat{Z} \notin \mathscr{M}_{1}[\Theta(\theta, m)]$. Thus, using the boundedness properties of $f$, one can find a fixed integer $m$ such that $P_{s}(W)>1-\varepsilon$ for all $s \in \Theta(\theta, b)$. Note that such a choice of $m$ implies that $\gamma(i, m)<2 \varepsilon$.

Now consider the set $F$ of indices $j$ such that $f(z) \in V(j)$ for some $z \in W$. The relations $r\left[f(z), f\left(\delta_{\theta}\right)\right]<m$ and $\theta \in A_{j}$ imply $r\left[f(z), f\left(\delta_{\theta}\right)\right]<m+a$. One has also $r\left[f\left(\delta_{j}\right), f(z)\right]<a+1$ for all pairs such that $f(z) \in V(j)$. Thus, $j \in F$ implies $r\left[f\left(\delta_{j}\right), f\left(\delta_{\theta}\right)\right]<m+2 a+1$. Write $\Pi=\Pi_{1}+\Pi_{2}$ where $\Pi_{1}$ is the sum $\sum\left\{\left[w(j) \Pi_{j}\right] ; j \in F\right\}$ and $\Pi_{2}=\sum\left\{\left[w(j) \Pi_{j}\right] ; j \notin F\right\}$.

According to the choice of $W$ made above, one has $\left\|\Pi_{2} P_{s}\right\|<2 \varepsilon$ for every $s \in \Theta(\theta, b)$. Eventually, one has also $\left\|\Pi_{j} P_{s}-P_{s}\right\|<2 \eta(j)$ for all $j \in F$ and $s \in \Theta(\theta, b)$. Thus $\left\|\Pi P_{s}-P_{s}\right\| \leq 4 \varepsilon+2 \sum\{\eta(j) ; j \in F\}$. Since each $\eta(j)$ tends to zero, to conclude it will be sufficient to show that the cardinality of $F$ remains bounded. To do this note that, for any fixed $c$, the set $S_{\omega}$ such that $q(\theta, \omega)<c$ have their cardinality bounded by some fixed integer.

Consider the experiment $\mathscr{E}_{S}=\left\{P_{s} ; s \in S\right\}$ where $S=\left\{s: r\left[f\left(\delta_{s}\right), f\left(\delta_{\theta}\right)\right] \leq m+\right. 2 a+1\}$. According to (G0) and according to the cardinality restriction just recalled, there is a fixed $k$ and a Gaussian experiment $\mathscr{G}_{S}=\left\{G_{s} ; s \in S\right\}$ such that all the following relations hold:
(i) $\Delta\left(\mathscr{E}_{S}, \mathscr{G}_{S}\right) \rightarrow 0$;
(ii) $\mathscr{G}_{S}$ has rank at most $k$;
(iii) if $q_{G}^{2}(s, t)=-8 \log \int \sqrt{d G_{s} d G_{t}}$, then $\sup \left\{q_{G}^{2}(s, t) ;(s, t) \in S \times S\right\} \leq b_{1}$, fixed.

The elements $j \in J$ are such that $r\left[f\left(\delta_{j_{1}}\right), f\left(\delta_{j_{2}}\right)\right] \geq a$ if $j_{1} \neq j_{2}$. Using the separating property of $f$, one concludes that there exists some positive fixed $\alpha>0$ such that eventually $q_{G}\left(j_{1}, j_{2}\right)>\alpha$ for any pair of distinct elements of $F$. Thus the cardinality of $F$ is bounded by the number of points at distance $\alpha$ from each other which can be fitted in the ball of radius $b_{1}$ of the Euclidean space $\mathbb{R}^{k}$.
