This concludes the proof of the theorem, with "completion sufficiency" instead of sufficiency.

One can modify the proof given here to obtain sufficiency. To do this, note that in the above proof, each $j \in J$ was assigned a number $m(j)$ and a set $A(j)=A_{j}[m(j)]$. Instead of the experiment $\mathscr{E}_{j}=\left\{P_{s} ; s \in A(j)\right\}$, let $\mathscr{E}_{j}^{*}$ be the experiment $\left\{P_{s}^{*} ; s \in A(j)\right\}$ with

$$
P_{s}^{*}=P_{s} \wedge\left[m(j) P_{j}\right]+\left\{1-\left\|P_{s} \wedge\left[m(j) P_{j}\right]\right\|\right\} P_{j}
$$

This is a dominated experiment. Thus $M\left(\mathscr{E}_{j}^{*}\right)$ is the space of equivalence classes of bounded measurable functions. A projection $\Pi_{j}$ obtained for $\mathscr{E}_{j}^{*}$ can be obtained as a conditional expectation yielding for each $v \in M\left(\mathscr{E}_{j}^{*}\right)$ a suitable bounded Borel function on $\mathscr{X}$, say $\bar{v}_{j}$. This is then composed with the map $f \circ \hat{Z}$.

Now there is some other function, say $v_{j}^{*}$, which is of the second Baire class and is equivalent to $\bar{v}_{j}$ for all the measure images of $\left\{P_{s}^{*} ; s \in A(j)\right\}$ by the transformation $f \circ \mathcal{Z}$. According to Montgomery, (see Kuratowski, p. 267) the $\operatorname{sum} v^{\prime}=\sum v_{j}^{*} I_{V(j)}$ is still of the second Baire class. Composing $v^{\prime}$ with $f \circ \hat{Z}$ one obtains an element $v^{\prime \prime}$ of the space of bounded $\mathscr{B}$-measurable functions. One can then define $Q_{\theta}$ by $\int v d Q_{\theta}=\int v^{\prime \prime} d Q_{\theta}$. Since for the points satisfying the conditions (A) to (G0) the relevant $m(j)$ will tend to infinity, the differences $\left\|P_{s}^{*}-P_{s}\right\|, s \in A(j)$ will tend to zero. The rest of the argument is unchanged.

Remark 1. Consider a situation where $\Theta$ is a product $\Theta=\Xi \times H$ where the elements $\eta \in H$ are considered nuisance parameters, as explained in Section 5. Assume also that C. Stein's orthogonality relations are satisfied. Then Theorem 3 admits an extension giving an asymptotic sufficiency relation for experiments $\left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta=(\xi, \eta), \xi \in \Xi\right\}$ where $\eta$ is the true, unknown, value of the nuisance parameter.

Remark 2. Theorem 3 admits further extensions in which the insufficiencies of the centerings $\hat{Z}$ or their images $\hat{T}=f(\hat{Z})$ do not actually tend to zero, but have a lim sup smaller than some preassigned $\alpha>0$. This happens when the accessibility requirement (A) is weakened, replacing the requirement (ii) of Definition 6 by the condition that $\Gamma_{\theta}\left(\mu_{s}-\delta_{s}\right)$ be eventually bounded by some number $\beta>0$. This may not be as satisfying for the theoretician, but may not be a hindrance for practical purposes. Further remarks in this direction will be made later in this section.

Remark 3. Theorem 3, by itself, does not imply that the centerings $\hat{Z}$ possess asymptotic sufficiency properties, since one might conceive of the possibility that no boundedly separating maps $f$ may exist. However, the technique of proof of Theorem 3 can be applied, with some modifications, to the centerings $\hat{Z}$,
yielding the same sort of asymptotic sufficiency. To carry out the argument, one would metrize $\Theta$ by the Hellinger distance and estimate $\theta$ by any point in the support of $\mathcal{Z}$. Of course, if $\Gamma$ is positive on the entire space $\mathscr{M}_{0}(\Theta)$, one can use it to define a distance and Theorem 3 becomes directly applicable. However, $\Gamma$ may not behave appropriately, as explained at the beginning of this section.

There is an important special case in which one can obtain asymptotic sufficiency properties of the kind described in Theorem 3 for statistics $f(\hat{Z})$ which are estimates of $\theta$ taking values in the set $\Theta$ itself. To describe it, we shall use the positive forms $\Gamma_{\theta}$ constructed in this section. The condition says that, for these forms $\Gamma_{\theta}$, the space $\Theta$ itself looks like a Hilbert space in the vicinity of $\theta$.

Definition 8. Condition (H) is satisfied at $\theta$ if there are subsets $S \subset \Theta$ such that
(i) $\sup \{q(\theta, s) ; s \in S\}$ and card $S$ remain bounded;
(ii) the pairs $\left(S, \Gamma_{\theta}\right)$ are free;
(iii) every $t \in \Theta$ such that $q(t, \theta)$ remains bounded is in the span of ( $S, \Gamma_{\theta}$ );
(iv) if $\mu \in \mathscr{M}_{1}(S)$ is such that $\|\mu\|$ remains bounded, then there are elements $\tau \in \Theta$ such that $\Gamma_{\theta}\left(\mu-\delta_{\tau}\right) \rightarrow 0$.

Note that if (H) and the accessibility condition (A) are satisfied, one can take for the sets $S$ of (H) any set $S_{\omega}$ with $q(\omega, \theta)$ bounded. Whether (H) is satisfied or not, one can construct functions $T^{*}$ with values in $\Theta$ as follows.

Return to the construction of $\Gamma_{\theta}$ and of the estimated forms $\tilde{\Gamma}$. It involves a set $J$ and quadratic $K_{j}, j \in J$, together with numbers $m(j)$. If $\tilde{\Gamma}=K_{j}$ for a $j \in J$, let $T^{*}$ be some element of $\Theta$ such that

$$
K_{j}\left(\hat{Z}-\delta_{T^{*}}\right) \leq[m(j)]^{-1}+\inf _{t}\left\{K_{j}\left(\hat{Z}-\delta_{t}\right) ; t \in \Theta\right\}
$$

When $\hat{Z}$ is strongly measurable, one can easily make the selection of values so that $T^{*}$ itself is measurable.

Proposition 2. Let $T^{*}$ be selected as just described, and let it be measurable. Assume that at all favorable points conditions (A) and (H) are satisfied. Then $T^{*}$ possesses all the asymptotic sufficiency properties stated in Theorem 3. In addition, for all favorable $\theta$, the distributions $\mathscr{L}\left(T^{*}-\theta \mid P_{\theta}\right)$ are asymptotically Gaussian and

$$
\mathscr{L}\left\{q^{2}\left(T^{*}, t\right) \mid P_{\theta}\right\}-\mathscr{L}\left[\chi_{k}^{2}(\lambda)\right]
$$

tends to zero for noncentral $\chi^{2}$ defined exactly as in Proposition 1.
Proof. Take a $j \in J$ such that $q(\theta, j) \leq 1$. If $\theta$ is favorable, the corresponding integer $m(j)$ tends to infinity. Thus if $b$ is fixed and if $\Theta(\theta, b)=\{s ; s \in \Theta$, $q(s, \theta) \leq b\}$, one will eventually have $\Theta(\theta, b) \subset \Theta[j, m(j)]$ (see Section 5, Lemma 1 ). Also, for a fixed $\varepsilon>0$, there is a fixed $b$ such that, except for cases of probability at most $\varepsilon$, the support of $\hat{Z}$ will be contained in $\Theta(\theta, b)$.

Considering first the second assertion in the proposition, we may take $b$ so large that $q(t, \theta)<b$. Then, on $\mathscr{M}_{0}[\Theta(\theta, b)]$ the differences $\tilde{\Gamma}-K_{j}$ will tend to zero in the sense that if $\|\mu\|$ remains bounded then $\tilde{\Gamma}(\mu)-K_{j}(\mu) \rightarrow 0$. Take an $\omega \in \Omega$ among those closest to $\theta$. Project $\mathscr{M}_{1}[\Theta(\theta, b)]$ orthogonally with respect
to $K_{j}$ on the affine space $F$ spanned by $S_{\omega}$. Let $\pi$ be the projection operation. Since the accessibility condition (A) is satisfied, one can verify without difficulty that $K_{j}(\hat{Z}-\pi \hat{Z})$ tends to zero in probability. According to Proposition 1 , the vector $\pi \hat{Z}$ is asymptotically normal. The appropriate $\chi^{2}$ behavior of $K_{j}\left(\pi \hat{Z}-\delta_{t}\right)$ follows easily.

According to the definition, Property (H) insures the existence of elements $\theta^{*} \in \Theta$ such that $K_{j}\left(\pi \hat{Z}-\delta_{\theta^{*}}\right)$ tends to zero in probability. This implies that $K_{j}\left(\hat{Z}-\delta_{T^{*}}\right)$ and $K_{j}\left(\pi \hat{Z}-\delta_{T^{*}}\right)$ tend to zero in probability. For any $t$ in a ball $\Theta(\theta, b)$, the difference $q^{2}\left(t, T^{*}\right)-K_{j}\left(\delta_{t}-\delta_{T^{*}}\right)$ tends to zero in probability. This gives the last statement of the proposition.

To obtain the sufficiency statements, one can repeat the proof of Theorem 3 , using the function $q$ as if it was a distance on $\Theta$, instead of using the metric $r$ of Theorem 3. The necessary modifications are easy and the result can be considered proven.

One of the unfortunate restrictions used to obtain the results stated so far in this section is the boundedness restriction on the cardinality of the sets $S_{\omega}$. It is, of course, possible to let this cardinality tend to infinity "slowly" as $v$ tends to infinity, however how slow is "slow" will depend on the local behavior of the experiments $\mathscr{E}$. Note that if the dimension $k$ of Proposition 1 tends to infinity, the variables $\Gamma_{\theta}\left(\hat{Z}-\delta_{t}\right)$ of that proposition will also tend to infinity in probability. However, approximations of the logarithms $\Lambda(s, t)$ in terms of $\left\langle\hat{Z}-\delta_{t}, \delta_{s}-\delta_{t}\right\rangle_{\Gamma_{\theta}}$ may still make perfect sense.

Because of the complexity of the situation, we shall not investigate such possibilities. However, we shall now mention two separate facts which may be usable occasionally. One of them relates to the $K$-control of certain asymptotically Gaussian experiments. The other refers to the possibility of using sets $S_{\omega}$ of bounded cardinality in a situation where infinite cardinality would be necessary for full coverage.

Consider subsets $S \subset \Theta$ subject to the condition (G1) of Definition 2 and let $\mathscr{G}_{S}=\left\{G_{s} ; s \in S\right\}$ be such that $\Delta\left(\mathscr{E}_{S}, \mathscr{G}_{S}\right)$ tends to zero. Let $\Lambda_{G}$ denote the logarithm of likelihood ratios attached to $\mathscr{G}_{S}$. By assumption, if $S$ has bounded cardinality, the joint distributions of $\Lambda(s, \theta)$ and $\Lambda_{G}(s, \theta)$ are at dual Lipschitz distances which tend to zero. However, for $\mu \in \mathscr{M}_{0}(S)$ and $\|\mu\| \leq 1$ and for $S$ of unbounded cardinality, this may not imply that the distributions of $\int \Lambda(s, \theta) \mu(d s)$ and $\int \Lambda_{G}(s, \theta) \mu(d s)$ are close to each other. We shall show below that if the $\mathscr{E}_{S}$ are tail-precompact one can replace them by other $L_{1}$ equivalent experiments $\mathscr{E}_{S}^{\prime}$ for which no such difficulty occurs. The $\mathscr{E}_{S}^{\prime}$ will have $\log$-likelihood ratios $\Lambda^{\prime}$ and a quadratic form $\Gamma^{\prime}$ defined from $\rho^{\prime}(s, t)= \int \sqrt{d P_{s}^{\prime} d P_{t}^{\prime}}$. We shall use the functions $W^{\prime}(s, \theta)=\Lambda^{\prime}(s, \theta)+\frac{1}{2} \Gamma^{\prime}\left(\delta_{s}-\delta_{\theta}\right)$.

Proposition 3. Let the experiments $\mathscr{E}_{S}$ be tail-precompact experiments satisfying (G0). Then there are experiments $\mathscr{E}_{S}^{\prime}=\left\{P_{s}^{\prime} ; s \in S\right\}$ such that
(i) $\sup \left\{\left\|P_{s}-P_{s}^{\prime}\right\| ; s \in S\right\} \rightarrow 0$.
(ii) Let $L^{\prime}(\mu)$ be the distribution under $P_{\theta}^{\prime}$ of $\int W^{\prime}(s, \theta) \mu(d s)$ and let $L_{G}(\mu)$ be



the distribution under $G_{\theta}$ of $\int W_{G}(s, \theta) \mu(d s)$. Then

$$
\sup \left\{\left\|L^{\prime}(\mu)-L_{G}(\mu)\right\|_{D} ; \mu \in \mathscr{M}_{0}(S) ;\|\mu\| \leq 1\right\}
$$

tends to zero.
As a consequence the experiments $\mathscr{E}_{S}$ are $K$-controlled for any quadratic $K$ associated with a Gaussian $\mathscr{G}_{S}$ such that $\Delta\left(\mathscr{E}_{S}, \mathscr{G}_{S}\right) \rightarrow 0$.

Proof. One can, without loss of generality, assume that the $\mathscr{E}_{S}$ are equiprecompact and that they satisfy (G1). Indeed one could find experiments $\mathscr{E}^{*}$ which are equi-precompact and such that $\Delta\left(\mathscr{E}_{S}^{*}, \mathscr{E}_{S}\right) \rightarrow 0$. Then one forms other experiments $\mathscr{E}^{\prime \prime}$ by taking transitions $T^{*}$ such that $\sup \left\{\left\|P_{s}-T^{*} P_{s}^{*}\right\| ; s \in S\right\}$ tends to zero. The resulting $\mathscr{E}^{\prime \prime}$ are still equi-precompact and such that sup $\left\{\left\|P_{s}-P_{s}^{\prime \prime}\right\| ; s \in S\right\} \rightarrow$ 0.

Assuming this equi-precompactness, (G0) implies (G1) and the existence of Gaussian $\mathscr{G}=\left\{G_{s} ; s \in S\right\}$ such that $\Delta\left(\mathscr{E}_{S}, \mathscr{G}\right) \rightarrow 0$. If $K$ is the form induced by $\mathscr{G}$ the quantity $\sup \left\{|K(\mu)-\Gamma(\mu)| ; \mu \in \mathscr{M}_{0}(S),\|\mu\| \leq 1\right\}$ tends to zero.

Leaving $S$ aside for a moment consider sets $T$ of cardinality at most $k$ and experiments $\mathscr{F}_{T}=\left\{Q_{t} ; t \in T\right\}$ and $\mathscr{G}_{T}=\left\{G_{t} ; t \in T\right\}$. Assume that $\mathscr{G}_{T}$ is Gaussian with an associated quadratic form $K$ which is bounded on $\left\{\mu ; \mu \in \mathscr{M}_{0}(T),\|\mu\| \leq 1\right\}$ by some number $b$. Take a $\theta \in T$ and let $W(t, \theta)=\Lambda(t, \theta)+\frac{1}{2} \Gamma\left(\delta_{t}-\delta_{\theta}\right)$ and $W_{G}(t, \theta)$ be the associated centered processes. Let $\beta_{\mu}$ be the dual Lipschitz distance between $L(\mu)=\mathscr{L}\left\{\int W(t, \theta) \mu(d t) \mid Q_{\theta}\right\}$ and $L_{G}(\mu)=\mathscr{L}\left\{\int W_{G}(t, \theta) \mu(d t) \mid G_{\theta}\right\}$.

According to Chapter 3, there is a number $\beta(\Delta, k, b)$ depending only on the items listed in the bracket such that $\beta_{\mu} \leq \beta\left[\Delta\left(\mathscr{F}_{T}, \mathscr{G}_{T}\right), k, b\right]$ for all $\mu \in \mathscr{M}_{0}(T)$ such that $\|\mu\| \leq 1$. For fixed $k$ and $b$, this number $\beta(\Delta, k, b)$ tends to zero if $\Delta$ tends to zero.

Applying this to the present case, take for $\Delta$ the value $\Delta=\Delta\left(\mathscr{E}_{S}, \mathscr{G}_{S}\right)$. Then there are some numbers $\beta(\Delta, k)$ such that (i) for each fixed $k$ the values $\beta(\Delta, k)$ tend to zero if $\Delta$ does, and (ii) $\beta(\Delta, k) \geq \sup _{T} \sup _{\mu}\left\{\beta_{\mu} ; \mu \in \mathscr{M}_{0}(T),\|\mu\| \leq 1\right\}$, for a supremum taken over all subsets $T \subset S$ whose cardinality does not exceed the fixed $k$.

Now consider an integer $m>0$ and a subset $T(m) \subset S$. Take $T(m)$ maximal among those subsets of $S$ whose distinct points are at distance at least $1 / m$ for the $L_{1}$-norm $\left\|P_{t}-P_{s}\right\|$. According to the equi-precompactness assumption, $T(m)$ is a finite set whose cardinality does not exceed a certain number $c(1 / m)$.

One can assume that the function $\beta(\Delta, k)$ is increasing in $k$ for each $\Delta$. One can also assume without loss of generality that $c(1 / m)$ is strictly increasing in $m$ and that it tends to infinity as $m$ does. For each $\Delta>0$ there is a largest integer $k(\Delta, m)$ such that $\beta[\Delta, k(\Delta, m)] \leq 1 / m$. This $k(\Delta, m)$ decreases as $m$ increases. Thus for any given $\Delta$ there is a largest integer $m(\Delta)$ for which $c(1 / m) \leq k(\Delta, k)$. Since $\beta(\Delta, k)$ tends to zero for any fixed $k$ as $\Delta \rightarrow 0$, the number $m(\Delta)$ will tend to infinity as $\Delta \rightarrow 0$.

Now return to the pair $\left(\mathscr{E}_{S}, \mathscr{G}_{S}\right)$ with distance $\Delta=\left(\mathscr{E}_{S}, \mathscr{G}_{S}\right)$. For the set $T$ take $T[m(\Delta)]$ and construct a function $s \mapsto t(s)$ by taking for $t(s)$ a point of $T$ closest in $L_{1}$-norm to $s$. Then let $P_{s}^{\prime}=P_{t(s)}$. This will yield $\left\|P_{s}^{\prime}-P_{s}\right\| \leq$
$[m(\Delta)]^{-1}$, tending to zero as $\Delta$ does. Also, for any $\mu \in \mathscr{M}_{0}(T)$ with $\|\mu\| \leq 1$, one has $\left|L^{\prime}(\mu)-L_{G}(\mu)\right|_{D} \leq[m(\mu)]^{-1}$.

Consider further the experiment $\mathscr{G}_{s}^{\prime}=\left\{G_{s}^{\prime} ; s \in S\right\}$ obtained by letting $G_{s}^{\prime}= G_{t(s)}$. Let $W_{G}^{\prime}$ be the corresponding process and let $L_{G}^{\prime}(\mu)$ be the distribution of $\int W_{G}^{\prime}(s, \theta) \mu(d s)$. By construction, for any $\mu \in \mathscr{M}_{0}(S)$ there is a $\lambda \in \mathscr{M}_{0}(T)$ such that $\|\mu\|=\|\lambda\|$ and such that $\int W_{G}^{\prime}(s, \theta) \mu(d s)=\int W_{G}^{\prime}(s, \theta) \lambda(d s)$. Therefore

$$
\sup \left\{\left|L^{\prime}(\mu)-L_{G}^{\prime}(\mu)\right|_{D} ; \mu \in \mathscr{M}_{0}(S),\|\mu\| \leq 1\right\} \leq[m(\Delta)]^{-1}
$$

The experiment $\mathscr{G}_{s}^{\prime}$ is also a Gaussian experiment with $\int W_{G}^{\prime}(s, \theta) \mu(d s)$ a centered Gaussian variable having variance $K^{\prime}(\mu)$. Clearly $K^{\prime}(\mu)-K(\mu) \rightarrow 0$ for $\mu \in \mathscr{M}_{0}(S),\|\mu\| \leq 1$. Therefore

$$
\sup \left\{\left|L_{G}^{\prime}(\mu)-L_{G}(\mu)\right|_{D} ; \mu \in \mathscr{M}_{0}(S),\|\mu\| \leq 1\right\}
$$

tends to zero as $m(\Delta)$ tends to infinity. This concludes the proof, except for the assertion of $K$-control. To obtain it, note that $\int W_{G}(s, \theta) \mu(d s)$ being centered Gaussian with variance $K(\mu)$, it will tend to zero if (and only if) $K(\mu)$ does. Now if $K(\mu) \rightarrow 0$, and $\|\mu\| \leq 1$, then $L^{\prime}(\mu)$ has a degenerate limit at zero so that $\int W^{\prime}(s, \theta) \mu(d s) \rightarrow 0$. Furthermore, for $\|\mu\| \leq 1, \Gamma(\mu) \rightarrow 0$ if and only if $K(\mu)$ does. The proof is now complete.

Remark 1. We do not know to what extent one can relax the tail-precompactness assumption if one requires (G1) instead of (G0).

Remark 2. The reader will note that the net $\{v\}$ plays no role in the proof of the proposition. In fact the method of proof is to ignore it and pass to the limit as $\Delta=\Delta\left(\mathscr{E}_{S}, \mathscr{G}_{S}\right)$ tends to zero.

Remark 3. The result of Proposition 3 can be used to complete the proof of Theorem 2 above.

Remark 4. Let $T=T[m(\Delta)]$ be the sets introduced in the proof of Proposition 3. Then the pairs ( $T, K$ ) span the pairs ( $S, K$ ) in the sense of Section 4, Definition 2. One can also modify the forms $K$ to insure that for $\mu \in \mathscr{M}_{0}(T)$ one has $K(\mu)=0$ if and only if $\mu=0$.

To do this, if $\mu \in \mathscr{M}_{0}(T)$ let

$$
\sigma^{2}(\mu)=\sum\left\{|\mu[\{t\}]|^{2} ; t \in T\right\}
$$

and

$$
\bar{K}(\mu)=K(\mu)+\Delta \sigma^{2}(\mu)
$$

Since $\sigma^{2}(\mu) \leq\|\mu\|^{2}$ the difference $\bar{K}(\mu)-K(\mu)$ will tend to zero whenever $\|\mu\|$ remains bounded. Assuming that the experiments $\mathscr{E}_{T}=\left\{P_{t} ; t \in T\right\}$ are homogeneous, Lemma 1 of Section 3 implies the existence of centerings $Z$ such that, almost surely,

$$
\Lambda(t, \theta)=-\frac{1}{2}\left[\bar{K}\left(Z-\delta_{t}\right)-\bar{K}\left(Z-\delta_{\theta}\right)\right]
$$

for all pairs $(t, \theta)$ in $T \times T$.
For the map $s \mapsto t(s)$ used in the proof of Proposition 3, this insures that $\left.\Lambda(s, \theta)+\frac{1}{2}\left[\bar{K}-\delta_{t(s)}\right)-\bar{K}\left(Z-\delta_{\theta}\right)\right]$ tends to zero in probability. It follows that $\Lambda(s, \theta)+\frac{1}{2}\left[K\left(Z-\delta_{s}\right)-K\left(Z-\delta_{\theta}\right)\right]$ tends to zero in probability. Thus everything behaves as if $S$ was of bounded cardinality.

This suggests the possibility of replacing condition (D), imposing a cardinality restriction on the sets $S_{\omega}$, by a condition of tail-compactness on the experiments $\left\{P_{s} ; s \in S\right\}$. This is possible if the forms $\Gamma_{\theta}$ and $K_{\omega}$ vary slowly enough as $\theta$ and $\omega$ vary.

The next result shows that, in tail-precompact cases, it may be possible to use construction sets of bounded cardinality, if one is willing to lose a bit of efficiency. Consider sets $T \subset S \subset \Theta$. Assume that $S$ satisfies (G1). Let $\mathscr{G}_{S}$ be Gaussian experiments such that $\Delta\left(\mathscr{E}_{S}, \mathscr{G}_{S}\right) \rightarrow 0$. Let $K$ be the quadratic form of the Gaussian experiment $\mathscr{G}_{S}$ and let $\Lambda_{G}(t, \theta)=\log \left(d G_{t} / d G_{\theta}\right)$.

Proposition 4. Let $\alpha$ and $b$ denote fixed numbers with $\alpha>0$. Assume that $\Delta\left(\mathscr{E}_{S}, \mathscr{G}_{S}\right) \rightarrow 0$ and that the $\mathscr{E}_{T}$ are tail-precompact. Assume also that for each $s \in S$ there are $\mu_{s} \in \mathscr{M}_{1}(T)$ such that $\|\mu\| \leq b$ and $K\left(\mu_{s}-\delta_{s}\right) \leq \alpha$. Taking $\theta \in T$ let $\mathscr{B}$ denote the $\sigma$-field generated by the $\Lambda(t, \theta) ; t \in T$. Then, for the experiments $\left\{P_{t} ; t \in S\right\}$, the insufficiency of $\mathscr{B}$ is eventually smaller than $3 \alpha^{1 / 3}$.

Proof. The assumption $K\left(\mu_{s}-\delta_{s}\right) \leq \alpha$ means that the difference $\Lambda_{G}(s, \theta) \int \Lambda_{G}(t, \theta) \mu_{s}(d s)$ has a variance inferior to $\alpha$. For the measure $G_{s}$ this same difference has a certain expectation, say $\eta(s)$.

If necessary, replace $\mathscr{E}_{S}$ by an $L_{1}$-equivalent homogeneous experiment which satisfies the requirements of Proposition 3. If this has been done one will eventually have

$$
P_{s}\left\{\left|\Lambda(s, \theta)-\int \Lambda(t, \theta) \mu_{s}(d t)-\eta(s)\right| \geq 2 \alpha^{1 / 3}\right\} \leq 2 \alpha^{1 / 3}
$$

Let $Y_{s}=\eta(s)+\int \Lambda(t, \theta) \mu_{s}(d t)$ and let $c$ be the largest $c \in[0, \infty]$ such that $\int \exp \left(Y_{s} \wedge c\right) d P_{\theta} \leq 1$. If $c$ is finite, let $d Q_{s}^{\prime}=\exp \left\{\left(Y_{s} \wedge c\right)\right\} d P_{\theta}$. If $c=\infty$ and the measure $Q_{s}^{\prime}$ just defined has mass $\left\|Q_{s}^{\prime}\right\|<1$, replace it by $Q_{s}=\left\|Q_{s}^{\prime}\right\|^{-1} Q_{s}^{\prime}$. This yields an experiment $\left\{Q_{s} ; s \in S\right\}$. Let $A$ be the set where $\left|\Lambda(s, \omega)-Y_{s}\right|<2 \alpha^{1 / 3}$. Write $\Lambda$ for $\Lambda(s, \theta)$ and $Y$ for $Y_{s}$ for simplicity. Then, according to the inequalities of Chapter 4, we shall have

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left\|Q_{s}-P_{s}\right\| & \leq 2 \alpha^{1 / 3}+2 \int_{A}\left(e^{\Lambda}-e^{Y \wedge c}\right) d P_{\theta} \\
& =2 \alpha^{1 / 3}+2 \int_{A} 1-\exp \left\{-[\Lambda-(Y \wedge c)]^{+}\right\} d P_{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

One can write $[\Lambda-(Y \wedge c)]^{+}=(\Lambda-\Lambda \wedge c)+[(\Lambda \wedge c)-(Y \wedge c)]^{+}$. Replacing $[(\Lambda \wedge c)-(Y \wedge c)]^{+}$by the bound $2 \alpha^{1 / 3}$, the integral term is seen to be inferior to

$$
2 \int_{A}\left\{1-\exp \left\{-[\Lambda-(\Lambda \wedge c)]-2 \alpha^{1 / 3}\right\}\right\} d P_{s}
$$

Because of the contiguity assumption imbedded in (G1), the numbers $c$ will tend to infinity. Thus the lim sup of the integral term is inferior to that of

$$
2 \int\left\{1-\exp \left[-2 \alpha^{1 / 3}\right]\right\} d P_{s} \leq 4 \alpha^{1 / 3}
$$

Therefore one will eventually have $\frac{1}{2}\left\|Q_{s}-P_{s}\right\| \leq 2 \alpha^{1 / 3}+\varepsilon$ for any $\varepsilon>0$ fixed. This yields the result as stated.

It is clear that one could insert the bounds of Proposition 4 in the computations of insufficiency carried out for the proof of Theorem 3. However, explicit bounds for the insufficiency of the centerings $\hat{Z}$ appear complicated. Thus we shall skip them.

Note also that if the cardinality of $T$ remains bounded, Proposition 4 holds without any tail precompactness assumption on the experiments $\mathscr{E}_{S}$ or $\mathscr{E}_{T}$. However, to be able to approximate a set $S$ by a set $T$ of bounded cardinality, one needs to restrict $S$ in some way and the results of Proposition 4 are mostly of interest in cases where the $\mathscr{E}_{S}$ are tail-precompact.

The results of Proposition 4 can be applied in particular to favorable points $\theta$, taking for the sets $S$ of the proposition balls of the type $\Theta(\theta, b)$ with $b$ fixed and for the sets $T$ the sets $S_{\omega}$ of the construction. This yields variants for Theorem 1 or Proposition 2. As to Proposition 1, it is already written in such a way as to allow a certain leeway in the approximability of elements $s \in \Theta(\theta, b)$.

This being as it may, let us pass to a different matter altogether. As previously noted, all the results depend on the availability of the sets $S_{\omega}$ used in the construction of $\hat{Z}$. Thus it appears necessary to mention some of the methods which might yield such sets. Here we shall describe briefly a method which relies on the use of local "reference norms." Such reference norms will also be encountered in Sections 7 and 8.

Before one constructs the $S_{\omega}$ one must obtain $\Omega$ itself. The method of $a_{i}$-skeletons described in Section 5 will readily yield such sets $\Omega$ even if one knows the function $q$ only very approximately. Thus we shall not insist on the construction of $\Omega$, especially since in examples covered by Sections 7 and 8 other constructions are very immediate. A construction of the sets $S_{\omega}$ will be described here using the function $q$ itself. One can readily replace it by a distance $r$ which satisfies conditions analogous to those used in Theorem 3.

To proceed to the construction, specify two numbers, $\varepsilon$ and $b$, with $0<\varepsilon< 1<b$. They will be kept fixed throughout, independently of $v$. Considering the ball $\Theta(\omega, b)=\{s ; s \in \Theta, q(s, \omega) \leq b\}$ assume given a quadratic form $Q_{\omega}$ on $\mathscr{M}_{0}[\Theta(\theta, b)]$. As first element of $S_{\omega}$ take the point $\omega$ itself. Then look for some $t \in \Theta(\omega, b)$ which almost maximizes $Q_{\omega}\left(\delta_{t}-\delta_{\omega}\right)$ among all possible $t \in \Theta(\omega, b)$. To be specific, and assuming that the diameter (for $Q_{\omega}$ ) of $\Theta(\omega, b)$ is not zero, select $t$ so that

$$
Q_{\omega}\left(\delta_{t}-\delta_{\omega}\right) \geq\left(1-\frac{1}{4}\right) \sup Q\left\{\left(\delta_{s}-\delta_{\omega}\right) ; s \in \Theta(\omega, b)\right\}
$$

Let this be the second element of $S_{\omega}$. Continuing recursively, suppose that $\omega=\omega_{1}, \omega_{2}, \ldots, \omega_{m}$ have been chosen. Let $H_{m}$ be the span of $\left\{\delta_{\omega_{i}} ; i=1,2, \ldots\right.$,
$m\}$ in $\mathscr{M}_{1}[\Theta(\omega, b)]$. Let $\Pi_{m} t$ be the orthogonal projection of $\delta_{t}$ on $H_{m}$ for the quadratic form $Q_{\omega}$. Select for $\omega_{m+1}$ a $t \in \Theta(\omega, b)$ such that

$$
Q_{\omega}\left[\delta_{t}-\Pi_{m}(t)\right] \geq\left(1-\frac{1}{2^{m+1}}\right) \sup \left\{Q_{\omega}\left(\delta_{s}-\Pi_{m} s\right) ; s \in \Theta(\omega, b)\right\}
$$

It may happen that for some $m$ the set $\Theta(\omega, b)$ is entirely equivalent for $Q_{\omega}$ to a subset of $H_{m}$. In this case the construction will stop at that $m$. Otherwise the process might continue indefinitely. However, here, we shall use the number $\varepsilon>0$. Letting

$$
\alpha(\omega, m)=\sup _{s}\left\{Q_{\omega}\left(\delta_{s}-\Pi_{m-1} s\right) ; s \in \Theta(\omega, b)\right\}
$$

we shall stop the construction at the last $m$, if any, such that $\alpha(\omega, m) \geq \varepsilon$. This last $m$ will be called $n(\varepsilon)$. If, however, $\alpha(\omega, m) \geq \varepsilon$ for all $m$, let $n(\varepsilon)=\infty$.

Let $S_{\omega, k}$ be the set $\left\{\omega_{1}, \omega_{2}, \ldots, \omega_{k}\right\}$ formed by the first $k$ points obtained in this manner, assuming that $k \leq n(\varepsilon)$. To state a result, let us say that the quadratic forms $Q_{\omega}$ satisfy Condition ( R ) if the following holds.

Definition 9. Condition (R): Assume that $\omega \in \Omega$ satisfies Condition (G1). Let $V=\Theta(\omega, b)$. Then there are Gaussian experiments $\mathscr{G}_{V}=\left\{G_{t} ; t \in V\right\}$ with associated quadratic forms $K$ such that
(i) $\Delta\left(\mathscr{E}_{V}, \mathscr{G}_{V}\right) \rightarrow 0$;
(ii) the forms $K$ and $Q_{\omega}$ are related on $\mathscr{M}_{0}(V)$. (See Section 4, Definition 1.)

Lemma 4. Let the conditions (G1) and (R) be satisfied at a point $\omega \in \Omega$. Let $\varepsilon$ and $b$ be fixed. Then for each fixed $k \leq n(\varepsilon)$ and for the quadratic form $K$ of Condition (R), the pairs ( $S_{\omega, k}, K$ ) are free.

If, in addition, the experiments $\left\{P_{t} ; t \in \Theta(\omega, b)\right\}$ are tail-precompact, then $n(\varepsilon)$ is eventually bounded and the sets $\left(S_{\omega, n(\varepsilon)}, K\right)$ are free.

Proof. Let $V=\Theta(\omega, b)$ and let $\mu$ be an element of $\mathscr{M}_{0}(V)$. Let $\mathscr{G}$ be the Gaussian experiment of Condition (R) and let $L(t, \omega)$ be $L(t, \omega)=\log \left(d G_{t} / d G_{\omega}\right)$. Then $K(\mu)$ is the variance of $\int L(t, \omega) \mu(d t)$. If $\|\mu\|$ remains bounded, so does $K(\mu)$ since (G1) holds. Condition (R) implies then that $Q_{\omega}(\mu)$ remains bounded. Conversely, consider a $\mu \in \mathscr{M}_{0}(V)$ with $\|\mu\|=1$. Then $Q_{\omega}(\mu)$ is at least equal to

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{j=1}^{k}\left|\mu\left(\omega_{j}\right)\right|^{2} Q_{\omega}\left(\delta_{\omega_{j}}-\Pi_{j-1} \omega_{j}\right) & \geq \frac{3}{4} \alpha(\omega, k) \sum_{j=1}^{k}\left|\mu\left(\omega_{j}\right)\right|^{2} \\
& \geq \frac{3}{4} \varepsilon \sum_{j=1}^{k}\left|\mu\left(\omega_{j}\right)\right|^{2} \geq \frac{3}{4} \varepsilon \frac{1}{k}
\end{aligned}
$$

This proves the first assertion. To obtain the second, note that there is some fixed $\alpha>0$ such that $Q_{\omega}(\mu) \geq \varepsilon$ eventually implies $K(\mu) \geq \alpha$. The construction itself implies that any two distinct elements $s$ and $t$ of $S_{\omega, n(\varepsilon)}$ are such that $Q_{\omega}\left(\delta_{s}-\delta_{t}\right) \geq \varepsilon$. Thus, eventually one will have $K\left(\delta_{s}-\delta_{t}\right) \geq(3 / 4) \alpha$ and the boundedness of $n(\varepsilon)$ follows from the tail-precompactness assumption.

The construction depends on the choice of numbers $\varepsilon$ and $b$. Roughly, one should select $b$ so large that all important points in the vicinity of $\omega$ be contained in the ball $\Theta(\omega, b)$. However, $b$ should not be too large. It is probably reasonable in most cases to attempt to select points $\omega_{j}$ such that the affinities $\int \sqrt{d P_{\omega_{j}} d P_{\omega}}$ be in a range such as $\frac{1}{4}$ to $\frac{3}{4}$, but one may have to use much larger values for the $q\left(\omega_{j}, \omega\right)$ if the immediate vicinity of $\omega$ does not have a sufficient span.

The number $\varepsilon$ is intended to be so small that inequalities of the type $Q_{\omega}\left(\delta_{s}-\delta_{t}\right)< \varepsilon$ imply that the differences $P_{s}-P_{t}$ are negligible for all practical purposes. For instance, if $Q_{\omega}\left(\delta_{s}-\delta_{t}\right)<\varepsilon$ implies $q(s, t) \leq 10^{-10}$, one will have $\left\|P_{s}-P_{t}\right\| \leq 10^{-10}$. Such a difference is essentially unobservable on the experiment $\mathscr{E}$, which, by assumption, covers all the available observations. One could also attempt to select the number $\varepsilon$ in such a manner as to control the local insufficiencies of the $\sigma$-fields generated by the logarithms $\Lambda(s, \omega) ; s \in S_{\omega}$. Proposition 4 yields some information on the possibilities.

There is at least one case where the sets $S_{\omega}$ constructed by the method described here will be asymptotically well behaved independently of the choice of the values of $\varepsilon$ and $b$. This is the case where Condition (H) of Definition 8 is satisfied. (Note, however, that in practical applications the selection of $\varepsilon$ and $b$ remains important.)

Lemma 5. Let $\omega \in \Omega$ satisfy the conditions (G1), (H), and (R). Then:
(i) For any choice of fixed $\varepsilon$ and $b$ the numbers $n(\varepsilon)$ are eventually bounded.
(ii) As long as $\varepsilon$ is sufficiently small, the values of the $n(\varepsilon)$ are eventually independent of $\varepsilon$ and $b$.
(ii) Let $K$ be the quadratic of an approximating Gaussian experiment. Then, for every fixed $c$ the pairs $\left[S_{\omega, n(\varepsilon)}, K\right]$ span the pairs $[\Theta(\omega, c), K]$.

Proof. Select an approximating Gaussian experiment with quadratic form $K$. Assume that $\varepsilon$ is so small that $Q_{\omega}(\mu) \leq \varepsilon$ implies $K(\mu)<10^{-1}$. Since the combination (G1), (H) implies the tail-precompactness of the experiments $\left\{P_{t} ; t \in \Theta(\omega, b)\right\}$, the numbers $n(\varepsilon)$ will eventually remain bounded. Let $S_{\omega}= S_{\omega, n(\varepsilon)}$. Consider also the sets $S$ of Condition (H). Take a fixed $c$, with $c \geq b$ so large that eventually $S \subset \Theta(\omega, c)$. According to (H), the pairs ( $S, K$ ) span the pairs $[\Theta(\omega, c), K]$. Since ( $S_{\omega}, K$ ) is free, one must eventually have $\operatorname{dim} \mathscr{M}_{0}\left(S_{\omega}\right) \leq \operatorname{dim} \mathscr{M}_{0}(S)$, so that $n(\varepsilon)$ will not exceed card $S$.

Let $\pi$ be the orthogonal projection of $\mathscr{M}_{1}\left(S_{\omega}\right)$ into $\mathscr{M}_{1}(S)$ and let $S^{\prime}=\left\{\pi \delta_{s}\right.$; $\left.s \in S_{\omega}\right\}$. Since ( $S, K$ ) spans ( $S_{\omega}, K$ ), the pairs ( $S^{\prime}, K$ ) are also free. To show that ( $S_{\omega}, K$ ) spans ( $S, K$ ) it is sufficient to show that card $S_{\omega}$ is eventually as large as card $S$. Since ( $S, K$ ) spans $[\Theta(\omega, c), K]$ all the statements of the lemma will follow.

To show that $\operatorname{card} S_{\omega}=\operatorname{card} S$, select first some $\mu_{\omega} \in \mathscr{M}_{1}(S)$ so that $K\left(\mu_{\omega}-\delta_{\omega}\right) \rightarrow 0$. Consider the ball $B=\left\{\mu ; \mu \in \mathscr{M}_{1}(S), K\left(\mu-\mu_{\omega}\right)<1\right\}$. According to (H), for each $\mu \in B$ there is some $\tau \in \Theta$ such that $\Gamma_{\omega}\left(\mu-\delta_{\tau}\right) \rightarrow 0$. This will eventually imply $\Gamma_{\omega}\left(\delta_{\tau}-\delta_{\omega}\right)<1$. Also, $\sup \left\{\left|\Gamma_{\omega}(\mu)-K(\mu)\right| ; \mu \in B\right\} \rightarrow$ 0 . Therefore $\tau \in \Theta(\omega, b)$ for $v$ large enough.

By construction, for such a $\tau$, there is a $\lambda \in \mathscr{M}_{1}\left(S_{\omega}\right)$ such that $Q_{\omega}\left(\delta_{\tau}-\lambda\right) \leq \varepsilon$ and therefore $K\left(\delta_{\tau}-\lambda\right)<10^{-1}$. It follows that one will have $K(\mu-\lambda)<$
$10^{-1}$. Since $K\left(\mu-\mu_{\omega}\right) \leq 1$, the values $K\left(\lambda-\mu_{\omega}\right)$ are eventually bounded. Therefore $K\left(\lambda-\delta_{\omega}\right)$ will remain bounded and so will $\|\lambda\|$. Now transport $\lambda$ to $S^{\prime}$ by the projection $\pi$. This yields some $\mu^{\prime} \in \mathscr{M}_{1}\left(S^{\prime}\right)$ such that, eventually, $K\left(\mu-\mu^{\prime}\right)<10^{-1}$. Therefore, for $v$ large, all the elements of $B$ are within square distance at most $10^{-1}$ from $\mathscr{M}_{1}\left(S^{\prime}\right) \subset \mathscr{M}_{1}(S)$. This is impossible unless $\mathscr{M}_{1}\left(S^{\prime}\right)=\mathscr{M}_{1}(S)$. The desired result follows.

An important consequence of Lemma 5 is that the constructions described here, leading to approximately sufficient statistics $\hat{Z}$ or $T^{*}$, are actually possible. To state the result formally, note that conditions such as (G1) and (H) do not involve the auxiliary estimates $\hat{\theta}$ and cannot imply anything about their existence. Otherwise, the situation is honorable.

Proposition 5. There exist sets $\Omega$ and systems $\left\{S_{\omega}, K_{\omega}, \Lambda_{\omega}^{\prime} ; \omega \in \Omega\right\}$ such that whenever a $\theta \in \Theta$ satisfies (G1) and (H), then it also satisfies all the conditions (A), (B), (D), and (FF).

Proof. One can select for the set $\Omega$ a suitable skeleton. The tail-precompactness condition implied by (G1) and (H) yields the relevant part of (D). The other statements then follow easily from Lemma 5, using for the quadratics $Q_{\omega}$ then forms $\Gamma_{\omega}$ themselves.

Many, if not most, of the parametric asymptotically normal families considered in the literature do satisfy conditions at least as strong as the combination (G1), (H). The condition (H) is usually enforced by assumptions requiring the points $\theta$ to be fixed and interior to a subset $\Theta$ of a Euclidean space. However, there are also cases in which the interesting points are situated on or near a boundary. In such cases Lemma 5 may not be applicable. Fortunately, in most circumstances, the construction of sets $S_{\omega}$ is not a very difficult matter.
.The results obtained in this section suggest that the entire experiment $\mathscr{E}=$ \{ $P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta$ \} may well be approximable by an experiment in which the observable variables are certain random measures $Z$ with values in $\mathscr{M}_{1}(\Theta)$ and distributions which are exactly Gaussian. We shall presently describe a partial result in that direction.

Consider subsets $S \subset \Theta$ and the quadratic form $\Gamma$ on $\mathscr{M}_{0}(S)$. A particularly interesting case is described by the following definition.

Definition 10. The form $\Gamma$ admits a positive substitute on $S$ if there is a positive
quadratic form $K$ defined on $\mathscr{M}_{0}(S)$ such that
(i) $K\left(\delta_{s}-\delta_{t}\right)$ remains bounded if and only if $q(s, t)$ does, and
(ii) if $\theta \in S$ satisfies (G1) and if $q(s, \theta)+q(t, \theta)$ remains bounded, then $K\left(\delta_{s}-\delta_{t}\right)- \Gamma\left(\delta_{s}-\delta_{t}\right)$ tends to zero.

If the entire set $S$ satisfies (G1), the form $\Gamma$ admits a positive substitute on $S$. However, here we are interested in sets that could be much larger. An instance already mentioned at the beginning of this section occurs in the study of product experiments. Explicitly, let $P_{\theta}$ be a product measure, $P_{\theta}=X_{j} p_{\theta, j}$. Let

$$
h_{j}^{2}(s, t)=\frac{1}{2} \int\left(\sqrt{d p_{s, j}}-\sqrt{d p_{t, j}}\right)^{2}
$$

and let $H^{2}=\sum_{j} h_{j}^{2}$. Consider the form $K(\mu)=-4 \iint H^{2}(s, t) \mu(d s) \mu(d t)$. Assume that for each $\theta$ that satisfies (G1) the boundedness of $q(s, \theta)$ implies that $\sup _{j} h_{j}(s, \theta)$ tends to zero. Then $K$ is a positive substitute for $\Gamma$ on $S$. In such a situation, the form $K$ can be used to define a Gaussian shift experiment $\mathscr{G}_{S}=\left\{G_{\theta} ; \theta \in S\right\}$, as explained in Chapter 10.

Briefly one completes $\mathscr{M}_{0}(S)$ for the semi-norm induced by $K$. Taking a quotient one obtains a Hilbert space $\mathscr{H}$. The canonical Gaussian cylinder measure of $\mathscr{H}$ yields a weakly defined variable $Y$ such that $\langle Y, \mu\rangle_{K}$ is Gaussian with expectation zero and variance $K(\mu)$. One takes for observable variable a vector $Z$ such that $\mathscr{L}(Z \mid \theta)=\mathscr{L}\left(Y+\delta_{\theta}\right)$. The logarithms of likelihood ratios are

$$
\log \frac{d G_{s}}{d G_{\theta}}=\left\langle Z-\delta_{s}, \delta_{s}-\delta_{\theta}\right\rangle_{K}-\frac{1}{2} K\left(\delta_{s}-\delta_{\theta}\right)
$$

Now suppose, for instance, that all the points $\theta \in S$ are favorable in the sense of Definition 4 and that $K$ is a positive substitute for $\Gamma$. Then one would be tempted to conclude that $\Delta\left(\mathscr{E}_{S}, \mathscr{G}_{S}\right)$ tends to zero. In fact, one can prove the following result.

Proposition 6. Assume that all points of the sets $S$ are favorable and accessible and that $\Gamma$ admits a positive substitute $K$ on $S$. Then $\delta\left(\mathscr{E}_{S}, \mathscr{G}_{S}\right)$ tends to zero. In order that $\delta\left(\mathscr{G}_{S}, \mathscr{E}_{S}\right)$ tends to zero, it is necessary and sufficient that the Gaussian shift experiments $\mathscr{G}_{S}$ admit estimates $\theta^{*}$ such that $q\left(\theta^{*}, \theta\right)$ stays bounded in $G_{\theta}$ probability uniformly for $\theta \in S$.

Proof. Recall that the index $v$ of the underlying net is suppressed from the notation. Thus the sets $S$ depend on $v$ and the assertion that all elements of $S$ are favorable implies uniform validity of the conditions of Definition 4.

In particular, given a fixed $\varepsilon>0$, there is a fixed $b(\varepsilon)<\infty$ and an $N(\varepsilon)$ such that $v \geq N(\varepsilon)$ implies $P_{\theta}\{q(\hat{\theta}, \theta) \geq b(\varepsilon)\} \leq \varepsilon$ for all $\theta \in S$, the estimates $\hat{\theta}$ being those of Condition (E). Indeed, take a fixed $\varepsilon>0$ and let $b^{\prime}(\varepsilon)$ denote the supremum of the numbers $b$ for which $P_{\theta}\{q(\hat{\theta}, \theta) \geq b\}>\varepsilon$ for some $\theta \in S$. Let $b(\varepsilon)=b^{\prime}(\varepsilon)-\varepsilon$. If $b(\varepsilon)$ does not remain bounded independently of the index $v$, there are $v$ 's and $\theta_{v} \in S_{v}$ for which (E) fails.

Similarly, the assumptions imply that the cardinality of the sets $S_{\omega}$ are eventually bounded by some fixed $k<\infty$. This implies that locally the set $S$ behaves asymptotically approximately as a subset of Euclidean $k$-space. The first assertion can then be obtained by repeating the last part of the argument in the proof of Theorem 3, substituting Proposition 8, Chapter 5 for the use of insufficiencies.

Similarly, if the experiments $\mathscr{G}_{S}$ can yield estimates that satisfy the condition corresponding to (E) uniformly on $S$, the patchwork result of Proposition 8, Chapter 5 will become available to show that $\delta\left(\mathscr{G}_{S}, \mathscr{E}_{S}\right)$ tends to zero. Finally, if $\delta\left(\mathscr{G}_{S}, \mathscr{E}_{S}\right)$ tends to zero, the experiments $\mathscr{G}_{S}$ must be able to yield estimates essentially as good as the $\hat{\theta}$ available on $\mathscr{E}_{S}$. Hence the result.

The unfortunate feature of Proposition 6 is that we do not know when the $\mathscr{G}_{S}$ can support adequate estimates. It will be shown in Chapter 16 that such estimates exist whenever the set $S$ metrized by $K$ have a bounded dimension in
the sense of Kolmogorov. It is true that here the condition (D) enforces a limit on the dimension of the balls $\{s: q(s, \theta) \leq b\}$ for $b<\infty$, fixed. However it does not restrict the number of such balls that can be fitted in $S$ at large increasing distances from one another.

Note that the boundedness of the Kolmogorov dimension does not imply that the Hilbert spaces are finite dimensional or that $S$ is very close to a subset of $\mathscr{H}$ of bounded linear dimension. A simple example can be obtained by taking a fixed $\alpha \in(0,1 / 2)$, say $\alpha=\frac{1}{4}$, and taking independent observations from the shift family with densities $c \exp \left\{-|x-\theta|^{\alpha}\right\}$ on the real line.

It should be emphasized that the Gaussian experiments $\mathscr{G}_{S}$ of Proposition 6 are Gaussian shift experiments, that is, homoscedastic. This may look strange at first. Consider, for instance, experiments $\mathscr{E}$ in which the observable variable is a single random variable $X$ with a Poisson distribution. Let $E_{\theta} X=\theta$ and let $\Theta$ be an interval such that, as $v \rightarrow \infty$, so does $\inf \{\theta: \theta \in \Theta\}$. Here the affinity $\int \sqrt{d P_{s} d P_{t}}$ has the form $\exp \left\{-\frac{1}{2}(\sqrt{s}-\sqrt{t})^{2}\right\}$. The form $\Gamma(\mu)$ is $-4 \iint(\sqrt{s}-\sqrt{t})^{2} \mu(d s) \mu(d t)$. It is positive. The Hilbert space $\mathscr{H}$ is of dimension unity; that is, $\mathscr{H}=\mathbb{R}$. The homoscedasticity is a result of the fact that to a $\mu \in \mathscr{M}_{0}(\Theta)$ corresponds $\int \sqrt{s} \mu(d s) \in \mathbb{R}$ and not $\int s \mu(d s)$. This leads easily to approximations of likelihood ratios in terms of $\sqrt{X}-\sqrt{\theta}$, with the resulting homoscedasticity.

When $\Gamma$ does not admit positive substitutes, other complications arise. In such a case one may consider approximating $\mathscr{E}$ by an heteroscedastic Gaussian experiment where the observable variable $Z$ is again a weakly defined random vector such that $\mathscr{L}\left\{\left\langle Z-\delta_{\theta}, \mu\right\rangle_{\Gamma_{\theta}} \mid \theta\right\}$ is centered Gaussian with variance $\Gamma_{\theta}(\mu)$ for each $\mu \in \mathscr{M}_{0}(\Theta)$. Here the forms $\Gamma_{\theta}$ are, for instance, those used in Lemma 2. Let $H_{\theta}$ be the distribution $\mathscr{L}(Z \mid \theta)$ obtained in this manner. In such a case, finding estimates $\theta^{*}$ with $q\left(\theta^{*}, \theta\right)$ bounded in $H_{\theta}$ probability
may be difficult. However, there is a further complication of purely local character. To describe it, select a $\theta$ and a fixed number $b$. Consider the experiments $\mathscr{E}_{S}=\left\{P_{t} ; t \in S\right\}$ where $S$ is the ball $\Theta(\theta, b)$. Let $\mathscr{H}_{S}=\left\{H_{t} ; t \in S\right\}$. Then, assuming that (G1) holds, $\mathscr{E}_{S}$ is approximated by the Gaussian shift experiment $\mathscr{G}_{S}$ whose quadratic form is $\Gamma_{\theta}$ itself. In order that $\Delta\left(\mathscr{G}_{S}, \mathscr{H}_{S}\right)$ tend to zero it is necessary that the forms $\Gamma_{s}, s \in S$ be very close to $\Gamma_{\theta}$. For instance, when $S$ is sufficiently rich, one cannot expect that $\Delta\left(\mathscr{G}_{S}, \mathscr{H}_{S}\right)$ will tend to zero unless $\Gamma_{s}$ and $\Gamma_{\theta}$ are interchangeable on $\mathscr{M}_{0}(S)$. If the dimension of $\mathscr{M}_{0}(S)$ does not remain bounded, interchangeability is not sufficient. When $\Theta$ fills out the entire space $\mathscr{M}_{1}(S)$ in a manner analogous to that expressed in Condition (H) of Definition 8, necessary and sufficient conditions on the $\Gamma_{s}$ can be easily obtained. However it is not entirely clear whether one can select the various $\Gamma_{s}$ in such a way that these conditions will be satisfied.

For these reasons, we shall not pursue the matter further here and shall only return to the problem in a particular case, in Section 8. It should be pointed out, however, that the problem is not devoid of interest, even for practical purposes. It is easy to find shift experiments where $\Theta$ is the real line, the observable variables $X_{1}, \ldots, X_{n}$ are independent, identically distributed with a
density $f(x-\theta)$ with respect to the Lebesgue measure, conditions (G1) and the other conditions of Lemma 4 are duly satisfied, but $q^{2}(s, t)=|s-t|^{\alpha}$ for some $\alpha, 0<\alpha<2$. For these, an ad hoc approximation theory is feasible, but a more general approach would be welcome.

## 7. Some Particular Cases

In the preceding sections, the sets $\Theta$ were arbitrary sets, devoid of any structure except, of course, of the structure induced by the experiments $\mathscr{E}=\left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ themselves. The large majority of examples considered in the literature refer to cases where $\Theta$ is a subset of a Euclidean space. Sometimes $\Theta$ is a differentiable manifold but this is largely reducible to the Euclidean case by embedding. The present section specializes the assumptions of Section 6 to such a situation.

Of course the specialization to a $\Theta$ contained in a Cartesian product $\mathbb{R}^{k}$ does not really specialize much of anything unless the structure of $\mathbb{R}^{k}$ becomes involved. Here the traditional assumptions are a mixture of requirements of a very different nature. Some assumptions, such as the LAN assumption given below, are truly relevant in that they involve the linear structure of $\mathbb{R}^{k}$ and allow the construction of Section 3 to be carried out in $\mathbb{R}^{k}$ instead of the space $\mathscr{M}(\Theta)$ of finite signed measures with finite support on $\Theta$.

Some other features of the traditional framework are more accidental. One of them refers to the use of norms of $\mathbb{R}^{k}$, with possible rescaling factor or matrices to indicate when two elements $s$ and $t$ of $\Theta$ are close to each other in the sense that the corresponding $P_{s}$ and $P_{t}$ are also close to each other.

Another accidental feature is that usually one keeps the sets $\Theta_{\nu}$ fixed all equal to a $\Theta$ independent of $v$. This allows expression of assumptions and results pointwise on $\Theta$, and leads to seemingly simpler mathematical statements. However, this is a feature inherited from historical happenstance. Whether it can be considered satisfactory depends on the psychology of the interested individual. It does not fit well with this author's interpretation of what the theorems mean in actual practice. One indication of the confusion created by the traditional way of handling convergence is the recurrent argument about whether one should look at power of tests for "fixed alternatives" or for "nearby alternatives." Such a discussion does not make sense in the framework of Section 6 because nothing in the setup is fixed. It does not make practical sense either because in practice one deals with one particular experiment, not a fictitious sequence in which it is embedded.

It is true that the results of Sections 3-6 have been stated in the form of limit theorems. However, they are close to approximation theorems in the sense that they state that if some appropriate items are "small" then certain other objects are nearly Gaussian, or nearly sufficient, etc. They do not say how "small" the items have to be to ensure the desired "nearness" because, unfortunately, that is not an easy problem, but they were meant as a step in that direction.

Standard texts and papers contain mostly pure limit theorems, often stated pointwise. We have also indulged in that pasttime in Chapter 17. In addition,



we are not entirely unaware of the fact that our use of spaces of measures $\mathscr{M}_{0}(\Theta)$ and $\mathscr{M}_{1}(\Theta)$ in the preceding sections will appear to some strange and foreign. Thus the discussion of special cases given here will be somewhat mixed. On one side we have attempted to point out the truly relevant features. On the other side we have made an effort to stay close to the most traditional assumptions.

One relevant aspect of the present specialization is the following. Consider, as in Section 3, an experiment $\mathscr{E}=\left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$, a subset $\Omega \subset \Theta$ and for each $\omega \in \Omega$ a finite subset $S_{\omega} \subset \Theta$, with $\omega \in S_{\omega}$. Take also the random functions $\Lambda_{\omega}^{\prime}$ and the auxiliary estimate $\hat{\theta}$ with values in $\Omega$. Now suppose that $\Theta$ is a subset of a Cartesian product $\mathbb{R}^{k}$ with $k$ finite.

Then it may appear desirable to find centerings $\widehat{T}$ with values in $\mathbb{R}^{k}$ itself instead of $\mathscr{M}_{1}(\Theta)$ and obtain for these $\hat{T}$ properties analogous to those attributed to $\hat{Z}$ in Sections 5 and 6.

In Section 3, the $\hat{Z}$ were obtained by solving systems of linear equations

$$
\left\langle Z_{\omega}-\delta_{\omega}, \delta_{s}-\delta_{\omega}\right\rangle_{K_{\omega}}=\Lambda_{\omega}^{\prime}(s, \omega)+\frac{1}{2} K_{\omega}\left(\delta_{s}-\delta_{\omega}\right)
$$

for quadratic forms $K_{\omega}$ on $\mathscr{M}_{0}\left(S_{\omega}\right)$. The analogous procedure here would be to solve, for $s \in S_{\omega}$ and $T_{\omega} \in \mathbb{R}^{k}$, a system of equations

$$
\left(T_{\omega}-\omega\right)^{\prime} \bar{M}_{\omega}(s-\omega)=\Lambda_{\omega}^{\prime}(s, \omega)+\frac{1}{2}(s-\omega)^{\prime} \bar{M}_{\omega}(s-\omega)
$$

where $\bar{M}_{\omega}$ is a possibly random symmetric matrix on $\mathbb{R}^{k}$.
The relation between the two procedures may be expressed in the following terms. Considering the space $\mathscr{M}(\Theta)$ of all finite signed measures with finite support on $\Theta$, let $\xi$ be the canonical integral map into $\mathbb{R}^{k}$ defined by $\xi(\mu)= \int s \mu(d s)$.

If the forms $K_{\omega}$ are defined by the relation

$$
K_{\omega}(\mu)=[\xi(\mu)]^{\prime} \bar{M}_{\omega}[\xi(\mu)],
$$

solving the systems (a) or the systems (b) are equivalent procedures and the solutions are related by the equation $T_{\omega}=\xi\left(Z_{\omega}\right)$.

The corresponding centerings $\hat{T}$ and $\hat{Z}$, obtained by substituting for $\omega$ its estimated value $\hat{\theta}$ are also related by the equation $\hat{T}=\xi(\hat{Z})$. No problem arises here. However it may well happen that the $K$-control conditions of Section 4 are satisfied on sets $S$ for quadratic forms $K$ which cannot be written in the form $K(\mu)=[\xi(\mu)]^{\prime} M[\xi(\mu)]$. Thus an assumption ensuring that $\hat{T}$ will perform the same services as $\hat{Z}$ may be considered a true restriction. It will be embedded in the condition called LAN below.

To state the condition, consider a net $\left\{\mathscr{E}_{v}\right\}$ of experiments $\mathscr{E}_{v}=\left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta_{v}\right\}$ and suppress the index $v$ so that, unless otherwise specified, everything depends on $v$, even the dimension $k$ of the space $\mathbb{R}^{k}$ which contains $\Theta$.

Definition 1. The experiments $\mathscr{E}$ satisfy the LAN condition at $\theta$ if $\Theta \subset \mathbb{R}^{k}$ and if there exist Euclidean norms (denoted $\|\circ\|$ ), random variables $X_{\theta}$, and nonrandom matrices $M_{\theta}$ with the following properties:

If $\theta+t$ remains in $\Theta$ and $\|t\|$ remains bounded, then
(i) $\Lambda(\theta+t, \theta)-\left\{t X_{\theta}-\frac{1}{2} t^{\prime} M_{\theta} t\right\}$ tends to zero in $P_{\theta}$ probability;
(ii) the Lévy distance between the distributions $\mathscr{L}\left[t^{\prime} X_{\theta} \mid \theta\right]$ and the Gaussian $\mathscr{N}\left[0, t^{\prime} M_{\theta} t\right]$ tends to zero;
(iii) $t^{\prime} M_{\theta} t$ remains bounded;
(iv) if $S \subset \Theta$ is a set of bounded cardinality such that $\sup \{\|s-\theta\| ; s \in S\}$ remains bounded, then the vector $\left\{s^{\prime} X_{\theta} ; s \in S\right\}$ is asymptotically Gaussian.

This definition involves certain Euclidean norms $\|t\|$ which depend on $v$. If the conditions hold for different values of $\theta \in \Theta$, the norms in question may also be different. Thus the norms should really be written as $\|t\|_{\theta, v}$. Actually the definition involves two different norms: the norms written $\|t\|$ and the norms $\left(t^{\prime} M_{\theta} t\right)^{1 / 2}$. This is inherited from historical happenstance and will be reviewed in more detail later in this section.

In standard formulations the norms $\|t\|$ play the role of indicators of the speed of convergence of good estimates. We shall call them reference norms. On the contrary, the square norms $t^{\prime} M_{\theta} t$ specify the covariance structure of approximating Gaussian experiments. In many situations the set $\Theta$ is sufficiently rich around $\theta$, making the requirement (iv) a consequence of the others, as was shown in Chapter 10.

Part of the intent behind Definition 1 is expressed by the following lemma. To state it, let $\xi$ be the map from $\mathscr{M}(\Theta)$ to $\Theta$ defined by the integrals $\xi(\mu)=\int s \mu(d s)$ and let $H$ be the quadratic form defined by

$$
H(\mu)=-\frac{1}{2} \iint\|s-t\|^{2} \mu(d s) \mu(d t)=\|\xi(\mu)\|^{2}
$$

for $\mu \in \mathscr{M}_{0}(\Theta)$. Recall that $\Gamma(\mu)$ is defined by $\Gamma(\mu)=-\frac{1}{2} \iint q^{2}(s, t) \mu(d s) \mu(d t)$. Lemma 1. Let the LAN condition be satisfied at $\theta \in \Theta$. Let $U(\theta, b)$ denote the ball $\{t, t \in \Theta,\|t-\theta\| \leq b\}$. Then, for each fixed $b$,
(i) There are Gaussian experiments $\mathscr{G}=\left\{G_{t} ; t \in U(\theta, b)\right\}$ such that, if $S \subset U(\theta, b)$ has bounded cardinality, $\Delta\left(\mathscr{E}_{S}, \mathscr{G}_{S}\right)$ tends to zero.
(ii) For each fixed $\varepsilon>0$ there is a fixed $\delta>0$ such that, eventually, the inequalities $\|\mu\| \leq 1, H(\mu) \leq \delta$ imply $\Gamma(\mu)<\varepsilon$ for $\mu \in \mathscr{M}_{0}[U(\theta, b)]$.
(iii) If $s$ and $t$ remain in $U(\theta, b)$, then $q^{2}(s, t)-(s-t)^{\prime} M_{\theta}(s-t)$ tends to zero. (iv) If $\mu \in \mathscr{M}_{1}[U(\theta, b)]$ and if $\|\mu\|$ remains bounded, then $\Gamma\left[\mu-\delta_{\xi(\mu)}\right]$ and $\Gamma\left(\mu-\delta_{\theta}\right)-[\xi(\mu)-\theta]^{\prime} M_{\theta}[\xi(\mu)-\theta]$ tend to zero.

Proof. Consider any set $S \subset U(\theta, b)$ such that card $S$ remains bounded. Conditions (i) and (iv) of the LAN assumption imply the joint asymptotic normality of the logarithms $\{\Lambda(s, \theta) ; s \in S\}$. The combination (ii), (iii) implies contiguity of the nets $\left\{P_{s}\right\}$ and $\left\{P_{\theta}\right\}$. This gives the statement (i) of the lemma concerning the asymptotic Gaussian character of the experiment $\left\{P_{s} ; s \in U(\theta, b)\right\}$.

Statement (iii) of the lemma just says that the covariance of the approximating Gaussian processes can be taken equal to that of a process $t \mapsto t^{\prime} Y$, where Yis Gaussian $\mathscr{N}\left(0, M_{\theta}\right)$. This is implied by LAN. Statement (ii) of the lemma is obtained from this by rephrasing the boundedness condition (iii) of the LAN condition. As to statement (iv) of the lemma, it is obtainable by integrating
the difference $q^{2}(s, t)-(s-t)^{\prime} M_{\theta}(s-t)$ of statement (iii). Hence the result.
Note that the part of statement (iv), Lemma 1, which says that $\Gamma\left[\mu-\delta_{\xi(\mu)}\right]$ tends to zero is very important. Indeed it says that one may work directly on the space $\mathbb{R}^{k}$ which contains $\Theta$ instead of on the space $\mathscr{M}_{1}(\Theta)$ of Section 6. We have also included in Lemma 1 a statement (ii) which is a very immediate consequence of the others for the following reasons. It turns out that when $\Theta$ is sufficiently rich around $\theta$, the combination (i)-(ii) of Lemma 1 already implies the LAN conditons. That is, it implies the existence of the variables $X_{\theta}$ and of the matrices $M_{\theta}$ for which the conditions are satisfied. This may be seen using the arguments given in Chapter 10. The role of the reference norms $\|t\|$ is then clear: Under conditions (i) and (ii) of the lemma, they insure that the quadratic forms $\mu \mapsto \Gamma(\mu)$ may be approximated by quadratic forms $\xi^{\prime}(\mu) M \xi(\mu)$, with $M$ a matrix on $\mathbb{R}^{k}$. To see this in a simple case, suppose that (i) and (ii) of Lemma 1 holds and let $S$ be a subset of $U(\theta, b)$ for a fixed $b$. Let $K$ be the quadratic form defined on $\mathscr{M}_{0}(S)$ by an approximating Gaussian experiment. Suppose that the pair ( $S, K$ ) is free in the sense of Section 4, Definition 1. Let $t$ be a point of $\Theta$ of the form $t=\int s \mu(d s)$ for some $\mu \in \mathscr{M}_{1}(S)$
with $\|\mu\|$ bounded. Then $H\left(\delta_{t}-\mu\right)=0$ and therefore $K\left(\delta_{t}-\mu\right)$ and $\Gamma\left(\delta_{t}-\mu\right)$ tend to zero. This implies that on the part of $U(\theta, b)$ in the local span of $S$ the logarithms of likelihood ratios of the Gaussian experiments satisfy conditions (i) and (iii) of the LAN assumption.

Here the statement of the LAN condition allows the dimension $k$ of the space $\mathbb{R}^{k}$ which contains $\Theta$ to depend on $v$. Thus $k$ may well be unbounded. We shall see in Chapter 14 that for bounded $k$ and under (i)-(ii) of Lemma 1, one can select suitable subsets $S \subset \Theta$ and reconstruct the variables $X_{\theta}$ and the matrices $M_{\theta}$ of the LAN condition. This is done there for exponential families, under conditions which are certainly satisfied here.

To summarize, (i)-(ii) of Lemma 1 often imply the existence of the objects $X_{\theta}, M_{\theta}$ of the LAN condition, the role of the reference norms being then to yield the desired linear correspondence between $\Theta$ and the logarithms of likelihood ratios.

Another aspect of the role of the reference norms is totally different. Note that, here, Lemma 1 gives assertions relative to the balls $U(\theta, b)$ instead of the balls $\Theta(\theta, b)=\{t ; q(t, \theta) \leq b\}$ used in Section 6. The LAN condition, or the combination (i)-(ii) of Lemma 1, imply that for each fixed $b$ there is a fixed $c$ such that, eventually $U(\theta, b) \subset \Theta(\theta, c)$. However, the reverse implication is not true. An example will be given later in this section.

It happens to be true that using auxiliary estimates $\hat{\theta}$ such that $\|\hat{\theta}-\theta\|$ remains bounded in $P_{\theta}$ probability, one could carry out a construction of centerings similar to those of Section 6 using only the LAN conditions. However, cases where the construction can be carried out but where there will be pairs ( $s, t)$ such that $q(s, t)$ tends to zero but $\|s-t\|$ does not, produce difficulties in the practical interpretation of the mathematical theorems. Thus we shall work mostly with the LAN condition supplemented by the following condition.
(S1) The norms $\|\cdot\|$ satisfy (S1) at $\theta$ if boundedness of one of the quantities $q(t, \theta)$ or $\|t-\theta\|$ implies that of the other.

When the reference norms of the LAN condition satisfy (S1), one can in principle dispense with them altogether, using instead the function $q^{2}(s, t)= -8 \log \int \sqrt{d P_{s} d P_{t}}$ as in Sections 5 and 6. This leads to a condition resembling the LAN condition but differing from it.

Definition 1*. The experiments $\mathscr{E}$ satisfy the conditions LAN* at $\theta \in \Theta \subset \mathbb{R}^{k}$ if there are random variables $X_{\theta}$ and nonrandom matrices $M_{\theta}$ satisfying all the requirements of Definition 1 except that the boundedness restrictions on the reference norms $\|t\|$ or $\sup \{\|s-\theta\| ; s \in S\}$ are replaced by the conditions that $q(\theta+t, \theta)$ and $\sup \{q(s, \theta) ; s \in S\}$ remain bounded.

Of course, if the reference norms of Definition 1 satisfy ( S 1 ), then LAN implies LAN*. Conversely LAN* implies that LAN is satisfied. One can take for reference norms the norms $t \mapsto\left(t^{\prime} M_{\theta} t\right)^{1 / 2}$ themselves and then (S1) is satisfied. Under LAN*, one can obtain a result similar to that of Lemma 1.

Lemma 2. Let the LAN* condition be satisfied at $\theta$. Then:
(i) Condition (G0) of Section 6 is satisfied at $\theta$.
(ii) If $q(s, \theta)+q(t, \theta)$ remains bounded, then $q^{2}(s, t)-(s-t)^{\prime} M_{\theta}(s-t)$ tends to zero.
(iii) If $\mu \in \mathscr{M}_{1}[\Theta(\theta, b)]$, with $b$ fixed, is such that $\|\mu\|$ remains bounded, then $\Gamma\left[\mu-\delta_{\xi(\mu)}\right]$ tends to zero.

The proof is the same as that of Lemma 1. One can also show that the conditions (i)-(ii) of Lemma 2 do imply the LAN* conditions in many cases.

Since LAN* implies (G0) we are back into the framework of Section 6. Note that the theorems of Section 6 use requirements called (B), (D), (E), (FF), and the accessibility requirement called (A). Of these, (B), (D), and (E) involve the auxiliary estimate with allowed range $\Omega$ and the construction sets $S_{\omega}, \omega \in \Omega$. They do not involve the quadratic forms and therefore need no modification to be applicable here. Conditions (FF) and (A) involve the quadratic forms. They are immediately translatable using quadratic forms on $\mathbb{R}^{k}$ instead of $\mathscr{M}_{0}(\Theta)$.

Therefore all the results of Section 6 become directly applicable here. The main difference is that under LAN* one may work directly in $\mathbb{R}^{k}$ instead of $\mathscr{M}_{1}(\Theta)$. Indeed, suppose that the construction of Section 6 yields a centering variable $\hat{Z}$ with values in $\mathscr{M}_{1}(\Theta)$. Let $\hat{T}=\xi(\hat{Z})=\int s \hat{Z}(d s)$ be the canonical image of $\hat{Z}$ in $\mathbb{R}^{k}$.

Assume that for $P_{\theta}$ the $L_{1}$-norms $\|\hat{Z}\|$ are bounded in probability and that if $q(t, \theta)$ remains bounded then

$$
\left.\Lambda(t, \theta)+\frac{1}{2}\left[\hat{Z}-\delta_{t}\right)-\Gamma\left(\hat{Z}-\delta_{\theta}\right)\right]
$$

tends to zero in $P_{\theta}$ probability. Then, if LAN* holds at $\theta$, the difference

$$
\Lambda(t, \theta)+\frac{1}{2}\left[(\widehat{T}-t)^{\prime} M_{\theta}(\widehat{T}-t)-(\widehat{T}-\theta)^{\prime} M_{\theta}(\widehat{T}-\theta)\right]
$$

also tends to zero in probability. This means that the centerings $\hat{T}$ possess the asymptotic normality and sufficiency of Theorems 1 to 3 of Section 6.

If one uses the LAN* assumptions, the reference norms of LAN do not appear in the furmulation. Thus, they are not essential. However, they play an important role in the standard formulation of conditions similar to LAN found in the literature. Thus a few words about them may be in order. Here are certain common practices.
(1) In the simples cases, one considers a fixed space $\mathbb{R}^{k}$ (independent of $v$ ). One takes reference norms which do not depend on $\theta$. They have the form $\|t\|_{\nu}=\alpha_{\nu}\|t\|_{0}$ where $\|t\|_{0}$ is a fixed Euclidean norm on $\mathbb{R}^{k}$ and where $\alpha_{\nu}$ are numbers tending to infinity. (The $\alpha_{v}$ may be the usual $\sqrt{v}$ for $v$ independent observations.)
(2) An analogous case, differing from (1) only in notation, is to assume $\mathbb{R}^{k}$ fixed and take $\|t\|_{\nu}=\left\|\alpha_{\nu} t\right\|_{0}$ where $\|\cdot\|_{0}$ is fixed and where the $\alpha_{\nu}$ are matrices with $\alpha_{v}^{-1}$ tending to zero.
(3) In more complex cases it may be necessary to use reference norms which depend on $\theta \in \Theta$. One may use norms $\|t\|_{\nu}=\left\|\alpha_{\nu}(\theta) t\right\|_{0}$ with matrices $\alpha_{\nu}(\theta)$ exactly as in (2) above, except that they depend on $\theta \in \Theta$.

If the LAN* conditions are satisfied at $\theta$ one can use for the norms in this setup the norms $\left(t^{\prime} M_{\theta} t\right)^{1 / 2}$. Thus (3) represents the general case, except that here $\mathbb{R}^{k}$ is fixed. It was not necessarily fixed in LAN*. Also, the matrices $M_{\theta}^{-1}$ of LAN* need not tend to zero.

In a formulation such as (3) above, the local character of the renorming makes various complications in the formulation of conditions and in the proofs. This is why we have avoided it by using the formulation of Sections 3 to 6 .

If the LAN* conditions hold at $\theta$ and if $q(t, \theta)$ remains bounded, rescaling matrices $\alpha_{\nu}(\theta)$ and $\alpha_{\nu}(t)$ to be used at $\theta$ and $t$ must be appropriately close to each other. Some information on this will be found in Section 8. However, we feel that it is easier not to use those matrices.

For $\Theta$ fixed in a fixed $\mathbb{R}^{k}$, there is a variant of the LAN condition which has been used by several authors, including J. Hajek. Assuming that the reference norms have the form $\left\|\alpha_{v}(\theta) t\right\|_{0}$, these authors require only that the conditions of the LAN assumption hold for sequences $t_{v}$ with $\theta+t_{v} \in \Theta$ and $t_{v}=\alpha_{v}^{-1}(\theta) s$ for fixed values of $s \in \mathbb{R}^{k}$.

Such assumptions were used by J. Hájek to obtain his local asymptotic minimaxity and admissibility results. Here, as pointed out in Chapter 7, only weak convergence of experiments is needed. Similarly, for instance, if $\alpha_{\nu}(\theta)=\alpha_{\nu}$ independent of $\theta$, or slowly varying, the theorems of Chapter 8 will still yield Hájek's famous convolution theorem.

However, more generally, we find it psychologically difficult to envisage proper practical use of the weakened assumptions of the type used by Hájek and will therefore retain the forms LAN or LAN* described above. Note also that the weak forms imply the strong form if one adds the requirement that if $\left\|t_{v}-t\right\|_{v} \rightarrow 0$ then $\left\|P_{\theta+t_{v}, v}-P_{\theta+t, v}\right\| \rightarrow 0$. Thus, the difference is visible but not substantial enough to warrant study of the weaker form.

It has been stated above that the LAN* condition is stronger than the LAN condition. Indeed the LAN condition implies that if $\|t-\theta\|$ is bounded the nets $\left\{P_{t}\right\}$ and $\left\{P_{\theta}\right\}$ are contiguous. Therefore $q(t, \theta)$ remains bounded. However,
the converse is not necessarily true. It is not even true if one assumed that $\Theta$ is a fixed open set in a fixed Euclidean space, with several further restrictions.

Here is an example exhibiting the following features.
(a) The parameter set $\Theta$ is a fixed open subset of the line. The LAN assumptions are satisfied for each fixed $\theta \in \Theta$ and for norms $\|t\|_{\nu}=\sqrt{\nu}|t|_{0}$, where
$|t|_{0}$ is the absolute value of $t$. The approximating Gaussian experiments are of rank one.
(b) There are estimates $\hat{\theta}_{\nu}$ such that $\sqrt{\nu}\left|\hat{\theta}_{\nu}-\theta\right|_{0}=\left\|\hat{\theta}_{\nu}-\theta\right\|_{\nu}$ is bounded in $P_{\theta}$ probability for each fixed $\theta \in \Theta$.
(c) For each fixed $\theta \in \Theta$ there are sequences $\left\{t_{\nu}\right\}, t_{\nu} \in \Theta$ such that $\left|t_{\nu}-\theta\right|_{0}$ tends to infinity but $q\left(t_{v}, \theta\right)$ tends to zero.
(d) For each fixed $\theta$, the condition (G0) of Section 6 is satisfied with approximating experiments of rank two.

To construct the example, for each integer $m \geq 1$ let $J_{m}$ be the interval $\left(4 m, 4 m+\frac{1}{4}\right)$ of the line. Let $\Theta=\bigcup_{m} J_{m}$ and let $\left\{r_{m} ; m=1,2, \ldots\right\}$ be an ordering of the rational numbers of $(0,1)$. If $\theta \in J_{m}$, let $p_{1}(\theta)=r_{m} \cos ^{2} 2 \pi \theta$, $p_{2}(\theta)=r_{m} \sin ^{2} 2 \pi \theta$, and $p_{3}(\theta)=1-r_{m}$. Let $P_{\theta, v}$ be the trinomial distribution with $v$ observations and probabilities $\left[p_{1}(\theta), p_{2}(\theta), p_{3}(\theta)\right]$.

It can be shown (see Chapter 17) that there exist estimates $\hat{\theta}_{v}$ such that, for each fixed $\theta$, the variables $\sqrt{v}\left[\hat{\theta}_{v}-\theta\right]$ are asymptotically normally distributed with expectations zero and bounded variances. It is trivial to verify that the LAN conditions hold with norms $\|t\|_{\nu}=\sqrt{\nu}|t|_{0}$. It is also trivial to verify that (G0) holds. However, the vectors $\left[p_{1}(\theta), p_{2}(\theta), p_{3}(\theta)\right], \theta \in \Theta$ form a dense subset of the simplex of possible probability vectors for a trinomial.

Thus fixing $\theta$ in a particular interval $J_{m}$, one can always find other points $t_{v} \notin J_{m}$ with $q_{v}\left(t_{v}, \theta\right)$ as small as one wishes. These $t_{v}$ will necessarily be such that $\left|t_{v}-\theta\right|_{0}$ tends to infinity if $q_{v}\left(t_{v}, \theta\right)$ tends to zero.

The existence of estimates $\hat{\theta}$ such that $\|\hat{\theta}-\theta\|_{\nu}=\sqrt{n}|\hat{\theta}-\theta|_{0}$ stays bounded in $P_{\theta}$ probability for each $\theta \in \Theta$ even though ( S 1 ) is not satisfied, does not violate the inequalities of Chapter 4. These say, for instance, that for any pair ( $\theta_{0}, \theta_{1}$ ) one must have

$$
E_{\theta_{0}}\left\|\hat{\theta}-\theta_{0}\right\|_{\nu}^{2}+E_{\theta_{1}}\left\|\hat{\theta}-\theta_{1}\right\|^{2} \geq\left\|\theta_{1}-\theta_{0}\right\|^{2} \int \frac{d P_{\theta_{0}} d P_{\theta_{1}}}{d\left(P_{\theta_{0}}+P_{\theta_{1}}\right)}
$$

For any estimate $\hat{\theta}$ such that $\sqrt{n}|\hat{\theta}-\theta|_{0}$ stays bounded in $P_{\theta}$ probability for each fixed $\theta$, there will be, for every $v$, many large values of $\theta$ at which $\hat{\theta}$ behaves very poorly. The mathematical limit theorems have a content which seems remote from practical applicability, unless one is willing for other reasons to ignore the large values of the parameter. This is one of the reasons we prefer LAN* to LAN.

The LAN or LAN* assumptions allow the spaces $\mathbb{R}^{k}$, and therefore their dimension $k$, to depend on $v$. This dimension might tend to infinity. However, most of the results of Section 6 require for their proofs the cardinality restriction (D) of Section 5. This effectively means that, locally, the part of $\mathbb{R}^{k}$ one looks at
belongs to a Euclidean space of bounded dimension. The dimension may vary from place to place, but the theorems of Section 6 ignore those places where the dimension tends to infinity. In the system most commonly encountered in the literature, one assumes that $k$ is fixed. The usual description involves a fixed set $\Theta$ in a fixed Euclidean space $\mathbb{R}^{k}$. Often $\Theta$ is assumed to be
open. One uses renorming constants independent of $\theta$. The convergence assumptions are usually pointwise convergence assumptions.

We shall now consider systems of assmptions of that general character, for subsets $\Theta$ of Euclidean spaces and for sets $\Theta$ which are differentiable manifolds. To state the assumptions recall that in Chapter 10 we had introduced a concept of tangency for sets. If $S_{v}$ and $T_{v}$ are sets of a normed space ( $\mathscr{X}_{v},|\cdot|_{v}$ ) they are called tangent at $a_{v}$ if for every fixed $b$ the Hausdorff distance between $S_{\nu} \cap U\left(a_{\nu}, b\right)$ and $T_{\nu} \cap U\left(a_{\nu}, b\right)$ tended to zero. Here $U\left(a_{\nu}, b\right)$ is the ball $\{x: \left.\left|x-a_{\nu}\right|_{\nu} \leq b\right\}$ and the Hausdorff distances are computed for the norms $|\cdot|_{\nu} \cdot$

A possible system of assumptions is as follows, dropping the subscripts $v$ as usual.
(PG) The experiment $\mathscr{E}=\left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ are pointwise asymptotically Gaussian if the following requirements are all satisfied:
(i) The sets $\Theta$ are fixed subsets of fixed space $\mathbb{R}^{k}$.
(ii) For each fixed $\theta \in \Theta$ the LAN conditions hold for reference norms which may depend on $\theta$.
(iii) For each fixed $\theta$, the reference norms satisfy condition (S1).

As previously explained, one could replace the conditions (ii)-(iii) by the condition that LAN* holds at each fixed $\theta$. We have not done so to point out results concerning the reference norms and for another reason, as follows. The condition (PG) allows the sets $\Theta$ to be entirely arbitrary. They may have as many kinks, dents, and spikes as one wishes. To obtain results similar to the usual ones, one needs to restrict them.

Thus, we shall say that the experiments $\mathscr{E}$ satisfy (PGC) if they satisfy (PG) and if for each $\theta$ there are fixed convex cones $C_{\theta}$ with vertex at $\theta$ such that $C_{\theta}$ and $\Theta$ are tangent at $\theta$ for the reference norms of the LAN condition.

We shall say that they satisfy (PGL) if they satisfy (PGC) for cones $C_{\theta}$ such that $C_{\theta}-\theta$ is a linear subspace of $\mathbb{R}^{k}$. A first remark is that (PGC) or (PGL) places a severe constraint on the reference norms.

Lemma 3. Let (PGC) be satisfied and let $\theta$ be a fixed element of $\Theta$ with associated reference norms $\|\cdot\|$, matrices $M_{\theta}$, and tangent cone $C_{\theta}$. If $\theta+t$ stays in $C_{\theta}$ and if $t^{\prime} M_{\theta} t$ stays bounded, so does $\|t\|$. Also, if the net $\{v\}$ is not pathological, or if (PGL) holds, if $t^{\prime} M_{\theta} t$ tends to zero, so does $\|t\|$.

Proof. For fixed $b$, let $d_{1}(b)$ be the Hausdorff distance between $\Theta \cap U(\theta, b)$ and $C_{\theta} \cap U(\theta, b)$ for the reference norms. Let

$$
d_{2}(b)=\sup \left\{\left|t^{\prime} M_{\theta} t-q^{2}(\theta+t, \theta)\right| ; \theta+t \in \Theta \cap U(\theta, b)\right\} .
$$

If such an integer exists, let $m$ be the largest integer for which $d_{1}(m)+ d_{2}(m) \leq 1$. Otherwise let $m=\infty$. Let $S$ be the set of points $s \in \Theta \cap U(\theta, m)$ such that $(s-\theta)^{\prime} M_{\theta}(s-\theta) \leq 1$. On $S$ we have $q^{2}(s, \theta) \leq 2$. Thus according to
the condition (S1), including in (PG), the quantity $\sup \{\|s-\theta\| ; s \in S\}$ remains bounded. Let $\bar{S}=\{t ; \inf [\|s-t\| ; s \in S] \leq 1\}$. Then $\sup \{\|s-\theta\| ; s \in \bar{S}\}$ is also bounded. Take any $s \in C_{\theta}$ such that $(s-\theta)^{\prime} M_{\theta}(s-\theta) \leq 1$. Either $s$ itself belongs to $C_{\theta} \cap U(\theta, m)$ or there is some number $\alpha<1$ such that $t=\theta+\alpha(s-\theta)$ belongs to $C_{\theta} \cap U(\theta, m)$. One may assume in addition that $\alpha$ has been taken within $1 / m$ of the supremum of the numbers $\beta$ such that $\theta+\beta(s-\theta) \in C_{\theta} \cap U(\theta, m)$.

In such a case, since $m$ tends to infinity, if $\|s-\theta\|$ tends to infinity, so does $\|t-\theta\|$. However, $t$ eventually belongs to the set $\bar{S}$. Therefore $\|t-\theta\|$ and $\|s-\theta\|$ must remain bounded. This gives the first assertion. To obtain the second statement take points $t$ such that $\theta+t \in C_{\theta}$ and let $\delta^{2}=t^{\prime} M_{\theta} t$. If $\delta^{2}$ is not zero, let $s=\delta^{-1} t$. If $\delta^{2}=0$ but the integer $m$ is not infinite, let $s=m t$. If both $\delta^{2}=0$ and $m=\infty$ let $s=\varepsilon^{-1} t$, where $\varepsilon>0$ tends to zero along the net $\{v\}$. In any case one will have $s^{\prime} M_{\theta} s \leq 1$. Therefore $\|s\|$ remains bounded and $\|t\|$ tends to zero.

In the (PGL) case the situation is simpler since on the linear space $C_{\theta}-\theta$ the norms $\left(t^{\prime} M_{\theta} t\right)^{1 / 2}$ are bounded by a multiple of $\|t\|$, because of the LAN assumptions. Conversely the first part of the lemma says that $\|t\|$ is bounded by a multiple of $\left(t^{\prime} M_{\theta} t\right)^{1 / 2}$. Thus $\|\cdot\|$ and the norm $t \mapsto\left(t M_{\theta} t\right)^{1 / 2}$ are related on $C_{\theta}-\theta$, in the sense of Section 4, Definition 1.

We have stated here two sets of assumptions, the set (PGC) and the stronger (PGL). We shall discuss below only the (PGL) system. However, there are many problems in which the most interesting points of $\Theta$ are its boundary points. At these, (PGC) may be satisfied for cones $C_{\theta}$ such that $C_{\theta}-\theta$ is a proper subset of its linear span. A classical instance involves a twodimensional parameter $\theta=(\xi, \eta)$ with $\xi \in(-\infty,+\infty)$ but $\eta \geq 0$. One may want to investigate tests of the hypothesis $\eta=0$ against the alternatives $\eta>0$. Here the cones $C_{\theta}$ are half-spaces.

One can readily find many examples of this kind, involving, for instance, $k$ dimensional parameters $\theta$ whose coordinates are restricted by order relations. In most such cases the solid angle of $C_{\theta}-\theta$ in its linear span stays away from zero. Then the constructions described below can still be carried out with obvious modifications. Thus, for simplicity, we shall concentrate on (PGL).
