Lemma 4. Let (PGL) be satisfied and let $\theta$ be fixed. Then Condition (H), Section 6 is satisfied at $\theta$.

Proof. The space $C_{\theta}$ is the translate of a linear subspace of $\mathbb{R}^{k}$. For each $\theta \in \Theta$, let $\tau(\theta)$ be the point of $C_{\theta}$ closest to $\theta$ for the reference norm. Let $\mathscr{G}$ be the Gaussian experiment whose covariance structure is given by $K(\mu)=\left|\int \tau(s) \mu(d s)\right|^{2}$, where $|t|$ on $C_{\theta}-\theta$ is the norm $\left(t^{\prime} M_{\theta} t\right)^{1 / 2}$ of the LAN assumptions. Take two fixed numbers such that $1<b<c$ and let $V=\{t ; q(t, \theta) \leq b\}$. Let $S=\{s ; q(s, \theta) \leq c\}$. Suppose that $\mu \in \mathscr{M}_{1}(S)$ is such that $K\left(\mu-\delta_{\theta}\right) \leq 1$.

Then $\eta(\mu)=\int \tau(s) \mu(d s)$ is a certain element of $C_{\theta}$. By assumption, there are elements $x$ of $\Theta$ such that $\|x-\eta(\mu)\| \rightarrow 0$ for the reference norms. This implies that $K\left(\mu-\delta_{x}\right) \rightarrow 0$. Thus Condition (H) is satisfied.

It follows from this lemma that if estimates $\hat{\theta}$ satisfying the conditions (E)
of Section 5 are available with their range $\Omega \subset \Theta$, one can readily construct the sets $S_{\omega}$ by the procedure described in Section 6, Lemmas 4 and 5. These sets will yield free pairs ( $S_{\omega}, K_{\omega}$ ). In addition the accessibility condition (A) of Section 6 will be satisfied.

In the present case, if the forms $K_{\omega}$ are not directly available, one can expand the sets $S_{\omega}$ by adjunction of midpoints and estimate the $K_{\omega}$. Therefore the possibility of using the constructions of Sections 3 to 6 and obtaining all the results of Section 6 depends only on the availability of suitable auxiliary estimates $\hat{\theta}$.

Note that these estimates need to have a range $\Omega$ which satisfies Condition (E) and are therefore discrete in a suitable manner. If $\hat{\theta}$ satisfies the required $q$-convergence restrictions, one can always discretize it as shown in Section 5. However, there is a particular case where the discretization is very easy. This is the case where (PGL) holds with universal reference norms. Specifically, suppose that there is a choice of reference norms $\|\cdot\|$ such that the LAN conditions hold at each fixed $\theta \in \Theta$ with these norms as reference norms. (Then, as we have seen above in Lemma 3, the norms $t \mapsto\left(t^{\prime} M_{\theta} t\right)^{1 / 2}$ and $t \mapsto\|t\|$ are related on each $C_{\theta}-\theta$. Thus the existence of universal reference norms places a definite restriction on the possible $M_{\theta}$.) With such universal norms one can discretize by the following simple procedure. Take a unit cube for the norm $\|\cdot\|$. Pave $\mathbb{R}^{k}$ by translates of these cubes, removing some faces to obtain a partition of $\mathbb{R}^{k}$. If $B$ is one of the cubes of the partition and if $B \cap \Theta$ is not empty, select a point $\omega \in B \cap \Theta$. This will give a suitable set $\Omega$. To discretize $\hat{\theta}$, first order $\Omega$ arbitrarily in a sequence (it is countable). Then replace $\hat{\theta}$ by the first point of $\Omega$ closest to it.

Generally speaking, a universal reference norm can be substituted for the function $q$ to indicate distances between points of $\Theta$. Such norms occur often in the form $\|t\|_{\nu}=\left\|\alpha_{\nu} t\right\|_{0}$ for matrices $\alpha_{\nu}$ as indicated previously. However, there do exist naturally occurring statistical problems where universal reference norms do not exist. Here is a very simple example exhibiting this property.

Let $X_{j} ; j \in J$ be independent Bernoulli variables. Let $\Theta$ be a Euclidean space $\mathbb{R}^{k}$ and let $z_{j} ; j \in J$ be specified nonrandom elements of the dual of $\mathbb{R}^{k}$. Assume that $P_{\theta}\left[X_{j}=1\right]=1-P_{\theta}\left[X_{j}=0\right]=e^{z_{j} \theta}\left[1+e^{z_{j} \theta}\right]^{-1}$. Here, as before, $v$ is suppressed, but it is there, tending to infinity. We assume, however, that $\Theta$ is independent of $v$.

It turns out that if points $\theta \in \Theta$ satisfy the condition (G0) of Section 6, then the appropriate square norms are $t^{\prime} M_{\theta} t=\sum_{j} p_{j}(\theta)\left[1-p_{j}(\theta)\right]\left[z_{j} t\right]^{2}$. Necessary and sufficient conditions for (G0) are easy to find. They say that, for each fixed $b$ and each fixed $\varepsilon>0$, the contribution to the product experiment $\left\{P_{\theta+t} ; t^{\prime} M_{\theta} t \leq b\right\}$ of those $j$ 's for which $\left[z_{j} t\right]^{2}>\varepsilon$ is asymptotically trivial. One can readily construct systems $\left\{z_{j} ; j \in J\right\}$ where for some pairs ( $s, t$ ) the matrices $M_{s}$ and $M_{t}$ are not related. Other examples occur, for instance, in the theory of supercritical branching processes where the $M_{\theta}$ are numbers of the form $[m(\theta)]^{\nu}$ with $v$ equal to the number of generations.

Universal reference norms, if they exist, are a great convenience. However,
since reference norms themselves are not indispensible, universal ones are certainly not indispensable either. They do play a role in the statement of the (PGL) conditions above, most particularly in the requirement of existence of linear affine tangent spaces. One could avoid referring to these tangent spaces, requiring instead that Condition (H) of Section 6 be satisfied.

The (PGL) condition uses the LAN assumptions which require the existence of certain random vectors $X_{\theta}$ and matrices $M_{\theta}$. One can rephrase (PGL) in such a way as to avoid mentioning these objects. To do this, one would require that (G0) and (H) hold at each fixed $\theta \in \Theta$. In addition, one would require the existence of Euclidean norms $\|\cdot\|$ such that (i) $q(s, \theta)$ is bounded if and only if $\|s-\theta\|$ is, and (ii) for each fixed $b$ and each fixed $\varepsilon>0$, there is a $\delta>0$ such that, eventually, the inequalities $\|\mu\| \leq 1,\left\|\int s \mu(d s)\right\| \leq \delta$ imply $\Gamma(\mu)<\varepsilon$ for $\mu \in \mathscr{M}_{0}[\Theta(\theta, b)]$. Here the norms taken on $\Theta \subset \mathbb{R}^{k}$ are reference norms, but their role is different. They insure the approximability of $\Gamma(\mu)$ by $\left|\int s \mu(d s)\right|^{2}$ for some other Euclidean norm $|\cdot|$ on $\mathbb{R}^{k}$.

The simplest situation covered by the (PGL) condition is the one in which the tangent spaces $C_{\theta}$ are all equal to the space $\mathbb{R}^{k}$ itself. This is the situation one encounters when $\Theta$ is an open subset of $\mathbb{R}^{k}$ and when the reference norms $\|\cdot\|$ are of the form $\|t\|_{\nu}=\left\|\alpha_{\nu} t\right\|_{0}$ for a fixed norm $\|\cdot\|_{0}$ and for number or matrices $\alpha_{\nu}$ such that $\alpha_{\nu}^{-1}$ tends to zero.

When (PGL) holds, but with linear spaces $C_{\theta}-\theta$ which are proper subspaces of $\mathbb{R}^{k}$, more complicated situations can arise. Some of them are cases which would often be described by saying that $\Theta$ is a "surface" or "curve" in $\mathbb{R}^{k}$ with tangent space $C_{\theta}$ at $\theta$. They also include certain cases where $\Theta$ is a differentiable manifold of class $C^{1}$ and dimension $m$, imbedded in a space $\mathbb{R}^{k}$ with $k \leq 2 m+1$. The case of differentiable manifolds can also be considered without embedding them in $\mathbb{R}^{k}$ as we shall now show.

The space $\Theta$ is a differentiable manifold of class $C^{1}$ and dimension $m$ if it is a topological space with a countable base such that each $\theta \in \Theta$ possesses a neighborhood homeomorphic to an open subset of $\mathbb{R}^{m}$. The various homeomorphisms are assumed to be $C^{1}$-compatible in the sense that, if $\phi$ and $\psi$ are two of them with overlapping domains, the maps $\phi \circ \psi^{-1}$ and $\psi \circ \phi^{-1}$ are continuously differentiable. The homeomorphisms provide "local maps" of the manifold, in the geographical sense of the term.

Assuming that $\Theta$ is such a $C^{1}$-manifold independent of $v$, one can consider pointwise assumptions generally similar to the (PGL) assumptions described above. To state them, note first that if $\xi$ is a local coordinate system, homeomorphism of an open set $V \subset \Theta$ into $\mathbb{R}^{k}$, then there is a Gaussian experiment $\mathscr{G}_{V}=\left\{G_{s} ; s \in V\right\}$ such that the covariance structure of $\mathscr{G}_{V}$ is given by the inner products $\xi^{\prime}(s) \xi(t)$ in $\mathbb{R}^{k}$. Let $\left\|\xi^{\prime}(s)\right\|^{2}=\xi^{\prime}(s) \xi(s)$ be the corresponding square norm. Taking this into account, here is a possible system of assumptions. We shall call them PGM for pointwise, Gaussian, on a manifold.
(PGM) Let $\Theta$ be a fixed $C^{1}$-manifold of dimension $m$ and let $\mathscr{E}=\left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ be a net of experiments indexed by $\Theta$. This net satisfies (PGM) if
(i) For each fixed $\theta \in \Theta$, each fixed $b$ and each fixed neighborhood $V$ of $\theta$ the



set $\Theta(\theta, b)=\{t ; q(t, \theta) \leq b\}$ is eventually contained in $V$.
(ii) There are auxiliary estimates $\hat{\theta}$ satisfying Condition (E) of Section 5.
(iii) For each fixed $\theta \in \Theta$, there are coordinate systems $\xi_{\theta}$ such that if $b$ is fixed and $S=\Theta(\theta, b)=\{t ; q(t, \theta) \leq b\}$ then $S$ is eventually in the domain of $\xi_{\theta}$ and the distance $\Delta\left(\mathscr{G}_{S}, \mathscr{E}_{S}\right)$ for the Gaussian experiment with covariance structure $\xi_{\theta}^{\prime}(s) \xi_{\theta}(t)$ tends to zero.
(iv) For each fixed $\theta$, if $x \in \mathbb{R}^{m}$ is such that $\left\|x-\xi_{\theta}(\theta)\right\|$ remains bounded, there are points $t \in \Theta$ such that $\left\|x-\xi_{\theta}(t)\right\|$ tends to zero and such that $q(t, \theta)$ remains bounded.

The first condition of (PGM) states that local problems for $q$ are also local on the manifold. Condition (ii) is self-explanatory. Condition (iii) yields the desired local Gaussian approximations. Condition (iv) is rather strong. Together with the other conditions it says that if $\xi_{\theta}$ is defined on an open set $V$, then the neighborhoods induced by $q$ on $\Theta$ have a certain fullness, resembling the neighborhoods induced in $\xi_{\theta}(V)$ by the standard norm of $\mathbb{R}^{k}$. It is very much in the spirit of the tangent space requirement of (PGL). However, it is stronger in the sense that it forces the approximating Gaussian experiments to have rank $m$ equal to the dimension of the manifold. On the contrary, (PGL) allows any rank at most equal to the dimension $k$ of the space $\mathbb{R}^{k}$ in which $\Theta$ lies. It is clearly possible to modify (PGM) (iv) to accommodate this possibility. However, for simplicity we shall not do so.

Condition (PGM) (iii) could also be stated using instead of the inner product $\xi_{\theta}^{\prime}(s) \xi_{\theta}(t)$, products of the type $\xi_{\theta}^{\prime}(s) M_{\theta} \xi_{\theta}(t)$ for matrices $M_{\theta}$ on $\mathbb{R}^{m}$. Note that, according to our general notational conventions, $v$ has been suppressed, thus $\left\|\xi_{\theta}(s)\right\|^{2}$ is really $\left\|\xi_{\theta, v}(s)\right\|^{2}$, depending on $v$. As long as the matrices $M_{\theta, v}$ are positive definite (nonsingular), $\xi_{\theta, v}^{\prime}(s) M_{\theta, v} \xi_{\theta, v}(s)$ is the same as $\left\|\eta_{\theta, v}(s)\right\|^{2}$ for some other local coordinate system. Thus, unless one is in a situation where the use of singular matrices $M_{\theta, \nu}$ is absolutely necessary, the formulation given here is just as general. For simplicity, we shall consider only the nonsingular case. Note, however, that in many situations, one will take fixed local coordinate systems $\eta_{\theta}$, matrices $M_{\theta, v}$ which depend on $v$ and use $\left\|\xi_{\theta}(s)\right\|_{\nu}^{2}=\eta_{\theta}^{\prime}(s) M_{\theta, \nu} \eta_{\theta}(s)$.

Lemma 5. Let the conditions (PGM) be satisfied. Then, for each fixed $\theta \in \Theta$, Condition (H) of Section 6 is satisfied.

Proof. Consider two fixed numbers $b$ and $c$ with $b<c$. Let $V=\{t ; q(t, \theta) \leq b\}$ and let $S=\{s ; q(s, \theta) \leq c\}$. If $\mu \in \mathscr{M}_{0}(S)$, let $K(\mu)=\left\|\xi_{\theta}(\mu)\right\|^{2}, \xi_{\theta}(\mu)= \int \xi_{\theta}(s) \mu(d s) \in \mathbb{R}^{m}$, for the local coordinate system of (PGM) (iii). If $\mu \in \mathscr{M}_{1}(S)$ is such that $K\left(\mu-\delta_{\theta}\right)<b$, the condition (PGM) (iv) yields the existence of elements $t \in \Theta$ such that $\left\|\xi_{\theta}(\mu)-\xi_{\theta}(t)\right\|$ tends to zero and such that $q(t, \theta)$ remains bounded. By (PGM) (iii), $q(t, \theta)-\left\|\xi_{\theta}(t)-\xi_{\theta}(\theta)\right\|$ tends to zero. Thus $t$ will eventually be an element of $V$. This implies the desired result.

In the conditions (PGM), we have included the requirement that the auxiliary estimates $\hat{\theta}$ satisfy Condition (E). These estimates take their values in a set $\Omega$ constructible, for instance, by the procedure described in Section 5. Once $\Omega$ is given, to continue the construction of centerings, one needs sets $S_{\omega}$ and quadratic forms $K_{\omega}$. Here, because of Lemma 5, the sets $S_{\omega}$ can be constructed
by the procedure described in Section 6 and leading to Lemma 5, Section 6.
To carry out this procedure one needs suitable quadratic forms $Q_{\omega}$. In the present case it may be easier to think in terms of local coordinate systems. Thus (for each $v$ ), one will assign each $\omega$ a local coordinate system $\eta_{\omega}$ with domain $V_{\omega}$ and carry out the orthogonalization procedure with this system, identifying each $\mu \in \mathscr{M}_{1}\left(V_{\omega}\right)$ with its canonical image $\eta_{\omega}(\mu)=\int \eta_{\omega}(s) \mu(d s)$ in $\mathbb{R}^{m}$. The systems $\eta_{\omega}$ cannot be taken arbitrarily. However, for the technique to work it will be sufficient that if $\theta$ is fixed and if $q(\omega, \theta)$ remains bounded, then
(i) for each fixed $b$, the ball $\Theta(\omega, b)$ is eventually in the domain $V_{\omega}$ of $\eta_{\omega}$;
(ii) on $\mathscr{M}_{0}[\Theta(\omega, b)]$ the norms $\mu \mapsto\left\|\eta_{\omega}(\mu)\right\|$ and $\mu \mapsto\left\|\xi_{\theta}(\mu)\right\|$ are related in the sense of Definition 1, Section 4.

To insure that the norms are related requires some knowledge about the local structure of $\mathscr{E}$. However, it does not require exact knowledge of the possible local coordinate systems $\xi_{\theta}$. Once the sets $S_{\omega}$ are obtained, one can in principle obtain quadratic forms $K_{\omega}$ either by studying locally the function $q$ or by the method of midpoints described in the previous sections. Here a midpoint $m=m(s, t)$ between two elements $s$ and $t$ of $S_{\omega}$ can be obtained by taking some $m \in \Theta$ such that $\left\|\eta_{\omega}(m)-\frac{1}{2}\left[\eta_{\omega}(s)+\eta_{\omega}(t)\right]\right\|$ tends to zero.

A consequence of the assumptions (PGM) is that instead of working in the space $\mathscr{M}_{1}(\Theta)$ to obtain centerings $\hat{Z}$ satisfying the requirements of Section 6, one can work locally in $\mathbb{R}^{m}$, solving linear equations in the coordinate systems $\eta_{\omega}$. One can then pull back these centerings into $\Theta$ itself. Indeed, if there are centerings $\hat{Z}$ in $\mathscr{M}_{1}(\Theta)$ such that $\Gamma_{\theta}\left(\hat{Z}-\delta_{\theta}\right)$ remains bounded (for the forms $\Gamma_{\theta}$ of Lemma 2, Section 6) in $P_{\theta}$ probability, there will be random elements $\hat{T}$ with values in $\Theta$ itself such that $\Gamma\left(\hat{Z}-\delta_{\theta}\right)-\left\|\xi_{\theta}(\hat{T})-\xi_{\theta}(\theta)\right\|^{2}$ will tend to zero in probability. If the $\hat{Z}$ provide an approximation $-\frac{1}{2}\left[\Gamma\left(\hat{Z}-\delta_{t}\right)-\Gamma\left(\hat{Z}-\delta_{\theta}\right)\right]$ to the logarithms of likelihood ratios $\Lambda(t, \theta)$, then the difference $-\frac{1}{2}\left\{\left\|\xi_{\theta}(\widehat{T})-\xi_{\theta}(t)\right\|^{2}-\left\|\xi_{\theta}(\widehat{T})-\xi_{\theta}(\theta)\right\|\right\}^{2}$ will also provide such an approximation.

In summary, all the theorems of Section 6 will apply in the (PGM) case provided that the sets $\left(S_{\omega}, K_{\omega}\right)$ be constructed with sufficient care. In addition, the approximately sufficient statistics $\hat{T}$ of Theorem 3, Section 6, can be taken to have values in $\Theta$ itself.

The reader may wonder where in the preceding argument we have used the $C^{1}$ character of the manifold. In fact we have not. However, without it, one will find it difficult to find local coordinate systems $\eta_{\omega}$ related to the $\xi_{\theta}$ of the assumptions whenever $q(\theta, \omega)$ remains bounded.

The manifold situation described by (PGM) can also be reduced to the case covered by (PGL). Indeed, according to a theorem of H. Whitney, any $C^{1}$ manifold of dimension $m$ can be properly and regularly imbedded in a Euclidean space $\mathbb{R}^{k}$ with $k \leq 2 m+1$. Here "properly" refers to the fact that inverse images of compacts are compacts. The term "regularly" means that, for each $\theta$, a suitable subset of the coordinate system of $\mathbb{R}^{2 m+1}$ is a local coordinate system
around $\theta$.
The systems (PGL) or (PGM) described here use only pointwise convergence requirements. Assertions derived from them will necessarily reflect this feature. This may be considered rather unsatisfactory in the applications. However, it is easy to modify requirements such as (PGM) to obtain uniformity of the convergences on certain sets.

Consider for instance any fixed family $\mathscr{S}$ of subsets of $\Theta$. Change (PGM) or (PGL), replacing everywhere the expression "For each fixed $\theta$ " by "For $\theta$ remaining in a fixed set $S \in \mathscr{S}$." Then, so modified, either (PGL) or (PGM) will allow the derivation of properties of asymptotic normality and asymptotic sufficiency valid uniformly on the sets $S \in \mathscr{S}$. Of course, the construction of the sets $S_{\omega}$ and of the forms $K_{\omega}$ of Section 3 needs to be carried out in such a way as to reflect the desired uniformities. For instance, if one constructs sets $S_{\omega}$ using local coordinate systems $\eta_{\omega}$, care has to be taken that they be related to the relevant $\xi_{\theta}$ (of (PGM) (iii)) uniformly on each fixed $S \in \mathscr{S}$. The situation should be clear.

One of the most common families $\mathscr{S}$ used to assert uniform convergences is the family of compact subsets of $\Theta$. In the (PGL) case another favorite is the family of bounded subsets of $\Theta$ for a fixed norm $\|\cdot\|_{0}$.

The logic behind the use of such families is somewhat thin. However, very often a crude but trustworthy method of estimation will effectively reduce the most relevant part of the parameter space drastically.

Thus the "logic" goes as follows. A crude, "robust", confidence set will limit the relevant portion of the parameter space to a compact or to a bounded set. As long as the convergence properties hold uniformly on such a set, everything will be all right. This argument is far from being impeccable.

Under assumptions similar to the (PGL) conditions, but with uniformity on certain sets, certain computation procedures, which could not be used under (PGL) alone, become available. Suppose, for instance, that one has a fixed norm $\|\cdot\|_{0}$ on $\mathbb{R}^{k}$ and that for each fixed $\varepsilon>0$ and $b$ the sets $\{t ; q(t, \theta) \leq b\}$ are eventually contained in $\left\{t:\|t-\theta\|_{0}<\varepsilon\right\}$. Assume also that the (PGL) assumptions are replaced by assumptions with uniform convergence on $\|\cdot\|_{0}$ bounded sets. Alternatively one may assume that $\Theta$ is locally compact and that the desired convergences hold uniformly on compacts.

In such a case the LAN conditions use vectors $X_{\theta}$ and matrices $M_{\theta}$ to yield approximations of the logarithm of likelihood ratios. The usual differences $\Lambda(t, \theta)-t^{\prime} X_{\theta}+\frac{1}{2} t^{\prime} M_{\theta} t$ must then tend to zero in $P_{\theta}$ probability uniformly for $\theta$ in bounded subsets of $\Theta$, or alternatively, for $\theta$ in compact subsets of $\Theta$. This implies that to construct centerings $\hat{Z} \in \mathscr{M}_{1}(\Theta)$ or $\hat{T} \in \Theta$ one may use the expressions $t^{\prime} X_{\theta}+\frac{1}{2} t^{\prime} M_{\theta} t$ themselves instead of the $\Lambda(\theta+t, \theta)$. This is sometimes a convenience. When the $X_{\theta}$ are the vectors of first derivatives of $\Lambda(\theta+t, \theta)$, the construction of $\hat{T}$ resembles one application of the NewtonRaphson method of solution for the maximum likelihood equations.

Under (PGL) only, it is not always possible to use the $t^{\prime} X_{\theta}-\frac{1}{2} t^{\prime} M_{\theta} t$ instead of $\Lambda(\theta+t, \theta)$. Indeed the computation will be carried out around some point $\omega \in \Omega$ such that $q(\theta, \omega)$ remains bounded, for the true value $\theta$ of the parameter.

However, (PGL) does not insure that the relevant differences $\Lambda(\omega+t, \omega)-t^{\prime} X_{\omega}+ \frac{1}{2} t^{\prime} M_{\omega} t$ will tend to zero in probability.

Finally, note that even if $\Theta$ is fixed and subset of a fixed $\mathbb{R}^{k}$, the conditions used in Section 6 are more general than conditions of the LAN type or of the (PGM) type. For instance, they allow approximations by Gaussian experiments whose rank is bounded but larger than the dimension $k$ of $\mathbb{R}^{k}$. In such cases the centerings $\hat{Z}$ of Section 6 may be asymptotically sufficient but not replaceable by centerings $\widehat{T}$ with values in $\mathbb{R}^{k}$. We shall encounter such possibilities in Chapter 17 in connection with what is called second-order efficiency.

With respect to the uniformity considerations mentioned above, note that the statements of Section 6 cover any sets $S$, depending on $v$ or not, on which the conditions (A), (B), (D), (E), and (FF) are uniformly satisfied. Therefore the cases discussed above are included in the cases discussed in Section 6.

## 8. Reduction to the Gaussian Case by Small Distortions

In this section we consider again a net $\left\{\mathscr{E}_{v}\right\}$ of experiments $\mathscr{E}_{v}=\left\{P_{\theta, v} ; \theta \in \Theta_{v}\right\}$ and suppress the index $v$. The general framework used is intermediate between those of Section 6 and Section 7. It allows us to carry out the global approximation of $\mathscr{E}$ by an heteroscedastic Gaussian experiment mentioned at the end of Section 6. This is first done directly. However, extending an argument of A. Wald, one can also show that small distortions of the values of the estimates can be used to replace approximations of distributions in Prohorov or dual Lipschitz distance by approximations in $L_{1}$-norm.

One could carry out the argument in a more general setup. However, to retain some simplicity we have used a Euclidean framework with objects similar to the universal reference norms of Section 7.

We shall assume throughout that $\Theta$ may depend on $v$ but that it is a subset of a certain space $\mathbb{R}^{k}$ with $k$ independent of $v$. The space $\mathbb{R}^{k}$ is supposed to be provided with a Euclidean norm $t \mapsto\|t\|$ which depends on $v$.

As in Section 6, no assertions will be made for certain $\theta \in \Theta$, depending on the suppressed index $v$. Those particular $\theta$ for which results are stated will be called "good" and subjected to the requirements listed below. We shall use the function $q$ and the form $\Gamma$ of Section 6.
(1) If for $t \in \Theta$ one of the quantities $q(t, \theta)$ or $\|t-\theta\|$ remains bounded, so does the other.
(2) Let $\Theta(\theta, b)=\{t ; q(t, \theta) \leq b\}$ for a fixed $b$. Then given $\varepsilon>0$, fixed, there is a fixed $\delta>0$ such that, eventually, the relations $\mu \in \mathscr{M}_{0}[\Theta(\theta, b)],\|\mu\| \leq 1$ and $\left\|\int s \mu(d s)\right\|<\delta$ imply $|\Gamma(\mu)|<\varepsilon$.
(3) At $\theta$ the condition (G1) of Section 6 is satisfied.
(4) There are linear affine subspaces $F_{\theta} \subset \mathbb{R}^{k}$ such that if $b$ is fixed and if $U(\theta, b)=\left\{t ; t \in \mathbb{R}^{k},\|t-\theta\| \leq b\right\}$, then the Hausdorff distance (computed for $\|\cdot\|)$ between $\Theta \cap U(\theta, b)$ and $F_{\theta} \cap U(\theta, b)$ tends to zero.

The combination of (1) and (2) above implies immediately that one can work in the space $\mathbb{R}^{k}$ itself instead of in the space $\mathscr{M}_{1}(\Theta)$. It also implies that at the points $\theta$ in question, Condition (H) of Section 6 is satisfied. Thus, according to Lemma 5 of Section 6 one can construct suitable systems $\left\{S_{\omega}, K_{\omega}, \Lambda_{\omega}^{\prime}\right\}$ and, if auxiliary estimates $\hat{\theta}$ are available, carry out the construction of centerings $\hat{Z}$ or $\hat{T}=\int s \hat{Z}(d s)$ as in Sections 3 and 6.

Definition 1. Points $\theta \in \Theta$ will be called "good" if they satisfy the requirements (1) to (4) above, and if in addition the systems $\left\{\hat{\theta}, S_{\omega}, K_{\omega}, \Lambda_{\omega}^{\prime}\right\}$ satisfy all the requirements (A), (B), (D), (E), and (FF) of Section 6.

Sets $S \subset \Theta$ will be called uniformly good if all possible choices of points $\theta \in S$, depending on $v$, yield good points. In the present context Theorem 1 and 3 of Section 6 can be restated as follows.

Theorem 1. Let centerings $\hat{Z}$ be constructed by the method of Section 3 and let $\hat{T}=\int s \hat{Z}(d s)$. Then there are experiments $\mathscr{F}=\left\{P_{\theta}^{\prime} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ such that
(i) $\hat{T}$ is sufficient for $\mathscr{F}$.
(ii) $\mathscr{L}\left(\widehat{T} \mid P_{\theta}^{\prime}\right)=\mathscr{L}\left(\widehat{T} \mid P_{\theta}\right)$ for all $\theta$.
(iii) $\sup _{\theta \in S}\left\|P_{\theta}-P_{\theta}^{\prime}\right\| \rightarrow 0$ for all uniformly good sets $S$.
(iv) For all $\operatorname{good} \theta$, the variables $\|\hat{T}-\theta\|$ are eventually bounded in $P_{\theta}$-probability. Also there are quadratic forms $M_{\theta}$ on $\mathbb{R}^{k}$ such that if $q(s, \theta)$ remains bounded, then

$$
\Lambda(s, \theta)+\frac{1}{2}\left[M_{\theta}(\widehat{T}-s)-M_{\theta}(\widehat{T}-\theta)\right]
$$

tends to zero in $P_{\theta}$ and $P_{s}$ probability.
These statements imply a certain asymptotically Gaussian behavior for the centerings $\hat{T}$. This is also implied by the method of construction: If $\theta$ is good and if $t \in \Theta$ is such that $\|t-\theta\|$ remains bounded then, under $P_{\theta}$, the variables $\langle t-\theta, \hat{T}-\theta\rangle_{M_{\theta}}$ are asymptotically Gaussian $\mathscr{N}\left[0, M_{\theta}(t-\theta)\right]$. However, this does not say how the components of $\widehat{T}-\theta$ orthogonal to the tangent spaces $F_{\theta}$ will behave. Here is a supplementary bit of information.

Lemma 1. Let $\hat{T}=\int s \hat{Z}(d s)$ be as described. For the point $\theta$ and the norms $\|\cdot\|$, let $\phi_{\theta}$ be the orthogonal projections of $\mathbb{R}^{k}$ onto $F_{\theta}$. Then $\left\|\left(1-\phi_{\theta}\right)(\widehat{T}-\theta)\right\|$ tends to zero in $P_{\theta}$ probability for all good $\theta$.

Proof. According to Section 6, Theorem 1, the $L_{1}$-norms $\left\|\hat{Z}-\delta_{\theta}\right\|$ are eventually bounded in $P_{\theta}$ probability. Each value of $\hat{Z}$ is obtained from a $Z_{\omega}$ with values in a space $\mathscr{M}_{1}\left(S_{\omega}\right)$. With probability as large as one wishes, the set $S_{\omega}$ will be contained in a ball $U(\theta, b)=\{t ;\|t-\theta\| \leq b\}$ for some fixed $b$. By the tangency requirement, the distance $\sup _{s} \inf _{t}\left\{\|t-s\| ; t \in F_{\theta}, s \in S_{\omega}\right\}$ will then tend to zero. Thus inf $\left\{\left\|t-\int s \hat{Z}(d s)\right\| ; t \in \theta+F_{\theta}\right\}$ also tends to zero in probability. The result follows.

The statistics $\hat{T}$ take values in $\mathbb{R}^{k}$ but not necessarily in $\Theta$. We shall also use other statistics with values in the closure $\bar{\Theta}$ of $\Theta$. For each $t \in \mathbb{R}^{k}$ there are elements $s \in \bar{\Theta}$ such that $\|t-s\|=\inf \{\|t-\theta\| ; \theta \in \bar{\Theta}\}$. If there are several such
elements $s$ they form a compact subset of $\bar{\Theta}$ which may be totally ordered, for intance, by the lexicographical order of the coordinates of $\mathbb{R}^{k}$. One may then assign to each $t \in \mathbb{R}^{k}$ the first point $\psi(t) \in \bar{\Theta}$ such that the distance from $t$ to $\psi(t)$ is minimum. We shall call $\psi$ the "projection" of $\mathbb{R}^{k}$ into $\bar{\Theta}$, even though it does not have all the usual qualities of projections. Clearly $\psi$ is measurable. Now let $T^{*}=\psi(\hat{T})$. This is a random element with values in $\bar{\Theta}$.

Corollary. All the statements in Theorem 1 remain true if one replaces $\hat{T}$ by $T^{*}$.
Proof. The asymptotic sufficiency statement follows from Section 6, Theorem 3. The differences $M_{\theta}(\widehat{T}-s)-M_{\theta}(\widehat{T}-\theta)$ can be replace by $M_{\theta}\left(T^{*}-s\right)$ -$M_{\theta}\left(T^{*}-\theta\right)$ since $\left\|\hat{T}-T^{*}\right\|$ tends to zero in probability and since the forms $M_{\theta}$ are bounded on $\left(\mathbb{R}^{k},\|\cdot\|\right)$.

We shall now construct an heteroscedastic Gaussian experiment which will be used as approximation for $\mathscr{E}$ itself. First note that if $\theta$ is a good point and if $q(t, \theta)$ remains bounded, then $t$ is also a good point. Second, recall that the construction of $\hat{T}=\int s \hat{Z}(d s)$ used a set $\Omega$ and finite subsets $S_{\omega} \subset \Theta$. For each $\omega \in \Omega$ let $F_{\omega}$ be the affine subspace $F_{\omega}=\left\{\int s \mu(d s) ; \mu \in \mathscr{M}_{1}\left(S_{\omega}\right)\right\}$ spanned by $S_{\omega}$. Let $\phi_{\omega}$ be the orthogonal projection of $\mathbb{R}^{k}$ onto $F_{\omega}$. Consider $\phi_{\omega}(\widehat{T})$ and its distribution $\bar{Q}_{\omega}=\mathscr{L}\left[\phi_{\omega}(\widehat{T}) \mid P_{\omega}\right]$. Let $\mathscr{G}_{\omega}$ be the set of all Gaussian distributions centered at $\omega$ and carried by $F_{\omega}$.

Lemma 2. If $\theta$ is a good point and if $q(\theta, \omega)$ remains bounded, then, eventually, there are measures $\bar{G}_{\omega}$ such that

$$
\left\|\bar{G}_{\omega}-\bar{Q}_{\omega}\right\|_{D}=\inf \left\{\left\|G-\bar{Q}_{\omega}\right\|_{D} ; G \in \mathscr{G}_{\omega}\right\}
$$

for the dual Lipschitz norms induced by the reference norm $\|\cdot\|$.
Proof. We already know, from Theorem 1 above, that $\inf \left\{\left\|Q_{\omega}-G\right\|_{D}\right.$ : $\left.G \in \mathscr{G}_{\omega}\right\}$ tends to zero. Also, given a fixed $\varepsilon>0$ there is a fixed $b$ such that eventually $\bar{Q}_{\omega}\{\|\hat{T}-\theta\|>b\}<\varepsilon$. This will yield $G\{t ;\|t-\theta\|>2 b\}<2 \varepsilon$ for any $G \in \mathscr{G}_{\omega}$ sufficiently close to $\bar{Q}_{\omega}$. Thus, the infimum can be taken in a compact subset of $\mathscr{G}_{\omega}$ and the result follows.

This suggests assigning to each $\theta \in \Theta$ a Gaussian measure $\bar{G}_{\theta}$ on $\mathbb{R}^{k}$ as follows. Order the points of $\Omega$ and take for $\omega$ the first point of $\Omega$ closest to $\theta$. Let $\alpha(\omega)=\inf \left\{\left\|\bar{Q}_{\omega}-G\right\|_{D} ; G \in \mathscr{G}_{\omega}\right\}$. If the infimum is attained let $\bar{G}_{\omega}$ be a point in $\mathscr{G}_{\omega}$ attaining it. If the infimum is not attained but $\alpha(\omega)>0$, take a $\bar{G}_{\omega} \in \mathscr{G}_{\omega}$ with $\left\|\bar{Q}_{\omega}-\bar{G}_{\omega}\right\|_{D} \leq 2 \alpha(\omega)$. Finally, if the infimum is not attained and $\alpha(\omega)=0$, take an $\varepsilon>0$ and a $\bar{G}_{\omega} \in \mathscr{G}_{\omega}$ with $\left\|\bar{Q}_{\omega}-\bar{G}_{\omega}\right\|_{D}<\varepsilon$. (When the net $\{v\}$ is nonpathological one may make $\varepsilon$ depend on $v$ and tend to zero, but this is not really needed here.)

Now if $\omega$ is the first point of $\Omega$ closest to $\theta$, let $\bar{G}_{\theta}$ be $\bar{G}_{\omega}$ recentered at $\theta$. This yields a certain heteroscedastic experiment $\overline{\mathscr{H}}=\left\{\bar{G}_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$. However, note that for different values $\omega_{i}$ of $\omega$ the sets $F_{\omega_{i}}$ may well be disjoint, even if the $\omega_{i}$ are good and such that $q\left(\omega_{1}, \omega_{2}\right)$ remains bounded. Thus, the experiment $\overline{\mathscr{H}}$ does not reflect the properties of $\mathscr{E}$. To smooth it we shall replace the $\bar{G}_{\theta}$ by measures which are less concentrated.

One possibility is to modify each $\bar{G}_{\omega}$ as follows. Suppose that $\mathscr{L}\left(\bar{X}_{\omega}\right)=\bar{G}_{\omega}$. Let $U$ be a Gaussian vector such that $E U=0$ and $E\left(v^{\prime} U\right)^{2}=\|v\|^{2}$ with $U$ independent of $\bar{X}$. Replace $\bar{X}$ by $X_{\omega}=\bar{X}+a\left(1-\phi_{\omega}\right) U$ where $a$ is any fixed constant. This is not entirely satisfactory since now $\bar{X}-\psi(\bar{X})$ will not tend to zero. It would be more appealing to use an $X_{\omega}=\bar{X}+a(\omega)\left(1-\phi_{\omega}\right) U$, with $a(\omega)$ tending to zero as $v \rightarrow \infty$. This can readily be done in many cases. However, $a(\omega)$ must tend to zero slowly enough. For instance, for each fixed $b, a(\omega)$ should go to zero slower than the Hausdorff distance between $F_{s} \cap U(\omega, b)$ and $F_{t} \cap U(\omega, b)$ if $\|s-\omega\|$ and $\|t-\omega\|$ remain bounded. (Here $U(\omega, b)=\left\{t \in \mathbb{R}^{k} ;\|t-\omega\| \leq b\right\}$.) Under the same conditions on $s$ and $t$ the ratios $a(s)[a(t)]^{-1}$ should tend to unity. In special cases it is possible to find such numbers. However, in general, a construction appears complicated. Thus, we shall use a fixed $a>0$ and the distributions $G_{\omega}$ of $X_{\omega}=\bar{X}+a\left(1-\phi_{\omega}\right) U$. We shall take for $G_{\theta}$ the distribution of $X_{\omega}+\theta-\omega$ where $\omega$ is the first point of $\Omega$ closest to $\theta$.

Each $G_{\theta}$ defines a Gaussian shift experiment $\left\{G_{\theta, t} ; t \in \mathbb{R}^{k}\right\}$ if one takes for $G_{\theta, t}$ the distribution $G_{\theta}$ shifted to have its center at $t+\theta$. If the $G_{\theta, t} ; t \in \mathbb{R}^{k}$ are mutually absolutely continuous, then the logarithms of likelihood ratios have the form

$$
\log \frac{d G_{\theta, t}}{d G_{\theta, 0}}=t^{\prime} N_{\theta} X-\frac{1}{2} t^{\prime} N_{\theta} t
$$

where $X \in \mathbb{R}^{k}$ and where $N_{\theta}$ is some matrix on $\mathbb{R}^{k}$.
Lemma 3. Let $\left\{G_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ be constructed as described. Let $\theta$ be a good point of $\Theta$ and let $s \in \Theta$ be such that $\|s-\theta\|$ remains bounded.

Then
(i) if $\|v\|$ remains bounded $v^{\prime} N_{s} v-v^{\prime} N_{\theta} v$ tends to zero;
(ii) the difference $N_{\theta}^{-1} N_{s}-I$ where $I$ is the identity map tends to zero on $\mathbb{R}^{k}$ normed by $\|\cdot\|$.

Proof. Because of the particular construction used, one may as well assume that both $s$ and $\theta$ belong to $\Omega$. They were assigned affine spaces $F_{s}$ and $F_{\theta}$, respectively, with distributions $\bar{G}_{s}$ and $\bar{G}_{\theta}$ carried by these spaces. The conditions imposed on $\theta$ imply that at $\theta$ the LAN condition and the condition (S1) of Section 7 are satisfied. Thus the logarithms of likelihood ratios $\Lambda(s, \theta)$ are approximated by expressions of the type

$$
\left[\phi_{\theta}(s-\theta)\right]^{\prime} M_{\theta} \phi_{\theta}(\widehat{T}-\theta)-\frac{1}{2}\left[\phi_{\theta}(s-\theta)\right]^{\prime} M_{\theta}\left[\phi_{\theta}(s-\theta)\right]
$$

for certain matrices $M_{\theta}$ on $F_{\theta}-\theta$. It follows from the argument of Lemma 3, Section 7, that, restricted to $F_{\theta}-\theta$, the norms $v \mapsto\|v\|$ and $v \mapsto\left(v^{\prime} M_{\theta} v\right)^{1 / 2}$ are related. The matrix $M_{\theta}$ is an approximation to the inverse covariance matrix of $\bar{G}_{\theta}$. Thus, under the conditions imposed here the matrices $N_{\theta}$ will eventually exist. Also the norms $\|\cdot\|$ and $v \mapsto\left(v^{\prime} N_{\theta} v\right)^{1 / 2}$ will be related. The same applies to the point $s$. Therefore, to prove the results it is sufficient to show that if $\|v\|$ remains bounded then $v^{\prime} N_{s} v-v^{\prime} N_{\theta} v$ will tend to zero. This amounts to the
same thing as showing that for $\|v\|$ bounded the distributions $\mathscr{L}\left[v^{\prime} X_{\theta} \mid \theta\right]$ and $\mathscr{L}\left[v^{\prime} X_{s} \mid s\right]$ have a difference which tends to zero. However, this is immediate.

To state the next result, recall that for good points $\theta$, the logarithms of likelihood ratios $\Lambda(t, \theta)$ of $\mathscr{E}$ admit an approximation of the type $\Lambda(t, \theta) \sim -\frac{1}{2}\left\{M_{\theta}\left(T^{*}-t\right)-M_{\theta}\left(T^{*}-\theta\right)\right\}$, where $M_{\theta}$ is a quadratic form on $\mathbb{R}^{k}$. We shall now show that the heteroscedastic experiment admits a similar approximation.

Proposition 1. Let $\mathscr{H}$ be the experiment $\mathscr{H}=\left\{G_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$. Let $\mathscr{H}^{*}= \left\{H_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ where $H_{\theta}$ is the image of $G_{\theta}$ on $\bar{\Theta}$ by the projection $\psi$. Let $L$ be the logarithm of likelihood ratio for $\mathscr{H}^{*}$. Then, for every good $\theta$, and for $\|t-\theta\|$ bounded,
(i) The dual Lipschitz norms $\left\|\mathscr{L}\left(T^{*} \mid P_{\theta}\right)-H_{\theta}\right\|_{D}$ tend to zero.
(ii) The difference

$$
L(t, \theta)+\frac{1}{2}\left[M_{\theta}\left(X^{*}-t\right)-M_{\theta}\left(X^{*}-\theta\right)\right\}
$$

tend to zero in $H_{\theta}$ probability ( with $\mathscr{L}\left(X^{*}\right)=H_{\theta}$ ).
Proof. Statement (i) is immediate from the construction. To obtain statement (ii) consider first the logarithms $L^{\prime}(t, \theta)=\log \left(d G_{t} / d G_{\theta}\right)$. These can be written in the form

$$
\begin{aligned}
L^{\prime}(t, \theta)= & \frac{1}{2} \log \left(\frac{\operatorname{det} N_{t}}{\operatorname{det} N_{\theta}}\right)-\frac{1}{2}(X-t)^{\prime}\left(N_{t}-N_{\theta}\right)(X-t) \\
& +(t-\theta) N_{\theta}(X-\theta)-\frac{1}{2}(t-\theta)^{\prime} N_{\theta}(t-\theta)
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $N_{\theta}^{-1} N_{t}-I$ tends to zero, the term involving the ratio of determinants will tend to zero. Similarly the quadratic term $(X-t)^{\prime}\left(N_{t}-N_{\theta}\right)(X-t)$ tends to zero in probability. Thus $L^{\prime}(t, \theta)$ is approximated by $(t-\theta)^{\prime} N_{\theta}(X-\theta)- \frac{1}{2}(t-\theta)^{\prime} N_{\theta}(t-\theta)$. If $\phi_{\theta}$ is the projection on the space $F_{\theta}$, then

$$
(t-\theta)^{\prime} N_{\theta}(X-\theta)-\left[\phi_{\theta}(t)-\phi_{\theta}(\theta)\right]^{\prime} N_{\theta}\left[\phi_{\theta}(X)-\phi_{\theta}(\theta)\right]
$$

will tend to zero in probability. However, on the space $F_{\theta}-\theta$ the difference $u^{\prime} N_{\theta} u-M_{\theta}(u)$ tend to zero for all $u \in F_{\theta}$ such that $\|u\|$ remains bounded. Because of the tangency requirement $\phi_{\theta}(X)-\psi(X)$ tends to zero in probability and $\phi_{\theta}(\theta)-\theta \rightarrow 0$. Thus we can assert that

$$
L^{\prime}(t, \theta)+\frac{1}{2}\left\{M_{\theta}[\psi(X)-t]-M_{\theta}[\psi(X)-\theta]\right\}
$$

tends to zero in probability for $G_{\theta}$ and for $G_{t}$.
Consider a fixed number $b$ and the set $S=\{t ; t \in \Theta,\|t-\theta\| \leq b\}$. Since either $X$ or $X^{*}=\psi(X)$ are distinguished statistics for $\mathscr{H}_{S}=\left\{G_{s} ; s \in S\right\}$ and since the differences $\left\|\mathscr{L}\left(T^{*} \mid P_{s}\right)-\mathscr{L}\left(X^{*} \mid G_{s}\right)\right\|_{D}$ tend to zero, one can also assert that $\Delta\left(\mathscr{H}_{S}, \mathscr{E}_{S}\right) \rightarrow 0$.

The experiment $\mathscr{H}_{S}^{*}=\left\{H_{s} ; s \in S\right\}$, where $H_{s}$ is the projection of $G_{s}$ on $\bar{\Theta}$ by $\psi$, is weaker than $\mathscr{H}_{S}$. However, since $X^{*}=\psi(X)$ is distinguished for $\mathscr{H}_{S}$,
the difference $\Delta\left(\mathscr{H}_{S}, \mathscr{H}_{S}^{*}\right)$ tends to zero. The conclusion concerning $L(t, \theta)= \log \left(d H_{t} / d H_{\theta}\right)$ follows, for instance, from Chapter 4, Proposition 5.

An immediate consequence is as follows.
Corollary. Let $S$ be uniformly good. Then $\Delta\left(\mathscr{E}_{S}, \mathscr{H}_{S}\right)$ and $\Delta\left(\mathscr{E}_{S}, \mathscr{H}_{S}^{*}\right)$ tend to zero.
Proof. In the present case $q(\widehat{T}, \theta)$ is bounded in probability. Also, for the experiments $\mathscr{H}_{S}$ or $\mathscr{H}_{S}^{*}$, the variables $M_{\theta}\left(X^{*}-\theta\right)$ are bounded in $G_{\theta}$ probability. The result follows then from Chapter 5, Proposition 8.

According to this corollary, for any uniformly good set $S$ and for $Q_{\theta}= \mathscr{L}\left(T^{*} \mid P_{\theta}\right)$ there are randomizations, say $W$, such that $\sup _{s}\left\{\left\|W H_{s}-Q_{s}\right\| ; s \in S\right\}$ tend to zero. There are other randomizations such that $\sup _{s}\left\{\left\|H_{s}-W^{\prime} Q_{s}\right\|\right.$; $s \in S\}$ tends to zero.

We shall now show that these randomizations $W$ and $W^{\prime}$ can be constructed in such a way that they displace the points of $\bar{\Theta}$ by amounts which tend to zero as $v \rightarrow \infty$. To proceed, we need a preliminary lemma. Let $C$ be a compact metric space and let $B(x, \varepsilon) \subset C$ be the closed ball of radius $\varepsilon$ centered at $x$.

Lemma 4. Let $\varepsilon$ and $\alpha$ be two positive numbers and let $\mu$ and $v$ be two finite positive measures on $C$. There is a Markov kernel $W$, mapping $C$ into itself such that
(i) $W\left\{[B(x, \varepsilon)]^{c} \mid x\right\}=0$ for all $x \in C$,
(ii) $\|v-v \wedge W \mu\| \leq \alpha$, if and only if
(iii) $v(S) \leq \mu\left(S^{\varepsilon}\right)+\alpha$ for every closed subset $S$ of $C$.

Proof. If (i) holds, the mass of $W \mu$ contained in the set $S$ arises only from the mass of $\mu$ carried by $S^{\varepsilon}$. Thus (iii) is obviously necessary for the validity of (i) and (ii). To prove the converse note first that

$$
\|v-v \wedge W \mu\|=\sup _{\gamma \in \Phi}[\langle\gamma, v\rangle-\langle\gamma, W \mu\rangle]
$$

where $\Phi$ is the set of continuous functions $\gamma$ such that $0 \leq \gamma \leq 1$. Let $\mathscr{K}$ be the set of all kernels which satisfy condition (i) of the lemma. Their equivalence classes form a weakly closed convex subset of the space of all transitions from the $L$-space of $\mu$ to the space of Radon measures on $C$. Thus an application of the minimax theorem yields the equality

$$
\inf _{W \in \mathscr{K}} \sup _{\gamma \in \Phi}\langle\gamma, v-W \mu\rangle=\sup _{\gamma \in \Phi} \inf _{W \in \mathscr{K}}\langle\gamma, v-W \mu\rangle .
$$

Now take a particular $\gamma \in \Phi$ and a sequence of numbers $\beta_{i}$ such that $0=\beta_{0}< \beta_{1}<\cdots<\beta_{i}<\beta_{i+1}<\cdots \leq \beta_{m}=1$. Let $S_{j}$ be the set $S_{j}=\left\{x ; \gamma(x) \geq \beta_{j}\right\}$. Let $u$ and $v$ be the functions

$$
\begin{aligned}
& u=\sum_{i=1}^{m}\left(\beta_{i}-\beta_{i-1}\right) I_{S_{i}} \\
& v=\sum_{i=1}^{m}\left(\beta_{i}-\beta_{i-1}\right) I_{S_{i}^{e}} .
\end{aligned}
$$

If condition (iii) holds, one has $\langle u, v\rangle \leq\langle v, \mu\rangle+\alpha$. However, $\langle v, \mu\rangle$ itself is not larger than $\langle u, W \mu\rangle$ for a kernel $W$ constructible as follows.

Start with the last set $S_{m}$. Map all of $S_{m}^{\varepsilon}$ into $S_{m}$ taking for the image of a point $x \in S_{m}^{\varepsilon}$ a point of $S_{m}$ closest to it. This can be done in a measurable fashion. Continue downwards. When arriving at the set $S_{j}$ there will be only a certain part of $S_{j}^{\varepsilon}$ which has not been used before. Map this leftover into $S_{j}$ as was done for $S_{m}$. Note that the inequality $j<k$ implies $S_{k} \subset S_{j}$. Thus, the map so constructed always sends all the elements of $S_{j}^{\varepsilon}$ into $S_{j}$. The image of $\mu$ by this map is a certain measure, say $\mu^{\prime}$. The integral $\left\langle u, \mu^{\prime}\right\rangle=\sum\left(\beta_{j}-\beta_{j-1}\right) \mu^{\prime}\left(S_{j}\right)$ is at least equal to $\sum\left(\beta_{j}-\beta_{j-1}\right) \mu\left(S_{j}^{\varepsilon}\right)=\langle v, \mu\rangle$. Thus there is a kernel $W \in \mathscr{K}$ such that $\langle u, v\rangle \leq\langle u, W \mu\rangle+\alpha$. Now let the mesh $\varepsilon^{\prime}=\sup _{i}\left(\beta_{i}-\beta_{i-1}\right)$ of the division tend to zero. The function $u$ tends to $\gamma$ uniformly. Thus, for any $\alpha^{\prime}>\alpha$ there will be a kernel $W \in \mathscr{K}$ such that $\langle\gamma, v\rangle \leq\langle\gamma, W \mu\rangle+\alpha^{\prime}$. The result follows.

Note that the lemma involves only the difference $v-v \wedge W \mu$. However,

$$
\|v-W \mu\|=2\|v-v \wedge W \mu\|+\|\mu\|-\|v\| \leq 2\|v-v \wedge W \mu\|+\|\mu-v\|_{D}
$$

Thus the bound in (ii) of the lemma will be sufficient for our purposes. Using this lemma, we shall proceed to the construction of Markov kernels which represent small distortions of the underlying space $\bar{\Theta} \subset \mathbb{R}^{k}$ and allow approximate passage in $L_{1}$-norm from the $H_{\theta}$ to the $Q_{\theta}$ and conversely. For the passage from $\left\{Q_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ to $\left\{H_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ the construction is as follows.

Take an orthonormal basis of $\mathbb{R}^{k}$ for the reference norms $\|\cdot\|$. It defines a unit cube. Pave the entire space $\mathbb{R}^{k}$ with translates of this unit cube. Let the cubes of the pavement be $C_{j}^{\prime} ; j=1,2, \ldots$. Let $C_{j}$ be the cube which has the same center as $C_{j}^{\prime}$ but twice the diameter. More precisely if $C_{j}^{\prime}$ is centered at $t_{j}$ then $C_{j}-t_{j}=2\left(C_{j}^{\prime}-t_{j}\right)$. Using the cubes $C_{j}^{\prime}$ and $C_{j}$ one can find a partition of unity given by functions $\gamma_{j}$ such that (1) the support of $\gamma_{j}$ is entirely contained in $C_{j}$, (2) $\gamma_{j} \geq 0$ and $\sum \gamma_{j}=1$, (3) there is a fixed number $a$ such that $\left|\gamma_{j}(x)-\gamma_{j}(y)\right| \leq a\|x-y\|$ for all $j$ and all $x, y$ elements of $\mathbb{R}^{k}$.

If the cube $C_{j}$ intersects $\Theta$, select some point $\tau_{j} \in \Theta \cap C_{j}$. If $C_{j}$ does not intersect $\Theta$ but if $\Theta \cap C_{j}$ is not empty, take some $\varepsilon \in(0,1)$ and let $\tau_{j} \in \Theta$ be some point at distance less than $\varepsilon$ from $\Theta \cap C_{j}$. For simplicity of notation, let $Q_{j}$ be the measure $Q_{\theta}$ with $\theta=\tau_{j}$. Define $H_{j}$ similarly. If a measure $\mu$ has density $g$ with respect to a measure $m$, write $\mu=g \cdot m$. Using this notation, let $\lambda_{j}=\gamma_{j} \cdot Q_{j}$ and $\mu_{j}=\gamma_{j} \cdot H_{j}$.

Let $\alpha_{j}$ be the minimum of the numbers $\alpha$ such that there is a Markov kernel $W$ mapping $C_{j}$ into $C_{j}$ in such a way that (1) $\left\|\mu_{j}-W \lambda_{j}\right\| \leq 2 \alpha$, (2) if $B(x, \alpha)$ is the ball $B(x, \alpha)=\left\{y ; y \in \bar{\Theta} \cap C_{j},\|y-x\| \leq \alpha\right\}$ then for all $x \in C_{j}$ one has $W[B(x, \alpha) \mid x]=1$. Here, taking the cubes $C_{i}$ closed, all transitions admit Markov kernel representations. Thus there is a certain kernel $W_{j}$ with $\left\|\mu_{j}-W_{j} \lambda_{j}\right\| \leq 2 \alpha_{j}$ and $W_{j}\left[B\left(x, \alpha_{j}\right) \mid x\right] \equiv 1$. One can piece the $W_{j}$ together obtaining a certain kernel $W$ which operates on a measure $m$ by the formula $W m=\sum_{j} W_{j}\left(\gamma_{j} \cdot m\right)$.



Theorem 2. Construct $Q_{\theta}, H_{\theta}$, and the kernel $W$ as explained above. Then, for every fixed number $b$ and every good $\theta$ there are numbers $\alpha$ tending to zero such that the quantities
(i) $\sup _{t}\left\{\left\|H_{t}-W Q_{t}\right\| ;\|t-\theta\| \leq b\right\}$
(ii) $\sup _{x}\left\{W\left\{[B(x, \alpha)]^{c} \mid x\right\} ;\|x-\theta\| \leq b\right\}$
tend to zero. The same result holds, for different kernels, if the roles of $H_{\theta}$ and $Q_{\theta}$ are reversed.

Proof. Given a fixed $\varepsilon>0$ and $b$ there is some fixed number $c$ such that, eventually,

$$
\sup _{t}\left\{Q_{t}\left[\left\|T^{*}-\theta\right\| \geq c\right] ;\|t-\theta\| \geq b\right\}<\varepsilon
$$

and

$$
\sup _{t}\left\{H_{t}\left[\left\|X^{*}-\theta\right\| \geq c\right] ;\|t-\theta\| \leq b\right\}<\varepsilon
$$

Thus to study the effect of the kernel $W$ it will be sufficient to study its effect in cubes $C_{j}$ which contain points of $\mathbb{R}^{k}$ at distance at most $c$ from $\theta$. The number of such cubes remains bounded. Thus, it will be enough to consider one cube at a time. Let it be called $C=C_{j}$ and let $\gamma=\gamma_{j}$ be the element of the Lipschitzian partition of unity corresponding to it. For the cube $C$ we have selected
certain measures $Q=Q_{j}$ and $H=H_{j}$, yielding $\lambda=\gamma \cdot Q$ and $\mu=\gamma \cdot H$ on $C$. By Proposition 1 the dual Lipschitz distance $\|H-Q\|_{D}$ tends to zero and so does the equivalent Prohorov distance between $H$ and $Q$. Thus the Prohorov distance between $\lambda$ and $\mu$ tends to zero. According to Lemma 4, the corresponding kernel $W_{j}$ is associated with a number $\alpha_{j}$ which tends to zero and is such that, for all $x \in C$, the probabilities $W_{j}\left[B\left(x, \alpha_{j}\right) \mid x\right]$ tend to unity. This yields the second statement of the theorem. To obtain the first, take a fixed $\varepsilon>0$. Then there is a finite set $S \subset\{s:\|s-\theta\| \leq b\}$ such that if $\|t-\theta\| \leq b$ then $\inf \left\{\left\|Q_{s}-Q_{t}\right\| ; s \in S\right\}<\varepsilon$. The cardinality of $n$ remains bounded. Enlarging $S$ if necessary, one can also assume that inf $\left\{\left\|H_{s}-H_{t}\right\| ; s \in S\right\}<\varepsilon$.

According to Proposition 1, for each $s \in S$, one can find a function $g_{s}$ such that (i) $\left\|Q_{s}-g_{s} \cdot Q\right\|+\left\|H_{s}-g_{s} \cdot H\right\|<\varepsilon$ and (ii) $\left|g_{s}(x)-g_{s}(y)\right| \leq c_{1}\|x-y\|$ for some fixed number $c_{1}$, (iii) $0 \leq g_{s}(x) \leq c_{1}$.

The inequality just written for measures $Q_{s}$ and $H_{s}$ implies also $\left\|\lambda_{s}-g_{s} \cdot \lambda\right\|+ \left\|\mu_{s}-g_{s} \cdot \mu\right\|<\varepsilon$ for $\lambda_{s}=\gamma \cdot Q_{s}$ and $\mu_{s}=\gamma \cdot H_{s}$. Now consider the action of $W_{j}$ on $g_{s} \cdot \lambda$. The difference $g_{s} \cdot \mu-W_{j} g_{s} \cdot \lambda$ is equal to $g_{s} \cdot\left[\mu-W_{j} \lambda\right]+\left[g_{s} \cdot W_{j} \lambda\right]-\left[W_{j} g_{s} \lambda\right]$. By construction $\left\|g_{s} \cdot\left[\mu-W_{j} \lambda\right]\right\| \leq c_{1}\left\|\mu-W_{j} \lambda\right\|$ tends to zero. To evaluate the remaining term, take some function $u, 0 \leq u \leq 1$ and consider the integrals $J_{1}=\left\langle u, g_{s} \cdot W_{j} \lambda\right\rangle$ and $J_{2}=\left\langle u, W_{j} g_{s} \lambda\right\rangle$. Omitting the indices $s$ and $j$ for simplicity, these integrals can be written in the form

$$
\begin{aligned}
J_{1} & =\iint u(y) g(y) W(d y \mid x) \lambda(d x) \\
J_{2} & =\iint u(y) g(x) W(d y \mid x) \lambda(d x)
\end{aligned}
$$

It follows that

$$
\left|J_{1}-J_{2}\right| \leq\|\lambda\| \sup _{x, y}\left\{|g(y)-g(x)| ;\|x-y\|<\alpha_{j}\right\}
$$

The desired result follows, since $\alpha_{j} \rightarrow 0$.
Remark. For part of the argument just carried out, we have used the fact that the likelihood ratios $d H_{s} / d H$ and $d Q_{s} / d H$ are very close to each other. This was taken from Proposition 1 which gives the approximate form of the likelihood ratios. However, one can modify the argument to make it applicable to situations other than the Gaussian case.

Here we are working in a compact cube $C$ with two families of measures $\left\{\lambda_{s}\right\}$ and $\left\{\mu_{s}\right\},\|s-\theta\| \leq b$. These families are tail-precompact in the sense of Section 6. Thus the problem can be reduced to families $\left\{\lambda_{s} ; s \in S\right\}$ and $\left\{\mu_{s} ;\right. s \in S\}$ for sets $S$ of bounded cardinality.

For the corresponding families $\left\{Q_{s}\right\}$ and $\left\{H_{s}\right\}$, the random element in $\mathbb{R}^{k}$ is a distinguished asymptotically sufficient statistic in the sense of Chapter 7, Section 3. The approximability of likelihood ratios by functions which satisfy Lipschitz conditions follows then from Chapter 7, Section 3, Theorem 1. The approximate equality of $d Q_{s} / d Q$ and $d H_{s} / d H$ can be obtained from Chapter

4, Proposition 5 if one knows for instance that the distance between the binary experiments ( $Q, Q_{s}$ ) and ( $H, H_{s}$ ) tends to zero. In summary, it is not the asymptotic normality which is essential. With appropriate compactness, the distinguished character of the statistic, convergence to zero of dual Lipschitz distances, and convergence of binary experiments will yield the same result. Carried out in this manner, the argument is similar to an argument used by W. Müller to obtain evaluations of the distance between two experiments.

As mentioned previously, the argument used here is closely related to an argument of Wald who passed from measures analogous to our $Q_{\theta}$ to Gaussian measures by set transformations. Wald's purpose was to prove a statement such as $\Delta\left(\mathscr{H}^{*}, \mathscr{E}\right) \rightarrow 0$. This can be done in a simpler manner.

When $\Theta$ is the entire space $\mathbb{R}^{k}$, or when the tangent spaces $F_{\theta}$ are all of $\mathbb{R}^{k}$, the measures $H_{\theta}$ are actually Gaussian, nonsingular. One can then pass from them to the $Q_{\theta}$, as in Theorem 2, but using Markov kernels which are nonrandomized, that is, given by functions.

The measures $H_{\theta}$ are projections of the Gaussian measures $G_{\theta}$ on the set $\bar{\Theta}$. Thus they are not exactly Gaussian. One could recover approximate versions of the $G_{\theta}$ from the $H_{\theta}$ by appropriate randomization. Also in those cases where the variables $X=\bar{X}_{\omega}+\alpha(\omega)\left(1-\phi_{\omega}\right) U$ are constructed with coefficient $\alpha(\omega)$ which tend to zero, Theorem 2 could be stated with $G_{\theta}$ instead of $H_{\theta}$. The proof needs only trivial modifications.

Theorem 2 says in particular that for any uniformly good sets $S$ the distance $\Delta\left(\mathscr{H}_{S}^{*}, \mathscr{E}_{S}\right)$ will tend to zero. However, it says somewhat more. Suppose for instance that one is contemplating a testing problem and that for the projection $\left\{H_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ of the heteroscedastic Gaussian experiment one would use test functions $\beta$. Most of the test functions used for the Gaussian case are "almost smooth" in the sense that there are equi-Lipschitzian functions $\beta^{\prime}$ such that $\int\left|\beta-\beta^{\prime}\right| d H_{\theta}$ is small for all good $\theta$. Then one can use $\beta^{\prime}$ or $\beta$ for the experiment $\left\{Q_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ and be assured that for all good points the optimality properties of $\beta$ for $\left\{H_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ will become asymptotic optimality properties for $\left\{Q_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$. Several examples will be given in the next section.

In the preceding results we have kept $\Theta$ as an index set for the experiments $\mathscr{E}$ and their various approximations. This is necessary for the global theory. However, as is visible in Theorem 2 and in the next section, once the estimates $T^{*}$ are obtained, a large part of the description of their properties uses only local arguments. For these it is often more convenient to change the parameter space and work with Gaussian experiments linearly indexed by a Euclidean space. This can be achieved using the technique described in Chapter 10, Section 3. To proceed with ease, let us first extend the domains of definition of the experiments to the closure $\bar{\Theta}$ of $\Theta$. When the net $\{v\}$ is nonpathological, this can be done by the interpolation method used in Chapter 10, Section 3.

It can slso be done by a different procedure. Consider for instance the family $\left\{Q_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ and a point $t \in \bar{\Theta} \backslash \Theta$. If $\varepsilon>0$, let $V(\varepsilon)=\left\{Q_{\theta} ;\|t-\theta\|<\varepsilon\right\}$ and let $\overline{V(\varepsilon)}$ be its vague closure on the space $\mathbb{R}^{k}$ compactified by adjunction of a point at infinity. Then $\bigcap_{\varepsilon} \overline{V(\varepsilon)}$ contains at least one point. We shall take for $Q_{t}$
any element of $\bigcap_{\varepsilon} \overline{V(\varepsilon)}$. Since the experiments $\mathscr{E}^{*}=\left\{Q_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ are obviously regular in the sense of Chapter 10, Section 3 at any good $\theta$, the particular choice of $Q_{t} \in \bigcap_{\varepsilon} \overline{V(\varepsilon)}$ is asymptotically immaterial.

Now consider a good point $\theta$ and the linear affine tangent space $F_{\theta}$ assigned to it. We already have a projection map $\psi$ which sends $F_{\theta}$ into $\bar{\Theta}$. Thus we can transform the experiment $\mathscr{E}^{*}$ into an experiment $\mathscr{E}^{\prime \prime}=\left\{Q_{s}^{\prime \prime} ; s \in F_{\theta}\right\}$ indexed by $F_{\theta}$, taking for $Q_{s}^{\prime \prime}$ the measure $Q_{\psi(s)}$. It may be more convenient to consider instead $\mathscr{E}^{\prime}=\left\{Q_{s}^{\prime} ; s \in F_{\theta}\right\}$ where $Q_{s}^{\prime}$ is the distribution $\mathscr{L}\left\{\phi_{\theta}\left(T^{*}\right) \mid \psi(s)\right\}$ of the projection of $T^{*}$ on $F_{\theta}$.

Similarly, consider an experiment indexed by $F_{\theta}$. For instance, take the experiment $\mathscr{G}_{\theta}=\left\{G_{\theta, t} ; t \in F_{\theta}\right\}$ where

$$
\log \frac{d G_{\theta, t}}{d G}=(t-\theta)^{\prime} N_{\theta} X-\frac{1}{2}(t-\theta)^{\prime} N_{\theta}(t-\theta)
$$

as for Lemma 3. Let $\phi_{\theta}$ be the orthogonal projection on $F_{\theta}$ and let $G_{\theta, t}^{\prime}$ be the image of $G_{\theta, t}$ on $F_{\theta}$ by this projection. This gives a Gaussian experiment $\mathscr{G}_{\theta}^{\prime}$ indexed by $F_{\theta}$ and given by Gaussian measures on $F_{\theta}$. One can reindex it by $\bar{\Theta}$ obtaining an experiment $\overline{\mathscr{H}}_{\theta}=\left\{\bar{H}_{\theta, s} ; s \in \bar{\Theta}\right\}$ by taking for $\bar{H}_{\theta, s}$ the measure $G_{\theta, t}^{\prime}$, where $t=\phi_{\theta}(s)$. The following result is then quite obvious.

Lemma 5. Let $\theta$ be a good point of $\Theta$ and let $b$ denote a fixed number. Let $A=\{t ; t \in \boldsymbol{\Theta},\|t-\theta\| \leq b\}$ and let $B=\left\{t ; t \in F_{\theta},\|t-\theta\| \leq b\right\}$. Then
$\Delta\left(\mathscr{E}_{B}^{\prime}, \mathscr{G}_{\theta, B}^{\prime}\right)$ and $\Delta\left(\mathscr{E}_{A}, \overline{\mathscr{H}}_{\theta, A}\right)$ both tend to zero.

## 9. The Standard Tests and Confidence Sets

In this section we review briefly the standard methods of construction of tests and confidence sets under the conditions used in Section 6 or under the conditions described in Section 8. The general prescription is to use the statistics $T^{*}$ of Section 6, Proposition 2, or the corresponding $T^{*}$ of Section 8 and treat them as if they were Gaussian with (locally) constant covariance matrices. The optimal tests used in the Gaussian case are usually very smooth. This insures good asymptotic performance at all the "good" points of $\Theta$.

Tests are fine if that is what one really wants to do. However, they are not very informative. Confidence "balls" or "cylinders" yield more information. To obtain asymptotic equivalents of the standard Gaussian confidence sets is very easy. These sets can in addition be used to provide tests. If testing $\Theta_{0} \subset \Theta$ against an alternative $\Theta_{1} \subset \Theta$, one rejects $\Theta_{i}$ if the confidence set does not intersect $\Theta_{i}$. (This may loose some power. It may also point out the need for further thinking if, as will happen, both $\Theta_{i}$ are rejected.)

Since the asymptotic results are obtained by simple translation from the Gaussian ones, we shall consider first the Gaussian shift case with an experiment $\mathscr{G}=\left\{G_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ where $\Theta$ is an entire space $\mathbb{R}^{m}$ and where $G_{\theta}$ is the

Gaussian measure centered at $\theta$ with covariance matrix equal to the identity of $\mathbb{R}^{m}$. Let $X$ be a random variable with values in $\mathbb{R}^{m}$ and distribution $G_{\theta}$.

The standard "isotropic" confidence balls are given by regions $\{\theta: \mid X- \left.\left.\theta\right|^{2} \leq c^{2}\right\}$ where $|x|$ is the norm of $x$ in $\mathbb{R}^{m}$ and where $c^{2}$ is a cutoff point such that $\operatorname{Prob}\left[\chi_{m}^{2} \leq c^{2}\right]=1-\alpha$. Here $\chi_{m}^{2}$ is a central $\chi^{2}$ with $m$ degrees of freedom. When some parameters are considered nuisance parameters, one uses, instead of balls, confidence cylinders. For instance, suppose that $L$ is a linear subspace of $\mathbb{R}^{m}$ embodying the directions of the nuisance parameters. Let $(I-\Pi)$ be the orthogonal projection of $\mathbb{R}^{m}$ onto $L$. One uses as confidence cylinders sets of the form $\left\{\theta:|\Pi(X-\theta)|^{2} \leq c^{2}\right\}$ where $\operatorname{Prob}\left[\chi_{k}^{2} \leq c^{2}\right]=1-\alpha$ for a $\chi^{2}$ with a number of degrees of freedom $k$ equal to the dimension of $\Pi \mathbb{R}^{m}$.

These are the confidence sets used in the analysis of variance (linear models) and associated there with the name of Scheffé. Here the situation is simpler since the covariance system is entirely known. In the analysis of variance one usually assumes only that it is known up to a multiplicative constant. In cases where the statistician is interested in privileged directions in the space $\mathbb{R}^{m}$ one may use instead of balls or circular cylinders the rhomboids associated with Tukey's name. For reasons of space economy, we shall not discuss these here. The discussion would parallel the usual one of Scheffé's text.

Still in the exactly Gaussian shift case the standard tests are obtainable by use of the likelihood ratio tests. To test the hypothesis that $\theta \in \Theta_{0}$ against the alternative $\theta \in \Theta_{1}$ one accepts $\Theta_{0}$ if the quantity

$$
\inf \left\{|X-\theta|^{2} ; \theta \in \Theta_{1}\right\}-\inf \left\{|X-\theta|^{2} ; \theta \in \Theta_{0}\right\}
$$

exceeds a suitable calculated constant. For instance, in testing $\Theta_{0}$ against $\Theta_{1}=\Theta \backslash \Theta_{0}$, one accepts $\Theta_{0}$ if inf $\left\{|X-\theta|^{2} ; \theta \in \Theta_{0}\right\} \leq c^{2}$ for a suitable constant $c^{2}$. For $\Theta_{0}$ equal to a linear subspace of $\mathbb{R}^{m}$ and for $\Theta_{1}=\Theta \backslash \Theta_{0}$ this is the same thing as accepting $\Theta_{0}$ if confidence cylinders for the parameters in the orthogonal complement of $\Theta_{0}$ intersect $\Theta_{0}$ itself.

For arbitrary $\Theta_{0}$ and $\Theta_{1}$, the performance of the above described testing procedure may be difficult to evaluate. The use of confidence balls or cylinders still permits computation of suitable cutoff constants and use of a test procedure which is often reasonable, even though some power may be lost.

As can be seen, the Gaussian constructions depend on the use of the variable $X$ with expectation $\theta$. It depends also on the use of a quadratic form $x \mapsto|x|^{2}$ which provides a norm on $\mathbb{R}^{m}$. If the experiment $\mathscr{G}=\left\{G_{\theta} ; \theta \in \mathbb{R}^{m}\right\}$ was modified so that $G_{\theta}$ is now Gaussian with expectation $\theta$ and covariance matrix $C$, the appropriate quadratic form would be $x^{\prime} C^{-1} x$. This can easily be checked, for instance, by reducing the present case to the previous one by the transformation $x \mapsto C^{-1 / 2} x$.

To be specific, the quadratic forms are those which occur in the logarithms of likelihood ratios. If they happen to be singular, this means that the experiment provides no information in certain directions.

At this point it should be clear how to translate the Gaussian prescriptions to make them applicable to the asymptotically Gaussian case: One provides oneself with centering variables $\hat{Z}$ (or $T^{*}$ ) which will play the same role as $X$ above. One uses, instead of $|X-\theta|^{2}$, quadratic expressions constructed from the approximations of the logarithms of likelihood ratios. For instance, in the cases covered by the assumptions of Section 6, one may use the quadratic forms $\tilde{\Gamma}\left(\hat{Z}-\delta_{\theta}\right)$ of Section 6, Lemma 2. It is not always appropriate to use the quadratics $\hat{K}\left(\hat{Z}-\delta_{\theta}\right)$ of Section 5, Theorem 2 or the quadratics $\Gamma\left(\hat{Z}-\delta_{\theta}\right)$ of Section 6, Theorem 1, for reasons that should be apparent from the discussion at the beginning of Section 6. We shall return to this matter later on.

In the Gaussian case, for arbitrary sets $\Theta_{0}$ and $\Theta_{1}$, little is known about the behavior of the likelihood ratio test. For special cases, several optimality properties have been established. Here we shall follow the work of A. Wald who considered the Gaussian case, and, under assumptions different from those used here, made the translation to the asymptotically Gaussian case. A first, simple, but crucial, remark is incorporated in the next lemma.

Consider the case where $\Theta=\mathbb{R}^{m}$ and where, for each given $\theta$, the distribution of the observation $X$ is the distribution of center $\theta$ and covariance matrix identity on $\mathbb{R}^{m}$. Let $\Theta_{0}$ be a linear subspace of $\mathbb{R}^{m}$. Then each $\theta \in \Theta$ can be written in the form $\theta=s+t$, where $s \in \Theta_{0}$ and where $t$ is orthogonal to $\Theta_{0}$. Similarly $X=Y+Z$ with a vector $Y$ which takes values in $\Theta_{0}$ and where $Z$ takes values in the space $\Theta_{0}^{\perp}$ orthogonal to $\Theta_{0}$.

For any $s \in \Theta_{0}$ and any number $b>0$ let $S(s, b)$ be the sphere $\{x: x=y+z$,
$\left.z \in \Theta_{0}^{\perp},|z|=b\right\}$. If $\Theta_{0}^{\perp}$ has $m_{1}$ dimensions, this is a translate of the sphere of $m_{1}-1$ dimensions $S(b)=\left\{z: z \in \Theta_{0}^{\perp},|z|=b\right\}$ of $\Theta_{0}^{\perp}$. On $S(b)$ there is a probability measure $\mu_{b}$, invariant by the orthogonal transformations of $\Theta_{0}^{\perp}$. It is the distribution of $b|Z|^{-1} Z$ for a variable with $\mathscr{N}(0, I)$ distributions on $\Theta_{0}^{\perp}$. Let $\mu_{b, s}$ be the translate of $\mu_{b}$ to $S(s, b)$.

Let $\phi$ be any test function defined on $\mathbb{R}^{m}$. For each $\theta \in \mathbb{R}^{m}$, let $\beta(\phi, \theta)= E\left(\phi \mid G_{\theta}\right)$ be the power of $\phi$. For each $s \in \Theta_{0}$ and each $b>0$, let $A(\phi, s, b)= \int \beta(\phi, \theta) \mu_{b, s}(d \theta)=\int \beta(\phi, s+t) \mu_{b}(d t)$. This will be called the average power of $\phi$ on the sphere $S(s, b)$. Let $\phi_{0}$ be the test which rejects $\Theta_{0}$ if $\inf \{|X-s|$; $\left.s \in \Theta_{0}\right\}>c$ and let $\alpha=E\left(\phi_{0} \mid G_{s}\right), s \in \Theta_{0}$ be its level.

Lemma 1. For any test $\phi$ such that $E\left(\phi \mid G_{s}\right) \leq \alpha$ for all $s \in \Theta_{0}$, one has

$$
A(\phi, s, b) \leq A\left(\phi_{0}, s, b\right)
$$

for all $b$ and all $s \in \Theta_{0}$.
Proof. Fix a value $s \in \Theta_{0}$ and a $t \in \Theta_{0}^{\perp}$. The likelihood ratio $d G_{s+t} / d G_{s}$ is equal to $\exp \left\{t^{\prime} X-\frac{1}{2}|t|^{2}\right\}=\exp \left\{t^{\prime} Z-\frac{1}{2}|t|^{2}\right\}$ for the decomposition $X=Y+Z$, with $Y$ taking values in $\Theta_{0}$ and $Z$ in $\Theta_{0}^{\perp}$. Thus the average power may be written

$$
A(\phi, s, b)=\iint G_{s}(d y) G_{0}(d z) \phi(y+z)\left\{\int \exp \left\{t^{\prime} z-\frac{1}{2}|t|^{2}\right\} \mu_{b}(d t)\right\}
$$

The function

$$
\int \exp \left\{t^{\prime} z-\frac{1}{2}|t|^{2}\right\} \mu_{b}(d t)
$$

depends only on $|z|$, because of the rotation invariance of $\mu_{b}$. Also, changing $z$ into $-z$, it may be put in the form

$$
\left.e^{-(1 / 2) b^{2}} \int \operatorname{Ch}\left\{\left[t^{\prime} z /|z|\right]|z|\right]\right\} \mu_{b}(d t)
$$

and is therefore an increasing function of $|z|$.
Thus

$$
A(\phi, s, b)=\iint \phi(y+z) f_{b}[|z|] G_{s}(d y) G_{0}(d z)
$$

for a certain increasing function $f_{b}[|z|]$ of $|z|$. The corresponding requirement $E\left(\phi \mid G_{s}\right) \leq \alpha$ can be written

$$
\iint \phi(y+z) G_{s}(d y) G_{0}(d z) \leq \alpha
$$

Thus, according to the Neyman-Pearson lemma, a function $\phi$ which maximizes $A(\phi, s, b)$ subject to $E\left(\phi \mid G_{s}\right) \leq \alpha$ must be of the form $\phi(y+z)=1$ if $|z|>c_{1}, \phi(y+z)=0$ if $|z|<c_{1}$, and $\phi(y+z)=\gamma, 0 \leq \gamma \leq 1$, if $|z|=c_{1}$. This
implies the result as stated, since $A(\phi, s, b)$ will be maximized by taking $c_{1}$ as small as possible and therefore such that $E\left(\phi \mid G_{s}\right)=E\left(\phi_{0} \mid G_{s}\right)=\alpha$.

Corollary 1. Among tests such that $E\left(\phi \mid G_{s}\right) \leq \alpha, s \in \Theta_{0}$, and such that the power $E\left(\phi \mid G_{\theta}\right)$ is constant over each of the surfaces $S(s, b)$, the test $\phi_{0}$ maximizes that constant power.

In these statements, the spheres $S(s, b)$ play a very special role. The following statement, also due to Wald, seems to avoid this special role.

Consider the family $\Phi$ of tests $\phi$ such that $E\left(\phi \mid G_{s}\right) \leq \alpha$ for $s \in \Theta_{0}$. For each $\theta \in \Theta \backslash \Theta_{0}$ let $\bar{\beta}(\theta)=\sup \left\{E\left(\phi \mid G_{\theta}\right) ; \phi \in \Phi\right\}$. This is the maximum power achievable for testing $\Theta_{0}$ against $\theta$ under the restriction that the level of $\phi$ be at most $\alpha$. The "regret" of a test $\phi$ is

$$
r(\phi)=\sup \left\{\bar{\beta}(\theta)-\beta(\phi, \theta) ; \theta \in \Theta \backslash \Theta_{0}\right\}
$$

A test is most stringent at level $\alpha$ if its regret is minimum.
Corollary 2. The test $\phi_{0}$ of Lemma 1 is most stringent at level $\alpha$.
Proof. Let $\theta=s+t, s \in \Theta_{0}, t \in \Theta_{0}^{\perp}$. One can easily verify that $\bar{\beta}(\theta)$ is the maximum achievable power for a test of the simple hypothesis $\{s\}$ against the simple alternative $\{s+t\}$. The power $\bar{\beta}(\theta)$ of such a level $\alpha$ test depends only on $|t|$. Similarly, the difference $\bar{\beta}(\theta)-\beta\left(\phi_{0}, \theta\right)$ depends only on $|t|$. Now suppose that $r(\phi)<r\left(\phi_{0}\right)-2 \varepsilon$. Then, there is some $b$ such that $\sup \{\bar{\beta}(s+t)- \left.\beta\left(\phi_{0}, s+t\right) ;|t|=b\right\} \geq r\left(\phi_{0}\right)-\varepsilon$ for all $s$. Also

$$
\bar{\beta}(s+t)-\beta(\phi, s+t) \leq r\left(\phi_{0}\right)-2 \varepsilon \leq \bar{\beta}(s+t)-\beta\left(\phi_{0}, s+t\right)-\varepsilon
$$

for all $s$ and all $|t|=b$. However, integrating this with respect to the measure $\mu_{b}$ of Lemma 1 yields a contradiction. Hence the result.

In fact we shall need a slight modification of Corollary 2 as follows.
Take number $b$ and consider the family $\Phi(b)$ of tests $\phi$ such that $E\left(\phi \mid G_{s}\right) \leq \alpha+b^{-1}$ for all $s \in \Theta_{0}$ such that $|s| \leq b$. Let $\bar{\beta}(\theta, b)=\sup \left\{E\left(\phi \mid G_{\theta}\right) ; \phi \in \Phi(b)\right\}$ and let $r(\phi, b)$ denote the corresponding regret

$$
r(\phi, b)=\sup \left\{\bar{\beta}(\theta, b)-\beta(\phi, \theta) ; \theta \in \Theta \backslash \Theta_{0},|\theta| \leq b\right\}
$$

Corollary 3. Given any $\varepsilon>0$, there are numbers $b$ such that

$$
r\left(\phi_{0}, b\right) \leq r(\phi, b)+\varepsilon
$$

for every test $\phi \in \Phi(b)$
This follows from Corollary 2 and from the compactness of the space of tests. The test $\phi_{0}$ which accepts $\Theta_{0}$ if $\inf \left\{|X-s| ; s \in \Theta_{0}\right\}<c$ is admissible, by A . Birnbaum's theorem (Chapter 10, Section 6). It is nonrandomized and well defined by its power function since the experiment $\left\{G_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ is complete.

Of course there are very many other admissible nonrandomized tests. Take any measurable acceptance region $A$ such that the sections $A y=\{z ; y+z \in Z\}, y \in \Theta_{0}, z \in \Theta_{0}^{\perp}$ are convex and contain a ball $\{y+z ;|z| \leq \varepsilon\}$ for some $\varepsilon>0$.

Then $A$ is admissible. This follows from an extension of Chapter 10, Section 6, Lemma 1.

There is another situation often considered in the literature, where one takes for $\Theta_{0}$ a linear subspace of dimension $m-1$ in $\Theta=\mathbb{R}^{m}$ and where one wishes to test $\Theta_{0}$ against one of the half spaces $\Theta_{0}$ determines. This half space can be written $\Theta_{1}=\left\{y+\alpha u ; y \in \Theta_{0}, \alpha>0\right\}$ where $u$ is a unit vector of $\Theta$ orthogonal to $\Theta_{0}$. The standard test for the Gaussian situation in this case is to accept $\Theta_{0}$ if the inner product $u^{\prime} X$ satisfies $u^{\prime} X \leq a$ for a suitably chosen number $a$, cutoff point in the upper tail of the Gaussian distributon. Here if

$$
\alpha=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2 \pi}} \int_{a}^{\infty} \exp \left\{-\frac{1}{2} x^{2}\right\} d x
$$

the test in question is uniformly most powerful among all tests of level $\alpha$. This is very easily checked.

Statements relative to tests can be converted into statements relative to confidence sets by the classical method of $\mathbf{J}$. Neyman. Consider, for instance, a linear subspace $\Theta_{0}$ of $\mathbb{R}^{m}$ and the orthogonal projection $\Pi$ of $\mathbb{R}^{m}$ on the orthogonal complement $\Theta_{0}^{\perp}$ of $\Theta_{0}$. To test the hypothesis that $\Pi \theta=\xi$, one uses the acceptance region $\left\{x \in \mathbb{R}^{m} ;|\Pi x-\xi| \leq c\right\}$. This yields the confidence ball $\left\{\xi ; \xi \in \Theta_{0}^{\perp},|\Pi x-\xi| \leq c\right\}$ for the projection $\xi=\Pi \theta$ of $\theta$. For a test of level $\alpha$ such confidence balls have probability of coverage $1-\alpha$. The test procedure for $\Theta_{0}$ is equivalent to the procedure which accepts the hypothesis $\Theta_{0}$ if the confidence ball contains the origin of $\Theta_{0}^{\perp}$.

The optimality statements relative to the test criteria are directly convertible into optimality statements for the confidence sets. Consider, for instance, confidence sets $C(X)$ such that $P_{\theta}[\theta \in C(X)] \geq 1-\alpha$ for all $\theta \in \Theta$. Consider also $P_{\theta}[t \in C(X)]$ for $t \neq \theta$. Then the balls $\{\theta ;|X-\theta| \leq c\}$ minimize expressions such as $\sup _{t}\left\{P_{\theta}[t \in C(X)] ;|t-\theta|=b\right\}$, and the corresponding "regret" functions. These balls also enjoy another optimality property: If $\lambda$ is the Lebesgue measure of $\mathbb{R}^{m}$ the confidence balls $\{\theta ;|X-\theta| \leq c\}$ with coverage probability $1-\alpha$ minimize the maximum expected "volume" $\sup _{\theta} E_{\theta}\{\lambda[C(X)]\}$. This can be seen as follows. Give $\theta$ a Gaussian prior distribution with inverse covariance matrix $\beta^{2} I$ on $\mathbb{R}^{m}$. The conditional distribution of $\theta$ given $X=x$ is then a Gaussian distribution centered at $\left(1+\beta^{2}\right)^{-1} x$ with inverse covariance matrix $\left(1+\beta^{2}\right) I$. The marginal distribution of $X$ is a certain centered Gaussian distribution $H$.

Consider the problem of finding a function $\phi, 0 \leq \phi \leq 1$, defined on $\mathbb{R}^{m} \times \mathbb{R}^{m}$, which minimizes $E_{H} \int \phi(X, t) d t$ subject to the condition that $E \phi(X, \theta) \geq 1-\alpha$. Using the Neyman-Pearson lemma, one sees that there is some constant $c$ such that $\phi(x, \theta)=1$ if $\left(1+\beta^{2}\right)\left|\theta-\left(x / 1+\beta^{2}\right)\right|^{2}<c^{2}$ and $\phi(x, \theta)=0$ if $\left|\theta-\left(x / 1+\beta^{2}\right)\right|$ satisfies the reverse inequality. Here $c^{2}$ is the cutoff point such that $P\left[\chi_{m}^{2} \geq c^{2}\right]=\alpha$. Also, for every $x$, the conditional expected volume of the set $\left\{\theta:\left(1+\beta^{2}\right)\left|\theta-\left(x / 1+\beta^{2}\right)\right|^{2} \leq c^{2}\right\}$ is of the form $K\left[c^{2}\left(1+\beta^{2}\right)^{-1}\right]^{m / 2}$ for a certain coefficient $K$ which depends only on the dimension $m$. This is equal
to $\int \phi(x, t) d t$. Thus $E_{H} \int \phi(X, t) d t$ has for minimum value $K\left[c^{2}\left(1+\beta^{2}\right)^{-1}\right]^{m / 2}$ under the sole restriction that $E \phi(X, \theta) \geq 1-\alpha$. Letting $\beta$ tend to zero gives the minimax result that $\sup _{\theta} E \lambda[C(X)] \geq K c^{m}$. This is achieved by the confidence balls described previously. Note, however, that Joshi has shown that the confidence sets in question are not admissible.

Having obtained results which refer to an exactly Gaussian experiment, it remains to translate them for approximately Gaussian ones. To do so, we shall return to the conditions used in Section 6. Let us recall briefly what these conditions are. The construction of centerings $\hat{Z}$ uses an auxiliary estimate $\hat{\theta}$ with values in a subset $\Omega \subset \Theta$, pairs ( $S_{\omega}, K_{\omega}$ ), and approximate logarithms of likelihood ratios $\Lambda_{\omega}^{\prime}$. The assertions of Section 6 refer only to certain points $\theta \in \Theta$ called favorable points. If $\theta$ is such a point and if $q(\omega, \theta)$ remains bounded, Condition (B) requires that $S_{\omega}$ has a bounded diameter (for $q$ ), that the $\Lambda_{\omega}^{\prime}$ are indeed approximations for the logarithms of likelihood ratios attached to the experiment $\mathscr{E}$.
