Conditions (D) and (E) require that $\hat{\theta}$ converges at the appropriate speed and that the sets $S_{\omega}$ and $\Omega(\theta, b)=\{\omega: \omega \in \Omega, q(\omega, \theta) \leq b\}$ have bounded cardinalities. Condition ( FF ) requires that the pairs ( $S_{\omega}, K_{\omega}$ ) be free and that, on their domain, the quadratic forms $K_{\omega}$ be close approximations of the form $\Gamma$.

Points $\theta$ at which all these requirements and the Gaussian approximability requirement (G1) hold were called favorable. Section 6 also contains further requirements. One of them is that when $q(\omega, \theta)+q(s, \theta)$ remains bounded then $s$ is in the span of $\left(S_{\omega}, \Gamma\right)$.

This was called requirement (A). Another requirement, called (H), says that around $\theta$, the set $\Theta$ becomes dense in the Hilbert spaces $\mathscr{M}_{1}[\Theta(\theta, b)]$ normed through quadratic forms $\Gamma_{\theta}$. These forms, constructed before the statement of Lemma 2, Section 6, are positive on the entire space $\mathscr{M}_{0}(\Theta)$. Locally they resemble the form $\Gamma$. Section 6 also uses estimates $\tilde{\Gamma}$ of the forms $\Gamma_{\theta}$.

For each point $\theta$, take an $\omega \in \Omega$ among those closest to $\theta$ and consider the space $\mathscr{M}_{1}\left(S_{\omega}\right)$ of the corresponding set $S_{\omega}$. Let $F_{\theta}=\delta_{\theta}-\delta_{\omega}+\mathscr{M}_{1}\left(S_{\omega}\right)$ and let $\phi_{\theta}$ be the projection of $\mathscr{M}_{1}(\Theta)$ orthogonally with respect to $\Gamma_{\theta}$ on the space $F_{\theta}$.

If $\theta$ is a favorable point there will eventually exist a uniquely defined Gaussian measure $G_{\theta}$ carried by $F_{\theta}$ and centered at $\delta_{\theta}$ such that if $G_{\theta, \mu}$ is $G_{\theta}$ shifted by a $\mu \in \mathscr{M}_{0}\left(S_{\omega}\right)$ then

$$
\log \frac{d G_{\theta, \mu}}{d G_{\theta}}+\frac{1}{2} \Gamma_{\theta}(\mu)
$$

is linear in $\mu$. Let $X$ be a random vector with distribution $G_{\theta}$.
Lemma 2. Let $\theta$ be a favorable point at which the accessibility requirement ( $\mathbf{A}$ ) is satisfied. Let $\Theta_{i} ; i=0,1$ be two subsets of $\Theta$ such that $\theta \in \Theta_{0}$. Let $U$ be the statistic

$$
U=\inf _{t}\left\{\widetilde{\Gamma}\left(\hat{Z}-\delta_{t}\right) ; t \in \Theta_{1}\right\}-\inf _{t}\left\{\widetilde{\Gamma}\left(\hat{Z}-\delta_{t}\right) ; t \in \Theta_{0}\right\} .
$$

Let $V$ be a statistic defined by

$$
V=\inf _{t}\left\{\Gamma_{\theta}\left(X-\delta_{t}\right) ; t \in \Theta_{1}\right\}-\inf _{t}\left\{\Gamma_{\theta}\left(X-\delta_{t}\right) ; t \in \Theta_{0}\right\}
$$

for a random vector $X$ distributed according to the measure $G_{\theta}$ defined above. Then
(i) If $\inf \left\{q(t, \theta) ; t \in \Theta_{1}\right\}$ tends to infinity, $U$ tends to infinity in $P_{\theta}$ probability.
(ii) If inf $\left\{q(t, \theta) ; t \in \Theta_{1}\right\}$ stays bounded, then the dual Lipschitz distance between $\mathscr{L}\left(U \mid P_{\theta}\right)$ and $\mathscr{L}\left(V \mid G_{\theta}\right)$ tends to zero.

Proof. According to Section 6, Theorem 1 and Proposition 2, the variables $\tilde{\Gamma}\left(\hat{Z}-\delta_{\theta}\right)$ are asymptotically distributed as $\chi^{2}$ variables with a bounded number of degrees of freedom. Therefore, since $\theta \in \Theta_{0}$, the term $\inf _{t}\{\tilde{\Gamma}(\hat{Z}- \left.\left.\delta_{t}\right) ; t \in \Theta_{0}\right\}$ is bounded in probability. For any $t \in \Theta_{1}$ one may write $[\widetilde{\Gamma}(\widehat{Z}- \left.\left.\delta_{t}\right)\right]^{1 / 2} \geq\left[\widetilde{\Gamma}\left(\delta_{\theta}-\delta_{t}\right)\right]^{1 / 2}-\left[\widetilde{\Gamma}\left(\hat{Z}-\delta_{\theta}\right)\right]^{1 / 2}$. The construction of Section 6 insures that $\tilde{\Gamma}\left(\hat{Z}-\delta_{t}\right)$ cannot stay bounded in probability unless $q(\theta, t)$ does. This yields the first assertion.

For the second assertion, take a fixed $\varepsilon>0$ and a fixed number $b$ so large that $\widetilde{\Gamma}\left(\hat{Z}-\delta_{\theta}\right)<b$ except for cases of probability at most $\varepsilon$. Suppose also that the ball $\Theta_{1}(\theta, b)=\left\{t ; t \in \Theta_{1}, q^{2}(t, \theta) \leq b\right\}$ is not empty. Under the restriction $\tilde{\Gamma}\left(\hat{Z}-\delta_{\theta}\right)<b$ the two infima of the definition of $U$ are unchanged if one replaces the set $\Theta_{i}$ by the sets $\Theta_{i}(\theta, 4 b)$. We may also assume that $b$ is so large that $S_{\omega} \subset \Theta(\theta, b)$.

Consider now the projection $\phi_{\theta} \hat{Z}=Z^{*}$ of $\hat{Z}$ on the subspace $F_{\theta}$. The accessibility requirement implies, as in Section 6, that $\tilde{\Gamma}\left(Z^{*}-\hat{Z}\right) \rightarrow 0$. Also $\widetilde{\Gamma}-\Gamma_{\theta} \rightarrow 0$ in probability and the dual Lipschitz distance between $\mathscr{L}\left(Z^{*} \mid P_{\theta}\right)$ and $\mathscr{L}\left(X \mid G_{\theta}\right)$ tends to zero. This implies the second statement of the lemma.

The assertions of Lemma 2 can be restated in an informal way as follows. If $\theta \in \Theta_{0} \cup \Theta_{1}$ is a favorable point satisfying the accessibility requirement (A), then the test statistic $U$ behaves asymptotically in the same way as the likelihood ratio test for the corresponding Gaussian problem. It follows then from general considerations that whenever the likelihood ratio test in the limit experiment exhibits optimality properties, the tests based on the statistics $U$ will be asymptotically optimal in an analogous manner. Using the results recalled above for the exact Gaussian case, we shall presently give an example of such a situation. For this we need to single out points of $\Theta$ at which the sets $\Theta_{0}$ or $\Theta_{1}$ have the general appearance of finite dimensional spaces. This was the object of the condition called (H) in Section 6. Condition (H) involves sets $S \subset \Theta_{i}$ such that the pairs ( $S, \Gamma_{\theta}$ ) are free.

Suppose that $\theta$ is a favorable point of $\Theta$, that Condition (A) holds at $\theta$, and that both $\Theta_{i}$ satisfy ( H ) at $\theta$. Suppose also that $\theta \in \Theta_{0} \subset \Theta_{1}$. Condition ( H ) will involve pairs ( $S^{0}, \Gamma_{\theta}$ ) for $\Theta_{0}$ and pairs ( $S^{1}, \Gamma_{\theta}$ ) for $\Theta_{1}$. The dimension of the Hilbert spaces $\mathscr{M}_{0}\left(S^{i}, \Gamma_{\theta}\right)$ will be some bounded numbers $k_{i}$. We shall



let $k=k_{1}-k_{0}$.
Now assuming $\Theta_{0} \subset \Theta_{1}$ consider the statistics

$$
U=\inf _{t}\left\{\tilde{\Gamma}\left(\hat{Z}-\delta_{t}\right) ; t \in \Theta_{0}\right\}-\inf _{t}\left\{\tilde{\Gamma}\left(\hat{Z}-\delta_{t}\right) ; t \in \Theta_{1}\right\}
$$

Construct also the statistics $T^{*}$ of Section 6, Proposition 2 and statistics

$$
U^{*}=\inf _{t}\left\{q^{2}\left(T^{*}-t\right) ; t \in \Theta_{0}\right\}-\inf _{t}\left\{q^{2}\left(T^{*}-t\right) ; t \in \Theta_{1}\right\}
$$

It is a consequence of Lemma 2 that if $\theta \in \Theta_{1}$ is a favorable point satisfying (A) and if $\inf _{t}\left\{q(\theta, t) ; t \in \Theta_{0}\right\}$ tends to infinity, then both $U$ and $U^{*}$ tend to infinity in probability. Thus it will be enough to look at the behavior of $U$ and $U^{*}$ near the hypothesis $\Theta_{0}$.

Proposition 1. Let $\theta \in \Theta_{0} \subset \Theta_{1}$ be a favorable point at which the accessibility condition (A) holds. Assume also that both $\Theta_{i}$ satisfy Condition (H) at $\theta$. Then
(i) $U-U^{*}$ tends to zero in probability.
(ii) If $q(\theta, t)$ remains bounded, the difference $\mathscr{L}\left(U \mid P_{t}\right)-\mathscr{L}\left(\chi_{k}^{2}(\lambda)\right)$ tends to zero in dual Lipschitz distance for a $\chi^{2}$ with $k=k_{1}-k_{0}$ degrees of freedom and noncentrality parameter $\lambda=\inf _{s}\left\{q^{2}(t, s) ; s \in \Theta_{0}\right\}$.

Proof. This is an immediate consequence of Lemma 2 of the present section and of Proposition 2, Section 6.

In the present framework, the dimension $k=k_{1}-k_{0}$ may vary with $v$. However for any fixed $\alpha \in(0,1)$ one can find numbers $c$, depending on $k$, such that $\operatorname{Pr}\left\{\chi_{k}^{2} \geq c\right\}=\alpha$. Define a test function $\phi_{0}$ by $\phi_{0}=0$ if $U^{*} \leq c$ and $\phi_{0}=1$ if $U^{*}>c$.

Given a fixed number $b$, let $\psi(b)$ be the family of test functions $\phi$ such that $\int \phi d P_{t} \leq \alpha+b^{-1}$ for all $t \in \Theta_{0}$ such that $q(t, \theta) \leq b$. Define a regret function $r(\phi, b)$ as for the Corollary 3 of Lemma 1. The following statement gives an optimality property for $\phi_{0}$.

Proposition 2. Let the conditions of Proposition 1 be satisfied at $\theta$. Then, for each fixed $b$,
(i) the test $\phi_{0}$ eventually belongs to $\psi(b)$;
(ii) for any fixed $\varepsilon>0$, there is a $b$ such that eventually $r\left(\phi_{0}, b\right) \leq r(\phi, b)+\varepsilon$, for all $\phi \in \psi(b)$.

Proof. Consider the affine spaces $F_{\theta}$ provided by Condition (H). To each $\mu \in F_{\theta}$ assign a $\tau(\mu) \in \Theta$ so that $\Gamma_{\theta}[\mu-\tau(\mu)] \rightarrow 0$ if $\Gamma_{\theta}\left(\mu-\delta_{\theta}\right)$ remains bounded. For each fixed $b$, this yields an experiment, say $\mathscr{F}_{\theta, b}=\left\{P_{\mu}^{\prime} ; \mu \in F_{\theta}, \Gamma_{\theta}\left(\mu-\delta_{\theta}\right) \leq b\right\}$. There is a Gaussian shift experiment $\mathscr{G}_{\theta}$, linearly indexed by $F_{\theta}$, such that, for all fixed $b$, the distances $\Delta\left(\mathscr{G}_{\theta, b}, \mathscr{F}_{\theta, b}\right)$ tend to zero. Corollary 3 of Lemma 1 is applicable to $\mathscr{G}_{\theta}$, yielding a suitable fixed $b$. It follows that the statement of Proposition 2 would apply to the experiments $\mathscr{F}_{\theta, b}$. Since for any $t \in \Theta(\theta, b)$, $\Gamma_{\theta}\left\{\delta_{t}-\tau\left[\phi_{\theta}\left(\delta_{t}\right)\right]\right\}$ tends to zero, the result applies also to $\mathscr{E}$, hence the result by Proposition 1.

There are analogues of Proposition 2 phrased in terms of average or constant power over surfaces, as in Lemma 1 and its corollaries. The situation should be clear.

The testing problem described here could be handled in a different manner. As mentioned above, Condition ( H ) involves pairs ( $S^{0}, \Gamma_{\theta}$ ) where $S^{0}$ is a finite subset of $\Theta_{0}$. One could then use the space $\mathscr{M}_{0}\left(S^{0}\right)$ as the space of nuisance directions and apply Theorem 2 of Section 6. Under the assumptions made here, the conclusions of Proposition 1 and Proposition 2 are stronger than those obtainable by the "nuisance direction" method. However the latter approach has definite merits in cases where $\Theta_{0}$ behaves locally as a Hilbert space with unbounded dimension with an orthogonal complement of finite, bounded, dimension.

Instead of tests one may also consider estimation problems for the parameter $\theta$. It is an easy consequence of the results of Chapter 10 that, around points $\theta$ which are favorable and satisfy ( H ), the estimates $T^{*}$ will be asymptotically minimax for a large variety of loss functions. However point estimates are usually not a most satisfactory answer to a specific practical problem and one may wish to use confidence balls, or cylinders.

For the case of confidence balls, consider favorable points $\theta$ satisfying Condition ( H ). To such points we have assigned a space $F_{\theta}=\delta_{\theta}-\delta_{\omega}+\mathscr{M}_{1}\left(S_{\omega}\right)$ for an $\omega$ nearest to $\theta$. Let $k(\theta)$ be the linear dimension of $F_{\theta}$ and let $c(k)$ be such that $\operatorname{Pr}\left[\chi_{k}^{2} \geq c(k)\right]=\alpha$ for a fixed $\alpha \in(0,1)$. If $k(\theta)$ is independent of $\theta$ one can use confidence balls of the type $\left\{t ; \tilde{\Gamma}\left(\hat{Z}-\delta_{t}\right) \leq c(k)\right\}$ or $\left\{t ; q^{2}(t\right.$, $\left.\left.T^{*}\right) \leq c(k)\right\}$. According to the results of Section 6, for any favorable $\theta$ satisfying (H) the probability $P_{\theta}\left\{q^{2}\left(\theta, T^{*}\right) \leq c[k(\theta)]\right\}$ will tend to $1-\alpha$. If $k(\theta)$ varies as $\theta$ does, the situation may be more complex. If it is indeed important to maintain a coverage probability $1-\alpha$, one may want to use balls of the type $B^{\prime}\left(T^{*}\right)=\left\{t ; q^{2}\left(t, T^{*}\right) \leq c\left[k\left(T^{*}\right)\right]\right\}$. Here again, for any favorable $\theta$ satisfying (H) the probability $P_{\theta}\left[\theta \in B^{\prime}\left(T^{*}\right)\right]$ will tend to $1-\alpha$.

As to optimality properties, let us consider, for simplicity, the case where the dimension $k(\theta)$ does not depend on $\theta$. Then each $F_{\theta}$ has a certain dimension $k(\theta)=k$ and one can take confidence balls $B\left(T^{*}\right)=\left\{t ; q^{2}\left(t, T^{*}\right) \leq c(k)\right\}$. Consider also the balls $\bar{B}=\left\{\mu ; \mu \in \mathscr{M}_{1}(\Theta), \tilde{\Gamma}(\hat{Z}-\mu) \leq c(k)\right\}$.

Let $\theta$ be a favorable point satisfying (H). Then it can easily be verified that the Hausdorff distance, computed for $\Gamma_{\theta}$, between $B\left(T^{*}\right)$ and $\bar{B}$, tends to zero in $P_{\theta}$ probability. The coverage probabilities $P_{\theta}\left[\theta \in B\left(T^{*}\right)\right]$ and $P_{\theta}\left(\delta_{\theta} \in \bar{B}\right)$ both tend to $1-\alpha$.

In the space $F_{\theta}$ let $\lambda$ be the Lebesgue measure, normalized so that, for instance, the ball $\left\{\mu ; \Gamma_{\theta}\left(\mu-\delta_{\theta}\right) \leq 1\right\}$ has measure unity. Let $\phi_{\theta}$ be the orthogonal projection onto $F_{\theta}$. Consider arbitrary measurable confidence sets, say $V$, defined on $\mathscr{E}$ and their projections $V_{\theta}=\phi_{\theta}(Q)$ on $F_{\theta}$. Finally let $\bar{B}_{\theta}=\bar{B} \cap F_{\theta}$.

Proposition 3. Let $\theta$ be a favorable point satisfying ( H ). Assume that if $q(t, \theta)$ remains bounded, then $\liminf _{\nu} P_{t}(t \in V) \leq 1-\alpha$. Then $\liminf E_{\theta} \lambda\left(V_{\theta}\right)- \lambda\left(\bar{B}_{\theta}\right) \geq 0$.

Proof. For a favorable $\theta$ satisfying Condition (H) one can reindex the experiments $\left\{P_{t} ; t \in \Theta(\theta, b)\right\}$ by using as parameter space the sets $F_{\theta}$ as indicated in the proof of Proposition 2. For the corresponding approximating Gaussian
experiments, the assertion concerning the Lebesgue measure of confidence sets has been proved earlier in this section. The result relative to $\mathscr{E}$ follows then as in the proof of Proposition 2. Alternatively one may use the minimax result of Chapter 7, Section 4, Theorem 1.

The confidence balls $B\left(T^{*}\right)$ or $\bar{B}$ possess other optimality properties, obtainable by the usual translation of the properties of the corresponding tests. We shall leave them to the care of the reader.

Even in a situation such as the one considered for Proposition 1, one may wish to use confidence balls instead of test criteria. For instance, one would reject $\Theta_{0}$ if $B\left(T^{*}\right)$ does not intersect $\Theta_{0}$. Here some caution may be necessary since when the dimension $k_{0}$ of Proposition 1 is large the number of degrees of freedom $k(\theta)$ of the $\chi^{2}$ approximation to $q^{2}\left(T^{*}, \theta\right)$ may be substantially larger than the $k_{1}-k_{0}$ of Proposition 1. Thus, for the same level, some power is lost. It can be recovered by using a two-step procedure: If $B\left(T^{*}\right)$ does not intersect $\Theta_{0}$, one rejects $\Theta_{0}$ at once. If, however, $B\left(T^{*}\right)$ intersects $\Theta_{0}$, one carries out the test procedure as in Proposition 2. Such a procedure does not affect the level of the test and it provides supplementary information whenever $\Theta_{0}$ is rejected.

The reader will note that in the preceding arguments we have always used the quadratic forms $\Gamma_{\theta}$ or $\widetilde{\Gamma}$ introduced for Lemma 2 in Section 6. Alternately, on $\Theta$ itself, we have used the nonrandom function $q$. When the centerings $\hat{Z}$ are obtained from a system ( $S_{\omega}, K_{\omega}$ ) where $K_{\omega}$ is determined by a midpoint method, or a similar procedure, it is very tempting to use $\hat{K}=K_{\hat{\theta}}$ instead of $\widetilde{\Gamma}$. This cannot be done unless the domain of definition of the $K_{\omega}$ is substantial so that at least expressions of the kind $\hat{K}\left(\hat{Z}-\delta_{t}\right), t \in \Theta$ have a meaning. In the Euclidean framework described in Section 7 describing $K_{\omega}$ on $\mathscr{M}_{0}\left(S_{\omega}\right)$ automatically determines it on the entire $\mathscr{M}_{0}(\Theta)$ space. This is also true in the framework of Section 8 when the tangent sets are the entire space. If they are not the entire space, one can still extend the relevant $K_{\omega}$ to the entire space $\mathscr{M}_{0}(\Theta)$, using a technique analogous to the one used to construct the $N_{\theta}$ of Section 8, Lemma 3.

In any event, assuming that the $K_{\omega}$ are definable on the entire space $\mathscr{M}_{0}(\Theta)$, care should be taken to make them positive, otherwise the difficulties mentioned in Section 6 may be bothersome. For instance, it could happen that $\Gamma_{\theta}\left(\hat{Z}-\delta_{\theta}\right)$ remains bounded in probability, that $q(s, \theta) \rightarrow \infty$, but that $\hat{K}\left(\hat{Z}-\delta_{s}\right)$ tends to zero.

To obtain a result involving such forms, let us return to Condition ( M ) and Theorem 2 of Section 5. Assume that forms $\hat{K}$ have been obtained as described there.

Lemma 3. Assume that Condition ( M ) is satisfied and that the forms $\hat{K}$ are positive and defined on the entire space $\mathscr{M}_{0}(\Theta)$. Then they may be substituted for $\tilde{\Gamma}$ in the statements of Propositions 1, 2, and 3.

The proof is immediate and will be left to the reader.
The preceding lemma is important for two entirely separate reasons. One of them is simply that in practical situations it may be very easy to obtain the
forms $\hat{K}$ and very difficult to obtain the $\tilde{\Gamma}$ or the $\Gamma_{\theta}$ or even a good approximation to the function $q$.

Another reason is totally different. The results of Sections 6, 7, 8, and the present one depend on the asymptotic normality assumption included in (G1). This implies the $K$-control of the local experiments for nonrandom quadratic forms. However it is not at all impossible, and even fairly usual, that for a given $\nu$, the experiment $\mathscr{E}=\left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ would admit better approximations with random forms. Here we should remind the reader again that in practice $\nu$ never tends to infinity. It is not even there, and the limit theorems given in the general literature and here are only psychological substitutes for unavailable approximation theorems. Thus, one should, if necessary, use quadratic approximations in terms of random quadratic forms.

For random quadratic forms one can use the conditions (B), (C), (D), (E) of Section 5. One can also use instead of ( G 1 ) the requirement that for every fixed $b$ the experiments $\left\{P_{t} ; t \in \Theta(\theta, b)\right\}$ be $K_{\theta}$-controlled in the sense of Section 4, Definition 3, for suitable random forms $K_{\theta}$. It is then possible to reword the conditions (FF) and (H), using the random $K_{\theta}$ instead of the nonrandom $\Gamma_{\theta}$ or Γ.

The necessary modifications being easy, we shall not insist on their details and call "stochastically favorable" any $\theta \in \Theta$ at which the $K_{\theta}$-controlled condition hold together with (B), (C), (D), (E), and the analogous of (FF) and (H). Then one may construct centerings $\hat{Z}$ as in Section 3. Suppose also that we have constructed approximations $\hat{K}$ of the $K_{\theta}$, that these $\hat{K}$ are positive and defined on the entire set $\mathscr{M}_{0}(\Theta)$.

Under such conditions one may wonder what happens to the tests or confidence regions used in Propositions 1, 2, and 3. The answer is that not too much is known. Of course the centering $\hat{Z}$ are such that $K_{\theta}\left(\hat{Z}-\delta_{\theta}\right)$ remains bounded in $P_{\theta}$ probability, implying a certain form of consistency. Of course also, the Bayes estimates, or other Bayes procedure will have the same general form as in the asymptotically Gaussian cases, with substitution of $\hat{K}$ for $\tilde{\Gamma}$.

For suitable prior distributions, the posterior distributions will still be asymptotically Gaussian, as will result, for instance, from Theorems 2 and 3 of Chapter 12, Section 4. However, little else is known in the general case.

There is a particular case in which something more can be said. It is the case where if $q(t, \theta)$ remains bounded then the difference between the distributions $\mathscr{L}\left(K_{\theta} \mid t\right)$ and $\mathscr{L}\left(K_{\theta} \mid \theta\right)$ tends to zero. Here, because of the boundedness of dimension included in (D) and the analogous of (FF) and (H) it is enough to assume that if $b$ is fixed, $\mu \in \mathscr{M}_{0}[\Theta(\theta, b)]$ such that $\|\mu\|$ remains bounded, then the dual Lipschitz distance between $\mathscr{L}\left(K_{\theta}(\mu) \mid t\right)$ and $\mathscr{L}\left(K_{\theta}(\mu) \mid \theta\right)$ tends to zero.

This is by far the most common situation in cases of $K_{\theta}$-control, as one can see using the invariance results of Chapter 8, Section 4. In such a case one can look at the experiments $\mathscr{E}_{\theta, b}=\left\{P_{t} ; t \in \Theta(\theta, b)\right\}$ and use the spaces $F_{\theta}$ of Proposition 1. It can then be shown that the $\mathscr{E}_{\theta, b}$ are approximable by experiments $\mathscr{F}_{\theta, b}=\left\{Q_{t} ; t \in \Theta(\theta, b)\right\}$ where, for suitable centerings $Z$ and
quadratic forms $K$ one has exactly

$$
\log \frac{d Q_{t}}{d Q_{\theta}}=\exp \left\{-\frac{1}{2}\left[K\left(Z-\delta_{t}\right)-K\left(Z-\delta_{\theta}\right)\right]\right\}
$$

with conditional distributions $\mathscr{L}\left\{\left\langle Z-\delta_{\theta}, \mu\right\rangle_{K} \mid K, \theta\right\}$ which are Gaussian with expectation zero and variance $K(\mu)$. Because of this feature, this is often called the "asymptotically mixed normal case."

The fact that the distributions $\mathscr{L}\left(K_{\theta} \mid t\right)$ and $\mathscr{L}\left(K_{\theta} \mid t\right)$ differ little if $q(t, \theta)$ remains bounded, can be stated by saying that $K_{\theta}$ or $\hat{K}$ are asymptotically "ancillary" statistics for the experiments $\mathscr{E}_{\theta, b}=\left\{P_{t} ; t \in \Theta(\theta, b)\right\}$. More precisely, in the present case, the experiments $\left\{\mathscr{L}\left(K_{\theta} \mid t\right) ; t \in \Theta(\theta, b)\right\}$ are asymptotically trivial, so that, by itself, $K_{\theta}$ gives little information as to the value of $t$.

In such a case it is perfectly legal, but not mandatory, to carry out a study of decision procedures conditionally, given the value of $K_{\theta}$. One would then use tests and confidence balls built on quadratic expressions $\hat{K}\left(\hat{Z}-\delta_{\theta}\right)$ as if, given $\hat{K}$, the distribution $\mathscr{L}\left\{\left\langle Z-\delta_{\theta}, \mu\right\rangle_{\hat{K}} \mid \theta\right\}$ were exactly Gaussian with expectation zero and variances $\hat{K}(\mu)$. In particular one would use $\hat{K}\left(\hat{Z}, \delta_{\theta}\right)$ as if, given $\widehat{K}$, it was distributed according to a $\chi^{2}$ distribution.
(Note that we have not said that the experiments $\{\mathscr{L}(\hat{K} \mid t) ; t \in \Theta(\theta, b)\}$ are asymptotically trivial. This depends on how $\hat{K}$ is constructed. It is also clear that the experiments $\{\mathscr{L}(\hat{K} \mid \theta) ; \theta \in \Theta\}$ may be far from trivial.) There are, however, various features which make the situation much less satisfactory than the Gaussian ones. Here is, for instance, a statement suggested by a result of A. Swansen.

Suppose that $\Theta$ is the line and that the experiments $\mathscr{E}$ admit an approximation such that

$$
\Lambda(t, \theta)+\frac{1}{2} \sigma^{2}\left[(X-t)^{2}-(X-\theta)^{2}\right] \rightarrow 0
$$

for variables $\sigma^{2}$ and $X$. Assume also that $\mathscr{L}\left(\sigma^{2} \mid \theta\right)$ has a limit carried by $(0, \infty)$ and that $\mathscr{L}\left(\sigma^{2} \mid t\right)$ tends to this same limit whenever $|t-\theta|$ remains bounded. Assume further that the $\left\{P_{t}\right\}$ and $\left\{P_{\theta}\right\}$ are contiguous whenever $|t-\theta|$ remains bounded. Consider a fixed $\theta$ and two other fixed values $s$ and $t$ such that $\theta<s<t$. For a fixed level $\alpha$, the Neyman-Pearson test of $\theta$ against $s$ rejects $\theta$ if $T_{1}=\sigma^{2}\left[(X-\theta)-\frac{1}{2}(s-\theta)\right]$ exceeds a certain constant $c_{1}$.

Similarly, the Neyman-Pearson test of $\theta$ against $t$ rejects $\theta$ if $T_{2}=\sigma^{2}[(X- \left.\theta)-\frac{1}{2}(t-\theta)\right]$ exceeds another constant $c_{2}$. The difference $T_{1}-T_{2}$ is equal to $\frac{1}{2} \sigma^{2}(t-s)$. Given $T_{1}-T_{2}$, in the approximating mixed normal experiment, the sum $\frac{1}{2}\left(T_{1}+T_{2}\right)$ has a Gaussian distribution with variance $\sigma^{-2}$. For a given constant $b$ and any two constants ( $c_{1}, c_{2}$ ) the lines $T_{1}-T_{2}=b$ always intersect either the quadrant $\left\{x, y ; x \geq c_{1}, y \leq c_{2}\right\}$ or the quadrant $\left\{x, y ; x \leq c_{1}, y \geq c_{2}\right\}$. Thus, unless $\sigma^{2}$ is not random, the set $\left\{T_{1} \geq c_{1}, T_{2}<c_{2}\right\} \cup\left\{T_{1}<c_{1}, T_{2} \geq c_{2}\right\}$ will have positive probability. It results from this that the tests based on the
two $T_{i}$ cannot be asymptotically equivalent. This behavior carries over to tests on the experiments $\mathscr{E}$ themselves.

Therefore, contrary to what happens in the asymptotically Gaussian case, there is no asymptotically most powerful test of $\theta$ against an alternative $t \in \left(s_{1}, s_{2}\right)$ with $\theta<s_{1}<s_{2}$ fixed. Conditionally, given $\sigma^{2}$, there are such tests. However their unconditional behavior is subject to difficulties. Thus, even though it is always legal to condition on whatever one pleases, it may not be what one really wants to do.

## 10. Minimum $\chi^{2}$ and Relatives

The method of construction of the centerings $\hat{Z}$ described in Section 3 is closely related to two widely used methods. One of them is the maximum likelihood method. Another is the minimum $\chi^{2}$ method. In fact, when midpoints are used, the method of Section 3 could be described as follows. For a given $\omega \in \Omega$ one takes the set $\widetilde{S}_{\omega}$ obtained from $S_{\omega}$ by adjoining to it its midpoints. One fits a quadratic expression to the logarithm $\Lambda(s, \omega), s \in \widetilde{S}_{\omega}$ and one takes for $Z_{\omega}$ the point in $\mathscr{M}_{1}\left(S_{\omega}\right)$ which maximizes this quadratic. This is carried out only at the point $\omega=\hat{\theta}$. Changing signs, one can also say that $\hat{Z}$ minimizes a certain quadratic expression. In the asymptotically Gaussian case, the expression in question is asymptotically distributed according to a $\chi^{2}$ distribution. Note that the method of Section 3 does not coincide with the maximum likelihood method since the function one maximizes is only deemed to be an approximation to the likelihood function. This makes a substantial difference in the results one can obtain. Maximum likelihood estimates are often very badly behaved. Proofs that they do work use very severe restrictions. However, there is clearly a kinship between the method of Section 3, the maximum likelihood method, and the minimum $\chi^{2}$ method. Because of this relation we give in this section a few results which can often be used to simplify investigation of properties of the minimum $\chi^{2}$ or more precisely asymptotically least-squares type of estimates.

The assertions are roughly as follows.
(a) Instead of minimizing a function $f$, one may minimize a function $g$ approximately equal to $f$.
(b) Instead of minimizing $g$ on the set $S_{1}$, one can minimize it on a set $S_{2}$ tangent to $S_{1}$.
(c) If $g$ is approximately quadratic on a linear space $S_{2}$, the minimization yields a linear projection onto $S_{2}$.

Having established this, we give some indications on the asymptotic distributions of the resulting $\chi^{2}$, including some results of Chernoff and Lehmann, Dzhaparidze and Nikulin, Rao and Robson, and D. S. Moore.

The framework used here will be similar to that of Section 8. It involves the following pieces, which together form a structure to be called $\mathscr{S}$.
(1) A set $\Theta$ and an experiment $\mathscr{E}=\left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ given by measures on a space $(\mathscr{W}, \mathscr{B})$.
(2) A real vector space $\mathscr{X}$ with a norm $|\cdot|$.
(3) A map $\xi$ from $\Theta$ to $\mathscr{X}$.
(4) A random vector available on $\mathscr{E}$ and taking values in $\mathscr{X}$.
(5) A function $Q$ defined on the product $\mathscr{X} \times \mathscr{X} \times \Theta \times \mathscr{W}$ and such that $Q(x, y, \theta, W) \in[0, \infty]$.

It is assumed that the intent of the statistician is to find estimates $T$ of $\xi(\theta)$ by minimizing the expression $Q$ with respect to $\theta$.

In the sequel we shall always suppress the dependence on the underlying random element $W$ and write, for instance, $Q(X, \xi(\theta), \theta)$ instead of $Q(X(W)$, $\xi(\theta), \theta, W)$. Note that here $\xi$ is a nonrandom map from $\Theta$ to $\mathscr{X}$. One could consider random maps at the cost of some complication.

For purpose of taking limits, we shall consider not just one structure $\mathscr{S}$ of the type described above, but a sequence or net $\left\{\mathscr{S}_{v}\right\}$ of such structures. As usual, the index $v$ will be suppressed, and everything will vary with $v$ unless otherwise labeled. It will be assumed that the actual distributions of the variables $W$ are induced by measures $P_{\theta_{v}, v}$ (written simply $P_{\theta}$ ) for a certain $\theta_{v} \in \Theta_{v}$. For simplicity, the value $\xi_{v}\left(\theta_{v}\right)$ will be taken as origin of $\mathscr{X}$ (except in the very last part of this section).

With these conventions, consider not one, but two functions $Q$, say $Q^{0}$ and $Q^{1}$. For $i=0,1$, let $R^{i}(t)$ be the expression

$$
R^{i}(t)=Q^{i}(X, \xi(t), t)-\inf \left\{Q^{i}(X, \xi(\tau), \tau) ; \tau \in \Theta\right\}
$$

Let $\Delta(t)$ be the difference

$$
\Delta(t)=Q^{0}(X, \xi(t), t)-Q^{1}(X, \xi(t), t) .
$$

Lemma 1. If $\hat{\theta}$ is any estimate of $\theta$, the following inequalities hold.
(i) For every $t \in \Theta$ one has

$$
Q^{0}[X, \xi(\hat{\theta}), \hat{\theta}] \leq Q^{0}[X, \xi(t), t]+|\Delta(t)|+|\Delta(\hat{\theta})|+R^{1}(\hat{\theta})
$$

(ii) If $Q^{0}(x, \xi, t) \leq Q^{1}(x, \xi, t)$ for all triplets $(x, \xi, t)$, then if $\xi(\theta)$ is the origin of $\mathscr{X}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q^{0}(X, \xi(\hat{\theta}), \hat{\theta}] \leq Q^{1}[X, \xi(\hat{\theta}), \hat{\theta}] & \leq \inf _{t} Q^{1}[X, \xi(t), t]+R^{1}(\hat{\theta}) \\
& \leq Q^{1}(X, 0, \theta)+R^{1}(\hat{\theta})
\end{aligned}
$$

The proof is a simple computation.
Now consider the net of structures $\mathscr{S}$ with "true value" of the parameter $\theta$ such that $\xi(\theta)=0$.

Assumption 1. The nets $|X|$ and $Q^{1}[X, \xi(\theta), \theta]$ are bounded in $P_{\theta}$ probability. Assumption 2. There is a fixed number $b$ such that for every fixed $c \in(0, \infty)$, the set where $Q^{i}(x, \xi(t), t) \geq b|x-\xi(t)|$ for all $t$ and for all $x$ such that $|x| \leq c$, has a $P_{\theta}$ inner measure tending to unity.

For $c \in(0, \infty)$ let $U(c)$ be the ball $U(c)=\{t \in \Theta ; \xi(t) \leq c\}$ and let $\beta(c)$ be the expression

$$
\beta(c)=\sup \left\{\left|Q^{1}(x, y, t)-Q^{0}(x, y, t)\right| ; t \in U(c),|x|+|y| \leq c\right\} .
$$

Assumption 3. For every fixed $c \in(0, \infty)$ and every fixed $\varepsilon>0$, the outer probability $P_{\theta}[\beta(c)>\varepsilon]$ tends to zero.

Definition 1. For each $v$, let $\hat{\theta}$ be a function from $\mathscr{W}$ to $\Theta$. This will be called a $Q^{i}$ net or sequence if $R^{i}(\hat{\theta})$ tends to zero in $P_{\theta}$ probability.

The following proposition shows that, in the computation of $Q^{i}$ nets or sequences, one can replace $Q^{1}$ by $Q^{0}$.

Proposition 1. Let the assumptions (1), (2), and (3) be satisfied. Then (i) For any $Q^{i}$-sequence $\hat{\theta}$, the $|\xi(\theta)|$ are bounded in $P_{\theta}$ probability.
(ii) $A$ sequence $\hat{\theta}$ is a $Q^{1}$-sequence if and only if it is a $Q^{0}$-sequence.

Proof. Assumption 1 and the inequality (ii) of Lemma 1 insure that $Q^{1}(X, \xi(\hat{\theta})$, $\hat{\theta}$ ) will remain bounded in probability. Thus, taking a fixed $\varepsilon>0$ and eliminating sets of probability at most $\varepsilon$, there will be a fixed $c$ such that $|X| \leq c$ and $b|X-\xi(\hat{\theta})| \leq Q^{1}(X, \xi(\hat{\theta}), \hat{\theta}) \leq c$. It follows that $|X-\xi(\hat{\theta})|$ remains bounded in probability, and so does $\xi(\hat{\theta})$. For the function $Q^{0}$, one can assume again, eliminating sets of probability at most $\varepsilon$, that $b|X-\xi(t)| \leq Q^{0}(X, \xi(t), t)$ for all $t$ and for $|X| \leq c$. In this range, $Q^{0}$ and $Q^{1}$ are close to one another, according to Assumption 3. Thus, if $\theta^{*}$ is a $Q^{0}$-sequence, $Q^{0}\left[X, \xi\left(\theta^{*}\right), \theta^{*}\right]$ is bounded in probability and the differences $Q^{1}(X, \xi(\hat{\theta}), \hat{\theta})-Q^{0}\left[X, \xi\left(\theta^{*}\right), \theta^{*}\right]$ will tend to zero in probability. The result follows.

Assumption 2, above, is rather strong. It has been used only to insure that if $Q^{i}(X, \xi(\hat{\theta}), \hat{\theta})$ remains bounded in probability, so does $|\xi(\hat{\theta})|$ and could be replaced by an assumption to this effect.

In the situations which mimic asymptotically the usual least-squares methods the functions $Q^{i}$ are locally approximable by quadratic expressions in the differences $X-\xi(t)$. To investigate this situation we shall use another assumption.

Assumption 4. For the given true $\theta$, there is an $\mathscr{X}$, a norm $\rho$ with the following properties:
(i) The norm $\rho$ is of Hilbertian type. That is,

$$
\rho^{2}\left(\frac{x+y}{2}\right)+\rho^{2}\left(\frac{x-y}{2}\right)=\frac{1}{2}\left[\rho^{2}(x)+\rho^{2}(y)\right] .
$$

(ii) The norms $\rho$ and $|\cdot|$ are related (according to Section 4, Definition 1).
(iii) For each fixed $c$ the expression

$$
\sup \left\{\left|Q^{i}(x, y, t)-\rho^{2}(x-y)\right| ; t \in U(c),|x|+|y| \leq c\right\}
$$

tends to zero in $P_{\theta}$ probability.
If this assumption holds, and if the distributions are induced by $P_{\theta}$, Proposition 1 says that any $Q^{i}$-sequence is a $\rho$-sequence and conversely. Thus to investigate the properties of $Q^{i}$-sequences, it is enough to investigate those of $\rho$-sequences.

Consider then elements $a \in \mathscr{X}$ and subsets $C \subset \mathscr{X}$. Both $a$ and $C$ may be random. Call $C$ asymptotically tangent to $\xi(\Theta)$ at $a$ if for each fixed $c$, the

Hausdorff distance (for $|\cdot|$ ) between $\xi(\Theta) \bigcap U(a, c)$ and $C \bigcap U(a, c)$ tends to zero. Here $U(a, c)$ is the ball of radius $c$ centered at $a$.

Proposition 2. Let assumptions (1) to (4) be satisfied. Let $|a|$ be bounded in probability and let $C$ be asymptotically tangent to $\xi(\Theta)$ at $a$. Let $\{b\}$ be any sequence of numbers such that $b \rightarrow \infty$. Then a sequence $\{\hat{\theta}\}$ is a $Q^{i}$-sequence if and only if

$$
\rho[X-\xi(\hat{\theta})]-\inf \{\rho(X-u) ; u \in C \cap U(a, b)\}
$$

tends to zero in probability.
Proof. According to Proposition 1, one may ignore the $Q^{i}$ and work with the norm $\rho$. Now since $a$ is bounded in probability, except for cases of small probability, for $v$ large and $b_{1}$ sufficiently large, but fixed, the balls $U\left(a, b_{1}\right)$ will contain a ball centered at the origin of $\mathscr{X}$. The sets $C$ and $\xi(\Theta)$ are also tangent at the origin of $\mathscr{X}$ and, excepting again sets of small probability, $C \cap U\left(a, b_{1}\right)$ will contain points which are very close to $\xi(\theta)=0$. It follows from this and from the argument of Proposition 1 that any sequence $\{u\}$ such that

$$
\rho(X-u)-\inf _{s}\{\rho(X-s) ; s \in C \bigcap U(a, b)\}
$$

will be such that $|u|$ is also bounded in probability. Thus, given a fixed $\varepsilon>0$, excepting sets of probability at most $\varepsilon$, one can assume that $u \in C \cap U\left(b_{1}\right)$ and $\xi(\hat{\theta}) \in \xi(\Theta) \cap U\left(b_{1}\right)$, for some fixed $b_{2}$. However, since the sets $C$ and $\xi(\Theta)$ are asymptotically tangent at $\xi(\theta)=0$, the differences

$$
\inf \left\{\rho(X-s) ; s \in C \cap U\left(b_{2}\right)\right\}-\inf \left\{\rho(X-s) ; s \in \xi(\Theta) \cap U\left(b_{2}\right)\right\}
$$

tend to zero in probability. The result follows.
Note that the preceding Proposition 2 does not make any use of particular properties of the sets $C$, or of the norm $\rho$, except for the tangency properties and for the conditions (ii) and (iii) of Assumption 4. The Hilbertian properties of $\rho$ are not used.

Without them, and without further assumptions on the sets $C$, it may very well happen that although the values $\inf \{\rho(X-u) ; u \in C\}$ are well determined, the set of values $u$ which achieve or almost achieve the infimum are very large. To pass to a more usual, and more restricted situation, we shall introduce another assumption.

Assumption 5. There are complete linear subspaces $V$ of $\mathscr{X}$ which are asymptotically tangent to $\xi(\Theta)$ at the point $\xi(\theta)=0$.

The following proposition is very easy, but important. Note first that if $V$ is complete for the norm $|\cdot|$ it will also be complete for the related $\rho$, at least for $\nu$ sufficiently large. Thus there will exist an orthogonal projection of $\mathscr{X}$ into $V$ for the norm $\rho$. Let $\Pi_{\rho}$ be this projection. It is a linear map.

Proposition 3. Let assumptions (1) to (5) be satisfied. Then for every sequence $\hat{\theta}$ which is a $Q^{i}$-sequence, the difference $\xi(\hat{\theta})-\Pi_{\rho} X$ tends to zero in probability.

Proof. Let $u_{1}$ and $u_{2}$ be two elements of $V$ such that

$$
\rho\left(X-u_{i}\right) \leq \inf \{\rho(X-v) ; v \in V\}+\varepsilon
$$

Then, according to the median inequality

$$
\rho^{2}\left(u_{1}-u_{2}\right) \leq 2 \varepsilon(D+2 \varepsilon)
$$

for $D=\inf \{\rho(X-v) ; v \in V\}$.
According to Proposition 2, the difference $\rho[X-\xi(\hat{\theta})]-D$ tends to zero in probability. Hence the result.

Up to this point nothing would be changed in the arguments if one assumed that the norms $\rho$, the maps $\xi$, and the sets $V$ were also random elements. However, to say something about optimality properties it is most convenient to assume that the maps $\xi$ and the norms $\rho$ are nonrandom. Then $V$ can also be taken nonrandom.

The familiar least-squares situation assumes that the vectors $X$ have first and second moments. Here we shall assume something of the same nature, but a bit weaker. However to simplify various statements, we shall consider only the case where the spaces $(\mathscr{X}, \rho)$ are fixed.

Assumption 6. The spaces $\mathscr{X}$ and $V$ and the norm $\rho$ are fixed, independent of $v$. For each element $y$ of the dual $\mathscr{Y}$ of $(\mathscr{X}, \rho)$, the distributions $\mathscr{L}\left\{\langle y, X\rangle \mid P_{\theta}\right\}$ have a limit with a finite second moment $\sigma^{2}(y)$.

Under these circumstances, consider a $Q^{i}$-sequence $\{\hat{\theta}\}$. According to the foregoing propositions, the behavior of $\xi(\hat{\theta})$ is the same as that of $\Pi X$ where $\Pi$ is the orthogonal projection of $\mathscr{X}$ onto $V$ for the norm $\rho$. Thus $\langle y, \xi(\hat{\theta})\rangle$ will have a limiting distribution, with a second moment equal to $\sigma^{2}(y \Pi)$. Here $y \Pi$ is defined as usual by $\langle y \Pi, x\rangle=\langle y, \Pi x\rangle$.

For any given $y$, one may try to select the functions $Q$ in such a way that among all possible choices the mean-square error $\sigma^{2}(y \Pi)$ of the limiting distribution be as small as possible. A $Q$-sequence with this property will be called relatively optimal for $y$. The optimality is very "relative" in that it refers only to those estimates which can be obtained through construction of $Q$ sequences. Nothing in the assumptions made here prevents $X$ or $Q$ from having hidden in their makeup perfect or almost perfect information about $\theta$. For instance, one could have a vector $X$ whose decimal expansions (in a suitable basis) contain, after the $v$ th decimals, a one-to-one function of $t \in \Theta$. Under the assumptions made here, these decimals are essentially ignored.

Another aspect of this relative optimality is that the estimates $\hat{\theta}$ are locally essentially functions of $X$ alone. However, nothing prevents $X$ from being hopelessly insufficient for the experiments $\left\{P_{t} ; t \in \Theta\right\}$.

Now note that, if all the assumptions 1 to 6 are satisfied, there is on $\mathscr{X}$ a cylindrical measure $\mathscr{L}(Z)$ such that $\sigma^{2}(y)=E|\langle y, Z\rangle|^{2}$. Thus $\sigma$ is a seminorm of Hilbertian type and $\mathscr{L}\{\langle y, \xi(\hat{\theta})\rangle \mid \theta\}$ tends to $\mathscr{L}\{\langle y, \Pi Z\rangle\}$ for the projection $\Pi$ of $\mathscr{X}$ onto $V$ orthogonally for $\rho$. Therefore one will always have $\sigma^{2}(y \Pi) \geq s^{2}(y)$ where $s^{2}(y)=\inf _{\pi} \sigma^{2}(y \pi)$ taken over the set of all $\rho$-continuous



linear projections of $\mathscr{X}$ onto $V$. This leads to a first remark, usually considered part of the Gauss-Markov theorem.

Proposition 4. Let the assumptions 1 to 6 be satisfied. Let $V^{0}$ be the polar of $V$ in the dual $\mathscr{Y}$ of $\mathscr{X}$. Then $\sigma^{2}(y \Pi)=s^{2}(y)$ if and only if $y \Pi$ is orthogonal to $V^{0}$ for the semi-norm $\sigma$. If this is true for every $y \in \mathscr{Y}$ and every closed linear subspace $V \subset \mathscr{X}$, then $\sigma$ is a multiple of the norm $q_{1}$ defined on $\mathscr{Y}$ by $q_{1}(y)=\sup \{|\langle y, x\rangle| ; \rho(x) \leq 1\}$.

Proof. Let $\pi$ be any continuous linear projection of $\mathscr{X}$ onto $V$. The transpose of $\pi$ is a projection of $\mathscr{Y}$ into itself. The same is true of $I-\pi$, and in fact $I-\pi$ is a projection of $\mathscr{Y}$ onto $V^{0}$. Indeed $z \in V^{0}$ implies

$$
\langle z(I-\pi), x\rangle=\langle z, x\rangle-\langle z, \pi x\rangle=\langle z, x\rangle
$$

for all $x \in \mathscr{X}$. Thus $V^{0}$ is invariant. Also $x \in V$ implies $(I-\pi) x=0$, hence $\langle z(I-\pi), x\rangle=0$ for all $z \in \mathscr{Y}$, that is, $z(I-\pi) \in V^{0}$.

One consequence of this is that for the specified point $y$ of the proposition one can write $y \pi=y-v$ for some $v \in V^{0}$. Thus

$$
s^{2}(y) \geq s_{0}^{2}(y)=\inf \left\{\sigma^{2}(y-v) ; v \in V^{0}\right\}
$$

Now assume that $y \notin V^{0}$. Take an $\varepsilon>0$ and a particular $w \in V^{0}$ such that $\sigma^{2}(y-w) \leq s_{0}^{2}(y)+\varepsilon$. In the two-dimensional space spanned by $y$ and $w$ there is some projection $p$ such that $y p=w$. One can extend this $p$ to the linear space $L$ spanned by $y$ and $V^{0}$, getting a projection of $L$ onto $V^{0}$. To do this, let $q$ be the polar norm of $\rho$. Any $z \in L$ can be written in the form $z=\alpha y+\beta w+\gamma v$ where $v \in V^{0}$ is orthogonal to $w$ for $q_{1}$. Then one can take $z p=(\alpha+\beta) w$. The space $L$ is closed in $\mathscr{Y}$. One can write each $z \in \mathscr{Y}$ as $z=z_{1}+z_{2}$ with $z_{1} \in L$ and $z_{2}$ orthogonal to $z_{1}$ for $q_{1}$. Then $z p=z_{1} p$ yields a projection of $\mathscr{Y}$ onto $V^{0}$ such that $y p=w$. It follows that $s^{2}(y)=s_{0}^{2}(y)$. It follows also that if $\sigma^{2}(y \Pi)=s^{2}(y)$ then $y \Pi=y-v$ for a $v$ which achieves the minimum $s_{0}^{2}(y)=\sigma^{2}(y-v)$. Thus $y-v$ is $\sigma$-orthogonal to $V^{0}$ as claimed.

For the second statement of the proposition note that for any two elements $y$ and $z$ of $\mathscr{Y}$ the projections $y \Pi$ and $z(I-\Pi)$ are orthogonal for the polar norm $q_{1}$ defined by $\rho$. Thus, if the assumed relations hold for all $y$ and $V$, the orthogonality relations defined by $\sigma$ and $q_{1}$ are the same. Looking at twodimensional subspaces of $\mathscr{Y}$, one sees then that there is some constant $a$ such that $\sigma(y)=a q_{1}(y)$ for all $y$. Hence the result.

Remark 1. The proportionality of $\sigma$ and $q_{1}$ is obtained here by varying $V$. For a given $V$ it may well happen that the projection $\Pi$ induced by $\rho$ is such that
the transpose of $I-\Pi$ is an orthogonal projection of $\mathscr{Y}$ onto $V^{0}$ for the seminorm $\sigma$, even though $\sigma$ and $q_{1}$ are far from being proportional.

Remark 2. For a fixed infinite dimensional space ( $\mathscr{X}, \rho$ ), it may be difficult, or impossible, to enforce the proportionality of $\sigma$ and $q_{1}$ as well as the validity of the assumptions 1 to 6 . Indeed, the assumptions 1 to 6 imply that $\rho(Z)$ is almost surely finite. Thus the cylindrical measure $\mathscr{L}(Z)$ admits a Radon extension on ( $\mathscr{X}, \rho$ ). If in addition $E \rho(Z)$ is finite, as would be the case, for
instance, for a Gaussian $Z$, the function $y \mapsto \sigma(y)$ is continuous for the weak topology $w(\mathscr{Y}, \mathscr{X})$ of $\mathscr{Y}$ when restricted to $q_{1}$-balls of $\mathscr{Y}$. Thus $\sigma$ cannot be proportional to $q_{1}$ unless $\mathscr{X}$ is finite dimensional.

Remark 3. For spaces $\mathscr{X}$ of bounded dimension it is usually easy to find functions $Q$ which yield the desired proportionality relations of $\sigma$ and $q_{1}$ at a particular choice of the point $\theta$. This does not necessarily imply that it is easy to find functions $Q$ with the same property for all choices of $\theta$.

Because of space limitations, we shall not dwell further on the matter of estimation through asymptotic analogues of the least-square method. However, the same technique is also used for testing purposes and a few words must be said on this subject.

Although distribution assumptions play a minimal role in estimation problems, they are very important in testing. Thus we shall replace Assumption 6 above by the following.

Assumption 7. The dimension of the spaces $\mathscr{X}$ remain bounded. There are Gaussian random vectors $X^{\prime}$, with values in $\mathscr{X}$ such that the dual Lipschitz distance between $\mathscr{L}\left(X^{\prime}\right)$ and $\mathscr{L}\left\{X-\xi(\theta) \mid P_{\theta}\right\}$ tends to zero. Furthermore $E X^{\prime}=0$ and $E\left\{\left\langle y, X^{\prime}\right\rangle\right\}=\sigma^{2}(y)$ for a norm $\sigma$ whose polar can play the role of the norm $\rho$ of Assumption 4.
(Here dual Lipschitz distances are computed either for the norm $|\cdot|$ or the norm $\rho$.)

Proposition 5. Let the assumptions 1 to 5 and 7 be satisfied. Then:
(i) the dual Lipschitz distance between $\mathscr{L}[\xi(\hat{\theta}) \mid \theta]$ and $\mathscr{L}\left(X^{\prime \prime}\right)$ tends to zero for a Gaussian variable $X^{\prime \prime}=\Pi_{\rho} X^{\prime}$ with mean zero and variance function $E\left|\left\langle y, X^{\prime \prime}\right\rangle\right|^{2}=\inf \left\{\sigma^{2}(y-w) ; w \in V^{0}\right\}$.
(ii) The distance between $\mathscr{L}[Q(X, \xi(\theta), \theta) \mid \theta]$ and a central $\chi^{2}$ distribution with number of degrees of freedom equal to the dimension of $\mathscr{X}$ tends to zero.
(iii) The distance between $\mathscr{L}\{Q[X, \xi(\hat{\theta}), \hat{\theta}] \mid \theta\}$ and a central $\chi^{2}$ distribution with number of degrees of freedom $\operatorname{dim} \mathscr{X}-\operatorname{dim} V$ tends to zero.

Proof. According to Proposition 3, the difference $\xi(\hat{\theta})-\Pi_{\rho} X$ tends to zero. Thus $\mathscr{L}[\xi(\hat{\theta}) \mid \theta]$ behaves in the same manner as $\mathscr{L}\left(\Pi_{\rho} X^{\prime}\right)$. This, and Proposition 4 , yields the first statement. For the other statements one may replace the functions $Q$ by the square norms $\rho^{2}$. Thus it is sufficient to investigate the behavior of $\rho^{2}\left(X^{\prime}\right)$ and $\rho^{2}\left(\Pi_{\rho} X^{\prime}\right)$ for the Gaussian variable $X^{\prime}$. This gives immediately the desired result.

The statistics $Q(X, \xi(\hat{\theta}), \hat{\theta})$ are often used as tests of goodness of fit. The reduction of the number of degrees of freedom from $\operatorname{dim} \mathscr{X}$ to $\operatorname{dim} \mathscr{X}-\operatorname{dim} V$ should not be ignored. It was the occasion of a major misunderstanding between R.A. Fisher and K. Pearson in the case of contingency tables. We shall return to it below.

The $\chi^{2}$ type tests are also used to test hypotheses $\Theta_{0} \subset \Theta$ against $\Theta \backslash \Theta_{0}$. If $\Theta_{0}$ admits a tangent linear space $V_{0} \subset V$ at $\theta$ the difference

$$
\inf \left\{Q(X, \xi(t), t) ; t \in \Theta_{0}\right\}-\inf \{Q(X, \xi(t), t) ; t \in \Theta\}
$$

will also behave as a $\chi^{2}$, with a number of degrees of freedom $\operatorname{dim} V-\operatorname{dim} V_{0}$.

The theory parallels the usual linear hypotheses theory of the Gaussian case and need not be repeated (see Section 9).

Note well, however, that the asymptotic $\chi^{2}$ behavior of $Q[X, \xi(\hat{\theta}), \hat{\theta}]$ was obtained under the assumption that $\hat{\theta}$ is a $Q$-sequence, almost minimizing $Q(X, \xi(t), t)$ for $t \in \Theta$.

It often happens that one would prefer to use a statistic $Q\left[X, \xi\left(\theta^{*}\right), \theta^{*}\right]$ where $\theta^{*}$ is a good estimate of $\theta$, obtained by another method entirely. This happens, for instance, in the standard $\chi^{2}$-tests of goodness of fit for densities on the line. Suppose, for instance, that one observes independent identically distributed observations $\omega_{1}, \omega_{2}, \ldots, \omega_{n}$ on the line and that one wants to test the hypothesis that they are issued from a Gaussian distribution with parameters $\theta=\left(\mu, \sigma^{2}\right)$, unknown. A possible test is a $\chi^{2}$ test based on cell frequencies obtainable by dividing the line into $k+1$ disjoint intervals. Let $X$ be the vector of observed cell frequencies. Let $\xi(\theta)$ be the vector of cell probabilities under the hypothesis that $\theta$ is true. The standard $\chi^{2}$ of K . Pearson would be

$$
Q[X, \xi(\theta)]=\sum_{i=1}^{k+1} \frac{n\left[X_{i}-\xi_{i}(\theta)\right]^{2}}{\xi_{i}(\theta)}
$$

where $X_{i}$ is the $i$ th coordinate of $X$. Then, under $\theta$, the quantity $Q(X, \xi(\theta))$ is approximately $\chi^{2}$ with $k$ degrees of freedom. If $\hat{\theta}$ is a $Q$-sequence then $Q[X, \xi(\hat{\theta})]$ is approximately $\chi^{2}$ with $k-2$ degrees of freedom.

In this case it is much easier to compute the maximum likelihood estimate, say $\theta^{*}$, based on the ungrouped observations. However Chernoff and Lehmann have shown that $Q\left[X, \xi\left(\theta^{*}\right)\right]$ need not behave asymptotically as a $\chi^{2}$ variable. Dzhaparidze and Nikulin have proposed to subtract from $Q\left[X, \xi\left(\theta^{*}\right)\right]$ a suitable nonnegative quadratic expression, reverting it to a $\chi^{2}$ with $k-2$ degrees of freedom. On the contrary, Rao and Robson have proposed adding to $Q\left[X, \xi\left(\theta^{*}\right)\right]$ a nonnegative quadratic, to revert to a $\chi^{2}$ with $k$ degrees of freedom. D.S. Moore and others have proposed to use a quadratic
$\left[X-\xi\left(\theta^{*}\right)\right]^{\prime} M\left[X-\xi\left(\theta^{*}\right)\right]$ where $M$ is an estimate of the inverse covariance matrix of $X-\xi\left(\theta^{*}\right)$. (Here the covariance matrix of $X-\xi\left(\theta^{*}\right)$ is singular on the $k+1$ dimensional space. However this is immaterial since one works only on the space where $\sum_{i=1}^{k+1}\left[X_{i}-\xi_{i}\left(\theta^{*}\right)\right]=0$.)

We shall now present some results which refer to a similar situation. Note that here we are dealing with two random elements: the vector $X$ and the estimate $\theta^{*}$. In the usual situations both are asymptotically normally distributed. Thus, it appears useful to look first at the exactly Gaussian situation. A Gaussian analogue could be described as follows.

Let $\mathscr{X}$ and $\mathscr{Z}$ be two finite dimensional vector spaces and let $\binom{X}{Z}$ be a random vector such that $X$ has values in $\mathscr{X}$ and $Z$ has values in $\mathscr{Z}$. It will be assumed that the joint distribution $P$ of $\binom{X}{Z}$ varies in a class $\mathscr{D}_{1}$ and that the null hypothesis specifies that $P \in \mathscr{D}_{0} \subset \mathscr{D}_{1}$.

Two standard assumptions would be
Standard Condition $(1 \mathrm{~N})$. For every $P \in \mathscr{D}_{1}$, the joint distribution $\mathscr{L}\left\{\left.\binom{X}{Z} \right\rvert\, P\right\}$
is Gaussian, with

$$
E_{p}\binom{X}{Z}=\binom{\xi}{\zeta}
$$

and with a covariance matrix

$$
E_{p}\binom{X-\xi}{Z-\zeta}\binom{X-\xi}{Z-\zeta}^{\prime}=\left(\begin{array}{cc}
A & C \\
C^{\prime} & B
\end{array}\right)
$$

independent of $P$.
(The use of matrix notation and transpose assumes that bases have been selected in $\mathscr{X}$ and $\mathscr{Z}$.)

Standard Condition $(2 \mathrm{~N})$. There is a linear map $S$ from $\mathscr{Z}$ to $\mathscr{X}$ such that if $P \in \mathscr{D}_{0}$ then $E_{p} X=S\left(E_{p} Z\right)$. Furthermore, as $P$ varies through $\mathscr{D}_{0}$ the expectation $E_{p} Z$ varies through the entire space $\mathscr{Z}$.

We shall assume that there is a particular $P_{0} \in \mathscr{D}_{0}$ such that $E_{p_{0}}\binom{X}{Z}=\binom{0}{0}$. It is convenient to introduce quadratic norms as follows. For $u$ in the dual $\mathscr{X}^{\prime}$ of $\mathscr{X}$, let $\|u\|^{2}=E_{P_{0}}(u X)^{2}$. For $v \in \mathscr{Z}^{\prime}$, let $|v|^{2}=E_{P_{0}}(v Z)^{2}$. Define the corresponding polar norms by $\|x\|=\sup \left\{|u x| ; u \in \mathscr{X}^{\prime},\|u\| \leq 1\right\}$ and similarly for $|z|, z \in \mathscr{Z}$. If $A$ is nonsingular, then $\|u\|^{2}=u A u^{\prime}$ and $\|x\|^{2}=x^{\prime} A^{-1} x$. Here it is convenient to assume that both $A$ and $B$ are nonsingular. This can always be achieved at little cost, reducing the dimensions of the spaces $\mathscr{X}^{\prime}$ and $\mathscr{Z}^{\prime}$ if necessary. Even if this is assumed, the entire matrix $\left(\begin{array}{cc}A & C \\ C & B\end{array}\right)$ may still be singular. For simplicity we shall assume below that such is not the case. However we shall occasionally point out a few differences arising from the singularity of that matrix. Note that, under the standard condition (1 N) above,
the vector $\binom{X-\xi}{Z-\xi}$ is always in the range of the matrix $\left(\begin{array}{cc}A & C \\ C & B\end{array}\right)$ but that, for instance, $\binom{X-S \zeta}{Z-\zeta}$ may not be, if $P \in \mathscr{D}_{1} \backslash \mathscr{D}_{0}$.

With this notation, the various $\chi^{2}$-types of goodness of fit mentioned above take the following forms:
(a) The K. Pearson formula: $Q_{p}=\|X-S Z\|^{2}$.
(b) The Pearson-Fisher formula: $Q_{F}=\inf \left\{\|X-S \zeta\|^{2} ; \zeta \in \mathscr{Z}\right\}$.

The value $S \hat{\zeta}$ which achieves the minimum in that last expression is the orthogonal projection $\Pi X$ of $X$ on the range $S \mathscr{Z}$ for the norm $\|\cdot\|$. Thus, by Pythagoras' theorem one may write

$$
Q_{P}=Q_{F}+\|\Pi X-S Z\|^{2}
$$

The Dzhaparidze-Nikulin formula consists in computing $Q_{P}$ and $\|\Pi X-S Z\|^{2}$ separately and recovering $Q_{F}$ by difference. Let $M$ be the covariance matrix of $X-S Z$. The Rao-Robson-Moore formula would give a criterion

$$
Q_{R M}=(X-S Z)^{\prime} M^{-1}(X-S Z) .
$$

Rao and Robson also suggested another formula:

$$
Q_{R R}=Q_{P}+(X-S Z)^{\prime} K(X-S Z),
$$

with $K=R^{\prime}\left[B-C^{\prime} A^{-1} C\right]^{-1} R, R=C^{\prime} A^{-1}$. By construction one always has the inequalities $Q_{F} \leq Q_{P} \leq Q_{R R}$.

All these formulas appear to be intended for use in the case where, under $\mathscr{D}_{1}$, the range of possible values of $\left(\frac{\xi}{\xi}\right)$ is the entire product space $\mathscr{X} \times \mathscr{Z}$. Under the standard conditions $(1 \mathrm{~N})$ and $(2 \mathrm{~N})$, the hypothesis $\mathscr{D}_{0}$ is a linear hypothesis on Gaussian expectations. Thus, assuming that $\mathscr{D}_{1}$ yields all of $\mathscr{X} \times \mathscr{Z}$, one could also use the Neyman-Pearson likelihood ratio test based on the expression

$$
Q_{L R}=\inf _{\zeta \in Z}\binom{X-S \zeta}{Z-\zeta}^{\prime}\left(\begin{array}{cc}
A & C \\
C^{\prime} & B
\end{array}\right)^{-1}\binom{X-S \zeta}{Z-\zeta}
$$

This looks complicated, but can be simplified easily. Let $U=X-S Z$ and let $V=Z-T U$ for a linear map $T$ selected so that $U$ and $V$ are uncorrelated. Explicitly

$$
E_{P_{0}} Z\left(X^{\prime}-Z^{\prime} S^{\prime}\right)=T E_{P_{0}}(X-S Z)\left(X^{\prime}-Z^{\prime} S^{\prime}\right)
$$

so that $C^{\prime}-B S^{\prime}=T M$.
Expressed in terms of $U$ and $V$, the hypothesis $\mathscr{D}_{0}$ says that $E_{P} U=0$ and $E_{P} V=\zeta$. The quadratic form to be minimized to obtain $Q_{L R}$ takes the form

$$
U^{\prime} M^{-1} U+(V-\zeta)^{\prime} M_{1}^{-1}(V-\zeta)
$$

for some matrix $M_{1}$, covariance of $V$. Thus $Q_{L R}=U^{\prime} M^{-1} U$ and the minimum is achieved for $\hat{\zeta}=V=Z-T(X-S Z)$. In particular one obtains the equality $Q_{R M}=Q_{L R}$.

Note that when $M$ is singular, but $X-S Z$ is in the range of $M$, there is no difficulty in defining $(X-S Z)^{\prime} M^{-1}(X-S Z)$. If on the contrary $X-S Z$ is not in the range of $M$, one should take $Q_{L R}=\infty$ and not use a generalized inverse.

To obtain a formula of the Rao-Robson type, introduce a variable $W= Z-R X$ with $C^{\prime}=R A$ so that $X$ and $W$ are uncorrelated. Then the quadratic form in the exponent of the normal densities takes the form

$$
(X-S \zeta)^{\prime} A^{-1}(X-S \zeta)+(W-\phi)^{\prime}\left(B-C^{\prime} A^{-1} C\right)^{-1}(W-\phi)
$$

with $\phi=E_{P} W=(I-R S) \zeta$ under $\mathscr{D}_{0}$.
If in this formula one replaces $\zeta$ by the estimate $Z$, one obtains

$$
(X-S Z)^{\prime}\left[A^{-1}+R^{\prime}\left(B-C^{\prime} A^{-1} C\right)^{-1} R\right](X-S Z)
$$

This is the Rao-Robson statistic $Q_{R R}$. However $Q_{R R}$ and $Q_{L R}$ may be different since the value $\hat{\zeta}$ which yield minimum $Q_{L R}$ is not $Z$ but $Z-T(X-S Z)$. The difference $Z-\hat{\zeta}$ has a covariance matrix $T M T^{\prime}=(C-S B)^{\prime} M^{-1}(C-S B)$
which vanishes identically only if $C=S B$. It is only in this case that the formula was suggested by Rao and Robson.

Let us recall that when, under the general hypothesis $\mathscr{D}_{1}$, the range of the expectations is the entire space $\mathscr{X} \times \mathscr{Z}$, the tests based on $Q_{L R}$ have a variety of optimality such as being most stringent, having best average power over certain surfaces, etc. Thus the tests based on $Q_{L R}$ or the equivalent $Q_{R M}$ seem reasonable. Of course if the range of the expectations is not the entire $\mathscr{X} \times \mathscr{Z}$ but a linear subspace of it, the appropriate likelihood ratio test need not be given by $Q_{L R}$ (see Section 9).

Assuming that what one wants to use is $Q_{L R}=Q_{R M}$, the question arises whether $Q_{L R}=Q_{R R}$. This is partly because the form $Q_{R R}$, or variants of it to be mentioned below, may be much easier to compute than $Q_{R M}$. In addition the identity $Q_{L R}=Q_{R M}$ has a variety of other implications, as we shall see. Let us first note the following.

Lemma 2. Let the standard conditons (1 N) and (2 N) be satisfied. Assume also that the matrix $\left(\begin{array}{cc}A & C \\ C & \end{array}\right)$ is nonsingular. Then $Q_{L R}$ is identically equal to $Q_{R R}$ if and only if $C=S B$. This is equivalent to the fact that the variable $Z$ is sufficient for $\left\{\mathscr{L}\left[\left.\binom{X}{Z} \right\rvert\, P\right] ; P \in \mathscr{D}_{0}\right\}$.

Proof. In the nonsingular case the quadratic form one minimizes to obtain $Q_{L R}$ achieves its minimum at the unique point $\hat{\zeta}$. We have seen above that the difference $Z-\hat{\zeta}$ has covariance matrix $(C-S B)^{\prime} M^{-1}(C-S B)$. This gives the first assertion. For the second assertion, let us show that the equality $C=S B$ implies the desired sufficiency. The converse will be proved below (Lemma 3) in a more general situation. The logarithm of likelihood ratio of a measure $P \in \mathscr{D}_{0}$ with expectation $\binom{S \zeta}{\zeta}$ to the measure $P_{0}$ has the form

$$
\zeta^{\prime} M_{1}^{-1}[Z-T(X-S Z)]-\frac{1}{2} \zeta^{\prime} M_{1}^{-1} \zeta,
$$

where $M_{1}$ and $T$ are the matrices used in the alternate expressions of quadratic forms used to obtain the form of $Q_{L R}$ above. The map $T$ is defined by $T M= (C-S B)^{\prime}$. Hence $T=0$ and the logarithm of likelihood ratio becomes simply $\zeta^{\prime} B^{-1} Z-\frac{1}{2} \zeta^{\prime} B^{-1} \zeta$. The sufficiency of $Z$ follows.

It is interesting that the sufficiency of $Z$ for the experiment $\left\{\mathscr{L}\left[\left.\binom{X}{Z} \right\rvert\, P\right]\right.$; $\left.P \in \mathscr{D}_{0}\right\}$ already implies many relations under conditions that are weaker than the standard conditions (1) and (2). For instance, it may be used to conclude to the existence of the map $S$. Alternately, assuming the existence of $S$, it may be used to conclude to joint normality of $X$ and $Z$. Let us take as basic assumptions the following

Standard Condition (3N). If $P \in \mathscr{D}_{0}$ then $Z-\zeta$ is Gaussian $\mathscr{N}(0, B)$ and $X-\xi$ is Gaussian $\mathscr{N}(0, A)$. The matrices $A$ and $B$ are independent of $P \in \mathscr{D}_{0}$. Furthermore $B$ is nonsingular.

Standard Condition $(\mathbf{4 N})$. As $P$ varies through $\mathscr{D}_{0}$, the expectation $\zeta=E_{p} Z$ ranges through the entire space $\mathscr{Z}$. There is a $P_{0} \in \mathscr{D}_{0}$ such that $E_{P_{0}}\binom{X}{Z}=\binom{0}{0}$.

Standard Condition $(5 \mathrm{~N})$. For the family $\mathscr{L}\left[\left.\binom{X}{Z} \right\rvert\, P\right] ; P \in \mathscr{D}_{0}$, the vector $Z$ is a sufficient statistic.

Standard Condition $(\mathbf{6 N})$. For the particular measure $P_{0}$ the joint distribution $\mathscr{L}\left[\left.\binom{X}{Z} \right\rvert\, P_{0}\right]$ is Gaussian.

Standard Condition $(\mathbf{7 N})$. There is a linear map $S$ from $\mathscr{Z}$ to $\mathscr{X}$ such that $P \in \mathscr{D}_{0}$ implies $E_{P} X=S\left(E_{P} Z\right)$.

Lemma 3. Let the standard conditions ( 3 N ) to ( 5 N ) hold. Then conditions ( 6 N ) and ( 7 N ) are equivalent. If either one of them holds, so do the standard conditions ( 1 N ) and ( 2 N ) for $P$ restricted to lie in $\mathscr{D}_{0}$ and in addition the vector $Z$ and $X-S Z$ are independent for all $P \in \mathscr{D}_{0}$.

Proof. We shall use the norm $|\cdot|$ and $\|\cdot\|$ defined earlier. According to the sufficiency condition ( 5 N ), for every $P \in \mathscr{D}_{0}$ one can write

$$
\frac{d P}{d P_{0}}=\frac{d \mathscr{L}(Z \mid P)}{d \mathscr{L}\left(Z \mid P_{0}\right)}
$$

According to Condition $(3 \mathrm{~N})$, this Radon-Nikodym density is

$$
\frac{d P}{d P_{0}}=\exp \left\{Z^{\prime} B^{-1} \zeta-\frac{1}{2} \zeta^{\prime} B^{-1} \zeta\right\}
$$

Suppose now that Condition $(7 \mathrm{~N})$ holds and compute $E_{P} \exp \{v X\}$ for some $v \in \mathscr{X}^{\prime}$. According to Condition (3 N), this may be written

$$
E_{P} \exp \{v X\}=\exp \left\{v S \zeta+\frac{1}{2}\|v\|^{2}\right\} .
$$

(Here $\|v\|$ is the norm of $v$ in $\mathscr{X}^{\prime}$, namely $\|v\|^{2}=v A v^{\prime}$.) The sufficiency property gives an alternate expression

$$
E_{P} \exp \{v X\}=E_{P_{0}} \exp \left\{v X+Z^{\prime} B^{-1} \zeta-\frac{1}{2} \zeta^{\prime} B^{-1} \zeta\right\}
$$

Equating the two, one obtains

$$
E_{P_{0}} \exp \left\{v X+Z^{\prime} B^{-1} \zeta\right\}=\exp \left\{\frac{1}{2}\left[|\zeta|^{2}+\|v\|^{2}+2 v S \zeta\right]\right\} .
$$

This shows that, under $P_{0}$, the vectors $X$ and $Z$ are jointly normally distributed, with $E\left[(v X)\left(Z^{\prime} B^{-1} \zeta\right)\right]=v S \zeta$ and therefore $C=S B$. Thus conditions $(3 \mathrm{~N}),(4 \mathrm{~N}),(5 \mathrm{~N})$, and $(7 \mathrm{~N})$ imply that Condition $(6 \mathrm{~N})$ holds. The result so obtained also completes the proof of Lemma 2.

Now suppose that instead of ( 7 N ) one assumes that ( 6 N ) holds. Then, for $v \in \mathscr{X}^{\prime}$ and $w \in \mathscr{Z}$ one has

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{P} \exp \left\{v X+w^{\prime} Z\right\}= & E_{P_{0}} \exp \left\{v X+Z^{\prime}\left(w+B^{-1} \zeta\right)-\frac{1}{2} \zeta^{\prime} B^{-1} \zeta\right\} \\
= & \exp \left\{\frac{1}{2}\|v\|^{2}+\frac{1}{2}\left[\left(w+B^{-1} \zeta\right)^{\prime} B\left(w+B^{-1} \zeta\right)-\frac{1}{2} \zeta^{\prime} B^{-1} \zeta\right]\right. \\
& \left.+v C\left(w+B^{-1} \zeta\right)\right\}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $C=E X Z^{\prime}$. It follows that $X$ and $Z$ are jointly Gaussian for all $P \in \mathscr{D}_{0}$. Also, putting $w=0$ in this formula, one obtains

$$
E_{P} \exp \{v X\}=\exp \left\{\frac{1}{2}\left[\|v\|^{2}+2 v C B^{-1} \zeta\right]\right\}
$$

This implies $E_{P} X=C B^{-1} \zeta$ and therefore $E_{P} X=S \zeta$ for $C=S B$. The proof of the lemma is now complete.

To state some consequences, let us say that ( $N_{0}$ ) holds if the conditions ( 3 N ) to ( 5 N ) are satisfied and if either one (and therefore both) of ( 6 N ) and ( 7 N ) also holds. Let $\Pi$ be the projection of $\mathscr{X}$ onto its subspace $S \mathscr{Z}$ orthogonally for the norm $\|\cdot\|$.

Corollary 1. Let ( $N_{0}$ ) hold. Then the Pearson statistic $Q_{P}=\|X-S Z\|^{2}$ can be written

$$
Q_{P}=Q_{F}+\|\Pi X-S Z\|^{2}
$$

where $Q_{F}=\|X-\Pi X\|^{2}$ and where $X-\Pi X$ and $\Pi X-S Z$ are independent for $P \in \mathscr{D}_{0}$.

Proof. The formula $Q_{P}=Q_{F}+\|\Pi X-S Z\|^{2}$ is Pythagoras' formula. By construction $X-\Pi X$ and $\Pi X$ are uncorrelated, and therefore independent. Thus, it will be sufficient to show that $X-\Pi X$ and $S Z$ are independent. However,

$$
E_{P_{0}}(X-\Pi X) Z^{\prime} S^{\prime}=(I-\Pi)\left(E_{P_{0}} X Z^{\prime}\right) S^{\prime}=(I-\Pi) C S^{\prime}=(I-\Pi) S B S^{\prime} .
$$

This vanishes since the range of $S$ is the same as the range of $\Pi$.
Corollary 2. When ( $N_{0}$ ) holds, the statistic $Q_{F}$ has a $\chi^{2}$ distribution with number of degrees of freedom $\operatorname{rank} A-\operatorname{dim} S \mathscr{Z}$. The independent term $\|\Pi X-S Z\|^{2}$ has the same distribution as a sum $\sum_{j=1}^{k} c_{j} w_{j}^{2}$, where the $w_{j}$ are independent, Gaussian $\mathcal{N}(0,1)$, where $0 \leq c_{j} \leq 1$ and where the number $k$ is $\operatorname{dim} S \mathfrak{Z}$.

Proof. The sufficiency condition ( 5 N ) insures that $A$ cannot be singular on $S \mathscr{Z}$. The first assertion follows easily. For the second assertion note that

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{P_{0}}(\Pi X-S Z)(\Pi X-S Z)^{\prime} & =\Pi A \Pi^{\prime}-\Pi S B S^{\prime} \Pi^{\prime} \\
& =\Pi\left(A-S B S^{\prime}\right) \Pi^{\prime} \\
& \leq \Pi A \Pi^{\prime}
\end{aligned}
$$

where the inequality means that the difference between the two matrices is positive semi-definite. This implies that the eigenvalues of $A-S B S^{\prime}$ are smaller than those of $A$ and implies the assertion concerning the size of the numbers $c_{j}$.

Corollary 3. Let ( $N_{0}$ ) hold and let the matrix $\left(\begin{array}{cc}A & C \\ C & B\end{array}\right)$ be nonsingular. Then for $P \in \mathscr{D}_{0}$,

$$
Q_{L R}=Q_{P}+(X-S Z)^{\prime} K(X-S Z),
$$

where $K=A^{-1} S\left[B^{-1}-S^{\prime} A^{-1} S\right]^{-1} S^{\prime} A^{-1}$. The variable $Q_{L R}$ has a $\chi^{2}$ distribution with a number of degrees of freedom equal to $\operatorname{dim} \mathscr{X}$.

Proof. Let $M=E_{P_{0}}(X-S Z)(X-S Z)^{\prime}$. We have already observed that $M^{-1}=A^{-1}+K$ with $K=A^{-1} C\left(B-C^{\prime} A^{-1} C\right)^{-1} C^{\prime} A^{-1}$ and $C=S B$. Replacing all occurrences of $C$ by $S B$ yields the alternate form of $K$ used here. The result follows.

As mentioned previously, there is no great difficulty in making sure that both matrices $A$ and $B$ are nonsingular. However the combined matrix $\left(\begin{array}{ll}A & C \\ C & B\end{array}\right)$ may still be singular. In this case $M$ is also singular and the number of degrees of freedom of $Q_{L R}$ is not $\operatorname{dim} \mathscr{X}$ but $\operatorname{rank} M$.

Note, however, that when $M$ is singular the random vector $X-S Z$ takes its values in the range of $M$. Thus there is no difficulty in defining ( $X- S Z)^{\prime} M^{-1}(X-S Z)$. Also, by ( 5 N ), the matrix $A$ is necessarily nonsingular on the range of $S$. Therefore $A^{-1} S$ is always defined and so is $B^{-1}-S^{\prime} A^{-1} S$. The procedure by which we obtained the identity $M^{-1}=A^{-1}+K$ shows also that

$$
Q_{L R}=\|X-S X\|^{2}+\inf _{\phi}(W-\phi)^{\prime} D^{-1}(W-\phi)
$$

where $D$ is the covariance matrix of $W=Z-R X$ and $\phi=(I-R S) \zeta$ with $\zeta \in \mathscr{Z}$.

Here again, because of the absolute continuity implied by ( 5 N ), the vector $W-\phi$ takes values in the range of $D$ for all $P \in \mathscr{D}_{0}$ with a corresponding $\zeta \in \mathscr{Z}$. One concludes that, whether the matrix $M$ is singular or not, the equality $M^{-1}=A^{-1}+K$ always makes sense on the range where $X-S Z$ takes its values under $\mathscr{D}_{0}$. For values outside this range $Q_{L R}$ should be taken equal to infinity and the same must be done for the alternate form $Q_{R R}$. We shall now describe a possible asymptotic analogue of strictly Gaussian situation just studied here. For this, let us revert to a net $\left\{\mathscr{E}_{v}\right\}$ of experiments $\mathscr{E}_{v}=\left\{P_{\theta, v} ; \theta \in \Theta_{v}\right\}$ with the index $v$ suppressed from the notation, as usual.

Let us assume that the vectors $X$ and the maps $\xi$ satisfy all the assumptions (1) to (5) of the present section when the distributions are induced by the true value $\theta \in \Theta$ of the parameter. At the beginning of this section, it was convenient to assume that the true value $\theta$ was such that $\xi(\theta)$ was the origin of $\mathscr{X}$. This is no longer convenient here. Thus we shall let $\xi(\theta)$ be any element of $\mathscr{X}$. However
we shall still assume that $\mathscr{L}\left\{X-\xi(\theta) \mid P_{\theta}\right\}$ satisfies the asymptotic normality requirement, with a bound on the dimension of $\mathscr{X}$, described in Assumption 7.

Recall that Proposition 2 of Section 6 involves a certain estimate $T^{*}$, with values in $\Theta$ itself. At all favorable points satisfying Condition (H), this estimate is asymptotically sufficient, distinguished, and asymptotically normally distributed. This suggests that the arguments carried out in Lemma 2 and 3 and their corollaries may become approximately applicable to tests of goodness of fit based on quadratic expressions in $X-\xi\left(T^{*}\right)$.

To make this precise, measure distances in $\mathscr{X}$ according to a norm $\|x\|^{2}= x^{\prime} A^{-1} x$ where $A^{-1}$ is the inverse of the covariance of a good normal approximation to $\mathscr{L}\left[X-\xi(\theta) \mid P_{\theta}\right]$. Measure distances in $\Theta$ or $\mathscr{M}_{1}(\Theta)$ according to the quadratic form $\Gamma_{\theta}$ of Section 6, Lemma 2.

We have mentioned the estimate $T^{*}$ of Proposition 2, Section 6. One can also use the centerings $\hat{Z}$ of Section 6. For favorable points $\theta$ which satisfy (H), the difference $\Gamma_{\theta}\left(\hat{Z}-\delta_{T^{*}}\right)$ tends to zero in $P_{\theta}$ probability. Thus except for the fact that under the conditions used here $\Theta$ has no linear structure of its own, our next assumption is an asymptotic normality assumption for the pair $\left[X-\xi(\theta), T^{*}-\theta\right]$.

Assumption 8. The true value $\theta$ is a favorable point satisfying (H). There are centered Gaussian distributions G such that the dual Lipschitz distance between $G$ and $\mathscr{L}\left\{\left[X-\xi(\theta), \hat{Z}-\delta_{\theta}\right] \mid P_{\theta}\right\}$ tends to zero.

This is analogous to a combination of the conditions ( 5 N ) and ( 6 N ) used for Lemma 3.

One immediate consequence of Assumptions 7 and 8 is that if $t \in \Theta$ is such that $q(t, \theta)$ remains bounded, then $\mathscr{L}\left\{\left[X-\xi(\theta), \hat{Z}-\delta_{\theta}\right] \mid P_{t}\right\}$ is also asymptotically approximable by a Gaussian distribution, with the same covariance structures as under $P_{\theta}$ and with expectations modified according to the prescriptions which follow Proposition 6, Chapter 6.

In the present situation, the role of the function $\xi$ is to provide an approximate centering of the variables $X$. This is expressed by Assumption 7 for the true value $\theta$. One could strength Assumption 7, requiring that $\mathscr{L}\left\{X-\xi(t) \mid P_{t}\right\}$ be asymptotically centered Gaussian for all $t$ such that $q(t, \theta)$ remains bounded. We shall refer to this as Assumption 7S. Another possibility is to use a symmetry requirement.

Assumption 9. If $q(t, \theta)$ remains bounded then the dual Lipschitz distance between $\mathscr{L}\left\{X-\xi(t) \mid P_{t}\right\}$ and $\mathscr{L}\left\{\xi(t)-X \mid P_{t}\right\}$ tends to zero.

Whenever Assumptions 7,8, and 9 are satisfied, so is 7 S . We are now in a position to make assertions similar to the basic ones of Lemmas 2 and 3.

Proposition 6. Let Assumptions 7,8, and 9 be satisfied. Then, for any $t \in \Theta$ such that $q(t, \theta)$ remains bounded, the distributions $\mathscr{L}\left\{X-\xi\left(T^{*}\right) \mid P_{t}\right\}$ are asymptotically approximable by centered Gaussian distributions with covariance structure independent of $t$. Furthermore the variables $X-\xi\left(T^{*}\right)$ and $T^{*}$ are asymptotically independent.
(The asymptotic independence means that the joint distributions are approx-



imable by product distributions in the sense of dual Lipschitz distances computed for the metrics used here.)

Proof. According to Section 6, the quantities $\Gamma_{\theta}\left(\hat{Z}-\delta_{T^{*}}\right)$ tend to zero in probability. Also the logarithms of likelihood ratios $\Lambda(t, \theta)$ are approximable by

$$
\left\langle\hat{Z}-\delta_{\theta}, \delta_{t}-\delta_{\theta}\right\rangle_{\Gamma_{\theta}}-\frac{1}{2} \Gamma_{\theta}\left(\delta_{t}-\delta_{\theta}\right)
$$

According the Assumption 8, the joint distributions $\mathscr{L}\left\{\left[X-\xi(\theta), \hat{Z}-\delta_{\theta}\right] \mid P_{\theta}\right\}$ are approximable by a certain centered Gaussian distribution, say $G_{\theta}$. Under $P_{t}$, the distribution $\mathscr{L}\left\{\left[X-\xi(\theta), \hat{Z}-\delta_{\theta}\right] \mid P_{t}\right\}$ is approximable by a Gaussian distribution with the same covariance structure as $G_{\theta}$. The expectation of $X-\xi(\theta)$ is shifted by an amount equal to the covariance, under $G_{\theta}$, of $X-\xi(\theta)$ and $\left\langle\hat{Z}-\delta_{\theta}, \delta_{t}-\delta_{\theta}\right\rangle_{\Gamma_{\theta}}$. According to Assumption 9, this means that $\xi(t)-\xi(\theta)$ is approximable by $S\left(\delta_{t}-\delta_{\theta}\right)$ where $S$ is the linear map defined by

$$
S\left(\delta_{t}-\delta_{\theta}\right)=E[X-\xi(\theta)]\left\langle\hat{Z}-\delta_{\theta}, \delta_{t}-\delta_{\theta}\right\rangle_{\Gamma_{\theta}}
$$

where the expectation is taken under $G_{\theta}$. For the norms used here one may write

$$
\left\|S\left(\delta_{t}-\delta_{\theta}\right)\right\|^{2} \leq E\|X-\xi(\theta)\|^{2} \Gamma_{\theta}\left(\delta_{t}-\delta_{\theta}\right)
$$

Therefore, since $\Gamma_{\theta}\left(\delta_{T^{*}}-\delta_{\theta}\right)$ is bounded in probability, one can also say that

$$
\xi\left(T^{*}\right)-\xi(\theta)-S\left(\delta_{T^{*}}-\delta_{\theta}\right)
$$

tends to zero in probability. Replacing $\delta_{T^{*}}$ by $\hat{Z}$, this implies the desired asymptotically normal behavior for $X-\xi\left(T^{*}\right)$.

Now the approximating normal distributions of $X-\xi\left(T^{*}\right)$ can be taken centered, independently of which $P_{t}$ is used. Thus the covariances between $X-\xi\left(T^{*}\right)$ and the inner product $\left\langle\hat{Z}-\delta_{\theta}, \delta_{t}-\delta_{\theta}\right\rangle_{\Gamma_{\theta}}$ are all asymptotically negligible. This gives the asserted independence and concludes the proof of the proposition.

Remark. Here have used Assumptions 7, 8, and 9. One could instead assume that 7 S holds, that $\theta$ is favorable and satisfies $(\mathrm{H})$, and that there are linear maps $S$ satisfying the conditions obtained in the course of the preceding proof. This may be a useful alternative if verification of the joint asymptotic normality of Assumption 8 is difficult.

To pass to the testing problems, the preceding argument suggests the use of a test statistic $Q=\left[X-\xi\left(T^{*}\right)\right]^{\prime} \hat{M}_{1}\left[X-\xi\left(T^{*}\right)\right]$ where $\hat{M}_{1}$ is a consistent estimate of the inverse of the covariance matrix of $X-\xi\left(T^{*}\right)$ under a Gaussian approximation.
Here we have suitable estimates of the inverse covariance matrices for the variables $\hat{Z}$ or $T^{*}$. They are given by the $\tilde{\Gamma}$ of Section 6. We may also have suitable
estimates, say $\widehat{A_{1}}$, for the inverse covariance matrices $A^{-1}$ of the normal approximations for $X$. However $X-\xi\left(T^{*}\right)$ itself may tend to degenerate. In such a case it may be difficult, or dangerous, to try to find estimates of its inverse covariance matrix.

Thus, for the remainder of this section we shall assume that the Gaussian approximations to $\mathscr{L}\left\{X-\xi\left(T^{*}\right) \mid P_{\theta}\right\}$ do not tend to degenerate in the space $\mathscr{X}$ metrized by $\|\cdot\|$ with $\|x\|^{2}=x^{\prime} A^{-1} x$.

Under this supplementary assumption, and assuming that the maps $\xi$ are known, it is possible to find estimates of the linear maps $S$ which occur in the proof of Proposition 6.

A procedure is as follows. Consider the sets $S_{\omega}$ used in the constructions of Section 6.
