For each $S_{\omega}$ one can make a linear map $L_{\omega}$ of $\mathscr{M}_{0}\left(S_{\omega}\right)$ into $\mathscr{X}$ by writing $L_{\omega}\left(\delta_{s}-\delta_{\omega}\right)=\xi(s)-\xi(\omega)$. Let $\hat{\omega}$ be a point of $\Omega$ closest to $T^{*}$ (or if one so wishes, let $\hat{\omega}$ be equal to the auxiliary estimate $\hat{\theta})$. Then $\hat{S}=L_{\omega}$ is a linear map from $\hat{Z}=\mathscr{M}_{0}\left(S_{\hat{\omega}}\right)$ to $\mathscr{X}$. On $\mathscr{M}_{0}\left(S_{\hat{\omega}}\right)$ itself we have, from Section 6, a quadratic form $\tilde{\Gamma}$ estimate of the "inverse covariance matrix" of $\hat{Z}$. In the basis supplied by $S_{\hat{\omega}}$ this $\tilde{\Gamma}$ can be expressed as a matrix, say $\widehat{B}_{1}$.

Suppose also given a suitable estimate $\hat{A_{1}}$ of $A^{-1}$ on $\mathscr{X}$. Then one can form $\hat{B}_{1}-\hat{S}^{\prime} \hat{A}_{1} \hat{S}$ on $\hat{Z}$ and a corresponding matrix $\hat{K}=\hat{A_{1}} \hat{S}\left[\hat{B}_{1}-\hat{S}^{\prime} \hat{A_{1}} \hat{S}\right]^{-1} \hat{S}^{\prime} \hat{A}_{1}$ on $\mathscr{X}$. Then $\hat{A}_{1}+\hat{K}$ will be an estimate of the covariance matrix in an approximating Gaussian distribution for $X-\xi\left(T^{*}\right)$. Alternatively, one may use the estimates $\left[\hat{A}_{1}^{-1}-\hat{S} \hat{B}_{1}^{-1} \hat{S}^{\prime}\right]^{-1}$ for the inverse covariance of $X-\xi\left(T^{*}\right)$. A choice between the two possible procedures can usually be made on the basis of ease in computability. If the dimension of $\mathscr{Z}$ is much smaller than that of $\mathscr{X}$, computation of $\hat{A}_{1}+\hat{K}$ may be easier than the alternative procedure.

Note that the foregoing argument takes place entirely under the assumption that the distributions are induced by the $\left\{P_{t} ; t \in \Theta\right\}$ of the experiments $\mathscr{E}$, but that the aim of the construction is precisely to test whether the $P_{t}$ fit the observations. In other words, Proposition 6 corresponds to Lemmas 2 and 3 proved for the class $\mathscr{D}_{0}$ in the normal case. The optimality properties proved in the exact Gaussian case can be translated into asymptotic optimality properties for testing $\mathscr{E}$ against a larger class $\mathscr{E}_{1}$ provided that one assumes that under $\mathscr{E}_{1}$ as well as under $\mathscr{E}$ the approximating Gaussian distributions for $X, T^{*}$ give a local approximation to the experiments $\mathscr{E}_{1}$. Otherwise one can still claim certain optimality results, but only for tests restricted by continuity requirements in ( $X, T^{*}$ ). More precisely let ( $X_{1}, T_{1}$ ) be random variables having a joint Gaussian distribution, approximation to the distribution of ( $X, T^{*}$ ) under the alternatives. Then the optimality properties of the exact Gaussian case will hold approximately for ( $X, T^{*}$ ) if one takes into consideration only those tests $\phi$ such that $\phi\left(X, T^{*}\right)$ and $\phi\left(X_{1}, T_{1}\right)$ have approximately the same distribution. However it is easy to construct examples where, for instance, $T^{*}$ gives very specific information on the alternatives and where this information is entirely missing in ( $X_{1}, T_{1}$ ).

In practical situations, finding the estimate $\hat{K}$ may be considered a bother-
some task and one may be tempted to use the quadratic $Q_{P}=\left[X-\xi\left(T^{*}\right)\right]^{\prime} \hat{A_{1}}\left[X-\xi\left(T^{*}\right)\right]$. The asymptotic behavior of this can be obtained from Corollary 2 of Lemma 2. Note also that

$$
Q_{F} \leq Q_{P} \leq\left[X-\xi\left(T^{*}\right)\right]^{\prime}\left[\hat{A}_{1}+\hat{K}\right]\left[X-\xi\left(T^{*}\right)\right]=Q_{R R}
$$

with $Q_{F}=\inf _{t}[X-\xi(t)]^{\prime} \hat{A}_{1}\left[X-\xi\left(T^{*}\right)\right]$. Thus, $Q_{P}$ is bracketed by two statistics with approximate $\chi^{2}$ distributions whose degrees of freedom are, respectively, $\operatorname{dim} \mathscr{X}-\operatorname{rank} S$ and $\operatorname{dim} \mathscr{X}$. These inequalities may make it possible to use $Q_{P}$ to obtain tests with either conservative or optimistic level. However, little can be said about loss in power.

Finally, one may want to evaluate the power of the tests based on $Q_{R R}$. If the alternatives considered are measures $P^{\prime}$ such that ( $P^{\prime}, P_{\theta}$ ) is asymptotically Gaussian with logarithm of likelihood ratio $\Lambda=\log \left[d P^{\prime} / d P_{\theta}\right]$, then $Q_{R R}=\left[X-\xi\left(T^{*}\right)\right]^{\prime}\left(\hat{A_{1}}+\hat{K}\right)\left[X-\xi\left(T^{*}\right)\right]$ will have, under $P^{\prime}$, a distribution approximable by a noncentral $\chi^{2}$ with noncentrality parameter $\eta^{\prime}\left(A^{-1}+K\right) \eta$, where $\eta$ is the covariance of $\Lambda$ and $X-\xi\left(T^{*}\right)$ in the approximating Gaussian distributions.

## CHAPTER 12

## Posterior Distributions and Bayes Solutions

## 1. Introduction

In the first three chapters of this work, prior distributions with finite support and the corresponding Bayes procedures were used to establish some of the main theorems relating to comparison of experiments. Bayes procedures with respect to more general prior distributions have played a very important role in the development of statistics, including its asymptotic theory.

For experiments carried out in a single stage the use of such general priors is not entirely indispensable for the establishment of the main results. However, there are many problems where a glance at posterior distributions, for suitable priors, yields immediately interesting information. Also, this technique can hardly be avoided in sequential analysis.
This chapter is not intended to give a full account of the theory relating to Bayes procedures. It gives only a few facts on convergence of posterior distributions, relying mostly on properties of convergence of joint distributions. A few additional facts will be found in the chapter on independent identically distributed observations, where, for instance, properties of convergence for individual values of the parameter $\theta$ will be encountered.

The present chapter is divided as follows. Section 2 recalls a few inequalities relating $L_{1}$-distances of posterior distributions to $L_{1}$-distances of joint distributions. It also contains an argument which often permits "localization" of the
computations. Section 3 describes some asymptotic properties of the risk functions of Bayes procedures, with a general lemma which says that, under suitable circumstances, the choice of prior distributions is asymptotically unimportant. Section 4 deals with the convergence of posterior distributions to Gaussian ones. It contains a theorem related to what is called the Bernsteinvon Mises theorem.

All the way through we have assumed that the prior distributions are finite positive measures of arbitrary finite mass, and not necessarily probability measures. This is for technical reasons. It is convenient to be able to truncate measures or to multiply them by constants. The main arguments of Sections 2 and 3 allow the masses to tend to infinity. Unfortunately the more involved arguments of Section 4 do not allow this flexibility.

## 2. Inequalities on Conditional Distributions

Let $\mathscr{A}$ be a $\sigma$-field carried by a set $\mathscr{X}$ and let $\mathscr{B}$ denote another $\sigma$-field on a set $\Theta$. Conditional distributions of $\theta$ given $x$ are given by Markov kernels $x \mapsto F_{x}$ which are maps from $\mathscr{X}$ to the space of probability measures on $(\Theta, \mathscr{B})$ subject to the condition that for each $B \in \mathscr{B}$ the real function $x \mapsto F_{x}(B)$ is $\mathscr{A}$-measurable.

Let $S$ be a finite positive measure on the product $\sigma$-field $\mathscr{A} \times \mathscr{B}$. The projection, or marginal, of $S$ on $(\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{A})$ is a certain measure $S^{\prime}$. Similarly $S$ has a marginal $S^{\prime \prime}$ on $(\Theta, \mathscr{B})$. For such a measure, we shall call disintegration of $S$ with respect to the projection on $\mathscr{X}$ any Markov kernel $x \mapsto F_{x}$ such that

$$
S(d x, d \theta)=S^{\prime}(d x) F_{x}(d \theta)
$$

Such disintegrations do not always exist. However we shall be concerned here only with situations in which they do exist and are essentially uniquely defined.

One situation in which disintegrations do exist is the one in which $S$ is cross dominated, in the sense that $S$ is absolutely continuous with respect to the product $S^{\prime} \otimes S^{\prime \prime}$ of its marginals. In this case $S$ may be written in the form

$$
S(d x, d \theta)=f(x, \theta) S^{\prime}(d x) S^{\prime \prime}(d \theta)
$$

for a suitable positive jointly measurable function $f$. One can then rewrite $S$ as $S(d x, d \theta)=S^{\prime}(d x) F_{x}(d \theta)=P_{\theta}(d x) S^{\prime \prime}(d \theta)$, taking for instance

$$
F_{x}(d \theta)=\frac{f(x, \theta)}{f^{\prime}(x)} S^{\prime \prime}(d \theta)
$$

with $f^{\prime}(x)=\int f(x, \theta) S^{\prime \prime}(d \theta)$.
This case distinguishes itself from the general case in which disintegrations exist by the strong measurability of the map $x \mapsto F_{x}$. Specifically there is a set $A \in \mathscr{A}$ such that $S^{\prime}(\mathscr{X} \backslash A)=0$ and such that the range $\left\{F_{x} ; x \in A\right\}$ is separable for the $L_{1}$-norm. Another case in which disintegrations exist is given by the following lemma.

Lemma 1. Assume that $\Theta$ is a Borel subset of a Polish space and that $\mathscr{B}$ is the $\sigma$-field of Borel subsets of $\Theta$. Let $S$ be a finite measure on $\mathscr{A} \times \mathscr{B}$. Then $S$ admits a disintegration in the form $S(d x, d \theta)=S^{\prime}(d x) F_{x}(d \theta)$. Furthermore if $x \mapsto F_{x}^{*}$ provides another such disintegration then the $L_{1}$-norm $\left\|F_{x}^{*}-F_{x}\right\|$ vanishes almost everywhere for $S^{\prime}$.

Proof. Since this is a well-known result we shall just sketch a proof. Let $C$ be the space of bounded continuous functions on $\Theta$. If there is a Markov kernel $x \mapsto F_{x}$ yielding the desired disintegration, then for each $\gamma \in C$, the integral $\int \gamma(\theta) F_{x}(d \theta)$ gives a version of the conditional expectation of $\gamma$ given the $\sigma$-field $\mathscr{A}$ for the measure $S$.

Now note that, according to Lusin, there is an increasing sequence of compacts $K_{n} \subset \Theta$ such that $S\left(\mathscr{X} \times K_{n}\right)$ increases to the total mass $\|S\|$ of $S$. Let $D$ be a countable set of elements $v \in C$ such that $0 \leq v \leq 1$ and such that for each $K_{n}$ the set $D$ is uniformly dense in the interval $[0,1]$ of the space $C\left(K_{n}\right)$ of continuous functions on $K_{n}$. Such a set exists since each $C\left(K_{n}\right)$ is separable. Assume that the function 1 belongs to $D$ and let $V$ be the space of finite rational combinations of elements of $D$.

Select an integer $n$. For each $v \in V$ let $T_{n} v$ be a bounded $\mathscr{A}$-measurable function which is a conditional expectation of $v I_{K_{n}}$ for the measure $S$. Since $V$ is countable one may select the representatives $T_{n} v$ so that the map $v \mapsto T_{n} v$ is rationally linear, such that $v_{1} \leq v_{2}$ implies $T_{n} v_{1} \leq T_{n} v_{2}$ and such that $\left\|T_{n} v\right\| \leq\|v\|$ for all $v \in V$. One may also assume that $T_{n} v \leq T_{n+1} v$ for all positive elements $v \in V$ and that $\lim _{n} T_{n} 1=1$.

The $T_{n}$ extend to the entire space $C$ by continuity. They become positive linear maps. The evaluations $v \mapsto\left(T_{n} v\right)(x)$ at points $x \in \mathscr{X}$ become positive linear functionals of norm at most unity.

Now, if a filter or net $\gamma_{\nu} \in C$ decreases to zero pointwise on $\Theta$, the functions $\gamma_{v} I_{K_{n}}$ decrease to zero uniformly on $\Theta$. Thus each $v \mapsto\left(T_{n} v\right)(x)$ is representable by an integral, say $\left(T_{n} v\right)(x)=\int v(\theta) M_{n}(x, d \theta)$ where $M_{n}(x, d \theta)$ is a positive Radon measure carried by the compact $K_{n} \subset \Theta$. As $n$ increases the $M_{n}(x, \cdot)$ increase to a limit $F_{x}$ which yields the desired kernel.

Whether the conditions of Lemma 1 are satisfied or whether the measure $S$ is cross dominated, the effective determination of $x \mapsto F_{x}$ requires only a countable number of operations with a countable number of $\mathscr{A}$-measurable real functions. Thus, in both cases it is no great loss of generality to assume that $\mathscr{A}$ itself is countably generated. If so, by suitable completion, one could replace ( $\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{A}$ ) by a Polish space with its Borel sets. Thus, for the remainder of this chapter we shall assume that the following standard Polish assumption is satisfied.

The Polish Assumption. The spaces $(\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{A})$ and $(\Theta, \mathscr{B})$ are Borel subsets of Polish spaces with their $\sigma$-fields of Borel subsets.

One could proceed with weaker requirements, especially in Section 3. However the gain in generality would be out of line with the restrictions of Section 4 as well as with the main intent of this chapter.

Consider then a pair $(\mathscr{X}, \Theta),(\mathscr{A}, \mathscr{B})$ subject to the Polish assumptions, and two finite positive measures $S_{i}, i=1,2$ on $\mathscr{A} \times \mathscr{B}$. They have disintegration of the form

$$
S_{i}(d x, d \theta)=S_{i}^{\prime}(d x) F_{x, i}(d \theta)
$$

We shall be interested in the differences of the conditional distributions $F_{x, i}$ as measured by expressions of the type $\int\left\|F_{x, 1}-F_{x, 2}\right\| S_{i}^{\prime}(d x)$ where the norms under the integral sign are $L_{1}$-norms.

To investigate these it is convenient to introduce a third measure, say $S$, which dominates both $S_{1}$ and $S_{2}$ so that one may write

$$
S_{i}(d x, d \theta)=\phi_{i}(x, \theta) S(d x, d \theta)
$$

The measure $S$ itself admits a disintegration $S(d x, d \theta)=S^{\prime}(d x) F_{x}(d \theta)$. Using this it follows that a possible disintegration of $S_{i}$ is given by the relation

$$
\phi_{i}^{\prime}(x) F_{x, i}(d \theta)=\phi_{i}(x, \theta) F_{x}(d \theta)
$$

with

$$
\phi_{i}^{\prime}(x)=\int \phi_{i}(x, \theta) F_{x}(d \theta)
$$

A first, crude but useful, result is given by the following lemma, which duplicates a result already mentioned in Chapter 4.

Lemma 2. Let $S_{1}$ and $S_{2}$ be as described and let $J$ be the integral

$$
J=\frac{1}{2} \int\left\|F_{x, 1}-F_{x, 2}\right\|\left[S_{1}^{\prime}(d x)+S_{2}^{\prime}(d x)\right]
$$

Then

$$
\left\|S_{1}-S_{2}\right\|-\left\|S_{1}^{\prime}-S_{2}^{\prime}\right\| \leq J \leq\left\|S_{1}-S_{2}\right\|+\left\|S_{1}^{\prime}-S_{2}^{\prime}\right\| .
$$

Proof. In the present case the function $x \mapsto\left\|F_{x, 1}-F_{x, 2}\right\|$ is a measurable function obtainable as a supremum over a countable subset of the space $C^{b}(\Theta)$ of bounded continuous functions on $\Theta$.

To obtain the result it is enough to use the relations (1) and (2) and the identity

$$
\phi_{1}^{\prime} F_{1}-\phi_{2}^{\prime} F_{2}=\frac{1}{2}\left(\phi_{1}^{\prime}+\phi_{2}^{\prime}\right)\left(F_{1}-F_{2}\right)+\frac{1}{2}\left(\phi_{1}^{\prime}-\phi_{2}^{\prime}\right)\left(F_{1}+F_{2}\right)
$$

where, for simplicity, the arguments $x$ and $\theta$ have been omitted. Integrating on $\theta$ first and then on $x$ gives the desired relations.

As can easily be seen, the inequality of Lemma 2 does not capture some essential features of the situation, since, for instance, one could always modify the distributions so that $S_{1}^{\prime}$ and $S_{2}^{\prime}$ are the same without modifying at all the conditional kernels $x \mapsto F_{x, i}$. Here is another relation which is also very usable, and for which the above objection to Lemma 2 does not arise.

Let $A_{i}$ be the set $A_{i}=\left\{x ; 0<\phi_{i}^{\prime}(x)<\infty\right\}$ and let $A=A_{1} \cap A_{2}$. For $x \in A$ let $r$ be the function

$$
\begin{aligned}
r(x, \theta) & =\frac{\phi_{2}(x, \theta)}{\phi_{1}(x, \theta)} \frac{\phi_{1}^{\prime}(x)}{\phi_{2}^{\prime}(x)} \\
& =\int \frac{\phi_{2}(x, \theta)}{\phi_{2}(x, \xi)} \frac{\phi_{1}(x, \xi)}{\phi_{1}(x, \theta)} F_{x, 2}(d \xi)
\end{aligned}
$$

where, in the last expression the integrand is taken equal to zero if either $\phi_{2}(x, \xi)=0$ or $\phi_{1}(x, \theta)=0$. Since for every $x \in A$ the measures $F_{x, i}$ are probability measures, the relations given in Chapter 4 yield that

$$
\left\|F_{x, 1}-F_{x, 2}\right\|=2 \int\{1-[1 \wedge r(x, \theta)]\} F_{x, 1}(d \theta)
$$

To translate this into a statement directly applicable to passages to the limit, consider not one pair $(\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{A}),(\Theta, \mathscr{B})$ with measures $S_{i}$ but a sequence, or a net of such structures. In this case all the entities involved acquire an additional subscript, say $v$, whose function is to tend to infinity along the net.

Note also that $F_{x, i}$ is almost uniquely defined on $A_{i}$ but that $F_{x, 1}$, for instance, could be taken arbitrarily on the set $A_{2} \cap A_{1}^{c}$. Thus one could make $F_{x, 1}$ and $F_{x, 2}$ agree on the complement of $A=A_{1} \cap A_{2}$. If so, we shall say that the $F_{x, i}$ have been adjusted. With this convention one has the following result.

Lemma 3. Consider a net of pairs ( $S_{1, v}, S_{2, v}$ ) on spaces ( $\mathscr{X}_{v} \times \Theta_{v}, \mathscr{A}_{v} \times \mathscr{B}_{v}$ ) with disintegrations $S_{i, v}(d x, d \theta)=S_{i, v}^{\prime}(d x) F_{x, i, v}(d \theta)$ which are adjusted. Let $r_{v}$ be defined as in relation (3). Then in order that $\int\left\|F_{x, 1, v}-F_{x, 2, v}\right\| S_{1, v}^{\prime}(d x) \rightarrow 0$ it is necessary and sufficient that the functions $r_{v}$ tend to unity in measure for the measures $S_{1, v}$. It is sufficient that the corresponding ratios

$$
R_{v}(x, \theta, \xi)=\frac{\phi_{2, v}(x, \theta)}{\phi_{2, v}(x, \xi)} \frac{\phi_{1, v}(x, \xi)}{\phi_{1, v}(x, \theta)}
$$

tend to unity in measure for the measures $S_{1, v}^{\prime}(d x) F_{x, 1, v}(d \theta) F_{x, 2, v}(d \xi)$ on $\mathscr{X}_{v} \times \Theta_{v} \times \Theta_{v}$.

Proof. The assertion concerning $r_{v}$ follows from the identity (4) written above. For the second assertion let $R_{v}$ be the ratio

$$
R_{v}(x, \theta, \xi)=\frac{\phi_{2, v}(x, \theta)}{\phi_{2, v}(x, \xi)} \frac{\phi_{1, v}(x, \xi)}{\phi_{1, v}(x, \theta)}
$$

Let $\bar{R}_{v}(x, \theta, \xi)=1 \wedge R_{v}(x, \theta, \xi)$, so that

$$
r_{v}(x, \theta)=\int R_{v}(x, \theta, \xi) F_{x, 2, v}(d \xi) \geq \bar{r}_{v}(x, \theta)=\int \bar{R}_{v}(x, \theta, \xi) F_{x, 2, v}(d \xi)
$$

Then the identity (4) gives the inequality

$$
\frac{1}{2} \int\left\|F_{x, 1, v}-F_{x, 2, v}\right\| S_{1, v}^{\prime}(d x) \leq \int\left[1-\bar{r}_{v}\right] S_{1, v}(d x, d \theta)
$$

To show that this tends to zero, it is enough to show that $\bar{r}_{\nu}$ tend to unity in measure for $S_{1, v}$. However, this is certainly true if $\bar{R}_{v}$ tends to unity for the triple product measure $S_{1, \nu}^{\prime} F_{x, 1, \nu} F_{x, 2, \nu}$ of the statement of the lemma. The result follows.

Remark 1. In this lemma the norms $\left\|F_{x, 1}-F_{x, 2}\right\|$ could be integrated with respect to any finite measure instead of $S_{1}^{\prime}$. With the appropriate substitutions the result remains valid.

Remark 2. The measures $S_{i}$ can also be disintegrated in the form $S_{i}(d x, d \theta)= P_{\theta . i}(d x) \mu_{i}(d \theta)$. Therefore, if $\rho$ is the Radon-Nikodym density $\rho=d \mu_{2} / d \mu_{1}$, the ratio $\phi_{2}(x, \theta) / \phi_{1}(x, \theta)$ is also equal to the product $\left(d P_{\theta, 2} / d P_{\theta, 1}\right) \rho$. The ratio $R(x, \theta, \xi)$ which occurs in the statement of Lemma 3 could then be written in the form of a product of $(\rho(\theta) / \rho(\xi))$ by the quotient of likelihood ratios

$$
\left(\frac{d P_{\theta, 2}}{d P_{\xi, 2}}\right)\left(\frac{d P_{\theta, 1}}{d P_{\xi, 1}}\right)^{-1}
$$

In particular, if $\mu_{1}$ and $\mu_{2}$ are the same, then the posterior distributions $F_{x, i}$ will be alike if the two experiments $\mathscr{E}_{i}=\left\{P_{\theta, i} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}, i=1,2$ have logarithms of likelihood ratios which behave alike.

There is a special case of the inequality of Lemma 2 which deserves particular mention. It arises when one wishes to truncate the measures $S_{i}$ to a given subset $D$ of the product $\mathscr{X} \times \Theta$. Suppose, for instance, that the Polish structure of $\Theta$ is induced by a certain distance, which will be noted here in the form $\left|\theta_{1}-\theta_{2}\right|=\operatorname{dist}\left(\theta_{1}, \theta_{2}\right)$. It frequently happens that one can find estimates $T$ available on $\mathscr{E}_{i}$ and such that $S_{i}\{|T-\theta|>b\}<\varepsilon$, for a suitable number $b$. In such a case one may want to remove from consideration the set $\{(x, \theta) ;|T(x)-\theta|>b\}$ or equivalently truncate the $S_{i}$ to the set $D=\{(x, \theta) ;|T(x)-\theta| \leq b\}$.

Considering such a set $D$ and measures $S_{i}$ which are disintegrated in the form

$$
S_{i}(d x, d \theta)=S_{i}^{\prime}(d x) F_{x, i}(d \theta)=P_{\theta, i}(d x) \mu_{i}(d \theta)
$$

one can truncate any one of the measures $S_{i}, F_{x, i}$, or $P_{\theta, i}$ to $D$ or to the appropriate sections of $D$. This would yield certain measures which will be noted $\hat{S}_{i}, \hat{F}_{x, i}$, and $\hat{P}_{\theta, i}$. The measure $\hat{S}_{i}$ possesses a marginal $\hat{S}_{i}^{\prime}$ on $\mathscr{X}$ and a disintegration of the type $\widehat{S}_{i}(d x, d \theta)=\widehat{S}_{i}^{\prime}(d x) F_{x, i}^{*}(d \theta)$.

The probability measures $F_{x, i}^{*}$ are not necessarily equal to the truncated measures $\widehat{F}_{x, i}$. However, the relation $\widehat{F}_{x, i}=\left\|\widehat{F}_{x, i}\right\| F_{x, i}^{*}$ must hold almost everywhere $\hat{S}_{i}^{\prime}$. The measures $F_{x, i}^{*}$ themselves can be selected so that if $D_{x}=\{\theta:(x, \theta) \in D\}$ then $F_{x, i}^{*}\left(D_{x}\right)=1$ almost everywhere.

Thus, except on a set of measure zero, one can write $\left\|\hat{F}_{x, i}-F_{x, i}^{*}\right\|=1- \left\|\widehat{F}_{x, i}\right\|$ and $\left\|F_{x, i}^{*}-F_{x, i}\right\|=2\left[1-\left\|\widehat{F}_{x, i}\right\|\right]=2 F_{x, i}\left(D_{x}^{c}\right)$. This gives, among other
things, the relation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int\left\|F_{x, i}^{*}-F_{x, i}\right\| S_{i}^{\prime}(d x) & =2 \int F_{x, i}\left(D_{x}^{c}\right) S_{i}^{\prime}(d x) \\
& =2\left[\left\|S_{i}\right\|-\left\|\widehat{S}_{i}\right\|\right]=2 S_{i}\left(D^{c}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

When $D$ has the form $D=\{(x, \theta) ;|T(x)-\theta| \leq b\}$ the construction of the measures $F_{x, i}^{*}$ is a "local" problem involving only the restriction of the measures $S_{i}$ to a set $\{(x, \theta) ;|T(x)-\theta| \leq 2 b\}$.

One can also obtain results where all the computations, except for a final integration, are "local" in $\theta$. For this purpose consider a countable partition $\left\{B_{v} ; v=1,2, \ldots\right\}$ of the set $\Theta$. Let $B_{v}^{*}=\left\{x ;(x, \theta) \in D\right.$ for some $\left.\theta \in B_{v}\right\}$ and let $\bar{B}_{v}=\left\{\theta ; x \in B_{v}^{*},(x, \theta) \in D\right\}$. Note that if $D=\{(x, \theta) ;|T(x)-\theta| \leq b\}$ and if $B_{v}$ has diameter $k b$ then $\bar{B}_{v}$ has diameter at most $(k+4) b$.

For each $v$ let $a_{v, i}=\mu_{i}\left(B_{v}\right)$ and let $W_{v, i}$ be any positive measure carried by $B_{v}^{*}$ such that

$$
a_{v, i} W_{v, i}=\int_{B_{v}} \hat{P}_{\theta, i} \mu_{i}(d \theta)
$$

If $\theta \in B_{v}$ let $v_{i}(\theta)$ be defined by the relation

$$
v_{i}(\theta)=\int\left\|F_{x, 1}^{*}-F_{x, 2}^{*}\right\| W_{v, i}(d x)
$$

Then, the integral $J_{i}^{*}=\int\left\|F_{x, 1}^{*}-F_{x, 2}^{*}\right\| \widehat{S}_{i}^{\prime}(d x)$ may also be written in the form $J_{i}^{*}=\int v_{i}(\theta) \mu_{i}(d \theta)$.

The determination of $v_{i}(\theta)$ for $\theta \in \underline{B}_{v}$ involves only the behavior of $S_{i}$ on the intersection of $D$ with the set $B_{v}^{*} \times \bar{B}_{v}$. Thus, showing that the $v_{i}$ are small for each $\theta$ and application of suitable Lebesgue type convergence theorems will often yield convergence to zero of the $J_{i}^{*}$.

## 3. Asymptotic Behavior of Bayes Procedures

In this section we shall consider sequences of spaces $\left(\mathscr{X}_{n} \times \Theta_{n}, \mathscr{A}_{n} \times \mathscr{B}_{n}\right)$ where $\sigma$-field $\mathscr{A}_{n} \times \mathscr{B}_{n}$ carries a positive finite measure $S_{n}$. It will be assumed throughout that $\mathscr{X}_{n}$ and $\Theta_{n}$ are Borel subsets of Polish spaces and that the $\sigma$-fields $\mathscr{A}_{n}$ and $\mathscr{B}_{n}$ are the Borel $\sigma$-field. If $f_{n}$ is an $S_{n}$-integrable function defined on $\mathscr{X}_{n} \times \Theta_{n}$, one can introduce, in addition to $S_{n}$, another measure $S_{n, 1}$ which has density $f_{n}$ with respect to $S_{n}$.

Our present aim is to show that if $f_{n}$ can be "estimated" in a suitable sense, then (i) the posterior distributions of $\theta$ given $x$ for $S_{n}$ and $S_{n, 1}$ are asymptotically equivalent, and (ii) for bounded loss functions, procedures which are asymptotically Bayes for $\left\{S_{n}\right\}$ have the same property for $\left\{S_{n, 1}\right\}$. We shall use the terms "sequences" throughout. However, the discussion applies just as well to nets
or filters. The following definitions will be used. Let $S_{n}$ be disintegrated in the form

$$
S_{n}(d x, d \theta)=S_{n}^{\prime}(d x) F_{x, n}(d \theta)=P_{\theta, n}(d x) \mu_{n}(d \theta)
$$

Consider also another Markov kernel $x \mapsto G_{x, n}$.
Definition 1.The sequence of Markov kernels $\left\{x \mapsto G_{x, n}\right\}$ is adapted to $\left\{S_{n}\right\}$ if the integrals

$$
\int\left\|F_{x, n}-G_{x, n}\right\| S_{n}^{\prime}(d x)
$$

tend to zero as $n \rightarrow \infty$.
For the next definition, consider the space $\mathscr{F}$ of sequences $f=\left\{f_{n}\right\}$ where for each $n, f_{n}$ is a measurable real valued function defined on $\mathscr{X}_{n} \times \Theta_{n}$ and such that $\int\left|f_{n}\right| d S_{n}<\infty$. This space $\mathscr{F}$ is a vector lattice for operations carried out pointwise. On $\mathscr{F}$ one can introduce two semi-norms $\alpha$ and $\beta$ as follows:

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \beta(f)=\sup _{n} \int\left|f_{n}\right| d S_{n} \\
& \alpha(f)=\limsup _{n} \alpha_{n}\left(f_{n}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\alpha_{n}\left(f_{n}\right)$ is the infimum

$$
\alpha_{n}\left(f_{n}\right)=\inf _{\phi} \int\left|f_{n}-\phi\right| d S_{n}
$$

taken over all $\mathscr{A}_{n}$-measurable functions $\phi$.
Definition 2. A sequence $f=\left\{f_{n}\right\} \in \mathscr{F}$ will be called quasi-bounded if for each $\varepsilon>0$ there is a number $k(\varepsilon)$ and an $N(\varepsilon)$ such that $\int\left\{\left|f_{n}\right|-\left|f_{n}\right| \wedge k\right\} d S_{n}<\varepsilon$ for all $n \geq N(\varepsilon)$. It will be called accessible if $\alpha(f)=0$.
(Note that since $\left\|S_{n}\right\|$ may tend to infinity, the integrals $\int f_{n} d S_{n}$ may also tend to infinity even if $\left|f_{n}\right| \leq k$ for all $n$.)

According to the definition of the semi-norm $\alpha$, to say that $\left\{f_{n}\right\}$ is accessible is to say that for the measures $S_{n}$ one can estimate $f_{n}(x, \theta)$ closely by a function of $x$ only. The following simple lemma gives one useful property of accessible sequences.

Lemma 4. Let $f=\left\{f_{n}\right\} \in \mathscr{F}$ be a sequence such that $f_{n} \geq 0$. Define measures $S_{f, n}$ by the relation

$$
S_{f, n}(d x, d \theta)=f_{n}(x, \theta) S_{n}(d x, d \theta)
$$

If the sequence $f$ is quasi-bounded and accessible, then every sequence $\{x \mapsto \left.G_{x, n}\right\}$ of Markov kernels adapted to $\left\{S_{n}\right\}$ is also adapted to $\left\{S_{f, n}\right\}$. Conversely if the kernels $\left\{x \mapsto F_{x, n}\right\}$ are adapted to $\left\{S_{f, n}\right\}$ then $f$ is accessible.

Proof. Let $f_{n}^{\prime}=\int f_{n}(x, \theta) F_{x, n}(d \theta)$ and let $S_{f, n}(d x, d \theta)=S_{f, n}^{\prime}(d x) H_{x, n}(d \theta)$ be the disintegration of $S_{f, n}$. The kernels $H_{x, n}$ may be taken equal to $\left[f_{n}^{\prime}(x)\right]^{-1} f_{n}(x, \theta) F_{x, n}(d \theta)$. Thus



$$
\int\left\|F_{x, n}-H_{x, n}\right\| S_{f, x}^{\prime}(d x)=\int\left|f_{n}-f_{n}^{\prime}\right| d S_{n}
$$

It follows that if the kernels $x \mapsto F_{x, n}$ are adapted to $\left\{S_{f, n}\right\}$ the numbers $\alpha_{n}\left(f_{n}\right) \leq \int\left|f_{n}-f_{n}^{\prime}\right| d S_{n}$ must tend to zero.

In the other direction, let $\phi_{n}$ be any non-negative $\mathscr{A}_{n}$-measurable $S_{n}$-integrable function. Let $S_{1, n}$ be the measure which has density $\phi_{n}$ with respect to $S_{n}$. The kernels $x \mapsto F_{x, n}$ provide a common disintegration for $S_{n}$ and $S_{1, n}$. Thus, according to Lemma 2 one may write

$$
\int\left\|F_{x, n}-H_{x, n}\right\|\left[S_{1, n}^{\prime}(d x)+S_{f, n}^{\prime}(d x)\right] \leq 4 \int\left|f_{n}-\phi_{n}\right| d S_{n}
$$

Suppose now that $\left\{f_{n}\right\}$ is quasi-bounded and that $\left\{x \mapsto G_{x, n}\right\}$ is adapted to $\left\{S_{n}\right\}$. Take an $\varepsilon>0$ and a number $k$ such that eventually $\int\left[f_{n}-f_{n} \wedge k\right] d S_{n}< \varepsilon$. The marginal distribution $S_{f, n}^{\prime}$ may be written as a sum $V_{n}+W_{n}$ where $V_{n}$ is the marginal of $\left(f_{n} \wedge k\right) d S_{n}$ and where $\left\|W_{n}\right\| \leq \varepsilon$ for sufficiently large $n$. Since $V_{n} \leq k S_{n}^{\prime}$ one can also write

$$
\int\left\|G_{x, n}-H_{x, n}\right\| S_{f, n}^{\prime}(d x) \leq 4 \int\left|f_{n}-\phi_{n}\right| d S_{n}+2 \varepsilon+k \int\left\|G_{x, n}-F_{x, n}\right\| d S_{n}^{\prime}
$$

for all sufficiently large values of $n$. Since $\int\left|f_{n}-\phi_{n}\right| d S_{n}$ can be made as close to $\alpha_{n}\left(f_{n}\right)$ as desired, the result follows.

The following lemma gives some indications on the structure of the space $\mathscr{F}_{a}$ of accessible sequences.

Lemma 5. The space $\mathscr{F}_{a}$ of accessible sequences is a vector lattice for operations carried out pointwise. The subspace of $\mathscr{F}_{a}$ formed by sequences $f$ such that $\beta(f)<\infty$ is complete for the norm $\beta$.

Proof. The first assertion is immediate. The second follows from the obvious inequality

$$
\alpha_{n}\left(f_{n}\right) \leq \int\left|f_{n}\right| d S_{n} \leq \beta(f)
$$

To obtain results on the behavior of risk functions consider, in addition to the systems $\left(\mathscr{X}_{n}, \Theta_{n}, \mathscr{A}_{n}, \mathscr{B}_{n}, S_{n}\right)$, sets of possible decisions $Z_{n}$ and loss functions $W_{n}$ defined on $\Theta_{n} \times Z_{n}$.

It will be assumed below that $Z_{n}$ carries a certain $\sigma$-field, say $\mathscr{C}_{n}$. Furthermore, we shall consider only situations where (i) $W_{n}$ is $\mathscr{A}_{n} \times \mathscr{C}_{n}$-measurable and (ii) $0 \leq W_{n} \leq 1$ for all values of $n$.

Let $\mathscr{D}_{n}$ be the space of decision procedures representable as Markov kernels $\rho=\left\{x \mapsto \rho_{x}\right\}$ from $\left(\mathscr{X}_{n}, \mathscr{A}_{n}\right)$ to probability measures on $\mathscr{C}_{n}$. An element $\rho$ of $\mathscr{D}_{n}$ admits a risk function $R_{n}(\theta, \rho)$ defined by

$$
R_{n}(\theta, \rho)=\iint W_{\theta, n}(z) \rho_{x}(d z) P_{\theta, n}(d x)
$$

where $P_{\theta, n}$ is the probability measure which occurs in the disintegration $S_{n}(d x, d \theta)=P_{\theta, n}(d x) \mu_{n}(d \theta)$ of $S_{n}$. If $f_{n} \geq 0$ is $S_{n}$-integrable, let $\bar{R}\left(f_{n}, \rho\right)$ be the average risk

$$
\bar{R}\left(f_{n}, \rho\right)=\iiint W_{\theta, n}(z) \rho_{x}(d z) f_{n}(x, \theta) S_{n}(d x, d \theta)
$$

and let $r\left(f_{n}, \rho\right)$ be the regret

$$
r\left(f_{n}, \rho\right)=\bar{R}\left(f_{n}, \rho\right)-\inf _{\sigma}\left\{\bar{R}\left(f_{n}, \sigma\right) ; \sigma \in \mathscr{D}_{n}\right\}
$$

Definition 3. A sequence $\left\{\rho_{n}\right\}, \rho_{n} \in \mathscr{D}_{n}$ will be called asymptotically Bayes for the sequence $\left\{f_{n}, S_{n}\right\}$ if $\lim _{n} r\left(f_{n}, \rho_{n}\right)=0$.

With these definitions and conditions one can assert the following.
Proposition 1. A sequence $\left\{\rho_{n}\right\}, \rho_{n} \in \mathscr{D}_{n}$ which is asymptotically Bayes with respect to $\left\{1, S_{n}\right\}$ is also asymptotically Bayes with respect to any sequence $\left\{f_{n}, S_{n}\right\}, f_{n} \geq 0$ such that $\left\{f_{n}\right\}$ is accessible and quasi-bounded.

Proof. Taking a suitable integer $k$ and replacing $f_{n}$ by $f_{n} \wedge k$, one sees that it will be sufficient to prove the result under the additional assumption that $0 \leq f_{n} \leq k$ for all $n$. The assertion is then an immediate consequence of the following simple lemma.

Lemma 6. Let $k \geq 0$ be a constant and let $f_{n}$ and $g_{n}$ be two positive integrable functions such that $f_{n}+g_{n}=k$. Then, for any decision procedure $\rho \in \mathscr{D}_{n}$ one has

$$
r_{n}\left(f_{n}+g_{n}, \rho\right) \leq r_{n}\left(f_{n}, \rho\right)+r_{n}\left(g_{n}, \rho\right) \leq r_{n}\left(f_{n}+g_{n}, \rho\right)+\alpha_{n}\left(f_{n}\right)
$$

Proof. In the inequality as asserted, the subscript $n$ plays no role. Thus it will be dropped in the following argument.

Consider, besides $\rho$, two decision procedures $\rho^{\prime}$ and $\rho^{\prime \prime}$ and an $\mathscr{A}$-measurable function $\phi$ such that $0 \leq \phi \leq 1$. Let $\rho^{\prime \prime \prime}$ be the decision procedure $\rho^{\prime \prime \prime}= \phi \rho^{\prime}+(1-\phi) \rho^{\prime \prime \prime}$. Define a function $V$ on $\mathscr{X} \times \Theta$ by

$$
V(x, \theta)=\int W_{\theta}(z) \rho_{x}(d z)
$$

Let $V^{\prime}, V^{\prime \prime}$, and $V^{\prime \prime \prime}$ be the corresponding functions for the other decision procedures introduced above. Then $V^{\prime \prime \prime}=\phi V^{\prime}+(1-\phi) V^{\prime \prime}$. This gives the relation

$$
\begin{aligned}
r(f+g, \rho) \geq & \bar{R}(f+g, \rho)-\bar{R}\left(f+g, \rho^{\prime \prime \prime}\right) \\
= & {\left[\bar{R}(f, \rho)-\bar{R}\left(f, \rho^{\prime}\right)\right]+\left[\bar{R}(g, \rho)-\bar{R}\left(g, \rho^{\prime \prime}\right)\right] } \\
& +\int[(1-\phi) f-\phi g]\left[V^{\prime}-V^{\prime \prime}\right] d S
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $0 \leq W \leq 1$, one has also $\left|V^{\prime}-V^{\prime \prime}\right| \leq 1$. Keeping $\phi$ fixed and varying $\rho^{\prime}$ and $\rho^{\prime \prime}$, this yields

$$
r(f+g, \rho) \geq r(f, \rho)+r(g, \rho)-\int|(1-\phi) f-\phi g| d S
$$

The integral on the right may be written in the form $\int \mid(1-\phi) h-\phi(1- h) \mid(f+g) d S$, where $h=f(f+g)^{-1}$. Since $f+g=k$ by assumption, the integral in question is also equal to $k \int|h-\phi| d S$. One of the asserted inequalities follows by taking an infimum over $\phi$. The other inequality is trivial. Hence the result.

There is a special case in which the above assertions take a particularly elegant form. It occurs when the spaces ( $\Theta_{n}, \mathscr{B}_{n}$ ) and the prior measures $\mu_{n}$ are independent of $n$, so that $S_{n}$, defined on $\mathscr{A}_{n} \times \mathscr{B}$ has the form

$$
S_{n}(d x, d \theta)=P_{\theta, n}(d x) \mu(d \theta)
$$

In this case let $\mathscr{L}$ be the space of real valued functions defined on $\Theta$ and $\mu$-integrable. If $f \in \mathscr{L}$ one can define a semi-norm

$$
\alpha(f)=\lim \sup _{n} \inf _{\phi} \int|f-\phi| d S_{n}
$$

as before. Thus, taking sequences $\left\{f_{n}\right\}$ identically equal to $f$ in the previous definitions, we shall say that $f \in \mathscr{L}$ is accessible if $\alpha(f)=0$. Let $\mathscr{L}_{a}$ be the space of all accessible elements of $\mathscr{L}$.

According to Lemma 5 one can assert that there is a $\sigma$-field $\mathscr{B}_{a}$ contained in the completion $\overline{\mathscr{B}}$ of $\mathscr{B}$ for $\mu$ such that $\mathscr{L}_{a}$ is precisely the space of all $\mathscr{B}_{a}$ measurable $\mu$-integrable functions. If $\phi$ is a bounded $\mathscr{B}$-measurable function, let $\tilde{\phi}$ be its conditional expectation given $\mathscr{B}_{a}$ for the measure $\mu$. Explicitly $\tilde{\phi}$ is $\mathscr{B}_{a}$-measurable and such that $\int \tilde{\phi} f d \mu=\int \phi f d \mu$ for all $f \in \mathscr{L}_{a}$.

Letting $\mathscr{M}_{a}$ be the space of bounded $\mathscr{B}_{a}$-measurable functions and letting $M_{a}$ be the space of their $\mu$-equivalence classes one can topologize $\mathscr{M}_{a}$ by the weak topology $w\left(M_{a}, \mathscr{L}_{a}\right)$ induced by $\mathscr{L}_{a}$. For this topology the unit ball of $M_{a}$ is compact and sequentially compact. Thus a sequence $\phi_{n} \in M_{a}$ such that $\left|\tilde{\phi}_{n}\right| \leq 1$ always admits cluster points which are limits of subsequences. In this particular case, Proposition 1 implies the following.

Proposition 2. Let the sequence ( $\Theta_{n}, \mathscr{B}_{n}, \mu_{n}$ ) be identically equal to a given space $(\Theta, \mathscr{B}, \mu)$ and let the general assumptions of Proposition 1 be satisfied. Let $\rho_{n}$ and $\rho_{n}^{\prime}$ be two decision procedures, elements of $\mathscr{D}_{n}$, and let $\phi_{n}$ be the function

$$
\phi_{n}\left(\rho_{n}, \rho_{n}^{\prime} ; \theta\right)=R_{n}\left(\theta, \rho_{n}^{\prime}\right)-R_{n}\left(\theta, \rho_{n}\right) .
$$

Then $\left\{\rho_{n}\right\}$ is asymptotically Bayes for $\left\{1, S_{n}\right\}$ if and only if for every sequence $\left\{\rho_{n}^{\prime}\right\}$ every cluster point of the sequence $\tilde{\phi}_{n}\left(\rho_{n}, \rho_{n}^{\prime}\right)$ in $M_{a}$ is nonnegative. If so, $\rho_{n}$ is also asymptotically Bayes with respect to $\left\{f, S_{n}\right\}$ for any $f \geq 0, f \in \mathscr{L}_{a}$.

This result may be applied in particular when $\mathscr{B}$ is the $\sigma$-field of Borel sets of $\Theta$ for a certain metric. Then the inclusion $\mathscr{B} \subset \mathscr{B}_{a}$ is equivalent to the existence of estimates of $\theta$ which are consistent in an appropriately weak sense.

Specifically, $\mathscr{B} \subset \mathscr{B}_{a}$ if for every bounded Lipschitzian function $f$ defined on $\Theta$ there are estimates $T_{n}$ such that $\int\left|f\left(T_{n}\right)-f(\theta)\right| S_{n}(d x, d \theta)$ tends to zero. In such a situation it is not necessary to introduce the conditional expectations $\tilde{\phi}$ and one may just say that the cluster points of the sequence $\left\{\phi_{n}\right\}$ are almost everywhere non-negative.

To conclude this section, let us mention a result which can be interpreted as saying that Bayes estimates tend to converge at the "right rate". Since actual properties of Bayes estimates depend on the structure of the loss functions $W_{n}$, the result is stated in terms of estimates built using only the posterior distributions $F_{x, n}$ or approximations to them.

Let $T_{n}$ be a measurable map from $\left(\mathscr{X}_{n}, \mathscr{A}_{n}\right)$ to $\left(\Theta_{n}, \mathscr{B}_{n}\right)$ and let $\left\{\delta_{n}\right\}$ be a sequence of numbers, $\delta_{n}>0$. Let us say that the $T_{n}$ converge at the rate $\delta_{n}$ if for every $\varepsilon>0$ there is a $b<\infty$ such that

$$
\limsup _{n} S_{n}\left\{\left|T_{n}-\theta\right|>b \delta_{n}\right\}<\varepsilon
$$

Here, as in the previous section, the expression $|t-\theta|$ is used to denote the distance between $t$ and $\theta$ for a metric which induces the Borel structure $\left(\Theta_{n}, \mathscr{B}_{n}\right)$.

Now consider a Markov kernel $x \mapsto G_{x, n}$ from $\left(\mathscr{X}_{n}, \mathscr{A}_{n}\right)$ to $\left(\Theta_{n}, \mathscr{B}_{n}\right)$. For each $(x, n)$ let $\gamma_{0}(x, n)$ be the infimum of the numbers $\gamma$ for which there is a ball $B \subset \Theta_{n}$ of diameter at most $\gamma$ such that $G_{x, n}(B)>\frac{1}{2}$. Let $\gamma(x, n)$ be some number such that $\gamma(x, n)>\gamma_{0}(x, n)$. For instance, one may take $\gamma(x, n)=(1+(1 / n)) \gamma_{0}(x, n)$ if $\gamma_{0}(x, n)>0$. If, on the contrary, $\gamma_{0}(x, n)=0$, then $\gamma(x, n)$ can be taken arbitrarily small but strictly positive.

Using this definition of $\gamma(x, n)$ there is always some ball $B(x, n)$ of diameter at most $\gamma(x, n)$ such that $G_{x, n}[B(x, n)]>\frac{1}{2}$. Take for estimate of $\theta$ any point $\bar{\theta}_{n} \in B(x, n)$.

If $\bar{\theta}_{n}$ is selected very arbitrarily the map $x \mapsto \bar{\theta}_{n}(x)$ may or may not be measurable. It is easy to see that one can always insure the measurability of the map in question. However there is no need to do so if one is willing to substitute outer measures for actual measures in the definition of rates of convergence. This leads to the following assertion.

Proposition 3. Let $\bar{\theta}_{n}$ be constructed as described for a sequence of kernels $\left\{x \mapsto G_{x, n}\right\}$ which are adapted to $\left\{S_{n}\right\}$. If there are estimates $T_{n}$ which converge at the rate $\delta_{n}$, then $\bar{\theta}_{n}$ converges at the same $\delta_{n}$ rate.

Proof. Let $\varepsilon \in(0,1)$ be such that $4 \varepsilon<1$. Let $b$ denote a number such that, if $n$ is sufficiently large, one has $S_{n}\left\{\left|T_{n}-\theta\right| \geq b \delta_{n}\right\}<\varepsilon / 2$. Assume also that $n$ is taken so large that $\int\left\|F_{x, n}-G_{x, n}\right\| S_{n}^{\prime}(d x)<\varepsilon / 2$. Let $D(x, n)$ be the set $D(x, n)=\left\{\theta:\left|T_{n}(x)-\theta\right|<b \delta_{n}\right\}$. Disintegration of $S_{n}$ yields $\int F_{x, n}\left[D^{c}(x, n)\right] S_{n}^{\prime}(d x)< \varepsilon / 2$. Hence, for the values of $n$ under consideration,

$$
\int G_{x, n}\left[D^{c}(x, n)\right] S_{n}^{\prime}(d x)<\varepsilon
$$

Now let $A_{n}$ be the set of values of $x$ such that $G_{x, n}\left[D^{c}(x, n)\right] \geq \frac{1}{2}$. If $x$ does not belong to $A_{n}$, the probability for $G_{x, n}$ of the ball $D(x, n)$ exceeds $\frac{1}{2}$. Thus
the number $\gamma_{0}(x, n)$ used to construct $\bar{\theta}_{n}$ cannot exceed $2 b \delta_{n}$. In addition, any ball $B(x, n)$ for which $G_{x, n}[B(x, n)]>\frac{1}{2}$ cannot be disjoint from $D(x, n)$. Thus, in this case $\left|\bar{\theta}_{n}-\theta\right| \leq 4 b \delta_{n}$. Finally $S_{n}^{\prime}\left(A_{n}\right) \leq 2 \varepsilon$, by Markov's inequality. This implies the result as claimed.

Remarks. The reader may note that if Proposition 3 is stated using prior distributions which are probability measures, then it actually involves two rates of convergence, with very different functions.

Indeed, let $S_{n}$ be a measure which has a disintegration of the form $P_{\theta, n}(d x) \mu_{n}(d \theta)$. Let $\pi_{n}$ be the probability measure $c_{n} \mu_{n}$ with $c_{n}\left\|\mu_{n}\right\|=1$. Then the disintegration takes the form $c_{n}^{-1} P_{\theta, n}(d x) \pi_{n}(d \theta)$ and the assertion $S_{n}\left\{\left|\bar{\theta}_{n}-\theta\right| \geq 4 b \delta_{n}\right\} \leq \varepsilon$ takes the form $\operatorname{Pr}\left\{\left|\bar{\theta}_{n}-\theta\right| \geq 4 b \delta_{n}\right\} \leq \varepsilon c_{n}$ for the probability $\operatorname{Pr}$ computed for the measure $P_{\theta, n}(d x) \pi_{n}(d \theta)$.

Thus, if one uses only probabilistic priors, the assertion of Proposition 3 means that the tails of $\left|\bar{\theta}_{n}-\theta\right|$ decrease almost as rapidly as possible.

## 4. Approximately Gaussian Posterior Distributions

In his "Theorie Analytique," Laplace noted that large numbers of observations often lead to posterior distributions which resemble Gaussian distributions. The subject was taken up later, but in a different framework, by S . Bernstein and R. von Mises. This section contains a theorem related to Laplace's assertion but expressed in terms of joint distributions.

Here we shall consider again pairs $\left(\mathscr{X}_{n}, \mathscr{A}_{n}\right),\left(\Theta_{n}, \mathscr{B}_{n}\right)$ which satisfy the Polish assumptions. However ( $\Theta_{n}, \mathscr{B}_{n}$ ) will be fixed, independently of $n$, and taken equal to $(\Theta, \mathscr{B})$ where $\Theta$ is a certain $k$-dimensional Euclidean space and $\mathscr{B}$ is the family of Borel subsets of $\Theta$. (Taking for $\Theta$ a measurable subset of $R^{k}$ does not represent an increase in generality.)

Consider then on $\mathscr{A}_{n} \times \mathscr{B}$ a positive finite mesure $S_{n}$ disintegrated as usual in the form $S_{n}^{\prime}(d x) F_{x, n}(d \theta)=P_{\theta, n}(d x) \mu_{n}(d \theta)$. For each $n$ let $x \mapsto G_{x, n}$ be a

Markov kernel in which $G_{x, n}$ is a certain Gaussian measure on the Euclidean space $\Theta=R^{k}$. The purpose of this section is to investigate under what conditions one may assert that the kernels $\left\{x \mapsto G_{x, n}\right\}$ are adapted to $\left\{S_{n}\right\}$ in the sense of Definition 1, Section 3, that is such that

$$
\int\left\|F_{x, n}-G_{x, n}\right\| S_{n}^{\prime}(d x) \rightarrow 0
$$

Necessary and sufficient conditions for this will be given below, but only under some restrictive assumptions.

One of the restrictive assumptions is as follows. Let $\mu_{n, s}$ be the marginal measure $\mu_{n}$ shifted by $s$. That is, $\mu_{n, s}$ is defined by the equality $\int \gamma(\theta) \mu_{n, s}(d \theta)= \int \gamma(\theta+s) \mu_{n}(d \theta)$.

Assumption (A). The sequence $\left\|\mu_{n}\right\|$ is bounded. For every number $c \in (0, \infty)$ the quantity $\sup _{s}\left\{\left\|\mu_{n, s}-\mu_{n}\right\| ;|s| \leq c\right\}$ tends to zero as $n$ tends to infinity.

It is clear that Gaussian kernels may well be adapted to $\left\{S_{n}\right\}$ even if this assumption is not satisfied. The boundedness of the sequence $\left\|\mu_{n}\right\|$ is used here only because it simplifies the arguments and the statements of other assumptions. The role of the second part of Assumption (A) will become clearer later. It is a way of insuring that $\mu_{n}$ behaves locally very much like the Lebesgue measure.

In many applications Assumption (A) is not too much of a hindrance. Indeed, in practically all the theorems available in the literature the original parameter is not our $\theta$ but another parameter, say $\omega, \omega \in R^{k}$, related to our $\theta$ by a relation $\omega=\delta_{n} \theta$ where the $\delta_{n}$ are numbers, $\delta_{n}>0$, which tend to zero as $n \rightarrow \infty$. One then takes a fixed probability measure, say $v$, as prior distribution for $\omega$. The corresponding prior for $\theta$ is the image $\mu_{n}$ of $v$ by the scale change $\theta=\delta_{n}^{-1} \omega$. The assertion that $\left\|\mu_{n, s}-\mu_{n}\right\| \rightarrow 0$ is equivalent to the assertion that $v$ is dominated by the Lebesgue measure. In other words, (A) is rather natural if one thinks of the usual parameter before rescaling.

To construct Gaussian approximations, one needs to specify their centering and their covariances. Thus we shall assume given, for each integer $n$, a measurable map $x \mapsto \Gamma_{n}(x)$ from $\left(\mathscr{X}_{n}, \mathscr{A}_{n}\right)$ to the space of symmetric positive definitive nonsingular matrices on $R^{k}$. If $T_{n}$ is a measurable map from $\left(\mathscr{X}_{n}, \mathscr{A}_{n}\right.$ ) to $R^{k}$, the Gaussian measure $G_{x, n}$ associated with ( $T_{n}, \Gamma_{n}$ ) will be given by a density

$$
g_{n}(x, \theta)=\frac{\left|\operatorname{det} \Gamma_{n}\right|^{1 / 2}}{(2 \pi)^{k / 2}} \exp \left\{-\frac{1}{2}\left(\theta-T_{n}\right) \Gamma_{n}\left(\theta-T_{n}\right)^{\prime}\right\}
$$

with respect to the Lebesgue measure $\lambda$ of $R^{k}$.
Here again, an arbitrary restriction will be used. Namely, we shall investigate only the cases where the proposed $G_{x, n}$ satisfy the following compactness requirement.

Assumption (B). For each $\varepsilon>0$ there is an integer $N$ and a number $b<\infty$ such that $n \geq N$ implies

$$
S_{n}\left\{\left\|\Gamma_{n}\right\|+\left\|\Gamma_{n}^{-1}\right\|>b\right\}<\varepsilon
$$

To list the other conditions used below, introduce the measures $S_{n, t}$ obtained by shifting $S_{n}$ by the amount $t$. Explicitly

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int u(x, \theta) S_{n, t}(d x, d \theta) & =\int u(x, \theta+t) S_{n}(d x, d \theta) \\
& =\int u(x, \xi) P_{\xi-t, n}(d x) \mu_{n, t}(d \xi)
\end{aligned}
$$

The conditional distribution of $\theta$ given $x$ for $S_{n, t}$ is the distribution $F_{x, n}$ arising in $S_{n}$ but shifted by the amount $t$.

Condition ( $\mathbf{C}_{\mathbf{0}}$ ). For every sequence $\left\{s_{n}\right\}$ such that $s_{n} \rightarrow 0$, one has $\left\|S_{n, s_{n}}-S_{n}\right\| \rightarrow 0$.

Condition ( $\mathbf{C}_{\mathbf{1}}$ ). For every $t \in R^{k}$ the sequences $\left\{S_{n, t}\right\}$ and $\left\{S_{n}\right\}$ are contiguous.
To state the next condition, let $\bar{\Lambda}_{n, t}$ be the logarithm of likelihood ratio

$$
\bar{\Lambda}_{n, t}=\log \frac{d S_{n, t}}{d S_{n}}
$$

Condition (D). The sequence $\left\{S_{n}\right\}$ satisfies Condition (D) for the maps $\Gamma_{n}$ if for every pair ( $s, t$ ) of elements of $R^{k}$ the difference

$$
\bar{\Lambda}_{n, s+t}-\bar{\Lambda}_{n, s}-\bar{\Lambda}_{n, t}+s \Gamma_{n} t^{\prime}
$$

tends to zero in probability for $\left\{S_{n}\right\}$.
Condition (E). The sequence $\left\{S_{n}\right\}$ satisfies Condition (E) if there are measurable functions $\hat{\theta}_{n}$ from $\left(\mathscr{X}_{n}, \mathscr{A}_{n}\right)$ to $\Theta$ such that for every $\varepsilon>0$ there are numbers $N, b$ for which $n \geq N$ implies

$$
S_{n}\left\{\left|\hat{\theta}_{n}-\theta\right|>b\right\}<\varepsilon
$$

Finally, the desired result is expressible by the following:
Condition (F). The sequence $\left\{S_{n}\right\}$ satisfies Condition (F), for the matrices $\Gamma_{n}$, if there are maps $T_{n}$ such that, for the corresponding Gaussian measures $G_{x, n}$ the quantities $\int\left\|F_{x, n}-G_{x, n}\right\| S_{n}^{\prime}(d x)$ tend to zero.

It will be shown below that when Assumption (A) is satisfied the combination ( $\mathrm{B}, \mathrm{F}$ ) is equivalent to the combination ( $\mathrm{C}_{0}, \mathrm{C}_{1}, \mathrm{D}, \mathrm{E}$ ). A first step is as follows. Consider two sequences of measures $\left\{S_{n}\right\}$ and $\left\{S_{n}^{*}\right\}$. Call them equivalent if $\left\|S_{n}-S_{n}^{*}\right\| \rightarrow 0$.

Proposition 4. Let $\left\{S_{n}\right\}$ and $\left\{S_{n}^{*}\right\}$ be equivalent. Then if one of the two sequences satisfies any one of the conditions (A), (B), ( $\mathrm{C}_{i}$ ), (E), or (F), so does the other. Also, if one of the two satisfies the pair of conditions ( $\mathrm{C}_{1}$ ) and ( D ), so does the other.

The proof is immediate and will be left to the reader. In one direction the relation between the various conditions is given by the following result.

Theorem 1. Assume that the sequence $\left\{S_{n}\right\}$ satisfies the conditions (A), (B), and (F). Then $\left\{S_{n}\right\}$ also satisfies all the conditions $\left(\mathrm{C}_{0}\right),\left(\mathrm{C}_{1}\right),(\mathrm{D})$, and (E).

Proof. Let $W_{n}$ be the measure defined by $W_{n}(d x, d \theta)=S_{n}^{\prime}(d x) G_{x, n}(d \theta)$ for the Gaussian kernels $x \mapsto G_{x, n}$ of Condition (F). According to Proposition 4, it will be enough to show that the required conditions are satisfied by the sequence $\left\{W_{n}\right\}$.

For the Gaussian measure $G_{x, n}$, the quadratic $\left(\theta-T_{n}\right) \Gamma_{n}\left(\theta-T_{n}\right)^{\prime}$ has a chi-square distribution with $k$ degrees of freedom. This, coupled with the boundedness of $\left\|\Gamma_{n}^{-1}\right\|$ asserted by (B), implies that the estimability relation (E) is satisfied. The continuity condition $\left(\mathrm{C}_{0}\right)$ results from the fact that when $\left\|\Gamma_{n}(x)\right\|$ is not too large, shifting $G_{x, n}$ by a small $s$ disturbs it little.

For the remaining assertions, let $W_{n, t}$ be $W_{n}$ shifted by $t$ and let $V_{n, t}$ be the logarithm of likelihood ratio of $W_{n, t}$ with respect to $W_{n}$. Direct computation shows that $V_{n, t}=t \Gamma_{n}\left(\theta-T_{n}\right)^{\prime}-\frac{1}{2} t \Gamma_{n} t^{\prime}$. It follows that (D) is satisfied. Also,
since (B) holds and since $\left[\theta-T_{n}(x)\right] \Gamma_{n}(x)\left[\theta-T_{n}(x)\right]^{\prime}$ has a chi-square distribution, the distributions of the $V_{n, t}$ are a relatively compact sequence. Thus $\left(\mathrm{C}_{1}\right)$ holds. This concludes the proof of the theorem.

To prove a result in the opposite direction it will be necessary to produce estimates $T_{n}$ and carry out various computations. These become simpler if, taking advantage of Proposition 4, one first replaces the sequence $\left\{S_{n}\right\}$ by an equivalent smooth approximation.

Here $\left\{S_{n}\right\}$ will be called "smooth" if
(i) the conditional distributions $F_{x, n}$ may be written $F_{x, n}(d \theta)=f_{n}(x, \theta) \lambda(d \theta)$ for the Lebesgue measure $\lambda$ and for functions $f_{n}$ which satisfy in $\theta$ a Lipschitz condition independent of $x$ and are such that $f_{n}(x, \theta)>0$, except perhaps on a subset of $\mathscr{X}$ independent of $\theta$;
(ii) the marginal distributions $\mu_{n}$ have the form $\mu_{n}(d \theta)=\phi_{n}(\theta) \lambda(d \theta)$ for functions $\phi_{n}$ such that (a) $\phi_{n}(\theta)>0$ for all $\theta$, (b) $\sup _{n, \theta}\left|\phi_{n}(\theta)\right|<\infty$, and (c) the $\phi_{n}$ satisfy a Lipschitz condition independent of $n$.

Lemma 7. Let the conditions (A) and ( $\mathrm{C}_{0}$ ) be satisfied. Then there are smooth sequences $\left\{S_{n}^{*}\right\}$ such that $\left\|S_{n}-S_{n}^{*}\right\| \rightarrow 0$.

Proof. Let $\sigma$ be a standard Gaussian distribution on the space $R^{k}$. According to (A), there is some sequence $\left\{b_{n}\right\}, b_{n} \rightarrow \infty$, such that $\sup \left\{\left\|\mu_{n, s}-\mu_{n}\right\|\right.$; $\left.|s| \leq b_{n}^{2}\right\} \rightarrow 0$. Let $v_{n}$ be the convolution $v_{n}=\int \mu_{n, s b_{n}} \sigma(d s)$. Then $\left\|v_{n}-\mu_{n}\right\| \rightarrow$ 0 and $v_{n}$ has with respect to the Lebesgue measure $\lambda$ a strictly positive Lipschitzian density.

For the other statement, one can assume that $S_{n}$ has been replaced by $\bar{S}_{n}$ defined by $\bar{S}_{n}(d x, d \theta)=P_{\theta, n}(d x) v_{n}(d \theta)$. Taking the same measure $\sigma$, let $\left\{\varepsilon_{n}\right\}$ be a sequence such that $\varepsilon_{n}>0$ but $\varepsilon_{n} \rightarrow 0$. Replace $P_{\theta, n}$ by $\bar{P}_{\theta, n}= \int P_{\theta+\varepsilon_{n} s, n} \sigma(d s)$. Then if ( $\mathrm{C}_{0}$ ) holds, the integrals

$$
\int\left\|\bar{P}_{\theta, n}-P_{\theta, n}\right\| v_{n}(d \theta)
$$

will tend to zero. This insures the smoothness condition (i) except perhaps for the fact that the densities $f_{n}(x, \theta)$ may vanish at places which depend on $\theta$.

To avoid this, take some probability measure $P_{n}$ which dominates the entire family $\bar{P}_{\theta, n}$ and replace $\bar{P}_{\theta, n}$ by $(1-(1 / n)) \bar{P}_{\theta, n}+(1 / n) P_{n}$. Finally convolute again with respect to some Gaussian measure with covariance matrices which tend to zero. Then, excepting a fixed set of measure zero in $\mathscr{X}_{n}$, all the smoothness conditions are satisfied.

According to this lemma we shall assume that $\left\{S_{n}\right\}$ is smooth whenever convenient. The next result is also of a technical nature.

Proposition 5. Assume that the conditions (A), ( $\mathrm{C}_{0}$ ), ( $\mathrm{C}_{1}$ ), (D), and (E) are satisfied. Then (B) is also satisfied.

Proof. Assume that $\left\{S_{n}\right\}$ is smooth. One can assume in addition that $\left\|S_{n}\right\|=1$. Consider then the two measures $S_{n, t}$ and $S_{n, s}$ for $s=-t$. Since $s+t=0$ implies $\bar{\Lambda}_{n, s+t}=0$ it follows from (D) that $A_{n}(t)+t \Gamma_{n} t^{\prime} \rightarrow 0$ in measure if $A_{n}(t)=\bar{\Lambda}_{n, s}+\bar{\Lambda}_{n, t}$.

Let $\left\{u_{j} ; j=1,2, \ldots, k\right\}$ be an orthonormal basis of $R^{k}$. Let $k$ be the cube formed by the vectors whose maximum coordinate norm does not exceed one in
this basis. Then every $t$ in the unit ball $B$ of $R^{k}$ is a convex combination of the vertices of $K$.

Now for each such vertex $v$ the sum $A_{n}(v)+v \Gamma_{n} v^{\prime}$ tends to zero in measure. Also, by the contiguity condition $\left(\mathrm{C}_{1}\right), A_{n}(v)$ is bounded in probability. Thus $v \Gamma_{n} v^{\prime}$ is bounded in probability. It follows that $\sup \left\{t \Gamma_{n} t^{\prime} ; t \in B\right\}$, which is smaller than the supremum of $v \Gamma_{n} v^{\prime}$ over the vertices of $K$, is also bounded in measure. Thus $\left\{\left\|\Gamma_{n}\right\|\right\}$ is bounded in measure for $\left\{S_{n}\right\}$.

For the second assertion, concerning $\left\|\Gamma_{n}^{-1}\right\|$, consider the experiment $\mathscr{F}_{n}= \left\{S_{n, t} ; t \in R^{k}\right\}$. Here $S_{n}$ is the distribution of a pair ( $x, \theta$ ) and $S_{n, t}$ is the corresponding distribution of $(x, \theta+t)$. Thus, if $\hat{\theta}_{n}$ is as in Condition (E), the difference $\hat{t}_{n}=\theta-\hat{\theta}_{n}$ is an estimate of $t$. The statement that $S_{n}\left\{\left|\hat{\theta}_{n}-\theta\right|>b\right\}<\varepsilon$ is the same as the statement that $S_{n, t}\left\{\left|\hat{\theta}_{n}-(\theta-t)\right|>b\right\}<\varepsilon$. Thus according to (E), given $\varepsilon$ there is a number $b$ such that $S_{n, t}\left\{\left|\hat{t}_{n}-t\right|>b\right\}<\varepsilon$.

Let $L_{n}(s)=\bar{\Lambda}_{n, s}+\frac{1}{2} s \Gamma_{n} s^{\prime}$. Then (D) says that $L_{n}(s+t)-\left[L_{n}(s)+L_{n}(t)\right] \rightarrow$ 0 . Thus, according to the usual argument (see Chapter 10), one may write $L_{n}(s)$ in the form $L_{n}(s)=s Z_{n}+\varepsilon_{n}$ for variables $\varepsilon_{n}$ which tend to zero in measure and for random vectors $Z_{n}$ which have a relatively compact sequence of distributions.

Taking a subsequence if necessary, one may assume that the joint distributions $\mathscr{L}\left[Z_{n}, \Gamma_{n} \mid S_{n}\right]$ tend to a limit, say $F=\mathscr{L}(Z, \Gamma)$. Then the experiments $\mathscr{F}_{n}$ have a limit $\mathscr{F}=\left\{F_{t} ; t \in R^{k}\right\}$ with

$$
F_{t}(d z, d \gamma)=\exp \left\{t z-\frac{1}{2} t \gamma t^{\prime}\right\} F(d z, d \gamma)
$$

For this limit experiment $\mathscr{F}$, consider the problem of estimating $t$ with a gain function $g(\hat{t}-t)$ with $g(t)=\exp \left\{-\left(\alpha^{2} / 2\right)\|t\|^{2}\right\}$. Take a Gaussian prior $\mathscr{N}\left(0,\left(1 / \sigma^{2}\right) I\right)$ on $R^{k}$. Then the posterior distribution of $t$ for the experiment $\mathscr{F}$ is a Gaussian distribution whose covariance matrix is the inverse of $M= \gamma+\sigma^{2} I$.

The Bayes posterior gain $\beta$ is given by the expression

$$
\beta^{2}(\alpha, \sigma)=\frac{\operatorname{det} M}{\operatorname{det}\left(\alpha^{2}+M\right)}=\prod_{j}\left(\frac{\delta_{j}+\sigma^{2}}{\delta_{j}+\sigma^{2}+\alpha^{2}}\right)
$$

where the $\delta_{j}$ are the eigenvalues of $\gamma$. Letting $\sigma$ tend to zero, it follows that the maximin gain in $\mathscr{F}$ is at most equal to the expectation of $\prod_{j}\left(\delta_{j} / \delta_{j}+\alpha^{2}\right)^{1 / 2}$. Thus, if for the limiting distribution $F=\mathscr{L}(Z, \Gamma)$ the matrix $\Gamma$ may be singular, the maximin gain will be inferior to the probability that $\Gamma$ is nonsingular. This is true for every $\alpha>0$.

Returning to loss functions $l(\hat{t}-t)=1-g(\hat{t}-t)$ one can assert that the minimax risk for $\mathscr{F}$ is at least equal to a certain number $\varepsilon_{0}$ equal to the probability that $\Gamma$ is singular.

Now, according to Chapter 7, Section 4, Theorem 1, the minimax risk of $\mathscr{F}$ is smaller than the limit of the minimax risks for the experiments $\mathscr{F}_{n}$. For these one may write

$$
\begin{aligned}
E l\left(\hat{t}_{n}-t\right) & =E l\left(\hat{t}_{n}-t\right) I\left\{\left|\hat{t}_{n}-t\right| \leq b\right\}+E l\left(\hat{t}_{n}-t\right) I\left\{\left|\hat{t}_{n}-t\right|>b\right\} \\
& \leq 1-\exp \left\{-\frac{1}{2} \alpha^{2} b^{2}\right\}+\varepsilon
\end{aligned}
$$

if $\varepsilon \geq S_{n, t}\left\{\left|\hat{t}_{n}-t\right|>b\right\}$.
If $\varepsilon_{0}>0$ one can select $b$ so that $\varepsilon<\varepsilon_{0} / 4$ and then select $\alpha$ so that $l(\alpha \beta)< \varepsilon_{0} / 4$. This would lead to a risk smaller than $\varepsilon_{0}$. This contradiction proves that $\varepsilon_{0}=\operatorname{Prob}(\Gamma$ is singular $)$ must be zero and the desired result follows.

To proceed to the construction of estimates $T_{n}$ which can be used as centerings for the Gaussian distributions $G_{x, n}$, we shall first randomize the estimate $\hat{\theta}_{n}$ provided by Condition (E). This randomization, which will be removed eventually, gives rise to the feature expressed in the following lemma.

Let $\xi$ be a random vector uniformly distributed on the unit ball of $R^{k}$ independently of the other variables $(x, \theta)$. Let $\bar{\theta}_{n}=\hat{\theta}_{n}+\xi$ and let $V_{n}$ be the random vector $\bar{\theta}_{n}-\theta$.

Lemma 8. Let $\phi_{n}$ be a jointly measurable function defined on $\mathscr{X}_{n} \times R^{k} \times R^{k}$. Assume that for each fixed $v \in R^{k}$ the sequence $\left\{\phi_{n}(x, \theta, v)\right\}$ tends to zero in measure for $\left\{S_{n}\right\}$. Assume also that the estimate $\hat{\theta}_{n}$ satisfies Condition (E). Then $\left\{\phi_{n}\left(x, \theta, V_{n}\right)\right\}$ tends to zero in measure.

Proof. Let $v$ be the Gaussian distribution which has expectation zero and identity covariance matrix on $R^{k}$. Let $\pi_{x, \theta, n}$ be the conditional distribution of $V_{n}=\bar{\theta}_{n}-\theta$ given the pair ( $x, \theta$ ). For each fixed number $b$ one can find a number c such that $\left|\theta_{n}(x)-\theta\right| \leq b$ implies $\pi_{x, \theta, n} \leq c v$. Thus, assuming $0 \leq \phi_{n} \leq 1$, one can write

$$
E \phi_{n}\left(x, \theta, V_{n}\right) \leq S_{n}\left\{\left|\hat{\theta}_{n}-\theta\right|>b\right\}+c E \int \phi_{n}(x, \theta, v) v(\mathrm{dv})
$$

The desired result follows.
For the next assertion, let $\Lambda_{n}(t, \theta)$ be the logarithm of likelihood ratio

$$
\Lambda_{n}(t, \theta)=\log \frac{d P_{t, n}}{d P_{\theta, n}}
$$

in the disintegration $S_{n}(d x, d \theta)=P_{\theta, n}(d x) \mu_{n}(d \theta)$ of $S_{n}$.
Theorem 2. Assume that the sequence $\left\{S_{n}\right\}$ satisfies all the conditions (A), ( $\mathrm{C}_{0}$ ), $\left(\mathrm{C}_{1}\right)$, (D), and (E). Then there are measurable functions $T_{n}$ from $\left(\mathscr{X}_{n}, \mathscr{A}_{n}\right)$ to $R^{k}$ such that for every bounded sequence $\left\{t_{n}\right\}$ the difference

$$
\Lambda_{n}\left(\theta+t_{n}, \theta\right)-t_{n} \Gamma_{n}\left(T_{n}-\theta\right)^{\prime}+\frac{1}{2} t_{n} \Gamma_{n} t_{n}^{\prime}
$$

tends to zero in measure for $\left\{S_{n}\right\}$. Furthermore these estimates $T_{n}$ satisfy Condition (E).

Proof. The function $\Lambda_{n}$ is related to the logarithms of likelihood ratio $\bar{\Lambda}_{n, t}$ of Condition (D) by the equality



$$
\Lambda_{n}(\theta-t, \theta)=\bar{\Lambda}_{n, t}-\log \frac{d \mu_{n, t}}{d \mu_{n}}
$$

Since (A) holds, the term involving $\mu_{n, t}$ and $\mu_{n}$ tends to zero in measure as $n \rightarrow \infty$.

Let $L_{n}$ be defined by

$$
L_{n}(s, \theta, v)=\Lambda_{n}[\theta+v+s, \theta+v]+\frac{1}{2} s \Gamma_{n} s^{\prime}
$$

and consider the difference

$$
D_{n}(s, t ; v)=L_{n}(s+t, \theta, v)-L_{n}(s, \theta, v)-L_{n}(t, \theta, v)
$$

The expression $D_{n}(s, t ; 0)$ is asymptotically equivalent to the corresponding difference computed from the logarithms $\bar{\Lambda}_{n}$. Thus, according to Condition (D), the $D_{n}(s, t ; 0)$ tend to zero for $\left\{S_{n}\right\}$. This implies that $D_{n}(s, t ; v)$ tends to zero for $\left\{S_{n, v}\right\}$ hence also for $\left\{S_{n}\right\}$ since Condition $\left(\mathrm{C}_{1}\right)$ holds. Also, according to Condition $\left(\mathrm{C}_{0}\right), D_{n}\left(s_{n}, t_{n} ; v\right)$ will tend to zero as long as the sequences $\left\{s_{n}\right\}$ and $\left\{t_{n}\right\}$ remain bounded.

Letting $V_{n}=\bar{\theta}_{n}-\theta$, as in Lemma 8 one can conclude that $D_{n}\left(s_{n}, t_{n} ; V_{n}\right)$ still tends to zero in measure. Define a function $M_{n}$ by the relation

$$
M_{n}(s)=L_{n}\left(s, \theta, V_{n}\right)=\Lambda_{n}\left(\bar{\theta}_{n}+s, \bar{\theta}_{n}\right)+\frac{1}{2} s \Gamma_{n} s^{\prime}
$$

Let $\left\{u_{j} ; j=1,2, \ldots, k\right\}$ be a basis for $R^{k}$. Then if the $\alpha_{j}$ are rational numbers the expression $M_{n}\left(\sum \alpha_{j} u_{j}\right)-\sum \alpha_{j} M_{n}\left(u_{j}\right)$ tends to zero for $\left\{S_{n}\right\}$. Consider the vector $\left[M_{n}\left(u_{1}\right), M_{n}\left(u_{2}\right), \ldots, M_{n}\left(u_{k}\right)\right]$ and write it in the form $\left(T_{n}-\bar{\theta}_{n}\right) \Gamma_{n}$. The above says that if $s_{n}$ is a rational sum $\sum \alpha_{j} u_{j}$ then $M_{n}\left(s_{n}\right)- s_{n} \Gamma_{n}\left(T_{n}-\bar{\theta}_{n}\right)^{\prime}$ tends to zero for $\left\{S_{n}\right\}$. However, the contiguity condition $\left(\mathrm{C}_{1}\right)$ applied to $\Gamma_{n}\left(T_{n}-\bar{\theta}_{n}\right)^{\prime}$ and the continuity condition ( $\mathrm{C}_{0}$ ) applied to $M_{n}\left(s_{n}\right)$ imply that the convergence in question holds for any bounded sequence $\left\{s_{n}\right\}$.

Consider also the expressions

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \Lambda_{n}\left(\bar{\theta}_{n}+s_{n}, \bar{\theta}_{n}\right)-\Lambda_{n}\left(\theta+s_{n}, \theta\right)+s_{n} \Gamma_{n}\left(\bar{\theta}_{n}-\theta\right)^{\prime} \\
& \quad=\Lambda_{n}\left(\theta+s_{n}+V_{n}, \theta+V_{n}\right)-\Lambda_{n}\left(\theta+s_{n}, \theta\right)+s_{n} \Gamma_{n} V_{n}^{\prime}
\end{aligned}
$$

These tend to zero for $\left\{S_{n}\right\}$ for the same reasons as above. Thus, by addition one can conclude that

$$
\Lambda_{n}\left(\theta+s_{n}, \theta\right)-s_{n} \Gamma_{n}\left(T_{n}-\theta\right)^{\prime}+\frac{1}{2} s_{n} \Gamma_{n} s_{n}^{\prime}
$$

tend to zero for $\left\{S_{n}\right\}$ whenever $\sup \left|S_{n}\right|<\infty$.
Since, by ( $\mathrm{C}_{1}$ ) the $\Lambda_{n}\left(\theta+s_{n}, \theta\right)$ are bounded in probability, and since, by Proposition 5 the norms $\left\|\Gamma_{n}^{-1}\right\|$ are similarly bounded, one concludes that the sequence $\left\{T_{n}\right\}$ satisfies Condition (E).

This would conclude the proof of the theorem. However, here $T_{n}$ is defined on the product of $\mathscr{X}_{n}$ by the unit ball of $R^{k}$ and not on $\mathscr{X}_{n}$ itself. It remains then to show that one may replace the above $T_{n}$ by a function of $x$ only.

For this purpose, consider the basis $\left\{u_{j}\right\}$ and the differences

$$
\Delta_{n, j}(x, \theta, \xi)=\Lambda_{n}\left(\theta+u_{j}, \theta\right)-u_{j} \Gamma_{n}\left(T_{n}-\theta\right)^{\prime}+\frac{1}{2} u_{j} \Gamma_{n} u_{j}^{\prime} .
$$

Let

$$
\sigma_{n}(x, \xi)=\sum_{j} \int\left[1 \wedge\left|\Delta_{n, j}\right|\right] F_{x, n}(d \theta)
$$

for the posterior distributions $F_{x, n}$. Finally, if $\pi$ is the distribution of $\xi$ let $\sigma_{n}(x)=\int \sigma_{n}(x, \xi) \pi(d \xi)$. For each $x$ one can find some element $\xi_{n}(x)$ such that $\sigma_{n}\left[x, \xi_{n}(x)\right] \leq \sigma_{n}(x)+(1 / n)$.

All the functions involved being jointly measurable, it is not difficult to select $\xi_{n}$ in a measurable way. If $T_{n}$ is written as $T_{n}(x, \xi)$, let $T_{n}^{*}(x)=T_{n}\left[x, \xi_{n}(x)\right]$.

Then

$$
\Lambda_{n}\left(\theta+u_{j}, \theta\right)-u_{j} \Gamma_{n}\left(T_{n}^{*}-\theta\right)^{\prime}+\frac{1}{2} u_{j} \Gamma_{n} u_{j}^{\prime}
$$

tends to zero for each $j$. It follows that $T_{n}^{*}-T_{n} \rightarrow 0$ for $S_{n}$. This completes the proof of the theorem.

We are now in a position to state a converse of Theorem 1 as follows.
Theorem 3. Let $\left\{S_{n}\right\}$ satisfy the conditions (A), $\left(\mathrm{C}_{0}\right),\left(\mathrm{C}_{1}\right),(\mathrm{D})$, and (E) for the sequence $\left\{\Gamma_{n}\right\}$. Let $T_{n}$ be estimates satisfying the relation of Theorem 2. Let $G_{x, n}$ be the Gaussian measure associated with ( $T_{n}, \Gamma_{n}$ ). Then $\int\left\|F_{x, n}-G_{x, n}\right\| S_{n}^{\prime}(d x)$ tends to zero as $n \rightarrow \infty$.

Proof. According to Proposition 5 the sequence $\left\{\Gamma_{n}\right\}$ satisfies the compactness requirement (B). Thus, fixing an $\varepsilon>0$ and removing a set which has $S_{n}$ measure at most $\varepsilon$, one sees that it will be sufficient to prove the assertion under the stronger requirement that there is a number $b$ for which $\left\|\Gamma_{n}\right\|+\left\|\Gamma_{n}^{-1}\right\| \leq b$ for all $x$ and $n$.

Let $W_{n}(d x, d \theta)=S_{n}^{\prime}(d x) G_{x, n}(d \theta)$. Since $T_{n}$ satisfies the condition (E) (for $S_{n}$ and $W_{n}$ both) it is sufficient to show that if $S_{n}$ and $W_{n}$ are both truncated to the sets $D_{n}(m)=\left\{(x, \theta) ;\left|T_{n}(x)-\theta\right| \leq m\right\}$ then the difference of the truncated measures tend to zero.

One may also assume that $S_{n}$ is smooth in the sense of Lemma 7 and write it in the form $S_{n}(d x, d \theta)=S_{n}^{\prime}(d x) f_{n}(x, \theta) \mu_{n}(d \theta)$ for a function $f_{n}$ such that

$$
\int f_{n}(x, \theta) S_{n}^{\prime}(d x)=\int f_{n}(x, \theta) \mu_{n}(d \theta)=1
$$

One can assume also that $\mu_{n}$ has the form $\mu_{n}(d \theta)=\phi_{n}(\theta) \lambda(d \theta)$ for certain functions $\phi_{n}>0$ which satisfy a Lipschitz condition and are bounded independently of $n$.

According to Assumption (A), for every bounded sequence $\left\{t_{n}\right\}$ the ratio $\phi_{n}\left(\theta+t_{n}\right) \phi_{n}^{-1}(\theta)$ tends to unity for the measures $\left\{S_{n}\right\}$. If $\eta$ has a uniform distribution on the unit ball $B$ of $R^{k}$, it follows from Lemma 8 that $\phi_{n}\left(T_{n}+\eta\right) \phi_{n}^{-1}(\theta)$ also tends to unity in measure. Let $\beta_{n}$ be a measurable function defined on $\left(\mathscr{X}_{n}, \mathscr{A}_{n}\right)$ such that $\beta_{n}(x)$ is a median of $\phi_{n}\left(T_{n}+\eta\right)$ given $T_{n}$. Then $\beta_{n}(x) \phi_{n}^{-1}(\theta)$ tends to unity in measure. Now let $g_{n}$ be the Gaussian density such that $G_{x, n}(d \theta)=g_{n}(x, \theta) \lambda(d \theta)$.

Fix an integer $m$ and let $J_{n}$ be the indicator of the corresponding strip $D_{n}(m)=\left\{(x, \theta) ;\left|T_{n}(x)-\theta\right| \leq m\right\}$. Consider the measure $M_{n}$ defined by truncating $S_{n}$ to $D_{n}(m)$ so that $M_{n}=J_{n} S_{n}$. Disintegrate $M_{n}$ in the form $M_{n}(d x, d \theta)= M_{n}^{\prime}(d x) F_{x, n}^{*}(d \theta)$. Let $N_{n}$ be the measure defined by the relation

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{n}(d x, d \theta) & =S_{n}^{\prime}(d x) g_{n}(x, \theta) J_{n}(x, \theta) \beta_{n}(x) \lambda(d \theta) \\
& =N_{n}^{\prime}(d x) H_{x, n}(d \theta)
\end{aligned}
$$

For the pair ( $M_{n}, N_{n}$ ) the ratio called $R$ in the statement of Lemma 3, Section 2 can be written in the form

$$
R_{n}(x, \theta, \xi)=J_{n}(x, \theta) \frac{g_{n}(x, \theta)}{g_{n}(x, \xi)} \frac{f_{n}(x, \xi)}{f_{n}(x, \theta)} \frac{\beta_{n}(x)}{\phi_{n}(\theta)}
$$

We already know that $\beta_{n}(x)\left[\phi_{n}(\theta)\right]^{-1}$ tends to unity for $\left\{S_{n}\right\}$.
That the ratios

$$
\frac{g_{n}(x, \theta)}{g_{n}(x, \xi)}\left[\frac{f_{n}(x, \theta)}{f_{n}(x, \xi)}\right]^{-1}
$$

tend to unity for the product measures $S_{n}^{\prime}(d x) F_{x, n}(d \theta) H_{x, n}(d \xi)$ follows immediately from the approximation of $\Lambda_{n}(\theta+\xi, \theta)$ given in Theorem 2. Thus Lemma 3, Section 2 is applicable. It yields that $\int\left\|F_{x, n}^{*}-H_{x, n}\right\| M_{n}^{\prime}(d x) \rightarrow 0$ as $n \rightarrow \infty$.

Now $H_{x, n}$ is just the Gaussian measure $G_{x, n}$ truncated to the set $\{\theta: \mid \left.\theta-T_{n}(x) \mid \leq m\right\}$. Also $S_{n}^{\prime}-M_{n}^{\prime}$ is a positive measure which has mass equal to $S_{n}\left[D_{n}^{c}(m)\right]<\varepsilon$. Thus the assertion that $\left\|S_{n}-W_{n}\right\| \rightarrow 0$ follows from the inequalities of Section 2 by letting $m$ tend to infinity. This concludes the proof of the theorem.

It will be shown, in later chapters, that one can derive from Theorem 3 many of the results associated with the label of "Bernstein-von Mises theorems." However, these are usually stated as results valid for the measures $P_{\theta, n}$ for fixed values of $\theta$. Here, the integral $\int\left\|F_{x, n}-G_{x, n}\right\| S_{n}^{\prime}(d x)$ may also be written $\iint\left\|F_{x, n}-G_{x, n}\right\| P_{\theta, n}(d x) \mu_{n}(d \theta)$. Thus the integrals $\int\left\|F_{x, n}-G_{x, n}\right\| P_{\theta, n}(d x)$ tend to zero in $\mu_{n}$ measure, but not necessarily for any fixed $\theta$. In spite of this it will be possible to recover most of the usual results by simple arguments valid in these particular cases.

## CHAPTER 13

## An Approximation Theorem for Certain Sequential Experiments

## 1. Introduction

The purpose of this chapter is to investigate passages to the limit for a restricted class of sequential experiments. We consider experiments which, if they were stopped by a nonrandom variable, would be approximable by Gaussian or Koopman-Darmois families.

The most important result is a theorem according to which one may restrict oneself to stopping rules which depend only on the vectors occurring in the Koopman-Darmois approximation. It is also shown that the sequential experiments in question can be approximated by using suitable stopping times on a process with independent increments.

A sequential experiment with a preassigned stopping rule is, of course, just another experiment in the sense of Chapter 1. However, here one must worry not only about the type of the experiment but also about the cost associated with the stopping variable. Thus the limit theorems given here concern both the limits of the experiment types and the limiting distributions for the stopping variables.

Section 2 describes the general framework with the main assumptions. It also introduces the processes with independent increments used later on.

Section 3 gives the main auxiliary lemmas. It shows that under the conditions imposed here, limiting processes automatically acquire certain independence properties. Also replacing stopping times by dyadic approximations does not introduce major disturbances.

Section 4 contains the two reduction theorems. Section 5 describes some consequences in the Gaussian case.

## 2. Notations and Assumptions

Exponential families, or Koopman-Darmois families, are homogeneous experiments $\left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ which have a structure describable as follows. Assume that the $P_{\theta}$ are measures on a certain space ( $\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{A}$ ) and assume that $P=P_{\theta_{0}}$ is a particular element of the family. Then the likelihood ratios have the form

$$
\log \frac{d P_{\theta}}{d P}=\xi(\theta) S-\beta[\xi(\theta)]
$$

where $S$ is a measurable map from ( $\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{A}$ ) to a certain Euclidean space and where $\xi$ is a map from $\Theta$ to the dual of the range of $S$. The function $\beta$ is a numerical function defined at least on the convex hull of the range of $\xi$.

For the applications which may be made of the results of the present chapter, it is not natural to assume that the map $\xi$ is injective. For instance, in the testing problem described in Section 5, it may happen that $\theta$ is a pair consisting of a real parameter $\lambda$ and a probability measure $\mu$ on the line. The image $\xi(\theta)$ may consist of a pair ( $\lambda, \sigma^{2}$ ) where $\sigma^{2}$ is the variance of $\mu$.

However, keeping the set $\Theta$ and the map $\xi$ throughout introduces major notational nuisances even though the necessary modifications of argumentation are entirely trivial. Thus, except in Section 5, we shall proceed as if $\xi$ was injective and work directly on the range $\Xi=\xi(\Theta)$ without any further reference to $\Theta$ itself. The reader should have no difficulty in returning to the general case.

With this reduction the structures to be studied are as follows. One is given a certain subset $\boldsymbol{\Xi}$ of a Euclidean space $H$, with dual $H^{\prime}$, and a real function $\beta$ defined on the convex hull of $\Xi$.

For each integer $n$ one considers a system $\mathscr{S}_{n}$ composed of the following pieces:
(1) A set $\mathscr{X}_{n}$, together with an increasing family $\mathscr{A}_{n}(t) ; t \in[0, \infty)$ of $\sigma$-fields of subsets of $\mathscr{X}_{n}$. It will be assumed that $\mathscr{A}_{n}(0)$ is always the trivial $\sigma$-field $\left\{\varnothing, \mathscr{X}_{n}\right\}$. Also it will be assumed that the $\mathscr{A}_{n}(t)$ are right continuous, so that $\mathscr{A}_{n}(s)=\bigcap_{t>s} \mathscr{A}_{n}(t)$.
(2) For each $\mathscr{A}_{n}(t)$ and each $\xi \in \Xi$ one is given a probability measure $P_{n}[\xi, t]$ on the $\sigma$-field $\mathscr{A}_{n}(t)$. These measures are assumed to be coherent in the sense that if $s<t$ then $P_{n}[\xi, s]$ is the restriction to $\mathscr{A}_{n}(s)$ of $P_{n}(\xi, t)$ which is defined on the larger $\mathscr{A}_{n}(t)$.
(3) For each pair $(n, t)$, there is given a measurable map $S_{n}(t)$ of $\left(\mathscr{X}_{n}, \mathscr{A}_{n}(t)\right)$ into the dual $H^{\prime}$ of the Euclidean space $H$.

For each $n$ one will consider stopping times $T_{n}$ adapted to the increasing family $\left\{\mathscr{A}_{n}(t) ; t \in[0, \infty)\right\}$. Specifically $T_{n}$ is a function from $\mathscr{X}_{n}$ to $[0, \infty)$ such that the set $\left\{T_{n} \leq t\right\}$ belongs to $\mathscr{A}_{n}(t)$. The $\sigma$-field $\mathscr{A}_{n}\left(T_{n}\right)$ associated to the stopping time $T_{n}$ is the $\sigma$-field of sets $A$ such that $A \cap\left\{T_{n} \leq t\right\} \in \mathscr{A}_{n}(t)$ for all $t$.

Here we have considered directly a situation where the increasing $\sigma$-fields are indexed by a continuous time. The more standard situation where one considers an increasing sequence, say $\mathscr{A}_{n}^{\prime}(k) ; k=0,1,2, \ldots$ reduces immediately to the above. However, passages to the limit on such sequences are often carried out by a rescaling of integers $k$. For instance, if $k$ denotes the number of observations taken and $c_{n}$ is the cost of each observation, one introduces a "time scale" by taking a time equal to $c_{n} k$.
