One then lets $\mathscr{A}_{n}(t)$ be the $\sigma$-field $\mathscr{A}_{n}^{\prime}(k)$ for $k$ equal to the integer part of $c_{n}^{-1} t$. Note that in this situation the right continuity of the $\sigma$-fields $\mathscr{A}_{n}(t)$ is automatic.

Note that in the above description the set $\Xi$ does not depend on the index $n$ of the system $\mathscr{S}_{n}$. We shall place on $\Xi$ the following restriction.

Assumption 1. The origin 0 of the space $H$ belongs to $\Xi$. It is an interior point of the closure of $\Xi$.

Given a system $\mathscr{S}_{n}$, for each $t$ and each $\xi \in \Xi$ one may define RadonNikodym densities as follows. Let $g_{n}(\xi, t)$ be the density of $P_{n}(\xi, t)$ with respect
to $P_{n}[0, t]+P_{n}[\xi, t]$, on the $\sigma$-field $\mathscr{A}_{n}(t)$. This is a function defined on $\mathscr{X}_{n}$. It is assumed to be $\mathscr{A}_{n}(t)$-measurable.

For the dominating measure $P_{n}[0, t]+P_{n}[\xi, t]$ the family $g_{n}(\xi, s), s \leq t$ satisfies the martingale equalities. Also, $0 \leq g_{n}(\xi, s) \leq 1$. Thus, since we have assumed that the $\mathscr{A}_{n}(t) ; t \in[0, \infty)$ are right continuous one can select versions of the $g_{n}(\xi, t)$ in such a way that the functions $t \mapsto g(\xi, t)$ are right continuous.

We shall assume that the $g_{n}(\xi, t)$ are selected in this manner and take for density of $P_{n}(\xi, t)$ with respect to $P_{n}(0, t)$ the function $f_{n}(\xi, t)=g_{n}(\xi, t)\left[1-g_{n}(\xi, t)\right]^{-1}$ which takes its values in $[0, \infty]$.

Now consider a stopping time $T_{n}$ adapted to the family $\left\{\mathscr{A}_{n}(t)\right\}$. It defines a $\sigma$-field $\mathscr{A}_{n}\left(T_{n}\right)$. Let $P_{n}\left[\xi, T_{n}\right]$ be the restriction of the coherent family $P_{n}[\xi, t]$, $t \in[0, \infty)$ to that $\sigma$-field. This is also a probability measure.

One can easily verify that the evaluation $f_{n}\left(\xi, T_{n}\right)$ is a version of the density of $P_{n}\left[\xi, T_{n}\right]$ with respect to $P_{n}\left[0, T_{n}\right]$. (Here "density" means, as usual, density of the part of $P_{n}\left[\xi, T_{n}\right]$ which is dominated by $P_{n}\left[0, T_{n}\right]$.)

A proof could be carried out as follows. Return to the densities $g_{n}(\xi, t)$ with respect to the sum $P_{n}(\xi, t)+P_{n}[0, t]$. If $T_{n}$ is given, let $T_{n, m}$ be the dyadic stopping time which takes the value $k 2^{-m}$ if $(k-1) 2^{-m} \leq T_{n}<k 2^{-m}$ for integer values of $k$. For $T_{n, m}$ the corresponding density is obviously equivalent to $g_{n}\left(\xi, T_{n, m}\right)$. The limit of $g_{n}\left(\xi, T_{n, m}\right)$ as $m \rightarrow \infty$ is $g_{n}\left(\xi, T_{n}\right)$. Hence the assertion.

For a given stopping time $T_{n}$, the family $\left\{P_{n}\left[\xi, T_{n}\right] ; \xi \in \Xi\right\}$ is a certain experiment which will be noted $\mathscr{E}_{n}\left(T_{n}\right)$. On the measures $P_{n}[\xi, t]$ we shall make the following assumptions.

Assumption 2. For each fixed pair ( $\xi, t$ ) the sequences $\left\{P_{n}[\xi, t]\right\}$ and $\left\{P_{n}[0, t]\right\}$, $n=1,2, \ldots$ are contiguous.

Assumption 3. For each fixed pair ( $\xi, t$ ) the differences

$$
f_{n}(\xi, t)-\exp \left\{\xi S_{n}(t)-t \beta(\xi)\right\}
$$

tend to zero in probability as $n \rightarrow \infty$.
We shall also need a stronger assumption as follows.
Assumption 4. For each fixed pair ( $\xi, t$ ) the expression

$$
\sup _{s \leq t}\left|f_{n}(\xi, s)-\exp \left\{\xi S_{n}(s)-s \beta(\xi)\right\}\right|
$$

tends to zero in probability as $n \rightarrow \infty$.
Assumption 4, which is stronger than 3, will give us the most satisfactory conclusions. However there are many circumstances in which 4 may fail but 3 becomes satisfied without any particular precautions. As we shall show, it permits conclusions which are still very usable provided one is willing to restrict the stopping times $T_{n}$ to a mild extent. The following definition is introduced for technical reasons.

Definition 1. Let Assumptions 1, 2, and 3 be satisfied and let $T_{n}$ be a stopping time adapted to the family $\mathscr{A}_{n}(t) ; t \in[0, \infty)$. The sequence $\left\{T_{n}\right\}$ will be called regular if
(1) the sequence of distribution $\mathscr{L}\left[T_{n} \mid \xi\right]$ is relatively compact on $[0, \infty)$;
(2) the differences

$$
f_{n}\left(\xi, T_{n}+\alpha\right)-\exp \left\{\xi S_{n}\left(T_{n}+\alpha\right)-\left(T_{n}+\alpha\right) \beta(\xi)\right\}
$$

tend to zero in probability for every $\alpha \in[0,1]$.
Note that any sequence $\left\{T_{n}\right\}$ whose values are restricted to lie in a fixed finite set $D$ is automatically regular. Also, when Assumption 4 is satisfied, any sequence $\left\{T_{n}\right\}$ with a relatively compact sequence of distributions is regular.

Passages to the limit will introduce a certain process $S$ which has independent increments and is such that

$$
E \exp \{\xi S(t)\}=\exp \{t \beta(\xi)\}
$$

for all $t \in[0, \infty)$ and $\xi \in \Xi$. In fact we shall need a particular version of this process, with an increasing family of $\sigma$-fields $\mathscr{F}_{t}$ as follows.

Let $\Omega$ be the set of functions from $[0, \infty)$ to $H^{\prime}$ which have left limits and are right continuous. Let $\Omega_{1}$ be the product $\Omega_{1}=\Omega \times[0,1]$ of $\Omega$ by the interval $[0,1]$. On $\Omega_{1}$ let $\mathscr{F}_{t}$ be the $\sigma$-field generated by all the evaluation maps $(\omega, \gamma) \mapsto (\omega(s), \gamma) \in H^{\prime} \times[0,1]$ for $s \leq t$. Let $\mathscr{F}_{t+}=\bigcap_{s}\left\{\mathscr{F}_{s} ; s>t\right\}$.

On the set $\Omega_{1}$ we shall define a measure $P_{0}^{1}=P_{0} \otimes \lambda$ where $\lambda$ is the Lebesgue measure of $[0,1]$ and where $P_{0}$ is the distribution of the process with independent increments $\{S(t) ; t \in[0, \infty)\}$ mentioned above. On the $\sigma$-fields $\mathscr{F}_{t}$ or $\mathscr{F}_{t+}$ the measure $P_{\xi, t}^{1}$ will be the measure which has density $\exp \{\xi \omega(t)-t \beta(\xi)\}$ with respect to the restriction $P_{0, t}^{1}$ of $P_{0}^{1}$ to $\mathscr{F}_{t}$ or $\mathscr{F}_{t+}$.

All the way through we shall restrict our considerations to stopping times $T_{n}$ such that the sequences of distributions $\left\{\mathscr{L}\left[T_{n} \mid \xi\right]\right\}$ are relatively compact on $[0, \infty)$. In many problems of interest one considers stopping variables $N_{n}$ which are integer valued with an assigned cost equal to an expectation $E\left[c_{n} N_{n} \mid \xi\right]$. It is then presumed that one will preferentially use variables with small cost. To assume that $E\left[c_{n} N_{n} \mid \xi\right]$ remains bounded for each $\xi$ would lead to a stronger restriction than the compactness requirement described here. However, expectations may be easily distorted by large values which are very infrequent. Thus, the compactness requirement imposed on the distributions $\mathscr{L}\left[T_{n} \mid \xi\right]$ seems more appropriate.

## 3. Basic Auxiliary Lemmas

It will be assumed throughout that the systems $\mathscr{S}_{n}$ satisfy Assumptions 1, 2 , and 3 of the preceding section. Also, the only stopping times considered will be assumed to satisfy the compactness requirement that the sequences of distributions $\mathscr{L}\left[T_{n} \mid \xi\right] ; n=1,2, \ldots$ are relatively compact on $[0, \infty)$.

Lemma 1. Under the above assumptions, for every $\xi \in \Xi$ the sequences $\left\{P_{n}\left[\xi, T_{n}\right]\right\}$ and $\left\{P_{n}\left[0, T_{n}\right]\right\}$ are contiguous.

Proof. Let $A_{n}$ be a set $A_{n} \in \mathscr{A}_{n}\left(T_{n}\right)$. Assume that there is some $\varepsilon>$ 0 such that $P_{n}\left[\xi, T_{n} ; A_{n}\right] \geq 2 \varepsilon$ infinitely often. Take a number $b$ such that,
for the distribution induced by the $P_{n}[\xi, t]$ the probability that $T_{n} \geq b$ does not exceed $\varepsilon$. Let $B_{n}=A_{n} \cap\left[T_{n} \leq b\right]$. Then $P_{n}\left[\xi, T_{n} ; B_{n}\right] \geq \varepsilon$ infinitely often. However $P_{n}\left[\xi, T_{n} ; B_{n}\right]=P_{n}\left[\xi, b ; B_{n}\right]$. Also $\left\{P_{n}[\xi, b]\right\}$ and $\left\{P_{n}[0, b]\right\}$ are assumed to be contiguous. Thus $P_{n}\left[0, T_{n} ; A_{n}\right] \geq P_{n}\left[0, b ; B_{n}\right]$ cannot tend to zero. Interchanging the roles of $\xi$ and 0 in the above argument yields the conclusion as stated.

For the next assertions, consider stopping times $T_{n}$ and a fixed number $\alpha \geq 0$. Let $X_{n}=S_{n}\left(T_{n}\right)$ and $Y_{n}=S_{n}\left(T_{n}+\alpha\right)-S_{n}\left(T_{n}\right)$. Also let $U_{n}$ be some $\mathscr{A}_{n}\left(T_{n}\right)$-measurable map to some fixed Euclidean space.

Proposition 1. Let Assumptions 1, 2, and 3 be satisfied. Assume also that the sequence $\left\{T_{n}\right\}$ is regular in the sense of Definition 1 and that the joint distributions $\mathscr{L}\left[U_{n}, T_{n}, X_{n}, Y_{n} \mid 0\right]$ converge to a limit $L_{0}=\mathscr{L}[U, T, X, Y \mid 0]$.

Then, for each $\xi \in \Xi$ the distributions $\mathscr{L}\left[U_{n}, T_{n}, X_{n}, Y_{n} \mid \xi\right]$ converge to a limit $L_{\xi}$. The density of $L_{\xi}$ with respect to $L_{0}$ is given by the expression $\exp \{\xi(x+y)-(t+\alpha) \beta(\xi)\}$. The experiment $\left\{L_{\xi} ; \xi \in \Xi\right\}$ is the limit of the experiments $\mathscr{E}_{n}\left(T_{n}+\alpha\right)$.

For the limiting distributions $L_{\xi}$ the triplet ( $U, T, X$ ) and the variable $Y$ are independent. The Laplace transform of $Y$ is given by the relation $E_{0} \exp \{\xi Y\}= \exp \{\alpha \beta(\xi)\}$ for all $\xi$ interior to the closure of $\Xi$.

Proof. Since the sequence $\left\{T_{n}\right\}$ is regular, the difference between the likelihood ratios $f_{n}\left(\xi, T_{n}+\alpha\right)$ and $\exp \left\{\xi\left(X_{n}+Y_{n}\right)-\left(T_{n}+\alpha\right) \beta(\xi)\right\}$ tends to zero in probability. By Lemma 1 the corresponding sequences of measures are contiguous. Thus the first assertion is a consequence of Proposition 5, Chapter 6.

The assertion concerning the limits of the experiments is a consequence of the fact that here the pairs ( $S_{n}\left(T_{n}+\alpha\right), T_{n}$ ) form a distinguished sequence of asymptotically sufficient statistics (see Chapter 7).

To prove the independence of ( $U, T, X$ ) and $Y$, write the distribution $L_{0}$ of the quadruplets ( $U, T, X, Y$ ) in the form

$$
L_{0}(d u, d t, d x, d y)=M(d u, d t, d x) K(d y \mid u, t, x)
$$

for a marginal distribution $M$ and a conditional distribution $K$. Let $g$ be an arbitrary bounded measurable function defined on the space of triplets $(u, t, x)$. Consider the expectation $E_{\xi} g[U, T, X]$ for the distribution $L_{\xi}$.

Applying the first part of the proposition for $\alpha=0$ this may be written

$$
E_{\xi} g(U, T, X)=\int g(u, t, x) \exp \{\xi x-t \beta(\xi)\} M(d u, d t, d x)
$$

Using the same argument for a fixed $\alpha>0$ the same expectation may be written

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \iint g(u, t, x) \exp \{\xi(x+y)-(t+\alpha) \beta(\xi)\} d K d M \\
& \quad=\int g(u, t, x) \exp \{\xi x-t \beta(\xi)\} \phi(\xi, u, t, x) d M
\end{aligned}
$$

with

$$
\phi(\xi, u, t, x)=\int \exp \{\xi y-\alpha \beta(\xi)\} K(d y \mid u, t, x)
$$

Since $g$ is arbitrary, identification of the two expressions shows that $\phi(\xi, u, t, x)=$ 1 for almost all triplets $(u, t, x)$. This argument may be applied to each $\xi$ of a countable set dense in $\Xi$. Thus, eliminating a set which has $M$ measure zero, one concludes that the Laplace transform $\int \exp \{\xi y\} K(d y \mid u, t, x)$ is equal to $\exp \{\alpha \beta(\xi)\}$ on the countable dense set and therefore also for every $\xi$ interior to the closure of $\Xi$. This concludes the proof of the proposition.

Remark 1. In Proposition 1 we have used just one number $\alpha$. One could also consider a finite sequence, say $0=\alpha_{0}<\alpha_{1}<\cdots<\alpha_{m}$. In this situation let $\mathbf{Y}_{n}$ be the vector which has coordinates $Y_{n}^{i}=S_{n}\left(T_{n}+\alpha_{i}\right)-S_{n}\left(T_{n}+\alpha_{i-1}\right), i= 1,2, \ldots, m$. It is a corollary of Proposition 1 that in the limiting distribution of ( $U_{n}, T_{n}, X_{n}, \mathbf{Y}_{n}$ ) the triplet ( $U, T, X$ ) is independent of $\mathbf{Y}$. Also the coordinates of $\mathbf{Y}$ are mutually independent. This can be shown by repeating the argument of Proposition 1, but an easier argument is to apply Proposition 1 recursively starting with $Y^{m}$ and working down.

Remark 2. According to the first part of the proposition, the limit of the experiments $\mathscr{E}_{n}\left(T_{n}\right)$ is entirely determined by the limit of the joint distributions $\mathscr{L}\left[T_{n}, S_{n}\left(T_{n}\right) \mid 0\right]$ whenever $\left\{T_{n}\right\}$ is assumed to be regular. It does not necessarily follow, however, that if the $\mathscr{E}_{n}\left(T_{n}\right)$ tend to a limit so do the said joint distributions.

Note also that the definition of "regularity" involves two separate pieces. One of them has to do with the convergence to zero of the differences

$$
f_{n}\left(\xi, T_{n}\right)-\exp \left\{\xi S_{n}\left(T_{n}\right)-T_{n} \beta(\xi)\right\} .
$$

The other refers to the relative compactness of the sequences $\mathscr{L}\left(T_{n} \mid \xi\right)$. It will be convenient to know that under suitable circumstances this relative compactness may be deduced from the fact that the sequences $\mathscr{L}\left[T_{n}, S_{n}\left(T_{n}\right) \mid 0\right]$ (at $\xi=0$ only) are relatively compact. A specific statement is as follows.

Lemma 2. Let Assumptions 1, 2, and 3 be satisfied. Let $\left\{T_{n}\right\}$ be a regular sequence of stopping times. Let $\tau_{n}$ be another sequence of stopping times such that
(i) for every real number $b$, the sequence $\left\{\tau_{n} \wedge b\right\}$ is regular;
(ii) the Prohorov distance between $\mathscr{L}\left[\tau_{n}, S_{n}\left(\tau_{n}\right) \mid 0\right]$ and $\mathscr{L}\left[T_{n}, S_{n}\left(T_{n}\right) \mid 0\right]$ tends to zero.

Then $\left\{\tau_{n}\right\}$ is regular and the Prohorov distance between $\mathscr{L}\left[\tau_{n}, S_{n}\left(\tau_{n}\right) \mid \xi\right]$ and $\mathscr{L}\left[T_{n}, S_{n}\left(T_{n}\right) \mid \xi\right]$ tends to zero for every $\xi \in \Xi$.

Proof. Suppose that for a given $\xi$ the sequence $\mathscr{L}\left[\tau_{n} \mid \xi\right]$ is not relatively compact. Then, there is an $\varepsilon_{0}>0$ an a sequence $n(r) ; r=1,2, \ldots$, such that $n(r) \rightarrow \infty$ and such that for every $b \in(1, \infty)$ the probability under $\xi$ that $\tau_{n}>2 b$ is eventually larger than $2 \varepsilon_{0}$.

Taking a further subsequence if necessary, one may assume that along the subsequence the distributions $\mathscr{L}\left[T_{n}, S_{n}\left(T_{n}\right) \mid 0\right]$ have a certain limit, say $L_{0}$.

Since for $T_{n}$ itself the sequences $\mathscr{L}\left[T_{n} \mid \xi\right]$ are relatively compact, one may select a $b$ such that eventually the probability under $\xi$ that $T_{n}>b$ is less than $\varepsilon_{0}$. This $b$ may be taken such that it is a point of continuity of the limit of $\mathscr{L}\left[T_{n} \mid 0\right]$ along the subsequence $\{n(r)\}$.

Now consider the stopping times $\tau_{n}^{\prime}=\tau_{n} \wedge(2 b)$ and the joint distributions $\mathscr{L}\left[\tau_{n}, S_{n}\left(\tau_{n}\right) \mid \xi\right]$ and $\mathscr{L}\left[\tau_{n}^{\prime}, S_{n}\left(\tau_{n}^{\prime}\right) \mid \xi\right]$ on the space $[0, \infty) \times H^{\prime}$. The parts of these distributions carried by the set $[0, b] \times H^{\prime}$ coincide since $\tau_{n}^{\prime}=\tau_{n}$ unless $\tau_{n}>2 b$. In particular, if $J$ is the indicator of $[0, b] \times H^{\prime}$, the measures $J \mathscr{L}\left[\tau_{n}^{\prime}, S_{n}\left(\tau_{n}^{\prime}\right) \mid 0\right]$ and $J \mathscr{L}\left[\tau_{n}, S_{n}\left(\tau_{n}\right) \mid 0\right]$ both converge to the same limit $J L_{0}$ as $J \mathscr{L}\left[T_{n}, S_{n}\left(T_{n}\right) \mid 0\right]$.

Let $I_{n}$ be the indicator of the set $\left\{\tau_{n} \leq b\right\}$ and consider the difference

$$
I_{n}\left[f_{n}\left(\xi, \tau_{n}\right)-\exp \left\{\xi S_{n}\left(\tau_{n}\right)-\tau_{n} \beta(\xi)\right\}\right]
$$

This difference remains unchanged if one replaces $\tau_{n}$ by $\tau_{n}^{\prime}$. By the regularity assumption made on $\tau_{n}^{\prime}$, the differences in question all tend to zero as $n$ tends to infinity.

However, since the $\tau_{n}^{\prime}$ are bounded by $2 b$, the sequences $\left\{P_{n}\left[\xi, \tau_{n}^{\prime}\right]\right\}$ and $\left\{P_{n}\left[0, \tau_{n}^{\prime}\right]\right\}$ are contiguous and the limit of $E_{\xi} I_{n}$ is given by the expression

$$
\int_{\tau \leq b} \exp \{\xi S-\tau \beta(\xi)\} d L_{1}
$$

where $L_{1}$ is the limit of the sequences $\mathscr{L}\left[\tau_{n}^{\prime}, S_{n}\left(\tau_{n}^{\prime}\right) \mid 0\right]$. This coincides with $\int_{\tau \leq b} \exp \{\xi S-\tau \beta(\xi)\} d L_{0}$ and cannot be larger than $\varepsilon_{0}$. The contradiction so obtained proves the desired assertion.

Proposition 2. Let $T_{n}$ and $T_{n}^{*}$ be stopping times adapted to the family $\mathscr{A}_{n}(t)$; $t \in[0, \infty)$. Let Assumptions 1, 2, and 3 be satisfied. Assume that $T_{n}-T_{n}^{*}$ tends to zero in probability as $n \rightarrow \infty$. Assume also that both $\left\{T_{n}\right\}$ and $\left\{T_{n}^{*}\right\}$ are regular. Then the difference $S_{n}\left(T_{n}\right)-S_{n}\left(T_{n}^{*}\right)$ tends to zero in probability.

Proof. Select a number $\alpha>0$ and consider the stopping times $T_{n}^{1}=T_{n} \wedge T_{n}^{*}$, $T_{n}^{2}=T_{n} \vee T_{n}^{*}$, and $T_{n}^{3}=T_{n}^{1}+\alpha$. Note that if both $\left\{T_{n}\right\}$ and $\left\{T_{n}^{*}\right\}$ are regular so are all the sequences $\left\{T_{n}^{i}\right\}, i=1,2,3$.

Let $F$ be a finite subset of $\Xi$ which contains the origin and a basis for the linear space $H$ spanned by $\Xi$. Each of the stopping times mentioned above defines an experiment. For the purposes of the present proof we shall let $\mathscr{E}_{n}\left(T_{n}^{i}\right)$ be the experiment defined by $T_{n}^{i}$ but with a set of indices restricted to $F$ instead of the entire $\Xi$.

For the ordering of experiments introduced in Chapter 2, one has $\mathscr{E}_{n}\left(T_{n}^{1}\right) \leq \mathscr{E}_{n}\left(T_{n}\right) \leq \mathscr{E}_{n}\left(T_{n}^{2}\right)$ and a similar inequality where $T_{n}$ is replaced by $T_{n}^{*}$. Let ( $\mathscr{E}^{1}, \mathscr{E}^{2}, \mathscr{E}^{3}$ ) be a cluster point of the sequence of triplets $\left\{\mathscr{E}_{n}\left(T_{n}^{i}\right) ; i=1,2,3\right\}$. It is clear that $\mathscr{E}^{1} \leq \mathscr{E}^{2}$. To show that one has also $\mathscr{E}^{2} \leq \mathscr{E}^{3}$, consider the set $A_{n}=\left\{T_{n}^{2} \leq T_{n}^{3}\right\}$. This set belongs to the $\sigma$-fields $\mathscr{A}_{n}\left(T_{n}^{i}\right)$ for $i=2$ and $i=3$ both. On it the measures $P_{n}\left(\xi, T_{n}^{i}\right), i=2,3$ coincide. Thus the distance between $\mathscr{E}_{n}\left(T_{n}^{2}\right)$ and $\mathscr{E}_{n}\left(T_{n}^{2} \wedge T_{n}^{3}\right)$ does not exceed $\sup _{\xi \in F} P_{n}\left[\xi, T_{n}^{2} ; A_{n}^{c}\right]$. Since this tends to zero as $n \rightarrow \infty$, the inequality $\mathscr{E}^{2} \leq \mathscr{E}^{3}$ follows.



To continue, let us show that given $\varepsilon>0$ one may select $\alpha$ so small that $\delta\left(\mathscr{E}^{1}, \mathscr{E}^{3}\right)<\varepsilon$. For this purpose let $Y_{n}=S_{n}\left(T_{n}^{3}\right)-S_{n}\left(T_{n}^{1}\right)$ and let $L_{0}$ be a cluster point of the sequence $\mathscr{L}\left[T_{n}^{1}, S_{n}\left(T_{n}^{1}\right), Y_{n} \mid 0\right]$ selected so that it corresponds to the cluster point $\left(\mathscr{E}^{1}, \mathscr{E}^{2}, \mathscr{E}^{3}\right)$.

According to Proposition 1 the experiment $\mathscr{E}^{3}$ can be realized by measures $L_{\xi}, \xi \in F$ such that the density of $L_{\xi}$ with respect to $L_{0}$ is

$$
g_{3}(t, x, y)=\exp \{\xi(x+y)-(t+\alpha) \beta(\xi)\}
$$

The experiment $\mathscr{E}^{1}$ can be realized on the same space using densities

$$
g_{1}(t, x, y)=\exp \{\xi x-t \beta(\xi)\}
$$

The Hellinger affinity between $g_{1}$ and $g_{3}$ is equal to

$$
\exp \left\{\alpha\left[\beta\left(\frac{\xi}{2}\right)-\frac{1}{2} \beta(\xi)\right]\right\}
$$

This can be made as close to unity as desired by taking $\alpha$ small enough. Therefore, given $\varepsilon>0$ there is some $\alpha$ such that $\delta\left(\mathscr{E}^{1}, \mathscr{E}^{3}\right)<\varepsilon$. It follows that the distances $\Delta\left[\mathscr{E}_{n}\left(T_{n}^{1}\right), \mathscr{E}_{n}\left(T_{n}^{2}\right)\right]$ tend to zero and the same conclusion applies to the smaller distances $\Delta\left[\mathscr{E}_{n}\left(T_{n}\right), \mathscr{E}_{n}\left(T_{n}^{*}\right)\right]$.

To conclude we shall show that $S_{n}\left(T_{n}\right)-S_{n}\left(T_{n}^{1}\right)$ tends to zero in probability. Since the same argument can be applied with $T_{n}$ replaced by $T_{n}^{*}$ the desired result will follow.

For the purpose, take a particular $\xi \in F$ and let $\mu_{n}=P_{n}\left[0, T_{n}\right]+P_{n}\left[\xi, T_{n}\right]$. Then $P_{n}\left[\xi, T_{n}\right]$ has with respect to $\mu_{n}$ a density $V_{n}$ on $\mathscr{A}_{n}\left(T_{n}\right)$ and a density $V_{n}^{1}$ on $\mathscr{A}_{n}\left(T_{n}^{1}\right)$. Let $\delta_{n}$ be the deficiency $\delta\left[\mathscr{E}_{n}\left(T_{n}^{1}\right), \mathscr{E}_{n}\left(T_{n}\right)\right]$. According to Chapter 4, Lemma 1 these densities are such that $\int\left|V_{n}^{1}-V_{n}\right|^{2} d \mu_{n} \leq 4 \delta_{n}$. Thus $V_{n}^{1}-V_{n}$ tends to zero in quadratic mean. By contiguity the differences

$$
\log \frac{V_{n}^{1}}{1-V_{n}^{1}}-\log \frac{V_{n}}{1-V_{n}}
$$

will also tend to zero in probability.
However, according to the regularity assumption made on the times $T_{n}$ the above difference of logarithms is approximable by the expression

$$
\xi\left[S_{n}\left(T_{n}^{1}\right)-S_{n}\left(T_{n}\right)\right]-\left(T_{n}^{1}-T_{n}\right) \beta(\xi) .
$$

Thus $\xi\left[S_{n}\left(T_{n}^{1}\right)-S_{n}\left(T_{n}\right)\right]$ tends to zero in probability. As $\xi$ varies in $F$ which contains a basis of $H$, this yields the desired conclusion.

## 4. Reduction Theorems

In this section we consider a sequence $\left\{\mathscr{E}_{n}\left(T_{n}\right)\right\}$ of experiments which satisfy Assumptions 1, 2, and 3 of Section 2 for a regular sequence $\left\{T_{n}\right\}$. It is shown that,
as far as limit of distributions $\mathscr{L}\left[T_{n}, S_{n}\left(T_{n}\right) \mid \xi\right]$ are concerned, one could replace the $\sigma$-fields $\left\{\mathscr{A}_{n}(t) ; t \in[0, \infty)\right\}$ by $\sigma$-fields $\left\{\mathscr{B}_{n}(t) ; t \in[0, \infty)\right\}$ where $\mathscr{B}_{n}(t)$ is the $\sigma$-field generated by $\left\{S_{n}(s) ; s \leq t\right\}$ and by a variable uniformly distributed on $[0,1]$ independently of everything else.

It is also shown that if $\mathscr{L}\left[T_{n}, S_{n}\left(T_{n}\right) \mid 0\right]$ has a limit, then this limit can be realized by a suitable stopping time of the process with independent increments $S$ with $\sigma$-fields $\mathscr{F}_{t++}$ introduced in Section 2.

To proceed, let $D$ be a fixed subset of $[0, \infty)$. Assume that $D$ is discrete. That is, $D$ does not have any accumulation points. The space $\mathscr{W}$ of functions from
$D$ to $H^{\prime}$ is a factor of the space $\Omega$ used in Section 2. Similarly $\mathscr{W}_{1}=\mathscr{W} \times[0,1]$ is a factor of the space $\Omega_{1}$ of Section 2. On $\mathscr{W}_{1}$ one can introduce $\sigma$-fields $\mathscr{D}_{t}$, $t \in D$ such that $\mathscr{D}_{t}$ is generated by the coordinate evaluations $(\omega, \gamma) \mapsto(\omega(s), \gamma)$, $s \leq t$.

Let $W=\{W(t) ; t \in D\}$ be a process with independent increments such that $E \exp \{\xi W(t)\}=\exp \{t \beta(\xi)\}$ for all $t \in D$ and all $\xi \in \Xi$. Let $P_{0}$ be the distribution of $W$ in the space $\mathscr{W}$ and let $P_{0}^{1}=P_{0} \otimes \lambda$ be the product of $P_{0}$ by the Lebesgue measure $\lambda$ on $[0,1]$.

Let $P_{\xi}^{1}$ be the measure which has density $\exp \{\xi \omega(t)-t \beta(\xi)\}$ with respect to $P_{0}^{1}$ on the $\sigma$-field $\mathscr{D}_{t}$. Here a generic element $w_{1}$ of $\mathscr{W}_{1}$ has the form $\omega_{1}=(\omega, \gamma)$ for $\omega \in \mathscr{W}$ and $\gamma \in[0,1]$ and $\omega(t)$ is the evaluation at $t$ of $\omega$.

Proposition 3. Let Assumptions 1, 2, and 3 be satisfied. Let $T_{n}$ be a stopping time adapted to the $\sigma$-fields $\left\{\mathscr{A}_{n}(t) ; t \in[0, \infty)\right\}$. Assume that $T_{n}$ takes its values in the set $D$ only. Also assume that the distributions $\mathscr{L}\left[T_{n}, S_{n}\left(T_{n}\right) \mid 0\right]$ converge to a limit and, if $D$ is infinite, that $\mathscr{L}\left[T_{n} \mid \xi\right]$ is relatively compact for all $\xi \in \Xi$. Then there is on $\mathscr{W}_{1}$ a stopping time $\tau$ adapted to the $\sigma$-fields $\left\{\mathscr{D}_{t} ; t \in D\right\}$ such that
(i) the distribution $L_{0}$ of $(\tau, w(\tau))$ for $P_{0}^{1}$ is the limit of the sequence $\mathscr{L}\left[T_{n}, S_{n}\left(T_{n}\right) \mid 0\right]$;
(ii) the experiment given by the measures $L_{\xi}$ such that

$$
d L_{\xi}=\exp \{\xi \omega-\tau \beta(\xi)\} d L_{0}
$$

is the limit of the experiment $\mathscr{E}_{n}\left(T_{n}\right)$.
Proof. Let $\mathscr{V}$ be the space of functions from $D$ to the product $\{0,1\} \times H^{\prime}$ of the two point set $\{0,1\}$ by the Euclidean space $H^{\prime}$. For $t \in D$ let $U_{n}(t)$ be the indicator of the event $\left\{T_{n} \leq t\right\}$. Then the process $\left\{U_{n}(t), S_{n}(t) ; t \in D\right\}$ is a process with trajectories in $\mathscr{V}$. For the measures $P_{n}[0, t], t \in D$ this process has a certain distribution, say $M_{n}$. For convergence of finite dimensional distributions, the sequence $M_{n}$ has at least one cluster point. Select such a point and call it $M$.

On the space $\mathscr{V}$, let $\mathscr{G}_{t}$ be the $\sigma$-field generated by the coordinate evaluations $\{\omega(s) ; s \leq t\}$ where $\omega(s)$ is the coordinate in $H^{\prime}$ of the point $(u(s), \omega(s))$ of $\{0,1\} \times H^{\prime}$. Let $\mathscr{G}_{t}^{\prime}$ be the $\sigma$-field generated by the differences $\omega(s)-\omega(t)$ for $s \in D, s>t$. Finally, let $\mathscr{G}$ be the smallest $\sigma$-field containing all the $\mathscr{G}_{t}$ and $\mathscr{G}_{t}^{\prime}$.

Let $V=\{[U(t), W(t)] ; t \in D\}$ be a random element which has distribution $M$. For each $t \in D$ let $\phi_{t}$ be a conditional expectation $E_{0}\{U(t) \mid \mathscr{G}\}$. We claim
that one can select versions $\phi_{t}^{\prime}$ of the $\phi_{t}$ so that (i) $\phi_{t}^{\prime}$ is $\mathscr{G}_{t}$ measurable and (ii) $0 \leq \phi_{s}^{\prime} \leq \phi_{t}^{\prime} \leq 1$ for all pairs $s<t$.

To prove this, fix a value $t \in D$ and let $(f, g)$ be a pair of bounded functions defined on $\mathscr{V}$ and such that $f$ is $\mathscr{G}_{t}$-measurable and $g$ is $\mathscr{G}_{t}^{\prime}$-measurable. Applying Proposition 1 one sees that the pairs $(f, U(s)) ; s \leq t$ are independent of $g$. Thus the conditional expectations $\phi_{t}$ must satisfy the relations

$$
E_{0}\left[f \phi_{t} g\right]=E_{0} f U(t) g=E_{0}[f U(t)] E_{0} g
$$

Let $\psi_{t}=E_{0}\left[f U(t) \mid \mathscr{G}_{t}\right]$. Since $f$ is $\mathscr{G}_{t}$-measurable one has also $E_{0} f U(t)= E_{0}\left[f \psi_{t}\right]$. However, $f \psi_{t}$ being $\mathscr{G}_{t}$-measurable, Proposition 1 implies that $E_{0} f \psi_{t} g= E_{0}\left(f \psi_{t}\right) E_{0}(g)$. It follows that for all pairs $(f, g)$ one has $E_{0}\left[f \phi_{t} g\right]=E_{0}\left[f \psi_{t} g\right]$. Thus $\phi_{t}=\psi_{t}$ almost everywhere.

By construction, $U_{n}(s) \leq U_{n}(t)$ whenever $s<t$. Thus $U(s) \leq U(t)$ almost surely. It follows that $\phi_{s} \leq \phi_{t}$ and therefore $\psi_{s} \leq \psi_{t}$ almost surely. Now let $\phi_{t}^{\prime}=\sup _{s}\left\{\psi_{s} ; s \leq t, s \in D\right\}$. Then $\phi_{t}^{\prime}=\phi_{t}=\psi_{t}$ almost surely and $\phi_{s}^{\prime} \leq \phi_{t}^{\prime}$ everywhere if $s \leq t$.

The space $\mathscr{V}$ used here can be mapped into the space $\mathscr{W}$ of functions from $D$ to $H^{\prime}$ by retaining only the second coordinates $w(s)$ of the pairs $(u(s), w(s))$. The cluster point $M$ selected above induces then on $\mathscr{W}$ or, respectively, $\mathscr{W}_{1}= \mathscr{W} \times[0,1]$ a certain distribution which by Proposition 1 again is no other than the measure $P_{0}$ or, respectively, $P_{0}^{1}$.

For each pair $(w, \gamma) \in \mathscr{W}_{1}$ let $\tau(w, \gamma)$ be the first $t \in D$ such that $\phi_{t}^{\prime} \geq \gamma$. This defines a certain stopping time $\tau$ adapted to the $\sigma$-fields $\left\{\mathscr{D}_{t} ; t \in D\right\}$. However $\tau$ may take infinite values if $D$ is itself unbounded. On the space $\mathscr{V}$ itself let $T$ be the stopping time such that $\{T \leq t\}$ is equivalent to $U(t)=1$.

We claim that the distributions $\mathscr{L}[T, w(T)]$ and $\mathscr{L}[\tau, w(\tau)]$ are the same and that in particular $\tau$ is almost surely finite. To prove this, let $f$ be a bounded measurable function defined on the space $D \times H^{\prime}$ where $(T, w(T))$ and $(\tau, w(\tau)$ ) take their values. Fix a particular $t \in D$ and assume that $f\left(t^{\prime}, x\right)=0$ unless $t^{\prime}=t$. Let $s$ be the element of $D$ which immediately precedes $t$. Then

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{0} f[T, \omega(T)] & =E_{0}[U(t)-U(s)] f[t, \omega(t)] \\
& =E_{0}\left[\phi_{t}^{\prime}-\phi_{s}^{\prime}\right] f[t, \omega(t)]
\end{aligned}
$$

However, for the stopping time $\tau$ the conditional probability given $\omega \in \mathscr{W}$ that $\tau=t$ is also equal to $\phi_{t}^{\prime}-\phi_{s}^{\prime}$. Thus $E_{0}\left(\phi_{t}^{\prime}-\phi_{s}^{\prime}\right) f[t, w(t)]=E_{0} f[\tau, w(\tau)]$. This being true for all $t \in D$ implies $\mathscr{L}[T, w(T)]=\mathscr{L}[\tau, w(\tau)]$ as claimed. The additional statement of the proposition concerning the measures $L_{\xi}$ is a consequence of Proposition 1, and the proof is therefore complete.

To proceed further we shall use a lemma concerning stopping times adapted to the family $\left\{\mathscr{D}_{t} ; t \in D\right\}$ of $\sigma$-fields defined on $\mathscr{W}_{1}$.

Lemma 3. Let $D$ be finite. Let $P$ be a probability measure on $\mathscr{W}_{1}$ and let $\tau$ be a stopping time adapted to the $\sigma$-fields $\left\{\mathscr{D}_{t} ; t \in D\right\}$. Let $\varepsilon>0$ be a given number. Then there are stopping times $\tau^{\prime}$ adapted to the family $\left\{\mathscr{D}_{t} ; t \in D\right\}$ such that
(i) $P\left[\tau^{\prime} \neq \tau\right]<\varepsilon$;
(ii) for each $t \in D$ the boundary of the set $\left\{\tau^{\prime}=t\right\}$ has $P$-measure zero.

Proof. Let $\mathscr{W}^{t}$ be the space of functions from the subset $\{s ; s \in D, s \leq t\}$ of $D$ to $H^{\prime}$. Let $\mathscr{W}_{1}^{t}=\mathscr{W}^{t} \times[0,1]$. Then $\mathscr{W}_{1}^{t}$ is a factor of $\mathscr{W}_{1}$. The set $\{\tau \leq t\}$, which belongs to $\mathscr{D}_{t}$, is a cylinder with a base $A_{t}$ in $\mathscr{W}_{1}^{t}$. Let $r$ be the number of elements of $D$. There exist pairs ( $K_{t}, G_{t}$ ) such that (i) $K_{t}$ is compact, (ii) $G_{t}$ is open in $\mathscr{W}_{1}^{t}$, (iii) $K_{t} \subset A_{t} \subset G_{t}$, and (iv) $P\left(G_{t} \backslash K_{t}\right)<\varepsilon r^{-2}$.

For $\alpha>0$ let $K_{t}^{\alpha}$ be the set of points of $\mathscr{W}_{1}^{t}$ whose distance to $K_{t}$ does not exceed $\alpha$. For $\alpha$ small enough $K_{t}^{\alpha} \subset G_{t}$. Also, at least one of the $K_{t}^{\alpha} \subset G_{t}$ must have a boundary which has $P$-measure zero. Select such a $K_{t}^{\alpha}$ and let $B_{t}$ denote the cylinder of $\mathscr{W}_{1}$ which has base $B_{t}$. Let $C_{t}=\bigcup_{s}\left\{B_{s} ; s \leq t, s \in D\right\}$. Then the boundary of $C_{t}$ has measure zero. The symmetric difference between $C_{t}$ and the cylinder based on $A_{t}$ has measure smaller than $\varepsilon r^{-1}$. Define the stopping time $\tau^{\prime}$ by the relations $\left\{\tau^{\prime} \leq t\right\}=C_{t}$. Then $P\left(\tau^{\prime} \neq \tau\right)<\varepsilon$ and $\tau^{\prime}$ satisfies the requirements of the lemma.

A combination of the foregoing lemmas with Proposition 3 will allow us to show that as long as one is interested only in the limits of the experiments $\mathscr{E}_{n}\left(T_{n}\right)$ and the limit of the distributions $\mathscr{L}\left(T_{n} \mid \xi\right)$, it is possible to restrict attention to those stopping times $T_{n}$ which, except for randomization, are adapted to the $\sigma$-fields generated by the process $\left\{S_{n}(t) ; t \in[0, \infty)\right\}$.

Specifically, if $\mathscr{X}_{n}$ is the underlying space for the $\sigma$-fields $\mathscr{A}_{n}(t)$, let $\mathscr{X}_{n, 1}$ be the product of $\mathscr{X}_{n}$ by the interval $[0,1]$. Let $\mathscr{B}_{n}(t)$ be the $\sigma$-field generated on $\mathscr{X}_{n, 1}$ by the evaluations $\left\{S_{n}(s) ; s \leq t\right\}$ and by the evaluation of the coordinate in $[0,1]$. On $\mathscr{B}_{n}(t)$, let $P_{n}^{\prime}[\xi, t]$ be the product of $P_{n}[\xi, t]$ by the Lebesgue measure on $[0,1]$.

Theorem 1. Let Assumptions 1, 2, and 4 be satisfied and let $T_{n}$ be a stopping time adapted to the family $\mathscr{A}_{n}(t) ; t \in[0, \infty)$. Assume that for each $\xi$ the sequences $\left\{\mathscr{L}\left[T_{n} \mid \xi\right]\right\}$ are relatively compact on $[0, \infty)$. Then there are stopping times $\tau_{n}$ of the family $\mathscr{B}_{n}(t) ; t \in[0, \infty)$, such that the Prohorov distances between $\mathscr{L}\left[T_{n}, S_{n}\left(T_{n}\right) \mid \xi\right]$ and $\mathscr{L}\left[\tau_{n}, S_{n}\left(\tau_{n}\right) \mid \xi\right]$ tend to zero as $n \rightarrow \infty$ for all $\xi \in \Xi$.

Proof. Define dyadic approximations to $T_{n}$ as follows. Let $m$ be an integer. Let $T_{n, m}=k 2^{-m}$ if the integer $k$ is such that $(k-1) 2^{-m} \leq T_{n}<k 2^{-m}$. Let $T_{n, m}^{\prime}=T_{n, m} \wedge 2^{m}$. Then $T_{n, m}^{\prime}$ takes its values in the finite set $D_{m}=\left\{k 2^{-m}\right.$; $\left.k=1,2, \ldots, 2^{2 m}\right\}$.

Suppose that for each $m$ one has constructed stopping times $\tau_{n, m}$ adapted to the $\mathscr{B}_{n}(t)$ and such that the Prohorov distance $\pi_{n}(m)$ between $\mathscr{L}\left[T_{n, m}^{\prime}\right.$, $\left.S_{n}\left(T_{n, m}^{\prime}\right) \mid 0\right]$ and $\mathscr{L}\left[\tau_{n, m}, S_{n}\left(\tau_{n, m}\right) \mid 0\right]$ converges to zero. Then one can find an increasing sequence $\left\{m_{n}\right\}$ such that $m_{n} \rightarrow \infty$ and such that $\pi_{n}\left(m_{n}\right)$ still tends to zero. The distance between $\mathscr{L}\left[\tau_{n, m_{n}}, S_{n}\left(\tau_{n, m_{n}}\right) \mid 0\right]$ and $\mathscr{L}\left[T_{n}, S_{n}\left(T_{n}\right) \mid 0\right]$ will tend to zero since here Proposition is applicable.

Thus according to Lemma 2 it is sufficient to prove the result under the additional assumption that $T_{n}$ takes its values in a fixed finite set $D$. If so, let $\tau_{n}$ be adapted to the $\sigma$-fields $\mathscr{B}_{n}(t) ; t \in[0, \infty)$ and let $\rho_{n}\left(\tau_{n}\right)$ be the Prohorov distance between $\mathscr{L}\left[T_{n}, S_{n}\left(T_{n}\right) \mid 0\right]$ and $\mathscr{L}\left[\tau_{n}, S_{n}\left(\tau_{n}\right) \mid 0\right]$. Let $\rho_{n}$ be the infimum
of $\rho_{n}\left(\tau_{n}\right)$ when $\tau_{n}$ varies over all such adapted stopping times $\tau_{n}$.
Suppose that $\rho_{n}$ does not tend to zero. Then there is an $\varepsilon_{0}>0$ and a subsequence, say $n(r), r=1,2, \ldots$, such that $\rho_{n(r)} \geq 2 \varepsilon_{0}$ for all $r$. Taking a further subsequence, if necessary, one may assume that along the subsequence in question the joint distributions $\mathscr{L}\left[T_{n(r)}, S_{n(r)}\left(T_{n(r)}\right) \mid 0\right]$ converge to a limit, say $L_{0}$.

Then, according to Proposition 3, there is a stopping time $\tau$ of the limit process in the space $\mathscr{W}_{1}$ associated to $D$ such that (i) $\tau$ is adapted to the $\sigma$-fields $\mathscr{D}_{t}, t \in D$ and (ii) $\mathscr{L}\left[\tau, w(\tau) \mid P_{0}\right]=L_{0}$. According to Lemma 3 there is another stopping time, say $\tau^{\prime}$, which is similarly adapted and such that the $L_{1}$ distance $\left\|\mathscr{L}\left[\tau, \omega(\tau) \mid P_{0}\right]-\mathscr{L}\left[\tau^{\prime}, \omega\left(\tau^{\prime}\right) \mid P_{0}\right]\right\|$ is smaller than $\varepsilon_{0}$ while the sets $\left\{\tau^{\prime} \leq t\right\}$ have negligible boundaries for $P_{0}$.

These stopping times may be transformed into stopping times adapted to the $\mathscr{B}_{n}(t) ; t \in D$ since $\mathscr{B}_{n}(t)$ is larger than the inverse image of $\mathscr{D}_{n}(t)$ by the map induced by the process $S_{n}(t) ; t \in[0, \infty)$. Alternatively one may map this process into $\mathscr{W}$ and use the $\tau^{\prime}$ directly on $\mathscr{W}_{1}=\mathscr{W} \times[0,1]$.

Under Assumptions 1, 2, and 3, the joint distributions of the vectors $S_{n}(t)$; $t \in D$ always converge. It follows that the distributions $\mathscr{L}\left[\tau^{\prime}, S_{n}\left(\tau^{\prime}\right) \mid 0\right]$ converge to the limit $\mathscr{L}\left[\tau^{\prime}, w\left(\tau^{\prime}\right) \mid 0\right]$ available on the limiting processes. Thus for $n$ of the form $n(r)$ sufficiently large, the Prohorov distance between $\mathscr{L}\left[\tau^{\prime}, S_{n}\left(\tau^{\prime}\right) \mid 0\right]$ and $\mathscr{L}\left[T_{n}, S_{n}\left(T_{n}\right) \mid 0\right]$ will be at most $\varepsilon_{0}$, contradicting the assumption that $\rho_{n} \geq 2 \varepsilon_{0}$. Since one passes to the distributions $\mathscr{L}\left[T_{n}, S_{n}\left(T_{n}\right) \mid \xi\right]$ by a contiguity argument, the desired result follows.

If in the above Theorem 1 one replaces the strong Assumption 4 by the weaker Assumption 3, one cannot expect that the result as stated will remain valid. However a large part of the importance of this result is that it insures that (i) the differences $\mathscr{L}\left(T_{n} \mid \xi\right)-\mathscr{L}\left[\tau_{n} \mid \xi\right]$ tend to zero and (ii) if $\left(\mathscr{E}^{1}, \mathscr{E}^{2}\right)$ is a cluster point of the sequences $\left\{\mathscr{E}_{n}\left(\tau_{n}\right), \mathscr{E}_{n}\left(T_{n}\right)\right\}$ then $\mathscr{E}^{1}=\mathscr{E}^{2}$.

Now suppose that $\left\{\mathscr{L}\left[T_{n} \mid \xi\right]\right\}$ is relatively compact for each $\xi$ and consider the dyadic times $T_{n, m}$ introduced at the beginning of the proof of Theorem 1. If Assumptions 1, 2, and 3 hold, these times are always regular. Since $T_{n} \leq T_{n, m} \leq T_{n}+2^{-m}$, the experiments $\mathscr{E}_{n}\left(T_{n, m}\right)$ are always stronger than the $\mathscr{E}_{n}\left(T_{n}\right)$. Also, the Prohorov distance between $\mathscr{L}\left[T_{n} \mid \xi\right]$ and $\mathscr{L}\left[T_{n, m} \mid \xi\right]$ never exceeds $2^{-m}$.

Thus one could expect that there would exist stopping times $\tau_{n}$ of the $\sigma$ fields $\mathscr{B}_{n}(t) ; t \in[0, \infty)$ such that
(i) $\mathscr{L}\left[T_{n} \mid \xi\right]-\mathscr{L}\left[\tau_{n} \mid \xi\right] \rightarrow 0$;
(ii) if $\left(\mathscr{E}^{1}, \mathscr{E}^{2}\right)$ is a cluster point of the sequence $\left\{\mathscr{E}_{n}\left(\tau_{n}\right), \mathscr{E}_{n}\left(T_{n}\right)\right\}$ then $\delta\left(\mathscr{E}^{1}, \mathscr{E}^{2}\right)=$ 0.

We have not been able to prove this in general under the assumptions of pointwise convergence used here. However, here is a variant which is still useful.

Assumption 5. The systems $\mathscr{S}_{n}$ satisfy Assumption 5 if $\Xi$ is a locally compact subset of $H$ and if for every fixed $t$ and every sequence $\left\{\xi_{n}\right\}$ which converge to a limit $\xi \in \Xi$ the $L_{1}$-norms $\left\|P_{n}\left[\xi_{n}, t\right]-P_{n}[\xi, t]\right\|$ tend to zero as $n \rightarrow \infty$.

Under this assumption pointwise convergence can be replaced by uniform convergence on compacts. Since the latter is metrizable, diagonal arguments
become available. They give the following.
Theorem 2. Let Assumptions 1, 2, 3, and 5 be satisfied. Let $T_{n}$ be a stopping time of the family $\mathscr{A}_{n}(t) ; t \in[0, \infty)$. Assume that for each $\xi \in \Xi$ the sequences $\left\{\mathscr{L}\left[T_{n} \mid \xi\right]\right\}$ are relatively compact. Then there are stopping times $\tau_{n}$ adapted to the family $\mathscr{B}_{n}(t) ; t \in[0, \infty)$ with the following properties:
(i) the Prohorov distances between $\mathscr{L}\left[\tau_{n} \mid \xi\right]$ and $\mathscr{L}\left[T_{n} \mid \xi\right]$ tend to zero uniformly on the compacts of $\Xi$, and
(ii) if $\left(\mathscr{E}^{1}, \mathscr{E}^{2}\right)$ is a weak cluster point of the sequence $\left[\mathscr{E}_{n}\left(\tau_{n}\right), \mathscr{E}_{n}\left(T_{n}\right)\right]$, then $\delta\left(\mathscr{E}^{1}, \mathscr{E}^{2}\right)=0$.

Proof. Let $T_{n, m}$ be the dyadic time defined by $T_{n, m}=k 2^{-m}$ if $(k-1) 2^{-m} \leq T_{n}<k 2^{-m}$ and let $T_{n, m}^{\prime}=T_{n, m} \wedge 2^{m}$. Construct stopping times $\tau_{n, m}$ exactly as for the proof of Theorem 1. For these times which take values in the fixed finite set $D_{m}$, the use of Assumption 4 is not necessary. It may be replaced by Assumption 3. Thus, by the argument of Theorem 1, for any finite subset $K$ of $\Xi$, the supremum

$$
\sup _{\xi \in K} \pi\left[\mathscr{L}\left(T_{n, m}^{\prime} \mid \xi\right), \mathscr{L}\left(\tau_{n, m} \mid \xi\right)\right]
$$

of the relevant Prohorov distances tends to zero. Similarly the distances $\Delta_{k}\left[\mathscr{E}_{n}\left(T_{n, m}^{\prime}\right), \mathscr{E}_{n}\left(\tau_{n, m}\right)\right]$ for experiments with a set of indices restricted to the finite set $K$ will also tend to zero.

In the present case Assumption 5 implies that in the above statements the finite set $K$ may be replaced by any compact subset of $\Xi$. Thus since the compacts of $\Xi$ admit a countable base, there exists a sequence $\{m(n)\}, m(n) \rightarrow \infty$ such that for every compact subset $K \subset \Xi$ the expressions

$$
\sup _{\xi \in K} \pi\left[\mathscr{L}\left(T_{n, m(n)}^{\prime} \mid \xi\right), \mathscr{L}\left(\tau_{n, m(n)} \mid \xi\right)\right]
$$

and

$$
\Delta_{K}\left[\mathscr{E}_{n}\left(T_{n, m(n)}^{\prime}\right), \mathscr{E}_{n}\left(\tau_{n, m(n)}\right)\right]
$$

both tend to zero as $n \rightarrow \infty$.
Now $T_{n} \leq T_{n, m(n)}^{\prime} \leq T_{n}+2^{-m(n)}$ except if $T_{n}>2^{m(n)}$. Under Assumption 5 the probability under $\xi$ that $T_{n}>2^{m(n)}$ tends to zero uniformly on compacts. This gives the first assertion. The same inequalities imply that for any cluster point ( $\mathscr{E}, \mathscr{E}^{\prime}$ ) of the sequence $\left\{\mathscr{E}_{n}\left(T_{n, m(n)}\right), \mathscr{E}_{n}\left(T_{n, m(n)}^{\prime}\right)\right\}$ the experiments $\mathscr{E}$ and $\mathscr{E}^{\prime}$ are equivalent. Since $\mathscr{E}_{n}\left[T_{n, m(n)}\right]$ is always better than $\mathscr{E}_{n}\left(T_{n}\right)$ this implies the second statement and concludes the proof of the theorem.

The foregoing results refer to the possibility of substituting the $\sigma$-fields $\mathscr{B}_{n}(t)$ for the $\sigma$-fields $\mathscr{A}_{n}(t)$ themselves. A related question is the possibility of realizing the limits of the experiments $\mathscr{E}_{n}\left(T_{n}\right)$ by stopping times of the process with independent increments described in Section 2.

This process was defined there by taking the space $\Omega$ of functions from $[0, \infty)$ to $H^{\prime}$ which are right continuous and have left limits. The process $S$, with values in $\Omega$ is a process with independent increments such that

$$
E \exp \{\xi S(t)\}=\exp \{t \beta(\xi)\}
$$

for all $t$ and all $\xi \in \Xi$. The space $\Omega_{1}$ is the product $\Omega_{1}=\Omega \times[0,1]$ and an element $\omega_{1}$ of $\Omega_{1}$ is a pair $\omega_{1}=(\omega, \gamma)$ with $\omega \in \Omega$ and $\gamma \in[0,1]$. The distribution $P_{0}$ is the product measure, product of the distribution of $S$ by the Lebesgue measure $\lambda$ on $[0,1]$. The distributions $P_{\xi}$ are defined through the natural densities $\exp \{\xi \omega(t)-t \beta(\xi)\}$. Let then $T_{n}$ be a stopping time adapted to the family $\mathscr{A}_{n}(t) ; t \in[0, \infty)$.

Assume that for each $\xi \in \Xi$ the sequences $\left\{\mathscr{L}\left[T_{n} \mid \xi\right]\right\}$ are relatively compact. Denote $\mathscr{L}\left(T_{n}\right)$ the function $\xi \mapsto \mathscr{L}\left(T_{n} \mid \xi\right)$ and consider sequences of pairs $\left[\mathscr{L}\left(T_{n}\right), \mathscr{E}_{n}\left(T_{n}\right)\right]$.

Let $\{v\}$ be a subnet of the sequence $\{n\}$. Denote $\left[\mathscr{L}\left(T_{v}\right), \mathscr{E}_{v}\left(T_{v}\right)\right]$ the corresponding net of pairs and say that these converge to a limit $[\mathscr{L}(T), \mathscr{E}]$ if $\mathscr{L}(T)$ is a function $\mathscr{L}(T)=\xi \mapsto \mathscr{L}(T \mid \xi)$ such that $\mathscr{L}\left(T_{\nu} \mid \xi\right) \rightarrow \mathscr{L}(T \mid \xi)$ for each $\xi$ and if $\mathscr{E}_{\nu}\left(T_{\nu}\right) \rightarrow \mathscr{E}$ weakly.

Under the compactness requirement imposed on the $T_{n}$ every net $\{v\}$ finer than $\{n\}$ always admit a finer subnet which converges. The limit is a cluster point of the sequence $\left[\mathscr{L}\left(T_{n}\right), \mathscr{E}_{n}\left(T_{n}\right)\right]$.

Consider such a cluster point $[\mathscr{L}(T), \mathscr{E}]$. Let us say that it is realizable on $\Omega_{1}$ if there is a stopping time $\tau$ defined on $\Omega_{1}$, adapted to the right continuous $\sigma$-fields $\mathscr{F}_{t+}$ and such that
(i) $\mathscr{L}[\tau \mid \xi]=\mathscr{L}(T \mid \xi)$ for each $\xi$;
(ii) the experiment $\mathscr{E}(\tau)$ induced by $\tau$ is such that $\delta[\mathscr{E}(\tau), \mathscr{E}]=0$.

Proposition 3 of this section says in particular that if Assumptions 1, 2, and 3 are satisfied and if $T_{n}$ is restricted to lie in a finite set $D$ independent of $n$, then every cluster point of the sequence $\left[\mathscr{L}\left(T_{n}\right), \mathscr{E}_{n}\left(T_{n}\right)\right]$ is realizable on $\Omega_{1}$. The following result is in this sense an improvement of Proposition 3.

Theorem 3. Let Assumptions 1, 2, and 3 be satisfied. Let $T_{n}$ be a stopping time of the family $\mathscr{A}_{n}(t) ; t \in[0, \infty)$. Assume that for each $\xi \in \Xi$ the sequences $\mathscr{L}\left[T_{n} \mid \xi\right]$ are relatively compact. Then every cluster point of the sequence $\left\{\mathscr{L}\left(T_{n}\right), \mathscr{E}_{n}\left(T_{n}\right)\right\}$ is realizable on $\Omega_{1}$.

Proof. Let $\overline{\mathscr{V}}_{0}$ be the space of functions from $[0, \infty)$ to the product $\{0,1\} \times H^{\prime}$ of the two point set $\{0,1\}$ by the Euclidean space $H^{\prime}$. Each $v \in \overline{\mathscr{V}}_{0}$ is a pair $(u, w)$ where $u$ is a function from $[0, \infty)$ to $\{0,1\}$ and $w$ is a function from $[0, \infty)$ to $H^{\prime}$. Let $\overline{\mathscr{V}}$ be the subspace of $\overline{\mathscr{V}_{0}}$ formed by elements $(u, w)$ such that $w \in \Omega$, that is, $w$ is right continuous with left limits. Let $U_{n}(t)$ be the indicator of the set $\left\{T_{n} \leq t\right\}$ and let $V_{n}$ be the process given by $V_{n}(t)=\left\{U_{n}(t), S_{n}(t) ; t \in[0, \infty)\right\}$.

Let $\{v\}$ be a subnet of $\{n\}$ such that $\left\{\mathscr{L}\left(T_{\nu}\right), \mathscr{E}_{\nu}\left(T_{\nu}\right)\right\}$ converges to a limit $(\mathscr{L}(T), \mathscr{E})$. One may assume, taking a further subnet if necessary, that along the net in question the finite dimensional distributions for $\xi=0$ of the processes $V_{v}$ all converge.

Then, according to Proposition 1, the distribution of the second coordinates $\{w(s) ; s \in[0, \infty)\}$ in the pairs $\{u(s), w(s) ; s \in[0, \infty)\}$ of $\overline{\mathscr{V}}$ is always the measure $P_{0}$ of the process with independent increments introduced in Section 2.

On $\overline{\mathscr{V}}_{\text {itself let }} \overline{\mathscr{G}}_{t}$ be the $\sigma$-field generated by the evaluation maps $(u, w) \mapsto w(s)$ for $s \leq t$. Let $\mathscr{G}_{t, m}$ be the $\sigma$-field obtained from the same evaluation maps but for $s \leq t$ and for $s$ restricted to lie in the dyadic set $D_{m}=\left\{k 2^{-m} ; k=0,1,2, \ldots\right\}$.

Let $T_{n, m}$ be equal to $k 2^{-m}$ if $(k-1) 2^{-m} \leq T_{n}<k 2^{-m}$. Note that if $t \in D_{m}$ the events $\left\{T_{n} \leq t\right\}$ and $\left\{T_{n, m} \leq t\right\}$ are the same. Thus $U_{n}(t)$ is also the indicator of $\left\{T_{n, m} \leq t\right\}$. For a fixed $m$ the times $T_{n, m}$ satisfy the conditions of Proposition 3. Thus one can apply the argument of the proof of this proposition to the $T_{n, m}$ and the $\sigma$-fields $\mathscr{G}_{t, m}$.

However, a glance at the proof shows that the conclusions remain entirely valid if, still using $T_{n, m}$, one replaces everywhere the $\sigma$-fields $\mathscr{G}_{t, m}$ by the larger $\sigma$-fields $\overline{\mathscr{G}}_{t}$. Then, $\mathscr{\mathscr { G }}$ denoting the smallest $\sigma$-field which contains all the $\overline{\mathscr{G}}_{t}$, the construction of Proposition 3 uses conditional expectations of the type $\phi_{t}= E_{0}[U(t) \mid \mathscr{G}]$. They may be taken $\overline{\mathscr{G}}_{t}$-measurable for each $t \in D_{m}$. The resulting stopping times, say $\tau_{m}$, defined on $\Omega_{1}$ are adapted to the family $\left\{\mathscr{F}_{t} ; t \in D_{m}\right\}$. However since the same $\sigma$-field $\mathscr{G}$ is used for all values of $m$ one concludes easily that $\tau_{m+1} \leq \tau_{m}$ almost surely.

Now, according to Proposition 1, for each fixed $m$ the distribution $\mathscr{L}\left[\tau_{m}, w\left(\tau_{m}\right) \mid \xi\right]$ is the same as the limit of the $\mathscr{L}\left[T_{n, m}, S_{n}\left(T_{n, m}\right) \mid \xi\right]$ along the net $\{v\}$. In particular, $\mathscr{L}\left[\tau_{m} \mid \xi\right]$ is the limit along this net of $\mathscr{L}\left[T_{n, m} \mid \xi\right]$.

Let $\tau=\lim _{m} \tau_{m}$. Then $\tau$ is a stopping time of the $\sigma$-fields $\mathscr{F}_{t+}$. Also, since the elements of $\Omega_{1}$ are right continuous, the distribution $\mathscr{L}[\tau, w(\tau) \mid \xi]$ is the limit of the $\mathscr{L}\left[\tau_{m}, w\left(\tau_{m}\right) \mid \xi\right]$. Hence $\mathscr{L}[\tau \mid \xi]$ is the limit of $\mathscr{L}\left[T_{n} \mid \xi\right]$ along the net $v$.

The assertion concerning the experiments $\mathscr{E}_{n}\left(T_{n}\right)$ follows again from the facts that (i) the $\mathscr{E}_{n}\left(T_{n, m}\right)$ are stronger than the $\mathscr{E}_{n}\left(T_{n}\right)$ and (ii) the experiment generated by $\tau$ is the limit of the experiments generated by the $\tau_{m}$.

The foregoing Theorem 3 uses only the weak Assumption 3. Under the stronger Assumption 4 or under Assumption 5 one can say somewhat more and obtain a partial converse as follows.

Theorem 4. Let Assumptions 1, 2, and 3 be satisfied. Assume in addition that either Assumption 4 holds or that Assumption 5 does. Let $\tau$ be a (real valued) stopping time defined on $\Omega_{1}$ and adapted to the family $\mathscr{F}_{t_{+}} ; t \in[0, \infty)$.

Then there are stopping times $T_{n}$ of the family $\mathscr{B}_{n}(t) ; t \in[0, \infty)$ such that for each $\xi \in \Xi$ the distributions $\mathscr{L}\left[T_{n}, S_{n}\left(T_{n}\right) \mid \xi\right]$ tend to $\mathscr{L}[\tau, w(\tau) \mid \xi]$.

Proof. For each integer $m$ let $\tau_{m}=k 2^{-m}$ if $(k-1) 2^{-m} \leq \tau<k 2^{-m}$. Let $\tau_{m}^{\prime}=\tau_{m} \wedge 2^{m}$. This $\tau_{m}^{\prime}$ takes its values in the finite set $D_{m}^{\prime}=\left\{k 2^{-m} ; k=0,1,2, \ldots, 2^{2 m}\right\}$.

Since $\tau_{m}$ is finite, an argument similar to that of Lemma 1 shows that on the space $\Omega_{1}$ and on the $\sigma$-field generated by $\tau_{m}$ the measures corresponding to various values of $\xi$ are all mutually absolutely continuous. Also, by the right continuity of the paths in $\Omega_{1}$ the limit of $\mathscr{L}\left[\tau_{m}^{\prime}, w\left(\tau_{m}^{\prime}\right) \mid \xi\right]$ is $\mathscr{L}[\tau, w(\tau) \mid \xi]$.

Now according to the proof of Theorem 1, for each $\varepsilon>0$ there is some modification $\tau_{m}^{\prime \prime}$ of $\tau_{m}^{\prime}$ such that $\left\|\mathscr{L}\left[\tau_{m}^{\prime \prime}, w\left(\tau_{m}^{\prime \prime}\right) \mid 0\right]-\mathscr{L}\left[\tau_{m}^{\prime}, w\left(\tau_{m}^{\prime}\right) \mid 0\right]\right\|$ is inferior to $\varepsilon 2^{-m}$ and such that the sets $\left\{\tau_{m}^{\prime \prime}<t\right\}$ have negligible boundaries. These $\tau_{m}^{\prime \prime}$ can be transferred to the $\sigma$-fields $\mathscr{B}_{n}(t)$, yielding stopping times $T_{n, m}$ such that $\mathscr{L}\left[T_{n, m}, S_{n}\left(T_{n, m}\right) \mid \xi\right] \rightarrow \mathscr{L}\left[\tau_{m}^{\prime \prime}, S_{n}\left(\tau_{m}^{\prime \prime}\right) \mid \xi\right]$ for each $\xi$.

Thus, applying a diagonal argument one can obtain stoppings times $T_{n}$ such that $\mathscr{L}\left[T_{n}, S_{n}\left(T_{n}\right) \mid 0\right]$ converges to $\mathscr{L}[\tau, w(\tau) \mid 0]$. When Assumption 4 is satisfied this gives the desired result by application of Lemma 2.

When Assumption 5 is satisfied, one can select a sequence $m(n), m(n) \rightarrow \infty$ such that, writing $T_{n}=T_{n, m(n)}$, the Prohorov distances between $\mathscr{L}\left[T_{n}, S_{n}\left(T_{n}\right) \mid \xi\right]$ and $\mathscr{L}\left[\tau_{m(n)}^{\prime \prime}, S_{n}\left(\tau_{m(n)}^{\prime \prime}\right) \mid \xi\right]$ tend to zero uniformly on the compacts $K \subset \Xi$. This concludes the proof of the theorem.

## 5. Remarks on Possible Applications

In this section we shall consider applications of the results of Section 4 to cases where the function $\xi \mapsto \beta(\xi)$ used previously is a quadratic. The corresponding experiments with a fixed sample size would then be asymptotically Gaussian in the sense of Chapter 10. For simplicity we shall also restrict our attention to the case where the set $\Xi$ is the entire Euclidean space $H$.

It is a common observation that under the conditions so described, and of course assuming the validity of assumptions such as $1,2,3$, or even 4 of Section 2 , sequential estimation procedures are not much better than their fixed sample size analogues. To be specific consider the problem of estimating $\xi$ with a loss function $W_{\xi}(z)=\|\xi-z\|^{2}$.

Assume that $\beta(\xi)$ is the square norm $\beta(\xi)=\frac{1}{2}\|\xi\|^{2}$. Let $\left\{T_{n}\right\}$ be stopping times such that the sequences $\left\{\mathscr{L}\left[T_{n} \mid \xi\right]\right\}$ are all relatively compact. The experiments they define have limits which are weaker than an experiment, say $\mathscr{F}$, realizable by measures $F_{\xi}$ such that

$$
F_{\xi}(d x, d \tau)=\exp \left\{\xi x-\frac{\tau}{2}\|\xi\|^{2}\right\} F_{0}(d x, d \tau)
$$

For such an experiment $\mathscr{F}$ and for given prior distributions, the posterior distributions are the same as if $\tau$ had been determined in advance.

Taking Gaussian priors, one concludes easily that, for the given estimation problem, the minimax risk is at least equal to

$$
\liminf _{\sigma \rightarrow 0} E\left[\left.\frac{p}{\tau+\sigma^{2}} \right\rvert\, \xi\right] \geq p\left\{\sup _{\xi} E[\tau \mid \xi]\right\}^{-1}
$$

where $p$ is the dimension of the space $H$.
Thus, nothing much is gained in the minimax sense by replacing a fixed sample size procedure by a sequential one which has the same maximum expected sample size. The formula given above even suggests that such a substitution may make the risks deteriorate. On the contrary, under similar assumptions it is well known that for testing problems sequential procedures may improve upon the fixed sample analogues.

For instance, assume $\beta(\xi)=\frac{1}{2}\|\xi\|^{2}$ for a Euclidean norm $\|\xi\|$ on $H$. Consider the limit process $S$ with independent increments of Section 2 for this case. It is a Wiener process with values in $H^{\prime}$.

Suppose that, for this limiting situation, it is desired to test the hypothesis $\xi=0$ against the hypothesis that $\xi$ is a certain fixed vector $u$ such that $\|u\|=1$.

In this case, according to Wald and Wolfowitz, the sequential probability ratio test which has fixed probabilities of errors, say $\alpha$ and $\beta$, minimizes the expected sample time under the hypotheses $\xi=0$ and $\xi=u$, both.

Taking $\alpha=\beta$ small, one can see that the expected sample size for the sequential probability ratio test is

$$
E[\tau \mid 0]=2 \log \left(\frac{1-\alpha}{\alpha}\right)
$$

By contrast, a fixed sample size procedure with the same error probabilities will have a sample size approximately equal to $4|\log \alpha \sqrt{2 \pi}|$. If, for instance, $\alpha$ is taken equal to .01 , the latter sample size is about 1.6 times the expected sample size of the sequential probability ratio test.

This is a very definite improvement. However, it is not spectacular enough in itself to be a strong recommendation for the use of sequential procedures in practice.

Indeed it is very rare, except under certain conditions in industrial production, that one can use truly "sequential" procedures. In most cases performance of the required measurements on the test item, or animal, or human being requires a prohibitive length of time. Thus one is led to use multiple stage procedures which involve only a few distinct decision stages. These will not usually lead to the spectacular ameliorations of the risks described just above. The reason for their use is entirely different and might be better described as follows.

First, in the test of $\xi=0$ against $\xi=u$ described above it may happen that the true $\xi$ is not $u$ but a certain $c u$ with $c$ substantially larger than unity. Then the sequential test will take this into account and terminate much more rapidly than a predetermined fixed sample size procedure aimed at the pair $(\xi=0$, $\xi=u\}$.

What is more, and this both for testing and estimation purposes, it may happen that the quadratic expression $\xi \mapsto \beta(\xi)$ is assumed to exist but that its actual form is not known. A sequential procedure will then still permit achieving either a desired expected square deviation loss or preassigned probabilities of error. Single stage procedures cannot generally afford this flexibility.

To describe in some detail a situation where sequential procedures may be used we shall consider systems which are, for fixed sample size, subject to the assumptions used in Chapter 11 for the obtention of asymptotically Gaussian distinguished estimates.

Consider a situation where for each integer $n$ one has a set $\Theta_{n}$ and a map $\xi_{n}$ from $\Theta_{n}$ into a Euclidean space $H$. Let $\mathscr{X}_{n}$ be a set with an increasing family of $\sigma$-fields $\left\{\mathscr{A}_{n}(t) ; t \in[0, \infty)\right\}$. For each $t$ and each $\theta \in \Theta_{n}$, let $Q_{n}(\theta, t)$ be a probability measure on $\mathscr{A}_{n}(t)$. Assume that these measures are coherent in the sense that $Q_{n}(\theta, s)$ is the restriction of $Q_{n}(\theta, t)$ to $\mathscr{A}_{n}(s)$ if $s<t$.

We shall assume as before that the families $\mathscr{A}_{n}(t)$ are right continuous. Also, for simplicity in stating the other conditions, we shall assume that $\Theta_{n}$ is a certain



fixed set $\Theta$ independent of $n$. Under these restrictions one may use the following assumption.

Assumption (A). For each fixed $t$ the sequences of experiments $\left\{Q_{n}(\theta, t) ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$, $n=1,2, \ldots$ with attached maps $\xi_{n}$ satisfy the pointwise asymptotic normality requirements in the condition G.P. of Chapter 11, Section 7.

This assumption (A) implies the existence of suitable preliminary estimates, say $\hat{\xi}(n, t)$, of $\xi_{n}(\theta)$, existence of tangent spaces, etc., as detailed in the assumptions of Chapter 11, Section 7.

Here, and for the sake of simplicity again, we shall restrict ourselves to the "full rank" case covered by the following condition.

Assumption (B). Let $\theta$ be fixed and let $U_{n}$ be a ball of radius $c$ centered at $\xi_{n}(\theta)$. Then the Hausdorff distance between $U_{n}$ and $U_{n} \cap \xi_{n}(\Theta)$ tends to zero as $n \rightarrow \infty$.

The foregoing assumptions (A) and (B) involve only what happens at each particular time point $t$. To apply the results of the preceding sections, we need to impose in addition a link between the successive values of $t$. For this purpose consider first a fixed $t$, a fixed $\theta$, and sequences $\theta_{n}^{i}, i=1,2,3$ such that the differences $\xi_{n}\left(\theta_{n}^{i}\right)-\xi_{n}(\theta)$ remains bounded as $n \rightarrow \infty$. Let $\Lambda_{n}\left(\theta_{n}^{i}, \theta ; t\right)$ be the logarithm of the density of $Q_{n}\left(\theta_{n}^{i}, t\right)$ with respect to $Q_{n}(\theta, t)$. The condition G.P. of Chapter 11, Section 7 already implies the existence of matrices, say $\Gamma_{n}(\theta, t)$, with the following property. Let

$$
L_{n}\left(\theta_{n}^{i}, \theta ; t\right)=\Lambda_{n}\left(\theta_{n}^{i}, \theta ; t\right)+\frac{1}{2}\left[\xi_{n}\left(\theta_{n}^{i}\right)-\xi_{n}(\theta)\right] \Gamma_{n}(\theta, t)\left[\xi_{n}\left(\theta_{n}^{i}\right)-\xi_{n}(\theta)\right]^{\prime}
$$

Then if $\xi_{n}\left(\theta_{n}^{3}\right)-\frac{1}{2}\left[\xi_{n}\left(\theta_{n}^{1}\right)+\xi_{n}\left(\theta_{n}^{2}\right)\right]$ tends to zero, so does

$$
L_{n}\left[\theta_{n}^{3}, \theta ; t\right]-\frac{1}{2}\left\{L_{n}\left[\theta_{n}^{1}, \theta ; t\right]+L_{n}\left[\theta_{n}^{2}, \theta ; t\right]\right\} .
$$

Here we shall assume that the $\Gamma_{n}(\theta, t)$ are linked in the following special manner.

Assumption ( $\mathbf{C}$ ). There are matrices $\Gamma_{n}(\theta)$ independent of $t$ such that the matrices $\Gamma_{n}(\theta, t)$ of condition G.P. can be taken equal to the product $t \Gamma_{n}(\theta)$.

Keeping $t$ fixed for the moment, it was shown in Chapter 11 that condition G.P. implies the existence of estimates, say $X_{n}(t)$, of the vector $\xi_{n}(\theta)$ such that, when $\theta$ is fixed and $\left|\xi_{n}\left(\theta_{n}\right)-\xi_{n}(\theta)\right|$ bounded, the logarithm of likelihood ratios $\Lambda_{n}\left[\theta_{n}, \theta ; t\right]$ are approximated by the quadratic

$$
\begin{aligned}
- & \frac{1}{2}\left\{\left[X_{n}(t)-\xi_{n}\left(\theta_{n}\right)\right] \Gamma_{n}(\theta, t)\left[X_{n}(t)-\xi_{n}\left(\theta_{n}\right)\right]^{\prime}\right. \\
& \left.-\left[X_{n}(t)-\xi_{n}(\theta)\right] \Gamma_{n}(\theta, t)\left[X_{n}(t)-\xi_{n}(\theta)\right]^{\prime}\right\}
\end{aligned}
$$

According to the same Chapter 11, one can also construct estimates, say $K(n, t)$, of the matrices $\Gamma_{n}(\theta, t)$ such that (i) the above approximation to $\Lambda_{n}\left[\theta_{n}, \theta ; t\right]$ remains valid if $K(n, t)$ is substituted for $\Gamma_{n}(\theta, t)$, and (ii) the $K(n, t)$ are measurable functions of $X_{n}(t)$. Here we shall assume that for each value of $t$ one has selected pairs ( $X_{n}(t), K(n, t)$ ) of estimates as described.

Using the approximation of $\Lambda_{n}\left(\theta_{n}, \theta ; t\right)$ provided by $X_{n}(t)$ and $K(n, t)$, one sees that the essentially random part of $\Lambda_{n}\left(\theta_{n}, \theta ; t\right)$ is asymptotically equivalent to the expression

$$
\left[\xi_{n}\left(\theta_{n}\right)-\xi_{n}(\theta)\right] K(n, t)\left[X_{n}(t)-\xi_{n}(\theta)\right]^{\prime}
$$

Thus, if $\eta_{n}$ represents the difference $\xi_{n}\left(\theta_{n}\right)-\xi_{n}(\theta)$ and $S_{n}(t)=K(n, t)\left[X_{n}(t)-\xi_{n}(\theta)\right]^{\prime}$ we are in a situation similar to that covered by Assumptions 1,2, and 3 of Section 2. One difference is that the set which plays the role of $\Xi$ is the image of $\Theta$ by the map $\theta^{\prime} \mapsto \xi_{n}\left(\theta^{\prime}\right)-\xi_{n}(\theta)$. This set will usually depend on $n$. However, as shown in Chapter 11 one can readily extend the range of definition of the experiments. Thus we may as well proceed as if the image of $\Theta$ was the entire Euclidean space $H$.

Another difference is that in Assumption 3 the function $\beta$ was kept independent of $n$. Here the function which takes the place of $\beta$ is the function $\eta \mapsto \frac{1}{2} \eta \Gamma_{n}(\theta) \eta^{\prime}$. It depends on $n$. However the G.P. assumptions imply that the norms $\left\|\Gamma_{n}(\theta)\right\|+\left\|\Gamma_{n}^{-1}(\theta)\right\|$ remain bounded. Thus, along appropriate subsequences the $\Gamma_{n}(\theta)$ will converge to limits and all the arguments of Section 4 will remain applicable to such subsequences.

In the present case, and for a fixed value of $\theta$, Assumption 4 of Section 2 is not automatically satisfied. However, Assumption 5 is verified as can be seen by inspection of the conditions in Chapter 11, Section 7.

For Assumption 4 itself the following remarks are in order. Let $m$ be an integer. If one is willing to observe the processes for a length of time $t \geq 2^{-m}$, then one can modify the preliminary estimates $\hat{\xi}(n, t)$ and the estimates $K(n, t)$ to avoid undue fluctuations. For this purpose let $t(m)$ be the dyadic number $t(m)=k 2^{-m}$ such that $k 2^{-m} \leq t<(k+1) 2^{-m}$. Replace $\hat{\xi}(n, t)$ by $\hat{\xi}[n, t(m)]$ and $K(n, t)$ by $t[t(m)]^{-1} K[n, t(m)]$.

Note also that the construction of the estimates $X_{n}(t)$ relies on matching a quadratic expression to logarithms of likelihood ratios around the estimated value $\hat{\xi}[n, t(m)]$. Thus, those points will satisfy the requirement expressed in Assumption 4, but other points need not do so.

In order to be able to satisfy Assumption 4 one could use a requirement such as the following. Let

$$
L_{n}\left(\theta_{n}^{i}, \theta ; t\right)=\Lambda_{n}\left(\theta_{n}^{i}, \theta ; t\right)+\frac{t}{2}\left[\xi_{n}\left(\theta_{n}^{i}\right)-\xi_{n}(\theta)\right] \Gamma_{n}(\theta)\left[\xi_{n}\left(\theta_{n}^{i}\right)-\xi_{n}(\theta)\right]^{\prime}
$$

Then if $\xi_{n}\left(\theta_{n}^{3}\right)-\frac{1}{2}\left[\xi_{n}\left(\theta_{n}^{1}\right)+\xi_{n}\left(\theta_{n}^{2}\right)\right]$ tends to zero and $\sup _{n}\left|\xi_{n}\left(\theta_{n}^{i}\right)-\xi_{n}(\theta)\right|< \infty$, the expression

$$
\sup _{t \leq b} L_{n}\left[\theta_{n}^{3}, \theta ; t\right]-\frac{1}{2}\left\{L_{n}\left(\theta_{n}^{1}, \theta ; t\right)+L_{n}\left(\theta_{n}^{2}, \theta ; t\right)\right\}
$$

tends to zero in probability for each $b \in(0, \infty)$. Under such a requirement appropriate constructions can lead to estimates $X_{n}(t)$ for which Assumption 4 will be satisfied.

In the present case it has not been assumed that the passage from a $\sigma$-field $\mathscr{A}_{n}(s)$ to the larger $\mathscr{A}_{n}(t)$ for $s<t$ is effected by making observations which are independent of $\mathscr{A}_{n}(s)$. However, even if this was the case, the process $S_{n}(t)= K(n, t)\left[X_{n}(t)-\xi_{n}(\theta)\right]^{\prime}$ would not necessarily be a process with independent increments. To put it differently, with the notation of Assumption 3, even if the processes $\log f_{n}(\xi, t)$ do have independent increments, this property is not automatically inherited by the processes $S_{n}(t)$.

Under the assumptions (A), (B), and (C), if $\theta$ is the true value of the parameter, the estimates $X_{n}(t)$ are approximately distributed like Gaussian vectors with expectations $\xi_{n}(\theta)$ and inverse covariance matrices $t \Gamma_{n}(\theta)$ estimated by $K(n, t)$.

The corresponding approximation to the distribution of the process $\left\{S_{n}(t)\right.$; $t \in[0, \infty)\}$ is that of a Gaussian process with independent increments and covariance $t \Gamma_{n}(\theta)$ at the point $t$. This covariance structure is not independent of $\theta$.

This being said, let us suppose that the statistician desires to estimate the value $\xi_{n}(\theta)$ with a certain precision, measured for instance by requiring that the determinant, $\operatorname{det} t \Gamma_{n}(\theta)$, of the "information matrix" $t \Gamma_{n}(\theta)$, be at least as large as some number $a>0$.

The procedure which suggests itself is to use a stopping time $T_{n}^{\prime}$ defined as the first $t$ for which $\operatorname{det} K(n, t) \geq a$. Then one uses $X_{n}\left(T_{n}^{\prime}\right)$ as an estimate of $\xi_{n}(\theta)$.

Let $t_{n}(\theta)$ be the smallest $t$ for which $\operatorname{det} t \Gamma_{n}(\theta) \geq a$. Under the assumptions (A), (B), and (C), the positive part $\left[T_{n}^{\prime}-t_{n}(\theta)\right]^{+}$tends to zero in probability. However the assumptions are too weak to imply that $\left|T_{n}^{\prime}-t_{n}(\theta)\right|$ tends to zero in probability. Even if it does, the difference $X_{n}\left(T_{n}^{\prime}\right)-\xi_{n}(\theta)$, which ought to be asymptotically distributed approximately as a Gaussian vector with mean zero and inverse covariance matrix $t_{n}(\theta) \Gamma_{n}(\theta)$, will not necessarily behave in that manner, unless Assumption 4 is also satisfied.

However, even under the weak assumptions used here one can approach this behavior as closely as one wishes without increasing the needed observation time very substantially.

To see this, select an $\varepsilon \in(0,1)$ and an integer $m$ such that $2^{-m}<\varepsilon^{2}$. Let $T_{n}$ be the first time $t$ of the form $k 2^{-m}, k$ integer, $k 2^{-m} \geq \varepsilon$ for which $\operatorname{det} K(n, t) \geq a$. Then, for all sequences such that $\inf _{n} t_{n}(\theta)>\varepsilon$, the probability that $\left|T_{n}-t_{n}(\theta)\right| \leq 22^{-m}$ tends to unity.

If $\inf _{n} t_{n}(\theta)>\varepsilon$ and if the times $t_{n}(\theta)$ stay at positive distance from the dyadic numbers of the form $k 2^{-m}$, one can say more. Let $t_{n}(\theta, m)$ be the smallest number of the form $k 2^{-m}$ which is at least equal to $t_{n}(\theta)$. Then, as $n \rightarrow \infty$, the distribution of $X_{n}\left(T_{n}\right)-\xi_{n}(\theta)$ is approximable by a Gaussian distribution which has expectation zero and inverse covariance matrix $t_{n}(\theta, m) \Gamma_{n}(\theta)$.

If $\inf _{n} t_{n}(\theta)>\varepsilon$, but $t_{n}(\theta)$ gets close to the dyadic number, the situation is more complex. To indicate what may happen suppose that $t_{n}(\theta)$ has a fixed value $t>\varepsilon$ of the form $t=k 2^{-m}$. Then the only values that $T_{n}$ will take with probabilities which do not tend to zero are the values $k 2^{-m}$ and $(k+1) 2^{-m}$. Let $\left(Y_{n, 1}, Y_{n, 2}\right)$ be the pair $\left(Y_{n, 1}, Y_{n, 2}\right)=\left\{X_{n}\left(k 2^{-m}\right)-\xi_{n}(\theta), X_{n}\left[(k+1) 2^{-m}\right]-\xi_{n}(\theta)\right\}$.

The joint distribution of the pair ( $Y_{n, 1}, Y_{n, 2}$ ) can be approximated by a certain Gaussian distribution, say $\mathscr{L}\left(Z_{n, 1}, Z_{n, 2}\right)$, which has expectation zero and a covariance structure describable as follows. The inverse covariance of $Z_{n, 1}$ is $t \Gamma_{n}(\theta)$. That of $Z_{n, 2}$ is $\left(t+2^{-m}\right) \Gamma_{n}(\theta)$. The conditional expectation of $Z_{n, 1}$ given $Z_{n, 2}$ is $Z_{n, 2}$ itself and the conditional covariance of $Z_{n, 1}-Z_{n, 2}$ given $Z_{n, 2}$ is $2^{-m}\left(t+2^{-m}\right)^{-1}\left[t \Gamma_{n}(\theta)\right]^{-1} \leq \varepsilon\left[t \Gamma_{n}(\theta)\right]^{-1}$. Since $\left[t \Gamma_{n}(\theta)\right]^{-1}$ is considered "reasonable", the covariance $\varepsilon\left[t \Gamma_{n}(\theta)\right]^{-1}$ may be considered "small". Thus $Z_{n, 1}-Z_{n, 2}$ is a small random vector and, within the approximation so afforded, the difference $Y_{n, 1}-Y_{n, 2}$ may be neglected.

In this example the sequential nature of the experimentation was used not to reduce the expected observation time but to stabilize the matrices $t \Gamma_{n}(\theta)$ which are unknown.

A similar stabilization can also be carried out for certain testing problems. We shall now discuss briefly a testing situation analogous to one discussed by Avinash Singh.

Keeping the assumptions (A), (B), and (C) let us suppose that one is interested in a particular subset $\Theta_{0}$ of $\Theta$. Assume also that the image $\xi_{n}\left(\Theta_{0}\right)$ is a certain fixed linear subspace $H_{0}$ of codimension unity in $H$.

Consider a vector $u$ which does not belong to $H_{0}$ and the half-space $\left\{w ; w=v+\lambda u, v \in H_{0}, \lambda>0\right\}$ bounded by $H_{0}$ and containing $u$.

Suppose further that one is given a small number $\alpha \in(0,1)$ and that one desires to obtain tests $\phi_{n}$ satisfying the following requirements.

Assume throughout that the true values $\theta_{n}$ of the parameter are such that there is a certain fixed $\theta_{0}$ for which the differences $\xi_{n}\left(\theta_{n}\right)-\xi_{n}\left(\theta_{0}\right)$ remain bounded. Then, if $\theta_{n} \in \Theta_{0}$ one would like to have

$$
\limsup _{n} E\left(\phi_{n} \mid \theta_{n}\right) \leq \alpha
$$

If, on the contrary, $\xi_{n}\left(\theta_{n}\right)$ has the form $\xi_{n}\left(\theta_{n}\right)=v_{n}+\lambda_{n} u, v_{n} \in H_{0}, \lambda_{n} \geq 1$, one would like to insure that

$$
\liminf E\left(\phi_{n} \mid \theta_{n}\right) \geq 1-\alpha
$$

To obtain an appropriate construction consider first the case where the experiments corresponding to the successive values of $t$ are exactly Gaussian shift experiments. Using the estimates $X_{n}(t)$ introduced above this would mean that the differences $X_{n}(t)-\xi_{n}(\theta)$ are exactly Gaussian with expectation zero and a covariance matrix $\left(t \Gamma_{n}\right)^{-1}$ independent of $\theta$.

If $\theta_{i, n}, i=1,2$ are two values of $\theta$, the corresponding likelihood ratios would have the form

$$
\log \frac{d G_{n}\left(\theta_{2, n}, t\right)}{d G_{n}\left(\theta_{1, n}, t\right)}=t \Delta_{n} \Gamma_{n}\left[X_{n}(t)-\xi_{n}\left(\theta_{1, n}\right)\right]^{\prime}-\frac{t}{2} \Delta_{n} \Gamma_{n} \Delta_{n}^{\prime}
$$

where $\Delta_{n}$ is the difference $\Delta_{n}=\xi_{n}\left(\theta_{2, n}\right)-\xi_{n}\left(\theta_{1, n}\right)$. In such a case, define on $H$ a norm by the relation $\|v\|^{2}=v \Gamma_{n} v^{\prime}$ and let $a_{n}$ be the component of the vector $u$ which is orthogonal to $H_{0}$ for this norm.

Consider pairs $\left(\theta_{1, n}, \theta_{2, n}\right)$ with $\xi_{n}\left(\theta_{1, n}\right) \in H_{0}$ and $\xi_{n}\left(\theta_{2, n}\right) \in H_{0}+u$. The corresponding pairs $\left\{G_{n}\left[\theta_{1, n}, t\right], G_{n}\left[\theta_{2, n}, t\right]\right\}$ are least informative if $\xi_{n}\left(\theta_{2, n}\right)$ has the form $\xi_{n}\left(\theta_{2, n}\right)=\xi_{n}\left(\theta_{1, n}\right)+a_{n}$. For such a pair the logarithms of likelihood ratio would take the form

$$
\Lambda_{n}(t)=t a_{n} \Gamma_{n} Z_{n}(t)-\frac{t}{2} a_{n} \Gamma_{n} a_{n}^{\prime}
$$

where $Z_{n}(t)=\left[X_{n}(t)-\xi_{n}\left(\theta_{1, n}\right)\right]^{\prime}$ and where $\left\|a_{n}\right\|^{-1} a_{n} \Gamma_{n} Z_{n}(t)=Y_{n}(t)$ is the projection of the vector $Z_{n}(t)$ on the line which carries the vector $a_{n}$, orthogonally for the norm defined by $\Gamma_{n}$.

A sequential test $\phi_{n}$ yielding the performance $E\left[\phi_{n} \mid \theta_{1, n}\right]=\alpha$ and $E\left[\phi_{n} \mid \theta_{2, n}\right]= 1-\alpha$ and minimizing the expected observation time under these restrictions would be given by a sequential probability ratio test.

Specifically, according to Wald and Wolfowitz, let $A$ be the number $A= \log 1-\alpha / \alpha$, and let $\tau$ be the first time $t$ such that $\left|\left\|a_{n}\right\| t Y_{n}(t)-(t / 2)\left\|a_{n}\right\|^{2}\right| \geq A$. The optimal test $\phi_{n}$ consists of stopping at the time $\tau$ and taking the obvious terminal decision.

This would solve the problem as posed if the experiments were exactly Gaussian shift with known covariance structure. Under Assumptions (A), (B), and ( C) only, suppose that the true values $\theta_{n}$ are such that for some $\theta_{0}$ the quantities $\sup _{n}\left|\xi_{n}\left(\theta_{n}\right)-\xi_{n}\left(\theta_{0}\right)\right|$ remain bounded. Then, around $\xi_{n}\left(\theta_{0}\right)$ the conclusions of Section 4 become applicable, with approximating Gaussian experiments which have inverse covariance matrices of the type $t \Gamma_{n}=t \Gamma_{n}\left(\theta_{0}\right)$.

According to Section 4, if $\xi_{n}\left(\theta_{n}\right) \in H_{0}$ or $\xi_{n}\left(\theta_{n}\right) \in H_{0}+u$ are possibilities, then no sequential tests can have a limiting performance which is better than that of the Gaussian tests described above for $\Gamma_{n}=\Gamma_{n}\left(\theta_{0}\right)$. Also, if $\theta_{0}$ was known, Theorem 4 would imply that the behavior of the Gaussian tests could be mimicked on the original experiments within differences which tend to zero as $n$ tends to infinity. Indeed, here Assumption 5 of Section 4 is satisfied.

Here $\theta_{0}$ or $\Gamma_{n}\left(\theta_{0}\right)$ remain unknown. However, just as was the case for the estimation problem considered previously, given an $\varepsilon>0$ one can at least achieve the desired performance within an $\varepsilon$.

For the purpose, select some point $v \in H_{0}$, say $v=0$. Let $K(n, t)$ be the estimate of $t \Gamma_{n}(\theta)$ available on the $\sigma$-field $\mathscr{A}_{n}(t)$ and let $\Lambda_{n}^{*}(t)$ be the approximation to the logarithms of likelihood ratios

$$
\Lambda_{n}^{*}(t)=a_{n}(t) K(n, t) X_{n}^{\prime}(t)-\frac{1}{2} a_{n}(t) K(n, t) a_{n}^{\prime}(t)
$$

where $a_{n}(t)$ is the component of $u$ which is orthogonal to $H_{0}$ for the matrix $K(n, t)$.

It would be pleasant to take a stopping time $T_{n}^{\prime}$ which stops as soon as $\left|\Lambda_{n}^{*}(t)\right| \geq A$. However, since Assumption 4 of Section 2 need not hold, one needs to proceed more cautiously using only discrete stopping times. A possibility is as follows. If $\varepsilon \in[0,1]$ is given, take an integer $m$ such that $2^{-m}<\varepsilon^{2}$.

Let $T_{n}$ be the first time $t$ satisfying the conditions (i) $t \geq \varepsilon$, (ii) $t=k 2^{-m}$ for some $k$, and (iii) $\left|\Lambda_{n}^{*}(t)\right| \geq A$. Under the assumption that the true value $\theta_{n}$ is such that $\sup _{n}\left|\xi_{n}\left(\theta_{n}\right)-\xi_{n}\left(\theta_{0}\right)\right|<\infty$ for some suitable $\theta_{0}$, the performance of the above testing procedure may be described as follows.
