Let $\theta_{1, n}$ be such that $\xi_{n}\left(\theta_{1, n}\right)$ is the point of $H_{0}$ closest to $\xi_{n}\left(\theta_{n}\right)$ for the square norm $\|v\|^{2}=v \Gamma_{n}\left(\theta_{n}\right) v^{\prime}$. For the norm just defined let $a_{n}$ be the component of $u$ which is orthogonal to $H_{0}$ and let $b_{n}$ be the component of $\xi_{n}\left(\theta_{n}\right)$ which is orthogonal to $H_{0}$. Note that $\sup _{n}\left\{\left\|a_{n}\right\|+\left\|a_{n}\right\|^{-1}\right\}<\infty$.

On the contrary, $\left\|b_{n}\right\|$ may become arbitrarily large. If so, for each $t>0$ the expression $\Lambda_{n}^{*}(t)$ will tend in probability to $+\infty$. The time $T_{n}$ will tend to the first dyadic number $k 2^{-m} \geq \varepsilon$ and the probability of rejecting $H_{0}$ will tend to unity.

Suppose on the contrary that $\left\|b_{n}\right\|$ remains bounded. Write $X_{n}(t)=\xi_{n}\left(\theta_{n}\right)+ Y_{n}(t)$. Then for any fixed $t$ the expression $\Lambda_{n}^{*}(t)$ is approximated by $c_{n} t+V_{n}(t)$ where

$$
c_{n}=a_{n} \Gamma_{n}\left(\theta_{n}\right) b_{n}^{\prime}-\frac{1}{2} a_{n} \Gamma_{n}\left(\theta_{n}\right) a_{n}^{\prime}
$$

and

$$
V_{n}(t)=t a_{n} \Gamma_{n}\left(\theta_{n}\right) Y_{n}^{\prime}(t)
$$

The distribution of the process $t \mapsto V_{n}(t)$ is approximated by that of a Gaussian process $t \mapsto W_{n}(t)$ which has independent increments, expectation zero and variance $E W_{n}(t)=t\left\|a_{n}\right\|^{2}$. The limiting behavior of the times $T_{n}$ is the same as that of times $\tau_{n}$ defined as the first times of the form $t=k 2^{-m} \geq \varepsilon$ for which $\left|c_{n} t+W_{n}(t)\right| \geq A$.

Since $W_{n}$ has continuous paths, taking $\varepsilon$ sufficiently small one can insure that the behavior of $\tau_{n}$ is as close as one wishes to that of a stopping time $\tau_{n}^{\prime}$ which is the first $t \in[0, \infty)$ for which $\left|c_{n} t+W_{n}(t)\right| \geq A$. One can also insure that the probabilities of error for $\tau_{n}$ and $\tau_{n}^{\prime}$ are as close to each other as desired.

In those cases where Assumption 4 is satisfied and $K(n, t)$ converges uniformly for $t$ in compacts, there-is no need to pass through the dyadic system, and the limiting behavior will be exactly that associated with the times $\tau_{n}^{\prime}$.

## CHAPTER 14

## Approximation by Exponential Families

## 1. Introduction

In this chapter we give an extension of some of the results of Chapters 10 and 11, replacing the Gaussian shift families used there by other exponential families. The results obtained cover only a restricted domain. To go further, much additional work appears necessary.

Why exponential families occupy such an important place in the statistical literature is not entirely clear. They are often called Koopman-Darmois families from the names of two authors who recognized their special connection with sufficient statistics. This connection has been described most convincingly by E.B. Dynkin, L. Brown, J. Denny, and J. Pfanzagl among other authors. However, it arises only when one considers product experiments.

Exponential families have also been used in asymptotic arguments, especially when considering questions relating to "second" or "third order" efficiency. In this situation they occur when one approximates logarithms of likelihood ratios by limited Taylor expansions which are finite sums of the type $\sum \xi_{j}(\theta) T_{j}(x)$.

Otherwise, direct attempts to use exponential families as approximations do not seem to have been made. At the time of this writing, it appears too early to predict whether such approximations will prove practically very useful, except, of course, in the case of asymptotically mixed normal experiments (see Chapter 11).

One reason is simply that to use an approximation one needs to know certain of its properties. There does exist a large literature on exponential families. For the "complete" ones much information can be found in the book by E.L. Lehmann. Further information can be found in the book by O. BarndorffNielsen. The "incomplete" case is not nearly as well understood.

Many interesting but difficult problems arise. For them, we can only refer the reader to the work of R . Wijsman and to the beautiful book of Yu. V. Linnik.

Some other features were elaborated by Chentsov who introduces on spaces of measures certain affine connections and associates the corresponding geodesics with exponential families. The statistical implications of this geodesic character do not seem to have been fully explored.

In any event, the present chapter is not a treatise on the subject of exponential families. It is included here in the hope that it may turn out to be useful, and for the specific reasons given in more detail below.

Section 2 deals with a problem which is not necessarily related to exponential families. It just expands on an argument already used in Chapter 10, Section 2, Theorem 1, indicating that, under contiguity restrictions, approximability of logarithms of likelihood ratios by $\mathscr{B}$-measurable functions implies approximate sufficiency of $\mathscr{B}$.

Section 3 introduces the concept of rank of exponential families and elaborates some of the properties of families which have a given rank and are further restricted by bounds on the tails of the logarithms of likelihood ratios.

Section 4 revolves around the equivalence between two approximation properties, one involving logarithms of likelihood, the other involving approximability in the sense of the distance between experiments by the families of rank $k$ of Section 3.

Section 5 considers special exponential families of rank unity and shows that for these one can carry out a program similar to the "global" program carried out in Chapter 11. The interesting feature is that the computation of asymptotically sufficient statistics can be carried out without actual knowledge
of approximating exponential experiments. What is needed is just the assumption that the approximation exists. This is similar to the observation made in Chapter 11 according to which the procedure of construction of the sufficient statistics does not require special knowledge. There, the proof that they will work depends on the assumption that Gaussian shift approximation is possible. Extension of this technique to more general situations may be of interest, but it is not given here.

## 2. A Lemma on Approximate Sufficiency

Let $\left\{P_{t} ; t \in V\right\}$ be an experiment indexed by some set $V$. Assume that the $P_{t}$ are probability measures on a $\sigma$-field $\mathscr{A}$. Take a particular element of $V$ as origin and denote it by zero.

It often happens that the logarithms of likelihood ratios $\Lambda(t)=\log \left(d P_{t} / d P_{0}\right)$ admit approximations by some functions, say $L(t)$, which are measurable with respect to a sub- $\sigma$-field $\mathscr{B}$ of $\mathscr{A}$. Such cases were already encountered in Chapter 10. Further cases will be encountered in Section 4 below. The question then arises whether $\mathscr{B}$ is approximately sufficient in the sense of Chapter 5.

To show that this is indeed the case under suitable restrictions, we repeat here the argument used in Chapter 10, Section 2, Theorem 1. The basic inequality is as follows.

Let $P$ and $Q$ be two probability measures on a $\sigma$-field $\mathscr{A}$. Let $f$ be a nonnegative measurable function such that $0<\int f d P \leq \infty$. Define a number $a$ as follows. If $\int f d P \leq 1$, let $a=\infty$. If $\int f d P>1$ let $a$ be the supremum of the numbers $c \geq 1$ such that $\int(f \wedge c) d P \leq 1$. Since $f \wedge(c+\varepsilon)-(f \wedge c) \leq \varepsilon$, the integral $\int(f \wedge c) d P$ is a continuous increasing function of $c$. Thus $\int(f \wedge a) d P=1$. Let $M^{a}$ be the measure which has density $f \wedge a$ with respect to $P$. Similarly let $Q^{a}=Q \wedge(a P)$ with $Q^{\infty}$ equal to the part of $Q$ which is dominated by $P$, that is, $\sup _{c<\infty} Q \wedge(c P)$.

Finally, let $\phi$ be the Radon-Nikodym density of $Q^{\infty}$ with respect to $P$ and let $\alpha$ and $\beta$ be the numbers defined, respectively, by

$$
\begin{aligned}
\alpha & =\int\left[1-1 \wedge\left(\frac{f}{\phi}\right)\right] d Q^{\infty} \leq \inf _{\varepsilon>0}\{\varepsilon+Q[f<(1-\varepsilon) \phi]\} \\
\beta & =\int\left[1-1 \wedge\left(\frac{\phi}{f}\right)\right] d P \leq \inf _{\varepsilon>0}\{\varepsilon+P[\phi<(1-\varepsilon) f]\}
\end{aligned}
$$

Lemma 1. The notation being as just described, let $M=\left\|M^{a}\right\|^{-1} M^{a}$. Then one has

$$
\frac{1}{2}\|M-Q\| \leq 1-\left\|Q^{a}\right\|+\alpha
$$

and, for every $b>a$,

$$
1-\left\|Q^{a}\right\| \leq \frac{1}{2}\|M-Q\| \leq 1-\left\|Q^{b}\right\|+a \beta
$$

Proof. By construction $M$ is always a probability measure. Thus one can write $\|M-Q\|=2\|Q-Q \wedge M\|$.

If $\left\|M^{a}\right\|<1$ one has $M>M^{a}$ and therefore $Q-Q \wedge M \leq Q-Q \wedge M^{a}= \left[Q-Q^{a}\right]+\left[Q^{a}-Q^{a} \wedge M^{a}\right]$. This yields

$$
\|Q-Q \wedge M\| \leq 1-\left\|Q^{a}\right\|+\left\|Q^{a}-Q^{a} \wedge M^{a}\right\|
$$

with equality holding whenever $M=M^{a}$.
The Radon-Nikodym density of $Q^{a} \wedge M^{a}$ with respect to $Q^{a}$ is the ratio $g_{a}= (f \wedge \phi \wedge a) / \phi \wedge a$, which is at least equal to $1 \wedge(f / \phi)$. Therefore,

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left\|Q^{a}-Q^{a} \wedge M^{a}\right\| & =\int\left[1-g_{a}\right] d Q^{a} \\
& \leq \int\left[1-1 \wedge\left(\frac{f}{\phi}\right)\right] d Q^{a} \\
& \leq \varepsilon+Q^{a}\left[\frac{f}{\phi}<1-\varepsilon\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

This yields the first inequality.
To obtain the second inequality, note that $b>a$ implies that $a$ is finite and therefore that $M=M^{a}$. Thus $\|Q-Q \wedge M\|=1-\left\|Q^{a}\right\|+\left\|Q^{a}-Q^{a} \wedge M^{a}\right\| \geq 1-\left\|Q^{a}\right\|$. However, $\|Q-Q \wedge M\|=\|M-Q \wedge M\|$ and this last quantity may be written

$$
\begin{aligned}
\|M-Q \wedge M\| & =\int\left[1-\frac{f \wedge \phi \wedge a}{f \wedge a}\right] d M \\
& \leq \int\left[1-1 \wedge\left(\frac{\phi}{f}\right)\right] d M \\
& \leq a \int\left[1-1 \wedge\left(\frac{\phi}{f}\right)\right] d P
\end{aligned}
$$

since $M=M^{a} \leq a P$. This completes the proof of the lemma.
The conditions used here do not afford any control over the term $1-\left\|Q^{a}\right\|$. This is why we have given the second inequality.

To describe a possible consequence of Lemma 1, consider not one pair ( $P, Q$ ) but a sequence $\left\{\left(P_{n}, Q_{n}\right)\right\}$ of pairs of probability measures on $\sigma$-fields $\mathscr{A}_{n}$. The terms corresponding to $f$ and $\phi$ become then $f_{n}$ and $\phi_{n}$ and the number $a$ becomes $a_{n}$, giving a measure $M_{n}$.

Corollary 1. Assume that the sequences $\left\{P_{n}\right\}$ and $\left\{Q_{n}\right\}$ are contiguous and that $\log \left(\phi_{n} / f_{n}\right)$ tends in probability to zero under $Q_{n}$. Then $\left\|M_{n}-Q_{n}\right\| \rightarrow 0$.

Proof. The terms $\alpha_{n}$ and $\beta_{n}$ corresponding to $\alpha$ and $\beta$ above, both tend to zero as $n \rightarrow \infty$. Take an $\varepsilon>0$ and, using the contiguity assumption select a $b$ such that $1-\left\|Q_{n}^{b}\right\|<\varepsilon$ for all $n$. Then either $a_{n} \geq b$, in which case $1-\left\|Q_{n}^{a_{n}}\right\|<\varepsilon$, or $a_{n}<b$, in which case $\left\|M_{n}-Q_{n}\right\| \leq 2 \varepsilon+2 b \beta_{n}$. Since $\beta_{n} \rightarrow 0$, so does $b \beta_{n}$, and the result follows.

Remark. In the Gaussian situation covered by Chapter 10, Section 2, Theorem 1 , one could assert that the level points $a_{n}$ tended to infinity. This is not
necessarily the case here. However, fix $\varepsilon>0$ and take for $b_{n}$ the smallest value such that $1-\left\|Q_{n}^{b}\right\| \leq \varepsilon$. The above argument shows that under the conditions of the corollary one will have $a_{n} \geq b_{n}$ for all large enough $n$.

To state another corollary, consider an experiment $\mathscr{E}=\left\{P_{t}: t \in V\right\}$ with logarithms of likelihood ratios $\Lambda(t)$ and with approximate versions of $L(t)$ of the $\Lambda(t)$. Let $a(t)$ be the supremum of the numbers $c$ such that $\int c \wedge \exp \{L(t)\} d P_{0} \leq$ 1. Let $\eta$ be the number

$$
\eta=\sup _{t} \inf _{\varepsilon>0} \varepsilon+P_{t}\{|L(t)-\Lambda(t)|>\varepsilon\}
$$

Corollary 2. Assume that the functions $L(t)$ are all $\mathscr{B}$-measurable. Then the
insufficiency of $\mathscr{B}$ for the experiment $\mathscr{E}$ is not larger than

$$
2\left\{\eta+\sup _{t} 1-\left\|\left(c(t) P_{0}\right) \wedge P_{t}\right\|\right\}
$$

Proof. This is an immediate consequence of Lemma 1 since the function $c(t) \wedge \exp \{L(t)\}$ are also $\mathscr{B}$-measurable.

The reader will note that the foregoing corollary still contains the unevaluable expressions $1-\left\|\left(c(t) P_{0}\right) \wedge P_{t}\right\|$. However, these can often be bounded by the procedure used in Corollary 1 or variants of it.

Also note that in Lemma 1 the measure $Q$ is not replaced by a measure which has exactly $f$ for density with respect to $P$. Similarly, in Corollary 2, the measures used to approximate the $P_{t}$ do not have exactly $\exp \{L(t)\}$ for likelihood ratios.

It is occasionally convenient to be able to assert that the $P_{t}$ are close to measures which have exactly $\exp \{L(t)\}$ for likelihood ratio. This is rather difficult to achieve with probability measures, but one can in certain cases replace the $P_{t}$ by probability measures $Q_{t}$ which have logarithms of likelihood ratios equal to $L(t)+c(t)$, where the $c(t)$ are suitable normalization constants. A particular result akin to the one given in Chapter 10, Section 2, Proposition 2 can be described as follows.

Let $\mathscr{E}=\left\{P_{t} ; t \in V\right\}$ be an experiment indexed by the set $V$. Let $\Lambda(t, s)$ be the logarithm of likelihood ratio of $P_{t}$ with respect to $P_{s}$. In the present situation it is convenient to define $\Lambda(t, s)$ as a measurable function with values in $[-\infty,+\infty]$. The infinite values are taken to be actually achieved. For instance, $\Lambda(t, s)=+\infty$ on the set which carries the part of $P_{t}$ which is $P_{s}$ singular.

We shall also assume that one has selected a particular element of $V$ for reference. To avoid excess indices this element will be called $u$. The logarithm of likelihood ratio $\Lambda(t, u)$ will be abbreviated $\Lambda(t, u)=\Lambda(t)$. It will be assumed that for each $t$ one is given a measurable real valued approximation $L(t)$ to $\Lambda(t)$. By real valued is meant in particular that $L(t)$ is finite.

The procedure described below is a modification of the truncation procedure used in Chapter 10, Section 2, Proposition 2. The basic idea is as follows. One selects a particular finite subset of the set $V$, say $F$, such that the reference



point $u$ belongs to $F$. Then one eliminates entirely the parts of the measures situated on the complement of the set $W$ where

$$
|\Lambda(t, u)-[L(t)-L(u)]| \leq \log 2
$$

for all $t \in F$.
This yields inequalities for approximations to the measures $P_{t}, t \in F$. To get inequalities for other values $t \in V$, one relies on Hölder inequalities or other convexity arguments. In the above, the number $\log 2$ has no mysterious significance. It could be replaced by any other number $b \in(0, \infty)$. The $\log 2$ has been chosen to avoid cumbersome coefficients. Here $P_{u}$ is taken as reference measure and $L(t)$ is an approximation to $\Lambda(t, u)=\Lambda(t)$. Thus we shall assume that $L(u)$ is identically zero.

Construct measures $m, \mu_{t}, M_{t}$, and $Q_{t}$ as follows. Let $S(t)=\{|\Lambda(t)-L(t)| \leq \log 2\}$ for every $t \in V$. Let $W=\cap\{S(s) ; s \in F\}$ and let $w$ be the indicator of $W$. Similarly let $w_{t}$ be the indicator of the set $W \cap S(t)=W_{t}$. Let $m$ be the measure defined by $d m=w d P_{u}$. Write $d M_{t}=\exp \{L(t)\} d m$ and $d \mu_{t}= w_{t} \exp \{L(t)\} d m=w_{t} d M_{t}$. Finally, if $\left\|M_{t}\right\|<\infty$, let $Q_{t}$ be the probability measure $Q_{t}=\left\|M_{t}\right\|^{-1} M_{t}$.

To describe the relations between these measures we need numbers $\varepsilon_{1}$ and $\varepsilon_{2}$ defined as follows. For $\alpha \in[0,2]$ let $\eta_{t}(\alpha)$ be the number

$$
\eta_{t}(\alpha)=\left[e^{\alpha}-1\right] P_{t}\{|\Lambda(t)-L(t)|<\alpha\}+P_{t}\{\alpha \leq|\Lambda(t)-L(t)| \leq \log 2\}
$$

Let $\dot{\varepsilon}_{1}$ be the number

$$
\varepsilon_{1}=\sup _{t \in V} \inf _{\alpha} \eta_{t}(\alpha)
$$

Let $\varepsilon_{2}$ be the number

$$
\varepsilon_{2}=\sup _{s, t} P_{t}\left\{|\Lambda(t, s)-[L(t)-L(s)]|>\frac{1}{2} \log 2\right\}
$$

Here the supremum is taken for all pairs ( $s, t$ ) of elements of $V$. However, if the finite set $F$ has been selected, one could also restrict the pairs so that $t \in V$, but $s \in F \subset V$. With these notations, one can assert the following.

Lemma 2. For every $t \in V$ and $s \in F$ one has
(i) $\left\|P_{t}-\mu_{t}\right\| \leq \varepsilon_{1}+(2 r+1) \varepsilon_{2}$,
(ii) $M_{s}\left(W \backslash W_{t}\right) \leq 4 \varepsilon_{2}$, where $r+1$ is the number of elements of the finite set $F$.

Proof. Consider an arbitrary triplet $(s, t, u)$ of elements of $V$ and a real number $b$. Let $S(s, t ; b)$ be the set

$$
S(s, t ; b)=\{|\Lambda(t, s)-[L(t)-L(s)]| \leq b\}
$$

Consider the sets

$$
C=S(t, u ; 2 b) \cap S(s, u ; 2 b)
$$

and

$$
B=S(t, u ; b) \cap S(s, t ; b) .
$$

Since for each $t \in V$ the function $L(t)$ is assumed to be finite, on the sets $B$ or $C$ the sum $\Lambda(s, t)+\Lambda(t, u)$ is equivalent to $\Lambda(s, u)$. Taking the equivalence classes $\dot{B}$ and $\dot{C}$ with respect to $P_{s}+P_{t}+P_{u}$, one concludes that $\dot{B} \subset \dot{C}$.

Now take $b=\frac{1}{2} \log 2$ so that $S(t, u ; 2 b)$ is the set $S(t)$ used in the construction of $\mu_{t}$ and $M_{t}$. By definition of $\varepsilon_{2}$ one has $P_{t}\left\{[S(s, t ; b)]^{c}\right\} \leq \varepsilon_{2}$ and $P_{t}\left\{[S(t, u ; b)]^{c}\right\} \leq \varepsilon_{2}$. Therefore $P_{t}\left(C^{c}\right) \leq P_{t}\left(B^{c}\right) \leq 2 \varepsilon_{2}$. This can be applied to all triplets $(s, t, u)$ for $s \in F$ and $t \in V$. It yields $P_{t}\left(W^{c}\right) \leq(2 r+1) \varepsilon_{2}$ and also $P_{t}\left(W \backslash W_{t}\right) \leq 2 \varepsilon_{2}$.

Now, if one replaces $\Lambda(t)$ by $L(t)$ on the set $W_{t}$, the corresponding measures will differ by at most $\varepsilon_{1}$. Thus

$$
\left\|P_{t}-\mu_{t}\right\| \leq \varepsilon_{1}+(2 r+1) \varepsilon_{2}
$$

yielding the first inequality.
For the second, note that the above argument yields $P_{s}\left(W \backslash W_{t}\right) \leq 2 \varepsilon_{2}$ for $s \in F$ but that $M_{s} \leq 2 P_{s}$. This yields the second inequality and completes the proof of the lemma.

In the above construction the logarithm of likelihood ratio of $M_{t}$ with respect to $M_{s}$ is exactly equal to $L(t)-L(s)$ on the set $W$ retained as basic space. However, even though one can conclude that $\mu_{t}$ is close to $P_{t}$ for all $t \in V$, one cannot say the same for $M_{t}$. Obviously $M_{t}$ and $P_{t}$ will be close to each other only when the value $M_{t}\left(W \backslash W_{t}\right)$ is small. This is, of course, true for $t$ in the finite set $F$ but need not be true for $t \in V$. However, application of the above to approximations by exponential families suggests the following.

Lemma 3. Assume that there are numbers $b$ and $\xi_{s} ; s \in F$ such that $\xi_{s} \geq 0$, $\sum \xi_{s}=1$, and

$$
L(t) \leq b+\sum_{s} \xi_{s} L(s)
$$

on the set $W$. Then assuming $\varepsilon_{1}+(2 r+1) \varepsilon_{2}<1$ one has
(i) $M_{t}\left(W \backslash W_{t}\right) \leq 4 e^{b} \varepsilon_{2}$
(ii) $\left\|P_{t}-Q_{t}\right\| \leq 2 \varepsilon_{1}+2 \varepsilon_{2}\left[2 r+1+4 e^{b}\right]$.

Proof. Hölder's inequality implies that

$$
M_{t}\left(W \backslash W_{t}\right) \leq e^{b} \prod_{s \in F}\left[M_{s}\left(W \backslash W_{t}\right)\right]^{\xi_{s}}
$$

Thus the first inequality written above follows from the second inequality of Lemma 2.

To obtain the second statement, combine the inequalities of Lemma 2 with the one derived above. This gives

$$
\left\|P_{t}-M_{t}\right\| \leq \varepsilon_{1}+\left[(2 r+1)+4 e^{b}\right] \varepsilon_{2}
$$

Now $Q_{t}=\left\|M_{t}\right\|^{-1} M_{t}$ and for all values of $\left\|M_{t}\right\|>0$ this gives

$$
\left\|P_{t}-\right\| M_{t}\left\|^{-1} M_{t}\right\| \leq\left\|P_{t}-M_{t}\right\|+\left|\left\|M_{t}\right\|-1\right|
$$

This completes the proof of the lemma.
The reader can readily verify that the above inequalities can be used to derive limit theorems under a variety of conditions. However, just as was the case for Lemma 1, the results seem most easily usable under contiguity restrictions. Even though Lemmas 2 and 3 had been originally intended to avoid such restrictions, one cannot claim that their application has been successful in noncontiguous cases.

## 3. Homogeneous Experiments of Finite Rank

This section contains some technical lemmas about the structure of homogeneous experiments of "finite rank" in a sense described below. It also contains further technical material on compactness properties of certain families of experiments of bounded rank. The results will be used in Section 4 to describe certain features of approximations by exponential families.

Consider a set $V$ and an experiment $\mathscr{E}=\left\{P_{t} ; t \in V\right\}$ indexed by $V$. In Chapter 3 we represented such an experiment by a Choquet conical measure, say $m$, on the space $\mathscr{F}\{V ;[0, \infty)\}$. When the experiment is homogeneous the conical measure $m$ is $\sigma$-smooth on the space $\mathscr{F}\{V ;(0, \infty)\}$. The logarithmic transformation carried out coordinate wise induces an isomorphism between the multiplicative group structure of $\mathscr{F}\{V ;(0, \infty)\}$ and the additive group structure of the space $\mathscr{F}\{V ; \mathbb{R}\}$ of all real valued functions on $V$. Furthermore, if both spaces are given their product topologies, the isomorphism is bicontinuous.

The convenience of the vector space structure of $\mathscr{F}\{V ; \mathbb{R}\}$ and the fact that one often looks at direct products of experiments, have led statisticians to work on $\mathscr{F}\{V ; \mathbb{R}\}$ instead of $\mathscr{F}\{V ;(0, \infty)\}$. We shall do so here, introducing what will be called $\log$ versions of the experiment $\mathscr{E}$.

To obtain such a version, select a particular element, say $u$, of $V$ and let $Q_{u}$ be the joint distribution, on the product $\sigma$-field of $\mathscr{F}\{V ; \mathbb{R}\}$, and under $P_{u}$, of the logarithms of likelihood ratios

$$
\Lambda(t)=\log \left(d P_{t} / d P_{u}\right)
$$

Using $Q_{u}$ one can define an experiment $\mathscr{E}^{\prime}=\left\{Q_{t} ; t \in V\right\}$ as follows. If $z \in \mathscr{F}\{V, \mathbb{R}\}$, let $l_{t}(z)$ be its value at $t \in V$. Define $Q_{t}$ by $Q_{t}(d z)=\exp \left\{l_{t}(z)-l_{u}(z)\right\} Q_{u}(d z)$.

The experiment $\mathscr{E}^{\prime}$ is the $\log$ version of $\mathscr{E}$ based on $u$. Each choice of $u$ gives such a version. They are obviously all equivalent and equivalent to the original experiment $\mathscr{E}$.

Call flat of $\mathscr{F}\{V, \mathbb{R}\}$ any translate of a linear subspace of $\mathscr{F}\{V, \mathbb{R}\}$. We shall be interested below in situations where there exists a smallest closed flat which
carries all the measures $Q_{t} ; t \in V$. The rank of $\mathscr{E}$ will be the dimension of this flat, that is, the linear dimension of the linear space of which the flat is a translate.

When $V$ is finite or countable it is easy to give a precise meaning to the statement "the smallest closed flat which carries the $Q_{t}$." Indeed, in these cases, the conical measure $m$ of $\mathscr{E}$ admits a localization which is a finite Radon measure on $\mathscr{F}\{V ;(0, \infty)\}$, for the product topology of this space. Thus all the $Q_{t}$ admit extensions $\bar{Q}_{t}$ which are Radon measures on $\mathscr{F}\{V, \mathbb{R}\}$. The intersection, say $H$, of the flats $H^{\prime}$ such that $\bar{Q}_{u}\left(H^{\prime}\right)=1$ is also such that $\bar{Q}_{u}(H)=1$ and $\bar{Q}_{t}(H)=1$ for all $t$.

If $V$ is uncountable, the situation is more complicated. Choquet has given examples of conical measures which cannot be localized by Radon measures.

Also, D. Maharam and J.B.H. Kempermann have given examples of probability measures which are $\sigma$-smooth, but not $\tau$-smooth on $\mathscr{F}\{V ; \mathbb{R}\}$. Although we have not checked it, one may conceive that similar difficulties could occur for the measures $Q_{u}$ used above. In any event, we shall be interested in experiments of finite rank. For those, no real difficulty occurs as can be seen from the following results.

First note the following. Take two points, say $u$ and $v$ of $V$. The point $u$ gives a log version $\mathscr{E}^{\prime}=\left\{Q_{t} ; t \in V\right\}$. The point $v$ gives another version, say $\mathscr{E}^{\prime \prime}=\left\{Q_{t}^{\prime} ; t \in V\right\}$. The passage from $Q_{u}$ to $Q_{v}^{\prime}$ can be effected in two steps as follows. First one multiplies $Q_{u}$ by $\exp \left\{l_{v}(z)-l_{u}(z)\right\}$, obtaining $Q_{v}$. Then $Q_{v}^{\prime}$ is the image of $Q_{v}$ by the map $z \mapsto \gamma(z)$ which consists in adding to each coordinate $l_{t}(z)$ of $z$ the amount $l_{u}(z)-l_{v}(z)$.

The map $\gamma$ is a linear, bijective, bicontinuous, and bimeasurable transformation of $\mathscr{F}\{V ; \mathbb{R}\}$ onto itself. Thus, if $Q_{u}$ admits a Radon extension $\bar{Q}_{u}$, the measure $Q_{v}^{\prime}$ admits a Radon extension $\bar{Q}_{v}^{\prime}$. If $H$ is the smallest closed flat carrying $\bar{Q}_{u}$, its image by $\gamma$ is the smallest closed flat carrying $\bar{Q}_{v}^{\prime}$.

Thus, whenever the conical measure $m$ of the experiment $\mathscr{E}$ admits a finite Radon localization on $\mathscr{F}\{V ;(0, \infty)\}$, one can speak of the rank of $\mathscr{E}$ without reference to the particular logarithmic version used. It is clear from this that $\operatorname{rank} \mathscr{E}=\operatorname{rank} \mathscr{F}$ if $\mathscr{E}$ and $\mathscr{F}$ are equivalent. Note also the following.

Proposition 1. Let $\mathscr{E}$ be an homogeneous experiment indexed by V. Let $k$ be an integer. Assume that, for every finite subset $S$ of $V$, the rank of $\mathscr{E}_{S}$ does not exceed $k$. Then the log versions of $\mathscr{E}$ admit Radon extensions and the rank of $\mathscr{E}$ is equal to $\sup _{S} \operatorname{rank} \mathscr{E}_{S}, S$ finite.

Proof. We already know that for $S$ finite the rank of $\mathscr{E}_{S}$ is well defined. To prove the result one may assume that $k=\sup _{S} \operatorname{rank} \mathscr{E}_{S}$ for a supremum taken over finite subsets of $V$. Let $F$ be a particular finite subset of $V$ selected so that (i) $\operatorname{rank} \mathscr{E}_{F}=k$ and (ii) $F$ has the smallest possible cardinality subject to (i).

Now consider any two finite subsets, say $S$ and $T$, of $V$ with a point $u \in S$ and $S \subset T$. For $P_{u}$, the distribution of the log likelihood vectors $\{\Lambda(s) ; s \in S\}$ and $\{\Lambda(t) ; t \in T\}$ are probability measures $M_{S}$ and $M_{T}$ carried, respectively, by $\mathscr{F}\{S, \mathbb{R}\}$ and $\mathscr{F}\{T, \mathbb{R}\}$. The measure $M_{S}$ is the image of $M_{T}$ by the projection operation which replaces an element of $\mathscr{F}\{T, \mathbb{R}\}$ by its restriction to $S$.

By assumption $M_{T}$ is carried by a certain flat $H_{T}$ of dimension at most $k$.

The restriction of $H_{T}$ to $S$ gives a flat which carries $M_{S}$ and must therefore contain the minimal flat $H_{S}$ associated with $M_{S}$. If both $H_{S}$ and $H_{T}$ have exactly rank $k$ the restriction of $H_{T}$ to $S$ must then be exactly $H_{S}$ and the projection map $\pi_{S, T}$ of $\mathscr{F}\{T, \mathbb{R}\}$ into $\mathscr{F}\{S, \mathbb{R}\}$ is a bicontinuous bijection of $H_{T}$ onto $H_{S}$.

Now, start with $F$ and a point $u \in F$. Let $H$ be the subset of $\mathscr{F}\{V, \mathbb{R}\}$ formed by elements $z$ such that for every $S$ finite, $S \supset F$, the restriction of $z$ to $S$ belongs to $H_{S}$. This is a flat of $\mathscr{F}\{V, \mathbb{R}\}$ which has dimension exactly $k$ and is closed in $\mathscr{F}\{V, \mathbb{R}\}$. The inverses of the projection maps $\pi_{F, S}$ put together yield a continuous map of $H_{F}$ onto $H$ which transports the measure $M_{F}$ to $H$. This is a Radon measure, since it is the image of a Radon measure by a continuous map. The result follows.

Remark 1. Since we have taken a set $F$ of minimal cardinality, one can also argue easily that on $H$ the coordinate functions are $l_{t}$ and are continuous functions of the $l_{s} ; s \in F$.

Remark 2. The concept of rank introduced above is obviously related to some concept of dimension for sufficient statistics. However, it is certainly not a topological concept of dimension. Indeed J. Denny has shown that the unit cube in $\mathbb{R}^{k}$ can be mapped in a bicontinuous way onto $[0,1]$ by a map which is also one to one except on a subset of $\mathbb{R}^{k}$ which has Lebesgue measure zero.

To go further, we shall need two small geometrical lemmas as follows. Let $F$ be a $k$-dimensional flat in a linear space and let $x_{1}, x_{2}, \ldots, x_{k+1}$ be the vertices of a nondegenerate simplex $S$ in $F$. Assume $k \geq 1$. Let $a$ be the center of $S$, that is, $a=(1 /(k+1)) \sum x_{j}$. For a given $\alpha>0$ let $U_{0}=a+\alpha(S-a)$ and let $U_{j}=x_{j}+\alpha(S-a)$.

Lemma 4. There is an $\alpha>0$ depending on $k$ only such that for every system $\left\{y_{j} ; j=1, \ldots, k+1\right\}$ with $y_{j} \in U_{j}$ the simplex spanned by the $y_{j}$ contains $U_{0}$.

This is easily verified, for instance, by induction on $k$.
Lemma 5. Let $F$ be as above. Let $\mathscr{C}$ be the family of bounded subsets of $F$. There is some number $b(k)$ depending only on the dimension of $F$ such that for each $C \in \mathscr{C}$ one can find elements $x_{0}, x_{1}, \ldots, x_{k}$ of $C$ such that every $z \in C$ has the form $z=\sum_{j=0}^{k} c_{j} x_{j}$ with $\sum_{j} c_{j}=1$ and $\sum_{j}\left|c_{j}\right| \leq b(k)$.

Proof. The asserted property is clearly true for one dimension. We shall carry out a proof by induction, assuming that it is true for $1,2, \ldots, k-1$. Consider then a bounded set $B$ that is exactly $k$-dimensional. Take in $B$ a set $\left\{x_{0}, x_{1}, \ldots, x_{k}\right\}$ such that each $y \in B$ may be written

$$
y=x_{0}+\sum_{j=1}^{k} \beta_{j}(y)\left(x_{j}-x_{0}\right)
$$

with, say, $\sup _{y \in B} \sum\left|\beta_{j}(y)\right|$ almost as small as it can be made by suitable choice of the $x_{j}$. Let $F_{k-1}$ be the flat spanned by the $x_{0}, x_{1}, \ldots, x_{k-1}$. Then, since $B$ is truly $k$-dimensional, $x_{k}-x_{0}$ cannot be parallel to $F_{k-1}$. Let $\Pi$ be the projection of $F$ onto $F_{k-1}$ parallel to $x_{k}-x_{0}$. This gives a set $\Pi B$ that is exactly $(k-1)$ dimensional. By the recurrence assumption this $\Pi B$ contains vertices $v_{0}, v_{1}, \ldots, v_{k-1}$ such that each $z \in \Pi B$ may be written $z=\sum_{j=0}^{k-1} c_{j}(z) v_{j}$ with
$\sum_{j=0}^{k-1}\left|c_{j}(z)\right| \leq b(k-1)$ (and $\left.\sum_{j}(z)=1\right)$. Each $v_{j}$ is the projection $v_{j}=\Pi w_{j}$ of some $w_{j} \in B$. Let $F^{\prime}$ be the flat spanned by the $w_{j}, j=0,1, \ldots, k-1$. This flat $F^{\prime}$ cannot be parallel to $x_{k}-x_{0}$ since its projection on $F_{k-1}$ is $F_{k-1}$ itself. Now
consider the line $L$ through $x_{0}$ and $x_{k}$ and the projection $\Pi^{\prime}$ onto $L$ parallel to $F^{\prime}$. For $\varepsilon>0$, the image $\Pi^{\prime} B$ of $B$ on $L$ contains $\Pi^{\prime} w_{0}$ and an image $\Pi^{\prime} w_{k}$ such that for any $w \in B$ one has $\left|\Pi^{\prime} w-\Pi^{\prime} w_{0}\right| \leq(1+\varepsilon)\left|\Pi^{\prime} w_{k}-\Pi^{\prime} w_{0}\right|$. If $\Pi^{\prime \prime}$ is the projection on $F^{\prime}$ parallel to $\left(x_{k}-x_{0}\right)$, the set $\left\{w_{0}, \ldots, w_{k-1}\right\}$ has in $F^{\prime}$ and $\Pi^{\prime \prime} B$ the same behavior as the set $\left\{v_{0}, \ldots, v_{k-1}\right\}$ in $\Pi B$. Thus any point $y \in B$ can be written $y=w_{0}+\sum_{j=1}^{k-1} \gamma_{j}(y)\left(w_{j}-w_{0}\right)+\gamma_{k}(y)\left(w_{k}-w_{0}\right)$ with $\left|\gamma_{k}(y)\right| \leq 1+\varepsilon$, and $\sum_{j=1}^{k-1}\left|\gamma_{j}(y)\right|+\left|1-\sum_{j=1}^{k-1} \gamma_{j}(y)\right| \leq b(k-1)$. This yields $b(k) \leq(2+\varepsilon)+b(k-1)$ and concludes the proof of the lemma.

A consequence of the above is as follows.
Proposition 2. Let $N$ be a directed set and let $V$ be an arbitrary set. For each $v \in N$ let $\mathscr{E}_{v}$ be an experiment of finite rank indexed by $V$.

Assume that the experiment types $\mathscr{E}_{v}$ of the $\mathscr{E}_{v}$ converge weakly (in the sense of Chapter 3) to an experiment type $\mathscr{E}$. If $\mathscr{E}$ is homogeneous, then

$$
\operatorname{rank} \mathscr{E} \leq \lim _{v} \inf \operatorname{rank} \mathscr{E}_{v}
$$

Proof. By Proposition 1 it is enough to prove the result for $V$ finite. Take a particular point $u \in V$ and consider the logarithmic versions of $\mathscr{E}_{v}$ based on that point. Then the log likelihood based on $u$ have a certain distribution $Q^{v}$. Similarly $\mathscr{E}$ yields a distribution $Q$. Since $Q$ is carried by $\mathscr{F}\{V, \mathbb{R}\}$ the weak convergence of the $\mathscr{E}_{\nu}$ to $\mathscr{E}$ implies that $Q^{\nu}$ converges in the ordinary sense to $Q$. Indeed the transformation from the unit simplex $U^{V}$ which sends $U^{V}$ to the logarithmic space sends the Blackwell canonical measures of the $\mathscr{E}_{v}$ to $Q^{v}$ and this transformation is almost surely continuous for $Q$.

Let $F$ be a minimal flat of $\mathscr{F}\{V, \mathbb{R}\}$ which carries $Q$. It has some dimension $k$. Then there is in $F$ a simplex, say $x_{1}, \ldots, x_{k+1}$, such that for each $j$ and each open neighborhood $U_{j}$ of $x_{j}$ one has $Q\left(U_{j}\right)>0$. By Lemma 3 one can select the $U_{j}$ so small that any choice of points $y_{j} \in U_{j}$ gives a simplex containing a certain fixed nondegenerate simplex of $F$. Let $2 \varepsilon=\inf _{j} Q\left(U_{j}\right)$. Convergence of $Q^{v}$ to $Q$ implies that eventually $Q^{v}\left(U_{j}\right)>\varepsilon$ for all $j=1,2, \ldots, k+1$. Thus any flat carrying $Q^{v}$ must contain $F$ and the result follows.

Remark. Homogeneous experiments of rank unity may converge to nonhomogeneous limits. Also homogeneous experiments of rank unity may converge to the trivial noninformative experiment which has rank zero.

To proceed in the opposite direction, first note the following. Take a point $u \in V$ and a flat of dimension $k$, say $F$, in $\mathscr{F}\{V, \mathbb{R}\}$. Assume that $l_{u}(z)=0$ for all $z \in F$.

Take an arbitrary open relatively compact subset, say $G$, of $F$ and let $\lambda$ be a Lebesgue measure on $G$. This measure can be used to define other measures $\mu_{t}$ by the relation $\mu_{t}(d z)=\exp \left\{l_{t}(z)-l_{u}(z)\right\} \lambda(d z)$. Normalizing, one obtains probability measures $Q_{t}=\left\|\mu_{t}\right\|^{-1} \mu_{t}$. These are carried not necessarily by $F$
itself, but by a translate $F_{1}$ of $F$. The family $\left\{Q_{t} ; t \in V\right\}$ is the log version at $u$ of a certain experiment which has exactly rank $k$. Each of the evaluation maps $l_{t}, t \in V$ is of course bounded on the support of $Q_{u}$. However it is not necessarily true that for each $z \in F_{1}$ the function $t \mapsto l_{t}(z)$ be a bounded function of $t$.

In the remainder of this section, we shall consider experiments for which such a boundedness condition holds. They occur as follows.

Let $\mathscr{E}=\left\{P_{t} ; t \in V\right\}$. For two points $s$ and $t$ of $V$ let $\Lambda(t, s)$ be the logarithm of likelihood ratio $\Lambda(t, s)=\log d P_{t} / d P_{s}$, with appropriate infinite values allowed to cover singular parts. Let $\zeta$ be a non-negative decreasing function defined on $(0, \infty)$ and tending to zero at infinity.

Definition. Given $V$ and $\zeta$ the class $\mathscr{K}(\zeta)$ is the class of all experiments $\mathscr{E}$ indexed by $V$ and such that for every triplet ( $s, t, u$ ) of elements of $V$ one has

$$
P_{u}\{|\Lambda(t, s)|>b\} \leq \zeta(b)
$$

The class $\mathscr{K}(\zeta)$ has the following property which will be used below. Take any net $\left\{\mathscr{E}_{\nu}\right\}$ with $\mathscr{E}_{\nu} \in \mathscr{K}(\zeta)$. For each $\mathscr{E}_{\nu}$ take a pair ( $P_{s_{\nu}}, P_{t_{\nu}}$ ) where $s_{\nu}$ and $t_{\nu}$ are arbitrarily selected in $V$. Then the nets $\left\{P_{s_{v}}\right\},\left\{P_{t_{v}}\right\}$ are contiguous.

Conversely, let $\mathscr{K}$ be any class of experiments indexed by $V$. Assume that $\mathscr{K}$ possesses the property just described for nets $\mathscr{E}_{\nu}$ and arbitrary pairs. Then there is some function $\zeta$, as described, such that $\mathscr{K} \subset \mathscr{K}(\zeta)$.

Let $u$ be a particular element of $V$ and let $F$ be a flat of dimension $k$ in $\mathscr{F}\{V, \mathbb{R}\}$. Let $\mathscr{K}(\zeta, F)$ be the subset of $\mathscr{K}(\zeta)$ formed by experiments whose log version at $u$ is carried by $F$.

Lemma 6. Let $F$ be a given flat of dimension $k$. Assume that there is in $\mathscr{K}(\zeta, F)$ at least one experiment of rank exactly $k$. Then for each $z \in F$ one has

$$
\sup _{t}\left\{\left|l_{t}(z)\right| ; t \in V\right\}<\infty
$$

Proof. Take a particular $\mathscr{E}$ of rank $k$ in $\mathscr{K}(\zeta, F)$ and let $\left\{Q_{t} ; t \in V\right\}$ be its log version based on $u$. There is some set $\left\{a_{j} ; j=1,2, \ldots, k+1\right\}$ of elements of $F$ such that each $a_{j}$ is in the support of the measure $Q_{u}$ and such that $F$ is the flat spanned by the $a_{j}$. Let $a_{0}$ be the center $a_{0}=(1 /(k+1)) \sum_{j} a_{j}$. Form neighborhoods $U_{j} ; j=0,1, \ldots, k+1$ according to the prescription of Lemma 4. Let $2 \varepsilon=\inf _{j \geq 1} Q_{u}\left(U_{j}\right)$ and let $b$ denote a number such that $\zeta(b)<\varepsilon$. Then $Q_{t}\left(U_{j}\right)>\varepsilon$ for all $t$ and all $j=1,2, \ldots, k+1$. Therefore, for each $t \in V$ each $U_{j}$ must contain some point, say $z_{t, j}$ such that $\left|l_{t}\left(z_{t, j}\right)\right| \leq b$. By convexity this implies that $\left|l_{t}(z)\right| \leq b$ for all $z$ in the simplex $U_{0}$. This implies the desired result.

According to this lemma, as long as we are considering only experiments $\mathscr{E}$ which belong to the class $\mathscr{K}(\zeta)$ and have finite rank it is not necessary to work on the entire space $\mathscr{F}\{V, \mathbb{R}\}$ but only on the subspace $\mathscr{B}\{V, \mathbb{R}\}$ of bounded numerical functions defined on $V$.

Lemma 7. Let $F$ be a flat of dimension $k$. Assume that there is in $\mathscr{K}(\zeta, F)$ at least one experiment of rank exactly $k$. Then there is a subset $\left\{s_{0}, s_{1}, \ldots, s_{k+1}\right\}$ of $V$ such that on $F$ every $l_{t}$ has the form

$$
l_{t}(z)=\sum_{j=1}^{k+1} \beta(t, j)\left[l_{s_{j}}(z)-l_{s_{0}}(z)\right]
$$

for coefficients $\beta(t, j)$ such that $\sum_{j}|\beta(t, j)| \leq 2 b(k+1)$ where $b(k+1)$ is the bound of Lemma 5.

Alternatively, one can find a subset $\left\{t_{0}, t_{1}, \ldots, t_{k}\right\}$ and a bounded function $t \mapsto A(t)$ such that

$$
l_{t}(z)=\sum_{j=1}^{k} \beta(t, j)\left[l_{t_{j}}(z)-l_{t_{0}}(z)\right]-A(t)
$$

with coefficients $\beta(t, j)$ such that $\sum|\beta(t, j)| \leq 2 b(k)$.
Note. The second expression given above is more in accord than the first with the usual expressions used for exponential families. However, the first turns out to be more convenient for certain purposes, as will be seen below.

Proof. Take a nondegenerate simplex with vertices $a_{1}, \ldots, a_{k+1}$ in $F$ and consider the vector $x(t)=\left\{l_{t}\left(a_{i}\right) ; i=1,2, \ldots, k+1\right\}$. According to Lemma 6 the set $\{x(t) ; t \in V\}$ is a bounded subset of the ( $k+1$ )-dimensional space $\mathbb{R}^{k+1}$. Thus, by Lemma 5, there is a subset $\left\{s_{0}, s_{1}, \ldots, s_{k+1}\right\} \subset V$ such that every $x(t)$ has the form

$$
x(t)=\sum_{j=0}^{k+1} c(t, j) x\left(s_{j}\right)
$$

for coefficients $c(t, j)$ such that $\sum_{j} c(t, j)=1$ and $\sum_{j}|c(t, j)| \leq b(k+1)$. Taking $x\left(s_{0}\right)$ as origin, the above expression may be rewritten

$$
x(t)=\sum_{j=1}^{k+1} c(t, j)\left[x\left(s_{j}\right)-x\left(s_{0}\right)\right]+x\left(s_{0}\right)
$$

However $l_{u}(z) \equiv 0$ identically on $F$. Thus

$$
0=x(u)=\sum_{j=1}^{k+1} c(u, j)\left[x\left(s_{j}\right)-x\left(s_{0}\right)\right]+x\left(s_{0}\right) .
$$

Taking a difference yields

$$
x(t)=\sum_{j=1}^{k+1} \beta(t, j)\left[x\left(s_{j}\right)-x\left(s_{0}\right)\right]
$$

with $\beta(t, j)=c(t, j)-c(u, j)$.
Every $l_{t}$ is affine on $F$, so that if $\left\{\gamma_{i} ; i=1, \ldots, k+1\right\}$ is any set of numbers such that $\sum \gamma_{i}=1$, one can write

$$
l_{t}\left\{\sum \gamma_{i} a_{i}\right\}=\sum \gamma_{i} l_{t}\left(a_{i}\right)
$$

Thus, the relation written above for the $x(t)$ yields the relation

$$
l_{t}=\sum_{j=1}^{k+1} \beta(t, j)\left[l_{s_{j}}-l_{s_{0}}\right]
$$

on the entire space $F$.
The alternate form can be obtained by a similar procedure. One takes an arbitrary element, say $a$, of $F$ and consider on the linear space $F-a$ the family of linear functionals $\left\{l_{t} ; t \in V\right\}$. These form a bounded set to which one can apply Lemma 5. Taking differences as was done above, and using the fact that $l_{u}$ still vanishes identically on $F-a$, one obtains a representation

$$
l_{t}=\sum_{j=1}^{k} \beta(t, j)\left[l_{t_{j}}-l_{t_{0}}\right]
$$

on $F-a$. This yields the desired result on $F$ with a function $A$ given by

$$
A(t)=\sum_{j=1}^{k} \beta(t, j)\left[l_{t_{j}}(a)-l_{t_{0}}(a)\right]-l_{t}(a)
$$

A corollary of Lemma 7 is as follows.
Corollary 1. Let $\varepsilon>0$ be a given number. Then there is a number $b(\varepsilon)$ such that the ball $K_{\varepsilon}=\left\{x: \sup _{t}|x(t)| \leq b(\varepsilon)\right\}$ of $\mathscr{B}\{V, \mathbb{R}\}$ has the following property. Take any experiment $\mathscr{E}$ of the class $\mathscr{K}(\zeta)$. Assume that the rank of $\mathscr{E}$ does not exceed $k$. Let $\left\{Q_{t} ; t \in V\right\}$ be the $\log$ version of $\mathscr{E}$ at $u$. Then

$$
Q_{t}\left(K_{\varepsilon}^{c}\right)<\varepsilon \quad \text { for all } t \in V .
$$

Proof. Each $\mathscr{E} \in \mathscr{K}(\zeta)$ of rank $r \leq k$ defines a minimal flat $F$ to which Lemma 7 may be applied, yielding some set $\left\{s_{0}, s_{1}, \ldots, s_{\boldsymbol{r}+1}\right\}$. Since $\mathscr{E}$ belongs to $\mathscr{K}(\zeta)$, there is a number $c(\varepsilon)$ depending only on $\varepsilon$ and $\zeta$, such that

$$
Q_{t}\left\{\left|l_{s_{j}}-l_{s_{0}}\right|>c(\varepsilon)\right\}<\frac{\varepsilon}{k+2}
$$

for all $t \in V$ and all $j=0,1,2, \ldots, k+1$. This implies $Q_{t}\left\{\sup _{j}\left|l_{s_{j}}-l_{s_{0}}\right|>c(\varepsilon)\right\}< \varepsilon$. However, if $\left|l_{s_{j}}(z)-l_{s_{0}}(z)\right| \leq c(\varepsilon)$ then $\left|l_{t}(z)\right| \leq b(\varepsilon)=2 c(\varepsilon) b(k+1)$ for all $t \in V$. The result follows.

Corollary 2. Let $F$ be a flat of dimension $k$ or less, such that $\mathscr{K}(\zeta, F)$ contains at least one experiment. Then there is in $F$ some element $a(F)$ such that $\left|l_{t}[a(F)]\right| \leq b\left(\frac{1}{4}\right)$, for all $t \in V$.

This is immediate from the above.
The combination of Lemma 7 and the above corollaries can be used to induce a correspondence between the flats $F$ of dimension $k$ which carry the log version of an $\mathscr{E} \in \mathscr{K}(\zeta)$ of rank $k$ as follows.

Take such an $F$ and an element $a(F)$ of it selected to satisfy the restriction of Corollary 2. Construct a set $T(F)=\left\{t_{0}(F), \ldots, t_{k}(F)\right\}$ as was done in the
proof of the second part of Lemma 7. Let $G(F)$ be the linear space $G(F)= F-a(F)$ and let $\phi_{j, F}$ be the linear functional $\phi_{j, F}=l_{t_{j}(F)}-l_{t_{0}(F)}$.

For the norm $\|z\|_{F}=\sup _{j}\left|\phi_{j, F}(z)\right|$ the spaces $G(F)$ are all isomorphic to the space $H=\mathbb{R}^{k}$ with its maximum coordinate norm. Having ordered the set $T(F)$ one can identify the $j$ th coordinate of the image of $z$ in $\mathbb{R}^{k}$ to $\phi_{j, F}(z)$. (The isomorphism, say $\phi_{F}$, so obtained depends on the choice of $T(F)$ and of the choice of order for its elements, but this is immaterial in the sequel.)

On the space $\mathscr{B}\{V ; \mathbb{R}\}$ itself, let $\|z\|=\sup \left\{\left|l_{t}(z)\right| ; t \in V\right\}$ be the uniform norm of $z$. By construction, the norms so obtained satisfy the inequality

$$
\frac{1}{2}\|z\|_{F} \leq\|z\| \leq 2 b(k)\|z\|_{F}
$$

for every $z \in G(F)=F-a(F)$.
A consequence of this isomorphism situation can be expressed in terms of covering numbers as follows. For an experiment $\mathscr{E}=\left\{P_{t} ; t \in V\right\}$ and an $\varepsilon>0$, let $N(\mathscr{E}, \varepsilon)$ be the smallest number of sets of diameter $\leq \varepsilon$ which can cover $\mathscr{E}$. Here the metric used on $\mathscr{E}$ is the one induced by the $L_{1}$-norm.

Proposition 3. Let $k$ and $\varepsilon$ be given. Let $\mathscr{K}_{k}(\zeta)$ be the class of experiments $\mathscr{E} \in \mathscr{K}(\zeta)$ which have rank $k$. There is some number $M(k, \varepsilon)$ such that $N(\mathscr{E}, \varepsilon) \leq M(k, \varepsilon)$ for all $\mathscr{E} \in \mathscr{K}_{k}(\zeta)$.

Proof. Let $\varepsilon$ be a number $\varepsilon \in\left(0, \frac{1}{4}\right)$ and let $\varepsilon^{\prime}=\varepsilon / 8$. According to Corollary 1 of Lemma 7, there is a set $K_{\varepsilon^{\prime}}$ such that if $\mathscr{E}$ has $\log$ version $\left\{Q_{t} ; t \in V\right\}$ then $Q_{t}\left(K_{\varepsilon^{\prime}}^{c}\right)<\varepsilon^{\prime}$ for all $t \in V$. Let $\mu_{t}$ be the truncation of $Q_{t}$ to $K_{\varepsilon^{\prime}}$ so that $\mu_{t}(A)=Q_{t}\left(A \cap K_{\varepsilon^{\prime}}\right)$ and $\left\|Q_{t}-\mu_{t}\right\|<\varepsilon^{\prime}$.

This $\mu_{t}$ is carried by a flat $F$. By the shift $z \mapsto z-a(F)$, with $a(F)$ selected according to Corollary 2 above, the measure $\mu_{t}$ is sent onto a measure $\mu_{t}^{\prime}$ on $G(F)=F-a(F)$. Now use the isomorphism $\psi_{F}$. This sends $\mu_{t}^{\prime}$ onto a measure $v_{t}$ on the space $H=\mathbb{R}^{k}$.

By all these transformations the set $K_{\varepsilon^{\prime}}$ is sent onto a certain compact subset of $H$. This set is certainly contained in the ball $K$ of radius $2\left[b\left(\frac{1}{4}\right)+b\left(\varepsilon^{\prime}\right)\right]$ of $H$.

Now consider the log likelihood of $Q_{t}$ with respect to $Q_{u}$. On $\mathscr{B}\{V ; \mathbb{R}\}$ this is given by the linear functional $l_{t}$. On $G(F)$, the corresponding function is an affine function of the form $f_{t}^{\prime}(x)=l_{t}(x)+l_{t}[a(F)]$ with $\left|l_{t}[a(F)]\right| \leq b\left(\frac{1}{4}\right)$ by the Corollary 2 of Lemma 7. Of course, $\left|l_{t}(x)\right| \leq\|x\|$.

Now apply the isomorphism $\psi_{F}$ to send $G(F)$ onto $H$. The affine functions $f_{t}^{\prime}$ defined on $G(F)$ become affine functions, say $f_{t}$, defined on $H$ itself. They have the form

$$
f_{t}=g_{t}+a(t) \quad \text { where } g_{t} \text { is linear on } H
$$

and $a(t)$ is a suitable number. By construction $|a(t)| \leq b\left(\frac{1}{4}\right)$ and, on the space $H$ with its norm, the $g_{t}$ satisfy the relation

$$
\left|g_{t}(x)\right| \leq 2 b(k)\|x\| .
$$



Let $D$ be the space of all the affine functions which are defined on $H$ and satisfy the two inequalities just derived. Their restrictions to the compact $K$ form a compact set for uniform convergence on $K$. Thus, given $\varepsilon$ there is a finite subset, say $\left\{f_{j} ; j=1,2, \ldots, m\right\} \subset D$ such that, for each $f \in D$, one can find an $f_{j}$ with $\sup \left\{\left|f(x)-f_{j}(x)\right| ; x \in K\right\}<\varepsilon / 4$. The sets $D$ and $K$ do not depend on the experiment $\mathscr{E}$ selected in $\mathscr{K}_{k}(\zeta)$ and the $f_{j}$ can be selected using $D$ and $K$ only.

Now, returning to the experiment $\mathscr{E}$, the measures $v_{t}$ have densities of the form $f_{t} \in D$ with respect to the measure $v_{u}$. Replacing $f_{t}$ by the closest $f_{j}$ gives a measure $f_{j} d v_{u}$ which differs from $d v_{t}=f_{t} d v_{u}$ by at most $\varepsilon / 4$. Returning to the $Q_{t}$, the assertion of the proposition follows immediately.

The precompactness property just proved could suggest that the subclass of $\mathscr{K}(\zeta)$ formed by experiments of rank at most $k$ is compact for the distance $\Delta$ between experiments. However, this is not always the case. One can construct an example of a sequence of experiments $\mathscr{E}_{n}$ which converges weakly to a limit $\mathscr{E}$ but does not converge for the distance $\Delta$ as follows.

Let $p_{n}$ be the $n$th prime number. Let $S_{n}$ be the set $S_{n}=\left\{\left(j / p_{n}\right) ; j=1,2, \ldots\right.$, $\left.p_{n}-1\right\}$ and let $V=\bigcup S_{n}$. For $t \in V$ let $P_{t}$ be the Gaussian measure $\mathscr{N}(t, 1)$ on the line. This gives a certain experiment $\mathscr{E}$ which can be used to define a suitable function $\zeta$ for which $\mathscr{E} \in \mathscr{K}(\zeta)$. Construct $\mathscr{E}_{n}$ as follows.

Let $s_{n}=1 / p_{n}$ and $t_{n}=\left(p_{n}-1\right) / p_{n}$. If $t$ is not one of the points $s_{n}$ or $t_{n}$ let $P_{t, n}=P_{t}$. Otherwise, let $P_{s_{n}, n}=P_{t_{n}}$ and $P_{t_{n}, n}=P_{s_{n}}$.

If $S$ is any fixed finite subset of $V$, there is some $N$ such that $n \geq N$ implies $P_{s, n}=P_{s}$ for all $s \in S$. However, let $v_{n}=2 / p_{n}$. Then $\left\|P_{s_{n}}-P_{v_{n}}\right\| \rightarrow 0$ but $\left\|P_{s_{n}, n}-P_{v_{n}}\right\|$ does not.

On the contrary, one can show that the class $\mathscr{K}[\zeta, F]$ of all experiment types $\mathscr{E} \in \mathscr{K}(\zeta)$ which are carried by a fixed flat $F$ of dimension $k$ is compact for the distance $\Delta$. In fact, we shall give below a slightly more general result, about the class $\mathscr{K}^{*}(\zeta, F)$ of experiment types from $\mathscr{K}(\zeta)$ which are carried by translates of the flat $F$. The reason for the additional generality is as follows.

Take a particular flat $F$ which carries the $\log$ version of an experiment $\mathscr{E}= \left\{P_{t} ; t \in V\right\}$ of rank equal to the dimension of $F$. The $P_{t}$ themselves may be measures on some arbitrary space ( $\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{A}$ ). The second representation given in Lemma 7 says that one can write the densities in the form

$$
\log \left\{\left(\frac{d P_{t}}{d P_{u}}\right) x\right\}=\sum_{j} \beta(t, j) \phi_{j}(x)-A(t)
$$

where the $\phi_{j}$ are measurable functions on $(\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{A})$ and where $A(t)$ is a certain number depending on $t$. Specifying the functions $t \mapsto \beta(t, j), j=1,2, \ldots, k$ does not entirely specify what $F$ is, but it determines the class of flats which are translates of $F$. A look at what happens for the Gaussian cases, where for instance $V$ is a linear space and the $t \mapsto \beta(t, j)$ are linear, shows that it is convenient to have a result which applies to linearly indexed Gaussian experiments without specifying the covariance structure (which is imbedded in the
function $A$ ). Thus the following result is a generalization of results already used many times in the Gaussian case.

Proposition 4. Let $F$ be a flat of dimension $k$. Assume that there is in $\mathscr{K}(\zeta, F)$ at least one experiment of rank exactly $k$ and let $\mathscr{K}^{*}(\zeta, F)$ be the set of experiment types $\mathscr{E} \in \mathscr{K}(\zeta)$ whose log version at $u$ is carried by a translate of $F$. Then $\mathscr{K}^{*}(\zeta, F)$ is compact for the distance $\Delta$ between experiment types.

Note. The experiments in $\mathscr{K} *(\zeta, F)$ have ranks which can be any one of the integers from zero to $k$.

Proof. Let $\varepsilon$ be a number $\varepsilon \in\left(0, \frac{1}{4}\right)$. According to Corollary 1 of Lemma 7, there is a ball $K_{\varepsilon}$ of $\mathscr{B}\{V, \mathbb{R}\}$ such that $Q_{t}\left(K_{\varepsilon}^{c}\right)<\varepsilon$ for all $t$ and for all $\mathscr{E}= \left\{Q_{t} ; t \in V\right\}$ which are log versions of experiments of rank at most $k$ in $\mathscr{K}(\zeta)$. Taking such an $\mathscr{E}$, define $\mu_{t}$ by $\mu_{t}(A)=Q_{t}\left(A \cap K_{\varepsilon}\right)$ and let $Q_{t}^{\prime}=\left\|\mu_{t}\right\|^{-1} \mu_{t}$. The experiment $\mathscr{E}^{\prime}=\left\{Q_{t}^{\prime} ; t \in V\right\}$ has a log version which is carried by a translate of the flat which carried $\mathscr{E}$. Also $\Delta\left(\mathscr{E}, \mathscr{E}^{\prime}\right)<2 \varepsilon$ and the $\log$ version of $\mathscr{E}^{\prime}$ is now entirely carried by a certain ball $K_{\varepsilon}^{\prime}=\left\{x: x \in \mathscr{B}\{V ; \mathbb{R}\}, \sup _{t}\left|l_{t}(x)\right| \leq b(\varepsilon)+2\right\}$.

Thus to prove the result it will be sufficient to prove that for any given $b$ the class, say $\mathscr{C}(F, b)$ of experiment types $\mathscr{E}$ whose log version is carried by a translate of $F$ and by the ball $\left\{x: \sup _{t}\left|l_{t}(x)\right| \leq b\right\}$ is a compact class for the distance $\Delta$.

If $\mathscr{E}=\left\{P_{t} ; t \in V\right\}$ is such a log version, carried by a translate $F(\mathscr{E})$ of $F$, the coordinate functions $l_{t}$ are integrable for $P_{u}$. The function $a(\mathscr{E})$ defined by

$$
l_{t}[a(\mathscr{E})]=\int l_{t}(z) P_{u}(d z)
$$

is well determined. It belongs to $F(\mathscr{E})$. The translates $F(\mathscr{E})-a(\mathscr{E})$ are all identical to a certain linear subspace, say $H$, of $\mathscr{B}\{V ; \mathbb{R}\}$.

If a net $\left\{\mathscr{E}_{v}\right\}, \mathscr{E}_{v} \in \mathscr{C}(F, b)$ converges to a limit $\mathscr{E}$, the corresponding log versions $\mathscr{E}_{v}=\left\{P_{t, v} ; t \in V\right\}$ are such that the measures $P_{u, v}$ converge vaguely to the corresponding limit $P_{u}$ in the sense that for any finite set $S \subset V$ the joint distributions of the vector $\left\{l_{s} ; s \in S\right\}$ converge. In particular $l_{t}\left[a\left(\mathscr{E}_{v}\right)\right] \rightarrow l_{t}[a(\mathscr{E})]$ for each $t \in V$.

Considering such a net, let $M_{v}$ be the image on $H$ of the measure $P_{u, v}$ by the map $z \mapsto z-a\left(\mathscr{E}_{v}\right)$. Let $M$ be the corresponding measure for the limit experiment. Then, since $a\left(\mathscr{E}_{v}\right) \rightarrow a(\mathscr{E})$ pointwise on $V$, the $M_{v}$ also converge vaguely to $M$.

According to the second part of Lemma 7 , to the flat $F$ corresponds a finite set $\left\{t_{0}, t_{1}, \ldots, t_{k}\right\}$ and maps $t \mapsto \beta(t, j)$. These depend on $F$ only. On the space $H$, the measures which represent an experiment $\mathscr{E}$ are those which for $t \in V$ have densities

$$
\exp \left\{\sum_{j} \beta(t, j) \phi_{j}+l_{t}[a(\mathscr{E})]\right\}
$$

with respect to the measure $M$. The $\phi_{j}$ are the linear functions $\phi_{j}=l_{t_{j}}-l_{t_{0}}$.

In the present case all the measures $M_{v}$ and $M$ are carried by a certain fixed compact $K$ of $H$. The functions $\sum \beta(t, j) \phi_{j}, t \in V$ form a bounded equicontinuous set on $H$. Thus,

$$
\int \exp \left\{\sum \beta(t, j) \phi_{j}\right\} d M_{v} \rightarrow \int \exp \left\{\sum \beta(t, j) \phi_{j}\right\} d M
$$

uniformly for $t \in V$.
However the numbers $l_{t}\left[a\left(\mathscr{E}_{v}\right)\right]$ may also be obtained from the relation

$$
\exp \left\{-l_{t}\left[a\left(\mathscr{E}_{v}\right)\right]\right\}=\int \exp \left\{\sum \beta(t, j) \phi_{j}\right\} d M_{v}
$$

Thus one can conclude that for any pair ( $s_{v}, t_{v}$ ) of elements of $V$ the difference $\left\|P_{s_{v}, v}-P_{t_{v}, v}\right\|-\left\|P_{s_{v}}-P_{t_{v}}\right\|$ tend to zero.

It follow easily that $\mathscr{C}(F, b)$ is compact for the weak convergence of experiments. Applying Lindae's theorem and Proposition 3 one concludes that $\mathscr{C}(F, b)$ is also compact for the distance $\Delta$. This concludes the proof of the proposition.

Remark. We have assumed that $F$ carries an experiment of rank equal to the dimension of $F$. In the present case $F$ can be replaced by any one of its translates. Thus the assumption in question could be replaced by the assumption that the elements of $F$ are bounded on $V$.

## 4. Approximation by Experiments of Finite Rank

In this section we shall retain the general notation of Section 3 and consider the class $\mathscr{K}(\zeta)$ of experiments with logarithmic tails bounded by a given function $\zeta$.

The purpose of the section can be briefly described as follows: A net $\left\{\mathscr{E}_{v}\right\}$ of experiments indexed by $V$ is such that $\Delta\left(\mathscr{E}_{v}, \mathscr{E}\right) \rightarrow 0$ for some experiment $\mathscr{E} \in \mathscr{K}(\zeta, F)$ with dimension of $F$ equal to $k$ only if the log versions of the $\mathscr{E}_{v}$ concentrate around the flat $F$. In other words, the logarithms of likelihood ratios of the $\mathscr{E}_{v}$ tend to satisfy approximately the same linear relation as those of $\mathscr{E}$.

Conversely when the logarithms of likelihood ratios tend to satisfy the required relations, the experiments converge at least weakly and even for the distance $\Delta$ if conditions of the Lindae type are satisfied.

For a fixed set $V$ we have often used the weak topology of $\mathbb{E}(V)$. Since $\mathbb{E}(V)$ is compact, this topology is compatible with a uniquely determined uniform structure. This is clearly the structure obtained by taking for vicinities of the diagonal of $\mathbb{E}(V) \times \mathbb{E}(V)$ sets of the type $\Delta\left(\mathscr{E}_{S}, \mathscr{F}_{S}\right)<\varepsilon$, for finite sets $S \subset V$.

By abuse of language we shall often say that the difference between $\mathscr{E}_{v}$ and $\mathscr{F}_{\nu}$ tends to zero weakly if for each $S$ and $\varepsilon$ there is a $\nu(S, \varepsilon)$ such that $\nu \geq \nu(S, \varepsilon)$ implies $\Delta\left(\mathscr{E}_{S, v}, \mathscr{F}_{S, v}\right)<\varepsilon$. With these conventions one can state the following.

Proposition 5. Let $\mathscr{K}_{k}(\zeta)$ be the set of experiment types which belong to $\mathscr{K}(\zeta)$ and have rank at most $k$. Let $\left\{\mathscr{E}_{v}\right\}$ be a net of experiments $\mathscr{E}_{v}=$
$\left\{P_{t, v} ; t \in V\right\}$ such that $\mathscr{E}_{v} \in \mathscr{K}_{k}(\zeta)$. Let $\left\{\mathscr{F}_{v}\right\}$ be another net, with the same set of indices, such that $\mathscr{F}_{v}=\left\{Q_{t, v} ; t \in V\right\}$ is an arbitrary element of $\mathbb{E}(V)$.

The following statements are all equivalent:
(i) $\Delta\left(\mathscr{E}_{v}, \mathscr{F}_{v}\right) \rightarrow 0$.
(ii) For arbitrary sets $S_{v} \subset V$ of cardinality at most $k+2$, the distances $\Delta\left(\mathscr{E}_{S_{v}, v}, \mathscr{F}_{S_{v}, v}\right)$ tend to zero.
(iii) The difference between $\mathscr{E}_{v}$ and $\mathscr{F}_{v}$ tends to zero weakly and for arbitrary pairs $S_{v}=\left(s_{v}, t_{v}\right) \subset V$ the distances $\Delta\left(\mathscr{E}_{S_{v}, v}, \mathscr{F}_{S_{v}, v}\right)$ tend to zero.

Proof. It is clear that (i) implies both (ii) and (iii). To show that (ii) ⇒ (iii) ⇒ (i) one can assume without loss of generality that $\mathscr{E}_{v} \rightarrow \mathscr{E}$ weakly and that $\mathscr{F}_{v} \rightarrow \mathscr{F}$ weakly. In this case, since $\mathscr{K}_{k}(\zeta)$ is weakly compact, the experiment $\mathscr{E}$ belongs to $\mathscr{K}_{k}(\zeta)$. Taking some point $u \in V$ as origin, $\mathscr{E}$ has a $\log$ version $\left\{P_{t} ; t \in V\right\}$ carried by some flat $F$ of dimension at most $k$. One can assume that $F$ is the minimal flat carrying $\mathscr{E}$. Then, according to Lemma 7, there is some set $\left\{t_{0}, t_{1}, \ldots, t_{k}\right\}$ and functions such that the logarithms of likelihood ratios of $\mathscr{E}$ have the form

$$
\sum_{j} \beta(t, j)\left(l_{t_{j}}-l_{t_{0}}\right)-A(t)
$$

The coefficients $\beta(t, j)$ and $A(t)$ are entirely determined by the joint distributions of the linear functions $l_{t}, l_{t_{0}}, \ldots, l_{t_{k}}$. Thus $\mathscr{E}$ is entirely determined by its restrictions $\mathscr{E}_{S}$ where $S$ is of cardinality at most $k+2$. It follows by compactness for the weak topology that (ii) ⇒ (iii). To show that (iii) ⇒ (i) it is now sufficient to use Proposition 3, Section 3, and Lindae's theorem.

To state the next result we need additional notations and conventions as follows. Fix a point $u \in V$. For each experiment $\mathscr{E}$ of the class $\mathscr{K}_{k}(\zeta)$ let $F(\mathscr{E})$ be the minimal flat which carries the $\log$ version of $\mathscr{E}$ at $u$. Let $\Phi$ be the class of all such flats.

For each $F \in \Phi$ of dimension $r \leq k$, select an element $a(F) \in F$ and a finite set $T(F)=\left\{t_{0}, t_{1}, \ldots, t_{r}\right\}$ according to the prescriptions of Lemma 7 and its Corollary 1. Then, for this particular $F$ the logarithms of likelihood ratio will take on $F$ the form

$$
L_{t}(z)=\sum_{j} \beta(t, j) \phi_{j}(z)-A(t)
$$

where

$$
\phi_{j}(z)=l_{t_{j}}(z)-l_{t_{0}}(z)
$$

and where

$$
A(t)=\sum_{j} \beta(t, j) \phi_{j}[a(F)]-l_{t}[a(F)]
$$

The coefficients $\beta$ are entirely determined by the choice of the pair $\{a(F), T(F)\}$. For an experiment $\mathscr{E} \in \mathscr{K}(\zeta, F)$ the form of $L_{t}$ used above is relevant only on
$F$ itself. However $L_{t}(z)$ is obviously well defined on the entire space $\mathscr{F}\{V ; \mathbb{R}\}$. To accommodate experiments which may not be homogeneous, we shall extend the definition of $L_{t}$ to the entire space $\mathscr{F}\{V ;[-\infty,+\infty]\}$, letting $L_{t}(z)=-\infty$ for all $z$ which take infinite values.

The functions $L_{t}$ so defined depend only on the flat $F$ and the pair $\{a(F), T(F)\}$. They will occasionally be denoted $L_{t, F}$. However if $F=F\left(\mathscr{E}_{v}\right)$ is the minimal flat of an experiment $\mathscr{E}_{v} \in \mathscr{K}_{k}(\zeta)$ we shall abbreviate the notation to $L_{t, v}$ instead of $L_{t, F_{v}}$. Assuming that such a selection has been effected one can assert the following.

Theorem 1. Let the function $\zeta$ and the integer $k$ be fixed. Assume that functions $L_{t, F}$ have been selected as described above. For each homogeneous experiment $\mathscr{F}$ indexed by $V$ consider its $\log$ version $\mathscr{F}=\left\{Q_{t} ; t \in V\right\}$ at $u$. Then for each $\varepsilon>0$ there exists a $\delta>0$ with the following property. Suppose that $\Delta(\mathscr{E}, \mathscr{F})<\delta$ for some $\mathscr{E} \in \mathscr{K}_{k}(\zeta)$ with minimal flat $F$ and associated functions $L_{t, F}$. Then

$$
Q_{s}\left\{\left|l_{t}(z)-L_{t, F}(z)\right|>\varepsilon\right\}<\varepsilon
$$

for all pairs $(s, t)$ of elements of $V$.
Proof. If the conclusion of the theorem does not hold, there exists a particular $\varepsilon>0$, a sequence of pairs ( $\mathscr{E}_{\nu}, \mathscr{F}_{\nu}$ ) and a sequence of pairs ( $s_{\nu}, t_{\nu}$ ) such that $\Delta\left(\mathscr{E}_{v}, \mathscr{F}_{v}\right) \rightarrow 0$ but such that if $L_{t, v}$ is the function assigned to $\mathscr{E}_{v}$ then

$$
Q_{s_{v}, v}\left\{\left|l_{t_{v}}(z)-L_{t_{v}, v}(z)\right|>\varepsilon\right\} \geq \varepsilon
$$

This last inequality involves only the set $S_{v}=\left\{u, s_{v}, t_{v} ; t_{0}\left(F_{v}\right), \ldots, t_{r}\left(F_{v}\right)\right\}$ where for $j=0,1, \ldots, r$ the $t_{j}\left(F_{v}\right)$ are the elements of the set $T\left(F_{v}\right)$ selected for $F_{v}=F\left(\mathscr{E}_{v}\right)$.

Thus it is sufficient to consider the experiments $\mathscr{E}_{v}^{\prime}$ and $\mathscr{F}_{v}^{\prime}$ obtained from $\mathscr{E}_{v}$ and $\mathscr{F}_{v}$ by restricting the set of indices to elements $t$ of $S_{v}$. Letting $m$ be the cardinality of $S_{v}$ one can identify $S_{v}$ to the interval $\{1,2, \ldots, m\}$ of the integers and identify $\mathbb{R}^{S_{v}}$ to $\mathbb{R}^{m}$, with the order of indices selected in the listing of $S_{v}$ above. We shall use on $\mathbb{R}^{S_{v}}=\mathbb{R}^{m}$ its maximum coordinate norm.

The condition that $\Delta\left(\mathscr{E}_{v}^{\prime}, \mathscr{F}_{v}^{\prime}\right) \rightarrow 0$ implies that the Prohorov distance between the corresponding distributions of likelihood ratios tends to zero. However, because the $\mathscr{E}_{v}$ are in the class $\mathscr{K}(\zeta)$, one can also say that the Prohorov distance between the distributions of logarithms of likelihood ratios tends to zero. Let $F_{v}^{\prime}$ be the flat which carries $\mathscr{E}_{v}^{\prime}$ in $\mathbb{R}^{S_{v}}$. There is also a certain compact $K$ of $\mathbb{R}^{S_{v}}=\mathbb{R}^{m}$ such that if $\mathscr{E}_{v}^{\prime}=\left\{P_{t, v} ; t \in S_{v}\right\}$ then $P_{t, v}\left(K^{c}\right)<\varepsilon / 4$ for all $t$. Let $\alpha>0$ be a number and let $G_{v}$ be the set of points whose distance to $K \cap F_{\nu}^{\prime}$ is less than $\alpha$. Convergence in the Prohorov sense implies that for some $v(\alpha)$ and $v \geq v(\alpha)$ one has also $Q_{s_{v}, v}\left(G_{v}^{c}\right)<\varepsilon / 2$. However $L_{t, v}(z)=l_{t}(z)$ on $F_{v}^{\prime}$ and one can certainly select $\alpha$ so small that on $G_{v}$ one has $\left|l_{t}(z)-L_{t, v}(z)\right|<\varepsilon$ for all $t \in S_{v}$ (since the $l_{t}$ are a bounded set of linear functionals on $\mathbb{R}^{m}$ ). This contradicts the assumed inequality, thereby concluding the proof of the theorem.

Remark. In this theorem we have assumed that the experiments $\mathscr{F}_{v}$ are homogeneous. It is clear that this restriction can be removed, by the simple
procedure of defining logarithmic versions on $\mathscr{F}\{V ;[-\infty,+\infty]\}$ with adequate choices of infinite values for the logarithms of likelihood ratios.

Theorem 2. Let $N$ be a directed set. For each $v \in N$ let ( $\mathscr{E}_{v}, \mathscr{F}_{v}$ ) be a pair such that $\mathscr{E}_{v} \in \mathscr{K}_{k}(\zeta)$ and such that $\mathscr{F}_{v}$ be an experiment $\mathscr{F}_{v}=\left\{Q_{t, v} ; t \in V\right\}$ on a measurable space $\left(\mathscr{X}_{\nu}, \mathscr{A}_{\nu}\right)$.

Assume that $\Delta\left(\mathscr{E}_{v}, \mathscr{F}_{v}\right) \rightarrow 0$. Then there are experiments $\mathscr{F}_{v}^{\prime}=\left\{Q_{t, v}^{\prime} ; t \in V\right\}$ carried by the same space ( $\mathscr{X}_{v}, \mathscr{A}_{v}$ ) such that
(i) $\sup _{t}\left\{\left\|Q_{t, v}-Q_{t, v}^{\prime}\right\| ; t \in V\right\} \rightarrow 0$;
(ii) the logarithmic version of $\mathscr{F}_{v}^{\prime}$ is carried by a flat which is a translate of the flat $F\left(\mathscr{E}_{v}\right)$ which carries the $\log$ version of $\mathscr{E}_{v}$.

Proof. One may assume without loss of generality that $\mathscr{F}_{\nu}$ is homogeneous. Fixing a point $u$ for origin of $\log$ versions, each $\mathscr{E}_{v}$ is carried by a flat $F_{v}=F\left(\mathscr{E}_{v}\right)$. For $F_{v}$ select a point $a\left(F_{v}\right) \in F_{v}$ and a finite set $T\left(F_{v}\right)=\left\{t_{0, v}, \ldots, t_{r, v}\right\}$ exactly as explained for Theorem 1.

Passing to logarithmic versions let

$$
\alpha_{v}=\sup _{s, t} Q_{s, v}\left\{\left|l_{t}-L_{t, F_{v}}\right|>\log 2\right\}
$$

and let

$$
c_{\nu}=[4 b(k)]^{-1} \log \left|\log \alpha_{\nu}\right|
$$

where $b(k)$ is the coefficient of Lemma 5, Section 3.
Let $\Lambda_{t, v}$ be the logarithm of likelihood ratio $\Lambda_{t, v}=\log \left[d Q_{t, v} / d Q_{u, v}\right]$ and let $B_{v}$ be the subset of $\mathscr{X}_{v}$ where all the $\Lambda_{t, v}, t \in T\left(F_{v}\right)$ satisfy the inequality $\left|\Lambda_{t, v}\right| \leq c_{v}$. Let $M_{t, v}$ be the measure defined by

$$
d M_{t, v}=I_{B_{v}} \exp \left\{L_{t, F_{v}}\right\} d Q_{u, v}
$$

where $L_{t, \boldsymbol{F}_{v}}$ which was defined on $\mathscr{F}(V ; \mathbb{R})$ is now interpreted as a function on $\mathscr{X}_{v}$, substituting for $z$ in $L_{t, v}$ the process $s \mapsto \Lambda_{s, v}$. Let $Q_{t, v}^{\prime}= \left\|M_{t, v}\right\|^{-1} M_{t, v}$. We claim that the experiment $\mathscr{F}_{v}^{\prime}=\left\{Q_{t, v}^{\prime} ; t \in V\right\}$ satisfies the conditions of the theorem. It is clear that $\mathscr{F}_{\nu}^{\prime}$ has a $\log$ version carried by a translate of $F_{\nu}$. Thus to prove the result it will be enough to show that $\sup _{t}\left\|M_{t, v}-Q_{t, v}\right\| \rightarrow 0$.

Now, according to Section 2, replacing $\Lambda_{t, v}$ by $L_{t, F_{v}}$ on the set where $\left|\Lambda_{t, v}-L_{t, F_{v}}\right| \leq \log 2$ changes the measure $Q_{t, v}$ only by an amount which is smaller than $\eta_{t, v}$ with
$\eta_{t, v}=\inf _{\alpha}\left\{\left[e^{\alpha}-1\right] Q_{t, v}\left\{\left|\Lambda_{t, v}-L_{t, F_{v}}\right|<\alpha\right\}+Q_{t, v}\left\{\alpha \leq\left|\Lambda_{t, v}-L_{t, F_{v}}\right| \leq \log 2\right\}\right\}$
According to Theorem 1 the numbers $\sup _{t} \eta_{t, v}$ tend to zero.
Replacing $\Lambda_{t, v}$ by $L_{t, F_{v}}$ on the part of $B_{v}$ where $\left|\Lambda_{t, v}-L_{t, F_{v}}\right|>\log 2$ introduces a difference which is at most equal to $2 \alpha_{\nu} \exp \left\{b_{\nu}\right\}$ where $b_{\nu}$ is a bound for $L_{t, F_{v}}$ on $B_{v}$. Now $L_{t, F_{v}}$ has the form

$$
L_{t, F_{v}}(z)=\sum_{j} \beta(t, j)\left(l_{t_{j}, v}-l_{t_{0}, v}\right)-A_{v}(t)
$$

where $\sum_{j}|\beta(t, j)| \leq 2 b(k)$ and where $A_{v}(t)$ is bounded by some number $b$ independent of $v$ and $t$. Thus, on $B_{v}$ one has $\left|L_{t, F_{v}}\right| \leq b+\log \left|\log \alpha_{v}\right|$. It follows that $2 \alpha_{\nu} \exp \left\{b_{\nu}\right\}$ does tend to zero. To conclude, it remains to show that $\sup _{t} Q_{t, v}\left(B_{v}^{c}\right)$ tends to zero. However, computation of $Q_{t, v}\left(B_{v}^{c}\right)$ involves only the finite set $\left\{u, t, t_{0, v}, \ldots, t_{r, v}\right\}$, and the result follows from the fact that the corresponding probabilities for $\mathscr{E}_{\nu}$ tend to zero. This concludes the proof of the theorem.

According to the preceding theorems if an experiment $\mathscr{F}$ is close to an experiment $\mathscr{E}$ of the class $\mathscr{K}_{k}(\zeta)$, the logarithms of likelihood ratios of $\mathscr{F}$ are close to those of $\mathscr{E}$. The converse of this is not true without further assumptions of contiguity type. However, with appropriate contiguity it is true. A possible version is as follows.

Proposition 6. Let $\left\{\mathscr{F}_{v}\right\}, \mathscr{F}_{v}=\left\{Q_{t, v} ; t \in V\right\}$ be a net of experiments of the class $\mathscr{K}(\zeta)$. Let $F_{\nu}$ be a flat of dimension $r \leq k$ such that there is at least one $\mathscr{E} \in \mathscr{K}(\zeta)$ of rank $k$ whose log version is carried by $F_{v}$. Select points $t_{0, v}, \ldots, t_{r, v}$ as usual. Assume that

$$
\sup _{s, t} Q_{s, v}\left\{\left|l_{t}-L_{t, F_{v}}\right|>\varepsilon\right\}
$$

tends to zero for each $\varepsilon>0$. Then there is an experiment $\mathscr{E}_{\nu} \in \mathscr{K}_{k}(\zeta)$ carried by $F_{v}$ such that $\Delta\left(\mathscr{E}_{v}, \mathscr{F}_{v}\right) \rightarrow 0$.

Proof. This is an immediate consequence of Proposition 5.

## 5. Construction of Distinguished Sequences of Estimates

The results given in Sections 3 and 4 show that, locally, one can use general exponential families in much the same way as was done for Gaussian shift families in Chapter 10.

It also appears feasible to carry out a "global" program, similar to that of Chapter 11. For this, one would need suitable preliminary estimates and enough information on the form of the local exponential families. Since the necessary arguments are not presently available in detail, such a program will not be carried out here.

However, for the sake of illustrating the possibilities we shall treat a special case which shows that one can proceed to estimate the flats which carry the local families and then construct distinguished sequences of statistics almost exactly as in Chapter 11.

The special situation considered here is as follows. We shall assume given a sequence $\left\{\mathscr{E}_{n}\right\}, \mathscr{E}_{n}=\left\{P_{\theta, n} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ indexed by the same set $\Theta$, together with a sequence of numbers $\delta_{n}, \delta_{n}>0$ such that $\delta_{n} \rightarrow 0$. The experiments $\mathscr{E}_{n}$ will be
subject to the following assumptions.
(A1) The set $\Theta$ is an open subset of the real line.
(A2) There are auxiliary estimates $\theta_{n}^{*}$ such that for each $\theta \in \Theta$ and each $\varepsilon>0$ there exist numbers $n(\theta, \varepsilon)$ and $b(\theta, \varepsilon)$ for which

$$
P_{\theta, n}\left\{\left|\theta_{n}^{*}-\theta\right|>\delta_{n} b(\theta, \varepsilon)\right\}<\varepsilon
$$

for all $n \geq n(\theta, \varepsilon)$.
It will be assumed that the estimates $\theta_{n}^{*}$ are $\delta_{n}$-discrete in the sense that there is some integer $m$ such that no translate of the interval $\left[-\delta_{n}, \delta_{n}\right]$ contains more than $m$ possible values of $\theta_{n}^{*}$. This additional restriction can always be met by computing $\theta_{n}^{*}$ only up to $\log _{10}\left(m / \delta_{n}\right)$ decimals. Let $\mathscr{F}_{\theta, b, n}$ be the experiment formed by the measures $P_{\theta+\delta_{n} t, n}$ where $t$ is restricted so that $|t| \leq b$ and $\theta+\delta_{n} t \in \Theta$.
(A3) For each $\theta$ and $b$ the experiments $\mathscr{F}_{\theta, b, n}$ converge to a limit $\mathscr{F}_{\theta, b}$ which is an exponential family of rank unity.

We shall denote $\mathscr{F}_{\theta}$ the experiment obtained by piecing the index sets of the $\mathscr{F}_{\theta, b}$ together. Then $\mathscr{F}_{\theta}$ has the form $\mathscr{F}_{\theta}=\left\{F_{\theta, t} ; t \in \mathbb{R}\right\}$ for a certain family indexed by the entire line and $\mathscr{F}_{\theta, b}$ is $\left\{F_{\theta, t} ; t \in \mathbb{R},|t|<b\right\}$.

It has been shown in Chapter 8, Section 4 that (A1) and (A3), together with a measurability condition on the $P_{\theta, n}$, imply that the limit experiments $\mathscr{F}_{\theta}$ satisfy for almost all $\theta$ an invariance requirement which forces the families $\left\{F_{\theta, t} ; t \in \mathbb{R}\right\}$ to be of a special form.

Here we shall assume that the invariance requirement is satisfied for all $\theta \in \Theta$. Taking into account the results of Chapter 8, Section 5, Proposition 6, this may be stated as follows.

For $\alpha \in(0, \infty)$ and $\beta \in(-\infty,+\infty), \beta \neq 0$ let $L(\cdot, \cdot ; \alpha, \beta)$ be the function defined on $\mathbb{R} \times \mathbb{R}$ by

$$
L(x, t ; \alpha, \beta)=-\frac{\alpha^{2}}{\beta^{2}}\left\{e^{\beta(x-t)}-1-\beta(x-t)\right\}
$$

If $\beta=0$ let $L(\cdot, \cdot ; \alpha, 0)$ be the limit

$$
L(x, t ; \alpha, 0)=-\frac{\alpha^{2}}{2}(x-t)^{2}
$$

Let $G(t ; \alpha, \beta)$ be the probability measure whose density with respect to the Lebesgue measure on $\mathbb{R}$ is proportional to the function $x \mapsto \exp L(x, t ; \alpha, \beta)$.
(A4) For each $\theta \in \Theta$ there are numbers $\alpha(\theta)>0$ and $\beta(\theta)$ such that $\mathscr{F}_{\theta}$ is equivalent to the experiment $\{G[t ; \alpha(\theta), \beta(\theta)] ; t \in \mathbb{R}\}$.

Taking a logarithmic version of $\mathscr{F}_{\theta}$ with respect to the base point $t=0$, one could also say that the $\log$ version of $\mathscr{F}_{\theta}$ is carried by the flat $\{a+\gamma b ; \gamma \in \mathbb{R}\}$ where $a$ is the function $t \mapsto-\left(\alpha^{2} / \beta\right) t$ and $b$ is the function $t \mapsto-e^{-\beta t}$. Here both $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are functions of $\theta$ and the flat in question changes with $\theta$.

Returning to $\mathscr{E}_{n}=\left\{P_{\theta, n} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$, let $\Lambda_{n}(t, s)$ be the logarithm of likelihood ratio

$$
\Lambda_{n}(t, s)=\log \frac{d P_{t, n}}{d P_{s, n}}
$$

Select a number $v_{n}$. For each integer $j$ let $X_{n}(j)$ be the random variable

$$
X_{n}(j)=\Lambda_{n}\left[\theta_{n}^{*}+j v_{n} \mid \theta_{n}^{*}\right]
$$

Let $D_{n}$ be the difference $D_{n}=X_{n}(1)-X_{n}(-1)$ and let $S_{n}$ be the sum $S_{n}=X_{n}(1)+X_{n}(-1)$. Define a variable $\gamma_{n}$ by the relation

$$
\exp \left\{-\gamma_{n}\right\}=\frac{X_{n}(2)-2 X_{n}(1)+X_{n}(0)}{X_{n}(1)-2 X_{n}(0)+X_{n}(-1)}
$$

In this expression $X_{n}(0)$ is equal to zero by construction. We have written it out to exhibit the second difference structure of the formula.

With these variables one can construct an estimate $T_{n}$ by the relations

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{n} & =\theta_{n}^{*}-v_{n} W_{n} \\
W_{n} & =\frac{1}{\gamma_{n}} \log \left\{1+\frac{1}{\gamma_{n}}\left[\cosh \gamma_{n}-1\right] \frac{D_{n}}{S_{n}}+\frac{1}{\gamma_{n}}\left[\sinh \gamma_{n}-\gamma_{n}\right]\right\}
\end{aligned}
$$

Our claim is that under the conditions (A1)-(A4) and with appropriate choice of the numbers $v_{n}$, the sequence $\left\{T_{n}\right\}$ will be a distinguished sequence of estimates.

The reader may wonder about the very peculiar form of $W_{n}$ above. It is obtained through the following procedure. The variables $X_{n}(j)$ give particular values of logarithms of likelihood ratios. One fits to these values a curve which would be the correct logarithm of likelihood curve if the experiments $\mathscr{F}_{\theta, b, n}$ were exactly equal to the limits $\mathscr{F}_{\theta, b}$. Since $\theta$ is not known the coefficients $\alpha(\theta)$ and $\beta(\theta)$ which enter into $\mathscr{F}_{\theta}$ are estimated from the $X_{n}(j)$. This done, one takes for $T_{n}$ the point which maximizes the fitted curve. This is exactly the technique that was used in Chapter 11, except that here the fitted curve is not quadratic.

The procedure depends on the choice of the units $v_{n}$. If the sequence $\left\{\delta_{n}\right\}$ is known, one can take for $v_{n}$ something of the form $v_{n}=u \delta_{n}, u$ fixed, equal to one for instance. For applications where $\delta_{n}$ is not known, one could fix a value $v_{n}$ by computing norms $\left\|P_{\theta^{*}, n} \wedge P_{\theta^{*}+v_{n}, n}\right\|$ and selecting $v_{n}$ so that these norms be neither too large nor too small; for instance, approximately equal to one-half.

This last procedure is probably what one would have to do in practice, since usual problems come with a given experiment, without any $n$ tending to infinity and therefore without a sequence $\left\{\delta_{n}\right\}$. The following proposition summarizes the situation.

Proposition 7. Let conditions (A1)-(A4) be satisfied. Select $u_{n}$ as indicated above and construct the corresponding statistic $T_{n}$. Then:
