(i) for each $\theta \in \Theta$ the sequences of distributions $\mathscr{L}\left\{\delta_{n}^{-1}\left(T_{n}-\theta\right) \mid \theta\right\}$ are relatively compact.
(ii) The $T_{n}$ are asymptotically sufficient in the sense that there are families
$\mathscr{E}_{n}^{\prime}=\left\{Q_{\theta, n} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ such that $T_{n}$ is actually sufficient for $\mathscr{E}_{n}^{\prime}$ and such that for each $\theta$ and $b$ the differences

$$
\sup _{t}\left\{\left\|P_{t, n}-Q_{t, n}\right\| ;|t-\theta| \leq b \delta_{n}\right\}
$$

tend to zero as $n \rightarrow \infty$.
(iii) For each fixed $\theta$ and $b$, the $T_{n}$ are a distinguished sequence of statistics for the experiments $\mathscr{F}_{\theta, n, b}$.

Proof. Fix a value $\theta$ and the corresponding numbers $\alpha=\alpha(\theta)$ and $\beta=\beta(\theta)$. Let $\Lambda_{n}$ be the function defined by $t \mapsto \Lambda_{n}(t)=\Lambda_{n}\left[\theta+\delta_{n} t ; \theta\right]$ for the likelihood ratios of the measures $\left\{P_{s, n} ; s \in \Theta\right\}$.

The corresponding functions for the limit experiments $\mathscr{F}_{\theta}$ are the functions

$$
\begin{aligned}
L(t ; y) & =-\frac{\alpha^{2}}{\beta^{2}}[\exp \{\beta(y-t)\}-1-\beta(y-t)]+\frac{\alpha^{2}}{\beta^{2}}[\exp \{\beta y\}-1-\beta y] \\
& =-\frac{\alpha^{2}}{\beta^{2}}\left\{[\exp \{-\beta t\}-1] e^{\beta y}+\beta t\right\}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $y$ would be the observable variable in the experiment $\mathscr{F}_{\boldsymbol{\theta}}$.
The above expression for $L(t, y)$ makes it clear that the value of $t$ which maximizes the function $t \mapsto L(t, y)$ is the value $t=y$. One can also rewrite this expression by passing to the space of logarithms of likelihood ratios as in Sections 3 and 4. Taking any two arbitrary points, say $r$ and $s$, with $r \neq s$, the logarithms of likelihood ratios take the form

$$
L(t)=a f(t)\left(l_{s}-l_{r}\right)-A(t)
$$

where $a$ is a number which depends on the choice of the pair ( $r, s$ ) and where $f$ is the function defined by $f(t)=e^{-\beta t}-1$. The corresponding function $A$ is then given by

$$
A(t)=\frac{\alpha^{2}}{\beta}\{t-(s-r) a f(t)\}
$$

The limit experiment $\mathscr{F}_{\theta}$ is homogeneous. Thus, for any two bounded sequences $\left\{s_{n}\right\}$ and $\left\{t_{n}\right\}$ the sequences $\left\{P_{s_{n}, n}\right\}$ and $\left\{P_{t_{n}, n}\right\}$ are contiguous. Also all the results of Sections 4 and 5 are applicable to sets $V$ which are bounded subsets of the line.

In particular, it follows from Section 4, Theorem 1 that for any fixed triplet $(r, s, t)$ the differences

$$
\Lambda_{n}(t)-a f(t)\left[\Lambda_{n}(s)-\Lambda_{n}(r)\right]+A(t)
$$

tend to zero in probability.
In these differences one can also replace the triplet ( $r, s, t$ ) by a variable triplet, say $\left(r_{n}, s_{n}, t_{n}\right)$, and the difference will still tend to zero as long as the triplets remain bounded. What is more, one can substitute triplets $\left(r_{n}, s_{n}, t_{n}\right)$ which



are random, as long as
(i) they stay bounded in probability, and
(ii) for any bounded set $B$ there is an integer $m(B)$ such that the triplet ( $r_{n}, s_{n}, t_{n}$ ) has in $B$ at most $m(B)$ possible values.

Now consider some value $t$, a number $u$, and the points of the form $t+j u$ where $j$ is a positive or negative integer. The second differences of the function $f$ take the form

$$
f[t+(j+1) u]-2 f(t+j u)+f[t+(j-1) u]=2[\cosh \gamma-1] e^{-\beta(t+j u)}
$$

with $\gamma=\beta u$.
The second differences of $A(t)$ are similar, except for the factor $-\left(\alpha^{2} / \beta\right) .(s- r) a$. Consider then, pairs $\left(t_{n}, u_{n}\right)$ of random variables such that
(i) $\left(t_{n}, u_{n}\right)$ is bounded in probability,
(ii) $\left|u_{n}\right|$ stays bounded away from zero, and
(iii) in any bounded set $B$ the number of possible values of ( $t_{n}, u_{n}$ ) remains bounded.

Let $R_{n}$ be the ratio

$$
R_{n}^{\prime}=\frac{\Lambda_{n}\left(t_{n}+2 u_{n}\right)-2 \Lambda_{n}\left(t_{n}+u_{n}\right)+\Lambda_{n}\left(t_{n}\right)}{\Lambda_{n}\left(t_{n}+u_{n}\right)-2 \Lambda_{n}\left(t_{n}\right)+\Lambda_{n}\left(t_{n}-u_{n}\right)}
$$

Checking that the denominator does not tend to zero, one concludes from the above that $R_{n}^{\prime}-\exp \left\{-\beta u_{n}\right\}$ tends to zero in probability so that $\gamma_{n}=-\log R_{n}^{\prime}$ is an estimate of $\beta u_{n}$.

In this expression we have used the logarithms of likelihood ratios $\Lambda_{n}(t)$ taken with respect to the given point $\theta$. However, let $t_{n}$ be the random variable $t_{n}=\delta_{n}^{-1}\left(\theta_{n}^{*}-\theta\right)$ where $\theta_{n}^{*}$ is our preliminary estimate of $\theta$. Then the variables called $X_{n}(j)$ in the description of the construction of the estimate $T_{n}$ are asymptotically equivalent to the differences $X_{n}^{\prime}(j)=\Lambda_{n}\left(t_{n}+j u_{n}\right)-\Lambda_{n}\left(t_{n}\right)$, with $u_{n}=\delta_{n}^{-1} v_{n}$.

Thus, in the preceding argument one may replace the ratio $R_{n}^{\prime}$ by the ratio

$$
R_{n}=\frac{X_{n}(2)-2 X_{n}(1)+X_{n}(0)}{X_{n}(1)-2 X_{n}(0)+X_{n}(-1)}
$$

Consider also the ratio $\left(D_{n} / S_{n}\right)$ where $D_{n}=X_{n}(1)-X_{n}(-1)$ and $S_{n}= X_{n}(1)+X_{n}(-1)$. This is asymptotically equivalent to

$$
V_{n}=\frac{\Lambda_{n}\left(t_{n}+u_{n}\right)-\Lambda_{n}\left(t_{n}-u_{n}\right)}{\Lambda_{n}\left(t_{n}+u_{n}\right)-2 \Lambda_{n}\left(t_{n}\right)+\Lambda_{n}\left(t_{n}-u_{n}\right)}
$$

Take any two fixed points, say $r$ and $s$ with $r \neq s$, and define a variable $Y_{n}$ by the relation

$$
\Lambda_{n}(s)-\Lambda_{n}(r)=-\frac{\alpha^{2}}{\beta^{2}}\left\{\left(e^{-\beta s}-e^{-\beta r}\right) e^{\beta Y_{n}}-\beta(s-r)\right\}
$$

If $\theta$ is fixed, the coefficients $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are known, thus knowledge of $Y_{n}$ is then equivalent to the knowledge of $\Lambda_{n}(s)-\Lambda_{n}(r)$. A simple computation shows then that the ratio $V_{n}$ is asymptotically equivalent to

$$
V_{n}^{\prime}=\left[\cosh \beta u_{n}-1\right]^{-1}\left[\left(\beta u_{n}\right) \exp \left\{-\beta\left(Y_{n}-t_{n}\right)\right\}-\sinh \beta u_{n}\right]
$$

In this expression one can replace $\beta u_{n}$ by the estimate $\gamma_{n}$ obtained above and define a variable $Z_{n}$ by the relation

$$
\left[\cosh \gamma_{n}-1\right] V_{n}=\gamma_{n} \exp \left\{-\frac{\gamma_{n}}{u_{n}}\left(Z_{n}-t_{n}\right)\right\}-\sinh \gamma_{n}
$$

That is, $Z_{n}=t_{n}-u_{n} W_{n}$ with

$$
W_{n}=\frac{1}{\gamma_{n}} \log \left\{1+\frac{1}{\gamma_{n}}\left(\cosh \gamma_{n}-1\right) V_{n}+\frac{1}{\gamma_{n}}\left(\sinh \gamma_{n}-\gamma_{n}\right)\right\}
$$

Since both $\gamma_{n}-\beta u_{n}$ and $V_{n}-V_{n}^{\prime}$ tend to zero in probability, it follows that $Z_{n}-Y_{n}$ tends to zero in probability.

Replacing $Y_{n}$ by $Z_{n}$ in the expression of $\Lambda_{n}(s)-\Lambda_{n}(r)$ one sees that

$$
\Lambda_{n}(s)-\Lambda_{n}(r)+\frac{\alpha^{2}}{\beta^{2}}\left[\left(e^{-\beta s}-e^{-\beta r}\right) e^{\beta Z_{n}}-\beta(s-r)\right]
$$

tends to zero in probability. By construction, $t_{n}=\delta_{n}^{-1}\left(\theta_{n}^{*}-\theta\right)$ so that $Z_{n}=\delta_{n}^{-1}\left(T_{n}-\theta\right)$, where $T_{n}$ is the estimate described before the statement of the proposition.

By assumption the sequences $\left\{t_{n}\right\}$ and $\left\{u_{n}\right\}$ were bounded in probability. The assumption that $\left\{u_{n}\right\}$ stays away from zero implies that the denominators in $V_{n}$ also stay away from zero. Thus, by contiguity, the ratios $V_{n}$ are bounded in probability. The coefficients $\gamma_{n}$ may tend to zero. They will do so if $\beta=$ 0 . However under all circumstances $\left\{W_{n}\right\}$ remains bounded in probability. It follows that $\left\{Z_{n}\right\}$ itself is bounded in probability. This gives the first statement of the proposition.

To obtain the second statement, note that the computations carried above for $\Lambda_{n}(s)-\Lambda_{n}(r)$ already show that locally $Z_{n}$ or $T_{n}$ have the required asymptotic sufficiency properties. Since $\delta_{n}^{-1}\left(T_{n}-\theta\right)$ is bounded in probability, the global statement follows from the argument carried out in Chapter 11, Section
4. The remaining statement (iii) is clear and the proposition may be considered proved.

It should be clear from the method of proof used here that it is possible to carry out similar arguments for other exponential families and in $k$ dimensions instead of one.

Note, however, that here the rank of the local exponential approximations was the same as the dimension of the underlying space $\Theta$. One can readily construct examples where the rank of the local exponential families is larger than the dimension of $\Theta$. In such a case there is not much hope to find statistics which are estimates of $\theta$ and have properties of asymptotic sufficiency similar to the ones described in Proposition 7.

To illustrate the situation, we shall describe briefly a structure which has been used widely for the case of independent identically distributed observations.

Consider experiments $\mathscr{E}_{n}=\left\{P_{\theta, n} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ indexed by an open subset of the line. Let $\left\{\delta_{n}\right\}, \delta_{n}>0$, be a sequence which tends to zero. Fixing a particular $\theta$, let $\Lambda_{n}(t)$ be the expression

$$
\Lambda_{n}(t)=\log \frac{d P_{\theta+\delta_{n} t, n}}{d P_{\theta, n}}
$$

In the most commonly studied case where the $P_{\theta, n}$ are obtained from $N$ identically distributed observations, one takes $n=N$ and $\delta_{n}=(1 / \sqrt{n})$ and one assumes that the densities are differentiable as many times as necessary. Then $\Lambda_{n}$ has a Taylor expansion of the type

$$
\Lambda_{n}(t)=\sum_{j=1}^{m} t^{j} X_{n, j}+\varepsilon_{n}
$$

where $\varepsilon_{n}$ is considered "small" and where the $X_{n, j}$ are suitable random variables obtainable from the derivatives of $\Lambda_{n}$ evaluated at the point $t=0$.

It turns out also that if one takes $n=N$ and $\delta_{n}=(1 / \sqrt{n})$, the main random term is the first one involving $t X_{n, 1}$. The other ones are of "higher order," but one may want to look at them closely and keep all the terms $t^{j} X_{n, j}$ for $j \leq m$ with $m$ equal to, say 2 or 3 or 4 . To declare the remainder terms "negligible" is to pass to the limit in a particular way.

In the present context it amounts to the following: Let us assume that as $n$ changes the number of observations $N$ does, but so does the underlying distribution of the individual observations. In this case passages to the limit as $n$ tends to infinity do not necessarily imply any "rates of convergence" or statements to the effect that $X_{n, 4}$ is of "higher order" than $X_{n, 2}$. They may still imply that there are functions $A_{n}(t)$ such that, for suitable variables $X_{n, j}$, the differences

$$
\Lambda_{n}(t)-\sum_{j=1}^{m} t^{j} X_{n, j}+A_{n}(t)
$$

tend to zero in probability.
It is easy to find experiments which would satisfy conditions of the type described here. For instance, suppose that for the value $\theta$ of the parameter, one is given variables $Y_{n, j} ; j=1,2, \ldots, m$, which are jointly normally distributed with expectation zero and with a certain covariance matrix $\Gamma_{n}$. Assume that passing from $\theta$ to $\theta+\delta_{n} t$ changes the distributions shifting $Y_{n, j}$ by an amount $c_{n, j} t^{j}$.

Then $\Lambda_{n}(t)$ will have exactly the form $\sum_{j=1}^{m} t^{j} X_{n, j}-A_{n}(t)$, where the $X_{n, j}$ are the transforms $X_{n, j}=c_{n, j} \sum_{i} \Gamma_{n, j, i} Y_{n, i}$ for the matrix $\Gamma_{n}$, and $A_{n}(t)$ is the corresponding "quadratic"

$$
A_{n}(t)=\frac{1}{2} \sum_{i} \sum_{j} c_{n, j} c_{n, i} t^{i} t^{j} \Gamma_{n, j, i}
$$

Assume that we are indeed in a situation where the logarithms of likelihood $\Lambda_{n}(t)$ are approximable by $\sum_{j=1}^{m} t^{j} X_{n, j}-A_{n}(t)$, with the form of $A_{n}$ just described and with variables $X_{n, j}$ which are asymptotically Gaussian as indicated. Then certainly the vector $\left\{X_{n, j} ; j=1,2, \ldots, m\right\}$ is asymptotically sufficient and distinguished for the local experiments.

Assume, in addition, that we are given auxiliary estimates, say $\theta_{n}^{*}$, which satisfy the condition (A2) used previously in this section.

Under conditions which are not too severe one could proceed to construct estimates $T_{n}$ by the general procedure described here. Namely one would compute log likelihood ratios $\Lambda_{n}\left(\theta_{n}^{*}+\delta_{n} v_{j} ; \theta_{n}^{*}\right)$ for a suitable set of numbers $v_{j}$. One would fit to them the approximation indicated by the assumed approximate form of the $\Lambda_{n}(t)$ and take $T_{n}$ close to the point at which the approximation is maximized.

Even if this is workable, when $m>1$ the resulting $T_{n}$ will not generally be asymptotically sufficient. To be asymptotically sufficient, it should allow us to recover the values of the $X_{n, j}$. This is unlikely to be possible, unless they have been imbedded in a recoverable form in $T_{n}$. This could be achieved by an artificial insertion of the decimal expansions of the $X_{n, j}$ in that of $T_{n}$, starting with decimals of sufficiently high order.

However, short of tricks of this sort which would not often lead to distinguished sequences of statistics, there is little hope that one could recover the values of the $X_{n, j}$ from a single statistic $T_{n}$.

In such cases, to achieve asymptotic sufficiency one needs not only $T_{n}$ but also some other variables. The general qualities of $T_{n}$ will reflect this, even though for particular loss functions it may be a perfectly reasonable estimate.

## CHAPTER 15

## Sums of Independent Random Variables

## 1. Introduction

This chapter collects a few facts, of the central limit theorem type, about distributions of sums of independent random variables.

Some of these facts will be used in Chapters 16 and 17 to study the distributions of logarithms of likelihood ratios in product experiments. However, these logarithms behave in a special manner which makes their study simpler than the general case considered here. The general classical central limit theorem can be obtained by a combination of three rather simple propositions.

One of these asserts that if $X_{j} ; j=1,2, \ldots$ are independent and such that $\sum\left\{P\left[X_{j} \neq 0\right]\right\}^{2}$ is small, then the distribution of $\sum X_{j}$ can be approximated, in
$L_{1}$-norm by the distribution whose characteristic function is $\exp \left\{\sum\left(\phi_{j}-1\right)\right\}$, where $\phi_{j}$ is the characteristic function of $X_{j}$.

Another proposition is Paul Lévy's form of the normal approximation criterion: let $S$ be the sum $\sum X_{j}$ and let $l$ be its interquartile range. Then the distribution of $S$ is close to a Gaussian one for the Kolmogorov-Smirnov norm whenever there is a small $\varepsilon>0$ such that $\sum P\left[\left|X_{j}\right|>\varepsilon l\right]<\varepsilon$.

The third proposition involves splitting variables $X_{j}$ into a "small" part and a part that is rarely different from zero. To do this, suppose that $X_{j}$ is defined on a probability space ( $\mathscr{X}_{j} ; \mathscr{A}_{j} ; \mu_{j}$ ) and that the underlying probability space is the product of the $\left(\mathscr{X}_{j} ; \mathscr{A}_{j} ; \mu_{j}\right)$. Let $A_{j} \in \mathscr{A}_{j}$. Then $X_{j}$ may be written $X_{j}=\left(1-I_{A_{j}}\right) X_{j}+I_{A_{j}} X_{j}$. If $\xi_{j}$ is a Bernoulli variable such that $\operatorname{Pr}\left[\xi_{j}=1\right]=1-P\left[\xi_{j}=0\right]=\mu_{j}\left(A_{j}\right)$, then $X_{j}$ has the same distribution as $Y_{j}=\left(1-\xi_{j}\right) V_{j}^{\prime}+\xi_{j} W_{j}^{\prime}$, where the $\xi_{j}, V_{j}^{\prime}$, and $W_{j}^{\prime}$ are independent, such that $\mathscr{L}\left(V_{j}^{\prime}\right)=\mathscr{L}\left[X_{j} \mid A_{j}^{c}\right]$ and $\mathscr{L}\left(W_{j}^{\prime}\right)=\mathscr{L}\left[X_{j} \mid A_{j}\right]$. If $E V_{j}^{\prime}=a_{j}$ is defined this may also be written

$$
Y_{j}=a_{j}+\left(1-\xi_{j}\right) V_{j}+\xi_{j} W_{j}
$$

with $V_{j}=V_{j}^{\prime}-a_{j}$ and $W_{j}=W_{j}^{\prime}-a_{j}$.
Now consider other Bernoulli variables $\eta_{j}$ with $\mathscr{L}\left(\eta_{i}\right)=\mathscr{L}\left(\xi_{i}\right)$, independent of all the previous variables and write the sum $\sum Y_{j}$ in the form

$$
\sum Y_{j}=\sum a_{j}+\sum\left(1-\eta_{j}\right) V_{j}+\sum \xi_{j} W_{j}+\sum\left(\eta_{j}-\xi_{j}\right) V_{j} .
$$

Here $\sum\left(1-\eta_{j}\right) V_{j}$ and $\sum \xi_{j} W_{j}$ are independent.
The classical central limit theorem can be proved by selecting the $A_{j}$ so that (i) the $V_{j}$ are small, (ii) the probabilities $\operatorname{Pr}\left[\xi_{j}=1\right]$ are small. The proof consists of applying the exponentiation proposition to the $\xi_{j} W_{j}$ and the Gaussian approximation to the $\sum\left(1-\eta_{j}\right) V_{j}$. Then one argues that $\sum\left(\eta_{j}-\xi_{j}\right) V_{j}$ may be neglected.

Another look at the situation suggests that the central limit theorem is just an extension of the remark that if $|x|$ is small then $e^{x}-(1+x)$ is even of smaller order. Thus, after suitable centering, the theorem becomes an expression of the fact that if $P_{j}$ is almost entirely concentrated near zero then the convolution product $\prod P_{j}$ can be approximated by the convolution exponential $\exp \left\{\sum\left(P_{j}-I\right)\right\}$. In this framework the Gaussian distributions appear as a partially "degenerate case" when all the mass of the measures $\sum_{j}$ becomes concentrated arbitrarily close to zero.

The above arguments quickly lead to the general form of the classical central limit theorem. In fact, the arguments can be made very elementary. However, to obtain necessary as well as sufficient conditions for the validity of the approximations or limit theorems it appears convenient to use Fourier transforms. Thus we have used these objects rather liberally.

The reader who would find himself repulsed by the length of certain proofs may safely skip them. The statements of the main results are supposed to be understandable by themselves and the proofs are really not terribly difficult.

The content of the chapter is as follows. In Section 2 we give some elementary facts concerning concentration functions and proceed to prove an inequality which originated with Lévy, was improved by Kolmogorov, and then by Esseen.

We first give an argument which is a slight modification of the one used by Esseen. However, Kolmogorov's original inequality can be proved by a combinatorial argument valid in vector spaces of arbitrary dimension. To indicate the possibilities we have given part of an argument due to M. Kanter.

Esseen's argument may be considered a refinement of the usual one which gives necessary and sufficient conditions for shift compactness of distributions of sums. To prepare the ground for Chapter 16 we apply the result to variables written in a split form as explained above.

Section 3 gives conditions for compactness and shift compactness.
Section 4 deals with the approximation of the distribution of a sum by the accompanying Poisson exponentials. It starts with two simple observations.

One of them concerns variables $X_{j}$ such that $\sum\left\{P\left[X_{j} \neq 0\right]\right\}^{2}$ is small. The other concerns variables $X_{j}$ such that $\left|X_{j}\right| \leq l$ for some small $l$. Combined, they already give the central limit theorem. However, to indicate other possibilities we also give a theorem relative to the case where it is only assumed that $\sup _{j} P\left[X_{j} \neq 0\right]$ is small, and conclude on a remark about the case where the convolution product $\prod_{j} P_{j}$ is not changed much if one of the terms of the product is deleted.

Section 5 gives further results on limiting distributions, emphasizing the passage from $\sum X_{j}$ to sums $\sum f\left(X_{j}\right)$ for functions $f$ which are twice Peano differentiable at zero.

Section 6 gives some special indications on sums of stochastic processes.

## 2. Concentration Inequalities

Many approximation theorems can be derived or guessed by using the principle that if $Z=X+Y$ with $Y$ small and $X$ very dispersed then the distribution of $Z$ differs little from that of $X$. We start by two simple remarks of this type. To apply them one needs to know how concentrated $X$ may be. For sums of many independent variables, this leads to the consideration of the inequalities of Kolmogorov and Esseen.

Let $\mu$ be a finite signed measure on the line. The Kolmogorov-Smirnov norm of $\mu$ is defined by

$$
|\mu|_{K}=\sup \{|\mu(-\infty, x]| ;-\infty<x<+\infty\} .
$$

Let $S^{\alpha} \mu$ be the measure $\mu$ shifted by the amount $\alpha$, so that if $\mu$ is the distribution of a variable $X$ then $S^{\alpha} \mu$ is the distribution of $X+\alpha$.

We shall call modulus of continuity under shift of the measure $\mu$ the function defined for $\tau \geq 0$ by

$$
\Gamma_{\mu}(\tau)=\sup \left\{\left|\mu-S^{\alpha} \mu\right|_{K} ;|\alpha| \leq \tau\right\}
$$

For a probability measure $P$ the modulus $\Gamma(P, \tau)$ is equal to $\sup _{x} P\{(x, x+ \tau]\}$.
The concentration function $C_{P}$ of the probability measure $P$ is defined by

$$
C_{P}(\tau)=\sup _{x} P\{[x, x+\tau]\}
$$

Obviously $\Gamma_{P}(\tau) \leq C_{P}(\tau)$ and $C_{P}(\tau) \leq \Gamma_{P}\left(\tau^{\prime}\right)$ whenever $\tau<\tau^{\prime}$. If $X$ is a random variable with distribution $P$, we shall often write $C_{X}$ instead of $C_{P}$ and similarly for $\Gamma$. The following lemmas summarize some of the elementary important properties of the functions $\Gamma$ or $C$.

Lemma 1 (P. Lévy). If $X$ and $Y$ are independent then $C_{X+Y} \leq\left(C_{X}\right) \wedge\left(C_{Y}\right)$. Also, $\Gamma_{X+Y} \leq\left(\Gamma_{X}\right) \wedge\left(\Gamma_{Y}\right)$.

Lemma 2. Let $X$ and $Y$ be two random variables with respective distributions $P=\mathscr{L}(X)$ and $Q=\mathscr{L}(Y)$. Then for every $\tau \geq 0$ one has

$$
|P-Q|_{K} \leq \operatorname{Pr}\{|X-Y|>\tau\}+\left[\Gamma_{X}(\tau)\right] \wedge\left[\Gamma_{Y}(\tau)\right]
$$

Lemma 3. Let $P, Q$, and $W$ be three probability measures on the line. Let $P W$ be the convolution product of $P$ by $W$. Define $Q W$ analogously. Let $\gamma$ be the minimum of the moduli of continuity of $[P-Q]^{+}$and $[P-Q]^{-}$. Then for every $\tau \geq 0$ one has

$$
\left[2 C_{W}(\tau)-1\right]|P-Q|_{K} \leq|P W-Q W|_{K}+C_{W}(\tau) \gamma(\tau)
$$

Proof. For the first lemma, let $J$ be any interval of the line and let $J-a= \{x ; x+a \in J\}$. Then

$$
\operatorname{Pr}[X+Y \in J]=E \operatorname{Pr}\{X \in J-Y \mid Y\}=E \operatorname{Pr}\{Y \in J-X \mid X\}
$$

This gives the desired result.
For the second lemma one can write, for instance,

$$
\begin{aligned}
\operatorname{Pr}[Y \leq x] & \leq \operatorname{Pr}[X \leq x+\tau]+\operatorname{Pr}[|X-Y|>\tau] \\
& \leq \operatorname{Pr}[X \leq x]+\Gamma_{X}(\tau)+\operatorname{Pr}[|X-Y|>\tau]
\end{aligned}
$$

Replacing $x$ by $x-\tau$ and interchanging the roles of $X$ and $Y$ one obtains four versions of the above inequality. They can be combined to give the result of Lemma 2.

For the third lemma let $\mu_{1}=[P-Q]^{+}$and $\mu_{2}=[P-Q]^{-}$. Let $F(x)= \mu_{1}\{(-\infty, x]\}, G(x)=\mu_{2}\{(-\infty, x]\}$, and $H=F-G$. Then $H(x)=P\{(-\infty, x]\}- Q\{(-\infty, x]\}$.

If $H$ is identically zero, or if $2 C_{W}(\tau)-1 \leq 0$, the inequality of the lemma certainly holds. If not, suppose that for some $x \in(-\infty,+\infty)$ one has $H(x)> \delta>0$. Then, for $u \geq 0$ one may write

$$
\begin{aligned}
F(x+u)-G(x+u)= & {[F(x+u)-F(x)]+[F(x)-G(x)] } \\
& -[G(x+u)-G(x)] \\
> & \delta-[G(x+u)-G(x)]>\delta-\Gamma_{\mu_{2}}(u)
\end{aligned}
$$

Similarly

$$
\begin{aligned}
F(x-u)-G(x-u)= & F(x)-G(x)+[G(x)-G(x-u)] \\
& -[F(x)-F(x-u)]>\delta-\Gamma_{\mu_{1}}(u)
\end{aligned}
$$

Thus in both cases there is an interval of length at least $\tau$ in which $H$ is larger than $\delta-\gamma(\tau)$. This implies the existence of a number $y$ such that

$$
\int H(y+u) W(d u) \geq[\delta-\gamma(\tau)] C_{W}(\tau)-\left\{\sup _{x}|H(x)|\right\}\left[1-C_{W}(\tau)\right]
$$

Therefore, in this case

$$
|P W-Q W|_{K} \geq[\delta-\gamma(\tau)] C_{W}(\tau)-|P-Q|_{K}\left[1-C_{W}(\tau)\right]
$$

If $|P-Q|_{K}=\sup _{x} H(x)$ the inequality asserted in the lemma follows by letting $\delta$ tend to $|P-Q|_{K}$. If on the contrary $|P-Q|_{K}=\sup _{x}[-H(x)]$, the same result follows by interchanging the roles of $P$ and $Q$. Hence the result.

Lemma 2 says that whether $X$ and $X-Y$ are independent or not, the distributions $\mathscr{L}(X)$ and $\mathscr{L}(Y)$ are close to each other when $X-Y$ is small and one of the variables $X$ and $Y$ is not very concentrated.

Lemma 3 says that adding a small independent variable will not change the Kolmogorov distances too drastically, if $\gamma$ is small. Note that one has always $\gamma \leq\left(\Gamma_{P}\right) \wedge\left(\Gamma_{Q}\right)$.

Remark. Here we have measured differences between probability measures by the Kolmogorov-Smirnov distance. This is a very convenient norm since it is invariant by all increasing one-to-one transformations of the line.

A weaker distance is the Lévy distance $\lambda(P, Q)$ defined as the infimum of the numbers $\varepsilon$ such that

$$
P\{(-\infty, x-\varepsilon]\}-\varepsilon \leq Q\{(-\infty, x]\} \leq P\{(-\infty, x+\varepsilon]\}+\varepsilon
$$

for all $x \in(-\infty,+\infty)$. Convergence for the Lévy distance is equivalent to convergence of integrals of bounded continuous functions.

The Lévy distance is shift invariant but definitely not scale invariant. It is often possible to relate the Lévy and Kolmogorov distances. For instance, an argument similar to that of Lemma 3 shows that if $\lambda(P, Q)>\varepsilon$ then

$$
|P W-Q W|_{K} \geq \varepsilon C_{W}(\varepsilon)-\left[1-C_{W}(\varepsilon)\right]
$$

For the Lévy distance one has the following often used property: If $X_{n}-Y_{n} \rightarrow$ 0 in probability, then $\lambda\left[\mathscr{L}\left(X_{n}\right), \mathscr{L}\left(Y_{n}\right)\right] \rightarrow 0$. This is often called Slutzky's theorem. The property in question was explicitly mentioned by Lévy as the reason for his choice of distance.

Since that time it has been shown that another distance, the Prohorov distance, is intimately related to the Slutzky result. For any pair ( $X, Y$ ) the Prohorov distance between $\mathscr{L}(X)$ and $\mathscr{L}(Y)$ is the infimum of the numbers
$\varepsilon \geq 0$ such that there be a joint distribution with marginals $\mathscr{L}(X)$ and $\mathscr{L}(Y)$, respectively, and such that $\operatorname{Pr}\{|X-Y|>\varepsilon\} \leq \varepsilon$.

If $A^{\varepsilon}$ denotes the set of points at distance $\varepsilon$ or less from $A$, the Prohorov distance $\pi(P, Q)$ is also the infimum of numbers $\varepsilon$ such that $P(A) \leq Q\left(A^{\varepsilon}\right)+\varepsilon$ for all measurable $A$. It is stronger than the Lévy distance but not comparable to the Kolmogorov distance. Lemma 1, above, says that adding independent random variables diminishes the concentration. We shall now give more precise results in this direction.

Since the situation is not as neat as one would wish, we have given two arguments. One of them is a modification by M. Kanter of an argument of Rogozin, which itself improved upon a previous result of Kolmogorov. This result of Kolmogorov was an improvement on results of P. Lévy and Doeblin.

The Kolmogorov-Rogozin-Kanter argument is a combinatorial one applicable to random variables which take their values in arbitrary vector spaces. However, it does not make any use of the small values of the variables. Another argument, due to C. G. Esseen, does take into account the small values. However, no dimension-free extension of it is known.

We shall start with the Esseen argument for which the following notation will be needed. If $X$ is a real random variable, $X^{s}$, called $X$ symmetrized, will be the variable $X^{s}=X-X^{\prime}$ with $X$ and $X^{\prime}$ independent and such that $\mathscr{L}\left(X^{\prime}\right)=\mathscr{L}(X)$. For any $X$ and any $\tau>0$ let $D^{2}(X, \tau)=E\left[1 \wedge|X / \tau|^{2}\right]$. For $\tau=0$ let $D^{2}(X, 0)=\lim _{\tau} D^{2}(X, \tau)=P[X \neq 0]$.

Note that $D^{2}(X, \tau)$ is a continuous decreasing function of $\tau$. On the contrary, $\tau^{2} D^{2}(X, \tau)=E\left[\tau^{2} \wedge|X|^{2}\right]$ is a continuous increasing function of $\tau$.

If $\mu$ is a median of $X$ then $P\left[\left|X^{s}\right| \geq \tau\right] \geq \frac{1}{2} P\{|X-\mu| \geq \tau\}$. Thus

$$
D^{2}[X-\mu, \tau] \leq 2 D^{2}\left(X^{s}, \tau\right)
$$

for all $\tau$. Note also that

$$
D^{2}\left(X^{s}, \tau\right) \geq \inf _{a} D^{2}[X-a, \tau]
$$

for all $\tau$.
We shall also need a similar quantity defined for infinitely divisible distributions. It is well known (and will be a consequence of the results proved in Section 4) that for $\tau>0$ an infinitely divisible random variable $S$ has a characteristic function of the form

$$
E \exp \{i t S\}=\exp \left\{i t m-\frac{\sigma^{2} t^{2}}{2}+\int\left[e^{i t x}-1-i t x u(x, \tau)\right] M(d x)\right\}
$$

where $u(x, \tau)=1$ if $|x| \leq \tau$ and $u(x, \tau)=0$ otherwise, and where $M$ is a certain positive measure on the locally compact space $(-\infty, 0) \cup(0, \infty)$. This $M$ is called the Lévy measure of $S$. The following proposition easily yields a bound on the concentration of infinitely divisible distributions.

Proposition 1. Let $S$ be an infinitely divisible random variable such that

$$
E \exp \{i t S\}=\exp \left\{i t m-\frac{\sigma^{2} t^{2}}{2}+\int\left[e^{i t x}-1-i t x u(x, \tau)\right] M(d x)\right\}
$$

Let $\lambda=\sigma^{2}+\int\left(1 \wedge x^{2}\right) M(d x)$. Then

$$
E[1-|S-a|]^{+} \leq \sup _{0 \leq y \leq 1} E\left[1-\left|Z_{y}\right|\right]^{+} \leq\left(\frac{\pi}{2 \lambda}\right)^{1 / 2}
$$

where $Z_{y}$ is a random variable of the form $Z_{y}=y\left(N-N^{\prime}\right)$ with $N$ and $N^{\prime}$ independent Poisson variables such that $E N=E N^{\prime}=\lambda / 2 y^{2}$.

Proof. The function $[1-|x|]^{+}$has a Fourier transform $2 \pi h(t)$ with

$$
2 \pi h(t)=\int[1-|x|]^{+} e^{i t x} d x=\left(\frac{2}{t} \sin \frac{t}{2}\right)^{2} \geq 0
$$

By integration this yields

$$
\begin{aligned}
E[1-|S-a|]^{+} & =\int e^{-i t a} \phi(t) h(t) d t \\
& \leq \int|\phi(t)| h(t) d t
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\phi(t)=E \exp \{i t S\}$.
Let $f(t, x)$ be the function which is equal to $\cos t x-1$ for $|x| \geq 1$ and to $x^{-2}[\cos t x-1]$ for $0<|x| \leq 1$. For $x=0$, taking the limiting value $f(t, 0)= -\left(t^{2} / 2\right)$. Let $\bar{M}$ be the measure obtained by writing $\bar{M}(A)=\frac{1}{2}[M(A)+ M(-A)]$. Taking $\lambda$ as defined, let $V$ be the probability measure such that $V[\{0\}]=\sigma^{2} / \lambda, \lambda V(d x)=x^{2} \bar{M}(d x)$ for $0<|x| \leq 1$, and $\lambda V(d x)=\bar{M}(d x)$ for $|x| \geq 1$.

With this notation, that previous inequality reads

$$
E[1-|S-a|]^{+} \leq \int\left[\exp \left\{\lambda \int f(t, x) V(d x)\right\}\right] h(t) d t
$$

Since geometric means do not exceed arithmetic means, this yields

$$
E[1-|S-a|]^{+} \leq \int J(x) V(d x)
$$

where

$$
J(x)=\int[\exp \{\lambda f(t, x)\}] h(t) d t
$$

Now consider $J(x)$ for $|x| \geq 1$. Then

$$
\begin{aligned}
J(x) & =\int \exp \{\lambda(\cos t x-1)\} h(t) d t \\
& =E\left[1-\left|Z_{x}\right|\right]^{+}
\end{aligned}
$$



for the variable $Z_{x}=x\left(N-N^{\prime}\right)$ with $N$ and $N^{\prime}$ independent Poisson, $E N= E N^{\prime}=\lambda / 2$. Since the only values that $Z_{x}$ can take are multiples of $|x| \geq 1$, the value $E\left[1-\left|Z_{x}\right|\right]^{+}$is equal to $P\left\{N-N^{\prime}=0\right\}$ and thus independent of $x$.

Thus

$$
E[1-|S-a|]^{+} \leq \sup _{0 \leq x \leq 1} J(x)
$$

as asserted in the proposition. To obtain the second assertion it will be sufficient to show that $J(x) \leq(\pi / 2 \lambda)^{1 / 2}$ for all $x \in[0,1]$. This is a consequence of the following lemmas.

Lemma 4. If $Z$ is any random variable then

$$
\begin{aligned}
E[1-|Z|]^{+} & \leq E \frac{2(1-\cos \pi Z)}{(\pi Z)^{2}} \\
& =\frac{1}{\pi} \int\left(E e^{i t Z}\right)\left[1-\left|\frac{t}{\pi}\right|\right]^{+} d t
\end{aligned}
$$

Proof. The equality comes from the Fourier transform identity

$$
\frac{2(1-\cos c x)}{(c x)^{2}}=\frac{1}{c} \int e^{i t x}\left[1-\left|\frac{t}{c}\right|\right]^{+} d t
$$

which is easily verifiable. To prove the inequality it is sufficient to show that for $0 \leq x \leq \pi$ one has

$$
1-\frac{x}{\pi} \leq \frac{2(1-\cos x)}{x^{2}}
$$

or equivalently that in the same range the function $g(x)=2(1-\cos x)- x^{2}+\left(x^{3} / \pi\right)$ remains positive.

Now

$$
g^{\prime}(x)=2 \sin x-2 x+3 \frac{x^{2}}{\pi}
$$

and

$$
g^{\prime \prime}(x)=6 \frac{x}{\pi}-2(1-\cos x)
$$

Note that $g^{\prime \prime}(\pi / 2)=1$. Also $g^{\prime \prime}$ decreases only in the interval ( $x_{1}, x_{2}$ ) where $x_{1}<\pi / 2<x_{2}$ are such that $\sin x_{1}=\sin x_{2}=(3 / \pi)$. Since $g^{\prime \prime \prime}(x) \geq -2\left(1-(3 / \pi)\right.$, one has $g^{\prime \prime}\left(x_{2}\right) \geq 1-2(1-(3 / \pi)) \geq 0$. Thus $g^{\prime \prime}(x)>0$ in the interval $[0, \pi]$. It follows that $g^{\prime}(x) \geq 0$ and therefore that $g(x) \geq g(0)=0$. This gives the desired result.

This leads to the following upper bound for the integrals $J(x)=E[1- \left.\left|Z_{x}\right|\right]^{+}$of Proposition 1.

Lemma 5. For every $x \in(0,1]$ one has

$$
J(x)=\int \exp \left\{\frac{\lambda}{x^{2}}(\cos t x-1)\right\} h(t) d t \leq\left(\frac{\pi}{2 \lambda}\right)^{1 / 2}
$$

Proof. According to the preceding lemma one has

$$
J(x) \leq \frac{1}{\pi} \int_{|t| \leq \pi} \exp \left\{-\frac{\lambda}{x^{2}}(1-\cos t x)\right\} d t
$$

The term in the exponent may be written in the form

$$
\frac{\lambda t^{2}}{2}\left[\frac{2(1-\cos t x)}{t^{2} x^{2}}\right]=\frac{\lambda t^{2}}{2}\left(\frac{2}{t x} \sin \frac{t x}{2}\right)^{2}
$$

Since $\sin u / u$ decreases for $u \in(0, \pi / 2)$, the term $[(2 / t x) \sin (t x / 2)]^{2}$ is at least equal to $(2 / \pi)^{2}$.

Thus

$$
\begin{aligned}
J(x) & \leq \frac{1}{\pi} \int \exp \left\{-\left(\frac{2}{\pi}\right)^{2} \lambda \frac{t^{2}}{2}\right\} d t \\
& =\left(\frac{\pi}{2 \lambda}\right)^{1 / 2}
\end{aligned}
$$

This concludes the proof of the lemma.
The foregoing Proposition 1 can be used to give a bound on the concentration of sums of independent variables. For this purpose, recall that we have denoted $D^{2}(X, \tau)$ the expression $D^{2}(X, \tau)=E\left\{1 \wedge(X / \tau)^{2}\right\}$. With this notation one can state the following.

Theorem 1. Let $X_{j} ; j=1,2, \ldots$ be independent random variables with sum $S=\sum X_{j}$. Let $\mu_{j}$ be a median of $X_{j}$ and let $X_{j}^{s}$ be the symmetrized variable associated with $X_{j}$. Let $B^{2}(\tau)$ be any one of the three numbers

$$
\begin{aligned}
& B_{1}^{2}(\tau)=\sum_{j} D^{2}\left(X_{j}^{s}, \tau\right) \\
& B_{2}^{2}(\tau)=\sum_{j} \inf _{x} D^{2}\left(X_{j}-x, \tau\right) \\
& B_{3}^{2}(\tau)=\frac{1}{2} \sum_{j} D^{2}\left(X_{j}-\mu_{j}, \tau\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

Then

$$
C_{S}(\tau) \leq \frac{2 \sqrt{\pi}}{B(\tau)} \leq \frac{3.55}{B(\tau)}
$$

Proof. Let $\phi_{j}$ be the characteristic function of $X_{j}$. Then

$$
\left|\Pi \phi_{j}\right|^{2}=\Pi\left|\phi_{j}\right|^{2} \leq \exp \left\{\sum_{j}\left|\phi_{j}\right|^{2}-1\right\}
$$

Now $\left|\phi_{j}\right|^{2}$ is the characteristic function of the symmetrized variable $X_{j}^{s}$ and $\sum\left\{\left|\phi_{j}\right|^{2}-1\right\}$ takes the form

$$
\sum\left[\left|\phi_{j}\right|^{2}-1\right]=\int(\cos t x-1) M(d x)
$$

for a symmetric measure $M=\sum_{j} \mathscr{L}\left(X_{j}^{s}\right)$. Thus

$$
|\phi(t)| \leq \exp \left\{\frac{1}{2} \int(\cos t x-1) M(d x)\right\}
$$

and the argument of Proposition 1 becomes applicable. It gives $E[1-\mid S- a \mid]^{+} \leq(\pi / 2 \lambda)^{1 / 2}$ for a $\lambda$ equal to $\frac{1}{2} B_{1}^{2}=\frac{1}{2} \sum E\left[1 \wedge\left|X_{j}^{s}\right|\right]^{2}$. Since $[1-|x|]^{+}$is larger than $1 / 2$ in an interval of length unity, this gives $C_{S}(1) \leq 2 \sqrt{\pi} / B_{1}$ as claimed. The passage to other values $\tau>0$ is just a change of scale. For $\tau=0$ the result follows by continuity. This completes the proof of the theorem.

Remark. The argument applies also to the infinitely divisible variable $S$ considered in Proposition 1. With the notation of that Proposition, let $\lambda(\tau)$ be defined by

$$
\lambda(\tau)=\frac{1}{\tau^{2}}\left[\sigma^{2}+\int\left(\tau^{2} \wedge x^{2}\right) M(d x)\right]
$$

Then

$$
C_{S}^{2}(\tau) \leq \frac{2 \pi}{\lambda(\tau)}
$$

for all $\tau>0$. An inequality of this nature can also be obtained by combining the Berry-Esseen bounds with the inequality of Kolmogorov given in Theorem 2 below. This was the method used by the present author, before Esseen's argument became available.

Note that the result says in particular that if $C_{S}(\tau)>0$ for some $\tau>0$, then $\lambda(\tau)$ must be finite for all $\tau>0$. Also $S$ has a continuous distribution $C_{S}(0)=0$ whenever either $\sigma^{2}>0$ or $\|M\|=\infty$.

As mentioned previously the above method of proof, which depends heavily on the Fourier transformation, has not been extended to give analogous results valid in vector spaces independently of their dimension. Also, the result given in the last corollary above is not the best available. Thus we shall sketch another approach along the lines indicated by M. Kanter, who himself used a lemma of D. J. Kleitman.

Let $C$ be a set in a vector space $V$ and let $x_{1}, x_{2}, \ldots, x_{n}, \ldots$ be elements of $V$. Let us say that such a system has property ( $K$ ) if for every integer $k \geq 1$ every set $C+y_{j} ; j=1,2, \ldots, k$ of disjoint translates of $C$ and every $x_{i}$ there is some index $r$ such that $C+y_{r}-x_{i}$ is disjoint from $\bigcup_{j}\left\{C+y_{j} ; j=1,2, \ldots, k\right\}$.

For each $n$ and $k$ let $F_{n}(k)$ be the maximum over $m$ of sums of the type $\sum_{i}\left\{\binom{n}{i} ; m \leq i<m+k\right\}$. This is also the sum of the largest $k$ of the coefficients $\binom{n}{i}$.

Lemma 6. Let $C$ and $\left\{x_{j} ; j=1,2, \ldots, n\right\}$ have Property ( $\mathbf{K}$ ). Consider sums $S=\sum_{i=1}^{n} \varepsilon_{i} x_{i}$ where $\varepsilon_{i} \in\{0,1\}$. Then for any $k$ disjoint translates $C+y_{j}$ of $C$ the number of sums $S$ which fall in the union $\bigcup_{j}\left\{C+y_{j} ; j=1, \ldots, k\right\}$ does not exceed $F_{n}(k)$.

Proof. Let $G_{n}(k)$ be the maximum possible number of such sums. Clearly $G_{1}(k)=F_{1}(k)$ for all values of $k$. Write the sum $S=\sum_{i=1}^{n} \varepsilon_{i} x_{i}$ as $S=S^{\prime}+\varepsilon_{n} x_{n}$ and consider a set of disjoint translates $C+y_{j} ; j=1, \ldots, k$. If $S$ falls in their union and $\varepsilon_{n}=0$, then $S^{\prime} \in \bigcup_{j}\left(C+y_{j}\right)=A$. Consider the index $r$ such that $C+y_{r}-x_{n}$ is disjoint from the above union. Then it can happen that $\varepsilon_{n}=1$ and $S^{\prime} \in C+y_{r}-x_{n}$. Otherwise if $S \in A$ and $\varepsilon_{n}=1$, one has $S^{\prime} \in \bigcup_{j \neq r}\left[C+y_{j}-x_{n}\right]$. Thus the number of ways $S$ can fall in the union $A$ does not exceed the sum of the number of ways $S^{\prime}$ can fall in $\bigcup_{j \neq r}\left[C+y_{j}-x_{n}\right]$ with the number of ways it can fall in $A \cup\left(C+y_{r}-x_{n}\right)$. This shows that $G_{n}(k) \leq G_{n-1}(k-1)+G_{n-1}(k+1)$.

Now there is a case where the above recursion relation is satisfied with equality instead of inequality. This is the case where $C=\{0\}$ and where $x_{j} \equiv 1$ on the line. Indeed in this case, let $k$ be given and let $m$ be an integer.

According to the above argument, if $S \in[m, m+k)$, then either $\varepsilon_{n}=1$ and $S^{\prime} \in[m, m+k-1)$, or $\varepsilon_{n}=1$ and $S^{\prime}=m-1$, or $\varepsilon_{n}=0$ and $S^{\prime} \in[m, m+k)$.

In the present case we can place the integer $m$ in such a way that $[m, m+k)$ yields the maximum $F_{n}(k)$ of the sums $\left\{\sum_{i}\binom{n}{i} ; i \in[m, m+k)\right\}$ and also so that the intervals $[m-1, m+k)$ and $[m, m+k-1)$ yields the maximum of the corresponding sums of the coefficients $\binom{n-1}{i}$. This means that $F_{n}(k)= F_{n-1}(k-1)+F_{n-1}(k+1)$. By recurrence one concludes that $G_{n}(k) \leq F_{n}(k)$ as claimed in the statement of the lemma.

Here are two possible applications of Lemma 6.
Lemma 7. If $C$ is a convex symmetric set in a vector space $V$ and if all the $x_{j}$ are such that $x_{j} \notin 2 C$ then the system $\left\{C, x_{j} ; j=1,2, \ldots\right\}$ enjoys Property (K). Similarly, if $V$ is the real line and $C$ is the interval ( $-1,1$ ], then $\left\{C, x_{j}\right.$, $j=1,2, \ldots\}$ will enjoy Property (K) if $\left|x_{j}\right| \geq 2$.

Proof. The second statement is obvious. To prove the first, consider $k$ disjoint translates $C+y_{j}, j=1,2, \ldots, k$ and a point $x$ such that, whatever may be $j$, there is some other index $f(j)$ such that $C+y_{j}-x$ and $C+y_{f(j)}$ are not disjoint. Starting from $j=1$, let $f_{1}=1, f_{2}=f(1), f_{3}=f\left(f_{2}\right)$, and so forth until one reaches an index $f_{m}=1$. Since $\left(C+y_{j}-x\right) \cap\left(C+y_{f(j)}\right)$ is not empty and since $C$ is symmetric one can assert that $y_{f(j)}-y_{j}+x \in 2 C$. Summing over the values constructed above and dividing by $m$, one concludes that $x \in 2 C$. Hence the result.

We shall apply the foregoing two lemmas to real valued variables, but it should be clear that the argument applies also to symmetrically distributed variables in arbitrary spaces $V$ for a symmetric convex set $C$ (under appropriate measurability restrictions).

Proposition 2. Let $X_{j} ; j=1,2, \ldots$ be independent real random variables. Assume that $\mathscr{L}\left(X_{j}\right)=\mathscr{L}\left(-X_{j}\right)$ and let $S=\sum X_{j}$. Let $N_{j}$ equal one or zero according to
whether $\left|X_{j}\right|>1$ or $\left|X_{j}\right| \leq 1$. Let $N=\sum N_{j}$ and let $q(n)=2^{-n}\binom{n}{m}$ where $m$ is the integer part $m=[n / 2]$ of $n / 2$.

Then

$$
C_{S}(2) \leq E q(N) \leq\left\{\frac{1-e^{-\lambda}}{\lambda}\right\}^{1 / \lambda},
$$

where $\lambda=E N=\sum P\left\{\left|X_{j}\right|>1\right\}$.
Proof. Consider a sum of the form $S=\sum_{j=1}^{n} \gamma_{j} x_{j}$ with $\gamma_{j}= \pm 1$. Then $\sum x_{j}+\sum \gamma_{j} x_{j}$ is of the form $\sum \varepsilon_{j}\left(2 x_{j}\right)$ with coefficients $\varepsilon_{j}$ which take values zero or unity. Thus, according to Lemmas 6 and 7 , if the $x_{j}$ 's are fixed, $\left|x_{j}\right|>1$, and if the $\gamma_{j}$ 's are independently distributed with $P\left[\gamma_{j}=-1\right]=P\left[\gamma_{j}=1\right]=\frac{1}{2}$, the probability that $S$ fall in a given translate $[x-1, x+1]$ of $C=[-1,+1]$ is at most $q(n)=2^{-n}\binom{n}{\left[\frac{n}{2}\right]}$.

To pass to the general case note that if $S=\sum X_{j}$, then $S$ has the same distribution as $T=\sum_{j} \gamma_{j} X_{j}$. One can look at $T$ conditionally, given all the values $X_{j}=x_{j}$ and the sum $\sum \gamma_{i} X_{i}$ taken over those $i$ such that $\left|x_{i}\right| \leq 1$. If $N$ is the number of $X_{j}$ 's such that $\left|X_{j}\right|>1$, the conditional probability of falling in $[x-1, x+1]$ will be bounded by $q(N)$. Hence $C_{S}(2) \leq E q(N)$.

It remains to give an upper bound for this expectation. For this purpose, let $r(n)=\sqrt{n+1} q(n)$. Note, by inspection, that $r(0), r(1)$, and $r(2)$ are all smaller than unity. A simple computation shows also that $r(2 m+2) \leq r(2 m)$ and that $r(2 m+1) \leq r(2 m-1)$. Thus $r(n) \leq 1$ for all $n$. In other words $q(n) \leq(n+1)^{-1 / 2}$ and $C_{S}(2) \leq E(1 / \sqrt{N+1})$.

Now $N=\sum_{j} N_{j}$ is a Poisson binomial variable with expectation $E N=\lambda= \sum p_{j}, p_{j}=P\left\{\left|X_{j}\right|>1\right\}$. Using the equality

$$
\frac{1}{\sqrt{n+1}}=\frac{1}{\Gamma\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)} \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-(n+1) v} \frac{d v}{\sqrt{v}}
$$

one sees that $E(N+1)^{-1 / 2} \leq E(M+1)^{-1 / 2}$ where $M$ is a Poisson variable such that $E M=\lambda$. Thus

$$
C_{S}(2) \leq E \frac{1}{\sqrt{M+1}} \leq\left\{E\left(\frac{1}{M+1}\right)\right\}^{1 / 2}=\left(\frac{1-e^{-\lambda}}{\lambda}\right)^{1 / 2}
$$

This concludes the proof of the proposition.
Remark 1. Using the half open intervals $(x-1, x+1]$ mentioned in Lemma 7, we can also say that

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Gamma_{S}(2) & \leq\left(\frac{1-e^{-\mu}}{\mu}\right)^{1 / 2} \\
\text { where } \mu & =\sum P\left\{\left|X_{j}\right| \geq 1\right\}
\end{aligned}
$$

Remark 2. M. Kanter has refined the above argument to show that

$$
C_{S}(2) \leq e^{-\lambda}\left[I_{0}(\lambda)+I_{1}(\lambda)\right]
$$

where $I_{0}(\lambda)$ and $I_{1}(\lambda)$ are the modified Bessel functions. This bound is attained for the symmetrized Poisson variable $X^{s}=X-X^{\prime}$ where $X$ is Poisson with expectation $\lambda / 2$.

A consequence of the above arguments is the following result which refines slightly a result of Kolmogorov and Rogozin.

Theorem 2. Let $X_{j} ; j=1,2, \ldots$ be independent random variables. Let $S=\sum X_{j}$. Then for every $\tau \geq 0$ one has

$$
C_{S}^{2}(\tau) \leq \frac{1-e^{-\lambda(\tau)}}{\lambda(\tau)}
$$

and

$$
\Gamma_{S}^{2}(\tau) \leq \frac{1-e^{-\lambda(\tau-)}}{\lambda(\tau-)}
$$

where $\lambda(\tau)=\sum_{j}\left[1-C_{X_{j}}(\tau)\right]$.
Proof. Each $X_{j}$ can be written in the form $X_{j}=f_{j}\left(\xi_{j}\right)$ where $f_{j}$ is an increasing function defined on $[-1,+1]$ and where $\xi_{j}$ is uniformly distributed on $[-1,1]$. For $u \in[0,1]$ let $g_{j}(u)=\frac{1}{2}\left[f_{j}(u)+f_{j}(-u)\right]$ and $h_{j}(u)=\frac{1}{2}\left[f_{j}(u)-f_{j}(-u)\right]$.

Let $u_{j} ; j=1,2, \ldots$ be independent variables, uniformly distributed on $[0,1]$, and let $\gamma_{j}$ be independent variables which take values $(-1)$ and $(+1)$ with probability $1 / 2$. Then the distribution of $X_{j}$ is the same as that of $Y_{j}=g_{j}\left(u_{j}\right)+ \gamma_{j} h_{j}\left(u_{j}\right)$.

Conditioning on the values taken by the $u_{j}$ and then taking an expectation just as in Proposition 2, one sees that $C_{S}(\tau) \leq E q(N)$ and $\Gamma_{S}(\tau) \leq E q\left(N^{\prime}\right)$, where $N$ is the number of $u_{j}$ such that $f_{j}\left(u_{j}\right)-f_{j}\left(-u_{j}\right)>\tau$ and $N^{\prime}$ is the number of $u_{j}$ such that $f_{j}\left(u_{j}\right)-f_{j}\left(-u_{j}\right) \geq \tau$.

Now if $u$ is such that $f_{j}(u)-f_{j}(-u) \leq \tau$, then $P\left[f_{j}(-u) \leq X_{j} \leq f_{j}(u)\right] \geq u$. Hence $C_{X_{j}}(\tau) \geq u$ and $1-C_{X_{j}}(\tau) \leq P\left[f_{j}\left(u_{j}\right)-f_{j}\left(-u_{j}\right)>\tau\right]$.

Similarly if $f_{j}(u)-f_{j}(-u)<\tau$ then $f_{j}(u)-f_{j}(-u) \leq \tau^{\prime}$ for some $\tau^{\prime}<\tau$. Thus, letting $\tau^{\prime}$ increase to $\tau$ one has $P\left\{f_{j}\left(u_{j}\right)-f_{j}\left(-u_{j}\right) \geq \tau\right\} \geq 1-C_{X_{j}}(\tau-$ 0 ).

The result follows then by bounding the expectation $E q(N)$ or $E q\left(N^{\prime}\right)$ just as in the proof of Proposition 2.

Remark. The passage from $\lambda(\tau)$ to $\lambda(\tau-)$ which results in the substitution of the modulus $\Gamma$ for the concentration function $C$ is not a major improvement. The technical reason for mentioning it here is as follows. The function $\tau \mapsto \lambda(\tau)$ is decreasing and right continuous, but it may have discontinuities.

Thus, in arguments where one would like to choose a specific value, say $s$, for $\lambda$, it may not be possible to achieve this value exactly. However if $s \leq \lambda(0)$ there is always a $\tau$ such that $\lambda(\tau) \leq s$ and such that $\lambda\left(\tau^{\prime}\right) \geq s$ for all $\tau^{\prime}<\tau$. Thus one can assert that $\Gamma_{S}^{2}(\tau) \leq\left(1-e^{-s}\right) s^{-1}$ for that particular $\tau$. This is an occasional convenience.

The main applications of the foregoing inequalities will be found in subsequent sections. To conclude the present one we shall give three results which
illustrate the use of the inequalities. One of them refers to the splitting procedure used by Lévy to prove the central limit theorem. It is as follows.

Let $X$ be a random variable defined on some probability space ( $\Omega, \mathscr{A}, P$ ). Let $A$ be an event, $A \in \mathscr{A}$. Then $X$ is the sum of the two terms $X I_{A}$ and $X\left(1-I_{A}\right)$. Let $\alpha=P\left(A^{c}\right), G=\mathscr{L}(X \mid A)$, and $H=\mathscr{L}\left(X \mid A^{c}\right)$.

The distribution $F$ of $X$ may be written in the form $F=(1-\alpha) G+ \alpha H$. Alternatively, let $(\xi, \eta, U, V)$ be independent random variables such that $\mathscr{L}(U)=G, \mathscr{L}(V)=H, \mathscr{L}(\xi)=\mathscr{L}(\eta)$, and $P[\xi=1]=1-P[\xi=0]=\alpha$. Then $F$ is the distribution of $(1-\xi) U+\xi V=(1-\eta) U+\xi V+(\eta-\xi) U$.

For the next statement we shall consider not one variable $X$ but a finite independent sequence $\left\{X_{j} ; j=1,2, \ldots\right\}$ such that

$$
\mathscr{L}\left(X_{j}\right)=F_{j}=\left(1-\alpha_{j}\right) G_{j}+\alpha_{j} H_{j}=\mathscr{L}\left[\left(1-\xi_{j}\right) U_{j}+\xi_{j} V_{j}\right]
$$

for independent variables $\left(\xi_{j}, \eta_{j}, U_{j}, V_{j}\right)$.
Proposition 3. Let the variables $X_{j} ; j=1,2, \ldots$ be as just described. Let $\alpha= \sup _{j} \alpha_{j}$. Assume either that each $X_{j}$ admits zero for a median or alternatively that each $U_{j}$ admits zero for a median. Let $S=\sum_{j} X_{j}$ and let $T$ be the sum $T=\sum_{j}\left(1-\eta_{j}\right) U_{j}+\sum_{j} \xi_{j} V_{j}$.

Then the Kolmogorov norm $|\mathscr{L}(S)-\mathscr{L}(T)|_{K}$ satisfies the inequality

$$
|\mathscr{L}(S)-\mathscr{L}(T)|_{K} \leq 13 \alpha^{1 / 3}
$$

Proof. Take a number $\tau \geq 0$. Let $U_{j}=U_{j}^{\prime}+U_{j}^{\prime \prime}$ where $U_{j}^{\prime}=U_{j}$ if $\left|U_{j}\right| \leq \tau$ and where $U_{j}^{\prime}=0$ otherwise. Let $\sigma_{j}^{2}=E\left(U_{j}^{\prime}\right)^{2}$. Then the variance of $\sum\left(\eta_{j}-\xi_{j}\right) U_{j}^{\prime}$ is equal to

$$
\begin{aligned}
E\left[\sum\left(\eta_{j}-\xi_{j}\right) U_{j}^{\prime}\right]^{2} & =2 \sum \alpha_{j}\left(1-\alpha_{j}\right) \sigma_{j}^{2} \\
& \leq 2 \alpha(1-\alpha) \sum \sigma_{j}^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

Also,

$$
\sum P\left[\left(\eta_{j}-\xi_{j}\right) U_{j}^{\prime \prime} \neq 0\right] \leq 2 \alpha(1-\alpha) \sum_{j} P\left[\left|U_{j}\right|>\tau\right]
$$

Thus, using Chebyshev's inequality we can write

$$
P\left\{\left|\sum\left(\eta_{j}-\xi_{j}\right) U_{j}\right|>\tau\right\} \leq 2 \alpha(1-\alpha) D^{2}(\tau)
$$

where $D^{2}(\tau)$ is the quantity

$$
D^{2}(\tau)=\sum_{j} E\left[1 \wedge\left(\frac{U_{j}}{\tau}\right)^{2}\right]
$$

An application of Lemma 2 yields

$$
|\mathscr{L}(S)-\mathscr{L}(T)|_{K} \leq\left(\Gamma_{S}(\tau) \wedge \Gamma_{T}(\tau)\right)+2 \alpha(1-\alpha) D^{2}(\tau)
$$

Thus to obtain the result of the proposition, it will be enough to get appropriate bounds on $\Gamma_{S}$ and $\Gamma_{T}$.

Let

$$
B^{2}(\tau)=\sum_{j} E\left[1 \wedge\left(\frac{X_{j}}{\tau}\right)^{2}\right] .
$$

Then $B^{2}(\tau) \geq(1-\alpha) D^{2}(\tau)$. Also, the concentration of $T$ is smaller than that of $\sum\left(1-\eta_{j}\right) U_{j}$. When the $X_{j}$ have medians equal to zero, Theorem 1 says that

$$
C_{S}(\tau) \leq \frac{2 \sqrt{2 \pi}}{D(\tau) \sqrt{1-\alpha}}
$$

When the $U_{j}$ have median zero, the same inequality holds for $C_{T}$. Suppose then that $2 \alpha D^{2}(0) \leq 13 \alpha^{1 / 3}$. Then $P\left[\sum\left(\xi_{j}-\eta_{j}\right) U_{j} \neq 0\right] \leq 2 \alpha D^{2}(0) \leq 13 \alpha^{1 / 3}$ and the result is acquired. If, on the contrary, $2 \alpha D^{2}(0)>13 \alpha^{1 / 3}$, there is a value $\tau>0$ such that

$$
(1-\alpha)^{3 / 2} D^{3}(\tau)=\frac{1}{\alpha} \sqrt{\frac{\pi}{2}}
$$

This value minimizes the expression

$$
2\left\{\frac{\sqrt{2 \pi}}{D(\tau) \sqrt{1-\alpha}}+\alpha(1-\alpha) D^{2}(\tau)\right\}
$$

which becomes equal to $4\left[2^{1 / 3}+2^{-1 / 3}\right] \pi^{1 / 3} \alpha^{1 / 3}$.
Using this value of $\tau$ in the preceding expressions and evaluating the constants yields the statement of the proposition.

Remark. Here we have assumed either that the $X_{j}$ or that the $U_{j}$ are centered at medians. The first part of the argument does not depend on this at all. The second part can be carried out with a variety of other centerings.

The above argument says in effect that for small $\alpha$ the terms $\sum\left(1-\xi_{j}\right) U_{j}$ and $\sum \xi_{j} V_{j}$ may be dealt with as if they were independent.

The following result concerns the approximation of the distribution of a sum by the accompanying Poisson exponential. Such approximations will be encountered again in Sections 3, 4, and 5. The result is given here because it illustrates repeated applications of the inequalities derived above.

In its statement, product of measures are convolution products and the exponential is the usual convolution exponential.

Proposition 4. Let $N_{j}$ be a probability measure and let $\alpha_{j} \in[0,1]$. Then the Kolmogorov norm satisfies the inequality

$$
\left|\prod_{j}\left[\left(1-\alpha_{j}\right) I+\alpha_{j} N_{j}\right]-\exp \left\{\sum \alpha_{j}\left(N_{j}-I\right)\right\}\right|_{K} \leq C \alpha^{1 / 3},
$$

where $\alpha=\sup \alpha_{j}$ and where $C$ is a coefficient $C \leq 7$.
Proof. The following proof is long, but we do not know of any simpler proof. A more elaborate argument of Zaitsev and Arak gives a bound of the form $C \alpha$. This is a considerable improvement, but the proof is complex. To alleviate the notation, for any finite signed measure $\mu$ we shall denote pois $\mu$ the exponential

$$
\text { pois } \mu=\exp \{\mu-\|\mu\|\} \text {. }
$$

In the present case one can assume that $\alpha$ is so small that the supremum $p$ of the numbers $p_{j}=\alpha_{j}\left(1-\alpha_{j}\right)^{-1}$ is strictly inferior to unity. Then one can write $N_{j}^{\prime}=\left(1-\alpha_{j}\right) I+\alpha_{j} N_{j}=\left(1-\alpha_{j}\right)\left(I+p_{j} N_{j}\right)$ and take logarithms. This introduces the expansions

$$
\begin{aligned}
& A_{j}=\sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \frac{p_{j}^{2 k+1}}{2 k+1} N_{j}^{2 k+1} \\
& B_{j}=\sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \frac{p_{j}^{2 k+2}}{2 k+2} N_{j}^{2 k+2}
\end{aligned}
$$

Let $A=\sum_{j} A_{j}$ and $B=\sum_{j} B_{j}$. Finally, let $N=\prod_{j}\left[\left(1-\alpha_{j}\right) I+\alpha_{j} N_{j}\right]$. Then one can write

$$
N \text { pois } B=\text { pois } A \text {. }
$$

The measure $B$ involves only even powers of the $N_{j}$. We shall first replace them by odd powers. For this purpose, let $\beta_{j}=-\frac{1}{2} \log \left(1-p_{j}^{2}\right)$ and $B^{\prime}= \sum_{j} \beta_{j} N_{j}$. Let

$$
B^{\prime \prime}=\sum_{j} \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \frac{p_{j}^{2 k+2}}{2 k+2} N_{j}^{2 k+1}
$$

We claim that

$$
\mid N \text { pois } B-N \text { pois }\left.\left(B^{\prime}+B^{\prime \prime}\right)\right|_{K} \leq 3 \frac{1}{(2 m)^{1 / 3}}
$$

where $m$ is the integer part of $p^{-1}$.
To prove this, introduce random variables $Z_{i, j, k} ; i=1,2, \ldots ; j=1,2, \ldots ; k= 1,2, \ldots$, which are all independent and such that $\mathscr{L}\left(Z_{i, j, k}\right)=N_{j}$.

Let $\left\{v_{j, k}\right\}$ be independent Poisson variables, independent of the $Z_{i, j, k}$, and such that $E v_{j, k}=p_{j}^{2 k+2} /(2 k+2)$. Let $v_{j}^{\prime}=\sum_{k} v_{j, k}$ and $v_{j}^{\prime \prime}=\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}(2 k+1) v_{j, k}$. Let $X$ and $Y$ be the sums

$$
X=\sum_{j} \sum_{k}^{v_{j}^{\prime}} Z_{1, j, k}, \quad Y=\sum_{j} \sum_{k}^{v_{j}^{\prime \prime}} Z_{2, j, k}
$$

Then $\mathscr{L}(X)=$ pois $\left(B^{\prime}\right), \mathscr{L}(Y)=$ pois $\left(B^{\prime \prime}\right)$, and $\mathscr{L}(X+Y)=$ pois $B$. Of course, $X$ and $Y$ are not independent. Let $S$ and $V$ be two other independent
random variables, independent of the previous ones and such that $\mathscr{L}(S)=N$ and $\mathscr{L}(V)=\mathscr{L}(Y)$. Then one can write

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text { pois } A=N \text { pois } B & =\mathscr{L}[S+X+Y] \\
N \text { pois }\left[B^{\prime}+B^{\prime \prime}\right] & =\mathscr{L}[S+X+V]
\end{aligned}
$$

Note in addition that $B^{\prime \prime} \leq p A$ so that pois $B^{\prime \prime}$ is more concentrated than pois $(p A)$.

Let $m$ be the integer part of $p^{-1}$ and let $q=m^{-1}$. Let $C$ be the concentration function of $V$ and let $\Gamma$ be the modulus of continuity of pois $A$. It follows from Theorem 2 that for all $\tau>0$ one has $\Gamma(\tau) \leq\{m[1-C(\tau-)]\}^{-1 / 2}$.

Take an $\varepsilon>0$ and consider first the case where $1-C(0) \leq \varepsilon$. Then $P\{\mid Y- V \mid \neq 0\} \leq 2 \varepsilon$ and therefore

$$
|\mathscr{L}[S+X+Y]-\mathscr{L}[S+X+V]|_{K} \leq 2 \varepsilon .
$$

If on the contrary $1-C(0)>\varepsilon$ there is a $\tau>0$ such that $1-C(\tau-) \geq \varepsilon$ and $1-C(\tau) \leq \varepsilon$. This gives $\Gamma(\tau) \leq[m \varepsilon]^{-1 / 2}$. Also, there is some $x$ such that $P[|Y-x|>\tau / 2] \leq \varepsilon$ and therefore $P\{|Y-V|>\tau\} \leq 2 \varepsilon$. It follows then from Lemma 2 that

$$
|\mathscr{L}[S+X+Y]-\mathscr{L}[S+X+V]|_{K} \leq 2 \varepsilon+\frac{1}{\sqrt{m \varepsilon}}
$$

Taking $\varepsilon^{3}=(16 m)^{-1}$ one sees that under all circumstances

$$
|\mathscr{L}(S+X+Y)-\mathscr{L}(S+X+V)|_{K} \leq 3\left(\frac{q}{2}\right)^{1 / 3}
$$

with $q=1 / m$.
For the next step, note that both $A$ and $B^{\prime}+B^{\prime \prime}$ involve only odd powers of the measures $N_{j}$. We shall carry out an argument in which powers of the type $N_{j}^{2 k+1}$ are replaced by $(2 k+1) N_{j}$. Since the argument will be applied to $A$ and $B^{\prime \prime}$ it is written in a somewhat more general form.

Lemma 8. Let $m$ be the integer part of $p^{-1}$ and let $m q=1$. Let

$$
\delta(q)=\frac{6}{2^{1 / 3}} q^{2 / 3} \frac{1}{\left(1-q^{2}\right)^{1 / 3}\left(1-q^{2 / 3}\right)}
$$

For each $j$ let $a_{j, k} ; k=0,1,2, \ldots$ be non-negative numbers such that $a_{j, o} p^{2 k} \geq a_{j, k}$ for all $k$. Let $F$ and $G$ be the measures

$$
\begin{aligned}
F & =\sum_{j} \sum_{k}(2 k+1) a_{j, k} N_{j} \\
G & =\sum_{j} \sum_{k} a_{j, k} N_{j}^{2 k+1}
\end{aligned}
$$

Then

$$
\mid \text { pois } F-\text { pois }\left.G\right|_{K} \leq \delta(q)
$$



Proof. Introduce independent variables $Z_{i, j, k}$ such that $\mathscr{L}\left(Z_{i, j, k}\right)=N_{j}$ as before. Let $\left\{\mu_{j, k}\right\}$ be independent Poisson variables such that $E\left(\mu_{j, k}\right)=a_{j, k}$. Let $\mu_{j}=\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}(2 k+1) \mu_{j, k}$. Then pois $G$ is the distribution of $\sum_{j} \sum_{r=1}^{\mu_{j}} Z_{i, j, r}$. The sum $\mu_{j}$ can also be written in the form

$$
\mu_{j}=n_{j, 0}+2 n_{j, 1}+\cdots+2 n_{j, k}+\cdots
$$

with $n_{j, k}=\sum_{r \geq k} \mu_{j, r}$. This $n_{j, k}$ is a Poisson variable with expectation $E n_{j, k}=\sum_{r \geq k} a_{j, r}$.

The distribution pois $G$ can then be written as the distribution of

$$
X_{0}+\left(X_{1}+Y_{1}\right)+\left(X_{2}+Y_{2}\right)+\cdots
$$

where $X_{r}=\sum_{j} \sum_{k}^{n_{j, r}} Z_{1, r, k}$ and $Y_{r}=\sum_{j} \sum_{k}^{n_{j, r}} Z_{2, r, k}$. Consider variables $n_{j, r}^{\prime}$ and $n_{j, r}^{\prime \prime}$ which are independent and independent of all the previous ones and such that $\mathscr{L}\left(n_{j, r}^{\prime}\right)=\mathscr{L}\left(n_{j, r}^{\prime \prime}\right)=\mathscr{L}\left(n_{j, r}\right)$. Define $X_{r}^{\prime}$ and $Y_{r}^{\prime}$ by the formulas

$$
X_{r}^{\prime}=\sum_{j} \sum_{k}^{n_{j . r}^{\prime}} Z_{3, j, k}, \quad Y_{r}^{\prime}=\sum_{j} \sum_{k}^{n_{j . r}^{\prime \prime}} Z_{4, j, k}
$$

Then pois $F$ is the distribution of the sum

$$
X_{0}+\left(X_{1}^{\prime}+Y_{1}^{\prime}\right)+\cdots+\left(X_{r}^{\prime}+Y_{r}^{\prime}\right)+\cdots
$$

For $r>0$ consider the sums $S_{r}$ and $T_{r}$ defined, respectively, by

$$
S_{r}=X_{0}+\sum_{0<k<r}\left(X_{k}^{\prime}+Y_{k}^{\prime}\right)+\sum_{k \geq r}\left(X_{k}+Y_{k}\right)
$$

and

$$
T_{r}=X_{0}+\sum_{0<k \leq r}\left(X_{k}^{\prime}+Y_{k}^{\prime}\right)+\sum_{k>r}\left(X_{k}+Y_{k}\right)
$$

These sums differ only at the $r$ th place and

$$
S_{r}-T_{r}=\left(X_{r}-X_{r}^{\prime}\right)+\left(Y_{r}-Y_{r}^{\prime}\right)
$$

For $r \geq 1$ the variable $X_{r}$ has a distribution pois $L_{r}$ for a measure

$$
L_{r}=\sum_{j} b_{j, r} N_{j}
$$

with

$$
b_{j, r}=\sum_{k \geq r} a_{j, k} \leq a_{j, 0} \sum_{k \geq r} p^{2 k} \leq a_{j, 0} \frac{p^{2 r}}{1-p^{2}}
$$

Also, $\mathscr{L}\left(X_{r}\right)=\mathscr{L}\left(Y_{r}\right)=\mathscr{L}\left(X_{r}^{\prime}\right)=\mathscr{L}\left(Y_{r}^{\prime}\right)$. Let $m_{r}$ be the integer part of $\left(1-p^{2}\right) p^{-2 \boldsymbol{r}}$ 。

It follows from the above that the modulus of continuity $\Gamma_{r}$ of $S_{r}$ is smaller than the modulus of continuity of a sum of $m_{r}$ independent variables which have the same distribution as $X_{r}$.

Let $C_{r}$ be the concentration function of $X_{r}$. Take a number $\varepsilon>0$. If $1-C_{r}(0) \leq \varepsilon$, then $P\left\{\left|X_{r}-X_{r}^{\prime}+Y_{r}-Y_{r}^{\prime}\right| \neq 0\right\} \leq 4 \varepsilon$ and therefore $\mid \mathscr{L}\left(S_{r}\right)- \left.\mathscr{L}\left(T_{r}\right)\right|_{K} \leq 4 \varepsilon$.

If on the contrary $1-C_{r}(0)>\varepsilon$, there is a number $\tau>0$ such that $1- C_{r}(\tau-) \geq \varepsilon$ and $1-C_{r}(\tau) \leq \varepsilon$. There is also some $x$ such that $P\left\{\left|X_{r}-x\right|>\right. \tau / 2\} \leq \varepsilon$ and therefore $P\left\{\left|S_{r}-T_{r}\right|>2 \tau\right\} \leq 4 \varepsilon$.

Applying Theorem 2 and Lemma 2 one concludes that

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left|\mathscr{L}\left(S_{r}\right)-\mathscr{L}\left(T_{r}\right)\right|_{K} & \leq 4 \varepsilon+\Gamma_{r}(2 \tau) \\
& \leq 4 \varepsilon+2\left[m_{r} \varepsilon\right]^{-1 / 2}
\end{aligned}
$$

Now take $\varepsilon^{3}=\left(16 m_{r}\right)^{-1}$. The above inequality becomes

$$
\left|\mathscr{L}\left(S_{r}\right)-\mathscr{L}\left(T_{r}\right)\right|_{K} \leq \frac{6}{\left(2 m_{r}\right)^{1 / 3}}
$$

which is therefore valid in all cases. It follows by addition that

$$
\mid \text { pois } F-\text { pois }\left.G\right|_{K} \leq \frac{6}{2^{1 / 3}} \sum_{r=1}^{\infty}\left(\frac{1}{m_{r}}\right)^{1 / 3}
$$

We have previously used the integer part $m$ of $p^{-1}$. It can easily be verified that $m_{r} \geq m^{2 r}-m^{2 r-2}$. Thus, with $q=1 / m$, one can write

$$
\frac{1}{m_{r}} \leq \frac{1}{1-q^{2}} q^{2 r}
$$

This yields

$$
\mid \text { pois } F-\text { pois }\left.G\right|_{K} \leq \frac{6}{2^{1 / 3}} q^{2 / 3} \frac{1}{\left(1-q^{2}\right)^{1 / 3}\left(1-q^{2 / 3}\right)}
$$

as claimed.
It is now easy to conclude the proof of the proposition. Let $N^{*}=\exp \left\{\sum \alpha_{j}\left(N_{j}-I\right)\right\}$. If one applies the above lemma to pois $A$ one finds that | pois $A-N^{*}$ pois $\left.R\right|_{K} \leq \delta(q)$ for a measure $R=\frac{p^{2}}{1-p^{2}} N$. Similarly

$$
\mid N \text { pois }\left(B^{\prime}+B^{\prime \prime}\right)-N \text { pois }\left.R\right|_{K} \leq \delta(q)
$$

since $B^{\prime \prime}$ can be replaced by the measure $R_{1}$ defined by

$$
R_{1}=\sum_{j}\left(\sum_{k=0}^{\infty} p_{j}^{2 k+2} \frac{2 k+1}{2 k+2}\right) N_{j}
$$

and since $B^{\prime}+R_{1}=R$. It follows that

$$
\mid N \text { pois } R-N^{*} \text { pois }\left.R\right|_{K} \leq 2 \delta(q)+3\left(\frac{q}{2}\right)^{1 / 3}
$$

Now note that the measures $\sum \alpha_{j} N_{j}$ and $R$ satisfy the inequality $n R \leq \sum \alpha_{j} N_{j}$ where $n$ is the largest integer such that $n p \leq 1-p$. That is, $n=m-1$, for $m$ equal to the integer part of $p^{-1}$.

Take a number $\varepsilon>0$. Let $c$ be the concentration function of pois $R$ and let $\Gamma$ be the modulus of continuity of $N^{*}=$ pois $\left\{\sum \alpha_{j} \mathrm{~N}_{j}\right\}$. If $1-c(0) \leq \varepsilon$ then

$$
\left|N-N^{*}\right|_{K} \leq \mid N \text { pois } R-N^{*} \text { pois }\left.R\right|_{K}+2 \varepsilon \text {. }
$$

If on the contrary $1-c(0)>\varepsilon$, there is a $\gamma>0$ such that $1-c(\gamma-) \geq \varepsilon$ and $1-c(\gamma) \leq \varepsilon$. Then Lemma 3 says that

$$
[2 c(\gamma)-1]\left|N-N^{*}\right|_{K} \leq \mid N \text { pois } R-N^{*} \text { pois }\left.R\right|_{K}+\Gamma(\gamma) c(\gamma) .
$$

This yields the inequality

$$
(1-2 \varepsilon)\left|N-N^{*}\right|_{K} \leq 2 \delta(q)+\frac{3}{2}\left(\frac{q}{2}\right)^{1 / 3}+\frac{1}{\sqrt{n \varepsilon}}
$$

where $n=m-1$, with $m q=1$. Taking the value $\varepsilon=(4 n)^{-1 / 3}$ and writing $m / m-1$ as $1 / 1-q$ this gives

$$
(1-2 \varepsilon)\left|N-N^{*}\right|_{K} \leq 2 \delta(q)+3\left(\frac{q}{2}\right)^{1 / 3}+\frac{1}{(1-q)^{1 / 3}}(2 q)^{1 / 3}
$$

in all cases.
To obtain the final result, note that one may assume that $7 \alpha^{1 / 3} \leq 1$ or equivalently $\alpha \leq(343)^{-1}$. This gives a corresponding bound on $q$ and the result stated in the proposition follows by appropriate arithmetic.

As a further illustration of the use of the concentrations inequalities, consider an index $v$ which takes its values in some directed set. For each $v$ let $\left\{X_{v, j} ; j=1,2, \ldots\right\}$ be a sequence of independent identically distributed random variables. Let $n_{v}$ be a nonrandom integer and let $N_{v}$ be an integer valued random variable. It is not assumed that $N_{v}$ is a stopping variable of the $X_{v, j}$. The dependence between $N_{v}$ and the $X_{v, j}$ is left entirely arbitrary.

Proposition 5. Assume that $\mathscr{L}\left(X_{v, j}\right)=\mathscr{L}\left(-X_{v, j}\right)$ for all $j$ and v. Assume also that $n_{v} \rightarrow \infty$ and that $N_{v} / n_{v} \rightarrow 1$ as $v$ tends to infinity. Then the Kolmogorov distance between $\mathscr{L}\left[\sum_{j} X_{v, j} ; j \leq N_{v}\right]$ and $\mathscr{L}\left[\sum_{j} X_{v, j} ; j \leq n_{v}\right]$ tends to zero as $v$ tends to infinity.

Proof. Select a number $\varepsilon \in(0,1)$. Eliminating cases which have a probability tending to zero one may assume that the variable $N_{v}$ satisfies the inequality $n_{v}^{\prime} \leq N_{v} \leq n_{v}^{\prime \prime}$ where $n_{v}^{\prime}$ is the integer part of $n_{v}(1-\varepsilon)+1$ and $n_{v}^{\prime \prime}$ is the integer part of $n_{\nu}(1+\varepsilon)$.

For any integer $n$ let $S_{v}(n)$ be the sum $S_{v}(n)=\left\{\sum_{j} X_{v, j} ; j \leq n\right\}$. Then, by symmetry,

$$
\begin{aligned}
P\left\{\sup _{m}\left[\left|S_{v}(m)-S_{v}\left(n_{v}\right)\right| ; n_{v}^{\prime} \leq m \leq n_{v}^{\prime \prime}\right]>2 \tau\right\} & \leq 2 P\left\{\left|S_{v}\left(n_{v}^{\prime \prime}\right)-S_{v}\left(n_{v}^{\prime}\right)\right|>\tau\right\} \\
& =2 P\left\{\left|S_{v}\left(n_{v}^{\prime \prime}-n_{v}^{\prime}\right)\right|>\tau\right\}
\end{aligned}
$$

Thus,

$$
P\left\{\left|S_{v}\left(N_{v}\right)-S_{v}\left(n_{v}\right)\right|>2 \tau\right\} \leq 2 P\left\{\left|S_{v}\left(n_{v}^{\prime \prime}-n_{v}^{\prime}\right)\right|>\tau\right\} .
$$

Let $q$ be the integer part of $(2 \varepsilon)^{-1}$ and let $Y_{v}$ be a random variable which has the same distribution as $S\left(n_{\nu}^{\prime \prime}-n_{\nu}^{\prime}\right)$. Then, since $n_{\nu}^{\prime \prime}-n_{\nu}^{\prime} \leq 2 \varepsilon n_{\nu}$, the concentration function of $S_{\nu}\left(n_{\nu}\right)$ is smaller than that of a sum of $q$ independent terms distributed as $Y_{v}$.

Thus, by Lemma 2 and Proposition 2

$$
\left|\mathscr{L}\left[S_{v}\left(N_{v}\right)\right]-\mathscr{L}\left[S_{v}\left(n_{v}\right)\right]\right|_{K} \leq 2\left\{\frac{1}{\sqrt{q \beta_{v}(\tau)}}+\beta_{v}(\tau)\right\},
$$

where $\beta_{v}(\tau)=P\left\{\left|Y_{v}\right|>\tau\right\}$. Selecting $\tau$ appropriately, this may be made smaller than a quantity approximately equal to $4 q^{-1 / 3}$. The result follows by letting $\varepsilon$ tend to zero.

The nonsymmetric case is more complicated, but results of the same type have been obtained by H . Wittenberg.

## 3. Compactness and Shift-Compactness

In this section we consider only real valued random variables, even though the arguments extend readily to $\mathbb{R}^{k}$ and, with additional efforts, at least partially to Banach valued variables (see Section 6).

It is convenient to metrize the space of finite signed measures on $\mathbb{R}$ by the dual Lipschitz norm $\|\mu\|_{D}=\sup _{f}\left|\int f d \mu\right|$ where $f$ is allowed to vary through the space of functions which satisfy the conditions $|f| \leq 1,|f(x)-f(y)| \leq|x-y|$.

The same space of finite signed measures can also be topologized by the weak topology induced by the bounded continuous functions. Explicitly, $\mu_{v} \rightarrow \mu_{0}$ if $\int f d \mu_{v} \rightarrow \int f d \mu_{0}$ for every bounded continuous $f$.

It is well known, and easily verified that the weak topology and the topology induced by the dual Lipschitz norm coincide on the space of finite positive measures. This is entirely untrue on the space of signed measures.

It is immediate that a set $S$ of probability measures is relatively compact (for either one of the topologies described here) in the entire space of probability measures if and only if it is "tight." This means that the set is bounded and that for each $\varepsilon>0$ there is a compact $K$ such that $P\left(K^{c}\right)<\varepsilon$ for all $P \in S$.

For signed measures the situation is more complex. A set $S$ is relatively compact in the space of signed measures for the weak topology if and only if it is tight. (See Section 6 for a proof.) However, a set $S$ may be relatively compact for the dual Lipschitz norm without being even bounded.

A related concept is that of shift-compactness. In terms of random variables, this is as follows. Let $A$ be a set. For each $\alpha \in A$ let $X_{\alpha}$ be a random variable.

The set $\left\{\mathscr{L}\left(X_{\alpha}\right): \alpha \in A\right\}$ is called shift-compact if there are numbers $b_{\alpha}$ for which the set $\left\{\mathscr{L}\left(X_{\alpha}-b_{\alpha}\right) ; \alpha \in A\right\}$ is relatively compact. One says also that the set $\left\{X_{\alpha} ; \alpha \in A\right\}$ is shift-compact, even though this is an abuse of language. With these definitions, one can obtain results as follows.

Let $A$ be an arbitrary set. For each $\alpha \in A$ let $X_{\alpha, j} ; j=1,2, \ldots$ be a finite sequence of random variables. Let $P_{\alpha, j}=\mathscr{L}\left(X_{\alpha, j}\right)$ and let $I$ be the probability measure concentrated at the origin of the line.

Lemma 1. Let $M_{\alpha}$ be the signed measure $M_{\alpha}=\sum_{j}\left(P_{\alpha, j}-I\right)$. If the set $\left\{M_{\alpha} ; \alpha \in A\right\}$ is tight, then so is the set $\left\{\mathscr{L}\left(\sum_{j} X_{\alpha, j}\right) ; \alpha \in A\right\}$.

Proof. Select an $\varepsilon>0$. Then there is a number $b$ such that $M_{\alpha}\left\{[-b, b]^{c}\right\}<\varepsilon$ for all $\alpha$. Also, there is some number $m$ such that $\sup _{\alpha}\left\|M_{\alpha}\right\| \leq m$. Let $N_{\alpha}$ be the number of indices $j$ such that $X_{\alpha, j} \neq 0$. Then $E N_{\alpha} \leq m$. It follows that $P\left\{\Sigma\left|X_{\alpha, j}\right| \geq m b / \varepsilon\right\}<2 \varepsilon$. This implies the desired result.

For the next assertion, consider a sequence of independent random variables $X_{n, j} ; j=1,2, \ldots ; n=1,2, \ldots$, and let $S_{n}=\sum_{j} X_{n, j}$. For each $(n, j)$ let $m_{n, j}$ be a number which will be used as centering constant. Let $Y_{n, j}=X_{n, j}-m_{n, j}$.

If $\tau>0$, let $a_{n, j}(\tau, m)=E Y_{n, j} I\left[\left|Y_{n, j}\right| \leq \tau\right]$ and

$$
D_{n}^{2}(\tau, m)=\sum_{j} E\left\{1 \wedge\left(\frac{Y_{n, j}}{\tau}\right)^{2}\right\}
$$

Finally let $M_{n}(\tau, m)$ be the restriction to the set $(-\tau,+\tau)^{c}$ of the sum of measures $\sum_{j} \mathscr{L}\left(Y_{n, j}\right)$ and let $\mu_{n, j}$ be a median of $X_{n, j}$. (In this notation, the occurrence of the symbol $m$ in $a_{n, j}(\tau, m)$ and the other entities is meant to indicate that they depend on the double sequence $\left\{m_{n, j}\right\}$.)

Proposition 1. The sum $S_{n}=\sum_{j} X_{n, j}$ is shift compact if and only if there are centering constants $m_{n, j}$ such that
(i) for some $\tau>0$ (and thus for all!) one has $\sup _{n} D_{n}^{2}(\tau, m)<\infty$;
(ii) for some $\tau>0$ (and thus for all!), the sequence of measures $\left\{M_{n}(\tau, m)\right\}$ is relatively compact on the line.

If (i) and (ii) hold for some choice of the $m_{n, j}$, the sequences for which they hold are precisely those for which $\sup _{n} \sum\left|m_{n, j}-\mu_{n, j}\right|^{2}<\infty$. Finally, if (i) and
(ii) hold, the sequence $\left\{\mathscr{L}\left(S_{n}\right)\right\}$ is relatively compact if and only if
(iii) for some $\tau>0$ (and thus for all!), the sequence of numbers $\sum_{j}\left[m_{n, j}+a_{n, j}(\tau, m)\right]$ is relatively compact.

Proof. To show that the conditions are sufficient, let $\left(\xi_{n, j}, U_{n, j}, V_{n, j}\right)$ be independent variables such that
(a) $\mathscr{L}\left(U_{n, j}\right)=\mathscr{L}\left\{Y_{n, j}| | Y_{n, j} \mid \leq \tau\right\}$,
(b) $\mathscr{L}\left(V_{n, j}\right)=\mathscr{L}\left\{Y_{n, j}| | Y_{n, j} \mid>\tau\right\}$,
(c) $\alpha_{n, j}=P\left[\xi_{n, j}=1\right]=1-P\left[\xi_{n, j}=0\right]=P\left[\left|Y_{n, j}\right|>\tau\right]$.

Then $Y_{n, j}$ has the same distribution as $\left(1-\xi_{n, j}\right) U_{n, j}+\xi_{n, j} V_{n, j}$. Let $S_{n}^{\prime}$ be the $\operatorname{sum} S_{n}^{\prime}=\sum_{j}\left(1-\xi_{n, j}\right) U_{n, j}$ and let $S_{n}^{\prime \prime}=\sum_{j} \xi_{n, j} V_{n, j}$.

When condition (ii) is satisfied, the sums $\sum_{j}\left[\mathscr{L}\left(\xi_{n, j} V_{n, j}\right)-I\right]$ form a tight set. Thus, by Lemma 1, the same is true of the sequence $\left\{\mathscr{L}\left(S_{n}^{\prime \prime}\right)\right\}$. If (i) holds, the variance of $S_{n}^{\prime}$ is bounded independently of $n$. Thus $\left\{S_{n}^{\prime}\right\}$ is shift compact. Therefore the combination (i)-(ii) implies the shift-compactness of $S_{n}^{\prime}+S_{n}^{\prime \prime}$ and the relative compactness of the distributions of $S_{n}^{\prime}+S_{n}^{\prime \prime}-E S_{n}^{\prime}$. However $E S_{n}^{\prime}=\sum_{j} a_{n, j}(\tau, m)$. Returning to $\sum_{j} X_{n, j}=\sum_{j} m_{n, j}+\sum Y_{n, j}$ gives assertion (iii) of the proposition.

The "thus for all" statements in (i) and (ii) follow from the remark that $D_{n}(\tau, m)$ is a decreasing function of $\tau$ but $\tau D_{n}(\tau, m)$ is an increasing function of $\tau$. The "thus for all" in statement (iii) is then a consequence of the argument given above for $S_{n}^{\prime}$.

To prove the necessity of the conditions, take centering $m_{n, j}=\mu_{n, j}$, a median of $X_{n, j}$. Let $Y_{n, j}^{\prime}$ be independent copies of the $Y_{n, j}=X_{n, j}-\mu_{n, j}$ and let $X_{n, j}^{s}=Y_{n, j}-Y_{n, j}^{\prime}$. Let $S_{n}^{s}=\sum_{j} X_{n, j}^{s}$. It is readily verifiable that $S_{n}$ is shift-compact if and only if the distributions $\mathscr{L}\left(S_{n}^{s}\right)$ form a relatively compact sequence. Also, since the $Y_{n, j}$ have medians zero,

$$
2 P\left\{\left|X_{n, j}^{s}\right|>t\right\} \geq P\left[\left|Y_{n, j}\right|>t\right]
$$

for all $t$.
Thus

$$
D_{n}^{2}(\tau)=\sum_{j} E 1 \wedge\left(\frac{X_{n, j}^{s}}{\tau}\right)^{2} \geq \frac{1}{2} D_{n}^{2}(\tau, \mu)
$$

Since $C_{S_{n}}^{2}(\tau) \leq 4 \pi D_{n}^{-2}(\tau)$, condition (i) is clearly necessary.
To prove the necessity of (ii) it is enough to show that for each $\varepsilon>0$ there is a number $b<\infty$ such that $\sup _{n} \sum_{j} P\left[\left|X_{n, j}^{s}\right|>b\right]<\varepsilon$. For this purpose, let $P_{n}=\mathscr{L}\left[\sum_{j}\left(X_{n, j}^{s}\right)\right]$ and let $P_{n}^{k}$ be the convolution of $k$ copies of $P_{n}$. Then, for any fixed $k$, the sequence $\left\{P_{n}^{k}\right\}$ will also be relatively compact. Take $k$ such that $\varepsilon k>4$ and let $b$ be such that $P_{n}^{k}\{[-b,+b]\} \geq \frac{1}{2}$.

According to the concentration inequality given in Theorem 2, Section 2, for the symmetric case, this implies

$$
\frac{1}{4} \leq\left[P_{n}^{k}\{[-b,+b]\}\right]^{2} \leq\left\{k \sum_{j} P\left[\left|X_{n, j}^{s}\right|>b\right]\right\}^{-1}
$$

hence $\sum_{j} P\left[\left|X_{n, j}^{s}\right|>b\right] \leq 4 / k<\varepsilon$.
To complete the proof of the proposition it remains to show that in the shiftcompact case the centerings $m_{n, j}$ will give (i) and (ii) if and only if $\sum\left|m_{n, j}-\mu_{n, j}\right|^{2}$ remains bounded. This can easily be checked using the properties of the function $1 \wedge x^{2}$ and will be left to the reader.
(Note that the compactness condition (ii) would be satisfied if $\sup _{n} \mid m_{n, j}- \mu_{n, j} \mid<\infty$; however, condition (i) gives a stronger requirement.)

Remark. The choice of centering constants $m_{n, j}$ is still left fairly arbitrary in the preceding arguments. It is often convenient to select constants in the following manner.

Suppose that all the medians $\mu_{n, j}$ satisfy the condition $\left|\mu_{n, j}\right|<c$ for some fixed constant $c$. Let $u$ be the indicator of an interval, or set, which contains the interval $[-2 c,+2 c]$ and is contained in an interval $\left[-c_{1}, c_{1}\right], c_{1}>2 c$.

Define $m_{n, j},\left|m_{n, j}\right| \leq c_{1}$ as the conditional expectation such that

$$
m_{n, j} E u\left(X_{n, j}\right)=E X_{n, j} u\left(X_{n, j}\right) .
$$

For $\tau>2 c_{1}$ one has

$$
E \tau^{2} \wedge\left(X_{n, j}-\mu_{n, j}\right)^{2} \geq E\left(X_{n, j}-m_{n, j}\right)^{2} u\left(X_{n, j}\right)
$$

Thus, if the conditions (i) and (ii) are satisfied for the medians $\mu_{n, j}$, they are also satisfied for the constants $m_{n, j}$ defined here.

In the proof of the foregoing Proposition 1, we have used a particular splitting of the random variables $X_{n, j}$, separating out the parts where $\left|Y_{n, j}\right|>\tau$ from those where $\left|Y_{n, j}\right| \leq \tau$. It is often convenient to consider more general splittings, for instance, those of the type $X=X I_{A}+X\left(1-I_{A}\right)$, described before Proposition 3, Section 2. Our next result refers to such general splittings.

Consider a double sequence $\left\{X_{n, j}\right\} ; j=1,2, \ldots$ and $n=1,2, \ldots$ where for each $n$ the variables $X_{n, j} ; j=1,2, \ldots$ are independent. Suppose that $X_{n, j}$ is written in the form

$$
X_{n, j}=\left(1-\xi_{n, j}\right) U_{n, j}+\xi_{n, j} V_{n, j}
$$

where (i) for a given $n$ all the variables $\xi_{n, j}, U_{n, j}, V_{n, j}, j=1,2, \ldots$ are independent, and (ii) $P\left[\xi_{n, j}=1\right]=1-P\left[\xi_{n, j}=0\right]=\alpha_{n, j}$.

Let $\mu_{n, j}$ be a median of $X_{n, j}$. Define other numbers $m_{n, j}$ as follows. Select a number $\varepsilon_{0} \in(0,1)$. If $\alpha_{n, j}<\varepsilon_{0}$ and $1-\alpha_{n, j} \geq \varepsilon_{0}$, let $m_{n, j}$ be a median of $U_{n, j}$.
