If $\alpha_{n, j} \geq \varepsilon_{0}$ but $1-\alpha_{n, j}<\varepsilon_{0}$, let $m_{n, j}$ be a median of $V_{n, j}$. If $\alpha_{n, j} \geq \varepsilon_{0}$ and $1-\alpha_{n, j} \geq \varepsilon_{0}$, select in an arbitrary way either a median of $U_{n, j}$ or a median of $V_{n, j}$ and call it $m_{n, j}$. Consider the following sums:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{1, n} & =\sum_{j}\left(1-\xi_{n, j}\right)\left(U_{n, j}-m_{n, j}\right) \\
S_{2, n} & =\sum_{j} \xi_{n, j}\left(V_{n, j}-m_{n, j}\right) \\
T_{1, n} & =\sum_{j}\left(1-\xi_{n, j}\right)\left(U_{n, j}-\mu_{n, j}\right) \\
T_{2, n} & =\sum_{j} \xi_{n, j}\left(V_{n, j}-\mu_{n, j}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

Proposition 2. Let $S_{n}=\sum_{j} X_{n, j}$. If the sequence $\left\{S_{n}\right\}$ is shift-compact then all the sequences $\left\{S_{i, n}\right\}$ and $\left\{T_{i, n}\right\}, i=1,2$ are also shift-compact.

Proof. According to Proposition 1, the shift-compactness of $\left\{S_{n}\right\}$ is reflected by inequalities on the measures $M_{n}=\sum_{j} \mathscr{L}\left(X_{n, j}-\mu_{n, j}\right)$. Since $\Sigma_{j} \mathscr{L}\left[\left(1-\xi_{n, j}\right)\left(U_{n, j}-\mu_{n, j}\right)\right] \leq M_{n}$, the shift compactness of $S_{n}$ implies that of the $T_{i, n}$.

For the sums $S_{i, n}$ it is clearly sufficient to prove the result assuming that $\left(1-\alpha_{n, j}\right) \geq \varepsilon_{0}$ and that $m_{n, j}$ is a median of $U_{n, j}$. The general case reduces to this by splitting the index set of the summation into two disjoint sets, according to whether $m_{n, j}$ is a median of $U_{n, j}$ or of $V_{n, j}$.

Assuming this reduction done, let $F_{n, j}=\mathscr{L}\left(X_{n, j}\right)$ and write $F_{n, j}=\left(1-\alpha_{n, j}\right) G_{n, j}+ \alpha_{n, j} H_{n, j}$. Let $F_{n, j}^{\prime}=\mathscr{L}\left(-X_{n, j}\right)$ and similarly for $G_{n, j}^{\prime}$ and $H_{n, j}^{\prime}$. Then one can write

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{n, j} F_{n, j}^{\prime}= & \left(1-\alpha_{n, j}\right)^{2} G_{n, j} G_{n, j}^{\prime}+\alpha_{n, j}\left(1-\alpha_{n, j}\right)\left[G_{n, j} H_{n, j}^{\prime}+G_{n, j}^{\prime} H_{n, j}\right] \\
& +\alpha_{n, j}^{2} H_{n, j} H_{n, j}^{\prime} \\
\geq & \varepsilon_{0}^{2} G_{n, j} G_{n, j}^{\prime}
\end{aligned}
$$

It follows then from the proof of Proposition 1 that the shift-compactness of $\left\{S_{n}\right\}$ implies the shift-compactness of $\sum_{j}\left(U_{n, j}-m_{n, j}\right)$.

This in turn implies the shift-compactness of $\sum_{j}\left(1-\xi_{n, j}\right)\left[U_{n, j}-m_{n, j}\right]$, because the $U_{n, j}-m_{n, j}$ must satisfy conditions (i) and (ii) of Proposition 1 and because

$$
\mathscr{L}\left[\left(1-\xi_{n, j}\right)\left(U_{n, j}-m_{n, j}\right)\right]-I \leq \mathscr{L}\left(U_{n, j}-m_{n, j}\right)-I
$$

This completes the proof of the Proposition.
Remark. The proposition can be supplemented by the observation that if $\sup _{j}\left[\alpha_{n, j}\left(1-\alpha_{n, j}\right)\right] \rightarrow 0$ then the Prohorov distance between $\mathscr{L}\left(T_{1, n}, T_{2, n}\right)$ and the product measure $\mathscr{L}\left(T_{1, n}\right) \otimes \mathscr{L}\left(T_{2, n}\right)$ tends to zero and similarly for $\mathscr{L}\left(S_{1, n}, S_{2, n}\right)$ and $\mathscr{L}\left(S_{1, n}\right) \otimes \mathscr{L}\left(S_{2, n}\right)$. This can be seen by the evaluation which begins the proof of Proposition 3, Section 2.

## 4. Poisson Exponentials and Approximation Theorems

Let $\mu$ be a finite signed measure on the line. One can define the convolution exponential $e^{\mu}=\exp \{\mu\}$ by the usual series expansion $\sum \mu^{n} / n!$, where $\mu^{n}$ is the $n$th convolution power of $\mu$.

Let $I$ be the probability measure which gives mass unity to the origin. The exponential $\exp \{P-I\}$ has the following interpretation. Take independent variables $\left\{N, X_{j} ; j=1,2, \ldots\right\}$ such that $N$ is Poisson with $E N=1$ and such that $\mathscr{L}\left(X_{j}\right)=P$. Let $\sum^{N} X_{j}$ be equal to zero if $N=0$ and to the sum of the first $N$ of the $X_{j}$ if $N \geq 1$. Then $\exp \{P-I\}$ is the distribution of $\sum^{N} X_{j}$.

The main observation leading to the central limit theorem is that if $P-I$ is "small" then the difference between $\exp \{P-I\}$ and $P=I+(P-I)$ is of
smaller order. Thus one may expect to be able to approximate a convolution product $\Pi P_{j}$ by the exponential $\exp \left\{\sum\left(P_{j}-I\right)\right\}$.

A most natural interpretation of the requirement that $P-I$ be small is that the $L_{1}$-norm $\|P-I\|$ itself is small. If $P=\mathscr{L}(X)$ then $\|P-I\|$ is equal to $2 \operatorname{Pr}[X \neq 0]$.

Consider then a variable $\xi$ which is such that $\operatorname{Pr}[\xi=1]=1-\operatorname{Pr}[\xi= 0]=\alpha$ and a variable $v$ which is a Poisson variable with expectation $\alpha$. The minimum of the measures $\mathscr{L}(\xi)$ and $\mathscr{L}(v)$ is the measure which gives mass $(1-\alpha)$ to the origin and mass $\alpha e^{-\alpha}$ to the point unity. Thus $\|\mathscr{L}(\xi)-\mathscr{L}(v)\|= 2 \alpha\left(1-e^{-\alpha}\right)$ and one can easily construct a joint distribution for a pair $(\xi, v)$ with marginals $\mathscr{L}(\xi)$ and $\mathscr{L}(v)$ but such that $\operatorname{Pr}[\xi \neq v]=\alpha\left(1-e^{-\alpha}\right) \leq \alpha^{2}$. It follows immediately that if $\|P-I\|=2 \alpha$ then $\|\exp \{P-I\}-P\| \leq 2 \alpha^{2}$.

Taking products leads to the following proposition. It has been stated here for a semigroup instead of the line, since we shall need it later for the additive semigroup $[-\infty,+\infty)$.

Let $\mathscr{S}$ be an abelian semigroup noted additively. Assume that $\mathscr{S}$ carries a $\sigma$-field $\mathscr{A}$ for which addition is a measurable operation. Take all distributions on $\mathscr{S}$ as measures defined on $\mathscr{A}$.

Let $X_{j} ; j=1,2, \ldots, n$ be independent random variables with values in $\mathscr{S}$. Let $P_{j}=\mathscr{L}\left(X_{j}\right)$. Let $P=\Pi P_{j}$ be the convolution product of the $P_{j}$ and let $Q$ be the convolution exponential $Q=\exp \left\{\sum\left(P_{j}-I\right)\right\}$, where $I$ is the probability measure giving mass unity to the neutral element 0 of $\mathscr{S}$.

Proposition 1. In the situation just described let $\alpha_{j}=\operatorname{Pr}\left[X_{j} \neq 0\right]$. Then the $L_{1}$-norm $\|P-Q\|$ satisfies the inequality

$$
\|P-Q\| \leq 2\left\{1-\Pi\left[1-\alpha_{j}\left(1-e^{-\alpha_{j}}\right)\right]\right\} \leq 2 \sum \alpha_{j}^{2}
$$

Proof.The following argument is not the simplest possible, but it is rather elementary.

One may write $P_{j}=I+\alpha_{j}\left(M_{j}-I\right)$ where $M_{j}$ is the conditional distribution of $X_{j}$ given that $X_{j} \neq 0$. Then $P_{j}=\mathscr{L}\left(\xi_{j} V_{j}\right)$ where $\xi_{j}$ and $V_{j}$ are independent variables such that $\mathscr{L}\left(V_{j}\right)=M_{j}$ and such that $P\left[\xi_{j}=1\right]=1-P\left[\xi_{j}=0\right]=\alpha_{j}$. The distribution $\exp \left\{P_{j}-I\right\}=\exp \left\{\alpha_{j}\left(M_{j}-I\right)\right\}$ is the distribution of a sum $\sum_{i}{ }^{j_{j}} V_{j, i}$ where $v_{j}$ has a Poisson distribution with $E v_{j}=\alpha_{j}$ and where the $V_{j, i}$ are independent copies of $V_{j}$.

Pair the variables $\xi_{j}$ and $v_{j}$ so that $P\left[\xi_{j} \neq v_{j}\right]=\alpha_{j}\left(1-e^{-\alpha_{j}}\right)$. Then $\sum_{j} \xi_{j} V_{j, 1}$ and $\sum_{j} \sum_{i}^{\nu_{j}} V_{j, i}$ differ only if at least one $\xi_{j}$ differs from the corresponding $\nu_{j}$. This yields

$$
\begin{aligned}
P\left[\sum_{j} \xi_{j} V_{j, 1} \neq \sum_{j} \sum_{i}^{\nu_{j}} V_{j, i}\right] & \leq 1-\prod_{j}\left[1-\alpha_{j}\left(1-e^{-\alpha_{j}}\right)\right] \\
& \leq \sum \alpha_{j}^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

and gives the desired result.

To handle a more general case, for variables $X_{j}$ which rarely differ much from zero, we shall use characteristic functions. There are other possibilities. However, the reader should note that most if not all of them rely on the elementary observation that if $\left\{X_{j}, Y_{j} ; j=1,2, \ldots, n\right\}$ are random variables such that $S_{n}=\sum X_{j}$ and $T_{n}=\sum Y_{j}$, then for any function $f$ one can write

$$
\begin{aligned}
E f\left(S_{n}\right)-E f\left(T_{n}\right) & =E \sum_{k}\left[f\left(U_{k}+X_{k}\right)-f\left(U_{k}+Y_{k}\right)\right] \\
\text { with } U_{k} & =\left(\sum_{j<k} X_{j}\right)+\left(\sum_{j>k} Y_{j}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

The elementary proof of Lindeberg uses functions $f$ which are three times differentiable, with bounded third derivatives. It has the merit of being applicable directly for Hilbert space valued variables ( $X_{j}, Y_{j}$ ). It also applies readily to martingale differences.

The proof given below, intended for the line, uses functions $f$ of the form $f(x)=\exp (i t x)$. One can also couch that kind of proof in terms of semigroups and Markov operators. However, contrary to some opinions expressed in the literature, the proofs are not different in essence.

The guiding remark concerning the distributions $\mathscr{L}\left(X_{j}\right)=P_{j}$ and $\mathscr{L}\left(Y_{j}\right)= \exp \left(P_{j}-I\right)$ is as follows. Assume that $X_{j}$ has moments up to and including the fourth moments. Assume also that $E X_{j}=0$. Then $E X_{j}^{k}=E Y_{j}^{k}$ for $k=1$, 2,3, and $E Y_{j}^{4}=E X_{j}^{4}+3\left(E X_{j}^{2}\right)^{2}$. Also, if $\phi_{j}$ is the characteristic function of $X_{j}$ and $\psi_{j}=\exp \left(\phi_{j}-1\right)$ that of $Y_{j}$, then $\left|\psi_{j}-\phi_{j}\right| \leq \frac{1}{2}\left|\phi_{j}-1\right|^{2}$. Thus, in some ways, $P_{j}$ and $\exp \left(P_{j}-I\right)$ are "close to each other." This is exploited in the following argument.

Consider a finite sequence $\left\{X_{j} ; j=1,2, \ldots\right\}$ of independent random variables subject to the following restrictions.
(A) The distribution $L_{j}$ of $X_{j}$ is a convex combination $L_{j}=\left(1-\alpha_{j}\right) M_{j}+\alpha_{j} N_{j}$ of two probability measures $M_{j}$ and $N_{j}$.
(B) There is a number $b \geq 0$ such that $M_{j}[-b,+b]=1$ and $\int x M_{j}(d x)=0$.

To state the next results, it will be convenient to introduce the following notation.



$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{j}^{\prime} & =\alpha_{j} I+\left(1-\alpha_{j}\right) M_{j} \quad \text { and } \quad N_{j}^{\prime}=\left(1-\alpha_{j}\right) I+\alpha_{j} N_{j} \\
P & =\prod_{j} L_{j}=\mathscr{L}\left(\sum X_{j}\right) \\
Q & =\exp \left\{\sum_{j}\left(L_{j}-I\right)\right\} \\
P^{\prime} & =\prod_{j} M_{j}^{\prime} N_{j}^{\prime} \\
Q^{*} & =\left(\prod_{j} N_{j}^{\prime}\right) \exp \left\{\sum_{j}\left(M_{j}^{\prime}-I\right)\right\} \\
\sigma_{j}^{2} & =\int x^{2} M_{j}^{\prime}(d x)=\left(1-\alpha_{j}\right) \int x^{2} M_{j}(d x) \\
& \quad s^{2}=\sum_{j} \sigma_{j}^{2} \quad \text { and } \quad \delta=\left(\sum \sigma_{j}^{4}\right) s^{-4} \\
& \quad \alpha=\sup _{j} \alpha_{j} .
\end{aligned}
$$

As explained in Section 2, the variable $X_{j}$ has the same distribution as $\left(1-\xi_{j}\right) U_{j}+\xi_{j} V_{j}=\left(1-\eta_{j}\right) U_{j}+\xi_{j} V_{j}+\left(\eta_{j}-\xi_{j}\right) U_{j}$, where the $\left(\xi_{j}, \eta_{j}, U_{j}, V_{j}\right)$ are independent, such that $\mathscr{L}\left(U_{j}\right)=M_{j}, \mathscr{L}\left(V_{j}\right)=N_{j}$ and the $\xi_{j}$ and $\eta_{j}$ are the appropriate Bernoulli variables. In this notation, $P$ is the distribution of $\left[\sum\left(1-\xi_{j}\right) U_{j}\right]+\left[\sum \xi_{j} V_{j}\right]$. The measure $P^{\prime}$ is the distribution of $\left[\sum\left(1-\eta_{j}\right) U_{j}\right]+$ [ $\sum \xi_{j} V_{j}$ ]. The measure $Q^{*}$ exponentiates the $M_{j}$ but not the $N_{j}$.

The coefficient $\delta$ is some sort of measure of relative disparity of the $\sigma_{j}^{2}$. It may be written

$$
\delta=\sum_{j}\left(\frac{\sigma_{j}^{2}}{s^{2}}\right)\left(\frac{\sigma_{j}^{2}}{s^{2}}\right)
$$

and is therefore a weighted average of the ratios $\sigma_{j}^{2} / s^{2}$. If all the $\sigma_{j}^{2}$ were equal, and the summation extended for $j=1,2, \ldots, n$, then $\delta$ would be $(1 / n)$.

We shall need also a smoothing operation. For it, let $\tau$ be a non-negative number and let $G$ be the probability measure whose characteristic function $\widetilde{G}$ is equal to

$$
\widetilde{G}(t)=[1-b|t|]^{+} \exp \left\{-\frac{\tau^{2} t^{2}}{3}\right\}
$$

Proposition 2. Assume that the variables $X_{j}$ satisfy conditions ( $A$ ) and ( $B$ ). Then the Kolmogorov norms satisfy the inequalities
(i) $\left|P G-P^{\prime} G\right|_{K} \leq \frac{20}{7 \pi} K \alpha$,
(ii) $\left|P^{\prime} G-Q^{*} G\right|_{K} \leq \frac{10}{7 \pi} K^{2} \delta$,
(iii) $|P G-Q G|_{K} \leq\left|Q G-Q^{*} G\right|_{K}+\frac{10}{7 \pi}\left[2 K \alpha+K^{2} \delta\right]$,
where $K$ is the coefficient defined by

$$
s^{2}=K\left(s^{2}+\tau^{2}\right)
$$

Proof. The third inequality is a consequence of the other two. To prove these, let $F_{j}(\lambda)$ be the measure

$$
F_{j}(\lambda)=\left[\lambda I+(1-\lambda) M_{j}^{\prime}\right] \exp \left\{\lambda\left(M_{j}^{\prime}-I\right)\right\}
$$

for $\lambda \in[0,1]$. The derivative of $F_{j}$ with respect to $\lambda$ is equal to ( $1- \lambda)\left(M_{j}^{\prime}-I\right)^{2} \exp \left\{\lambda\left(M_{j}^{\prime}-I\right)\right\}$. Thus, by Taylor's formula

$$
Q^{*}-P^{\prime}=\left(\prod_{j} N_{j}^{\prime}\right) \int_{0}^{1}(1-\lambda)\left[\sum_{k} S_{k}(\lambda)\left(M_{k}^{\prime}-I\right)^{2}\right] d \lambda
$$

where $S_{k}(\lambda)$ is the measure

$$
S_{k}(\lambda)=\left[\prod_{j \neq k} F_{j}(\lambda)\right] \exp \left\{\lambda\left(M_{k}^{\prime}-I\right)\right\}
$$

Similarly the difference $P^{\prime}-P$ can be written in the form

$$
P^{\prime}-P=\sum_{k} \alpha_{k}\left(1-\alpha_{k}\right) R_{k}\left(M_{k}-I\right)\left(N_{k}-I\right)
$$

for a probability measure $R_{k}$ defined by

$$
R_{k}=\left[\prod_{j<k} L_{j}\right]\left[\prod_{j>k}\left(M_{j}^{\prime} N_{j}^{\prime}\right)\right]
$$

In these expressions measures can be replaced by their Fourier transforms. For any measure $m$ let $\tilde{m}$ be its Fourier transform and note the following inequalities, obtainable by Taylor expansions.

First,

$$
\tilde{M}_{j}^{\prime}(t)=1-\frac{1}{2} \sigma_{j}^{2} t^{2}\left[1+\frac{1}{3}|b t| \varepsilon(t)\right]
$$

for some complex number $\varepsilon(t)$ such that $|\varepsilon(t)| \leq 1$. For all values $t$ such that $|b t| \leq 1$, this implies

$$
\left|\tilde{M}_{j}^{\prime}(t)\right| \leq 1-\frac{1}{3} \sigma_{j}^{2} t^{2} \text { and }\left|\tilde{M}_{j}^{\prime}(t)-1\right| \leq \frac{2}{3} \sigma_{j}^{2} t^{2}
$$

In the same range of values of $t$, both $R_{k}$ and $S_{k}(\lambda)$ have Fourier transforms inferior in modulus to

$$
\exp \left\{-\frac{1}{3} \sum_{j \neq k} \sigma_{j}^{2} t^{2}\right\} \leq e^{1 / 3} \exp \left\{-\frac{s^{2} t^{2}}{3}\right\}
$$

This yields

$$
\left|\widetilde{P}(t)-\widetilde{P}^{\prime}(t)\right| \leq \frac{40}{21} \alpha s^{2} t^{2} \exp \left\{-\frac{1}{3} s^{2} t^{2}\right\}
$$

and

$$
\left|\tilde{P}^{\prime}(t)-\tilde{Q}^{*}(t)\right| \leq \frac{20}{63} \delta s^{4} t^{4} \exp \left\{-\frac{1}{3} s^{2} t^{2}\right\}
$$

Since the Fourier transform of the measures $P G, P^{\prime} G$, and $Q^{*} G$ are all integrable, one can apply to them the ordinary Fourier inversion formula. This yields

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left|P G-P^{\prime} G\right|_{K} & \leq \frac{40}{21 \pi} \alpha \int_{0}^{\infty}\left(s^{2} t^{2}\right) \exp \left\{-\frac{1}{3}\left(s^{2}+\tau^{2}\right) t^{2}\right\} \frac{d t}{t} \\
& =\frac{20}{7 \pi} \alpha K
\end{aligned}
$$

Similarly,

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left|P^{\prime} G-Q^{*} G\right|_{K} & \leq \frac{20}{63 \pi} \delta \int_{0}^{\infty}\left(s^{4} t^{4}\right) \exp \left\{-\frac{1}{3}\left(s^{2}+\tau^{2}\right) t^{2}\right\} \frac{d t}{t} \\
& =\frac{10}{7 \pi} \alpha K^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

This yields the result as stated.
There is a variant of the above proposition in which one replaces the Poisson exponentials $\exp \left\{M_{j}^{\prime}-I\right\}$ by Gaussian measures. To state it, let $\Psi_{j}$ be the Gaussian measure with mean zero and variance $\sigma_{j}^{2}$. Let $\Psi$ be the convolution product of the $\Psi_{j}$ so that $\Psi=\mathscr{N}\left(0, s^{2}\right), s^{2}=\sum \sigma_{j}^{2}$. Let $\Phi$ be the measure $\Psi \prod_{j} N_{j}^{\prime}$.

Proposition 3. Let the variables $X_{j}$ satisfy conditions ( $A$ ) and ( $B$ ). Then

$$
|P G-\Phi G|_{K} \leq \frac{20}{7 \pi} K \alpha+\frac{1}{3} K \frac{b}{\sqrt{s^{2}+\tau^{2}}}
$$

Proof. The proof is the same as that of Proposition 2 except that now we have to bound the difference between $P^{\prime}$ and $\Phi$ instead of the difference between $P^{\prime}$ and $Q^{*}$.

A Taylor expansion argument shows that the Fourier transforms satisfy the inequality

$$
\left|\tilde{M}_{j}^{\prime}(t)-\tilde{\psi}_{j}(t)\right| \leq \frac{1}{6}\left[b+\frac{4}{\sqrt{2 \pi}} \sigma_{j}\right] \sigma_{j}^{2}|t|^{3}
$$

in which $\sigma_{j}$ itself does not exceed $b$. Thus, in the range where $|b t| \leq 1$ one may write

$$
\left|\tilde{P}^{\prime}(t)-\tilde{\Phi}(t)\right| \leq \frac{1}{6}\left(1+\frac{4}{\sqrt{2 \pi}}\right) b s^{2}|t|^{3} \exp \left\{-\frac{1}{3} s^{2} t^{2}\right\}
$$

Applying the Fourier inversion formula one obtains

$$
\left|P^{\prime} G-\Phi G\right|_{K} \leq \frac{1}{3} K \frac{b}{\sqrt{s^{2}+\tau^{2}}}
$$

This gives the result with the coefficient $K=s^{2} / s^{2}+\tau^{2}$ as before Note that in this expression it will not be possible to insure that $b / \sqrt{s^{2}+\tau^{2}}$ is small for small $\tau$ unless $b / s$ is already small. It will be shown later that this is closely related to the necessary condition for approximability by Gaussian measures.

The conclusion of Proposition 2 involves the norm $\left|Q G-Q^{*} G\right|_{K}$. This can be bounded using Proposition 1 which implies that the norm $\left\|Q-Q^{*}\right\|$ does not exceed $2 \sum \alpha_{j}^{2}$. Another bound is given by Proposition 4, Section 2, which says that $\left|Q-Q^{*}\right|_{K} \leq 7 \alpha^{1 / 3}$ with $\alpha=\sup _{j} \alpha_{j}$.

In some particular cases one can use much better bounds. For instance, if the $X_{j}$ are identically distributed, a result of Prohorov, extended by J. Kerstan, shows that $\left\|Q-Q^{*}\right\| \leq 3 \alpha$.

The crude bound $\left\|Q-Q^{*}\right\| \leq 2 \sum \alpha_{j}^{2}$ of Proposition 1 is already sufficient to yield the usual central limit theorems. An immediate corollary of the foregoing inequalities can be stated as follows. There is also a corresponding corollary for Proposition 3.

Theorem 1. Assume that the variables $X_{j}$ satisfy conditions ( $A$ ) and ( $B$ ). Then

$$
|P-Q|_{K} \leq 2 \min \left[\sum \alpha_{j}^{2}, 7 \alpha^{1 / 3}\right]+\frac{20}{7 \pi}\left[2 K \alpha+K^{2} \delta\right]+\frac{3}{2} \Gamma[7 b+(3.4) \tau]
$$

Also

$$
|P-Q|_{K} \leq(14.1) \alpha^{1 / 3}+18 D^{-2 / 3}(b)
$$

Proof. The first assertion follows from Propositions 1 and 2 above, and Lemma 3, Section 2, if one can verify that the number $\gamma$ such that $C_{G}(\gamma)=\frac{3}{4}$ is smaller than $7 b+(3.4) \tau$. The distribution $G$ is that of a variable $b W_{1}+\tau W_{2} \sqrt{2 / 3}$ where $W_{1}$ has density $(1 / \pi)\left((1-\cos x) / x^{2}\right)$ and where $W_{2}$ is a standard Gaussian variable. From the normal tables one sees that $P\left[W_{2}>1.96\right] \leq .025$. For $W_{1}$ one can write

$$
P\left[W_{1} \geq y\right]=\frac{1}{\pi}\left[\frac{1-\cos y}{y}+\int_{y}^{\infty} \frac{\sin x}{x} d x\right]
$$

Using the tables of Jahnke and Emde for the sine-integral, one can see that $P\left[W_{1} \geq 3.5\right]<.1$. This gives $P\left\{\left|b W_{1}+\tau W_{2} \sqrt{2 / 3}\right| \geq[(3.5) b+(1.7) \tau]\right\} \leq .25$ and implies the desired result.

For the second statement, note that, according to Proposition 1, Section 2, the modulus of continuity of $Q$ satisfies the inequality

$$
\Gamma(\lambda) \leq \frac{\sqrt{2 \pi}}{D(\lambda)}
$$

with

$$
D^{2}(\lambda)=\sum_{j} \int\left[1 \wedge \frac{x^{2}}{\lambda^{2}}\right] P_{j}(d x)
$$

The coefficient $K=s^{2} /\left(s^{2}+\tau^{2}\right)$ does not exceed unity and the term $K^{2} \delta$ is always inferior to $b^{2} / \tau^{2}$. Thus to obtain the second result it is sufficient to select $\tau$ so that both $\left(b^{2} / \tau^{2}\right)$ and $\Gamma[(7) b+(3.4) \tau]$ are small.

Now take $\tau=x b$. One can write

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Gamma\{[7+(3.4) x] b\} & \leq[8+(3.4) x] \Gamma(b) \\
& \leq[8 \sqrt{2 \pi}+(3.4) \sqrt{2 \pi} x][D(b)]^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

This gives

$$
\frac{20}{7 \pi} K^{2} \delta+\frac{3}{2} \Gamma[7 b+(3.4) \tau] \leq \frac{20}{7 \pi} \frac{1}{x^{2}}+\frac{3(3.4) \sqrt{2 \pi}}{2 D(b)} x+\frac{12 \sqrt{2 \pi}}{D(b)}
$$

Selecting $x$ to minimize this expression one obtains a bound

$$
3\left\{\frac{20}{7}\left[\frac{3}{2}(3.4)\right]^{2}\right\}^{1 / 3}[D(b)]^{-2 / 3}+(12 \sqrt{2 \pi})[D(b)]^{-1}
$$

Taking into account the fact that the inequality is trivial unless $[D(b)]^{2 / 3} \geq$ 18 and $14 \alpha^{1 / 3} \leq 1$, one can obtain the result as stated by simple arithmetic.

The preceding propositions used only the Kolmogorov norms. As a result, Theorem 1 is applicable only to cases where the minimum $\Gamma_{p}(b) \wedge \Gamma_{Q}(b)$ of the moduli of continuity is small. This seems to be in the nature of things. Some remarks on the situation will be found at the end of this section. However, one can also use other distances. For the Lévy distance or an analogue of it, one can readily obtain the following result.

Proposition 4. Assume that the variables $X_{j}$ satisfy conditions ( $A$ ) and ( $B)$. Then for every $m \geq 15$ one can write

$$
P\{(-\infty, x-m b]\}-\varepsilon \leq Q\{(-\infty, x]\} \leq P\{(-\infty, x+m b]\}+\varepsilon,
$$

for an $\varepsilon$ such that

$$
\varepsilon \leq 9.38 \alpha^{1 / 3}+\frac{12.67}{m}
$$

Proof. Suppose for instance that for some $x$ one has

$$
P\{(-\infty, x-m b]\}>Q\{(-\infty, x]\}+\varepsilon
$$

Then $P\{(-\infty, y]\}>Q\{(-\infty, y]\}+\varepsilon$ for all $y$ such that $x-m b \leq y \leq x$. It follows as in Lemma 3, Section 2, that $G$ being the measure used for Proposition 2 one has

$$
|Q G-P G|_{K}+\left[1-C_{G}(m b)\right] \geq C_{G}(m b) \varepsilon .
$$

Using the bound in Proposition 2 above, this gives

$$
C_{G}(m b) \varepsilon \leq\left[1-C_{G}(m b)\right]+\frac{10}{7 \pi} K^{2} \delta+7 \alpha^{1 / 3}+\alpha
$$

The distribution $G$ is that of a variable $b W_{1}+\tau(\sqrt{2 / 3}) W_{2}$ as in the proof of Theorem 1. Taking $\tau=x b$ and using the fact that $K^{2} \delta \leq(b / \tau)^{2}$, one can bound
$1-C_{G}(m b)+(10 / 7 \pi) K^{2} \delta$ by

$$
\frac{10}{7 \pi} \frac{1}{x^{2}}+\operatorname{Pr}\left[\left|W_{1}\right| \geq \frac{m}{4}\right]+\operatorname{Pr}\left\{\left|x \sqrt{\frac{2}{3}} W_{2}\right| \geq \frac{m}{4}\right\} \leq \frac{16}{\pi} \frac{1}{m}+\frac{10}{7 \pi} \frac{1}{x^{2}}+\frac{32}{3 m^{2}} x^{2}
$$

The value of $x$ that minimizes that expression is given by $x^{2}=(15 / 7 \pi)^{1 / 2} m / 4$. For this value one obtains a bound

$$
\frac{1}{m}\left\{\frac{16}{\pi}+\frac{16}{3}\left(\frac{15}{7 \pi}\right)^{1 / 2}\right\} \leq(9.5) \frac{1}{m}
$$

It can be verified, as in the proof of Theorem 1 , that if $m \geq 14$, then, for the choice of $x$ made here, $C_{G}(m b) \geq \frac{3}{4}$. For those values of $\alpha$ for which the inequality is operative this gives

$$
\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon & \leq \frac{4}{3}\left[(7.03) \alpha^{1 / 3}+\frac{9.5}{m}\right] \\
& \leq 9.38 \alpha^{1 / 3}+\frac{12.67}{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

As a corollary one can obtain the following result of Kolmogorov.
Proposition 5. Let $X_{j} ; j=1, \ldots$ be arbitrary independent random variables such that the Lévy distance between $\mathscr{L}\left(X_{j}\right)$ and the probability measure concentrated at zero does not exceed $\varepsilon$. Let $a_{j}$ be a number, $\left|a_{j}\right| \leq \varepsilon$, such that $E\left\{\left(X_{j}-a_{j}\right) I\left[\left|X_{j}\right| \leq \varepsilon\right]\right\}=0$. Let $F_{j}=\mathscr{L}\left(X_{j}-a_{j}\right), P=\prod_{j} F_{j}$, and $Q=\exp \left\{\sum\left(F_{j}-I\right)\right\}$. Then the Lévy distance between $P$ and $Q$ does not exceed $13 \varepsilon^{1 / 3}$.

Proof. To say that the Lévy distance between $\mathscr{L}\left(X_{j}\right)$ and the mass one at zero does not exceed $\varepsilon$ is to say that $P\left\{\left|X_{j}\right|>\varepsilon\right\} \leq 2 \varepsilon$. Thus in the preceding result we may center the $X_{j}$ at the expectations obtained from the variables
truncated at $\varepsilon$ and take $b=2 \varepsilon$. Then both conditions (A) and (B) are satisfied for the measures $F_{j}$. The above Proposition 4 yields the desired inequality.

In all the preceding results we have centered the variables on some form of truncated expectation. One can also allow nonzero expectations as long as the sum of their squares is small. A possibility is as follows.

Theorem 2. Let $\left\{X_{j} ; j=1,2, \ldots\right\}$ be a finite sequence of independent random variables having distributions $P_{j}=\mathscr{L}\left(X_{j}\right)$. Let $b$ denote a positive number. Assume that there exist numbers $\alpha_{j} \in[0,1]$ and probability measures $M_{j}$ and $N_{j}$ such that $P_{j}=\left(1-\alpha_{j}\right) M_{j}+\alpha_{j} N_{j}$ and such that $M_{j}$ is entirely concentrated on the interval $[-b,+b]$. Let $a_{j}=\int x M_{j}(d x)$. Let $P=\mathscr{L}\left(\sum X_{j}\right)=\prod_{j} P_{j}$ and let $Q$ be the exponential $Q=\exp \left\{\sum_{j}\left(P_{j}-I\right)\right\}$.

Then

$$
|P-Q|_{K} \leq 20\left[\alpha^{1 / 3}+\beta^{1 / 3}+\varepsilon^{1 / 3}\right]
$$

with $\alpha=\sup _{j} \alpha_{j}, \beta=D^{2}(b)$, and

$$
\varepsilon=\frac{\left(\sum a_{j}^{2}\right)}{b^{2} D^{2}(b)}
$$

the quantity $D^{2}(b)$ being defined by

$$
D^{2}(b)=\sum_{j} \int\left(1 \wedge\left(\frac{x}{b}\right)^{2}\right) P_{j}(d x)
$$

Proof. Let $Y_{j}=X_{j}-a_{j}$ and let $\bar{P}_{j}=\mathscr{L}\left(Y_{j}\right)$. Then $\bar{P}_{j}=\left(1-\alpha_{j}\right) \bar{M}_{j}+ \alpha_{j} \bar{N}_{n}$, where $\bar{M}_{j}$ is $M_{j}$ shifted by the amount ( $-a_{j}$ ) and similarly for $\bar{N}_{j}$ and $\bar{P}_{j}$. Let $\bar{Q}=\exp \left\{\sum_{j}\left(\bar{P}_{j}-I\right)\right\}$. One can apply Proposition 2 to $\bar{P}=\prod \bar{P}_{j}$ and $\bar{Q}$ obtaining

$$
|\bar{P} G-\bar{Q} G|_{K} \leq 7 \alpha^{1 / 3}+\frac{10}{7 \pi}\left[2 K \alpha+K^{2} \delta\right]
$$

with appropriate definition for $\delta$.
Let $S$ be the shift by the amount $a=\sum_{j} a_{j}$. The two norms $|\bar{P} G-\bar{Q} G|_{K}$ and $|S \bar{P} G-S \bar{Q} G|_{K}$ are the same, and $S \bar{P}$ is the distribution $P$ of $\sum X_{j}$.

For each $j$ let $Y_{j, i}, i=1,2, \ldots$ be independent variables such that $\mathscr{L}\left(Y_{j, i}\right)= \mathscr{L}\left(Y_{j}\right)$ and let $v_{j}$ be an independent Poisson variable, independent of the $Y_{j, i}$ such that $E v_{j}=1$. Then $\bar{Q}$ is the distribution of

$$
T=\sum_{j} \sum_{i}^{v_{j}} Y_{j, i}
$$

while $Q$ is the distribution of $T+\Delta$ with $\Delta=\sum_{j} v_{j} a_{j}$. This variable $\Delta$ has expectation $a=\sum a_{j}$ and variance $v=\sum a_{j}^{2}$.

It follows from Chebyshev's inequality and Lemma 2, Section 2, that, for every $\lambda>0$

$$
|Q G-S \bar{Q} G|_{K} \leq \frac{v}{\lambda^{2}}+\Gamma_{0}(\lambda)
$$

where $\Gamma_{0}$ is the minimum of the moduli of continuity of $Q G$ and $\bar{Q} G$. Letting

$$
D^{2}=\sum_{j} \int\left(1 \wedge \frac{x^{2}}{b^{2}}\right) P_{j}(d x)+\frac{2}{3} \frac{\tau^{2}}{b^{2}}
$$

it follows from Proposition 1, Section 2 that

$$
\Gamma_{0}(\lambda) \leq\left[\frac{\lambda}{b}+1\right] \frac{\sqrt{2 \pi}}{D}
$$

Selecting $\lambda$ appropriately, this gives

$$
|Q G-S \bar{Q} G|_{K} \leq \frac{\sqrt{2 \pi}}{D}+(3.5)\left(\frac{v}{b^{2} D^{2}}\right)^{1 / 3}
$$

and therefore

$$
|P G-Q G|_{K} \leq 7 \alpha^{1 / 3}+\frac{10}{7 \pi}\left[2 K \alpha+K^{2} \delta\right]+\frac{\sqrt{2 \pi}}{D}+(3.5)\left(\frac{v}{b^{2} D^{2}}\right)^{1 / 3}
$$

One can then remove the convolution by $G$ exactly as in Theorem 1, obtaining

$$
|P-Q|_{K} \leq(14.1) \alpha^{1 / 3}+18 D^{-2 / 3}+\frac{2 \sqrt{2 \pi}}{D}+7 \varepsilon^{1 / 3}
$$

Since one may assume that $18 D^{-2 / 3} \leq 1$, and since $D \geq D(b)$, this can also be written as

$$
|P-Q|_{K} \leq(14.1) \alpha^{1 / 3}+(19.2) D^{-2 / 3}(b)+7 \varepsilon^{1 / 3}
$$

implying the result stated in the theorem.
One can obtain results analogous to Proposition 5 in the same manner.
It should be mentioned that for certain applications the bounds given here are unnecessarily crude. For instance, if the variables $X_{j}$ are identically distributed a result of Prohorov implies that the bound $7 \alpha^{1 / 3}$ of Proposition 9 can be replaced by $3 \alpha$. Also, in this case all the $\sigma_{j}^{2}$ which enter in the computation of $\delta=\sum_{j}\left[\sigma_{j}^{2} / s^{2}\right]^{2}$ are the same, so that $\delta$ is exactly equal to ( $1 / n$ ) and there is no need at all to introduce the Gaussian variable which occurs in the distribution $G$ used for smoothing. In this case Proposition 7 would give a bound at most equal to

$$
3 \alpha+\frac{10}{7 \pi}\left[2 \alpha+\frac{1}{n}\right]
$$

One can also select $b$ in such a way that the concentration $\Gamma[7 \mathrm{~b}]$ is small. In fact, taking a suitable coefficient $c$, one can find numbers $x_{1}$ and $x_{2}$ such that the measure $P_{j}$ may be written as a convex combination

$$
P_{j}=\frac{c}{n^{1 / 3}} N_{1, j}+\frac{c}{n^{1 / 3}} N_{2, j}+\left(1-\frac{2 c}{n^{1 / 3}}\right) M_{j}
$$

with $M_{j}$ carried by $\left[x_{1}, x_{2}\right], N_{1, j}$ carried by $\left(-\infty, x_{1}\right]$, and $N_{2, j}$ carried by $\left[x_{2}, \infty\right)$. Let then

$$
a_{j}=\int x M_{j}(d x) \text { and } Y_{j}=X_{j}-a_{j}
$$

If $P=\mathscr{L}\left(\sum Y_{j}\right)$ and $Q=\exp \left\{\Sigma_{j}\left[\mathscr{L}\left(Y_{j}\right)-I\right]\right\}$ it can be checked that, for an appropriate $c$ one may assert that

$$
|P-Q|_{K} \leq \frac{40}{n^{1 / 3}}
$$

To terminate this section, let us note the following. In all the propositions given here we have used a decomposition of the form $P_{j}=\left(1-\alpha_{j}\right) M_{j}+\alpha_{j} N_{j}$, where $\alpha_{j}$ is small and where $P_{j}$ is carried by an interval $[-b,+b]$ such that $b$ is presumably "small" compared to the dispersion of the sum $\sum_{j} X_{j}$. This is inherited from the usual central limit theorems which consider only asymptotically negligible variables. However, one may wonder if such restrictions are really needed for the approximation of $P=\mathscr{L}\left(\sum X_{j}\right)$ by the accompanying infinitely divisible distribution $Q=\exp \left\{\Sigma_{j}\left(P_{j}-I\right)\right\}$.

A partial answer is as follows. Suppose that the $X_{j}$ have symmetric distributions, that is, $\mathscr{L}\left(X_{j}\right)=\mathscr{L}\left(-X_{j}\right)$. Let $R_{k}$ be the convolution product $R_{k}= \prod_{j \neq k} P_{j}$ in which $P_{k}$ has been omitted. One can then show the three quantities $q_{1}=|P-Q|_{K}, q_{2}=\sup _{k}\left|R_{k}-P\right|_{K}$, and $q_{3}=\inf _{b}\left\{(14.1) \alpha^{1 / 3}+18 D^{-2 / 3}(b)\right\}$ (of Theorem 1) are all simultaneously small if any one of them is small.

In particular, approximation of $P$ by $Q$ is possible if and only if eliminating any one variable from the sum does not change the distribution much. This is valid also if instead of using the Kolmogorov distance one uses the Lévy distance throughout.

Without the symmetry assumption we do not know exactly what happens. However, if $\sup _{k}\left|R_{k}-P\right|_{K}$ is small, one can recenter the $X_{j}$ to make $|P-Q|_{K}$ small.

## 5. Limit Theorems and Related Results

In this section we shall consider not one convolution product $P=\prod_{j} P_{j}$ but a sequence $\left\{P_{n}\right\}, P_{n}=\prod_{j} P_{j, n}$ of such products, the purpose being to identify the possible limits of the sequence $\left\{P_{n}\right\}$. We already know, from Section 3, that, for small variables which are reasonably centered, the limit of the $P_{n}$ will be the same as that of the accompanying Poisson exponentials $Q_{n}=\exp \left\{\Sigma_{j}\left(P_{j, n}-I\right)\right\}$. However, conditions for convergence need to be
spelled out. This is rather easy, except for the choice of centering constants. We have devoted much space to the possible choices.

The argument will be carried out using only the compactness conditions of Section 2, Propositions 1 and 2 of Section 3, and the following result relative to infinitely divisible distributions.

To state it we shall consider triplets $\left(a, \sigma^{2}, M\right)$ consisting of a number $a$, a number $\sigma^{2} \geq 0$, and a Lévy measure $M$. This last object is a positive Radon measure on the locally compact space $(-\infty, 0) \cup(0, \infty)$ subject to the additional restriction that $\int\left(1 \wedge x^{2}\right) M(d x)<\infty$.

To a pair ( $\sigma^{2}, M$ ) corresponds a Khintchin measure $K$ defined by $K[\{0\}]= \sigma^{2}$ and $K(d x)=\left(1 \wedge x^{2}\right) M(d x)$ for $x \neq 0$. Let $u$ be a measurable function, $0 \leq u \leq 1$, such that $u(x)=1$ in a neighborhood of zero and $u(x)=0$ for all $|x|$ sufficiently large.

Let $\psi$ be the function

$$
\psi(t)=i a t-\frac{\sigma^{2}}{2} t^{2}+\int\left[e^{i t x}-1-i t x u(x)\right] M(d x)
$$

and let $\phi=\exp \{\psi\}$. This is the characteristic function of a certain infinitely divisible measure $P$.

Proposition 1. Let $P_{1}$ and $P_{2}$ be two probability measures with respective characteristic functions $\phi_{j}=\exp \left\{\psi_{j}\right\}$, where $\psi_{j}$ has the form described above. If the corresponding triplets $\left(a_{j}, \sigma_{j}^{2}, M_{j}\right)$ are different, then $P_{1}$ and $P_{2}$ are different. If ( $\sigma_{1}^{2}, M_{1}$ ) differs from ( $\sigma_{2}^{1}, M_{2}$ ) then $P_{2}$ is not a translate of $P_{1}$.

Proof. Note first that if $\phi=\exp \{\psi\}$, then $\phi$ never vanishes. Also, both $\phi$ and $\psi$ are continuous functions of the real variable $t$. Thus there is a unique version of $\log \phi(t)$ which is continuous and such that $\log \phi(0)=0$. This gives the function $\psi$ itself.

Let $s$ be any positive number. Consider the second differences

$$
\omega_{s}(t)=\psi(t)-\frac{1}{2}[\psi(t+s)+\psi(t-s)]
$$

This may be written

$$
\omega_{s}(t)=\sigma^{2} \frac{s^{2}}{2}+\int e^{i t x}[1-\cos s x] M(d x)
$$

Alternatively, introducing the Khintchin measure $K$ corresponding to ( $\sigma^{2}, M$ ) and the function $f(s, x)$ defined by $f(s, 0)=s^{2} / 2$ and $f(s, x)=(1-\cos s x)\left(1 \vee\left(1 / x^{2}\right)\right)$ for $x \neq 0$, one may write

$$
\omega_{s}(t)=\int e^{i t x} f(s, x) K(d x)
$$

Note that $\omega$ is left unchanged if one performs a shift on the measures $P$. Also, by the uniqueness theorem for Fourier transform, $\omega_{s}$ determines the measure $f(s, x) K(d x)$. This being true for each $s$, the measures $K$, or equivalently the



pairs ( $\sigma^{2}, M$ ) are well determined by the differences $\omega_{s}$. That $a$ cannot be changed without changing the measure $P$ follows easily.

Note. The value of $a$ depends on the function $u$ chosen for the formula. However if $v$ is another function satisfying the same conditions as $u$ and if $b$ is the corresponding constant, then $b-a=\int x[v(x)-u(x)] M(d x)$.

The foregoing proposition has an immediate consequence relative to sequences $\left\{P_{n}\right\}$ of infinitely divisible distributions. Let $\phi_{n}$ be the characteristic function of $P_{n}$, so that $\phi_{n}$ corresponds to a triplet ( $a_{n}, \sigma_{n}^{2}, M_{n}$ ) or equivalently to a pair $\left(a_{n}, K_{n}\right)$ where $K_{n}$ is the Khintchin measure associated with $\left(\sigma_{n}^{2}, M_{n}\right)$.

Corollary 1. Let $S(\xi)$ be the shift by the amount $\xi$. There are numbers $\xi_{n}$ such
that $S\left(\xi_{n}\right) P_{n}$ tends to a limit $P$ (for the Lévy distance) if and only if the Khintchin measures $K_{n}$ converge, for the Lévy distance, to a limit $K$. Then $P$ is infinitely divisible with Khintchin measure K.

Proof. One may assume that the function $u$ used in the formula has been taken continuous. Then, if $K_{n} \rightarrow K$ the corresponding integrals converge Conversely, according to Proposition 1, Section 3, shift-compactness of the $P_{n}$ implies relative compactness of the $K_{n}$. Thus one may extract convergent subsequences. If $K$ is such a limit, then $K$ is already well defined when $P$ is known up to a shift. Hence the result.

This being acquired, let us pass to double sequences $\left\{X_{n, j} ; j=1,2, \ldots ; n=\right. 1,2, \ldots\}$ of independent random variables, where $j$ is allowed to vary over some finite set which depends on $n$.

Such a double array is called "infinitesimal," or "uniformly asymptotically negligible" (U.A.N. for short) if, for each $\varepsilon>0$, the quantity

$$
\sup _{j} P\left\{\left|X_{n, j}\right|>\varepsilon\right\}
$$

tends to zero as $n \rightarrow \infty$.
Let $P_{n, j}$ be the distribution of $X_{n, j}$. Suppose that $P_{n, j}$ is written as a convex combination $P_{n, j}=\left(1-\alpha_{n, j}\right) M_{n, j}+\alpha_{n, j} N_{n, j}$ where $M_{n, j}$ and $N_{n, j}$ are probability measures and where $\alpha_{n, j} \in[0,1]$.

Definition 1. The decomposition $P_{n, j}=\left(1-\alpha_{n, j}\right) M_{n, j}+\alpha_{n, j} N_{n, j}$ will be called a Lévy splitting of the $P_{n, j}$ if the following two conditions are satisfied:
(i) There is a number $c<\infty$ such that all the $M_{n, j}$ are carried by the interval $[-c,+c]$;
(ii) $\alpha_{n}=\sup _{j} \alpha_{n, j}$ tends to zero as $n \rightarrow \infty$.

The splitting will be called regular if in addition the sums $\sum_{j} \alpha_{n, j}$ remain bounded.

For an arbitrary sequence $\left\{P_{n, j}\right\}$ one cannot always find Lévy splittings. However, for the situation considered in this section there are always many regular Lévy splittings as asserted by the following lemma.

Lemma 1. Let the $X_{n, j}$ be U.A.N. Assume in addition that the sums $S_{n}= \sum_{j} X_{n, j}$ are shift-compact. Then for any $c>0$ there are regular Lévy splittings which satisfy the conditions of Definition 1 for this constant $c$.

Proof. Let $\mu_{n, j}$ be a median of $X_{n, j}$. The U.A.N. condition implies that $\sup _{j}\left|\mu_{n, j}\right|$ tends to zero. Then it follows from Proposition 1, Section 3, that one can form a regular Lévy splitting by taking $M_{n, j}=\mathscr{L}\left\{X_{n, j}| | X_{n, j} \mid<c\right\}$ and $N_{n, j}=\mathscr{L}\left\{X_{n, j}| | X_{n, j} \mid \geq c\right\}$.

For an arbitrary Lévy splitting, consider independent variables $\left(\xi_{n, j}, \bar{U}_{n, j}, \bar{V}_{n, j}\right)$ such that
(i) $\alpha_{n, j}=P\left[\xi_{n, j}=1\right]=1-P\left[\xi_{n, j}=0\right]$,
(ii) $\mathscr{L}\left(\bar{U}_{n, j}\right)=M_{n, j}$,
(iii) $\mathscr{L}\left(\bar{V}_{n, j}\right)=N_{n, j}$.

Let $a_{n, j}=E \bar{U}_{\underline{n}, j}=\int x M_{n, j}(d x)$ and construct new variables $U_{n, j}=\bar{U}_{n, j}- a_{n, j}$ and $V_{n, j}=\bar{V}_{n, j}-a_{n, j}$. Then $X_{n, j}$ has the same distribution as $a_{n, j}+ \left(1-\xi_{n, j}\right) U_{n, j}+\xi_{n, j} V_{n, j}$.

Let $F_{n, j}$ be the distribution of $\left(1-\xi_{n, j}\right) U_{n, j}+\xi_{n, j} V_{n, j}$ so that $F_{n, j}= \mathscr{L}\left(X_{n, j}-a_{n, j}\right)$. For any number $h$ let $S(h)$ be the shift by the amount $h$. To such a system one can associate the accompanying infinitely divisible distribution

$$
Q_{n}=S\left(a_{n}\right) \exp \left\{\Sigma_{j}\left(F_{n, j}-I\right)\right\}
$$

with $a_{n}=\sum_{j} a_{n, j}$ and a Khintchin measure $K_{n}$ defined by

$$
K_{n}(d x)=\left(1 \wedge x^{2}\right) \sum_{j} F_{n, j}(d x)
$$

Note that for different splittings the corresponding measures $Q_{n}$ differ only in that the shifts $a_{n, j}$ used to pass from $P_{n, j}$ to $F_{n, j}$ may be different.

Lemma 2. Assume that the $X_{n, j}$ are U.A.N. and that the sums $S_{n}=\sum_{j} X_{n, j}$ are shift-compact. Consider two Lévy splittings, with associated objects ( $\left.a_{n, j}, Q_{n}, K_{n}\right)$ and $\left(a_{n, j}^{\prime}, Q_{n}^{\prime}, K_{n}^{\prime}\right)$. Then the sums $\sum_{j}\left(a_{n, j}-a_{n, j}^{\prime}\right)^{2}$ tend to zero. The Lévy distance $\lambda\left(Q_{n}, Q_{n}^{\prime}\right)$ and $\lambda\left(K_{n}, K_{n}^{\prime}\right)$ tend to zero. If, in addition, the splittings are regular then $\sum_{j}\left|a_{n, j}-a_{n, j}^{\prime}\right|$ remains bounded.

Proof. If $Q_{n}$ is the distribution of a variable $T_{n}$, then one can note, as in the proof of Theorem 2, Section 4, that $Q_{n}^{\prime}$ is the distribution of $T_{n}+\Delta_{n}$ where $E \Delta_{n}=0$ and variance $\Delta_{n}=\sum_{j}\left(a_{n, j}-a_{n, j}^{\prime}\right)^{2}$.

Thus, if $\sum_{j}\left(a_{n, j}-a_{n, j}^{\prime}\right)^{2} \rightarrow 0$, the Lévy distance $\lambda\left(Q_{n}, Q_{n}^{\prime}\right)$ will tend to zero. Then, according to the Corollary of Proposition 1 above, the distance $\lambda\left(K_{n}, K_{n}^{\prime}\right)$ will also tend to zero.

Therefore, it will be enough to prove the assertions concerning the differences $a_{n, j}-a_{n, j}^{\prime}$. To do this, take a fixed number $c>0$ and a splitting $P_{n, j}= \left(1-\alpha_{n, j}\right) M_{n, j}+\alpha_{n, j} N_{n, j}$ where $M_{n, j}$ is carried by $[-c,+c]$.

Write $P_{n, j}=\left(1-\alpha_{n, j}^{\prime}\right) M_{n, j}^{\prime}+\alpha_{n, j}^{\prime} N_{n, j}^{\prime}$ where $M_{n, j}^{\prime}$ is a probability measure and $\left(1-\alpha_{n, j}^{\prime}\right) M_{n, j}^{\prime}$ is precisely the part of $P_{n, j}$ carried by $[-c,+c]$. These $M_{n, j}^{\prime}$ give centering constants $a_{n, j}^{\prime}=\int x M_{n, j}^{\prime}(d x)$.

Since $M_{n, j}$ itself is carried by $[-c,+c]$, one may write

$$
M_{n, j}^{\prime}=\left(1-\beta_{n, j}\right) M_{n, j}+\beta_{n, j} R_{n, j}
$$

where $1-\beta_{n, j}=\left(1-\alpha_{n, j}^{\prime}\right)^{-1}\left(1-\alpha_{n, j}\right)$ and $R_{n, j}$ is obtained by renormalizing the part of $N_{n, j}$ which is carried by $[-c,+c]$. If $\mathscr{L}\left(Z_{n, j}\right)=M_{n, j}$, let $M_{n, j}^{*}=\mathscr{L}\left(-Z_{n, j}\right)$ and let $W_{n, j}$ be the convolution $R_{n} M_{n, j}^{*}$.

The difference $a_{n, j}^{\prime}-a_{n, j}$ is equal to

$$
\beta_{n, j}\left[\int x R_{n, j}(d x)-\int x M_{n, j}(d x)\right]=\beta_{n, j} \int x W_{n, j}(d x)
$$

Thus

$$
\left(a_{n, j}-a_{n, j}^{\prime}\right)^{2} \leq \beta_{n, j}^{2} \int x^{2} W_{n, j}(d x)
$$

Convoluting $M_{n, j}^{\prime}$ with its symmetric, one sees that the variance of $M_{n, j}^{\prime}$ is equal to

$$
\left(1-\beta_{n, j}\right)^{2} \operatorname{var} M_{n, j}+\beta_{n, j}^{2} \operatorname{var} R_{n, j}+2 \beta_{n, j}\left(1-\beta_{n, j}\right) \int x^{2} W_{n, j}(d x)
$$

If the sums $S_{n}$ are shift-compact the sum of the variances of the $M_{n, j}^{\prime}$ must remain bounded. In particular

$$
\sup _{n} \sum_{j} \beta_{n, j}\left(1-\beta_{n, j}\right) \int x^{2} W_{n, j}(d x)<\infty
$$

Since $\sup _{j} \beta_{n, j} \rightarrow 0$, it follows that $\sum_{j}\left(a_{n, j}-a_{n, j}^{\prime}\right)^{2} \rightarrow 0$. This relation implies that $\sum_{j}\left(a_{n, j}-a_{n, j}^{\prime}\right)^{2}$ must tend to zero for any two arbitrary Lévy splittings.

The further assertion concerning the sums $\sum\left|a_{n, j}-a_{n, j}^{\prime}\right|$ for regular Lévy splittings arises from the fact that if

$$
P_{n, j}=\left(1-\alpha_{n, j}^{\prime}\right) M_{n, j}^{\prime}+\alpha_{n, j}^{\prime} N_{n, j}^{\prime}=\left(1-\alpha_{n, j}\right) M_{n, j}+\alpha_{n, j} N_{n, j}
$$

then

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left\|\left(1-\alpha_{n, j}^{\prime}\right) M_{n, j}^{\prime}-\left(1-\alpha_{n, j}\right) M_{n, j}\right\| & =\left\|\alpha_{n, j} N_{n, j}-\alpha_{n, j}^{\prime} N_{n, j}^{\prime}\right\| \\
& \leq \alpha_{n, j}+\alpha_{n, j}^{\prime}
\end{aligned}
$$

Thus, if both $M_{n, j}$ and $M_{n, j}^{\prime}$ are carried by $[-c,+c]$ one has

$$
\left|a_{n, j}-a_{n, j}^{\prime}\right| \leq 2 c\left[\alpha_{n, j}+\alpha_{n, j}^{\prime}\right]
$$

This concludes the proof of the Lemma.
We are now in a position to state a basic approximation theorem as follows.
Theorem 1. Assume that the $X_{n, j}$ are U.A.N. and that the sums $\sum_{j} X_{n, j}$ are shift-compact. Consider an arbitrary Lévy splitting of the $P_{n, j}$ with associated objects $a_{n, j}, a_{n}=\sum_{j} a_{n, j}$ and

$$
Q_{n}=S\left(a_{n}\right) \exp \left\{\sum_{j}\left[S\left(-a_{n, j}\right) P_{n, j}-I\right]\right\}
$$

Then the Lévy distance between $Q_{n}$ and the convolution product $P_{n}= \Pi_{j} P_{n, j}$ tends to zero as $n \rightarrow \infty$.

Proof. Let $P_{n, j}=\left(1-\alpha_{n, j}^{\prime}\right) M_{n, j}^{\prime}+\alpha_{n, j}^{\prime} N_{n, j}^{\prime}$ be another Lévy splitting which is regular and such that $M_{n, j}^{\prime}$ is carried by the interval $[-b / 2,+b / 2]$. Let $G$ be the distribution which has characteristic function

$$
\widetilde{G}(t)=[1-|b t|]^{+} \exp \left\{-\frac{\tau^{2} t^{2}}{3}\right\}
$$

According to Proposition 2, Section 4, the Kolmogorov norms satisfy the inequality

$$
\left|P_{n} G-Q_{n}^{\prime} G\right|_{K} \leq \frac{10}{7 \pi}\left[2 \alpha_{n}^{\prime}+\delta_{n}\right]+\sum_{j}\left(\alpha_{n, j}^{\prime}\right)^{2}
$$

with $\alpha_{n}^{\prime}=\sup _{j} \alpha_{n, j}^{\prime}$ and with

$$
\delta_{n}=\sum_{j}\left[\frac{\sigma_{n, j}^{2}}{\tau^{2}+\sigma_{n, j}^{2}}\right]^{2}
$$

where $\sigma_{n, j}^{2}$ is $\left(1-\alpha_{n, j}^{\prime}\right)$ times the variance of $M_{n, j}^{\prime}$. In particular, $\delta_{n} \leq \tau^{-2} \sup _{j} \sigma_{n, j}^{2}$. Thus, for fixed values $b>0$ and $\tau>0$, the three quantities $\alpha_{n}^{\prime}, \delta_{n}$, and $\sum\left(\alpha_{n, j}^{\prime}\right)^{2}$ all tend to zero. It follows that the Lévy distance $\lambda\left(P_{n} G, Q_{n}^{\prime} G\right)$ tends to zero. Therefore, according to Lemma 2 above, the distances $\lambda\left(P_{n} G, Q_{n} G\right)$ also tend to zero. However, $Q_{n}$ does not involve the constant $b$ at all. Thus, letting both $b$ and $\tau$ tend to zero we conclude that $\lambda\left(P_{n}, Q_{n}\right) \rightarrow 0$. This is the desired result.

Remark. The above statement is, of course, an immediate consequence of Proposition 5, Section 4. However the inequalities used above are very simple. They do not make use of Proposition 4, Section 2.

The above theorem is usable in many circumstances, but it is not always convenient for identification of limits when limits exist. For this purpose the following two propositions may be helpful.

Let $L_{n}=\sum_{j} P_{n, j}$. Take a bounded neighborhood of zero, say $W$, on the line at let $\sigma_{n, j}^{2}(W)$ be the variance of the part of $P_{n, j}$ which is carried by $W$. Let $s_{n}^{2}(W)=\sum_{j} \sigma_{n, j}^{2}(W)$.

Proposition 2. Let the $X_{n, j}$ be U.A.N. and let $P_{n}$ be the distribution of $\sum_{j} X_{n, j}$. There are numbers $\xi_{n}$ such that $S\left(\xi_{n}\right) P_{n}$ converges to a limit $Q$ if and only if
(i) the measures $L_{n}$ converge to a limit $L$ in the sense that $\int \gamma(x) L_{n}(d x) \rightarrow$
$\int \gamma(x) L(d x)$ for every bounded continuous function $\gamma$ which vanishes on a neighborhood of zero;
(ii) the limit $\sigma^{2}=\lim _{W} \lim _{n} s_{n}^{2}(W)$, taken as $n \rightarrow \infty$ and then $W$ shrinks to zero, exists.

The limit $Q$ has then a characteristic function of the form

$$
\tilde{Q}(t)=\exp \left\{i a t-\frac{\sigma^{2} t^{2}}{2}+\int\left[e^{i t x}-1-i t x u(x)\right] L(d x)\right\}
$$

Proof. Take any arbitrary Lévy splitting and the corresponding measure $Q_{n}=S\left(a_{n}\right) \exp \left\{\sum_{j}\left(F_{n, j}-I\right)\right\}$. This corresponds to a Lévy measure $L_{n}^{*}= \sum_{j} F_{n, j}$ where $F_{n, j}=S\left(-a_{n, j}\right) P_{n, j}$. Since $\sup _{j}\left(a_{n, j}\right) \rightarrow 0$, convergence of $L_{n}$ to $L$ in the indicated sense, is equivalent to convergence of $L_{n}^{*}$ to $L$. To conclude, it is sufficient to note that the two conditions (i) and (ii) stated here are equivalent to the convergence of the Khintchin measures $K_{n}^{*}(d x)=\left(1 \wedge x^{2}\right) L_{n}^{*}(d x)$. The result follows then from the corollary of Proposition 1.

The preceding result characterizes the possible limits of the sequence $\left\{P_{n}\right\}$ except for the shift $a$ to be introduced in the final formula. One possible evaluation of the shift constants is as follows.

Proposition 3. Assume that the $X_{n, j}$ are U.A.N. and that they satisfy the conditions of Proposition 2.

Let $P_{n, j}=\left(1-\alpha_{n, j}\right) M_{n, j}+\alpha_{n, j} N_{n, j}$ be a regular Lévy splitting of the $P_{n, j}$ selected so that the sums $\sum_{j} \alpha_{n, j} N_{n, j}$ converges to a limit as $n \rightarrow \infty$. Let $a_{n, j}=\int x M_{n, j}(d x)$ and $a_{n}=\sum_{j} a_{n, j}$. Then the convolution product $P_{n}=\mathscr{L}\left(\sum_{j} X_{n, j}\right)$ converges to a limit if and only if the numbers $a_{n}$ converge to a limit.

Proof. Let $u$ be a continuous function defined on the line and such that $0 \leq u \leq 1$. For the given Lévy splitting, there is a constant $c$ such that all the $M_{n, j}$ are carried by $[-c,+c]$. Select $u$ so that $u(x) \equiv 1$ for $x \in[-2 c,+2 c]$ and so that $u(x)=0$ for $|x| \geq 4 c$. Let $M_{n, j}^{*}=S\left(-a_{n, j}\right) M_{n, j}$ and $N_{n, j}^{*}=S\left(-a_{n, j}\right) N_{n, j}$. The infinitely divisible distribution $Q_{n}$ of Theorem 1 may be written $Q_{n}= S\left(a_{n}\right) \exp \left\{L_{n}^{*}-\left\|L_{n}^{*}\right\|\right\}$, where $L_{n}^{*}=L_{n}^{\prime}+L_{n}^{\prime \prime}$ with $L_{n}^{\prime}=\sum_{j}\left[\left(1-\alpha_{n, j}\right) M_{n, j}^{*}\right]$ and $L_{n}^{\prime \prime}=\sum_{j} \alpha_{n, j} N_{n, j}^{*}$. Since $\sup _{j}\left|a_{n, j}\right| \rightarrow 0$, the sequence $L_{n}^{\prime \prime}$ has the same limit as $\sum_{j} a_{n, j} N_{n, j}$ itself. The measure $Q_{n}$ has a characteristic function $\widetilde{Q}_{n}$ which may be written in the form

$$
\begin{aligned}
\log \widetilde{Q}_{n}(t)= & i a_{n} t+\int\left[e^{i t x}-1-i t x u(x)\right] L_{n}^{\prime}(d x)+\int\left[e^{i t x}-1\right] L_{n}^{\prime \prime}(d x) \\
& +i t \int x u(x) L_{n}^{\prime}(d x)
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $\int x u(x) M_{n, j}^{*}(d x)=\int x M_{n, j}^{*}(d x)=0$, the last term in this expression vanishes. By Proposition 2, the two remaining integral terms converge. Thus $\tilde{Q}_{n}$ will converge if and only if $a_{n}$ does. This gives the desired result.

When the conditions of Proposition 2 are satisfied it is easy to find Lévy splittings such that $\sum_{j} \alpha_{n, j} N_{n, j}$ converges. For instance, if $L_{n}$ converges to $L$, one can select any two points $\tau_{i}, \tau_{1}<0<\tau_{2}$ such that $L\left[\left\{\tau_{1}\right\}\right]=L\left[\left\{\tau_{2}\right\}\right]=0$ and let $\alpha_{n, j} N_{n, j}$ be the part of $P_{n, j}$ situated outside [ $\tau_{1}, \tau_{2}$ ]. One can also take a continuous function $u$ such that $u(x)=0$ for $|x|$ small, $u(x)=1$ for $|x|$ large, and $0 \leq u(x) \leq 1$ for all $x$. One lets $\alpha_{n, j} N_{n, j}$ be the measure which has density $u$ with respect to $P_{n, j}$.

One of the most important particular cases of the above results is the case where the limits are Gaussian distributions. We shall give here a statement in the spirit and with the notation of Proposition 2.

Proposition 4. Let the variables $X_{n, j}$ be U.A.N. There are numbers $\xi_{n}$ such that $S\left(\xi_{n}\right) P_{n}$ converges to a Gaussian limit if and only if
(i) for every $\varepsilon>0$ the sum $\sum_{j} P\left\{\left|X_{n, j}\right|>\varepsilon\right\}$ tends to zero,
(ii) the limit $\sigma^{2}=\lim _{W} \lim _{n} s_{n}^{2}(W)$, taken as $n$ tends to infinity and then $W$ shrinks to zero, exists. Then the limiting distribution is a Gaussian distribution with variance $\sigma^{2}$.

Proof. This follows from Proposition 2 and from the uniqueness statement of Proposition 1.

Note that the centering constants $a_{n, j}$ can still be selected as before. However, to obtain the variance $\sigma^{2}$ of the limiting distribution one can proceed differently. Indeed if (i) is satisfied it is clear that

$$
\sigma^{2}=\lim _{n} s_{n}^{2}(W)
$$

for every relatively compact neighborhood $W$ of zero.
It does not follow, however, that the variance $\sigma^{2}$ of the limiting distribution will also be the limit of the variances, say $\sigma_{n}^{2}$, of the measures $P_{n}$ themselves. The necessary and sufficient condition insuring this, and (i) at the same time, is known as the Lindeberg condition.

It is as follows. Take an arbitrary relatively compact neighborhood of zero, say $W$, and let $a_{n, j}$ be a number such that

$$
E\left(X_{n, j}-a_{n, j}\right) I\left[X_{n, j} \in W\right]=0 .
$$

The Lindeberg condition says that for every $\varepsilon>0$, the sums

$$
\sum_{j} E\left(X_{n, j}-a_{n, j}\right)^{2} I\left\{\left|X_{n, j}\right|>\varepsilon\right\}
$$

must tend to zero.
In brief, the Lindeberg condition says that not only the variables $\sum\left(X_{n, j}-a_{n, j}\right) I\left\{\left|X_{n, j}\right|>\varepsilon\right\}$ are negligible, but their second moments are also negligible.

In the following chapters we shall encounter situations where one knows that $\sum X_{n, j}$ converges to a limit, but where the variables of main interest are of the form $\sum_{j} f\left(X_{n, j}\right)$, where $f$ is a reasonably behaved function (see for instance Chapter 16, Section 2).

As in Chapters 10 and 16 we shall consider uniquely functions $f$ which belong to the class $P D[0,2]$. Explicitly, we shall consider functions $f$ such that
(i) $f(0)=0$,
(ii) there are numbers $f^{\prime}$ and $f^{\prime \prime}$ such that if

$$
\rho(x)=\frac{1}{x^{2}}\left|f(x)-f(0)-x f^{\prime}-\frac{x^{2}}{2} f^{\prime \prime}\right|,
$$

then $\rho(x) \rightarrow 0$ as $x \rightarrow 0$.
Note that these two conditions imply that there is some number $c_{f}>0$ such that $\sup \left\{|f(x)| ;|x| \leq c_{f}\right\}<\infty$. The first immediate remark is as follows.

Lemma 3. Assume that $f$ is a measurable function which belongs to the class $P D[0,2]$. Let $P_{n, j}=\left(1-\alpha_{n, j}\right) M_{n, j}+\alpha_{n, j} N_{n, j}$ be an arbitrary Lévy splitting of the $P_{n, j}$ such that $M_{n, j}$ is carried by the interval [ $-c_{f},+c_{f}$ ].

Then, denoting fm the image by $f$ of the measure $m$, the decomposition

$$
f P_{n, j}=\left(1-\alpha_{n, j}\right) f M_{n, j}+\alpha_{n, j} f N_{n, j}
$$

is $\alpha$ Lévy splitting of $f P_{n, j}$. If the splitting of $P_{n, j}$ is regular, the corresponding splitting of $f P_{n, j}$ is also regular.

This is very obvious.
Another remark is as follows. Let $P_{n, j}=\left(1-\alpha_{n, j}\right) M_{n, j}+\alpha_{n, j} N_{n, j}$ be a Lévy splitting of $P_{n, j}$ such that $M_{n, j}$ is carried by an interval $\left[-c_{f},+c_{f}\right]$ where $f$ remains bounded. Let $b_{n, j}=\int f(x) M_{n, j}(d x)$ and let $F_{n, j}=\mathscr{L}\left[f\left(X_{n, j}\right)-b_{n, j}\right]$.

Lemma 4. Assume that $f$ is a real valued measurable function of the class $P D[0,2]$. Assume that the Lévy splitting of $P_{n, j}$ is as described above and that the variables $X_{n, j}$ are U.A.N. Let $b_{n}=\sum_{j} b_{n, j}$. Then the Lévy distance between the distribution of $\sum_{j} f\left(X_{n, j}\right)$ and the infinitely divisible distribution $S\left(b_{n}\right) \exp \left\{\sum_{j}\left(F_{n, j}-I\right)\right\}$ converges to zero as $n \rightarrow \infty$.

Proof. This follows readily from Proposition 5 of Section 4 and from the computations on truncated means carried out in Lemma 2 of the present section.

To state another result, consider the following conditions.
(A) The $X_{n, j}$ are U.A.N. and the distributions $P_{n}=\mathscr{L}\left(\sum X_{n, j}\right)$ converge to a limit $P$.
(B) There is a Lévy splitting of the $X_{n, j}$ with centering constants $a_{n, j}$ such that $\sum_{j} a_{n, j}^{2} \rightarrow 0$ as $n \rightarrow \infty$.
(C) The function $f$ is of the class $P D[0,2]$ with differential coefficients $f^{\prime}$ and $f^{\prime \prime}$.
(D) The function $f$ is continuous.

Let $u$ be a continuous function defined on the line, such that $0 \leq u \leq 1$ and such that $u(x)=1$ for $|x|$ small and $u(x)=0$ for $|x|$ large.

To such a function corresponds a Lévy splitting of $P_{n, j}=\mathscr{L}\left(X_{n, j}\right)$ of the form $P_{n, j}=\left(1-\alpha_{n, j}\right) M_{n, j}+\alpha_{n, j} N_{n, j}$, with $\left(1-\alpha_{n, j}\right) M_{n, j}(d x)=u(x) P_{n, j}(d x)$. According to Lemma 2, the centering constants $a_{n, j}=\int x M_{n, j}(d x)$ will be such that $\sum_{j} a_{n, j}^{2} \rightarrow 0$ whenever (B) is satisfied. Also, the limiting distribution $P$ has a Fourier transform which may be written

$$
\log \tilde{P}(t)=i a t-\frac{1}{2} \sigma^{2} t^{2}+\int\left[e^{i t x}-1-i t x u(x)\right] L(d x)
$$

with $a=\lim _{n} \sum_{j} a_{n, j}$.
Proposition 5. Let the conditions (A), (B), (C), (D) be satisfied. Then the distribution $R_{n}=\mathscr{L}\left\{\Sigma_{j} f\left(X_{n, j}\right)\right\}$ converges to a limit $R$ which has a Fourier transform given by the expression

$$
\log \tilde{R}(t)=i b t-\frac{1}{2}\left(f^{\prime}\right)^{2} \sigma^{2} t^{2}+\int\left[e^{i t f(x)}-1-i t f^{\prime} x u(x)\right] L(d x)
$$

with $b=a f^{\prime}+\frac{1}{2} \sigma^{2} f^{\prime \prime}$.
Proof. It is easy to check that the distributions $R_{n}$ are shift-compact. Consider also a sequence $\left\{\varepsilon_{n}\right\}, \varepsilon_{n}>0$ which tends to zero so slowly that $\sup _{j} P\left\{\left|X_{n, j}\right|>\varepsilon_{n}\right\}$ tends to zero. Let $\beta_{n, j}=P\left\{\left|X_{n, j}\right|>\varepsilon_{n}\right\}$ and let $M_{n, j}^{\prime}= \mathscr{L}\left\{X_{n, j}| | X_{n, j} \mid \leq \varepsilon_{n}\right\}$. This yields a Lévy splitting of $P_{n, j}$ in the form $P_{n, j}= \left(1-\beta_{n, j}\right) M_{n, j}^{\prime}+\beta_{n, j} N_{n, j}^{\prime}$ and a corresponding splitting for the measure $R_{n, j}= f P_{n, j}$ image of $P_{n, j}$ by $f$.

For the latter, the corresponding centering constants are numbers $b_{n, j}^{\prime}$ which may be written

$$
\begin{aligned}
b_{n, j}^{\prime} & =\int f(x) M_{n, j}^{\prime}(d x) \\
& =\int\left[x f^{\prime}+\frac{1}{2} x^{2} f^{\prime \prime}+x^{2} \rho(x)\right] M_{n, j}^{\prime}(d x)
\end{aligned}
$$

Here $\rho(x)=x^{-2}\left(f(x)-f(0)-x f^{\prime}-\frac{1}{2} x^{2} f^{\prime \prime}\right)$ tends to zero as $x \rightarrow 0$.
Since $\sum_{j} a_{n, j}^{2} \rightarrow 0$, the sums $s_{n}^{2}=\sum_{j} s_{n, j}^{2}$ with $s_{n, j}^{2}=\int x^{2} M_{n, j}^{\prime}(d x)$ remain bounded. Thus the $b_{n, j}^{\prime}$ are equivalent to $b_{n, j}^{\prime \prime}=\left(f^{\prime}\right) \int x M_{n, j}^{\prime}(d x)+\frac{1}{2}\left(f^{\prime \prime}\right) s_{n, j}^{2}$. It follows easily that $\sum_{j}\left(b_{n, j}^{\prime}\right)^{2} \rightarrow 0$.

Applying Lemma 2 again one sees that the measure $P_{n}$ is approximated by $\exp \left\{L_{n}-\left\|L_{n}\right\|\right\}$ with $L_{n}=\sum_{j} P_{n, j}$. Also the measure $R_{n}$ is approximated by $Q_{n}=\exp \left\{f L_{n}-\left\|f L_{n}\right\|\right\}$. This $Q_{n}$ has a Fourier transform $\widetilde{Q}_{n}$ given by the expression

$$
\begin{aligned}
\log \widetilde{Q}_{n}(t)= & \int\left[e^{i t f(x)}-1\right] L_{n}(d x) \\
= & \int\left[e^{i t f(x)}-1-i t x\left(f^{\prime}\right) u(x)\right] L_{n}(d x) \\
& +i t\left(f^{\prime}\right) \int x u(x) L_{n}(d x)
\end{aligned}
$$

The integral $\int x u(x) L_{n}(d x)$ is simply the sum $\sum_{j} \bar{a}_{n, j}$ with $\bar{a}_{n, j}=\int x u(x) P_{n, j}(d x)= \left(1-\alpha_{n, j}\right) a_{n, j}$. Since $\left|\Sigma_{j} \alpha_{n, j} a_{n, j}\right|^{2} \leq \sum \alpha_{n, j}^{2} \sum a_{n, j}^{2} \rightarrow 0$, the integral $\int x u(x) L_{n}(d x)$ converges to the limit $a$ which occurs in the representation of $\tilde{P}$.

For the remaining term note that as $x \rightarrow 0$ the expression

$$
\frac{1}{x^{2}}\left[\exp \{i t f(x)\}-1-i t x\left(f^{\prime}\right)-\frac{1}{2} i t x^{2}\left(f^{\prime \prime}\right)-\frac{t^{2}}{2} x^{2}\left(f^{\prime}\right)^{2}\right]
$$

tends to zero. Also, $\lim _{\varepsilon} \lim _{n} \int x^{2} L_{n}(d x)=\sigma^{2}$. Thus $\log \widetilde{Q}_{n}(t)$ converges to

$$
i t\left[a\left(f^{\prime}\right)+\frac{1}{2} \sigma^{2}\left(f^{\prime \prime}\right)\right]-\frac{1}{2}\left(f^{\prime}\right)^{2} \sigma^{2} t^{2}+\int\left[e^{i t f(x)}-1-i t x f^{\prime} u(x)\right] L_{n}(d x)
$$

This completes the proof of the proposition.
Remark. The above Proposition 5 can be applied in particular to the case where the limiting distribution $P$ is a Gaussian distribution, that is, to the case where the Lévy measure $L$ vanishes.

In this case, one can easily verify that the continuity condition ( D ) is unnecessary. In fact, one may use functions $f$ which satisfy the $P D[0,2]$ condition but are otherwise totally arbitrary. For instance, they may be allowed to take infinite values and they need not be measurable.

To terminate this section, let us mention another easy but useful fact. Consider variables $X_{n, j}$ which are independent, as before, but take their values in a space $\mathbb{R}^{k}$ instead of $\mathbb{R}$. Let $X_{n, j, r}$ be the $r$ th coordinate of $X_{n, j}$.

Proposition 6. Assume that the $X_{n, j}$ are U.A.N. and that for each $r$ the distributions $\mathscr{L}\left(\sum_{j} X_{n, j, r}\right)$ are shift-compact and approximable by Gaussian distributions. Then the same is true of the distribution of the sums $\sum_{j} X_{n, j}$ themselves.

Proof. This follows readily from the Gaussian convergence criterion given in Proposition 4.

## 6. Sums of Independent Stochastic Processes

Some of the theorems given in the preceding sections, and especially Section 5 , extend readily to variables $X_{n, j}$ which take their values in finite dimensional spaces and even certain special Banach spaces such as Hilbert spaces. However, certain arguments do not extend. To give an account of the situation would necessitate a separate volume.

However two of the techniques used here, namely symmetrization and replacement of a product $\prod P_{j}$ by the accompanying $\operatorname{exponential} \exp \left\{\sum\left(P_{j}-I\right)\right\}$, can often be used to obtain results rapidly and with a minimum of computation in situations which are of statistical interest.

One may think, for instance, of the Poissonization operation which converts empirical distribution functions into Poisson processes with independent increments. For the latter, bounds on oscillations can be readily obtained from the symmetrization arguments of P . Lévy. The technique applies just as well to empirical cumulatives obtained from independent observations which are not
identically distributed. Because of such possible applications, it appears reasonable to give a few results on this particular subject.

There are, however, several difficulties. One of them occurs from the fact that in separable Banach spaces relative compactness of $\exp \left\{\sum\left(P_{j}-I\right)\right\}$ will imply that of $\prod P_{j}$, but the converse is not generally true. The other bothersome feature is that the "natural" space for empirical cumulatives, namely the set of bounded functions which are continuous on the right and have limit on the left (càd làg functions), with the uniform norm is not a separable Banach space.

This entails various difficulties with problems of measurability. One problem can be described as follows. Let $D$ be the space of (bounded) càd làg functions on $[0,1)$, with the uniform norm. The empirical cumulative, for one observation from the Lebesgue distribution $\lambda$ on [ 0,1 ], is a $\operatorname{map} \phi$ from $[0,1]$ to $D$. There is also a certain cardinal $\mathbb{A}_{0}$ which is the first on which there is a $\sigma$-additive probability measure $\mu$, defined on all the subsets of $\mathbb{A}_{0}$, such that $\mu(A)=[\mu(A)]^{2}$ for all $A \subset \mathbb{A}_{0}$ but $\mu[\{x\}]=0$ for every point $x \in \mathbb{A}_{0}$. This cardinal is enormous, larger than $c=2^{\mathbb{A}_{\mathrm{o}}}, 2^{c}$, and everything which can be obtained from there by exponentiation as many times as you please. It follows then from a theorem of Fremlin and Solovay that there must be open subsets $G \subset D$ such that $\phi^{-1}(G)$ is not Lebesgue measurable.

The impossibility of extending the image of $\lambda$ by $\varphi$ to all Borel subsets of $D$ is also compatible with the axioms of set theory. It holds, for instance, if the power of the continuum does not exceed $\mathbb{A}_{\Omega}$ where $\Omega$ is the first uncountable ordinal.

Because of this situation several devices have been used. For instance, Skorohod used on $D$ a much weaker metric that makes it a complete separable space. One can do that in many cases, but it is awkward because for the Skorohod topology, addition is not continuous. Another possibility is to use on $D$ the $\sigma$-field generated by the balls. Then addition is not measurable. For these and other reasons we shall only give a sketch of some possibilities, referring the interested reader to treatises such as the book by Araujo and Giné and the book by D. Pollard.

Let $\mathscr{X}$ be a locally convex linear space and let $\mathscr{Y}$ be its dual. Let $F$ be a finite subset of $\mathscr{Y}$ and let $T_{F}$ be the linear map from $\mathscr{X}$ to $\mathbb{R}^{F}$ defined by the evaluations $T_{F}(x)=\{\langle y, x\rangle ; y \in F\}$. Give $\mathbb{R}^{F}$ its maximum coordinate norm and let $\Lambda_{F}$ be the space of bounded numerical functions which are defined on $\mathbb{R}^{F}$ and satisfy there a Lipschitz condition. Let $H$ be the space of functions that are defined on $\mathscr{X}$ and are of the form $\gamma=f \circ T_{F}$ for some finite set $F$ and some $f \in \Lambda_{F}$. Let us call "cylindrical measure" any bounded real valued finitely additive measure $\mu$ defined on the $\sigma$-field generated by $H$ on $\mathscr{X}$ and subject to the restriction that for any fixed $F$ the restriction of $\mu$ to the $\sigma$-field generated by the elements of $H$ issued from $\Lambda_{F}$ is $\sigma$-smooth. It amounts to the same to define $\mu$ as a linear functional on $H$, with the $\sigma$-smoothness property on the subspace of $H$ generated by any fixed $\Lambda_{F}$.

On cylindrical measures one may define a variety of metrics and topologies. One very simple one is called the half-space metric: It is obtained from the norm $\|\mu\|_{h}=\sup \{|\mu(A)| ; A=\{x \in \mathscr{X} ;\langle\varphi, x\rangle \leq a$, for some $\varphi \in \mathscr{Y}$ and some $a \in \mathbb{R}\}\}$.



It is a norm. If $\mathscr{X}$ was the real line, it would be equivalent to the KolmogorovSmirnov norm. A particular reason for using this norm is as follows: Any cylindrical probability $P$ on $\mathscr{X}$ can be generated by a linear stochastic process $\{X(y) ; y \in \mathscr{Y}\}$ such that the map $y \mapsto X(y)$ from $\mathscr{Y}$ to random variables is linear. The cylindrical probability is the "law" of the process. Such processes can be added and modified in a number of ways.

Now consider independent linear processes $X_{j} ; j=1,2, \ldots, n$ and their sum $S$. For random variables, Proposition 4 and 5 of Section 2 give bounds for certain Kolmogorov norms. Upon multiplication by a factor 2, these bounds remain valid for the half-space metric for any vector space $\mathscr{X}$. The same is true of the approximation theorem, Theorem 1 of Section 4, provided that adequate centerings are available.
(One can center the $X_{j}(y)$ individually, but that need not yield a centering with good linearity properties.) One can define other norms usable for different purposes. For instance, let $\mathscr{X}$ be a Banach space and let $B$ be the unit ball of its dual. For $F \subset B$ let $H_{1, F}$ be the subset of $H$ formed by functions $\gamma=f \circ T_{F}$ where on $\mathbb{R}^{F}$ the function $f$ satisfies $|f|<1$ and the Lipschitz condition $\left|f\left(u_{1}\right)-f\left(u_{2}\right)\right| \leq\left|u_{1}-u_{2}\right|$ (for the sup norm of $\mathbb{R}^{F}$ ). Define then a norm by

$$
\|\mu\|_{B}=\sup _{F \subset B} \sup _{\gamma \in H_{1, F}}|\langle\mu, \gamma\rangle|
$$

where, of course, $F$ denotes a finite set. This gives what could be called a weak dual Lipschitz norm on the cylindrical measures. It has been shown by Bartoszynski (1961) and more generally by E. Caby that if $\mathscr{X}$ is a separable Banach space, the weak norm $\|\cdot\|_{B}$ induces on probability measures carried by $\mathscr{X}$ the same topology as the Prohorov metric. This will follow from results given below. Let us note, however, that convergence of probability measures may be defined in other ways. One favorite is an extension of a distance once proposed by Lévy almost as follows:

Let $X_{i}$ be a process with distribution $P_{i}, i=1,2$. Let $d\left(P_{1}, P_{2}\right)$ be the infimum of numbers $\varepsilon$ such that there exist a probability space ( $\Omega, \mathscr{G}, \mu$ ) and versions $\widetilde{X}_{i}$ of $X_{i}$ defined on ( $\Omega, \mathscr{G}, \mu$ ) satisfying $\mathscr{L}\left(\widetilde{X}_{i}\right)=P_{i}$ and $E^{*} 1 \wedge \left\|\tilde{X}_{1}-\tilde{X}_{2}\right\|<\varepsilon$.

Here $\left\|\tilde{X}_{1}-\tilde{X}_{2}\right\|=\sup _{y \in B}\left\|\tilde{X}_{1}(y)-\tilde{X}_{2}(y)\right\|$. Since this need not be measurable, one takes for the expectation $E^{*}$ an upper integral. Distances of this type have been studied by Kantorovitch and Rubinstein (1961), Strassen (1965), and Dudley (1968). These authors have shown that if the $X_{i}$ are given by strongly measurable maps from a probability space to the Banach space $\mathscr{X}$, then $d\left(P_{1}, P_{2}\right)=\sup \left|\int f\left(d P_{1}-d P_{2}\right)\right|$ for a supremum taken over all real valued functions $f$ that satisfy $|f| \leq 1$ and $\left|f\left(x_{1}\right)-f\left(x_{2}\right)\right| \leq\left\|x_{1}-x_{2}\right\|$ for the norm
of $\mathscr{X}$. Another possibility, closely related to the above, will be described later. For it and for many other purposes, one needs to restrict the class of allowable
cylinder measures.
Consider the Banach space $\mathscr{X}$, its dual $\mathscr{Y}$, and the topological dual $\mathscr{Z}$ of $\mathscr{Y}$. Also let $\mathscr{Y}^{*}$ be the algebraic dual of $\mathscr{Y}$. The space $\mathscr{X}$ can be imbedded isometrically in $\mathscr{Z} \subset \mathscr{Y}^{*}$. The balls $\{z ; z \in \mathscr{Z},|z| \leq c\}$ are compact for the $w(\mathscr{Z}, \mathscr{Y})$ topology. They are the closure in $\mathscr{Y}^{*}$ for the $w\left(\mathscr{Y}^{*}, \mathscr{Y}\right)$ topology of the corresponding balls of $\mathscr{X}$. The elements of our space $H$ are uniformly continuous on $\mathscr{X}$ for the $w\left(\mathscr{Y}^{*}, \mathscr{Y}\right)$ uniform structure. Thus each $\gamma \in H$ possesses a uniquely determined continuous extension to $\mathscr{Y}^{*}$ and therefore to $\mathscr{Z} \subset \mathscr{Y}^{*}$. Let $\hat{H}$ be the space of these extensions. A bounded linear functional defined on $H$ is also a bounded linear functional on $\hat{H}$.

Let $\mu$ be a positive linear functional defined on $H$. Assume $\mu(1)=1$. Then $\mu$ will be called a weak Radon expectation if it is the restriction to $\hat{H}$ of a Radon measure on $\mathscr{Z}$ for the $w(\mathscr{Z}, \mathscr{Y})$ topology. A weak Radon expectation will be called strong on $\mathscr{X}$ (resp. $\mathscr{Z}$ ) if it is the restriction to $\hat{H}$ of a Radon measure on $\mathscr{X}$ (resp. $\mathscr{Z}$ ) with its strong topology.

As we shall see below (Lemma 1), a cylindrical probability $\mu$ yields a weak Radon expectation if and only if the corresponding process $\{X(y) ; y \in \mathscr{Y}\}$ admits a version with sample paths bounded on the unit ball $\mathscr{B}$ of $\mathscr{Y}$. In such a case one can find probability spaces $(\Omega, \mathscr{G}, P)$ and maps $Z: w \rightarrow \mathscr{Z}(w) \in \mathscr{Z}$ such that the processes $X$ and $Z$ have the same law. In fact the Radon parent $\bar{\mu}$ of $\mu$ provides such a probability space with a map that is the identity map.

Note that if $\mu$ is a weak Radon expectation on $\hat{H}$ the Radon measure $\bar{\mu}$ which is its parent is entirely determined by the values of $\mu$ on $\hat{H}$. Also, $w(\mathscr{Z}, \mathscr{Y})$ closed convex subsets of $\mathscr{Z}$ are in the domain of $\bar{\mu}$. Similarly the open balls of the type $\left\{z ;\left\|z-z_{0}\right\|<c\right\}$ in $\mathscr{Z}$ are in the domain of $\bar{\mu}$.

Finally, the addition map from $\mathscr{Z} \times \mathscr{Z}$ to $\mathscr{Z}$ is $w(\mathscr{Z}, \mathscr{Y})$ continuous. Thus, for weak Radon expectations, or the corresponding random elements, there is no difficulty in defining convolution or addition.

Weak Radon expectations have nice properties. However, in passing from processes to them, one should exercise caution. Consider, for instance, a probability space ( $\Omega, \mathscr{G}, P$ ) and a map $\omega \mapsto Z(\omega)$ from $\Omega$ to the space $\mathscr{Z}$. Assume that the map is scalarly measurable in the sense that for each $f \in \hat{H}$ the map $\omega \mapsto f[Z(\omega)]$ is $\mathscr{G}$-measurable. Then $\mu(f)=\int f[Z(\omega)] P(d \omega)$ is well determined for every $f \in \hat{H}$ as it is for every bounded $g$ such that $\omega \mapsto g[Z(\omega)]$ is $\mathscr{G}$ measurable. However it is not necessarily true, that for functions $g$ that satisfy that requirement, $\operatorname{Eg}(Z)$ is equal to the value $\bar{\mu}(g)$ given by the weak Radon extension of $\mu$.

Returning to the definition of convergence in law, let $Z_{n}$ be defined on ( $\left.\Omega_{n}, \mathscr{G}_{n}, P_{n}\right)$ with values in $\mathscr{Z}$. Assume that the $Z_{n}$ are scalarly measurable. A definition of convergence related to the Dudley distance described earlier is as follows $Z_{n} \rightarrow Z_{\infty}$ in law if for every bounded strongly continuous function $f$ defined on $\mathscr{Z}$ the upper integrals $\int^{*} f\left(Z_{n}\right) d P_{n}$ converge to $\int^{*} f\left(Z_{\infty}\right) d P$. (See Dudley, 1968.)

Note that such a definition depends strongly on the choice of spaces ( $\Omega_{n}, \mathscr{G}_{n}, P_{n}$ ). It is conceivable that its conditions would be satisfied for the
weak Radon extensions $\bar{\mu}_{n}$ of the laws $\mu_{n}$ of the processes and not for the original ( $\Omega_{n}, \mathscr{G}_{n}, P_{n}$ ). (This might conceivably even happen if $\mu_{\infty}$ is strong Radon on $\mathscr{X}$, although we do not have good examples.)

A characterization of weak Radon measures can be obtained through the following result, which is an extension of a theorem of E. Mourier. For further extensions see the work of A. de Acosta.

Consider a real vector space $\mathscr{Y}$ and its algebraic dual $\mathscr{Y}^{*}$. For each finite set $F \subset \mathscr{Y}$ let $T_{F}$ be the map from $\mathscr{Y}^{*}$ to $\mathbb{R}^{F}$ defined by $T_{F}(z)=\{\langle z, y\rangle ; y \in F\}$. Define $\Lambda_{F}$ as before and let $H$ be the set of functions defined on $\mathscr{Y}^{*}$ by expressions of the type $f \circ T_{F}$ with $f \in \Lambda_{F}, F$ finite. Now let $V$ be a convex symmetric subset of $\mathscr{Y}$. Let $P$ be a positive linear functional defined on $H$ and such that $P 1=1$.

Let us say that $P$ is $\varepsilon$-polarized by $V$ if for every finite set $F \subset V$ and every $f \in \Lambda_{F}, 0 \leq f \leq 1$ which vanishes on the unit cube $\{u ;|u| \leq 1\}$ of $\mathbb{R}^{F}$ one has $P\left(f \circ T_{F}\right) \leq \varepsilon$.

If $P$ is the distribution of a linear process $y \mapsto Z(y)$ indexed by $\mathscr{Y}$, to say that $P$ is $\varepsilon$-polarized is to say that for every finite set $F \subset V$ one has

$$
P\{\sup [|Z(y)| ; y \in F]>1\} \leq \varepsilon .
$$

Lemma 1. With the above notation, assume that $V$ is absorbing and let $K=V^{0}$ be its polar in $\mathscr{Y}^{*}$. Then $P$ is $\varepsilon$-polarized by $V$ if and only if for every $\gamma \in H, 0 \leq \gamma \leq 1$ which vanishes on $K$, one has $P \gamma \leq \varepsilon$. Equivalently, there is a probability measure $P_{1}$ which is a Radon measure on $K$ for $w\left(\mathscr{Y}^{*}, \mathscr{Y}\right)$ and a positive linear functional $P_{2}$ on $H, P_{2} 1=1$, such that $P$ is the restriction to $H$ of $(1-\varepsilon) P_{1}+\varepsilon P_{2}$.

Proof. Let $F$ be a finite subset of $V$ and let $L$ be the subset $T_{F} \mathscr{Y}^{*}$ of $\mathbb{R}^{F}$ with the norm inherited from $\mathbb{R}^{F}$. Since $V$ is absorbing the image $T_{F} K$ is a compact subset of $L$. Consider a function $f$ defined on $L$. Assume that $0 \leq f \leq 1$, that $f$ vanishes on $T_{F} K$, and that $f$ satisfies on $L$ a Lipschitz condition $\mid f\left(u_{1}\right) f\left(u_{2}\right)|\leq c| u_{1}-u_{2} \mid$. Then $f$ can be extended to the entire space $\mathbb{R}^{F}$, yielding a function $\bar{f}$ which has the same bounds as $f$ and satisfies the same Lipschitz condition. Taking the minimum of $\bar{f}$ with another suitable Lipschitz function which vanishes on the unit cube $\{u:|u| \leq 1\}$ of $\mathbb{R}^{F}$ one can replace $\bar{f}$ by a Lipschitz function $g$ such that $0 \leq g \leq 1, g=f$ on $L$ and $g=0$ on the unit cube.

Let then $\gamma$ be some element of $H$ such that $0 \leq \gamma \leq 1$ and such that $\gamma$ vanishes on $K$. This $\gamma$ has the form $\gamma=f \circ T_{F}$ for some finite set $F \subset \mathscr{Y}$ and some $f \in \Lambda_{F}$. One may assume that $0 \leq f \leq 1$. Since $V$ is absorbing, one can assume that $F \subset V$. According to the remark made above on $T_{F} \mathscr{Y}^{*}$, one can also assume that the elements of $F$ are linearly independent, so that $\mathbb{R}^{F}=T_{F} \mathscr{Y}$.

By construction $f$ vanishes on $T_{F} K$. Thus, given an $\varepsilon^{\prime}>0$, there is some neighborhood $U$ of $T_{F} K$ such that $0 \leq f(u) \leq \varepsilon^{\prime}$ for all $u \in U$. However, $T_{F} K$ being compact and convex, it is the intersection of the closed half-spaces which contain it. Thus, by compactness again, there exist elements $v_{j} ; j=1, \ldots, m$ of
the dual of $\mathbb{R}^{F}$ such that the polygonal set $S=\left\{u \in \mathbb{R}^{F} ;\left|\left\langle v_{j}, u\right\rangle\right| \leq 1\right\}$ satisfies the inclusion relations $T_{F} K \subset S \subset U$. Letting $g=f-f \wedge \varepsilon^{\prime}$ one sees that $g$ vanishes on $S$ and that $P\left(f \circ T_{F}\right) \leq P\left(g \circ T_{F}\right)+\varepsilon^{\prime}$. Thus, $\varepsilon^{\prime}$ being arbitrary, to prove the desired result it will be sufficient to show that polygonal sets $S$ satisfying the above inclusion relations can be obtained as intersections of $\mathbb{R}^{\boldsymbol{F}}= T_{\boldsymbol{F}} \mathscr{Y}$ by the unit cube in a suitable $\mathbb{R}^{\boldsymbol{F}_{1}}, F_{1} \subset V$.

To proceed, we shall give $\mathscr{Y}$ the strongest locally convex topology definable on it. This topology $\mathscr{T}$ has for basis at the origin the convex symmetric absorbing subsets of $\mathscr{Y}$.
