The dual of $(\mathscr{Y}, \mathscr{T})$ is $\mathscr{Y}^{*}$ and our set $V$ is a neighborhood of the origin.

The set $F$ used above spans a certain linear subspace $M$ of $\mathscr{Y}$. The dual of $\mathbb{R}^{F}$ is a certain linear space $M$, which can be identified to $M$ according to the diagram

$$
\begin{gathered}
\mathscr{Y}^{*} \stackrel{T_{F}}{\mapsto} \mathbb{R}^{F} \\
\mathscr{Y} \supset M \stackrel{t_{T}}{\mapsto} M_{1} .
\end{gathered}
$$

Let $\bar{V}$ be the closure of $V$ in $\mathscr{Y}$. According to the bipolar theorems one has $K^{0}=\bar{V}$ and $\left({ }^{t} T_{F}\right)^{-1} \bar{V}=\left(T_{F} K\right)^{0}$. Since all the sets are closed convex and symmetric, one can also write the second relation in the form $T_{F} K= \left(\left({ }^{t} T\right)^{-1} \bar{V}\right)^{0}$.

If $y$ belongs to $\bar{V}$, then the entire open segment $\{z ; z=t y, t \in(-1,+1)\}$ belongs to the interior $W$ of $V$ (for the topology $\mathscr{T}$ ). Thus $\bar{V} \cap M=(\overline{W \cap M})$. Obviously $\left({ }^{t} T\right)^{-1} \bar{V}=\left({ }^{t} T_{F}\right)^{-1}(\bar{V} \cap M)$. It follows that if $G$ is the open set $G=\left({ }^{t} T_{F}\right)^{-1}(W \cap M)$, then $\left({ }^{t} T_{F}\right)^{-1} \bar{V}=\bar{G}$ and therefore $T_{F} K=G^{0}$.

Consider finite sets $\Gamma$ of the form $\Gamma=F \cup F_{1}$ where $F_{1}$ is a finite subset of $W \cap M$ consisting of pionts $y_{j}={ }^{t} T x_{j}$ with $x_{j} \in G$. The set $T K$, being equal to $G^{0}$, is the intersection of the sets of the form $\left[\left({ }^{t} T\right)^{-1} \Gamma\right]^{0}$. These are compact polyhedra. Thus, for any given neighborhood $U$ of $T K$ one of them must be such that $T K \subset\left[\left({ }^{t} T\right)^{-1} \Gamma\right]^{0} \subset U$. However $\left[\left({ }^{t} T\right)^{-1} \Gamma\right]^{0}$ is also the intersection of $\mathbb{R}^{F}$ with the unit cube in the corresponding space $\mathbb{R}^{\Gamma}$. This concludes the proof of the lemma except for the assertion concerning Radon measures. The latter follows from the usual decomposition of $P$ in a part "carried by $K$ " and a part carried by the complement of $K$. Hence the result.

Corollary. Let $\mu$ be a positive linear functional defined on $H$ and such that $\langle\mu, 1\rangle=1$. It is a weak Radon expectation if and only if for every $\varepsilon>0$ there is a multiple $c B$ of the unit ball $B$ of $\mathscr{Y}$ such that $\mu$ is $\varepsilon$-polarized by $c B$.

Remark. The reader should be careful not to confuse the weak Radon expectations described above with the restrictions to $H$ of measures which are Radon measures on $\mathscr{X}$ for $w(\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{Y})$. These are already strong Radon measures on $\mathscr{X}$.

Another corollary of Lemma 1 is as follows.
Lemma 2. Let $\mu$ and $v$ be two weak Radon expectations on $H$. Assume that $\|\mu-v\|_{B} \leq \varepsilon \leq 1 / 4$. Let $K$ be a $w(\mathscr{Z}, \mathscr{Y})$ closed convex symmetric subset of
$\mathscr{Z}$. Let $K_{1}=\{z ; z \in \mathscr{Z}, \inf [\|z-t\| ; t \in K] \leq 2 \sqrt{\varepsilon}\}$. Then $\bar{v}\left(K_{1}\right) \geq \bar{\mu}(K)-\sqrt{\varepsilon}$.
Proof. Let $V$ be the closed ball $V=\{z ;\|z\| \leq 2 \sqrt{\varepsilon}\}$. Let $\left\{z_{i} ; i=1,2, \ldots, m\right\}= S$ be a finite subset of $\mathscr{Z}$ contained in the complement of $K_{1}=K+V$. For each $i$, there is some element $y_{i} \in \mathscr{Y}$ such that $\left\|y_{i}\right\|=1$ and some number $\alpha_{i}$ such that $\left\langle y_{i}, z\right\rangle \leq \alpha_{i}$ for all $z \in K$ but $\left\langle y_{i}, z\right\rangle \geq \alpha_{i}$ for all $z \in z_{i}+V$.

Let $\phi_{i}(z)$ be defined by

$$
\phi_{i}(z)=1 \wedge\left\{\left|\left\langle y_{i}, z\right\rangle\right|-\left|\left\langle y_{i}, z\right\rangle\right| \wedge \alpha\right\} .
$$

This is a Lipschitz function which vanishes on $K$ and takes at $z_{i}$ a value at least equal to $2 \sqrt{\varepsilon}$. Let $\psi_{i}=1-\left[(2 \sqrt{\varepsilon})^{-1} \phi\right] \wedge 1$. This is unity on $K$ and zero at $z$. Define $\psi_{S}=\min _{i} \psi_{i}$. This function $\psi_{S}$ can be written in the form $\psi_{S}=f \circ T_{F}$ for some finite set $F \in B$ and for some $f \in \Lambda_{F}$ such that $|f| \leq 1$ and $\left|f\left(u_{1}\right)-f\left(u_{2}\right)\right| \leq \sqrt{\varepsilon}\left|u_{1}-u_{2}\right|$.

From the definition of the distance $\|\mu-v\|_{D}$ it follows that

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left\langle v, \psi_{S}\right\rangle & \geq\left\langle\mu, \psi_{S}\right\rangle-\varepsilon^{-1 / 2}\|\mu-v\|_{D} \\
& \geq\left\langle\mu, \psi_{S}\right\rangle-\sqrt{\varepsilon} \\
& \geq \bar{\mu}(K)-\sqrt{\varepsilon}
\end{aligned}
$$

However, as the set $S$ increases, the corresponding functions $\psi_{S}$ decrease to some limit which is zero on $K_{1}^{c}$. Thus the $\left\langle v, \psi_{S}\right\rangle$ have a limit inferior to $\bar{v}\left(K_{1}\right)$. This yields the desired result.

An immediate consequence of Lemma 2 is that, for weak Radon expectations on $H$, the topology induced by the norm is stronger than the topology of pointwise convergence defined by the space of bounded functions whose restrictions to the $w(\mathscr{Z}, \mathscr{Y})$ compacts of $\mathscr{Z}$ are $w(\mathscr{Z}, \mathscr{Y})$ continuous.

The following consequence of Lemma 2 will be essential to the arguments given in the remainder of this section. For any set $K \subset \mathscr{Z}$ let $K^{\varepsilon}$ be the set $K^{\varepsilon}=\{z ;[\inf \|z-t\| ; t \in K] \leq \varepsilon\}$.

Lemma 3. Let $\mathscr{E}$ be the space of all weak Radon expectations on H. Let $\Phi$ be a filter on $\mathscr{E}$. Assume that along $\Phi$ and for each $h \in H$ the expectations $\langle v, h\rangle$ converge to a limit $\langle\phi, h\rangle$. Then the following statements are equivalent: (i) $\phi$ is a strong Radon expectation on $\mathscr{Z}$ and $\|v-\phi\|_{B}$ converges to zero along $\Phi$;
(ii) for each $\varepsilon>0$ there is a strongly compact set $K \subset \mathscr{Z}$ and a set $S \in \Phi$ such that $v\left(K^{\varepsilon}\right) \geq 1-\varepsilon$ for all $v \in S$;
(iii) for each $\varepsilon>0$ there is a strongly compact set $K \subset \mathscr{Z}$ such that for every $\alpha>0$ there is an $S_{\alpha} \in \Phi$ such that $v\left(K^{\alpha}\right) \geq 1-\varepsilon$ for all $v \in S_{\alpha}$.

Proof. If $\phi$ is a strong Radon expectation on $\mathscr{Z}$, there is a convex symmetric strongly compact set $K$ such that $\phi(K) \geq 1-\alpha^{2} / 4$. Thus (iii), and its weaker version (ii), follows by direct application of Lemma 2.

Conversely, let (ii) be satisfied. Then for given $\varepsilon>0$ for each integer $n$ there is a strongly compact symmetric convex set $K_{n}$ and a set $S_{n} \in \Phi$ such that $v \in S_{n}$ implies $v\left(C_{n}\right) \geq 1-\varepsilon 2^{-n}$ for the set $C_{n}=\left\{z:\left[\inf \|z-t\| ; t \in K_{n}\right] \leq \varepsilon 2^{-n}\right\}$.

One can assume $K_{n} \subset K_{n+1}$. Let $h, 0 \leq h \leq 1$, be an element of $H$ which vanishes on $C_{n}$. Then $\langle v, h\rangle \leq \varepsilon 2^{-n}$ for $v \in S_{n}$ and therefore $\langle\phi, h\rangle \leq \varepsilon 2^{-n}$. Thus $\phi$ is at least a weak Radon expectation on $H$ and $\phi\left(C_{n}\right) \geq 1-\varepsilon 2^{-n}$.

Let $K=\bigcap_{n} C_{n}$. Then $K$ is a strongly compact convex symmetric set such that $\phi(K) \geq 1-\varepsilon$. Note also that $K_{1} \subset K_{n} \subset C_{n}$ for each $n$. Thus $K_{1} \subset K$ and $K^{\varepsilon} \supset C_{1}$. This implies $v\left(K^{\varepsilon}\right) \geq 1-\varepsilon$ for all $v \in S_{1}$.

Now let $H_{1}$ be the subset of $H$ used to define the norm on the dual of $H$. Each $h \in H_{1}$ is such that $|h| \leq 1$ and $\left|h\left(z_{1}\right)-h\left(z_{2}\right)\right| \leq\left\|z_{1}-z_{2}\right\|$. Thus, there is a finite subset $\left\{h_{j} ; j \in J\right\}$ such that for each $h \in H_{1}$ there is an $h_{j}$ satisfying the inequality $\left|h(z)-h_{j}(z)\right|<\varepsilon$ for all $z \in K$ and therefore also $\left|h(z)-h_{j}(z)\right|<2 \varepsilon$ for all $z \in K^{\varepsilon}$. For such an $h$ one has

$$
|\langle v, h\rangle-\langle\phi, h\rangle| \leq v\left[\left(K^{\varepsilon}\right)^{c}\right]+\phi\left[\left(K^{\varepsilon}\right)^{c}\right]+\left|\left\langle v, h_{j}\right\rangle-\left\langle\phi, h_{j}\right\rangle\right| .
$$

There is a set $S \in \Phi$ such that $\sup _{j}\left|\left\langle v, h_{j}\right\rangle-\left\langle\phi, h_{j}\right\rangle\right|<\varepsilon$ for all $v \in S$. Thus $|\langle v, h\rangle-\langle\phi, h\rangle| \leq 3 \varepsilon$ for all $v \in S \cap S_{1}$, or equivalently $\|v-\phi\|_{B} \leq 3 \varepsilon$ for $v \in S \cap S_{1}$. The desired result follows.

We are now in a position to extend the symmetrization principle to the present situation. Before proceeding to its statement, let us show that there is something to prove and that the result is not valid without restriction.

Let $\left\{X_{k} ; k=1,2, \ldots\right\}$ be a sequence of independent identically distributed variables which take value ( -1 ) with probability $1 / 4$ and value 1 with probability $3 / 4$. Consider the space $m$ of bounded sequences of real numbers with the uniform norm. This is the dual of the space $l_{1}$ of summable sequences. Thus $m$ can play the role of our space $\mathscr{Z}$. Let $\left\{u_{k} ; k=1,2 \ldots\right\}$ be the ordinary basis of $m$. Explicitly, $u_{k}$ is the sequence whose $k$ th entry is unity, all the others being zero. If $\xi=\left\{\xi_{k} ; k=1,2, \ldots\right\}$ is a sequence such that $\xi_{k}^{2}=1$, define a random element of $m$ by $Z(\xi)=\sum_{k} \xi_{k} X_{k} u_{k}$. It has a distribution $\mathscr{L}[Z(\xi)]=P_{\xi}$ which is a weak Radon expectation on $m$.

It is easily verified that for any pair ( $a, b$ ) of elements of $m$ and for $\xi \neq \eta$ one has $\|\mathscr{L}(a+Z(\xi))-\mathscr{L}(b+Z(\eta))\|_{B} \geq 1 / 2$. However the convolution $P_{\xi} P_{(-\xi)}$ is independent of $\xi$. For simplicity of language we shall call the norm $\|\mu\|_{B}$ the norm attached to the metric of $\mathscr{X}$ or $\mathscr{Z}$.

Theorem 1. Let $\left\{P_{v}\right\}$ and $\left\{Q_{v}\right\}$ be two sequences of weak Radon expectations on $(H, \mathscr{Z})$. Assume that the convolution $P_{\nu} Q_{\nu}$ converges for the norm attached to the metric of $\mathscr{Z}$ to a limit $\phi$ which is a strong Radon expectation on $\mathscr{X}$. Then there are constants $a_{\nu} \in \mathscr{Z}$ with the following property. Let $P_{\nu}^{\prime}$ be $P_{\nu}$ shifted by $a_{v}$. (That is, if $P_{v}=\mathscr{L}\left(X_{v}\right)$, then $P_{v}^{\prime}=\mathscr{L}\left(X_{v}+a_{v}\right)$ ).

Then, every subsequence $\left\{P_{\nu_{k}}^{\prime}\right\}$ contains a further subsequence which converges, for the norm attached to the metric of $\mathscr{Z}$, to a limit which is a strong Radon expectation on $\mathscr{X}$.

Proof. For each integer $m$ there is a strongly compact subset $K_{m}$ of $\mathscr{X}$ such that $\phi\left(K_{m}\right) \geq 1-\left(1 / 2^{2 m+2}\right)$. One can assume that $K_{m}$ is convex and symmetric and that $K_{m} \subset K_{m+1}$. Let $C_{m}$ be the subset of $\mathscr{Z}$ formed by points which are at distance at most $2^{-m}$ of $K_{m}$ and let $\phi_{v}=P_{v} Q_{v}$.

According to Lemma 2, for each integer $n$ there is some other integer $N_{n}$ such that $v \geq N_{n}$ implies $\phi_{v}\left(C_{m}\right) \geq 1-2^{-2 m}$ for all $m \leq n$. Let $A_{m}(v)$ be the set of points $z \in \mathscr{Z}$ such that $P_{v}\left(C_{m}-z\right) \geq 1-2^{-m}$. This is a $w(\mathscr{Z}, \mathscr{Y})$ closed subset of $\mathscr{Z}$. Thus it is $Q_{\nu}$-measurable. Writing $\phi_{\nu}\left(C_{m}\right)=\int P_{\nu}\left(C_{m}-z\right) Q_{\nu}(d z)$ one sees that $Q_{v}\left[A_{m}(v)\right] \geq 1-2^{-m}$. Thus $A(v, n)=\cap\left\{A_{m}(v) ; m \leq n\right\}$ is not empty.

One can assume that the $N_{n}$ are selected so that $N_{n}<N_{n+1}$. Then to each $v$ corresponds an integer $n(v)$ defined by the relation $N_{n} \leq v<N_{n+1}$. Let $a_{v}$ be any element of $A[v, n(v)]$. Then, by construction, $P_{v}\left(C_{m}-a_{v}\right) \geq 1-2^{-m}$ for every $m \leq n(v)$.

Now let $P_{\nu}^{\prime}$ be $P_{\nu}$ shifted by $a_{\nu}$. Then $P_{\nu}^{\prime}\left(C_{m}\right) \geq 1-2^{-m}$ for all $m \leq n(\nu)$. Since $n(v) \rightarrow \infty$ as $v \rightarrow \infty$, the expectations $P_{v}^{\prime}$ satisfy the condition (ii) of Lemma 3. It follows then from Lemma 3 that if a filter finer than the tail filter of the $P_{\nu}^{\prime}$ converges pointwise on $H$ to a limit $\mu$, this limit will be a strong Radon expectation on $\mathscr{X}$ itself. In addition, the convergence takes place in the sense of the norm attached to the metric of $\mathscr{Z}$. Here we have asserted in addition that one can extract convergent subsequences. This is easily seen as follows.

Let $H_{1}$ be the subset of $H$ used to define the norm. Restricted to each $K_{m}$, the space $H_{1}$ is separable. Thus there is a countable subset $D \subset H_{1}$ which is dense in $H_{1}$ for the uniform convergence on the sets $K_{m}$. Convergence on this set $D$ already implies convergence on all of $H_{1}$. Hence the result.

Remark. The theorem asserts existence of elements $a_{\nu} \in \mathscr{Z}$ which can be used as centerings. They are not necessarily elements of $\mathscr{X}$, as can be seen by taking for $P_{v}$ the mass unity at $a_{v}$ and for $Q_{v}$ the mass unity at ( $-a_{v}$ ). Of course, if each $Q_{\nu}$ is already a strong Radon measure on $\mathscr{X}$ the $a_{\nu}$ can be taken to be elements of $\mathscr{X}$.

There are also many other situations where it is easy to see that the $a_{v}$ can be taken in $\mathscr{X}$ itself. One case is as follows. Let $S$ be any subset of the unit ball of $\mathscr{Y}$ such that $\|z\|=\sup \{|\langle z, y\rangle| ; y \in S\}$ for all $z \in \mathscr{Z}$. Let $X_{v}$ be variables with distribution $P_{v}$ on $\mathscr{Z}$. Assume that the real random variables $\left\langle X_{v}, y\right\rangle$ have medians which converge to zero uniformly for $y \in S$. Assume also that the $P_{v}$ converge pointwise on $H$ to a limit $P$ which is a centered Gaussian distribution. Then one can take $a_{v}=0$.

To see this, take a subsequence, say $\{n\} \subset\{v\}$, such that along $\{n\}$ the $P_{\nu}^{\prime}=\mathscr{L}\left(X_{\nu}+a_{\nu}\right)$ converge to a limit $P^{\prime}$. Then $P^{\prime}$ is a Gaussian measure centered at some $a \in \mathscr{Z}$. The medians of $\left\langle X_{\nu}+a_{\nu}, y\right\rangle$ converge uniformly for $y \in S$ to the limit $\langle a, y\rangle$. Thus $\left\|a_{v}-a\right\| \rightarrow 0$ and $\mathscr{L}\left(X_{v}+a\right)$ has the same limit $P^{\prime}$ as $X_{v}+a_{v}$. But then $\mathscr{L}\left(X_{v}\right)$ itself converges along $\{n\}$ to the limit $P$. The convergence takes place in the sense of the norm attached to the metric of $\mathscr{X}$ or $\mathscr{Z}$.

Another example illustrating the possibilities is as follows. Assume that the distributions $\mathscr{L}\left\{\left\langle X_{\nu}, y\right\rangle\right\}$ converge to limits $L_{y}$ uniformly for $y \in S$. Assume also that on the line the family $\left\{L_{y} ; y \in \mathscr{Y}\right\}$ is relatively compact. Take a subsequence $\{n\}$ along which the $P_{\nu}^{\prime}$ converge to a limit. Then using the identity $E \exp i t\left\langle X_{v}+a_{v}, y\right\rangle=\exp \left\{i t a_{v}\right\} E \exp \left\{i t\left\langle X_{v}, y\right\rangle\right\}$ one sees that there is some $a \in \mathscr{Z}$ such that, along $\{n\}$, one has $\left\|a_{v}-a\right\| \rightarrow 0$.

Here again one concludes that the $P_{v}=\mathscr{L}\left(X_{v}\right)$ already converge to a limit on $\mathscr{Z}$. This limit is not necessarily carried by $\mathscr{X}$ itself but by a translate of $\mathscr{X}$. However, under various conditions, such as symmetry of the limiting distribution, existence of truncated expectations which lie in $\mathscr{X}$, and so forth, one can conclude in addition that the limit is situated on $\mathscr{X}$. This should be sufficient to indicate some of the possibilities. We shall now pass to some results on the usual Poissonization operation.

Let $\left\{P_{n, j} ; j=1,2, \ldots, k_{n} ; n=1,2, \ldots\right\}$ be a double sequence of weak Radon expectations on $(H, \mathscr{Z})$. The Poissonization operation amounts to replace the convolution product $P_{n}=\prod_{j} P_{n, j}$ by the exponential $Q_{n}=\exp \left\{\sum_{j}\left(P_{n, j}-I\right)\right\}$. In finite dimensional spaces, and for variables which are suitably centered, relative compactness of the sequences $\left\{P_{n}\right\}$ and $\left\{Q_{n}\right\}$ are equivalent statements. This equivalence already breaks down in the Banach space $c_{0}$ of sequences which tend to zero at infinity. In fact, Araujo and Giné have obtained a result as follows. Let $\mathscr{X}$ be a separable space and consider Radon measures $P_{n, j}$ which are symmetric on $\mathscr{X}$. Then the relative compactness of $\left\{P_{n}\right\}$ implies that of $\left\{Q_{n}\right\}$ for all choices of the $P_{n, j}$ if and only if the space $c_{0}$ is not finitely representable in $\mathscr{X}$.

However, in the symmetric case and for separable $\mathscr{X}$, one can show that relative compactness of $\left\{Q_{n}\right\}$ always implies that of $\left\{P_{n}\right\}$. The only known method of proof of this result relies heavily on results analogous to Theorem 1 above. Thus the results to be given below will contain restrictions on the limits of the measures $Q_{n}$. It is not known whether other methods of proof could lead to more general statements.

To proceed, consider again a Banach space $\mathscr{Y}$ with dual $\mathscr{Z}$. Recall that the array $\left\{P_{\nu, j}\right\}$ is called infinitesimal or U.A.N. if for each $\varepsilon>0$ the quantities $\sup _{j} P_{v, j}\{z ;\|z\|>\varepsilon\}$ tend to zero as $v$ tends to infinity.

Theorem 2. Let $\left\{P_{v, j} ; j=1,2, \ldots, k_{v} ; v=1,2, \ldots\right\}$ be a double sequence of weak Radon expectations on ( $H, \mathscr{Z}$ ). Let $Q_{\nu}$ be the Poisson exponential $Q_{\nu}=\exp \left\{\sum_{j}\left(P_{v, j}-I\right)\right\}$. Assume that as $v$ tends to infinity the sequence $Q_{v}$ converges to a limit $Q$ for the norm attached to the metric of $\mathscr{Z}$. Assume also that the limit $Q$ is a strong Radon measure. Let $P_{v}=\prod_{j} P_{v, j}$.

Then there are elements $a_{\nu}$ of $\mathscr{Z}$ such that if $P_{\nu}^{\prime}$ is $P_{\nu}$-shifted by $a_{\nu}$, every subsequence of $\left\{P_{\nu}^{\prime}\right\}$ contains a further subsequence which converges to a strong

Radon measure on $\mathscr{Z}$. If, in addition, the $P_{v, j}$ form an infinitesimal array, convergence of $Q_{v}$ to $Q$ implies convergence of $P_{v}$ to the same limit $Q$.

The first statement in the theorem is awkward to repeat. Thus we shall abbreviate it by saying that $\left\{P_{v}\right\}$ is shift-compact with strong limits.

Proof. Let $X_{v, j, k} ; k=1,2, \ldots ; j=1,2, \ldots$ be independent variables such that $\mathscr{L}\left(X_{v, j, k}\right)=P_{v, j}$. Let $m_{v, j}$ be Poisson variables independent of the $X_{v, j, k}$ and independent among themselves. Then $P_{v}$ is the distribution of $S_{v}=\sum_{j} X_{v, j, 1}$ and $Q_{v}$ is the distribution of

$$
T_{v}=\sum_{j}\left\{\sum_{k}\left[X_{v, j, k} ; k \leq m_{n, j}\right]\right\} .
$$

Let $X_{v, j, k}^{\prime}$ be other variables which are also independent and independent of all the previous variables, but such that $\mathscr{L}\left(X_{v, j, k}^{\prime}\right)=\mathscr{L}\left(X_{v, j, k}\right)$. Let $T_{v}^{\prime}$ be the sum corresponding to $T_{v}$.

Then for any $w(\mathscr{Z}, \mathscr{Y})$ closed convex symmetric subset $K$ of $\mathscr{Z}$ one may write

$$
\operatorname{Pr}\left\{T_{v}-T_{v}^{\prime} \in(2 K)^{c}\right\} \leq 2 \operatorname{Pr}\left\{T_{v} \in K^{c}\right\}
$$

An application of the criterion (ii) of Lemma 3 shows that convergence of $Q_{v}$ implies shift-compactness with strong limit of the distributions $\mathscr{L}\left(T_{v}-T_{v}^{\prime}\right)$. Now

$$
T_{v}-T_{v}^{\prime}=\sum_{j}\left\{\sum_{k}\left[\left(X_{v, j, k}-X_{v, j, k}^{\prime}\right) ; k \leq m_{n, j}\right]\right\}
$$

is the Poissonized sum corresponding to variables $Y_{v, j}=X_{v, j}-X_{v, j}^{\prime}$ which are symmetrically distributed. An application of Theorem 1 shows that the first assertion will hold if it holds for these symmetrized variables.

Introduce independent variables $Y_{v, j, k}$ as was done previously for the $X_{v, j, k}$. Let $n_{v, j}$ be independent Poisson variables, independent of all the previously mentioned variables, and such that $E n_{v, j}=\log 2$. Let $\xi_{v, j}=\min \left(1, n_{v, j}\right)$. The sum $\bar{S}_{v}=\sum_{j} Y_{v, j}$ may be written in the form $\bar{S}_{v}=S_{v}^{0}+S_{v}^{1}$, with $S_{v}^{1}= \sum_{j}\left\{\sum_{k}\left[Y_{\nu, j, k} ; k \leq \xi_{\nu, j}\right]\right\}$ and with $S_{\nu}^{0}$ obtained similarly, replacing $\xi_{\nu, j}$ by $1- \xi_{\nu, j}$. Then $\mathscr{L}\left(S_{\nu}^{0}\right)=\mathscr{L}\left(S_{\nu}^{1}\right)$. The inequality

$$
\operatorname{Pr}\left\{S_{v}^{0}+S_{v}^{1} \in(2 K)^{c}\right\} \leq 2 \operatorname{Pr}\left\{S_{v}^{1} \in K^{c}\right\}
$$

and the criterion (ii) of Lemma 3 shows that it will be enough to prove that $S_{v}^{1}$ has the desired shift-compactness property.

Now the distribution of $\bar{T}_{v}=\sum_{j}\left\{\sum_{k}\left[Y_{v, j, k} ; k \leq n_{v, j}\right]\right\}$ is a factor of that of $T_{v}-T_{v}^{\prime}$. Thus by Theorem 1, $\left\{\mathscr{L}\left(\bar{T}_{v}\right)\right\}$ is shift-compact with strong limits. One can also write $\bar{T}_{\nu}=S_{\nu}^{1}+S_{\nu}^{2}$, where $S_{\nu}^{2}$ is the sum

$$
S_{v}^{2}=\sum_{j}\left\{\sum_{k}\left[Y_{v, j, k} ; \xi_{v, j}<k \leq n_{v, j}\right]\right\} .
$$

Given $S_{v}^{1}$, the conditional distribution of $S_{v}^{2}$ is symmetric around $S_{v}^{1}$. It follows
that for any $w(\mathscr{Z}, \mathscr{Y})$ closed convex set $K \subset \mathscr{Z}$ one has $2 \operatorname{Pr}\left[\bar{T}_{v} \in K^{c}\right] \geq \operatorname{Pr}\left[S_{\nu}^{1} \in K^{c}\right]$. Thus, if the $\bar{T}_{\nu}$ satisfy the criterion (ii) of Lemma 3, so do the $S_{\nu}^{1}$. This completes the proof of the first assertion.

For the second assertion, let $\phi_{v, j}$ be the characteristic function $\phi_{v, j}(y)= E \exp \left\{i\left\langle X_{v, j}, y\right\rangle\right\}$ defined for $y \in \mathscr{Y}$. The corresponding characteristic functions for $P_{v}$ and $Q_{v}$ are, respectively, $\widetilde{P}_{v}=\prod_{j} \phi_{v, j}$ and $\widetilde{Q}_{v}=\exp \left\{\sum_{j}\left(\phi_{v, j}-1\right)\right\}$. If the array is infinitesimal and if $Q_{v} \rightarrow Q$, then $\widetilde{Q}_{v} \rightarrow \widetilde{Q}$ and $\widetilde{Q}_{v}-\widetilde{P}_{v} \rightarrow 0$ uniformly on the balls of $\mathscr{Y}$.

Thus we are in a situation where the Remark following Theorem 1 is applicable, showing that $P_{v}$ tends to a limit which cannot be other than $Q$ itself. This concludes the proof of the theorem.

In the preceding arguments, we have asserted or used the existence of appropriate centerings. It is often desirable to have some approximate evaluation of the centering constant. Thus we shall end by a result of Lévy which gives such an indication.

Proposition 1. Let $X$ be a random vector whose distribution is a weak Radon expectation on $(H, \mathscr{Z})$. For each $y \in \mathscr{Y}$, let $\mu(y)$ be a median of $\langle y, X\rangle$. Let $X^{\prime}$ be independent of $X$, with $\mathscr{L}(X)=\mathscr{L}\left(X^{\prime}\right)$. Then for any set $A \subset \mathscr{Y}$ one has

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{2} P\left\{\sup _{y \in A}|\langle y, X\rangle-\mu(y)| \geq b\right\} & \leq P\left\{\sup _{y \in A}\left|\left\langle y, X-X^{\prime}\right\rangle\right| \geq b\right\} \\
& \leq 2 P\left\{\sup _{y \in A}|\langle y, X\rangle-\mu(y)| \geq \frac{b}{2}\right\}
\end{aligned}
$$

Proof. Since it is assumed that $\mathscr{L}(X)$ is a weak Radon expectation, the result holds for an arbitrary $A$ if and only if it holds for finite sets.

In other words, it is enough to show that if $X_{j} ; j=1,2, \ldots, n$ is a finite set of real random variables with zero medians, then

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{2} P\left\{\sup \left|X_{j}\right| \geq b\right\} & =P\left\{\sup \left|X_{j}-X_{j}^{\prime}\right| \geq b\right\} \\
& \leq 2 P\left\{\sup \left|X_{j}\right| \geq \frac{b}{2}\right\}
\end{aligned}
$$

To show this, let $k$ be such that $\left|X_{j}\right|<b$ for $j<k$ but $\left|X_{k}\right| \geq b$. Given $X_{j}$; $j \leq k$ the event $X_{k} X_{k}^{\prime} \leq 0$ has a probability at least one-half. Thus

$$
P\left\{\sup \left|X_{j}\right| \geq b \text { and } X_{k} X_{k}^{\prime} \leq 0\right\} \geq \frac{1}{2} P\left\{\sup \left|X_{j}\right| \geq b\right\}
$$

However, $X_{k} X_{k}^{\prime} \leq 0$ implies $\left|X_{k}-X_{k}^{\prime}\right| \geq b$.
This gives the first inequality. For the second note that $\left|X_{j}\right|<b / 2$ and $\left|X_{j}^{\prime}\right|< b / 2$ implies $\left|X_{j}-X_{j}^{\prime}\right|<b$. This completes the proof of the proposition.

Remark. The above proof has been given in somewhat more detail than is really necessary for the following reason.

It is sometimes inconvenient to use exact values of medians. One can still obtain usable inequalities in other circumstances. Indeed, what the proof shows is this. Suppose that $k$ is the first of the indices $j$ for which $\left|X_{j}\right| \geq b$.

Assume that given $X_{j}$ for $j \leq k$ the conditional probability that $X_{k} X_{k}^{\prime} \leq 0$ is at least $\alpha>0$ then



$$
\alpha P\left\{\sup \left|X_{j}\right| \geq b\right\} \leq P\left\{\sup \left|X_{j}-X_{j}^{\prime}\right| \geq b\right\}
$$

Since here the $X_{j}$ and $X_{j}^{\prime}$ are independent, one can take for $\alpha$ the minimum of the probabilities $P\left[X_{j}^{\prime} \leq 0\right]$ and $P\left[X_{j}^{\prime} \geq 0\right]$.

## CHAPTER 16

## Independent Observations

## 1. Introduction

In this chapter we consider the situation where the statistician observes independent variables. In more formal terms, we consider experiments $\mathscr{E}=\left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ which are direct products of components $\mathscr{E}_{j}=\left\{p_{\theta, j} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$. Except as specifically mentioned otherwise, we limit ourselves to the case where each component $\mathscr{E}_{j}$ by itself yields little information.

Section 2 deals with binary experiments. It gives a number of results concerning the limiting distributions of likelihood ratios, links them with distributions of sums of the likelihood ratios or their square roots. It contains necessary and sufficient conditions for contiguity. It ends by a remark on joint limiting distributions of sums $\sum_{j} \Lambda_{j, n}, \sum_{j} Z_{j, n}$ for variables $Z_{j, n}$ defined on the component experiments.

Section 3 gives necessary and sufficient conditions for the asymptotic normality of logarithms of likelihood ratios and for the asymptotic normality of experiments. Section 4 collects a few lemmas relating distances to existence of tests.

Section 5 is devoted to proofs of existence of estimates which have a finite minimax risk when the loss function is obtained from sums of Hellinger distances. Section 6 gives bounds on the risk of Bayes estimates and estimates related to the maximum probability estimates of Weiss and Wolfowitz.

Section 7 gives a bound on the deviation between empirical cumulative and the average of the cumulatives of the observations.

Throughout the main assumptions placed on the components $\mathscr{E}_{j}$ are the assumptions (A), (B) stated in Section 2. However, occasionally, we give some refinements relative to the case where the $\mathscr{E}_{j}$ are all equivalent.

Further results, relative to the case where one observes independent identically distributed variables and let their number tend to infinity, are deferred to Chapter 17.

## 2. Limiting Distributions for Likelihood Ratios

Let $\mathscr{E}_{n}=\left(P_{0, n}, P_{1, n}\right)$ be a binary experiment. Assume that $\mathscr{E}_{n}$ is the direct product of experiments $\mathscr{E}_{j, n}$ with $\mathscr{E}_{j, n}=\left(p_{0, j, n}, p_{1, j, n}\right)$. Then, the Hellinger
affinity between $P_{0, n}$ and $P_{1, n}$ is the product $\rho\left(P_{0, n}, P_{1, n}\right)=\prod_{j} \rho\left(p_{0, j, n}, p_{1, j, n}\right)$ of the affinities between the components. Equivalently the Hellinger distances satisfy the relation

$$
1-H^{2}\left(P_{0, n}, P_{1, n}\right)=\prod_{j}\left[1-H^{2}\left(p_{0, j, n}, p_{1, j, n}\right)\right]
$$

It follows that the sequence $\left\{\mathscr{E}_{n}\right\}$ will not contain subsequences which converge to the perfect experiment if and only if there is some number $\beta>0$ such that $\prod_{j}\left\{1-H^{2}\left(p_{0, j, n}, p_{1, j, n}\right)\right\} \geq \beta$ for all values of $n$. Letting $h_{j, n}= H\left(p_{0, j, n}, p_{1, j, n}\right)$ for simplicity, this reads $\prod_{j}\left(1-h_{j, n}^{2}\right) \geq \beta$ and implies in particular $h_{j, n}^{2} \leq 1-\beta$.

Taking $c \geq|\log \beta|(1-\beta)^{-1}$ one can write

$$
\exp \left\{-c \sum_{j} h_{j, n}^{2}\right\} \leq \prod_{j}\left(1-h_{j, n}^{2}\right) \leq \exp \left\{-\sum_{j} h_{j, n}^{2}\right\} .
$$

This yields the following easy lemma.
Lemma 1. Assume that the sequence $\left\{\mathscr{E}_{n}\right\}$ does not contain any subsequences which tend to the perfect experiment. Then there are numbers $\beta>0$ and $b<\infty$ such that
(i) $\sup _{j, n} h_{j, n}^{2} \leq 1-\beta$
(ii) $\sup _{n} \sum_{j} h_{j, n}^{2} \leq b$.

Conversely if the above conditions (i) and (ii) are satisfied, no subsequence of $\left\{\mathscr{E}_{n}\right\}$ can converge to the perfect experiment.

Under the conditions of the preceding lemma it may still happen that all the $\mathscr{E}_{j, n}$ are trivial except one, say $\mathscr{E}_{1, n}$. In such a case the product structure of $\mathscr{E}_{n}$ is illusory. Thus we shall consider below sequences $\mathscr{E}_{n}$ subject to the following assumptions.

Assumption (A). As $n \rightarrow \infty$, the quantity $\sup _{j} h_{j, n}$ tends to zero.
Assumption (B). There is a number $b<\infty$ such that $\sup _{n} \sum_{j} h_{j, n}^{2} \leq b$. Clearly, Assumption (A) means that any $\mathscr{E}_{j, n}$ taken individually provides a negligible amount of information. Under this condition, Assumption (B) means that $\mathscr{E}_{n}$ stays away from the perfect binary experiment in which the measures are disjoint.

For each $j$ let $n_{j}$ be a Poisson variable which has expectation unity. Take the $n_{j}$ so that they are an independent set of variables. Then one can construct a Poissonized version $\mathscr{F}_{j, n}$ of $\mathscr{E}_{j, n}$ by first observating $n_{j}$ and replicating $\mathscr{E}_{j, n}$ a number of times equal to the value of $n_{j}$. The following lemma is immediate.

Lemma 2. Let the $\mathscr{E}_{j, n}$ satisfy Assumption (A). Then the distance between $\mathscr{E}_{n}=\prod_{j} \mathscr{E}_{j, n}$ and the accompanying infinitely divisible experiment $\mathscr{F}_{n}= \prod_{j} \mathscr{F}_{j, n}$ tends to zero as $n \rightarrow \infty$.

Proof. Let $\phi_{j, n}$ be the Hellinger transform of $\mathscr{E}_{j, n}$. This is logarithmically convex on $[0,1]$. Therefore $\phi_{j, n}(\alpha) \geq\left(1-h_{j, n}^{2}\right)^{2}$ for all $\alpha \in[0,1]$. It follows then from Chapter 9, Section 5, Lemma 5 that the Hellinger transforms of $\mathscr{E}_{n}$ and $\mathscr{F}_{n}$ differ by no more than $e \sup _{j} h_{j, n}^{2}$. The result follows.

Let $\Lambda_{j, n}=\log d p_{1, j, n} / d p_{0, j, n}$ considered as a random element with values in $[-\infty,+\infty)$ for the distribution $\mu_{j, n}$ induced by $p_{0, j, n}$. The Hellinger transform of $\mathscr{F}_{j, n}$ has the form

$$
\psi_{j, n}(\alpha)=\exp \left\{\int\left(e^{\alpha x}-1\right) \mu_{j, n}(d x)\right\}
$$

for all $\alpha \in(0,1)$. Thus the Hellinger transform of $\mathscr{F}_{n}$ is given by

$$
\psi_{n}(\alpha)=\exp \left\{\int\left(e^{\alpha x}-1\right) \mu_{n}(d x)\right\}
$$

with $\mu_{n}=\sum_{j} \mu_{j, n}$, for all $\alpha \in(0,1)$.
For various technical reasons it is not always convenient to study the logarithms $\Lambda_{j, n}$ directly. We shall also use several functions of them, and in particular the functions

$$
X_{j, n}=\exp \left\{\Lambda_{j, n}\right\}-1=\frac{d p_{1, j, n}}{d p_{0, j, n}}-1
$$

and the functions $Y_{j, n}=\left[d p_{1, j, n} / d p_{0, j, n}\right]^{1 / 2}-1$.
Considering the $Y_{j, n}$ as random variables for the distribution induced by $p_{0 . j, n}$ one sees immediately that $E Y_{j, n}=-h_{j, n}^{2}$ and $E Y_{j, n}^{2}=2 h_{j, n}^{2}-\beta_{j, n}$, where $\beta_{j, n}$ is the mass of the part of $p_{1, j, n}$ which is $p_{0, j, n}$-singular.

Assumption (A), above, means that the $Y_{j, n}$ form an infinitesimal array. Assumption (B) implies that the sum $\sum_{j} Y_{j, n}$ has a bounded expectation and a bounded second moment or variance. Thus the sequence of distributions $\mathscr{L}\left(\sum_{j} Y_{j, n}\right)$ is relatively compact. The $Y_{j, n}$ possess an additional property which simplifies considerably the application of central limit theorems. It is as follows.

Lemma 3. Let $V$ be a neighborhood of the origin of the line. For each pair ( $j, n$ ) let $u_{j, n}$ be a function which is unity on $V$ and is otherwise such that $0 \leq u_{j, n} \leq 1$. Let $Y_{j, n}^{*}=Y_{j, n} u_{j, n}\left(Y_{j, n}\right)$.

Then, when the assumptions (A) and (B) are satisfied the sum $\sum_{j}\left|E Y_{j, n}^{*}\right|^{2}$ tends to zero as $n \rightarrow \infty$.

Proof. Note first that $E Y_{j, n}=-h_{j, n}^{2}$. Thus $\sum_{j}\left|E Y_{j, n}\right|^{2} \leq\left(\sup _{j} h_{j, n}^{2}\right) \sum_{j} h_{j, n}^{2}$ tends to zero as $n \rightarrow \infty$. Now let $v_{j, n}=1-u_{j, n}$. On the support of $v_{j, n}$ the variable $Y_{j, n}$ satisfies the inequality $\left|Y_{j, n}\right| \geq \varepsilon$ for some $\varepsilon$ which depends on $V$ only.

Thus

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left|E\left\{Y_{j, n} v_{j, n}\left(Y_{j, n}\right)\right\}\right|^{2} & \leq \frac{1}{\varepsilon^{2}}\left|E\left\{\left|Y_{j, n}^{2} v_{j, n}\left(Y_{j, n}\right)\right|\right\}\right|^{2} \\
& \leq \frac{1}{\varepsilon^{2}}\left|E Y_{j, n}^{2}\right|^{2} \\
& \leq \frac{4}{\varepsilon^{2}} h_{j, n}^{4}
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore $\sum_{j}\left|E\left\{Y_{j, n} v_{j, n}\left(Y_{j, n}\right)\right\}\right|^{2}$ does tend to zero as $n \rightarrow \infty$. The result as stated then follows from the inequality

$$
\left|E\left\{Y_{j, n} u_{j, n}\left(Y_{j, n}\right)\right\}\right|^{2} \leq 2\left|E Y_{j, n}\right|^{2}+2\left|E\left\{Y_{j, n} v_{j, n}\left(Y_{j, n}\right)\right\}\right|^{2}
$$

A consequence of this lemma is that to obtain the limiting distribution of $\sum_{j} Y_{j, n}$ there is no need to bother about centerings at truncated expectations. If $M_{j, n}=\mathscr{L}\left(Y_{j, n}\right)$ one approximates $\mathscr{L}\left(\sum_{j} Y_{j, n}\right)$ by $\exp \left\{\sum_{j}\left(M_{j, n}-I\right)\right\}$. The variance of the Gaussian component in the limit is given by $\lim _{\varepsilon \rightarrow 0} \lim _{n} E Y_{j, n}^{2} I\left[\left|Y_{j, n}\right|<\varepsilon\right]$.

The fact that the sum of second moments of the $Y_{j, n}$ remain bounded implies also that one can write the Khintchin-Lévy expressions in a simpler form. If $M$ is the limit of the Lévy measures $M_{n}$, the limiting distribution $P$ of $\sum_{j} Y_{j, n}$ has a Fourier transform which may be written

$$
\log \widetilde{P}(t)=i a t-\frac{\sigma^{2}}{2} t^{2}+\int\left(e^{i t y}-1-i t y\right) M(d y)
$$

where $a$ is the limit of $\sum_{j} E\left(Y_{j, n}\right)$, that is, $a=-\lim _{n} \sum_{j} h_{j, n}^{2}$. We are also in a situation where Proposition 5 of Section 5, Chapter 15 is directly applicable.

For $P D[0,2]$ functions from $[-1,+\infty)$ to $(-\infty,+\infty)$ Proposition 5, Section 5, Chapter 15 yields the following.

Proposition 1. Let Assumptions (A) and (B) be satisfied. Let $f$ be a continuous map from $[-1,+\infty)$ to $(-\infty,+\infty)$. Assume that $f$ belongs to the class $P D[0,2]$ with differential coefficients at zero equal to $f^{\prime}$ and $f^{\prime \prime}$.

Assume also that $\mathscr{L}\left(\sum Y_{j, n}\right)$ converges to a limit $P$ whose Fourier transform $\widetilde{P}$ has the expression

$$
\log \widetilde{P}(t)=i a t-\frac{1}{2} \sigma^{2} t^{2}+\int\left[e^{i t y}-1-i t y\right] M(d y)
$$

Then $\mathscr{L}\left\{\sum_{j} f\left(Y_{j, n}\right)\right\}$ converges to a limit $P_{f}$ whose Fourier transform has the expression

$$
\log \widetilde{P}_{f}(t)=i b_{f} t-\frac{1}{2} \sigma^{2} t^{2}\left(f^{\prime}\right)^{2}+\int\left[e^{i t f(y)}-1-i t y f^{\prime}\right] M(d y)
$$

with $b_{f}=a f^{\prime}+\frac{1}{2} \sigma^{2} f^{\prime \prime}$.
Proof. This can be seen by direct application of Chapter 15, Section 5, Proposition 5 if one notes that in the present case there is no need to introduce the function called $u$ in that proposition. Note that the function used in the Lévy integral is taken equal to $\exp \{\operatorname{itf}(y)\}-1-i t y f^{\prime}$, not to $\exp \{i t f(y)\}-1-\operatorname{itf}(y)$. To be able to use the latter form one may need to reintroduce the function $u$ mentioned above.

Remark. The function $f$ used here is supposed to be finite. Thus Proposition 1 does not cover the transformation $y \mapsto 2 \log (1+y)$ which yields the likelihood ratios. For these it is easier to proceed directly, using Lemma 2 above. However, one could also extend the validity of Proposition 1 as follows.

The set $[-\infty,+\infty)$ can be considered as an additive semigroup with the usual topology induced by the transformation $y \mapsto 2 \log (1+y)$. Convergence of measures on $[-\infty,+\infty)$ can be defined by saying that $\mu_{n} \rightarrow \mu_{0}$ if $\int \gamma d \mu_{n} \rightarrow \int \gamma d \mu_{0}$ for all functions $\gamma$ in the space $C^{b}\{[-\infty,+\infty)\}$ of bounded continuous functions on $[-\infty,+\infty)$. The logarithms $\Lambda_{j, n}$ are variables with values in the semigroup $[-\infty,+\infty)$.

One can readily extend the definition of the class $P D[0,2]$ by saying that a function $f$ from $[-1,+\infty)$ to $[-\infty,+\infty)$ belongs to $\mathrm{PD}[0,2]$ if $f(0)=0$ and if $f$ is twice Peano differentiable at zero. Let $f$ be a continuous $P D[0,2]$ function from $[-1,+\infty)$ to $[-\infty,+\infty)$. Then the set $G=\{y:|f(y)|<\infty\}$ is an open neighborhood of zero in $[-1,+\infty)$.

If the other conditions of Proposition 1 are satisfied, the sum $\sum_{j} f\left(Y_{j, n}\right)$ will still have a limiting distribution $P_{f}$ on $[-\infty,+\infty)$. It can easily be verified that $P_{f}$ is the convolution of two terms, say $Q_{0}$ and $Q_{1}$, as follows.

The term $Q_{0}$ is carried by ( $-\infty,+\infty$ ). Its Fourier transform has the expression

$$
\log \tilde{Q}_{0}(t)=i b_{f} t-\frac{1}{2}\left(f^{\prime}\right)^{2} \sigma^{2} t^{2}+\int_{G}\left[e^{i t f(y)}-1-i t y f^{\prime}\right] M(d y)
$$

The term $Q_{1}$ is a probability measure which gives mass $e^{-\lambda}$ to zero and mass $1-e^{-\lambda}$ to $-\infty$, for $\lambda=M\left(G^{c}\right)$.

Since the

$$
X_{j, n}=\frac{d p_{1, j, n}}{d p_{0, j, n}}-1
$$

are often useful, we shall give the details of the passages between the limiting distributions $Q$ of $\sum_{j} X_{j, n}$ and $P$ of $\sum Y_{j, n}$. Here the $P D(0,2)$ function used is the function $f(y)=(1+y)^{2}-1=2 y+y^{2}$. The differential coefficients are $f^{\prime}=2$ and $f^{\prime \prime}=2$. Thus if the Fourier transform of $P$ has the form

$$
\log \widetilde{P}(t)=i a t-\frac{1}{2} \sigma^{2} t^{2}+\int\left(e^{i t y}-1-i t y\right) M(d y)
$$

the Fourier transform of $Q$ has the form

$$
\log \widetilde{Q}(t)=i\left(2 a+\sigma^{2}\right) t-2 \sigma^{2} t^{2}+\int\left[e^{i t\left(2 y+y^{2}\right)}-1-2 i t y\right] M(d y)
$$

The measure $M$ is a limit of Lévy measures $M_{n}$ such that $\sup \int y^{2} M_{n}(d y)< \infty$. Thus $\int y^{2} M(d y)<\infty$. It follows that one could introduce a term ity ${ }^{2}$ under the integral sign. Thus if $N$ is the limit of the Lévy measures $N_{n}=\sum_{j} \mathscr{L}\left(X_{j, n}\right)$ one could also rewrite the preceding expression in the form

$$
\log \widetilde{Q}(t)=i b t-2 \sigma^{2} t^{2}+\int\left[e^{i t x}-1-i t x\right] N(d x)
$$

with $b=2 a+\sigma^{2}+\int y^{2} M(d y)$.
Here $a$ is the limit of the integrals $\int y M_{n}(d y)$. However, because of accumulation of mass around zero, it is not usually equal to $\int y M(d y)$. Thus one cannot replace $2 a+\int y^{2} M(d y)$ by the simpler $\int x N(d x)$.

As already mentioned, Proposition 1 does not allow passage from the $Y_{j, n}$ to the $\Lambda_{j, n}$. One could use the more general version given in the remark which follows Proposition 1, but it is simpler to argue directly as follows.

Proposition 2. Let assumptions (A) and (B) be satisfied. Let $\mathscr{L}\left(\sum Y_{j, n}\right)$ have a limit $P$ with Fourier transform

$$
\log \widetilde{P}(t)=i a t-\frac{1}{2} \sigma^{2} t^{2}+\int\left[e^{i t y}-1-i t y\right] M(d y)
$$

Then $\mathscr{L}\left(\sum_{j} \Lambda_{j, n}\right)$ converges to a limit $\mathscr{L}(\Lambda)$ such that for all $\alpha \in(0,1)$ one has

$$
\log E e^{\alpha \Lambda}=\left(2 a-\sigma^{2}\right) \alpha+2 \sigma^{2} \alpha^{2}+\int\left[(1+y)^{2 \alpha}-1-2 \alpha y\right] M(d y)
$$

Proof. The limit $E e^{\alpha \Lambda}$ is the limit of the Hellinger transforms of the experiments $\mathscr{E}_{n}=\left\{P_{0, n}, P_{1, n}\right\}$. According to Lemma 2 one can replace those transforms by the transforms of the corresponding Poissonized experiments. These have the form

$$
\psi_{n}(\alpha)=\exp \left\{\int\left(e^{\alpha x}-1\right) \mu_{n}(d x)\right\}
$$

where $\mu_{n}=\sum \mathscr{L}\left(\Lambda_{j, n}\right)$. Thus they can be rewritten in the form

$$
\psi_{n}(\alpha)=\exp \left\{\int\left[(1+y)^{2 \alpha}-1\right] M_{n}(d y)\right\}
$$

with $M_{n}=\sum \mathscr{L}\left(Y_{j, n}\right)$. The result is then obtainable by writing

$$
\int\left[(1+y)^{2 \alpha}-1\right] M_{n}(d y)=-2 \alpha \sum_{j} h_{j, n}^{2}+\int\left[(1+y)^{2 \alpha}-1-2 \alpha y\right] M_{n}(d y)
$$

and passing to the limit.
The foregoing Proposition 1 allows passage from the sums $\sum_{j} Y_{j, n}$ to sums $\sum_{j} f\left(Y_{j, n}\right)$. There are many situations where passage in the reverse direction is needed. For instance, one may want to pass from $\sum X_{j, n}$ to $\sum Y_{j, n}$. That such a passage is possible is indicated by the following Lemma 4 and by Proposition 3.

Lemma 4. Let assumptions (A) and (B) be satisfied. Let $f$ be a continuous map from $[-1, \infty)$ to $[-\infty,+\infty)$. Assume that (i) $f$ is of the class $\mathrm{PD}[0,2]$, (ii) $f$ is injective, and (iii) the differential coefficient $f^{\prime}$ of $f$ at zero does not vanish. Then if the distributions $\mathscr{L}\left\{\sum f\left(Y_{j, n}\right)\right\}$ converge to a limit so do the
distributions $\mathscr{L}\left\{\sum Y_{j, n}\right\}$, and the two are related as described in Proposition 1 or the remark which follows it.

Proof. Since (A) and (B) are satisfied, the sequence $\mathscr{L}\left\{\sum\left(Y_{j, n}\right)\right\}$ is relatively compact. For a convergent subsequence, the limits are related as in Proposition 1 or its extension. However if $f^{\prime} \neq 0$ and if $f$ is injective, the expressions given there for the limit of $\mathscr{L}\left\{\sum f\left(Y_{j, n}\right)\right\}$ determine uniquely the terms $a, \sigma^{2}$, and $M$. Hence the result.

This lemma can be applied in particular to the logarithms of likelihood ratios $\Lambda_{n}=\sum_{j} \Lambda_{j, n}$. However here one can, in addition, check ( A ) and (B) easily. Condition (A) is equivalent to the statement that the array $\left\{\Lambda_{j, n}\right\}$ is infinitesimal. Then condition (B) just says that the limiting distribution of $\Lambda_{n}$ is not entirely concentrated at $-\infty$.

The preceding Lemma 4 still makes direct use of the conditions (A) and (B). It is sometimes possible to proceed without them. The following proposition gives a particular result in this direction.

Proposition 3. Let $f$ be a continuous strictly increasing map from $[-1, \infty)$ to $(-\infty,+\infty)$. Assume that $f$ belongs to the class PD $[0,2]$. Assume in addition that $f(y) \rightarrow \infty$ as $y \rightarrow \infty$ and that $f^{\prime} \neq 0$. Suppose also that there is some number $\alpha>0$ such that for all $(j, n)$ one has $E\left\{f\left(Y_{j, n}\right) I\left[Y_{j, n} \leq \alpha\right]\right\}<0$. Then, if the array $\left\{f\left(Y_{j, n}\right)\right\}$ is infinitesimal and if $\mathscr{L}\left\{\sum_{j} f\left(Y_{j, n}\right)\right\}$ converges, so does $\mathscr{L}\left\{\sum_{j} Y_{j, n}\right\}$, and the limits are related as in Proposition 1.

Proof. If $\left\{f\left(Y_{j, n}\right)\right\}$ is infinitesimal, so is $Y_{j, n}$. Thus under the conditions given
here, Assumption (A) is satisfied. The convergence of the distributions of $\sum_{j} f\left(Y_{j, n}\right)$ implies also that the Lévy measures $M_{n}=\sum_{j} \mathscr{L}\left(Y_{j, n}\right)$ converge to a limit $M$ in the sense that $\int \gamma d M_{n} \rightarrow \int \gamma d M$ for every bounded continuous function which vanishes on a neighborhood of zero.

Since $f$ is continuous, it is bounded on $[-1, \alpha]$. Let $m_{j, n}$ be the truncated expectation $m_{j, n}=E f\left(Y_{j, n}\right) I\left[Y_{j, n} \leq \alpha\right]$. Then, by the central limit theorems of Chapter 15, Section 5, the sum $\left|\sum_{j} m_{j, n}\right|$ remains bounded. Since the $m_{j, n}$ are all negative, this implies that $\sum_{j} m_{j, n}^{2} \rightarrow 0$. In this argument one may take $\alpha>0$ as small as one wishes. Also, if $\varepsilon>0$, then the terms $m_{j, n}^{\prime}= \operatorname{Ef}\left(Y_{j, n}\right) I\left[Y_{j, n}<-\varepsilon\right]$ are always such that $\sum\left(m_{j, n}^{\prime}\right)^{2} \rightarrow 0$.

It follows that for every $\varepsilon>0$ the sum of squares of the truncated expectations $m_{j, n}(\varepsilon)=E f\left(Y_{j, n}\right) I\left[\left|Y_{j, n}\right| \leq \varepsilon\right]$ converges to zero. Therefore in the central limit theorem, truncated variances may be replaced by the truncated second moments $\sigma_{j, n}^{2}=E\left[f\left(Y_{j, n}\right)\right]^{2} I\left\{\left|Y_{j, n}\right| \leq \varepsilon\right\}$, and $\sum_{j} \sigma_{j, n}^{2}$ must remain bounded. Now, near zero, $f$ has an expansion $f(y)=y f^{\prime}+\left(y^{2} / 2\right) f^{\prime \prime}+y^{2} \rho(y)$ with $\rho(y) \rightarrow 0$ as $y \rightarrow 0$. Therefore, if $\varepsilon$ is small enough and $|y| \leq \varepsilon$, one can assert that $|f(y)| \geq \frac{1}{2}|y|\left|f^{\prime}\right|$. It follows that $\sum_{j} E Y_{j, n}^{2} I\left[\left|Y_{j, n}\right| \leq \varepsilon\right]$ remains bounded. Let $Z_{j, n}=Y_{j, n} I\left\{\left|Y_{j, n}\right| \leq \varepsilon\right\}$. The expansion of $f$ yields also that

$$
m_{j, n}(\varepsilon)=E f\left(Z_{j, n}\right)=\left(E Z_{j, n}\right) f^{\prime}+\frac{1}{2}\left(E Z_{j, n}^{2}\right) f^{\prime \prime}+\varepsilon^{\prime} E Z_{j, n}^{2} .
$$

Since $\sum m_{j, n}(\varepsilon)$ is bounded and since $f^{\prime} \neq 0$, one concludes from this that $\Sigma_{j} E Z_{j, n}$ remains bounded. Taking squares, it follows also that $\sum\left(E Z_{j, n}\right)^{2}$ does tend to zero.

Thus the central limit theorem applies directly to the $Y_{j, n}$ without recenterings. It follows, in particular, that the sum of truncated expectations $a_{j, n}= E Y_{j, n} I\left[\left|Y_{j, n}\right| \leq 1\right]$ must remain bounded. However, $a_{j, n} \leq-h_{j, n}^{2}$. Therefore $\sup _{n} \sum_{j} h_{j, n}^{2}<\infty$. Thus condition (B) is also satisfied. This concludes the proof of the proposition.

Proposition 2, above, can be applied in particular to functions of the likelihood ratios such as $X_{j, n}=d p_{1, j, n} / d p_{0, j, n}-1$ or other powers such as $\left(d p_{1, j, n} / d p_{0, j, n}\right)^{\alpha}-$ 1 with $\alpha \in(0,1]$.

Passages to the limit for the $\sum Y_{j, n}$ introduce limit Lévy measures $M$ and terms $a, \sigma^{2}$. One may wonder whether these satisfy special conditions. To express these, note that $a$ is the limit of $-\sum h_{j, n}^{2}$. The limit of the variances $\sum h_{j, n}^{2}\left(2-h_{j, n}^{2}\right)-\beta_{n, j}$ is always larger than the limiting variance $\sigma^{2}+ \int y^{2} M(d y)$. Thus it is necessary that $M$ be carried by $[-1, \infty)$ and that $b=2 a+\sigma^{2}+\int y^{2} M(d y) \leq 0$. Otherwise the terms $a, \sigma^{2}$, and $M$ are unrestricted.

Let us pass now to the conditions they must satisfy if the sequences $\left\{P_{0, n}\right\}$, $\left\{P_{1, n}\right\}$ are contiguous. Here the term $b=2 a+\sigma^{2}+\int y^{2} M(d y)$ will play a special role. Recall that it is also the term which enters in the Fourier transform of the limit of the $\mathscr{L}\left(\sum X_{j, n}\right)$. This has the form

$$
\log \widetilde{Q}(t)=i b t-2 \sigma^{2} t^{2}+\int\left[e^{i x t}-1-i x t\right] N(d x)
$$

Proposition 4. Let conditions (A) and (B) be satisfied. Assume that $\mathscr{E}_{n}= \left\{P_{0, n}, P_{1, n}\right\}$ converges to a limit $\mathscr{E}=\left\{P_{0}, P_{1}\right\}$. Then the mass $m$ of the part of $P_{1}$ which
is dominated by $P_{0}$ can be written in any one of the following two equivalent forms:
(i) $m=e^{b}$,
(ii) $m=\lim _{c \rightarrow \infty} \lim _{n} \prod_{j}\left[1-\alpha_{j, n}(c)\right]$,
where $\alpha_{j, n}(c)$ is $p_{1, j, n}\left[X_{j, n} \geq c\right]$.
Remark. The quantity $\alpha_{j, n}(c)$ can be expressed in terms of $p_{0, j, n}$ instead of $p_{1, j, n}$. In fact, $1-\alpha_{j, n}(c)=E\left(1+X_{j, n}\right) I\left[X_{j, n}<c\right]$ is the mass of the part of $p_{1, j, n}$ situated where the likelihood ratio $1+X_{j, n}$ is smaller than $c$.

The second expression given here has a more direct intuitive content than the first. It also remains valid, whenever $\mathscr{E}$ is not perfect, whether or not condition (A) is satisfied. However, the expression (i) is much more convenient for a variety of technical arguments.

Proof. Convergence of $\mathscr{E}_{n}$ to $\mathscr{E}$ implies convergence of the Hellinger transforms and therefore of the distributions $\mathscr{L}\left\{\sum Y_{j, n}\right\}$. According to Proposition 2 the limit Hellinger transform $\phi(\alpha)$ has an expression given by

$$
\log \phi(\alpha)=\left(2 a-\sigma^{2}\right) \alpha+2 \sigma^{2} \alpha^{2}+\int\left[(1+y)^{2 \alpha}-1-2 \alpha y\right] M(d y)
$$

The mass of $P_{1}$ which is dominated by $P_{0}$ is the limit $m=\lim \{\phi(\alpha) ; \alpha<1$, $\alpha \rightarrow 1\}$. Passage to the limit under the integral sign presents no difficulty since, on $[-1, \infty)$, the integrand $(1+y)^{2 \alpha}-1-2 \alpha y$ is bounded by some multiple of $y^{2}$. Thus

$$
\log m=\lim _{\alpha} \log \phi(\alpha)=2 a+\sigma^{2}+\int y^{2} M(d y)=b
$$

This gives the first expression for $m$.
To obtain the second expression, consider the limiting distribution $Q$ of $\sum_{j} X_{j, n}$. From the form of this distribution one sees that $b$ is just the expectation $b= \int x Q(d x)$. Thus, according to Chapter 15, Section 5, $b$ is also equal to the iterated limit

$$
b=\lim _{c \rightarrow \infty} \lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} \sum_{j} m_{j, n}(c)
$$

where $m_{j, n}(c)=E X_{j, n} I\left[X_{j, n}<c\right]$. Introduce the probabilities $\gamma_{j, n}(c)= \operatorname{Pr}\left[X_{j, n} \geq c\right]$. Then

$$
1-\alpha_{j, n}(c)=1-\gamma_{j, n}(c)+m_{j, n}(c)
$$

Since $\sup _{j}\left\{\gamma_{j, n}(c)+\left|m_{j, n}(c)\right|\right\}$ tends to zero (by Assumption (A)), the product $\prod_{j}\left[1-\alpha_{j, n}(c)\right]$ can be replaced by $\exp \left\{\sum_{j}\left[m_{j, n}(c)-\gamma_{j, n}(c)\right]\right\}$. However, $\lim _{c \rightarrow \infty} \lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} \sum_{j} \gamma_{j, n}(c)=0$. Thus

$$
\lim _{c} \lim _{n} \Pi\left[1-\alpha_{j, n}(c)\right]=\exp \left\{\lim _{c} \lim _{n} \sum_{j} m_{j, n}(c)\right\}=e^{b} .
$$

This concludes the proof of the proposition.
Remark 1. The quantity $b=\int x Q(d x)=2 a+\sigma^{2}+\int y^{2} M(d y)$ can also be interpreted as follows. The variance of $\sum_{j} Y_{j, n}$ is $\sum\left\{h_{j, n}^{2}\left(2-h_{j, n}^{2}\right)-\beta_{j, n}\right\}$, where $\beta_{j, n}$ is the mass of the part of $p_{1, j, n}$ which is $p_{0, j, n}$-singular. Thus $-2 a$ is the limit of var $\left[\sum_{j} Y_{j, n}\right]+\sum \beta_{j, n}$.

It follows that $-b$ is the sum of two parts, one representing the limit of the sum $\sum_{j} \beta_{j, n}$ of singular parts and another representing the difference between the limit of variances and the variance of the limit.

Remark 2. The second expression given in Proposition 4 can also be obtained as follows. Consider the limiting distribution of $\Lambda_{n}=\sum_{j} \Lambda_{j, n}$ under $P_{0, n}$. This has a mass at $(-\infty)$ precisely equal to $1-\exp \{-M[\{-1\}]\}$. Thus the part of $P_{0}$ which is dominated by $P_{1}$ has mass $\exp \{-M[\{-1\}]\}$. The expression (ii) in Proposition 4 is obtained from this by interchanging the roles of $P_{0, n}$ and $P_{1, n}$.

Corollary. Let condition (A) be satisfied. Then $\left\{P_{0, n}\right\}$ and $\left\{P_{1, n}\right\}$ are contiguous sequences if and only if the following conditions hold.
(i) Condition (B) is satisfied.
(ii) The sum of singular parts $\sum \beta_{j, n}$ tends to zero.
(iii) The variance of the limiting distributions of the $\sum Y_{j, n}$ is the limit of the variances.
(iv) The limit Lévy measures $M$ gives mass zero to the point ( -1 ).

This is an immediate consequence of Proposition 4 and of the remarks made above.

In the preceding arguments we have used only variables which are functions of the logarithms of likelihood ratios $\Lambda_{j, n}$. It often happens that one wishes to consider joint distributions of sums $\sum_{j} \Lambda_{j, n}, \sum_{j} Z_{j, n}$ where the $Z_{j, n}$ are random variables defined on the component experiments $\mathscr{E}_{j, n}=\left\{p_{0, j, n}, p_{1, j, n}\right\}$. Thus the $Z_{j, n}$ are mutually independent.

If conditions (A) and (B) are duly satisfied and if, in addition, the array $\left\{Z_{j, n}\right\}$ is infinitesimal, joint limiting distributions under $P_{0, n}$, if any, will be infinitely divisible. Thus if ( $\Lambda, Z$ ) is a pair having a limiting distribution $F_{0}$, and if $\Lambda$ is real, the characteristic function $\phi_{0}(s, t)=E \exp \{i s \Lambda+i t Z\}$ takes the form

$$
\begin{aligned}
\log \phi_{0}(s, t)= & i\left[A_{0} s+B_{0} t\right]-\frac{1}{2}\left[C_{0} s^{2}+2 D_{0} s t+E_{0} t^{2}\right] \\
& +\iint\left[e^{i s x+i t y}-1-i(s x+t y) u(x, y)\right] L_{0}(d x, d y)
\end{aligned}
$$

where $u$ is a suitable function $0 \leq u \leq 1$ as usual.
Under the measures $P_{1, n}$ the limiting distributions will have a similar form yielding a characteristic function $\phi_{1}$ which, for the same choice of $u$, involves terms $A_{1}, B_{1}, \ldots$ and so forth.

The following proposition gives the relation between $\phi_{0}$ and $\phi_{1}$. Since its validity does not actually depend on the special structure used here we have stated it more generally.

Proposition 5. Let $\left\{\mathscr{E}_{n}\right\}$ be a sequence of experiments $\mathscr{E}_{n}=\left\{P_{0, n}, P_{1, n}\right\}$. Let $\Lambda_{n}=\log d P_{1, n} / d P_{0, n}$, and let $Z_{n}$ be a random variable defined on $\mathscr{E}_{n}$.

Assume that the sequences $\left\{P_{0, n}\right\},\left\{P_{1, n}\right\}$ are contiguous and that, under $\left\{P_{0, n}\right\}$, the pair ( $\Lambda_{n}, Z_{n}$ ) has a limiting distribution $F_{0}$ which is infinitely divisible with characteristic function $\phi_{0}$.

Then, under $\left\{P_{1, n}\right\}$ the limiting distribution $F_{1}$ of the pair ( $\Lambda_{n}, Z_{n}$ ) is infinitely divisible, with characteristic function $\phi_{1}$ related to $\phi_{0}$ as follows:
(i) $A_{1}=A_{0}+C_{0}+\int\left(e^{x}-1\right) x u(x, y) L_{0}(d x, d y)$,
(ii) $B_{1}=B_{0}+D_{0}+\int\left(e^{x}-1\right) y u(x, y) L_{0}(d x, d y)$,
(iii) the quadratic expression is unchanged, that is $C_{1}=C_{0}, D_{1}=D_{0}$, and $E_{1}=E_{0}$,
(iv) the new Lévy measure $L_{1}$ is such that $L_{1}(d x, d y)=e^{x} L_{0}(d x, d y)$.

Proof. We already know by Chapter 6, Section 3, Proposition 5, that the pair ( $\Lambda_{n}, Z_{n}$ ) has under $P_{1, n}$ a limiting distribution $F_{1}$ with $F_{1}(d x, d y)=$



$e^{x} F_{0}(d x, d y)$.
Thus $\phi_{1}(s, t)=\int \exp \{i s x+i t y\} F_{1}(d x, d y)$ is obtainable by substituting + 1 for is in $\phi_{0}(s, t)$. Here, for any given $t$, the function $\phi_{0}$ is an analytic function for values of (is) whose real part $\operatorname{Re}($ is $)$ remains in ( 0,1 ). It is also continuous as long as $\operatorname{Re}(i s) \in[0,1]$. The same remark applies to the expressions involved in the Lévy representation. Thus $\phi_{1}(s, t)$ can be obtained by the purely formal substitution of $s-i$ for $s$ in $\phi_{0}(s, t)$.

For the integral term, this yields

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \iint\left\{e^{x} e^{i s x+i t y}-1-[x+i s x+i t y] u(x, y)\right\} L_{0}(d x, d y) \\
& =\iint\left[e^{i s x+i t y}-1-(i s x+i t y) u(x, y)\right] e^{x} L_{0}(d x, d y) \\
& \quad+\iint\left[e^{x}-1-x u(x, y)\right] L_{0}(d x, d y) \\
& \quad+\iint\left(e^{x}-1\right)(i s x+i t y) u(x, y) L_{0}(d x, d y)
\end{aligned}
$$

The linear and quadratic terms yield a term which is independent of $s$ and $t$ and has the form $A_{0}+\frac{1}{2} C_{0}$.

The quadratic term yields in addition a linear term $i\left(C_{0} s+D_{0} t\right)$. The total of the constant terms is

$$
A_{0}+\frac{1}{2} C_{0}+\iint\left[e^{x}-1-x u(x, y)\right] L_{0}(d x, d y)
$$

This must be equal to zero, since $\phi_{1}(0,0)=1$. The result as stated follows by assembling the terms in appropriate order. Hence the proposition.

The reader will notice that the integral terms involved in the passage from $A_{0}$ to $A_{1}$ or $B_{0}$ to $B_{1}$ do depend on the choice of the function $u$ introduced to insure integrability. However there are several interesting cases where one can simplify the formula.

For instance, it may happen that the integrals $\iint_{|x|<\varepsilon} x L_{0}(d x, d y)$ and $\iint_{|y|<\varepsilon} y L_{0}(d x, d y)$ do exist. In such a case one may take $u$ identically equal to zero. Then $A_{1}=A_{0}+C_{0}$ and $B_{1}=B_{0}+D_{0}$.

Another case where simplification occurs is the case where the Lévy measure $L_{0}$ is concentrated on the coordinate axes. Then $B_{1}=B_{0}+D_{0}$. This happens in particular when the distribution of $Z_{n}$ tends to a Gaussian distribution. One can state that as follows:

Corollary. Let the conditions of Proposition 5 be satisfied. Assume in addition that under $P_{0, n}$ the limiting distribution of $Z_{n}$ is Gaussian $\mathscr{N}\left(B_{0}, E_{0}\right)$. Then under $P_{1, n}$ the limiting distribution of $Z_{n}$ is also Gaussian, with the same variance and with an expectation $B_{1}=B_{0}+D_{0}$.

The preceding corollary can be used, with some additional restrictions, in conjunction with a projection theorem of J. Hájek.

Let $\mathscr{E}_{n}=\left\{P_{0, n}, P_{1, n}\right\}$ and let $L_{2, n}$ be the Hilbert space of equivalence classes of $P_{0, n}$ square integrable functions. Assume that $\mathscr{E}_{n}$ is a direct product of
experiments $\mathscr{E}_{n, j}=\left\{p_{0, j, n}, p_{1, j, n}\right\}, j \in J_{n}$ finite. Assume also that conditions (A) and (B) are satisfied.

Let $\omega_{n, j}$ be the observable random element for $\mathscr{E}_{n, j}$. Let $\mathscr{S}_{n}$ be the subset of $L_{2, n}$ formed by functions of the type $\sum_{j} l_{j}\left(\omega_{n, j}\right)$. This is a closed linear subspace of $L_{2, n}$. Every $Z_{n} \in L_{2, n}$ admits an orthogonal projection, say $\hat{Z}_{n}$ on $\mathscr{S}_{n}$. One can readily verify that

$$
\hat{Z}_{n}-E \hat{Z}_{n}=\sum_{j} E\left[\left(Z_{n}-\mu_{n}\right) \mid \omega_{n, j}\right]
$$

with $\mu_{n}=E Z_{n}=E \hat{Z}_{n}$.
Now suppose that the conditions of the preceding corollary are satisfied by $Z_{n}$. Assume also that the $Z_{n}^{2}$ are equi-integrable so that the variance of their limiting distribution is the limit of their variances.

Assume that the $\hat{Z}_{n}$ also satisfy the conditions of the corollary. Then the amount of shift $D_{0}$ generated by using $P_{1, n}$ instead of $P_{0, n}$ will be the same for $Z_{n}$ and $\hat{Z}_{n}$.

## 3. Conditions for Asymptotic Normality

In this section we shall retain the notations of Section 2. In particular we shall use the variables $X_{j, n}, Y_{j, n}$, and $\Lambda_{j, n}$ introduced there for experiments $\mathscr{E}_{n}= \left\{P_{0, n}, P_{1, n}\right\}$ which are direct products of experiments $\mathscr{E}_{j, n}=\left\{p_{0, j, n}, p_{1, j, n}\right\}$.

We shall be interested here in conditions which insure that the experiments $\mathscr{E}_{n}=\left\{P_{0, n}, P_{1, n}\right\}$ are asymptotically Gaussian in the sense of Chapter 10.

This property can be split into two separate conditions. First the sequences $\left\{P_{0, n}\right\},\left\{P_{1, n}\right\}$ must be contiguous. Second the distributions $\mathscr{L}\left\{\Lambda_{n} \mid P_{0, n}\right\}$ must be approximable by Gaussian distributions.

For brevity, let us say that random elements $\xi_{n}$ taking values in $[-\infty,+\infty)$ are asymptotically normal if there are numbers $a_{n}$ and $\sigma_{n}^{2}$ such that the difference between $\mathscr{L}\left(\xi_{n}\right)$ and $\mathscr{N}\left(a_{n}, \sigma_{n}^{2}\right)$ tends to zero. Call the $\xi_{n}$ asymptotically constant if they are asymptotically normal with an approximating $\mathscr{N}\left(a_{n}, \sigma_{n}^{2}\right)$ in which $\sigma_{n}^{2}=0$. With this convention one can state the following.

Proposition 1. Let assumptions (A) and (B) (of Section 2) be satisfied. Then the following statements are all equivalent.
(i) The sums $\Lambda_{n}=\sum_{j} \Lambda_{j, n}$ are asymptotically normal.
(ii) There is some PD $[0,2]$ function $f$ satisfying the restrictions that $\inf _{y}\{|f(y)|$; $|y|>\varepsilon\}>0$ for all $\varepsilon>0$, for which the sums $\sum_{j} f\left(Y_{j, n}\right)$ are asymptotically normal and their distributions relatively compact on $(-\infty,+\infty)$.
(iii) Let $Z_{j, n}=\Lambda_{j, n}-2 Y_{j, n}+Y_{j, n}^{2}$. Then $\sum Z_{j, n}$ is asymptotically normal.
(iv) The sums $\sum Z_{j, n}$ are asymptotically constant.

Proof. Under the conditions (A) and (B), the cluster points of the sequence $\left\{\mathscr{L}\left(\Lambda_{n}\right)\right\}$ can possess nonzero masses at $(-\infty)$. However, the entire distribution cannot be situated at $(-\infty)$. The same remark applies to the $Z_{j, n}$ and indeed to all sums $\sum f\left(Y_{j, n}\right)$ where $f$ is a $P D[0,2]$ function which remains bounded on
compact subsets of $(-1,+\infty)$. Thus in all the statements given in the proposition, the approximating sequences $\mathscr{N}\left(a_{n}, \sigma_{n}^{2}\right)$ are relatively compact sequences on $(-\infty,+\infty)$.

Thus any one of the statements (i) to (iv) implies that for every $\varepsilon>0$ the sums $\sum_{j} \operatorname{Pr}\left\{\left|Y_{j, n}\right|>\varepsilon\right\}$ tend to zero. If so, the sums $\sum Y_{j, n}$ are asymptotically normal. By the argument given in Chapter 10, Section 5, Lemma 1, if $f$ is any arbitrary $P D[0,2]$ function from $[-1,+\infty)$ to $[-\infty,+\infty)$, the sum $\sum f\left(Y_{j, n}\right)$ are also asymptotically normal.

The asymptotic constancy asserted in statement (iv) follows from the usual computations of limiting expectations and variances. If $\sum Y_{j, n}$ behave asymptotically like $\mathcal{N}\left(a_{n}, \sigma_{n}^{2}\right)$, then $\sum Z_{j, n}$ behaves like $\mathscr{N}\left(2 \sigma_{n}^{2}, 0\right)$. This completes the proof of the Proposition.

Note that particular cases of statement (ii) would be the asymptotic normality of $\sum Y_{j, n}$ or $\sum X_{j, n}$. In the following corollary the term $\beta_{j, n}$ is the mass of $p_{1, j, n}$ which is $p_{0, j, n}$-singular.

Corollary. Let Assumption (A) be satisfied. Then the following statements are all equivalent.
(i) The experiments $\mathscr{E}_{n}$ converge to a Gaussian experiment.
(ii) The sums $\sum X_{j, n}$ have a limiting distribution which is Gaussian with expectation equal to zero.
(iii) The sums $\sum Y_{j, n}$ have a limiting distribution which is Gaussian with a variance equal to the limit of the variances of the $\sum_{j} Y_{j, n}$. Furthermore $\sum \beta_{j, n}$ tends to zero.

Proof. As explained above, $\mathscr{E}_{n}$ converges to a Gaussian experiment if and only if two conditions are satisfied. The sequences $\left\{P_{0, n}\right\},\left\{P_{1, n}\right\}$ must be contiguous. The sums $\Lambda_{n}=\sum \Lambda_{j, n}$ must have a limiting distribution which is Gaussian.

Now if either $\mathscr{L}\left(\sum X_{j, n}\right)$ or $\mathscr{L}\left(\sum Y_{j, n}\right)$ converges to a Gaussian limit so does $\mathscr{L}\left(\Lambda_{n}\right)$. Thus it remains to check the contiguity. In the two cases considered here, this follows immediately from Section 2, Proposition 4 and its corollaries.

Remark. The statement relative to $\sum X_{j, n}$ does not involve the singular terms $\beta_{j, n}$. For the $Y_{j, n}$, on the contrary, it is necessary to take them into consideration. An example is as follows. Let $p_{0, j, n}$ be the uniform distribution on $[0,1]$. Let $p_{1, j, n}$ be the uniform distribution on $(1-(1 / n))^{-2}$. Then, under $p_{0, j, n}$ the variable $Y_{j, n}$ is the constant $Y_{j, n}=-(1 / n)$. Letting $j$ go from 1 to $n$ gives $\sum Y_{j, n}=-1$.

This is indeed asymptotically Gaussian with variance zero, equal to the limit of the variances. However, the limiting experiment is clearly not a Gaussian one. The corresponding sum $\sum X_{j, n}$ is equal to $-2+(1 / n)$. Its limiting distribution has expectation -2 .

It would thus appear that statement (ii) of the corollary would be the easiest one to use in practice. This need not be so. The asymptotic normality condition of (iii) is equivalent to the usual Lindeberg-Feller condition. In the present case one can replace it by the simpler condition that for any $\varepsilon>0$ the $\operatorname{sum} \sum_{j} E Y_{j, n}^{2} I\left[\left|Y_{j, n}\right|>\varepsilon\right]$ tends to zero.

Explicitly $\sum Y_{j, n}$ will have a limiting normal distribution, with variance of the limit equal to the limit of the variances if and only if $\sum_{j} E Y_{j, n}^{2}$ tends to a limit and, for every $\varepsilon>0$, the sums $\sum E Y_{j, n}^{2} I\left\{\left|Y_{j, n}\right|>\varepsilon\right\}$ tend to zero.

By contrast the $X_{j, n}$ need not satisfy Lindeberg condition. Of course, convergence of $\mathscr{E}_{n}$ to a Gaussian experiment can be expressed in many other ways. For instance, one may state the following.

Proposition 2. Let condition (A) be satisfied. Then the following statements are all equivalent.
(i) $\Lambda_{n}=\sum \Lambda_{j, n}$ has a limiting Gaussian distribution $\mathscr{N}\left(-\frac{1}{2} \sigma^{2}, \sigma^{2}\right)$.
(ii) $\sum_{j} X_{j, n}$ has a limiting Gaussian distribution $\mathscr{N}\left(0, \sigma^{2}\right)$.
(iii) $\sum Y_{j, n}$ has a limiting Gaussian distribution $\mathscr{N}\left(-\frac{1}{2}\left(\sigma^{2} / 4\right), \sigma^{2} / 4\right)$.
(iv) The experiments $\mathscr{E}_{n}$ converge to the experiment $\mathscr{E}=\{\mathscr{N}(0,1), \mathscr{N}(\sigma, 1)\}$.

Proof. The relations between the various limiting distributions can be established easily using either Chapter 10, Section 5, Lemma 1, or Chapter 15, Section 5, Proposition 5. The relations between the expectations and the variances imply the necessary contiguity. The form of the limit $\mathscr{E}$ is obtainable from the fact that if under $P_{0, n}$ the $\Lambda_{n}$ converge to $\mathscr{N}\left(-\frac{1}{2} \sigma^{2}, \sigma^{2}\right)$, then, by Proposition 5, Section 2, under $P_{1, n}$, they converge to $\mathscr{N}\left(\frac{1}{2} \sigma^{2}, \sigma^{2}\right)$.

So far we have studied only binary experiments $\left\{P_{0, n}, P_{1, n}\right\}$. For the remainder of this section we shall consider experiments $\mathscr{E}_{n}=\left\{P_{s, n} ; s \in S\right\}$ where $S$ is either a subset of a vector space or a totally arbitrary set, but where the $\mathscr{E}_{n}$ are direct products of experiments $\mathscr{E}_{j, n}=\left\{p_{s, j, n} ; s \in S\right\}$.

Definition 1. Let $h_{j, n}(s, t)$ be the Hellinger distance between $p_{s, j, n}$ and $p_{t, j, n}$. The double array $\left\{\mathscr{E}_{j, n}\right\}$ will be called infinitesimal on $S$ if for every pair ( $s, t$ ) of elements of $S$ the supremum $\sup _{j} h_{j, n}(s, t)$ tends to zero as $n$ tends to infinity.

Lemma 1. Let $\mathscr{E}_{n}=\left\{P_{s, n} ; s \in S\right\}$ be a sequence of experiments which are direct products of $\mathscr{E}_{j, n}$. Assume that the array $\mathscr{E}_{j, n}$ is infinitesimal and that for each pair ( $s, t$ ) the binary experiments $\left\{P_{s, n}, P_{t, n}\right\}$ converge to a Gaussian experiment. Then $\mathscr{E}_{n}$ converges weakly to a Gaussian shift experiment.

Proof. Let $\Lambda_{n}(s, t)$ be the logarithm of likelihood ratio $\log d P_{s, n} / d P_{t, n}$. Consider three points $s, t$, and $u$ of $S$. By contiguity, one will have $\Lambda_{n}(s, u)= \Lambda_{n}(s, t)+\Lambda_{n}(t, u)$ except on sets whose probability tends to zero. According to the pairwise convergence assumptions, all the distributions $\mathscr{L}\left[\Lambda_{n}(s, t) \mid P_{t, n}\right]$, $\mathscr{L}\left[\Lambda_{n}(t, u) \mid P_{t, n}\right]$, and $\mathscr{L}\left[\Lambda_{n}(s, u) \mid P_{u, n}\right]$ converge to certain Gaussian limits. Since the $\Lambda_{n}$ are sums of the corresponding infinitesimal $\Lambda_{j, n}$, this implies that joint distributions such as $F_{n}=\mathscr{L}\left[\Lambda_{n}(s, u), \Lambda_{n}(s, t), \Lambda_{n}(t, u) \mid P_{t, n}\right]$ are approximable by trivariate Gaussian distributions.

Extract a subsequence, say $\{m\} \subset\{n\}$, such that $F_{m}$ converges to a Gaussian limit with expectations $\mu(s, u), \mu(s, t), \mu(t, u)$ and with an appropriate covariance matrix. Now consider the same triplet, but with distributions $G_{n}$ induced by $P_{u, n}$. Along the same subsequence $\{m\}$, the $G_{m}$ will have a Gaussian limit with the same covariance structure as for $F_{m}$. The expectations are increased by the appropriate covariances. For instance, $\mu(s, u)$ is increased by the covariance, say $\gamma(s, u)$, in the limiting distributions of $\Lambda_{m}(s, u), \Lambda_{m}(u, t)$ under $P_{t, m}$. Thus $\mu(s, u)+\gamma(s, u)$ must be the expectation in the limit of
$\mathscr{L}\left[\Lambda_{n}(s, u) \mid P_{u, n}\right]$, which exists by assumption. It follows that $\gamma(s, u)$ does not depend on the particular subsequence $\{m\}$ chosen to insure convergence. By addition, one concludes that the covariances in the cluster points of subsequences $\mathscr{L}\left\{\Lambda_{m}(s, t), \Lambda_{m}(t, u) \mid P_{t, m}\right\}$ are also all the same. Therefore the joint distributions actually converge. The result follows.

Now take a particular element $u \in S$ and keep it fixed as "origin." For any $s \in S$, let $Y_{j, n}(s)$ be the variable $\sqrt{\left(d p_{s, j, n} / d p_{u, j, n}\right)}-1$ considered as a random variable for the distributions induced by $P_{u, n}$.

Proposition 3. Assume that the array $\left\{\mathscr{E}_{j, n}\right\}$ is infinitesimal on $S$. Assume also that for each pair ( $u, s$ ) the experiments $\left\{P_{u, n}, P_{s, n}\right\}$ tend to a Gaussian limit. Finally, assume that for each pair ( $s, t$ ) the expectations $\sum_{j} E Y_{j, n}(s) Y_{j, n}(t)$ converge to a limit $\frac{1}{4} \Gamma(s, t)$. Then the experiments $\mathscr{E}_{n}$ converge to a limit $\mathscr{E}$. This $\mathscr{E}$ is the Gaussian shift experiment associated with the kernel $\Gamma$.

Proof. Consider a subsequence $\{m\} \subset\{n\}$ such that the joint distributions $\mathscr{L}\left[\sum_{j} Y_{j, n}(s), \sum_{j} Y_{j, n}(t)\right]$ converge. Then the limit is a Gaussian distribution. The Lindeberg-type conditions satisfied by the $Y_{j, n}$ imply that the covariance of the limiting distribution is no other than $\frac{1}{4} \Gamma(s, t)$. One concludes that for any finite set $\left\{s_{1}, s_{2}, \ldots, s_{k}\right\} \subset S$ the joint distributions $\mathscr{L}\left\{\sum Y_{j, n}\left(s_{i}\right) ; i=1,2, \ldots, k\right\}$ converge to a Gaussian limit with covariances as described.

Now let $\Lambda_{n}(s)=\log d P_{s, n} / d P_{u, n}$, considered also as a random variable for the distribution induced by $P_{u, n}$. It follows that the joint distribution of the $\Lambda_{n}(\mathrm{~s})$, for any finite set of values of $s$ converge to joint Gaussian distributions. The covariances are given by the kernel $\Gamma$ itself. This concludes the proof of the proposition.

Let us recall (see Chapter 10) that the limit experiment $\mathscr{E}$ associated with $\Gamma$ is the following. The measure $G_{u}$ is the distribution of a process $\{\xi(s) ; s \in S\}$ which is a Gaussian process with expectation zero and covariance $E_{u} \xi(s) \xi(t)= \Gamma(s, t)$. For another point, say $t \in S$, the distribution $G_{t}$ is the distribution of the process $\xi(s)+\Gamma(s, t)$.

The most commonly used form of the preceding proposition refers to the case where the set $S$ is a subset of a vector space and where one wants the limit experiment to be linearly indexed by $S$. That is, one wishes that passing from one parameter value $t_{1}$ to a value $t_{2}$ changes the expectation of the process $\xi$ by a linear function of $t_{2}-t_{1}$.

Since this occurs frequently, we summarize the situation for this case. Let $Y_{j, n}(s)$ be the modified square root of likelihood ratios used before. Let $\beta_{j, n}(s)$ be the mass of the part of $p_{s, j, n}$ which is $p_{u, j, n}$-singular.

Theorem 1. Let $S$ be a subset of a vector space with origin $u \in S$. Let the array $\left\{\mathscr{E}_{j, n}\right\}$ be infinitesimal with direct product $\mathscr{E}_{n}$.

In order that $\mathscr{E}_{n}$ converges weakly to a Gaussian limit $\mathscr{E}$ linearly indexed by $S$, it is necessary and sufficient that the following conditions be all satisfied.
(i) $\sum \beta_{j, n}(s) \rightarrow 0$, for each $s \in S$.
(ii) For each $s \in S$, the $Y_{j, n}(s)$ satisfy the Lindeberg condition that $\sum_{j} E\left[Y_{j, n}(s)\right]^{2} I\left\{\left|Y_{j, n}(s)\right|>\varepsilon\right\}$ tends to zero.
(iii) Let $\rho_{j, n}(s, t)$ be the Hellinger affinity between $p_{s, j, n}$ and $p_{t, j, n}$ and let
$C_{n}(s, t)=\sum_{j} \rho_{j, n}(s, t)$. Then the quantity

$$
C_{n}(s, t)-C_{n}(s, u)-C_{n}(u, t)+C_{n}(u, u)
$$

has a limit $\frac{1}{4} \Gamma(s, t)$.
(iv) The function $\Gamma$ is the restriction to $S \times S$ of a bilinear function.

Proof. This would be just a summary of results which have already been established, except for the fact that we have replaced the sum $\sum E Y_{j, n}(s) Y_{j, n}(t)$ by the second differences of the function $C_{n}$. To see that this is possible, let $V_{j, n}=1+Y_{j, n}$ be the square root of the likelihood ratio itself. Then

$$
E Y_{j, n}(s) Y_{j, n}(t)=E V_{j, n}(s) V_{j, n}(t)-E V_{j, n}(s)-E V_{j, n}(t)+1
$$

Of course, $E V_{j, n}(s)=\rho_{j, n}(s, u)$. For the product $V_{j, n}(s) V_{j, n}(t)$ one can write

$$
E V_{j, n}(s) V_{j, n}(t)=\rho_{j, n}(s, t)-\int \sqrt{d \mu_{j, n}(s) d \mu_{j, n}(t)}
$$

where $u_{j, n}(s)$ is the part of $p_{s, j, n}$ which is $p_{u, j, n}$-singular. By Hölder's inequality, one has

$$
\int\left[d \mu_{j, n}(s) d \mu_{j, n}(t)\right]^{1 / 2} \leq\left\{\beta_{j, n}(s) \beta_{j, n}(t)\right\}^{1 / 2}
$$

Therefore

$$
\sum \int\left[d \mu_{j, n}(s) d \mu_{j, n}(t)\right]^{1 / 2} \leq\left[\sum_{j} \beta_{j, n}(s)\right]\left[\sum_{j} \beta_{j, n}(t)\right]
$$

This tends to zero, showing that this term can be neglected in the passage to the limit. Thus the replacement of $\sum_{j} E Y_{j, n}(s) Y_{j, n}(t)$ by the second difference of the function $C_{n}$ is justified and this concludes the proof of the theorem.

Remark 1. The above results admit variations in which one considers convergences such as $\Delta\left(\mathscr{E}_{n}, \mathscr{E}\right) \rightarrow 0$. The necessary modifications are obvious.

Remark 2. The only part of Theorem 1 involving the linear relation between $S$ and the expectations of the Gaussian experiment is statement (iv). To check that such a relation exists, it is often more convenient to study first sums such as $\Lambda_{n}(s)+\Lambda_{n}(-s)$ or $\sum\left[Y_{j, n}(s)+Y_{j, n}(-s)\right]$ which should then be asymptotically constant.

Suppose, for instance, that $S$ is an open convex set symmetric around $u=0$. Then $-\left[\Lambda_{n}(s)+\Lambda_{n}(-s)\right]$ should tend to a nonrandom limit $K(s)$ such that $K(\lambda s)=\lambda^{2} K(s)$ for $\lambda$ real and $s \in S, \lambda s \in S$. This $K(s)$ is then equal to $\Gamma(s, s)$ and $\Gamma(s, t)$ can be obtained by polarization. Such a convergence is however not sufficient to imply that the experiments are asymptotically Gaussian.

Here we have kept the sets $S$ fixed, independent of $n$. There is no difficulty in passing to sets $S_{n}$ that depend on $n$, except of course for Lemma 1 since its purpose is to prove convergence of distributions for a fixed set $S$. If the $S_{n}$ vary with $n$, one cannot speak of convergences to limits, but one can still speak of approximations. Considering sequences $\mathscr{E}_{n}=\left\{P_{s, n} ; s \in S_{n}\right\}$ where $\mathscr{E}_{n}$ is a product
of experiments $\mathscr{E}_{n, j}=\left\{p_{s, j, n} ; s \in S_{n}\right\}$, one can still say that $\mathscr{E}_{n}$ is asymptotically weakly Gaussian if
(i) for any two sequences $\left\{s_{n}\right\},\left\{t_{n}\right\}$ of elements of $S_{n}$, the sequences of measures $\left\{P_{s_{n}, n}\right\},\left\{P_{t_{n}, n}\right\}$ are contiguous, and
(ii) for any sets $\left\{s_{0, n}, \ldots, s_{k, n}\right\} \subset S_{n}$ of fixed finite cardinality the joint distributions under $s_{0, n}$ of the logarithms $\Lambda_{n}\left(s_{j, n}, s_{0, n}\right) ; j=1, \ldots, k$ are asymptotically approximable by Gaussian distributions.

The linearity relations involved, for instance, in parts (iii) and (iv) of Theorem 1 above can also be rewritten to allow $S_{n}$ to vary with $n$. A possibility is as follows. As in Chapter 11, let $\mathscr{M}_{0}\left(S_{n}\right)$ be the space of finite signed measures $\lambda$ with finite support in $S_{n}$ and with mass $\lambda\left(S_{n}\right)=0$. Let $H_{n}^{2}(s, t)=\sum_{j} h_{j, n}^{2}(s, t)$ and let $\Gamma_{n}(\lambda)=-\frac{1}{2} \iint H_{n}^{2}(s, t) \lambda(d s) \lambda(d t)$.
