This can be taken as a form that defines the covariance system of an approximating Gaussian experiment. Suppose now that $S_{n}$ is a subset of some vector space $V_{n}$ and that for certain $\lambda_{n} \in \mathscr{M}_{0}\left(S_{n}\right)$ one has $\int s \lambda_{n}(d s)=0$ in $V_{n}$. Then, if the total masses $\left\|\lambda_{n}\right\|$ remain bounded and the cardinality of the supports of the $\lambda_{n}$ remains bounded, one should have $\Gamma_{n}\left(\lambda_{n}\right)$ tending to zero. When $V_{n}$ carries suitable reference norms, instead of $\int s \lambda_{n}(d s)=0$, one can substitute the condition $\left|\int s \lambda_{n}(d s)\right|_{n} \rightarrow 0$. This is a stronger linearity restriction than the first.

With such terminology, the above results have an important consequence that was brought to my attention by a suggestion of Prof. Grace Lo Yang. Consider two double arrays $\left\{\mathscr{E}_{j, n}\right\}$ and $\left\{\mathscr{F}_{j, n}\right\}$, both indexed by $S_{n}$.

Proposition 4. Assume that (i) the double array $\left\{\mathscr{E}_{n, j}\right\}$ is infinitesimal (according to Definition 1, above), and (ii) $\mathscr{F}_{j, n}$ is weaker than $\mathscr{E}_{j, n}$. Then if the products $\mathscr{E}_{n}$ are weakly asymptotically normal so are the products $\mathscr{F}_{n}$ of the $\mathscr{F}_{j, n}$. If in addition $S_{n}$ is a subset of a vector space and $\mathscr{E}_{n}$ satisfies linearity restrictions, then $\mathscr{F}_{n}$ satisfies the same linearity restrictions.

Proof. Let $p_{s, j, n}$ be the measure attached to $s$ in $\mathscr{E}_{j, n}$. The assumption that the $\mathscr{E}_{n}$ are asymptotically Gaussian may be rewritten as follows: The required contiguity holds and for any pair ( $s, t$ ) of elements of $S_{n}$ and for $\varepsilon>0$, let

$$
\delta(s, t, j, n, \varepsilon)=\left\|p_{s, j, n}-\left[(1+\varepsilon) p_{t, j, n}\right] \wedge p_{s, j, n}\right\| .
$$

The condition is that for all such pairs the sums $\sum_{j} \delta(s, t, j, n, \varepsilon)$ tend to zero. (This must be applied to both pairs $(s, t)$ and $(t, s)$.) Now let $T_{j, n}$ be a transition that yields $\mathscr{F}_{j, n}$ from $\mathscr{E}_{j, n}$. Let $q_{s, j, n}$ be the corresponding measure. By Chapter 6, Section 2, Proposition 1, one has

$$
\left\|q_{s, j, n}-\left[(1+\varepsilon) q_{t, j, n}\right] \wedge q_{s, j, n}\right\| \leq \delta(s, t, j, n, \varepsilon)
$$

By Chapter 6, Section 3, Lemma 2, the contiguity properties of $\mathscr{E}_{n}$ remain valid in $\mathscr{F}_{n}$. This gives the first assertion. To prove the second, select a particular $s \in S_{n}$. Then, by the above one can proceed as if for all $t \in S_{n}$ one has $p_{t, j, n}=p_{t, j, n} \wedge\left[2 p_{s, j, n}\right]$. We shall assume this below. Since $\delta\left(\mathscr{E}_{j, n}, \mathscr{F}_{j, n}\right)=0$, one can also represent the $\mathscr{E}_{j, n}$ and $\mathscr{F}_{j, n}$ by measures, say $W_{t, j, n}$, on a product $\sigma$ field $\mathscr{G}_{j, n} \times \mathscr{B}_{j, n}$ in such a way that the marginals on $\mathscr{G}_{j, n}$ give $\mathscr{E}_{j, n}$, the marginals
on $\mathscr{B}_{j, n}$ give $\mathscr{F}_{j, n}$, and $\mathscr{G}_{j, n}$ is a sufficient $\sigma$-field. We shall let $1+f_{j, n}(t)$ denote the density

$$
\frac{d W_{t, j, n}}{d W_{s, j, n}}
$$

taken either on $\mathscr{G}_{j, n} \times \mathscr{B}_{j, n}$ or on $\mathscr{G}_{j, n}$. The density taken on $\mathscr{B}_{j, n}$ will be written $1+g_{j, n}(t)$.

It is then easily checked that the linearity restrictions can be written in terms of the processes $\bar{f}_{n}(t)=\sum_{j} f_{j, n}(t)$ and the analogous processes $\bar{g}_{n}(t)$. For instance, for $\lambda_{n} \in \mathscr{M}_{0}\left(S_{n}\right)$, with $\left\|\lambda_{n}\right\|$ and the cardinality of the support of $\lambda_{n}$ remaining bounded, the relation $\Gamma_{n}\left(\lambda_{n}\right) \rightarrow 0$ means that $\int \bar{f}_{n}(t) \lambda_{n}(d t)$ tends in probability (for $P_{s, n}$ ) to zero. Now, with the restriction $\sup _{n}\left\|\lambda_{n}\right\|< \infty$, and with the boundedness assumption $p_{t, j, n}=p_{t, j, n} \wedge\left[2 p_{s, j, n}\right]$, the variables $\left|\int \bar{f}_{n}(t) \lambda_{n}(d t)\right|$ are uniformly integrable. If they tend in probability to zero so does $\mathrm{E}_{s}\left|\int \bar{f}_{n}(t) \lambda_{n}(d t)\right|$ and also $E_{s}\left\{\int \bar{f}_{n}(t) \lambda_{n}(d t) \mid \mathscr{B}_{n}\right\}$ for a conditional expectation taken given the product $\sigma$-field $\mathscr{B}_{n}=X_{j} \mathscr{B}_{j, n}$. However, $E\left[\bar{f}_{n}(t) \mid \mathscr{B}_{n}\right]=\bar{g}_{n}(t)$. Hence the result.

Note that Proposition 4 implies that if the $\mathscr{E}_{n}$ satisfy the LAN conditions of Chapter 11, Section 7 for reference norms $\|\cdot\|_{n}$, so will the $\mathscr{F}_{n}$. However, this does not mean that the $\mathscr{F}_{n}$ will satisfy their own LAN* conditions, even if these are satisfied by the $\mathscr{E}_{n}$.

## 4. Tests and Distances

This section collects a few facts about the power of tests in direct products of experiments. The facts may be of interest by themselves. They will also be used in the next section to construct certain estimates.

Consider a measurable space $(\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{A})$ and two subsets $A$ and $B$ of the space of probability measures on $(\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{A})$. To such sets we shall attach numbers as follows. The set $A \cup B$ generates an $L_{1}$-space, say $L$, with dual $M$. In keeping with the conventions used throughout, any element $\phi \in M$ such that $0 \leq \phi \leq 1$ will be called a test, even though it need not always be representable by a bounded measurable function.

If $\phi$ is such a test, let $\pi(A, B, \phi)$ be the number

$$
\pi(A, B, \phi)=\sup \{\langle(1-\phi), P\rangle+\langle\phi, Q\rangle ; P \in A, Q \in B\}
$$

Let $\pi(A, B)=\inf _{\phi} \pi(A, B, \phi)$, for an infimum taken over all tests $\phi \in M$. Let $\operatorname{conv} A$ be the convex hull of $A$.

Lemma 1. The number $\pi(A, B)$ is equal to $\sup \{\|P \wedge Q\| ; P \in \operatorname{conv} A, Q \in$ conv $B\}$. Furthermore there is a test $\phi \in M$ such that $\pi(A, B, \phi)=\pi(A, B)$.

Proof. It is clear that $\pi(A, B, \phi)=\pi[\operatorname{conv} A, \operatorname{conv} B, \phi]$. Thus $\pi(A, B)$ is the minimax risk for the decision problem where the risk function is $\langle(1-\phi), P\rangle+ \langle\phi, Q\rangle=1+\langle\phi, Q-P\rangle$ and $Q-P$ is allowed to take all values in the convex set $\operatorname{conv} B-\operatorname{conv} A$. Thus, by the minimax theorem

$$
\begin{aligned}
\pi(A, B) & =\sup \left\{\inf _{\phi}[\langle(1-\phi), P\rangle+\langle\phi, Q\rangle] ; P \in \operatorname{conv} A, Q \in \operatorname{conv} B\right\} \\
& =\sup \{\|P \wedge Q\| ; P \in \operatorname{conv} A, Q \in \operatorname{conv} B\}
\end{aligned}
$$

The existence of a minimax $\phi$ follows from the fact that the set of tests is a weakly compact subset of $M$. Hence the result.
(Another proof can be obtained from the remark made below.)
Remark. The reader will note that the term $\phi$ of the lemma belongs to $M$, but that it need not be representable by a bounded measurable function, unless one modifies $\mathscr{X}$ as explained in Chapter 1. This representability is of course automatic if $A \cup B$ is dominated by a finite or $\sigma$-finite measure. There are also other cases where one can achieve a similar effect with bounded measurable functions only. One case which occurs often in the study of robust procedures is as follows. One starts with a uniform lattice $\Gamma$ of bounded functions on a set $\mathscr{X}$. The measures are then identified with the linear functionals they define on $\Gamma$. Let $\Gamma^{\prime}$ be the dual of $\Gamma$ and let $A$ and $B$ be two sets of probability measures identified with subsets of $\Gamma^{\prime}$.

Let $\widetilde{A}$ and $\widetilde{B}$ be the closed convex hulls of $A$ and $B$, respectively, for the $w\left(\Gamma^{\prime}, \Gamma\right)$ topology. Assume that $\beta=\sup \{\|P \wedge Q\| ; P \in \widetilde{A}, Q \in \widetilde{B}\}<\varepsilon$. Then, there is an element $\phi$ of $\Gamma$ itself such that $0 \leq \phi \leq 1$ and such that $\langle(1- \phi), P\rangle+\langle\phi, Q\rangle<\varepsilon$ for all $P \in \widetilde{A}$ and $Q \in \widetilde{B}$. To prove this, note that $\widetilde{A}, \widetilde{B}$ and the difference $C=\widetilde{A}-\widetilde{B}$ are $w\left(\Gamma^{\prime}, \Gamma\right)$ compact convex sets. The relation $\|P \wedge Q\| \leq \beta$ can also be written $\frac{1}{2}\|P-Q\| \geq 1-\beta$. Thus, if $\gamma$ is any number $\gamma<1-\beta$, the set $C$ is disjoint from the ball $V=\left\{\mu ; \mu \in \Gamma^{\prime}, \frac{1}{2}\|\mu\| \leq \gamma\right\}$. This ball is also a $w\left(\Gamma^{\prime}, \Gamma\right)$ compact convex subset. Thus there is a $\psi \in \Gamma$ such that $\|\psi\| \leq 1$ and

$$
\sup \{\langle\psi, v\rangle ; v \in V\}<\inf \{\langle\psi, \mu\rangle ; \mu \in C\} .
$$

In particular $\langle\psi, \mu\rangle>2 \gamma$ for all $\mu \in C$. Let $\phi=\frac{1}{2}(1+\psi)$. This gives $0 \leq \phi \leq 1$ and $\langle\phi, P-Q\rangle>\gamma$ or equivalently $\langle(1-\phi), P\rangle+\langle\phi, Q\rangle<1-\gamma$. The result follows since one may take for $1-\gamma$ any number such that $\beta<1-\gamma<\varepsilon$.

The number $1-\pi(A, B)$ has a nice intuitive meaning, since it is one-half the $L_{1}$-distance $\inf \{\|P-Q\| ; P \in \operatorname{conv} A, Q \in \operatorname{conv} B\}$. However, it is not always easily computable and does not behave conveniently in direct product situations. Thus we shall also use another number $\rho(A, B)$ given by

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho(A, B) & =\sup \left\{\int \sqrt{d P d Q} ; P \in \operatorname{conv} A, Q \in \operatorname{conv} B\right\} \\
& =1-\inf \left\{h^{2}(P, Q) ; P \in \operatorname{conv} A, Q \in \operatorname{conv} B\right\}
\end{aligned}
$$

for the Hellinger distance $h$.
The relations between Hellinger distances and $L_{1}$-distances imply that $\pi(A, B) \leq \rho(A, B)$ and $\rho^{2} \leq \pi(2-\pi)$, so that, to a certain extent, $\pi$ and $\rho$ may be used interchangeably. One usable property of the function $\rho$ may be stated as follows.

Consider, besides $(\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{A})$, another space $(\mathscr{Y}, \mathscr{B})$. Let $A_{1}$ and $B_{1}$ be sets of probability measures on ( $\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{A}$ ) and let $A_{2}$ and $B_{2}$ be sets of probability measures on $(\mathscr{Y}, \mathscr{B})$. For $A_{1}$ and $A_{2}$ let $A_{1} \otimes A_{2}$ be the set of direct products $P_{1} \otimes P_{2}, P_{1} \in A_{1}, P_{2} \in A_{2}$. Use a similar notation for $B_{1} \otimes B_{2}$.

Lemma 2. For the sets $A_{1}, A_{2}, B_{1}, B_{2}$ one has the inequality

$$
\rho\left(A_{1} \otimes A_{2}, B_{1} \otimes B_{2}\right) \leq \rho\left(A_{1}, A_{2}\right) \rho\left(B_{1}, B_{2}\right) .
$$

Proof. An element $M$ of the convex hull of $A_{1} \otimes A_{2}$ may be written in the form $M=\int\left(P_{1, s} \otimes P_{2, s}\right) \mu(d s)$, where $\mu$ is a probability measure on a finite set. One can disintegrate the expression $P_{1, s}(d x) \mu(d s)$ in the form $P_{1}(d x) \mu(d s \mid x)$. where $\bar{P}_{1}=\int P_{1, s} \mu(d s)$ and where $\mu(d s \mid x)$ represents the conditional distribution of $s$ given $x$. Thus we may write $M$ in the form $M(d x, d y)=\bar{P}_{1}(d x) \bar{P}_{2}(d y, x)$, where $\bar{P}_{2}(\cdot, x)=\int P_{2, s}(\cdot) \mu(d s \mid x)$. Similarly an element $N=\int\left(Q_{1, t} \otimes Q_{2, t}\right) v(d t)$ of the convex hull of $B_{1} \otimes B_{2}$ can be written

$$
N(d x, d y)=\bar{Q}_{1}(d x) \bar{Q}_{2}(d y, x)
$$

Let $\gamma(x)=\int\left[\bar{P}_{2}(d y, x) \bar{Q}_{2}(d y, x)\right]^{1 / 2}$. The affinity $\rho(M, N)=\int \sqrt{d M d N}$ is then equal to

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho(M, N) & =\int\left[\bar{P}_{1}(d x) \bar{Q}_{1}(d x)\right]^{1 / 2} \gamma(x) \\
& \leq \rho\left(\bar{P}_{1}, \bar{Q}_{1}\right) \sup _{x} \gamma(x)
\end{aligned}
$$

Now, for each $x$, the number $\gamma(x)$ is the affinity between elements $\bar{P}_{2}(\cdot, x)$ and $\bar{Q}_{2}(\cdot, x)$ which belong, respectively, to the convex hulls of $\dot{A}_{2}$ and $B_{2}$. Thus $\rho(M, N) \leq \rho\left(\bar{P}_{1}, \bar{Q}_{1}\right) \rho\left(A_{2}, B_{2}\right)$, and the desired result follows.

Remark 1. There is another version of the preceding lemma in which convex hulls are replaced by somewhat larger sets definable as follows. If $A$ is a set of probability measures on $(\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{A})$, let $\tilde{A}$ be the set of measures $P$ of type $P=\int P_{s} \mu(d s)$, where $s \mapsto P_{s}$ is a map from some probability space ( $S, \mathscr{S}, \mu$ ) such that $s \mapsto P_{s}(C)$ is $\mathscr{S}$-measurable for all $C \in \mathscr{A}$. Proceed similarly for $B$ and
define $\bar{\rho}(A, B)=\inf \left\{\int \sqrt{d P d Q} ; P \in \widetilde{A}, Q \in \widetilde{B}\right\}$. Then again

$$
\bar{\rho}\left(A_{1} \otimes A_{2}, B_{1} \otimes B_{2}\right) \leq \bar{\rho}\left(A_{1}, A_{2}\right) \bar{\rho}\left(B_{1}, B_{2}\right)
$$

One could carry out the proof exactly in the same manner as above, except for the circumstance that one can no longer use the existence of the conditional distributions $\mu(d s \mid x)$. A possible proof is as follows. Replace the $\sigma$-field $\mathscr{A}$ by a field $\mathscr{F}$ generated by a finite partition of $\mathscr{X}$ by elements of $\mathscr{A}$. One obtains affinities such as $\rho_{\mathscr{F}}(M, N)$ and $\rho_{\mathscr{F}}\left(\bar{P}_{1}, \bar{Q}_{1}\right)$ computed on $\mathscr{F} \times \mathscr{B}$ or $\mathscr{F}$ instead of $\mathscr{A} \times \mathscr{B}$ or $\mathscr{A}$. For these, the preceding proof applies. As $\mathscr{F}$ is refined, $\rho_{\mathscr{F}}(M, N)$ decreases to $\rho(M, N)$ and $\rho_{\mathscr{F}}\left(\bar{P}_{1}, \bar{Q}\right)$ decreases to $\rho\left(\bar{P}_{1}, \bar{Q}_{1}\right)$. The result follows.



Remark 2. An exactly analogous inequality can be obtained for Hellinger transforms using $\int(d P)^{1-\alpha}(d Q)^{\alpha}$ instead of the affinity $\int \sqrt{d P d Q}$. The proof is the same.

Note that here $\rho(A, B)$ has been defined as an infimum of $\int \sqrt{d P d Q}$ for $P$ and $Q$ varying in the convex hulls of $A$ and $B$, respectively. Because of this feature the following easy result will be useful.

Lemma 3. In the space of probability measures on $(\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{A})$, the Hellinger ball of radius $r<1$ centered at a particular $P_{0}$ is a convex set.

Proof. Denoting the Hellinger distance by $h$ let $B=\left\{P: h^{2}\left(P_{0}, P\right) \leq r^{2}\right\}$. This may also be written $B=\left\{P: \int \sqrt{d P_{0} d P} \geq 1-r^{2}\right\}$. Let $Q=\int P \mu(d P)$ where $\mu$ is some probability measure (with finite support) on $B$. Then

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int \sqrt{d P_{0} d Q} & =\int_{\mathscr{X}}\left(d P_{0}\right)^{1 / 2}\left[\int_{B} d P \mu(d P)\right]^{1 / 2} \\
& =\int_{\mathscr{X}}\left[\int_{B}\left(d P_{0} d P\right) \mu(d P)\right]^{1 / 2} \\
& \geq \int_{\mathscr{X}} \int_{B}\left[d P_{0} d P\right]^{1 / 2} \mu(d P) \\
& =\int_{B}\left(\int_{\mathscr{X}} \sqrt{d P_{0} d P}\right) \mu(d P)
\end{aligned}
$$

Thus $\int \sqrt{d P_{0} d Q} \geq 1-r^{2}$ and $Q \in B$ as claimed.
For the purposes of our next section and many other purposes, Lemma 2 is important in that it yields the following corollary.

Lemma 4. Let $\left\{\mathscr{X}_{j}, \mathscr{A}_{j}\right\} ; j=1,2, \ldots$ be measurable spaces. For each $j$, let ( $A_{j}, B_{j}$ ) be a pair of sets of probability measures on $\mathscr{A}_{j}$. Let $A$ be the set $A=\left\{P_{1} \otimes\right.$
$\left.P_{2} \otimes \cdots\right\}$ where $P_{j} \in A_{j}$. Define $B$ similarly. Then

$$
\pi(A, B) \leq \prod_{j} \rho\left(A_{j}, B_{j}\right)
$$

Proof. This follows from Lemma 2 applied recursively and from the inequality $\pi(A, B) \leq \rho(A, B)$.

One can obtain a very similar result without Lemma 2, using instead the following elementary remark.

Lemma 5. Let $X_{1}, X_{2}, \ldots, X_{n}$ be independent variables such that $0 \leq X_{j} \leq$ 1 for all $j$. Let $p$ be defined by $n p=\sum_{j=1}^{n} E X_{j}$. If $p<1 / 2$ and if $S=\sum_{j=1}^{n} X_{j}$, then

$$
\operatorname{Pr}\left[S \geq \frac{n}{2}\right] \leq[4 p(1-p)]^{n / 2}
$$

Proof. First consider a fixed $t \geq 0$. Let $p_{j}=E X_{j}$ and $\phi_{j}(t)=E e^{t X_{j}}$. By convexity, among all the variables $X_{j}, 0 \leq X_{j} \leq 1$ such that $p_{j}=E X_{j}$, the maximum value of $\phi_{j}(t)$ is equal to the binomial value $\phi_{j}(t)=\left[1+p_{j}\left(e^{t}-1\right)\right]$.

For all $n$-tuplets $\left\{p_{1}, \ldots, p_{n}\right\}$ such that $\sum p_{j}=n p$ the maximum of $\prod_{j} \phi_{j}(t)$ is attained when all the $p_{j}$ are equal to $p$. To see this, consider a product

$$
\left[1+p_{1}\left(e^{t}-1\right)\right]\left[1+p_{2}\left(e^{t}-1\right)\right]=1+\left(p_{1}+p_{2}\right)\left(e^{t}-1\right)+p_{1} p_{2}\left(e^{t}-1\right)^{2}
$$

For $p_{1}+p_{2}$ fixed, the maximum is obtained when $p_{1} p_{2}$ is maximum; that is, when $p_{1}=p_{2}$. It follows that the maximum possible value of $\prod_{j} \phi_{j}(t)$, for fixed $t$, is simply equal to $\left[(1-p)+p e^{t}\right]^{n}$. An application of Markov's inequality yields

$$
P\left[S \geq \frac{n}{2}\right] \leq\left\{\left[(1-p)+p e^{t}\right] e^{-t / 2}\right\}^{n}
$$

Now, take the minimum of the right hand side for $t \geq 0$. The minimum is attained for $e^{t}=p^{-1}(1-p)$. This yields $P(S \geq(n / 2)) \leq 2^{n}[p(1-p)]^{n / 2}$ as claimed.

The foregoing argument shows that for the Laplace transform $\prod \phi_{j}(t)$ the maximum value is obtainable when all the $p_{j}=E X_{j}$ are equal. There is a very general result of W. Hoeffding which shows that this type of behavior is to be expected. It is as follows.

Lemma 6 (Hoeffding). Let $X_{1}, X_{2}, \ldots, X_{n}$ be independent variables such that $\operatorname{Pr}\left[X_{j}=1\right]=p_{j}=1-\operatorname{Pr}\left[X_{j}=0\right]$. Let $g$ be a symmetric function defined on $\mathbb{R}^{n}$. Then $\operatorname{Eg}\left[X_{1}, X_{2}, \ldots, X_{n}\right]$ is a function of $p_{1}, p_{2}, \ldots, p_{n}$ which, under the restriction $n p=\sum_{j=1}^{n} p_{j}$, reaches its maximum for an $n$-tuple $\left\{p_{1}, \ldots, p_{n}\right\}$ such that (i) $r$ of the $p_{j}$ 's are equal to zero, (ii) $s$ of them are equal to unity, and (iii) the remaining ones are all equal to some number $\alpha=(n-r-s)^{-1} n p-s$.

Proof. The function $\operatorname{Eg}\left(X_{1}, X_{2}, \ldots, X_{n}\right)$ is a symmetric function of $p_{1}, p_{2}, \ldots, p_{n}$. Also it is linear affine (that is of the form $A+B p_{j}$ ) in each of the $p_{j}$ separately. It achieves its maximum at some point of the set of $n$-tuples $\left\{p_{1}, \ldots, p_{n}\right\}$ such that $n p=\sum p_{j}$ and $0 \leq p_{j} \leq 1$. Suppose that $\left\{p_{1}, p_{2}, \ldots, p_{n}\right\}$ is such a maximizing point. It may have a certain number of coordinates equal to zero or unity. By symmetry one may assume that the $p_{j}$ such that $p_{j} \neq p_{j}^{2}$ are $p_{1}, p_{2}, \ldots, p_{m}$. Then the values of the variables $X_{j}, j>m$ are deterministic. Fixing them, one is reduced to the consideration of a symmetric function, say $\Phi$ of the first $m$ arguments. This $\Phi$ is also linear affine in each argument. Thus it will be enough to show that if such a $\Phi$ reaches its maximum at an interior point of a set $\sum_{j=1}^{m} p_{j}=\lambda, 0 \leq p_{j} \leq 1$, such that $p_{j} \neq p_{k}$ for some pair $(j, k)$, then $\Phi$ is constant over the entire set. By symmetry, again, suppose $0<p_{1}<p_{2}<1$ and $\operatorname{expand} \Phi$ as $\Phi\left(p_{1}, p_{2}, \ldots, p_{m}\right)=\Phi_{0}+\left(p_{1}+p_{2}\right) \Phi_{1}+p_{1} p_{2} \Phi_{2}$. Let $S_{2}=p_{1}+p_{2}$. Varying $p_{1}$ and $p_{2}$ in such a way that $S_{2}$ remains constant, one sees that $\Phi_{2}$ must vanish. Then $\Phi\left(p_{1}, p_{2}, \ldots, p_{m}\right)=\Phi_{0}+S_{2} \Phi_{1}$ where $\Phi_{0}$ and $\Phi_{1}$ are symmetric functions of $p_{3}, \ldots, p_{m}$.

Proceeding by induction, suppose that, at the point $\left(p_{1}, \ldots, p_{m}\right)$ one may write $\Phi\left(p_{1}, p_{2}, \ldots, p_{m}\right)=\Phi_{0}+S_{k} \Phi_{1}$ where $\Phi_{0}$ and $\Phi_{1}$ are symmetric functions of $p_{k+1}, \ldots, p_{m}$ and where $S_{k}=p_{1}+p_{2}+\cdots+p_{k}$. Then $\Phi_{0}\left(p_{k+1}, \ldots, p_{m}\right)$ is the value $\Phi\left(0,0, \ldots, 0, p_{k+1}, \ldots, p_{m}\right)$, where the first $k$ coordinates have been put
equal to zero. Similarly $\Phi_{1}\left(p_{k+1}, \ldots, p_{m}\right)$ is $\Phi\left(0,0, \ldots, 0,1, p_{k+1}, \ldots, p_{m}\right)$ where all but one of the first $k$ coordinates is zero and the exceptional one is unity. The expression $\Phi_{0}+S_{k} \Phi_{1}$ does not change value if one replaces $p_{1}, p_{2}, \ldots, p_{k}$ by $x_{1}, x_{2}, \ldots, x_{k}$, provided that $s=p_{1}+p_{2}+\cdots+p_{k}=x_{1}+x_{2}+\cdots+x_{k}$.

Expanding in terms of $p_{k+1}$, one can write

$$
\Phi=\Phi_{00}+p_{k+1} \Phi_{0,1}+S_{k} \Phi_{1,0}+p_{k+1} S_{k} \Phi_{1,1}
$$

where the $\Phi_{i, j}$ are functions of $p_{k+2}, p_{k+3}, \ldots, p_{m}$ and $S_{k}=x_{1}+\cdots+x_{k}$. By symmetry the value of this expression is unchanged if one interchanges $x_{1}$ and $p_{k+1}$. Note also that, from the expressions of $\Phi_{0}$ and $\Phi_{1}$ given above, one has $\Phi_{1,0}=\Phi_{0,1}$. Thus $\Phi$ becomes

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Phi & =\Phi_{00}+\left(p_{k+1}+S_{k}\right) \Phi_{0,1}+p_{k+1} S_{k} \Phi_{11} \\
& =\Phi_{00}+\left(p_{k+1}+S_{k}\right) \Phi_{0,1}+x_{1}\left(s+p_{k+1}-x_{1}\right) \Phi_{11}
\end{aligned}
$$

Here $x_{1}$ is arbitrary. Therefore $\Phi_{11}$ must vanish. It follows that $\Phi$ depends only on the sum of the $p_{j}$. This yields the desired result without difficulty.

Hoeffding has used this result to show that if $S=\sum X_{j}$ and $c>n p$ then the probability $P[S \geq c]$ is always inferior to the corresponding probability for the binomial case $p_{j} \equiv p, \sum p_{j}=n p$. This gives a better bound than Lemma 5. However the bound of Lemma 5 is often simpler to handle.

To apply Lemma 5 consider a sequence of pairs $\left(A_{j} \cdot B_{j}\right), j=1,2, \ldots, n$ as in the statement of Lemma 4. Let $\phi_{j}$ be a test such that $\left\langle 1-\phi_{j}\right\rangle P+\left\langle\phi_{j}, Q\right\rangle \leq \pi\left(A_{j}, B_{j}\right)$ for all $P \in A_{j}$ and $Q \in B_{j}$. Assume that $\pi\left(A_{j}, B_{j}\right) \leq \alpha<1 / 2$. Then $\left\langle\phi_{j}, Q\right\rangle \leq \alpha$. Consider the test $\phi$ which accepts the hypothesis $A_{1} \otimes A_{2} \otimes \cdots \otimes A_{n}$ if $\sum_{j} \phi_{j}<n / 2$ and accepts $B_{1} \otimes B_{2} \otimes \cdots \otimes B_{n}$ if $\sum_{j} \phi_{j} \geq n / 2$. Then according to Lemma 5, for every $Q$ in the direct product $B=B_{1} \otimes B_{2} \otimes \cdots \otimes B_{n}$ one has $\langle\phi, Q\rangle \leq[4 \alpha(1-\alpha)]^{n / 2}$. For all $P$ in $A=A_{1} \otimes A_{2} \otimes \cdots \otimes A_{n}$ one has $\langle 1-\phi, P\rangle \leq[4 \alpha(1-\alpha)]^{n / 2}$. This gives in particular

$$
\pi(A, B) \leq 2\left[4 \alpha\left(1-\alpha^{\prime}\right]^{n / 2}\right.
$$

In the case considered here, one could also use the inequality $\rho^{2}\left(A_{j}, B_{j}\right) \leq \alpha(2-\alpha)$ and apply Lemma 4. This would give $\pi(A, B) \leq[\alpha(2-\alpha)]^{n / 2}$. This bound is typically smaller than the one obtained above, simply because from $\left\langle 1-\phi_{j}, P\right\rangle+\left\langle\phi_{j}, Q\right\rangle \leq \alpha$ one cannot conclude $\left\langle\phi_{j}, Q\right\rangle \leq \alpha / 2$. Thus, in the cases where one wants to treat $A$ and $B$ symmetrically, we shall ordinarily use Lemma 4. On the contrary, when considering measures $Q_{\theta, j}$ which depend on a parameter $\theta$ and tests such that $\left\langle\phi_{j}, Q_{\theta, j}\right\rangle$ is bounded by a function $\alpha(\theta)$, Lemma 5 may be much preferable.

The foregoing arguments refer to direct products. Thus, they are in particular applicable to the case of infinitely divisible experiments. However, for these, some special techniques, giving better results, are also available.

Let $\mathscr{E}=\left\{P_{\theta}: \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ be an infinitely divisible experiment which is pairwise imperfect. Then the positive function $K$ defined on $\Theta \times \Theta$ by $K^{2}(s, t)= -\log \int \sqrt{d P_{s} d P_{t}}$ is a metric or pseudometric on $\Theta$, as shown in Chapter 9. To
relate it to existence of tests, we shall first prove a small lemma concerning Poisson experiments.

Consider a set $\mathscr{X}$ with a ring $\mathscr{A}$ and two sets $A$ and $B$ of finitely additive measures $\mu$ on $\mathscr{A}$. For each $\mu$, let $P_{\mu}$ be the distribution of the Poisson process of base $\mu$.

Lemma 7. Assume that $A$ and $B$ are convex sets. Let $\hat{A}$ be the convex hull of $\left\{P_{\mu} ; \mu \in A\right\}$. Define $\hat{B}$ similarly. Then

$$
\sup \left\{\int \sqrt{d P d Q} ; P \in \hat{A}, Q \in \hat{B}\right\}
$$

does not exceed

$$
\sup \left\{\exp \left[-\frac{1}{2} \int(\sqrt{d \mu}-\sqrt{d v})^{2}\right] ; \mu \in A, v \in B\right\}
$$

Proof. Since $P \in \widehat{A}$ and $Q \in \widehat{B}$ are finite convex combinations of the corresponding distributions of Poisson processes, one sees that it is sufficient to consider the case where $A$ and $B$ are convex hulls of finite sets. It is also sufficient to prove the result assuming that all the measures $\mu \in A$ and $v \in B$ are finite. The general case is obtainable by a passage to the limit.

Consider also an integer $n$ and the measure $P_{\mu / n}$ associated with $\mu / n$. This $P_{\mu / n}$ can be obtained as follows. First one selects an integer $N$ according to a Poisson distribution such that $E N=\|\mu / n\|$. There, one places $N$ points in $\mathscr{X}$ independently of each other according to the measure $\|\mu\|^{-1} \mu$.

The probability that $N \geq 2$ is at most $\frac{1}{2}\|\mu / n\|^{2}$. Consider convex combinations $\bar{P}=\sum \alpha_{j} P_{\mu_{j} / n}$ and $\bar{Q}=\sum_{j} \beta_{j} P_{v_{j} / n}$ with $\mu_{j} \in A$ and $v_{j} \in B$. Let $\sigma$ be the maximum of $\sup _{j}\left\|\mu_{j}\right\|$ and $\sup _{j}\left\|v_{j}\right\|$. Then $\bar{P}(N \geq 2)+\bar{Q}(N \geq 2) \leq \sigma^{2} / n^{2}$. Thus, if $\sigma$ is much smaller than $n$, this part of the space contributes to the affinity $\bar{\rho}_{n}=\int \sqrt{d \bar{P} d \bar{Q}}$ a term at most equal to $\sigma^{2} / n^{2}$.

The part of the space where $N=1$ contributes at most $(1 / n) \int \sqrt{d \xi d \eta}$, where $\xi=\sum \alpha_{j} \mu_{j}$ and $\eta=\sum \beta_{j} v_{j}$. The part where $N=0$ contributes $\left\{\sum \alpha_{j} e^{-\left\|\mu_{j}\right\| / n} \sum \beta_{j} e^{-\left\|v_{j}\right\| / n}\right\}^{1 / 2}$. This is smaller than $\left(1-\frac{\|\xi\|}{n}+\frac{\sigma^{2}}{2 n^{2}}\right)^{1 / 2}\left(1-\frac{\|n\|}{n}+\frac{\sigma^{2}}{2 n^{2}}\right)^{1 / 2} \leq\left\{\left(1-\frac{\|\xi\|}{n}\right)\left(1-\frac{\|n\|}{n}\right)+2 \frac{\sigma^{2}}{n^{2}}\right\}^{1 / 2}$, as soon as $\sigma<n$.
Similarly the affinity $\rho_{n}$ between $P_{\xi / n}$ and $P_{\eta / n}$ is larger than

$$
\exp \left\{-\frac{1}{2}[\|\xi\|+\|\eta\|]\right\}\left\{1+\frac{1}{n} \int \sqrt{d \xi d \eta}\right\}
$$

One concludes that there is some number $b$ such that $\bar{\rho}_{n} \leq \rho_{n}\left(1+b^{2} / n^{2}\right)$. According to Lemma 2, the affinity $\rho$ between measures in $\hat{A}$ and $\hat{B}$ is at most equal to the supremum of $\left(\bar{\rho}_{n}\right)^{n}$ over all the finite convex combinations of the type $\sum \alpha_{j} P_{\mu_{j} / n}$ and $\sum \beta_{j} P_{v_{j} / n}$ considered here. Also, $\rho_{n}^{n}$ is the affinity between the measures $P_{\xi}$ and $P_{\eta}$ which belong, respectively, to $\hat{A}$ and $\hat{B}$. Thus $\sup \left\{\int \sqrt{d P d Q} ; P \in \widehat{A}, Q \in \widehat{B}\right\}$ does not exceed

$$
\left(1+\frac{b^{2}}{n^{2}}\right)^{n} \sup \left\{\int \sqrt{d P_{\xi} d P_{\eta}} ; \xi \in A, \eta \in B\right\}
$$

The result follows.
Returning to the infinitely divisible experiment $\mathscr{E}=\left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ and the distance $K$ defined by $K^{2}(s, t)=-\log \int \sqrt{d P_{s} d P_{t}}$, this yields the following result.

Proposition 1. Let $\mathscr{E}=\left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ be infinitely divisible and pairwise imperfect. Let $s$ and $t$ be two elements of $\Theta$ such that $K(s, t)>b$. Let $A= \{\theta: \theta \in \Theta ; K(s, \theta) \leq a\}$ and let $B=\{\theta: \theta \in \Theta ; K(t, \theta) \leq a\}$. Then, if $2 a<b$, $\pi(A, B) \leq \rho(A, B) \leq \exp \left\{-(b-2 a)^{2}\right\}$

Proof. It is sufficient to carry out the proof for the case where $\mathscr{E}$ is a Poisson experiment corresponding to measures $\mu$ on a ring $\mathscr{A}$. Thus we shall argue as if $\Theta$ was a subset of this space $\mathscr{M}$ of measures. Now the sets $\bar{A}=\{\mu: K(s, \mu) \leq a\}$ and $\underline{B}=\{\mu ; K(t, \mu) \leq a\}$ are convex sets in $\mathscr{M}$. The distance between elements $\mu \in \bar{A}$ and $v \in \bar{B}$ is at least $b-2 a$. Hence the result.

The conclusion of Proposition 1 suggests that when $\mathscr{E}$ is a direct product of experiments $\mathscr{E}_{i}=\left\{p_{\theta, i} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ the performance of tests might be describable in terms of $K^{2}(s, t)=-\log \int \sqrt{d P_{s} d P_{t}}$. Such a description is not available, because Lemma 7 has no equivalent here. However, we shall now give some results in terms of another distance, called $H$.

This distance $H$ will be defined by

$$
H^{2}(s, t)=\sum_{j} h_{j}^{2}(s, t) \text { for } h_{j}^{2}(s, t)=\frac{1}{2} \int\left(\sqrt{d p_{s, j}}-\sqrt{d p_{t, j}}\right)^{2}
$$

One has always $H(s, t) \leq K(s, t)$. Also, if all the $h_{j}^{2}$ are smaller than some $\alpha \in(0,1)$ one has $H^{2}(s, t) \geq \alpha|\log (1-\alpha)|^{-1} K^{2}(s, t)$.

Note, however, that $H$ is not a function of the type of $\mathscr{E}$. It does depend on the particular representation of $\mathscr{E}$ as a direct product of factors $\mathscr{E}_{j}$. One could avoid the arbitrariness by replacing $H(s, t)$ by the supremum, say $\bar{H}(s, t)$, of all the values $H(s, t)$ obtainable from decompositions of $\mathscr{E}$ into direct products. The arguments given below would also apply to $\bar{H}$.

Proposition 2. Assume that $\mathscr{E}$ is a direct product of $m$ experiments $\mathscr{E}_{j}$ that are all of the same type. Let $A$ and $B$ be two subsets of $\Theta$ such that (i) for the distance $H, \operatorname{diam} A \leq a$ and $\operatorname{diam} B \leq a$, and (ii) there is a pair ( $s, t$ ), $s \in A, t \in B$, such that $H(s, t) \geq b>2 a$. Then

$$
\rho(A, B) \leq \exp \left\{-(b-2 a)^{2}\right\}
$$

Proof. Let $A_{j}$ and $B_{j}$ be the sets of measures corresponding to $A$ and $B$ for the $j$ th factor $\mathscr{E}_{j}$ alone. Then $\rho\left(A_{j}, B_{j}\right) \leq 1-(1 / m)(b-2 a)^{2}$, by Lemma 3 . It follows then from Lemma 2 that

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho(A, B) & \leq \prod \rho\left(A_{j}, B_{j}\right) \leq\left[\rho\left(A_{j}, B_{j}\right)\right]^{m} \leq\left[1-\frac{1}{m}(b-2 a)^{2}\right]^{m} \\
& \leq \exp \left\{-(b-2 a)^{2}\right\}
\end{aligned}
$$

Remark 1. This looks very much like Proposition 1. However the distances involved are different.

Remark 2. Let $\mathscr{E}_{j}=\left\{p_{\theta, j} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ where the $p_{\theta, j}$ are measures on a certain space $\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{A}$. The proof of the proposition is unchanged if one replaces the set $\mathscr{A}_{j}$ by the Hellinger ball of center $p_{s, j}$ and radius $a / m$ in the entire space of probability measures on ( $\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{A}$ ). This suggests investigating tests between Hellinger balls of that nature. It turns out that minimax tests between these balls have a very simple structure. They depend only on the centers of the balls and their diameter. They can be determined fairly easily for practical applications and lead to tests having certain "robustness" properties.

We start with a very simple geometrical observation. Consider a space ( $\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{A})$ and two Hellinger balls $A=\left\{P ; \rho\left(P, P_{c}\right) \geq \alpha\right\}$ and $B=\left\{Q ; \rho\left(Q, Q_{c}\right) \geq \beta\right\}$ for centers $P_{c}$ and $Q_{c}$, respectively. These balls are taken in the space of all probability measures on $\mathscr{A}$. To avoid trivialities, we shall assume $\alpha \beta>0$ and $\max (\alpha, \beta)<1$.

Lemma 8. Assume that the Hellinger balls $A$ and $B$ are disjoint. Then there is a uniquely determined pair $P_{m} \in A, Q_{m} \in B$ such that

$$
\rho_{m}=\rho\left(P_{m}, Q_{m}\right)=\rho(A, B)=\sup \{\rho(P, Q) ; P \in A, Q \in B\}
$$

The measures $P_{m}$ and $Q_{m}$ are mutually absolutely continuous and equivalent to $P_{c}+Q_{c}$.

Proof. For the inner product defined by the affinity, the set of all positive finite measures on $(\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{A})$ is part of a Hilbert space, say $\mathscr{H}$. Write $\sqrt{d P}$ for the element of $\mathscr{H}$ which corresponds to $P$ and let the inner product $\langle\sqrt{d P}, \sqrt{d Q}\rangle= \rho(P, Q)$.

Let $\rho_{c}=\rho\left(P_{c}, Q_{c}\right)=\cos \omega$ and let $\alpha=\cos \varepsilon, \beta=\cos \eta$. Let $\hat{A}$ be the set of points $y \in \mathscr{H}$ which have norm $\|y\| \leq 1$ in $\mathscr{H}$ and are such that $\left\langle y, \sqrt{d P_{c}}\right\rangle \geq \alpha=\cos \varepsilon$. Define $\hat{B}$ similarly. Clearly $A \subset \hat{A}$ and $\hat{A}$ is a cap on the unit ball of $\mathscr{H}$ cut off by a hyperplane orthogonal to the ray from the origin to the point $\sqrt{d P_{c}}$. If there are points of $\hat{A}$ and $\hat{B}$ closest to each other, they are already in the two-dimensional space $E$ spanned by $\sqrt{d P_{c}}, \sqrt{d Q_{c}}$ and the origin of $\mathscr{H}$. This is visible by projecting $\mathscr{H}$ orthogonally on $E$.

In the plane $E$ the vector $(\sin \omega)^{-1}\left(\sqrt{d Q_{c}}-\rho_{c} \sqrt{d P_{c}}\right)$ is a unit vector orthogonal to $\sqrt{d P_{c}}$. It follows that the point of $E \cap \hat{A}$ closest to $E \cap \hat{B}$ is the combination

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sqrt{d P_{m}} & =(\cos \varepsilon) \sqrt{d P_{c}}+\frac{\sin \varepsilon}{\sin \omega}\left(\sqrt{d Q_{c}}-\rho_{c} \sqrt{d P_{c}}\right) \\
& =(\sin \omega)^{-1}\left[\sin (\omega-\varepsilon) \sqrt{d P_{c}}+\sin \varepsilon \sqrt{d Q_{c}}\right] \\
& =\left[\alpha-\rho_{c} \sqrt{\frac{1-\alpha^{2}}{1-\rho_{c}^{2}}}\right] \sqrt{d P_{c}}+\sqrt{\frac{1-\alpha^{2}}{1-\rho_{c}^{2}}} \sqrt{d Q_{c}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Similarly the point of $E \cap \hat{B}$ closest to $E \cap \hat{A}$ is given by

$$
\sqrt{d Q_{m}}=(\sin \omega)^{-1}\left\{\sin (\omega-\eta) \sqrt{d Q_{c}}+\sin \eta \sqrt{d P_{c}}\right\}
$$

One verifies immediately that $P_{m} \in A$ and $Q_{m} \in B$. This establishes the existence of the pair $\left(P_{m}, Q_{m}\right)$. For the statement concerning mutual absolute continuity, note that since $A \cap B=\varnothing$ one must have $\omega>\varepsilon+\eta$ so that both $\sin (\omega-\varepsilon)$ and $\sin (\omega-\eta)$ must be positive. This concludes the proof of the lemma.

One can say a little bit more. Note for instance that the Radon-Nikodym density $d Q_{m} / d P_{m}$ is bounded above and bounded away from zero. It satisfies the inequality

$$
\sqrt{\frac{d Q_{m}}{d P_{m}}} \leq \max \left\{\frac{\sin \eta}{\sin (\omega-\varepsilon)}, \frac{\sin (\omega-\eta)}{\sin \varepsilon}\right\}
$$

Another important remark is that the pair ( $P_{m}, Q_{m}$ ) is entirely determined by the centers $\left(P_{c}, Q_{c}\right)$ and the radii of the balls. This pair is not affected if one imbeds the measures in a different $L$-space, for instance by enlarging the $\sigma$-field $\mathscr{A}$ or by imbedding in the second dual of the space of all finite signed measures on ( $\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{A}$ ).

The pair ( $P_{m}, Q_{m}$ ) plays here the role of "most unfavorable pair" for the sets $(A, B)$. For certain convex sets, namely those which are the sets of all probability measures bounded above by a two alternating capacity, Huber and Strassen have shown that tests between "most unfavorable pairs" actually yield minimax tests between the convex sets. Here the Hellinger ball $A$ is bounded above by the two alternating capacity $v(S)=\sup \{P(S) ; P \in A\}$. However, $A$ is not the entire set $\{P ; P \leq v\}$. As pointed out to me by R . Traxler, this is readily visible if $\mathscr{X}$ is a three point set. In spite of this, L . Birgé was able to prove the following result.

Theorem 1. With the notation and assumptions of Lemma 8, let $\phi$ be a measurable function defined on $(\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{A})$ with the following properties:
(i) For the measure $P_{c}+Q_{c}$, the function $\phi$ is equivalent to $\sqrt{d Q_{m} / d P_{m}}$.
(ii) Let $m=\operatorname{essinf} \phi$ and $M=\operatorname{ess} \sup \phi$ for $P_{c}+Q_{c}$. Then $m \leq \phi(x) \leq M$ for all $x \in \mathscr{X}$.

For such a $\phi$, one has $\int \phi d P \leq \rho_{m}$ and $\int \phi^{-1} d Q \leq \rho_{m}$ for all $P \in A$ and $Q \in B$.
The proof is somewhat long and will be divided into successive lemmas. To proceed, for probability measures $P$ and $Q$ and for a measurable function $\phi$ from $(\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{A})$ to $[0, \infty]$, let

$$
F[\phi, P, Q, a]=a \int \phi d P+(1-a) \int \phi^{-1} d Q
$$

where $a \in[0,1]$. Writing $v=P+Q$ and $d P=f d v, d Q=g d v$, the function $F$ can also be written

$$
F[\phi, P, Q, a]=\int\left[a \phi f+(1-a) g \phi^{-1}\right] d v
$$

It follows that $\inf _{\phi} F(\phi, P, Q, a)$ is attained for $\phi^{2}=(1-a / a)(g / f)$. The value of the minimum is $2 \sqrt{a(1-a)} \int \sqrt{f g d v}=2 \sqrt{a(1-a)} \rho(P, Q)$. As a function of $a$, this achieves a maximum at $a=1 / 2$ and the achieved value is $\rho(P, Q)$.

Now, let $\mathscr{B}$ denote a convex cone of positive measurable functions $\psi$ defined on ( $\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{A}$ ) and such that $\psi \in \mathscr{B}$ implies that $\psi+\psi^{-1}$ is bounded. Assume that $\mathscr{B}$ is a lattice, that $\mathscr{B}$ contains all functions $\psi$ identically equal to positive constants and that the $\sigma$-field generated by $\mathscr{B}$ is $\mathscr{A}$.

Let $V$ be the vector space spanned by $\mathscr{B}$. This is a vector lattice. Positive finite measures on $\mathscr{A}$ can be identified with elements of the Riesz dual $V^{*}$ of $V$.

Lemma 9. Let $A$ and $B$ denote Hellinger balls according to the assumptions of Lemma 8. Then

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho_{m} & =\inf _{\psi \in \mathscr{B}} \sup _{P, Q, a} F(\psi, P, Q, a) \\
& =\sup _{P, Q, a} \inf _{\psi \in \mathscr{B}} F(\psi, P, Q, a)
\end{aligned}
$$

where the supremum is taken for $P \in A, Q \in B$, and $a \in[0,1]$.
Proof. Let $\bar{A}=\left\{P ; P \in V^{*}, P \geq 0,\|P\|=1, \rho\left(P, P_{c}\right) \geq \alpha\right\}$. Let $\bar{B}$ be the corresponding set obtained from $B$. We have already noted that
$\rho_{m}=\sup \{\rho(P, Q) ; P \in A, Q \in B\}=\sup \{\rho(P, Q) ; P \in \bar{A}, Q \in \bar{B}\}=\rho\left(P_{m}, Q_{m}\right)$
By construction, $\bar{A}$ and $\bar{B}$ are convex sets. They are compact for the weak topology $w\left(V^{*}, V\right)$. Also $F$ is a convex function on the convex set $\mathscr{B}$. For each $\psi \in \mathscr{B}$, it is continuous on $\bar{A} \times \bar{B} \times[0,1]$.

An application of the minimax theorem yields the equality

$$
\inf _{\psi} \sup _{\xi} \int F(\psi, P, Q, a) d \xi=\sup _{\xi} \inf _{\psi} \int F(\psi, P, Q, a) d \xi
$$

where $\xi$ represents a probability measure on the compact $\bar{A} \times \bar{B} \times[0,1]$. However, $\int F(\psi, P, Q, a) d \xi$ is also equal to $F(\psi, \bar{P}, \bar{Q}, \bar{a})$ for some point $(\bar{P}, \bar{Q}, \bar{a}) \in \bar{A} \times \bar{B} \times[0,1]$. This gives the desired result.

The introduction of the arbitrary $a \in[0,1]$ could be avoided. However it yields the following corollary.

Corollary. For each integer $k$, there is some $\psi_{k} \in \mathscr{B}$ such that $\int \psi_{k} d P \leq \rho_{m}+2^{-k}$ and $\int \psi_{k}^{-1} d Q \leq \rho_{m}+2^{-k}$ for all $P \in A$ and $Q \in B$.

For the next lemma, recall that $\mu=P_{c}+Q_{c}$ and let $\phi$ be the particular function in the statement of Theorem 1.

Lemma 10. Let $P \in A$ and $Q \in B$ be such that $P+Q$ is dominated by $\mu$. Then

$$
\int \phi d P \leq \rho_{m} \text { and } \int \phi^{-1} d Q \leq \rho_{m}
$$

Proof. One has $0<m \leq \phi \leq M<\infty$. Thus every one of the functions $\psi_{k}$ in the corollary can be written in the form $\psi_{k}=u_{k} \phi$. The corollary applies in particular to the most unfavorable pair ( $P_{m}, Q_{m}$ ) yielding

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho_{m} & \leq \frac{1}{2}\left\{\int \psi_{k} d P_{m}+\int \psi_{k}^{-1} d Q_{m}\right\} \\
& =\int \frac{1}{2}\left(u_{k}+\frac{1}{u_{k}}\right) \sqrt{d P_{m} d Q_{m}} \leq \rho_{m}+2 \cdot 2^{-k}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since

$$
\frac{1}{2}\left(u+\frac{1}{u}\right)=1+\frac{(u-1)^{2}}{2 u}
$$

it follows that

$$
\int \frac{\left(u_{k}-1\right)^{2}}{2 u_{k}} \sqrt{d P_{m} d Q_{m}} \leq 2 \cdot 2^{-k}
$$

Therefore $u_{k}$ tends almost surely $\mu$ to unity as $k \rightarrow \infty$.
Now, return to the inequality $\int \psi_{k} d P \leq \rho_{m}+2^{-k}$ and pass to the limit as $k \rightarrow \infty$. By Fatou's lemma, this gives $\int \phi d P \leq \rho_{m}$. The same argument applies to $\int \phi^{-1} d Q$. Hence the result.

It remains to verify what happens for elements $P \in A$ which possess a nonzero part singular with respect to $\mu$.

Lemma 11. Let $P \in A$ be written as $P=P^{\prime}+P^{\prime \prime}$ with $0 \leq P^{\prime} \ll \mu$ and $P^{\prime \prime} \wedge \mu=0$. Then

$$
\int \phi d P^{\prime}+M\left\|P^{\prime \prime}\right\| \leq \rho_{m}
$$

Proof. Consider $P^{*}=P^{\prime}+\left\|P^{\prime \prime}\right\| \pi$ where $\pi$ is an arbitrary probability measure on $(\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{A})$. If $P \in A$, then $\rho\left(P, P_{c}\right)=\int \sqrt{d P^{\prime} d P_{c}} \geq \alpha$. Thus $\int \sqrt{d P^{*} d P_{c}} \geq \alpha$ and $P^{*} \in A$.

Now take for $\pi$ any probability measure dominated by $\mu$. According to the previous Lemma 10 one has $\int \phi d P^{*}=\int \phi d P^{\prime}+\left\|P^{\prime \prime}\right\| \int \phi d \pi \leq \rho_{m}$. This is true for every $\pi$ dominated by $\mu$. The result follows by concentrating $\pi$ on sets $\{x ; \phi(x) \geq M-\varepsilon\}$. The same argument gives a bound for $\int \phi^{-1} d Q, Q \in B$. This concludes the proof of Theorem 1.

Note that Birgé's argument provides bounds on the error probabilities. These could be obtained directly, without using Theorem 1. However, Theorem 1 provides a method of construction of the tests. Note also that these tests are given by measurable functions and not by elements of the dual $M(\mathscr{E})$ of $L(\mathscr{E})$. This is true independently of any domination hypotheses on $\mathscr{E}$.

Theorem 1 provides error bounds for the elements of the balls $A$ and $B$. We shall need below additional information for measures $P$ which may be totally arbitrary. The necessary result is, again, due to Birgé.

It will be convenient to use the following notation. Consider two probability measures $P_{c}$ and $Q_{c}$. Let $\omega$ be the angle defined by $\omega=\operatorname{arc} \cos \rho\left(P_{c}, Q_{c}\right)$ and let $\xi$ be a number $0<\xi \leq \frac{1}{3}$. Let $A$ be the ball

$$
A=\left\{P: \rho\left(P, P_{c}\right) \geq \cos \xi \omega\right\}=\left\{P: h^{2}\left(P, P_{c}\right) \leq 2 \sin ^{2} \frac{\xi \omega}{2}\right\}
$$

Let $B$ denote the ball $B=\left\{Q: h^{2}\left(Q, Q_{c}\right) \leq 2 \sin ^{2}(\xi \omega / 2)\right\}$. With this notation the function $\phi$ of Lemma 8 and Theorem 1 can be written

$$
\phi=\frac{[\sin (1-\xi) \omega] \sqrt{d Q_{c}}+(\sin \xi \omega) \sqrt{d P_{c}}}{[\sin \xi \omega] \sqrt{d Q_{c}}+[\sin (1-\xi) \omega] \sqrt{d P_{c}}}
$$

The least favorable pair ( $P_{m}, Q_{m}$ ) is given by $\sqrt{d P_{m}}=(\sin \omega)^{-1}\{[\sin (1- \left.\xi) \omega] \sqrt{d P_{c}}+[\sin \xi \omega] \sqrt{d Q_{c}}\right\}$ and by the analogous expression for $\sqrt{d Q_{m}}$. We assume that the actual bounds of $\phi$ are the same as the essential bounds for $P_{m}+Q_{m}$.

Lemma 12. Let $P_{c}, Q_{c}, \xi$, and $\omega$ be as described, with $\xi \leq 1 / 3$. Let $h= h\left(P_{c}, Q_{c}\right), d=h\left(P_{m}, Q_{m}\right), r=h\left(P_{m}, P_{c}\right)=\sqrt{2} \sin (\xi \omega / 2)$, and $R(\xi)=[\sin (1- 2 \xi) \pi / 4][(1-2 \xi) \sin \pi / 4]^{-1}$.

Let $P$ be an arbitrary probability measure. Let $C=h\left(P, P_{c}\right)$ and $z=r^{2} / C^{2}$ Let $y$ be the solution of the equation

$$
y^{2}(y+1) R^{2}(\xi)=\frac{4}{z}
$$

Then

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int \phi d P & \leq\left(1-D^{2}\right)+2\left(\frac{1-2 \xi}{\xi}\right)\left[1+\frac{3}{y}+\frac{1}{y(y+1)}\right][1-z]^{+} C^{2} \\
& \leq 1-(1-2 \xi)^{2} h^{2}+4\left(\frac{1-2 \xi}{\xi}\right) C^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

Proof. The case where $P$ belongs to the ball $A=\left\{P: h\left(P, P_{c}\right) \leq r\right\}$, with $r=h\left(P_{c}, P_{m}\right)$, is already covered by Theorem 1. Thus we can assume $C= h\left(P, P_{c}\right)>r$. If so, let $P^{*}$ be the measure $P^{*}=(1-\beta) P+\beta P_{c}$, with $\beta= C^{-2}\left[C^{2}-r^{2}\right]$. Writing the affinity between $P^{*}$ and $P_{c}$ and using the concavity of square roots one sees that $P^{*} \in A$. Thus it will be sufficient to bound the difference $\int \phi d\left(P-P^{*}\right)=\beta \int \phi d\left(P-P_{c}\right)$.

Write $d P=f d P_{c}+d P^{\prime}$ where $P^{\prime}$ is the part of $P$ that is $P_{c}$-singular. Select a number $x>1$. Let $S_{1}$ be the complement of the set $\{\sqrt{f} \leq x\}$ and let $S=\{\phi>1\} \cap S_{1}, T^{\prime}=\{\phi>1,1 \leq \sqrt{f} \leq x\}$, and $T^{\prime \prime}=\{\phi<1,0 \leq \sqrt{f}<1\}$.

On the part of the set $S$ where $x<\sqrt{f}<\infty$ one can write $f-1 \leq ((x+1) /(x-1))(\sqrt{f}-1)^{2}$. Since

$$
\phi \leq \frac{\sin (1-\xi) \omega}{\sin \xi \omega} \leq \frac{1-\xi}{\xi}
$$

one can write

$$
\int(\phi-1) d\left(P-P_{c}\right) \leq \frac{x+1}{x-1}\left(\frac{1-2 \xi}{\xi}\right) \int(\sqrt{f}-1)^{2} d P_{c}
$$



On the part of the space where $P_{c}$ vanishes, one has $d P-d P_{c}=(\sqrt{d P}- \left.\sqrt{d P_{c}}\right)^{2}$. Thus, on the set $S$, one obtains a bound

$$
\int_{S}(\phi-1) d\left(P-P_{c}\right) \leq\left(\frac{x+1}{x-1}\right)\left(\frac{1-2 \xi}{\xi}\right) \int_{S}\left(\sqrt{d P}-\sqrt{d P_{c}}\right)^{2}
$$

Now consider the set $T^{\prime}$. The inequality $\phi>1$ is equivalent to $\sqrt{d Q_{c}}> \sqrt{d P_{c}}$. This, together with the relation $\sin (1-\xi) \omega+\sin \xi \omega \geq \sin \omega$, implies that on $T^{\prime}$ one has $d P_{c} \leq d P_{m}$. This gives

$$
\int_{T^{\prime}}(\phi-1) d\left(P-P_{c}\right) \leq(x+1) \int_{T^{\prime}}(\phi-1)(\sqrt{f}-1) \sqrt{d P_{c} d P_{m}}
$$

On the set $T^{\prime \prime}$ one has

$$
\sqrt{d P_{c}} \leq \frac{\sin \omega}{\sin (1-\xi) \omega} \sqrt{d P_{m}} \leq \frac{1}{1-\xi} \sqrt{d P_{m}}
$$

and $(\sqrt{f}+1) \leq 2$. Thus,

$$
\int_{T^{\prime \prime}}(\phi-1) d\left(P-P_{c}\right) \leq \frac{2}{1-\xi} \int(\phi-1)(\sqrt{f}-1) \sqrt{d P_{m} d P_{c}}
$$

Letting $T=T^{\prime} \cup T^{\prime \prime}$ and $\lambda=\max (x+1,(2 / 1-\xi))$, this yields

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int_{T}(\phi-1) d\left(P-P_{c}\right) & \leq \lambda \int_{T}(\phi-1)(\sqrt{f}-1) \sqrt{d P_{m} d P_{c}} \\
& \leq \lambda\left\{\int_{T}(\phi-1)^{2} d P_{m} \int_{T}(\sqrt{f}-1)^{2} d P_{c}\right\}^{1 / 2}
\end{aligned}
$$

For any set $X$ let $I(X)=\int_{X}(\phi-1)^{2} d P_{m}$ and $J(X)=\int_{X}(\sqrt{f}-1)^{2} d P_{c}$. Note that $S$ and $T$ are disjoint and that $J(S)+J(T) \leq 2 h^{2}\left(P, P_{c}\right)=2 C^{2}$. Using this, one can write the above bounds in the form

$$
\int(\phi-1) d\left(P-P_{c}\right) \leq A\left[2 C^{2}-J(T)\right]+\lambda\{I(T) J(T)\}^{1 / 2}
$$

with $A=(x+1 / x-1)(1-2 \xi / \xi)$.
It will be shown later that if $\xi \leq 1 / 3$ all acceptable values of $x$ are such that $x+1 \geq 2 / 1-\xi$, so that $\lambda=(x+1)$. We shall use this value below.

Maximizing this as a function of $J(T)$ gives the inequality

$$
\int(\phi-1) d\left(P-P_{c}\right) \leq 2\left(\frac{1-2 \xi}{\xi}\right) \frac{x+1}{x-1} C^{2}+\frac{\xi}{2(1-2 \xi)}\left(x^{2}-1\right) D^{2}
$$

The definition of $P_{m}$ and $Q_{m}$ shows that $D^{2}=2 \sin ^{2}(1-2 \xi)(\omega / 2)$. Similarly, $h^{2}=h^{2}\left(P_{c}, Q_{c}\right)=2 \sin ^{2}(\omega / 2)$. This suggests introducing the ratio

$$
R(\xi, \omega)=\frac{\sin (1-2 \xi) \frac{\omega}{2}}{(1-2 \xi) \sin \frac{\omega}{2}}
$$

so that $D^{2}=R^{2}(\xi, \omega)(1-2 \xi)^{2} h^{2}$.
Note that $R(\xi, \omega)$ increases as $\omega$ increases. Thus

$$
R(\xi, \omega) \leq R(\xi)=\frac{\sin (1-2 \xi) \frac{\pi}{4}}{(1-2 \xi) \sin \frac{\pi}{4}}
$$

With this notation, one can write

$$
\int(\phi-1) d\left(P-P_{c}\right) \leq\left(\frac{1-2 \xi}{\xi}\right)\left\{2 \frac{x+1}{x-1} C^{2}+\frac{x^{2}-1}{2} R^{2}(\xi) \xi^{2} h^{2}\right\}
$$

Here $\xi^{2} h^{2} \leq 2 \sin ^{2}(\xi \omega / 2)=r^{2}$. Thus, we shall replace $\xi^{2} h^{2}$ by $r^{2}$. Introducing the ratio $z=r^{2} / C^{2}$ and the term $\beta=C^{-2}\left(C^{2}-r^{2}\right)=1-z$, we conclude that

$$
\beta \int(\phi-1) d\left(P-P_{c}\right) \leq C^{2}\left(\frac{1-2 \xi}{\xi}\right)\left\{2\left(1+\frac{2}{y}\right)+\frac{y(y+2)}{2} R^{2}(\xi) z\right\}(1-z)
$$

where $y=x-1$ should be such that $y \geq 2 \xi / 1-\xi$.
The value of $y$ that minimizes the above expression is a solution of the equation

$$
y^{2}(y+1)=\frac{4}{R^{2}(\xi) z}
$$

The assumed relation $P \notin A$ implies $z<1$. Also, the term $R^{2}(\xi)$ satisfies the inequalities $1 \leq R^{2}(\xi) \leq \pi^{2} / 8$. Thus the minimizing $y$ will satisfy the desired inequality $x+1=y+2 \geq 2 / 2-\xi$ as soon as $\xi \leq 1 / 3$. Instead of solving for $y$, one can use $z$ as a function of $y$. This yields

$$
\beta \int(\phi-1) d\left(P-P_{c}\right) \leq 2 C^{2}\left(\frac{1-2 \xi}{\xi}\right) Q(y)
$$

with

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q(y) & =(1-z)\left[1+\frac{3}{y}+\frac{1}{y(y+1)}\right] \\
& =\left[1+\frac{3}{y}+\frac{1}{y(y+1)}\right]\left[1-\frac{4}{y^{2}(y+1) R^{2}(\xi)}\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

To get an upper bound on $Q(y)$ one can replace $R^{2}(\xi)$ by its maximum value $\pi^{2} / 8<1.25$ and maximize $Q$ in the range $y^{2}(y+1) \geq 4$.

Direct computation shows that the maximum of $Q$ is attained in the range $2.2 \leq y \leq 2.3$ and that the maximum is smaller than 2 . This completes the proof of the lemma.

Our next proposition concerns the situation where the experiment $\mathscr{E}= \left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ is a direct product of experiments $\mathscr{E}_{j}=\left\{p_{\theta, j} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}, j \in J$, as in Proposition 2, except that here the $\mathscr{E}_{j}$ need not be of the same type. We shall metrize $\Theta$ by the function $H$ defined by $H^{2}(s, t)=\sum_{j} h^{2}\left(p_{s, j}, p_{t, j}\right)$ with $h^{2}\left(p_{s, j}, p_{t, j}\right)=\frac{1}{2} \int\left(\sqrt{d p_{s, j}}-\sqrt{d p_{t, j}}\right)^{2}$.

Note that $\exp \left\{-H^{2}(s, t)\right\}$ would be the affinity between pairs of measures ( $\left.\pi_{s}, \pi_{t}\right)$ in the Poisson experiment $\mathscr{P}=\left\{\pi_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ where the observable variables of $\mathscr{E}_{j}$ are replicated $v_{j}$ times independently, with $v_{j}$ distributed according to a Poisson distribution with expectation unity. The experiments $\mathscr{E}$ and $\mathscr{P}$ are not comparable, but one feels intuitively that $\mathscr{P}$ cannot be "much better" than $\mathscr{E}$. Thus we should expect analogues of Proposition 1.

One considers two subsets $A$ and $B$ of $\Theta$ and one wants to construct tests to distinguish $A$ from $B$. To the set $A$ corresponds a certain set $A_{j}=\left\{p_{\theta, j} ; \theta \in A\right\}$ of measures on the component $\mathscr{E}_{j}$. Similarly $B$ yields a set $B_{j}$. Here it may not be possible to construct appropriate tests by looking only at the pairs ( $A_{j}, B_{j}$ ) on the components. To see this, consider a space $(\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{A})$ and two distinct probability measures $p$ and $q$ on $(\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{A})$. Let $h^{2}(p, q)=\frac{1}{2} \int(\sqrt{d p}-\sqrt{d q})^{2}$. Let $\Theta$ consist of a pair ( $s, t$ ) and of the integers $1,2, \ldots, n$. Take $n$ independent observations $X_{j}$ so that, for $s$, the distribution of $X_{j}$ is always $p$. For $t$, let $\mathscr{L}\left(X_{j} \mid t\right)=q$. For the integer $m$, let $\mathscr{L}\left(X_{j} \mid m\right)=q$ except if $j=m$, in which case $\mathscr{L}\left(X_{j} \mid m\right)=p$.

Take for $A$ the set reduced to the point $s$ itself. Take $B=A^{c}$. Then, for any $\theta \in B$ one has $H^{2}(s, \theta) \geq(n-1) h^{2}(p, q)$. Also, diam $B \leq h(p, q)$. However, for each $j$ the sets $A_{j}$ and $B_{j}$ overlap and diam $B_{j}=h(p, q)$.

If in addition the experiment ( $p, q$ ) itself is infinitely divisible, so is $\mathscr{E}$, and Propositions 1 and 2 provide bounds for probabilities of error in tests of $A$ against $B$. However, these cannot be guessed at by looking only at the pairs ( $\left.A_{j}, B_{j}\right)$. Here is, however, a result applicable to such a situation. It is due to L. Birgé.

Consider a pair ( $s, t$ ) and let $H(s, t)=b$ and $h_{j}=h\left(p_{s, j}, p_{t, j}\right)$. In the component $\mathscr{E}_{j}$, the pair ( $s, t$ ) yields a pair of measures ( $p_{s, j}, p_{t, j}$ ). Select a number $\xi$ such that $0<\xi \leq \frac{1}{3}$ and let $\overline{A_{j}}$ be the whole Hellinger ball $\overline{A_{j}}= \left\{p: h^{2}\left(p, p_{s, j}\right) \leq 1-\cos \left(\xi \omega_{j}\right)\right\}$ where $\omega_{j} \in(0, \pi / 2)$ is the angle such that $1- \cos \omega_{j}=h^{2}\left(p_{s, j}, p_{t, j}\right)$. Let $\bar{B}_{j}$ be the corresponding ball centered at $p_{t, j}$. Lemma 8 and Theorem 1 yield a certain function $\phi_{j}$ such that

$$
\int \phi_{j} d p \leq \cos \left[(1-2 \xi) \omega_{j}\right] \quad \text { for all } p \in A_{j}
$$

and

$$
\int \phi_{j}^{-1} d q \leq \cos \left[(1-2 \xi) \omega_{j}\right] \quad \text { for all } q \in B_{j}
$$

Let $\psi$ be the function equal to zero if $\prod_{j} \phi_{j}<1$. Take $\psi=1$ if $\prod \phi_{j}>1$. If $\prod \phi_{j}=1$ take $\psi$ arbitrarily in $[0,1]$. This gives a certain test available on the experiment $\mathscr{E}$.

Proposition 3. Let $\mathscr{E}=\left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ be a direct product of experiments $\mathscr{E}_{j}=\left\{p_{\theta, j} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}, j \in J$. Take a number $\xi \in\left[0, \frac{1}{3}\right]$ and let $\psi$ be the test described before the statement of this proposition. Then for any number $q$ such that
$0<q<1-4 \xi$, there is a number $\gamma>0$ such that $\int \psi d P_{\theta} \leq \exp \left\{-q b^{2}\right\}$ whenever $H(\theta, s) \leq \gamma b$ and $\int(1-\psi) d P_{\theta} \leq \exp \left\{-q b^{2}\right\}$ whenever $H(\theta, t) \leq \gamma b$.

In addition, there are choices of $\xi$ such that $H\left(\theta^{\prime}, s\right) \leq \frac{1}{18} H(s, t)$ and $H\left(\theta^{\prime \prime}, t\right) \leq \frac{1}{18} H(s, t)$ imply, respectively,

$$
\int \psi d P_{\theta^{\prime}} \leq \exp \left\{-\frac{1}{2} b^{2}\right\}
$$

and

$$
\int(1-\psi) d P_{\theta^{\prime \prime}} \leq \exp \left\{-\frac{1}{2} b^{2}\right\}
$$

Proof. Let $s$ and $t$ be two elements of $\Theta$. In the experiment $\mathscr{E}_{j}$, they yield pairs ( $p_{s, j}, p_{t, j}$ ) that can play the role of the ( $P_{c}, Q_{c}$ ) of Lemma 12. Using the notation of that lemma, this yields numbers $h_{j}=h\left(p_{s, j}, p_{t, j}\right)$, angles $\omega_{j}$ such that $2 \sin ^{2}\left(\omega_{j} / 2\right)=h_{j}^{2}$, and radii $r_{j}=\sqrt{2} \sin \left(\xi \omega_{j} / 2\right)$. For $C_{j}=h\left(p_{\theta, j}, p_{s, j}\right)$ the inequality of Lemma 12 yields

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int\left(\prod \phi_{j}\right) d P_{\theta} & \leq \prod_{j}\left\{1-(1-2 \xi)^{2} h_{j}^{2}+4\left(\frac{1-2 \xi}{\xi}\right) C_{j}^{2}\right\} \\
& \leq \exp \left\{-(1-2 \xi)^{2} b^{2}+4\left(\frac{1-2 \xi}{\xi}\right) \gamma^{2} b^{2}\right\}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $b^{2}=\sum h_{j}^{2}=H^{2}(s, t)$ and $\gamma^{2} b^{2}=\sum C_{j}^{2}$. The first statement follows immediately.

For the second, take $\gamma=1 / 18$. Direct computation shows that the coefficient of $b^{2}$, equal here to $(2 / 81)+(1-2 \xi)^{2}-(1 / 81 \xi)$ is larger than $1 / 2$ in the range $.031 \leq \xi \leq .11$.

Remark 1. As mentioned previously, Proposition 3 is a result of L. Birgé. The present author had previously obtained a result of the same general nature. However, it used balls $\{\theta: H(\theta, s) \leq .025\}$ of fixed size, independent of the distance $b$ between $s$ and $t$. The fact that the balls used in the present proposition are bigger, with radii proportional to the distance between $s$ and $t$, is a major improvement. However it is not the main improvement. The tests previously used by the present author relied on a complicated construction of subproducts. The tests of Proposition 3 are built on the components $\mathscr{E}_{j}$ themselves in a very direct and simple manner. They use functions $\log \phi_{j}$ such that $\left|\log \phi_{j}\right| \leq \log (1-\xi / \xi)$ independently of the pair $(s, t)$. This gives them certain "robustness" properties, partly expressed in Lemma 12.

Remark 2. We shall need later a variant of Proposition 3 involving two sets, $A$ and $B$, such that $18 \max (\operatorname{diam} A, \operatorname{diam} B) \leq \operatorname{diam}(A \cup B)$. The same result applies, for an appropriate choice of points $s \in A, t \in B$.

## 5. Estimates for Finite Dimensional Parameter Spaces

The theory expounded in Chapter 11 depends heavily on the availability of estimates $\hat{\theta}$ such that $-\log \int \sqrt{d P_{\theta} d P_{\hat{\theta}}}$ stays bounded in probability. Such estimates can readily be constructed in very many special cases. However, general methods of construction are lacking and general necessary and sufficient conditions of existence are not known. Here we shall consider a special case for an experiment $\mathscr{E}=\left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ which is a direct product of experiments $\mathscr{E}_{j}= \left\{p_{\theta, j} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ for $j \in J$. We shall assume that $\Theta$ satisfies certain dimensionality restrictions and construct estimates by methods that have a distant kinship with Grenander's method of "sieves" with a touch of robustness added.

We start by a description of a variant of the maximum likelihood method. It suffers from some difficulties which can be remedied by a technique due to L . Birgé. We also describe another procedure, previously proposed by the present author, in which one decreases the diameter of confidence sets by successive application of testing procedures. The bounds obtainable in both cases are similar, but one can argue that each of the two procedures offers certain advantages.

The computation of bounds on the risks will be carried out using Proposition 3, Section 4 and the distance $H$ defined by

$$
H^{2}(s, t)=\frac{1}{2} \sum \int\left(\sqrt{d p_{s, j}}-\sqrt{d p_{t, j}}\right)^{2}
$$

for Hellinger distances computed on the components $\mathscr{E}_{j}$ of $\mathscr{E}$. One could also use other distances. For instance, if $\mathscr{E}$ is infinitely divisible, one can use the distance $K$ defined by $K^{2}(s, t)=-\log \int \sqrt{d P_{s} d P_{t}}$. One can also replace $H$ by the supremum $\bar{H}$ of all the distances of the type $H$ computed on decompositions of $\mathscr{E}$ as a direct product. When the number of terms in the direct product $\mathscr{E}=\prod \mathscr{E}_{j}$ is finite, the distance $H$ is necessarily bounded. However, this need not be the case for $\bar{H}$ or $K$. Thus, it is necessary to describe procedures applicable to sets $\Theta$ with infinite diameter. However, in most cases, one would not want to apply the method described here directly. One would first reduce the effective size of $\Theta$ by any available ad hoc but trustworthy method, possibly involving convergence of empirical cumulatives or such devices. In addition, minimax risks are obtainable as limits of risks on finite sets. Thus, occasionally, we have described the results only in the simpler case where $\operatorname{diam} \Theta<\infty$.

It should also be mentioned that, using $H$, the procedure does not necessarily insure that the resulting estimate $\hat{\theta}$ will be such that $K(\hat{\theta}, \theta)$ remains bounded in probability. This will be so if the individual distances $h\left(p_{s, j}, p_{t, j}\right)$ are kept bounded away from unity, and will be automatic in the identically distributed case. It will also happen in the particular cases described earlier in this chapter, where boundedness of $H(s, t)$ implies that $\sup _{j} h\left(p_{s, j}, p_{t, j}\right)$ tends to zero.

The constructions will involve covers of $\Theta$ by sets whose diameter or radius is bounded by a selected number, say $a$. Tests will be used to reject some of these sets. Taking into account Proposition 3 of Section 4, we shall use tests between
products of Hellinger balls. In many instances one will consider a fixed value $\xi \in\left(0, \frac{1}{10}\right)$ and for pairs of points $(s, t) \in \Theta \times \Theta$ one will construct measures $\mu_{s, t, j}$ by the formula

$$
\sqrt{d \mu_{s, t, j}}=\left(\sin \omega_{j}\right)^{-1}\left\{\sin \left[(1-\xi) \omega_{j}\right] \sqrt{d p_{s, j}}+\sin \xi \omega_{j} \sqrt{d p_{t, j}}\right\}
$$

with $1-\cos \omega_{j}=h^{2}\left(p_{s, j}, p_{t, j}\right)$. A measure $v_{s, t, j}$ is defined similarly, exchanging the roles of $p_{s, j}$ and $p_{t, j}$. This provides a likelihood ratio $\psi(s, t, j)= d \mu_{s, t, j} / d v_{s, t, j}$ and a function $\psi(s, t)=\prod_{j} \psi(s, t, j)$.

One possibility for the construction of estimates is as follows. One could call it a buffered maximum likelihood method. Take two numbers $a$ and $\tau$ with $0<a<\tau$ and $\tau \geq 18 a$ in general (or $\tau \geq 8 a$ in the identically distributed case). Let $\Omega \subset \Theta$ be maximal among subsets of $\Theta$ whose distinct points $s$ and $t$ satisfy $H(s, t) \geq \tau$. For a pair $(s, t) \in \Omega \times \Omega$, consider the corresponding function $\psi(s, t)$. Let $\phi_{s, t}=1$ if $\psi(s, t)>1$, let $\phi_{s, t}=0$ if $\psi(s, t)<1$. If $\psi(s, t)=1$ take $\phi_{s, t}$ such that $\phi_{s, t}=\phi_{s, t}^{2}=1-\phi_{t, s}$. Let $A_{\omega}=\{\theta: H(\theta, \omega) \leq a\}$. Let $S$ be the union of the sets $A_{s} ; s \in \Omega$ such that $\phi_{s, t}=1$ for all $t \in \Omega$ for which $H(t, s) \geq \tau$. The relation $\phi_{s, t}=1-\phi_{t, s}$ implies that $\phi_{s, t} \phi_{t, s}=0$. Thus the diameter of $S \cap \Omega$ cannot exceed $\tau$. There is an awkward possibility that $S=\varnothing$. Otherwise one could use $S$ as a confidence set for $\theta$.

The method is clearly a cousin of Grenander's method of "sieves". Grenander takes sets analogous to a skeleton $\Omega$ and applies the maximum likelihood method on $\Omega$. In our case, the likelihood ratios used are not the densities $d P_{s} / d P_{t}$ but the $\psi(s, t)$. This can make a very important difference in the performance of the method. One could also consider the procedure a distant relative of the maximum probability technique of Weiss and Wolfowitz. These authors use, instead of the densities $d P_{s} / d P_{t}$, densities $d Q_{s} / d Q_{t}$ where $Q_{s}$ is an average of the $P_{\theta}$ over a neighborhood of $s$.

Birgé proceeds with a pair ( $\Omega, a$ ) as above and with tests $\phi_{s, t}$ such that $\phi_{s, t}=\phi_{s, t}^{2}=1-\phi_{t, s}$. These can be, but need not be, the tests defined above using the ratios $\psi(s, t)$. Let $X(s, t)=H(s, t) \phi_{t, s}$ and $X(s)=\sup \{X(s, t) ; t \in \Omega\}$. Finally, let $X^{*}=\inf \{(X(s) ; s \in \Omega\}$. If there are points $\omega \in \Omega$ such that $X(\omega)=X^{*}$, pick one of them as the estimated value $\hat{\omega}$ of $\theta$. If there are no points with $X(\omega)=X^{*}$, take an $\varepsilon>0$ and pick $\hat{\omega} \in \Omega$ so that $X(\hat{\omega}) \leq X^{*}+\varepsilon$. Here again the existence of an $s \in \Omega$ such that $\phi_{s, t}=1$ for all $t \in \Omega, t \neq s$, is uncertain, but the procedure compensates by taking an $\hat{\omega}$ closest to the points $t$ which are preferred to it.

One could instead use the "buffered maximum likelihood method" with a variable $\tau$, taking a value $\tau$ such that the corresponding set $S$ be nonempty. We shall describe later a procedure which does just about that, but uses a sequence $\left\{\Omega_{v} ; v=0, \pm 1, \pm 2, \ldots\right\}$ of skeletons (or an analogous sequence of covers of $\Theta$ ) to reduce the influence of the selection of the number $a$.

For the present we shall describe some properties of Birgé's construction.
The first lemmas do not make reference to special choices of test functions $\phi_{s, t}$, even though later on we shall only use those arising from the densities $\psi(s, t)$.

To describe the performance of the procedures, consider a particular $\theta \in \Theta$. Assign to it an $\omega \in \Omega$ such that $H(\theta, \omega) \leq a$. For each $x>0$ let

$$
\beta(x)=\sup _{t}\left\{\int \phi_{t, \omega} d P_{\theta} ; t \in \Omega, H(\omega, t) \geq x\right\}
$$

Let $M_{\theta}(x)$ be the cardinality of the set $\{t: t \in \Omega, H(\omega, t) \leq x\}$. We shall assume below that $M_{\theta}(x)<\infty$ for each $x<\infty$. In this case, there is no difficulty in choosing $\hat{\omega}$ in a measurable manner. However, if one happens to choose $\hat{\omega}$ in a nonmeasurable manner, the statements given below are still valid, replacing such integrals as $E_{\theta} H^{2}(\hat{\omega}, \omega)$ by upper integrals.

Lemma 1. Assume that $H(\omega, \theta) \leq a$, that $M_{\theta}$ is finite, and that the integral $\alpha(x)=\int I[y>x] \beta(y) d M_{\theta}(y)$ tends to zero as $x \rightarrow \infty$. Then, with $P_{\theta}$ probability unity, there is an $\hat{\omega}$ such that $X(\hat{\omega})=\inf _{s}\{X(s) ; s \in \Omega\}$. Also,

$$
P_{\theta}\{H(\hat{\omega}, \omega)>x\} \leq \alpha(x)
$$

Finally, for any positive continuous increasing function $g$ defined on $[0, \infty]$, one has

$$
E_{\theta} g[H(\hat{\omega}, \omega)] \leq g(\tau)+\int I[y>\tau][g(y)-g(\tau)] \beta(y) d M_{\theta}(y)
$$

for every $\tau>0$.
Proof. Let $y>x$ be such that $H(\omega, t)=y$ for some $t \in \Omega$. Then $\int \phi_{t, \omega} d P_{\theta} \leq \beta(y)$. Let $B$ denote the event $\left\{\phi_{\omega, t}=1\right.$; all $t$ such that $\left.H(\omega, t)>x\right\}$. When this event occurs one has $X^{*} \leq X(\omega) \leq x$. Also, $X(s) \geq H(s, \omega) \phi_{\omega, s}=H(s, \omega)>x$ for all $s$ such that $H(s, \omega)>x$. Thus, there is an $\hat{\omega}$ such that $X(\hat{\omega})=X^{*}$. It satisfies $H(\hat{\omega}, \omega) \leq x$. The event $B^{c}$ has a probability at most equal to $\alpha(x)=\int I[y>x] \beta(y) d M_{\theta}(y)$. Hence the first two assertions.

To obtain the second, let $Z=H(\hat{\omega}, \omega)$ and let $F(z)=P(Z \leq z)$. Then

$$
E_{\theta} g(Z) \leq g(\tau)+\int[g(z)-g(\tau)] I[z>\tau] d F(z)
$$

There is a positive measure $\mu$ such that

$$
[g(z)-g(\tau)]^{+}=\int I[0<t<z] \mu(d t)
$$

The integral $\int[g(z)-g(\tau)] I[z>\tau] d F(z)$ may be written
$\iint I[0<t<z] I[z>\tau] \mu(d t) d F(z)=\int\left\{\int I[\tau<t<z] d F(z)\right\} I(\tau<t) \mu(d t)$.
Thus, if $\alpha(t)=\int I(y>t) \beta(y) d M_{\theta}(y) \geq P[Z>t]$, one will have

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int[g(z)-g(\tau)] I[z>\tau] d F(z) & \leq \int \alpha(t) I[\tau<t] \mu(d t) \\
& =\int[g(z)-g(\tau)] I[z>\tau] \beta(z) d M_{\theta}(z)
\end{aligned}
$$

This completes the proof of the lemma.
Since $M_{\theta}$ will not be readily computable, it is convenient to supplement Lemma 1 by the following simple observation.

Lemma 2. Let the assumptions of Lemma 1 be satisfied. Assume that for $y \geq \tau$, the function $y \mapsto g(y) \beta(y)$ decreases as $y$ increases. Then

$$
E_{\theta} g[H(\hat{\omega}, \omega)] \leq g(\tau)+\int I(y>\tau) g(y) \beta(y) d M(y)
$$

for any increasing function $M$ such that $M(y)-M(\tau) \geq M_{\theta}(y)-M_{\theta}(\tau)$ for all $y>\tau$.

This can be proved by the "integration by parts" technique that was used to prove Lemma 1.

Note that in Birgé's construction the balls $A_{s}$ and $A_{t}$ involved in the test procedures are all of the same radius $a$, independently of the distance $H(s, t)$. Thus, for any fixed choice of $a$, and $c<1$, one could select tests $\phi_{s, t}$ in such a way that for large values of $x$ one would have $\beta(x)<\exp \left\{-c x^{2}\right\}$. This may be valuable if $M_{\theta}(x)$ grows rapidly when $x$ becomes large. However, here, we shall limit ourselves to a particular choice of tests $\phi_{s, t}$, namely the choice indicated by Proposition 3, Section 4, in which one tests between the possibly larger balls $A_{s}^{\prime}=\{\theta ; H(\theta, s) \leq(1 / 18) H(s, t)\}$ and $A_{t}^{\prime}=\{\theta ; H(\theta, t) \leq(1 / 18) H(s, t)\}$ (with 18 replaced by 8 in the identically distributed case). This is not the "optimal" choice in some cases, but it will simplify computations. It also gives tests $\phi_{s, t}$ with certain robustness properties, as mentioned in Section 4. Also, for simplicity, we shall limit our considerations to the choice of function $g$ given by $g(x)=x^{2}$.

Corollary. With the choice of tests $\phi_{s, t}$ just described and with $M$ as in Lemma 2, one has

$$
P_{\theta}\{H(\hat{\omega}, \omega)>x\} \leq \int I(y>x) \exp \left\{-\frac{1}{2} y^{2}\right\} d M(y)
$$

for all $x \geq 18 a$. Also

$$
E_{\theta} H^{2}(\hat{\omega}, \theta) \leq\left(1+\frac{a}{\tau}\right)^{2}\left\{\tau^{2}+\int I(y>\tau) y^{2} \exp \left[-\frac{1}{2} y^{2}\right] d M(y)\right\}
$$

for all $\tau \geq \max (\sqrt{2}, 18 a)$.
Proof. This follows immediately from the preceding lemmas and from Proposition 3, Section 4.

The preceding corollary gives a finite bound for $E_{\theta} H^{2}(\hat{\omega}, \theta)$ for functions $y \mapsto M(y)$ that may increase fairly rapidly as $y$ increases. However, in some cases at least, it will be the rather moderate values $y$ which will lead to large values of the integrals. To see this, consider the case when $\mathscr{E}$ is a direct product of $n$ experiments $\mathscr{E}_{j}=\left\{p_{\theta, j} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ that are all of the same type. Let $r(s, t)$ be defined by $r^{2}(s, t)=\frac{1}{2} \int\left(\sqrt{d p_{s, j}}-\sqrt{d p_{t, j}}\right)^{2}$, so that $H^{2}(s, t)=n r^{2}(s, t)$. Take a fixed number $a$, say $a=1$ and consider the integral

$$
\int I\left[\tau_{1} \leq y \leq \tau_{2}\right] y^{2} \exp \left\{-\frac{y^{2}}{2}\right\} d M(y)
$$

with $\tau_{1}=8, \tau_{2}=100$. (Here the term $18 a$ of the corollary may be replaced by $8 a$, hence the choice of interval.) In this range, $y^{2} \exp \left\{-\left(y^{2} / 2\right)\right\} \geq \left(10^{4}\right) \exp \left\{-5 \times 10^{3}\right\}$, a fixed quantity. The value of $M(y)$ is approximately the number of points at distance unity from each other which may be fitted in the ball $\{t: H(t, \omega) \leq y\}$. In terms of the individual Hellinger distance $r$, this is the number of points at distance $1 / \sqrt{n}$ from each other which may be fitted in the ball $\{t ; r(t, \omega) \leq y / \sqrt{n}\}$. One can, as will be done below, bound $M(100)-M(8)$ by the number of points at distance $1 / \sqrt{n}$ from each other that may be fitted in the ball $\{t ; r(t, \omega) \leq 100 / \sqrt{n}\}$. However, this number may increase rapidly as $n$ increases, and so can $E_{\theta} H^{2}(\hat{\omega}, \theta)$. This observation suggests that Lemmas 1 and 2 and their corollary will lead to simple usable results only under dimensionality restrictions of the kind described below.

Definition 1. The dimension $D(\tau)$ of $\Theta$ for the pair ( $H, \tau$ ) is the smallest number $D$ such that every set of diameter $2 \delta \geq 2 \tau$ can be covered by no more than $2^{D}$ sets of diameter $\delta$.

The definition says "smallest" instead of "infimum" because $2^{D}$ has to be either infinity or an integer. Thus, in the finite case, there is a smallest integer $2^{D}$ with the required property. Otherwise one lets $D=\infty$. Note also that $D(\tau)=0$ means that the diameter diam $\Theta$ does not exceed $\tau$. It is also clear that $D(\tau)$ decreases as $\tau$ increases.

Assuming that $D(\tau)$ is finite for some $\tau$, take a number $a$ such that $D(a)< \infty$. Take a subset $\Omega \subset \Theta$ maximal among those whose distinct points $s$ and $t$ satisfy $H(s, t)>a$. Construct an estimate $\hat{\omega}$ according to Birgé's procedure, using the tests provided by Proposition 3, Section 4 for balls $A_{s}= \left\{\theta ; \theta \in \Theta, H(\theta, s) \leq \frac{1}{18} H(s, t)\right\}$. The following proposition gives an indication on the performance of the procedure.

It is to be expected that, when the selected number $a$ is large, the bounds on the risk will involve $a$ directly. If, on the contrary, $a$ is small, the dimension $D(a)$ will play an important role. The transition between the two cases will be described here in terms of two numbers $\xi_{1}$ and $\xi_{2}$. Writing $D(a)=D$ for short, let

$$
A=\frac{D}{D+2} \log \left[\frac{16(D+2)}{a^{2}}\right]+\frac{2}{D+2} \log \frac{D}{2}
$$

If $A>1$, let $\xi_{1}>1$ be the solution of the equation $\xi_{1}-\log \xi_{1}=A$. Let $\xi_{2}$ be the solution of the equation

$$
\xi_{2}-\frac{2}{D+2} \log \xi_{2}=\frac{2 D}{D+2} \log 72+\frac{2}{D+2} \log \frac{D}{2}
$$

(These are the functions which arise in the proof. One could use simpler expressions at the cost of some accuracy.) Note that if $A \geq 1$ one has $A<\xi_{1}< 2 A$.

Proposition 1. Let $\hat{\omega}$ be constructed by Birgé's method as explained and let $\xi_{i}, i=1,2$ be the numbers described above. Then
(i) if $A \leq 1$ or if $\xi_{1} \leq \xi_{2}$ one has

$$
E_{\theta} H^{2}(\hat{\omega}, \theta) \leq\left(\frac{19}{18}\right)^{2}\left\{(18 a)^{2}+4\left(\frac{D+1}{D}\right)\right\}
$$

(ii) if $A>1$ and $\xi_{1}>\xi_{2}$ one has

$$
E_{\theta} H^{2}(\hat{\omega}, \theta) \leq\left(\frac{19}{18}\right)^{2}\left\{(D+2) \xi_{1}+4\left(\frac{D+1}{D}\right)\right\}
$$

Remark. In the identically distributed case, one can use the coefficient 8 instead of 18. This yields a similar result, replacing $\left(\frac{19}{18}\right)$ by $\left(\frac{9}{8}\right)$ and replacing the value $\xi_{2}$ by the solution of the equation

$$
\xi-\frac{2}{D+2} \log \xi=\frac{10 D}{D+2} \log 2+\frac{2}{D+2} \log \frac{D}{2}
$$

The bound in (i) is then replaced by

$$
\left(\frac{9}{8}\right)^{2}\left\{(8 a)^{2}+4\left(\frac{D+1}{D}\right)\right\}
$$

Proof. Consider an arbitrary $a>0$ and apply the construction as described. To obtain a bound on the risk, it will be enough to bound the function $M_{\theta}$ of Lemma 1. Here a ball of center $\omega$ and radius $x$ can be covered by no more than $(4 x / a)^{D}$ sets of diameter $a$. Two distinct points of the set $\Omega$ cannot belong to the same set of diameter $a$. Thus $M_{\theta}(x) \leq(4 x / a)^{D}$. To apply Lemma 2, take a function $M$ possessing a jump of size $(4 \tau / a)^{D}$ at $\tau$. For $y>\tau$ let $d M(y)=(4 D / a)(4 y / a)^{D-1} d y$, with $D=D(a)$ for short.

The conditions of Lemma 2 leaves us free to choose $\tau$ at will, except for the restriction that $\tau^{2} \geq 2$. To apply the corollary we need in addition that $\tau \geq 18 a$. Under these restrictions, one obtains

$$
E_{\theta} H^{2}(\hat{\omega}, \theta) \leq\left(1+\frac{a}{\tau}\right)^{2}\left\{\tau^{2}\left[1+\left(\frac{4 \tau}{a}\right)^{D} e^{-\tau^{2} / 2}\right]+D \int_{\tau}^{\infty} e^{-y^{2} / 2}\left(\frac{4 y}{a}\right)^{D} y d y\right\}
$$

Writing $\tau^{2}=2 z, y^{2}=2 x$ and performing one integration by parts, one can rewrite the above inequality in the form



$$
E_{\theta} H^{2}(\hat{\omega}, \theta) \leq 2\left(1+\frac{a}{\tau}\right)^{2}\left\{z+\int_{z}^{\infty} f(x) d x+\frac{2}{D} f(z)\right\}
$$

with $f(z)=(D / D+2)\left(32 / a^{2}\right)^{D / 2} e^{-z} z^{D / 2+1}$.
The integral term may also be written in the form

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int_{z}^{\infty} f(x) d x & =f(z) \int_{z}^{\infty} e^{-(x-z)}\left(\frac{x}{z}\right)^{D / 2+1} d x \\
& =f(z) \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-x}\left(1+\frac{x}{z}\right)^{D / 2+1} d x
\end{aligned}
$$

The only values of $z$ to be considered here are those such that $\tau^{2} \geq 2$ and $\tau \geq 18 a$, that is $z \geq \max \left\{1, \frac{1}{2}(18 a)^{2}\right\}$. We shall restrict our attention to values $z \geq \max \left\{D+2, \frac{1}{2}(18 a)^{2}\right\}$. In this case

$$
\int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-x}\left(1+\frac{x}{z}\right)^{D / 2+1} d x \leq \frac{2 z}{2 z-(D+2)} \leq 2
$$

Now consider the function $f$ itself. Writing $z=(D+2 / 2) \xi$, one can write $\log f$ in the form $-\log f(z)=(D+2 / 2)\{[\xi-\log \xi]-A\}$, where $A$ is the term

$$
A=\frac{D}{D+2} \log \frac{16(D+2)}{a^{2}}+\frac{2}{D+2} \log \frac{D}{2}
$$

It follows that if $A \leq 1$, one has $f(z) \leq 1$ for all $z>0$. If $A>1$, the equation $\xi-\log \xi=A$ admits two roots $\xi_{0}$ and $\xi_{1}$ such that $\xi_{0} \leq 1 \leq \xi_{1}$. The term $z+\int_{z}^{\infty} f(x) d x$ achieves a local maximum at $z_{0}=(D+2 / 2) \xi_{0}$ and a local minimum at $z_{1}=(D+2 / 2) \xi_{1}$. For all $z \geq z_{1}$, one has $f(z) \leq 1$.

In summary, for all $z \geq \max \left\{D+2, z_{1}, \frac{1}{2}(18 a)^{2}\right\}$ one will have $f(z) \leq 1$ and

$$
E_{\theta} H^{2}(\hat{\omega}, \theta) \leq 2\left(1+\frac{a}{\tau}\right)^{2}\left\{z+2\left(\frac{D+1}{D}\right)\right\}
$$

In particular, if $A<1$ in which case $z_{1}$ does not exist, or if $1 \leq A \leq 2-\log 2$, in which case $\xi_{1} \leq 2$, one has

$$
E_{\theta} H^{2}(\hat{\omega}, \theta) \leq\left(\frac{19}{18}\right)^{2}\left\{\max \left[2(D+2),(18 a)^{2}\right]+4\left(\frac{D+1}{D}\right)\right\} .
$$

When $A \geq 2-\log 2$, the term $2(D+2)$ should be replaced by $2 z_{1}=(D+2) \xi_{1}$. One can check that the inequality $2(D+2) \geq(18 a)^{2}$ already implies $A \geq 2.1$. Similarly the inequality $(D+2) \xi_{1} \geq(18 a)^{2}$ can be written $16(D+2) a^{-2} \xi_{1} \geq(8 \times 9)^{2}$. Letting $\xi_{2}$ be the solution of the equation

$$
\xi_{2}-\frac{2}{D+2} \log \xi_{2}=\frac{2 D}{D+2} \log (8 \times 9)+\frac{2}{D+2} \log \frac{D}{2}
$$

one sees that $(D+2) \xi_{1} \geq(18 a)^{2}$ implies $\xi_{1} \geq \xi_{2}$.
From this one concludes that if $A \leq 1$ or if $\xi_{1}$ exists but $\xi_{1}<\xi_{2}$, one will have

$$
