E_{\theta} H^{2}(\hat{\omega}, \theta) \leq\left(\frac{19}{18}\right)^{2}\left\{(18 a)^{2}+4\left(\frac{D+1}{D}\right)\right\} .
$$

If on the contrary $\xi_{1}$ exists and $\xi_{1} \geq \xi_{2}$, then

$$
E_{\theta} H^{2}(\hat{\omega}, \theta) \leq\left(\frac{19}{18}\right)^{2}\left\{(D+2) \xi_{1}+4\left(\frac{D+1}{D}\right)\right\}
$$

This completes the proof of the proposition. The remark concerning the identically distributed case arises from the fact that, in that case, the requirement $\tau \geq 18 a$ can be replaced by $\tau \geq 8 a$. The argument remains the same.

One should note that the above proposition provides upper bounds, but upper bounds calculated in a very rough manner. The entire procedure relies on tests between balls of radius $a$ centered, respectively, at $s$ and $t$. However, the tests $\phi_{s, t}$ specified in the construction of the estimate $\hat{\omega}$ of Proposition 1 are tests between balls of radius $\frac{1}{18} H(s, t)$ centered at $s$ and $t$, respectively. For a fixed value $s \in \Omega$ and for values $t \in \Omega$ such that $H(s, t)$ is much larger than $18 a$, this means that a large number of tests are either needlessly used, or at least needlessly counted in the computations of the bounds. On the other hand, the formulas for the test functions, or for the functions $\psi(s, t)$ can be written down at once. Thus, in families described by tractable analytic expressions, one may hope to determine the estimate $\hat{\omega}$ by analytic methods.

One of the awkward features of the method is the dependence of its performance on the choice of the number $a$. This is not serious if the ratios $[D(a)+2] a^{-2}$ can be readily computed or approximated. Then one can select an adequate value of $a$, neither too large nor too small. We shall now present another construction in which the choice of value $a$ is rather irrelevant. The performance of the procedure does depend on the choice of another number, called $q$. However, a moderately poor choice of $q$ is not too serious.

The general procedure, which antedates Birgé's results, had been proposed on several grounds. One of the technical grounds is that the number of tests involved in it ought to be somewhat smaller than the number needed for covers by balls of fixed radius. Another is that using several covers by sets of successively smaller diameters ought to make the performance of the procedure fairly independent of the choice of the diameters. However, there was also a metastatistical reason. It seems to be a good principle of statistical practice to solve problems by steps: In a first step, one uses gross methods whose performance can be trusted even if the detailed assumptions made on the form of the experiment at hand are not really satisfied. Such gross methods should already permit, in most cases, to eliminate a large part of the parameter space as irrelevant. Then one proceeds with somewhat more refined methods and so forth recursively. This metastatistical principle is not logically justifiable, except perhaps in a Bayesian framework. However, it seems sensible.

The procedure described below does not quite conform to this principle, but it inherits some of its spirit, since it consists of constructing successively smaller and smaller confidence sets, until the possibilities of refinement seem exhausted. The construction had originally been described using partitions of $\Theta$. To facilitate comparisons with the results of Proposition 1, we shall use a variant with a sequence of skeletons $\Omega_{v}$.

Take two numbers $a>0$ and $q>1$. For each $v=0, \pm 1, \pm 2, \ldots$, let $\Omega_{v}$ be a maximal subset of $\Theta$ subject to the restriction that if $(s, t) \in \Omega_{v} \times \Omega_{v}$, then $H(s, t)>q^{v} a$. In the general direct product case, let $C \geq 18$. In the identically distributed case, let $C \geq 8$. For each pair $(s, t) \in \Omega_{v} \times \Omega_{v}$ such that $H(s, t) \geq C q^{v} a$ construct tests $\phi_{v, s, t}$ such that $\phi_{v, s, t}=\phi_{v, s, t}^{2}=1-\phi_{v, t, s}$ and such that

$$
\int\left(1-\phi_{v, s, t}\right) d P_{\theta} \leq \exp \left\{-\frac{1}{2} H^{2}(s, t)\right\}
$$

if $H(\theta, s) \leq q^{v} a$ and

$$
\int \phi_{v, s, t} d P_{\theta} \leq \exp \left\{-\frac{1}{2} H^{2}(s, t)\right\}
$$

if $H(\theta, t) \leq q^{\nu} a$. The tests $\phi_{\nu, s, t}$ will be taken the same as those used earlier in this section.

If $\operatorname{diam} \Theta<\infty$, there is a smallest $v_{0}$ such that $\operatorname{diam} \Theta \leq q^{v_{0}} a$. Then, $\Omega_{v_{0}}$ has only one point. Let $T_{\nu_{0}}=\Theta$. Now proceed to construct a sequence $T_{v_{0}} \supset T_{v_{0}-1} \supset \cdots \supset T_{v+1}$ and intermediate sets $V$ as follows. If $T_{m}$ has been constructed for all $m \geq v+1$, let $\Omega_{v}^{*}$ be the set of points of $\Omega_{v}$ whose distance to $T_{\nu+1}$ is at most $q^{\nu} a$. If $s \in \Omega_{\nu}^{*}$ and $C q^{\nu} a \leq x<C q^{\nu+1} a$, let

$$
\phi_{x}(s)=\inf \left\{\phi_{v, s, t} ; t \in \Omega_{v}^{*}, H(s, t)>x\right\}
$$

with $\phi_{x}(s)=1$ if the defining set is empty. Let $U_{v}(x)=\left\{s: s \in \Omega_{v}^{*}, \phi_{x}(s)=1\right\}$ and let $V^{\prime}(x)$ be the set of points of $\Theta$ at distance at most $q^{v} a$ from $U_{v}(x)$. Finally, define $T_{v}$ as $T_{v+1} \cap V^{\prime}\left(C q^{v} a\right)$, and $V(x)$ as $T_{v+1} \cap V^{\prime}(x)$. By construction $x<y$ implies $V(x) \subset V(y)$.

Lemma 3. Assume that $\operatorname{diam} \Theta<\infty$. Then for $x>0$, one has $\operatorname{diam} V(x) \leq (1+(2 / C)) x$. Also if the sets $\Omega_{v}$ are finite and if $\theta \in V(y)$ for all $y>x$, then $\theta \in V(x)$.

Proof. Consider two points $s$ and $t$ of $\Omega_{\nu}^{*}$ such that $H(s, t)>x$ for an $x \in\left[C q^{v} a, C q^{v+1} a\right)$. The construction of $\phi_{x}$ implies $\phi_{x}(s) \phi_{x}(t)=0$. Thus $U_{v}(x)$ has diameter at most $x$ and $V^{\prime}(x)$ has diameter at most $x+2 q^{v} a$. This gives the first result. For the second, note that $H(s, t)>x$ already implies $H(s, t)>y$ for some $y>x$. If the number of pairs involved is finite, there will be a $y>x$ such that $H(s, t)>y$ for all such pairs. Hence the result.

The preceding description has the merit of exhibiting the successive reduction in size of the proposed confidence sets. However, it applies only to the case where $\operatorname{diam} \Theta<\infty$. If $\operatorname{diam} \Theta=\infty$, a modification is needed.

Consider the skeleton $\Omega_{v}$ and the balls $A_{v, \omega}=\left\{\theta ; \theta \in \Theta, H(\theta, \omega) \leq q^{v} a\right\}$. One can well-order $\Omega_{v}$ in an arbitrary way and replace $A_{v, \omega}$ by the set $B_{v, \omega}= A_{v, \omega} \cap\left\{\bigcup A_{v, \alpha} ; \alpha<\omega\right\}^{c}$. This gives a partition of $\Theta$ by sets $B_{v, \omega}$ of diameter at most $q^{v} a$. It is still true that $\omega \in B_{v, \omega}$. Now consider a number $x$ such that $x \in\left[C q^{\nu} a, C q^{\nu+1} a\right)$. Define $\psi_{x}(s)$ as the infimum of the $\phi_{\nu, s, t}$ such that $t \in \Omega_{\nu}$ and $x<H(s, t) \leq(C+2) q^{\nu+1} a+2 q^{\nu} a$. Let $\psi_{x}(s)=1$ if the defining set is empty. Let $T_{\nu}^{*}(x)=\bigcup\left\{B_{\nu, s} ; \psi_{x}(s)=1\right\}$ and let $T_{\nu}^{\prime}=T_{\nu}^{*}\left(C q^{\nu} a\right)$. Let $T_{v}=\bigcap_{m \geq \nu} T_{m}^{\prime}$ and $V(x)=T_{v+1} \bigcap T_{v}^{*}(x)$. Here we have replaced the balls $A_{v, s}$ by the smaller sets $B_{v, s}$. This replacement can also be carried out in the construction described above for $\operatorname{diam} \Theta<\infty$.

If diam $V(x)>(1+(2 / C)) x$, there are two elements $s$ and $t$ of $V(x)$ such that $y=H(s, t)>((C+2) / C) x$. For each $m>v$, they belong to particular sets, say $B_{m, s^{*}}$ and $B_{m, t^{*}}$ of the partition. The pair ( $s^{*}, t^{*}$ ) satisfies

$$
y-2 q^{m} a \leq H\left(s^{*}, t^{*}\right) \leq y+2 q^{m} a
$$

Such a pair will be subject to test if

$$
C q^{m} a \leq H\left(s^{*}, t^{*}\right) \leq(C+2) q^{m+1} a+2 q^{m} a
$$

This will certainly happen if

$$
C q^{m} a+2 q^{m} a \leq y \leq(C+2) q^{m+1} a
$$

Letting $z=[(C+2) a]^{-1} y$, the preceding inequality becomes $q^{m} \leq z \leq q^{m}(C+2 / C+2) q=q^{m+1}$. It follows that if $y$ is sufficiently large, there will be some $m>v$ for which one of the two sets $B_{m, s^{*}}$ or $B_{m, t^{*}}$ will be eliminated. For smaller $y$, with $y \geq((C+2) / C) x$, the elimination will take place for one of the sets $B_{\nu, s^{*}}$ or $B_{\nu, t^{*}}$. Therefore $s$ and $t$ cannot both belong to $V(x)$. One concludes, as before, that $\operatorname{diam} V(x) \leq((C+2) / C) x$. The second conclusion of Lemma 3 remains valid, with the same proof.

This suggests defining confidence sets $\hat{V}$ as follows. If card $\Omega_{v} \leq \infty$ for all $v$ and if $T_{v} \neq \varnothing$ for all $v$, let $\bar{T}_{v}$ be the closure of $T_{v}$ in the completion $\bar{\Theta}$ of $\Theta$. Take $\hat{V}=\bigcap_{v} \bar{T}_{v}$. This is a set consisting of a single element of $\bar{\Theta}$. Otherwise, let $x$ be the smallest value such that (1) if $x \in\left[C q^{v} a, C q^{v+1} a\right)$, then card $\Omega_{v} \cap B<\infty$ for any bounded subset $B$ of $\Theta$, and (2) $V(x)$ is not empty. Then let $\hat{V}=V(x)$. If a point estimate is desired, let $\hat{\theta}$ be any point of $\hat{V}$.

To describe the performance of the procedure, consider a particular $\theta \in \Theta$.
For each $v$, this $\theta$ belongs to a particular element of the partition $\left\{B_{v, \omega} ; \omega \in \Omega_{v}\right\}$. Let this element be $B_{v, \omega(v)}$. Introduce increasing functions $N_{v, \theta}$ as follows. If $x \in\left[C q^{\nu} a,(C+2) q^{\nu+1} a+2 q^{m} a\right)$ let $N_{\nu, \theta}(x)$ be the number of points $t \in \Omega_{\nu}$ such that $C q^{v} a \leq H[\omega(v), t] \leq x$. Outside the interval in question, extend $N_{v, \theta}$ monotonically, using its lower and upper bounds in the interval. Let $N_{\theta}(x)=\sum_{v} N_{v, \theta}(x)$. This leads to an analogue of Lemma 1 as follows.

Lemma 4. For every $x>0$, one has

$$
P_{\theta}[\theta \notin V(x)] \leq \int I(y>x) e^{-y^{2} / 2} d N_{\theta}(y)
$$

Furthermore, if $g$ is any positive, continuous, increasing function defined on $[0, \infty)$ then, for $\tau>0$, one has

$$
E_{\theta} g\left[\left(\frac{C}{C+2}\right) H(\hat{\theta}, \theta)\right] \leq g(\tau)+\int I(y>\tau)[g(y)-g(\tau)] e^{-y^{2} / 2} d N_{\theta}(y)
$$

Proof. The second statement follows from the first exactly as in the proof of Lemma 1. To prove the first assertion, consider the case where $\operatorname{diam} \Theta<\infty$ and the relation $\theta \notin V(x)$. Assume that $x \in\left[C q^{v} a, C q^{v+1} a\right)$ and consider values $m>v$. There may be a largest $m$ such that $\phi_{x(m)}[\omega(m)]=0$ for $x(m)=C q^{m} a$. In this case $\omega(m+1)$ has been retained in the testing procedure, $\theta \in T_{m+1}$, and $\operatorname{diam} T_{m+1} \leq(C+2) q^{m+1} a$. The elements $t \in \Omega_{m}$ used in the testing procedure are all such that $H[\omega(m), t]>C q^{m} a$. Also, they are all within $q^{m} a$ of $T_{m+1}$. Thus, they are all in the corona $\left\{C q^{m} a<H[\omega(m), t] \leq(C+2) q^{m+1} a+2 q^{m} a\right\}$. For each one of them the probability that the corresponding function $\phi_{m, \omega(m), t}$ is zero is at most $\exp \left\{-\frac{1}{2} H^{2}[\omega(m), t]\right\}$. Since $\phi_{x(m)}[\omega(m)]=1$ implies $\theta \in T_{m}$, one sees that the probability that $\theta \notin T_{v+1}$ is at most $\int I\left[y>C q^{\nu+1} a\right] \exp \left[-\frac{1}{2} y^{2}\right] d N_{\theta}(y)$.

The part corresponding to tests $\phi_{x}[\omega(v)]$ can be bounded in a similar manner, yielding the desired result. When $\operatorname{diam} \Theta=\infty$, the computation is exactly the same. It will be omitted.

The bounds given in Lemma 4 and all the preceding lemmas and propositions involve probabilities computed for measures $P_{\theta}$ belonging to the family $\left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$. One can also obtain bounds for measures $P^{\prime}$ which are not in the family. For general $P^{\prime}$ the bounds are not interesting. However, here, $\mathscr{E}$ is a direct product $\mathscr{E}=X_{j}\left\{\mathscr{E}_{j} ; j \in J\right\}$ and one can obtain bounds for measures $P^{\prime}$ which are direct products $P^{\prime}=X\left\{p_{j}^{\prime} ; j \in J\right\}$ on the same index set $J$.

Assume that this is the case and compute distances $\delta(t)$ for $t \in \Theta$ by

$$
\delta^{2}(t)=\frac{1}{2} \sum_{j} \int\left(\sqrt{d p_{j}^{\prime}}-\sqrt{d p_{t, j}}\right)^{2}
$$

Let $\zeta_{0}=\inf \{\delta(t) ; t \in \Theta\}$. If there are points $\theta^{\prime}$ such that $\delta\left(\theta^{\prime}\right)=\zeta_{0}$, select such a point $\theta^{\prime}$. If not take a $\zeta>\zeta_{0}$ and select a $\theta^{\prime}$ with $\delta\left(\theta^{\prime}\right) \leq \zeta$.

Lemma 5. Let $V$ be constructed as explained by a stepwise method using a $C \geq 36$. Then for every $x \geq 36 \zeta$ one has

$$
P^{\prime}\left\{\theta^{\prime} \notin V(x)\right\} \leq \int I(y>x) e^{-y^{2} / 2} d N_{\theta}(y)
$$

The last assertion of Lemma 4 is also valid for $\tau>36 \zeta$.
Remark. The validity of Lemma 5 depends very essentially on the choice of tests which satisfy the requirements of Proposition 3, Section 4.

Proof. Proceed as in the proof of Lemma 4, letting $v$ be such that $x \in \left[C q^{v} a, C q^{v+1} a\right)$. Let $\omega(m)$ be such that $\theta^{\prime} \in B_{m, \omega(m)}$. Then, for all $m \geq v$, the pairs $[\omega(m), t]$ used in the construction of $V(x)$ satisfy the inequality $H[\omega(m), t] \geq \max \left[x, C q^{m} a\right]$. Let $\phi$ be one of the test functions, for a pair $(\omega, t)$. According to Proposition 3, Section 4, one will have $\int(1-\phi) d P^{\prime} \leq$
$\exp \left\{-\frac{1}{2} H^{2}(\omega, t)\right\}$ for any product probability $P^{\prime}$ such that $\delta(\omega) \leq \frac{1}{18} H(\omega, t)$. For $m \geq v$ this last inequality holds whenever $H[\omega(m), t] \geq 18\left[\zeta+q^{m} a\right]$ or $x \geq 18\left[\zeta+q^{m} a\right]$. For $C \geq 36$ this is satisfied whenever $x \geq 36 \zeta$.

There is, of course, an analogous result for the procedure proposed by Birgé. With the notation of Lemmas 1 and 2 it would look as follows.

Lemma 6. Assume that $P^{\prime}$ is as in Lemma 5. Construct an estimate $\hat{\omega}$ by Birgé's method with balls of radius $a$ and with the tests of Proposition 3, Section 4. Then for all $x \geq 18(\zeta+a)$ one has

$$
P^{\prime}\{H(\hat{\omega}, \omega)>x\} \leq \int I(y>x) \exp \left\{-\frac{1}{2} y^{2}\right\} d M(y)
$$

for an $\omega \in \Omega$ such that $\delta(\omega) \leq \zeta+a$.
Lemmas 5 and 6 can be considered as statements of "robustness" of the methods described here. Note that for the stepwise testing procedure to obtain the robustness we increased the coefficient $C$ from 18 to 36 . This makes the performance of the procedure a bit worse if $P^{\prime}$ is really in the family $\left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$.

It can be noted that the assertions in Lemmas 1 and 4 differ in that Lemmas 1 and 2 involve a function $M_{\theta}$ while Lemma 4 uses $N_{\theta}$. For the same value of the number $a$ one would expect $N_{\theta}$ to grow slower than $M_{\theta}$. Indeed, $N_{\theta}$ is obtained by counting the number of points at distances larger than $q^{\nu} a$ from each other which may be fitted in a set $\left\{t: C q^{\nu} a \leq H(\omega, t) \leq(C+2) q^{\nu+1} a+2 q^{\nu} a\right\}$. For the same set the function $M_{\theta}$ counts the number of points which can be fitted with distances larger than $a$. If $v \geq 1$, this latter number is larger than the first. However the various coronas do overlap and the sum $N_{\theta}$ may increase faster than $M_{\theta}$. We shall not pursue the comparison and pass to an inequality obtainable by a crude evaluation of $N_{\theta}$. Define a function $L$ by $L(\tau)=\max \left\{2 q^{2}, L^{\prime}(\tau)\right\}$ where

$$
L^{\prime}(\tau)=2 q^{2}\left\{D\left(\frac{\tau}{C}\right) \log [4[(C+2) q+2]]-\log \left[q^{2} \log q^{2}\right]\right\}
$$

If such a number exists, let $\tau(q)$ be defined by the requirement that $L[\tau(q)+ \varepsilon] \leq \tau^{2}(q) \leq L[\tau(q)-\varepsilon]$ for all $\varepsilon>0$. Since the inequality $L(\tau)<\tau^{2}$ cannot hold for all $\tau>0$, the number $\tau(q)$ will exist unless $\operatorname{diam} \Theta<\infty$ and $L(\tau)>\tau^{2}$ for all $\tau$. In this case take $\tau(q)=q^{-1} \operatorname{diam} \Theta$.

Proposition 2. Let $D(\tau)$ be finite for some $\tau>0$. Construct an estimate $\hat{\theta}$ as described. Then, for the number $\tau(q)$ defined above, one has

$$
E_{\theta} H^{2}(\hat{\theta}, \theta) \leq\left(\frac{C+2}{C}\right)^{2} q^{2}\left[2+\tau^{2}(q)\right]
$$

(With $C \geq 18$ in the general direct product case and $C \geq 8$ in the identically distributed case.)

Proof. Consider values $\tau^{2} \geq 2$ and the bound

$$
E_{\theta}\left(\frac{C}{C+2}\right)^{2} H^{2}(\hat{\theta}, \theta) \leq \tau^{2}+\int I(y>\tau) y^{2} e^{-y^{2} / 2} d N_{\theta}(y)
$$

Let $x_{m}=C q^{m} a$. As long as $2 \leq \tau^{2} \leq x_{m}$, one can replace the function $N_{\theta}$ in the interval $\left[x_{m}, x_{m+1}\right)$ by a function which has only one jump at the point $x_{m}$, the value of the jump being the number of points of $\Omega_{m}$ in the corona $\left\{t: C q^{m} a \leq H[\omega(m), t] \leq(C+2) q^{m+1} a+2 q^{m} a\right\}$. If $D_{m}$ is the dimension $D_{m}=D\left(q^{m} a\right)$, the number of points in question does not exceed $\{4[(C+2) q+2]\}^{D_{m}}$. Replacing this in the above bound for a value $\tau=x_{m} \geq \sqrt{2}$ one obtains an expression $B_{m}=x_{m}^{2}+A_{m}[4(C+2) q+2]^{D_{m}}$, where $A_{m}$ is the sum

$$
A_{m}=\sum_{v \geq m}\left(C q^{v} a\right)^{2} \exp \left\{-\frac{1}{2}\left(C q^{v} a\right)^{2}\right\}
$$

If one has not only $x_{m}^{2} \geq 2$ but also $x_{m-1}^{2} \geq 2$, one can bound $A_{m}$ by the integral

$$
\begin{aligned}
J_{m} & =\int_{m-1}^{\infty}\left(C q^{x} a\right)^{2} \exp \left\{-\frac{1}{2}\left(C q^{x} a\right)^{2}\right\} d x \\
& =\frac{1}{(\log q)} \exp \left\{-\frac{1}{2} C^{2} a^{2} q^{2(m-1)}\right\}
\end{aligned}
$$

Thus $\left(C q^{m-1} a\right)^{2} \geq 2$ implies

$$
B_{m} \leq x_{m}^{2}+[4(C+2) q+2]^{D_{m}} \frac{1}{\log q} \exp \left\{-\frac{x_{m}^{2}}{2 q^{2}}\right\}
$$

Consider the function $f_{m}$ defined on $[0, \infty)$ by

$$
f_{m}(x)=x^{2}+[4(C+2) q+2]^{D_{m}} \frac{1}{\log q} \exp \left\{-\frac{x^{2}}{2 q^{2}}\right\}
$$

Let $K_{m}=D_{m} \log 4[(C+2) q+2]-\log \left[q^{2} \log q^{2}\right]$. If $K_{m} \geq 0$, the function $f_{m}$ achieves a minimum at a value $\tau_{m}$ defined by $\tau_{m}^{2}=2 q^{2} K_{m}$. In addition, whenever $x \geq \tau_{m}$ one has $f_{m}(x) \leq x^{2}+2 q^{2}$. Let

$$
L^{\prime}(\tau)=2 q^{2}\left\{D\left(\frac{\tau}{C}\right) \log [4[(C+2) q+2]]-\log \left[q^{2} \log q^{2}\right]\right\}
$$

and $L(\tau)=\max \left\{2 q^{2}, L^{\prime}(\tau)\right\}$. According to the above argument, if $\tau_{m} \geq 0$, one has $\tau_{m}^{2}=L^{\prime}\left(x_{m}\right)$. Suppose that there exists a number $\tau(q)$ satisfying $L[\tau(q)+\varepsilon] \leq \tau^{2}(q) \leq L[\tau(q)-\varepsilon]$ for all $\varepsilon>0$. Then there is a smallest integer $m$ such that $x_{m}^{2} \geq L\left(x_{m}\right)$. For such an integer $m$ one has $x_{m}^{2} \geq 2 q^{2}$, hence $x_{m-1}^{2} \geq 2$. Therefore the bound obtained on $B_{m}$ is applicable here. For this $m$, one has also $x_{m-1}^{2}<L\left(x_{m-1}\right)$, hence $x_{m-1} \leq \tau(q) \leq x_{m}$. Since $x_{m}^{2}=q^{2} x_{m-1}$ this yields the inequality $x_{m}^{2} \leq q^{2} \tau^{2}(q)$. In addition $x_{m}^{2} \geq L\left(x_{m}\right)$ implies $x_{m}^{2} \geq L^{\prime}\left(x_{m}\right)=\tau_{m}^{2}$, hence $B_{m} \leq x_{m}^{2}+2 q^{2} \leq q^{2}\left[2+\tau^{2}(q)\right]$.

This completes the proof of the proposition, except for the case where $L(\tau)> \tau^{2}$ for all $\tau$. If diam $\Theta=\infty$, this would imply $D(\tau)=\infty$ for all $\tau$ contrary to assumption. If, on the contrary, $\operatorname{diam} \Theta<\infty$, but $L(\tau)>\tau^{2}$ for all $\tau$, we have
taken $\tau^{2}(q)=q^{-2}[\operatorname{diam} \Theta]^{2}$ so that the result of the proposition is trivially true. Hence the assertion.

In Proposition 2 we have taken a value $\tau(q)$ defined as an approximate solution of $\max \left[2 q^{2}, L^{\prime}(\tau)\right]=\tau^{2}$. For $q$ sufficiently small and certainly for $1<q \leq e$, the quantity $L^{\prime}(\tau)$ will always be larger than $2 q^{2}$, unless $D(\tau / C)=0$ in which case $\operatorname{diam} \Theta \leq \tau / C$. It is clear from the form of the bound that one wants to select $q$ close to unity, but not so close that the term $-\log \left[q^{2} \log q^{2}\right]$ starts dominating the rest. For purposes of comparison with Proposition 1, we shall take, arbitrarily, $q_{0}=1+(1 / 20)$. The function $L$ is then approximately equal to $(9.92) D(\tau / 18)+5.06$. This leads to a choice of $\tau=\tau\left(q_{0}\right)$ such that if $b=\tau / 18$, then $b^{-2}[D(b)+2]$ is approximately $32.7+(1.5) b^{-2}$.

In Proposition 1 one can attempt to select the value $a$ so that the effect of the dimension is just being felt. In the notation of that proposition, this means that $(18 a)^{2}$ is approximately equal to $[D(a)+2] \xi_{1}$. For large values of $D(a)$ this implies selecting $a$ so that $a^{-2}[D(a)+2]$ is close to 37.9. Thus the number $b$ defined above and the "best" choice of $a$ in Proposition 1 are roughly in the same range. Of course, in both cases, the computation of the bounds is rather brutal and wasteful and comparisons of such bounds mean relatively little. In summary, Birgé's method has the merit of simplicity. The alternate procedure has the merit of performing independently of the choice of a basic radius for the covering balls. The performances of the two procedures, as described by the available bounds, are roughly comparable. Intuitively the stepwise procedure ought to be somewhat more economical, but the situation is
complex. One can attempt to compare the two procedures by comparing the functions $M_{\theta}$ of Lemma 1 with the functions $N_{\theta}$ of Lemma 4. It then appears that for large values of $x$ the function $x \mapsto N_{\theta}(x)$ does indeed grow slower than $x \mapsto M_{\theta}(x)$. However, the situation may be reversed for small values of $x$. More specifically, let $k$ be the integer part of $(\log q)^{-1} \log \left\{[(C+2) q+3] C^{-1}\right\}$. (This is 5 if $C=18$ and $q=1.05$.) Let $a$ be the number which gives the best results in the bounds of Proposition 1 and work with a given dimension $D$. It is possible to argue that for $x \geq C q^{k} k^{1 / D} a$ the growth of $N_{\theta}(x)$ is slower than that of $M_{\theta}(x)$. However, for smaller values, $N_{\theta}$ may be affected by the attempt to use balls of radius smaller than $a$. For these, the associated tests are not very good and the performance of the procedure suffers accordingly.

Either Proposition 1 or 2 can be used to obtain bounds for the simplest case where the function $D(\tau)$ is bounded above independently of $\tau$. This gives results of the following type.

Corollary. Let $\mathscr{E}=\left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ be a direct product such that the dimension $D(\tau)$ of $\Theta$ for $(H, \tau)$ never exceeds $D_{0}$. Then there are estimates $\hat{\theta}$ such that

$$
E_{\theta} H^{2}(\hat{\theta}, \theta) \leq 14 D_{0}+10
$$

Proof. This is obtainable by taking $q=1.05$ and $C=18$ in the bound of Proposition 2. One could also select $a$ appropriately in Proposition 1 and obtain a bound of the type

$$
E_{\theta} H^{2}(\hat{\theta}, \theta) \leq 9.54 D+(2.92) \log D+(8.70)+\frac{4.46}{D}
$$

The bounds given above are valid for any arbitrary direct product $\mathscr{E}$. For the identically distributed case, somewhat better coefficients can be obtained. The bounds can also be improved for special pairs $(\Theta, H)$, for instance, those isomorphic to Euclidean cubes. It should be clear that achievable performance for estimates is related to much more complex properties than those expressed by a dimension. However, relying on dimension only, Propositions 1 and 2 appear to yield acceptable results, except that the coefficients may be oversize. For instance in the $D$-dimensional Gaussian situation, and using the metric $K$ instead of the $H$ used here, the analogue of Proposition 2 gives a bound $E_{\theta} K^{2}(\hat{\theta}, \theta) \leq 14 D+10$ while the standard estimate satisfies $E_{\theta} K^{2}(\hat{\theta}, \theta)=D / 8$, exactly.

As mentioned before, the very same computations yield minimax bounds for the Poisson experiment $\mathscr{P}$ that accompanies the product $\mathscr{E}=\prod \mathscr{E}_{j}$. One can even get somewhat better bounds for $\mathscr{P}$ than for $\mathscr{E}$. Here the situation is unsatisfactory. Even though $\mathscr{E}$ and $\mathscr{P}$ are generally not comparable, one would not expect that estimates based on $\mathscr{P}$ could be much better than similar estimates on $\mathscr{E}$. In fact, for a large variety of methods, procedures which work well on $\mathscr{P}$ work also on $\mathscr{E}$. This will be shown in Section 7 for methods based on distances between empirical measures or procedures in which one estimates $\theta$ by minimizing a "contrast" of the form $\sum_{j} \psi\left(X_{j}, \theta\right)$.

For the relation between a product $\mathscr{E}=\prod_{j} \mathscr{E}_{j}$ and its accompanying Poisson experiment, not much is known in general. However, the bounds of Propositions 1 and 2 do imply a relation in the identically distributed case. To state it, consider an experiment $\mathscr{E}=\mathscr{E}^{n}$, direct product of $n$ replicates of an experiment $\mathscr{E}=\left\{p_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$. The accompanying experiment $\mathscr{P}^{n}$ is one in which one observes a Poisson process based on a measure $n p_{\theta}$.

Let $\Theta$ be metrized by the distance $h$ defined by $h^{2}(s, t)=\frac{1}{2} \int\left(\sqrt{d p_{s}}-\sqrt{d p_{t}}\right)^{2}$. The distance $H$ of Propositions 1 and 2 is then $H=\sqrt{n} h$. Let $d(\tau)$ be the dimension of $\Theta$ for the pair ( $h, \tau$ ) and let $l$ be the function defined by $l(\tau)=9.97 d(\tau / 18)+2.23$. For each $n$ select a number $\tau_{n}$ such that $l\left(\tau_{n}+\varepsilon\right) \leq n \tau_{n}^{2} \leq l\left(\tau_{n}-\varepsilon\right)$ for all $\varepsilon>0$, if such a number exists. Otherwise let $\tau_{n} \sqrt{n}$ be the diameter of $\Theta$ for the distance $h$.

Proposition 3. Let $\mathscr{E}^{n}$ be the direct product of $n$ copies of the experiment $\mathscr{E}=\left\{p_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ and let $\mathscr{P}^{n}$ be the accompanying Poisson experiment. Then, for the number $\tau_{n}$ defined above and for every $\varepsilon>0$, one has

$$
\Delta\left(\mathscr{E}^{n}, \mathscr{P}^{n}\right) \leq(10.6)(d+1) n^{-1 / 4}
$$

where $d=d\left[\left(\tau_{n}-\varepsilon\right) / 18\right]$.
Proof. Actually we shall prove a stronger result which can be described as follows. One may consider an infinite sequence $X_{1}, X_{2}, \ldots, X_{n}, \ldots$ of independent
random elements which, if $\theta$ is true, have all the distributions $p_{\theta}$. The experiment $\mathscr{E}^{n}$ is equivalent to observing exactly the first $n$ of them. For $\mathscr{P}^{n}$, one first draws a Poisson variable $N$ with $E N=n$. Then one observes the first $N$ of the $X_{j}$. An experiment $\mathscr{F}$ better than both $\mathscr{E}^{n}$ and $\mathscr{P}^{n}$ is to observe the first $m=\max (n, N)$ of the $X_{j}$.

If so, it is clear that the three experiments $\mathscr{E}^{n}, \mathscr{P}^{n}$, and $\mathscr{F}$ can be represented on the same probability space ( $\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{A}$ ) by a family of measures $\left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ in such a way that $\mathscr{E}^{n}$ corresponds to the restrictions of the $P_{\theta}$ to a certain $\sigma$-field $\mathscr{B}_{1}$ and $\mathscr{P}^{n}$ corresponds to another $\sigma$-field $\mathscr{B}_{2}$. Since $\mathscr{E}^{n}$ is not affected if one adjoins to it the observations of the Poisson variable $N$, we shall assume that $\mathscr{B}_{1}$ is generated by $N$ and $\left\{X_{1}, \ldots, X_{n}\right\}$ and that $\mathscr{B}_{2}$ is generated by $N$ and $\left\{X_{1}, \ldots, X_{N}\right\}$.

Now, in both cases, $\mathscr{F}$ can be obtained from the weaker experiment by observing an additional number, say $v_{i}$, of the random variables $X_{j}$. In the case of $\mathscr{E}^{n}$, the number $v_{1}=[N-n]^{+}$. In the case of $\mathscr{P}^{n}$, the number $v_{2}$ is $[N-n]^{-}$. Now according to Proposition 6, Section 4, Chapter 5, and the remarks which follow it, the insufficiency of $\mathscr{B}_{1}$ in $\mathscr{F}$ does not exceed $\sqrt{2 \beta v_{0}}$ where $v_{0}=\sup _{\theta} E_{\theta} v_{1}$ and where $\beta=\inf _{\hat{\theta}} \sup _{\theta} E_{\theta} h^{2}(\hat{\theta}, \theta)$. Here we have $n \beta \leq 14 d+10$ for $d=d\left[\left(\tau_{n}-\varepsilon\right) / 18\right]$. Also $E v_{i} \leq \sqrt{E v_{i}^{2}} \leq \sqrt{n}$. Thus, the insufficiency of $\mathscr{B}_{1}$ in $\mathscr{F}$ satisfies the inequality

$$
\eta\left(\mathscr{B}_{1}, \mathscr{F}\right) \leq \frac{1}{n^{1 / 4}}[28 d+20]^{1 / 2}
$$

Repeating the argument, one obtains the same bound for $\eta\left(\mathscr{B}_{2}, \mathscr{F}\right)$. The assertion follows then from the inequality $\Delta\left(\mathscr{E}^{n}, \mathscr{P}^{n}\right) \leq \eta\left(\mathscr{B}_{1}, \mathscr{F}\right)+\eta\left(\mathscr{B}_{2}, \mathscr{F}\right)$. Hence the result.

Remark. The bound obtained here applies in particular to the situation considered in Chapter 9 for a finite set $\Theta$. However, it does not resolve the problem of finding the function $\eta$ described in the corollary of Lemma 5, Section 5, Chapter 9.

The bound applies also to the case where the dimension of $\Theta$ for the pair $(h, \tau)$ is bounded by some $D$ independent of $\tau$. In this case, it says that $\Delta\left(\mathscr{E}^{n}, \mathscr{P}^{n}\right) \leq C n^{-1 / 4}$ where $C$ is a certain constant. The rate $n^{-1 / 4}$ seems very crude. One would expect from general considerations, and from the relations between deficiencies and insufficiencies of Chapter 5, that $\Delta\left(\mathscr{E}^{n}, \mathscr{P}^{n}\right)$ would decrease like $n^{-1 / 2}$. We do not know of any argument giving such a result. Nor do we know of any argument applicable to the general direct product case in which such a result would be very useful.

## 6. The Risk of Formal Bayes Procedures

According to the general theorems of Chapter 2, Bayes procedures and their limits form a complete class. Thus it ought to be possible to obtain the minimax results of Section 5 as a consequence of results obtainable on the risks of Bayes procedures. This section gives indications in that direction. Although it does



not yield bounds as good as those of Section 5, even under more restrictive assumptions, it does provide usable bounds on the risks of Bayes procedures and maximum probability estimates.

We consider an experiment $\mathscr{E}=\left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ and assume that $\mathscr{E}$ is a direct product of experiments $\mathscr{E}_{j}=\left\{p_{\theta, j} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$. As in Section 5, $\Theta$ will be metrized by the distance $H$ defined by

$$
H^{2}(s, t)=\sum_{j} \frac{1}{2} \int\left(\sqrt{d p_{s, j}}-\sqrt{d p_{t, j}}\right)^{2}
$$

Just as in Section 5, this distance could be replaced everywhere by $\bar{H}$, or, in the infinitely divisible case, by $K$.

Let $\mathscr{B}$ denote the Borel field of $(\Theta, H)$ and let $\mu$ be a positive measure on $\mathscr{B}$. Let $(\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{A})$ be a measurable space carrying the $P_{\theta}$. We shall assume throughout that the following conditions hold.
(A1) The function $H$ is a metric for which $\Theta$ is complete. Furthermore, for each $\tau>0$, the dimension $D(\tau)$ of $\Theta$ for $(H, \tau)$ is finite.

When these conditions are satisfied one can define a joint measure on $\mathscr{A} \times \mathscr{B}$ by the integrals $\int_{B} P_{\theta}(A) \mu(d \theta)$. This measure will be denoted $P_{\theta}(d x) \mu(d \theta)$, as in Chapter 12. One can also define a marginal measure $S$ by $S(A)=\int P_{\theta}(A) \mu(d \theta)$ and, for any subset $C \in \mathscr{B}$, a measure $M_{C}=\int_{C} P_{\theta} \mu(d \theta)$. If $0<\mu(C)<\infty$, the measure $P_{C}=[\mu(C)]^{-1} M_{C}$ is a probability measure. We shall proceed below under assumptions which imply that the following condition (A2) is satisfied.
(A2) The measure $\mu$ is finite on bounded subsets of $\Theta$ and the measure $S$ is $\sigma$-finite.

Note that when (A1) holds the space $\Theta$ is a locally compact space. It is also countable at infinity. Thus, whenever (A1), (A2) hold, the measure $P_{\theta}(d x) \mu(d \theta)$ admits a disintegration of the form $P_{\theta}(d x) \mu(d \theta)=S(d x) F_{x}(d \theta)$, where for each $x$ the measure $F_{x}$ is a probability measure on $(\Theta, \mathscr{B})$. It is to insure the existence of this disintegration that we have added a completeness requirement in (A1). If $(\Theta, H)$ was not complete, one could always complete it. This would not affect in any way the other parts of the requirement (A1). The bounds given in this section will all be derived from the following simple assertion.

Lemma 1. Let (A1), (A2) be satisfied. Let $V$ and $C$ be two measurable subsets of $\Theta$. Let $\omega$ be a measurable function defined on $(\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{A})$ and such that $0 \leq \omega \leq 1$. Then:
(i) the map $x \mapsto F_{x}(C)$ is a version of the Radon-Nikodym density $d M_{C} / d S$;
(ii) if $0<\mu(V)<\infty$, for any probability measure $Q$ on $(\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{A})$ one has

$$
\int F_{x}(C) Q(d x) \leq \frac{1}{2}\left\|P_{V}-Q\right\|+\int[1-\omega(x)] Q(d x)+\frac{1}{\mu(V)} \int \omega(x) M_{C}(d x)
$$

Proof. The first statement follows from the defining equality

$$
\int_{B} P_{\theta}(A) \mu(d \theta)=\int_{A} F_{x}(B) S(d x)
$$

valid for all $A \in \mathscr{A}$ and $B \in \mathscr{B}$. To prove the second write $F_{x}(C)= [1-\omega(x)] F_{x}(C)+\omega(x) F_{x}(C)$. This yields

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int F_{x}(C) Q(d x) \leq & \int[1-\omega(x)] Q(d x)+\int \omega(x) F_{x}(C) P_{V}(d x) \\
& +\int \omega(x) F_{x}(C)\left[Q(d x)-P_{V}(d x)\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

The last term on the right is bounded by $\frac{1}{2}\left\|P_{V}-Q\right\|$. The term $\int \omega(x) F_{x}(C) P_{V}(d x)$ equals $\int \omega\left(d M_{C} / d S\right) d P_{V}$. Since $M_{C}+\mu(V) P_{V} \leq S$, one can take $d P_{V} / d S \leq [\mu(V)]^{-1}$. The result follows.

Note the occurrence of the term $\frac{1}{2}\left\|P_{V}-Q\right\|$. It is a considerable nuisance. However, it cannot be entirely removed. To see how it may occur, consider an interval $[-k a,+k a]$ of the line. Take both $k$ and $a$ large and take for $\mu$ the Lebesgue measure on $[-k a,+k a]$. Let $P_{\theta}$ be the uniform distribution on $\left[\theta-\frac{1}{2}, \theta+\frac{1}{2}\right]$ and let $C$ be the complement of $[-a, a]$ in $[-k a,+k a]$. Let $Q$ be a distribution such that $\left\|P_{0}-Q\right\| \leq \varepsilon$ but such that $Q$ admits either a point mass, or a density with very high value somewhere in $\{\theta: 2 a<\theta \leq(k-2) a\}$. On this set $d M_{C} / d S$ is close to unity. The integral $\int F_{x}(C) d Q=\int\left(d M_{C} / d S\right) d Q$ contains a term with a value close to $\frac{1}{2}\left\|P_{0}-Q\right\|$.

In this example $\mathscr{E}=\left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ is not written as a product of components $\mathscr{E}_{j}=\left\{p_{\theta, j} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$. However, even if $\mathscr{E}$ is such a product and if one considers only product measures $Q=X_{j} q_{j}$ a similar situation may arise. Suppose, for instance, that there is a $t \in V$ such that $\left\|P_{t}-P_{V}\right\|$ is small. Let $q_{j}=\left(1-\varepsilon_{j}\right) p_{t, j}+\varepsilon_{j} r_{j}$ where $r_{j}$ is a probability measure disjoint from the $p_{\theta, j} ; \theta \in B=C^{c}$ and from the integrals $\int_{B} p_{\theta, j} \mu(d \theta)$. Then $Q$ will have a part $R$ of mass $\alpha=\left[1-\prod\left(1-\varepsilon_{j}\right)\right]$ disjoint from $P_{B}$. Unless special precautions are taken, on the part carrying $R$ one can have $d M_{C} / d S=1$ and $\int\left(d M_{C} / d S\right) d R=\|R\|$.

From the inequalities of Lemma 1, it will be possible to obtain various bounds on the risk of formal Bayes estimates. Here we shall limit ourselves to formal Bayes estimates for the loss function $H^{2}(\theta, t) ;(\theta, t) \in \Theta \times \Theta$. One could use a variety of other loss functions. By a formal Bayes estimate will be meant a function $x \mapsto \bar{\theta}(x)$ such that

$$
\int H^{2}[\theta, \bar{\theta}(x)] F_{x}(d \theta)=\inf _{t} \int H^{2}(\theta, t) F_{x}(d \theta)
$$

Here $\int H^{2}(\theta, t) F_{x}(d \theta)$ is lower semicontinuous as a function of $t$. Since $\Theta$ is locally compact there exists a $\bar{\theta}(x)$ which achieves the minimum value. It is also easy to see that the function $x \mapsto \bar{\theta}(x)$ can be chosen to be $S$ equivalent to a measurable function. Indeed, consider the problem of minimizing $\int_{A}\left[\int H^{2}[\theta, t(x)] F_{x}(d \theta)\right] S(d x)$ where $A$ is a set such that $0<S(A)<\infty$. According Chapter 2, the minimum is always achievable by some transition from the band generated by $S$ to measures on $\Theta$. However, in the present situation all transitions are representable by Markov kernels.

Even if $\bar{\theta}$ is not selected in a measurable manner, the results given below are still valid provided one interprets expectations such as $E_{\theta} H^{2}(\bar{\theta}, \theta)$ as upper
integrals. The following trivial lemma records a usable relation.
Lemma 2. Let $\hat{\theta}(x)$ be any point in $\Theta$ such that $\int H^{2}[\theta, \hat{\theta}(x)] F_{x}(d \theta) \leq \int H^{2}\left(\theta, \theta_{0}\right) F_{x}(d \theta)$. Then

$$
E_{\theta_{0}} H^{2}\left(\theta_{0}, \hat{\theta}\right) \leq 4 \iint H^{2}\left(\theta, \theta_{0}\right) F_{x}(d \theta) P_{\theta_{0}}(d x)
$$

Proof. This follows from the triangular inequality $H\left(\theta_{0}, \hat{\theta}\right) \leq H\left(\theta_{0}, \theta\right)+ H(\theta, \hat{\theta})$.

Letting $C_{t}$ be the set $\left\{\theta ; \theta \in \Theta, H^{2}\left(\theta_{0}, \theta\right) \geq t\right\}$, the bound of Lemma 2 can also be written in the form $4 \int_{0}^{\infty} F_{x}\left(C_{t}\right) P_{\theta_{0}}(d x) d t$. If one attempts to substitute in this expression the bound of Lemma 1, one obtains a term of the type $\int\left\|P_{V}-P_{\theta_{0}}\right\| d t$ which will often be infinite unless one makes $V$ depend on $t$. This is what we shall do below.

To proceed, take a particular $\theta_{0}$ and a number $z \geq 0$. Let $B_{n}=\left\{\theta: H^{2}\left(\theta, \theta_{0}\right)<z+n\right\}$ for $n=0,1,2, \ldots$. Let $A_{n}$ be the corona $A_{n}=B_{n+1} \backslash B_{n}$ and let $V_{n}$ be the ball of radius $\alpha_{n}$ centered at $\theta_{0}$.

Lemma 3. Let $D_{n}$ be the dimension of $\Theta$ for the pair $\left(H,(18)^{-1}(z+n)^{1 / 2}\right)$. Assume that $\left(18 \alpha_{n}\right)^{2} \leq z+n$ and let

$$
K(n)=\left[72\left(\frac{z+n+1}{z+n}\right)^{1 / 2}\right]^{D_{n}}+\frac{\mu\left(A_{n}\right)}{\mu\left(V_{n}\right)}
$$

Then

$$
\int F_{x}\left(A_{n}\right) P_{\theta_{0}}(d x) \leq 2 \alpha_{n}+K(n) \exp \left\{-\frac{1}{2}(z+n)\right\}
$$

Proof. Let $18 a_{n}=(z+n)^{1 / 2}$. Let $U_{0}$ be the ball of radius $a_{n}$ centered at $\theta_{0}$, and let $U(\theta)$ be the ball of radius $a_{n}$ centered at $\theta \in A_{n}$. According to Section 4, Proposition 3, there is a test function $\phi_{\theta}$ such that $\int\left(1-\phi_{\theta}\right) d P_{s} \leq \exp \left\{-\frac{1}{2}(z+n)\right\}$ for $s \in U_{0}$ and $\int \phi_{\theta} d P_{t} \leq \exp \left\{-\frac{1}{2}(z+n)\right\}$ for $t \in U(\theta)$. Take a maximal subset $A^{\prime}$ of $A_{n}$ subject to the requirement that distinct points of $A^{\prime}$ are at distance strictly larger than $a_{n}$. The cardinality $N_{n}$ of $A^{\prime}$ is at most $\left\{(4 \times 18)(z+n+1 / z+n)^{1 / 2}\right\}^{D_{n}}$. Let $\omega=\inf \left\{\phi_{\theta} ; \theta \in A^{\prime}\right\}$. Then, certainly, $\int \omega d P_{t} \leq \exp \left\{-\frac{1}{2}(z+n)\right\}$ for all $t \in A_{n}$. Also $\int(1-\omega) d P_{s} \leq N_{n} \exp \left\{-\frac{1}{2}(z+n)\right\}$ for all $s \in U_{0}$. This implies $\int \omega d M_{A_{n}} \leq \mu\left(A_{n}\right) \exp \left\{-\frac{1}{2}(z+n)\right\}$ and $\int(1-\omega) d P_{V} \leq N_{n} \exp \left\{-\frac{1}{2}(z+n)\right\}$ for all sets $V \subset U_{0}$. In addition, $\left\|P_{V_{n}}-P_{\theta_{0}}\right\| \leq 2 \sqrt{2} \alpha_{n}$. The results follow by application of Lemma 1.

The inequality of Lemma 3 suggests that it will be possible to obtain bounds on $E_{\theta_{0}} H^{2}\left(\theta_{0}, \theta\right)$ whenever the ratios $\mu\left(A_{n}\right) / \mu\left(V_{n}\right)$ do not increase too rapidly even for balls $V_{n}$ of radius $\alpha_{n}$ such that $\sum n \alpha_{n}$ remains bounded. To obtain specific bounds, we shall use the following definition.

Definition 1. The measure $\mu$ exhibits slow growth and decay if there are positive constants $K_{i}, \tau_{i}, r_{i}, i=1,2$ such that $r_{1}+r_{2} \leq 1 / 4$ and such that
(i) if $B$ is contained in a ball of radius $x>1$ then $\mu(B) \leq K_{1} x^{2 \tau_{1}} e^{r_{1} x^{2}}$,
(ii) if $B$ contains a ball of radius $x^{3}<1$ then $\mu(B) \geq K_{2} x^{\tau_{2}} \exp \left\{-\left(r_{2} / x\right)\right\}$.

If $\mu$ has slow growth and decay for constants $r_{i}, i=1,2$ such that $r_{1}+r_{2}=$ 0 , we shall also say that $\mu$ is algebraically related to $H$. We shall occasionally refer separately to "slow growth" or "slow decay" for the separate statements (i) and (ii) of the foregoing definition.

Lemma 4. Assume that condition (A1) is satisfied and that $\mu$ exhibits slow growth. Then the measure $S=\int P_{\theta} \mu(d \theta)$ is $\sigma$-finite.

Proof. Take a particular $\theta_{0} \in \Theta$ and consider the balls $B_{n}$ and the coronas $A_{n}$ of Lemma 3, with $z=0$. The proof of Lemma 3 uses a test function $\omega_{n}$ such that $\int \omega_{n} d P_{t} \leq \exp \left\{-\frac{1}{2} n\right\}$ for all $t \in A_{n}$ and such that $\int\left(1-\omega_{n}\right) d P_{s} \leq N_{n} \exp \left\{-\frac{1}{2} n\right\}$ for $H^{2}\left(\theta_{0}, s\right) \leq(18)^{-2} n$. One can assume that $\omega_{n}=\omega_{n}^{2}$. Let $\phi_{n}=\inf _{m \geq n} \omega_{m}$. Then $\int \phi_{n} d P_{t} \leq \exp \left\{-\frac{1}{2} n\right\}$ for all $t$ such that $H^{2}\left(\theta_{0}, t\right) \geq n$. Also, there is a number $C_{1}$ independent of $n$ such that $\int\left(1-\phi_{n}\right) d P_{s} \leq C_{1} \exp \left\{-\frac{1}{2} n\right\}$ for all $s$ such that $H^{2}\left(\theta_{0}, s\right) \leq(18)^{-2} n$. These relations imply in particular that $\int \phi_{n} d S<\infty$ for all $n$.

Now consider a function $\psi, 0 \leq \psi \leq 1$, such that $\phi_{n} \psi=0$ for all $n$. Suppose that $\int \psi d S \geq 2 \varepsilon>0$. Then there is some integer $k$ such that $\int_{B_{k}} \psi(x) P_{\theta}(d x) \mu(d \theta) \geq \varepsilon$. However,

$$
\int_{B_{k}}\left(1-\phi_{n}\right) \psi(x) P_{\theta}(d x) \mu(d \theta) \leq \mu\left(B_{k}\right) C_{1} \exp \left\{-\frac{1}{2} n\right\}
$$

if $18 B_{k} \subset B_{n}$. Taking $n$ large, this contradicts the assumption $\int \psi d S \geq 2 \varepsilon$. Thus $S$ is the sum of the finite measures $\phi_{n} d S$. Hence the result.

Theorem 1. Let the conditions (A1) be satisfied and let $\mu$ have slow growth and decay. Let $\beta=4\left(\tau_{1}+\tau_{2}+1\right)$ and let $z$ be any number satisfying the two inequalities
(i) $z \geq 18 D(\sqrt{z} / 18)+4.78$;
(ii) $z \geq 8\left[\log \left(K_{1} \sqrt{e} / K_{2}\right)\right]^{+}+2 \beta \log \beta$.

Then for every $\theta \in \Theta$ one has

$$
E_{\theta} H^{2}(\bar{\theta}, \theta) \leq(16 / 3)+14 z
$$

Proof. Apply the inequality of Lemma 3 using a point $\theta_{0}$ and a ball $V_{n}$ of center $\theta_{0}$ and radius $\alpha_{n}=(z+n+1)^{-3}$. The ratio $\mu\left(A_{n}\right) / \mu\left(V_{n}\right)$ is bounded by

$$
K^{\prime}(n)=\frac{K_{1}}{K_{2}}(z+n+1)^{\tau_{1}+\tau_{2}} \exp \left\{\left(r_{1}+r_{2}\right)(z+n+1)\right\}
$$

Let $J_{n}=\int\left\{\int_{A_{n}} H^{2}\left(\theta, \theta_{0}\right) F_{x}(d \theta)\right\} P_{\theta_{0}}(d x)$. Then, according to Lemma 3

$$
\begin{aligned}
J_{n} \leq & \frac{2}{(z+n+1)^{2}}+\left\{\left[72\left(\frac{z+n+1}{z+n}\right)^{1 / 2}\right]^{D_{n}}+K^{\prime}(n)\right\} \\
& \times(z+n+1) \exp \left\{-\frac{1}{2}(z+n)\right\}
\end{aligned}
$$

Also, $\frac{1}{4} E_{\theta} H^{2}\left(\bar{\theta}, \theta_{0}\right) \leq(z+1)+\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} J_{n}$.
For the term which involves a dimension $D_{n}$ note that $D_{n} \leq D_{0}=D(\sqrt{z} / 18)$. Thus, the sum $\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} J_{n}$ can be bounded by the sum of three terms $T_{i}, i=1$, 2,3 as follows:

$$
\begin{aligned}
& T_{1}=2 \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \frac{1}{(z+n+1)^{2}} \leq \frac{2}{z} \\
& T_{2}=\sqrt{e}\left[72\left(\frac{z+1}{z}\right)^{1 / 2}\right]^{D_{0}} \sum_{n=0}^{\infty}(z+n+1) \exp \left\{-\frac{1}{2}(z+n+1)\right\} \\
& T_{3}=\sqrt{e} \frac{K_{1}}{K_{2}} \sum_{n=0}^{\infty}(z+n+1)^{\tau_{1}+\tau_{2}+1} \exp \left\{-\frac{1}{4}(z+n+1)\right\}
\end{aligned}
$$

Consider only those values of $z$ such that $z \geq 2+4\left(\tau_{1}+\tau_{2}+1\right)$. Then $T_{2}$ can be bounded by

$$
T_{2}^{\prime}=\sqrt{e}[72 \times \sqrt{7 / 6}]^{D} \int_{z}^{\infty} x e^{-x / 2} d x
$$

where $D \leq D(\sqrt{z} / 18)$. Similarly, $T_{3}$ can be bounded by

$$
T_{3}^{\prime}=\sqrt{e} \frac{K_{1}}{K_{2}} \int_{z}^{\infty} x^{\tau_{1}+\tau_{2}+1} e^{-x / 4} d x
$$

Suppose that in addition to the relation $z \geq 2+4\left(\tau_{1}+\tau_{2}+1\right)$, one selects $z$ so that

$$
z \geq 2 \log z+(8.71) D\left(\frac{\sqrt{z}}{18}\right)+1
$$

and

$$
z \geq 4\left(\tau_{1}+\tau_{2}+1\right) \log z+4 \log \left(\frac{K_{1}}{K_{2}}\right)+2
$$

Then $T_{2}^{\prime} \leq 2 z(z-2)^{-1}$ and $T_{3}^{\prime} \leq 4 z\left[z-4\left(\tau_{1}+\tau_{2}+1\right)\right]^{-1}$. Therefore, for such a choice of $z$, we obtain the inequality

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{4} E_{\theta_{0}} H^{2}\left(\bar{\theta}, \theta_{0}\right) & \leq 1+\frac{2}{z}+2 z\left\{\frac{1}{z-2}+\frac{2}{z-4\left(\tau_{1}+\tau_{2}+1\right)}+\frac{1}{2}\right\} \\
& \leq 1+\frac{2}{z}+\frac{5}{2} z \leq \frac{4}{3}+\frac{7}{2} z
\end{aligned}
$$

It remains to evaluate a bound for the values $z$ which satisfy all the desired inequalities. A rather crude argument is as follows. Consider the equation $z=\mathrm{a} \log z+b$ for $a \geq 0, b \geq 0$, and $(b / a)+\log a \geq 0$. It has a solution $z \geq 1$
such that $z \leq 2 b+2 a \log a$. Putting this in the lower bounds used for $z$, one sees that

$$
E_{\theta_{0}} H^{2}\left(\bar{\theta}, \theta_{0}\right) \leq \frac{16}{3}+14 z
$$

provided that $z$ satisfies the three inequalities
(i) $z \geq 2+4\left(\tau_{1}+\tau_{2}+1\right)$;
(ii) $z \geq(17.42) D(\sqrt{z} / 18)+2+4 \log 2$;
(iii) $z \geq 8 \log \left[K_{2} \vee\left(K_{1} \sqrt{e}\right) / K_{2}\right]+8\left(\tau_{1}+\tau_{2}+1\right) \log 4\left(\tau_{1}+\tau_{2}+1\right)$.

Since (iii) already implies that (i) is satisfied, the result as stated follows. The inequality of Theorem 1 has a general structure analogous to the minimax inequality of Section 5. The numerical coefficients should not be taken too seriously. They could be cut down to better size by more accurate computations. Taking this into account, the relation $z \geq 18 D(\sqrt{z} / 18)+4.78$ is very similar to the corresponding relation in Section 5. However, in the present situation, we also need the inequality $z \geq 4 \beta \log z+4 \log \left(K_{1} \sqrt{e} / K_{2}\right)$ which involves the special behavior of the measure $\mu$ for the coefficients ( $K_{i}, \tau_{i}, r_{i}$ ) of Definition 1. As we shall see, under the condition (A1), it is not too difficult to construct measures $\mu$ satisfying the growth condition of Definition 1 with $r_{1}=0$. To satisfy the decay condition is more bothersome and will be practicable only if $D(\tau)$ does not grow too fast as $\tau \rightarrow 0$.

Theorem 1 gives a bound for the risk $E_{\theta} H^{2}(\bar{\theta}, \theta)$. This seems reasonable in a framework where one uses Bayes estimates with $H^{2}$ as loss function. However, we shall argue later on that this risk may be a poor indicator of the actual behavior of the estimate. It might be preferable to look at probabilities such as $P_{\theta}\left[F_{x}(C)>\alpha\right]$ for $C=\left\{t: H^{2}(t, \theta) \geq z\right\}$. It is clear that the argument of Theorem 1 yields bounds for these probabilities. We shall not carry out the computation, since it is the same as the one carried out for Theorem 2 below.

Theorem 1 admits several variants which turn out to be useful under special circumstances. One of them yields bounds for $(1 / \mu(V)) \int_{V} E_{\theta} H^{2}(\bar{\theta}, \theta) \mu(d \theta)$ for a ball $V$ centered at $\theta_{0}$.

Take, for instance, a ball $V$ of radius $\alpha$ centered at $\theta_{0}$. Assume $\mu(V)>0$. Let $z$ be a number which satisfies all the inequalities
(i) $z \geq(18 \alpha)^{2}$;
(ii) $z \geq 2 \log z+(8.71) D\left(\frac{\sqrt{z}}{18}\right)+1$;
(iii) $z \geq 2+4\left(\tau_{1}+1\right) \log z+4 \log \frac{K_{1}}{\mu(V)}$.

Then

$$
\frac{1}{\mu(V)} \int_{V} E_{\theta} H^{2}(\bar{\theta}, \theta) \mu(d \theta) \leq \frac{16}{3}+14 z
$$

Note that here only the "slow growth" condition on $\mu$ is involved, except for the term $\log \left[K_{1} / \mu(V)\right]$ of inequality (iii). A consequence of such an inequality is that any ball $V$ subject to the above requirement contains at least some point $\theta$ such that $E_{\theta} H^{2}(\bar{\theta}, \theta) \leq(16 / 3)+14 z$. We shall give below a construction for a measure $\mu$ with slow growth and decay. It requires that as $\tau \rightarrow 0$ the
function $\tau^{1 / 3}|\log \tau| D(\tau)$ remains small. When this is not the case, information on the fact that there are points $\theta \in V$ with $E_{\theta} H^{2}(\bar{\theta}, \theta)$ small may still be of interest.

To show that Theorem 1 is not empty, it remains to show that, under suitable conditions, measures of slow growth and decay do exist. Take a number $\gamma$ in ( 0,1 ). For each $v=0,1,2, \ldots$, let $\Omega_{v}$ be a maximal subset of $\Theta$ subject to the requirement that if $s$ and $t$ are any two distinct elements of $\Omega_{v}$, then $H(s, t)>\gamma^{v}$. If $B$ is any ball of radius $1 / 4$, the cardinality of $B \cap \Omega_{v}$ is at most $g(v) \leq \exp \left\{|\log \gamma| v D\left(\gamma^{\nu}\right)\right\}$.

Let $\mu_{v}$ be the measure which gives mass $\gamma^{v}[g(v)]^{-1}$ to each element of $\Omega_{v}$ and let $\mu=\sum_{v=0}^{\infty} \mu_{v}$.

Lemma 5. Let $\mu$ be as just described. Then:
(i) For any ball $B$ of radius $x \geq 1$, one has

$$
\mu(B) \leq(1-\gamma)^{-1}(16 x)^{D(1 / 4)}
$$

(ii) If $x=\gamma^{m}$ for some integer $m \geq 0$ and if $V$ is a ball of radius $x$, then

$$
\mu(V) \geq x \exp \{-D(x)|\log x|\}
$$

Proof. This follows immediately from the construction.
Corollary. Assume that $4 x^{1 / 3}|\log x| D(x) \leq \gamma^{1 / 3}$ for all $x \leq 1$. Then $\mu$ exhibits slow growth and decay with coefficients

$$
K_{1}=(1-\gamma)^{-1}(16)^{D(1 / 4)}, \tau_{1}=\frac{1}{2} D\left(\frac{1}{4}\right), r_{1}=0
$$

and

$$
K_{2}=\gamma^{-1}, \tau_{2}=3, r_{2}=\frac{1}{4}
$$

If $\sup _{x} D(x)<\infty$, then $\mu$ and $H$ are algebraically related.
This follows from Lemma 5 by simple computations. Note that $K_{1}$ and $\tau_{1}$ are functions of the dimension $D(1 / 4)$. One could replace the skeletons $\Omega_{\nu}$ by other skeletons with distances $H(s, t)>4 a \gamma^{v}$ instead of $\gamma^{v}$. Then the dimension involved here would be $D(a)$ instead of $D(1 / 4)$.

Thus far we have considered formal Bayes procedures only for the loss function $H^{2}$. One could carry out similar arguments for other loss functions. We shall not pursue the matter here. However, we shall presently discuss another type of estimate introduced by Weiss and Wolfowitz under the name of "maximum probability estimates." It is a formal Bayes estimate for a particular type of gain function and occurs as follows.

Let $\mu$ be a measure satisfying the requirements (A2) and (A3) of this section. For each $\theta \in \Theta$, let $V(\theta)$ be a measurable subset of $\Theta$ and let $Q_{\theta}$ be the measure defined on ( $\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{A}$ ) by

$$
Q_{\theta}=\int_{V(\theta)} P_{t} \mu(d t)
$$

Letting $S=\int P_{t} \mu(d t)$, the maximum probability estimates are obtained by maximizing with respect to $\theta$ a version of Radon-Nikodym densities $d Q_{\theta} / d S$. The technique is related to a formal Bayes technique as can readily be seen from the fact that $d Q_{\theta} / d S$ and $x \mapsto F_{x}[V(\theta)]$ are versions of the same RadonNikodym densities. Equivalently, one could take the gain function $I[\theta \in V(t)]$ on $\Theta \times \Theta$ and maximize the average gain

$$
\int I[\theta \in V(t)] K(d t, x) P_{\theta}(d x) \mu(d \theta)
$$

When the measures $\mu[V(\theta)]$ are independent of $\theta$, one can also consider maximum probability estimates as maximum likelihood estimates for a smoothed version $\left\{Q_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ of the family $\left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$.

Here we shall consider only the situation where the sets $V(t)$ resemble balls centered at $t$. More precisely, let $B(t, a)$ be the ball of radius $a$ centered at $t$. We shall assume that the following requirement is satisfied.
(A3) There are two numbers $a$ and $b, 0<a \leq b<\infty$ such that $B(t, a) \subset V(t) \subset B(t, b)$.

Theorem 2. Let conditions (A1), (A2), and (A3) be satisfied. Assume that the measure $\mu$ exhibits slow growth and decay. Let $\varepsilon \in(0,1)$ be a preselected number. Taking an arbitrary choice of Radon-Nikodym densities, let $\hat{\theta}$ be any estimate such that

$$
\frac{d Q_{\hat{\theta}}}{d S} \geq(1-\varepsilon) \sup _{t} \frac{d Q_{t}}{d S}
$$

Let $y$ be number which satisfies all the inequalities
(i) $a y^{6} \geq 1$,
(ii) $y^{2} \geq(17.20) D\left(\frac{y}{18}\right)+10.90$,
(iii) $y^{2} \geq 8\left(\tau_{1}+\tau_{2}+3\right)\left\{\log 4\left(\tau_{1}+\tau_{2}+3\right)+4\left[\log \frac{K_{1} \sqrt{e}}{K_{2}}\right]^{+}\right\}$.

Then

$$
P_{\theta_{0}}\left\{H\left(\hat{\theta}, \theta_{0}\right) \geq y+b\right\} \leq \frac{1}{y^{6}}\left[3+\frac{1.25}{1-\varepsilon}\right]
$$

Proof. Let $C=\left\{\theta ; H\left(\theta, \theta_{0}\right) \geq y\right\}$ and let $U$ be a ball of radius $\alpha \leq a$ centered at $\theta_{0}$. Then $V\left(\theta_{0}\right) \supset U$ and $V(t) \subset C$ for any $t$ such that $H\left(t, \theta_{0}\right) \geq y+b$. It follows that for each value $t$, one has $Q_{t} \leq M_{C}=\int_{C} P_{\theta} \mu(d \theta)$. Also, $M_{U} \leq Q_{\theta_{0}}$. Therefore $H(\hat{\theta}, \theta)<y+b$ unless $d M_{C} / d M_{U}>(1-\varepsilon)$.

Let $A$ be the set where $d M_{C} / d M_{U}>1-\varepsilon$ and write $Q=[\mu(U)]^{-1} M_{U}$ and $P=P_{\theta_{0}}$ for simplicity. Then, for any test function $\omega$ one may write

$$
P(A) \leq \int(1-\omega) d P+\frac{1}{2}\|P-Q\|+\int \omega I_{A} d Q
$$

On the set $A$ one has $d M_{C} \geq(1-\varepsilon) \mu(U) d Q$. Therefore

$$
\int \omega I_{A} d Q \leq[(1-\varepsilon) \mu(U)]^{-1} \int \omega d M_{C}
$$

Now consider the coronas $A_{n}=\left\{\theta ; z+n \leq H^{2}\left(\theta, \theta_{0}\right)<z+n+1\right\}$. If the radius $\alpha$ of $U$ satisfies $(18 \alpha)^{2} \leq z$, the argument of Lemma 3 provides a test function $\omega$ such that $\int \omega d P_{t} \leq \exp \left\{-\frac{1}{2}(z+n)\right\}$ for all $t \in A_{n}$ and all $n=0,1$, $2, \ldots$ In addition

$$
\int(1-\omega) d P \leq \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} N_{n} \exp \left\{-\frac{1}{2}(z+n)\right\}
$$

for a coefficient $N_{n} \leq\left[72(z+n+1 / z+n)^{1 / 2}\right]^{D_{n}}$. Assuming $z \leq y^{2}$, the first inequality yields

$$
\int \omega d M_{C} \leq \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \mu\left(A_{n}\right) \exp \left\{-\frac{1}{2}(z+n)\right\}
$$

and therefore $P(A) \leq 2 \alpha+T_{1}(z)+T_{2}(z)$ with

$$
\begin{aligned}
& T_{1}(z) \leq \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} N_{n} \exp \left\{-\frac{1}{2}(z+n)\right\} \\
& T_{2}(z) \leq \frac{1}{(1-\varepsilon)} \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \frac{\mu\left(A_{n}\right)}{\mu(U)} \exp \left\{-\frac{1}{2}(z+n)\right\}
\end{aligned}
$$

Take values $z$ such that $(z+1)^{-3} \leq a$ and let $\alpha=(z+1)^{-3}$. Then, using the slow growth and decay condition, one can bound $T_{2}(z)$ by

$$
\frac{K_{1} \sqrt{e}}{K_{2}} \int_{0}^{\infty}(z+x)^{\tau_{1}+\tau_{2}} \exp \left\{-\frac{1}{4}(z+x)\right\} d x
$$

It follows that for $z>4\left(\tau_{1}+\tau_{2}\right)$ one has

$$
(1-\varepsilon) T_{2}(z) \leq \frac{4 K_{1} \sqrt{e}}{K_{2}} \frac{z}{z-4\left(\tau_{1}+\tau_{2}\right)} z^{\tau_{1}+\tau_{2}} \exp \left\{-\frac{1}{4} z\right\}
$$

Finally, if in addition $z \geq 4\left(\tau_{1}+\tau_{2}+3\right) \log z+4 \log \left(K_{1} \sqrt{e} / K_{2}\right)$, one can write

$$
(1-\varepsilon) T_{2}(z) \leq \frac{z}{z-4\left(\tau_{1}+\tau_{2}\right)} \frac{1}{z^{3}}
$$

Similarly, the term $T_{1}(z)$ can be bounded by

$$
T_{1}(z) \leq\left[72\left(\frac{z+1}{z}\right)^{1 / 2}\right]^{D} \frac{\sqrt{e}}{\sqrt{e}-1} \exp \left\{-\frac{1}{2} z\right\}
$$

for a number $D=D(\sqrt{z} / 18)$. Taking $z \geq 10.90+17.20 D$ yields $T_{1}(z) \leq z^{-3}$. In summary, consider all the inequalities
(i) $z \geq y^{2}$,
(ii) $(z+1)^{-3} \leq a$,
(iii) $z \geq(17.20) D\left(\frac{\sqrt{z}}{18}\right)+10.90$,
(iv) $z \geq 4\left(\tau_{1}+\tau_{2}+3\right) \log z+4\left[\log \frac{K_{1} \sqrt{e}}{K_{2}}\right]^{+}$.

Under these inequalities one can write

$$
P(A) \leq \frac{1}{z^{3}}\left[3+\frac{1.25}{1-\varepsilon}\right]
$$

The inequality called (iv) above is certainly satisfied whenever

$$
z \geq 8\left(\tau_{1}+\tau_{2}+3\right)\left\{\log 4\left(\tau_{1}+\tau_{2}+3\right)+4\left[\log \frac{K_{1} \sqrt{e}}{K_{2}}\right]^{+}\right\}
$$

This yields the result as stated.
Remark 1. When in the triplet ( $K_{2}, \tau_{2}, r_{2}$ ) the number $r_{2}$ equals zero, better bounds can be obtained. Instead of $y^{-6}$, one obtains terms involving $\exp \left\{-\frac{1}{4} y^{2}\right\}$.

Remark 2. A variant of the procedures described here would be to look for balls $B(t, a)$ such that $F_{x}[B(t, a)] \geq 1 / 2$. For each $t$, let $a(t)$ be the infimum of the numbers $a$ with $F_{x}[B(t, a)] \geq 1 / 2$ and let $a^{*}=\inf _{t} a(t)$. Select for estimate $\theta^{*}$ a point such that $a\left(\theta^{*}\right) \leq(1+\varepsilon) a^{*}$. It is clear that such an estimate $\theta^{*}$ will have properties analogous to those described in Theorem 2.

It is abundantly clear from examples that the dimensionality restrictions used here and in Section 5 cannot entail reasonable behavior for the most propagandized method of estimation, namely maximum likelihood. However, one can obtain some results for a modification that U. Grenander has called the method of "sieves". It is as follows. Take in $\Theta$ a set $S_{\alpha}$ which is maximal among sets whose distinct points are at distance strictly larger than $\alpha$. Take for estimate of $\theta$ some point $\hat{\theta}$ of $S_{\alpha}$ such that $d P_{\hat{\theta}} / d P_{s} \geq(1-\varepsilon)^{2}$ for all $s \in S_{\alpha}$.

Proposition 1. Let $\hat{\theta}$ be constructed as just described. Then if $z>1$ and $z \geq(D(\alpha) / 2)\left[\log z+\log \left(16 / \alpha^{2}\right)\right]+|\log \beta|$, one has

$$
P_{\theta}\left\{H^{2}(\hat{\theta}, \theta)>z\right\} \leq(5.7) \alpha+\frac{1.8}{1-\varepsilon} \beta
$$

Proof. Let $\theta_{0}$ be the true value of $\theta$ and let $P=P_{\theta_{0}}$. Consider $A_{n}= \left\{\theta: z+n \leq H^{2}\left(\theta, \theta_{0}\right)<z+n+1\right\}$. Let $X(\theta)=\left[d P_{\theta} / d P\right]^{1 / 2}$. Then $E X(\theta)= \int \sqrt{d P_{\theta} d P} \leq \exp \left\{-H^{2}\left(\theta, \theta_{0}\right)\right\}$. Thus, for any $\theta \in A_{n}$ one has $P\{X(\theta) \geq v\} \leq (1 / v) e^{-(z+n)}$. The number $N_{n}$ of elements of $S_{\alpha}$ contained in $A_{n}$ does not exceed $\left\{4\left((z+n+1)^{1 / 2} / \alpha\right)\right\}^{D(\alpha)}$. Thus if $C(z)=\left\{\theta ; \theta \in S_{\alpha}, H^{2}\left(\theta, \theta_{0}\right)>z\right\}$, one has

$$
P\{\sup [X(\theta) ; \theta \in C(z)] \geq v\} \leq \frac{1}{v} \sum_{n=0}^{\infty}\left[\frac{16(z+n+1)}{\alpha^{2}}\right]^{D(\alpha) / 2} e^{-(z+n)}
$$

Assuming $z \geq D(\alpha)$, this gives



$$
P\{\sup [X(\theta) ; \theta \in C(z)] \geq v\} \leq \frac{1}{v} K(\alpha) J(z)
$$

with $K(\alpha)=\left(16 / \alpha^{2}\right)^{D(\alpha) / 2}$ and $J(z)=\int_{0}^{\infty}(z+x)^{D(\alpha) / 2} e^{-(z+x)} d x$.
On the other hand, there is in $S_{\alpha}$ some element $t$ such that $H\left(t, \theta_{0}\right) \leq \alpha$ and therefore $\frac{1}{2}\left\|P-P_{t}\right\| \leq \alpha \sqrt{2}$. According to the basic inequalities of Chapter 4, this yields $P\left\{X^{2}(t) \leq(1-\eta / 1+\eta)\right\} \leq(\alpha 2 \sqrt{2} / \eta)$ for $0<\eta<1$. Taking $v=(1-\eta / 1+\eta)^{1 / 2}(1-\varepsilon)$, one obtains the relation

$$
P\{\hat{\theta} \in C(z)\} \leq \frac{\alpha 2 \sqrt{2}}{\eta}+\frac{1}{1-\varepsilon}\left(\frac{1+\eta}{1-\eta}\right)^{1 / 2} K(\alpha) J(z)
$$

For the value $\eta=\frac{1}{2}$, This can be written

$$
P\{\hat{\theta} \in C(z)\} \leq \alpha 4 \sqrt{2}+\frac{\sqrt{3}}{1-\varepsilon} K(\alpha) J(z)
$$

Here again, for $z \geq D(\alpha)$, one can assert that $J(z) \leq(2 z / 2 z-D(\alpha)) z^{D / 2} e^{-z}$. Thus, whenever $z \geq(D(\alpha) / 2) \log z+\log (K(\alpha) / \beta)$, one will have

$$
P\{\hat{\theta} \in C(z)\} \leq \alpha 4 \sqrt{2}+\frac{\sqrt{3}}{1-\varepsilon} \beta
$$

This yields the result as stated.
We have already observed, in Section 5, that in practical situations it would not be reasonable to construct estimates directly by the methods suggested here. In most practical situations one can rule out at a glance a huge portion of the parameter space. A refined construction needs to be carried out only on the part of $\Theta$ which is at all relevant. For instance, consider an experiment in which $n$ observations are taken from a family $\left\{p_{\theta} ; \theta \in R\right\}$ where $p_{\theta}$ is presumably $\mathscr{N}(\theta, 1)$. Suppose that the upper and lower observed quartiles are within 10 of a certain number $\theta^{*}$. If the actual distributions do not differ radically from a distribution symmetric around $\theta$, there is no practical loss in treating the problem as if the median $\theta$ was assumed to lie between $\theta^{*}-10^{137}$ and $\theta^{*}+10^{137}$.

To put all of this into mathematical terms requires more space than is available here. However, note that, especially when $H^{2}$ is unbounded, a risk such as $E_{\theta} H^{2}(\hat{\theta}, \theta)$ may be grossly affected by small deviations from the assumptions. It is then preferable to look at bounds on probabilities such as $P_{\theta}\{H(\hat{\theta}, \theta) \geq y\}$ for the relevant values of $y$. In such a case a first elimination of a part of $\Theta$ by a crude method with small probability of eliminating the true $\theta_{0}$ or its vicinity is an effective way of reducing the problem without danger. Such methods are available in particular cases. We shall also discuss in Section 7 some methods based on the behavior of empirical measures. In spite of all of this one should never neglect purely physical considerations. They usually limit the effectively relevant $\Theta$ to a manageable size.

The computations carried out in this section assume generally that the actual distribution of the observations is a member of the family $\left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$. For
the constructions proposed in Section 5 it was possible to make a variety of assertions concerning their behavior if the actual distributions are a product $P^{\prime}=X_{\left\{p_{j}^{\prime} ; j \in J\right\} \text { which need not be one of the } P_{\theta} \text {. Measuring the distances by } \delta(t) \text { with }}$

$$
\delta^{2}(t)=\frac{1}{2} \sum \int\left(\sqrt{d p_{j}^{\prime}}-\sqrt{d p_{t, j}}\right)^{2}
$$

as in Section 5, let $\zeta_{0}=\inf \{\delta(t) ; t \in \Theta\}$. Lemmas 5 and 6 of Section 6 say that Birgé's estimates, or the estimates obtained by the stepwise procedure with $C \geq 36$ do behave very well if $\zeta_{0}$ is not too large. This is true even if one measures their performance by a risk $E^{\prime} H^{2}\left(\hat{\theta}, \theta^{\prime}\right)$ where $\theta^{\prime} \in \Theta$ satisfies $\delta\left(\theta^{\prime}\right) \leq \zeta$ for $\mathrm{a} \zeta>\zeta_{0}$, or for the expectation $E^{\prime} \delta(\hat{\theta})$ itself.

Here no such assertion can be made about $E^{\prime} \delta(\hat{\theta})$, whether $\hat{\theta}$ is the formal Bayes estimate, the maximum probability estimate, or the method of sieves estimate. The bounds on probabilities given in Theorem 2 or Proposition 1 remain valid with suitable modifications. For instance, the bound of Theorem 2 will become

$$
P^{\prime}\{\delta(\hat{\theta}) \geq y+b+\zeta\} \leq \frac{C}{y^{6}}+\sqrt{2}\left(1-e^{-\zeta}\right)
$$

for a suitable coefficient $C$ and for appropriate lower bounds on $y$. Similarly, the bound of Proposition 1 will become

$$
P^{\prime}\left\{\delta(\hat{\theta}) \geq z+\zeta_{\alpha}\right\} \leq(5.7)\left(1-e^{-\zeta}\right)+\frac{1.8}{1-\varepsilon} \beta
$$

for values of $z$ satisfying the conditions of Proposition 1 and for $\zeta_{\alpha}= \inf _{t}\left\{\delta(t) ; t \in S_{\alpha}\right\}$.

We have not given here the analogous bounds for the formal Bayes estimates $\bar{\theta}$. They could be computed as in Theorem 2 and would be subject to the same kind of modifications. However in all cases, $E^{\prime} \delta(\bar{\theta})$ or $E^{\prime} H^{2}\left(\bar{\theta}, \theta^{\prime}\right)$ may be arbitrarily large. This is particularly annoying in the case of Bayes estimates that attempt to minimize an average risk $\int E_{\theta} H^{2}(\bar{\theta}, \theta) \mu(d \theta)$. As explained after the statement of Lemma 1, the difficulty occurs from the fact that the term $\frac{1}{2}\left\|P_{V}-Q\right\|$ cannot be entirely omitted in that lemma.

In order to construct the measures $\mu$ of Lemma 5, we had to assume that the dimension $D(\tau)$ of $(\Theta, H)$ at $\tau$ does not increase too fast as $\tau \rightarrow 0$. Some restriction of this general nature seems unavoidable. Fortunately there are many cases in which the restriction is satisfied. They include in particular those cases where $D(\tau)$ remains bounded so that $\sup _{\tau} D(\tau) \leq D<\infty$. Cases of this nature can often be recognized in a simple manner using the following type of arguments.

Let $\xi$ be a continuous strictly increasing function from $[0, \infty)$ to $[0, \infty)$. Assume $\xi(0)=0$. Let us say, with Karamata, that $\xi$ varies regularly at zero if there is some function $h$ such that, for every $x>0, \lim _{t \rightarrow 0} \xi(t x) / \xi(t)=h(x)$, with $0<h(x)<\infty$. It can be shown that $h$ must then be a power, $h(x)= x^{p}$, and that $\xi$ is a product $x^{p} \eta(x)$ where $\lim _{t \rightarrow 0} \eta(t x) / \eta(t)=1$. Analogous
definitions and results apply to the case where $t \rightarrow \infty$, with $\lim _{t \rightarrow \infty} \xi(t)=\infty$. Now consider on $\Theta$ two distances, the distance $H$ itself and another distance, say $\delta$.

Definition. The distances $H$ and $\delta$ are regularly related if there is a function $\xi$ which varies regularly at zero and infinity and numbers $0<a \leq b<\infty$ such that

$$
a \delta(s, t) \leq \xi[H(s, t)] \leq b \delta(s, t)
$$

for all pairs $(s, t)$ of elements of $\Theta$.
Lemma 6. Let $H$ and $\delta$ be regularly related. Then $\Theta$ has bounded dimension for $H$ if and only if it has bounded dimension for $\delta$.

Proof. Let $A \subset \Theta$ have diameter $2 c$ for $H$. Then, denoting by $\Delta$ the diameters for $\delta$, one has $\Delta(A) \leq(1 / a) \xi(2 c)$. Similarly if $B$ has diameter $c$ for $H$ then $\Delta(B) \geq(1 / b) \xi(c)$. Thus if $D^{\prime}$ is a bound for the dimension of $\Theta$ for $\delta$ the set $A$ can be covered by at most $[(2 b / a)(\xi(2 c) / \xi(c))]^{D^{\prime}}$ sets of $H$-diameter $c$. It follows that the dimension $D$ of $\Theta$ for $H$ satisfies

$$
D \leq D^{\prime}\left\{\log _{2} \frac{2 b}{a}+\sup _{c} \log _{2} \frac{\xi(2 c)}{\xi(c)}\right\}
$$

As $c$ tends to zero or infinity the function $\log \xi(2 c) / \xi(c)$ tends to a certain constant. Thus $\sup _{c} \log \xi(2 c) / \xi(c)$ is finite and the result follows in one direction. The proof in the other direction is analogous.

To give examples, let us consider the case where $\Theta$ is a subset of a Euclidean space with Euclidean distance $\delta$. Of course, $\mathbb{R}^{k}$ has finite Kolmogorov dimension. Thus if the map $\theta \mapsto P_{\theta}$ is well behaved, one can expect that $\Theta$ will also have finite dimension for $H$.

A particular case is as follows. Note that for the metric $H$ the set $\Theta$ is a subset of a certain Hilbert space, say $\mathscr{H}$. Take then for $\Theta$ a compact subset of a Euclidean space and assume the map $\theta \mapsto P_{\theta}$ from $(\Theta, \delta)$ to $\mathscr{H}$ is one-to-one and continuously differentiable. Assume also that the derivative is nonsingular. Then the map is a Lipschitz map in both directions and Lemma 6 applies.

This is, of course, a very particular situation, but we shall encounter it often in the so-called "regular case" for independent identically distributed observations. There are many other situations in which the map $\theta \mapsto P_{\theta}$ does not satisfy an ordinary Lipschitz condition but where the conditions of Lemma 6 are still satisfied.

One type of example can be obtained by letting $\mathscr{E}_{j}=\left\{p_{\theta, j} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ be given by the densities $[1-|x+\theta|]^{+}$for $x$ real and for $\theta$ in a bounded subset of the line. Another class of examples can be obtained by letting $\Theta$ be a subset of $\mathbb{R}^{k}$ and taking densities with respect to Lebesgue measure $\lambda$ of the form $C \mid x- \left.\theta\right|^{\alpha} \exp \left\{-|x-\theta|^{2}\right\}$, where $|x|$ is the Euclidean norm of $x$ and $\alpha$ is some number which may be positive or negative as long as $\int_{|x| \leq 1}|x|^{\alpha} \lambda(d x)<\infty$, that is, as long as $\alpha>-k$.

One can also mention the case of cusps of the type $e^{-|x-\theta|^{2}}$ studied by Prakasa Rao. Note that in such cases the Hilbert space $\mathscr{H}$ associated with
$(\Theta, H)$ is infinite dimensional. However the curve $\theta \mapsto P_{\theta}$ in $\mathscr{H}$ has finite metric dimension.

A question that arises naturally is whether all the finite dimensional $(\Theta, H)$ can be obtained by a Euclidean parameterization, with regular relations between the distances. A result of this nature has been obtained by $\mathbf{P}$. Assouad.

This author uses objects which are more general than distances. Let $A$ be the class of all positive symmetric functions $f$ defined on $\Theta \times \Theta$ such that (i) $f(\theta, \theta)=0$, (ii) there is some power $p>0$, a distance $d$, and numbers $0<a<b<\infty$ such that $a d \leq f^{p} \leq b d$. (One says, then, that $f^{p}$ is equivalent to $d$.) For all the objects of the class $A$, one can define the dimension of $\Theta$ as if they were metrics. Assouad's result can be paraphrased as follows.

Let $f$ be any object of the class $A$ for which the dimension of $\Theta$ is finite. Then there is a number $\alpha>0$, a Euclidean space $\mathbb{R}^{k}$, and an injective map $\phi$ from $\Theta$ to $\mathbb{R}^{k}$ such that $f^{\alpha}(s, t)=\delta[\phi(s), \phi(t)]$ for the Euclidean distance $\delta$ of $\mathbb{R}^{k}$. In other words, $(\Theta, H)$ has finite metric dimension if and only if it can be regularly embedded in a Euclidean space.

Note, however, that in such an embedding $\Theta$ may become a very "thin" subset of $\mathbb{R}^{k}$. Thus the problem also arises of finding which measures $\mu$ could be algebraically (or almost) related to $H$. We do not have a full answer to this problem and will only mention the following simple result.

Lemma 7. Let $(\Theta, \delta)$ be the entire Euclidean space $\mathbb{R}^{k}$ with Lebesgue measure $\lambda$. Assume that $H$ and $\delta$ are regularly related. Assume also that $\xi$ varies regularly at infinity and that it is unbounded. Then $\lambda$ is algebraically related to $H$.

Proof. To prove this it is enough to repeat the argument of Lemma 6 and note that, for $x$ large, $\xi(x) \leq c_{1} x^{p}$ for some $p>0$, and, for $x$ small, $\xi(x) \geq c_{2} x^{q}$ for some $q>0$.

The result does not actually carry over to a situation where $(\Theta, \delta)$ is an open subset of $\mathbb{R}^{k}$. This is simply because of boundary effects. However in such a case, for each $\theta \in \Theta$ there will be some $\varepsilon>0$ such that if $V$ is the ball of center $\theta$ and radius $x<\varepsilon$ for $H$, then $\lambda(V) \geq[(1 / b) \xi(x)]^{k}$.

It follows then that all the arguments used in Theorems 1 or 2 are still valid, but that the bounds obtained will depend on $\theta$. If one assumes only that $\Theta$ is a measurable subset of $\mathbb{R}^{k}$, a similar assertion can still be made for almost all $\theta \in \Theta$.

The results of Sections 5 and 6 rely heavily on metric dimension restrictions. It is very easy to find examples where such restrictions are violated and yet very good estimates exist. However, results of Birgé (1980) and Assouad (1983) indicate that such examples must have a special structure. Also, no general formulations using restrictions other than the metric dimension ones are presently available. The results of Birgé and Assouad rely on lower bounds for risk functions provided either by Fano's lemma or by a lemma of Assouad on experiments where the parameter sets form "hypercubes". For the sake of completeness we shall give these lower bounds.

Fano's lemma uses the expressions called Kullback-Leibler information numbers. For two probability measures $P$ and $Q$, the Kullback-Leibler information
number $K(P, Q)$ is $\int \log (d P / d Q) d P$. Let $J(P, Q)=K(P, Q)+K(Q, P)$.
Lemma 8 (Fano). Let $\Theta$ be a set of cardinality n. Consider the problem of estimating $\theta$ with a loss function $W$ such that $W(s, t)=0$ if $s=t$, and $W(s, t)=1$ otherwise. Then the minimax risk is at least

$$
\begin{aligned}
1 & -\frac{1}{\log (n-1)}\left[\log 2+\frac{1}{2 n^{2}} \sum_{j \neq k} J\left(P_{j}, P_{k}\right)\right] \\
& \geq 1-\frac{1}{\log (n-1)}\left[\log 2+\frac{1}{2} \max _{j, k} J\left(P_{j}, P_{k}\right)\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

Note that this lemma does not use any restriction on $\Theta$, except for the cardinality. Assouad's lemma assumes that $\Theta$ is a "hypercube" of dimension $r$. This is the set of sequences $\theta=\left\{\xi_{1}, \ldots, \xi_{r}\right\}$ where $\xi_{j}$ takes values zero or unity. For two such sequences, say $\theta^{\prime}=\left\{\xi_{1}^{\prime}, \ldots, \xi_{r}^{\prime}\right\}$ and $\theta^{\prime \prime}=\left\{\xi_{1}^{\prime \prime}, \ldots, \xi_{r}^{\prime \prime}\right\}$ we shall use the loss $W\left(\theta^{\prime}, \theta^{\prime \prime}\right)=\sum_{j}\left|\xi_{j}^{\prime}-\xi_{j}^{\prime \prime}\right|$ that counts the number of places where the sequences differ.

Lemma 9 (Assouad). Let $\Theta$ be a hypercube of dimension $r$. Let $W$ be the loss function just defined. Then the minimax risk is at least

$$
\frac{r}{2} \inf \left\{\left\|P_{s} \wedge P_{t}\right\| ;(s, t) \in \Theta \times \Theta, W(s, t)=1\right\}
$$

Proof. For Fano's lemma, use a prior distribution that gives mass $1 / n$ to each of the points of $\Theta$. For the estimation problem described in the lemma, the Bayes risk is then

$$
R=1-\frac{1}{n}\left\|\max P_{j}\right\|=1-\frac{1}{n} \int\left(\max f_{j}\right) d S
$$

for $S=\sum_{j=1}^{n} P_{j}$ and $f_{j}=d P_{j} / d S$.
This suggests looking for bounds for max $f_{j}$ for numbers $f_{j}$ such that $f_{j} \geq 0$ and $\sum_{j=1}^{n} f_{j}=1$. For this, consider the sum $T=\sum_{j=1}^{n} f_{j}\left|\log f_{j}\right|$. It can also be written, if the maximum occurs at $k$,

$$
T=f_{k}\left|\log f_{k}\right|+q_{k}\left|\log q_{k}\right|+q_{k} \sum_{j \neq k} \frac{f_{j}}{q_{k}}\left|\log \frac{f_{j}}{q_{k}}\right|
$$

with $q_{k}=1-f_{k}$. Now, using either the convexity properties of $x \mapsto x \log x$ or a direct argument by recurrence on the number of terms, one sees that the last sum on the right cannot exceed $\log (n-1)$. This gives

$$
\begin{aligned}
T & \leq f_{k}\left|\log f_{k}\right|+q_{k}\left|\log q_{k}\right|+q_{k} \log (n-1) \\
& \leq \log 2+q_{k} \log (n-1)
\end{aligned}
$$

Equivalently

$$
T+[\log (n-1)] \max f_{j} \leq \log 2+\log (n-1)
$$

Now integrate with respect to the measure $S$. This gives

$$
[\log (n-1)] \frac{1}{n}\left\|\max _{j} P_{j}\right\|+\frac{1}{n} \int T d S \leq[\log 2+\log (n-1)]
$$

Now consider the term

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{n} \int T d S & =-\frac{1}{n} \int\left[\sum_{j} f_{j} \log f_{j}\right] d S \\
& =-\frac{1}{n} \sum_{j} \int\left[\log \frac{d P_{j}}{d S}\right] d P_{j} \\
& =\frac{1}{n} \sum_{j} \int\left[\log \frac{d S}{d P_{j}}\right] d P_{j}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $S=n\left((1 / n) \sum P_{k}\right)$, a term such as $\log \left(d S / d P_{j}\right)$ is at least as large as $\log n+(1 / n) \sum_{k} \log d P_{k} / d P_{j}$. Thus

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{n} \int T d S & \geq \log n+\frac{1}{n^{2}} \sum_{j} \sum_{k} \int\left(\log \frac{d P_{k}}{d P_{j}}\right) d P_{j} \\
& =\log n-\frac{1}{n^{2}} \sum_{j \neq k} K\left(P_{j}, P_{k}\right) \\
& =\log n-\frac{1}{2 n^{2}} \sum_{j \neq k} J\left(P_{j}, P_{k}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore

$$
\begin{aligned}
{[\log (n-1)] \frac{1}{n}\left\|\max P_{j}\right\| } & \leq \log 2+\log (n-1)-\log n+\frac{1}{2 n^{2}} \sum_{j \neq k} J\left(P_{j}, P_{k}\right) \\
& =\log \left(\frac{n-1}{n}\right)+(\log 2)+\frac{1}{2 n^{2}} \sum_{j \neq k} J\left(P_{j}, P_{k}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

This gives the result as stated.
