To prove Assouad's lemma, place at each $\theta \in \Theta$ a prior mass equal to $2^{-r}$. A (nonrandomized) decision procedure $\varphi$ can be represented as a map $x \mapsto \varphi(x)= \left\{\varphi_{j}(x) ; j=1,2, \ldots, r\right\}$ where each $\varphi_{j}(x)$ takes values zero and unity.

For any $\theta \in \Theta$, let $\theta_{j}$ be its $j$ th coordinate. Then the Bayes risk is

$$
\frac{1}{2^{r}} \inf _{\varphi} \sum_{j=1}^{r}\left\{\int\left[1-\varphi_{j}(x)\right] \sum_{\theta_{j}=1} P_{\theta}(d x)+\int \varphi_{j}(x) \sum_{\theta_{j}=0} P_{\theta}(d x)\right\}
$$

However, each coordinate $\varphi_{j}$ can be chosen independently of the rest and therefore the inf and $\sum_{j=1}^{r}$ signs can be interchanged. Thus, consider one such term

$$
T_{j}=\inf _{\varphi} \int\left[1-\varphi_{j}(x)\right] \sum_{\theta_{j}=1} P_{\theta}(d x)+\int \varphi_{j}(x) \sum_{\theta_{j}=0} P_{\theta}(d x)
$$

Each of the inside summation signs runs over a set of cardinality $2^{-r+1}$. Thus $T_{j}$ is none other than $2^{r-1}\|\bar{P} \wedge \bar{Q}\|$, where $\bar{P}=\left(1 / 2^{r-1}\right) \sum_{\theta_{j}=1} P_{\theta}$ and $\bar{Q}=\left(1 / 2^{r-1}\right) \sum_{\theta_{j}=0} P_{\theta}$. These sums may also be written in a form

$$
\bar{P}=\frac{1}{2^{r-1}} \sum P_{s_{j}(\xi)}, \bar{Q}=\frac{1}{2^{r-1}} \sum P_{t_{j}(\xi)}
$$

where $s_{j}(\xi)$ is a sequence $s_{j}(\xi)=\left\{\xi_{1}, \xi_{2}, \ldots, \xi_{j-1}, 1, \xi_{j+1}, \ldots, \xi_{r}\right\}$ and $t_{j}(\xi)$ is the same sequence with the $j$ th coordinate changed to zero. Thus, by convexity

$$
\begin{aligned}
\|\bar{P} \wedge \bar{Q}\| & \geq \frac{1}{2^{r-1}} \sum\left\|P_{s_{j}(\xi)} \wedge P_{t_{j}(\xi)}\right\| \\
& \geq \inf \left\|P_{s_{j}(\xi)} \wedge P_{t_{j}(\xi)}\right\|
\end{aligned}
$$

This yields a Bayes risk at least equal to

$$
\frac{1}{2} \sum_{j=1}^{r} \inf \left\|P_{s_{j}(\xi)} \wedge P_{t_{j}(\xi)}\right\| \geq \frac{r}{2} \inf \left\{\left\|P_{s} \wedge P_{t}\right\| ;(s, t) \in \Theta \times \Theta, W(s, t)=1\right\}
$$

Hence the result.
These two lemmas have several features in common. One of them is that in the final bounds only distances $1-\left\|P_{s} \wedge P_{t}\right\|$ or discrepancies $J\left(P_{s}, P_{t}\right)$ between pairs of measures are involved. Another is that, other things being equal, the cardinality of $\Theta$ enters by its logarithm. For instance, in Fano's lemma, with the form of the bound involving $\max J\left(P_{j}, P_{k}\right)$, the gain
( $=1$ - risk) decreases almost as $C / \log [\operatorname{card} \Theta]$. In Assouad's lemma, the risk increases proportionally to $\log [\operatorname{card} \Theta]$. This is consistent with the behavior of the upper bounds derived in Section 5. In both lemmas, it is easy to take into account the direct product structure of an experiment. Indeed, for direct products, the $J\left(P_{j}, P_{k}\right)$ are the sum of the corresponding discrepancies taken in the components. In Assouad's lemma, the $\left\|P_{s} \wedge P_{t}\right\|$ cannot be computed easily, but they can be replaced by the lower bounds $\left\|P_{s} \wedge P_{t}\right\| \geq \frac{1}{2} \rho^{2}\left(P_{s}, P_{t}\right)$ where $\rho$ is the Hellinger affinity. These affinities multiply in the direct product situation.

The fact that, in both lemmas, only pairs of indices enter is nice but suggests that the bounds might be somewhat crude. It is unclear how much is lost in the process of using the convexity properties to pass from more general averages to quantities computable on pairs of measures. To illustrate the problem and at the same time give an example of "hypercube", let us consider the following situation. Let $\lambda$ be the Lebesgue measure on $(0,2]$. Divide the interval $(0,1]$ in $r$ equal intervals $\left.I_{j}=((j-1)) / r, j / r\right], j=1,2, \ldots, r$. Divide ( 1,2 ] similarly by intervals $\left.I_{j}^{\prime}=(1+(j-1)) / r, 1+j / r\right]$.

Let $\Theta$ be the set of sequences $\theta=\left\{\theta_{1}, \ldots, \theta_{r}\right\}$ where the coordinates $\theta_{j}$ take values zero or unity. For $\theta \in \Theta$, let $P_{\theta}$ be a measure, $d P_{\theta}=f_{\theta} d \lambda$, whose density on $I_{j} \subset(0,1]$ is given by $\frac{1}{2}+\left(\theta_{j}-\frac{1}{2}\right)$. For the value of $f_{\theta}$ on ( 1,2 ] consider the following possibilities. In possibility ( A ), the value of $f_{\theta}$ in $I_{j}^{\prime}$ is $\frac{1}{2}-\left(\theta_{j}-\frac{1}{2}\right)$. In possibility ( B ), let us look at the values of $\theta_{1}$ and $\theta_{2}$ and at the number $v$ of items $\theta_{2}, \theta_{3}, \ldots, \theta_{r}$ that are equal to unity. If $\theta_{1}=1$ and $\theta_{2}=1$, then the intervals $I_{2}^{\prime}, I_{3}^{\prime}, \ldots, I_{v+1}^{\prime}$ are assigned the value 0 . The remainder are assigned value one. If $\theta_{1}=1$ but $\theta_{2}=0$, then the $v$ intervals starting with $I_{r}^{\prime}, I_{r-1}^{\prime}$ to $I_{r-v+1}^{\prime}$ are assigned value zero, the remainder being assigned value 1 .

For $\theta_{1}=0$, reverse the assignment process starting up with $I_{2}^{\prime}, I_{3}^{\prime}, \ldots$ if $\theta_{2}=0$ and down with $I_{r}^{\prime}, I_{r-1}^{\prime}, \ldots$ if $\theta_{2}=1$. Now consider the averages of the type

$$
\bar{P}=\frac{1}{2^{r-1}} \sum_{\xi} P_{s_{1}(\xi)} \text { and } \bar{Q}=\frac{1}{2^{r-1}} \sum_{\xi} P_{t_{1}(\xi)}
$$

used in the proof of Lemma 9. For the assignment procedure ( A ) these differ only on the intervals $I_{1}$ and $I_{1}^{\prime}$. However, the individual $P_{s_{1}(\xi)}$ and $P_{t_{1}(\xi)}$ differ also only on these same intervals. Thus $\|\bar{P} \wedge \bar{Q}\|=\left\|P_{s_{1}(\xi)} \wedge P_{t_{1}(\xi)}\right\|$ and nothing is lost in the averaging process. For the assignment (B) the situation is very different. In the interval ( 1,2 ], if a measure of the type $P_{s_{1}(\xi)}$ starts with a string of zeroes followed by a string of ones, the corresponding $P_{t_{1}(\xi)}$ will start with a string of ones followed by a string of zeroes. The only places where they match is in the interval $[\min [v, r-1-v], \max [v,(r-1-v)]]$. The situation is similar if $P_{s_{1}(\xi)}$ starts with a string of ones. Thus, differences of the type $\left\|P_{s_{1}(\xi)}-P_{t_{1}(\xi)}\right\|$ will be close to unity except for a term that tends to zero as $1 / \sqrt{r}$ when $r$ becomes large. This means that the $\left\|P_{s_{1}(\xi)} \wedge P_{t_{1}(\xi)}\right\|$ will be rather small. However, the corresponding $\bar{P} \wedge \bar{Q}$ are large. Indeed, averaging the densities in $(1,2]$ for a fixed $\theta_{1}$ and fixed $\theta_{3}, \theta_{4}, \ldots, \theta_{r}$ over the values of $\theta_{2}$ alone gives densities that are essentially equal to $\frac{1}{2}$ except perhaps in the interval $[\min (v, r-1, v), \max (v,(r-1-v)]$. The values over these exceptional intervals also average out as $v$ varies. Thus $\bar{P} \wedge \bar{Q}$ is approximately $1-2 / r$.

This means that, depending on circumstances, very little or very much can be lost in the passage from the $\bar{P} \wedge \bar{Q}$ to their pairwise bounds. It is unfortunate that, at present, we have very few other tractable formulas for lower bounds. One of them that appeared promising, but did not fulfill its promises so far, is as follows.

Let $\mathscr{H}$ be a Hilbert space with distance between two elements $s$ and $t$ denoted $H(s, t)$. Take an experiment $\left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ indexed by a subset $\Theta$ of $\mathscr{H}$ and consider the problem of estimating $\theta$ for the loss function $H^{2}(s, t)$, with all values in $\mathscr{H}$ allowed as values of the estimates. Assuming $\Theta$ finite, place on it a uniform prior. Then, for $M=1 / n \sum\left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$, the Bayes risk is

$$
R=\frac{1}{2 n^{2}} \sum_{s} \sum_{t} H^{2}(s, t) \int \frac{d P_{s} d P_{t}}{d M}
$$

The occurrence of the sum $\sum_{\theta} P_{\theta}$ in the denominator makes this awkward to use. In such cases one could use lower bounds of the type

$$
R \geq \frac{1}{2}\left|\frac{1}{n^{2}} \sum_{s} \sum_{t} H(s, t) \int \sqrt{d P_{s} d P_{t}}\right|^{2}
$$

or

$$
R \geq \frac{1}{2} \frac{1}{n^{2}} \sum_{s} \sum_{t} H(s, t)\left(\int \sqrt{d P_{s} d P_{t}}\right)^{2}
$$

Unfortunately these bounds do not seem to yield anything that clearly shows the influence of the cardinality of $\Theta$ or its dimension.

To indicate how the bounds of Lemma 8 and 9 are related to dimension problems, let us consider an experiment $\mathscr{E}=\left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ that is a direct product of experiments $\mathscr{E}_{j} ; j \in J$ with $\mathscr{E}_{j}=\left\{p_{\theta, j} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$. Let $H$ be the distance defined on $\Theta$ by the sum of the square Hellinger distances on the components $\left(H^{2}(s, t)=\sum_{j} h_{j}^{2}(s, t) ;\right.$ see Section 5). Then $\exp \left\{-H^{2}(s, t)\right\}=\rho\left(Q_{s}, Q_{t}\right)$ for the Poissonized version $\mathscr{P}$ of $\mathscr{E}$ and the difference between $\rho\left(Q_{s}, Q_{t}\right)$ and $\rho\left(P_{s}, P_{t}\right)$ will be small if all the $h_{j}(s, t)$ are small.

Lemma 10. Assume that $a \geq 0$ is such that for every Poisson experiment $\mathscr{F}=\left\{F_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ such that $\rho\left(F_{s}, F_{t}\right) \equiv \exp \left\{-H^{2}(s, t)\right\}$, there are estimates $T_{\mathscr{F}}$ such that $\sup _{\theta} E_{\theta} H\left(T_{\mathscr{F}}, \theta\right) \leq a$. Then for every $b>a$ and $\Delta>2 b$ the number $m$ of points at mutual distances $\geq 2 b$ that can be fitted in a subset of $\Theta$ of diameter △ satisfies the inequality

$$
m \leq 1+\exp \left\{\frac{b}{b-a}\left[8 \Delta^{2}+\log 2\right]\right\}
$$

(This is a paraphrase of a result of Assouad and Birgé.)
Proof. If the minimax bound $a$ holds for all Poisson experiments described, it also holds for a limiting Gaussian experiment $\left\{G_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ with the same affinities. Now let $\Theta_{m}$ be a subset of $\Theta$ formed by $m$ points with mutual distances at least $2 b$. If the minimax risk for all of $\Theta$ is at most $a$, then it is at most $2 a$ on $\Theta_{m}$ for estimates $T$ that are constrained to take values in $\Theta_{m}$ itself. However, by Markov's inequality, one must then have $G_{\theta}(T \neq \theta) \leq 2 a / 2 b=a / b$.

According to Fano's lemma, one must have $a / b \geq 1-(1 / \log (m-1))\left[\log 2+K_{0}\right]$, where $K_{0}$ is $\sup \left\{J\left(G_{s}, G_{t}\right) ;(s, t) \in \Theta_{m} \times \Theta_{m}\right\}$. However, the experiment $\left\{G_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ with affinities $\exp \left\{-H^{2}(s, t)\right\}$ is a restriction to $\Theta$ of a standard Gaussian experiment corresponding to the Hilbert norm $\|\cdot\|=(\sqrt{8}) H$. For such an experiment it is easy to compute that $J\left(G_{s}, G_{t}\right)=8 H^{2}(s, t)$.

Now, if the diameter of $\Theta_{m}$ is at most $\Delta$, this will yield $K_{0} \leq 8 \Delta^{2}$. Thus

$$
\frac{1}{\log (m-1)}\left[8 \Delta^{2}+\log 2\right] \geq 1-\frac{a}{b}
$$

and

$$
\log (m-1) \leq \frac{b}{b-a}\left[8 \Delta^{2}+\log 2\right]
$$

This yields the desired result.
It is fairly clear from this that minimax bounds that use the Hellinger metric structure only will necessarily depend on dimensionality restrictions. It is unclear at the time of this writing what general features, besides the metric structure, could be used to refine the results.

## 7. Empirical Measures and Cumulatives

Empirical cumulatives are a favorite tool in the construction of estimates or tests. They arise as follows. Let $\mathscr{E}=\left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ be an experiment which is a direct product of experiments $\mathscr{E}_{j}=\left\{p_{\theta, j} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}, j=1,2, \ldots, n$. Assume that the $p_{\theta, j}$ are probability measures carried by a common space ( $\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{A}$ ) independent of $j$. If the $j$ th observation falls at a point $x_{j}$, the empirical measure is the measure $\mu_{n}$ which gives mass $1 / n$ to each $x_{j}$.

When $p_{\theta, j}$ is also independent of $j$, the empirical measure $\mu_{n}$, considered as a random element, is a sufficient statistic. In general this is not the case and $\mu_{n}$ may be a bizarre melange without much statistical relevance. However, it does occur in that form in studies of robustness where the $p_{\theta, j}$ are all close to a certain fixed $p_{\theta}$.

There is a procedure by which one can give statistical relevance to an empirical measure even if the $p_{\theta, j}$ are dependent on $j$ and even if the spaces $\left(\mathscr{X}_{j}, \mathscr{A}_{j}\right.$ ) carrying the $p_{\theta, j}$ also depend on $j$. To describe it in a particular case, assume that for each $j$ there is a finite measure $M_{j}$ on $\left(\mathscr{X}_{j}, \mathscr{A}_{j}\right)$ such that all the $p_{\theta, j}$ are dominated by $M_{j}$. Instead of working on the original spaces $\left(\mathscr{X}_{j}, \mathscr{A}_{j}\right)$, let us choose versions of the logarithms $\log \left(d p_{\theta, j} / d M_{j}\right)$ and work on the space $\mathscr{Z}=\{[-\infty,+\infty)\}^{\oplus}$, representing point $x_{j}$ by the function

$$
\theta \mapsto z_{j}(\theta)=\log \frac{d p_{\theta, j}}{d M_{j}}
$$

This gives new random elements $Z_{j}$ with values in $\mathscr{Z}$. Let $\mu_{n}$ be their empirical measure. Let $M$ be the product of the $M_{j}$, and let $P_{\theta}$ be the product of the $p_{\theta, j}$. Then $\log \left(d P_{\theta} / d M\right)$ is equivalent to $n \int z(\theta) \mu_{n}(d z)$. Thus $\mu_{n}$ obtained in this manner is always a sufficient statistic.

The passage to the space $\mathscr{Z}$ has other consequences that are of interest in the present context. Let $q_{\theta, j}$ be the distribution of $Z_{j}$ for $p_{\theta, j}$. Then $\mu_{n}$ may be considered an estimate of the average $\bar{q}_{\theta}=(1 / n) \sum_{j=1}^{n} q_{\theta, j}$. Since the density of $q_{\theta, j}$ with respect to $M_{j}$ is the evaluation $\exp \{z(\theta)\}$ at $\theta$ of $z \in \mathscr{Z}$ and since this is independnt of $j$, one sees that for any two points $s$ and $t$ of $\Theta$ one has $\left\|\bar{q}_{s}-\bar{q}_{t}\right\|=(1 / n) \sum_{j=1}^{n}\left\|p_{s, j}-p_{t, j}\right\|$. Similarly

$$
n \frac{1}{2} \int\left(\sqrt{d \bar{q}_{s}}-\sqrt{d \bar{q}_{t}}\right)^{2}=\frac{1}{2} \sum_{j} \int\left(\sqrt{d p_{s, j}}-\sqrt{d p_{t, j}}\right)^{2}=H^{2}(s, t)
$$



for the distance $H$ used in Sections 4 to 6 .
It thus appears that the empirical measure $\mu_{n}$, defined on $\mathscr{Z}$, may be an effective estimate of the averages $\bar{q}_{\theta}$ and therefore of $\theta$, whenever it can be shown that $\mu_{n}-\bar{q}_{\theta}$ converges to zero in a suitable sense. Except when $\Theta$ is finite or perhaps countable, one cannot expect $\mu_{n}-\bar{q}_{\theta}$ to converge in $L_{1}$-norm, but the mode of convergence of $\mu_{n}-\bar{q}_{\theta}$ certainly deserves investigation. We shall obtain below a variety of results. They will lead us to consider several classes of functions defined on $\mathscr{Z}$ and in particular the classes satisfying inequalities on "entropy with bracketing" of R.M. Dudley and the Vapnik-Chervonenkis classes.

For a start we shall consider empirical cumulatives on the line (or Euclidean spaces). For some purposes the consideration of empirical measures on $Z= \{[-\infty,+\infty)\}^{\Theta}$ can be reduced to the consideration of empirical measures on the line.

Suppose, for instance, that $\left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ is not only dominated by $M$ but also separable for the $L_{1}$-norm. Then one can work instead with $\mathscr{Z}_{S}=\{[-\infty,+\infty)\}^{S}$ where $S \subset \Theta$ is a countable set such that $\left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in S\right\}$ is dense in $\left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$.

Now, for its product topology, $\mathscr{Z}_{\boldsymbol{S}}$ is a Polish space. There exists a one-toone bimeasurable correspondence between $\mathscr{Z}_{\boldsymbol{S}}$ and the interval $[0,1]$ with the Borel sets. If $z(x)$ is the element of $\mathscr{Z}_{\boldsymbol{S}}$ which corresponds to the point $x \in[0,1]$, then

$$
\log \frac{d P_{\theta}}{d M}=n \int z_{\theta}(x) \mu_{n}(d x)
$$

for the empirical measure $\mu_{n}$ constructed in $[0,1]$ and for $\theta \in S$. This is still a sufficient statistic.

Instead of reducing to [ 0,1 ] one could also pass to Euclidean spaces, and this in a continuous manner if one is willing to lose some information. Indeed the conical measure of $\mathscr{E}$ admits on $\mathscr{Z}_{S}$ a representation which is the image of $M$. As such, it is a finite Radon measure, say $m$. Thus for every $\varepsilon>0$ there is some compact $K \subset \mathscr{Z}_{S}$ such that $m\left(K^{c}\right)<\varepsilon$. On the compact $K$ the evaluation maps $z \mapsto z_{\theta}, \theta \in S$ are equicontinuous and therefore approximable by a finite subcollection, say $z \mapsto z_{\theta}, \theta \in F$. Thus at the cost of some approximation one could work in $\mathbb{R}^{F}$. However, it is not clear how much information is lost in this procedure.

In any event we shall consider first the case of empirical measures obtained from independent real valued random variables. Extension of the argument to Euclidean spaces is possible, but it complicates matters to a certain extent. We shall leave it to the reader.

Although the literature on the independent identically distributed case is huge, the nonidentically distributed case seems to have received comparatively little attention. We shall start by a rather crude but readily available limit theorem.

For each integer $n$ let $X_{j, n} ; j=1,2, \ldots, n$ be independent variables. Let $F_{j, n}(t)=P\left[X_{j, n} \leq t\right]$ and $F_{n}(t)=(1 / n) \sum_{j} F_{j, n}(t)$. Let $K_{n}$ be the covariance kernel which, for $s \leq t$, has value

$$
K_{n}(s, t)=\frac{1}{n} \sum_{j} F_{j, n}(s)\left[1-F_{j, n}(t)\right]
$$

Define an empirical process $\hat{F}_{n}$ by $n \hat{F}_{n}(t)=$ number of $X_{j, n}$ such that $X_{j, n} \leq t$ and let $Y_{n}$ be the process defined by $Y_{n}(t)=\sqrt{n}\left[\hat{F}_{n}(t)-F_{n}(t)\right]$. Let $Z_{n}$ be a Gaussian process which has expectation zero and covariance kernel $K_{n}$.

Consider both $\mathrm{Y}_{n}$ and $Z_{n}$ as random vectors which take their values in the space $\mathscr{B}$ of bounded functions on $\mathbb{R}$ with its uniform norm. Metrize the space of measures on $\mathscr{B}$ by the norm $\|\cdot\|_{B}$ defined in Chapter 15, Section 6. With all these notations one can state the following.

Theorem 1. As $n$ tends to infinity the distance $\left\|\mathscr{L}\left(Y_{n}\right)-\mathscr{L}\left(Z_{n}\right)\right\|_{B}$ between the distributions of $Y_{n}$ and $Z_{n}$ tends to zero.

Proof. If the measures involved possess atoms, one can stretch them out and obtain new processes, say $\bar{Y}_{n}$ and $\bar{Z}_{n}$. The original $Y_{n}$ and $Z_{n}$ are then Lipschitz functions of $\bar{Y}_{n}$ and $\bar{Z}_{n}$. Thus it is enough to consider the nonatomic case. By a monotone transformation of the line, one can then reduce everything to the interval $[0,1]$ and assume that the average of the distributions of the $X_{j, n}$ is independent of $n$ and equal to the Lebesgue measure $\lambda$ of $[0,1]$. This is entirely immaterial to the assertion of the theorem, but will allow us to extract convergent subsequences.

This reduction being effected, let $U_{j, n}(t)=1 / \sqrt{n}$ if $X_{j, n} \leq t$ and let $U_{j, n}(t)=0$ otherwise. Then $Y_{n}=\sum_{j=1}^{n}\left(U_{j, n}-(1 / \sqrt{n} F)\right.$, where $F(t)=t$ for $t \in[0,1]$. Consider also another process, say $V_{n}$, obtained as follows. One takes independent
replicates, say $U_{j, n, k}, k=1,2, \ldots$ of the $U_{j, n}$ and Poisson variables $v_{j}$ with expectation $E v_{j}=1$ and one forms the sum

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{n} & =\sum_{j=1}^{n} \sum_{k=1}^{v_{j}}\left[U_{j, n, k}-\frac{1}{\sqrt{n}} F\right] \\
& =\sum_{j=1}^{n} \sum_{k=1}^{v_{j}} U_{j, n, k}-\sqrt{n} F-\frac{N-n}{\sqrt{n}} F
\end{aligned}
$$

where $N=\sum_{j=1}^{n} v_{j}$. The process $W_{n}=\sum_{j=1}^{n} \sum_{k=1}^{v_{j}} U_{j, n, k}$ can also be obtained from the standard Poisson process based on the measure $n \lambda$ on $[0,1]$ by dividing the jumps by $\sqrt{n}$.

If one symmetrizes $W_{n}$ one obtains another process $W_{n}-W_{n}^{\prime}$ which is also a process with independent increments. It is easy to see that $\mathscr{L}\left(W_{n}-W_{n}^{\prime}\right)$ converges on $\mathscr{B}$ to a certain limit carried by $C[0,1]$. To do this, one bounds the oscillations of $W_{n}-W_{n}^{\prime}$ in small intervals $[s, t]$ by the variation of $\left(W_{n}-W_{n}^{\prime}\right)(t)- \left(W_{n}-W_{n}^{\prime}\right)(s)$, using Lévy's reflection principle.

Thus $W_{n}$ is shift-compact with strong limits. This remains true of $V_{n}$ which differs from $W_{n}$ by the random term $((N-n) / \sqrt{n}) F$ and the centering $\sqrt{n} F$. Thus, according to Chapter 15, Section 6, Theorem 2, the sequence $\mathscr{L}\left(Y_{n}\right)$ is also shift-compact with strong limits.

A simple computation, using for instance the inequality $E\left|Y_{n}(t)-Y_{n}(s)\right|^{2} \leq |t-s|$, shows that the kernels $K_{n}$ are equicontinuous on $[0,1] \times[0,1]$. Thus one can extract subsequences which converge uniformly.

For such a subsequence, still denoted $\{n\}$ here, the finite dimensional distributions of the process $Y_{n}$ converge to the Gaussian distributions given by the limit kernel $K$. It follows that the Gaussian process $Z$ which has that kernel is also given by a strong Radon measure and that along the subsequence $\left\|\mathscr{L}\left(Y_{n}\right)-\mathscr{L}(Z)\right\|_{B} \rightarrow 0$.

To obtain the conclusion of the theorem it is enough to show that along the same subsequence $\left\|\mathscr{L}\left(Z_{n}\right)-\mathscr{L}(Z)\right\|_{B}$ also tends to zero. This is an easy consequence of the fact that $E\left|Z_{n}(t)-Z_{n}(s)\right|^{2} \leq|t-s|$, and the result can be considered proven.

In Theorem 1, we have used directly Theorem 2 of Chapter 15, Section 6. By retracing the steps of the proof of this latter result, one can obtain some additional information. One result is as follows.

Proposition 1. Let $Y_{n}$ be the process of Theorem 1. Then, for all $r \geq 0$ one has

$$
P\left\{\left\|Y_{n}\right\| \geq r+\frac{1}{\sqrt{n}}\right\} \leq \exp \left\{-\frac{r^{2}}{46}\right\}
$$

with $\left\|Y_{n}\right\|=\sup _{t} Y_{n}(t)$.
The proof will be omitted, since better results are given further on.
The proof used for Theorem 1 makes use of inequalities on fluctuations of sums of independent random variables. This gives a very simple proof. However, in situations where the sets used to look at empirical measures are not ordered, the technique fails and one needs to go back to more elaborate procedures, similar to those used to prove continuity of sample paths for processes defined on general metric spaces. We shall now give some results of this nature, taken from work of R.M. Dudley. First we need to expand on inequalities due to Bernstein, Bennett, and Hoeffding. Lemma 5 of Section 4 is a special case of these results.

Lemma 1. Let $X_{1}, X_{2}, \ldots, X_{n}$ be independent random variables such that $0 \leq X_{j} \leq 1$. Let $S=\sum X_{j}$ and $n p=\mathrm{ES}$. Assume that $p \leq \frac{1}{2}$. Then, for $0 \leq k \leq n(1-p)$ one has

$$
P\{S \geq n p+k\} \leq \exp \left\{-\frac{k^{2}}{2(n p+k)}\right\}
$$

and, for $0 \leq k \leq n p$, one has

$$
P\{S \leq n p-k\} \leq \exp \left\{-\frac{k^{2}}{2 n p(1-p)}\right\} .
$$

Proof. Proceed exactly as in the proof of Lemma 5, Section 4. This yields the inequality

$$
E e^{t S} \leq\left[1-p+p e^{t}\right]^{n}
$$

Thus, by Markov's inequality, for $0 \leq k \leq n(1-p)$,

$$
P[S \geq n p+k] \leq\left\{\left[1-p+p e^{t}\right] e^{-t(p+x)}\right\}^{n}
$$

with $x=k / n, 0 \leq x \leq 1-p$.
Let $f(x, z)=\log [1-p+p z]-(p+x) \log z$ and suppose first that $x<1-p$. For $x$ fixed, $0 \leq x<1-p$, the function $f$ achieves a minimum at the value $z(x)=(1-p)(p+x)[p(1-p-x)]^{-1}$. The value of the minimum is given by $-g(x)$, where

$$
g(x)=(p+x) \log \left(\frac{p+x}{p}\right)+(1-p-x) \log \left(\frac{1-p-x}{1-p}\right)
$$

For the value $x=1-p$ itself, the minimum is obtained by letting $z$ tend to infinity, and the corresponding value of the minimum is the limit value of $-g(x)$ as $x \rightarrow 1-p$.

Take two derivatives of $g$ with respect to $x$. Note that $g(0)=g^{\prime}(0)=0$ and that $g^{\prime \prime}(x)=[(p+x)(1-p-x)]^{-1}$. Thus, for $0 \leq x<1-p$ one has $g^{\prime \prime}(x) \geq(p+x)^{-1}$. Taylor's formula yields $g(x) \geq x^{2}[2(p+x)]^{-1}$. The first assertion follows by substitution. The second assertion follows similarly, noting that for $-p \leq x \leq 0$ one has $g^{\prime \prime}(x) \geq[p(1-p)]^{-1}$.

Remark. Let $p_{j}=E X_{j}$. Then under the conditions of Lemma 1, var $X_{j} \leq p_{j}\left(1-p_{j}\right)$ and $\sum_{j} p_{j}\left(1-p_{j}\right) \leq n p(1-p)$. If $Z$ is a Gaussian variable with $E Z=E S$ and $\operatorname{var} Z=\operatorname{var} S=\sigma^{2}$, then

$$
P[Z \geq n p+k] \leq \frac{\sigma}{k \sqrt{2 \pi}} \exp \left\{-\frac{k^{2}}{2 \sigma^{2}}\right\}
$$

Even if $\sigma^{2}$ is replaced by the upper bound $n p(1-p)$, this gives a bound for $P\{Z \geq n p+k\}$ which does not exceed that of Lemma 1.

We shall need later on a variant of Lemma 1 applicable to the hypergeometric distribution. It can be obtained easily by an argument due to Hoeffding as follows.

Let $A$ be a finite subset of the line, with $\operatorname{card} A=N$. Let $X_{1}, X_{2}, \ldots, X_{n}$ be $n$ independent variables each distributed uniformly on $A$. Let $S=\sum_{j=1}^{n} X_{j}$. Let $Y_{1}, Y_{2}, \ldots, Y_{n}$ be obtained by sampling at random without replacement from the population $A$ and let $T=\sum_{j=1}^{n} Y_{j}$.

Lemma 2 (Hoeffding). For every convex function $g$ one has $E g(T) \leq E g(S)$. Proof. Consider an experiment in which one selects a sample of size $n$ at random with replacement from the integers $1,2, \ldots, N$. This will produce a certain set of integers in a particular order. Let it be $m_{1}, m_{2}, \ldots, m_{n}$. Let $B_{1}$ denote the subset of $\{1,2, \ldots, n\}$ formed by those integers $j$ such that $m_{j}=m_{1}$. Let $v_{1}$ be the first integer such that $m_{v_{1}} \neq m_{1}$. Take for $B_{2}$ the set $B_{2}=\left\{j ; 1<j \leq n, m_{j}=m_{v_{1}}\right\}$. Proceed recursively, obtaining $K \leq n$ disjoint sets $B_{i} ; i=1,2, \ldots, K$ such that $\bigcup B_{i}=\{1,2, \ldots, n\}$. Consider also a sample of size $n$ taken at random without replacement from the population $A$. List its elements in a random order as $Y_{1}, Y_{2}, \ldots, Y_{n}$. For $j \in B_{i}$, let $X_{j}=Y_{i}$.

This procedures yields a sample $X_{1}, \ldots, X_{n}$ which has the same distribution as a sample of size $n$ taken at random with replacement from the population $A$. It is clear that given the set of $Y_{i}$ 's the conditional expectation of any $X_{j}$ is $n^{-1} T$ with $T=\sum_{i=1}^{n} Y_{i}$. Thus the assertion of the lemma follows from Jensen's inequality.

Corollary. Suppose that $A \subset[0,1]$ and that the sum $\sum\{x ; x \in A\}=N p$. Take a sample $Y_{1}, Y_{2}, \ldots, Y_{n}$ at random without replacement from $A$ and let $T=\sum_{i=1}^{n} Y_{i}$. Then for $0 \leq k \leq n(1-p)$

$$
P\{T \geq n p+k\} \leq \exp \left\{-\frac{k^{2}}{2(n p+k)}\right\}
$$

For $0 \leq k \leq n p$, one has

$$
P\{T \leq n p-k\} \leq \exp \left\{-\frac{k^{2}}{2 n p(1-p)}\right\}
$$

Proof. This follows from Lemma 2 applies to $E e^{t T}$ and from the derivation of Lemma 1 .

Now let us return to the empirical measure $\mu_{n}$ obtained from independent observations $\left(\xi_{1}, \xi_{2}, \ldots, \xi_{n}\right)$ on a set $(\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{A})$. Let $P_{j}$ be the distribution of $\xi_{j}$ and let $n P=\sum_{j=1}^{n} P_{j}$. For any $P$-integrable function $\phi$, let

$$
X(\phi)=\sqrt{n}\left\{\int \phi d \mu_{n}-\int \phi d P\right\}
$$

Lemma 3. Let $\left\{\phi_{1}, \phi_{2}, \ldots, \phi_{m}\right\}$ and $\left\{\psi_{1}, \psi_{2}, \ldots, \psi_{M}\right\}$ be two sets of measurable functions defined on $(\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{A})$. Assume that $0 \leq \phi_{j} \leq 1$ and $0 \leq \psi_{j} \leq 1$ for all $j$. Assume also that for each $\psi_{k}$ there is a $\phi_{j}$ such that $\int\left|\psi_{k}-\phi_{j}\right| d P \leq p \leq \frac{1}{2}$. Then, if $2 M^{2} \geq$ pe and $4 \log \left(2 M^{2} / p\right) \leq n p$, one has

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \operatorname{Pr}\left\{\sup _{k} \inf _{j}\left|X\left(\psi_{k}\right)-X\left(\phi_{j}\right)\right| \geq 4\left[p \log \frac{2 M^{2}}{p}\right]^{1 / 2}\right\} \\
& \quad \leq 2 \sqrt{2 p}\left[\log \frac{2 M^{2}}{p}\right]^{-1 / 2} \leq 2 \sqrt{2 p}
\end{aligned}
$$

Remark. This is usually stated for functions $\phi_{j}$ and $\psi_{k}$ which are indicator functions. However, there are statistical problems in which other functions are needed. In addition, the proof is the same.

Proof. To each $k$ assign a $j(k)$ with $\int\left|\psi_{k}-\phi_{j(k)}\right| d P \leq p$. Consider separately the functions $\omega_{k}=\left[\psi_{k}-\phi_{j(k)}\right]^{+}$and $\omega_{k}^{\prime}=\left[\psi_{k}-\phi_{j(k)}\right]^{-}$. According to Lemma 1, one can write

$$
\operatorname{Pr}\left\{\left|X\left(\omega_{k}\right)\right| \geq \varepsilon\right\} \leq 2 \exp \left\{-\frac{\varepsilon^{2}}{2[p+(\varepsilon / \sqrt{n})]}\right\}
$$

for all $\varepsilon \geq 0$. Consider only the case where $\varepsilon \leq p \sqrt{n}$. This gives

$$
\operatorname{Pr}\left\{\sup _{k}\left|X\left(\omega_{k}\right)\right| \geq \varepsilon\right\} \leq 2 M \exp \left\{-\frac{\varepsilon^{2}}{4 p}\right\}
$$

Consider the function of $\varepsilon$ defined by $\varepsilon+2 M \exp \left\{-\varepsilon^{2} / 4 p\right\}$. Letting $z= \varepsilon^{2} / 2 p$, one sees that the derivative of this function of $\varepsilon$ vanishes at a value such that $z=\log z+\log \left(2 M^{2} / p\right)$. Such a $z$ verifies the inequalities $\log \left(2 M^{2} / p\right) \leq z \leq 2 \log \left(2 M^{2} / p\right)$ as long as $2 M^{2} \geq p e$. Thus, for this particular choice of $\varepsilon= \sqrt{2 p z}$, the inequality $\varepsilon \leq p \sqrt{n}$ will be satisfied whenever $4 \log \left(2 M^{2} / p\right) \leq n p$. Substituting in the inequalities, a bit of algebra gives the desired result.

The foregoing Lemma 3 can be used repeatedly to pass from one family of functions to another. Lemma 4, below, gives an example of the procedure. To state it, consider a family $\mathscr{F}$ of measurable functions from $(\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{A})$ to $[0,1]$.

For each $x \in(0,1)$ let $K_{0}(x)$ be the minimal cardinality of a set $\mathscr{S} \in \mathscr{F}$ such that

$$
\sup _{f} \inf _{\phi}\left\{\int|f-\phi| d P ; \phi \in \mathscr{S}, f \in \mathscr{F}\right\} \leq x
$$

Let $L_{0}$ be the function $L_{0}(x)=\left\{\log \left[K_{0}^{2}(x) / 2 x\right]\right\}^{1 / 2}$.
Lemma 4. Let $m \geq 0$ be an integer and let $p$ be a number $p \in(0,1)$. Assume that $2 K_{0}^{2}\left(4^{m} p\right) \geq 4^{m} p e$ and that $4 \log \left[K_{0}^{2}(p) / 2 p\right] \leq n p$. Then there are subsets $\mathscr{S}_{i}, i=1,2$ of $\mathscr{F}$ and maps $f \mapsto \xi(f)$ from $\mathscr{S}_{1}$ to $\mathscr{S}_{2}$ with the following properties:
(1) $\operatorname{card} \mathscr{S}_{1} \leq K_{0}(p)$ and card $\mathscr{S}_{2} \leq K_{0}\left(4^{m} p\right)$;
(2) for each $f \in \mathscr{F}$ there is a $g \in \mathscr{S}_{1}$ such that $\int|f-g| d P \leq p$;
(3) if $b$ is the number $b=32 \int_{\sqrt{p} / 2}^{2 m-1} \sqrt{p} L_{0}\left(x^{2}\right) d x$, then, there is a set of probability at most $4 \sqrt{2 p}\left(2^{m}-1\right)$ outside of which one has

$$
\sup \left\{|X(f)-X[\xi(f)]| ; f \in \mathscr{S}_{1}\right\} \leq b
$$

(4) $\int|f-\xi(f)| d P \leq \frac{1}{3} p 4^{m+1}$ for all $f \in \mathscr{S}_{1}$.

Proof. For each integer $v=0,1,2, \ldots, m$ let $\left\{\phi_{v, k} ; k=1,2, \ldots, K_{0}\left(4^{v} p\right)\right\}$ be a subset of $\mathscr{F}$ such that $\inf _{k} \int\left|f-\phi_{\nu, k}\right| d P \leq 4^{\nu} p$ for all $f \in \mathscr{F}$. For each $v$ and each $\phi_{v, k}$ let $\phi_{v+1, j(k)}$ be such that $\int\left|\phi_{v, k}-\phi_{v+1, j(k)}\right| d P \leq 4^{v+1} p$. Following the successive pairs $\phi_{v, k}, \phi_{v+1, j(k)}$, one will assign to each $\phi_{0, k}$ a certain member $\phi_{m, \mu(k)}$ of the last set $\left\{\phi_{m, k} ; k=1,2, \ldots, K_{0}\left(4^{m} p\right)\right\}$ to be considered.

Note that the numbers $K_{0}\left(4^{v} p\right)$ decrease as $v$ increases, so that the inequalities assumed on $K_{0}(p)$ and $K_{0}\left(4^{m} p\right)$ allow application of Lemma 3 to each pair of sets $\left\{\phi_{v, k}\right\},\left\{\phi_{v+1, k}\right\}$. Lemma 3 says that except for cases having probability at most $2 \sqrt{2 p} \sqrt{4^{v+1}}$ one will have

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sup _{k}\left|X\left(\phi_{v, k}\right)-X\left(\phi_{v+1, j(k)}\right)\right| & \leq 4\left\{4^{v+1} p \log \left[\frac{2 K_{0}^{2}\left(4^{v} p\right)}{4^{v+1} p}\right]\right\}^{1 / 2} \\
& =4\left(4^{v+1} p\right)^{1 / 2} L_{0}\left(4^{v} p\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

The function $L_{0}\left(4^{v} p\right)$ is a decreasing function of $v$. It follows that

$$
\left(p 4^{v+1}\right)^{1 / 2} L_{0}\left(4^{v} p\right) \leq 4 \int_{v-1}^{v}\left(p 4^{y}\right)^{1 / 2} L_{0}\left(p 4^{y}\right) d y
$$

Change variables, letting $x=\left(p 4^{y}\right)^{1 / 2}$ and sum over values of $v$. This gives a bound $(32 / \log 4) \int_{\sqrt{p / 2}}^{2 \frac{m-1}{p}} L_{0}\left(x^{2}\right) d x$ for the differences $\left|X\left(\phi_{0, k}\right)-X\left(\phi_{m, \mu(k)}\right)\right|$, except for cases having a probability at most $2 \sqrt{2 p} \sum_{v=0}^{m-1} 2^{v+1}=4 \sqrt{2 p}\left(2^{m}-1\right)$. Hence the result.

The reader will note that Lemma 4 gives bounds on the differences $X(f)- X[\xi(f)]$ only for the functions $f$ of the subset $S_{1}$ of $\mathscr{F}$. To obtain bounds valid for the entire set $\mathscr{F}$, further conditions are needed. As a first example, we shall give part of a result of R.M. Dudley relative to classes of functions whose "entropy with bracketing" satisfies a suitable inequality. A similar result also due to Dudley applies to the Vapnik-Chervonenkis classes. For the latter, measurability considerations are nontrivial. For the "bracketing" families, no difficulties can occur. Since these already cover the most common families used in statistics, the result is very much worth stating separately.

Consider again a set $(\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{A})$ and probability measures $P_{j} ; j=1,2, \ldots, n$ with $n P=\sum P_{j}$. Let $\mathscr{F}$ be a class of measurable functions from ( $\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{A}$ ) to $[0,1]$. Consider "brackets" $(\psi, \omega)$ formed by measurable functions with $\psi \leq \omega$. (These need not be elements of $\mathscr{F}$, but one can assume without loss of generality that $0 \leq \psi \leq \omega \leq 1$. We shall do so.)

Let $K(x)$ be the minimum number of brackets needed to achieve the conditions:
(i) For each bracket $(\psi, \omega)$ one has $\int(\omega-\psi) d P \leq x$,
(ii) for each $f \in \mathscr{F}$ there is a bracket with $\psi \leq f \leq \omega$.

The function $K$ is the entropy function with bracketing. Since two elements $f$ and $g$ of $\mathscr{F}$ such that $\int|f-g| d P>x$ cannot be in the same bracket, one has necessarily $K_{0}(x) \leq K(x)$ for all $x$.

The result stated below will involve several functions related to $K$. One of them is the function $L$ defined by

$$
L(x)=\left[\log \frac{2 K^{2}(x)}{x}\right]^{1 / 2}
$$

Another function is given by

$$
\tau(x)=2 x^{-1 / 2}\left\{\log \left[\frac{2 K^{2}(x)}{x}\right]\right\}^{1 / 2}=2 x^{-1 / 2} L(x)
$$

Theorem 2. Let $\mathscr{F}$ be a class of measurable functions from ( $\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{A}$ ) to $[0,1]$ and let $K, K_{0}, \tau$, and $L$ be the functions defined above. Let $q$ be a number such that $\tau(q+\varepsilon) \leq \sqrt{n} \leq \tau(q-\varepsilon)$ for all $\varepsilon>0$. Then for any $m \geq 0$ such that $4^{m+2} q \leq 1$ there is a map $f \mapsto \phi_{f}$ from $\mathscr{F}$ to a subset $\mathscr{S} \subset \mathscr{F}$ such that:
(i) the cardinality of $\mathscr{S}$ is at most $K_{0}\left(4^{m} q\right)$,
(ii) $\int\left|f-\phi_{f}\right| d P \leq 2\left[1+\frac{1}{3}\left(4^{m}-1\right)\right] q$,
(iii) outside of a set of probability at most $2^{m+3} \sqrt{q}$

$$
\sup \left\{\left|X(f)-X\left(\phi_{f}\right)\right| ; f \in \mathscr{F}\right\} \leq c(m)
$$

with

$$
c(m) \leq 8 q \tau\left(q^{-}\right)+32 \int_{0}^{2 m \sqrt{q}} L_{0}\left(x^{2}\right) d x
$$

Proof. Take a number $p<1 / 2$. Let $\mathscr{S}_{2}$ be a set of brackets $(\psi, \omega), \psi \leq \omega$, such that for each $f \in \mathscr{F}$ there is a pair $\left(\psi_{f}, \omega_{f}\right) \in \mathscr{S}_{2}$ such that $\psi_{f} \leq f \leq \omega_{f}$. Assume
also that all the brackets used are such that $\int(\omega-\psi) d P \leq p$. By definition of $K$, this can be achieved with a set $\mathscr{S}_{2}$ of cardinality $K(p)$. For each $f \in \mathscr{F}$ select a bracket $\left(\psi_{f}, \omega_{f}\right) \in \mathscr{S}_{2}$ and some element $g_{f} \in \mathscr{F}$ such that $\psi_{f} \leq g_{f} \leq \omega_{f}$. One can write $X(f)-X\left(g_{f}\right)=\sqrt{n}\left(f-g_{f}\right) d \mu_{n}-\sqrt{n}\left(f-g_{f}\right) d P$. Therefore

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left|X(f)-X\left(g_{f}\right)\right| & \leq\left|X\left(\omega_{f}-\psi_{f}\right)\right|+2 \sqrt{n} \int\left|f-g_{f}\right| d P \\
& \leq\left|X\left(\omega_{f}-\psi_{f}\right)\right|+2 p \sqrt{n}
\end{aligned}
$$

Assuming that $p>q$ and $2 K^{2}(p) \geq p e$, Lemma 3 gives a bound

$$
\operatorname{Pr}\left\{\sup _{f}\left|X\left(\omega_{f}\right)-X\left(\psi_{f}\right)\right| \geq 2 p \tau(p)\right\} \leq 2 \sqrt{2 p}
$$

Thus, there is a set of probability at most $2 \sqrt{2 p}$ outside of which one has

$$
\left|X(f)-X\left(g_{f}\right)\right| \leq 2 p \sqrt{n}+2 p \tau(p)
$$

for every $f \in \mathscr{F}$.
Now form a double array $\left\{\phi_{v, k}\right\}$ of the kind used in Lemma 4. That is, take for $\left\{\phi_{v, k} ; k=1,2, \ldots, K_{0}\left(4^{v} p\right)\right\}$ a maximal subset of $\mathscr{F}$ subject to the restriction that if $j \neq k$ then $\int\left|\phi_{v, j}-\phi_{v, k}\right| d P>p 4^{v}$. Since $K_{0}(x) \leq K(x)$ the restrictions of Lemmas 3 and 4 will be satisfied. First apply Lemma 3 to the sets $\left\{g_{f}\right\}$ and $\left\{\phi_{0, k}\right\}$, Selecting for $k(f)$ some index such that $\left.\int \mid g_{f}-\phi_{0, k(f)}\right) \mid d P \leq p$. This will introduce bounds $\left|X\left(g_{f}\right)-X\left(\phi_{0, k(f)}\right)\right| \leq 4\left[p \log \left(2 K^{2}(p) / p\right)\right]^{1 / 2}$ except for cases having probability at most $2 \sqrt{2 p}$. Then apply Lemma 4, following a path $f \rightarrow g_{f} \rightarrow \phi_{0, k(f)} \rightarrow \cdots \rightarrow \phi_{m, k(f)}=\phi_{f}$.

According to the above argument and according to Lemma 4, outside of a set having probability at most $4 \sqrt{2 p} 2^{m}$, one has

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sup _{f}\left|X(f)-X\left(\phi_{f}\right)\right| & \leq 2 p \sqrt{n}+2 p \tau(p)+4 p^{1 / 2} L(p)+32 \int_{\sqrt{p} / 2}^{2^{m-1} \sqrt{p}} L_{0}\left(x^{2}\right) d x \\
& =2 p[\sqrt{n}+2 \tau(p)]+32 \int_{\sqrt{p} / 2}^{2^{m-1} \sqrt{p}} L_{0}\left(x^{2}\right) d x
\end{aligned}
$$

By construction, $\sqrt{n} \leq \tau(q-)$. If $3 p \leq 4 q$, one will have $p[\sqrt{n}+2 \tau(p)] \leq 4 q \tau(q-)$ and $2^{m-1} \sqrt{p} \leq 2^{m} \sqrt{q}$. The result follows by taking $p$ close enough to $q$.

Corollary. Assume that $\beta<1 / 2$ and that $2 \times 4^{m+2} q \leq \beta$ for some $m \geq 0$. Let $\mathscr{P}$ be the set of pairs $(f, g) \in \mathscr{F} \times \mathscr{F}$ such that $\int|f-g| d P \leq \beta / 2$. Then, outside of set having probability at most $4 \sqrt{2} \beta$ one will have

$$
|X(f)-X(g)| \leq 2 c(m)+4\left\{\beta \log \frac{2 K_{0}^{4}\left(q 4^{m}\right)}{\beta}\right\}^{1 / 2}
$$

for all pairs $(f, g) \in \mathscr{P}$.
Proof. Consider the map $f \mapsto \phi_{t} \in \mathscr{S}$ of Theorem 1 for a $p>q$, but $p$ very close to $q$. If $\int|f-g| d P<\beta / 2$ one will have $\int\left|\phi_{f}-\phi_{g}\right| d P<(\beta / 2)+ 4\left(1+4^{m}\right) q<\beta$. Let $\mathscr{S}(\beta)$ be the set of pairs $(\phi, \psi) \in \mathscr{S} \times \mathscr{S}$ such that $\int|\phi-\psi| d P<\beta$. The number of such pairs does not exceed $K_{0}^{2}\left(p 4^{m}\right) \leq K_{0}^{2}\left(q 4^{m}\right)$. Thus the argument of the proof of Lemma 2 yields an inequality

$$
\sup \{|X(\phi)-X(\psi)| ;(\phi, \psi) \in \mathscr{S}(\beta)\} \leq 4\left\{\beta \log \left[\frac{2 K_{0}^{4}\left(q 4^{m}\right)}{\beta}\right]\right\}^{1 / 2}
$$

except on a set of probability at most $2 \sqrt{2 \beta}$. Since $\left|X(f)-X\left(\phi_{f}\right)\right| \leq c(m)$ and $\left|X(g)-X\left(\phi_{g}\right)\right| \leq c(m)$ except for cases of probability at most $2^{m+3} \sqrt{q}$, the result follows.

Remark. It is more usual to write the results in terms of the function $x \mapsto [\log K(x)]^{1 / 2}$. This can be achieved by replacing our $L_{0}$ by the larger $L$ and noting that $x \log x$ is bounded for $x \in(0,1)$. We have used the functions $L$ and $L_{0}$ because they are the ones which occur in the proofs.

Any application of Theorem 2 will depend on the possibility of bounding the number $q$ and the product $q \tau(q-)$. For a fixed function $L$, Dudley uses the condition $\int_{0}^{1} L\left(x^{2}\right) d x<\infty$. Since $L(x)$ increases as $x$ decreases, such a condition implies that $2 \sqrt{x L}(x)=x \tau(x)$ tends to zero as $x \rightarrow 0$. In such a situation the value $q$ defined by $\tau(q+\varepsilon) \leq \sqrt{n} \leq \tau(q-\varepsilon)$ for $\varepsilon>0$ will go to zero as $n \rightarrow \infty$ and this in such a manner that $q \sqrt{n} \rightarrow 0$.

The foregoing Theorem 2 and its Corollary can be used to obtain limit theorems by a standard procedure. For each integer $n$ consider a space ( $\left.\mathscr{X}_{n}, \mathscr{A}_{n}\right)$ and probability measures $P_{j, n} ; j=1,2, \ldots, n$ on $\left(\mathscr{X}_{n}, \mathscr{A}_{n}\right)$. Let $\bar{P}_{n}=n^{-1} \sum_{j=1}^{n} P_{j, n}$. Let $\mathscr{F}_{n}$ be a class of measurable maps from $\left(\mathscr{X}_{n}, \mathscr{A}_{n}\right)$ to $[0,1]$. Let $K_{n}(x)$ be the entropy with bracketing of $\mathscr{F}_{n}$ at the level $x$. That is, $K_{n}(x)$ is the minimum number of brackets $(\psi, \omega)$ with $\psi \leq \omega, \int(\omega-\psi) d P_{n} \leq x$ such that each $f \in \mathscr{F}_{n}$ satisfies $\psi \leq f \leq \omega$ for some bracket. Let $L_{n}(x)= \left[\log \left(2 K_{n}^{2}(x) / x\right)\right]^{1 / 2}$.

Consider independent variables $\xi_{j, n}$ with $\mathscr{L}\left(\xi_{j, n}\right)=P_{j, n}$ and the empirical process $X_{n}(f)=(1 / \sqrt{n}) \sum_{j}\left[f\left(\xi_{j, n}\right)-E f\left(\xi_{j, n}\right)\right]$ defined for $f \in \mathscr{F}_{n}$. Consider also Gaussian processes $Z_{n}$ indexed by $\mathscr{F}_{n}$ and such that $E Z_{n}(f)=0$ and $E\left|Z_{n}(f)-Z_{n}(g)\right|^{2}=E\left|X_{n}(f)-X_{n}(g)\right|^{2}=(1 / n) \sum_{j} \operatorname{var}\left[f\left(\xi_{n, j}\right)-g\left(\xi_{n, j}\right)\right]$.

Metrize $\mathscr{F}_{n}$ by the norm $\int|f-g| d \bar{P}_{n}$. Then if the integral $\int_{0}^{1} L_{n}\left(x^{2}\right) d x$ is finite, the space $\mathscr{F}_{n}$ is precompact and therefore separable. The "trajectories" of the processes $X_{n}$ and $Z_{n}$ are functions from $\mathscr{F}_{n}$ to $(-\infty,+\infty)$. The process $X_{n}$ has bounded trajectories. Thus $X_{n}$ is a random element with values in the space $\mathscr{B}\left(\mathscr{F}_{n}\right)$ of bounded functions on $\mathscr{F}_{n}$. There is an analogous result for $Z_{n}$.

Lemma 5. If $\int_{0}^{1} L_{n}\left(x^{2}\right) d x<\infty$ then there is a Gaussian process $Z_{n}$ with the same expectation and covariance as $X_{n}$ such that the trajectories of $Z_{n}$ are bounded uniformly continuous functions on $\mathscr{F}_{n}$.

Proof. We have observed previously that the bounds on binomial tails used in Lemmas 2, 3, and 4 are not as good as the standard bounds on the Gaussian tails. Furthermore, the use of the binomial bounds was limited by the requirement that, in these lemmas, one needed to have an inequality of the type $\varepsilon \leq p \sqrt{n}$. The Gaussian bounds are valid without such a restriction. Thus, one can apply to $Z_{n}$ the result of Lemma 4 without the restriction $4 \log \left[2 K_{0}^{2} \beta_{1}\right] \leq n \beta_{1}$. This implies immediately the possibility of approximating the values of $Z_{n}$ on $\mathscr{F}_{n}$ by values on a finite subset of $\mathscr{F}_{n}$. The proof proceeds then as in Theorem 1 and its corollary without any reference to the inequalities $\psi \leq f \leq \omega$. (These inclusion inequalities do not yield any supplementary information here.) The proof can be carried out without difficulty.

According to this lemma one may consider $Z_{n}$ as a random element with values in the space $\Delta\left(\mathscr{F}_{n}\right)$ of bounded uniformly continuous functions on $\mathscr{F}_{n}$. This space can be metrized by the uniform norm $\sup \left\{|z(f)| ; f \in \mathscr{F}_{n}\right\}$ for which it becomes a separable Banach space. One can take on $\Delta\left(\mathscr{F}_{n}\right)$ the Borel $\sigma$-field and define the distribution $\mathscr{L}\left(Z_{n}\right)$ on this $\sigma$-field. For $X_{n}$ the measurability situation is more complex, but one can define the distribution $\mathscr{L}\left(X_{n}\right)$ as a probability measure on a suitable $\sigma$-field of the space $\mathscr{B}\left(\mathscr{F}_{n}\right)$ of bounded functions on $\mathscr{F}_{n}$. Thus $\mathscr{B}\left(\mathscr{F}_{n}\right)$ will also be considered as a Banach space for the uniform norm. The distance between $\mathscr{L}\left(X_{n}\right)$ and $\mathscr{L}\left(Z_{n}\right)$ can then be obtained by the dual Lipschitz norm corresponding to the uniform norm on $\mathscr{B}_{\left(\mathscr{F}_{n}\right)}$ (see the end of Chapter 15).

Theorem 3. Let $\mathscr{F}_{n}, X_{n}$, and $Z_{n}$ be as described. Assume that for each $\varepsilon>0$ there is a $\delta$ such that $\int_{0}^{\delta} L_{n}\left(x^{2}\right) d x<\varepsilon$ for all $n$. Then
(i) $\left\|\mathscr{L}\left(X_{n}\right)-\mathscr{L}\left(Z_{n}\right)\right\|_{D} \rightarrow 0$ as $n \rightarrow \infty$.
(ii) For each $\varepsilon>0$ there is an $N(\varepsilon)$ and a $\delta(\varepsilon)$ such that $n \geq N(\varepsilon)$ implies the existence of a set of probability at most $\varepsilon$ outside of which $\left|X_{n}(f)-X_{n}(g)\right| \leq \varepsilon$ for all pairs $(f, g)$ of elements of $\mathscr{F}_{n}$ such that $\int|f-g| d \bar{P}_{n} \leq \delta$.

Remark. Using a theorem of Strassen, one could rephrase statement (i), saying that one can find joint distributions for pairs $\left(X_{n}^{*}, Z_{n}^{*}\right)$ such that $\mathscr{L}\left(X_{n}^{*}\right)= \mathscr{L}\left(X_{n}\right), \mathscr{L}\left(Z_{n}^{*}\right)=\mathscr{L}\left(Z_{n}\right)$, and $\sup \left\{\left|X_{n}^{*}(f)-Z_{n}^{*}(f)\right| ; f \in \mathscr{F}_{n}\right\}$ tends to zero in probability.

Proof. Statement (ii) is an immediate consequence of the corollary of Theorem 1. For statement (i) note that for each $\varepsilon>0$, Theorem 1 implies the existence of subsets $\mathscr{S}_{n} \subset \mathscr{F}_{n}$ such that card $\mathscr{S}_{n}$ remains bounded and such that for suitable maps $T_{n}$ of $\mathscr{F}_{n}$ into $\mathscr{S}_{n}$ one will eventually have



$$
\sup \left\{\left|X_{n}(f)-X_{n}\left(T_{n} f\right)\right| ; f \in \mathscr{F}_{n}\right\}<\varepsilon
$$

outside of a set of probability at most $\varepsilon$. The same statement applies to the differences $Z_{n}(f)-Z_{n}\left(T_{n} f\right)$. If restricted to $\mathscr{S}_{n}$ the processes $\left\{X_{n}(f) ; f \in \mathscr{S}_{n}\right\}$ and $\left\{Z_{n}(f) ; f \in \mathscr{S}_{n}\right\}$ are random vectors of bounded dimension to which the ordinary central limit theorem applies. The spaces of functions $x \in \mathscr{B}\left(\mathscr{F}_{n}\right)$ of the type $f \mapsto x\left(T_{n} f\right)$ are spaces of bounded dimensionality in which one can approximate both $X_{n}$ and $Z_{n}$. The result follows.

Theorem 3 has been stated for measures $\bar{P}_{n}$ and families of functions $\mathscr{F}_{n}$ which may vary with $n$. If $\bar{P}_{n}=P$, fixed and $\mathscr{F}_{n}=\mathscr{F}$, fixed the equiintegrability condition for the functions $L_{n}$ is just the condition $\int_{0}^{1} L\left(x^{2}\right) d x< \infty$. This can readily be verified for a number of families $\mathscr{F}$, including, if $\mathscr{X}=\mathbb{R}^{k}$, families of parallelepipeds with faces parallel to coordinate planes, families of balls, etc., as long as the boundaries have $P$ measure equal to zero.

Even if $\bar{P}_{n}=P$, fixed, it is often convenient to consider families $\mathscr{F}_{n}$ which vary with $n$, and may increase as $n$ increases. Note that even if the integrals $\int_{0}^{1} L_{n}\left(x^{2}\right) d x$ are not uniformly convergent, Theorem 2 will still provide bounds which may be very usable.

Finally note that a variety of transformations preserve the integrability conditions on the functions $L_{n}$. One example is as follows. Let $\mathscr{F}$ be a family whose entropy with bracketing function is $K$ for a probability measure $P$ on $(\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{A})$. Let ( $\mathscr{Y}, \mathscr{B}$ ) be another space, carrying a measure $Q$. Let $K$ be a Markov kernel such that $P(A)=\int K(A, y) Q(d y)$ for $A \in \mathscr{A}$. Let $\mathscr{G}$ be the family of functions defined on $\mathscr{Y}$ by $\left\{\psi ; \psi(y)=\int \phi(x) K(d x, y), \phi \in \mathscr{F}\right\}$. Then the entropy with inclusion function $K^{\prime}$ of $\mathscr{G}$ for $Q$ satisfies $K^{\prime}(\alpha) \leq K(\alpha)$ for every $\alpha$.

## 8. Empirical Measures on Vapnik-Ccervonenkis Classes

In the preceding section we obtained a central limit theorem for empirical measures using Dudley's conditions on "entropy with bracketing." These conditions are sufficient but not necessary. We shall now consider another type of condition introduced in this domain by Vapnik and Cervonenkis. These authors were interested in generalizations of the Glivenko-Cantelli theorem. Dudley was able to show that the same conditions yield a central limit theorem. Furthermore Dudley and Durst showed that if one wants conditions independent of the underlying probability measure, the conditions in question are necessary.

Let $\mathscr{X}$ be a set and let $\mathscr{S}$ be a class of subsets of $\mathscr{X}$. Let $F=\left\{x_{1}, x_{2}, \ldots, x_{n}\right\}$ be a subset of $\mathscr{X}$ of cardinality $n$. Consider all the intersections $S \cap F, S \in \mathscr{S}$. This is a certain class of sets with cardinality $D(F, \mathscr{S})$ at most equal to $2^{n}$. With Steele, we shall say that $F$ is shattered by $\mathscr{S}$ if $D(F, \mathscr{S})=2^{n}$. The following lemma is a very remarkable result of Vapnik and Chervonenkis, Sauer and Shilah.

Lemma 1. Let $F$ be a subset of cardinality $n$. Let $\phi(k, n)=1+\sum_{j=0}^{k-1}\binom{n}{j}$. If for some integer $k, 1 \leq k \leq n$, one has $D(F, \mathscr{S}) \geq \phi(k, n)$ then there exists a subset $A \subset F$ which is shattered by $\mathscr{S}$ and has cardinality $k$.

Proof. The function $\phi$ satisfies the relation $\phi(n, n)=2^{n}$. Thus the result holds for $k=n$. The result also holds for $k=1$. In this case the assertion is that there is some $x \in F$ such that $x \in F \cap S_{1}$ but $x \notin F \cap S_{0}$ for a pair ( $S_{0}, S_{1} )$ of elements of $\mathscr{S}$. However, $\phi(1, n)=2$ implies the existence of the pair in question.

Proceeding by induction, assume that the lemma is valid for all pairs $(k, n)$ such that $k \leq n<N$ but that it does not hold for $N$ itself. If $F$ is any set and if $y \notin F$, let $\{F, y\}$ be the set obtained by adjoining $y$ to $F$. Consider a set $\{F, y\}$ where $F$ has cardinality $N-1$, and take a number $k \leq N-1$. If $\{F, y\}$ does not contain any set of cardinality $k$ shattered by $\mathscr{S}$, this is also true of $F$ itself. Thus the induction hypothesis implies $D(F, \mathscr{S})<\phi(k, N-1)$.

Now partition the subsets $F \cap S, S \in \mathscr{S}$, into two classes. In the first class place those $F \cap S$ such that one of the two sets $F \cap S$ and $\{F \cap S, y\}$, but not both are of the type $\{F, y\} \cap T$ for some $T \in \mathscr{S}$. The second class contains those $F \cap S$ such that there exist $S_{0}$ and $S_{1}$ in $\mathscr{S}$ with $F \cap S=\{F, y\} \cap S_{0}$ and $\{F \cap S, y\}=\{F, y\} \cap S_{1}$.

Let $\mathscr{S}_{2}$ be the family of sets $S$ for which $F \cap S$ is of the second class. Suppose that there is a set $A \subset F$ which is shattered by $\mathscr{S}_{2}$ and has cardinality $k-1$. For $S \in \mathscr{S}_{2}$ the pair ( $S_{0}, S_{1}$ ) of elements of $\mathscr{S}$ which yields $F \cap S=\{F, y\} \cap S_{0}$ and $\{F \cap S, y\}=\{F, y\} \cap S_{1}$ also yields $A \cap S=\{A, y\} \cap S_{0}$ and $\{A \cap S, y\}= \{A, y\} \cap S_{1}$. Thus one would have $D[\{A, y\}, \mathscr{S}]=2^{k}$, contrary to assumption. The induction assumption implies then that $D\left[F, \mathscr{S}_{2}\right]<\phi(k-1, N-1)$. The sets $F \cap S$ of the first class form a family of cardinality $a$. Those of the second class form a family of cardinality $b$, and $D(F, \mathscr{S})=a+b$ while $D[\{F, y\}, \mathscr{S}]=a+2 b$. The nonexistence of a shattered set of cardinality $k$ implies $a+b \leq \phi(k, N-1)-1$ and $b \leq \phi(k-1, N-1)-1$. This yields

$$
D[\{F, y\}, \mathscr{S}]<\phi(k, N-1)+\phi(k-1, N-1)-1 .
$$

However, $\phi(k, N)=\phi(k, N-1)+\phi(k-1, N-1)-1$. The result follows.
One corollary of Lemma 1 is as follows. For a triplet $\{\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{A}, \mathscr{S}\}, \mathscr{S} \subset \mathscr{A}$, let $m(n, \mathscr{S})=\sup _{F} D(F, \mathscr{S})$ where the supremum is taken over all sets $F \subset \mathscr{X}$ of cardinality $n$. Define also an integer $v(\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{S})$ as the largest integer $k$ (or $+\infty)$ such that $\mathscr{X}$ contains subsets of cardinality $k$ shattered by $\mathscr{S}$.

Corollary. Either $m(n, \mathscr{S})=2^{n}$ identically in $n$, or $m(n, \mathscr{S}) \leq \phi(v+1, n)-1$ for $n>v$.

Now $\phi(v+1, n)-1=\sum_{j=0}^{v}\binom{n}{j}$. It follows that $\phi(1, n)-1=n+1, \phi(2, n)- 1=1+n+(n(n-1) / 2)$, etc. One sees easily that $\phi(v+1, n)-1 \leq n^{v}+1$ for all $v \leq n$ and that $\phi(v+1, n)-1 \leq n^{v}$ for $2 \leq v \leq n$.

Definition. If $v(\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{S})$ is finite, the class $\mathscr{S}$ is called a Vapnik-Cervonenkis class (V.C. for short) of exponent $v$.

It is not difficult to see that on the line, intervals form a V.C. class. The same applies to quadrants with sides parallel to the coordinate axes in the plane,
and so forth. Another example of V.C. class is given by the following result, taken from Dudley's paper.

Proposition 1. Let $\mathscr{X}$ be a set of cardinality card $\mathscr{X}>m$. Let $\mathscr{E}$ be any vector space of real functions on $\mathscr{X}$. Assume that $\mathscr{E}$ has dimension $m$ and let $\mathscr{S}$ be the class of sets of the form $\{x: f(x)>0\}$ for $f \in \mathscr{E}$. Then no set $A \subset \mathscr{X}$ of cardinality $m+1$ is shattered by $\mathscr{S}$.

Proof. Restrict $\mathscr{E}$ to $A$, getting a subspace $\mathscr{E}_{A}$ of the space $\mathscr{F}(A, \mathbb{R})$ of all real functions on $A$. Since $\mathscr{E}_{A}$ has dimension at most $m$ it cannot be the entire space $\mathscr{F}(A, \mathbb{R})$. Give $\mathscr{F}(A, \mathbb{R})$ the inner product $\langle g, f\rangle=\sum_{x \in A} f(x) g(x)$. Then there is a $g$ which is orthogonal to every $f \in \mathscr{E}_{A}^{\circ}$. Let $B=\{x: x \in A, g(x)>0\}$. (If $B$ is empty, replace $g$ by $(-g)$.) If $\mathscr{S}$ shatters $A$, there is an $f \in \mathscr{E}_{A}$ such that $B=\{x: f(x)>0\}$. However, this implies $\sum_{x \in A} g(x) f(x)>0$, contradicting the orthogonality property.

Lemma 2 (Dudley). Let $\mathscr{S}$ be a V.C. class of exponent v. Let $\mathscr{S}_{1}= \left\{S_{1} \backslash S_{2} ; S_{i} \in \mathscr{S}\right\}$. Then $\mathscr{S}_{1}$ is a V.C. class of exponent at most $2 v$.

Proof. The numbers $D\left(F, \mathscr{S}_{1}\right)$ cannot exceed $1+D(F, \mathscr{S})[D(F, \mathscr{S})-1]$. Lemma 3 (Dudley). Let $P$ be any probability measure on ( $\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{A}$ ). Let $\mathscr{S}$ be a V.C. class of exponent v. For $x \in(0, \log 2)$, let $\left\{S_{j} ; j \in J\right\}$ be a maximal subset of $\mathscr{S}$ subject to the restriction that if $j \neq k$ then the symmetric difference $S_{j} \Delta S_{k}$ satisfies $P\left(S_{j} \Delta S_{k}\right)>x$. Then

$$
\log (\operatorname{card} J) \leq 2 v\left[\log v+\log \frac{2}{x}\right]
$$

Proof. Let $\left\{S_{j} ; j=1,2, \ldots, q\right\}$ be a finite subset of $\left\{S_{j} ; j \in J\right\}$. Let $n$ be such that $q^{2} e^{-n x}<2$. Take a sample $F$ of size $n$ from the distribution $P$. Then the probability that all symmetric differences $S_{j} \Delta S_{k}, j \neq k$, intersect $F$ is not zero. However, if all the sets $\left(S_{j} \Delta S_{k}\right) \cap F$ are nonempty, the family $\left\{S_{j} \cap F\right.$; $j=1,2, \ldots, q\}$ consists of $q$ distinct nonempty elements. Thus $q \leq m(n, \mathscr{S})$. This implies in particular that $J$ is a finite set and that one can take $q=\operatorname{card} J$. Restating the inequality, one can say that the relation $2 \log q \leq \log 2+n x$ implies $q \leq n^{v}$ (since the sets are nonempty). Now let $\xi=(1 / v) \log q$ and let $n_{0}$ be the first integer such that $n \geq(1 / x)[2 v \xi-\log 2]$. For this integer we have $\xi \leq \log n_{0}$ and therefore

$$
\xi \leq \log \left\{\frac{1}{x}[2 v \xi-\log 2]+1\right\} \leq \log \frac{2 v}{x} \xi
$$

if, as assumed, $x \leq \log 2$. This gives an inequality $\xi \leq \log \xi+a$, with $a=\log (2 v / x)$. The function $f(\xi)=\xi-\log \xi$ is such that $f(1)=1$. It is decreasing in the interval $\xi \in(0,1)$ and increasing, convex for $\xi \in[1, \infty)$.

Thus the inequality $\xi \leq \log \xi+a$ implies that $\xi$ is at most as large as the solution of the equation $y=\log y+a$ that is larger than unity. However, that solution is smaller than $2 a$. Thus we get

$$
\xi=\frac{1}{v} \log q \leq 2 \log \frac{2 v}{x} \text { and } \log q \leq 2 v \log \frac{2 v}{x}
$$

as claimed.
In the sequel, we shall work with sets $F=\left\{\left(x_{j}, y_{j}\right) ; j=1,2, \ldots, n\right\}$ of pairs of elements of $\mathscr{X}$. We shall let $F_{1}$ be set $F_{1}=\left\{x_{1}, x_{2}, \ldots, x_{n}\right\}$ of first coordinates and let $F_{2}$ be the set of second coordinates of the pairs. A set $S \in \mathscr{S}$ defines a pattern on $F$ by associating to $\left(x_{j}, y_{j}\right)$ score unity if $\left(x_{j}, y_{j}\right) \in S \times S^{c}$, score $(-1)$ if $\left(x_{j}, y_{j}\right) \in S^{c} \times S$, and value zero otherwise.

Lemma 4. Let $F_{1} \cup F_{2}$ consist of $2 n$ distinct elements of $\mathscr{X}$. Then the number of distinct patterns cut by $\mathscr{S}$ on $F$ does not exceed $D\left(F_{1} \cup F_{2}, \mathscr{S}\right)$.

Proof. Consider an $S \in \mathscr{S}$ and the intersection $S \cap\left(F_{1} \cup F_{2}\right)$. For a given $F$ this yields two sets $S \cap F_{1}$ and $S \cap F_{2}$ from which one can reconstruct in a well determined manner the sets $F \cap\left(S \times S^{c}\right), F \cap\left(S^{c} \times S\right)$ and so forth. Hence the result.

The following argument is adopted from Vapnik-Ccervonenkis and Pollard. Let $F=\left\{\left(x_{j}, y_{j}\right) ; j=1,2, \ldots, n\right\}$ be a subset of $\mathscr{X} \times \mathscr{X}$. Let $\varepsilon_{j}$ be independent random variables such that $\operatorname{Pr}\left[\varepsilon_{j}=1\right]=\operatorname{Pr}\left[\varepsilon_{j}=-1\right]=1 / 2$. Let $X$ be the signed measure $X=\sum_{j=1}^{n} \varepsilon_{j}\left(\delta_{x_{j}}-\delta_{y_{j}}\right)$.

Lemma 5. Assume that $F_{1} \cup F_{2}$ contains $2 n$ distinct points. Assume also that for each $S \in \mathscr{S}$ one has card $S \cap\left(F_{1} \cup F_{2}\right) \leq c$. Then

$$
\operatorname{Pr}\{\sup [|X(S)| ; S \in \mathscr{S}] \geq r\} \leq 2 D\left\{F_{1} \cup F_{2}, \mathscr{S}\right\} \exp \left\{-\frac{r^{2}}{2 c}\right\}
$$

Proof. Take a particular $S \in \mathscr{S}$ and let $U\left(x_{j}, y_{j}\right)$ be the score it attaches to $\left(x_{j}, y_{j}\right)$. Then $X(S)=\sum \varepsilon_{j} U\left(x_{j}, y_{j}\right)$. This has the same distribution as $\sum \varepsilon_{j}\left|U\left(x_{j}, y_{j}\right)\right|$. Equivalently $X(S)$ has the distribution of $2(Y-(N / 2))$, where $N=\sum\left|U\left(x_{j}, y_{j}\right)\right|$ and where $Y$ is binomial with $N$ trials each with probability of success $1 / 2$. It follows then from Lemma 1 , Section 7 that

$$
\operatorname{Pr}\{|X(S)| \geq r\} \leq 2 \exp \left\{-\frac{r^{2}}{2 N}\right\}
$$

A pair $\left(x_{j}, y_{j}\right)$ such that $\left|U\left(x_{j}, y_{j}\right)\right|=1$ may be such that $x_{j} \in S$. The number
of such pairs is at most card $S \cap F_{1}$. Similarly the number of pairs with $\left|U\left(x_{j}, y_{j}\right)\right|=1$ and $y_{j} \in S$ is at most card $S \cap F_{2}$. Thus $N \leq c$. Now if two sets $S$ and $S^{\prime}$ yield the same pattern, one has $X(S)=X\left(S^{\prime}\right)$. Therefore the number of different random variables of the type $X(S)$ does not exceed $D\left(F_{1} \cup F_{2}, \mathscr{S}\right)$. The result follows.

We are now in a position to obtain central limit theorems for V.C. classes. However, the general case presents some measurability problems. We shall return to these later, assuming for the time being that enough measurability is available to make the arguments valid.

Let $(\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{A})$ be a measurable space and let $p_{j} ; j=1,2, \ldots, n$ be $n$ probability measures defined on $\mathscr{A}$. Let $\xi_{j} ; j=1,2, \ldots, n$ be $n$ independent variables such that $\xi_{j}$ has distribution $p_{j}$. Let $\mathscr{S}$ be a class of subsets of $\mathscr{X}$ with $\mathscr{S} \subset \mathscr{A}$. We are interested in the unnormalized empirical process $Z$ defined on $\mathscr{S}$ by

$$
Z(S)=\sum_{j=1}^{n}\left[\delta_{\xi_{j}}(S)-p_{j}(S)\right]
$$

and in the norm $\|Z\|_{\mathscr{S}}=\sup \{|Z(S)| ; S \in \mathscr{S}\}$. Note that $Z(S)$ is well defined but that $\|Z\|_{\mathscr{Y}}$ need not be a random variable, even on the completion of the $\sigma$-fields.

The technique of proof will be to use a symmetrized version $X$ of $Z$ such that $X=Z-Z^{\prime}$, where $Z^{\prime}$ is a copy of $Z$ independent of $Z$. It will be obtained from variables $\eta_{j}, j=1,2, \ldots, n$ independent of the $\xi_{j}$ and such that $\mathscr{L}\left\{\xi_{j} ; j=1,2, \ldots, n\right\}=\mathscr{L}\left\{\eta_{j} ; j=1,2, \ldots, n\right\}$ by writing $Z^{\prime}(S)=\sum\left[\delta_{\eta_{j}}(S)-p_{j}(S)\right]$ and $X(S)=Z(S)-Z^{\prime}(S)=\sum\left(\delta_{\xi_{j}}-\delta_{\eta_{j}}\right)(S)$.

Several norms will be used, taking classes $\mathscr{S}_{\alpha}, \mathscr{S}_{\alpha}{ }^{*}$, etc., derived from $\mathscr{S}$. For the validity of the arguments the following conditions will be needed.
(A) Let $\varepsilon_{j}$ be independent variables, independent of the $\left\{\left(\xi_{k}, \eta_{k}\right) ; k=1,2, \ldots, n\right\}$ such that $P\left[\varepsilon_{j}=1\right]=P\left[\varepsilon_{j}=-1\right]=1 / 2$. Let $X^{*}=\sum \varepsilon_{j}\left(\delta_{\xi_{j}}-\delta_{\eta_{j}}\right)$. Then for all the variables used in the arguments, $X$ and $X^{*}$ generate the same distribution and Lemma 5 can be applied conditionally given the $\left(\xi_{j}, \eta_{j}\right)$.
(B) Let $m(S)$ be a median of $Z(S)$. Paul Lévy's symmetrization inequalities imply that whenever $\mathscr{S}$ is countable,

$$
\operatorname{Pr}\left\{\|Z-m\|_{\mathscr{S}} \geq x\right\} \leq 2 \operatorname{Pr}\left\{\|X\|_{\mathscr{S}} \geq x\right\} .
$$

It will be assumed here that the same relations hold for all the classes to which the symmetrization argument is applied below.

We shall return later to conditions which are sufficient to imply (A) and (B). Take a particular class $\mathscr{S}$ of subsets. Let $\bar{p}=(1 / n) \sum_{j=1}^{n} p_{j}$. Take a number $\alpha \in(0,1)$. Let $\mathscr{F}_{\alpha}=\left\{f_{i} ; i=1,2, \ldots, K_{0}(\alpha)\right\}$ be a maximal subset of $\mathscr{S}$ subject to the requirement that $i \neq k$ implies that the symmetric difference $f_{i} \Delta f_{k}$ has $\bar{p}$ measure $\bar{p}\left(f_{i} \Delta f_{k}\right)>\alpha$. Then, for each $S \in \mathscr{S}$ one can find an $f(S) \in \mathscr{F}_{\alpha}$ such that
$\bar{p}[S \Delta f(S)] \leq \alpha$. We shall assume that such a selection has been carried out, yielding a function $S \mapsto f(S)$ from $\mathscr{S}$ to $\mathscr{F}_{\alpha}$ and call $\mathscr{S}_{\alpha}$ the class of differences of the form $S \backslash f(S)$ and $f(S) \backslash S$.

Let $F=\left\{x_{1}, \ldots, x_{n} ; y_{1}, y_{2}, \ldots, y_{n}\right\}$ be the double sample. For each $A \in \mathscr{S}_{\alpha}$, let $U_{j}(A, F)=1$ if $x_{j} \in A, y_{j} \in A^{c}$. Let $U_{j}(A, F)=-1$ if $x_{j} \in A^{c}, y_{j} \in A$. Otherwise let $U_{j}(A, F)=0$. Let $N\left(F, \mathscr{S}_{\alpha}\right)$ be the number

$$
N\left(F, \mathscr{S}_{\alpha}\right)=\sup _{A}\left\{\sum_{j}\left|U_{j}(A, F)\right| ; A \in \mathscr{S}_{\alpha}\right\}
$$

and let $M\left(F, \mathscr{S}_{\alpha}\right)$ be the number of different patterns $\left\{U_{j}(A, F) ; j=1,2, \ldots, n\right\}$ cut by the sets $A \in \mathscr{S}_{\alpha}$ on $F$. These numbers $M\left(F, \mathscr{S}_{\alpha}\right)$ and $N\left(F, \mathscr{S}_{\alpha}\right)$ are functions of the sample $F$. They may or may not be random variables.

However, if one considers $F$ fixed, the inequalities of Lemma 5 are still applicable. To obtain inequalities applicable to the process $Z$ on $\mathscr{S}_{\alpha}$, let $\varepsilon$ be a num-
ber, $\varepsilon \in(0,1)$. Let $m(\varepsilon)$ be such that the outer probability that $M\left(F, \mathscr{S}_{\alpha}\right)> m(\varepsilon)$ is no larger than $\varepsilon$. Similarly take $v(\varepsilon)$ so that $\operatorname{Pr}^{*}\left\{N\left(F, \mathscr{S}_{\alpha}\right)>v(\varepsilon)\right\} \leq \varepsilon$.

Lemma 6. Let the conditions (A) and (B) be satisfied for $\mathscr{S}_{\alpha}$. Let $x^{2}(\varepsilon)= 2 \log (2 m(\varepsilon) / \varepsilon)$. Then

$$
v(8 \varepsilon) \leq 2[x(\varepsilon)+\sqrt{n \alpha+1}]^{2}
$$

and

$$
\operatorname{Pr}\left\{\sup _{S}\left[|Y(S)| ; S \in \mathscr{S}_{\alpha}\right] \geq \sqrt{2} x(\varepsilon)[x(\varepsilon)+\sqrt{n \alpha+1}]\right\}<10 \varepsilon
$$

Proof. Fix a sample $F$. For $S \in \mathscr{S}_{\alpha}$ let $v(F, S)=\sum_{j}\left|U_{j}(S, F)\right|$ be the number of pairs of $F$ separated by $S$. Let $N_{1}(S)=\sum_{i} \delta_{x_{1}}(S)$ and $N_{2}(S)= \sum \delta_{y_{i}}(S)$ so that $Y(S)=N_{1}(S)-N_{2}(S)$.

The inequality of Lemma 5 yields the relation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\operatorname{Pr}\{|Y(S)| & \left.\geq x \sqrt{v(F, S)} \text { for some } S \in \mathscr{S}_{\alpha} \mid F\right\} \\
& \leq 2 M\left(F, \mathscr{S}_{\alpha}\right) \exp \left\{-x^{2} / 2\right\}
\end{aligned}
$$

Thus, with the definition of $x(\varepsilon)$, we can write

$$
\operatorname{Pr}\left\{|Y(S)| \geq x(\varepsilon) \sqrt{v(F, S)} \text { for some } S \in \mathscr{S}_{\alpha}\right\} \leq 2 \varepsilon
$$

Now write $Y(S)=N_{1}(S)-N_{2}(S)$ and note that $\sqrt{v(F, S)} \leq \sqrt{N_{1}(S)+N_{2}(S)} \leq \sqrt{N_{1}(S)}+\sqrt{N_{2}(S)}$. This gives the relation

$$
\operatorname{Pr} .\left\{\sup \left[\left|\sqrt{N_{1}(S)}-\sqrt{N_{2}(S)}\right| ; S \in \mathscr{S}_{\alpha}\right] \geq x(\varepsilon)\right\} \leq 2 \varepsilon
$$

Let $\mu$ be an upper bound for the median of $N_{1}(S), S \in \mathscr{L}_{\alpha}$. Paul Lévy's symmetrization inequalities give

$$
\operatorname{Pr}\left\{\sup \left[\sqrt{N_{1}(S)} ; S \in \mathscr{S}_{\alpha}\right] \geq x(\varepsilon)+\mu\right\}<4 \varepsilon
$$

and a similar inequality for $\mathrm{N}_{2}(S)$. According to a result of Jogdeo and Samuels the median of $N_{i}(S)$ is bounded by $n \alpha+1$. Therefore,

$$
\operatorname{Pr}\left\{\sup \left[N_{1}(S)+N_{2}(S) ; S \in \mathscr{S}_{\alpha}\right] \geq 2[x(\varepsilon)+\sqrt{n \alpha+1}]^{2}\right\} \leq 8 \varepsilon .
$$

This yields the first statement. To obtain the second, apply Lemma 5 once again, noting that except for probability at most $\varepsilon+8 \varepsilon$ one will have $M\left(F, \mathscr{S}_{\alpha}\right) \leq m(\varepsilon)$ and $\sum\left|U_{j}(S, F)\right| \leq v(8 \varepsilon)$.

Remark. Paul Lévy's symmetrization inequalities and the result of Jogdeo and Samuels yield also the inequality
$\operatorname{Pr}\left\{\sup \left[|Z(S)| ; S \in \mathscr{S}_{\alpha}\right] \geq 1+y\right\} \leq 2 \varepsilon+2 \operatorname{Pr}\left\{2 N\left(F, \mathscr{S}_{\alpha}\right) \log \frac{2 M\left(F, \mathscr{S}_{\alpha}\right)}{\varepsilon}>y^{2}\right\}$,
where, if necessary, the probability signs are interpreted as outer probabilities.
The foregoing Lemma 6 can be combined with Lemma 4 of Section 7. Recall that this Lemma 4 uses functions $K_{0}$ and $L_{0}$ defined on the class $\mathscr{S}$ and the empirical process $X(S)=(1 / \sqrt{n}) Z(S)$. The combination yields the following statement.

Lemma 7. Let $\varepsilon$ be a fixed number $\varepsilon \in(0,1)$. Assume that $\alpha$ is such that $4 \alpha^{-1} L_{0}^{2}(\alpha) \leq n$. Then there is a map $\tau$ from $\mathscr{S}$ to $\mathscr{F}_{\varepsilon^{2}}$ such that

$$
\sup \{|X(S)-X[\tau(S)]| ; S \in \mathscr{S}\} \leq(32) \int_{0}^{\varepsilon} L_{0}\left(x^{2}\right) d x+\frac{2}{\sqrt{n}}(1+y)
$$

except for cases whose total probability does not exceed

$$
5 \varepsilon+\varepsilon \operatorname{Pr}\left\{2 N\left(F, \mathscr{S}_{\alpha}\right) \log \left[\frac{2 M\left(F, \mathscr{S}_{\alpha}\right)}{\varepsilon}\right] \geq y^{2}\right\}
$$

Proof. Lemma 4 of Section 7 involves the choice of a number $p \in(0,1)$ and an integer $m$. It can be applied here with $\alpha=p$ taken approximately equal to $\varepsilon^{2} 4^{-m}$.

In the preceding arguments the number of observations $n$ was kept fixed. To obtain central limit theorems one lets $n$ tend to infinity. In such a case the average measure $\bar{p}_{n}=(1 / n) \sum_{j} p_{j, n}$ depends on $n$ and so can the functions called $K_{0}$ and $L_{0}$ even if the class $\mathscr{S}$ is fixed. However, the class $\mathscr{S}$ itself may be made dependent on $n$. Thus, the functions $L_{0}$ become $L_{0, n}$, the class $\mathscr{S}$ becomes $\mathscr{S}_{n}$, and the classes $\mathscr{S}_{\alpha}$ become $\mathscr{S}_{\alpha, n}$. One can apply Lemma 7 for a fixed $\alpha$. However, better results can be obtained by letting $\alpha$ depend on $n$, as can be seen from Lemma 7 itself. The application of that lemma requires that $\alpha(n)$ be selected so that $4 \alpha^{-1}(n) L_{0, n}^{2}[\alpha(n)] \leq n$. Since $x^{-1} L_{0, n}^{2}(x)$ is a decreasing function of $x$, for all $n \geq 4$, there will be a number $\alpha^{\prime}(n)$ such that $x>\alpha^{\prime}(n)$ implies
$4 x^{-1} L_{0, n}^{2}(x) \leq n$ and $x<\alpha^{\prime}(n)$ implies $4 x^{-1} L_{0, n}^{2}(x) \geq n$. We shall take $\alpha(n)=\left(1+2^{-n}\right) \alpha^{\prime}(n)$. In order to obtain convergence theorems, the functions $L_{0, n}$ must be suitably restricted. We shall use the following assumption.

Assumption (C). For each $\varepsilon>0$ there is a $\delta>0$ independent of $n$ such that

$$
\int_{0}^{\delta} L_{0, n}\left(x^{2}\right) d x<\varepsilon
$$

This is the same equi-integrability assumption as the one made for Theorem 3, Section 7. However, the functions $L_{0, n}$ are smaller than the functions $L_{n}$ of that theorem.

Lemma 8. Let Assumption (C) be satisfied. Then $\sqrt{n} \alpha(n) \rightarrow 0$ as $n \rightarrow \infty$. Proof. One can write

$$
\int_{0}^{\sqrt{x}} L_{0, n}\left(\xi^{2}\right) d \xi \geq \sqrt{x} L_{0, n}(x)
$$

Thus $\sup _{n} L_{0, n}(x)<\infty$ for each $x \in(0,1)$. Also the inequality $4 x^{-1} L_{0, n}^{2}(x) \geq n$ implies $x \sqrt{n} \leq 2 \int_{0}^{\sqrt{x}} L_{0, n}\left(\xi^{2}\right) d \xi$. By continuity this last inequality applies also if $x$ is replaced by $\alpha^{\prime}(n)$. The result follows.

Having chosen the cutoff point $\alpha(n)$, one selects a subclass $F_{\alpha(n), n} \subset \mathscr{S}_{n}$ as explained previously, and a map $f_{n}$ from $\mathscr{S}_{n}$ to $\mathscr{F}_{\alpha(n), n}$. This yields classes $\mathscr{S}_{\alpha(n), n}$ of sets of the form $S \backslash f_{n}(S)$ or $f_{n}(S) \backslash S$ as before. Then one can map the $\mathscr{F}_{\alpha(n), n}$ to classes $\mathscr{F}_{\varepsilon^{2}, n}$ as in Lemma 7. The classes $\mathscr{S}_{\alpha(n), n}$ can be used to define variables $M_{n}=M\left[F_{n}, \mathscr{S}_{\alpha(n), n}\right]$ and $N_{n}=N\left[F_{n}, \mathscr{S}_{\alpha(n), n}\right]$ as before.

Theorem 1. Let $\left\{\mathscr{S}_{n}\right\}, n=1, \varepsilon, \ldots$ be a sequence of classes satisfying the measurability assumptions (A) and (B) and the entropy assumption (C).

Assume in addition that either $(1 / n) N_{n} \log M_{n}$ or that $(1 / \sqrt{n}) \log M_{n}$ tends to zero in probability. Then, for each fixed $\varepsilon>0$ there are classes $\mathscr{S}_{n}^{\prime} \subset \mathscr{S}_{n}$ and maps $\tau_{n}$ from $\mathscr{S}_{n}$ to $\mathscr{S}_{n}^{\prime}$ such that
(i) $\sup _{n} \operatorname{card} \mathscr{S}_{n}^{\prime}<\infty$;
(ii) $\lim _{n} \sup \operatorname{Pr}\left\{\sup \left[\left|X_{n}(S)-X_{n}\left[\tau_{n}(S)\right]\right| ; S \in \mathscr{S}_{n}\right]>\varepsilon\right\}<\varepsilon$.

Proof. Under the assumption that $(1 / n) N_{n} \log M_{n}$ tends to zero in probability, this follows immediately from Lemma 7. For the alternate case where $(1 / \sqrt{n}) \log M_{n}$ tends to zero, it will be sufficient to show that $(1 / \sqrt{n}) N_{n}$ also tends to zero. To show this, let $m_{n}(\varepsilon)$ be such that $\operatorname{Pr}\left[M_{n}>m_{n}(\varepsilon)\right]<\varepsilon$, let $v_{n}(\varepsilon)$ be such that $\operatorname{Pr}\left[N_{n}>\varepsilon\right]<\varepsilon$, and let $x_{n}^{2}(\varepsilon)=2 \log \left[\left(2 m_{n}(\varepsilon) / \varepsilon\right)\right]$. Then, according to Lemma 6,

$$
v_{n}(8 \varepsilon) \leq 2\left[x_{n}(\varepsilon)+\sqrt{n \alpha(n)+1}\right]^{2}
$$

and

$$
\frac{v_{n}(8 \varepsilon)}{\sqrt{n}} \leq 2\left\{\left[\frac{x_{n}^{2}(\varepsilon)}{\sqrt{n}}\right]^{1 / 2}+\left[\alpha(n) \sqrt{n}+\frac{1}{\sqrt{n}}\right]^{1 / 2}\right\}^{2}
$$

However, by assumption, $x_{n}^{2}(\varepsilon) / \sqrt{n} \rightarrow 0$ and, by Lemma $8, \alpha(n) \sqrt{n} \rightarrow 0$. This concludes the proof of the theorem.

Just as in Section 7, one cannot claim that the processes $(1 / \sqrt{n}) Z_{n}$ converge. But one can approximate their distribution by that of the Gaussian processes with the same covariance structure. One can modify the proofs to apply to certain classes that are not V.C. classes. Lemmas 6 and 7 admit versions where the quantities $\left[M\left(F_{2 n}, \mathscr{S}\right)\right]^{2}$ are replaced by the expectation of $\left[D\left(F_{2 n}, \mathscr{S}\right)\right]^{2}$, for the sample $F_{2 n}=\left\{\xi_{1}, \ldots, \xi_{n} ; \eta_{1}, \ldots, \eta_{n}\right\}$.

It is possible to obtain analogues of Theorem 1 in which the class $\mathscr{S}$ has been replaced by a class $\mathscr{F}$ of functions. Suppose, for instance, that $f \in \mathscr{F}$ implies $0 \leq f \leq 1$. For each $f$, let $S_{f}$ be the set $\{(x, t) ; f(x) \leq t\}$ in $\mathscr{X} \times[0,1]$. A possible modification of the V.C. condition is to require that $\mathscr{S}=\left\{S_{f} ; f \in \mathscr{F}\right\}$ be a V.C. class. The sums obtained from a sample $\xi_{j} ; j=1,2, \ldots, n$ would be $Y(f)=\sum_{j}\left[f\left(\xi_{j}\right)-E f\left(\xi_{j}\right)\right]$.

The procedure used in the proof of Lemma 4, Section 7 is applicable to the process $Y$ itself. Thus to obtain a result similar to Theorem 1 it is sufficient to
obtain an analogue of Lemma 6. The corresponding class of functions is a class $\mathscr{F}_{\alpha}{ }^{*}$ of functions of the type $\left(f_{1}-f_{2}\right)^{*}$, with $f_{i} \in \mathscr{F}$ and $E\left|f_{1}-f_{2}\right| \leq \alpha$.

On this class $\mathscr{F}_{\alpha}^{*}$ consider another process $\hat{Y}$ obtained by selecting independent variables $u_{1}, u_{2}, \ldots, u_{n}$ independent of the $\xi_{j}$ and uniformly distributed on $[0,1]$. Let $\hat{Y}(f)+\sum_{j} E f\left(\xi_{j}\right)$ be the number of pairs $\left(\xi_{j}, u_{j}\right)$ such that $(\xi, u) \in S_{f}$. Then, conditionally given $\xi_{1}, \xi_{2}, \ldots, \xi_{n}$, the variables $\hat{Y}(f)$ have expectation $Y(f)$ and a conditional median $M(f)$ such that $|Y(f)-M(f)| \leq 1$. If Lévy's symmetrization inequalities are valid, one can assert that

$$
P\left\{\sup _{f}|Y(f)| \geq x+1\right\} \leq 2 P\left\{\sup _{f}|\hat{Y}(f)| \geq x\right\}
$$

Since Lemma 6 applies to $\widehat{Y}$, this gives the desired result.
One can obtain results analogous to Theorem 1 for certain other classes of functions. This was done by Dudley, using a different approach. Dudley uses classes $\mathscr{F}$ such that there is a suitable $g$ for which $|f| \leq g$ for all $f \in \mathscr{F}$ and such that the sets $\{x: f(x) \leq t ; f \in \mathscr{F}, t \in(-\infty,+\infty)\}$ form a V.C. class.

The validity of Theorem 1 depends on the requirements called (A) and (B) above. They are automatically satisfied if $\mathscr{S}$ is countable. One can also force them to be satisfied by replacing the processes by separable processes with the same finite dimensional distributions. This is the procedure used by Pollard. This author gives $\mathscr{S}$ the pseudometric $P\left(A_{1} \Delta A_{2}\right)$, for which $\mathscr{S}$ is precompact. A theorem of Doob says then that there are separable versions with the same finite dimensional distributions. There is, however, a difficulty: If $Z$ and $Z^{\prime}$ are separable their difference $Z-Z^{\prime}$ need not be separable. Such a difficulty appears curable by selecting special versions or by going to the weak Radon measures of Chapter 15.

There are, however, situations where one would like to use the processes $Z$ as they are defined, eliminating perhaps only one set of measure zero from the probability spaces. For the condition called (A), the situation is fairly simple. The sample $\left(\xi_{j}, \eta_{j}\right) ; j=1,2, \ldots, n$ comes with the ordering of its index set $\{1,2, \ldots, n\}$.
