One can reorder it as $\left\{\xi_{1}, \xi_{2}, \ldots, \xi_{n}, \eta_{1}, \eta_{2}, \ldots, \eta_{n}\right\}$, putting it in one-to-one correspondence with the set $A$ of the integers $\{1,2, \ldots, 2 n\}$. The set $\mathscr{P}(A)$ of all subsets of $A$ can be ordered in a systematic manner.

This gives a one-to-one correspondence between the subsets of $\left\{\xi_{1}, \xi_{2}, \ldots, \xi_{n}, \eta_{1}, \ldots, \eta_{n}\right\}$ and those of $A$. Consider the sample as a point in the product $\mathscr{X}^{2 n}$ of $2 n$ copies of $\mathscr{X}$. Assign to each sample its $\mathscr{S}$-pattern defined as the ordered family of subsets of $A$ corresponding to sets of the form $S \cap\left\{\xi_{1}, \xi_{2}, \ldots, \xi_{n}, \eta_{1}, \ldots, \eta_{n}\right\}$.

Let us assume that this pattern map is measurable. Then one can decompose the set $\mathscr{X}^{2 n}$ into a finite family of measurable sets, each corresponding to a particular pattern. One can argue conditionally on each subset, and the condition (A) is satisfied.

For the condition (B) the situation is rather more complex. Dudley has given conditions involving Suslin sets. We shall sketch an argument of this type. The proofs will not be given in great detail since the reader who is sophisticated
enough to worry about the measurability difficulties is probably quite able to fill in whatever is necessary. To start with, let us recall a result on images of Radon measures.

Definition. $A$ subset $A$ of a Hausdorff space is called $K$-analytic if there are compacts $K_{m, n}$ and a continuous map from $B=\bigcap_{m} \bigcup_{n} K_{m, n}$ onto $A$. The set $A$ is called a Suslin set if it is the continuous image of a Polish space.

It is easy to check that all Suslin sets are $K$-analytic. (They are images of metric $K_{\sigma, \delta}$.) We shall use below the Suslin assumption. The reason for mentioning the $K$-analytic sets is that being the continuous image of a $K_{\sigma, \delta}$ is the property at the core of the proofs of measurability and of the proofs of our Proposition 3 below.

Proposition 2. Let $\mathscr{X}$ and $\mathscr{Y}$ be two Hausdorff spaces. Let $Q$ be a probability measure which is a Radon measure on $\mathscr{Y}$ and let $f$ be a continuous map of $\mathscr{X}$ onto $\mathscr{Y}$. Then there is a Radon probability $P$ on $\mathscr{X}$ such that $Q$ is the image of $P$ by $f$ if and only if $Q$ is carried by a countable union of images by $f$ of compacts of $\mathscr{X}$.

To prove this, one considers first the case where both $\mathscr{X}$ and $\mathscr{Y}$ are compact. In this case the map $f$ induces a positive linear map $T$ of $C(\mathscr{Y})$ into $C(\mathscr{X})$. The
map is injective. One defines $P$ on the image of $C(\mathscr{Y})$ by $\langle u, P\rangle=\langle v, Q\rangle$ if $u=T v$ and one extends it by the Hahn-Banach theorem.

For the general case one takes increasing compacts $K_{n}^{\prime}$, images $K_{n}^{\prime}=f\left(K_{n}\right)$ of compacts of $\mathscr{X}$, such that $Q\left(K_{n}^{\prime}\right)>1-2^{-n}$. Restricting $Q$ to $K_{n}^{\prime}$ one applies the previous method. This gives a sequence, say $\left\{M_{n}\right\}$, of positive measures such that $\left\|f\left(M_{n}\right)-Q\right\| \leq 2^{-n}$. One takes for $P$ any cluster point of the sequence $\left\{M_{n}\right\}$.

Proposition 3. Let $\mathscr{X}$ and $\mathscr{Y}$ be $K$-analytic and let $f$ be a continuous map of $\mathscr{X}$ onto $\mathscr{Y}$. Then every Radon probability $Q$ on $\mathscr{Y}$ is the image of a Radon probability $P$ on $\mathscr{X}$. All the $K$-analytic subsets of $\mathscr{Y}$ are $Q$-measurable.

Proof. Write $\mathscr{Y}=\phi(B)$ where $B$ is a $K_{\sigma, \delta}$, that is, $B=\bigcap_{m} \bigcup_{n} K_{m, n}$ for certain compacts $K_{m, n}$. One can assume that $K_{m, n} \subset K_{m, n+1}$ for all pairs ( $m, n)$. Then, for each $m$, the images $\phi\left(B \cap K_{m, n}\right)$ form an increasing sequence with union $\mathscr{Y}$. Given an $\varepsilon>0$, find an integer $n(1)$ such that the closure $D_{1}$ of $\phi\left[B \cap K_{1, n(1)}\right]$ has $Q$ measure $Q\left(D_{1}\right)>1-\varepsilon$. Now one can find an integer $n(2)$ such that if $D_{2}$ is the closure of $\phi\left[B \cap K_{1, n(1)} \cap K_{2, n(2)}\right]$ then $Q\left(D_{2}\right)>1-\varepsilon$. Continuing, one gets a sequence $C_{m}=K_{1, n(1)} \cap K_{2, n(2)} \cap \cdots \cap K_{m, n(m)}$ such that if $D_{m}$ is the closure of $\phi\left(B \cap C_{m}\right)$ then $Q\left(D_{m}\right)>1-\varepsilon$.

Now consider $A=\bigcap_{m} C_{m}$. It is a compact subset of $B$ and $\phi(A) \subset \bigcap_{m} D_{m}$. Let $V$ be a closed neighborhood of $\phi(A)$ in $\mathscr{Y}$. Then $\phi^{-1}(V)$ is a closed neighborhood of $A$ in $B$. If $\mathscr{Y}_{0}$ is the space containing the $K_{m, n}$, one can write $\phi^{-1}(V)=W \cap B$ for a certain neighborhood $W$ of $A$ in $\mathscr{Y}_{0}$. The $C_{m}$ being compact and decreasing, there is an $m$ such that $C_{m} \subset W$ and therefore $\phi\left(C_{m} \cap B\right) \subset V$. Since $V$ is closed, the closure $D_{m}$ of $\phi\left(C_{m} \cap B\right)$ is such that $D_{m} \subset V$. One concludes that $\bigcap_{n} D_{m} \subset V$ and therefore that $\bigcap_{m} D_{m} \subset \phi(A)$ since $\phi(A)$ being compact is the intersection of its closed neighborhoods. Thus



$\phi(A)=\bigcap_{m} D_{m}$ is such that $Q[\phi(A)] \geq 1-\varepsilon$. The existence of the measure $P$ is then a consequence of Proposition 2.

The measurability of $K$-analytic sets is demonstrable by the same argument. If $S \subset \mathscr{X}$ is $K$-analytic one can find a compact $K \subset S$ such that $Q(K) \geq Q^{*}(S)-\varepsilon$.

Returning to the processes $Z$ and $Z^{\prime}$, let us assume that $Z$ is defined on a Hausdorff space $\Omega$ carrying a Radon measure $P$. Let ( $Z^{\prime}, \Omega^{\prime}, P^{\prime}$ ) be a copy of the system ( $Z, \Omega, P$ ). Assume that the family $\mathscr{S}$ is also a Hausdorff space, and consider the assumption.

Assumption (S). For each $x \geq 0$, the set of pairs $(\omega, S) \in \Omega \times \mathscr{S}$ such that $Z(S) \geq x$ is a Suslin set. The same holds for the sets $\{Z(S) \leq-x\}$.

Lemma 8. If Assumption ( $S$ ) holds, then

$$
P\{\sup [Z(S) ; S \in \mathscr{S}] \geq x+1\} \leq 2 P\left\{\sup \left[Z(S)-Z^{\prime}(S)\right] \geq x\right\}
$$

Proof. Let $A$ be the set of pairs $(\omega, S)$ such that $Z(S) \geq x+1$. Its projection $B$ on $\Omega$ is a Suslin set. Suppose that $P(B)>0$ and let $\mu_{1}$ be the restriction of $P$ to $B$. Let $\mu$ be the probability measure $\mu=\left\|\mu_{1}\right\|^{-1} \mu$. According to Proposition 3, there is a probability measure $M$ on $A$ with projection $\mu$ on $B$. This gives a joint distribution $M \times P^{\prime}$ on $A \times \Omega^{\prime}$. Consider the difference $Z^{*}(S)-Z^{\prime}(S)$ where $Z^{*}$ is the function $Z$ used before, restricted to $A$, and given the distribution induced by $M$. The set $Z^{*}(S)-Z^{\prime}(S) \geq x$ in $A \times \Omega^{\prime}$ contains the set $V=\left\{(\omega, S) \in A, Z^{\prime}(S) \leq 1\right\}$. This set is measurable. Conditionally given ( $\omega, S$ ), the probability that $Z^{\prime}(S) \leq 1$ is at least $1 / 2$ since the median of $Z^{\prime}(S)$ is in $[-1,+1]$. Thus $\left(M \times P^{\prime}\right)(V) \geq 1 / 2$. Equivalently $\left(\mu \times P^{\prime}\right)(V) \geq \frac{1}{2}\|\mu\|$. Hence the result.

One can extend the domain of application of Lemma 8 by eliminating one set of measure zero in the probability space $\Omega$. Indeed, let $\Omega^{\prime \prime} \subset \Omega$ have probability unity. Let $\mathscr{S}^{\prime \prime}=\left\{S \cap \Omega^{\prime \prime} ; S \in \mathscr{S}\right\}$. If the system ( $\Omega^{\prime \prime}, \mathscr{S}^{\prime \prime}$ ) has property (S), the conclusion of the lemma still holds.

The assumption called (S) implies that both $\Omega$ and $\mathscr{S}$ are Suslin sets. Thus if $\Omega=\mathscr{X}^{n}$, the space $\mathscr{X}$ itself is Suslin. One can give conditions on the component measures $p_{j}$ implying Assumption (S). A possibility, used by Dudley, is as follows.

Definition. Call a system $\{\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{A}, p, \mathscr{S}\}$ a $(p, \in)$-Suslin system if
(1) both $\mathscr{X}$ and $\mathscr{S}$ are Suslin,
(2) the graph in $\mathscr{X} \times \mathscr{S}$ of the relation ( $x \in S$ ) is Borel,
(3) $\mathscr{A}$ is the $\sigma$-field generated by the Suslin sets,
(4) the function $S \mapsto p(S)$ is Borel.

The proof that if the $\left(\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{A}, p_{j}, \mathscr{S}\right)$ are $(p, \in)$-Suslin, then Assumption (S) holds, is fairly simple. It will be left to the reader.

One could have used " $K$-analytic" instead of "Suslin" everywhere, but then "Borel" would have to be replaced by $K$-analytic or something similar. Note, however, that under the Suslin-type hypotheses one can reduce the entire problem to one where $\mathscr{X}$ is the unit interval with nonatomic measures.

Indeed, let $\mathscr{X}$ be a continuous image of a Polish space $T$. By Proposition 3 one can regard $p_{j}$ as image of a probability measure $q_{j}$ on $T$. This $T$ itself is homeomorphic to a $G_{\delta}$ of the space $K=[0,1]^{\mathbb{N}}$ product of a countable family of intervals $[0,1]$. Thus one can look at $q_{j}$ as if it were a measure on $K$. One can also represent $K$ as continuous image of the Cantor set and therefore consider $q_{j}$ as image of a measure on $[0,1]$ carried by the Cantor set. One can replace atoms by intervals whose length is the size of the atom, with Lebesgue measure on these intervals. The family $\mathscr{S}$ is transferred to [ 0,1 ] by inverse images. This remark may be relevant to some of the arguments which follow.

The argument of Theorem 1 uses not only the class $\mathscr{S}$ but also certain classes $\mathscr{S}_{\alpha}{ }^{*}$ derived from $\mathscr{S}$. For these the following remarks may be useful.

Assume temporarily that $\mathscr{X}$ is a compact metric space. Metrize the space $\mathscr{F}(\mathscr{X})$ of its closed subsets by the Hausdorff distance. Then $\mathscr{F}(\mathscr{X})$ is a compact metric space. In the product $\mathscr{X} \times \mathscr{F}(\mathscr{X})$ the graph of the relation $(x \in S)$ is closed. For any probability measure $p$, the map $S \mapsto p(S)$ is upper semicontinuous on $\mathscr{F}(\mathscr{X})$.

Lemma 9. Let $\mathscr{S}$ be a subset of $\mathscr{F}(\mathscr{X})$. Assume that $\mathscr{S}$ topologized by the Hausdorff metric is Suslin. Let $A$ be an arbitrary p -measurable subset of $\mathscr{X}$. Let $\mathscr{S}^{\prime}=\{S \cap A ; S \in \mathscr{S}\}$. Then the system ( $A, \mathscr{S}^{\prime}, p$ ) is p-equivalent to a ( $p, \in)$-Suslin system.

Proof. Eliminating a set of measure zero, one can assume that $A$ is Borel, or even that it is a $K_{\sigma}$. Consider the map $S \rightarrow S^{\prime}=S \cap A$ from $\mathscr{S}$ to $\mathscr{S}^{\prime}$. It induces on $\mathscr{S}^{\prime}$ the topology quotient of that of $\mathscr{S}$. To each $S^{\prime}$ one can assign its closure $\overline{S^{\prime}}$ in $\mathscr{X}$. The map $S^{\prime} \mapsto \overline{S^{\prime}}$ is continuous. Thus the topology of $\mathscr{S}^{\prime}$ is a Hausdorff topology. Hence $\mathscr{S}^{\prime}$ is Suslin. The same argument applies also to the product $\mathscr{X} \times \mathscr{S}$ mapped into $\mathscr{X} \times \mathscr{S}^{\prime}$. Thus, the intersection $\left(A \times \mathscr{S}^{\prime}\right) \cap\left(\mathscr{X} \times \mathscr{S}^{\prime}\right)$ is Suslin. The graph of ( $x \in S$ ) in $A \times \mathscr{S}^{\prime}$ remains closed. Hence the result.

For the classes $\mathscr{S}_{\alpha}^{*}$, consider the finite set $\mathscr{F}_{\alpha}$ and an $F \in \mathscr{F}_{\alpha}$. Let $\mathscr{S}(F)$ be the class $\mathscr{S}(F)=\left\{S \cap F^{c} ; S \in \mathscr{S}\right\}$. One can apply Lemma 9 to each $\mathscr{S}(F)$. The result applies also to $\mathscr{S}_{\alpha}^{*}(F)$ which is the intersection of $\mathscr{S}(F)$ with the Borel set $\{S: p(S \Delta F) \leq \alpha\}$. Thus it applies to the finite union $\mathscr{S}_{\alpha}^{*}=\bigcup\left\{\mathscr{S}_{\alpha}(F) ; F \in \mathscr{F}_{\alpha}\right\}$.

From this one concludes that Lemma 8 will be applicable to any pair ( $\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{S}$ ) where (1) $\mathscr{X} \subset \mathscr{X}^{\prime}$, with $\mathscr{X}^{\prime}$ Polish and $\mathscr{X}$ universally measurable in $\mathscr{X}^{\prime}$, (2) the class $\mathscr{S}$ is a class of closed subsets of $\mathscr{X}$, (3) imbedding $\mathscr{X}^{\prime}$ in $K=[0,1]^{\mathbb{N}}$ one can give to $\mathscr{S}$ the quotient topology induced by the Hausdorff metric and $\mathscr{S}$ is Suslin for that topology.

Give two classes $\mathscr{S}_{1}$ and $\mathscr{S}_{2}$ with the required properties, one can also pass to classes such as $\mathscr{S}_{2}^{\prime}=\left\{S^{c} ; S \in \mathscr{S}_{2}\right\}$ by complementation and to classes such as $\left\{S_{1} \cap S_{2} ; S_{1} \in \mathscr{S}_{1}, S_{2} \in \mathscr{S}_{2}\right\}$ since the intersection operation is Borelian. To terminate, let us mention another usable result of Dudley.

Lemma 10. Let $\mathscr{X}$ be a locally compact separable metric space. Let $\Gamma$ be a class of continuous real functions defined on $\mathscr{X}$. Assume that $\Gamma$ carries a $\sigma$-field $\mathscr{B}$ for which ( $\Gamma, \mathscr{B}$ ) is a Suslin space and for which the maps $\gamma \mapsto \gamma(x)$ are measurable. Let $\mathscr{S}$ be a class of sets of the form $S=\{x ; \gamma(x)=0\}$. Then
$\mathscr{S}$ is a Suslin subspace of the space $\mathscr{F}$ of closed sets of $\mathscr{X}$.
Proof. It is sufficient to show that the map from $\Gamma$ to $\mathscr{F}$ defined by $S(\gamma)= \{x: \gamma(x)=0\}$ is measurable. For this it is sufficient to show that for each $F \in \mathscr{F}$ the set $\{y: S(y) \subset F\}$ is measurable. Let $V$ be the complement of $F$ and let $K_{n} ; n=1,2, \ldots$ be a countable family of compact sets such that $\bigcup_{n} K_{n}=V$. For each $K_{n}$ let $D_{n}$ be a countable dense set in $K_{n}$. Then $S(\gamma) \subset F$ if and only if for
every $n$ there is an integer $m$ such that $|\gamma(x)|>1 / m$ for all $x \in D_{n}$. This gives the desired result.

The symmetrization procedure used in this section has been used by $\mathbf{D}$. Pollard to obtain central limit theorems for empirical measures on certain classes of functions. Since Pollard himself has published an excellent and very readable text on the subject, we shall not enter into any details. In addition, there is a recent article of E. Giné and J. Zinn that reviews the present state of the matter. It should, however, be pointed out that Pollard, or Giné and Zinn consider only classes of functions that do not vary as the number of observations vary. It is possible to adapt their methods to the more general case. For recent information on classes of functions for which the central limit theorem holds, see the work of R.M. Dudley (1985).

## CHAPTER 17

## Independent Identically Distributed Observations

## 1. Introduction

The situation that has been most extensively studied in asymptotic statistics is one where the statistician deals with " $n$ independent identically distributed observations."

Without any further qualification this can be translated as follows: One has $n$ experiments $\mathscr{E}_{j}=\left\{P_{\theta, j} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}, j=1,2, \ldots, n$, each equivalent to $\mathscr{E}_{1}$ and one considers the direct product $\mathscr{E}$ of the $\mathscr{E}_{j}$.

To refer to this situation in a simple manner we shall say either that $\mathscr{E}$ is an $n$th power or that $\mathscr{E}$ is $n$-divisible. Here the experiments $\mathscr{E}_{j}$ may well depend on $n$. They could be of the form $\mathscr{E}_{j, n}=\left\{P_{\theta, j, n} ; \theta \in \Theta_{n}\right\}$ with everything including $\Theta_{n}$ varying as $n$ varies.

The situation most commonly described in the literature is much more restricted. One considers a fixed experiment $\mathscr{E}_{1}=\left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$, takes its $n$th power, and lets $n$ tend to infinity. This is the standard i.i.d. case. It is the main subject of study in this chapter. However, sometimes, and especially in Section 2 below, we have treated the case of general variable $n$th powers. It is true that the results available there do not go much beyond those of the more general direct product case of Chapter 16. The main difference, visible in Section 2, is a special representation of the Hilbert spaces generated by Hellinger distances.

The standard i.i.d. case is imposed on us by tradition and mathematical tractability. Although $n$th powers, with $n$ large, do occur in practical situations, the technique of proving limit theorems "as $n$ tends to infinity" for powers $\mathscr{E}_{1}^{n}$ of a fixed experiment $\mathscr{E}_{1}$ has little bearing on anything of practical import. Powers $\mathscr{E}_{n}^{n}$ of experiments $\mathscr{E}_{n}$ that vary with $n$ could be studied to suggest approximation theorems that might be of more value. However, since it is the standard i.i.d. case with its quaint concepts, such as "consistency," that occupies so much of the literature, we have devoted this chapter to it in order to illustrate the applicability of the general ideas of the present volume.

The specific contents of this chapter are as follows. Section 2 studies the local behavior of $n$th powers, using Hilbert spaces of square integrable functions and giving theorem about asymptotic normality. The only result in it that is intended only for the standard i.i.d. case is Lemma 6.

Section 3 studies in more detail the standard i.i.d. case where the parameter space $\Theta$ is a subset of a Euclidean space $\mathbb{R}^{k}$. The rate of convergence $\sqrt{n}$ plays a special role and so does a related condition of differentiability in quadratic mean.

Section 4 is a supplement to Section 3, indicating that asymptotic normality of experiments, with a linear indexing by $\mathbb{R}^{k}$, can occur for rates other than the standard $\sqrt{n}$, but that it occurs only for rates of the type $\sqrt{n} f(n)$ where $f$ is slowly varying.

Section 5 gives necessary and sufficient conditions for the existence of consistent estimates. Section 6 describes the use of empirical cumulatives to construct estimates that are consistent at the $\sqrt{n}$ rate. It also contains some remarks about the work of P.W. Millar on the "robustness' of such estimates.

The section ends with a brief description of a procedure introduced by $R$. Beran to construct efficient robust estimates. Section 7 is on the consistency and asymptotic normality of Bayes estimates. It shows that in nicely parameterized situations Bayes estimates are consistent, but it also contains a result of $D$. Freedman showing that the infinite dimensional case presents problems. It ends on a version of the Bernstein-von Mises theorem according to which posterior distributions tend to look Gaussian.

Section 8 is intended to show that maximum likelihood estimates can be very badly behaved. Section 9 extends some of the previous results to the case where the number $n$ of observations is random.

We had intended to include some description of the available results on higher order expansions, but this necessitates too much space and will not be found here.

## 2. Hilbert Spaces Around a Point

In this section we consider experiments $\mathscr{E}_{n}=\left\{p_{\theta, n} ; \theta \in \Theta_{n}\right\}$ and their $n$th power $\mathscr{E}_{n}^{n}=\left\{P_{\theta, n} ; \theta \in \Theta_{n}\right\}$, where $P_{\theta, n}$ is the direct product of $n$ copies of $p_{\theta, n}$. We first describe certain Hilbert spaces attached to $\mathscr{E}_{n}^{n}$. Certain of these spaces admit here particularly simple and concrete representations. We proceed to
study conditions for approximability by Gaussian experiments and conclude with a few lemmas on local behavior in the Hilbert spaces.

Theorem 1 of Chapter 11, Section 6, suggests the introduction of Hilbert spaces $\mathscr{H}_{n}$ as follows. Let $K_{n}^{*}(s, t)=-8 \log \int \sqrt{d P_{s, n} d P_{t, n}}$. Let $K_{n}$ be a kernel of positive type which is an approximation to $K_{n}^{*}$.

Let $\mathscr{M}_{0}\left(\Theta_{n}\right)$ be the space of finite signed measures $\mu$ with finite support in $\Theta_{n}$ and total algebraic mass $\mu\left(\Theta_{n}\right)=0$. Define a quadratic form $\Gamma_{n}$ by

$$
\Gamma_{n}(\mu)=-\frac{1}{2} \iint K_{n}(s, t) \mu(d s) \mu(d t)
$$

After identifying to zero those $\mu \in \mathscr{M}_{0}\left(\Theta_{0}\right)$ such that $\Gamma_{n}(\mu)=0$, one completes $\mathscr{M}_{0}\left(\Theta_{0}\right)$ for the norm $\sqrt{\Gamma_{n}(\mu)}$ and obtains that way a Hilbert space $\mathscr{H}_{n}$. Here a good candidate for the role of $K_{n}$ is $8 n h_{n}^{2}$, where $h_{n}^{2}(s, t)$ is the square Hellinger distance

$$
h_{n}^{2}(s, t)=\frac{1}{2} \int\left(\sqrt{d p_{s, n}}-\sqrt{d p_{t, n}}\right)^{2}
$$

The same Hilbert space is customarily described in another manner as follows. Assume that the $p_{\theta, n}$ are measures on a $\sigma$-field $\mathscr{A}_{n}$. Consider pairs $(\xi, \lambda)$ that consist of a positive finite measure $\lambda$ on $\mathscr{A}_{n}$ and of a measurable function $\xi$ such that $\int \xi^{2} d \lambda<\infty$. Identify two pairs ( $\xi_{1}, \lambda_{1}$ ) and ( $\xi_{2}, \lambda_{2}$ ) if $\int\left(\xi_{1} \sqrt{d \lambda_{1}}-\xi_{2} \sqrt{d \lambda_{2}}\right)^{2}=0$. Describe the equivalence classes so obtained by symbols of the type $\xi \sqrt{d \lambda}$ and use a square norm equal to $\|\xi \sqrt{d \lambda}\|_{n}^{2}= 4 n \int \xi^{2} d \lambda$. For two probability measures $p_{s, n}$ and $p_{t, n}$ on $\mathscr{A}_{n}$ one will have $\left\|\sqrt{d p_{s, n}}-\sqrt{d p_{t, n}}\right\|_{n}^{2}=8 n h_{n}^{2}(s, t)$ so that the Hilbert space $\mathscr{H}_{n}$ described previously can be imbedded isometrically in the space of symbols $\xi \sqrt{d \lambda}$.

When considering probability measures that are absolutely continuous with respect to a particular $p$ on $\mathscr{A}_{n}$ it is more convenient to introduce a different Hilbert space as follows. Let $\mathscr{L}_{2,0}(p)$ be the space of $\mathscr{A}_{n}$-measurable functions such that $\int u d p=0$ and $\int u^{2} d p=\|u\|^{2}<\infty$. Let $L_{2,0}(p)$ be the Hilbert space of their equivalence classes. For any $u \in \mathscr{L}_{2,0}(p)$ such that $\|u\| \leq 2 \sqrt{n}$, one can define a probability measure $p_{u, n}$ by

$$
d p_{u, n}=\left[1-c\left(\frac{\|u\|}{2 \sqrt{n}}\right)+\frac{u}{2 \sqrt{n}}\right]^{2} d p
$$

where $x \mapsto c(x)$ is the function on $[0,1]$ to $[0,1]$ defined by $[1-c(x)]^{2}+x^{2}=1$.
Conversely, any probability measure $q$ dominated by $p$ can be represented in the form $p_{u, n}$ for a $u \in \mathscr{L}_{2,0}(p)$ such that $\|u\| \leq 2 \sqrt{n}$ and $1-c(\|u\| / 2 \sqrt{n})+ (u / 2 \sqrt{n}) \geq 0$.

Any $u \in \mathscr{L}_{2,0}(p)$ that satisfies these two conditions will be termed eligible at $n$. If $u$ and $v$ are two eligible elements of $\mathscr{L}_{2,0}(p)$, the Hellinger distance $h_{n}(u, v)$ between the corresponding measures $p_{u, n}$ and $p_{v, n}$ is given by

$$
h_{n}^{2}(u, v)=\frac{1}{2}\left[c\left(\frac{\|v\|}{2 \sqrt{n}}\right)-c\left(\frac{\|u\|}{2 \sqrt{n}}\right)\right]^{2}+\frac{1}{8 n}\|u-v\|^{2} .
$$

Note that the function $c$ satisfies the relation $2 \mathrm{c}(x)=x^{2}+c^{2}(x)$. Thus, for $x$ small, $c(x)$ will behave approximately as $x^{2} / 2$. This implies, for instance, that if $\|u\|+\|v\|$ stays bounded then $\|u-v\|^{2}$ will behave in the same manner as $8 n h_{n}^{2}(u, v)$ as $n \rightarrow \infty$. It follows also that if $u$ and $v$ are eligible at $n$ and if $\min (\|u\|,\|v\|)<b \sqrt{n}$ for a $b<2$, then

$$
n h_{n}^{2}(u, v) \leq\left(\frac{1}{8}+\frac{2}{4-b^{2}}\right)\|u-v\|^{2} .
$$

The measures $p_{u, n}$ do not actually depend on $u$ as elements of $\mathscr{L}_{2,0}(p)$ but only on its equivalence class in $L_{2,0}(p)$. For notational purposes it is inconvenient to distinguish between a function and its equivalence class. Thus we shall abuse the notation very liberally except when distinctions become necessary. Thus, if $u \in L_{2,0}(p)$ is such that $\|u\| \leq 2 \sqrt{n}$, we shall let

$$
d p_{u, n}=\left[1-c\left(\frac{\|u\|}{2 \sqrt{n}}\right)+\frac{u}{2 \sqrt{n}}\right]^{2} d p
$$

and let $P_{u, n}$ be the corresponding direct product of $n$ copies of $p_{u, n}$.
It will be seen that the use of $\mathscr{H}_{n}$ cannot be avoided for global arguments, but that $L_{2,0}(p)$ is very convenient locally, mostly because of the normalization $\int u d p=0$. The following lemma is intended to show that the part of $L_{2,0}(p)$ that is eligible at $n$ can already provide many experiments of the type $\left\{P_{v, n}: v \in S \subset L_{2,0}(p)\right\}$.

Lemma 1. Assume that $p$ is nonatomic. Let $\mathscr{F}=\left\{Q_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ be an experiment subject to the following restrictions:
(i) There is a $t \in \Theta$ such that all the $Q_{\theta}$ are dominated by $Q_{t}$,
(ii) $\mathscr{F}$ is $n$-divisible,
(iii) the family $\left\{Q_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ is separable for the $L_{1}$-norm.

Then there is a map $\theta \mapsto v(\theta) \in L_{2,0}(p)$ such that $v(\theta)$ is eligible at $n$ and such that the product experiment $\mathscr{E}=\left\{P_{v(\theta), n} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ is equivalent to $\mathscr{F}$.

Proof. Let $\mathscr{F}_{n}=\left\{q_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ be such that its $n$th power is equivalent to $\mathscr{F}$. Take a countable dense set $\left\{q_{j} ; j \in J\right\}$. With $t \in J$ consider the joint distribution of the variables

$$
X_{j}=2 \sqrt{n}\left\{\sqrt{\frac{d q_{j}}{d q_{t}}}-\int \sqrt{d q_{j} d q_{t}}\right\}
$$

for the measure $q_{t}$.
Since $p$ is nonatomic, the $\sigma$-field $\mathscr{A}_{n}$ contains a sub- $\sigma$-field, say $\mathscr{B}$, on which $p$ is isomorphic to the Lebesgue measure $\lambda$ on the Borel subsets of $[0,1]$. On the Lebesgue space $\{[0,1], \lambda\}$ one can find random variables $\left\{v_{j} ; j \in J\right\}$ whose joint distribution is precisely the same as that of the $X_{j}$. Thus, this is also possible on $\left\{\mathscr{A}_{n}, p\right\}$. For arbitrary $\theta \in \Theta$, the corresponding variable $X_{\theta}$ is a limit in quadratic mean of a subsequence of the $X_{j}$. One takes the corresponding limit of the $v_{j}$ for $v(\theta)$. This gives the desired result.

The foregoing lemma shows that, for instance, all separable homogenous infinitely divisible experiments have representations of the form $\left\{P_{v, n} ; v \in S\right\}$ with $S \subset L_{2,0}(p)$ containing only eligible elements of $L_{2,0}(p)$.

On the other hand, it should be noted that the positivity requirement $1- c(\|v\| / 2 \sqrt{n})+(v / 2 \sqrt{n}) \geq 0$ will always exclude a sizeable portion of the unit ball of $L_{2,0}(p)$, at least if $p$ is nonatomic. To see this, it is enough again to use the Lebesgue space $\{[0,1], \lambda\}$. On this space define $u_{n}$ by $u_{n} \sqrt{2}=2^{n}$ on $\left(0,2^{-2 n}\right]$ and $u_{n} \sqrt{2}=-2^{n}$ on $\left(2^{-2 n}, 2^{-2 n+1}\right]$. Here $u_{n} \in L_{2,0}(\lambda),\left\|u_{n}\right\|=1$. However, any $v_{n}$ eligible at $n$ must be such that

$$
\left\|u_{n}-v_{n}\right\| \geq \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\left(1-\frac{2 \sqrt{n}}{2^{n}}\right)
$$

This is so in spite of the fact that if $p$ is fixed on a fixed $\mathscr{A}_{n}$ independent of $n$, any fixed $v$ in the unit ball of $L_{2,0}(p)$ is the limit of a sequence $\left\{u_{n}\right\}$ with $u_{n}$ eligible at $n$. In this connection it is convenient to use the following terminology, usable whether $p$ is fixed or not. Call two sequences $\left\{u_{n}\right\}$ and $\left\{v_{n}\right\}$ in $L_{2,0}(p)$ asymptotically equivalent if $\left\|u_{n}-v_{n}\right\| \rightarrow 0$ as $n \rightarrow \infty$.

Asymptotically equivalent sequences enjoy the following property. Let $\left\{\left(U_{n, j}, V_{n, j}\right) ; j=1,2, \ldots, n\right\}$ be independent replicates of the pair $\left(u_{n}, v_{n}\right)$. Let $S_{n}=(1 / \sqrt{n}) \sum_{j=1}^{n} U_{n, j}$ and $T_{n}=(1 / \sqrt{n}) \sum_{j=1}^{n} V_{n, j}$. Then $E\left|S_{n}-T_{n}\right|^{2}=\left\|u_{n}-v_{n}\right\|^{2}$ will tend to zero. Thus $S_{n}$ and $T_{n}$ have the same asymptotic behavior as far as distributions and second moments are concerned.

For instance, if the $U_{n, j}$ satisfy a Lindeberg condition, so do the $V_{n, j}$. Since, for ( $p, \mathscr{A}_{n}$ ) independent of $n$, fixed elements of $L_{2,0}(p)$ satisfy the Lindeberg condition, so do Cauchy sequences (for the $L_{2}$-norm).

The classical statistical literature shows a strong tendency to consider as specially important the case where ( $p, \mathscr{A}_{n}$ ) is independent of $n$ and where the elements $u_{n} \in L_{2,0}(p)$ converge. According to the preceding remark this will often lead to asymptotic normality. In this respect note the following result that is valid whether or $\operatorname{not}\left(p, \mathscr{A}_{n}\right)$ is fixed.

Take replicates $\left(p_{j}, \mathscr{A}_{n, j}\right), j=1,2, \ldots, n$, of $\left(p, \mathscr{A}_{n}\right)$. On $\mathscr{A}_{n, j}$ let $p_{v, n, j}$ be the measure defined by

$$
d p_{v, n, j}=\left[1-c\left(\frac{\left\|v_{n, j}\right\|}{2 \sqrt{n}}\right)+\frac{v_{n, j}}{2 \sqrt{n}}\right]^{2} d p_{j}
$$

where the $v_{n, j}$ are replicates of a $v_{n} \in L_{2,0}(p)$ with $\left\|v_{n}\right\| \leq 2 \sqrt{n}$. Consider the corresponding product measures $P_{v_{n}, n}$.

Lemma 2. Assume that $v_{n}$ is eligible at $n$. Then the binary experiments $\left\{P_{0, n}, P_{v_{n}, n}\right\}$ admit a Gaussian shift approximation that remains away from a perfect experiment if and only if
(i) $\sup \left\|v_{n}\right\|<\infty$,
(ii) the $v_{n}$ satisfy a Lindeberg condition in the sense that for every $\varepsilon>0$,

$$
\int v_{n}^{2} I\left[\left|v_{n}\right|>\varepsilon \sqrt{n}\right] d p \rightarrow 0
$$

The conditions (i) and (ii) remain sufficient to insure the Gaussian approximability if the eligibility condition is removed. In any event the difference $\log \left(d P_{v_{n}, n} / d P_{0, n}\right)-\left\{(1 / \sqrt{n}) \sum_{j=1}^{n} v_{n, j}-\frac{1}{2}\left\|v_{n}\right\|^{2}\right\}$ tends to zero in probability.

This is an immediate consequence of the results in Chapter 16, Section 3. The Lindeberg condition (ii) above can also be written in a different form that is sometimes more convenient.

Consider a $v \in L_{2,0}(p)$ with $\|v\| \leq 2 \sqrt{n}$. The corresponding measure $p_{v, n}$ has a density with respect to $p$ that satisfies

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{d p_{v, n}}{d p} & =\left[1-c\left(\frac{\|v\|}{2 \sqrt{n}}\right)+\frac{v}{2 \sqrt{n}}\right]^{2} \\
& \leq 2\left[1-c\left(\frac{\|v\|}{2 \sqrt{n}}\right)\right]^{2}+\frac{v^{2}}{2 n}
\end{aligned}
$$

Thus,

$$
n p_{v, n}\{|v| \geq \varepsilon \sqrt{n}\} \leq 2 n p[|v| \geq \varepsilon \sqrt{n}]+\frac{1}{2} \int v^{2} I(|v| \geq \varepsilon \sqrt{n}) d p
$$

Also, by Chebyshev's inequality

$$
n p[|v| \geq \varepsilon \sqrt{n}] \leq \frac{1}{\varepsilon^{2}} \int v^{2} I[|v| \geq \varepsilon \sqrt{n}] d p
$$

It follows that condition (ii) implies
(iii) For every $\varepsilon>0$, the quantity

$$
n\left(p_{v_{n}, n}+p\right)\{|v| \geq \varepsilon \sqrt{n}\}
$$

tends to zero as $n \rightarrow \infty$.
It will be shown below, as part of the proof of Lemma 3, that (iii) implies (ii). (Condition (iii) is more symmetrical than (ii), and can be reinterpreted to cover probability measures that have a part singular with respect to $p$.)

Lemma 3. Let $v \in L_{2,0}(p)$ be such that $0 \leq 1-c(\|v\| / 2 \sqrt{n})+(v / 2 \sqrt{n})$ and $n\left(p+p_{v, n}\right)\{|v|>\varepsilon \sqrt{n}\}<\varepsilon$ for an $\varepsilon \leq \frac{1}{4}$. Let $v^{\prime}=v I[|v| \leq \varepsilon \sqrt{n}]$ and let $v^{*}=v^{\prime}-\int v^{\prime} d p$. Then the corresponding product measures $P_{v, n}$ and $P_{v *, n}$ satisfy the inequality $\left\|P_{v^{*}, n}-P_{v, n}\right\|<2 \sqrt{2 \varepsilon}$.

Proof. Since $\left|v^{*}\right| \leq 2 \varepsilon \sqrt{n}$ one has $\left\|v^{*}\right\| \leq 2 \varepsilon \sqrt{n}$. This gives

$$
1-c\left[\frac{\left\|v^{*}\right\|}{2 \sqrt{n}}\right]=\left\{1-\frac{\left\|v^{*}\right\|}{4 n}\right\}^{1 / 2} \geq\left(1-\varepsilon^{2}\right)^{1 / 2}
$$

Thus

$$
1-c\left[\frac{\left\|v^{*}\right\|}{2 \sqrt{n}}\right]+\frac{v^{*}}{2 \sqrt{n}} \geq\left(1-\varepsilon^{2}\right)^{1 / 2}-\varepsilon>0 \quad \text { if } \varepsilon \in\left[0, \frac{1}{2}\right]
$$

Thus $v^{*}$ is eligible. If so, the Hellinger distance $h$ between the individual distributions is given by

$$
n h^{2}=\frac{1}{8} \int\left(v-v^{*}\right)^{2} d p+\frac{n}{2}\left\{c\left(\frac{\|v\|}{2 \sqrt{n}}\right)-c\left(\frac{\left\|v^{*}\right\|}{2 \sqrt{n}}\right)\right\}^{2}
$$

Now

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int\left(v-v^{*}\right)^{2} d p & \leq \int\left(v-v^{\prime}\right)^{2} d p \\
& =\int v^{2} I[v<-\varepsilon \sqrt{n}] d p+\int v^{2} I[v>\varepsilon \sqrt{n}] d p
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $v / 2 \sqrt{n} \geq-1$, the first integral is bounded by $4 n p\{v<-\varepsilon \sqrt{n}\}$. For the second, note that, in the range considered, $\left(d p_{v, n} / d p\right) \geq\left(v^{2} / 4 n\right)$. Thus the second integral is bounded by $4 n p_{v, n}\{v>\varepsilon \sqrt{n}\}$. Therefore the first term in the expression of $n h^{2}$ is at most

$$
\frac{1}{2} n\left\{p[|v|>\varepsilon \sqrt{n}]+p_{v, n}[|v|>\varepsilon \sqrt{n}]\right\} \leq \frac{1}{2} \varepsilon
$$

Note also, for further use, the bound $\left\|v-v^{*}\right\|^{2} \leq 4 \varepsilon$ and therefore $\|v\| \leq 2 \varepsilon \sqrt{n}+2 \sqrt{\varepsilon}$.

For the second term in $n h^{2}$, note that

$$
\begin{aligned}
c\left[\frac{\|v\|}{2 \sqrt{n}}\right]-c\left[\frac{\left\|v^{*}\right\|}{2 \sqrt{n}}\right] & =\left[1-\frac{\|v\|^{2}}{4 n}\right]^{1 / 2}-\left[1-\frac{\left\|v^{*}\right\|^{2}}{4 n}\right]^{1 / 2} \\
& =\frac{1}{4 n}\left[\left\|v^{*}\right\|^{2}-\|v\|^{2}\right]\left\{\left[1-\frac{\|v\|^{2}}{4 n}\right]^{1 / 2}\right. \\
& \left.+\left[1-\frac{\left\|v^{*}\right\|^{2}}{4 n}\right]^{1 / 2}\right\}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

The expression in the curly brackets is larger than the square root of

$$
2-\frac{\|v\|^{2}}{4 n}-\frac{\left\|v^{*}\right\|^{2}}{4 n} \geq 2-\varepsilon^{2}-\varepsilon\left(\sqrt{\varepsilon}+\frac{1}{\sqrt{n}}\right)^{2}
$$

For $0 \leq \varepsilon<\frac{1}{4}$, this larger than unity. Hence

$$
\left|c\left[\frac{\|v\|}{2 \sqrt{n}}\right]-c\left(\frac{\left\|v^{*}\right\|}{2 \sqrt{n}}\right)\right| \leq \frac{1}{4 n}\left\|v^{*}-v\right\|\left[\left\|v^{*}\right\|+\|v\|\right] .
$$

Finally,

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{n}{2}\left|c\left[\frac{\|v\|}{2 \sqrt{n}}\right]-c\left[\frac{\left\|v^{*}\right\|}{2 \sqrt{n}}\right]\right|^{2} & \leq \frac{1}{32 n}\left\|v^{*}-v\right\|^{2}\left[\left\|v^{*}\right\|+\|v\|\right]^{2} \\
& \leq \frac{1}{8 n} \varepsilon 16 \varepsilon n\left[\sqrt{\varepsilon}+\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{n}}\right]^{2} \\
& \leq 2 \varepsilon^{2} \leq \frac{1}{2} \varepsilon, \quad \text { if } 0<\varepsilon \leq \frac{1}{4} .
\end{aligned}
$$

This gives $n h^{2} \leq \varepsilon$. Therefore the Hellinger distance between $P_{v, n}$ and $P_{v^{*}, n}$ does not exceed $\varepsilon$. The $L_{1}$-norm is bounded by $(2 \sqrt{2}) \sqrt{\varepsilon}$, as claimed. Note that instead of the condition $n\left(p+p_{v, n}\right)[|v|>\varepsilon \sqrt{n}]<\varepsilon$, we could have used the condition $\int v^{2} I[|v|>\varepsilon \sqrt{n}] d p<4 \varepsilon$.

Our next lemma says that, for certain subsets $S$ of $L_{2,0}(p)$, one can replace the experiments $\left\{P_{v, n} ; v \in S\right\}$ by families of positive measures $\left\{Q_{v, n} ; v \in S\right\}$ for which the likelihood ratio structure is exactly the same as that of a Gaussian shift experiment. To make this explicit, let $X_{1}, X_{2}, \ldots X_{n}$ be independent identically distributed and let $Z_{n}$ be the empirical process defined by $Z_{n}(A)=(1 / \sqrt{n}) \sum_{j=1}^{n}\left[\delta_{X_{j}}(A)-p(A)\right]$. Let $\left\langle v, Z_{n}\right\rangle=\int v d Z_{n}$. Define $P_{v, n}$ as before and let $Q_{v, n}$ be the measure whose density with respect to $P_{0, n}$ is given by $\exp \left\{\left\langle v, Z_{n}\right\rangle-\frac{1}{2}\|v\|^{2}\right\}$.

Lemma 4. Let $\varepsilon \in\left[0, \frac{1}{4}\right]$ and $b \in[1, \infty)$ be fixed. Assume be $\varepsilon \leq 2$. Let $U_{n}(b, \varepsilon)$ be the set of $v \in L_{2,0}(p)$ that satisfy the following requirements:
(1) $\|v\|^{2} \leq b$,
(2) $|v / \sqrt{n}| \leq \varepsilon$.

Then for all $v \in U_{n}(b, \varepsilon)$ one has

$$
\left\|Q_{v, n}-P_{v, n}\right\| \leq K \sqrt{b \varepsilon}
$$

for a coefficient $K \leq 5$.
Remark. One can obtain an analogous result, with a coefficient $K$ that depends on $a$ and $b$ if Condition 2 above is replaced by the following set of requirements:
(i) $0 \leq 1-c[\|v\| / 2 \sqrt{n}]+v / 2 \sqrt{n}$,
(ii) $\int v^{2} I[|v|>\varepsilon \sqrt{n}] d p<\varepsilon$,
(iii) $n \int e^{v / \sqrt{ } n} I[v / \sqrt{n} \geq a] d p<\varepsilon$.

The computations are longer, but the principle of the proof is the same.
Proof. Consider the expression

$$
\exp \left\{\frac{v}{\sqrt{n}}\right\}=1+\frac{v}{\sqrt{n}}+\frac{1}{2} \frac{v^{2}}{n}+r_{n}(v)
$$

Taking an expectation under $p$, this yields

$$
\begin{aligned}
E \exp \left\{\frac{v}{\sqrt{n}}\right\} & =1+\frac{1}{2} \frac{\|v\|^{2}}{n}+E r_{n}(v) \\
& \leq \exp \left\{\frac{1}{2} \frac{\|v\|^{2}}{n}+E r_{n}(v)\right\}
\end{aligned}
$$



Taking a product, one obtains

$$
\left\|Q_{v, n}\right\| \leq \exp \left\{n E r_{n}(v)\right\}
$$

Now $\left|E r_{n}(v)\right| \leq \frac{1}{2}\left(e^{\varepsilon}-1\right) E\left(v^{2} / n\right)$ if $|v| \leq \varepsilon \sqrt{n}$. Thus, using condition (1), one obtains $\left|n E r_{n}(v)\right| \leq \frac{1}{2} b e^{1 / 4} \varepsilon$ and therefore

$$
\left\|Q_{v, n}\right\| \leq \exp \left\{\frac{1}{2} b e^{1 / 4} \varepsilon\right\} \leq 1+\frac{1}{2} b e^{5 / 4} \varepsilon
$$

for $\frac{1}{2} b e^{1 / 4} \varepsilon \leq 1$.
Now consider the affinity $\rho_{n}=\int \sqrt{d P_{v, n} d Q_{v, n}}$. It can be written $\rho_{n}=A_{n}^{n}$ with

$$
A_{n}=\int \exp \left\{\frac{v}{2 \sqrt{n}}-\frac{1}{4 n}\|v\|^{2}\right\}\left\{1-c\left(\frac{\|v\|}{2 \sqrt{n}}\right)+\frac{v}{2 \sqrt{n}}\right\} d p
$$

Expand $\exp \{v / 2 \sqrt{n}\}$ as before, obtaining $1+(v / 2 \sqrt{n})+\left(v^{2} / 8 n\right)+r_{n}(v / 2)$ and consider first the terms that do not involve $r_{n}$. They give

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{1} & =\int\left[1+\frac{v}{2 \sqrt{n}}+\frac{v^{2}}{8 n}\right]\left[1-c\left(\frac{\|v\|}{2 \sqrt{n}}\right)+\frac{v}{2 \sqrt{n}}\right] d p \\
& =1+\frac{\|v\|^{2}}{4 n}+\frac{1}{2} \int\left(\frac{v}{2 \sqrt{n}}\right)^{3} d p-\left\{c\left(\frac{\|v\|}{2 \sqrt{n}}\right)-\frac{\|v\|^{2}}{8 n}\right\} \\
& -c\left(\frac{\|v\|}{2 \sqrt{n}}\right) \frac{\|v\|^{2}}{8 n}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $v / \sqrt{n} \geq-\varepsilon$, the negative part of $\frac{1}{2} \int(v / 2 \sqrt{n})^{3} d p$ is bounded by $\frac{1}{16} \varepsilon\left(\|v\|^{2} / n\right)$.
To bound the term that involves the function $c$, note that it is defined by

$$
\begin{aligned}
c(x) & =1-\sqrt{1-x^{2}}=\frac{x^{2}}{1+\sqrt{1-x^{2}}} \\
& =\frac{x^{2}}{2}+\frac{x^{2}}{2} \frac{x^{2}}{\left[1+\sqrt{1-x^{2}}\right]^{2}} \\
& \leq \frac{x^{2}}{2}+\frac{x^{4}}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

Putting $x=\|v\| / 2 \sqrt{n}$ one obtains

$$
c\left[\frac{\|v\|}{2 \sqrt{n}}\right]-\frac{\|v\|^{2}}{8 n} \leq \frac{1}{2} \frac{\|v\|^{4}}{(4 n)^{2}} \leq \frac{\|v\|^{2}}{32 n} \varepsilon^{2}
$$

It remains to obtain a lower bound for the term

$$
T_{2}=\int r_{n}\left(\frac{v}{2}\right)\left[1-c\left(\frac{\|v\|}{2 \sqrt{n}}\right)+\frac{v}{2 \sqrt{n}}\right] d p
$$

Now

$$
r_{n}\left(\frac{v}{2}\right)=\frac{v^{2}}{4 n} \int_{0}^{1}(1-\xi)\left\{\exp \left\{\frac{\xi v}{2 \sqrt{n}}\right\}-1\right\} d \xi
$$

has the same sign as $v$. Thus it is sufficient to obtain an upper bound for the integral $\int\left[r_{n}(v / 2)\right]^{-} d p$. The inequality $\left[r_{n}(v / 2)\right]^{-} \leq\left(v^{2} / 4 n\right) \frac{1}{4} \varepsilon$ yields the bound $\left(\|v\|^{2} / 16 n\right) \varepsilon$. Taking all terms into account one obtains

$$
A_{n} \geq \exp \left\{-\frac{\|v\|^{2}}{4 n}\right\}\left\{1+\frac{\|v\|^{2}}{4 n}-\frac{2}{16} \frac{\|v\|^{2}}{n} \varepsilon-\frac{\|v\|^{2}}{32 n} \varepsilon^{2}-\frac{\|v\|^{2}}{8 n}\left(\varepsilon^{2}+\varepsilon^{4}\right)\right\}
$$

The inequality $e^{-x}(1+x) \geq 1-x^{2}$ gives

$$
A_{n} \geq 1-\left[\frac{\|v\|^{2}}{4 n}\right]^{2}-\frac{1}{8} \frac{\|v\|^{2}}{n}\left\{\varepsilon+\frac{5}{4} \varepsilon^{2}+\varepsilon^{4}\right\}
$$

Taking into account the restriction $\|v\|^{2} \leq b$, this yields

$$
\rho_{n}=A_{n}^{n} \geq 1-b\left\{\frac{\varepsilon}{8}+\varepsilon^{2}\right\} \geq 1-\frac{19}{128} b \varepsilon
$$

Finally the inequalities of Chapter 4 say that

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left\|Q_{v, n}-P_{v, n}\right\|^{2} & \leq 4\left\{\left\|\frac{1}{2}\left(Q_{v, n}+P_{v, n}\right)\right\|^{2}-\rho_{n}^{2}\right\} \\
& \leq 4\left\|Q_{v, n}+P_{v, n}\right\|\left\{\frac{1}{2}\left\|Q_{v, n}+P_{v, n}\right\|-\rho_{n}\right\}
\end{aligned}
$$

Here we have already seen that $\left\|Q_{v, n}\right\| \leq 1+\frac{1}{2} b e^{5 / 4} \varepsilon \leq 1+e^{1 / 4}$, since it is assumed that $\frac{1}{2} b e \varepsilon \leq 1$. This implies

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left\|Q_{v, n}-P_{v, n}\right\|^{2} & \leq 4 \times(2.29)\left[\frac{1}{4} b e^{5 / 4}+\frac{19}{128} b\right] \varepsilon \\
& =K^{2} b \varepsilon
\end{aligned}
$$

with $K^{2}=(2.29)\left[e^{5 / 4}+(19 / 32)\right]$. This implies the result as stated.
Note that the $Q_{v, n}$ are not probability measures. One could replace them by the probability measures $Q_{v, n}^{\prime}=\left\|Q_{v, n}\right\|^{-1} Q_{v, n}$. This would yield $\| Q_{v, n}^{\prime}- P_{v, n} \| \leq 2 K \sqrt{b \varepsilon}$. However, to maintain the simplicity of the formulas for likelihood ratios, we shall work directly with the $Q_{v, n}$. The likelihood ratio $d Q_{v, n} / d Q_{0, n}$ with $Q_{0, n}=P_{0, n}$ can be described as follows. For $n$ independent observations $X_{1}, \ldots, X_{n}$ let $Z_{n}$ be the empirical process defined by

$$
Z_{n}(A)=\frac{1}{\sqrt{n}} \sum_{j=1}^{n}\left[\delta_{X_{j}}(A)-p(A)\right]
$$

where $\delta_{x}$ is the Dirac mass at $x$. For $v \in \mathscr{L}_{2,0}(p)$, let $\left\langle v, Z_{n}\right\rangle=\int v d Z_{n}$. Then one can write

$$
\frac{d Q_{v, n}}{d Q_{0, n}}=\exp \left\{\left\langle v, Z_{n}\right\rangle-\frac{1}{2}\|v\|^{2}\right\}
$$

Note that, here, to get a version of the density $d Q_{v, n} / d Q_{0, n}$ we use an element of $\mathscr{L}_{2,0}(p)$, not its equivalence class in $L_{2,0}(p)$. However, indexing the measures by $\mathscr{L}_{2,0}(p)$ is inconvenient, since they depend only on the equivalence classes. Thus we shall keep the indexing by $L_{2,0}(p)$ but make choices for the variables $\left\langle v, Z_{n}\right\rangle$. For the case of the measures $Q_{v, n}$ of Lemma 4, this could be done in several manners. Since these functions $v$ are bounded, one could use a lifting. Another procedure is to work directly on $L_{2,0}(p)$ and note that the process $v \mapsto\left\langle v, Z_{n}\right\rangle$ is "linear" in the sense that for numbers $\alpha_{i}$ and elements $v_{i}$ one has $\left\langle v_{3}, Z_{n}\right\rangle=\alpha_{1}\left\langle v_{1}, Z_{n}\right\rangle+\alpha_{2}\left\langle v_{2}, Z_{n}\right\rangle$ almost surely if $\left\|v_{3}-\left(\alpha_{1} v_{1}+\alpha_{2} v_{2}\right)\right\|=$ 0 in $L_{2,0}(p)$. Thus the process $Z_{n}$ admits a realization by a probability measure $F_{n}$ on the algebraic dual $\mathscr{Y}$ of $L_{2,0}(p)$. This $F_{n}$ is uniquely determined on the $\sigma$-field generated by the $L_{2,0}(p)$ cylinder sets of $\mathscr{Y}$. Taking this into account, we shall assume that $P_{0, n}=Q_{0, n}$ has been transferred to $\mathscr{Y}$ in the form of $F_{n}$ and that the selected likelihood ratios are written

$$
\frac{d Q_{v, n}}{d Q_{0, n}}=\exp \left\{\left\langle v, Z_{n}\right\rangle-\frac{1}{2}\|v\|^{2}\right\}
$$

for $v \in L_{2,0}(p)$ and for a $Z_{n}$ with values in $\mathscr{Y}$.
Now note that there is on $L_{2,0}(p)$ a Gaussian shift experiment $\mathscr{G}=\left\{G_{v} ; v \in L_{2,0}(p)\right.$ \} canonically attached to $L_{2,0}(p)$. Its likelihood ratios can also be written in the form

$$
\frac{d G_{v}}{d G_{0}}=\exp \left\{\langle v, W\rangle-\frac{1}{2}\|v\|^{2}\right\}
$$

where $W$ is a random element (Gaussian) with values in $\mathscr{Y}$.
Take a subset $S \subset L_{2,0}(p)$ satisfying the requirements of Lemma 4. The representation of likelihood ratios given above shows that the families $\left\{Q_{v, n} ; v \in S\right\}$ and $\left\{G_{v} ; v \in S\right\}$ have the same likelihood ratio structure. Thus one would expect them to be close to each other in some suitable sense.

There is, however, a major difficulty: The measure $F_{n}$ representing $P_{0, n}$ on $\mathscr{Y}$ and the measure $G$ are disjoint. One can also look at the conical measures induced on the space $\mathscr{F}_{+}(S)$ of functions from $S$ to $[0, \infty)$. For a given $S$, let $M_{n}$ be the conical measure of $\left\{Q_{v, n} ; v \in S\right\}$ and let $M$ be that of $\left\{G_{v} ; v \in S\right\}$. Except if $S$ is very restricted, these measures will be disjoint conical measures. Indeed, unless $S$ is a G.B. set in the sense of Dudley, the paths $v \mapsto\langle v, W\rangle$ are almost surely unbounded, while $v \mapsto\left\langle v, Z_{n}\right\rangle$ has bounded paths for every $S$ that is bounded for the uniform norm (as in Lemma 4).

It is true that the distribution $F_{n}$ of the empirical process does converge to $G$ as $n \rightarrow \infty$. However, presently available central limit theorems for $F_{n}$ apply only to sets $S$ that are at least G.B. and G.C. with a restriction on the growth of their Kolmogorov entropy function (see Chapter 16). In spite of this one can obtain certain convergence theorems.

To state one, let us introduce the following definitions. Let $p$ be a probability measure on $\mathscr{A}_{n}$. (It is allowed to vary with $n$, since $\mathscr{A}_{n}$ does.)

Definition 1. Let $S_{n}$ be a subset of $L_{2,0}(p)$. The sequence $\left\{S_{n}\right\}$ will be said to satisfy a boundedness and uniform Lindeberg condition if
(i) $\sup _{n} \sup _{v}\left\{\|v\| ; v \in S_{n}\right\}<\infty$,
(ii) for every $\varepsilon>0$,

$$
\sup _{v} \int v^{2} I\{|v|>\varepsilon \sqrt{n}\} d p \rightarrow 0
$$

Definition 2. The sequence $\left\{S_{n}\right\}$ is tail equi-precompact if, $N_{n}(\varepsilon)$ denoting the number of sets of diameter $\varepsilon$ needed to cover $S_{n}$, one has

$$
\limsup _{n} N_{n}(\varepsilon)<\infty
$$

for every $\varepsilon>0$.
Theorem 1. Let $S_{n} \subset L_{2,0}(p)$ be such that $v \in S_{n}$ implies $\|v\| \leq 2 \sqrt{n}$. Assume that $S_{n}$ satisfies a boundedness and uniform Lindeberg condition. Let $\mathscr{F}_{n}= \left\{P_{v, n} ; v \in S_{n}\right\}$ and $\mathscr{G}_{n}=\left\{G_{v} ; v \in S_{n}\right\}$. Then $\Delta\left(\mathscr{F}_{n}, \mathscr{G}_{n}\right) \rightarrow 0$ if either one of the following restrictions hold:
(a) The $S_{n}$ are tail equi-precompact, or (b) The variables $\left\{\left\langle v, Z_{n}\right\rangle ; v \in S_{n}\right\}$ form an independent set for each $n$ sufficiently large.

Remark. The alternate condition (b) is not very interesting. It is given here only to show that the precompactness condition (a) is certainly not necessary. However, one can obtain other more interesting results. See the remarks that follow the proof.

Proof. Because of the Lindeberg condition, one might as well assume that the $v \in S_{n}$ satisfy the eligibility requirement

$$
1-c\left(\frac{\|v\|}{2 \sqrt{n}}\right)+\frac{v}{2 \sqrt{n}} \geq 0
$$

The first assertion is then an immediate consequence of the results of Chapter 16 if the cardinality of $S_{n}$ remains bounded. The precompact case follows by Lindae's argument (Chapter 6, Section 4). To handle the case where the $\left\langle v, Z_{n}\right\rangle, v \in S_{n}$ are independent, note that, according to Lemma 3, one can replace each $v$ by a $v^{*}$ with $\left|v^{*}\right| \leq \varepsilon \sqrt{n}$ without modifying $P_{v, n}$ appreciably. The same substitution changes $G_{v}$ to a $G_{v^{*}}$ with $\int \sqrt{d G_{v} d G_{v^{*}}} \geq \exp \left\{-\frac{1}{8}\left\|v-v^{*}\right\|^{2}\right\}$ and therefore $\left\|G_{v}-G_{v^{*}}\right\| \leq\left\|v-v^{*}\right\|$.

Thus, it is sufficient to consider the case where all $v \in S_{n}$ satisfy the restriction $|v| \leq \varepsilon \sqrt{n}$ for an $\varepsilon$ that will tend to zero as $n \rightarrow \infty$. According to Lemma 4, the experiment $\left\{P_{v, n} ; v \in S_{n}\right\}$ can be replaced by $\left\{Q_{v, n} ; v \in S_{n}\right\}$. Now consider the distribution function $\Phi_{v}$ of $\langle v, W\rangle$ under $G_{0}$ for a particular $v \in S_{n}$. Let $F_{v}$ be the distribution function of $\left\langle v, Z_{n}\right\rangle$ and apply the transformation $x \mapsto F_{v}^{-1} \Phi_{v}(x)$ to $\left\langle v, W_{n}\right\rangle$. This gives a new variable, say $Y_{v, n}$, whose distribution is exactly that of $\left\langle v, Z_{n}\right\rangle$. Let $R_{v, n}$ be the measure defined by

$$
d R_{v, n}=\exp \left\{Y_{v, n}-\frac{1}{2}\|v\|^{2}\right\} d G_{0}
$$

Since we have assumed independence of the $\left\langle v, Z_{n}\right\rangle$, the $\langle v, W\rangle$ are also independent. Thus the process $v \mapsto Y_{v, n} ; v \in S_{n}$ has under $G_{0}$ exactly the same joint distribution as the process $v \mapsto\left\langle v, Z_{n}\right\rangle ; v \in S_{n}$ under $P_{0, n}$. This means that, for the distance $\Delta$, the families $\left\{Q_{v, n} ; v \in S_{n}\right\}$ and $\left\{R_{v, n} ; v \in S_{n}\right\}$ are equivalent. To conclude that $\delta\left(\mathscr{G}_{n}, \mathscr{F}_{n}\right)$ tends to zero it will be sufficient to show that $\sup _{v}\left\{\left\|R_{v, n}-G_{v}\right\| ; v \in S_{n}\right\}$ tends to zero. However,

$$
\left\|R_{v, n}-G_{v}\right\|=\int\left|e^{Y_{v, n}}-e^{\langle v, W\rangle}\right| e^{-(1 / 2)\|v\|^{2}} d G_{0}
$$

It is easy to check that

$$
E 1 \wedge\left|Y_{v, n}-\langle v, W\rangle\right| \rightarrow 0 \quad \text { uniformly for } v \in S_{n}
$$

This will imply that $\sup _{v}\left\|R_{v, n}-G_{v}\right\| \rightarrow 0$ if the $e^{Y_{v, n}}$ and $e^{\langle v, W\rangle}$ are uniformly equi-integrable. This is easily checked by looking at moment generating functions. Hence the result is in one direction.

In the other direction, one first convolutes the distribution $F_{v}$ with a uniform distribution of small range. Then the smoothed $F_{v}$ can be transformed to match the Gaussian distribution of $\langle v, W\rangle$. The argument is essentially the same. Hence the result.

Remark 1. The second part of the result depends on finding a process $v \mapsto Y_{v} ; v \in S_{n}$ that is a function of $W$, has the same distribution as the process $v \mapsto\left\langle v, Z_{n}\right\rangle$, and is such that $\sup _{v} E 1 \wedge\left|Y_{v}-\langle v, W\rangle\right|$ is small. This can be contrasted with the requirement for the usual Prohorov type convergence in which the "sup" and " $E$ " would be interchanged.

Remark 2. Suppose that the $v \in S_{n}$ satisfy the positivity requirement $0 \leq 1$ -
$c(\|v\| / 2 \sqrt{n})+(v / 2 \sqrt{n})$, the uniform Lindeberg condition, and a boundedness requirement $v \leq a \sqrt{n}$ with $a<\infty$ independent of $n$. Suppose also that the $\left\langle v, Z_{n}\right\rangle ; v \in S_{n}$ form an independent set. Then the conclusion of the theorem will also apply to the convex hull $\widetilde{S}_{n}$ of $S_{n}$.

Indeed, here, there is no need to perform any truncation procedure, according to the remark that follows the statement of Lemma 4. In addition, if the distribution of the process $v \mapsto Y_{v} ; v \in S_{n}$ matches that of $v \mapsto\left\langle v, Z_{n}\right\rangle$, the same will be true for linear combinations of the $v$ 's. The Lindeberg condition remains true and the $\exp \left\langle v, Z_{n}\right\rangle$ are still equi-integrable for $v \in \widetilde{S}_{n}, n=1,2, \ldots$. It can also be shown that the result can be carried over to combinations $\Sigma_{j} \alpha_{j} v_{j}$ with $v \in S_{n}$ and $\Sigma_{j} \alpha_{j}^{2} \leq 1$.

Theorem 1 and the above remarks suggest that perhaps $\Delta\left(\mathscr{F}_{n}, \mathscr{G}_{n}\right)$ will tend to zero whenever the uniform Lindeberg condition and the boundedness restrictions $\|v\|^{2} \leq b$ are satisfied. At the time of this writing we do not know what the situation is. However, one can get an intermediate result.

According to Chapter 1, the distance between $\mathscr{F}_{n}$ and $\mathscr{G}_{n}$ can be obtained as follows. Let $\pi$ be a probability measure with finite support on $S_{n}$. Let $c(j, v) ; j \in J, v \in S_{n}$ be numbers such that $0 \leq c(j, v) \leq 1$. Compute the norm $v\left[\pi, C, \mathscr{F}_{n}\right]=\left\|\sup _{j \in J} \Sigma_{v} c(j, v) \pi_{v} P_{v, n}\right\|$. Compute $v\left[\pi, C, \mathscr{G}_{n}\right]$ similarly. Then

$$
\Delta\left(\mathscr{F}_{n}, \mathscr{G}_{n}\right)=\sup _{\pi, C}\left|v\left(\pi, C, \mathscr{F}_{n}\right)-v\left(\pi, C, \mathscr{G}_{n}\right)\right|
$$

for a supremum taken over all $\pi$ and $C$. Torgersen has used other distances $\Delta_{k}$ obtainable if one looks only at $k$-decision problems. $\Delta_{k}$ is defined exactly as above except that the cardinality of $J$ is restricted to be at most $k$.

Another distance $\Delta_{H}$ can be obtained as follows. Let $U$ be the unit ball of some Hilbert space. (It is sufficient to take the unit ball of $L_{2,0}(p)$.) Let $v \mapsto g(v)$ be a map from $S_{n}$ to $U$ and let $\pi$ be a finitely supported probability measure on $S_{n}$. Let $R\left(\pi, g, \mathscr{F}_{n}\right)$ be the Bayes risk

$$
R\left(\pi, g, \mathscr{F}_{n}\right)=\inf _{T} \sum_{v} \pi_{v} \int\|g(v)-T(x)\|^{2} P_{v, n}(d x)
$$

Define $R\left(\pi, g, \mathscr{G}_{n}\right)$ analogously. Then let

$$
\Delta_{\boldsymbol{H}}\left(\mathscr{F}_{n}, \mathscr{G}_{n}\right)=\sup _{g, \pi, U}\left|R\left(\pi, g, \mathscr{F}_{n}\right)-R\left(\pi, g, \mathscr{G}_{n}\right)\right|
$$

With these definitions one can prove the following.
Theorem 2. Assume that $S_{n}$ satisfies a boundedness condition $\|v\|^{2} \leq b$ and the uniform Lindeberg condition. Then for each fixed $k$ the distance $\Delta_{k}\left(\mathscr{F}_{n}, \mathscr{G}_{n}\right)+ \Delta_{\boldsymbol{H}}\left(\mathscr{F}_{\boldsymbol{n}}, \mathscr{G}_{\boldsymbol{n}}\right)$ tends to zero as $n \rightarrow \infty$.

The proof of this is long and rather laborious. It will be published elsewhere. Instead of approximating the experiments $\mathscr{F}_{n}$ by the Gaussian $\mathscr{G}_{n}$, one could use the Poissonized verison $\mathscr{P}_{n}$ of $\mathscr{F}_{n}$. The measures $P_{v, n}^{\prime}$ of $\mathscr{P}_{n}$ are obtained by
taking a random number $N$ with a Poisson distribution such that $E N=n$ and then making a direct product of $N$ copies of $P_{v, n}$.

Theorem 3. Assume that there is a fixed $b<\infty$ with $\|v\|^{2} \leq b$ for all $v \in S_{n}$. Then $\Delta\left(\mathscr{F}_{n}, \mathscr{P}_{n}\right) \rightarrow 0$ as $n \rightarrow \infty$.

Proof. Let $\mathbf{M}=\max (n, N)$ on the product $\mathscr{X}_{n}^{(M)}=\prod_{j=1}^{M} \mathscr{X}_{n, j}$ of $M$ copies of $\mathscr{X}_{n}$, let $P_{v, n}^{*}$ be the product of $M$ copies of $p_{v, n}$. Since $\operatorname{Pr}[M \geq 2 n]$ tends to zero, the affinity between $P_{v, n}^{*}$ and $P_{0, n}^{*}$ will stay bounded away from zero.

On $\mathscr{X}_{n}{ }^{(M)}$, the experiment $\mathscr{F}_{n}$ can be realized as follows. Let $f_{n, j} ; j= 1,2, \ldots$, be independent copies of $d p_{v, n} / d p$ on the replicate spaces $\left(\mathscr{X}_{n, j}, \mathscr{A}_{n, j}\right. )$. Then one can write $d P_{v, n}^{*} / d P_{0, n}^{*}=\prod_{j=1}^{M} f_{n, j}$. For $\mathscr{F}_{n}$, one proceeds in the same way, except that from $n+1$ on the $f_{n, j}$ are replaced by unity. In such a representation the affinity $\rho_{n}=\int \sqrt{d P_{v, n} d P_{v, n}^{*}}$ is given by $E\left(\prod_{j=n+1}^{M} f_{n, j}\right)^{1 / 2}$ for an expectation taken under $P_{0, n}^{*}$. There are numbers $\alpha_{n}$ tending to zero such that $\beta_{n}=\operatorname{Pr}\left[M \geq n+\alpha_{n} n\right] \rightarrow 0$. This yields

$$
E\left(\prod_{j=n+1}^{M} f_{n, j}\right)^{1 / 2} \geq E\left(\prod_{j=1}^{\alpha_{n} n} f_{n, j}\right)^{1 / 2}-\beta_{n}
$$

Thus $\rho_{n} \rightarrow 1$ since the affinity $\int \sqrt{d P_{v, n} d P_{0, n}}$ stays bounded away from zero.
The same argument can be applied to the measures $P_{v, n}^{\prime}$ of the Poissonized version. This yields the desired result.

Remark 1. The preceding theorem is particularly useful if one wants to split apart pieces of the underlying spaces $\left(\mathscr{X}_{n}, \mathscr{A}_{n}\right)$ since for the Poissonized version $\mathscr{P}_{n}$ they can be treated independently of the rest.

Remark 2. Assume that the $v \in S_{n}$ satisfy (i) $\|v\|^{2} \leq b$, (ii) $1-c(\|v\| / 2 \sqrt{n})+ (v / 2 \sqrt{n}) \geq 0$, (iii) $v \leq a \sqrt{n}$ for a fixed finite $a$ and the uniform Lindeberg condition. Then the density of the Poissonized measure $P_{v, n}^{\prime}$ can be replaced by $\exp \left\{\left\langle v, Z_{n}^{\prime}\right\rangle-\frac{1}{2}\|v\|^{2}\right\}$ where $Z_{n}^{\prime}$ is the process with independent pieces $Z_{n}^{\prime}= (1 / \sqrt{n}) \sum_{j=1}^{N}\left(\delta_{X_{j}}-p\right)$. This can be seen as in Lemma 4, using the remark that follows the statement of Lemma 2.

We shall conclude this section with a few remarks linked to the geometry of the Hilbert spaces $\mathscr{H}_{n}$ introduced previously. If the underlying spaces, for one individual observation, are spaces $\left(\mathscr{X}_{n}, \mathscr{A}_{n}\right)$, then $\mathscr{H}_{n}$ can be represented by the space of symbols $\xi \sqrt{d \mu}$ with the square norm $\|\xi \sqrt{d \mu}\|_{n}^{2}=4 n \int \xi^{2} d \mu$. We shall also use $\mathscr{H}_{n}{ }^{1}$ for the same set of elements with the square norm $\|\xi \sqrt{d \mu}\|^{2}= \int \xi^{2} d \mu$.

Lemma 5. Let $p_{n}, q_{n}$, and $r_{n}$ be three probability measures on $\mathscr{A}_{n}$. Assume that there is a number $\alpha \geq 0$ such that

$$
\left\|\left(\sqrt{d q_{n}}-\sqrt{d p_{n}}\right)+\alpha\left(\sqrt{d r_{n}}-\sqrt{d p_{n}}\right)\right\|_{n}
$$

tends to zero as $n \rightarrow \infty$. Then if $q_{n}^{\prime \prime}$ is the part of $q_{n}$ that is $p_{n}$ singular, the $L_{1}$-norms $n\left\|q_{n}^{\prime \prime}\right\|$ tends to zero as $n \rightarrow \infty$.

Proof. Let $m_{n}$ be the measure defined by $\sqrt{d m_{n}}=\left(\sqrt{d q_{n}}+\alpha \sqrt{d r_{n}}\right) / 1+\alpha$. The assumption is that $\left\|\sqrt{d m_{n}}-\sqrt{d p_{n}}\right\|_{n}^{2} \rightarrow 0$. The part $m_{n}^{\prime \prime}$ of $m_{n}$ that is $p_{n}$ singular contributes to that square norm a term $n\left\|m_{n}^{\prime \prime}\right\|$ which must tend to zero. Now $q_{n}$ is absolutely continuous with respect to $m_{n}$ and in fact $d q_{n} \leq (1+\alpha)^{2} d m_{n}$. Therefore, $n\left\|q_{n}^{\prime \prime}\right\| \leq(1+\alpha)^{2} n\left\|m_{n}^{\prime \prime}\right\|$ tends to zero.

In this lemma the elements $q_{n}$ and $r_{n}$ are "on opposite sides" of $p_{n}$ approaching $p_{n}$ at the same general rate. If the spaces $\mathscr{X}_{n}, \mathscr{A}_{n}$, and the $p_{n}$ are independent of $n$, equal to ( $\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{A}, p$ ), one can obtain a result of the same type with measures that approach $p$ at different rates.

Lemma 6 (Hájek). Let $(\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{A}, p)$ be fixed, independent of $n$. Let $q_{n}$ be probability measures on $\mathscr{A}$ such that $\left\|\sqrt{n d q_{n}}-\sqrt{n d p}-v\right\| \rightarrow 0$ for some $v \in \mathscr{H}^{1}$ (and the norm of $\mathscr{H}^{1}$ ). Assume that there are other probability measures $r_{m}$ and numbers $c_{m}$ such that $\left\|\sqrt{c_{m} d r_{m}}-\sqrt{c_{m} d p}+\beta v\right\| \rightarrow 0$ in $\mathscr{H}^{1}$. Then, if $\beta>0$, the mass $n\left\|q_{n}^{\prime \prime}\right\|$ for the parts of $q_{n}$ that are $p$ singular tends to zero as $n \rightarrow \infty$.

Proof. Since only sequences are involved, one may assume that all the $q_{n}, r_{n}$, and $p$ are dominated by one probability measure $\mu$. Take $f \geq 0$ and $g_{n} \geq 0$ such that $d p=f^{2} d \mu$ and $d q_{n}=g_{n}^{2} d \mu$. To say that $\sqrt{n d q_{n}}-\sqrt{n d p}$ tends to $v$ is to say that

$$
\int\left|\sqrt{n}\left(g_{n}-f\right)-w\right|^{2} d \mu \rightarrow 0
$$

for some $w \in L_{2}(\mu)$.
Let $Z_{1}$ be the indicator of the set $\{x ; w(x)<0, f(x)=0\}$ and let $Z_{2}$ be the indicator of $\{x ; w(x) \geq 0, f(x)=0\}$. The relation written above implies that $\int Z_{1}\left|\sqrt{n} g_{n}-w\right|^{2} d \mu \rightarrow 0$. However, $Z_{1}\left|\sqrt{n g_{n}}-w\right|^{2} \geq n Z_{1} g_{n}^{2}+Z_{1} w^{2}$. Thus $n \int Z_{1} g_{n}^{2} d \mu \rightarrow 0$ and $\int Z_{1} w^{2} d \mu=0$.

Proceed similarly for the sequence $r_{m}$, writing $d r_{m}=h_{m}^{2} d \mu$, with $h_{m} \geq 0$. Now

$$
\left\|Z_{2}\left[\sqrt{c_{m} d r_{m}}-\sqrt{c_{m} d p}+\beta v\right]\right\|^{2}=\int Z_{2}\left|\left(\sqrt{c_{m}}\right) h_{m}+\beta w\right|^{2} d \mu
$$

will tend to zero, implying $\int Z_{2} w^{2} d \mu=0$. This implies $\int\left(Z_{1}+Z_{2}\right) w^{2} d \mu=$ 0 . The function $Z_{1}+Z_{2}$ is the indicator of the set $\{x: f(x)=0\}$. This yields

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int\left[\sqrt{n}\left(g_{n}-f\right)-w\right]^{2} d \mu & \geq \int\left(Z_{1}+Z_{2}\right)\left[\sqrt{n}\left(g_{n}-f\right)-w\right]^{2} d \mu \\
& =\int\left(Z_{1}+Z_{2}\right) n g_{n}^{2} d \mu \\
& =n\left\|q_{n}^{\prime \prime}\right\|
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore $n\left\|q_{n}^{\prime \prime}\right\|$ must tend to zero.
As mentioned, the foregoing Lemma 6, although perhaps not exactly in this form, is due to J. Hájek. We shall meet it again in the next section in connection with differentiability in quadratic mean.

Our next lemma refers to the finite dimensional approximations used locally in Chapter 11 and "near isometries." We shall work again in the space $\mathscr{H}_{n}$ where the norm $\|\sqrt{d p}-\sqrt{d q}\|_{n}$ is the Hellinger distance multiplied by $\sqrt{8 n}$. Two sequences $\left\{S_{n}\right\}$ and $\left\{T_{n}\right\}$ as subsets of $\mathscr{H}_{n}$ will be called tangent around elements $p_{n}$ if, $B_{n}(a)$ denoting the ball of radius $a$ centered at $p_{n}$, the Hausdorff distance between $S_{n} \cap B_{n}(a)$ and $T_{n} \cap B_{n}(a)$ tends to zero as $n \rightarrow \infty$.

For near isometries one can consider two different properties. Consider sets $S_{n} \subset \mathscr{H}_{n}$ and let $\mathscr{H}_{n}^{\prime}$ be another Hilbert space. Let $\phi_{n}$ be a map from $S_{n}$ to $\mathscr{H}_{n}^{\prime}$. The maps $\phi_{n}$ will be called nearly isometric at $p_{n}$ if, for all fixed $a$ and all pairs $q_{n}, r_{n}$ of elements of $S_{n} \cap B_{n}(a)$, the differences

$$
\left\|\sqrt{d q_{n}}-\sqrt{d r_{n}}\right\|_{n}-\left\|\phi_{n}\left(q_{n}\right)-\phi_{n}\left(r_{n}\right)\right\|^{\prime}
$$

tend to zero as $n \rightarrow \infty$. There is a stronger concept describable as follows. Identify $\phi_{n}$ to its graph in $\mathscr{H}_{n} \times \mathscr{H}_{n}^{\prime}$. The graphs $\phi_{n}$ are near isometries if there are isometries $\psi_{n}$ of subsets of $\mathscr{H}_{n}$ into $\mathscr{H}_{n}^{\prime}$ such that the Hausdorff distance between the the graphs $\phi_{n}$ and $\psi_{n}$ tends to zero. This can be localized as before, taking intersections of $\phi_{n}$ and $\psi_{n}$ with the product $B_{n}(a) \times B_{n}^{\prime}\left(a^{\prime}\right)$ of balls in $\mathscr{H}_{n}$ and $\mathscr{H}_{n}^{\prime}$.

Lemma 7. Let $p_{n}$ be elements of $\mathscr{H}_{n}$ and let $S_{n} \subset \mathscr{H}_{n}$. Let $L_{n}$ be a linear affine subspace of $\mathscr{H}_{n}$ with $p_{n} \in L_{n}$. The following properties are equivalent for a fixed $k<\infty$ :
(1) There is a linear affine $L_{n}$ of dimension at most $k$ and subsets $T_{n} \subset L_{n}$ such that $S_{n}$ and $T_{n}$ are tangent around $p_{n}$.
(2) There are maps $\phi_{n}$ of $S_{n}$ into a Euclidean space $\mathbb{R}^{k}$ that are nearly isometric around $p_{n}$ and such that $\phi_{n}\left(p_{n}\right)=0$.
(3) There are maps $\phi_{n}$ of $S_{n}$ into a Euclidean space $\mathbb{R}^{k}$ such that $\phi_{n}\left(p_{n}\right)=0$ and such that the graphs $\phi_{n}$ are near isometries.

Proof. To show that (1) implies (3), associate to each $s \in S_{n}$ a point $t(s) \in L_{n}$ as close as possible to $s$. Taking $p_{n}$ as origin, this will give a map $\phi_{n}$ to a Euclidean space. For two elements $s_{i} ; i=1,2$ of $S_{n}$ one will have $\left\|t\left(s_{1}\right)-t\left(s_{2}\right)\right\|_{n} \leq\left\|s_{1}-s_{2}\right\|_{n}$. If the $\left\|s_{i}-p_{n}\right\|_{n}$ stay bounded, then, by assumption $\left\|s_{i}-t\left(s_{i}\right)\right\|_{n} \rightarrow 0$. Thus $\left\|s_{1}-s_{2}\right\|_{n}-\left\|t\left(s_{1}\right)-t\left(s_{2}\right)\right\|_{n}$ will also tend to zero. The projection $s \mapsto t(s)$ described here is an isometry of $L_{n}$ onto itself. Thus condition (3) is satisfied.

To show that (2) implies (1), consider pairs $\left(T_{n}, L_{n}\right)$ where $T_{n}$ is a subset of a $k$-dimensional affine subspace of $\mathscr{H}_{n}$, with $p_{n} \in L_{n}$. Let $d_{n}\left(T_{n}, j\right)$ be the Hausdorff distance between $T_{n} \cap B_{n}(j)$ and $S_{n} \cap B_{n}(j)$. Let $d_{n}\left(T_{n}\right)= \sum_{j=1}^{\infty} d_{n}\left(T_{n}, j\right) j^{-3}$. For each $n$ select a pair $\left(T_{n}, L_{n}\right)$ so that $d_{n}\left(T_{n}\right) \leq \inf _{T} d_{n}(T)+ (1 / n)$. Here the infimum is taken over subsets $T$ of $k$-dimensional affine spaces $L$ with $p_{n} \in L$. If $d_{n}\left(T_{n}\right)$ tends to zero for these selected $T_{n}$ then (1) holds.

If $d_{n}\left(T_{n}\right)$ does not tend to zero there is an $\alpha>0$ and a subsequence $\{m\} \subset \{n\}$
such that $d_{m}\left(T_{m}\right) \geq 3 \alpha$ for all $m$. Since $d_{m}\left(T_{m}, j\right) \leq 2 j$, there is some integer $\nu$ such that $\sum_{j=1}^{v} d_{m}\left(T_{m}, j\right) j^{-3} \geq 2 \alpha$ for all $m$. For sufficiently large $m$ this will imply $\inf _{T} \sum_{j=1}^{v} d_{m}(T, j) j^{-3} \geq \alpha$. Hence $\inf _{T} d_{m}(T, v) \geq c \alpha$ with $c^{-1}= \sum_{j=1}^{v}\left(1 / j^{3}\right)$. Now let $D_{m}$ be the image of $S_{m} \cap B_{m}(v)$ in $\mathbb{R}^{k}$ by the maps $\phi_{m}$ of condition (2) of the lemma and their closures $\bar{D}_{m}$. The $\bar{D}_{m}$ will stay within a fixed compact of $\mathscr{R}^{k}$. One can assume that the sequence $\{m\}$ has been so selected that the $\bar{D}_{m}$ converge for the Hausdorff distance to a limit $D \subset \mathbb{R}^{k}$.

This limit $D$ spans a linear subspace, say $M$, of $\mathbb{R}^{k}$. Let $r$ be the dimension of $M$ and let $u_{i} ; i=1,2, \ldots, r$ be $r$ linearly independent elements of $D$. By construction there are elements $s_{m, i} \in S_{m} \cap B_{m}(v)$ such that $\sup _{i} \| \phi_{m}\left(s_{m, i}\right) u_{i} \| \rightarrow 0$ as $m \rightarrow \infty$. These $s_{m, i}$ span a linear affine subspace $L_{m}$ of $\mathscr{H}_{m}$. Since $\inf _{T} d_{m}(T, v) \geq c \alpha$, there are elements $s_{m, r+1}$ of $S_{m} \cap B_{m}(v)$ such that the distance from $s_{m, r+1}$ to $L_{m}$ does not tend to zero. Let $u_{m, r+1}$ be elements of $D$ such that $\left\|\phi_{m}\left(s_{m, r+1}\right)-u_{m, r+1}\right\| \rightarrow 0$. Put $u_{0}=0$ and $s_{m, 0}=p_{m}$. Consider sets of numbers $\left\{\beta_{i} ; i=0,1, \ldots, r+1\right\}$ with $\Sigma \beta_{i}=0, \Sigma\left|\beta_{i}\right| \leq A$, and define
square norms

$$
\begin{aligned}
\|\beta\|_{m}^{2} & =-\frac{1}{2} \sum \sum\left\|s_{m, i}-s_{m, j}\right\|^{2} \beta_{i} \beta_{j} \\
\|\beta\|^{2} & =-\frac{1}{2} \sum \sum\left\|u_{i}-u_{j}\right\|^{2} \beta_{i} \beta_{j}
\end{aligned}
$$

where we have written $u_{r+1}$ for $u_{m, r+1}$. Since the maps $\phi_{m}$ are nearly isometric, the differences $\|\beta\|_{m}-\|\beta\|$ will tend to zero uniformly on the simplex $S=\left\{\beta ; \sum \beta_{i}=0, \sum\left|\beta_{i}\right| \leq A\right\}$. Consider also the simplex $S_{0}$ subset of $S$ where $\beta_{r+1}=0$. On $S_{0}$ norms $\|\beta\|$, and therefore $\|\beta\|_{m}$, cannot tend to zero unless $\sum\left|\beta_{i}\right|$ does. Since the distance of $s_{m, r+1}$ to $L_{m}$ does not tend to zero, this is also true on $S$ for the norms $\|\beta\|_{m}^{2}$ and therefore $\|\beta\|$. This is a contradiction since $u_{m, r+1}$ is a linear combination of the $u_{i}, i \leq r$. Hence the result.

In conjunction with a uniform Lindeberg condition, Lemma 7 can be used to give necessary and sufficient conditions for approximability of local experiments by finite dimensional ones, as needed for many of the results of Chapter 11.

Note that, except for Lemma 6, we have let the triplets $\left(\mathscr{X}_{n}, \mathscr{A}_{n}, p\right)$ vary with $n$ in arbitrary ways. When $\left(\mathscr{X}_{n}, \mathscr{A}_{n}, p\right)=(\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{A}, p)$ fixed, independent of $n$, certain other phenomena can occur. For instance, one may look at a fixed subset $S$ of the space of probability measures on $\mathscr{A}$. One has a fixed Hilbert space $\mathscr{H}$ where the square norm is eight times the square Hellinger distance for positive measures on $\mathscr{A}$.

Tangent spaces $T_{n}$ at $p$ become sets such that, $B_{n}^{1}(a)$ being the ball of radius $a / \sqrt{n}$ centered at $p$, the Hausdorff distance between $S \cap B_{n}^{1}(a)$ and $T_{n} \cap B_{n}^{1}(a)$ tends to zero faster than $1 / \sqrt{n}$. In such cases, the behavior of the sets $T_{n}$ is somewhat restricted.

Suppose for instance that they are cones with vertex at $p$. Let $m_{n}$ be any sequence of integers such that $0<\liminf _{n}\left(m_{n} / n\right) \leq \limsup \left(m_{n} / n\right)<\infty$. Then the distance between $T_{m_{n}} \cap B_{n}^{1}(a)$ and $T_{n} \cap B_{n}^{1}(a)$ (in $\mathscr{H}$ ) must tend to zero faster than $1 / \sqrt{n}$. Indeed, take $p$ as origin and let $\widetilde{S}$ be the image of $S$ by the map $q \mapsto \sqrt{d q}-\sqrt{d p}$ in $\mathscr{H}$. Then $\left(\sqrt{m_{n} / n}\right)(\sqrt{n} \widetilde{S})=\left(\sqrt{m_{n}}\right) \widetilde{S}$. Also $\left(\sqrt{m_{n} / n}\right) \widetilde{T}_{n}= \tilde{T}_{n}$. Thus if $\tilde{T}_{n} \cap B_{n}(a)$ is close to $(\sqrt{n} \widetilde{S}) \cap B_{n}(a)$, it is close to $\left(\sqrt{m_{n}} \widetilde{S}\right) \cap B_{n}(a)$ and therefore to $\tilde{T}_{m_{n}} \cap B_{n}(a)$. One can already obtain many important results by asking that $S$ admit a fixed tangent cone $T$. This will be the case for the differentiable families of Section 3. However, variable cones $T_{n}$ do occur in fairly simple examples. Take, for instance, the family $\left\{p_{\theta} ; \theta \in \mathbb{R}\right\}$ where $p_{\theta}$ has density $[1-|x-\theta|]^{+}$with respect to the Lebesgue measure on the line. Take $p=p_{1}$ as origin. For $\theta(n)=1+(n \log n)^{-1 / 2}$ the difference $\sqrt{d p_{\theta(n)}}-\sqrt{d p_{1}}$ contains a term that behaves in the same way as $\phi_{n}=\frac{1}{2}(x \log n)^{-1 / 2}$ for $\sqrt{\log n / n}<x<\varepsilon$. There is another similar term for $x$ close to 2 . The functions $\phi_{n}$ vary slowly, but they do not converge in $\mathscr{H}$.

For the next section we shall keep the underlying observation spaces ( $\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{A}$ ) fixed. Then, for $p$ fixed, one can look at the Hilbert space $\mathscr{H}(p)$ where measures



$q$ have been mapped into $\sqrt{d q}-\sqrt{d p}=\tilde{q}$ with $\|\tilde{q}\|$ equal to twice the Hellinger distance.

In Lemma 7 we have looked at finite dimensional situations involving maps of pieces of $\mathscr{H}(p)$ into Euclidean space. One can also generate finite dimensional experiments by mapping pieces of Euclidean spaces into $\mathscr{H}(p)$.

This will be the situation studied in Sections 3 and 4. In these sections one considers a subset $\Theta$ of a finite dimensional vector space $V$ and an experiment $\mathscr{E}=\left\{p_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ indexed by $\Theta$. For each $n$, one takes a number $\delta(n)>0$ so that $\delta(n) \rightarrow 0$ as $n \rightarrow \infty$. For a given norm on $V$, let $\mathscr{F}(\theta, n, c)$ be the experiment formed by the measures $p_{\theta+\delta(n) t}^{n}$ where $t$ varies in the set $\{t: \theta+\delta(n) t \in \Theta,|t| \leq c\}$. The problem considered is the possibility of approximating the $\mathscr{F}(\theta, n, c)$ by the restriction to the same set of indices of a linearly indexed Gaussian experiment.

It will be shown (Proposition 1, Section 4) that under mild restrictions on the set $\Theta$, the sequences $\delta(n)$ for which such an approximation is possible are fairly restricted. In fact $\sqrt{n} \delta(n)$ must be slowly varying at infinity.

This is, of course, because we insist on approximation by a linearly indexed Gaussian shift experiment. Approximation by Gaussian shift experiments which are indexed by $V$ in a totally arbitrary manner can occur for other rates of convergence. However, the simplest cases are those where $\sqrt{n} \delta(n)$ is not only slowly varying but essentially constant. This particular case is most important. It is the subject of the next section.

## 3. A Special Role for $\sqrt{n}$ : Differentiability in Quadratic Mean

Let $\mathscr{E}=\left\{p_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ be an experiment indexed by a subset $\Theta$ of a Euclidean space $\mathbb{R}^{k}$. Consider the product experiments $\mathscr{E}^{n}=\left\{p_{\theta}^{n} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$, a particular point $\theta_{0} \in \Theta$, and a sequence $\{\delta(n)\}, \delta(n)>0$, of numbers which tend to zero. Let $V(n, c)$ be the subset of $\mathbb{R}^{k}$ defined by $V(n, c)=\left\{t ;|t| \leq c, \theta_{0}+\delta(n) t \in \Theta\right\}$, where $|t|$ is the norm of $t$ in $\mathbb{R}^{k}$. Let $\mathscr{F}\left(\theta_{0}, n, c\right)$ be the experiment

$$
\mathscr{F}\left(\theta_{0}, n, c\right)=\left\{p_{\theta_{0}+\delta(n) t}^{n} ; t \in V(n, c)\right\}
$$

In the spirit of Chapter 10 one may inquire about the possibility of approximating the $\mathscr{F}\left(\theta_{0}, n, c\right)$ by Gaussian experiments $\mathscr{G}\left(\theta_{0}, n, c\right)=\left\{G_{t, n} ; t \in V(n, c)\right\}$ which are linearly indexed by $t$ in the sense that the likelihood ratios $d G_{t, n} / d G_{0, n}$ are equal to $\exp \left\{t S_{n}-\frac{1}{2}\|t\|^{2}\right\}$ for some random vector $S_{n}$ and some Euclidean norms or semi-norms $\|t\|$. This section is devoted to a study of such approximability for the special case where the sequence $\{\delta(n)\}$ is given by $\delta(n)=1 / \sqrt{n}$. (Some other cases occur in Section 4.)

It will be shown that a certain condition of differentiability in quadratic mean (DQM) yields the desired approximability. It will also be shown that the condition DQM must hold at almost every $\theta \in \Theta$, even though we provide examples where it is violated at particular points.

In fact, after deriving some consequences of DQM, we show that it is already implied almost everywhere by the much weaker requirement that if $\sqrt{n}\left|\theta_{n}-\theta_{0}\right|$ stays bounded then $\left\|p_{\theta_{n}}^{n} \wedge p_{\theta_{0}}^{n}\right\|$ does not tend to zero. We also give some conditions which are useful in checking whether DQM holds and compare it with other differentiability conditions used by Fisher, Doob, Dugué, Wald, and Cramér in the theory of maximum likelihood.

To introduce DQM, consider first an experiment $\mathscr{E}=\left\{p_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ where the $p_{\theta}$ are all dominated by some probability measure $\mu$. Then $d p_{\theta}=f_{\theta} d \mu$ for some function $f_{\theta} \geq 0$. Let $\theta \mapsto \xi(\theta)$ be the stochastic process obtained by substituting in $\sqrt{f_{\theta}(\omega)}$ a random element $\omega$ with distribution $\mu$. The process $\xi$ is a second-order process with $E \xi(s) \xi(t)=\rho(s, t)$, where $\rho(s, t)$ is the affinity $\rho(s, t)=\int \sqrt{d p_{s} d p_{t}}$. In this case, condition DQM holds at $\theta_{0}$ if the process $\xi$ is differentiable in quadratic mean at $\theta_{0}$.

More generally, if the $p_{\theta}$ are not dominated, the affinity $\rho(s, t)$ is still a covariance kernel for some second-order process $\xi$. Since differentiability in quadratic mean is a property of the kernel $\rho$, it does not matter which process $\xi$ is used provided $\rho(s, t)=E \xi(s) \xi(t)$. Thus, DQM can be stated as follows.

Condition DQM. The experiment $\mathscr{E}=\left\{p_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ satisfies DQM at $\theta_{0}$ if a process $\xi$ such that $\rho(s, t)=E \xi(s) \xi(t)$ is differentiable in quadratic mean at $\theta_{0}$.

The condition can be stated in a different form using the Hilbert space $\mathscr{H}$ of Section 2. In this case the experiment $\mathscr{E}$ gives a map $\theta \mapsto \sqrt{d p_{\theta}}$ from $\Theta$ to $\mathscr{H}$. Treat the elements of $\Theta$ as row vectors, with norms $|\theta|$. Consider also $k$-dimensional column vectors whose entries are elements of $\mathscr{K}$.

The map $\theta \mapsto \sqrt{d p_{\theta}}$ is called (strongly) (or Fréchet) differentiable at the point $\theta_{0}$ if there is a vector $V$ such that for $\theta \in \Theta$ one can write

$$
\sqrt{d p_{\theta}}-\sqrt{d p_{\theta_{0}}}-\left(\theta-\theta_{0}\right) V=\left|\theta-\theta_{0}\right| R\left(\theta, \theta_{0}\right)
$$

for a term $R \in \mathscr{H}$ such that $\left\|R\left(\theta, \theta_{0}\right)\right\|$ tends to zero as $\left|\theta-\theta_{0}\right| \rightarrow 0$. Thus one can restate DQM as follows.

Condition DQM. The experiment $\mathscr{E}=\left\{p_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ satisfies Condition DQM at $\theta_{0}$ if the map $\theta \mapsto \sqrt{d p_{\theta}}$ from $\Theta$ to $\mathscr{H}$ is differentiable at $\theta_{0}$.

Note that, in both cases, only the behavior on the set $\Theta$ is involved. We do not require that $\theta \mapsto \sqrt{d p_{\theta}}$ be the restriction to $\Theta$ of a map defined on $\mathbb{R}^{k}$ and differentiable at $\theta_{0}$ on $\mathbb{R}^{h}$.

If $\theta \mapsto \sqrt{d p_{\theta}}$ is differentiable at $\theta_{0}$, with vector derivative $V$, the square norms $4\|t V\|^{2}$ taken in $\mathscr{H}$ define a certain quadratic function of $t$ on $\mathbb{R}^{k}$. This yields a Euclidean semi-norm which will be denoted $\|t\|$ below.
