To this semi-norm is associated a Gaussian shift experiment $\mathscr{G}=\left\{G_{t} ; t \in \mathbb{R}^{h}\right\}$ in such a way that the likelihood ratios have the form $d G_{t} / d G_{0}=\exp \left\{t S-\frac{1}{2}\|t\|^{2}\right\}$ for some random vector $S$. Let $V\left(\theta_{0}, n, c\right)=\left\{t ;|t| \leq c, \theta_{0}+(t / \sqrt{n}) \in \Theta\right\}$ and let $\mathscr{G}\left(\theta_{0}, n, c\right)= \left\{G_{t} ; t \in V\left(\theta_{0}, n, c\right)\right\}$ and $\mathscr{F}\left(\theta_{0}, n, c\right)=\left\{p_{\theta_{0}+t / \sqrt{n}}^{n} ; t \in V\left(\theta_{0}, n, c\right)\right\}$.

We are interested in the consequences of DQM for the approximability of $\mathscr{F}\left(\theta_{0}, n, c\right)$ by $\mathscr{G}\left(\theta_{0}, n, c\right)$. To express them we need to introduce the contingent $C\left(\theta_{0}, \Theta\right)$ of $\Theta$ at the point $\theta_{0}$.

The set $C\left(\theta_{0}, \Theta\right)$ is a cone, formed by rays of the type $\{\alpha u ; \alpha \geq 0\}$ where $u$ is an element of $\mathbb{R}^{k}$ such that $|u|=1$. A ray $\{\alpha u ; \alpha \geq 0\}$ belongs to $C\left(\theta_{0}, \Theta\right)$ if there are elements $\theta_{n} \in \Theta$ and numbers $c_{n}$ tending to $+\infty$ such that $c_{n}\left(\theta_{n}-\theta_{0}\right)$ converges to $u$. The contingent is always a closed cone. For any $\theta_{n} \in \Theta$ write $d p_{\theta_{n}}=\left[1-c_{n}+\left(v_{n} / \sqrt{n}\right)\right]^{2} d p_{\theta_{0}}+d p_{\theta_{n}}^{\prime \prime}$ where $1-c_{n}+\left(v_{n} / \sqrt{n}\right) \geq 0$, with $v_{n} \in L_{2,0}\left(p_{\theta_{0}}\right)$, and where $p_{\theta_{n}}^{\prime \prime}$ is the part of $p_{\theta_{n}}$ that is singular with respect to $p_{\theta_{0}}$.

Theorem 1. Let $\mathscr{E}=\left\{p_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ satisfy condition DQM at $\theta_{0}$. Then in order that $\Delta\left[\mathscr{F}\left(\theta_{0}, n, c\right), \mathscr{G}\left(\theta_{0}, n, c\right)\right]$ tend to zero for a given $c \geq 2$, it is necessary and sufficient that if $\left|t_{n}\right| \leq c, \theta_{n}=\theta_{0}+t_{n} / \sqrt{n} \in \Theta$, the singular masses $n\left\|p_{\theta_{n}}^{\prime \prime}\right\|$ tend to zero.

This condition is automatically satisfied if there is a basis $\left\{u_{j} ; j=1, \ldots, k\right\}$ of $\mathbb{R}^{k}$ such that all the rays $\left\{\alpha u_{j} ; \alpha \geq 0\right\}$ and $\left\{-\alpha u_{j} ; \alpha \geq 0\right\}$ belong to the contingent $C\left(\theta_{0}, \Theta\right)$.
Proof. If $\theta_{n}=\theta_{0}+t_{n} / \sqrt{n} \in \Theta$, let $v_{n}\left(t_{n}\right)=\sqrt{n}\left(\sqrt{d p_{\theta_{n}}}-\sqrt{d p_{\theta_{0}}}\right)$ be the corresponding element of the Hilbert space $\mathscr{H}(p)$. The DQM condition says that there is a vector $V$ such that, whenever $\left|t_{n}\right| \leq c$, the norms $\left\|v_{n}\left(t_{n}\right)-t_{n} V^{\prime}\right\|$ taken in $\mathscr{H}(p)$ tend to zero. Thus the $v_{n}\left(t_{n}\right)$ form a relatively compact sequence in $\mathscr{H}(p)$ and the Lindeberg conditions of Section 2 are satisfied. Thus, if $n\left\|p_{\theta_{n}}^{\prime \prime}\right\| \rightarrow 0$, all the conditions of Section 2, Proposition 1 are satisfied. The assertion concerning the distances $\Delta$ follows immediately. The necessity of the condition $n\left\|p_{\theta_{n}}^{\prime \prime}\right\| \rightarrow 0$ is clear, since $\mathscr{G}$ is homogeneous. Let us show that it is automatically satisfied if the contingent $C\left(\theta_{0}, \Theta\right)$ is sufficiently rich.

For this purpose note first the following. Let $s=\xi \sqrt{d \mu}-\sqrt{d p}$ be an arbitrary element of $\mathscr{H}(p)$. Write $\mu=\mu^{\prime}+\mu^{\prime \prime}$ with $\mu^{\prime \prime}$ equal to the part of $\mu$ which is $p$ singular. This gives another element $\xi \sqrt{d \mu^{\prime}}-\sqrt{d p}$ of $\mathscr{H}(p)$. It will be denoted $\Pi \mathrm{s}$. The operation $\Pi$ is an orthogonal projection on a linear subspace $\mathscr{H}^{\prime}(p)$ of $\mathscr{H}(p)$.

Now consider a sequence $\left\{t_{n}\right\}$ such that $\left|t_{n}\right| \leq c$ and such that $\theta_{n}=\theta_{0}+ t_{n} / \sqrt{n} \in \Theta$. Assume that $t_{n} \rightarrow t$. Then $v_{n}\left(t_{n}\right)$ converges to a limit $v=t V$ in $\mathscr{H}(p)$. The projections $\Pi v_{n}\left(t_{n}\right)$ tend to $\Pi(t V)$. The difference between the norms $\|t V\|^{2}-\|\Pi(t V)\|^{2}$ is a positive semi-definite quadratic form on $\mathbb{R}^{k}$. If it vanishes for elements $\left\{u_{j} ; j=1,2, \ldots, k\right\}$ of a basis of $\mathbb{R}^{k}$, it vanishes identically on $\mathbb{R}^{k}$.

Now suppose that the contingent $C\left(\theta_{0}, \Theta\right)$ contains both rays $\left\{\alpha u_{j} ; \alpha \geq 0\right\}$ and $\left\{\alpha\left(-u_{j}\right) ; \alpha \geq 0\right\}$. Then there are elements $\theta_{k} \in \Theta$ and numbers $a_{k}$ tending to infinity such that $a_{k}\left(\theta_{k}-\theta_{0}\right) \rightarrow u_{j}$. There are also elements $s_{k} \in \Theta$ and numbers $b_{k}$ tending to infinity such that $b_{k}\left(s_{k}-\theta_{0}\right) \rightarrow\left(-u_{j}\right)$. It results then from Lemma 5 of Section 2 that $u_{j} V$ and $\Pi\left(u_{j} V\right)$ are the same, and that $n\left\|p_{\theta_{n}}^{\prime \prime}\right\| \rightarrow 0$. This concludes the proof of the theorem.

Remark 1. Let $S$ be the subset of $\Theta$ formed by points $\theta$ at which the contingent $C(\theta, \Theta)$ is not the entire space $\mathbb{R}^{k}$. Then, according to theorems of F . Rogers, the set $S$ is a countable union of sets of finite ( $k-1$ )-dimensional Hausdorff measure. In particular it has Lebesgue ( $k$-dimensional) measure zero. Thus the points at which $C(\theta, \Theta)$ does not satisfy the condition of the theorem are relatively scarce. However, they do occur in many practical cases. Take,
for instance, for $\Theta$ the quadrant $\{x \geq 0, y \geq 0\}$ in the plane. A more specific example is given below.

Remark 2. Let $\Theta=\Theta_{0} \cup \Theta_{1}$ with $\Theta_{0}=[-1,0], \Theta_{1}=[0,1]$ on the line. Let $q_{\theta}$ be the Gaussian measure $\mathcal{N}(\theta, 1)$ on the line and let $m$ be a probability measure which is singular with respect to the Lebesgue measure. Let $p_{\theta}= \left[1-|\theta|^{2}\right] q_{\theta}+|\theta|^{2} m$.

Then the map $\theta \mapsto \sqrt{d p_{\theta}}$ is differentiable on $\Theta_{0}$. It is also differentiable on $\Theta_{1}$. It is not differentiable at $\theta=0$ on $\Theta$ itself. If one writes $p_{\theta}^{\prime \prime}$ for the part of $p_{\theta}$ which is $p_{0}$ singular, then $n\left\|p_{1 / \sqrt{n}}^{\prime \prime}\right\|=1$. However the projections into the space $\mathscr{H}^{\prime}\left(p_{0}\right)$ are differentiable. Equivalently, if one substitutes into the square roots $\sqrt{d p_{\theta}(\omega) / d p_{0}}$ a variable $\omega$ distributed according to $p_{0}$, the process so obtained is differentiable in quadratic mean.

One could prove the first assertion of Theorem 1 in a more direct manner, obtaining supplementary information as follows.

Let $\mathscr{E}=\left\{p_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ be an experiment defined by measures $p_{\theta}$ on a $\sigma$-field $\mathscr{A}$. For each integer $j$, let $\mathscr{E}_{j}=\left\{p_{\theta, j} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ be a copy of $\mathscr{E}$ on a $\sigma$-field $\mathscr{A}_{j}$, copy of $\mathscr{A}$. Assume that, at $\theta_{0}$, the experiment $\mathscr{E}$ satisfies the condition DQM. Consider the Radon-Nikodym densities $f_{\theta}=d p_{\theta} / d p_{\theta_{0}}$ of the part of $p_{\theta}$ which is dominated by $p_{\theta_{0}}$.

Let $Y(\theta)=\sqrt{f_{\theta}}-1$ be considered as a random variable for the distribution induced by $p=p_{\theta_{0}}$. By projection of $\mathscr{H}(p)$ onto $\mathscr{H}^{\prime}(p)$ one sees that there are random vectors $W$ such that

$$
Y(\theta)=\left(\theta-\theta_{0}\right) W+\left|\theta-\theta_{0}\right| R\left(\theta, \theta_{0}\right)
$$

for remainder terms such that $\int R^{2}\left(\theta, \theta_{0}\right) d p \rightarrow 0$ as $\theta \rightarrow \theta_{0}$. The vector $W \sqrt{d p}$ is the projection on $\mathscr{H}^{\prime}(p)$ of the derivative $V$ in $\mathscr{H}(p)$. A first remark is as follows.

Lemma 1. Let condition DQM be satisfied at $\theta_{0}$. Let $t$ be an element of the linear space spanned by the contingent $C\left(\theta_{0}, \Theta\right)$ of $\Theta$ at $\theta_{0}$. Then

$$
\int t W d p=0
$$

Proof. Denote expectations under $p=p_{\theta_{0}}$ by $E$. Then $E Y(\theta)=\rho\left(\theta, \theta_{0}\right)-1$, where $\rho(s, t)=\int \sqrt{d p_{s} d p_{t}}=1-h^{2}(s, t)$ for the square Hellinger distance $h^{2}(s, t)=\frac{1}{2}\left\|\sqrt{d p_{s}}-\sqrt{d p_{t}}\right\|^{2}$. The DQM condition implies that, for any bounded sequence $t_{n}$ such that $\theta_{0}+t_{n} / \sqrt{n} \in \Theta$, the difference between $\left(2 /\left|t_{n}\right|^{2}\right) h^{2}\left(\theta_{0}+\left(t_{n} / \sqrt{n}\right), \theta_{0}\right)$ and $\left\|\left(t_{n} /\left|t_{n}\right|\right) V\right\|^{2}$ tends to zero.

Thus $\left|t_{n}\right|^{-2} h^{2}\left(\theta_{0}+\left(t_{n} / \sqrt{n}\right), \theta_{0}\right)$ remains bounded and $\left|t_{n}\right|^{-1} E Y\left(\theta_{0}+\left(t_{n} / \sqrt{n}\right)\right)$ tends to zero. However, $\left|t_{n}\right|^{-1}\left[Y\left(\theta_{0}+\left(t_{n} / \sqrt{n}\right)\right)-t_{n} W\right]$ tends to zero in quadratic mean. Thus, $E\left[\left(t_{n} /\left|t_{n}\right|\right) W\right] \rightarrow 0$. This gives the desired result.

To state the next result, for each integer $j$, let $\mathscr{E}_{j}=\left\{p_{\theta, j} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ be a copy of $\mathscr{E}=\left\{p_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ on $\sigma$-fields $\mathscr{A}_{j}$, copies of the $\sigma$-field $\mathscr{A}$ which carries the measures $p_{\theta}$. Generally, if $X$ is any term defined on $\mathscr{E}$, let $X_{j}$ be its copy of $\mathscr{E}_{j}$.

Let $\mathscr{E}^{n}$ be the product experiment formed by the product measures $p_{\theta}^{n}= \prod_{j=1}^{n} p_{\theta, j}$. Let $\Lambda_{n}\left(\theta, \theta_{0}\right)$ be the logarithm of likelihood ratio $\Lambda_{n}\left(\theta, \theta_{0}\right)=\log \left(d p_{\theta}^{n} / d p_{\theta_{0}}^{n}\right)$. This is a sum of the analogous logarithms taken on the components. Define a quadratic $\sigma^{2}(t)$ as follows:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma^{2}(t) & =2\left\{\|t V\|^{2}+\|t W \sqrt{d p}\|^{2}\right\} \\
& =2\left\{\|t V\|^{2}+E|t W|^{2}\right\}
\end{aligned}
$$

Theorem 2. Assume that $\mathscr{E}$ satisfies DQM at $\theta_{0}$. Then for every bounded sequence $\left\{t_{n}\right\}$ such that $\theta_{0}+t_{n} / \sqrt{n} \in \Theta$, the differences

$$
\Lambda_{n}\left(\theta_{0}+\frac{t_{n}}{\sqrt{n}}, \theta_{0}\right)-t_{n}\left(\frac{2}{\sqrt{n}} \sum_{j=1}^{n} W_{j}\right)+\frac{1}{2} \sigma^{2}\left(t_{n}\right)
$$

tend to zero in $p_{\theta_{0}}^{n}$ probability. In particular, the difference between the distributions $\mathscr{L}\left\{\Lambda_{n}\left(\theta_{0}+\left(t_{n} / \sqrt{n}\right), \theta_{0}\right) \mid \theta_{0}\right\}$ and the Gaussian $\mathscr{N}\left\{-\frac{1}{2} \sigma^{2}\left(t_{n}\right), E\left|t_{n} W\right|^{2}\right\}$ tends to zero as $n \rightarrow \infty$.

Proof. Consider first the variables $Y_{j}$. One can write

$$
Y_{j}\left(\theta_{0}+\frac{t_{n}}{\sqrt{n}}\right)=\frac{t_{n}}{\sqrt{n}} W_{j}+E Y\left(\theta_{0}+\frac{t_{n}}{\sqrt{n}}\right)+\left|\frac{t_{n}}{\sqrt{n}}\right| S_{n, j}
$$

where $S_{n, j}$ is a replicate of a variable $S_{n}$ such that $E S_{n}=0$ and such that $E S_{n}^{2} \rightarrow 0$.

Thus

$$
\sum_{j=1}^{n}\left[Y_{j}\left(\theta_{0}+\frac{t_{n}}{\sqrt{n}}\right)-\frac{t_{n}}{\sqrt{n}} W_{j}+E Y\left(\theta_{0}+\frac{t_{n}}{\sqrt{n}}\right)\right]
$$

has expectation zero and a variance $\left|t_{n}\right|^{2}$ var $S_{n}$ which tends to zero as $n \rightarrow \infty$.
Since the $W_{j}$ are identically distributed, $\mathscr{L}\left((1 / \sqrt{n}) \sum_{j=1}^{n} W_{j}\right)$ converges to a certain Gaussian distribution, say $\mathscr{L}(Z)$, with $E Z=0, E|t Z|^{2}=E|t W|^{2}$. Consider also $n E Y\left(\theta_{0}+\left(t_{n} / \sqrt{n}\right)\right)=-n h^{2}\left(\theta_{0}+\left(t_{n} / \sqrt{n}\right), \theta_{0}\right)$. According to the DQM condition

$$
n h^{2}\left(\theta_{0}+\frac{t_{n}}{\sqrt{n}}, \theta_{0}\right)-\frac{1}{2}\left\|t_{n} V\right\|^{2} \rightarrow 0
$$

The logarithm of likelihood ratios can be written

$$
\Lambda_{n}\left(\theta_{0}+\frac{t_{n}}{\sqrt{n}}, \theta_{0}\right)=2 \sum_{j=1}^{n} \log \left[1+Y_{j}\left(\theta_{0}+\frac{t_{n}}{\sqrt{n}}\right)\right]
$$

According to the usual Taylor expansion argument

$$
\sum_{j=1}^{n} \log \left[1+Y_{j}\left(\theta_{0}+\frac{t_{n}}{\sqrt{n}}\right)\right]-\sum_{j} Y_{j}\left(\theta_{0}+\frac{t_{n}}{\sqrt{n}}\right)+\frac{1}{2} E\left|t_{n} W\right|^{2}
$$

tends to zero in probability. Therefore

$$
\Lambda_{n}\left(\theta_{0}+\frac{t_{n}}{\sqrt{n}}, \theta_{0}\right)-t_{n}\left(\frac{2}{\sqrt{n}} \sum W_{j}\right)+\left\{\left\|t_{n} V\right\|^{2}+E\left|t_{n} W\right|^{2}\right\}
$$

tends to zero in probability. The result follows.
Remark. In this result it is also visible that if $\theta_{n}=\theta_{0}+t_{n} / \sqrt{n}$, the sequences $\left\{p_{\theta_{n}}^{n}\right\}$ and $\left\{p_{\theta_{0}}^{n}\right\}$ are contiguous if and only if the difference between $\left\|t_{n} V\right\|^{2}$ and $\left\|t_{n} W \sqrt{d p}\right\|^{2}$ tends to zero. This is the condition on convergence of singular parts of Theorem 1.

Up to this point, we have considered only what happens around a given point $\theta_{0}$. Here is a result relative to convergence on bigger sets.

For each $\theta \in \Theta$ and each pair $(n, c)$ let $V(\theta, n, c)=\left\{t ; t \in \mathbb{R}^{k},|t| \leq c, \theta+(t / \sqrt{n}) \in\right. \Theta\}$. Let $\mathscr{F}(\theta, n, c)$ be the experiment $\mathscr{F}(\theta, n, c)=\left\{F_{\theta, n, t} ; t \in V(\theta, n, c)\right\}$, where $F_{\theta, n, t}$ is the product measure $p_{\theta+t / \sqrt{n}}^{n}$. Assume that $\mathscr{E}=\left\{p_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ satisfies DQM at each $\theta \in \Theta$. Let $\sigma_{\theta}^{2}$ be the quadratic defined by

$$
\sigma_{\theta}^{2}(t)=4 \lim _{\varepsilon \rightarrow 0} \frac{1}{\varepsilon^{2}}\left\|\sqrt{d p_{\theta+\varepsilon t}}-\sqrt{d p_{\theta}}\right\|^{2}
$$

Let $\mathscr{G}_{\theta}=\left\{G_{\theta, t} ; t \in \mathbb{R}^{k}\right\}$ be a Gaussian shift experiment corresponding to the quadratic $\sigma_{\theta}^{2}$ and let $\mathscr{G}(\theta, n, c)$ be its restriction to the domain $t \in V(\theta, n, c)$. Let us say that the map $\theta \mapsto \sqrt{d p_{\theta}}$ satisfies DQM uniformly on a set $K \subset \Theta$ if there are vectors $V_{\theta}$ and remainders $R(t, \theta)$ such that $\theta \in K, \theta+t \in \Theta$ implies

$$
\sqrt{d p_{\theta+t}}-\sqrt{d p_{\theta}}=t V_{\theta}+|t| R(t, \theta)
$$

with $\sup \{\|R(t, \theta)\| ; \theta \in K, \theta+t \in \Theta,|t|<\varepsilon\}$ tending to zero as $\varepsilon \rightarrow 0$.
Proposition 1. Assume that $\Theta$ is an open subset of $\mathbb{R}^{k}$. Assume also that the condition DQM holds uniformly on the compact subsets $K \subset \Theta$. Then, for each compact $K \subset \Theta$ and each $c<\infty$

$$
\sup _{\theta \in K} \Delta[\mathscr{G}(\theta, n, c), \mathscr{F}(\theta, n, c)]
$$

tends to zero as $n \rightarrow \infty$.
Proof. For $\varepsilon>0$, let $K^{\varepsilon}$ be the set of points of $\mathbb{R}^{k}$ which are at distance at most $\varepsilon$ from $K$. There is an $\varepsilon>0$ such that $K^{\varepsilon} \subset \Theta$. Thus, there is an $N$ such that $n \geq N$ and $\theta \in K$ implies that both $\theta+(t / \sqrt{n})$ and $\theta-(t / \sqrt{n})$ belong to $\Theta$. The condition DQM implies that $\sqrt{n}\left\{\left(\sqrt{d p_{\theta+t / \sqrt{n}}}-\sqrt{d p_{\theta}}\right)+\left(\sqrt{d p_{\theta-t / \sqrt{n}}}-\sqrt{d p_{\theta}}\right)\right\}$ tends to zero in $\mathscr{H}\left(p_{\theta}\right)$ and thus uniformly for $\theta \in K$. It follows then from Section 2, Lemma 6 that if $\beta(\theta, t, n)$ is the part of $p_{\theta+t / \sqrt{n}}$ which is $p_{\theta}$ singular,
then $n \beta(\theta, t, n) \rightarrow 0$, uniformly for $\theta \in K$ as long as $|t| \leq c$. This takes care of one part of the asymptotic normality requirements.

Now note that $\theta \mapsto \sqrt{d p_{\theta}}$ is continuous, since it is differentiable. Thus the $\operatorname{map} \theta \mapsto(1 / \varepsilon)\left(\sqrt{d p_{\theta+\varepsilon t}}-\sqrt{d p_{\theta}}\right)$ is continuous for each $\varepsilon>0$. The map $\theta \mapsto t V_{\theta}$ is continuous, since it is a uniform limit on compacts of continuous maps. The vector $V_{\theta}$ has a representation $W_{\theta} \sqrt{d p_{\theta}}$ where $W_{\theta}$ is a random vector such that $\left\|t V_{\theta}\right\|^{2}=E\left[\left|t W_{\theta}\right|^{2} \mid p_{\theta}\right]$ for each $t \in \mathbb{R}^{k}$.

Fix an element $\theta \in K \subset \Theta$. Let $s$ be another element of $\Theta$ and let $p_{s}^{\prime}$ be the part of $p_{s}$ which is dominated by $p_{\theta}$. Let $Z_{s}=\left(d p_{s}^{\prime} / d p_{\theta}\right)^{1 / 2}$. Then

$$
\left\|t\left(W_{s} Z_{s}-W_{\theta}\right) \sqrt{d p_{\theta}}\right\|^{2} \leq\left\|t\left[W_{s} \sqrt{d p_{s}}-W_{\theta} \sqrt{d p_{\theta}}\right]\right\|^{2}
$$

tends to zero as $s \rightarrow \theta$.
Also $\int\left[Z_{s}-1\right]^{2} d p_{\theta}$ tends to zero. Thus $\mathscr{L}\left(W_{s} \mid p_{\theta}\right)$ converges to $\mathscr{L}\left(W_{\theta} \mid p_{\theta}\right)$. Since $\left\|p_{s}-p_{\theta}\right\| \rightarrow 0$, this implies that $\mathscr{L}\left(W_{s} \mid p_{s}\right)$ tends to $\mathscr{L}\left(W_{\theta} \mid p_{\theta}\right)$. It follows from this and from the fact that $\int\left|t W_{s}\right|^{2} d p_{s} \rightarrow \int\left|t W_{\theta}\right|^{2} d p_{\theta}$ that $\sup _{s \in K} \int\left(t W_{s}\right)^{2} I\left\{\left|t W_{s}\right|>b\right\} d p_{s}$ tends to zero as $b$ tends to infinity. This is true for each fixed $t$. Therefore it holds true uniformly for $t$ bounded. In conclusion, the Lindeberg conditions are satisfied uniformly on the compact $K$. The desired result follows.

Remark. The assumption of Proposition 1 can be replaced by the equivalent assumption that the condition DQM holds at each point of the open set $\Theta$ with a derivative $V_{\theta}$ which is a continuous function of $\theta$.

The preceding Proposition 1 and Theorems 1 and 2 allow approximations of the product experiments by Gaussian shift experiments around given points of $\Theta$. They do not yet permit the application of the results of Chapter 11 which require the availability of preliminary estimates. We shall return to this matter in Section 5 below.

For the time being, let us note the following. If an experiment $\mathscr{E}=\left\{p_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ satisfies the condition DQM at a particular point $\theta \in \Theta$, then for bounded sequences $\left\{t_{n}\right\}$, the measures $p_{\theta}^{n}$ and $p_{\theta+t_{n} / \sqrt{n}}^{n}$ do not entirely separate as $n \rightarrow \infty$. This lack of complete separation is a much weaker property than DQM, or than the asymptotic normality asserted by Theorem 1 and 2. However, it turns out that this weak property "almost" implies DQM in a sense described below. Consider again an experiment $\mathscr{E}=\left\{p_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ and the associated map $\theta \mapsto \sqrt{d p_{\theta}}$ to the Hilbert space $\mathscr{H}$.

Definition. The map $\theta \mapsto \sqrt{d p_{\theta}}$ satisfies condition ( $l$ ) at the point $\theta \in \Theta$ if

$$
\limsup _{|t-\theta| \rightarrow 0, t \in \Theta} \frac{1}{|t-\theta|}\left\|\sqrt{d p_{t}}-\sqrt{d p_{\theta}}\right\|<\infty
$$

If condition ( $l$ ) is satisfied at $\theta$, then for bounded sequences $\left\{t_{n}\right\}$ the norms $\sqrt{n}\left\|\sqrt{d p_{\theta+t_{n} / \sqrt{n}}}-\sqrt{d p_{\theta}}\right\|$ remain bounded. Therefore $\left\|p_{\theta}^{n} \wedge p_{\theta+t_{n} / \sqrt{n}}^{n}\right\|$ stays bounded away from zero for $n$ sufficiently large. Conversely, suppose that for every bounded sequence $\left\{t_{n}\right\}$ one has

$$
\liminf _{n}\left\|p_{\theta}^{n} \wedge p_{\theta+t_{n} \sqrt{n}}^{n}\right\|>0
$$

Then condition ( $l$ ) holds at $\theta$. Indeed, supposing the contrary, there would be some sequence $\left\{t_{n}\right\}$ such that $t_{n} \rightarrow 0, \theta+t_{n} \in \Theta$, but $\left|t_{n}\right|^{-1} \| \sqrt{d p_{\theta+t_{n}}} \sqrt{d p_{\theta}} \| \rightarrow \infty$. Letting $n$ be the integer part of $\left|t_{n}\right|^{-2}$ and rewriting $t_{n}$ as $t_{n}= \tau_{n} / \sqrt{n}$ gives a contradiction. It is clear that ( $l$ ) is implied by DQM. In the converse direction, one can prove the following.

Theorem 3. Let $S$ be a measurable subset of $\Theta \subset \mathbb{R}^{k}$ such that the map $f$ : $\theta \mapsto \sqrt{d p_{\theta}}$ satisfies condition ( $l$ ) (with respect to $\Theta$ ) at each point of $S$. Then the map $\theta \mapsto \sqrt{d p_{\theta}}$ is differentiable at all points of $S$ except perhaps those of a subset which has Lebesgue measure zero.

The proof of this result is relatively complex. Here, we shall just sketch the various steps leading to it. The lemmas stated below without proof can be found in the appendices of this volume. The statements involve the concept of approximate derivative.

This is defined as follows. Let $\lambda$ denote the Lebesgue measure of $\mathbb{R}^{k}$. Let $S(x, \varepsilon)$ be the ball of radius $\varepsilon$ and center $x$ in $\mathbb{R}^{k}$. Let $f$ be a function defined on a measurable subset $E \subset \mathbb{R}^{k}$ and taking values in a Banach space $\mathscr{X}$. Let $a$ be a vector $a=\left\{a_{1}, a_{2}, \ldots, a_{k}\right\}$ with $a_{j} \in \mathscr{X}$. One says that $a$ is an approximate derivative of $f$ at $x$ relative to $E$ if for every $\delta>0$ the ratio

$$
\frac{1}{\lambda[S(x, \varepsilon)]} \lambda\{y ; y \in E \cap S(x, \varepsilon),\|f(y)-f(x)-a(x-y)\|>\delta|y-x|\}
$$

tends to zero as $\varepsilon$ tends to zero. If $E$ is not measurable, the measures are replaced by the corresponding outer measures.

Here again, as was the case of for derivatives, the relations are relative to the set $E$ on which $f$ is considered. However, for approximate derivatives one can perform operations which are not feasible for derivatives. Suppose, for instance, that $E=\bigcup_{n} E_{n}$ for measurable sets $E_{n}$. Suppose also that, for each $n$, the function $f$ is approximated differentiable relatively to $E_{n}$ at almost all points of $E_{n}$. Then $f$ is approximately differentiable relative to $E$ at almost all points of $E$. The following lemma gives a connection between approximate and true derivatives.

Lemma 2. Let $f$ be a Banach-valued function defined on a set $\Theta \subset \mathbb{R}^{k}$. Let $A$ be a measurable subset of $\Theta$. Assume that $x \in A$ satisfies the following requirements:
(i) $f$ is approximately differentiable at $x$ relatively to $A$.
(ii) $x$ is a point of density of $A$.
(iii) There are numbers $\varepsilon>0, K<\infty$ such that the relations $y \in A, z \in \Theta$ and $\max \{|y-x|,|z-x|\}<\varepsilon$ imply $\|f(y)-f(z)\| \leq K|y-z|$. Then $f$ is differentiable at $x$ relatively to $\Theta$.

For a proof, see the appendices of this volume, or the book Theory of the Integral, by Saks.

This lemma can be applied in the present case as follows. Let $A_{n}$ be the subset of $S$ formed by points $\theta$ such that $t \in \Theta,|t-\theta|<n^{-1}$ implies that $|t-\theta|^{-1}\left\|\sqrt{d p_{t}}-\sqrt{d p_{\theta}}\right\| \leq n$. Then, under the assumptions of Theorem 3, one has $\bigcup_{n} A_{n}=S$. Furthermore, consider two points $\theta \in A_{n}, s \in A_{n}$, and a $t \in \Theta$. Assume that $2|s-\theta|<n^{-1}$ and $2|t-\theta|<n^{-1}$. Then $|s-t|<n^{-1}$ and therefore, by definition of $A_{n}$, one has $\left\|\sqrt{d p_{t}}-\sqrt{d p_{s}}\right\| \leq n|t-s|$. It follows that Lemma 2 is applicable to those points of density of $A_{n}$ where the approximate derivatives exist. Thus, we are reduced to proving existence of approximate derivatives on such sets as $A_{n}$.

One can cover $A_{n}$ by a countable family of measurable sets $A_{n, j}$ such that the diameter of $A_{n, j}$ is at most $n^{-1}$. On such a set $A_{n, j}$ the function $f$ defined by $\theta \mapsto f(\theta)=\sqrt{d p_{\theta}}$ satisfies the Lipschitz condition $|f(s)-f(t)|<n|s-t|$. Retaining only those points of $A_{n, j}$ which are points of density would eliminate only a set of measure zero.

Thus, it is sufficient to show that if $f$ is defined on a measurable set $B$ and satisfies there a Lipschitz condition, it is approximately differentiable at almost all points of $B$. This can be proved by induction on the number of dimensions of $\mathbb{R}^{k}$, if one can prove it for $k=1$. (See the appendices, or Saks' Theory of the Integral, p. 300.)

For the case where $k=1$, consider a compact set $B$ and a function $f$ from $B$ to $\mathscr{H}$ satisfying a Lipschitz condition $\|f(x)-f(y)\| \leq C|x-y|$ on $B$. Then $f$ is continuous and takes values in a separable subspace $\mathscr{H}_{1}$ of $\mathscr{H}$. One can extend $f$ subject to the same Lipschitz condition to an interval $J$ which contains $B$. This can be done, for instance, by linear interpolation on the open intervals of $J \backslash K$.

Consider then $f$ extended to $J$ with values in $\mathscr{H}_{1}$ and let $D$ be a countable dense subset of the unit ball of the dual $\mathscr{H}_{1}^{\prime}$ of $\mathscr{H}_{1}$. For each $g \in D$ the function $x \mapsto\langle g, f(x)\rangle$ is a real valued function satisfying the Lipschitz condition. Thus it is differentiable almost everywhere on $J$. It follows that there is a Lebesgue null subset $N$ of $J$ such that all the maps $\langle g, f\rangle, g \in D$, are differentiable at points of $J \backslash N$. However, the ratios $|x-y|^{-1}[f(x)-f(y)]$ remain in the weakly compact ball of radius $C$ of $\mathscr{H}_{1}$. Therefore, for almost all $x \in J$, the ratios $1 /[y-x][f(y)-f(x)]$ converge weakly to a limit $a(x)$ such that $\|a(x)\| \leq C$. The function $x \mapsto a(x)$ so obtained is bounded, weakly measurable, with values in the separable space $\mathscr{H}_{1}$. Thus it is integrable in the sense of Bochner. However Bochner integrals are strongly differentiable almost everywhere. This completes the sketch of proof for Theorem 3.

A corollary of this theorem is that, with the exception of some singular points, the rate of convergence $1 / \sqrt{n}$ is the slowest rate one needs to consider for experiments indexed by a Euclidean set $\Theta$.

To state this precisely, consider a sequence of numbers $\delta_{n}>0$ such that $\delta_{n} \rightarrow 0$ but $\sqrt{n} \delta_{n} \rightarrow \infty$. Assume that the experiment $\mathscr{E}=\left\{p_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ is such that, for all $\theta \in \Theta$ and all bounded sequences $\left\{t_{n}\right\}$, one has

$$
\liminf _{n}\left\|p_{\theta}^{n} \wedge p_{\theta+\delta_{n} t_{n}}^{n}\right\|>0
$$

Then

$$
\limsup _{|t-\theta| \rightarrow 0}|t-\theta|^{-1}\left\|\sqrt{d p_{t}}-\sqrt{d p_{\theta}}\right\|=0
$$

Therefore the condition DQM is satisfied at almost all points of $\Theta$ with a derivative equal to zero. Consider then any map $\phi$ from $[0,1]$ to $\Theta$ satisfying a Lipschitz condition or condition (l). Let $f(\theta)=\sqrt{d p_{\theta}}$ and let $\psi=f \circ \phi$ be the composed map. This $\psi$ has derivative zero almost everywhere on $[0,1]$. It is of the class $A C G^{*}$ and therefore constant.

If $\Theta$ is a connected set, this implies that the experiment $\mathscr{E}=\left\{p_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ is the trivial experiment. Without some sort of connectedness condition on $\Theta$ one cannot say that $\mathscr{E}$ is trivial. However, one can still say that the set $\left\{p_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ is rather "small", in a sense which can be made precise as follows.

Assume that $\Theta \subset \mathbb{R}^{k}$ is measurable and that condition ( $l$ ) is satisfied by the map $f: \theta \mapsto \sqrt{d p_{\theta}}$ at all points of $\Theta$. Let $C$ be the subset of $\Theta$ where the derivative of $f$ does not exist or where it exists but has rank strictly smaller than $k$. Then the image of $C$ by $f$ has $k$-dimensional Hausdorff measure zero in $\mathscr{H}$. (We shall not prove this here. The proof amounts to show that $f$ satisfies a condition similar to Lusin's condition ( $N$ ) and then applying the argument which gives Sard's theorem.)

All of this may suggest that, if convergence to a Gaussian experiment occurs for neighborhoods which shrink at the rate $1 / \sqrt{n}$, then the map $f$ must satisfy the condition DQM.

We shall presently show that such is not the case. With the notations of Proposition 1, the necessary and sufficient conditions for the convergence of $\Delta[\mathscr{F}(\theta, n, c), \mathscr{G}(\theta, n, c)]$ to zero can be stated as follows.
(a) For any bounded sequence $\left\{t_{n}\right\}$ the product measures $p_{\theta}^{n}$ and $p_{\theta+t_{n} / \sqrt{n}}^{n}$ form contiguous sequences.
(b) The logarithms of likelihood ratio $\log \left(d p_{\theta+t_{n} / \sqrt{n}}^{n} / d p_{\theta}^{n}\right)$ have distributions approximable by Gaussian ones.
(c) Let $C(s, t)$ be the affinity $C(s, t)=\int \sqrt{d p_{s} d p_{t}}$. There is a matrix $\Gamma$ such that if $s_{n} \rightarrow s$ and $t_{n} \rightarrow t$ then

$$
\lim _{\varepsilon \rightarrow 0} \varepsilon^{-2}\left\{C\left(\theta+\varepsilon s_{n}, \theta+\varepsilon t_{n}\right)-C\left(\theta+\varepsilon s_{n}, \theta\right)-C\left(\theta, \theta+\varepsilon t_{n}\right)+C(\theta, \theta)\right\}=\frac{1}{4} s^{\prime} \Gamma t
$$

The condition called (c) above is very close to the condition of differentiability in quadratic mean. However, in that condition, the increments $\varepsilon s_{n}$ and $\varepsilon t_{n}$ tend to zero essentially at the same rate. Thus the condition is formally weaker than the standard condition of differentiability in quadratic mean. It is also effectively weaker as shown by the following example.

Example. Let $(\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{A}, \lambda)$ be the probability space which consists of the interval $[0,1]$ with the Lebesgue measure $\lambda$. Let $\left\{\phi_{k}\right\}$ be an orthonormal sequence of functions. Specifically, assume that $\int \phi_{k} d \lambda=0$ and that $\int \phi_{j} \phi_{k} d \lambda$ is the Kronecker $\delta_{j, k}$. Let $g(t)=|\log t|^{1 / 2}$ for $t$ in an interval [ $0, t_{0}$ ] with $0<t_{0}<1$.



If $k \leq g(t)<k+1$, let $u(t)=[(k+1)-g(t)] \phi_{k}+[g(t)-k] \phi_{k+1}$. Let $\gamma(t)>0$ be defined by $\gamma^{2}(t)=E u^{2}(t)=[k+1-g(t)]^{2}+[g(t)-k]^{2}$. One can verify that $\gamma^{2}(t) \geq 1 / 2$.

Let $a(t)$ be the number defined by the relation $2 a(t)=t\left[1+a^{2}(t)\right]$, with $t / 2<a(t)<t$. Finally, let $X(t)$ be the function $X(t)=t\{(u(t) / \gamma(t))-a(t)\}$. In order that $1+X(t) \geq 0$, it is sufficient that $u(t) \geq \gamma(t) a(t)-(1 / t)$. This will be satisfied for small values of $t$ if the functions $\phi_{k}$ do not take very large negative values. If this positivity requirement for $1+X(t)$ is satisfied then $1+X(t)$ can be treated as the square root of the likelihood ratio, for a measure $p_{t}$ such that $d p_{t}=[1+X(t)]^{2} d \lambda$. Direct computation shows that the second difference involved in condition (c), above, converges to the limit st.

Now consider a particular choice of functions $\phi_{k}$ as follows. Let $\phi_{k}(x)=2^{k / 2}$ if $x \in\left(0,2^{-(k+1)}\right]$. Let $\phi_{k}(x)=-2^{k / 2}$ if $x \in\left(2^{-(k+1)}, 2^{-k}\right]$. For all other values of $x$, let $\phi_{k}(x)=0$.

It is fairly easy to check that for this particular choice of the functions $\phi_{k}$ the following relations hold.
(i) $1+X(t) \geq 0$ if $t \in[0,1 / 2)$ and $k \geq 2$.
(ii) For independent replicates $X_{j}(t)$ of $X(t)$ the sum $\sum_{j=1}^{n} X_{j}(t / \sqrt{n})$ has a distribution which converges to a Gaussian distribution with expectation $-\frac{1}{2} t^{2}$ and variance $t^{2}$.
(iii) The process $X$ is not differentiable in quadratic mean at $t=0$. In fact, $E\left[t^{-1} X(t)\right]^{2}$ tends to unity, but $t^{-m} X(t)$ tends to zero pointwise on $[0,1]$ for every integer $m$.
(iv) There is an $\varepsilon>0$ such that all the measures $p_{t}, t \in[0, \varepsilon]$ are mutually absolutely continuous.

It follows that the corresponding experiments $\mathscr{F}(0, n, c)$ do converge to the required Gaussian limit but that DQM is not satisfied at zero. One can also modify the choice of the orthonormal system $\left\{\phi_{k}\right\}$ to show that the contiguity condition (a) and the condition (c) together are insufficient to imply convergence to a Gaussian limit. For instance, one may obtain limits which have a Poisson component.

Finally, note that the behavior described here is not attributable to the fact that the point at which it occurs is a boundary point of the interval of definition. One can readily extend the definition of the function called $u$ to an interval ( $-\frac{1}{2},+\frac{1}{2}$ ) with the same kind of behavior at zero. (For instance, one could take $u(-t)=u(t)$ for $t>0$.)

In summary, the condition DQM is sufficient to imply asymptotic normality of the experiments $\mathscr{F}(\theta, n, c)$. It is not quite necessary. However, it must hold almost everywhere if the rate $\sqrt{n}$ is the right rate of convergence.

The following result shows that the conditions customarily used in the literature, such as Cramér's conditions, do in fact imply the differentiability in quadratic mean. To state the result, consider an experiment $\mathscr{E}=\left\{p_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ indexed by $\Theta \subset \mathbb{R}^{k}$ and given by measures $p_{\theta}$ on a space ( $\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{A}$ ). Make replicates $\left\{p_{\boldsymbol{\theta}, j} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ and ( $\mathscr{X}_{j}, \mathscr{A}_{j}$ ) of the entire system. Let $X$ be a random vector defined on $(\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{A})$ and let $X_{j}$ be its replicate on $\left(\mathscr{X}_{j}, \mathscr{A}_{j}\right)$. Assume that
the origin 0 of $\mathbb{R}^{k}$ belongs to $\Theta$.
Proposition 2. Let $\Lambda_{n}(t)$ be the logarithm of likelihood ratio of $p_{t}^{n}$ with respect to $p_{0}^{n}$. Assume that the random vectors $X_{j}$ are such that $E X_{j}=0$ and such that $E\left|t X_{j}\right|^{2}<\infty$ for all $t$. Assume, in addition, that if $t_{n} \rightarrow t$ then

$$
\Lambda_{n}\left(\frac{t_{n}}{\sqrt{n}}\right)-\frac{1}{\sqrt{n}} t_{n} \sum_{j=1}^{n} X_{j}+\frac{1}{2} E(t X)^{2}
$$

tends to zero in $p_{0}^{n}$ probability as $n \rightarrow \infty$. Then $\mathscr{E}$ satisfies the condition DQM at $\theta=0$.

Proof. The assumptions made imply the contiguity of the sequences $\left\{p_{0}^{n}\right\}$ and $\left\{p_{t / \sqrt{n}}^{n}\right\}$. They also imply that the affinity between $p_{0}^{n}$ and $p_{t_{n} / \sqrt{n}}^{n}$ tends to $\exp \left\{-\frac{1}{8} E(t X)^{2}\right\}$. Equivalently one can say that $n \int\left[\sqrt{d p_{t_{n} / \sqrt{n}}}-\sqrt{d p_{0}}\right]^{2}$ converges to $\frac{1}{4} E(t X)^{2}$. Thus to prove that DQM is satisfied it will be enough to show that if $Y(t)=\sqrt{d p_{t} / d p_{0}}-1$ then $\sqrt{n} Y\left(t_{n} / \sqrt{n}\right)$ tends to $\frac{1}{2} t X$ in $p_{0}$ measure. To show this, let $\lambda(t)=2 \log [1+Y(t)]$ and let $\lambda_{j}(t)$ be its copy on $\left(X_{j}, A_{j}\right)$.

The assumption made on the $\Lambda_{n}(t)$ can be restated by saying that if $t_{n} \rightarrow t$ then

$$
\sum_{j=1}^{n}\left\{\lambda_{j}\left(\frac{t_{n}}{\sqrt{n}}\right)-\frac{t_{n}}{\sqrt{n}} X_{j}+\frac{1}{2} \frac{1}{n} E(t X)^{2}\right\}
$$

tends to zero in probability. This implies that $\lambda_{j}\left(t_{n} / \sqrt{n}\right)-\left(t_{n} / \sqrt{n}\right) X_{j}$ tends to zero in probability. It also implies that, for every $\varepsilon>0$ the quantity

$$
E\left\{\sqrt{n}\left|\lambda\left(\frac{t_{n}}{\sqrt{n}}\right)-\frac{t_{n}}{\sqrt{n}} X\right|\right\}^{2} I\left\{\left|\lambda\left(\frac{t_{n}}{\sqrt{n}}\right)-\frac{t_{n}}{\sqrt{n}} X\right|<\varepsilon\right\}
$$

tends to zero. Therefore $\sqrt{n}\left[\lambda\left(t_{n} / \sqrt{n}\right)-\left(t_{n} / \sqrt{n}\right) X\right]$ tends to zero in $p_{0}$ probability. This implies that $\sqrt{n} Y\left(t_{n} / \sqrt{n}\right)$ converges to $\frac{1}{2} t X$ in $p_{0}$ probability, concluding the proof of the proposition.

The main difference between the situation considered in this proposition and the previous example is that, here, the linear term has a form $(t / \sqrt{n}) \sum X_{j}$ while in the example it has the form $(t / \sqrt{n}) \sum X_{n, j}$, with $X_{n, j}$ depending on $n$.

To terminate this section we shall give a few results which may occasionally be helpful in checking the DQM condition at least if $\Theta$ is an interval of the line. By the same token, we shall attempt to clarify what should be meant by the Fisher information number.

It is common practice to assume that the measures $p_{\theta}$ of an experiment $\mathscr{E}$ are given by densities $f(x, \theta)$ with respect to some finite or $\sigma$-finite measure $\mu$. It is also common practice to take derivatives with respect to $\theta$ for each fixed $x$ and call Fisher information the number

$$
i(\theta)=\int\left[\frac{\partial}{\partial \theta} f(x, \theta)\right]^{2} \frac{1}{f(x, \theta)} \mu(d x)
$$

Unfortunately, the literature is rather vague about what happens when there are points $\theta$, possibly dependent on $x$, at which the ordinary derivatives fail to exist. There is also a problem about using derivatives of $f$ or $\sqrt{f}$ or certain other functions of $f$.

To link some of the possibilities together, we shall first study derivatives of composed functions. The assertions involve assumptions of absolute continuity (in $\theta$ ) of the density function $f$ itself, or its square root or its logarithm. Since absolute continuity of $f$ does not always imply that of $\sqrt{f}$, we shall use instead of absolute continuity a much weaker condition, called Lusin's condition (N). A function $\phi$ from an interval $J$ of the line to the line satisfies condition (N) if for every set $S \subset J$ which has Lebesgue measure zero, the image $\phi(S)$ also has measure zero. This property is obviously preserved by composition. For any function $\phi$ defined on an interval of the line we shall use a derivative $\dot{\phi}$ defined as follows. If the ordinary derivative $\phi^{\prime}(t)$ exists and is finite at $t$, then $\dot{\phi}(t)=\phi^{\prime}(t)$. Otherwise $\dot{\phi}(t)=0$.

Suppose that $\psi$ maps an interval $I$ into an interval $J$ and that $\phi$ is defined on $J$. The composed function $g=\phi \circ \psi$ has a derivative $\dot{g}$ according to our prescription. It also possesses a "chain rule derivative" $\bar{g}$ which can be written $\bar{g}(t)=\dot{\phi}[\psi(t)] \dot{\psi}(t)$. This is zero unless both derivatives exist and are finite. The following lemma says that under suitable restrictions $g$ is still the indefinite integral of $\bar{g}$.

Lemma 3. Let $\phi$ be a function from an interval $J$ to the line and let $\psi$ be a function from an interval I to J. Assume that both $\phi$ and $\psi$ are continuous and that they satisfy condition ( $N$ ). Suppose also that $\phi$ admits a finite derivative almost everywhere on $J$. Let $g=\phi \circ \psi$. Then for any interval $[a, b] \subset I$ one has

$$
|g(b)-g(a)| \leq \int_{a}^{b}|\bar{g}(t)| d t
$$

If $\bar{g}$ is locally integrable then $g$ is the indefinite Lebesgue integral of $\bar{g}$. Proof. Let $s=\psi(t)$ be a point of $J$ at which the derivative $\phi^{\prime}(s)$ exists, is finite, and nonzero. At such a point one must have $\bar{g}(t)=\dot{g}(t)$. Let $S$ be the subset of $J$ where $\phi^{\prime}$ exists but is zero, and let $T$ be the subset where $\phi^{\prime}$ either does not exist or is infinite. Denoting the Lebesgue measure by $\lambda$, we have assumed that $\lambda(T)=0$. Since $\phi$ satisfies condition (N), this gives $\lambda[\phi(T)]=0$.

According to Theorem 6.5, page 227 of Saks' Theory of the Integral, one has also

$$
\lambda[\phi(S)] \leq \int_{S}\left|\phi^{\prime}(x)\right| d x=0
$$

Thus $\dot{g}(t)=\bar{g}(t)$ except for some set $A$ of values of $t$ whose image by $g$ has measure zero. It follows then from the same theorem of Saks that $\lambda[g(I)] \leq \lambda[g(A)]+\int_{A^{c}}|\bar{g}(t)| d t$.

This gives the first assertion of the lemma. For the second assertion, Saks also says (page 285) that when $g$ satisfies condition ( N ) it will be absolutely
continuous if $\int_{P} g^{\prime}(t) d t<\infty$, where $P$ is the set of points where $g$ has a finite non-negative derivative. This completes the proof of the lemma.

For a possible application of this lemma the reader should think of the case where $J=[0, \infty)$ and where $\phi(t)=\sqrt{t}$ with $\psi(t)$ equal to the value $f(x, t)$ of a probability density. (The proof for $\phi(t)=\sqrt{t}$ instead of a general $\phi$ is not much simpler than the general proof.)

With this we can return to the Fisher information. Assume that the space $(\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{A})$ carries a positive measure $\mu$ and let $f$ be a function defined on $\mathscr{X} \times I$, where $I$ is an interval of the line. Let $f$ be such that $f(x, t) \geq 0$ and such that $\int f(x, t) \mu(d x)=1$. Let $g(x, t)=\sqrt{f(x, t)}$.

For each fixed $x$ define a derivative $\bar{g}(x, t)$ as follows. If $f(x, t)>0$ and if the derivative $f^{\prime}(x, t)=d f(x, t) / d t$ exists and is finite, let

$$
\bar{g}(x, t)=\frac{1}{2} \frac{f^{\prime}(x, t)}{\sqrt{f(x, t)}}
$$

In all other cases put $\bar{g}(x, t)=0$. Let $s(t) \geq 0$ be defined by the integral $s^{2}(t)=\int|\bar{g}(x, t)|^{2} \mu(d x)$. The map $g(x, t)$ induces a map $t \mapsto G(t)$ to the Hilbert space $L_{2}(\mu)$ of equivalence classes of square-integrable functions with respect to $\mu$.

Proposition 3. Assume that, for almost every $x \in \mathscr{X}$, the map $t \mapsto f(x, t)$ is continuous and satisfies Lusin's condition ( $N$ ). Assume also that for every compact interval $[a, b] \subset I$ the integral $\int_{a}^{b} s(t) d t$ is finite.

Then:
(i) The map $t \mapsto G(t)$ is strongly differentiable at almost all points of $I$.
(ii) For almost all $t$ the function $x \mapsto \bar{g}(x, t)$ belongs to the class $G^{\prime}(t)$ of the derivative of $G$ in $L_{2}(\mu)$.
(iii) The function $G$ is an indefinite Bochner-Lebesgue integral of its derivative $G^{\prime}$.

Proof. The continuity of $t \mapsto f(x, t)$ implies that $f$ is jointly measurable and that the family of probability measures defined by $p_{t}(d x)=f(x, t) \mu(d x)$ is dominated by a probability measure. The value of $s^{2}(t)$ does not depend on which dominating measure is used. Thus it is enough to prove the result assuming that $\mu$ itself is a probability measure.

The derivative $\bar{g}$ is also jointly measurable. Therefore Fubini's theorem is applicable. It yields

$$
\int\left\{\int_{a}^{b}|\bar{g}(x, t)| d t\right\} \mu(d x) \leq \int_{a}^{b} s(t) d t
$$

From this follows that for almost all $x$ the integral $\int_{a}^{b}|\bar{g}(x, t)| d t$ is finite. One concludes then from Lemma 3 above and from Saks' Theory of the Integral, page 285, Theorem 7.7, that for almost all $x$ the map $t \mapsto g(x, t)$ is absolutely continuous and such that

$$
g(x, b)-g(x, a)=\int_{a}^{b} \bar{g}(x, t) d t
$$

Now let $v$ be a fixed element of $L_{2}(\mu)$, and let $t \in[a, b]$. The inner product $\langle v, G(t)\rangle$ takes the form

$$
\begin{aligned}
\langle v, G(t)\rangle & =\int v(x) g(x, a) \mu(d x)+\int\left\{v(x) \int_{a}^{t} \bar{g}(x, \tau) d \tau\right\} \mu(d x) \\
& =\int v(x) g(x, a) \mu(d x)+\int_{a}^{t}\left\{\int v(x) \bar{g}(x, \tau) \mu(d x)\right\} d \tau
\end{aligned}
$$

This relation shows that $G$ is the indefinite Pettis integral of the map $\bar{g}$ to $L_{2}(\mu)$ defined by $\tau \mapsto \bar{g}(\cdot, \tau)$. However, $\bar{g}$ is jointly measurable, hence $\bar{g}$ is almost separately valued. Its norm is integrable. Thus the Pettis integral is also a Bochner integral. Its derivative $G^{\prime}(t)$ exists for almost all $t$. From the above relations one sees that $G^{\prime}(t)$ is the class of $\bar{g}(x, t)$ for almost all $t$. This completes the proof of the proposition.

The foregoing Proposition 3 does not identify any of the points at which $G$ may be differentiable. Here is a further remark which does identify almost all of them.

Let us say that a point $\theta$ of an open interval $I$ is a Lebesgue point of the function $s$ if $s(\theta)<\infty$ and if

$$
\lim _{\varepsilon \rightarrow 0} \frac{1}{2 \varepsilon} \int_{-\varepsilon}^{+\varepsilon}|s(\theta+\tau)-s(\theta)| d \tau=0
$$

It is a theorem of Lebesgue that if $s$ is locally integrable, then almost all points of $I$ are Lebesgue points. With this definition and with the notation of Proposition 3 one can state the following.

Proposition 4. Assume that the measure $\mu$ is finite and that for almost all $x$ the map $t \mapsto f(x, t)$ is continuous and satisfies condition ( $N$ ). Let $\theta$ be a Lebesgue point of the function $t \mapsto s(t)$. Then the map $t \mapsto G(t)$ is differentiable at $t=\theta$ and the derivative $G^{\prime}(\theta)$ is the equivalence class of $\bar{g}(x, \theta)$.

Proof. Let $\phi_{\tau}$ be the function defined by $\phi_{\tau}(x)=(1 / \tau)[g(x, \theta+\tau)-g(x, \theta)]$. Write $\bar{g}(x)$ for $\bar{g}(x, \theta)$ and let $A$ be the subset of $\mathscr{X}$ on which $\lim _{\tau \rightarrow 0} \phi_{\tau}(x)= \bar{g}(x)$. The definition of $\bar{g}$ implies that $\lim _{\tau} \inf \left|\phi_{\tau}\right| \geq|\bar{g}|$. Thus, by Fatou's lemma

$$
\liminf _{\tau} \int \phi_{\tau}^{2} d \mu \geq \int \bar{g}^{2} d \mu
$$

To obtain an inequality in the opposite direction, consider a value $t>\theta$. Then

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left\|\frac{1}{t-\theta}\left[G_{t}-G_{\theta}\right]\right\|^{2} & =\frac{1}{(t-\theta)^{2}} \int|g(x, t)-g(x, \theta)|^{2} \mu(d x) \\
& =\int\left|\frac{1}{t-\theta} \int_{\theta}^{t} \bar{g}(x, u) d u\right|^{2} \mu(d x) \\
& \leq \int\left\{\frac{1}{t-\theta} \int_{\theta}^{t}|\bar{g}(x, u)| d u\right\}\left\{\frac{1}{t-\theta} \int_{\theta}^{t}|\bar{g}(x, v)| d v\right\} \mu(d x) \\
& =\frac{1}{(t-\theta)^{2}} \int_{\theta}^{t} \int_{\theta}^{t}\left\{\int|\bar{g}(x, u)||\bar{g}(x, v)| \mu(d x)\right\} d u d v
\end{aligned}
$$

Now

$$
\int|\bar{g}(x, u)||\bar{g}(x, v)| \mu(d x) \leq s(u) s(v)
$$

by Schwarz' inequality. Thus

$$
\left\|\frac{1}{t-\theta}\left[G_{t}-G_{\theta}\right]\right\|^{2} \leq \frac{1}{(t-\theta)^{2}} \int_{\theta}^{t} \int_{\theta}^{t} s(u) s(v) d u d v=\left|\frac{1}{t-\theta} \int_{\theta}^{t} s(u) d u\right|^{2}
$$

Therefore

$$
\left\|\frac{1}{t-\theta}\left[G_{t}-G_{\theta}\right]\right\| \leq s(\theta)+\frac{1}{t-\theta} \int_{\theta}^{t}|s(u)-s(\theta)| d u
$$

If $\theta$ is a Lebesgue point of $s$, this gives

$$
\limsup _{t \rightarrow \theta} \frac{1}{|t-\theta|}\left\|G_{t}-G_{\theta}\right\| \leq s(\theta)
$$

Repeating the argument for $t<\theta$, it follows that $\lim _{\tau \rightarrow 0} \int \phi_{\tau}^{2} d \mu=s^{2}(\theta)$. Consider now the function $\psi_{\tau}$ defined by $\psi_{\tau}=\inf _{|\varepsilon| \leq \tau}\left|\phi_{\varepsilon}\right|$. By construction of $\bar{g}$ we have $\lim _{\tau \rightarrow 0} \psi_{\tau} \geq|\bar{g}|$. Also, $\psi_{\tau}^{2}$ increases to a certain function $v^{2}$ equal to $|\bar{g}|^{2}$ on the set $A$ where $\phi_{\tau}$ tends to $\bar{g}$. The integrals $\int\left[\phi_{\tau}^{2}-\psi_{\tau}^{2}\right] d \mu$ tend to zero and $\int v^{2} d \mu=\int|\bar{g}|^{2} d \mu$. Thus, since $\bar{g}=0$ on $A^{c}$, we can conclude that $v=0$ almost everywhere on $A^{c}$. It follows that $\int_{A^{c}} \phi_{\tau}^{2} d \mu \leq \int_{A^{c}} \psi_{\tau}^{2} d \mu+\int\left[\phi_{\tau}^{2}-\psi_{\tau}^{2}\right] d \mu$ tends to zero as $\tau \rightarrow 0$. One concludes from this that $\phi_{\tau}$ tends to $\bar{g}$ in $\mu$-measure. Since $\int \phi_{\tau}^{2} d \mu \rightarrow \int \bar{g}^{2} d \mu$, it follows that $\int\left|\phi_{\tau}-\bar{g}\right|^{2} d \mu \rightarrow 0$. Hence the result.

The above statements admit certain converses. For instance, suppose that the map $t \mapsto G(t)$ admits almost everywhere on $I$ a weak derivative $G^{\prime}(t)$. Assume also that $t \mapsto G(t)$ is the indefinite Bochner integral of $G^{\prime}$. Then there is a version $g(x, t)$ of $G(t)$ such that the map $t \mapsto g(x, t)$ is absolutely continuous for almost all $x$ and to which all the above arguments can be applied.

Propositions 3 and 4 require a moderate amount of integrability of either the function $s(t)$ or the norm $\left\|G^{\prime}(t)\right\|$. This forces the densities $f(x, t)$ to be continuous in $t$. However differentiability of $G$ by itself does not imply such continuity
or integrability. There are processes which are differentiable in quadratic mean but with discontinuous trajectories even for a set of indices which is an interval $I=[a, b]$ of the line. If condition ( $l$ ) is satisfied at all points of $I$ the derivative $G^{\prime}$ and the value $G(a)$ determine the function $G$ entirely. It is a Denjoy-Perron integral of $G^{\prime}$, but it need not be a Bochner integral.

However, one should especially note that continuity of the trajectories is not at all implied by the differentiability in quadratic mean as soon as the parameter set $\Theta$ is allowed to have two dimensions or more. For instance, in $\mathbb{R}^{3}$ one can consider densities of the type $C|x-\theta|^{-1 / 2} \exp \left\{-|x-\theta|^{2}\right\}$ with respect to the Lebesgue measure. These satisfy DQM, but the trajectories have an infinite discontinuity at $\theta=x$. (This example was communicated to me by J. B. H. Kemperman.) For $\mathbb{R}^{2}$, the construction is somewhat more delicate. However, one can construct examples of shift families where the density is everywhere extremely smooth except that near zero it behaves like $\log |\log | x|\mid$. Such families satisfy the DQM condition, but the trajectories are discontinuous.

As to what meaning should be attached to the number called $s^{2}(\theta)= \int|\bar{g}(x, \theta)|^{2} \mu(d x)$ when it differs from the limit of $\left\|(1 / t-\theta)\left(G_{t}-G_{\theta}\right)\right\|^{2}$, the situation may be complex, but it appears that typically $s^{2}(\theta)$ is devoid of any reasonable meaning.

We have given in this section, following Theorem 3, an example of a situation where the local experiments $\mathscr{F}(\theta, n, c)$ do converge to a nondegenerate Gaussian experiment but where $\bar{g}(x, 0)=0$ everywhere. In fact, using smooth functions instead of piecewise linear interpolation, it is easy to construct a family of densities $f(x, \theta), \theta \in(-1,+1)$ where $f$ is, for each $x$, indefinitely differentiable as functions of $\theta$ with all the derivatives vanishing for $\theta=0$. That does not prevent the experiments $\mathscr{F}(0, n, c)$ from converging to a Gaussian experiment equivalent to the shift family $\mathscr{N}\left(t, \frac{1}{4}\right)$.

There are also classical examples where the derivatives exist and are zero almost everywhere but where the situation is entirely different from the Gaussian one. An example is the shift family obtained by shifting the uniform distribution on $[0,1]$. This example shows also that in Proposition 4 the continuity condition cannot be omitted.

Propositions 3 and 4 rely on fairly strong assumptions. However, they can still be convenient as can be judged by the shift families where $\theta \in \mathbb{R}$. Assuming that $f(x, \theta)=f(x-\theta)$ is a density with respect to Lebesgue measure, the function $s$ of Proposition 4 is a constant. The conditions reduce to continuity, condition (N) and the inequality $\int\left(\left|f^{\prime}(x)\right|^{2} / f(x)\right) d x<\infty$.

For parameters $\theta \in \mathbb{R}^{k}, k>1$, the situation is more complex. Sometimes the simplest method of proving differentiability in quadratic mean is to first prove differentiability in measure. Then one checks the convergence of the appropriate quadratic norms.

Some other techniques can also be useful. One is as follows. Let $\theta \mapsto f(\theta)= \sqrt{d p_{\theta}}$ be the usual map to the Hilbert space $\mathscr{H}$.

Assume that (i) the map $f$ satisfies a Lipschitz condition and (ii) there is a vector $V$ such that if $t \in[0, \infty)$ tends to zero then $1 / t\|f(\theta+t u)-f(\theta)-t u V\|$
tends to zero for each fixed unit vector $u$. Then $f$ is differentiable at $\theta$.
This almost reduces the problem to a one-dimensional one. The Lipschitz condition insures the validity of the integrability conditions of Propositions 3 or 4 . Thus one needs only to check that the functions $t \mapsto f(\theta+t u)$ are continuous and satisfy condition ( N ). Then one checks the linearity of the limits for the existence of the vector $V$. One could apply this in particular to the case of densities of the type $C|x-\theta|^{-1} \exp \left\{-|x-\theta|^{2}\right\}$ in $\mathbb{R}^{4}$. However, here, by Theorem 3, it is sufficient to check that the Lipschitz condition holds. Without the Lipschitz condition one could check that for every differentiable map $\phi$ from $[0,1]$ to $\Theta$ with $\phi(0)=\theta$, the composed map $t \mapsto f[\phi(t)]$ is differentiable.

## 4. Asymptotic Normality for Rates Other than $\sqrt{n}$

Let $\mathscr{E}=\left\{p_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ be an experiment indexed by an open subset $\Theta$ of $\mathbb{R}^{k}$. Let $\delta_{n}>0$ be a number. Assume that $\delta_{n} \rightarrow 0$ as $n \rightarrow \infty$. For each $c \in(0, \infty)$ and each $n$ let $\mathscr{F}(\theta, n, c)$ be the experiment $\mathscr{F}(\theta, n, c)=\left\{p_{\boldsymbol{\theta}+\delta_{\boldsymbol{n}}}^{\boldsymbol{n}} ; t \in \mathbb{R}^{\boldsymbol{k}}, \theta+\delta_{\boldsymbol{n}} t \in \Theta\right.$, $|t| \leq c\}$ indexed by the parameter $t$ for $\theta$ fixed. Let $\mathscr{G}=\left\{G_{t} ; t \in \mathbb{R}^{k}\right\}$ be a Gaussian experiment linearly indexed by $\mathbb{R}^{k}$.

We have just seen that when $\delta_{n}=1 / \sqrt{n}$ the distance between $\mathscr{F}(\theta, n, c)$ and the corresponding restriction $\mathscr{G}(\theta, n, c)$ tends to zero under a condition akin to differentiability in quadratic mean. The question arises naturally whether there are cases where such a convergence occurs for sequences $\left\{\delta_{n}\right\}$ such that $\sqrt{n} \delta_{n}$ tends to zero or infinity. Our first proposition below indicates some limitations on the possible rates $\delta_{n}$.

Proposition 1. Let $\mathscr{G}=\left\{G_{t} ; t \in \mathbb{R}^{k}\right\}$ be a Gaussian shift experiment linearly indexed by $t$. Assume that $s \neq t$ implies $G_{s} \neq G_{t}$. Assume also that, for some $c>0$, the distances $\Delta[\mathscr{F}(\theta, n, c), \mathscr{G}(\theta, n, c)]$ tend to zero as $n \rightarrow \infty$. Let $\phi(n)=\delta_{n} \sqrt{n}$. Then $\phi$ is a slowly varying function in the sense that if $m_{n}$ is a sequence of integers such that $0<\liminf \left(m_{n} / n\right) \leq \limsup \left(m_{n} / n\right)<\infty$, then $\phi\left(m_{n}\right) / \phi(n)$ tends to unity.

Proof. We shall write $\delta(n)$ instead of $\delta_{n}$. Let $h(s)$ be the Hellinger distance between $p_{\theta}$ and $p_{\theta+s}$. The assumption made implies that there is some Euclidean norm such that $n h^{2}\left(\delta(n) t_{n}\right)-\left\|t_{n}\right\|^{2}$ tends to zero whenever $\left|t_{n}\right|<c$. Subject to the same condition, the differences $m_{n} h^{2}\left(\delta\left(m_{n}\right) t_{n}\right)-\left\|t_{n}\right\|^{2}$ also tend to zero. However, one can write $m_{n} h^{2}\left[\delta\left(m_{n}\right) t\right]=\left(m_{n} / n\right) n h^{2}\left[\delta(n)\left(\delta\left(m_{n}\right) / \delta(n)\right) t\right]$. Thus, if $\delta\left(m_{n}\right) / \delta(n) \rightarrow 0$, the limit of $m_{n} h^{2}\left[\delta\left(m_{n}\right) t\right]$ would be zero. Similarly, writing

$$
n h^{2}[\delta(n) t]=\frac{n}{m_{n}} m_{n} h^{2}\left[\delta\left(m_{n}\right) \frac{\delta(n)}{\delta\left(m_{n}\right)} t\right]
$$

one sees that $\delta(n) / \delta\left(m_{n}\right)$ stays away from zero.
Taking a subsequence if necessary, assume that $\delta\left(m_{n}\right) / \delta(n)$ converges to a limit, say $a \in(0, \infty)$. Let $t_{n}=\left(\delta\left(m_{n}\right) / \delta(n)\right) t$. Then $t_{n} \rightarrow a t$ and $n h^{2}\left(\delta(n) t_{n}\right)$ tends to $a^{2}\|t\|^{2}$. This can also be stated by saying that $\left(n / m_{n}\right) m_{n} h^{2}\left[\delta\left(m_{n}\right) t\right]$ tends to $a^{2}\|t\|^{2}$. However, $m_{n} h^{2}\left[\delta\left(m_{n}\right) t\right]$ tends to $\|t\|^{2}$. It follows that $n / m_{n}$ tends
to $a^{2}$. Equivalently, $\left(n / m_{n}\right)\left[\delta(n) / \delta\left(m_{n}\right)\right]^{2}$ tends to unity, yielding the desired result.

In the preceding statement, the product $\delta_{n} \sqrt{n}$ may tend to any limit in $[0, \infty]$. When $\Theta$ is an interval of the line, it is easy to see that the assumptions of Proposition 1 do not imply any other restrictions on the variation of $\delta_{n} \sqrt{n}$. To show this, let $\Theta=[0,1)$ and let $\mathscr{E}=\left\{p_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ be an experiment which satisfies the asymptotic normality requirements of Theorem 2, Section 3 at $\theta=0$. Let $\Lambda_{n}(t)$ be the logarithm of likelihood ratio of $p_{t}^{n}$ with respect to $p_{0}^{n}$. Then, there are random variables $Z_{j}$ and a number $\sigma^{2}$ such that

$$
\Lambda_{n}\left(\frac{t}{\sqrt{n}}\right)-\frac{t}{\sqrt{n}} \sum_{j=1}^{n} Z_{j}+\frac{1}{2} \sigma^{2} t^{2}
$$

tends to zero in probability. Changing variables, let $s=t \phi(t)$ where $\phi$ is slowly
varying and such that $f(t)=t \phi(t)$ is continuous, strictly increasing with $f(0)=$ 0 . Define $\delta(n)$ by the relation $\delta(n)=(1 / \sqrt{n}) \phi(1 / \sqrt{n})$. For a fixed value of $s$ the relation $\delta(n) s=\left(t_{n} / \sqrt{n}\right) \phi\left(t_{n} / \sqrt{n}\right)$ defines a certain $t_{n}$. This relation can also be written $t_{n} \phi\left(t_{n} / \sqrt{n}\right)=\phi(1 / \sqrt{n})$ s. It is satisfied by a sequence $\left\{t_{n}\right\}$ such that $t_{n} \rightarrow s$. Since $t_{n}$ was uniquely defined, one concludes that $t_{n} \rightarrow s$.

Now let $\left\{p_{s}^{*} ; s \in[0, \phi(1))\right\}=\mathscr{E}^{*}$ be the experiment where $p_{s}^{*}=p_{t}$ where $s=t \phi(t)$. Let $\Lambda_{n}^{*}(s)$ be the logarithm of likelihood of $\left(p_{s}^{*}\right)^{n}$ with respect to $\left(p_{0}^{*}\right)^{n}$. One has $\Lambda_{n}^{*}[\delta(n) s]=\Lambda_{n}\left(t_{n} / \sqrt{n}\right)$ by construction. However, $\Lambda_{n}\left(t_{n} / \sqrt{n}\right)$ is asymptotically equivalent to $\left(t_{n} / \sqrt{n}\right) \sum_{j} Z_{j}-\frac{1}{2} \sigma^{2} t_{n}^{2}$. This differs from $(s / \sqrt{n}) \sum_{j} Z_{j}- \frac{1}{2} \sigma^{2} s^{2}$ by a quantity which tends to zero in probability. Therefore $\mathscr{E}^{*}$ satisfies the desired asymptotic normality requirement with the rate of convergence $\delta(n)$ such that $\delta(n) \sqrt{n}=\phi(1 / \sqrt{n})$.

The experiment $\mathscr{E}^{*}$ constructed here is of no particular interest. Rates $\delta(n)$ such that $\sqrt{n} \delta(n) \rightarrow \infty$ are not very interesting either since, according to Theorem 3, Section 3, they cannot hold on large sets without implying the triviality of the experiment. However, it happens that rates of convergence such that $\sqrt{n} \delta(n) \rightarrow 0$ do occur naturally.

One case in point is the case of the triangular densities $[1-|x-\theta|]^{+}$with respect to the Lebesgue measure on the line. One can verify that for these the appropriate rate of convergence is $\sqrt{n \log n}$ instead of $\sqrt{n}$.

A more interesting example is the one studied by Prakasa Rao. This author considers densities of the form $C \exp \left\{-|x-\theta|^{\alpha}\right\}$ with respect to the Lebesgue measure on the line. For $\alpha>\frac{1}{2}$ the condition DQM is satisfied. For $\alpha=\frac{1}{2}$ one can take $\delta(n)=(n \log n)^{-1 / 2}$ and still approximate the corresponding experiments $\mathscr{F}(\theta, n, c)$ of Proposition 1 by linearly indexed Gaussian experiments.

For $\alpha \in(0,1 / 2)$ the situation is quite different. The appropriate $\delta(n)$ has the form $\delta(n)=n^{-1 /(2 \alpha+1)}$. This precludes the possibility of approximation by a linearly indexed Gaussian shift experiment. However, the local experiments $\mathscr{F}(\theta, n, c)$ are still approximable by Gaussian shift experiments. Specifically, one can approximate the $\mathscr{F}(\theta, n, c)$ by a Gaussian shift experiment whose covariance
structure corresponds to that of a Gaussian process $\xi$ such that $E|\xi(s)-\xi(t)|^{2}= C|s-t|^{2 \alpha+1}$ for a suitable constant $C$.

The asymptotic behavior of the $n$th products of the experiments listed here can be obtained by direct computation. However, one can often reduce such computations to simpler forms according to the following principles.

Consider a $\sigma$-finite measure $\mu$ on a set $(\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{A})$. Let $p_{\theta}(d x)=f(x, \theta) \mu(d x)$ for a density $f$. Let us say that $\left\{p_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ accepts the rate of convergence $\delta(n)$ at $\theta$ if for $t_{n} \rightarrow t,|t|>0$ the square distances $n \int \mid f^{1 / 2}\left[x, \theta+\delta(n) t_{n}\right]- \left.f^{1 / 2}(x, \theta)\right|^{2} \mu(d x)$ remain bounded away from zero and infinity. Let us say that $\left\{p_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ degenerates at the rate $\delta(n)$ if $t_{n} \rightarrow t$ implies that the same distances tend to zero.

Now assume that $f$ has the form $f(x, \theta)=g(x, \theta)+h(x, \theta)$ where $g$ and $h$ are positive functions. Assume that $g$ accepts the rate $\delta(n)$ but that $h$ degenerates for it. Then $f$ will accept the rate $\delta(n)$ and, in all computations which involve asymptotic behavior for a finite set of values of $t$, one can neglect $h$.

This can be seen directly. It can also be seen by noting that for every set $F_{n}$ with $\sup _{n} \operatorname{card} F_{n}<\infty$ one can replace the experiment $\left\{p_{\theta+\delta(n) t}^{n} ; t \in F_{n}\right\}$ by the accompanying Poisson experiment $\mathscr{P}_{n}$.

The experiment $\mathscr{P}_{n}$ is the Poisson experiment based on measures which have densities $n f$ with respect to $\mu$. This $\mathscr{P}_{n}$ is the direct product of Poisson experiments $\mathscr{P}_{n}^{\prime}$ and $\mathscr{P}_{n}^{\prime \prime}$, respectively, based on measures with densities $n g$ and $n h$. However, by assumption, $\mathscr{P}_{n}^{\prime \prime}$ tends to the trivial experiment. Similar assertions can be made for densities $f$ which are products $f(x, \theta)=g(x, \theta) h(x, \theta)$ with analogous behavior for $g$ and $h$.

For the case of densities such as $[1-|x-\theta|]^{+}$or $C \exp \left\{-|x-\theta|^{\alpha}\right\}, 0<\alpha \leq \frac{1}{2}$, this means that one can neglect everything, except the immediate neighborhood of the "singular" points. These are $x=\theta-1$ and $x=\theta+1$ for the case $[1-|x-\theta|]^{+}$. For the other example, the only singular point is the point $x=\theta$.

As an example we shall briefly sketch what happens in the case of the densities $C \exp \left\{-|x-\theta|^{\alpha}\right\}$ for $\alpha>0$. Let $p_{\theta}$ be the corresponding measure and consider the quantity $n h^{2}(\delta(n) t)$ where $t>0$ and where $h(s)$ is the Hellinger distance between $p_{0}$ and $p_{s}$. The square Hellinger distance involves an integral taken from $-\infty$ to $+\infty$. Consider first the part of the integral for the interval $[2 \delta(n) t, \varepsilon]$ where $\varepsilon$ is a small positive number. This is roughly proportional to

$$
\begin{aligned}
A(n) & =n \int_{2 \delta(n) t}^{\varepsilon}\left[|x-\delta(n) t|^{\alpha}-|x|^{\alpha}\right]^{2} d x \\
& =\left\{\int_{2}^{b_{n}}\left[|y-1|^{\alpha}-|y|^{\alpha}\right]^{2} d y\right\} n|\delta(n) t|^{2 \alpha+1}
\end{aligned}
$$

with $b_{n}=\varepsilon[\delta(n) t]^{-1}$.
The difference $|y-1|^{\alpha}-|y|^{\alpha}$ behaves like $|y|^{\alpha}(1 / y)$, so that if $\alpha<\frac{1}{2}$, the function under the integral sign is integrable on ( $2, \infty$ ). It follows that $A(n)$ behaves approximately proportionally to $n[\delta(n)]^{2 \alpha+1}|t|^{2 \alpha+1}$ and stabilizes for



$n[\delta(n)]^{2 \alpha+1}=1$. If $\alpha=\frac{1}{2}$, the same argument shows that $A(n)$ is of the order of magnitude

$$
n[\delta(n)]^{2 \alpha+1}|t|^{2 \alpha+1}|\log [\delta(n)]|=t^{2} n \delta^{2}(n)|\log \delta(n)| .
$$

This stabilizes for $\delta(n)=[n \log n]^{-1 / 2}$.
If $\alpha>\frac{1}{2}$, the integral gives a term proportional to $[\delta(n)]^{-[2 \alpha-1]}$ which cancels out part of the term $n[\delta(n)]^{2 \alpha+1}$ leaving for $A(n)$ a quantity of the type $n[\delta(n)]^{2} t^{2}$ which behaves for $\delta(n)=1 / \sqrt{n}$.

This yields certain possible rates of convergence. To check that they are indeed the appropriate rates it is sufficient to show that the integrals $n \int_{0}^{2 \delta(n) t}\left[|x-\delta(n) t|^{\alpha}-|x|^{\alpha}\right]^{2} d x$ do not tend to infinity. In fact these integrals are equal to $n[\delta(n)]^{2 \alpha+1} \int_{0}^{2}\left[|y-1|^{\alpha}-|y|^{\alpha}\right]^{2} d y$. They always remain bounded and even tend to zero if $\alpha \geq \frac{1}{2}$.

The calculations carried out above also yield information on the covariance of the limiting processes. To check that these processes are indeed Gaussian is easy in the present case.

As the reader may have noticed, the two families $[1-|x-\theta|]^{+}$and $C \exp \left\{-|x-\theta|^{1 / 2}\right\}$ both lead to a rate of convergence $\delta(n)=[n \log n]^{-1 / 2}$. The function $\sqrt{\log n}$ is a slowly varying function, as required by Proposition 1. However, one can readily obtain a variety of other slowly varying functions by modifications of the above examples. For instance, one may consider families of the type $C \exp \left\{-|x-\theta|^{1 / 2} \phi(x-\theta)\right\}$, where $\phi \geq 0$ varies slowly at zero and is otherwise smooth.

There is, however, a reason for the fact that in the two families described here the rates are the same. It is as follows. Let $g$ be a non-negative function such that $g(0)=0$. If $f_{1}(x-\theta)=g^{2}(x-\theta)$, then for small values of $t$, the square Hellinger distances will contain a term of the type $\int_{0}^{\varepsilon}|g(x-t)-g(x)|^{2} d x$. If $f_{2}(x-\theta)=\exp \{-2 g(x-\theta)\}$, the Hellinger distances contain a term of the type

$$
\int_{0}^{\varepsilon} e^{-2 g(x)}\left|e^{-[g(x-t)-g(x)]}-1\right|^{2} d x
$$

which, if $g$ is continuous at zero, can be approximated by $\int_{0}^{\varepsilon} \mid g(x-t)- \left.g(x)\right|^{2} d x$. If $g$ is otherwise smooth and bounded away from zero, this shows that the two families given by $f_{1}$ and $f_{2}$ behave in a similar manner.

Finally, note that the mere existence of a discontinuity in the derivatives of the densities is not necessarily an indication that the DQM condition fails. Densities proportional to $(x-\theta)^{\alpha}(1+\theta-x)^{\beta}$ for $\theta<x<\theta+1$ and equal to zero otherwise satisfy the DQM condition if $\min (\alpha, \beta)>1$. We have also seen above that the densities $C \exp \left\{-|x-\theta|^{\alpha}\right\}$ satisfy the DQM condition for $\alpha>\frac{1}{2}$.

## 5. Existence of Consistent Estimates

Let $\mathscr{P}$ be a set of probability measures on a space $(\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{A})$. For any $p \in \mathscr{P}$, let $\left(\mathscr{X}^{n}, \mathscr{A}^{n}, p^{n}\right)$ be the direct product of $n$ replicates of ( $\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{A}, p$ ). Let $\phi$ be a numerical function defined on $\mathscr{P}$. For each $n$, let $T_{n}$ be a numerical $\mathscr{A}^{n}$
measurable function defined on $\mathscr{X}^{n}$. One says that $\left\{T_{n}\right\}$ is a consistent sequence of estimates of $\phi$ on $\mathscr{P}$ if, for each $p \in \mathscr{P}$, the $T_{n}$ tend in probability to $\phi(p)$. Equivalently $E\left\{\left|T_{n}-\phi(p)\right| \wedge 1 \mid p\right\} \rightarrow 0$.

The purpose of this section is to give necessary and sufficient conditions on $\phi$ for the existence of consistent estimates of $\phi$. Actually, we shall consider a more general problem as follows.

Let $\mathscr{F}$ be a family of subsets of $\mathscr{P}$. Assume that $\mathscr{F}$ is increasingly directed in the sense that if $F_{i} \in \mathscr{F}, i=1,2$, there exists an $F_{3} \in \mathscr{F}$ such that $F_{1} \cup F_{2} \subset F_{3}$. We shall look for conditions which are equivalent to the existence of estimates $T_{n}$ such that $\sup _{p \in F} E\left\{\left|T_{n}-\phi(p)\right| \wedge 1 \mid p\right\}$ tends to zero for every $F \in \mathscr{F}$. In this case $\left\{T_{n}\right\}$ is called uniformly consistent on the elements of $\mathscr{F}$. The answer to the problem will be given in terms of a certain uniform structure $\mathscr{U}$ which looks rather inaccessible. However, there are enough easily tractable special cases to make the results worthwhile.

The reader will note that here we have specified that the $T_{n}$ must be measurable functions defined on $\left(\mathscr{X}^{n}, \mathscr{A}^{n}\right)$. This departs from our usual definitions where $T_{n}$ could be any transition from the $L$-space of the experiment $\left\{p^{n} ; p \in \mathscr{P}\right\}$ to measures or more general objects on $\mathbb{R}$. If general transitions are used the structure $\mathscr{U}$ will need to be modified. We shall return to this problem later on in this section.

The structure $\mathscr{U}$ is defined in the following manner. For each integer $n$, let $\mathscr{U}_{n}$ be the structure defined on $\mathscr{P}$ by taking as vicinities of the diagonal of $\mathscr{P} \times \mathscr{P}$ sets of the form

$$
\left\{(p, q) ; \sup _{j \in J}\left|\int f_{j}\left(d p^{n}-d q^{n}\right)\right| \leq 1\right\},
$$

where $J$ is a finite set and where $f_{j}$ is a bounded measurable real function on $\left(\mathscr{X}^{n}, \mathscr{A}^{n}\right)$. The structure $\mathscr{U}$ is the union of the structures $\mathscr{U}_{n}$.

It is immediate that each $\mathscr{U}_{n}$ is a separated precompact structure on $\mathscr{P}$ and that the same is true of $\mathscr{U}$.

A first easy lemma is as follows. Let $Z$ be a metric space with a distance denoted by $\rho$. Let $\mathscr{B}$ denote the Borel field of ( $Z, \rho$ ).

Lemma 1. Let $\phi$ be a function from $\mathscr{P}$ to the metric space $(Z, \rho)$. Assume that $\phi$ is uniformly continuous for $\mathscr{U}$ on $\mathscr{P}$ and $\rho$ on $Z$. Then, for each $\varepsilon>0$, there exists an integer $N$ such that,for all $n \geq N$, there is a measurable function from $\left(\mathscr{X}^{n}, \mathscr{A}^{n}\right)$ to $(Z, \mathscr{B})$ such that $E_{p} 1 \wedge \rho\left(T_{n}, \phi(p)\right)<\varepsilon$ for all $p \in \mathscr{P}$.

Proof. Replacing $\rho$ by $1 \wedge \rho$, we may as well assume that $\rho \leq 1$. The uniform continuity of $\phi$ implies the existence of an integer $m$ and of a finite set $f_{j} ; j \in J$ of bounded measurable numerical functions defined on $\left(\mathscr{X}^{m}, \mathscr{A}^{m}\right)$ such that if $\left|\int f_{j}\left(d p^{m}-d q^{m}\right)\right| \leq 1$ for all $j \in J$ then $\rho[\phi(p), \phi(q)]<\varepsilon$. Let $\left\|f_{j}\right\|$ be the uniform norm of $f_{j}$ and let $M$ be the first integer such that $32 \sum_{j \in J}\left\|f_{j}\right\|^{2}<\varepsilon M$. Let $\xi_{r}, r=1,2, \ldots, M$ be $M$ independent observations each distributed on $\left(\mathscr{X}^{m}, \mathscr{A}^{m}\right)$ according to one of the measures $p^{m}, p \in \mathscr{P}$. Let $S_{j}=M^{-1} \sum_{r=1}^{M} f_{j}\left(\xi_{r}\right)$.

Then, according to Chebyshev's inequality

$$
\sum_{j \in J} \operatorname{Pr}\left\{\left|S_{j}-E f_{j}\left(\xi_{r}\right)\right| \geq \frac{1}{4}\right\} \leq \frac{16}{M} \sum_{j}\left\|f_{j}\right\|^{2} \leq \frac{\varepsilon}{2}
$$

Since $\mathscr{U}$ is precompact, there is some finite set, say $D \subset \mathscr{P}$, such that for each $p \in \mathscr{P}$ there is a $q \in D$ for which $\sup _{j \in J}\left|E_{p} f_{j}-E_{q} f_{j}\right|<\frac{1}{4}$. Consider for each $p$ the quantity $\gamma(p)=\sup _{j}\left|S_{j}-E_{p} f_{j}\right|$ and order the elements of $D$ in a sequence. Take the first $\hat{q}$ in $D$ such that $\gamma(\hat{q})=\min \{\gamma(q) ; q \in D\}$. This gives a measurable map from the space $\left(\mathscr{X}^{N}, \mathscr{A}^{N}\right), N=m M$, to $D$. Now let $p$ be the true distribution. Then according to the Chebyshev inequality $\gamma(p)<\frac{1}{4}$ except for cases
of total probability at most $\varepsilon / 2$. If $\gamma(p)<\frac{1}{4}$, then $\gamma(\hat{q})<\frac{1}{2}$. By addition one obtains $\left|E\left(f_{j} \mid \hat{q}\right)-E\left(f_{j} \mid p\right)\right|<\frac{1}{4}$ and therefore $\rho[\phi(\hat{q}), \phi(p)]<\varepsilon / 2$. The map $T_{N}$ given by $\phi(\hat{q})$ is measurable. It satisfies the inequality $E_{p} 1 \wedge \rho\left[T_{N}, \phi(p)\right]<\varepsilon$ for all $p \in \mathscr{P}$. This implies the desired result.

A corollary of this lemma is as follows.
Proposition 1. Let $\mathscr{F}$ be an increasingly directed family of subsets of $\mathscr{P}$. Let $\phi$ be a function from $\mathscr{P}$ to a metric space $(Z, \rho)$. Assume that there is a sequence $\left\{\phi_{k}\right\}$ of functions from $\mathscr{P}$ to ( $Z, \rho$ ) such that (i) each $\phi_{k}$ is uniformly continuous for $\mathscr{U}, \rho$ and (ii) the $\phi_{k}$ converge to $\phi$ uniformly on the elements of $\mathscr{F}$. Then, there are measurable maps $T_{n}$ from $\left(\mathscr{X}^{n}, \mathscr{A}^{n}\right)$ to $(Z, \rho)$ such that $\sup _{p \in F} E_{p} 1 \wedge \rho\left[T_{n}, \phi(p)\right]$ tends to zero for each $F \in \mathscr{F}$.

Proof. For each integer $k$ let $\varepsilon(k)=2^{-k}$. Let $N(k)$ be the integer provided by the proof of Lemma 1 for $\varepsilon=\varepsilon(k)$ and for $\phi_{k}$. One can assume without loss of generality that $N(k)<N(k+1)$. Then, for all integers $n$ such that $N(k) \leq n<N(k+1)$, Lemma 1 insures the existence of an estimate $T_{n}$ such that $E_{p} 1 \wedge \rho\left[\left(T_{n}\right), \phi_{k}(p)\right]<\varepsilon(k)$ for all $p \in \mathscr{P}$.

Changing the notation around, one can define an integer $k(n)$ to be the integer $k$ such that $N(k) \leq n<N(k+1)$. Then $E_{p} 1 \wedge \rho\left[T_{n}, \phi_{k(n)}(p)\right] \leq \varepsilon[k(n)]$ for all $p$. Also, for every $F \in \mathscr{F}$, the quantity $\varepsilon^{\prime}(n, F)=\sup \left\{\rho\left[\phi_{k(n)}(p), \phi(p)\right]\right.$; $p \in F\}$ tends to zero. It follows that

$$
\sup \left\{E_{p} 1 \wedge \rho\left[T_{n}, \phi(p)\right] ; p \in F\right\} \leq \varepsilon[k(n)]+\varepsilon^{\prime}(n, F)
$$

tends to zero, concluding the proof of the proposition.
Remark. In the proof of this proposition, one can keep the same $T_{n}$ for all integers $n$ such that $N(k) \leq n<N(k+1)$. One could also say that $\operatorname{Pr}\left\{\rho\left[T_{n}\right.\right.$, $\left.\left.\phi_{k}(p)\right] \geq \varepsilon(k)\right\}<\varepsilon(k)$. Thus since $\sum_{k} \varepsilon(k)<\infty$, one sees that $T_{n}$ tends to $\phi(p)$ almost surely. The same result can be achieved if one "improves" the $T_{n}$ as $n$ varies from $N(k)$ to $N(k+1)$, using the same technique as in Lemma 1, but bounding the probabilities by the Hájek-Renyi inequality instead of Chebyshev's inequality. This will also yield almost sure convergence. Almost sure convergence is of no great importance in most practical problem. However, it is a technical convenience in certain arguments and particularly in sequential investigations.

There is a special case of Proposition 1 which arises often enough to merit special mention. It is as follows. Let us say that $\mathscr{F}$ has countable cofinality
if there is a sequence $\left\{F_{k} ; k=1,2, \ldots\right\}, F_{k} \in \mathscr{F}$, such that each $F \in \mathscr{F}$ is contained in some $F_{k}$.

Proposition 2. Assume that $\mathscr{F}$ has countable cofinality. Then in order that there exist estimates $T_{n}$ such that $\sup \left\{E_{p} 1 \wedge \rho\left[T_{n}, \phi(p)\right] ; p \in F\right\} \rightarrow 0$ for each $F \in \mathscr{F}$, it is sufficient that for each $k$ the restriction $\phi_{k}$ of $\phi$ to $F_{k}$ be $\mathscr{U}$ uniformly continuous.

Proof. The proof is the same as that of Proposition 1 except that one uses estimates $T_{n}$ such that, if $N(k) \leq n<N(k+1)$ then $E_{p} 1 \wedge \rho\left[T_{n}, \phi(p)\right]<\varepsilon(k)$ for all $p \in F_{k}$.

Another corollary of the argument carried out in Lemma 1 can be used to obtain a result originally stated by Pfanzagl.

Consider the case where the $\sigma$-field $\mathscr{A}$ is countably generated. Then, there exists a sequence $\left\{\mathscr{A}_{k} ; k=1,2 \ldots\right\}$ of finitely generated $\sigma$-fields such that $\mathscr{A}_{k} \subset \mathscr{A}_{k+1}$ and such that the union of the $\mathscr{A}_{k}$ generates $\mathscr{A}$.

For each $p \in \mathscr{P}$, let $p_{k}$ be its restriction to $\mathscr{A}_{k}$. The map from $\mathscr{P}$ with the structure $\mathscr{U}_{1}$ to the restrictions to $\mathscr{A}_{k}$ for the $L_{1}$-norm is obviously uniformly continuous. Now note that, as $k \rightarrow \infty$, the $L_{1}$-norms $\left\|p-p_{k}\right\|$ tend to zero uniformly on all the $L_{1}$-precompact subsets of $\mathscr{P}$. Thus the canonical map from ( $\mathscr{P}, \mathscr{U}_{1}$ ) to ( $\mathscr{P}, L_{1}$-norm) is the limit of a sequence of $\mathscr{U}_{1}$ uniformly continuous functions, uniformly on the $L_{1}$-precompact subsets of $\mathscr{P}$. According to Lemma 1 and Proposition 1, this implies the existence of estimates $T_{n}$ that are uniformly consistent on the $L_{1}$-precompact subsets of $\mathscr{P}$.

Pfanzagl's result assumes the separability of $\mathscr{P}$ for the $L_{1}$-norm. This can be reduced to the above, since, in this case, one can replace the $\sigma$-field $\mathscr{A}$ by a countably generated one without modifying the structure of $\mathscr{P}$.

Before we state the next result, recall that any compact metric space $Z$ can be homeomorphically imbedded in the cube $K$ product of a countable sequence of intervals $[0,1]$. This cube is a convex set. For $K$, Proposition 3 gives a converse of Proposition 1. We shall return later to the role of the compactness of $K$ in this converse.
