Proposition 3. Let ( $Z, \rho$ ) be the cube $K$, countable product of intervals $[0,1]$ with a compatible metric $\rho$. Assume that there are estimates $T_{n}$ such that $\sup \left\{E_{p} 1 \wedge \rho\left[T_{n}, \phi(p)\right] ; p \in F\right\}$ tends to zero for each $F \in \mathscr{F}$. Then there is a sequence $\left\{\phi_{k}\right\}$ of $\mathscr{U}$-uniformly continuous functions such that $\phi_{k} \rightarrow \phi$ uniformly on the elements of $\mathscr{F}$.

Proof. The choice of metric on $K$ is indifferent. Thus we shall metrize $K$ by $\rho(x, y)=\sum 2^{-m}\left|x_{m}-y_{m}\right|$ where $x_{m}$ is the $m$ th coordinate of $x \in K$. Let $\phi_{n}(p)=E_{p}\left(T_{n}\right)$. This is an element of $K$. Also $\phi_{n}$ is $\mathscr{U}_{n}$-uniformly continuous and $\rho\left[\phi_{n}(p), \phi(p)\right] \leq E_{p} \rho\left[T_{n}, \phi(p)\right]$. The result follows.

For the situation considered in Proposition 2 where $\mathscr{F}$ has countable cofinality, this says that, restricted to each $F_{k}$, the function $\phi$ must be uniformly continuous for $\mathscr{U}$. Such an assertion does not remain valid if the compactness condition on the space $Z$ is entirely removed. An example is provided by the well known Glivenko-Cantelli theorem as follows.

Let $(Z, \rho)$ be the space of all cumulative distributions on $[0,1]$ metrized by the Kolmogorov-Smirnov norm. Let $\mathscr{P}$ be the set of all probability measures
on $[0,1]$. For each $p \in \mathscr{P}$ let $\phi(p)$ be its cumulative distribution. Let $T_{n}$ be the element of $Z$ obtained from the empirical cumulative $F_{n}$ by interpolating linearly between jumps. This is measurable. Then $\rho\left(T_{n}, F_{n}\right) \leq 1 / n$ and, according to the Glivenko-Cantelli theorem, $E_{p} \rho\left[T_{n}, \phi(p)\right] \leq \varepsilon_{n}$ with $\varepsilon_{n} \rightarrow 0, \varepsilon_{n}$ independent of $p$.

However, the function $\phi$ is not a uniformly continuous map for $\mathscr{U}$ and $\rho$. Indeed one can pass back from $Z$ to $\rho$ and let $\mathscr{U}(\rho)$ be the uniform structure defined on $\mathscr{P}$ by the norm $\rho$. Uniform continuity of $\phi$ would imply that $\mathscr{U}(\rho)$ is weaker than $\mathscr{U}$. However, $\mathscr{U}$ and $\mathscr{U}(\rho)$ are not comparable. Indeed the topology induced by $\mathscr{U}(\rho)$ is weaker than the topology induced by $\mathscr{U}_{1}$. Also $\mathscr{U}$ is precompact while $\mathscr{P}$ contains a set of points having the cardinality of the continuum at distance unity from each other for $\rho$.

It turns out, however, that the space $\Gamma[\mathscr{U}(\rho)]$ of bounded uniformly continuous numerical functions for $\mathscr{U}(\rho)$ is contained in the analogous space $\Gamma(\mathscr{U})$ for $\mathscr{U}$. This follows, for instance, from Proposition 3. In such cases, a weakened form of Proposition 3 is still available.

Let $\Gamma$ be the space of all bounded uniformly continuous functions on $(Z, \rho)$ to the line and let $\mathscr{V}$ be the smallest uniform structure on $Z$ which makes all the elements of $\Gamma$ uniformly continuous.

Lemma 2. Let ( $Z, \rho$ ) be an arbitrary metric space. Assume that $T_{n}$ is measurable from $\left(\mathscr{X}^{n}, \mathscr{A}^{n}\right)$ to $(Z, \rho)$ and that $E_{p} 1 \wedge \rho\left[T_{n}, \phi(p)\right]$ tends to zero uniformly on $\mathscr{P}$. Then $\phi$ is uniformly continuous for the structures ( $\mathscr{U}, \mathscr{V}$ ). In particular, $\phi$ is continuous for the topologies induced, respectively, by $\mathscr{U}$ and $\rho$.

Proof. For each $\gamma \in \Gamma$ let $\gamma \circ \phi$ be the composed function. Then, by Proposition 3, $\gamma \circ \phi$ is $\mathscr{U}$-uniformly continuous. This implies that $\phi$ is uniformly continuous for $\mathscr{U}$ and $\mathscr{V}$. However, the topology induced by $\mathscr{V}$ on $Z$ is the same as that induced by $\rho$. Hence the result.

Remark. The uniform continuity for ( $\mathscr{U}, \mathscr{V}$ ) can be a much more severe restriction than mere continuity of $\phi$.

In the preceding propositions we have argued about estimates which are consistent uniformly on certain subsets $F \in \mathscr{F}$ of $\mathscr{P}$. It is natural that in such a context uniformities would play an important role. However, a large part of the existing statistical literature deals with pointwise consistency, that is, with the situation in which our family $\mathscr{F}$ is the family of finite subsets of $\mathscr{P}$. One might think that in such a case the concepts arising from uniformities could be replaced by the corresponding topological concepts. This does not appear to be the case.

For instance, Propositions 1 and 3 say that a function $\phi$ from $\mathscr{P}$ to $[0,1]$ admits a pointwise consistent sequence of estimates if and only if it is a pointwise limit of a sequence of $\mathscr{U}$-uniformly continuous functions. Now there is no reason to expect that continuous functions would be limits of sequences of uniformly continuous functions. This is so on metric spaces. There every bounded continuous function is a limit of an increasing sequence of uniformly continuous functions. However, generally one needs to use increasing directed sets of uniformly continuous functions. The following example has several un-
satisfactory features, but it illustrates the point.
Let $\mathscr{X}$ be the interval $[0,1]$ and let $\mathscr{A}$ be the Borel field of $\mathscr{X}$. Let $\mathscr{P}$ be the family $\left\{\delta_{x} ; x \in \mathscr{X}\right\}$ where $\delta_{x}$ is the Dirac measure giving mass unity to $x$. One can identify $\mathscr{P}$ and $[0,1]$. In this case all the uniformities $\mathscr{U}_{n}$ coincide so that $\mathscr{U}$ coincides with $\mathscr{U}_{1}$. By definition $\mathscr{U}_{1}$ is the smallest structure which makes the bounded Borel functions continuous. It is a precompact structure. The pointwise limits of sequences of Borel functions are also Borel, hence $\mathscr{U}_{1}$ uniformly continuous. However, of each $x$, there is a Borel function $f$ such that $f(x)=1$ but $f(y)=0$ for all $y \neq x$. Thus the topology induced by $\mathscr{U}$ is discrete and every arbitrary function defined on $[0,1]$ is continuous.

The uniform structure $\mathscr{U}$ is not easily accessible. However there are many interesting situations where simpler structures can be used. One of them is as follows. Consider the topology $\mathscr{T}_{1}$ defined by $\mathscr{U}_{1}$. This is the topology where $p_{r} \rightarrow p$ if $\int f d p_{r} \rightarrow \int f d p$ for every bounded measurable function $f$.

Lemma 3. Let $L$ be the space of all finite signed measures on $(\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{A})$. Assume that $\mathscr{P}$ is relatively compact for $\mathscr{T}_{1}$ in $L$. Then, on $\mathscr{P}$ the structures $\mathscr{U}_{1}$ and $\mathscr{U}$ coincide.

Proof. According to the Dunford-Pettis-Grothendieck theorem the closure $\overline{\mathscr{P}}$ of $\mathscr{P}$ in $L$ is already compact for the $w(L, M)$ topology induced by the dual $M$ of $L$. Also there is some probability measure $\mu$ such that for each $\varepsilon>0$ there is a $c$ such that $\|\lambda-(c \mu) \wedge \lambda\| \leq \varepsilon$ for all $\lambda \in \overline{\mathscr{P}}$. Suppose that a filter $\lambda_{r}$ converges to $\lambda_{0} \in \overline{\mathscr{P}}$.

We shall now show that products $\lambda_{r} \otimes \lambda_{r}$, with two factors, converges to $\lambda_{0} \otimes \lambda_{0}$. The argument is the same for any direct product with a fixed finite number of factors.

Take $c$ so that $\|\lambda-(c \mu) \wedge \lambda\|<\varepsilon$ for all $\lambda \in \overline{\mathscr{P}}$. Then $\| \lambda \otimes \lambda-[(c \mu) \wedge \lambda] \otimes [c \mu \wedge \lambda] \|<2 \varepsilon$ for all $\lambda \in \overline{\mathscr{P}}$. Since $\varepsilon$ can be selected arbitrarily small, it will be sufficient to show that if $m_{v}=\lambda_{v} \wedge(c \mu)$ then $m_{v} \otimes m_{v}$ tends to $m_{0} \otimes m_{0}$. Now take a bounded measurable function $f$ defined on $\mathscr{X} \times \mathscr{X}$. There is a finite sum $\sum_{j} g_{j}(x) h_{j}(y)$ such that $\int\left|f-\sum g_{j} h_{j}\right| d(\mu \otimes \mu)<\varepsilon / c$ and therefore $\int\left|f-\sum g_{j} h_{j}\right| d\left(m_{v} \otimes m_{v}\right)<\varepsilon$ for all $v$. Thus it is sufficient to show that $\int g_{j} h_{j} d\left(m_{v} \otimes m_{v}\right)$ converges to $\int g_{j} h_{j} d\left(m_{0} \otimes m_{0}\right)$. This is obvious. This shows that $\overline{\mathscr{P}}$ is also compact for $\mathscr{U}_{n}$. Compact sets have a unique uniform structure compatible with their topology. Hence the result.

One immediate corollary is as follows.
Corollary. Assume that $\mathscr{F}$ has a cofinal sequence $\left\{F_{k}\right\}$ formed by sets which are $\mathscr{U}_{1}$ compact. Let $\phi$ be a map from $\mathscr{P}$ to an arbitrary metric space ( $Z, \rho$ ). Then, in order that there exists a sequence $\left\{T_{n}\right\}$ of estimates which is uniformly consistent on the elements of $\mathscr{F}$ it is necessary and sufficient that the restriction $\phi_{k}$ of $\phi$ to any $F_{k}$ be $\mathscr{U}_{1}$ continuous on that set $F_{k}$.

Proof. The sufficiency follows from Proposition 2 and the preceding Lemma 3. The necessity follows from Lemma 2.

In this statement the topology $\mathscr{T}_{1}$ induced by $\mathscr{U}_{1}$ has been used throughout. Suppose that all the occurrences of $\mathscr{T}_{1}$ are replaced by another topology $\mathscr{T}_{0}$ stronger than $\mathscr{T}_{1}$. Then the statement remains valid since the $F_{k}$ are now $\mathscr{T}_{0}$
compact and since on them $\mathscr{T}_{0}$ and $\mathscr{T}_{1}$ coincide. One could also use a separated topology $\mathscr{T}_{0}$ weaker than $\mathscr{T}_{1}$ provided that the $F_{k}$ be assumed to be $\mathscr{T}_{1}$ compact.

This remark can be rephrased in several ways. One of them leads to the following. Let $\Theta$ be locally compact countable at infinity. Let $\theta \mapsto p_{\theta}$ be an injection from $\Theta$ into the set $\mathscr{P}$ of probability measures on a space $(\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{A})$. Assume that $\theta \mapsto p_{\theta}$ is continuous for a topology stronger than $\mathscr{T}_{1}$ on $\mathscr{P}$. Then, there are estimates $\hat{\theta}_{n}$ of $\theta$ which are uniformly consistent on the compacts of $\Theta$.

The result remains valid if one assumes that $\theta \mapsto p_{\theta}$ is continuous for a separated topology weaker than $\mathscr{T}_{1}$ on $\mathscr{P}$, provided that one assumes that, for each compact $K \subset \Theta$, the set $\left\{p_{\theta} ; \theta \in K\right\}$ is $\mathscr{T}_{1}$ relatively compact. This means that there is a probability measure $\mu_{K}$ such that for each $\varepsilon>0$ there is a $c$ such that $\left\|p_{\theta}-\left(c \mu_{K}\right) \wedge p_{\theta}\right\|<\varepsilon$ for all $\theta \in K$. In the terminology of Chapter 6, Section 1, the set $\left\{p_{\theta} ; \theta \in K\right\}$ is "limited".

Instead of this limitation condition, one can use various other conditions. One of them was proposed by Hoeffding and Wolfowitz. It will be called the $\mathrm{H}-\mathrm{W}$ condition below. We shall describe it for the case where the underlying space ( $\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{A}$ ) is the line.

In that case it amounts to say in a precise way that the differences between densities associated with measures $p_{\theta}, \theta \in K$ do not change sign "too often". To describe it precisely, let $P$ and $Q$ be two probability measures on the line. Let $f$ and $g$ be their respective densities with respect to $P+Q$. If there are such intervals, let $I_{i}, i=1,2, \ldots, m$ be intervals such that $f-g$ has a constant sign on each $I_{i}$. Let $V$ be the complement of $\bigcup_{i=1}^{m} I_{i}$. For a given $\varepsilon>0$, let $J[P, Q ; \varepsilon]$ be the smallest value of $m$ such that $\min [P(V), Q(V)]<\varepsilon$. Otherwise, let $J[P, Q ; \varepsilon]=\infty$.

A family $\mathscr{P}$ of probability measures satisfies the $\mathrm{H}-\mathrm{W}$ condition if for each $\varepsilon>0$ the quantity $J(\varepsilon)=\sup \{J[P, Q ; \varepsilon] ; P \in \mathscr{P}, Q \in \mathscr{P}\}$ is finite. Hoeffding and Wolfowitz have shown that the $L_{1}$-norm $\|P-Q\|$ and the KolmogorovSmirnov norm are related by the inequality

$$
\|P-Q\| \leq 4 J[P, Q ; \varepsilon]|P-Q|_{K}+2 \varepsilon
$$

Thus, if a set $\mathscr{P}$ satisfies $\mathrm{H}-\mathrm{W}$, on it the $L_{1}$-norm and the KolmogorovSmirnov norm are equivalent.

Using this, one can formulate a variant of our previous statements as follows. Let $\Theta$ be locally compact countable at infinity. Let $\theta \mapsto p_{\theta}$ be an injection of $\Theta$ into the set of probability measures on the line. Assume that (i) $\theta \mapsto p_{\theta}$ is continuous for the Kolmogorov-Smirnov norm and, (ii) for each compact $K \subset \Theta$, the set $\left\{p_{\theta} ; \theta \in K\right\}$ satisfies the $\mathrm{H}-\mathrm{W}$ condition. Then there exist estimates $\hat{\theta}_{n}$ which are uniformly consistent on the compacts of $\Theta$.

As mentioned before, the preceding results were stated for estimates which are measurable functions, instead of the more general transitions used in the main body of the present work. One can obtain results similar to those of Propositions 1 and 3 for such transitions. In fact, let $M_{n}$ be the $M$-space associated with the product experiment $\mathscr{E}^{n}$. Let $V_{n}$ be a uniformly closed vector
sublattice of $M_{n}$. Assume that $V_{n}$ contains the constant and that, for $m<n$, the space $V_{m}$ can be obtained from $V_{n}$ by restriction to the $L$-space $L_{m}$ of $\mathscr{E}^{m}$. Then analogues of Propositions 1 and 3 are valid for the uniform structure $\mathscr{U}^{*}$ defined on $\mathscr{E}$ by all the $V_{n}$.

In the preceding paragraphs we have mentioned the Kolmogorov-Smirnov distance. It plays a very special role on the line, but becomes less satisfactory in two or more dimensions. One is led to consider other distances induced on the space of measures on a space ( $\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{A}$ ) by norms of the type $\|\mu\|_{\mathscr{Y}}= \sup _{A}\{|\mu(A)| ; A \in \mathscr{S}\}$ where $\mathscr{S}$ is a suitable class $\mathscr{S} \subset \mathscr{A}$.

Consider then a sample $\xi_{1}, \xi_{2}, \ldots, \xi_{n}$ of independent random variables distributed according to a certain probability measure $p$ on $(\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{A})$. Let $\mu_{n}$ be the associated empirical measure. The question arises of characterizing those classes $\mathscr{S} \subset \mathscr{A}$ for which $\left\|\mu_{n}-p\right\|_{\mathscr{S}}$ will tend to zero in probability. Except for some measurability difficulties, such a characterization was given by Vapnik and Crervonenkis. Their results were further refined by M. Steele. To describe them, let us return to the notation of Chapter 16, Section 8. There for any finite set $F$ and class $\mathscr{S}$ we have denoted $D(F, \mathscr{S})$ the number of distinct subsets of $F$ of the form $F \cap S, S \in \mathscr{S}$. Also $v(F, \mathscr{S})$ was the largest integer $k$ such that $F$ contains a subset of cardinality $k$ shattered by $\mathscr{S}$.

To show that certain conditions are necessary we shall use the following lemma. Let $F=\left\{x_{1}, x_{2}, \ldots, x_{2 n}\right\}$ be a set containing $2 n$ distinct points. Take a sample $E$ of size $n$ without replacement from the set $F$. For each $S \in \mathscr{S}$ let $Y(S)=\operatorname{card}(S \cap E)-\operatorname{card}[S \cap(F \backslash E)]$. Let $\|Y\|=\sup \{|Y(S)| ; S \in \mathscr{S}\}$.

Lemma 4. Assume that there is a subset $G \subset F$ of cardinality $k$ which is shattered by $\mathscr{S}$. Then

$$
\operatorname{Pr}\left\{\|Y\| \geq \frac{k}{2}-2 \varepsilon n\right\} \geq 1-\frac{2}{n \varepsilon^{2}}
$$

Proof. To select $E$ one can first determine the number $M$ of points of $E \cap G$ according to the hypergeometric distribution

$$
\operatorname{Pr}[M=m]=\frac{\binom{k}{m}\binom{2 n-k}{n-m}}{\binom{2 n}{n}}
$$

Then one selects at random without replacement ( $n-M$ ) points out of the $2 n-k$ points of $G^{c}$. Suppose that $E \cap G$ has been selected and that $M=\operatorname{card}(E \cap G)=m$. Take an $S \in \mathscr{S}$ such that $S \cap(G \cap E)=G \cap E$. Let $N$ be the cardinality of $S \cap G^{c} \cap E$. Then, given $M=m$, the variable $N$ has a hypergeometric distribution. If $K=\operatorname{card}\left(S \cap G^{c}\right)$ one has $E(N \mid M=m)= (n-m)(K / 2 n-k)$ and $\operatorname{var}[N \mid M=m] \leq(n-m / 4)$.

The corresponding value $Y(S)$ is $M+2 N-K$. Since $E M=(k / 2)$, one obtains $E Y(S)=(k / 2)$ and var $Y(S) \leq(n-m)+E[E(Y \mid M)-E(Y)]^{2} \leq 2 n$. Thus by Chebyshev's inequality $\operatorname{Pr}\{|Y(S)-(k / 2)| \geq \varepsilon n\} \leq\left(2 / n \varepsilon^{2}\right)$ and the result follows.

Now consider a sequence $\left\{\xi_{1}, \xi_{2}, \ldots, \xi_{n}, \ldots\right\}$ of independent identically distributed variables with distribution $p$ on $(\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{A})$. Let $F_{n}=\left\{\xi_{1}, \xi_{2}, \ldots, \xi_{n}\right\}$
and $\Delta_{n}=D\left(F_{n}, \mathscr{S}\right)$. Let $V_{n}=v\left(F_{n}, \mathscr{S}\right)$. Finally let $Z_{n}$ be the process $Z_{n}(S)=\sum_{j=1}^{n}\left(\delta_{\xi_{j}}-p\right)(S)$ and let $W_{n}=(1 / n) \sup \left\{\left|Z_{n}(S)\right| ; S \in \mathscr{S}\right\}$.

The following two lemmas are not entirely necessary for our purposes, but they clarify the situation. Assume that the measurability conditions (A) and (B) of Chapter 16, Section 8, are satisfied.

Lemma 5. As $n \rightarrow \infty$, the variable $W_{n}$ converge almost surely to a constant limit $W$.

Proof. Let $M_{k}=\sum\left\{\left(\delta_{\xi_{j}}-p\right) ; j \neq k, 1 \leq j \leq n+1\right\}$. By symmetry one sees that $\operatorname{Pr}\left\{Z_{n}=M_{j} \mid Z_{n+1}, Z_{n+2}, \ldots\right\}=(1 / n+1)$ for all $j=1,2, \ldots, n+1$. Thus

$$
\begin{aligned}
E\left\{\left\|Z_{n}\right\|_{\mathscr{S}} \mid Z_{n+1}, Z_{n+2}, \ldots\right\} & =\frac{n}{n+1} \sum_{j=1}^{n+1}\left\|M_{j}\right\|_{\mathscr{Y}} \geq \frac{1}{n+1}\left\|\sum_{j=1}^{n+1} M_{j}\right\|_{\mathscr{S}} \\
& =\frac{n}{n+1}\left\|Z_{n+1}\right\|_{\mathscr{S}}
\end{aligned}
$$

The convergence of $W_{n}=(1 / n)\left\|Z_{n}\right\|_{\mathscr{Y}}$ follows from the martingale theorem. The fact that the limit $W$ is constant follows from the fact that it is measurable with respect to the tail $\sigma$-field.

Lemma 6. The variables $n^{-1} V_{n}$ converge in $L_{1}$-norm to a limit $\alpha=\inf _{n} E n^{-1} V_{n}$. Proof. Let $F_{m, n}=\left\{\xi_{m+1}, \xi_{m+2}, \ldots, \xi_{n}\right\}$ for $m<n$. Let $f(m, n)=v\left(F_{m, n}, \mathscr{S}\right)$. If $A$ is a subset of $F_{n}=F_{m} \cup F_{m, n}$ which is shattered by $\mathscr{S}$, then both $F_{m}$ and $F_{m, n}$ are shattered by $\mathscr{S}$. Thus $V_{n} \leq V_{m}+f(m, n)$. Let $g_{n}=n^{-1} V_{n}$. Take an $\varepsilon>0$ and an integer $m$ such that $E g_{m}<\alpha+\varepsilon$. Let $n=k m+r$ where $k$ is an integer $k \geq 1$ and where $0 \leq r<m$. According to the subadditivity relation obtained above one has

$$
g_{n} \leq \frac{1}{n} f[k m, k m+r]+\frac{k m}{n} \frac{1}{k} \sum_{j=0}^{k-1} \frac{1}{m} f[j m,(j+1) m]
$$

Now, by definition $f(k m, k m+r) \leq r$. Hence, as $k \rightarrow \infty$ the first term on the
right tends to zero. The second term involves the average of $k$ independent random variables, all distributed in the same manner as $(1 / m) V_{m}$. Thus, as $k \rightarrow \infty$ the second term tends almost surely to $E(1 / m) V_{m} \leq \alpha+\varepsilon$. It follows that $\lim _{n} \sup g_{n} \leq \alpha+\varepsilon$ almost surely. This is true for arbitrary $\varepsilon>0$. Therefore $\lim _{n} \sup g_{n} \leq \alpha$ almost surely. Taking expectations in the same expressions one sees that $\lim _{n} E g_{n}=\alpha$. Since $0 \leq g_{n} \leq 1$, these two results imply that $\lim _{n} E\left(g_{n} \wedge \alpha\right)=\alpha$ and therefore that $\lim E\left[\alpha-g_{n} \wedge \alpha\right]=0$. Hence the result.

Remark. Applying Kingman's ergodic theorem for subadditive processes, one can show that $(1 / n) V_{n} \rightarrow \alpha$ almost surely and that $(1 / n) \log \Delta_{n}$ also possesses an almost sure constant limit.

We are now in a position to prove the following result, due to Vapnik and Červonenkis.

Theorem 1. Let the measurability conditions ( $A$ ) and ( $B$ ) of Chapter 16, Section 8, be satisfied. Then the following statements are all equivalent:
(1) $W_{n}=(1 / n) \sup \left\{\left|Z_{n}(S)\right| ; S \in \mathscr{S}\right\} \rightarrow 0$ almost surely (or in probability);
(2) $E(1 / n) V_{n} \rightarrow 0$;
(3) $(1 / n) \log \Delta_{n} \rightarrow 0$ in probability.

Proof. Let $\alpha$ be the limit of $E(1 / n) V_{n}$. Consider the processes $Z_{n}$ and $Z_{n}^{\prime}$ of Section 8, Chapter 16. Conditionally given a sample $\left\{\xi_{1}, \xi_{2}, \ldots, \xi_{n}, \xi_{n+1}, \ldots, \xi_{2 n}\right\}$ the distribution of $\left\|Z_{n}-Z_{n}^{\prime}\right\|=\sup \left\{\left|Z_{n}(S)-Z^{\prime}(S)\right| ; S \in \mathscr{S}\right\}$ is that considered in Lemma 4 above.

Thus, for $n$ sufficiently large and $\varepsilon>0$ one will eventually have

$$
\operatorname{Pr}\left\{\frac{1}{n}\left\|Z_{n}-Z_{n}^{\prime}\right\| \geq \frac{\alpha}{4}-3 \varepsilon\right\}>1-\frac{2}{n \varepsilon^{2}}-\varepsilon
$$

Taking $12 \varepsilon<\alpha$ one sees that $(1 / n)\left\|Z_{n}-Z_{n}^{\prime}\right\|$ cannot tend to zero. However

$$
\frac{1}{n}\left\|Z_{n}-Z_{n}^{\prime}\right\| \leq \frac{1}{n}\left\|Z_{n}\right\|+\frac{1}{n}\left\|Z_{n}^{\prime}\right\|
$$

Since the two terms on the right have the same distribution, neither one can tend to zero in probability. This shows that (1) ⇒ (2).

To show that (2) ⇒ (3), let us use the inequality of Lemma 1, Section 8, Chapter 16. It implies that

$$
2^{V_{n}} \leq \Delta_{n} \leq \phi\left(V_{n}+1, n\right)
$$

for the function $\phi(k, n)=1+\sum_{j=0}^{k-1}\binom{n}{j}$. If $2 k<n$, one can bound $\phi(k+1, n)$ by $1+(k+1)\binom{n}{k}$. Taking logarithms and applying Stirling's formula, one sees that if $(1 / n) V_{n} \rightarrow 0$ so does $(1 / n) \log \Delta_{n}$.

It remains to show that (3) ⇒ (1). To do this, let us return to Lemma 5,
Section 8, Chapter 16 and reintroduce the notation $Y_{n}=Z_{n}-Z_{n}^{\prime}$. The lemma says that, given the sample $F_{2 n}=\left\{\xi_{1}, \xi_{2}, \ldots, \xi_{2 n}\right\}$ one has

$$
\begin{aligned}
\operatorname{Pr} & \left\{\sup \left[\left|Y_{n}(S)\right| ; S \in \mathscr{S}\right] \geq n \varepsilon \mid F_{2 n}\right\} \leq 2 D\left(F_{2 n}, \mathscr{S}\right) \exp \left\{-\frac{n \varepsilon^{2}}{4}\right\} \\
& =2 \exp \left\{-\frac{n \varepsilon^{2}}{4}+\log \Delta_{2 n}\right\}
\end{aligned}
$$

if $(1 / n) \log \Delta_{2 n} \rightarrow 0$ in probability, this will imply that, for $n$ sufficiently large, one has

$$
\operatorname{Pr}\left\{\sup \left[\left|Y_{n}(S)\right| ; S \in \mathscr{S}\right] \geq n \varepsilon\right\} \leq \varepsilon+2 \exp \left\{-n\left(\frac{\varepsilon^{2}}{4}-\frac{\varepsilon^{2}}{8}\right)\right\}
$$

It follows that $(1 / n)\left\|Y_{n}\right\|_{\mathscr{Y}} \rightarrow 0$ in probability. Using Paul Lévy's symmetrization inequalities as in the proof of Lemma 6, Section 8, Chapter 16, one concludes that $(1 / n)\left\|Z_{n}\right\|_{\mathscr{S}} \rightarrow 0$ in probability and therefore almost surely. This concludes the proof of the theorem.



Note that Theorem 1 above enjoys a certain aesthetic beauty. However, the results of Chapter 16, Section 8 are essentially stronger. They give bounds on the tails of the variables $\left\|Z_{n}\right\|_{\mathscr{S}}$. They also allow the class $\mathscr{S}$ to vary as $n$ varies.

## 6. Estimates Converging at the $\sqrt{n}$-rate

In this section we consider again an experiment $\mathscr{E}=\left\{p_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ and the experiments $\mathscr{E}^{n}$ direct product of $n$ copies of $\mathscr{E}$. Let $\rho(s, t)=\int \sqrt{d p_{s} d p_{t}}, h^{2}(s, t)= 1-\rho(s, t)$, and $q_{n}^{2}(s, t)=-n \log \rho(s, t)$. In order to be able to apply the arguments of Chapter 11 and obtain asymptotically sufficient statistics, one needs to use auxiliary estimates $\hat{\theta}_{n}$ that are $q$-convergent in the sense that $q_{n}\left(\hat{\theta}_{n}, \theta\right)$ remains bounded in $p_{\theta}^{n}$ probability. In the present case $q_{n}^{2}\left(\hat{\theta}_{n}, \theta\right)$ will be bounded in probability if and only if $n h^{2}(\hat{\theta}, \theta)$ is. It has already been shown in Chapter 16, Section 5, that, when $\Theta$ has finite dimension $D$, there exist estimates $\hat{\theta}_{n}$ such that $E_{\theta} n h^{2}(\hat{\theta}, \theta) \leq 14 D+10$. However, the methods of Chapter 16 are complex. We shall presently investigate constructions of the "minimum distance" type which can also give good estimates under appropriate circumstances. We start with a lemma intended to permit a first localization of the problem. Then we show that under conditions similar to the DQM conditions of Sections 2 and 3, minimizing a Kolmogorov-Smirnov distance will generate suitable estimates. The use of Lemma 1 in this situation would be unnecessary if one would strengthen the assumptions a little. However it seems that many authors like to work with very local conditions, forgetting even to state the necessary identifiability restrictions.

Lemma 1 will allow us to write conditions similar to those most often found in the literature. It will also bypass certain difficulties due to the fact that one may have assumed that the maps used are proper for certain topologies, but that the topologies actually used are not the ones for which the assumptions were stated.

After the proof of Lemma 1, we proceed to a construction under the DQM conditions. We indicate some "robustness" properties. Finally, we give a short account of various methods, such as the method of moments, the method of minimum contrasts, and so forth.

To obtain a first localization, assume that $\Theta$ is metrized by a certain distance $d$. Let $\left\{\Theta_{k} ; k=1,2, \ldots\right\}$ be an increasing sequence of subsets of $\Theta$. Call it a localization sequence if $\bigcup \Theta_{k}=\Theta$ and if $\inf \left\{d(s, t) ; s \in \Theta_{k}, t \in \Theta_{k+1}^{c}\right\}>0$ for each $k$. When ( $\Theta, d$ ) is Euclidean one can take for $\Theta_{k}$ the ball of radius $k$ centered at the origin. More generally, when $\Theta$ is locally compact countable at infinity and metrizable one can find localization sequences $\left\{\Theta_{k}\right\}$ where $\Theta_{k}$ is a compact subset of $\Theta$. Indeed, there is an increasing sequence $\left\{K_{m}\right\}$ of compact subsets of $\Theta$ such that every compact subset of $\Theta$ is contained in one of the $K_{m}$. If $\Theta_{1}, \ldots, \Theta_{k}$ have been constructed one takes for $\Theta_{k+1}$ one of the $K_{m}$, with $m \geq k+1$ so that $K_{m}$ contains an open neighborhood of $\Theta_{k}$. This gives
a sequence that is a localization sequence for any metric compatible with the topology of $\Theta$.

Call a sequence $\left\{\theta_{n}^{*}\right\}$ of estimates $d$-uniformly consistent on a set $S \subset \Theta$ if for each $\varepsilon>0$ the quantity $\sup \left\{p_{\theta}^{n}\left[d\left(\theta_{n}^{*}, \theta\right)>\varepsilon\right] ; \theta \in S\right\}$ tends to zero as $n \rightarrow \infty$. Call it uniformly $q$-convergent on the set $S$ if for each $\varepsilon>0$ there are numbers $b(\varepsilon)$ and $n(\varepsilon)$ such that $n \geq n(\varepsilon)$ implies

$$
\sup \left\{p_{\theta}^{n}\left[n h^{2}\left(\theta_{n}^{*}, \theta\right) \geq b(\varepsilon)\right] ; \theta \in S\right\}<\varepsilon .
$$

Lemma 1. Assume that $\left\{\Theta_{k}\right\}$ is a localization sequence for a metric $d$. Assume in addition that
(a) the sequence $\left\{\theta_{n}^{*}\right\}$ is $d$-uniformly consistent on each $\Theta_{k}$, and
(b) for each fixed $k$, there is a sequence $\left\{T_{n, k}\right\}$ which is uniformly $q$-convergent on $\Theta_{k}$.

Then there is a sequence $\left\{T_{n}\right\}$ which is uniformly $q$-convergent on each $\Theta_{k}$. Proof. Let $m_{n}$ be the smallest integer $m$ such that $\theta_{n}^{*} \in \Theta_{m-1}$. Let $A_{k}= \Theta_{k} \cap \Theta_{k-1}^{c}$ and let $\varepsilon_{k}=\inf \left\{d(s, t) ; s \in \Theta_{k-2}, t \in \Theta_{k-1}^{c}\right\}$. If $\theta \in A_{k}$, then $p_{\theta}^{n}\left[d\left(\theta_{n}^{*}, \theta\right) \geq \varepsilon_{k} / 2\right]$ tends to zero. Therefore, eliminating cases of small probability, one will have $d\left(\theta_{n}^{*}, \theta\right)<\varepsilon_{k} / 2$ so that $\theta_{n}^{*} \notin \Theta_{k-2}$. This means that $m_{n} \geq k$. Similarly, excepting cases of small probability, one will have $m_{n} \leq k+2$. Now let $T_{n}=T_{n, m_{n}}$. Then $T_{n}=T_{n, j}$ for some $j$ such that $k \leq j \leq k+2$. However, by assumption, given $\varepsilon>0$, there is a $b_{j}$ such that $p_{\theta}^{n}\left[n h^{2}\left(T_{n, j}, \theta\right) \leq b_{j}\right]<\varepsilon$ for all $\theta \in \Theta_{j}$.

It follows that, for $\theta \in A_{k}$ one will have

$$
p_{\theta}^{n}\left[n h^{2}\left(T_{n}, \theta\right) \geq b_{k}+b_{k+1}+b_{k+2}\right] \leq 3 \varepsilon+\alpha_{n}
$$

where $\alpha_{n}=p_{\theta}^{n}\left[\left(m_{n}<k\right) \cup\left(m_{n}>k+2\right)\right]$. Since $\alpha_{n} \rightarrow 0$, the result follows.
Note. This is analogous to Section 5, Proposition 2 or Lemma 3. The lemma admits a number of variants. One of them uses the assumption (a) as above, but replaces (b) by the assumption that $T_{n, k}$ is $q$-convergent for each fixed $\theta \in \Theta_{k}$. The conclusion is then that $T_{n}$ is $q$-convergent for each fixed $\theta$. The proof is the same. It is clear that one can state analogous results for distances other than the Hellinger distance $h$ used here.

An easy consequence of Lemma 1 is as follows. For any $K \subset \Theta$, let $D(K, \tau)$ be its dimension for the pair ( $h, \tau$ ) as in Section 5, Chapter 16.

Lemma 2. Assume that $\Theta$ is locally compact, metrizable and countable at infinity. In addition assume that
(1) if $s \neq t$, then $p_{s} \neq p_{t}$;
(2) for each compact subset $K \subset \Theta$ one has $\sup _{\tau} D(K, \tau)<\infty$;
(3) the map from $\Theta$ topological to $\Theta$ metrized by $h$ is continuous.

Then there exist estimates $\hat{\theta}_{n}$ that are $q$-convergent uniformly on each compact subset of $\Theta$.

Proof. The existence of estimates that are uniformly consistent on compacts follows from Section 5, Lemma 3 and corollaries. Chapter 16, Section 5 provides estimates which can play the role of the $T_{n, k}$ of Lemma 1. The result follows from Lemma 1.

To obtain further results, let us consider the case where $\Theta$ is a subset of a Euclidean space. Assume also, temporarily that the observations take their value in a Euclidean space $\mathscr{X}$. Let $F_{\theta}$ be the cumulative distribution function corresponding to the measure $p_{\theta}$. If $F_{1}$ and $F_{2}$ are two cumulative distribution functions on $\mathscr{X}$, let $\left\|F_{1}-F_{2}\right\|_{K}=\sup _{x}\left|F_{1}(x)-F_{2}(x)\right|$ be their Kolmogorov distance.

If $\mathscr{E}=\left\{p_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ is an experiment indexed by $\Theta$, recall that condition DQM (Section 3) is satisfied at $\theta$ if the process $\xi$ of covariance kernel $\rho(s, t)= \int \sqrt{d p_{s} d p_{t}}$ is differentiable in quadratic mean at $\theta$.

Definition 1. A point $\theta \in \Theta$ will be called $D$-regular if it satisfies the following three requirements:
(i) DQM is satisfied at $\theta$;
(ii) the derivative in quadratic mean of $\xi$ has a nonsingular covariance matrix;
(iii) if $p_{t}^{\prime \prime}$ is the part of $p_{t}$ which is $p_{\theta}$ singular, then $|t-\theta|^{-2} p_{t}^{\prime \prime}$ tends to zero as $t \rightarrow \theta$.

The following lemma is related to inverse function theorems.
Lemma 3. Let $\theta$ be a $D$-regular point of $\Theta$. Then there is a number $c>0$ and an $\varepsilon>0$ such that $|t-\theta|<\varepsilon$ implies

$$
\left\|F_{t}-F_{\theta}\right\|_{K} \geq c|t-\theta|
$$

Proof. Let $\mu$ be a probability measure which dominates $p_{\theta}$. Let $f(x, t)$ be the density of $p_{t}$ with respect to $\mu$ and let $g(x, t)=\sqrt{f(x, t)}$. At a point $\theta$ where DQM holds one can write

$$
g(x, \theta+t)-g(x, \theta)=t V(x, \theta)+|t| R_{2}(x, \theta, t)
$$

with $\int V(x, \theta) \mu(d x)=0$ and with $\int R_{2}^{2}(x, \theta, t) \mu(d x)$ tending to zero as $|t| \rightarrow$ 0 . This implies

$$
f(x, \theta+t)-f(x, t)=2 t V(x, \theta) g(x, \theta)+|t| R(x, \theta, t)
$$

with $\delta_{1}(t, \theta)=\int|R(x, \theta, t)| \mu(d x)$ tending to zero as $t \rightarrow 0$.
Let $u$ be the unit vector $u=|t|^{-1} t$ and let $\phi(u, y)$ be the function $\phi(u, y)= \int I(x \leq y) u V(x, \theta) g(x, \theta) \mu(d x)$. Integrating the previous relations gives

$$
\left|F_{\theta+t}(y)-F_{\theta}(y)\right| \geq 2|\phi(u, y)|-|t| \delta(t, \theta)
$$

where $\delta(t, \theta)=\delta_{1}(t, \theta)+|t|^{-1}\left\|p_{\theta+t}^{\prime \prime}\right\|$. We have seen in Section 3 that DQM, or even the condition called ( $l$ ), implies that $|t|^{-1}\left\|p_{\theta+t}^{\prime \prime}\right\|$ tends to zero as $|t| \rightarrow 0$. Thus $\delta(t, \theta) \rightarrow 0$ as $t \rightarrow 0$. For the Kolmogorov norm one obtains

$$
\left\|F_{\theta+t}-F_{\theta}\right\| \geq[2 c(u, \theta)-\delta(t, \theta)]|t|
$$

with $c(u, \theta)=\sup _{y}|\phi(u, y)|$. Thus the result will follow if we can show that $\inf c(u, \theta) \geq c>0$.

Now suppose that $c(u, \theta)=0$ for some unit vector $u$. This implies $\phi(u, y) \equiv 0$ and therefore $u V(x, \theta)=0$ almost everywhere $\mu$. Part (iii) of the definition of
regular point shows that the singular parts $\lim _{\varepsilon}\left(1 / \varepsilon^{2}\right) p_{\theta+\varepsilon u}^{\prime \prime}$ do not contribute in the limit to the covariance of the derivative of the process of covariance kernel $\int \sqrt{d p_{s} d p_{t}}$. Thus $\int|u V(x, \theta)|^{2} f(x, \theta) \mu(d x)>0$ contrary to the assumption $c(u, \theta)=0$. The function $u \mapsto c(u, \theta)$ is lower semicontinuous in $u$. It reaches its minimum on the compact $|u|=1$. Hence $c(u, \theta) \geq c>0$. This concludes the proof of the lemma.

Remark 1. One could carry out the same argument without part (iii) of the definition of regular point at the cost of strengthening (ii) to read that $\dot{\xi} I\left(p_{\theta}>0\right)$ have a nonsingular covariance matrix.

Remark 2. The same argument can be applied to certain other distances. For instance, it applies to distances of the type $\int\left|F_{t}(y)-F_{\theta}(y)\right| v(d y)$ for suitable probability measures $v$. All that is needed is that $\int|\phi(u, y)| v(d y)=0$ implies $u V(x, \theta)=0$ almost surely $\mu$. A similar remark applies to the distances $\left[\int\left|F_{t}(y)-F_{\theta}(y)\right|^{2} v(d y)\right]^{1 / 2}$.

For the existence of estimates, one can assert the following.
Proposition 1. Assume that the set $\Theta$ is an intersection $\Theta=F \cap G$ with $F$ closed and $G$ open in $\mathbb{R}^{k}$. Assume also that the map $\theta \mapsto p_{\theta}$ is continuous for the Hellinger distance and that $s \neq t$ implies $p_{s} \neq p_{t}$.

Then there exist estimates $\hat{\theta}_{n}$ such that $\sqrt{n}\left|\hat{\theta}_{n}-\theta\right|$ is bounded in probability at each $D$-regular point of $\Theta$. In addition, if $K$ is a compact set of $D$-regular points on which DQM holds uniformly, then for each $\varepsilon>0$ there is a $b$ such that

$$
\sup _{n} \sup _{\theta \in K} p_{\theta}^{n}\left\{\sqrt{n}\left|\hat{\theta}_{n}-\theta\right|>b\right\}<\varepsilon
$$

Proof. The condition that $\Theta=F \cap G$ with $F$ closed and $G$ open is equivalent to the condition that $\Theta$ be locally compact. Thus, there exists a localization sequence $\left\{\Theta_{k}\right\}$ formed of compacts such that each compact subset of $\Theta$ is contained in one $\Theta_{k}$. We have already noted above (Lemma 2) that there exist estimates $\theta_{n}^{*}$ that are uniformly consistent on compacts.

Define $m_{n}$ to be the smallest integer such that $\theta_{n}^{*} \in \Theta_{m_{n}-1}$. Assuming temporarily that the sample space is Euclidean, let $F_{n}$ be the empirical cumulative distribution function for $n$ observations. Let $F_{\theta}$ be the cumulative of $p_{\theta}$. Take for $\hat{\theta}_{n}$ any point of $\Theta_{m_{n}}$ such that

$$
\sqrt{n}\left\|F_{n}-F_{\hat{\theta}_{n}}\right\|_{K} \leq \frac{1}{n}+\inf _{t}\left\{\sqrt{n}\left\|F_{n}-F_{t}\right\|_{K} ; t \in \Theta_{m_{n}}\right\}
$$

Since $\Theta$ is separable for the $L_{1}$-norm and therefore also for the KolmogorovSmirnov norm, there is no difficulty in selecting $\hat{\theta}_{n}$ in a measurable manner. However, the inequalities written below do not actually depend on the measurability of $\hat{\theta}_{n}$.

In all the cases where $\theta \in \Theta_{m_{n}}$, one will have

$$
\sqrt{n}\left\|F_{\hat{\theta}_{n}}-F_{\theta}\right\|_{K} \leq \frac{1}{n}+2 \sqrt{n}\left\|F_{n}-F_{\theta}\right\|_{K}
$$

Thus the variables $\sqrt{n}\left\|F_{\hat{\theta}_{n}}-F_{\theta}\right\|_{K}$ remain bounded in probability. The first assertion follows then by application of Lemma 3.

For the second assertion, let $K$ be a compact subset of regular points on which DQM holds uniformly. One can assume that the measure $\mu$ used in Lemma 3 dominates all the $p_{t}, t \in \Theta$. Then the quantity called $\delta(t, \theta)$ in the proof of Lemma 3 is such that $\sup \{\delta(t, \theta) ; \theta \in K,|t|<\varepsilon\}$ tends to zero as $\varepsilon \rightarrow 0$. The derivatives $V(x, \theta)$ are continuous in quadratic mean. It follows that if $t \in K$ converges to $\theta$ and if the unit vector $u$ converges to a limit $u_{0}$ then $u V(x, t) g(x, t)$ converges to $u_{0} V(x, \theta) g(x, \theta)$ in the first mean. This implies that the function $(u, \theta) \mapsto c(u, \theta)$ is lower semicontinuous. Since it cannot vanish, one has $\inf \{c(u, \theta) ;|u|=1, \theta \in K\}>0$. The result follows. To conclude, it remains to remove the assumption that the sample space is Euclidean. To do this take a countable dense subset $S$ of $\Theta$. Let $Z$ be the likelihood ratio function $Z(\theta)=d p_{\theta} / d \mu$ for $\theta \in S$. This has values in $[0, \infty]^{S}$ which is compact in its product topology. One can map $[0, \infty]^{S}$ in a one-to-one Borel way onto the interval $[0,1]$. The sufficiency of $Z$ implies that no information is lost in the process. Thus, the general case can be reduced to the case where $(\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{A})$ is the interval [ 0,1 ] with its Borel sets.

Remark 1. Let $\mathscr{S} \subset \mathscr{A}$ be a class of subsets of $\mathscr{X}$ and let $\left\|p_{s}-p_{t}\right\|_{\mathscr{S}}= \sup \left\{\left|p_{s}(S)-p_{t}(S)\right| ; S \in \mathscr{S}\right\}$. Then, of course, $\left\|p_{s}-p_{t}\right\|_{\mathscr{S}} \leq 2 h(s, t)$. The proof of Proposition 1 shows that there are classes $\mathscr{S}$ such that (i) for each regular $\theta$, there is an $\varepsilon>0$ and a $b>0$ such that $|t-\theta|<\varepsilon$ implies

$$
b h(t, \theta) \leq\left\|p_{t}-p_{\theta}\right\|_{\mathscr{Y}} \leq 2 h(t, \theta)
$$

(ii) the class $\mathscr{S}$ is a Vapnik-Corvonenkis class satisfying the measurability restrictions of Chapter 16, Section 8.

Instead of transforming $\mathscr{X}$ to $[0,1]$ as was done in the proof of Proposition 1 and using the Kolmogorov-Smirnov norm, one could use any such V. C. class and the norm induced by it. Property called (i) above is not excessively demanding. According to the proof of Lemma 3, it will hold whenever $\sup _{\mathscr{S}}\left|\int_{\mathscr{S}} u V(x, \theta) g(x, \theta) \mu(d x)\right|=0$ implies that $u V(x, \theta) g(x, \theta)=0$ almost everywhere $\mu$. This is true in particular if $v(S) \equiv 0$ for a difference $v$ of two probability measures implies that $v(A) \equiv 0$ for $A \in \mathscr{A}$.

Remark 2. The reader will note that all the assumptions of Proposition 1 are purely local except for the identifiability condition that $s \neq t$ implies $p_{s} \neq p_{t}$. This is in keeping with the type of conditions usually found in the literature. The assumptions of Proposition 1 allow strange examples such as the trinomial example of Chapter 11, Section 7.

It would be more reasonable to assume that the map $\theta \mapsto p_{\theta}$ be not only injective but also proper in an appropriate sense. For an injective map to be proper it is necessary and sufficient that it maps closed sets to closed sets. Then, for each $\theta$, if $t$ is far away from $\theta$, the measure $p_{t}$ is far away from $p_{\theta}$. One could, for instance, assume that the map $t \mapsto p_{t}$ be proper for the Hellinger distance on measures. This would not avoid the use of Lemma 1 in the proof of Proposition 1 since the map may not be proper for the Kolmogorov distance used in the
construction. If, however, $\theta \mapsto p_{\theta}$ is proper for the Kolmogorov distance, one can avoid Lemma 1 entirely. One can also assert somewhat more in the form of a robustness property as follows.

Assume that the observations $X_{1}, X_{2}, \ldots, X_{n}$ are independent, but that $\mathscr{L}\left(X_{j}\right)=p_{j, n}$, a measure which is not necessarily one of the $p_{\theta}, \theta \in \Theta$ and may depend on $j$. Let $\bar{p}_{n}=(1 / n) \sum_{j=1}^{n} p_{j, n}$. Assume for simplicity that the observation space ( $\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{A}$ ) is Euclidean.

Lemma 4. Let the assumptions of Proposition 1 be satisfied. Assume in addition
that the map $\theta \mapsto p_{\theta}$ is proper for the Kolmogorov-Smirnov norm. Define $\hat{\theta}_{n}$ by

$$
\sqrt{n}\left\|F_{n}-F_{\hat{\theta}_{n}}\right\|_{K} \leq \frac{1}{n}+\inf \left\{\sqrt{n}\left\|F_{n}-F_{t}\right\|_{K} ; t \in \Theta\right\}
$$

Then if $\sqrt{n}\left\|\bar{p}_{n}-p_{\theta}\right\|_{K}$ remains bounded for some D-regular point $\theta \in \Theta$, the variable $\sqrt{n}\left|\hat{\theta}_{n}-\theta\right|$ remains bounded in probability.

This follows immediately from the method of proof of Proposition 1 and from the inequalities of Chapter 16, Section 7. The same kind of result holds if one replaces the Kolmogorov-Smirnov norms everywhere by norms on suitable Vapnik-Červonenkis classes, as explained in the preceding remark. It also holds for the integrated norms $\left\{\int(F-G)^{2} d v\right\}^{1 / 2}$ mentioned in Remark 2, following Lemma 3.

Once in possession of estimates which are $q$-convergent, one can proceed to apply the constructions described in Chapter 11. It is clear that at every $D$-regular point the relevant local experiments are asymptotically Gaussian. Therefore all such $D$-regular points will be "good" in the sense of Chapter 11, Section 7 and all the results of Chapter 11 become applicable. We shall not repeat these results here. However, two remarks are necessary.

One of them relates to the use of the so-called "minimum distance method" for constructions of estimates as in Proposition 1 or Lemma 4. The other is a modification due to Beran of the local constructions of Chapter 11 intended to make the resulting estimates more "robust".

For the minimum distance methods, Millar has emphasized that they can yield a large variety of estimates. He works on the line with Hilbertian norms of the integral type (as mentioned in the remarks which follow Lemma 3) and obtains estimates that are asymptotically equivalent to the median, the HodgesLehmann estimates, the trimmed or Winsorized means, etc. He also demonstrates certain locally asymptotically minimax properties of the estimates. The framework used by Millar is approximately the following.

One considers a particular parametric family $\left\{p_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ of probability measures on a set $(\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{A})$. The set $\Theta$ is an open subset of $\mathbb{R}^{d}$. The observations $X_{1}, X_{2}, \ldots, X_{n}$ are independent with a common distribution $q_{n}$ which is not necessarily one of the $p_{\theta}$.

Let $\mathscr{M}$ be the set of all finite signed measures on the line and let $\|\cdot\|_{M}$ be a particular norm (or semi-norm) on $\mathscr{M}$. (In some cases $\|\cdot\|_{M}$ is not defined on
the entire $\mathscr{M}$ but only on a subspace containing the $p_{\theta}$, the $q_{n}$ and the possible empirical measures obtainable from the $q_{n}$.)

Typical assumptions would be
(M1) There is a particular $\theta_{0} \in \Theta$, say $\theta_{0}=0$, such that

$$
\sup _{n} \sqrt{n}\left\|p_{0}-q_{n}\right\|_{M}<\infty
$$

(M2) As a subset of $\mathscr{M}$ normed by $\|\cdot\|_{M}$, the family $\left\{p_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ admits at $p_{0}$ a tangent linear subspace $L$ of dimension $d$.
(M3) Let $\mu_{n}$ be the empirical measure obtained from the $X_{j}$. Then, given $c$, there is a number $b$ such that $E\left\{n\left\|q_{n}-\mu_{n}\right\|_{M}^{2} \mid q_{n}\right\} \leq b$ for all $n$ and all $q_{n}$ such that $\sqrt{n}\left\|p_{\theta}-q_{n}\right\|_{M}<c$.
(M4) The map $\theta \mapsto p_{\theta}$ from $\Theta$ Euclidean to ( $\mathscr{M},\|\cdot\|_{M}$ ) is injective and proper. (This last condition may be omitted if one desires only to estimate $p_{\theta}$, but not $\theta$ itself.) The minimum distance method applied to $\|\cdot\|_{M}$ yields estimates $\hat{\theta}_{n}$ such that

$$
\sqrt{n}\left\|\mu_{n}-p_{\hat{\theta}_{n}}\right\|_{M} \leq \frac{1}{n}+\inf \left\{\sqrt{n}\left\|\mu_{n}-p_{t}\right\|_{M} ; t \in \Theta\right\} .
$$

Under the conditions given here, the assumptions 1 to 3 of Chapter 11, Section 10 are satisfied and the results of that section are applicable. If, in addition, the norms $\|\cdot\|_{M}$ are of Hilbertian type, Assumptions 1 to 5 of Chapter 11, Section 10 are satisfied. Thus, in the Hilbertian case, if $\Pi$ is the orthogonal projection of $\mathscr{M}$ onto the tangent space $L$, the quantities $\sqrt{n}\left\|p_{\hat{\theta}_{n}}-\Pi \mu_{n}\right\|_{M}$ will tend to zero in $q_{n}$-probability and $\sqrt{n}\left(\Pi \mu_{n}-\Pi q_{n}\right)$ will be asymptotically normally distributed.

Millar considers loss functions whose essential features are that they are positive increasing functions of $n\left\|\pi_{0} q_{n}-p_{\hat{\theta}_{n}}\right\|_{M}^{2}$, where $\pi_{0} q_{n}$ is the point $p_{\theta}$ (assumed to exist) such that

$$
\left\|p_{\theta}-q_{n}\right\|_{M}=\inf _{t}\left\{\left\|p_{t}-q_{n}\right\|_{M} ; t \in \Theta\right\}
$$

Under supplementary assumptions Millar obtains for the minimum distance $\hat{\theta}_{n}$ a local asymptotic minimax result for such loss functions.

The supplementary assumptions imply that for $q_{n}$ satisfying (M1) the process $\sqrt{n}\left(\mu_{n}-q_{n}\right)$ has in $\mathscr{M}$ normed by $\|\cdot\|_{M}$ a limiting Gaussian distribution $\mathscr{L}(Z)$ independent of $q_{n}$. They also imply that, if $v \in L$, there are sequences $\left\{q_{n}\right\}$ satisfying (M1) such that $\left\|\sqrt{n}\left(q_{n}-p_{0}\right)-v\right\|_{M} \rightarrow 0$ and such that the experiments $\left(p_{0}, q_{n}\right)$ converge to $(\mathscr{L}(Z), \mathscr{L}(Z+v))$. The minimax results are obtained through application of results similar to those of Chapter 10.

Beran's results are of a different nature. We shall not give them in detail, but try to indicate their spirit, linking them to the framework of Chapter 11. Assume that the conditions of Lemma 4 are satisfied for certain distributions $p_{j, n}$. Assume also that there is a $D$-regular point $\theta_{0} \in \Theta$ such that $\sqrt{n}\left\|\bar{p}_{n}-p_{\theta_{0}}\right\|_{K}$ remains bounded. Take $\theta_{0}=0$ for simplicity.

Pave $\Theta$ by cubes, obtaining, as in Chapter 11, a skeleton $\Omega_{n} \subset \Theta$ whose distinct points are at distance at least $1 / \sqrt{n}$. Construct an estimate $\hat{\theta}_{n}$ as explained in Lemma 4 and discretize it, obtaining an estimate $\hat{\omega}_{n}$ with values in $\Omega_{n}$. This $\hat{\omega}_{n}$ will play the role of the auxiliary estimate called $\hat{\theta}$ in Chapter 11.

For each $\omega \in \Omega_{n}$ such that $\sqrt{n}|\omega|$ remains bounded, let $S_{\omega, n}=\left\{\omega+\left(u_{k} / \sqrt{n}\right)\right.$; $k=0,1,2, \ldots, d\}$ where $d$ is the dimension of $\Theta$, where $u_{0}=0$, and where $\left\{u_{k} ; k=1,2, \ldots, d\right\}$ is a basis of $\mathbb{R}^{d}$. If $\theta_{0}=0$ is inferior to $\Theta$, then, eventually, $S_{\omega, n} \subset \Theta$ and one can apply the constructions of Chapter 11. To carry them out one selects certain logarithms of likelihood ratios $\Lambda_{\omega, n}^{\prime}(s, \omega) ; s \in S_{\omega, n}$ and one solves linear equations of the type

$$
\left(T_{\omega}-\omega\right)^{\prime} \bar{M}_{\omega, n}(s-\omega)=\Lambda_{\omega, n}^{\prime}(s, \omega)+\frac{1}{2}(s-\omega)^{\prime} \bar{M}_{\omega, n}(s-\omega)
$$

for $s \in S_{\omega, n}$, as explained in Chapter 11, Sections 3 and 8.
If $P_{\theta}^{n}$ is the product of $n$ copies of $p_{\theta}$, the $\Lambda_{\omega, n}^{\prime}(s, \omega)$ should be approximations of the log likelihood

$$
\Lambda_{n}(s, \omega)=\log \left(\frac{d P_{s}^{n}}{d P_{\omega}^{n}}\right)
$$

Let $s=\omega+u_{k} / \sqrt{n}$ for a particular $k$. Let

$$
f_{n, j, s, \omega}^{*}=\left(\sqrt{\frac{d p_{s}}{d p_{\omega}}}-1\right)
$$

evaluated at the $j$ th observation. Let $f_{n, j, s, \omega}=f_{n, j, s, \omega}^{*} \wedge 1$. In the present case, for $\sqrt{n}|\omega|$ bounded, the differences

$$
\left[\Lambda_{n}(s, \omega)+\frac{1}{2}(s-\omega)^{\prime} \bar{M}_{\omega, n}(s-\omega)\right]-2 \sum_{j=1}^{n}\left[f_{n, j, s, \omega}^{*}-E_{\omega} f_{n, j, s, \omega}^{*}\right]
$$

tend to zero in $P_{\omega}^{n}$ probability. Also in these expressions one can replace $f_{n, j, s, \omega}^{*}$ by $f_{n, j, s, \omega}$. Thus one can replace the equations which determine $T_{\omega}$ by the equations

$$
\frac{1}{2}\left(T_{\omega}-\omega\right)^{\prime} \bar{M}_{\omega, n}(s-\omega)=\sum_{j=1}^{n}\left[f_{n, j, s, \omega}-E_{\omega} f_{n, j, s, \omega}\right]
$$

The matrices $\bar{M}_{\omega, n}$ are such that $(s-\omega)^{\prime} \bar{M}_{\omega, n}(s-\omega)$ is an estimate of the variance of $2 \sum_{j} f_{n, j, s, \omega}$. Here, if $s=\omega+\left(u_{k} / \sqrt{n}\right)$ and $t=\omega+\left(u_{l} / \sqrt{n}\right)$, we can take for $(s-\omega)^{\prime} M_{\omega, n}(t-\omega)$ the expectation $4 n E_{\omega} f_{n, j, s, \omega} f_{n, j, t, \omega}$. We shall use this choice of $\bar{M}_{\omega, n}$ throughout.

According to Chapter 11, if $T_{n}$ is determined by $T_{n}=T_{\hat{\omega}_{n}}$, the $T_{n}$ will be asymptotically sufficient, distinguished, and asymptotically normally distributed for the experiments $\left\{P_{\theta}^{n} ; \theta \in \Theta, \sqrt{n}|\theta| \leq b\right\}$.

Beran's idea is to replace the $f_{n, j, s, \omega}$ by other functions, say $g_{n, j, s, \omega}$ in such a way that:
(i) if there are $\theta$ such that $p_{j, n}=p_{\theta}$ and $\sup _{n} \sqrt{n}|\theta|<\infty$, then the differences

$$
\sum\left[f_{n, j, s, \omega}-E_{\omega} f_{n, j, s, \omega}\right]-\sum\left[g_{n, j, s, \omega}-E_{\omega} g_{n, j, s, \omega}\right]
$$

tend to zero in probability, and
(ii) if the distributions $p_{j, n}$ do not belong to the family $\left\{p_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ but remain close enough to $p_{0}$, the sum $\sum_{j} g_{n, j, s, \omega}$ still behave reasonably well.
In Beran's construction the functions call $f$ above are replaced by functions $g$ of the type $g=f \psi\left(a_{n} n^{\nu} f\right)$ where $\psi, a_{n}$, and $\gamma$ satisfy the following requirements:
(R1) $\gamma$ and $\psi$ are non random and $\frac{1}{4}<\gamma<\frac{1}{2}$.
(R2) The function $\psi$ is continuous at zero. It satisfies $\psi(0)=1,0 \leq \psi(x) \leq 1$, $\psi(x)=\psi(-x)$, and $\sup _{x} x \psi(x)=b<\infty$.
(R3) The $a_{n}$ are positive integer valued random variables such that $a_{n} \geq 1$.
For a nonrandom $c$, let $g_{n, j, s, \omega}(c)=f_{n, j, s, \omega} \psi\left[c n^{\nu} f_{n, j, s, \omega}\right]$ and let $m_{n, j, s, \omega}(c)= E_{\omega} g_{n, j, s, \omega}(c)$. Finally let $g_{n, j, s, \omega}^{*}=g_{n, j, s, \omega}\left(a_{n}\right)-m_{n, j, s, \omega}\left(a_{n}\right)$. The estimates $T_{n}^{*}$ will be determined by the equations

$$
\frac{1}{2}\left(T_{n}^{*}-\omega\right) \bar{M}_{\omega, n}(s-\omega)=\sum_{j} g_{n, j, s, \omega}^{*}
$$

where for $\omega$ are substituted the discretized estimate $\hat{\omega}_{n}$.
To describe the performance of $T_{n}^{*}$, consider the average $\bar{p}_{n}=(1 / n) \sum_{j} p_{j, n}$ and let $\theta \in \Theta$ be such that

$$
n \int\left(\sqrt{d \bar{p}_{n}}-\sqrt{d p_{\theta}}\right)^{2} \leq \inf _{t \in \Theta} n \int\left(\sqrt{d \bar{p}_{n}}-\sqrt{d p_{t}}\right)^{2}+2^{-n}
$$

For these estimates, Beran proves the following result.
Proposition 2. Let $0 \in \Theta$ be an interior point of $\Theta$ which is $D$-regular for the family $\left\{p_{t} ; t \in \Theta\right\}$. Let the conditions (R1), (R2), and (R3) be satisfied for random variables $a_{n}$ which remain bounded in probability for the product measures $Q_{n}=X_{j} p_{j, n}$. Assume that the conditions of Lemma 4 are satisfied. Assume also that $n \int\left(\sqrt{d \bar{p}_{n}}-\sqrt{d p_{0}}\right)^{2}$ remains bounded and let $\Gamma$ be the covariance matrix of the limit of $\mathscr{L}\left[\sqrt{n} T_{n} \mid P_{0}^{n}\right]$. Then, for the value $\theta$ defined above, the distributions $\mathscr{L}\left\{\sqrt{n}\left(T_{n}^{*}-\theta\right) \mid Q_{n}\right\}$ are approximable by centered Gaussian distributions with covariances $\Gamma_{n}$ such that the cluster points of $\Gamma-\Gamma_{n}$ are all positive semidefinite. If, furthermore, $\left\{P_{0}^{n}\right\}$ and $\left\{Q_{n}\right\}$ are contiguous, the differences $\sqrt{n}\left(T_{n}^{*}-T_{n}\right)$ tend to zero in probability.

The proof of this proposition is a rather complex one. We shall not reproduce it here. However, the proposition itself calls for several comments. Note first that the conditions used impose essentially no restrictions on the $p_{j, n}$ except that their averages $\bar{p}_{n}$ must be such that $n \int\left(\sqrt{d \bar{p}_{n}}-\sqrt{d p_{0}}\right)^{2}$ remains bounded. This allows a substantial amount of variability. However, if for instance the $p_{j, n}, j=1,2, \ldots, n$ are all copies of the same $\bar{p}_{n}$, this means that they are rather close to $p_{0}$.

It would be much preferable for applications to work on balls of the type $\sqrt{n}\left\|\bar{p}_{n}-p_{0}\right\| \leq b$ for norms such as the Kolmogorov-Smirnov norm or similar objects. Beran has shown that results of this kind are achievable if the densities $d p_{\theta} / d p_{0}$ are smooth functions of the observations or if they can be replaced by smooth functions without disturbing the performance of estimates of $\theta$.

Another comment is that the estimates $T_{n}^{*}$ of Proposition 2 are locally asymptotically minimax around $p_{0}$ in an obvious sense. That property does not depend on the choice of the numbers $\gamma$ (with $1 / 4<\gamma<1 / 2$ ). Neither does it depend on the choice of the integer valued variables $a_{n}$ as long as $a_{n} \geq 1$ and that $a_{n}$ remains bounded in probability. In fact, the last part of the proof consists in showing than $T_{n}^{*}$ computed from the $a_{n}$ and a $T_{n}^{\prime}$ computed as if $a_{n} \equiv 1$ differ by variables of order smaller than $(1 / \sqrt{n})$. Since any such choice of the $a_{n}$ will lead to the same asymptotic behavior on the Hellinger neighborhoods $n \int\left(\sqrt{d \bar{p}_{n}}-\sqrt{d p_{0}}\right)^{2} \leq c$, making the $a_{n}$ random appears irrelevant. Indeed it is, as far as Proposition 2 is concerned. However Beran's idea was to make $a_{n}$ random and large if the estimated distance between the $p_{j, n}$ and the "model" $\left\{p_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ appears large. This is obviously a step in the right direction, even if the provable results leave something to be desired.

Suppose for instance that one takes either a fixed Vapnik-Crervonenkis class $\mathscr{S}$ or classes $\mathscr{S}_{n}$ that satisfy the requirements of Chapter 16. Let $\mu_{n}$ be the empirical measure and let $a_{n}-1$ be the integer part of $\inf _{t}\left\{\sqrt{n}\left\|\mu_{n}-p_{t}\right\|_{\mathscr{S}_{n}} ; t \in \Theta\right\}$, where $\|\lambda\|_{\mathscr{S}_{n}}=\sup \left\{|\lambda(S)| ; S \in \mathscr{S}_{n}\right\}$. Then, if $\sqrt{n}\left\|\bar{p}_{n}-p_{0}\right\|_{\mathscr{S}_{n}}$ remains bounded, the $a_{n}$ will remain bounded in probability and, if the necessary additional requirements are satisfied, Proposition 2 will become applicable.

If on the contrary $\inf \sqrt{n}\left\|\bar{p}_{n}-p_{t}\right\|_{\mathscr{S}_{n}}$ tends to infinity, the $a_{n}$ will tend in probability to infinity. Here the hope would be that since the $g_{n, j, s, \omega}\left(a_{n}\right)$ of the construction became small (since $\left|g_{n, \mathrm{j}, s, \omega}\left(a_{n}\right)\right| \leq b a_{n}^{-1} n^{-\gamma}$ ), the correction made to pass from the auxiliary estimate $\hat{\omega}_{n}$ to the $T_{n}^{*}$ would also become small, so that the initially "good" performance of the $\hat{\omega}_{n}$ would be preserved.

The situation is more complex and not so easily amenable to simple solutions. However, let us note that a statistician who observes an overly large value of $\inf _{t}\left\{\left\|\mu_{n}-p_{t}\right\|_{\mathscr{S}_{n}} ; t \in \Theta\right\}$ would be warned that his "model" does not fit, even on the average. He would probably be better advised to think over the situation afresh. (For very large $n$, this may not mean that the basic physical assumptions are in error. It may simply be that the vagaries of the measurement processes have not been adequately taken into account. However, see the papers of J. Berkson.)

## 7. The Behavior of Posterior Distributions

Some information on the behavior of Bayes estimates has already been included in Chapters 12 and 16. Here we concentrate on some further information available in the independent identically distributed case. A first step is the identification of what was called the accessible $\sigma$-field in Chapter 12 with the $\sigma$-field generated by weak zero-one sets. With supplementary assumptions, this yields



a theorem of Doob on "almost sure consistency for almost all $\theta$." Using results of $\mathbf{D}$. Freedman it is pointed out that "almost all $\theta$ " may be unsatisfactory because of the topological meagerness of the sets involved.

Leaving aside the "almost all $\theta$ " we refer to Chapter 16 for some conditions which imply consistency for a particular fixed $\theta$. Another result relates to differentiability in quadratic mean, as in Section 6. Within this last framework we give some results of the Laplace-Bernstein-von Mises-type relative to asymptotic normality of posterior distributions.

Consider an arbitrary experiment $\mathscr{E}=\left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ given by probability measures on a space ( $\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{A}$ ). Let $\mathscr{E}^{n}=\left\{P_{\theta}^{n} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ be the direct product of $n$ copies of $\mathscr{E}$. Let $\mathscr{B}_{0}$ be the smallest $\sigma$-field of subsets of $\Theta$ such that all the maps $\theta \mapsto P_{\theta}(A)$ are measurable and let $\mathscr{B}$ denote some other $\sigma$-field such that $\mathscr{B}_{0} \subset \mathscr{B}$. Let $\mu$ be a probability measure on $\mathscr{B}$. Let $P_{\theta}^{\infty}$ be the product measure induced by the $P_{\theta}$ on the countable product $\left(\mathscr{X}^{\infty}, \mathscr{A}^{\infty}\right)$ of copies of the $(\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{A})$.

In Chapter 12 a numerical function $f$ defined on $\Theta$ was called "accessible" if there existed $\mathscr{A}^{n}$-measurable functions $h_{n}$ such that $\iint\left|f(\theta)-h_{n}(x)\right| P_{\theta}^{n}(d x) \mu(d \theta) \rightarrow 0$ as $n \rightarrow \infty$. The accessible functions are the $\mathscr{B}_{a}$-measurable $\mu$-integrable functions for a certain $\sigma$-field $\mathscr{B}_{a}$ contained in the $\mu$ completion of $\mathscr{B}$. To characterize this $\sigma$-field one can introduce zero-one sets as follows.

Definition 1. A set $A \in \mathscr{A}^{\infty}$ is called a strict zero-one set if $P_{\theta}^{\infty}(A)= \left[P_{\theta}^{\infty}(A)\right]^{2}$ identically in $\theta$. It is called a $\mu$-zero-one set if $\int\left\{P_{\theta}^{\infty}(A)-\left[P_{\theta}^{\infty}(A)\right]^{2}\right\} \mu(d \theta)=$ 0.

It is easy to see that if $A_{1}$ and $A_{2}$ are $\mu$-zero-one sets so are $A_{1} \cup A_{2}, A_{1} \cap A_{2}$, and $A_{i}^{c}$. Furthermore if $\left\{A_{n} ; n=1,2, \ldots\right\}$ is an increasing sequence of $\mu$-zeroone sets, then $\bigcup_{n} A_{n}$ is a $\mu$-zero-one set. Thus the $\mu$-zero-one sets form a $\sigma$-field $Z$.

Definition 2. A subset $B \subset \Theta$ is a $\mu$-one set of $\Theta$ if there is an $A \in Z$ such that $B=\left\{\theta: P_{\theta}(A)=1\right\}$.

Letting $\mathscr{B}_{z}$ denote that family of all $\mu$-one sets of $\Theta$, one checks easily that $\mathscr{B}_{z}$ is a $\sigma$-field. Let $S(d x, d \theta)=P_{\theta}^{\infty}(d x) \mu(d \theta)$.

Proposition 1. The accessible $\sigma$-field $\mathscr{B}_{a}$, the $\sigma$-field $\mathscr{B}_{z}$ of $\mu$-one sets, and the $\mu$-completion $\overline{\mathscr{B}}_{0}$ of $\mathscr{B}_{0}$ are the same. If $f$, defined on $\Theta$, is an accessible numerical function, then the conditional expectation $E_{S}\left(f \mid \mathscr{A}^{n}\right)$ tends to $f(\theta)$ almost surely $P_{\theta}^{\infty}$ for $\mu$-almost all $\theta \in \Theta$.

Note. In the formulation of the proposition, we have written $E_{S}\left(f \mid \mathscr{A}^{n}\right)$ for the conditional expectation for the measure $S$ given the cylinder $\sigma$-field generated by $\mathscr{A}^{n}$ on $\mathscr{X}^{\infty} \times \Theta$. A similar abuse of notation will be used below.

Proof. Let $f$ be accessible. Then it is the limit in $L_{1}$-norm for $S$ of a sequence of $\mathscr{A}^{n}$-measurable functions. Thus it is equivalent to an $\mathscr{A}^{\infty}$-measurable function, say $g$. By the martingale theorem $g_{n}=E_{S}\left(g \mid \mathscr{A}^{n}\right)$ converges almost surely $S$ to $g$. Let $D$ be the subset of $\mathscr{X}^{\infty} \times \Theta$ where $g_{n}-f$ does not converge to zero. It is a (completion) measurable set such that $\int_{D} P_{\theta}^{\infty}(d x) \mu(d \theta)=0$. Thus
$P_{\theta}^{\infty}$ of the section of $D$ at $\theta$ is zero for $\mu$ almost $\theta$ by Fubini's theorem. This
proves the last assertion.
If $f$ is accessible and $f(\theta)=f^{2}(\theta)$ identically in $\theta$, then the function $g$ defined above is such that $g=g^{2}$ almost everywhere. Hence $f$ is the indicator of a set $B \in \mathscr{B}_{z}$. It follows that $\mathscr{B}_{z} \subset \mathscr{B}_{a}$. The martingale argument used above also shows that $\mathscr{B}_{a} \subset \mathscr{B}_{z}$. It remains to show that $\mathscr{B}_{z}$ coincides with $\overline{\mathscr{B}}_{0}$. To do this, take a $B \in \overline{\mathscr{B}}_{0}$ and a $B_{0} \in \mathscr{B}_{0}$ such that $B$ and $B_{0}$ are equivalent. Since $B_{0} \in \mathscr{B}_{0}$, there is a countable family $\left\{A_{k} ; k=1,2 \ldots\right\}$ of sets $A_{k} \in \mathscr{A}$ such that $B_{0}$ is in the $\sigma$-field generated by the functions $\theta \mapsto P_{\theta}\left(A_{k}\right)$. For each $A_{k}$, the averages $(1 / n) \sum_{j=1}^{n} I_{A_{k}}\left(X_{j}\right)$ converge almost surely $P_{\theta}^{\infty}$ to $P_{\theta}\left(A_{k}\right)$ for all $\theta \in \Theta$. Thus the functions $\theta \mapsto P_{\theta}\left(A_{k}\right)$ are $\mathscr{B}_{a}$-measurable. It follows that $\mathscr{B}_{0} \subset \mathscr{B}_{a}$. Conversely, if $B=\left\{\theta: P_{\theta}^{\infty}(A)=1\right\}$ for some $A \in \mathscr{A}^{\infty}$, then $B \in \mathscr{B}_{0}$ since the maps $\theta \mapsto P_{\theta}^{n}(C)$ are $\mathscr{B}_{0}$-measurable for all $C \in \mathscr{A}^{n}$ and the maps $\theta \mapsto P_{\theta}^{\infty}(A)$ similarly measurable for $A \in \mathscr{A}^{\infty}$. This concludes the proof of the proposition.

From this proposition one can obtain the following results, which are slight modifications of a theorem of Doob.

Proposition 2. Let $\mathscr{A}$ be a countably generated $\sigma$-field . Assume that $P_{\theta}= P_{\theta^{\prime}}$ implies $\theta=\theta^{\prime}$. Let $\Theta$ be a topological Hausdorff space and let $\mathscr{B}$ denote its $\sigma$-field of Borel subsets. Then $\mathscr{B} \subset \mathscr{B}_{a}$ in each of the following two situations:
(1) $\Theta$ is a Borel subset of a complete separable metric space.
(2) $\Theta$ is arbitrary Hausdorff, but $\mu$ is a Radon measure.

Proof. The first case is clearly a particular case of the second since then $\mu$ is automatically a Radon measure. Thus it will be sufficient to prove (2). To do this let $\mathscr{D}$ be a countable field generating $\mathscr{A}$. Any probability measure on $\mathscr{A}$ can then be identified with an element $\{P(D) ; D \in \mathscr{D}\}$ of the space $\mathbb{R}^{\mathscr{D}}$ of real functions on $\mathscr{D}$. Give this space its product topology. It is metrizable and separable. By assumption, the map $\phi: \theta \mapsto\left\{P_{\theta}(D) ; D \in \mathscr{D}\right\}$ from $\Theta$ to $\mathbb{R}^{\mathscr{D}}$ is $\mu$-measurable. Thus there exists a sequence $\left\{K_{n} ; n=1,2, \ldots\right\}$ of compact subsets of $\Theta$ such that (1) the $K_{n}$ are disjoint, (2) $\mu\left[\left(\cup K_{n}\right)^{c}\right]=0$, (3) restricted to any $K_{n}$ the map $\phi$ is continuous. Since $\phi$ is injective, the inverse map $\phi^{-1}$ is continuous if restricted to a $\phi\left(K_{n}\right)$. Thus $\phi$ is measurable if restricted to the measurable set $\bigcup_{n} \phi\left(K_{n}\right)$. The result follows.

Corollary. Let the conditions of Proposition 2 be satisfied. Then there is a choice $F_{x, n}$ of conditional distributions of $\theta$ given $x$ in ( $\mathscr{X}^{n}, \mathscr{A}^{n}$ ) with the following properties. There is a set $\Theta_{0} \subset \Theta$ such that $\mu\left(\Theta_{0}\right)=0$ and for each $\theta \notin \Theta_{0}$ a set $S_{\theta} \in \mathscr{A}^{\infty}$ such that $P_{\theta}^{\infty}\left(S_{\theta}\right)=1$ and such that if $\omega \in S_{\theta}$ then $F_{x, n}(V) \rightarrow 1$ for every neighborhood $V$ of the point $\theta$.

Proof. Return to the compacts $K_{n}$ of the proof of Proposition 2. Let $T$ be the locally compact topological direct sum of the $K_{n}$. Since each $K_{n}$ is a continuous
injective image of $\phi\left(K_{n}\right)$, it is metrizable. Thus $T$ is a separable locally compact metrizable space. Since $\mu$ is carried by $T$, one can disintegrate the measures $P_{\theta}^{n}(d x) \mu(d \theta)$ in the form $S^{\prime}(d x) F_{x, n}(d \theta)$ for probability measures $F_{x, n}$ carried by $T$. The open subsets of $T$ have a countable basis, say $\left\{G_{k} ; k=1,2, \ldots\right\}$. According to Proposition 1, for each $G_{k}$ and almost all $\theta$ the values $F_{x, n}\left(G_{k}\right)$ will tend almost surely $P_{\theta}^{\infty}$ to 1 or zero according to whether $\theta \in G_{k}$ or $\theta \notin G_{k}$.

Since each neighborhood $V$ of a $\theta \in T$ contains a $G_{k}$, the result follows.
(Note that according to this argument, there is very little difference between the conditions (1) and (2) of Proposition 2.) The assertion of Proposition 2 and its corollary may appear satisfactory since it will imply that Bayes estimates are usually consistent for almost all points $\theta \in \Theta$. However, there may be exceptional points, even in the topological support of the measure $\mu$. Examples to this effect were given by Lorraine Schwartz and, later, by D. Freedman. This last author has investigated more particularly prior measures on the entire space $\Theta$ of all probability measures on the integers. Any $\theta \in \Theta$ is a sequence $\theta=\left\{\theta_{k} ; k=1,2, \ldots\right\}$ such that $\theta_{k} \geq 0$ and $\sum \theta_{k}=1$. The space $\Theta$ can be metrized by the $L_{1}$-norm, or the Hellinger distance. The topology defined by these distances is the weak topology of pointwise convergence of the coordinates $\theta_{k}, k=1,2, \ldots$.

The set $\mathscr{P}$ of probability measures on $\Theta$ can be metrized by the corresponding dual Lipschitz norms. Both $\Theta$ and $\mathscr{P}$ are complete separable metric spaces. One of the results obtained by D. Freedman is as follows: Call a subset of $\Theta \times \mathscr{P}$ meager if it is the countable union of closed subsets without interior points. Call a set "residual" if its complement is meager.

Proposition 3. (Freedman). The set of pairs $(\theta, \mu) \in \Theta \times \mathscr{P}$ such that $\lim _{n} \sup \int F_{x, n}(U) \theta^{\infty}(d x)=1$ simultaneously for all open subsets of $\Theta$ is residual in $\Theta \times \mathscr{P}$.

A corollary of this is that there is a residual set of prior measures $\mu \in \mathscr{P}$ such that the set of $\theta \in \Theta$ for which the posterior measures converge to $\theta$ is a meager set.

The proof of Proposition 3 relies on the following observations. Let $\Theta_{0, k}$ be the set of $\theta \in \Theta$ such that $\theta_{k}=0$. Let $\Theta_{0}(m)=\bigcup_{k \leq m} \Theta_{0, k}$ and $\Theta_{0}=\bigcup_{k} \Theta_{0, k}$. Then both $\Theta_{0}$ and $\Theta \backslash \Theta_{0}$ are dense in $\Theta$. Let $W(p, \bar{m})$ be the set of probability measures $\mu \in \mathscr{P}$ of the form $\mu=\alpha \delta_{p}+(1-\alpha) \pi$ with $\alpha \in(0,1)$ and with $\pi$ concentrated on $\Theta_{0}$. Then for any fixed $p$, the set $W(p)=\bigcup_{m} W(p, m)$ is dense in $\mathscr{P}$.

Let $\mathscr{P}_{1}$ be the set of $\mu \in \mathscr{P}$ such that $\mu\left(\Theta_{0}\right)<1$. On the set $\mathscr{P}_{1}$ the maps $\mu \mapsto F_{x, n, \mu}$ giving the posterior distributions given the sample $x=\left\{\xi_{1}, \xi_{2}, \ldots\right\}$ are continuous. Thus, for every open set $U$ the maps $(\theta, \mu) \mapsto Q_{n}(U, \theta, \mu)= \int F_{x, n, \mu}(U) \theta^{\infty}(d x)$ are lower semicontinuous on $\Theta \times \mathscr{P}_{1}$. As a consequence, for fixed $U, n$ and $j>0$ the sets $R(U, n, j)=\left\{(\theta, \mu) \in \Theta \times \mathscr{P}_{1}: Q_{n}(U, \theta, \mu) \leq 1-j^{-1}\right\}$ are closed sets and so are the sets $R_{\nu}(U, j)=\bigcap_{n \geq \nu} R(U, n, j)$.

Now consider any open subset $V$ of $\Theta \times \mathscr{P}_{1}$. There is a $\theta \in \Theta \backslash \Theta_{0}$, a $p \in U$, an integer $m$, and a $\mu \in W(p, m)$ such that $(\theta, \mu) \in V$. However, for $\theta \in \Theta \backslash \Theta_{0}$ all the integers $k \leq m$ will eventually occur with positive frequencies in the sample $x=\left\{\xi_{1}, \xi_{2}, \ldots\right\}$. If so, the posterior distribution $F_{x, n, \mu}, \mu \in W(p, m)$, will be entirely concentrated on $p$. This implies $\lim _{n} Q_{n}(U, \theta, \mu)=1$. It follows that the closed sets $R_{v}(U, j)$ have no interior points. The statement of the proposition is then obtainable by taking a union for $j \geq 1$ and a union over a countable base $\left\{U_{i}\right\}$ of the set $\Theta$.

Freedman has also constructed prior measures $\mu \in \mathscr{P}$ which, at least for some $\theta$ in their support, tend to force the posterior measures towards some
specific sub-probability $q=\left\{q_{k} ; k=1,2, \ldots\right\}$ with $\sum q_{k}<1$. Since according to Proposition 3, "nearly all" prior measures will lead to inconsistencies, it is perhaps surprising that one can devise prior measures $\mu$ for which $F_{x, n, \mu} \rightarrow \theta$ almost surely $\theta^{\infty}$ for all $\theta$. Freedman has given such a set of measures $\mu$. They are called "tail-free" measures and have the property that the $F_{x, n, \mu}$ distribution of the vector $\left\{\theta_{k} ; k \leq m\right\}$ depends only on the observed frequencies of the integers $k \leq m$ in the sample $x=\left\{\xi_{1}, \xi_{2}, \ldots\right\}$. The same kind of construction has been used by J. Fabius, T. Ferguson, K. Doksum, and a number of other authors, leading to the use of so-called Dirichlet priors.

In view of Propositions 2 and 3, it would seem natural to seek simple conditions that would imply consistency of the Bayes estimates at particular given points $\theta \in \Theta$. Some sufficient conditions were given by Lorraine Schwartz. One can obtain some of them by using the inequalities of Chapter 16, Section 6. However, consistency is not a very inspiring property. Thus we shall skip to a more interesting phenomenon, known as the Bernstein-von Mises theorem because it was first described by Laplace.

Consider again experiments $\mathscr{E}^{n}=\left\{P_{\theta}^{n} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$, direct products of $n$ copies of an experiment $\mathscr{E}=\left\{p_{\theta}: \theta \in \Theta\right\}$. Assume that $\Theta$ is a subset of $\mathbb{R}^{k}$ and proceed under the following restrictions.
(B1) The set $\Theta$ is the intersection of an open set with a closed subset of $\mathbb{R}^{k}$.
The equality $p_{s}=p_{t}$ implies $s=t$.
(B2) The map $t \mapsto p_{t}$ is continuous for the Euclidean topology of $\Theta$ and the weak topology of $L(\mathscr{E})$.
(B3) The particular point $\theta_{0}$ under study is a D-regular point in the sense of Section 6, Definition 1.
(B4) There is a compact $K \subset \Theta$ and uniformly consistent tests of $\theta_{0}$ against $K^{c}$ 。
Let $\Lambda_{n}(s, t)=\log d P_{s}^{n} / d P_{t}^{n}$. It has been shown in Section 6 that (B1)-(B4) implies the existence of centerings $T_{n}$ such that

$$
\Lambda_{n}\left(t_{n}, \theta_{0}\right)+\frac{n}{2}\left\{\left(T_{n}-t_{n}\right)^{\prime} \Gamma\left(T_{n}-t_{n}\right)-\left(T_{n}-\theta_{0}\right)^{\prime} \Gamma\left(T_{n}-\theta_{0}\right)\right\}
$$

tends to zero in probability whenever $\sqrt{n}\left(t_{n}-\theta_{0}\right)$ remains bounded. The matrix $\Gamma$ is a nonrandom matrix obtainable from the covariance of the derivatives in quadratic mean at the regular point $\theta_{0}$. According to Chapter 11 one could replace $\Gamma$ by suitable estimates $\hat{\Gamma}_{n}$. This suggests that, if the prior distributions are sufficiently smooth, the posterior distributions of $\theta$ may look like Gaussian distributions centered at $T_{n}$. This is indeed the case, but some conditions on the prior measure $\mu$ are clearly needed. A possibility is as follows.
(B5) The prior measure $\mu$ is independent of $n$. It has a density $f$ with respect to the Lebesgue measure $\lambda$ on $\mathbb{R}^{k}$.
(B6) Let $B(\varepsilon)$ be the ball of center $\theta$ and radius $\varepsilon$. There is some number $a \in(0, \infty)$ such that

$$
\lim _{\varepsilon \rightarrow 0} \frac{1}{\lambda[B(\varepsilon)]} \int I_{B(\varepsilon)}|f(t)-a| \lambda(d t)=0
$$

(B7) The set $\Theta$ is sufficiently thick at $\theta_{0}$ in the sense that

$$
\liminf _{\varepsilon \rightarrow 0} \frac{\lambda[B(\varepsilon) \cap \Theta]}{\lambda[B(\varepsilon)]}>0
$$

Under these conditions, let $F_{x, n}$ be the posterior distribution of $\theta$ given the observations $x \in \mathscr{X}^{n}$. Let $H_{x, n}$ be the measure defined by

$$
H_{x, n}(B)=\int_{B \cap \Theta} \exp \left\{-\frac{n}{2}\left(t-T_{n}\right)^{\prime} \Gamma\left(t-T_{n}\right)\right\} \lambda(d t)
$$

and let $G_{x, n}=\left\|H_{x, n}\right\|^{-1} H_{x, n}$.
Theorem 1. Let the conditions (B1) to (B7) be satisfied. Then $\left\|F_{x, n}-G_{x, n}\right\|$ tends to zero in $P_{\theta_{0}}^{n}$ probability as $n \rightarrow \infty$. The result remains valid if the nonrandom matrix $\Gamma$ is replaced by an estimate $\hat{\Gamma}_{n}$ such that $\hat{\Gamma}_{n} \rightarrow \Gamma$ in $P_{\theta_{0}}^{n}$ probability.

Remark. Results of this nature are often stated with "almost sure convergence" instead of "convergence in probability." This can be done, but only with suitable choices of the pairs ( $T_{n}, \widehat{\Gamma}_{n}$ ).

Proof. The proof involves three separate steps. First one shows that for every neighborhood $V$ of $\theta_{0}$ the values $F_{x, n}(V)$ tend to unity. Then one shows that if $W(n, b)$ is the ball of center $\theta_{0}$ and radius $b / \sqrt{n}$, the values $F_{x, n}\left[W\left(n, b_{n}\right)\right]$ tend to unity whenever $b_{n} \rightarrow \infty$. This reduces the problem to consideration of prior measures carried by balls $W(n, b)$. To these one applies the arguments of Chapter 12. Proceeding as in Chapter 16, Section 6, for any Borel subset $B \subset \Theta$ let $M_{B, n}=\int_{B} P_{t}^{n} \mu(d t)$ and $P_{B, n}=\left\|M_{B, n}\right\|^{-1} M_{B, n}$. If $\omega_{n}$ is any test function defined on $\left(\mathscr{X}^{n}, \mathscr{A}^{n}\right)$, one can write

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \int F_{x, n}\left(V^{c}\right) Q_{n}(d x) \leq \int\left[1-\omega_{n}(x)\right] Q_{n}(d x)+\frac{1}{\mu[W(n, b)]} \int \omega_{n}(x) M_{V^{c}}(d x) \\
& \text { with } Q_{n}=P_{W(n, b)}
\end{aligned}
$$

According to Section 5, there exist tests $\omega_{n}$ such that both $\int\left[1-\omega_{n}(x)\right] Q_{n}(d x)$ and $\int \omega_{n}(x) M_{V^{c}}(d x)$ tend to zero exponentially fast. Under the conditions (B6)(B7), this implies the convergence of $\int F_{x, n}\left(V^{c}\right) Q_{n}(d x)$ to zero. However, since $\left\{P_{\theta_{0}}^{n}\right\}$ and $\left\{Q_{n}\right\}$ are contiguous, this implies that $\int F_{x, n}\left(V^{c}\right) P_{\theta_{0}}^{n}(d x)$ tends to zero. Thus the posterior distribution of $\theta$ converges, in the Prohorov sense, almost surely $P_{\theta_{0}}^{\infty}$ to the Dirac mass at $\theta_{0}$.

Now consider a particular neighborhood $V$ on which the conclusions of Lemma 3, Section 6 are satisfied. That is, using a transformation to real observations as in the proof of Proposition 1, Section 6, take $V$ so that the cumulative distributions satisfy the inequality $\left\|F_{t}-F_{\theta_{0}}\right\|_{K}>c\left|t-\theta_{0}\right|$ for a $c>0$ and all $t \in V$. Let $\left(b_{n}\right\}$ be any sequence such that $b_{n} \rightarrow \infty$ and let $C_{n}=V \cap\left[W\left(n, b_{n}\right)\right]^{c}$. Let $F_{n}$ be the empirical cumulative and let $\omega_{n}$ be the test function equal to unity if $\sqrt{n}\left\|F_{n}-F_{\theta_{0}}\right\|_{K} \leq(c / 2) b_{n}$ and to zero otherwise.

According to Kiefer and Wolfowitz, there is a constant $A$ such that

$$
P_{t}^{n}\left\{\sqrt{n}\left\|F_{n}-F_{t}\right\|_{K} \geq r\right\} \leq A \exp \left\{-2 r^{2}\right\}
$$
