for all $t$ and $n$. Thus $\int\left(1-\omega_{n}\right) d P_{\theta_{0}}^{n} \rightarrow 0$. Also, for $t \in C_{n}$ one has

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sqrt{n}\left\|F_{n}-F_{\theta_{0}}\right\|_{K} & \geq c \sqrt{n}|t-\theta|-\sqrt{n}\left\|F_{n}-F_{t}\right\|_{K} \\
& \geq \frac{c}{2} \sqrt{n}|t-\theta|>\frac{c}{2} b_{n}
\end{aligned}
$$

whenever $\sqrt{n}\left\|F_{n}-F_{t}\right\|_{K}<(c / 2) \sqrt{n}|t-\theta|$. Thus

$$
P_{t}^{n}\left\{\sqrt{n}\left\|F_{n}-F_{\theta_{0}}\right\|_{K} \leq \frac{c}{2} b_{n}\right\} \leq A \exp \left\{-\frac{n}{2} c^{2}\left|t-\theta^{2}\right|\right\}
$$

For $V$ small enough, condition (B6) implies that $M_{C_{n}}=\int_{C_{n}} P_{t}^{n} f(t) \lambda(d t)$ is bounded by a multiple of $N_{C_{n}}=\int_{C_{n}} P_{t}^{n} \lambda(d t)$. Thus $\int \omega_{n}(x) M_{C_{n}}(d x)$ is bounded by a multiple of $J_{n}=\int_{C_{n}} \exp \left\{-\left(n c^{2} / 2\right)\left|t-\theta_{0}\right|^{2}\right\} \lambda(d t)$. Changing variables one sees that

$$
n^{k / 2} J_{n} \leq \int_{|s| \geq b_{n}} \exp \left\{-\frac{c^{2}}{2}|s|^{2}\right\} \lambda(d s)
$$

tends to zero as $n \rightarrow \infty$. It follows then from (B7) that

$$
\{\mu[W(n, b)]\}^{-1} \int \omega_{n}(x) M_{C_{n}}(d x) \rightarrow 0
$$

As a consequence, $\int F_{x, n}\left(C_{n}\right) P_{\theta_{0}}^{n}(d x) \rightarrow 0$. This is true for every sequence $\left\{b_{n}\right\}$ such that $b_{n} \rightarrow \infty$. Thus given $\varepsilon>0$ there will be a $b=b(\varepsilon)$ and an $n(\varepsilon)$ such that $n \geq n(\varepsilon)$ implies

$$
\int F_{x, n}\left[W^{c}(n, b)\right] P_{\theta_{0}}^{n}(d x)<\varepsilon
$$

Note, by inspection, that a similar statement can be made about the measures $G_{x, n}$. Thus it will be sufficient to investigate the difference $F_{x, n}-G_{x, n}$ restricted to sets such as $W(n, b)$, for $b$ fixed. Equivalently, it is sufficient to investigate what happens to the posterior distribution of $\theta$ if the prior measures are entirely concentrated on the sets $W(n, b)$.

Consider then a fixed value of $b$ and arbitrary probability measures, say $v_{n}$, on the set $W(n, b)$. Let $S_{n}(d x, d t)=P_{\theta_{0}}^{n}(d x) v_{n}(d t)$. Let $\phi_{1, n}(x, t)$ be a jointly measurable version of the likelihood ratio $d P_{t}^{n} / d P_{\theta_{0}}^{n}$. Let

$$
S_{n, 1}(d x, d t)=\phi_{1, n}(x, t) S_{n}(d x, d t)
$$

and

$$
S_{n, 2}(d x, d t)=\phi_{2, n}(x, t) S_{n}(d x, d t)
$$

where $\phi_{2, n}(x, t)=\exp \left\{-(n / 2)\left(t-T_{n}\right)^{\prime} \Gamma\left(t-T_{n}\right)\right\}$. The measures $S_{n, i}$ admit disintegrations of the form $S_{n, i}(d x, d t)=S_{n, i}^{\prime}(d x) F_{x, n, i}(d t)$. The ratios

$$
\frac{\phi_{2, n}\left(x, t_{n}\right)}{\phi_{2, n}\left(x, s_{n}\right)} \frac{\phi_{1, n}\left(x, s_{n}\right)}{\phi_{1, n}\left(x, t_{n}\right)}
$$

tend to unity in $P_{\theta_{0}}^{n}$ probability for any pairs ( $s_{n}, t_{n}$ ) subject to the conditions $\left(s_{n}, t_{n}\right) \in W(n, b) \times W(n, b)$. Therefore, according to Lemma 3, Section 2, Chapter 12, the integrals

$$
\int\left\|F_{x, n, 1}-F_{x, n, 2}\right\| S_{1, n}^{\prime}(d x)
$$

tend to zero. This implies the desired result since $\left\{S_{1, n}^{\prime}\right\}$ and $\left\{P_{\theta_{0}}^{n}\right\}$ are contiguous sequences.

Remark. The reader will note that the third part of the argument carried out here applies to any locally asymptotically Gaussian or even locally asymptotically mixed Gaussian experiments. The relations between the prior measure $\mu$ and the Lebesgue measure $\lambda$ intervene mostly in the second part of the argument. If the point $\theta_{0}$ is interior to $\Theta$, Theorem 1 says that the posterior distributions of $\theta$ are asymptotically Gaussian. If $\theta$ is not interior to $\Theta$, the Gaussian approximations are restricted to $\Theta$ and renormalized.

## 8. Maximum Likelihood

As shown in Section 7, suitable "regularity" assumptions imply that posterior distributions will resemble Gaussian distributions. This was duly noted by Laplace who suggested using posterior medians as estimates, but also noted that in the Gaussian case medians, expectations, and modes are one and the same. It was noted by many that finding the mode of the posterior density for a very flat prior is the same thing as finding a point which maximizes the "likelihood function", that is, if $x$ has density $f(x, \theta)$, the function $\theta \mapsto f(x, \theta)$.

The terms "likelihood" and "maximum likelihood" seem to have been introduced by R. A. Fisher who seems also to be responsible for a great deal of propaganda on the merits of the maximum likelihood method.

In view of Fisher's vast influence, it is perhaps not surprising that the presumed superiority of the method is still for many an article of faith promoted with religious fervor. This state of affairs remains, in spite of a long accumulation of evidence to the effect that maximum likelihood estimates are often useless, or grossly misleading. Since almost every textbook contains something about the reputed merits of the method and since its defenders are legion, we shall not elaborate here on this aspect of the situation. On the contrary we shall just recollect a few facts and examples intended to make the reader view the claims with some caution.

First note that the cases in which maximum likelihood estimates (m. l. e.) have been shown to possess some optimality, or even asymptotic optimality properties are rather limited. Their common features are not readily apparent.

One particularly interesting case occurs when one considers an experiment $\mathscr{E}=\left\{P_{\theta}: \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ where the family $\left\{P_{\theta}\right\}$ is the family of all probability measures
on the line and $\theta$ is the cumulative distribution function attached to $P_{\theta}$. Taking independent identically distributed observations $\left\{\xi_{j} ; j=1, \ldots, n\right\}$, it can readily be seen that the m. l. e. $\hat{\theta}$ is the empirical cumulative $F_{n}$. This is asymptotically minimax for a variety of loss functions.

There are a few other cases of this nature. However, if one looks for proofs of consistency one finds that the only available general ones are variants of a result of Wald. For the i. i. d. case Wald's assumptions are approximately as follows. One assumes that $\mathscr{E}=\left\{P_{\theta}: \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ is dominated by some $\sigma$-finite measure $\mu$ and one selects densities $f(\cdot, \theta)=d P_{\theta} / d \mu$.

It is then assumed that:
(1) $\Theta$ is a dense subset of a compact space $\bar{\Theta}$.
(2) Let $\left\{\xi_{1}, \xi_{2}, \ldots, \xi_{n}, \ldots\right\}$ be distributed according to $P_{\theta_{0}}$.

Then for each neighborhood $U$ of $\theta_{0}$ and each $t \in \bar{\Theta}, t \neq \theta_{0}$, there is a $\theta_{1} \in U$, a neighborhood $V$ of $t$, and an integer $k$ such that if

$$
g\left[x_{1}, x_{2}, \ldots, x_{k} ; t, V\right]=\sup \left\{\sum_{j=1}^{k} \log \frac{f\left(x_{j}, \theta\right)}{f\left(x_{j}, \theta_{1}\right)} ; \theta \in V \cap \Theta\right\}
$$

then

$$
E_{\theta_{0}}^{*} g\left(\xi_{1}, \ldots, \xi_{k} ; t, V\right)<0
$$

(Here $E^{*}$ denotes an upper expectation.)
Under these conditions. let $\hat{\theta}_{n}$ be such that for some fixed $c>0$ one has $\prod_{j=1}^{n} f\left(\xi_{j}, \hat{\theta}_{n}\right) \geq c \sup _{\theta} \prod_{j=1}^{n} f\left(\xi_{j}, \theta\right)$. Then eventually $\hat{\theta}_{n} \in U$ almost surely $P_{\theta_{0}}$.

Unfortunately the conditions (1) and (2) are rather restrictive. They are not comparable to the conditions used in Section 7 to prove consistency of Bayes estimates, but are in many ways more stringent and, as we shall see, cannot imply any asymptotic optimality properties. They seem to be a high price to pay to obtain something as trivial as consistency. However, if they are satisfied for a Euclidean set $\Theta$ and if, locally, the logarithms of likelihood ratios are approximable by quadratics in a sense that is very much stronger than what we have used so far, one can indeed obtain asymptotic efficiency properties for the m. l. e . This is the origin of much of the superiority claims found in the literature. This being said, let us pass to some simple examples which show that m. l. e. is not that trustworthy.

Example 1 (Neyman and Scott). Let $X_{1}, X_{2}, \ldots, X_{n}, Y_{1}, Y_{2}, \ldots, Y_{n}$ be independent Gaussian variables with $\mathscr{L}\left(X_{j}\right)=\mathscr{L}\left(Y_{j}\right)=\mathscr{N}\left(\mu_{j}, \sigma^{2}\right)$. A fairly "reasonable" estimate of $\sigma^{2}$ is $s^{2}=(1 / 2 n) \sum_{j}\left(X_{j}-Y_{j}\right)^{2}$ but the m. l. e. of $\sigma^{2}$ is $s^{2} / 2$, a fairly untenable choice if $n$ is at all large. Note that here we are dealing with a rather simple exponential family. However the observations are not identically distributed.

Example 2 (Kiefer and Wolfowitz). Let $\xi_{1}, \xi_{2}, \ldots, \xi_{n}$ be identically distributed on $\mathbb{R}$ with a density

$$
f(x, \theta)=\frac{1-\alpha}{\sqrt{2} \pi} \exp \left\{-\frac{1}{2}(x-\mu)^{2}\right\}+\frac{\alpha}{\sigma \sqrt{2 \pi}} \exp \left\{-\frac{1}{2 \sigma^{2}}(x-\mu)^{2}\right\},
$$

where $\theta=(\mu, \theta), \mu \in \mathbb{R}, \sigma>0$ and where $\alpha=\exp \{-\exp [\exp (\exp 137)]\}$. With such a choice of $\alpha$ one can claim that $f(x, \theta)$ is for all practical purposes just $\mathscr{N}(\mu, 1)$. For $\mathscr{N}(\mu, 1)$ the m. l. e. of $\mu$ is the average $\bar{\xi}$ of the observations. For $f$ the supremum $\sup _{\theta} \prod_{j=1}^{n} f\left(\xi_{j}, \theta\right)$ is infinite. It would be attained by letting $\sigma=0$ and $\hat{\theta}=\xi_{j}$ for any one of the observations $\xi_{j}$.

One could make $\alpha$ depend on $n$, taking for instance $\alpha_{n}=\exp \{-\exp \times[\exp (\exp 137 n)]\}$, showing that maximum likelihood displays a tremendous lack of "robustness" even in the most restricted definitions given of the term.

It should be noted that the density $f$ defined above (for fixed $\alpha$ ) satisfies the DQM conditions of Section 3. All points $(\mu, \sigma), \mu \in \mathbb{R}, \sigma>0$ are $D$-regular in the sense of Section 6. There do exist asymptotically sufficient estimates with all the properties mentioned in Section 6. Also the Bernstein-von Mises theorems of Section 7 do apply.

Similar statements can be made for the shift family

$$
f(x, \theta)=\frac{C \exp \left\{-|x-\theta|^{2}\right\}}{|x-\theta|^{1 / 2}}
$$

where $x$ and $\theta$ belong to $\mathbb{R}^{k}, k \geq 3$, and $|x-\theta|$ is the ordinary Euclidean norm of $x-\theta$.

Example 3. Let $f(x, \theta)$ be the three-parameter $\log$ normal distribution where $X$ has the distribution of $\theta_{1}+\exp \left\{\theta_{2}+\theta_{3} \mathrm{Y}\right\}$ with $\mathscr{L}(Y)=\mathscr{N}(0,1)$. Here again $\sup _{\theta} \prod_{j=1}^{n} f\left(\xi_{j}, \theta\right)$ is always infinite even though the DQM conditions are satisfied and even though there exist perfectly well behaved estimates. The log normal was certainly not invented on purpose to make trouble for maximum likelihood. The same can be said of the gamma density

$$
\frac{\beta^{\alpha}}{\Gamma(\alpha)} \exp \{-\beta(x-\theta)\}(x-\theta)^{\alpha-1} I(x \geq \theta)
$$

In Examples 2 and 3 one could argue that maximum likelihood estimates do not actually exist and that, consequently, the examples do not detract from the pronouncements of the m. l. e. supporters. It is clear, however, that for any given $n$ one can smooth out the examples in question and get m.l. e. estimates that do exist but do not behave in any particularly reasonable way. However, keeping to the standard i. i. d. case, one can find examples where the m. l. e. definitely exists and behaves in a most unreasonable manner. The following example is imitated from one originally described by Bahadur.

Example 4. Let $\phi(x)=\exp \left\{x^{-2}\right\}$ for $x \in(0,1]$. Let $c$ be a constant, $c \in(0,1)$, say $c=1 / 2$. Define recursively a decreasing sequence $\left\{a_{k}\right\}$ with $a_{0}=1$ and $\int_{a_{k+1}}^{a_{k}}[\phi(x)-c] d x=1-c$. For each integer $k$, let $f(x, k)$ be a density defined on $(0,1]$ by $f(x, k)=c$ everywhere except in the interval $\left(a_{k}, a_{k-1}\right]$ where $f(x, k)$ is equal to $\phi(x)$.

We claim that for i.i.d. observations $\xi_{j}$ from the family $\{f(\cdot, k) ; k=1,2, \ldots\}$ the m.l.e. estimate $\hat{k}_{n}$ always exists, but that, no matter what $k$ induces the distributions, $\hat{k}_{n}$ tends to infinity almost surely as $n \rightarrow \infty$.

The same example can be made into one where the observations are integer valued instead of continuous. One simply replaces $\phi(x)$ on $\left(a_{k}, a_{k-1}\right]$ by its average $\left[a_{k-1}-a_{k}\right]^{-1} \int_{a_{k}}^{a_{k-1}} \phi(x) d x$ and record only in which intervals the observations $\xi_{j}$ fall. This does not change the behvior of $\hat{k}_{n}$.

To show that $\hat{k}_{n}$ tends to infinity, let $J_{k}$ be the indicator of $\left(a_{k}, a_{k-1}\right]$. Note that for any two integers $r$ and $s$ the logarithm of likelihood ratio $\Lambda_{n}(s, r)$ has the form

$$
\Lambda_{n}(s, r)=\sum_{j}\left\{J_{s}\left(\xi_{j}\right) \log \left[\frac{\phi\left(\xi_{j}\right)}{c}\right]-J_{r}\left(\xi_{j}\right) \log \frac{\phi\left(\xi_{j}\right)}{c}\right\}
$$

Assume that the $\xi_{j}$ are taken from $f(\cdot, k)$. Then for fixed $r$, the quantity $(1 / n) \sum J_{r}\left(\xi_{j}\right) \log \left(\phi\left(\xi_{j}\right) / c\right)$ converges almost surely to some finite limit, say $A_{r}$. Now consider the variable $Z_{n}=\min _{j \leq n} \xi_{j}$ and the value $\hat{s}$ such that $Z_{n} \in\left(a_{s}, a_{s-1}\right]$. Then, for any $\varepsilon$ and for $n$ large enough one will have

$$
\frac{1}{n} \Lambda_{n}(\hat{s}, r) \geq \frac{1}{n} \log \left[\frac{\phi\left(a_{\hat{s}-1}\right)}{c}\right]-A_{r}-\varepsilon
$$

However, $(1 / n) \log \phi\left(a_{\hat{s}-1}\right)$ is approximately equal to $\left(n Z_{n}^{2}\right)^{-1}$ which tends almost surely to infinity. Thus $\hat{k}_{n}$ will eventually escape any interval $[1,2, \ldots, r]$ and the assertion follows.

Arguing that the spacing between consecutive order statistics around $Z_{n}$ is of the order $1 / n$ while the spacing between the $a_{\hat{s}}$ and $a_{\hat{s}-1}$ is of order $\exp \left\{-\varepsilon n^{2}\right\}$, one can see that $\hat{k}_{n}$ will often be equal to $\hat{s}$. For those authors who argue that the m. l. e. may not be good as an "estimate", but that it is excellent as a "summary of information" one may ask whether $Z_{n}$ does indeed gather much "information" in the present case.

In this example, the parameter $k$ takes only integer values. One can, however, interpolate between successive values of $k$ and obtain families $\{f(x, \theta), \theta \in (+1,+\infty)\}$ which satisfy locally all the Cramér type conditions but are still such that $\hat{\theta}_{n}$ exists and tends almost surely to infinity.

The Bahadur family of Example 4 can also be used to point out some very strange properties of the m . l. e . Consider the discretized version where $\phi$ is replaced in each interval ( $a_{k}, a_{k-1}$ ] by its average on that interval. Let $X_{j}=k$ if $\xi_{j}$ falls in $\left(a_{k}, a_{k-1}\right]$. The $X_{j}$ are i. i. d. integer valued with some frequency function $p_{k}$. The family $\left\{p_{k} ; k=1,2, \ldots\right\}$ is a discrete subset $S$ of the set $\mathscr{P}$ of all the probability measures on the integers $\{1,2, \ldots\}$. The maximum likelihood estimate $\hat{p}_{n}$ computed on the entire $\mathscr{P}$ is the empirical frequency function. It is consistent. If, however, one takes into account that it is known that the $X_{j}$ arise from some $p \in S$, one gets a maximum likelihood estimate $\hat{p}_{n}$ which is gloriously inconsistent! Another peculiar feature is that if one replaces the $\xi_{j}$



by variables $Y_{j}=\xi_{j}+\zeta_{j}$ where the $\zeta_{j}$ are $\mathscr{N}\left(0, \sigma^{2}\right), \sigma>0$ then m. l. e. of $k$ computed from the densities of the $Y_{j}$ becomes consistent.

All of this points out to the conclusion that the maximum likelihood principle, as such, cannot be considered a general principle in general estimation problems.

Here is still another example, pointed out to this author by M. Akahira which shows that even when the m.l. ex exists, is unique and consistent, it may not be very good, even as information gatherer.

Let $f(x, \theta)=c \exp \left\{-\frac{1}{2}(x-\theta)^{2}\right\}$ for $|x-\theta| \leq 1$ and zero otherwise. Let $\xi_{1}, \xi_{2}, \ldots, \xi_{n}$ be a sample with $X_{n}=\min _{j \leq n} \xi_{j}$ and $Y_{n}=\max _{j \leq n} \xi_{j}$. For such a sample all values of $\theta$ are ruled out except those $\theta$ such that $Y_{n}-1 \leq \theta \leq X_{n}+1$. The logarithm of likelihood ratios (for those values) depend also on the average $\bar{\xi}_{n}$ of the observations. The $X_{n}, Y_{n}$, and $\bar{\xi}_{n}$ are asymptotically independent. For $n$ large the m.l.e. $\hat{\theta}_{n}$ behaves essentially as if it was equal to $\varepsilon\left(Y_{n}-1\right)+(1-\varepsilon)\left(X_{n}+1\right)$ where $\varepsilon$ is independent of $\left(X_{n}, Y_{n}\right)$ taking values zero and unity with probability one half. It "forgets" a considerable amount of the "information" contained in the pair ( $X_{n}, Y_{n}$ ). The mid-range $\frac{1}{2}\left(X_{n}+Y_{n}\right)= \frac{1}{2}\left(Y_{n}-1\right)+\frac{1}{2}\left(X_{n}+1\right)$ is very much more concentrated around $\theta$ than the m . l. e. (but it also forgets much information).

## 9. Some Cases where the Number of Observations Is Random

Let $\left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ be an experiment given by measures on a space ( $\mathscr{Y}, \mathscr{B}$ ). Suppose that an observational procedure consists of ascertaining the value of certain random variables $X_{1}, X_{2}, \ldots, X_{n}, \ldots$ in succession but that observation of the entire sequence is not feasible. Then the statistician may use an integer valued random variable $N$ and record only the values of the $X_{j}$ for $j \leq N$. Such an $N$ may be equal to some nonrandom value $n$. It may be that the sets $\{N=k\}$ are elements of the $\sigma$-field generated by the variables $X_{1}, X_{2}, \ldots, X_{k}$ in which case $N$ is called a stopping time of the sequence $\left\{X_{n}\right\}$. It may also happen that $N$ is determined separately from the $X_{n}$ 's and that it is not a stopping time of the sequence $\left\{X_{n}\right\}$. In this section we shall be interested in cases where, under each $P_{\theta}$, the sequence $\left\{X_{n}\right\} ; n=1,2, \ldots$ is a sequence of independent identically distributed variables. It will also be assumed that ( $\mathscr{Y}, \mathscr{B}$ ) carries a variable $X_{0}$ uniformly distributed in [ 0,1 ] independently of all the other $X_{n}$ and of the value of $\theta$. This is added to the system to provide the possibility of randomizing the values of $N$ independently of $\theta$. Let $\mathscr{A}_{n}$ be the $\sigma$-field generated by $X_{0}$, $X_{1} \ldots, X_{n}$.

Definition. A $\mathscr{B}$-measurable function $N$ from $\mathscr{Y}$ to the integers $\{0,1,2, \ldots\}$ will be called a random time. It will be called a stopping time if, for each $n$, the set $A_{n}=\{N=n\}$ belongs to $\mathscr{A}_{n}$. The $\sigma$-field $\mathscr{B}(N)$ attached to $N$ is the $\sigma$-field generated by the sets $A_{n}$ and the intersections $A_{n} \cap B, B \in \mathscr{A}_{n}$.

Random times that are not stopping times are sometimes called "raw", "pre-
scient" of "dishonest." The last two adjectives refer to the fact that either the statistican has prophetic gifts or that he did observe variables $X_{j}, j>N$ but chose not to report the fact. However, one can readily conceive of situations where the procedure by which the value of $N$ is determined is quite separate from that which allows determination of the $X_{j}$ and not at all prescient or dishonest.

In such a case, let $\mathscr{F}_{n}$ be the $\sigma$-field generated by the sets $A_{0}, A_{1}, \ldots, A_{n}$ and the variables $X_{0}, X_{1}, \ldots, X_{n}$. Then $N$ is a stopping time of the sequence of $\sigma$ fields $\left\{\mathscr{F}_{n}\right\}$. One has $\mathscr{F}_{0} \subset \mathscr{F}_{1} \subset \cdots \subset \mathscr{F}_{n} \subset \cdots$ and the martingale arguments of Chapter 10, Section 5 are still applicable. The same is true of the results of Chapter 13. The structure of the likelihood ratios is describable as follows. Let $Q_{\theta}$ be the restriction of $P_{\theta}$ to $\mathscr{B}(N)$. Let $p_{\theta, j}$ be the restriction of $P_{\theta}$ to the $\sigma$-field generated by the variable $X_{j}$ alone. For two elements $\theta$ and $s$ of $\Theta$ let $f_{j}(s, \theta)$ be the likelihood ratio of $p_{s, j}$ to $p_{\theta, j}$. Then, on the set $A_{n}$, the density $d Q_{s} / d Q_{\theta}$ is $\prod_{j \leq n} f_{j}(s, \theta)$. Thus one may write $d Q_{s} / d Q_{\theta}=\prod_{j \leq N} f_{j}(s, \theta)$.

For asymptotic purposes, let the system $(\mathscr{Y}, \mathscr{B}),\left\{P_{\theta} ; \theta \in \bar{\Theta}\right\}$ be fixed but consider a sequence $\left\{N_{n}\right\}$ of stopping times such that the medians $\operatorname{med}_{\theta} N_{n}$ tend to infinity for all $\theta \in \Theta$. Let $Q_{\theta, n}$ be the restriction of $P_{\theta}$ to $\mathscr{B}\left(N_{n}\right)$. There are very few global results relative to the asymptotic behavior of the experiment $\left\{Q_{\theta, n} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$. However here is one of them.

Proposition 1. Assume that the $N_{n}$ are stopping times. Assume also that (i) there are nonrandom integers $v_{n}$ such that for every $\varepsilon>0$ there is a $b(\varepsilon)$ such that $\sup _{\theta} P\left\{\left|N_{n}-v_{n}\right| \geq \varepsilon v_{n}\right\} \leq \varepsilon$ for all $n \geq b(\varepsilon)$;
(ii) the set $\Theta$ metrized by the Hellinger distance $h\left(p_{\theta, j}, p_{s, j}\right)$ has finite metric dimension.

Then the distance between the experiments $\left\{Q_{\theta, n} ; \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ defined by $N_{n}$ and the experiments defined by $v_{n}$ tends to zero.

Proof. This is an immediate consequence of Chapter 16, Section 5, Proposition 1 and Chapter 5, Section 5.

Other results, of a local character, can be obtained from the behavior of random sums of random variables. Some of these results, due in part at least to Renyi, make use of the concept of "stable" and "mixing" convergence. To define these, let $V$ be a random variable defined on ( $\mathscr{Y}, \mathscr{B}$ ). It determines a Markov kernel $K$ from ( $\mathscr{Y}, \mathscr{B}$ ) to the line by the equality $K(y, S)=I\{V(y) \in S\}$. If $P$ is any probability measure on $(\mathscr{Y}, \mathscr{B})$ such a kernel defines a transition from $L_{1}(P)$ to the dual $C_{0}^{*}$ of the space $C_{0}(\mathbb{R})$ of bounded continuous functions which tend to zero at infinity. One can topologize the space of transitions by the topology of pointwise convergence on $C_{0}(\mathbb{R}) \times L_{1}(P)$.

Definition. Let $\left\{V_{n}\right\}$ be a sequence of random variables defined on ( $\mathscr{Y}, \mathscr{B}, P$ ). Let $K_{n}$ be the Markov kernel attached to $V_{n}$. Assume that the $V_{n}$ remain bounded in probability. Then the sequence $\left\{V_{n}\right\}$ is said to converge stably if the $K_{n}$ converge. The convergence is mixing if the limit $K$ of the $K_{n}$ is "independent of $\mathscr{B}$," that is, if for each $u \in C_{0}(\mathbb{R})$ the image $u K$ of $u$ is constant on $\mathscr{Y}$.

From any sequence $\left\{K_{n}\right\}$ of Markov kernels one can extract subsequences which converge pointwise on $C_{0}(\mathbb{R}) \times L_{1}(P)$. The limit is a transition from $L_{1}(P)$ to $C_{0}^{*}(\mathbb{R})$. In the present case, the assumed relative compactness of $\left\{\mathscr{L}\left(V_{n}\right)\right\}$
implies that the limit transitions are representable by standard Markov kernels from $(\mathscr{Y}, \mathscr{B})$ to $\mathbb{R}$. The convergence of the $K_{n}$ is equivalent to the convergence of $E u\left(V_{n}\right) f$ for every $u \in C_{0}(\mathbb{R})$ and $f \in L_{1}(P)$. It implies the convergence of $E u\left(V_{n}\right) f$ for $f \in L_{1}(P)$ and for bounded continuous $u$. To check that convergence is stable or mixing, the following simple observation is important.

Lemma 1 Let $\left\{\mathscr{L}\left(V_{n}\right)\right\}$ be relatively compact. Assume that for each fixed $k$, each $A \in \mathscr{A}_{k}$, and each $u \in C_{0}(\mathbb{R})$ the expectations $E u\left(V_{n}\right) I_{A}$ converge. Then $\left\{V_{n}\right\}$ converges stably. The convergence is mixing if under the same conditions $E u\left(V_{n}\right) I_{A}$ converges to a product $E u(V) E I_{A}$ and the $V_{n}$ are measurable with respect to the $\sigma$-field $\mathscr{A}_{\infty}$ generated by all the $\mathscr{A}_{k}$.

Proof. If $\mathrm{f} \in L_{1}(P)$ is $\mathscr{A}_{\infty}$ measurable, then the martingale convergence theorems imply that $E\left|f-E\left(f \mid \mathscr{A}_{k}\right)\right|$ tends to zero as $k \rightarrow \infty$. Otherwise $E\left(f \mid \mathscr{A}_{k}\right) \rightarrow E\left(f \mid \mathscr{A}_{\infty}\right)$ and the result follows by looking first at $E\left\{u\left(V_{n}\right) f \mid \mathscr{A}_{\infty}\right\}$.

Lemma 2 (Renyi). Let $X_{n, j} ; j=1,2, \ldots, k_{n}$ be random variables defined on ( $\mathscr{Y}, \mathscr{B}, P$ ). Assume that
(i) for each fixed $k$, the $X_{n, j} ; j \leq k$ are $\mathscr{A}_{k}$-measurable and the $X_{n, j} ; j>k$ are independent of $\mathscr{A}_{k}$;
(ii) for each $\varepsilon>0, \sup _{j} P\left\{\left|X_{n, j}\right|>\varepsilon\right\} \rightarrow 0$;
(iii) if $S_{n}=\sum_{j} X_{n, j}$ then the distribution of $S_{n}$ tends to a limit on the line.

Then the convergence of $S_{n}$ is stable and mixing.
Proof. Write $S_{n}=S_{n, k}+S_{n, k}^{\prime}$ with $S_{n, k}=\sum_{j \leq k} X_{n, j}$. Then $S_{n, k} \rightarrow 0$ and $E\left|u\left(S_{n, k}+S_{n, k}^{\prime}\right)-u\left(S_{n, k}^{\prime}\right)\right| \rightarrow 0$ for every $u \in C_{0}(\mathbb{R})$. However, if $A \in \mathscr{A}_{k}$ then $E u\left(S_{n, k}^{\prime}\right) I_{A}=E u\left(S_{n, k}^{\prime}\right) E I_{A}$. Hence the result.

Lemma 3. Let $X_{n, j} j=1,2 \ldots$ be variables satisfying condition (i) of Lemma 2. Let $\left\{N_{n}\right\}$ be a sequence of random times such that there are constants $b_{n}, b_{n} \rightarrow \infty$ for which the ratios $N_{n} b_{n}^{-1}$ tend in probability to a limit. Let $\xi$ be a random variable independent of $\mathscr{B}$ such that $\mathscr{L}(\xi)=\lim _{n} \mathscr{L}\left(N_{n} b_{n}^{-1}\right)$.

Assume, in addition, that $\mathscr{L}\left\{\sum_{j} X_{n, j} ; j \leq b_{n}\right\}$ is a relatively compact sequence and that for each $n$ the $X_{n, j} ; j=1,2, \ldots$, are identically distributed. Let $S_{n}=\sum\left\{X_{n, j} ; j \leq N_{n}\right\}$ and $S_{n}^{\prime}=\sum\left\{X_{n, j} ; j \leq b_{n} \xi\right\}$. Then $\left\{\mathscr{L}\left(S_{n}\right)\right\}$ is relatively compact and $\mathscr{L}\left(S_{n}\right)-\mathscr{L}\left(S_{n}^{\prime}\right) \rightarrow 0$.

Proof. Note first that boundedness in probability of $\sum\left\{X_{n, j} ; j \leq b_{n}\right\}$ implies that of $\sum\left\{X_{n, j} ; j \leq b_{n} c\right\}$ for any fixed $c$, hence that of $S_{n}^{\prime}$. Also, it is clearly sufficient to prove the result for the case where $N_{n} b_{n}^{-1}$ is bounded by some $c<\infty$ and where in consequence $\tau=\lim N_{n} b_{n}^{-1}$ is bounded by $c$.

Now take an $\varepsilon>0$ and let $\tau(\varepsilon)$ be $\tau(\varepsilon)=\varepsilon$ [integer part of $(\tau / \varepsilon)$ ]. Similarly, let $\xi(\varepsilon)=\varepsilon$ [integer part of $\xi / \varepsilon$ ]. Then, by Lemma 2, the distributions $\mathscr{L}\left\{\sum X_{n, j} ; j \leq b_{n} \tau(\varepsilon)\right\}$ and $\mathscr{L}\left\{\sum X_{n, j} ; j \leq b_{n} \xi(\varepsilon)\right\}$ have the same asymptotic behavior.

Now fix an integer $m$ and let $B(m)=\{\tau(\varepsilon)=\varepsilon m\}$ and $B^{\prime}(m)=\{\xi(\varepsilon)= \varepsilon m\}$. Let $W(n, m, k)=\sum\left\{X_{n, j} ; b_{n} \varepsilon m<j \leq b_{n} \varepsilon m+k\right\}$ and for an $\alpha>0$, let

$$
V_{m}(n, \alpha)=\sup _{k}\left\{|W(n, m, k)| ; k \leq b_{n} \alpha\right\}
$$

By Lemma 2 again, the limiting behavior of $I_{B(m)} V_{m}(n, \alpha)$ is the same as that of $I_{B^{\prime}(m)} V_{m}(n, \alpha)$. However, the latter expression has the same behavior as $I_{B^{\prime}(m)} V_{0}(n, \alpha)$. Now

$$
V_{0}(n, \alpha)=\sup _{k \leq b_{n} \alpha}\left\{\left|\sum X_{n, j} ; 0<j \leq k\right|\right\} .
$$

Therefore $\lim _{\alpha \rightarrow 0} \lim _{n} V_{0}(n, \alpha)=0$.
One concludes from this that given any $\delta>0$ there is an $\varepsilon>0$ such that

$$
\lim _{n} P\left\{\left|S_{n}-\sum\left[X_{n, j} ; j \leq \tau(\varepsilon)\right]\right|>\delta\right\}<\delta
$$

This implies the desired result.
In the foregoing lemma, the convergence in probability of $\tau_{n}=N_{n} b_{n}^{-1}$ cannot be replaced by convergence in distribution, even if $N_{n}$ is a stopping time of the sequence $X_{n, j} ; j=1,2, \ldots$. The stopping times suggested by practical considerations are often such that $N_{n} b_{n}^{-1}$ converges in distribution for a suitable choice of $b_{n}$. Convergence in probability is rare, but it does occur. Some of the stopping times discussed at the end of Chapter 13 in connection with achievement of a desired precision satisfy such convergence relations.

The foregoing lemmas, the results of Chapter 10, Section 5, and the methods of Chapter 13 can all be used to study the behavior of experiments obtained from independent identically distributed observations by suitable stopping times.

Consider, for instance, two possible values $\theta_{n}^{\prime}$ and $\theta_{n}^{\prime \prime}$ of $\theta$. Let $\left\{N_{n}\right\}$ be a sequence of stopping times. Let $p_{\theta, j}$ be the restriction of $P_{\theta}$ to the $\sigma$-field generated by the $j$ th observation $X_{j}$ alone and let $\phi_{n, j}$ be the Radon-Nikodym density of $p_{\theta_{n}^{\prime \prime}, j}$ relative to $p_{\theta_{n}^{\prime}, j}$. The $\sigma$-field $\mathscr{A}_{j}$ generated by $X_{0}, X_{1}, \ldots, X_{j}$ form an increasing sequence to which the methods of Chapter 10, Section 5 are applicable. The conditional densities $f_{n, j}$ introduced there have the form $f_{n, j}=I\left(N_{n}<j\right)+\phi_{n, j} I\left(N_{n} \geq 1\right)$, so that $f_{n, j}-1=\left(\phi_{n, j}-1\right) I\left(N_{n} \geq j\right)$. Section 5 of Chapter 10 relies on four conditions called (A), (B), (C), and (D) as follows, all convergences or boundedness conditions being in probability for $P_{\theta_{n}^{\prime}}$.
(A) $\sup _{j}\left|f_{n, j}-1\right| \rightarrow 0$,
(B) $\sum_{j}\left(f_{n, j}-1\right)^{2}$ remains bounded in probability,
(C) $\sum_{k} E_{k-1}\left(1-f_{n, k}\right) \rightarrow 0$,
(D) $\sum_{k} E_{k-1}\left(f_{n, k}-1\right) I\left(f_{n, k} \geq 2\right) \rightarrow 0$.

This suggests the following observation.
Lemma 4. Assume that there is a sequence $\left\{\delta_{n}\right\}, \delta_{n}>0, \delta_{n} \rightarrow 0$ of constants such that $\mathscr{L}\left\{\delta_{n} N_{n} \mid P_{\theta_{n}^{\prime}}\right\}$ is a relatively compact sequence. Let $v_{n}$ be the integer part of $\delta_{n}^{-1}$. If the conditions (A)-(D) are satisfied for the stopping times $v_{n}$, they are also satisfied for the stopping times $N_{n}$.

Proof. Given $\varepsilon>0$ there is a $b$ such that $N_{n} \leq b v_{n}$ except for cases having probability $\phi$. The validity of the conditions for $v_{n}$ implies their validity for $b v_{n}$. Hence the result.

It should be noted that here, just as in Chapter 10, the validity of (A)-(D) for the measures $P_{\theta_{n}^{\prime}}$ does not imply their validity for $P_{\theta_{n}^{\prime \prime}}$. The conditions in equation do not imply contiguity of the measure $Q_{\theta_{n}^{\prime}, n}$ and $Q_{\theta_{n}^{\prime \prime}, n}$ obtained by restricting the $P_{\theta_{n}^{\prime}}$ and $P_{\theta_{n}^{\prime \prime}}$ to the $\sigma$-fields $\mathscr{B}\left(N_{n}\right)$. An example can be constructed easily as follows.

Let $X_{j}, j=1,2, \ldots$ be independent $\mathscr{N}(\theta, 1)$ for $\theta \in[0, \infty)$. Take a large number $a>0$ and let $N_{n}$ be the first $k$ such that $\sum_{j \leq k} X_{j} \leq-a \sqrt{n}$. Then $N_{n} / n$ has a limiting distribution on ( $0, \infty$ ) if $\theta=0$. However, if $\theta>0$, the time $N_{n}$ is infinite with a probability that can be made as close to one as one wishes by selecting $a$ sufficiently large.

Note also that in order to be able to apply the results of Lemma 3 above, or Theorem 2, Section 5, Chapter 10, the measures $P_{\theta_{n}^{\prime}}$ must be kept equal to some fixed $P_{\theta}$. Of course, even for variables $\theta_{n}^{\prime}$ and $\theta_{n}^{\prime \prime}$, one can obtain contiguity if one assumes that (A) and (B) hold also in $P_{\theta_{n}^{\prime \prime}}$ probability.

The results of Chapter 13 can also be applied in the present situation and this in several different manners. To give an example, let $\theta$ be an interior point of a set $\Theta \subset \mathbb{R}^{p}$. Assume that ithe condition DQM holds at $\theta$ and that the covariance matrix of the derivative in quadratic mean at $\theta$ is nonsingular. The notation of Chapter 13 uses a time $t \in(0, \infty)$ and $\sigma$-fields $\mathscr{A}_{n}(t)$. These can be introduced here as follows: Take a sequence $\left\{\delta_{n}\right\}$ of numbers $\delta_{n}>0$ such that $\delta_{n} \rightarrow 0$. Let $\mathscr{A}_{n}(t)$ be the $\sigma$-field generated by the independent observations $X_{j}$ for $j \leq \delta_{n}^{-2} t$. Each of the measures $P_{\theta}$ can be restricted to $\mathscr{A}_{n}(t)$, yielding a measure $P_{\theta, n, t}$. The density of $P_{\theta+\delta_{n} \xi, n, t}$ with respect to $P_{\theta, n, t}$ corresponds to the function called $f_{n}(\xi, t)$ in Chapter 3. As in Section 3, let $W_{j}(\theta)$ be the derivative in quadratic mean for the measures that correspond to the $j$ th observation. Let $\frac{1}{4} \Gamma(\theta)$ be its covariance matrix. Then, according to Theorem 2, Section 3, for each fixed time $t$ and for $S_{n}(t)=2 \sum\left\{W_{j}(\theta) ; j \leq \delta_{n}^{-2} t\right\}$ the differences $f_{n}(\xi, t)-\exp \left\{\xi^{\prime} S_{n}(t)-(t / 2) \xi^{\prime} \Gamma(\theta) \xi\right\}$ tend to zero in probability (for $P_{\theta}$ and for $P_{\theta+\delta_{n} \xi}$ ). It is also easily checked here that Assumption 4 of Chapter 13 is satisfied. That is,

$$
\sup _{s \leq t}\left|f_{n}(\xi, s)-\exp \left\{\xi^{\prime} S_{n}(s)-\frac{s}{2} \xi^{\prime} \Gamma(\theta) \xi\right\}\right|
$$

tends to zero in probability. To obtain this it is sufficient to use Kolmogorov's inequalities wherever Chebyshev's inequality is used in Section 3. The stopping times $N_{n}$ used here get converted to stopping times $\tau_{n}=\delta_{n}^{2} N_{n}$ of the system $\left\{\mathscr{A}_{n}(t) ; t \in 0, \infty\right\}$.

All the results of Chapter 13 become applicable here to any stopping times $\tau_{n}$ satisfying the restriction that if $\left|\xi_{n}\right|$ remains bounded, then $\tau_{n}$ remains bounded in $P_{\theta+\delta_{n}} \xi_{n}$ probability.

Note, however, that this result is entirely local. It refers to shrinking neighborhoods of a particular point $\theta \in \Theta$. This may be sufficient for certain testing
problems, but it is not particularly helpful in estimation problems. For these one may seek other approximations in terms of asymptotically sufficient estimates.

Section 6 gives a list of conditions under which it is possible to obtain estimates $T_{m}$ with the property that (i) the $T_{m}$ are $\mathscr{A}_{m}$-measurable and (ii) if $\left|\xi_{m}\right|$ remains bounded then

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Lambda_{m}\left(\theta+\frac{\xi_{m}}{\sqrt{m}}, \theta\right)- & \frac{m}{2}\left\{\left(T_{m}-\theta-\frac{\xi_{m}}{\sqrt{m}}\right)^{\prime} \Gamma(\theta)\left(T_{m}-\theta-\frac{\xi_{m}}{\sqrt{m}}\right)\right. \\
& \left.-\left(T_{m}-\theta\right)^{\prime} \Gamma(\theta)\left(T_{m}-\theta\right)\right\}
\end{aligned}
$$

tends to zero in probability.
In the present notation, one can take for each time $t$ the integer part $m$ of $\delta_{n}^{-2} t$ and let $T_{n}(t)$ be equal to the $T_{m}$ of the above approximation. This will give an approximation such that, for each fixed $t$,

$$
f_{n}(\xi, t)-\exp \left\{\xi_{n}^{\prime} S_{n}^{*}(t)-\frac{t}{2} \xi_{n}^{\prime} \Gamma(\theta) \xi_{n}\right\}
$$

tends to zero if $S_{n}^{*}(t)=\left(\delta_{n}^{-1} t\right) \xi_{n}^{\prime} \Gamma(\theta)\left(T_{n}(t)-\theta\right)$ and if $\left|\xi_{n}\right|$ remains bounded. In such a situation, Assumption 4 of Chapter 13 may or may not be satisfied, depending on the manner of construction of the estimates $T_{m}$. However, the assumptions (1), (2), (3), and (5) of Chapter 13 are satisfied and the results of that Chapter become applicable to all stopping times that are "regular" in the sense of Chapter 13, Section 2, Definition 1.

Note that these results imply in particular that whatever may be achieved by regular stopping times can also be achieved asymptotically using stopping times of the sequence $\left\{T_{m} ; m=1,2, \ldots\right\}$. However, there are also other possibilities. For instance, the construction of $T_{m}$ itself relies on estimating the covariance matrices $\Gamma(\theta)$ of the Gaussian approximation formula. Let $K_{m}$ be such an estimate, available on the $\sigma$-field $\mathscr{A}_{m}$. One may select as stopping time the first $m$ such that $\operatorname{det}\left(m K_{m}\right) \geq a_{n}$. This gives a stopping time $N_{n}$ such that $N_{n} \rightarrow \infty$ if $a_{n} \rightarrow \infty$. The role of such a stopping time in providing a "preassigned precision" is detailed in Chapter 13, Section 5.

Under certain conditions one can insure the validity of Assumption 4 of Chapter 13. To do that, the preliminary estimates and the construction sets of Chapter 11 need to be chosen in some appropriate manner. One technical device that works very well is as follows. Suppose that a certain, large number of observations have already been accumulated. Construct a preliminary estimate $\hat{\theta}$ and select construction sets $S_{\omega}$ to carry out the procedure of Chapter 11. Estimate how many more observations will be taken before the process terminates. If that number is $k$, keep using the same estimate $\hat{\theta}$ and the same sets $S_{\omega}$ until the process stops or until $10^{-1} k$ observations have been taken, whichever comes first.

For independent identically distributed observations whose individual distributions satisfy the DQM conditions on an open set $\Theta \subset \mathbb{R}^{k}$, further results are
available. To state them take a sequence $\left\{\delta_{n}\right\}$, of numbers $\delta_{n}>0$ such that $\delta_{n} \rightarrow 0$. Let $\frac{1}{2} W_{j}(\theta)$ be the evaluation of the derivative in quadratic mean at $\theta$ for the $j$ th observation.

Let $\left\{N_{n}\right\}$ be a sequence of stopping times such that, for each $\theta \in \Theta$
(i) $\mathscr{L}\left\{\sum_{j \leq N_{n}} W_{j}(\theta), \delta_{n} N_{n} \mid P_{\theta}\right\}$ tends to a limit as $n \rightarrow \infty$;
(ii) if $\xi_{n}$ stays bounded then $\delta_{n} N_{n}=\tau_{n}$ remains bounded in $P_{\theta+\delta_{n} \xi_{n}}$ probability.

Then, according to Chapter 13, the experiments $\mathscr{F}_{n, \theta}=\left\{Q_{\theta+\delta_{n}} \xi|\xi| \leq b\right\}$, where $Q_{t}$ is the restriction of $P_{t}$ to the $\sigma$-field of $N_{n}$, converge to a limit $\mathscr{F}_{\theta}= \left\{F_{\theta, \xi} ;|\xi| \leq b\right\}$ such that $d F_{\theta, \xi}=\exp \left\{\xi^{\prime} S-(\tau / 2) \xi^{\prime} \Gamma \xi\right\} d F_{\theta, 0}$, where $F_{\theta, 0}= \mathscr{L}(S, \tau)=\lim _{n} \mathscr{L}\left\{\sum_{j \leq N_{n}} W_{j}(\theta), \delta_{n} N_{n} \mid \theta\right\}$ and where $\Gamma=E\left\{W_{j}(\theta) W_{j}^{\prime}(\theta) \mid \theta\right\}$.

Here $S$ and $\tau$ could be jointly distributed in an arbitrary way, subject to the contiguity restriction that $E_{\theta, 0} \exp \left\{\xi^{\prime} S-(\tau / 2) \xi^{\prime} \Gamma \xi\right\}=1$ for all $\xi \in \mathbb{R}^{k}$. However, according to the invariance theorems of Chapter 8, Section 4, for almost all $\theta \in \Theta$ the limiting experiment $\mathscr{F}_{\theta}$ will be shift-invariant and the limiting distribution $\mathscr{L}(S, \tau \mid \xi)$ shift-covariant.

It is easy to check that the shift-invariance of the experiments and the shiftcovariance of the distributions $\mathscr{L}(S, \tau \mid \xi)$ are the same thing. They are also equivalent to the fact that $\mathscr{F}_{\boldsymbol{\theta}}$ is "mixed normal", that is that, given $\tau$, the conditional distribution of $S$ under $F_{\theta, 0}$ is Gaussian with expectation zero and covariance matrix $\tau \Gamma$.

It follows that $\xi^{\prime} S-(\tau / 2) \xi^{\prime} \Gamma \xi$ could also be written in the form

$$
-\frac{1}{2}\left\{(V-\xi)^{\prime} \tau \Gamma(V-\xi)-V^{\prime} \tau \Gamma V\right\}
$$

for a suitable random vector $V$. It follows also from this that the experiments $\mathscr{F}_{n, \theta}=\left\{Q_{\theta+\delta_{n}} \xi|\xi| \leq b\right\}$ are $\tau_{n} \Gamma$-controlled in the sense of Chapter 11, Section 4, Definition 3. Here, of course, the limiting distribution $F_{\theta, 0}=\mathscr{L}[S, \tau]$ depends on $\theta$ and so does the matrix $\Gamma=\Gamma(\theta)=E\left\{W_{j}(\theta) W_{j}^{\prime}(\theta) \mid \theta\right\}$.

In the present circumstances one may inquire about the possibility of selecting stopping times that would, for a reasonable definition of "cost of the observations" yield the best limiting experiments.

One remark is that, if the assumptions used here and the assumptions of Section 6 are satisfied, one may as well use only stopping times of the sequence of asymptotically sufficient estimates $\left\{T_{n}\right\}$ of Section 6. Another remark is that, for the local limits $\mathscr{F}_{\theta}$, such mixed normal experiments are not often comparable.

To see this, consider the case where $\Theta$ is one-dimensional and where $\Gamma=\Gamma(\theta)$ is the identity matrix. Then the experiments $\mathscr{F}_{\theta}$ can be written in the form $\mathscr{F}=\left\{H_{\xi} ; \xi \in \mathbb{R}\right\}$.

$$
d H_{\xi}=\exp \left\{\xi x \sqrt{\tau}-\frac{1}{2} \tau \xi^{2}\right\} G(d x) M(d \tau)
$$

for $G=\mathscr{N}(0,1)$ and for a certain probability measure $M$ on $[0, \infty)$.
The Hellinger transform of $\mathscr{F}$ is easy to compute. For masses $\alpha_{j}, \alpha_{j}>0$, $\sum \alpha_{j}=1$ carried by points $\xi_{j}$, it takes the form

$$
\phi(z)=\int \exp \{-\tau z\} M(d \tau)
$$

where $z=\frac{1}{2}\left\{\sum \alpha_{j} \xi_{j}^{2}-\left(\sum \alpha_{j} \xi_{j}\right)^{2}\right\}$.
According to this, if $\sigma$ is a limit in distribution of stopping times $\delta_{n} N_{n}^{\prime}$ such that the limit experiment of $\sigma$ is better than that of $\tau$, one must have $E e^{-\sigma z} \leq E e^{-\tau z}$ for all $z \geq 0$.

A more careful argument gives additional information. Note that for a mixed normal experiment such as the one given by the $H_{\xi}$, the variable $u=x / \sqrt{\tau}$ is conditionally Gaussian with expectation $\xi$ and variance $\tau^{-1}$. Now consider an experiment $\mathscr{E}_{1}$ corresponding to a variable $\sigma$ and an experiment $\mathscr{E}_{2}$ corresponding to $\tau$. Suppose that $\delta\left(\mathscr{E}_{1}, \mathscr{E}_{2}\right)=0$. Then, an application of the
invariance results of Chapter 8 shows that one can find variables ( $\sigma, V, T$ ) with joint distributions such that $\mathscr{L}(\sigma)$ is the distribution of the stopping time in $\mathscr{E}_{1}, \mathscr{L}(T)=\mathscr{L}(\tau)$, and

$$
E \exp \left\{i s V+i t T-\frac{1}{2} \frac{s^{2}}{\sigma}\right\}=E \exp \left\{-\frac{1}{2} \frac{s^{2}}{T}+i t T\right\}
$$

identically in $s$ and $t$.
Except for sets of measure zero, this implies

$$
E\left\{\left.\exp \left\{i s V-\frac{1}{2} \frac{s^{2}}{\sigma}\right\} \right\rvert\, T\right\}=\exp \left\{-\frac{1}{2} \frac{s^{2}}{T}\right\}
$$

In particular, $E(V \mid T)=0$ and $E\left\{(1 / \sigma)+V^{2} \mid T\right\}=(1 / T)$. It follows that if $E \sigma^{-1}=E\left(\tau^{-1}\right)$ then $V$ is degenerate at zero and $\mathscr{L}(\sigma)=\mathscr{L}(\tau)$. Using the relation $E((1 / \sigma) \mid T) E(\sigma \mid T) \geq 1$, one sees also that

$$
E(\sigma \mid T) \geq \frac{T}{1-T E\left(V^{2} \mid T\right)} \geq T\left\{1+T E\left(V^{2} \mid T\right)\right\}
$$

Thus, if the experiment $\mathscr{E}_{1}$ corresponding to $\sigma$ is better than $\mathscr{E}_{2}$ corresponding to $\tau$, we must have $E \sigma \geq E \tau[1+\tau \beta(\tau)]$ where $\beta(\tau) \geq 0$ and where $E \beta(\tau)=0$ only if $\mathscr{E}_{1}$ and $\mathscr{E}_{2}$ are actually equivalent.

It is usual to evaluate the cost of $N_{n}$ observations proportionally to the expectation $E N_{n}$. An asymptotic evaluation through $E \sigma$ and $E \tau$ makes more sense in that it disregards some very large values of $\delta_{n} N_{n}$ that occur with negligible probabilities. The above arguments show that to obtain a limit with $\mathscr{L}(\sigma)$ strictly better than $\mathscr{L}(\tau)$, one must have $E \sigma>E \tau$ and $E(1 / \sigma)<E(1 / \tau)$. The argument applies in particular to stopping times that are not random and shows that if stopping times $N_{n}$ are such that $E N_{n}=n$, the experiments they generate cannot be comparable in the limit to the experiments with stopping time $n$ unless $N_{n} / n$ tends to unity. This does not mean that they should not be used, but only that the stopping times should ordinarily be selected with some specific purpose in mind.

## Appendix

## Results from Classical Analysis

## 1. The Language of Set Theory

In addition to the letters of various alphabets, with accents, subscripts, and superscripts, the most common symbols used in the sequel are the following. The implication sign ⇒ will be used for "implies," and the equivalence sign ⇔ "if and only if." For any relation $R$, the class of objects $x$ satisfying $R$ will be denoted $\{x: R\}$. The relation " $x \in y$ " between letters $x$ and $y$ is read " $x$ is an element of $y$ " or " $x$ belongs to $y$." The relations " $x$ is included in $y$ " or " $x$ is contained in $y$ " or " $x$ is a subset of $y$ " will be written " $x \subset y$ " or " $y \supset x$." Such a relation can be defined in terms of $\in$ by considering $x \subset y$ as equivalent to " $(u \in x) \Rightarrow(u \in y)^{\prime \prime}$. The standard $\cap$ and $\cup$ will be used to mean intersection and union.

It is intuitively convenient to treat many mathematical objects as populations, collections, or classes of other objects. Those particular classes which are the terms of set theory are called "sets" and subjected to various axioms and construction rules. To simplify the language, the word set will often be replaced by class or collection, the resulting lack of precision being usually without serious consequences.

An ordered pair $(x, y)$ consists of two letters; $x$ is called the first element or the first projection of the pair and $y$ the second element or second projection of the pair. A set whose elements are ordered pairs is called a graph. The graph $\{(x, y) ; x \in A, y \in B\}$ is denoted $A \times B$ and called the Cartesian product of $A$ and $B$. If a graph $f$ has the property that $x_{1}=x_{2} \Rightarrow\left(x_{1}, y_{1}\right)=\left(x_{2}, y_{2}\right)$, the graph is called functional. Such a graph is also called a function, or a map, or a mapping. The set $A$ of first coordinates of $f$ is called the domain of $f$. The set $B$ of second coordinates of $f$ is called the strict range of $f$. One also says that $f$ maps $A$ onto $B$.

The second coordinate of $(x, y) \in f$ is called the value of $f$ at $x$ and often denoted by $f(x)$, or $f_{x}$, or $f x$, or even $x f$. The map $f$ itself is often conveniently denoted by the symbol $x \mapsto f(x)$. If the domain of $f$ is $A$ we shall also say that $f$ is a map defined on $A$. Any graph $G$ possesses an inverse graph $G^{-1}$ obtained by interchanging the first and second coordinates of the pairs. A function $f$ whose inverse graph $f^{-1}$ is functional is called "one-to-one" or an injection.

A map $f$ defined on $A$ and having a range contained in $B$ determines another map, usually denoted by the same letter $f$, from the class of subsets of $A$ into the class of subset of $B$ by the relation

$$
S \mapsto f(S)=\{y: y=f(x), x \in S\} .
$$

The map $f$ defines also an inverse map, denoted $f^{-1}$, from the subsets of $B$ to the subsets of $A$ according to the prescription $T \rightarrow f^{-1}(T)=\{x: f(x) \in T\}$. Inverse maps defined in this fashion preserve the set operations $\cap, \cup$, and
complementation. The relations $S \subset f^{-1}[f(S)]$ and $f\left[f^{-1}(T)\right] \subset T$ always hold. The relation " $S \subset A \Rightarrow f^{-1}[f(S)]=S$ " is equivalent to the statement that $f$ is one-to-one. The relation " $T \subset B \Rightarrow f\left[f^{-1}(T)\right]=T$ " is equivalent to the statement that $f$ is a map of $A$ onto $B$.

Let $A, B$, and $C$ be three sets. Let $f$ be a map of $A$ into $B$, and let $g$ be a map of a subset of $B$ into $C$. The map $h$ whose elements are the pairs $(x, g[f(x)])$ for which $g[f(x)]$ is defined is called the map obtained by composition of $g$ and $f$. The map $h$ is often denoted $h=g \circ f$ or, when convenient, $h=g f$.

A family indexed by a set $\Lambda$ is a functional graph whose first coordinates are the elements of $\Lambda$ and whose second coordinates are terms $S_{\lambda}$. Such a family is usually denoted by a symbol of the type $\left\{S_{\lambda} ; \lambda \in \Lambda\right\}$ or $\left\{S_{\lambda}\right\}, \lambda \in \Lambda$.

A graph $R \subset A \times A$ is also called a relation between elements of $A$. Such a graph is called an equivalence relation if the following three requirements are satisfied:
(a) $(x, x) \in R$ for every $x \in A$,
(b) $(x, y) \in R$ and $(y, z) \in R \Rightarrow(x, z) \in R$,
(c) $(x, y) \in R \Rightarrow(y, x) \in R$.

A graph $R \subset A \times A$ is called an ordering of $A$ if the following requirements are satisfied.
(a) $x \in A \Rightarrow(x, x) \in R$,
(b) $(x, y) \in R$ and $(y, z) \in R \Rightarrow(x, y) \in R$,
(c) $(x, y) \in R$ and $(y, x) \in R \Rightarrow x=y$.

The relation $(x, y) \in R$ is then denoted by symbols of the type $x \leqq y$ or $x<y$ or $y>x$.

In an ordered set $A$, an element $a$ is called minimal (resp. maximal) if $b \in A$ and $b \leqq a$ (resp. $b \geqq a$ ) implies $b=a$. An element $a$ is called the smallest element of $A$ if $a \leqq x$ for every $x \in A$. Let $S$ be a subset of $A$. An element $s$ is called an upper bound of $S$ in $A$ if $x \leqq s$ for every $x \in S$. Such an upper bound is called the supremum of $S$ in $A$ and denoted $\sup S$ or $\sup \{s: s \in S\}$ if it is the smallest upper bound of $S$ in $A$.

An ordered set $A$ is called directed if for every two elements $x$ and $y$ of $A$ there is a $z \in A$ such that $x \leqq z$ and $y \leqq z$. The set $A$ is called a lattice if every finite subset of $A$ possesses a supremum and an infimum in $A$. A lattice $A$ is called complete (order complete) if every bounded subset (i.e., possessing an upper and lower bound) of $A$ possesses both an infimum and a supremum in A.

An ordered set $S$ is called totally ordered (or a chain) if for every pair ( $x, y$ ) either $x \leqq y$ or $y \leqq x$. The set $A$ is called well ordered if every nonempty subset of $A$ has a smallest element.

One of the most important propositions of the theory of ordered sets is variously known as the Hausdorff maximality principle or as Zorn's lemma and states the following.

The Maximality Principle. Let $A$ be an ordered set and let $S$ be a chain in $A$. There is a maximal subchain of $A$ which contains $S$.

Zorn's form of the theorem is as follows.
Zorn's Lemma. If every well ordered subset of $A$ has an upper bound in $A$, then



A possesses at least one maximal element.
Occasionally it is most convenient to use the more precise result of Zermelo. Zermelo's Theorem. For every set $A$ the Cartesian product $A \times A$ contains a graph which is a well ordering of $A$.
(We shall admit that every nonempty class is given together with a well determined distinguished element, thus obviating any recourse to weaker forms of the axiom of choice.)

Let $X$ be a set, and let $\mathscr{F}$ be a class of subsets of $X$. Some classes $\mathscr{F}$ having particular properties have received names as follows:
(a) Ring: $A \in \mathscr{F}$ and $B \in \mathscr{F}$ implies that $A \cap B, A \cup B$, and $A \cap B^{c}$ are elements of $\mathscr{F}$. (The notation $B^{c}$ is used for the complement of $B$ in $X$.)
(b) Field: ring such that $X \in \mathscr{F}$.
(c) $\sigma$-field: field such that the intersections of countable subclasses of $\mathscr{F}$ are also elements of $\mathscr{F}$.
(d) Topology: $A \in \mathscr{F}$ and $B \in \mathscr{F}$ imply $A \cap B \in \mathscr{F}$ and the unions of arbitrary subclasses of $\mathscr{F}$ are also elements of $\mathscr{F}$. Further, $X$ and the empty set $\varnothing$ are elements of $\mathscr{F}$,
(e) Filter base: $A \in \mathscr{F}$ and $B \in \mathscr{F}$ implies $A \cap B \supset C \in \mathscr{F}$. Further, $\varnothing$ is not an element of $\mathscr{F}$.
(f) Filter: filter base such that $A \in \mathscr{F}$ and $A \subset B$ implies $B \in \mathscr{F}$.
(g) Ultrafilter: filter such that if $A \subset X$, then either $A \in \mathscr{F}$ or $A^{c} \in \mathscr{F}$.

A cover $\mathscr{F}$ of a set $X$ is a class of subsets of $X$ such that every element of $X$ belongs to an element of $\mathscr{F}$. A cover formed of disjoint sets is called a partition. If $\mathscr{F}_{1}$ and $\mathscr{F}_{2}$ are two covers of $X$, the second $\mathscr{F}_{2}$ is called finer than the first if for every $G \in \mathscr{F}_{2}$ there is an $F \in \mathscr{F}_{1}$ such that $G \subset F$.

Let $X$ and $Y$ be two sets. Let $f$ be a map of $X$ into $Y$. If $\mathscr{F}$ is a class of subsets of $X$, the quotient or direct image class induced by $f$ on $Y$ is the class $\mathscr{G}$ of subsets $G$ of $Y$ such that $f^{-1}(G) \in \mathscr{F}$. With this definition, the quotient of a ring, field, $\sigma$-field, topology, filter, or ultrafilter is again correspondingly a ring, field, $\sigma$-field, topology, filter, or ultrafilter.

A filter $\mathscr{F}$ is an ultrafilter if and only if the inclusion $\mathscr{F} \subset \mathscr{F}^{\prime}$ where $\mathscr{F}^{\prime}$ is a filter implies that $\mathscr{F}=\mathscr{F}^{\prime}$. According to Zorn's lemma, for every filter $\mathscr{F}$ there is an ultrafilter $\mathscr{F}^{\prime}$ such that $\mathscr{F} \subset \mathscr{F}^{\prime}$.

A set which has received some structure by an appropriate selection of classes of subsets, graphs, and other relations is often called a space. Strictly speaking, a space consists not only of a set but also of the whole conglomeration of other sets and relations which define the structure. However, it is often possible and convenient to mention only the letter naming the set. For instance, a topological space consists of a set $X$ and a topology $\mathscr{T}$ which is a class of subsets of $X$. Nevertheless, we shall often say "let $X$ be a topological space" instead of "let $\{X, \mathscr{T}\}$ be a topological space."

In addition to structures defined by classes of subsets such as topological spaces, we shall be forced to deal with structures of a different kind such as algebraic structures or more complicated affairs. The most common algebraic structures used in the sequel are the following.
(a) Groups: A group $G$ consists of a set (usually denoted $G$ ) and a function
$(x, y) \rightarrow x y$ from $G \times G$ to $G$ such that
(1) $(x y) z=x(y z)$ (associativity),
(2) there is a neutral element $e$ (usually denoted 0 or 1 ) such that $e x=x e=x$,
(3) for every $x \in G$ there is a $y \in G$ such that $x y=y x=e$.

If the group is Abelian, that is, if $x y=y x$, the operation $(x, y) \rightarrow x y$ is usually denoted by $(x, y) \rightarrow x+y$, and the neutral element $e$ is denoted 0 and called the origin or zero of the group.
(b) Rings: A ring $R$ is a set carrying two operations $(x, y) \rightarrow x+y$ and $(x, y) \rightarrow x y$. For the first operation (addition) $R$ is an Abelian group. The second operation (multiplication) satisfies
(1) $(x y) z=x(y z)$,
(2) $x(y+z)=x y+x z$,
(3) $(y+z) x=y x+z x$.
(c) Fields: A field is a ring in which the nonzero elements of $R$ form a group for the multiplication operation.
(d) Vector space or linear space: A pair consisting of an Abelian group $A$ and a field $K$ with an operation $(\lambda, x) \rightarrow \lambda x$ from $K \times A$ to $A$ satisfying the
following conditions:
(1) $\lambda(x+y)=\lambda x+\lambda y$,
(2) $(\lambda+\mu) x=\lambda x+\mu x$,
(3) $\lambda(\mu x)=(\lambda \mu) x$,
(4) $0 x=0$ and $1 x=x$.

The field $K$ is called the field of scalars of the vector space. For our purposes $K$ will practically always be the field of real numbers or the field of complex numbers. In the first case we shall refer to $A$ as a vector space or a real vector space. In the second case we shall refer to $A$ as a complex vector space.
(e) Algebra: An algebra will be here a vector space which also carries a multiplication operation $(x, y) \rightarrow x y$ for which it is a ring and such that $\lambda(x y)=(\lambda x) y$ for $\lambda \in K$ and $(x, y) \in A \times A$.

The notion of isomorphism and the various morphisms (homomorphism, homeomorphism, etc.) refer to maps between structures of the same type. An isomorphism is defined by one-to-one maps which preserve the whole structure under consideration. When a set carries several structures, we shall spell out which ones are preserved. For instance, we shall say that certain Banach algebras are algebraically isomorphic to algebras of complex functions.

## 2. Topological Spaces

In a topological space $\{X, \mathscr{T}\}$ the elements of $\mathscr{T}$ are called open sets and their complements closed sets. If $x \in X$, every subset $V$ of $X$ which contains an open set to which $x$ belongs is called a neigborhood of $x$. The neighborhood of $x$ forms a filter $V_{\boldsymbol{x}}$. A filter $\mathscr{F}$ on $X$ converges to $x$ if $V_{\boldsymbol{x}} \subset \mathscr{F}$. The point $x$ is then called the limit of $\mathscr{F}$.

Let $S \subset X$. The largest open set contained in $S$ is called the interior of $S$ and denoted $S^{0}$. The smallest closed set containing $S$ is called the closure or
adherence of $S$ and is denoted $\bar{S}$. A point $x$ is called a cluster point of a filter $\mathscr{F}$ if $x \in \cap\{\bar{F} ; F \in \mathscr{F}\}$. A point $x$ is a cluster point of the filter $\mathscr{F}$ if and only if there is a filter $\mathscr{F}^{\prime}$ converging to $x$ such that $\mathscr{F} \subset \mathscr{F}^{\prime}$.

Let $\Delta$ be a set directed by an order relation $\leqq$. A family $\left\{x_{v}\right\} ; v \in \Delta$, indexed by the elements of $\Delta$ is called a direction, or a net in $X$. The sets $\left\{x_{\mu} ; v \leqq \mu\right\}$ form for $v \in \Delta$ the base of a filter. Every filter or base of a filter can be obtained in this way. A direction for which $\Delta$ is the set of integers in their natural order is called a sequence. The terms "limit," "cluster point," etc., applied to a direction refer to the filter associated with the direction. A topological space $X$ is called separated if distinct points of $X$ possess disjoint neighborhoods. The space $X$ is called regular if it is separated and if for every $x \in X$ and every closed set $F$ such that $x \notin F$ the point $x$ and the set $F$ have disjoint neighborhoods.

The space $X$ is called completely regular if it is separated, and such that if $x \in X$ and if $V$ is a neighborhood of $x$, there is a continuous numerical function from $X$ to $[0,1]$ equal to one at $x$ and to zero on $V^{c}$.

The space $X$ is called normal if disjoint closed subsets of $X$ possess disjoint neighborhoods.

A space $X$ possesses Lindelöf's property if every cover of $X$ by open sets admits a countable refinement. The space $X$ possesses the Borel-Lebesgue property if every cover of $X$ by open sets admits a finite refinement.

A space $X$ is called (countably) paracompact if every (countable) open cover of $X$ has a locally finite refinement.

A topological space $X$ is called connected if $X$ cannot be partitioned into two disjoint nonempty open sets.

Let $X$ and $Y$ be two topological spaces, and let $f$ be a map defined on $X$ and taking its values in $Y$. The map $f$ is called continuous if the preimages $f^{-1}(G)$ of open sets $G \subset Y$ are open sets in $X$. Equivalently, $f$ is continuous if and only if the direct image by $f$ of a filter $\mathscr{F}$ converging to $x$ is a filter converging to $f(x)$. A map $f$ is called sequentially continuous if $\left\{f\left(x_{n}\right)\right\}$ converges to $f\left(x_{0}\right)$ whenever $\left\{x_{n}\right\}$ is a sequence converging to $x_{0}$.

An injection $f$ of $X$ into $Y$ is called a homeomorphism of $X$ into $Y$ if both $f$ and its inverse map are continuous.

Let $\left\{X_{\lambda} ; \lambda \in \Lambda\right\}$ be a family of topological spaces. The topological product $E=\left\{X_{\lambda} ; \lambda \in \Lambda\right\}$ is the space of families $\left\{x_{\lambda} ; \lambda \in \Lambda\right\}\left(x_{\lambda} \in X_{\lambda}\right)$ with the smallest topology which makes all the projections $\left\{x_{\lambda} ; \lambda \in \Lambda\right\} \rightarrow x_{\lambda_{0}}$ continuous. For a filter $\mathscr{F}$ on $E$ to converge to $\left\{x_{\lambda} ; \lambda \in \Lambda\right\}$, it is necessary and sufficient that the direct image of $\mathscr{F}$ in the coordinate spaces $X_{\lambda}$ converge to the corresponding coordinates $x_{\lambda}$.

In a topological space $X$ the following conditions are equivalent:
(a) $X$ possesses the Borel-Lebesgue property,
(b) every ultrafilter on $X$ has a limit,
(c) every filter on $X$ has a cluster point,
(d) if $\mathscr{F}$ is a family of closed sets of $X$, either $\bigcap\{F ; F \in \mathscr{F}\}$ is nonempty or there is a finite subclass of $\mathscr{F}$ having an empty intersection.

A space having these properties is called quasicompact. It is called compact if
it is quasicompact and separated. On a compact space $X$, a separated topology weaker than the original topology of $X$ is identical to it.

A continuous injection of a compact space $X$ into a separated space is a homeomorphism.

A space $X$ is called countably compact if it possesses the Heine-Borel property: every countable open cover of $X$ possesses a finite refinement.

A space $X$ is called sequentially compact if every sequence $\left\{x_{n}\right\}$ in $X$ contains a subsequence $\left\{x_{n_{k}}\right\}$ which converges to a point of $X$.

A separated continuous image of a compact, sequentially compact, countably compact, or Lindelöf space is likewise a compact, sequentially compact, countably compact, or a Lindelöf space.

A subset $A$ of a topological space $X$ is called relatively compact in $X$ if $\bar{A}$ is compact. The set $A$ is called relatively countably compact (resp. relatively sequentially compact) in $X$ if every sequence $\left\{x_{n}\right\}$ in $A$ has a cluster point in $X$ (resp. contains a subsequence which converges to a point of $X$ ).

A space $X$ is called locally compact if every point of $X$ has a compact neighborhood.

Countable products of countably compact (resp. sequentially compact) spaces are countably (resp. sequentially) compact. Arbitrary products of compact spaces are compact. Compact spaces are normal.

According to Urysohn's lemma, if $X$ is a normal space and if $A$ and $B$ are disjoint closed subsets of $X$, there is a continuous map $f$ from $X$ to $[0,1]$ such that $f(x)=1$ for $x \in A$ and $f(x)=0$ for $x \in B$. According to Tietze's theorem, if $X$ is normal and if $f$ is a continuous map defined on a closed subset $A$ of $X$ and taking values in $[0,1]$, there is a continuous map $g$ from $X$ to $[0,1]$ which agrees with $f$ on $A$.

## 3. Uniform Spaces

Let $X$ be a set. Let $X \times X$ be the Cartesian product of $X$ by itself, and let $\Delta=\{(x, x) ; x \in X\}$ be the diagonal of $X \times X$. A uniform structure on $X$ is a filter $\mathscr{U}$ on $X \times X$ having the following properties:
(a) every element of $\mathscr{U}$ contains $\Delta$;
(b) if $V \in \mathscr{U}$, then $V^{-1}=\{(x, y):(y, x) \in V\}$ is an element of $\mathscr{U}$,
(c) if $V \in \mathscr{U}$, there is a $W \in \mathscr{U}$ such that $W \circ W=\{(x, z)$ : there is a $y$ such that $(x, y) \in W$ and $(y, z) \in W\}$ is a subset of $V$.

The elements of $\mathscr{U}$ are called vicinities of the diagonal of $X \times X$. A uniform structure $\mathscr{U}$ defines a topology $\mathscr{T}$ on $X$ as follows. The open sets of $\mathscr{T}$ are the sets $G$ such that if $x \in G$, then there is $V \in \mathscr{U}$ such that $V(x)=\{y ;(x, y) \in V\} \subset G$. A topology $\mathscr{T}$ and a uniform structure $\mathscr{U}$ satisfying this relation are called compatible. The notions of limit, compactness, etc. for a uniform structure $\mathscr{U}$ refer to the topology induced by $\mathscr{U}$.

On a uniform space $X$ a filter $\mathscr{F}$ is called a Cauchy filter if $\mathscr{F}$ contains sets "as small as one pleases"; that is, if for every $V \in \mathscr{U}$ there is an $F \in \mathscr{F}$ such that $F \times F \subset V$. A uniform space in which every Cauchy filter has a limit
is called complete. If $X$ is a uniform space, one can (by taking equivalence classes of Cauchy filters) form a space $\hat{X}$ which is complete and in which $X$ can be isomorphically imbedded as a dense subset. The space $\hat{X}$ is called the completion of $X$.

A compact space has one and only one uniform structure $\mathscr{U}$ compatible with its topology. It is obtained by letting $\mathscr{U}$ be the filter of neighborhoods of the $X \times X$ diagonal. A space $X$ is called precompact if its completion is quasicompact, or equivalently, if for every $V \in \mathscr{U}$, the space $X$ admits a finite cover $\left\{A_{i} ; i=1,2, \ldots, n\right\}$ such that $A_{i} \times A_{i} \subset V$. A separated uniform space is compact if and only if it is complete and precompact. A function $f$ from a uniform space $X_{1}$ to a uniform space $X_{2}$ is called uniformly continuous if for every $V \in \mathscr{U}_{2}$ there is a $W \in \mathscr{U}_{1}$ such that $(x, y) \in W$ implies $(f(x), f(y)) \in V$. On a compact space every continuous function is also uniformly continuous.

Let $X$ be a separated topological space. The following conditions are equivalent:
(a) there is at least one uniform structure compatible with the topology of $X$;
(b) the space $X$ is completely regular;
(c) every lower semicontinuous numerical function $f$ on $X$ is the pointwise supremum of a family of continuous functions.

Let $\left\{X_{\lambda} ; \lambda \in \Lambda\right\}$ be a family of precompact spaces. Let $S$ be a set, and for each $\lambda$ let $f_{\lambda}$ be a map from $S$ into $X_{\lambda}$. The smallest uniform structure for which the $f_{\lambda}$ are uniformly continuous is precompact.

If $S$ is a uniform space and $\mathscr{F}$ is the set of all uniformly continuous maps from $S$ to the interval $[0,1]$, the smallest uniform structure on $S$ for which all the elements of $\mathscr{F}$ are uniformly continuous is precompact. It is the largest precompact structure smaller than the original structure of $S$.

## 4. Metric Spaces

Let $X$ be a set. A function $\rho$ from $X \times X$ to the interval [ $0, \infty$ ) of the real line is called a metric or a distance if it satisfies the following requirements:
(a) $\rho(x, y)=\rho(y, x)$,
(b) $\rho(x, z) \leqq \rho(x, y)+\rho(y, z)$,
(c) $\rho(x, x)=0$,
(d) $\rho(x, y)=0$ implies $x=y$.

If (d) is not satisfied, then $\rho$ is called a pseudometric. A pseudometric $\rho$ defines on $X$ a uniform structure $\mathscr{U}$ consisting of the sets $V$ which contain a set $W$ of the form $W=\{(x, y): \rho(x, y)<\varepsilon\}$ for some $\varepsilon>0$. The structure $\mathscr{U}$ defines in turn a topology $\mathscr{T}$ on $X$. In these circumstances $\rho$ and $\mathscr{U}$ and $\rho$ and $\mathscr{T}$ are called compatible.

A uniform structure $\mathscr{U}$ is compatible with some pseudometric if and only if $\mathscr{U}$ possesses a countable base. The notions of limit, compactness, etc. for a pseudometric refer to the topology induced by it. Similarly, the notions of Cauchy filter, completeness, etc. for a pseudometric refer to the uniform structure induced by it.

In a metric space the notions of compactness, sequential compactness, and countable compactness coincide and so do the corresponding relative notions. This is also true in every topological space whose topology is finer than a topology induced by a metric.

Generally speaking, the use of nets or filters is unnecessary in metric spaces. One can content oneself with the consideration of sequences, Cauchy sequences, and the like. A metric space is always normal. Conversely, a normal space having a countable base has a metrizable topology; that is, there exists a metric compatible with its topology.

The metric spaces which are complete and have a countable base occur quite frequently. A topological space which is homeomorphic with a complete separable metric space is called Polish. Closed subsets of Polish spaces are Polish. Countable products of Polish spaces are Polish. A space is Polish if and only if it is homeomorphic to a $G_{\delta}$ ( $=$ countable intersection of open sets) of the cube $K^{N}$ countable product of intervals $[0,1]$.

A topological space whose topology is Hausdorff is called a Souslin space if it is a continuous image of a Polish space. A Souslin space which is a continuous one-to-one image of a Polish space is called a Lusin space.

Let $X$ be a Souslin space. Let $\mathscr{B}(X)$ be the smallest $\sigma$-field of subsets of $X$ which contains the closed sets of $X$. The elements of $\mathscr{B}(X)$ are called the Borel subsets of $X$. The Borel space $\{X, \mathscr{B}(X)\}$ is called the Borel space associated with the topological space $X$.

A Borel function $f$ from space $X$ to a space $Y$ is a map $f$ defined on $X$, taking values in $Y$, and such that $f^{-1}(B) \in \mathscr{B}(X)$ for every $B \in \mathscr{B}(Y)$. If $X$ and $Y$ are Souslin spaces for a map $f$ to be a Borel map, it is necessary and sufficient that its graph be a Souslin space. In particular, if $f$ is one-toone and a Borel function, then its inverse function is also a Borel function. If $X$ is a metrizable Lusin space, its associated Borel space depends only on the cardinality of $X$. Explicitly, if $X$ and $Y$ are metrizable Lusin spaces of the same cardinality (which is then either finite or countable or the cardinality of the continuum), there is a one-to-one Borel map of $X$ onto $Y$ whose inverse is a Borel map.

Theorem of the Borel Section. Let $X$ be a Polish space and $R$ an equivalence relation in $X$ such that
(a) the equivalence classes of $R$ are closed in $X$, and
(b) the union of the equivalence classes of closed sets are Borel sets.

Then there is a Borel subset of $X$ which has one and only one point in common with each equivalence class.

## Measurable Sections

Let $A$ and $B$ be Souslin spaces. Let $f$ be a continuous map from $A$ onto $B$. There is a map $g$ from $B$ into $A$ such that $g(y) \in f^{-1}\{y\}$ for every $y \in B$ (that is, $(f \circ g) y=y)$ and such that if $\mu$ is an arbitrary Radon measure on $B$, then $g$ is measurable in the sense of Lusin for $\mu$.

Let $X$ be a metrizable topological space. Let $A$ be a relatively compact subset of $X$, which is a Souslin space. There exists a compact metrizable space $K$, a decreasing sequence $\left\{B_{n}\right\}$ of subsets of $K$, and a continuous map $f$ from $K$ to $X$ such that
(a) $A=f\left[\cap B_{n}\right]$;
(b) each $B_{n}$ is a countable union of compact subsets of $K$.

Many of the properties of Souslin spaces extend to the $K$-analytic spaces. A $K$-analytic space is a continuous image of a $K_{\sigma \delta}$ (countable intersection of countable unions of compacts). The sets obtained by application of Souslin's operation $A$ to compact subsets of a topological space ( $K$-Souslinian sets) have similar properties.

## 5. Spaces of Functions

Let $F$ be a uniform space and let $E$ be an arbitrary set. Let $\mathscr{F}(E, F)$ be the space of maps from $E$ to $F$. On the set $\mathscr{F}$ it is usual to define several uniform structures by the following process. Let $\Sigma$ be a family of subsets of $E$. For each $S \in \Sigma$ and each vicinity $V$ of the diagonal of $F$ let $W(S, V)$ be the set of couples $(f, g)$ of elements of $\mathscr{F}$ such that $(f(x), g(x)) \in V$ for every $x \in S$. The sets $W$ so obtained form a base of a uniformity on $\mathscr{F}$ is called the uniformity of $\Sigma$ convergence. The associated topology is called the topology of $\Sigma$-convergence.

Among the families $\Sigma$ which are commonly used one can mention the following:
(a) The family of all sets reduced to one point. The $\Sigma$-uniformity (topology) is then called the uniformity (topology) of pointwise convergence.
(b) The family of all subsets, or the family reduced to the set $E$ itself. The $\Sigma$-umiformity is then called the uniformity of uniform convergence.
(c) When $E$ is topological, one can take for $\Sigma$ the family of compact subsets of $E$, obtaining then the uniformity of compact convergence.
(d) When $E$ is a uniform space, one can take for $\Sigma$ the family of precompact subsets of $E$, etc.

The uniform space obtained by placing on $\mathscr{F}(E, F)$ the uniform structure of $\Sigma$-convergence will usually be denoted by $\mathscr{F}_{\Sigma}(E, F)$. Of course the same structures can be defined on subspaces of $\mathscr{F}$. For instance, one can consider the space $C_{\Sigma}(E, F)$ of continuous functions from $E$ to $F$ with the uniform structure of $\Sigma$-convergence.

Suppose that $E$ is locally compact and $F$ separated. Let $\Sigma$ be the family of compact subsets of $E$. It is interesting to note that the topology of $C_{\Sigma}(E, F)$ does not depend on the uniform structure of $F$ but only of its topology. More generally, if $E$ is locally compact and $F$ is a separated topological space, one can define on $C(E, F)$ a topology called compact-open or compact convergence by taking as open subsets of $C(E, F)$ the sets of elements $f$ such that $f(K) \subset U$ for a compact $K$ of $E$ and an open $U$ of $F$.

A filter $\left\{f_{v}\right\}$ on $C(E, F)$ converges to $f_{0} \in C(E, F)$ for the topology of compact convergence if and only if $x_{\alpha} \rightarrow x_{0}$ implies $\lim _{\nu, \alpha} f_{\nu}\left(x_{\alpha}\right)=f_{0}\left(x_{0}\right)$. This
result is of great importance as it shows that in many cases one can replace an argument involving uniform convergence by an argument involving an ordinary type of convergence. The type of convergence described by the property that $\lim _{v} f_{v}=f_{0}$ if $\lim _{v, \alpha} f_{v}\left(x_{\alpha}\right)=f_{0}\left(x_{0}\right)$ whenever $x_{\alpha} \rightarrow x_{0}$, is called continuous convergence.

Let $E$ be an arbitrary set and let $F$ be a separated uniform space. If $F$ is complete so is $\mathscr{F}_{\Sigma}(E, F)$ for every family $\Sigma$. If $S$ is a subset of $\mathscr{F}(E, F)$ and $S$ is complete for the structure of $\Sigma_{1}$-convergence, then $S$ is also complete for $\Sigma_{2}$-convergence if $\Sigma_{1} \subset \Sigma_{2}$. If $S$ is compact for $\Sigma_{2}$-convergence, if $\Sigma_{1}$ covers $E$, and $\Sigma_{1} \subset \Sigma_{2}$, then $S$ is also compact for $\Sigma_{1}$ and on $S$ the two structures coincide.

Let $E$ be a countably compact space and let $F$ be a metrizable space. Let $A \subset C_{s}(E, F)$ where $C_{s}=C_{s}(E, F)$ is the space of continuous functions from $E$ to $F$ with the topology of pointwise convergence. Let $D$ be a dense subset of $E$. Assume that for each $x \in E$ the set $\{f(x) ; f \in A\}$ is relatively compact in F.

The following conditions are equivalent:
(1) $A$ is relatively countably compact in $C_{s}$;
(2) $A$ is relatively compact in $C_{s}$;
(3) if $\left\{x_{n}\right\}$ is a sequence in $D$ and $\left\{f_{k}\right\}$ is a sequence in $A$ and if both repeated limits $\lim _{k} \lim _{n} f_{k}\left(x_{n}\right)$ and $\lim _{n} \lim _{k} f_{k}\left(x_{n}\right)$ exist, then they are equal.

From this it follows that, under the same assumptions on $E$ and $F$ (or assuming only that every element of $\mathscr{F}(E, F)$ where restriction to the countably compact subsets of $E$ is continuous is already in $C(E, F)$ ), and for every family $\Sigma$ of subsets of $E$ which covers $E$, the relatively countably compact subsets of $C_{\Sigma}(E, F)$ coincide with the relatively compact subsets.

Another theorem of interest is the following. Let $E$ be countably compact and let $F$ be a separated uniform space. Let $\Sigma$ be a family of subsets of $E$ which covers $E$. Let $A$ be a relatively countably compact subset of $C_{\Sigma}(E, F)$. If for each $f \in A$ the subset $f(E)$ of $F$ has a metrizable topology, then $A$ is relatively sequentially compact.

Let $E$ be topological and let $F$ be uniform. A subset $A$ of $\mathscr{F}(E, F)$ is called equicontinuous at $x \in E$ if for every vicinity $V$ of the uniform structure of $F$ there exists a neighborhood $U$ of $x$ such that $y \in U$ implies $(f(y), f(x)) \in V$ for every $f \in A$. The subset $A$ is called equicontinuous if it is equicontinuous at each point. Let $A$ be equicontinuous and let $\Sigma$ be the family of compact subsets of $E$. Then on $A$ the structure of pointwise convergence and the structure of $\Sigma$-convergence coincide.
(Ascoli). Let $A$ be a subset of $C(E, F)$ which is relatively compact in $C(E, F)$ for the topology of compact convergence; then the restriction of $A$ to each compact set of $E$ is equicontinuous and such that for each $x \in E$ the set $\{f(x) ; f \in A\}$ is relatively compact. The converse is true if $E$ is locally compact.
(Dini). Let $E$ be topological and $R$ the real line. Let $\left\{f_{v}\right\}$ be a family of functions $f_{v} \in C(E, R)$ which is directed decreasingly and such that $\lim _{v} f_{v}(x)=0$ for each $x \in E$. Then $f_{\nu}$ converges to zero for the topology of compact convergence.
