(Stone). Let $E$ be compact. Let $H$ be a subset of $C(E, R)$ such that if $f$ and $g$ belong to $H$, then so do the pointwise supremum $f \vee g$ and the pointwise infimum $f \vee g$ of $f$ and $g$. Let \& be a positive number.

If $h \in C(E, R)$, in order that there be $f \in H$ such that $|f(x)-h(x)|<\varepsilon$ for every $x \in E$ it is necessary and sufficient that for each pair ( $x, y$ ) of elements of $E$ there be a function $g=g_{x, y}$ of $H$ such that $|g(x)-h(x)|<\varepsilon$ and $|g(y)-h(y)|<\varepsilon$.

## 6. Vector Spaces

The vector spaces used most frequently in the sequel are, of course, the usual finite dimensional Euclidean spaces. However, a wide variety of other spaces will also be encountered. It is assumed that the reader is familiar with the properties of the $n$-dimensional Euclidean space $R_{n}$.

We recall that a vector space (or linear space) (over the reals) is an Abelian group together with scalar multiplication by real numbers. Let $\mathscr{X}$ and $\mathscr{Y}$ be two vector spaces with the same scalar field. A map $T$ from $\mathscr{X}$ to $\mathscr{Y}$ is called linear if $T(\lambda x+\mu y)=\lambda T(x)+\mu T(y)$ for every couple $(x, y)$ of elements of $\mathscr{X}$ and every couple $(\lambda, \mu)$ of elements of the scalar field. A map $A$ from $\mathscr{X}$ to $\mathscr{Y}$ is called affine if $T=A-A(0)$ is linear. A linear map from $\mathscr{X}$ to the scalars is called a linear form or a linear functional.

Let $\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{Y}$, and $\mathscr{F}$ be three vector spaces. A map $\mathscr{B}$ from $\mathscr{X} \times \mathscr{Y}$ to $\mathscr{F}$ is called bilinear if it is linear in each variable separately. Bilinear maps from $\mathscr{X} \times \mathscr{Y}$ to the scalars are also called bilinear forms. The restriction of a bilinear form on $\mathscr{X} \times \mathscr{X}$ to the diagonal of $\mathscr{X} \times \mathscr{X}$ is also called a quadratic form. Forms of higher order (trilinear, etc.) are defined similarly.

A projection $P$ of $\mathscr{X}$ onto a linear subspace $\mathscr{Y}$ of $\mathscr{X}$ is a linear map from $\mathscr{X}$ onto $\mathscr{Y}$, leaving all points of $\mathscr{Y}$ invariant. Projections are characterized by the fact that they are idempotent, i.e., $P P=P$. A projection $P$ is well determined by the spaces $P \mathscr{X}$ and $(I-P) \mathscr{X}$, the letter $I$ denoting the identity map.

Let $S$ be a subset of a vector space $\mathscr{X}$, with scalar field $K$. A (finite) linear combination of elements of $S$ is an element of $\mathscr{X}$ of the form $\sum \lambda(x) x$ with $\lambda(x) \in K$ and $x \in S$ and $\lambda(x)=0$, except for a finite set of values of $x$. If, in addition, $K$ is the real line $R$ and $\lambda(x) \geqq 0$ and $\sum \lambda(x)=1$, the linear combination is called convex.

A set $S \subset \mathscr{X}$ is called linearly independent if for finite linear combinations of elements of $S$ the equality $\sum \lambda(x) x=0$ implies $\lambda(x) x=0$ for every $x$.

A base for a vector space $\mathscr{X}$ is a linearly independent set $B$ such that every $x \in \mathscr{X}$ is a linear combination of elements of $B$. Every vector space $\mathscr{X}$ has a base and any two bases of $\mathscr{X}$ have the same cardinality, called the linear dimension $(\operatorname{dim} \mathscr{X})$ of $\mathscr{X}$.

A real vector space $\mathscr{X}$ is algebraically isomorphic to the product of $n$ real lines when and only when $\operatorname{dim} \mathscr{X}=n$. Let $\mathscr{X}$ be a vector space and let $A$ and $B$ be two subsets of $\mathscr{X}$. For scalars $\lambda, \mu$ the set $\lambda A+\mu B$ is the set $\{z \in \mathscr{X}: z=\lambda x+\mu y, x \in A, y \in B\}$.

For the remainder of this section, $\mathscr{X}$ will be a real or complex vector space. In such a space a set $C$ is called a cone of vertex $x_{0}$ if $x \in C$ implies that $x_{0}+\lambda\left(x-x_{0}\right) \in C$ for every $\lambda>0$. The cone is called pointed if $x_{0} \in C$, and acute if no line $\left\{x_{0}+\lambda\left(x-x_{0}\right), \lambda\right.$ real $\}$ is contained in $C$. A set $C$ is called convex if for every $\lambda \in[0,1]$ the set $\lambda C+(1-\lambda) C$ is contained in $C$.

Let $C$ be a convex subset of a real vector space $\mathscr{X}$. Assume that $0 \in C$. The gauge of $C$ is the function defined by

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \gamma(x)=\inf \{\rho: \rho \in(0, \infty), x \in \rho C\} \text { if } x \in \rho C \text { for some } \rho, \text { and } \\
& \gamma(x)=\infty \text { if } x \text { does not belong to any } \rho C .
\end{aligned}
$$

Finite valued gauges of symmetric convex sets are called seminorms. Explicitly, and on complex spaces as well, a seminorm is a function $\rho$ from $\mathscr{X}$ to $[0, \infty)$ such that
(1) $p(\lambda x)=|\lambda| p(x)$,
(2) $p(x+y) \leqq p(x)+p(y)$.

If, in addition, $p(x)=0$ implies $x=0$, the seminorm is called a norm. A seminorm $\rho$ is called Hilbertian or Euclidean if it satisfies the median equality (also called the parallelogram identity):

$$
\rho^{2}(x+y)+\rho^{2}(x-y)=2\left[\rho^{2}(x)+\rho^{2}(y)\right] .
$$

An Hilbertian seminorm, on a real vector space $\mathscr{X}$, defines on $\mathscr{X} \times \mathscr{X}$ a symmetric bilinear form $M$, called the inner product defined by $\rho$, according to the equality

$$
4 M(x, y)=\rho^{2}(x+y)-\rho^{2}(x-y)
$$

Such a form is also positive semidefinite, i.e., $M(x, x) \geqq 0$. Conversely a positive semidefinite symmetric bilinear form $M$ on $\mathscr{X} \times \mathscr{X}$ defines a Hilbertian seminorm, according to $\rho^{2}(x)=M(x, x)$. If $\rho$ so defined is a norm, the bilinear form $M$ is called positive definite.

If $\mathscr{X}$ is a vector space, the space $\mathscr{X}^{\prime}$ of all linear forms defined on $\mathscr{X}$ is also a vector space for the natural definition of the operations. The space $\mathscr{X}^{\prime}$ is called the algebraic dual of $\mathscr{X}$. Let $\langle\alpha, x\rangle$ be the value at $x$ of the element $\alpha$ of $\mathscr{X}^{\prime}$. Considering $x$ as fixed and $\alpha$ as variable, the map $\alpha \rightarrow\langle\alpha, x\rangle$ is an element of the algebraic dual $\mathscr{X}^{\prime \prime}$ of $\mathscr{X}^{\prime}$. This map is often identified as $x$. When convenient, we shall write $\langle x, \alpha\rangle$ instead of $\langle\alpha, x\rangle$.

If $\mathscr{X}$ is finite dimensional, $\mathscr{X}^{\prime}$ is isomorphic to $\mathscr{X}$. Let $\mathscr{X}$ and $\mathscr{Y}$ be two vector spaces. Let $\mathscr{X}^{\prime}$ and $\mathscr{Y}^{\prime}$ be their respective algebraic duals. If $T$ is a linear map
from $\mathscr{X}$ to $\mathscr{Y}$, the relation $\langle\beta, T x\rangle=\left\langle T^{\prime} \beta, x\right\rangle$ for $x \in \mathscr{X}$ and $\beta \in \mathscr{Y}^{\prime}$ defines a linear map $T^{\prime}$ from $\mathscr{Y}^{\prime}$ to $\mathscr{X}^{\prime}$. This map is called the algebraic transpose of $T$. When $\mathscr{X}$ is not finite dimensional, its algebraic dual $\mathscr{X}^{\prime}$ is usually too large for convenient handling. It is then preferable to use topological considerations and restrict oneself to continuous linear functionals.



A topology $\mathscr{T}$ on a group $\mathscr{X}$ is called compatible with the group structure of $\mathscr{X}$ if the maps $(x, y) \rightarrow x y$ (or $x+y$ ) and $x \rightarrow x^{-1}$ (or $(-x)$ ) are continuous. Such a topology can be used to define on $\mathscr{X}$ a uniform structure by taking for filter of vicinities of the diagonal the sets $U \in \mathscr{X} \times \mathscr{X}$ such that $U U^{-1}$ (or $U-U)$ be contained in a neighborhood of the origin.

A topology $\mathscr{T}$ on a vector space $\mathscr{X}$ is compatible with the vector structure of $\mathscr{X}$ if $\mathscr{T}$ is compatible with the group structure of $\mathscr{X}$ and if the scalar multiplication $(\lambda, x) \rightarrow \lambda x$ is jointly continuous. A uniform structure is associated with $\mathscr{T}$ by using the group structure as above.

Let $K$ denote either the field of real numbers or the field of complex numbers. If $\mathscr{X}$ is a finite dimensional space over $K$, there is one and only one separated topology compatible with the linear structure of $\mathscr{X}$. This topology coincides with the topology induced by the isomorphism between $\mathscr{X}$ and $K^{n}$. Consequently $\mathscr{X}$ is complete and locally compact. Conversely, a locally compact separated linear space is necessarily finite dimensional.

Most, but not all, topologies we shall have to consider in the sequel are locally convex. That is, every neighborhood of the origin of the space $\mathscr{X}$ contains a convex neighborhood of the origin. Locally convex topologies on $\mathscr{X}$ coincide with the topologies which can be defined by families of seminorms, namely the gauges of the convex symmetric neighborhoods of the origin.

Let $\mathscr{X}$ be a real or complex vector space and let $\mathscr{Y}$ be a linear subspace of the algebraic dual of $\mathscr{X}$. The weak topology induced on $\mathscr{X}$ by $\mathscr{Y}$ is the weakest topology for which the elements of $\mathscr{Y}$ are continuous. This topology is locally convex. It is denoted by the symbol $w\{\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{Y}\}$ or $\sigma\{\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{Y}\}$. The Mackey topology $\tau\{\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{Y}\}$ is the strongest locally convex topology on $\mathscr{X}$ for which the dual (=space of continuous linear functionals) of $\mathscr{X}$ is $\mathscr{Y}$.

Consider a pair $\{\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{Y}\}, \mathscr{Y} \subset \mathscr{X}^{\prime}$ such that $\mathscr{Y}$ separates the points of $\mathscr{X}$. The polar $S^{0}$ of a subset $S$ of $\mathscr{X}$ in $\mathscr{Y}$ is the set of all $y \in \mathscr{Y}$ such that the real part of $\langle y, x\rangle$ is larger than or equal to $(-1)$ for all $x \in S$. That is $S^{0}=\{y: y \in \mathscr{Y}, \mathscr{R}\{\langle y, x\rangle\} \geq-1$ for $x \in S\}$. The polar of linear subspace $S$ is also called the linear space orthogonal to $S$.

Let $\mathscr{X}$ be a vector space with a locally convex topology $\mathscr{T}$. Let $\mathscr{X}^{*}$ be the dual of $\{\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{T}\}$. The topology $w\left\{\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{X}^{*}\right\}$ is often called the weakened topology of $\mathscr{X}$. The topology $w\left\{\mathscr{X}^{*}, \mathscr{X}\right\}$ is also called the vague or weak star topology of $\mathscr{X}^{*}$.

In a vector space $\mathscr{X}$ a set $S$ is called absorbing if for every $x \in \mathscr{X}$ there is a $\lambda_{0}>0$ such that $x \in \lambda S$ for every $\lambda$ such that $|\lambda|>\lambda_{0}$. The set $S$ is called balanced if $x \in S$ implies $\lambda x \in S$ for $|\lambda| \leqq 1$. In $\{\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{T}\}$, a set $B$ is called bounded if for every neighborhood $V$ of the origin there is a $\lambda$ such that $B \subset \lambda V$. A convex balanced absorbing closed set is called a barrel.

The strong topology $\beta\left\{\mathscr{X}^{*}, \mathscr{X}\right\}$ defined by $\mathscr{X}$ on $\mathscr{X}^{*}$ is the topology obtained by taking as neighborhoods of the origin of $\mathscr{X}^{*}$ the polars of the bounded sets of $\mathscr{X}$.

The $\gamma$-topology of $\mathscr{X}^{*}$, denoted $\gamma\left\{\mathscr{X}^{*}, \mathscr{X}\right\}$, is the topology of uniform convergence on the balanced convex compact subsets of $\mathscr{X}$. A sizable proportion of the theory of locally convex spaces can be derived from following basic theo-
rems.
(a) The Hahn-Banach Theorem. Let $\mathscr{X}$ be a real locally convex space, let $H$ be a linear affine subspace of $\mathscr{X}$, and let $C$ be a convex set whose interior $C^{0}$ is nonempty and does not intersect $H$. Then there is a closed hyperplane $P$ of $\mathscr{X}$ such that $H \subset P$ and $P \cap C^{0}=\varnothing$. Further, $C$ lies on one side of $H$.

We use the term hyperplane to mean a set of the form $P=\{x: \phi(x)=c\}$ for a linear functional $\phi$. That $P$ is closed means that $\phi$ is continuous.
(b) The Krein-Milman Theorem. Let $\mathscr{X}$ be a separated real locally convex space. Let $K$ be a compact subset of $\mathscr{X}$ whose closed convex hull $\widetilde{K}$ is also compact. Then every extreme point of $\widetilde{K}$ belongs to $K$, and $\widetilde{K}$ is the closed convex hull of its set of extreme points.
(An extreme point $x$ of $\tilde{K}$ is a point such that $x=\alpha x_{1}+(1-\alpha) x_{2}, \alpha \in(0,1)$, $x_{i} \in \widetilde{K}$ implies $x_{1}=x_{2}$.)
(c) The Bourbaki-Alaoglu Theorem. Let $\{\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{T}\}$ be locally convex with dual $\mathscr{X}^{*}$. A subset $S$ of $\mathscr{X}^{*}$ is equicontinuous if and only if $S$ is contained in the polar of a neighborhood of the origin of $\mathscr{X}$. The equicontinuous subsets of $\mathscr{X}^{*}$ are relatively compact in $\mathscr{X}^{*}$ for the weak topology $w\left\{\mathscr{X}^{*}, \mathscr{X}\right\}$.

Let $\mathscr{X}$ and $\mathscr{Y}$ be two vector spaces. Let $(x, y) \rightarrow\langle x, y\rangle$ be a bilinear form on $\mathscr{X} \times \mathscr{Y}$ which separates the points of $\mathscr{X}$ and the points of $\mathscr{Y}$. One says then that $\mathscr{X}$ and $\mathscr{Y}$ are in proper duality.
(d) Smulian's Theorem. A convex balanced subset $S$ of $\mathscr{Y}$ is $w\{\mathscr{Y}, \mathscr{X}\}$ relatively compact if and only if its polar $S^{0}$ in $\mathscr{X}$ absorbs $\mathscr{X}$ and is such that every linear functional which remains bounded on $S^{0}$ is an element of $\mathscr{Y}$.
(e) The Mackey-Arens Theorem. Let $\{\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{Y}\}$ be in proper duality. A locally convex topology $\mathscr{T}$ on $\mathscr{X}$ is such that $\{\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{T}\}$ has dual $\mathscr{Y}$ if and only if $\mathscr{T}$ is the topology of uniform convergence on a family of $w\{\mathscr{Y}, \mathscr{X}\}$ compact convex balanced subsets of $\mathscr{Y}$ which covers $\mathscr{Y}$.
(f) Mackey's Theorem. Let $\mathscr{X}$ and $\mathscr{Y}$ be in proper duality. Bounded sets in $\mathscr{X}$ are the same for every locally convex topology $\mathscr{T}$ for which the dual of $\{\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{T}\}$ is $\mathscr{Y}$.
(g) Let $\left\{\mathscr{X}_{1}, \mathscr{T}_{1}\right\}$ and $\left\{\mathscr{X}_{2}, \mathscr{T}_{2}\right\}$ be two separated locally convex spaces with respective duals $\mathscr{X}_{1}^{*}$ and $\mathscr{X}_{2}^{*}$. Let $T$ be a linear map from $\mathscr{X}_{1}$ to $\mathscr{X}_{2}$. If $T$ is $\left\{\mathscr{T}_{1}, \mathscr{T}_{2}\right\}$ continuous, then $T$ is also $\left\{w\left\{\mathscr{X}_{1}, \mathscr{X}_{1}^{*}\right\}, w\left\{\mathscr{X}_{2}, \mathscr{X}_{2}^{*}\right\}\right\}$ and $\left\{\tau\left\{\mathscr{X}_{1}, \mathscr{X}_{1}^{*}\right\}, \tau\left\{\mathscr{X}_{2}\right.\right.$, $\left.\left.\mathscr{X}_{2}^{*}\right\}\right\}$ continuous. Furthermore, its transpose is weakly and strongly continuous.

A separated locally convex space $\{\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{T}\}$ is called tonnelé if every barrel in $\mathscr{X}$ is a neighborhood of zero.
(h) Banach-Steinhaus' Principle of Uniform Boundedness. Let $\mathscr{X}$ and $\mathscr{Y}$ be two separated locally convex vector spaces. Let $\mathscr{L}$ be the space of all continuous linear maps from $\mathscr{X}$ to $\mathscr{Y}$ and let $\Sigma$ be a family of bounded subsets of $\mathscr{X}$. If $\mathscr{X}$ is tonnelé every pointwise bounded subset of $\mathscr{L}$ is equicontinuous and consequently bounded for the $\Sigma$-convergence.
(i) The Open-Mapping Theorem (Banach). Let $\mathscr{X}$ and $\mathscr{Y}$ be complete metrizable vector spaces. Let $T$ be a continuous linear map of $\mathscr{X}$ onto $\mathscr{Y}$. Then the image by $T$ of a neighborhood of zero in $\mathscr{X}$ is a neighborhood of zero in $\mathscr{Y}$.
(j) The Closed Graph Theorem (Banach). Let $\mathscr{X}$ and $\mathscr{Y}$ be complete metrizable vector spaces. A linear map $T$ from $\mathscr{X}$ into $\mathscr{Y}$ is continuous if and only if its graph is closed in $\mathscr{X} \times \mathscr{Y}$.
(k) Banach's Theorem. Let $\mathscr{X}$ be a separated complete locally convex vector space with dual $\mathscr{X}^{*}$. A linear form $\phi$ on $\mathscr{X}^{*}$ is $w\left\{\mathscr{X}^{*}, \mathscr{X}\right\}$ continuous if (and only if) its restrictions to equicontinuous subsets of $\mathscr{X}^{*}$ are continuous.
(l) Grothendieck's Theorem. Let $\mathscr{X}$ and $\mathscr{Y}$ be two vector spaces in proper duality. Let $\mathscr{F}$ be a cover of $\mathscr{X}$ by convex balanced bounded closed sets. The completion of $\mathscr{X}$ for the structure of $\mathscr{F}$-convergence is the space of linear functionals whose restrictions to the elements of $\mathscr{F}$ are continuous.

Among the classes of spaces of interest in the sequel let us mention the following.
(a) Fréchet spaces. A locally convex metrizable space is called a Fréchet space if it is complete. A locally convex separated complete space is a Fréchet space if and only if the neighborhoods of zero have a countable base.
(b) The combination $\{\mathscr{X}, \rho\}$ of a linear space and a norm $\rho$ for which $\mathscr{X}$ is complete is called a Banach space. A locally convex space $\mathscr{X}$ admits a Banach structure if and only if it is complete, separated, and such that there is a bounded neighborhood of the origin.
(c) A Banach space whose norm $\rho$ is Hilbertian is called a Hilbert space. For a space $\{\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{T}\}$ to admit a Hilbert structure it is necessary that it be complete, separated, and that there be a linear one-to-one continuous map $x \rightarrow x^{*}$ of $\mathscr{X}$ onto $\mathscr{X}^{*}$ with $\left\langle x^{*}, x\right\rangle>0$ for $x \neq 0$.

If a space $\mathscr{X}$ carries a Hilbertian seminorm $\rho$, it is customary to extend the use of the word projection to cover maps defined as follows. Let $H$ be a convex subset of $\mathscr{X}$ which is complete and separated for the structure induced by $\rho$. Then, for every $x \in \mathscr{X}$ there is a unique element $\pi x$ of $H$ such that

$$
\rho(x-\pi x)=\inf \{\rho(x-z) ; z \in H\} .
$$

The element $\pi x$ is called the orthogonal projection of $x$ on $H$. The uniqueness and existence of $\pi x$ follows easily from the equality

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho^{2}\left[\frac{1}{2}\left(z-z^{\prime}\right)\right] & =\rho^{2}\left[\frac{1}{2}(x-z)-\frac{1}{2}\left(x-z^{\prime}\right)\right] \\
& =\frac{1}{2}\left\{\rho^{2}(x-z)+\rho^{2}\left(x-z^{\prime}\right)\right\}-\rho^{2}\left[x-\frac{1}{2}\left(z+z^{\prime}\right)\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

When $H$ is a linear (affine) subspace of $\mathscr{X}$, the map $x \rightarrow \pi x$ is linear (affine). If $H$ is a linear subspace of $\mathscr{X}$, the map $x \rightarrow \pi x$ is a projection in the previously defined sense.

## 7. Vector Lattices

Let $\mathscr{E}$ be a set ordered by a relation written $\leqq$. A subset $S$ of $\mathscr{E}$ will be called bounded above (resp. below) in $\mathscr{E}$ if there is a $v \in \mathscr{E}$ such that $s \leqq v$ (resp.
$v \leqq s)$ for every $s \in S$. A set of the type $\{s ; s \in \mathscr{E}, u \leqq s \leqq v\}$ will be called an interval $[u, v]$ of $\mathscr{E}$. A map $f$ from an ordered set $\mathscr{E}$ to another ordered set $\mathscr{F}$ will be called "order bounded", or simply "bounded" if no confusion ensues if $f$ transforms bounded subsets of $\mathscr{E}$ into bounded subsets of $\mathscr{F}$.

Let $\mathscr{E}$ be a real vector space; an order relation is compatible with the vector structure of $\mathscr{E}$ if $x \leqq y$ implies $x+u \leqq y+u$ and $\lambda x \leqq \lambda y$ for $u \in \mathscr{E}$ and $\lambda \geqq 0$.

We recall that an ordered set $\mathscr{E}$ is called a lattice if every finite set of elements of $\mathscr{E}$ possesses both a supremum and an infimum. A lattice is called complete if all bounded subsets possess suprema and infima.

If $\mathscr{E}$ is a complete lattice, it is possible to define on $\mathscr{E}$ an order topology $\mathscr{T}_{0}(\mathscr{E})$ as follows. Let $\mathscr{F}$ be a filter on $\mathscr{E}$. Assume that $\mathscr{F}$ contains a bounded set. Let

$$
\begin{aligned}
\limsup \mathscr{F} & =\inf _{S \in \mathscr{F}} \sup S \\
\liminf \mathscr{F} & =\sup _{S \in \mathscr{F}} \inf S
\end{aligned}
$$

One says that $\mathscr{F}$ converges for the order structure of $\mathscr{E}$ if $\limsup \mathscr{F}= \liminf \mathscr{F}$. The common value of these two expressions is called the limit of $\mathscr{F}$. The topology $\mathscr{T}_{0}(\mathscr{E})$ is the finest topology such that, if $\mathscr{F}$ order converges to $x$, then $\mathscr{F}$ also converges topologically to $x$. A vector lattice is a real vector space with a lattice structure compatible with its vector structure.

In a vector lattice $\mathscr{E}$ the cone $P$ of positive elements is a decomposable semigroup according to the following properties.
(a) The addition is associative and commutative.
(b) There is in $P$ a zero element 0 such that $x+0=x$ and such that $x+y=0$ implies $x=y=0$ for $x$ and $y$ in $P$.
(c) If $u+v=x+y$, there are elements $w_{i}, i=1,2,3,4$ of $P$ such that $u= w_{1}+w_{2}, v=w_{3}+w_{4}, x=w_{1}+w_{3}, y=w_{2}+w_{4}$ : Any decomposable semigroup $P$ can be given an order structure by writing $u \leqq v$ if there is a $w \in P$ such that $v=u+w$.

Frederick Riesz's theory of linear operations applies directly to decomposable semigroups; however, to simplify the language we shall concern ourselves only with those decomposable semigroups which are positive cones of vector lattices.

Let $P$ be a decomposable semigroup. Call a numerical function $\phi$ distributive in $P$ if $\phi(x+y)=\phi(x)+\phi(y)$ for $x$ and $y$ in $P$. Let $P^{\prime}$ be the set of distributive maps from $P$ to $[0, \infty)$ with the pointwise addition operation.

Theorem 1 (F. Riesz). The space $P^{\prime}$ is also a decomposable semigroup. Furthermore, $P^{\prime}$ is a complete lattice and every bounded distributive numerical function on $P$ is the difference of two elements of $P^{\prime}$. If $P$ is the cone of positive elements of a vector lattice $\mathscr{E}$, every element of $P^{\prime}$ possesses a unique linear extension to $\mathscr{E}$.

The proof of this result can be found in Bourbaki, Integration or Schaefer, Banach Lattices. Let us mention only that the infimum $a$ of a set $S \subset P^{\prime}$ can be defined by

$$
a(x)=\inf \left\{\sum s_{j} x_{j} ; s_{j} \in S, \sum x_{j}=x, x_{j} \in P\right\}
$$

Let $E$ be a vector lattice. Denote $x \vee y$ (resp. $x \wedge y$ ) the supremum (resp. the infimum) of the two elements $x, y$. Furthermore, denote $x^{+}$the element $x^{+}=x \vee 0$ and $x^{-}$the element $x^{-}=(-x) \vee 0$. The element $|x|=x^{+}+x^{-}$will be called the absolute value of $x$. Two elements $x, y$ of $E$ will be called disjoint if $|x| \wedge|y|=0$.

Suppose that $E$ is a complete vector lattice. Let $S$ be a linear subspace of $E$. The space $S$ will be called a band of $E$ if $x \in S$ and $0 \leqq y \leqq|x|$ implies $y \in S$ and if $S$ contains the supremum in $E$ of its subsets which are bounded in $E$. The properties of bands issue from the following theorem.

Theorem 2 (F. Riesz). Let $E$ be a complete vector lattice. Let $S$ be a subset of the cone $E^{+}$of positive elements of $E$. Assume that $S+S \subset S$, that $0 \leqq y \leqq x, x \in S$ implies $y \in S$, and that $S$ contains the suprema of its bounded subsets. Then every positive element $x$ of $E$ can be written $x=y+z$ with $y \in S$ and $z \in E^{+}$disjoint from every element of $S$.

Theorem 3. Let $B$ be a band of a complete vector lattice $E$. Let $C$ be the set of elements of $E$ disjoint from all the elements of $B$. Then $C$ is also a band. Furthermore, there exist positive linear projections $p$ and $q$ on $B$ and $C$, respectively, such that $x=p x+q x$.

Let $S$ be an arbitrary subset of a complete vector lattice $E$. The positive cone of the band of $E$ generated by $S$ can be obtained as follows. Let $S_{1}$ be the set of $s \in E^{+}$such that $s \leqq \sum\left|s_{j}\right|$ for some finite subset $\left\{s_{j}\right\}$ of $S$. Let $S_{2}$ be the set of suprema in $E$ of bounded subsets of $S_{1}$. Then $S_{2}$ is the positive cone of the smallest band of $E$ containing $S$.

Returning to the situation where $E$ is an arbitrary vector lattice, the space $E^{*}$ of all bounded linear functionals on $E$ will be called the Riesz dual of $E$. Note that for $x \in E^{+}$and for $\phi$ and $\psi$ in $E^{*}$ the following hold:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\langle\phi \vee \psi, x\rangle & =\sup \left\{\langle\phi, u\rangle+\langle\psi, v\rangle ; u, v \in E^{+}, u+v=x\right\} \\
\langle\phi \wedge \psi, x\rangle & =\inf \left\{\langle\phi, u\rangle+\langle\psi, v\rangle ; u, v \in E^{+}, u+v=x\right\} \\
\left\langle\phi^{+}, x\right\rangle & =\sup \{\langle\phi, u\rangle ; 0 \leqq u \leqq x\} \\
\langle | \phi|, x\rangle & =\sup \{\langle\phi, u\rangle ;|u| \leqq x\}
\end{aligned}
$$

If $E$ is a complete vector lattice, for a positive linear functional $\phi$ to be $\mathscr{T}_{0}(E)$ continuous, it is necessary and sufficient that for every bounded and increasingly directed subset $S$ of $E$ the relation

$$
\langle\phi, \sup S\rangle=\sup \{\langle\phi, s\rangle ; s \in S\}
$$

holds. One can deduce from this that the linear functionals which are $\mathscr{T}_{0}(E)$ continuous are also bounded and form a band of $E^{*}$. For an arbitrary vector lattice $E$ the elements of $E$ are $\mathscr{T}_{0}\left(E^{*}\right)$-continuous on $E^{*}$.

Theorem 4 (Hahn-Riesz). Let $E$ be a complete vector lattice. Let $F$ be the space of $\mathscr{T}_{0}(E)$-continuous linear functionals on $E$. Let $f$ be an element of $F$.

Then $E$ is an ordered direct sum of three bands $E_{0}, E_{-}$, and $E_{+}$such that
(1) $x \in E_{0}$ and $0<y \leqq|x|$ implies $\langle f, y\rangle=0$,
(2) $x \in E_{+}$and $0<y \leqq|x|$ implies $\langle f, y\rangle>0$,
(3) $x \in E_{-}$and $0<y \leqq|x|$ implies $\langle f, y\rangle<0$.

In addition, if $f$ and $f^{\prime}$ belong to $F$, and $\left\{E_{0}, E_{-}, E_{+}\right\}$and $\left\{E_{0}^{\prime}, E_{-}^{\prime}, E_{+}^{\prime}\right\}$ are the corresponding decompositions of $E$, for $f$ and $f^{\prime}$ to be disjoint in $F$ it is necessary and sufficient that ( $E_{+}+E_{-}$) and ( $E_{+}^{\prime}+E_{-}^{\prime}$ ) have only the origin in common.

Proof. Let $E_{0}, E_{+}$, and $E_{-}$be defined, respectively, by the relations (1), (2), and (3) of the theorem. Clearly, any two of these sets have only the origin in common. In addition, if $E_{i}$ denotes any one of the three subsets, one has $E_{i}+E_{i} \subset E_{i}$ and $\sup S \in E_{i}$ for any bounded subset $S$ of $E_{i}$.

Let us prove this for $E_{+}$, for instance. If $x_{i}, i=1,2$ are two elements of $E_{+}$ and $0<y \leqq\left|x_{1}+x_{2}\right|$, then $0<y \leqq\left|x_{1}\right|+\left|x_{2}\right|$. Thus there are two elements $z_{1}$ and $z_{2}$ of $E$ such that $y=z_{1}+z_{2}$ and $0 \leqq z_{i} \leqq\left|x_{i}\right|$. This implies

$$
\langle f, y\rangle=\left\langle f, z_{1}\right\rangle+\left\langle f, z_{2}\right\rangle>0
$$

Therefore $x_{1}+x_{2} \in E_{+}$.
Let $S$ be a bounded subset of $E_{+}^{+}$. Assume $S$ directed increasingly. For every $y \in E^{+}$one can write

$$
\sup _{x}\langle x \wedge y ; x \in S\rangle=[\sup S] \wedge y
$$

Therefore, since $f$ is $\mathscr{T}_{0}(E)$-continuous,

$$
\sup \{\langle f, x \wedge y\rangle ; x \in S\}=\langle f, y \wedge(\sup S)\rangle
$$

Assume $0<y \leqq \sup S$. Then

$$
\langle f, y\rangle=\sup \{\langle f, x \wedge y\rangle ; x \in S\}>0
$$

because there is at least one $x \in S$ such that $0<x \wedge y \leqq x$.
Note that if $E_{1}$ and $E_{2}$ are two distinct elements of the triple $\left\{E_{0}, E_{+}, E_{-}\right\}$, then every element of $E_{1}$ is disjoint from every element of $E_{2}$. Indeed, if $x \in E_{1}^{+}$ and $y \in E_{2}^{+}$, then $x \wedge y \in E_{1}^{+} \cap E_{2}^{+}$.

To show that $E$ is the direct sum of $E_{0}, E_{+}$, and $E_{-}$, it remains to show that the set $B$ of elements of $E$ disjoint from all the elements of $E_{0}+E_{+}+E_{-}$ consists only of the origin of $E$. Assume, to the contrary, that $x \in B^{+}$is different from zero. Then $\left\langle f^{+}, x\right\rangle>0$ and $\left\langle f^{-}, x\right\rangle>0$. Otherwise $x$ would belong to $E_{0}+E_{+}+E_{-}$.

Since $f^{+}$and $f^{-}$are disjoint, for every $\varepsilon>0$ there is a pair ( $u_{1}, v_{1}$ ) of elements of $B^{+}$such that $u_{1}+v_{1}=x$ and $\left\langle f^{+}, u_{1}\right\rangle+\left\langle f^{-}, v_{1}\right\rangle<\varepsilon$. Similarly, there are two elements $u_{2}, v_{2}$ of $B^{+}$such that $v_{1}=u_{2}+v_{2}$ and $\left\langle f^{+}, u_{2}\right\rangle+\left\langle f^{-}, v_{2}\right\rangle<\varepsilon^{2}$. By induction, we can define a sequence $\left\{u_{n}\right\}$ and a sequence $\left\{v_{n}\right\}$ of elements of $B^{+}$such that $v_{n}=u_{n+1}+v_{n+1}$ and $\left\langle f^{+}, u_{n+1}\right\rangle+\left\langle f^{-}, v_{n+1}\right\rangle<\varepsilon^{n+1}$. The sequence $\left\{v_{n}\right\}$ decreases for $\mathscr{T}_{0}(E)$ to a limit $v$. Since $\left\langle f^{-}, v_{n}\right\rangle \leqq \varepsilon^{n}$, one
has $\left\langle f^{-}, v\right\rangle=0$. But $v \in B^{+}$and $\left\langle f^{-}, v\right\rangle=0$ implies $v=0$. Therefore, $w_{n}=u_{1}+u_{2}+\cdots+u_{n}$ converges to $x$ as $n$ increases.

It follows that $\left\langle f^{+}, x\right\rangle=\lim \left\langle f^{+}, w_{n}\right\rangle<\varepsilon(1+\varepsilon)$. Hence, $\left\langle f^{+}, x\right\rangle=0$, and consequently, $x=0$. This completes the proof of the first part of the theorem. For the second part let $f$ and $g$ be two disjoint elements of $F^{+}$and let $h=f-g$. Let $\left\{E_{+}, E_{0}, E_{-}\right\}$and $\left\{E_{+}^{\prime}, E_{0}^{\prime}, E_{-}^{\prime}\right\}$ be the decompositions of $E$ corresponding to $f$ and $g$. Note that $E_{-}=E_{-}^{\prime}=\{0\}$. Let $B=E_{+} \cap E_{+}^{\prime}$. Decompose $B$ in two bands $C_{+}$and $C_{-}\left(C_{0}=\{0\}\right)$ for $h$. If $x \in B^{+}$, then $x=u+v$ with $u \in C_{+}^{+}$and $v \in C_{-}^{+}$. Hence,

$$
\langle | h|, x\rangle=\langle | f-g|, x\rangle=\langle f-g, u\rangle-\langle f-g, v\rangle .
$$

The two elements $f$ and $g$ are disjoint if and only if $\langle | f-g|, x\rangle=\langle f+g, x\rangle$ for every $x \in E^{+}$. But then

$$
\langle f-g, u\rangle-\langle f-g, v\rangle=\langle f+g, u\rangle+\langle f+g, v\rangle ;
$$

that is,

$$
\langle f, v\rangle+\langle g, u\rangle=0
$$

This is impossible unless $u=v=x=0$.
Conversely, assume that $f$ and $f^{\prime}$ are elements of $F^{+}$such that $B=E_{+} \cap E_{+}^{\prime}= \{0\}$. Every $x \in E$ can be written $x=u+v+w$ with $u \in E_{+}, v \in E_{+}^{\prime}$ and $w$ disjoint from the elements of $E_{+}+E_{+}^{\prime}$.

It follows that

$$
\langle f, v+w\rangle+\left\langle f^{\prime}, u\right\rangle=0
$$

Therefore $f$ and $f^{\prime}$ are disjoint. This completes the proof of the theorem. Let $E$ be a vector lattice and let $S$ be a subset of $E$. We shall say that $S$ is a proper sublattice of $E$ or that $S$ and $E$ are coreticulated if for every pair ( $u, v$ ) of elements $S$ the supremum $u \vee v$ and the infimum $u \wedge v$ in $E$ are also elements of $S$.

Consider now two vector lattices $E$ and $F$ and a bilinear function $(x, y) \rightarrow \langle x, y\rangle$ defined on $E \times F$. We shall say that such a bilinear form places $E$ and $F$ in coreticulated duality if the following conditions are satisfied.
(a) $x \in E^{+}$and $y \in F^{+}$implies $\langle x, y\rangle \geqq 0$.
(b) If $\langle x, y\rangle=0$ for every $x \in E$, then $y=0$; if $\langle x, y\rangle=0$ for every $y \in F$, then $x=0$.
(c) For $x \in E^{+}$and $y \in F$ one has $\left\langle x, y^{+}\right\rangle=\sup \{\langle u, y\rangle ; 0 \leqq u \leqq x\}$; for $x \in E$ and $y \in F^{+}$one has $\left\langle x^{+}, y\right\rangle=\sup \{\langle x, v\rangle ; 0 \leqq v \leqq y\}$.

Equivalently, one can say that $E$ is identified with a proper sublattice of the Riesz dual $F^{*}$, and $F$ is identified with a proper sublattice of the Riesz dual $E^{*}$.

We shall say that $E$ and $F$ are in complete coreticulated duality if in addition for every bounded increasingly directed subset $S$ of $E$ (resp. $T$ of $F$ ) there is an element $u \in E$ (resp. $v \in F$ ) such that

$$
\langle u, y\rangle=\sup \{\langle x, y\rangle ; x \in S\} \quad \text { for every } y \in F^{+}
$$

(resp. $\langle x, v\rangle=\sup \{\langle x, y\rangle ; y \in T\}$ for every $x \in E^{+}$). Equivalently one can say that $E$ and $F$ are complete lattices and that the elements of $E$ (resp. $F$ ) are $\mathscr{T}_{0}(F)$ (resp. $\mathscr{T}_{0}(E)$ )-continuous.

Theorem 5. Let $E$ and $F$ be two vector lattices in complete coreticulated duality. Let $f$ be a linear functional on $E$. There is a $y \in F$ such that $f(x)= \langle x, y\rangle$ for every $x \in E$ if and only if there are two elements $u, v$ of $F$ such that

$$
\langle x, u\rangle \leqq f(x) \leqq\langle x, v\rangle
$$

for every $x \in E^{+}$.
Proof. Define two subsets of $F$ by the relations

$$
\begin{aligned}
& S=\left\{y \in F ;\langle x, y\rangle \leqq f(x) \text { for } x \in E^{+}\right\} \\
& T=\left\{y \in F ;\langle x, y\rangle \geqq f(x) \text { for } x \in E^{+}\right\}
\end{aligned}
$$

The set $S$ is increasingly directed and possesses a supremum $s \in S$. Similarly, $T$ is decreasingly directed and possesses an infimum $t \in T$.

Taking differences, one sees that it is enough to consider the case where

$$
0=\sup \{u ; u \leq f\} \leq f \leq t=\inf \{v ; v \geq f\}
$$

If $0=f$ or $f=t$ the result is proved. Otherwise consider $g=f-\frac{1}{2} t$. It gives a Hahn decomposition $\left\{E_{+}, E_{0}, E_{-}\right\}$of $E$ where $E_{+}$contains some nonzero positive element $a$ whose band $A$ in $E$ is contained in $E_{+}$. Let $\Pi$ be the projection of $E$ on $A$ and let $\phi(x)=\left\langle\Pi x, \frac{1}{2} t\right\rangle$. Then $\phi$ vanishes on $(I-\Pi) E$ and $(f-\phi)=g$ on $A$. Thus $f-\phi \geq 0$. By the assumption $0=\sup \{u ; u \leq f\}$, this implies $\phi=0$. Therefore $t$ vanishes on $A$ contrary to the definition of $E_{+}$.

Corollary. Let $E$ and $F$ be two vector lattices placed in coreticulated duality by a bilinear form $\langle x, y\rangle$. If $E$ and $F$ are in complete coreticulated duality, then every interval $J=\{y ; u \leqq y \leqq v\}$ of $F$ is $w(F, E)$-compact. Conversely, if every interval of $F$ is $w(F, E)$-compact and every interval of $E$ is $w(E, F)$-compact then $E$ and $F$ are in complete coreticulated duality.

Proof. The direct part of the proposition is an immediate consequence of the preceding theorem. Conversely, let $T$ be an increasingly directed bounded part of $F$. One can assume $T$ contained in an interval $J=[u, v]$ of $F$. If $J$ is $w(F, E)$ compact, the direction defined by $T$ converges for $w(F, E)$ to an element $y$ of $J$. For every $x \in E^{+}$and every $\varepsilon>0$ there is a $z \in T$ such that $z^{\prime}>z$ implies $\left|\left\langle x, z^{\prime}\right\rangle-\langle x, y\rangle\right|<\varepsilon$. Therefore $\langle x, y\rangle=\sup \{\langle x, t\rangle, t \in T\}$. It follows that $F$ is a complete lattice and that the elements of $E$ are $\mathscr{T}_{0}(F)$ continuous.

The preceding results involve order topologies which are sometimes awkward to manipulate. For certain purposes it is more convenient to use weaker topologies denoted by symbols such as $\omega(E, F)$ below.

Theorem 6 (Dieudonné). Let $E$ be a vector lattice and let $F$ be a band of the Riesz dual $E^{*}$ of $E$. Assume that $E$ and $F$ are in coreticulated duality. Let $\omega(E, F)$ be the structure of uniform convergence on the intervals of $F$. Let $G$ be
the space of $\mathscr{T}_{0}(F)$-continuous linear functionals on $F$. Then $G$ is identical to the $\omega(E, F)$ completion of $E$. The lattice operations in $G$ are $\omega(G, F)$-continuous. Further, if $J$ is an interval of $E$ and $\hat{J}$ is the corresponding interval of $G$, then $J$ is $\omega(G, F)$ dense in $\hat{J}$, and $\hat{J}$ can be identified to the completion of $J$.

Proof. According to Grothendieck's theorem, the $\omega(E, F)$ completion of $E$ consists exactly of those linear functionals whose restrictions to the intervals of $F$ are $w(F, E)$-continuous. Such a linear functional is also $\mathscr{T}_{0}(F)$-continuous. Conversely, any element of the completion of $E$ is a limit of elements of $E$. Since the elements of $E$ are $\mathscr{T}_{0}(F)$-continuous and since the limits are taken uniformly over intervals of $F$, the elements of the completion are also $\mathscr{T}_{0}(F)$ continuous.

Let $\mathscr{F}$ be a filter on $G$. If $\mathscr{F}$ converges to $x_{0}$ for $\omega(G, F)$, the image of $\mathscr{F}$ by the map $x \mapsto|x|$ converges to $\left|x_{0}\right|$ because $\left|x-x_{0}\right|$ converges to zero along $\mathscr{F}$ for the topology $\omega(G, F)$. Thus the map $x \rightarrow|x|$ is continuous. It follows that the map $(x, y) \mapsto x \wedge y=\frac{1}{2}(x+y)-\frac{1}{2}|x-y|$ is also continuous. This proves the first part of the theorem. The rest follows by noting that if $\left\{x_{v}\right\}$ is a filter converging to an $x$ such that $|x| \leqq a$, then $\left(x_{\nu} \wedge a\right) \vee(-a)$ converges to $x$.

Another result relative to the density of $J$ in $\widehat{J}$ can be obtained directly without using Grothendieck's theorem in the case where $E$ possesses a unit element. It is Nakano's theorem given in the next section.

Because of the importance of this case in statistical decision theory, we shall devote the next section to an elaboration of the structure of certain vector lattices with units. We shall also need the following result which occurs in the theory of integration due to Darboux.

Let $E$ be a partially ordered vector space; let $H$ be a linear subspace of $E$ with the induced order relation; and let $F$ be the set of elements $x \in E$ such that $u \leqq x \leqq v$ for some couple $(u, v)$ of elements of $H$. Let $\phi$ be a positive linear functional defined on $H$. For every $x \in F$ let

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \bar{\phi}(x)=\inf \{\phi(v) ; v \in H, v \geqq x\} \\
& \underline{\phi}(x)=\sup \{\phi(u) ; u \in H, u \leqq x\}
\end{aligned}
$$

Lemma. Assume that $x \notin H$ and let $\alpha$ be an arbitrary real number. Let $H_{1}$ be the linear space generated by $H$ and $x$. For every $y=u+\lambda x$ with $u \in H$ and $\lambda$ real let $\phi^{*}(y)=\phi(u)+\lambda \alpha$. The function so defined is a positive linear functional defined on $H_{1}$ if and only if $\underline{\phi}(x) \leqq \alpha \leqq \bar{\phi}(x)$.

The proof can be carried out by direct verification.
Let $\mathscr{D}=\mathscr{D}\{H, E, \phi\}$ be the set of $x \in F$ such that $\phi(x)=\bar{\phi}(x)$.
This will be called the Eudoxus hull of $H$ in $E$ for the functional $\phi$. If $\phi^{*}$ is any positive linear extension of $\phi$ to a space containing $x \in \mathscr{D}$, then $\phi^{*}(x)$ must be equal to $\underline{\phi}(x)=\bar{\phi}(x)$. However,

$$
\begin{aligned}
\phi(x+y) & =\inf \{\phi(v) ; v \in H, v \geqq x+y\} \\
& =\inf \{\phi(u+v) ; u, v \in H, u \geqq x, v \geqq y\} \\
& =\bar{\phi}(x)+\bar{\phi}(y)
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence, on $\mathscr{D}$ the function $\phi^{*}=\phi=\bar{\phi}$ is distributive, and $\mathscr{D}$ is a group. In
addition, $\bar{\phi}(\lambda x)=\lambda \bar{\phi}(x)$ for $\lambda>0$ and $\bar{\phi}(\lambda x)=\lambda \phi(x)$ for $\lambda<0$. Therefore, $\mathscr{D}$ is a linear subspace of $F \subset E$ and $\phi^{*}$ is linear positive on $\mathscr{D}$. We shall call the extension of $\phi$ so obtained the Eudoxus extension of $\phi$.

From this result it follows easily that $\phi$ always possesses a positive linear extension to the whole of $F$ (theorem of M. Riesz). Indeed, the family of subspaces of $F$ to which such as extension is possible has a maximal element, according to Zorn's lemma. This maximal element cannot be other than $F$ itself.

## 8. Vector Lattices Arising from Experiments

The purpose of this section is to describe certain mathematical structures which can be used as a schematization of the intuitive concept of experiment. From a mathematical point of view the structures described here are simply $L$ and $M$ spaces, and the results given below are due to Gelfand, Kakutani, and Stone. We also include a form of the Radon-Nikodym theorem taken from Dieudonné.

Statistical decision theory makes constant use of a basic mathematical structure which represents a single stage experiment. This is usually taken to be a family of probability measures on a particular $\sigma$-field of subsets of a certain set. Instead of this we shall find it convenient to represent an experiment by a vector lattice $E$ with a unit $I$ and a family $\left\{P_{\theta}: \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ of positive linear functionals on $E$.

Intuitively each $\theta$ represents a particular physical theory relative to the real world. The elements of $E$ are the bounded observable random variables. For $x \in E$ the value $x P_{\theta}$ of $P_{\theta}$ at $x$ is the expectation of the random variable $x$ for the theory $\theta$.

Let $L$ be the smallest band of the Riesz dual $E^{*}$ of $E$ which contains all the $P_{\theta}$ and let $M$ be the space of order bounded linear functionals on $L$. Each $x \in E$ defines a linear functional on the vector lattice $L$.

Definition 1. A single stage experiment consists of a set $\Theta$, a vector lattice $E$, and a family $\left\{P_{\theta}: \theta \in \Theta\right\}$ of positive linear functionals on $E$. It is assumed that $E$ possesses a unit $I$ and that the value $I P_{\theta}$ of $P_{\theta}$ at $I$ is unity.

Let $E^{*}$ be the Riesz dual of $E$. Let $L$ be the smallest band of $E^{*}$ which contains all the functionals $P_{\theta}$, and let $M$ be the Riesz dual of $L$. Each $x \in E$ defines a linear functional $\dot{x}$ on $L$ by the relation $\dot{x} \lambda=x \lambda$. This functional is an element of $M$. Two distinct elements of $E$ may correspond to the same linear functional on $L$. Let $H$ be the set of elements $x \in E$ such that $x \lambda=0$ for every $\lambda \in L$, and let $\stackrel{\circ}{E}=E / H$ be the quotient space obtained by identifying to zero the elements of $H$ and calling a class $\dot{x}$ positive if there is an $x \geqq 0$ such that $x \in \dot{x}$.

Lemma 1. The imbedding $x \mapsto \dot{x}$ of $E$ into $M$ preserves the lattice operations. The image of $E$ by this map is isomorphic to the quotient space $\stackrel{\circ}{E}=E / H$.

Proof. Let $u$ and $v$ be two disjoint positive elements of $E$ and let $h$ be a positive element of $L$. For $x \in E, x \geqq 0$, let $x f$ be the value $x f=\inf y h$, where the infimum is taken over the set of positive elements $y$ of $E$ which are smaller



than $x$ and such that $x-y$ and $u$ are disjoint in $E$. This definition implies the equality $\left(x_{1}+x_{2}\right) f=x_{1} f+x_{2} f$ for $x_{i} \geqq 0, x_{i} \in E$. Since the map $x \mapsto x f$ so defined is also positively homogeneous on $E^{+}$, it can be extended to a positive linear functional on $E$. Clearly, $0 \leqq f \leqq h$. Also $0 \leqq x \leqq u$ implies $x f=x h$ and $x \wedge u=0$ in $E$ implies $x f=0$. Thus, one can write $h=f+g$ with $f \geqq 0$, $g \geqq 0$, and $u g=v f=0$. In other words, if $u$ and $v$ are disjoint in $E$, the linear functionals they define on $L$ are also disjoint in $M$. It follows that $\dot{x} \wedge \dot{y}$ is the class of $x \wedge y$ and that the lattice operations are preserved.

For the other statements of the lemma note that if $x \lambda=0$ for every $\lambda \in L^{+}$, then $|x| \lambda=0$ for every $\lambda \in L$. Indeed, one can write $x=x^{+}-x^{-}$and, if $\lambda \in L^{+}$, find for each $\varepsilon>0$ a pair ( $\mu, v$ ) of elements of $L^{+}$such that $\lambda=\mu+v$ and $x^{+} \mu+x^{-} v \leqq \varepsilon$. Since by assumption $x \mu=x v=0$, this gives

$$
|x| \lambda=x^{+} \mu+x^{+} v+x^{-} \mu+x^{-} v=2\left[x^{+} \mu+x^{-} v\right] \leqq 2 \varepsilon
$$

It follows that $x \in H$ implies $|x| \in H$ and that $0 \leqq y \leqq x \in H$ implies $y \in H$. If $x \in E$ is such that $\dot{x}$ is a positive element of $M$, the negative part $x^{-}$of $x$ must be an element of $H$, since $\left(x^{+}\right)^{\circ}$ and $\left(x^{-}\right)^{\circ}$ are disjoint. It follows that $x$ and $x^{+}$are equivalent. Thus $\dot{x} \geqq 0$ if and only if there is a positive element belonging to the class $\dot{x}$. This completes the proof of the lemma.

According to this lemma, the spaces $\stackrel{\circ}{E}$ and $L$ are in coreticulated duality. The space $\stackrel{\circ}{E}$ possesses a unit element $\stackrel{\circ}{I}$ and $L$ is a band in the Riesz dual of $\stackrel{\circ}{E}$. In view of this we shall study below a structure $\{F, L, M\}$ composed of a vector lattice $F$ with unit $I$, of a band $L$ in the Riesz dual of $F$ and of the Riesz dual $M$ of $L$. Such a structure will be called a canonical triple if $F$ and $L$ are in coreticulated duality.

Let $\{F, L, M\}$ be a canonical triple. The topology $\omega(F, L)$ is the topology induced by the family of seminorms of the form $x \mapsto|x| \mu$ for $\mu \in L^{+}$. The topology $\omega(L, F)$ is induced by the single norm

$$
\|\mu\|=\sup \{|x \mu| ; x \in F,|x| \leqq I\}
$$

This norm has the property that, for $\mu$ and $v$ elements of $L^{+},\|\mu+v\|= \|\mu\|+\|v\|$.

Since $L$ is a band of $F^{*}$, it is complete for the norm; hence, $\{L,\|\cdot\|\}$ is a Banach space. Subsets of $L$ which are increasingly directed and bounded converge for the norm topology to their supremum in $L$. Indeed, if $\mu \in L^{+}$, then $\|\mu\|=I \mu$. Thus if $\mu$ increases to $v$, then $\|\mu\|=I \mu$ increases to $\|v\|=I v$ and $\|v-\mu\|=\|v\|-\|\mu\|$ tends to zero.

A linear functional defined on $L$ and continuous for the norm is also order bounded. Conversely, every order bounded linear functional on $L$ is automatically continuous for the norm, hence $\mathscr{T}_{0}(L)$-continuous.

It is obviously sufficient to prove this for positive functionals. Let $y$ be a positive functional on $L$. If $y$ is not continuous for the norm there is an $\varepsilon>0$ such that for every integer $n$ there is a $\mu_{n} \in L$ such that $\left\|\mu_{n}\right\| \leqq 2^{-n}$ and $y \mu_{n}>\varepsilon$. This implies $y \mu_{n}^{+}>\varepsilon$. Thus one can assume $\mu_{n}=\mu_{n}^{+}$. Let then $v_{n}=\sum_{j=1}^{n} \mu_{j}$.

The sequence $\left\{v_{n}\right\}$ increases to a limit $v$ such that $\|v\| \leqq 1$. Since $v \geqq v_{n}$ one must have $y v \geqq y v_{n} \geqq n \varepsilon$ for all values of $n$. This is impossible.

Consider now the space $M$ of all order bounded linear functionals on $L$. We have just proved that $M$ is the dual of the Banach space $L$ and that all the elements of $M$ are $\mathscr{T}_{0}(L)$-continuous. In this duality the space $M$ acquires a norm defined by

$$
\|x\|=\sup \{|x \mu| ;\|\mu\| \leqq 1\}=\inf \{\alpha ;|x| \leqq \alpha I\}
$$

The norm of $M$ possesses the property that if $x \geqq 0$ and $y \geqq 0$, then $\|x \vee y\|=\max \{\|x\|,\|y\|\}$.

Let $B=\{x: x \in F,|x| \leqq I\}$ be the unit ball of $F$. The unit ball $\bar{B}=\{x: x \in M,|x| \leqq I\}$ of $M$ is obtainable by taking the polar of $B$ in $L$ and then the polar of this in $M$, Thus $B$ is weakly ( $w(M, L)$ ) dense in $\bar{B}$; hence, $B$ is $\omega(M, L)$ dense in $\bar{B}$. The following theorem gives a more precise result.

Theorem 1 (Nakano). Let $\{F, L, M\}$ be a canonical triple of lattices. Explicitly, $F$ has a unit element $I, L$ is a full subspace of the Riesz dual $F^{*}$, and $M$ is the Riesz dual of $L$; further, $F$ and $L$ are in coreticulated duality. Let $B=\{x: x \in F ;|x| \leqq I\}$ be the unit ball of $F$ and let $\bar{B}=\{x: x \in M ;|x| \leqq I\}$ be the unit ball of $M$, If $F$ is identified to a subspace of $M$, then $B$ is $\mathscr{T}_{0}(M)$ dense in $\bar{B}$. Similarly, the positive part $B^{+}$of $B$ is $\mathscr{T}_{0}(M)$ dense in the positive part $\bar{B}^{+}$of $\bar{B}$.

Proof. The map $x \rightarrow \frac{1}{2}(x+I)$ is a one-to-one bicontinuous map of $B$ onto $B^{+}$and of $\bar{B}$ onto $\bar{B}^{+}$. Thus it is sufficient to show that $B$ is dense in $\bar{B}$. Let $C$ be the closure of $B$ in $M$ for $\mathscr{T}_{0}(M)$ and let $H=\bigcup_{n}(n C)$ be the linear space spanned by $C$. We shall show that $H$ and $M$ are identical.

Note first that since the map $x \mapsto x^{+}$of $M$ into itself is $\mathscr{T}_{0}(M)$-continuous, $x \in C$ implies $x^{+} \in C$. It follows that $H$ is coreticulated to $M$. Consider now a bounded increasingly directed subset $S$ of $H$ with supremum $s=\sup S$ in $M$. Let us show that $s \in H$. For this purpose one may assume that $S \subset \bar{B}$. Each $x \in S$ has the form $x=n y$ for some $n$ and some $y \in C$. If $\mathscr{F}$ is a filter on $B$ converging to $y \in C$, the image of $\mathscr{F}$ by the map $z \mapsto(n z \wedge I) \vee(-I)$ converges to $(n y \wedge I) \vee(-I)=x$. Thus $S \subset \bar{B} \cap H$ implies $S \subset C$; hence, $s=\sup S \in C \subset H$.

It follows from this that $H$ is completely coreticulated to $M$ so that the elements of $L$ are $\mathscr{T}_{0}(H)$-continuous. Thus $L$ and $H$ are in complete coreticulated duality. Finally, every element of $M$ is bounded by some multiple of $I$. Since $I \in H$, the corollary of the Hahn-Riesz theorem implies that $H=M$.

It has been shown above that $\bar{B} \cap H \subset C$; since $H=M$ this entails $\bar{B}=C$ as claimed.

One can interpret this result as follows: the ball $\bar{B}$ is the completion by Dedekind cuts of the ball $B$. To go further we shall show that the lattice structure of $M$ can be used to define a bilinear commutative multiplication on $M$. For this purpose we shall need the following lemma.

Lemma 2. For any element $x$ of $\bar{B}^{+}$the following properties are equivalent: (1) $x$ is an extreme point of $\bar{B}^{+}$,
(2) $x \wedge(I-x)=0$,
(3) $x$ is the component of $I$ on a band of $M$.

Proof. Let $y=I-x$ and let $u=x \wedge y$. Since $x+u \leqq x+y=1$, both $x-u$ and $x+u$ are elements of $\bar{B}^{+}$and $x=\frac{1}{2}[(x+u)+(x-u)]$. Thus if $x$ is extreme, $u=0$. Conversely, suppose that for some $v \in M$ both $x-v$ and $x+v$ are in $\bar{B}^{+}$. Then both $y-v$ and $y+v$ are elements of $\bar{B}^{+}$. In particular, $x \geqq v$ and $y \geqq v$ so that $v \leqq x \wedge y$. Also $x \geqq(-v)$ and $y \geqq(-v)$ so that $(-v) \leqq x \wedge y$. It follows that $|v| \leqq x \wedge y$. Thus if $x \wedge y=0$, both $x$ and $y$ must be extreme.

If $v \in \bar{B}^{+}$, the component of $I$ on the band generated by $v$ is the element $\bar{v}$ given by the formula

$$
\bar{v}=\sup _{n>0}(n v \wedge I)
$$

It follows that $v \leqq \bar{v} \leqq I$ and that $\bar{v} \wedge(I-\bar{v})=0$. Conversely, if $x \in \bar{B}^{+}$is such that $x \wedge(I-x)=0$, then the component of $I$ on the band generated by $x$ is $x$ itself since $I=x+(I-x)$. This completes the proof of the lemma.

Definition 2. An element $x \in M$ such that $0 \leqq x \leqq I$ and $x \wedge(I-x)=0$ will be called an indicator.

It follows easily from the preceding lemma that if $u$ and $v$ are two indicators such that $u \leqq v$, then $v-u$ is an indicator disjoint from $u$.

Lemma 3. Let $\lambda_{1}$ and $\lambda_{2}$ be two numbers such that $0<\lambda_{1}<\lambda_{2} \leqq 1$. Let $x$ be an element of $\bar{B}^{+}$and let $u_{i}$ be the component of $I$ on the band generated by $\left(x-\lambda_{i} I\right)^{+}$. Then

$$
u_{2} \leqq \frac{1}{\lambda_{2}-\lambda_{1}}\left[\left(x \wedge \lambda_{2} I\right)-\left(x \wedge \lambda_{1} I\right)\right] \leqq u_{1}
$$

Proof. Let $M_{0}$ and $M_{2}$, respectively, be the subspaces generated by $I$ -$\left(x-\lambda_{1} I\right)^{+}$and $\left(x-\lambda_{2} I\right)^{+}$and let $M_{1}$ be the band generated by the part of $\left(x-\lambda_{1} I\right)^{+}$disjoint from $M_{2}$. Let $y=\left(x-\lambda_{1} I\right)^{+}$and $z=\left(x-\lambda_{2} I\right)^{+}$and let $y_{i}$ (resp. $z_{i}$ ) be the projection of $y$ (resp. z) on $M_{i}$. Similarly, let $I_{i}$ be the projection of $I$ on $M_{i}$ so that $u_{1}=I_{1}+I_{2}$. Clearly $y=y_{1}+y_{2}$ and $z=z_{2}$. The component of $\left(x-\lambda_{2} I\right)^{-}$on $M_{2}$ vanishes; hence, $z_{2}$ is also equal to the component of $x-\lambda_{2} I$ on $M_{2}$. However,

$$
\left(x-\lambda_{2} I\right)=\left(x-\lambda_{1} I\right)-\left(\lambda_{2}-\lambda_{1}\right) I=-\left(x-\lambda_{1} I\right)^{-}+y-\left(\lambda_{2}-\lambda_{1}\right) I .
$$

Thus $z_{2}=y_{2}-\left(\lambda_{2}-\lambda_{1}\right) I_{2}$. It follows that

$$
\frac{1}{\lambda_{2}-\lambda_{1}}\left[\left(x-\lambda_{1} I\right)^{+}-\left(x-\lambda_{2} I\right)^{+}\right]=\frac{1}{\lambda_{2}-\lambda_{1}} y_{1}+I_{2} .
$$

Since $u_{2}=I_{2}$ and $(x-\lambda I)^{+}=x-(x \wedge \lambda I)$, the first part of the inequality follows. The second part follows, for instance, from the inequality $a \wedge(b+c) \leqq (a \wedge b)+(a \wedge c)$ valid for any three positive elements of $M$.

Proposition 1. Let $x$ be an element of $\bar{B}^{+}$and let $\varepsilon>0$ be a positive number. There exist indicators $u_{1}, u_{2}, \ldots, u_{n}$ and real numbers $\alpha_{j} ; j=1,2, \ldots, n$ such that $\alpha_{j} \geqq 0$ and $\sum \alpha_{j}=1$ and

$$
\sum_{j} \alpha_{j} u_{j} \leqq x \leqq \varepsilon I+\sum_{j} \alpha_{j} u_{j} .
$$

Proof. Let $\lambda_{j} ; j=0,1,2, \ldots, n$ be a finite sequence of numbers such that $0=\lambda_{0}<\lambda_{1}<\cdots<\lambda_{j+1}<\cdots<\lambda_{n}=1$. For $j=1,2, \ldots, n-1$ let

$$
w_{j}=\frac{1}{\lambda_{j+1}-\lambda_{j}}\left[\left(x \wedge \lambda_{j+1} I\right)-\left(x \wedge \lambda_{j} I\right)\right]
$$

Let $u_{j}$ be the component of $I$ on the subspace generated by $\left(x-\lambda_{j} I\right)^{+}= x-\left(x \wedge \lambda_{j} I\right)$. Clearly, $u_{0} \geqq u_{1} \geqq \cdots \geqq u_{j} \geqq u_{j+1} \geqq \cdots \geqq u_{n}=0$. According to the preceding lemma,

$$
u_{j+1} \leqq w_{j} \leqq u_{j}
$$

This implies that $x=\sum_{j=0}^{n-1}\left(\lambda_{j+1}-\lambda_{j}\right) w_{j}$ satisfies the inequalities

$$
\sum_{j=0}^{n-1}\left(\lambda_{j+1}-\lambda_{j}\right) u_{j+1} \leqq x \leqq \sum_{j=0}^{n-1}\left(\lambda_{j+1}-\lambda_{j}\right) u_{j}
$$

The difference of the right-hand and left-hand sides of these inequalities is

$$
\sum_{j=0}^{n-1}\left(\lambda_{j+1}-\lambda_{j}\right)\left(u_{j}-u_{j+1}\right)
$$

Since ( $u_{j}-u_{j+1}$ ) is disjoint from $u_{j+1}$, this sum is also equal to

$$
\sup _{0 \leqq j<n}\left(\lambda_{j+1}-\lambda_{j}\right)\left(u_{j}-u_{j+1}\right) \leqq\left[\sup _{j}\left(\lambda_{j+1}-\lambda_{j}\right)\right] I .
$$

Therefore the inequality of the proposition will be satisfied, provided that $\lambda_{j+1}-\lambda_{j}<\varepsilon$ for every $j$. Hence the result.

Theorem 2. Let $F$ be a vector lattice with unit element I. Let $L$ be a band of the Riesz dual of $F$. Assume that $F$ and $L$ are in coreticulated duality and let $M$ be the Riesz dual of $L$.

There is one and only one bilinear map $(u, v) \mapsto u v$ of $M$ into itself such that
(1) $I u=u I=u$,
(2) $u^{+} v^{+} \geqq 0$.

The multiplication so defined is commutative.
Note. This theorem shows that the lattice structure of $M$ determines the multiplicative structure of $M$ except for the choice of the identity element $I$.

Proof. As usual, it is convenient to write $x^{2}$ instead of $x x$. Suppose that $x$ is an indicator $x \in \bar{B}^{+}$. Then $x-x^{2}=x(I-x)=(I-x) x$. The positivity requirement (2) implies $x^{2} \geqq 0$ and $x-x^{2} \geqq 0$; hence, $0 \leqq x-x^{2} \leqq x$. Also $0 \leqq x-x^{2} \leqq I-x$. Therefore $x=x^{2}$. In other words, the indicators are necessarily idempotents. Suppose that $x$ and $y$ are two such indicators. Then
$0 \leqq x y \leqq(x I) \wedge(I y)=x \wedge y$. One verifies easily that $x \wedge y$ is also an indicator. Since $x-(x \wedge y)$ is then an indicator disjoint from $x \wedge y$, this implies that

$$
x y=\{(x \wedge y)+[x-(x \wedge y)]\}\{(x \wedge y)+[y-(x \wedge y)]\}=x \wedge y
$$

The same positivity requirement implies that for any two elements $u$ and $v$ of $M$ the inequality $\|u v\| \leqq\|u\|\|v\|$ holds. Further, since finite sums $\sum_{j} \alpha_{j} u_{j}$ (where the $u_{j}$ 's are indicators), are dense in $M$, the bilinear function in question is well determined by its values for indicators. Thus the bilinear function is precisely the one determined by the relation $(x, y) \mapsto x \wedge y$ for $x$ and $y$ indicators in $\bar{B}^{+}$. The proof of the proposition is now easily completed.

The indicators introduced above can be used directly to construct a compact Hausdorff space on which the elements of $M$ are representable by bounded continuous functions. This can be done without any recourse to the multiplication operation defined above. It is sufficient to call "points" families $t$ of indicators such that (1) $0 \notin t$, (2) if $u_{i} \in t$ for $i=1$, 2 then $u_{1} \wedge u_{2} \in t$, (3) if $u$ is an indicator, either $u \in t$ or $I-u \in t$.

Instead of using this procedure we shall obtain the same representation through properties of the multiplication introduced above. The following theorem can also be used for other purposes.

Theorem 3. Let $A$ be a commutative real algebra with an identity I. Suppose that $A$ is partially ordered in such a way that $u \geqq 0$ and $v \geqq 0$ implies $u v \geqq 0$. Furthermore, assume that $A$ is linearly spanned by the set $\{u: u \in A, 0 \leqq u \leqq I\}$. Let $S$ be the set of linear functionals $\phi$ defined on $A$ such that $I \phi=1$ and $u \phi \geqq 0$ for $u \geqq 0$.

An element $\phi$ of $S$ is an extreme point of $S$ if and only if it is a multiplicative functional, that is $\phi$ is extreme if and only if

$$
(x y) \phi=(x \phi)(y \phi)
$$

for every pair ( $x, y$ ) of elements of $A$.
Proof. If $\phi \in S$ and $x \in M$, then $(x-\lambda I)^{2} \geqq 0$ and $0 \leqq(x-\lambda I)^{2} \phi$ for every real $\lambda$. Therefore

$$
x^{2} \phi \geqq(x \phi)^{2}
$$

for every $x \in A$.
Suppose that $\phi=\frac{1}{2}(\psi+\omega)$ with $\psi$ and $\omega$ elements of $S$. Then

$$
x^{2} \phi-(x \phi)^{2}=\frac{1}{2}\left[x^{2} \psi-(x \psi)^{2}\right]+\frac{1}{2}\left[x^{2} \omega-(x \omega)^{2}\right]+\frac{1}{4}[x(\psi-\omega)]^{2} .
$$

If $\phi$ is multiplicative, this implies that $x(\psi-\omega)=0$ for every $x \in A$. Thus $\phi$ must be extreme.

Conversely, suppose that $\phi \in S$ and that $0 \leqq u \leqq I$. Let $v=I-u$, and define $\psi$ and $\omega$ by

$$
\begin{aligned}
x \psi & =[1-(v \phi)][(u x) \phi]+[1+(u \phi)][(v x) \phi], \\
x \omega & =[1+(v \phi)][(u x) \phi]+[1-(u \phi)][(v x) \phi] .
\end{aligned}
$$

It is easily checked that both $\psi$ and $\omega$ belong to $S$ and that $2 \phi=\psi+\omega$. For $\psi$ and $\omega$ to be equal it is necessary that

$$
(v \phi)[(u x) \phi]=(u \phi)[(v x) \phi]
$$

or equivalently,

$$
(u x) \phi=(u \phi)(x \phi),
$$

for every $x \in M$. If $\phi$ is extreme, this must be true for every $u$ such that $0 \leqq u \leqq I$ hence, by linear extension for every $u \in A$. This concludes the proof of the theorem.

Suppose now that $A$ is a vector lattice which is also a commutative algebra and that the identity $I$ for multiplication is also a strong unit for the lattice $A$. Then $A$ is automatically generated by the positive part $\{u: 0 \leqq u \leqq I\}$ of its unit ball. The set $S$ is a $w\left(A^{*}, A\right)$ compact subset of the dual $A^{*}$ of $A$. Since $w\left(A^{*}, A\right)$ limits of multiplicative functionals are also multiplicative the set $Z$ of extreme points of $S$ is a $w\left(A^{*}, A\right)$ compact subset of $A^{*}$.

Let $C=C(Z)$ be the space of continuous numerical functions on $Z$. To each $u \in A$ corresponds an element $u^{\prime}$ of $C$ by the usual evaluation map: $u^{\prime}(z)=u z$. It is clear that the map $u \mapsto u^{\prime}$ is positive, linear, and multiplicative. In addition, the map $u \mapsto u^{\prime}$ is injective. Indeed, $u^{\prime}(z) \equiv 0$ is equivalent to $u z=0$ for every $z \in Z$ hence, by the Krein-Milman theorem to $u s=0$ for every $s \in S$. Since $S$ generates $A^{*}$ linearly, this implies $u=0$.

Therefore, if $A$ is a lattically ordered algebra with an identity $I$ which is a strong unit, the map $u \mapsto u^{\prime}$ of $A$ into $C(Z)$ is an isomorphism of the algebraic structure of $A$ onto a subalgebra of $C(Z)$.

In this case every element $\mu$ of $A^{*}$ having norm $\|\mu\| \leqq 1$ can be written $\mu=\mu^{+}-\mu^{-}$with $\mu^{+} \in S$ and $\mu^{-} \in S$ and $\left\|\mu^{+}\right\|+\left\|\mu^{-}\right\|=\|\mu\| \leqq 1$. Thus for every $u \in A$ one can also write $\|u\|=\sup \{|u \mu| ; u \in S\}$; this implies (by KreinMilman again)

$$
\|u\|=\sup \{|u z| ; z \in Z\}
$$

It follows that the map $u \mapsto u^{\prime}$ is also norm preserving. Thus if $A$ is complete for the norm, so is its image in $C(Z)$. By the Stone-Weierstrass theorem this implies that the image of $A$ is the whole of $C(Z)$.

If $u$ and $v$ are positive elements of $A$ such that $u^{\prime} \wedge v^{\prime}=0$ in the lattice $C(Z)$, then $u \wedge v=0$ in $A$. Conversely, if $u \wedge v=0$ in $A$, then $u^{\prime} \wedge v^{\prime}=0$, at least if $A$ is complete. As a summary one can state the following.

Theorem 4. Let $A$ be a real lattically ordered commutative algebra with an identity $I$ which is also a strong unit for the lattice structure. Suppose that $A$ is complete for the norm induced by $I$. Let $Z$ be the set of positive multiplicative linear functionals $z$ defined on $A$ and such that $I z=1$. Then $Z$ is a compact

Hausdorff space for the $w(Z, A)$ topology and the $u \mapsto u^{\prime}$ defined by $u^{\prime}(z)=u z$ is an isomorphism of $A$ onto $C(Z)$ for the lattice, algebraic, and Banach space structures of $A$.

This theorem applies in particular to the space $M$ defined by an experiment. In this case the compact space $Z$ is a Stonian space which can be used as the "set of all possible results" of the experiment.

In the conventional treatment of statistical problems the vector lattice $E$ is itself a vector lattice of numerical functions on a certain set. The lattice and algebraic operations are performed point by point. According to the preceding theorem, the whole space $M$ can be represented in this fashion by the space of continuous functions on a certain compact space $Z$. One may also want to use other representations of $M$ by algebra or lattices of functions on other sets. In this connection one may mention the following results.

If $X$ is an arbitrary set, the space $\mathscr{B}(X)$ of bounded numerical functions on $X$ is always a Banach space, a vector lattice, and a real commutative algebra with an identity for the pointwise operations.

An isomorphism of $M$ into $\mathscr{B}(X)$ for the vector lattice structure is also an isomorphism for the algebraic properties; conversely, an isomorphism of the lattice structure is an algebraic isomorphism. To see this it is sufficient to note that in $M$ and in $\mathscr{B}(X)$ the relations $u \geqq 0$ and $u=v^{2}$ are equivalent and that two elements $u, v$ have a product $u v$ equal to zero if and only if they are disjoint.

Proposition 2. Let $s \mapsto s^{\prime}$ be a one-to-one positive linear map of $M$ onto a linear subspace $Q$ of the space $\mathscr{B}(X)$ of bounded numerical functions on a set $X$. For
$\mu \in L$ define $\mu^{\prime}$ by the equation $s^{\prime} \mu^{\prime}=s \mu$. Assume $Q^{+}$is the image of $M^{+}$. Then $\mu^{\prime}$ is a linear function on $Q$ which is $\tau$-smooth in the sense that for every decreasingly directed family $\left\{s_{v}^{\prime}\right\}$ of elements of $Q$ which converges pointwise to zero on $X$ the values $s_{\nu}^{\prime} \mu^{\prime}$ tend to zero.

Proof. That $\mu^{\prime}$ is well defined and linear is obvious. For the other statement one can assume that $\mu \in L^{+}$. If $s^{\prime} \leqq t^{\prime}$ are two elements of $Q$, then $t^{\prime}-s^{\prime} \in Q$ and $t^{\prime}-s^{\prime} \geqq 0$ in $Q$ implies that $t-s \geqq 0$ in $M$; otherwise, $\left[(t-s)^{-}\right]^{\prime}$ would be zero. Consequently, if $s_{i}^{\prime}, i=1,2,3$ are three elements of the family $\left\{s_{v}^{\prime}\right\}$ such that $s_{3}^{\prime} \leqq s_{1}^{\prime} \leqq s_{2}^{\prime}$, then $s_{3} \leqq s_{1} \leqq s_{2}$. Let $u=\inf _{v} s_{v}$. Since $\mu$ is positive on $M$, the values $s_{v}^{\prime} \mu^{\prime}=s_{v} \mu$ decrease to the limit $u \mu$. To $u$ corresponds $u^{\prime}$ which must be smaller than all the $s_{v}^{\prime}$ and hence equal to zero. This implies $u=0$, hence the result.

The preceding proposition assumes only that the map $s \mapsto s^{\prime}$ is a positive linear map injecting $M$ into $\mathscr{B}(X)$. Suppose now that the map is actually an isomorphism of the algebraic (or vector lattice) structure of $M$, and that the space $Q$ separates the points of $X$. The dual map sends the dual of $Q$ normed into the dual of $M$ normed and provides an injection of $X$ into $Z$. It will be shown in the next sections that the image of $X$ in $Z$ by such a map is a set $A$ which must be dense in $Z$ and such that if $\mu$ is an arbitrary element of $L^{+}$and $K$ an arbitrary compact subset of $Z \cap A^{c}$, then the Radon measure $\mu(K)$ of $K$ for $\mu$ must be zero. Conversely, if $A$ is a dense subset of $Z$, the map which to each $u \in C(Z)$ associates its restriction $u$ to $A$ provides an isomorphism of
the algebraic structure of $M$ identified to $C(Z)$. It follows that for every dense subset $A$ of $Z$ the compacts $K \subset A^{c}$ must be negligible for every $\mu \in L^{+}$.

There is a particular form of isomorphism of $M$ spaces with spaces of function that has special importance. It is the isomorphism provided by the lifting theorem of D. Maharam and A. and C. Ionescu Tulcea. To state it, let $\{\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{A}, P\}$ be a probability space. Let $\mathscr{M}$ be the space of all bounded $P$ integrable numerical functions on $\mathscr{X}$. Let $M$ be the corresponding space of equivalence classes.

Theorem 5. There exists a positive linear map $s \mapsto \phi(s)$ from $M$ to $\mathscr{M}$ such that $\phi(s) \in s$ and such that $\phi$ is an isomorphism of the lattice, algebraic, and Banach structure of $M$.

Proof. Call a subset $S \subset \mathscr{M}$ a selection set if it has the following properties:
(i) it is a vector space and an algebra for pointwise operations;
(ii) it contains the constants;
(iii) the unit ball $\{s: s \in S ;|s| \leq I\}$ is complete for the norm $\|s\|=\langle | s|, P\rangle$;
(iv) if $s \in S$ is equivalent to zero, it is identically zero.

The space of constants is a selection set. We shall prove that every selection set is contained in a maximal selection set. To do this, let $\left\{S_{\alpha} ; \alpha \in A\right\}$ be a well ordered family of selection sets. Let $S=\bigcup_{\alpha} S_{\alpha}$. If $A$ has noncountable cofinality, $S$ is also a selection set. Indeed, properties (i), (ii), and (iv) are obviously preserved and (iii) holds since any Cauchy sequence in $S$ is already contained in some $S_{\alpha}$.

If $A$ has countable cofinality, one may write $S=\bigcup_{n} S_{n}, n=1,2, \ldots$. Let $\bar{S}$ be the algebra of equivalence classes of $S$. Let $\bar{T}$ be the closure of $\bar{S}$ in $M$ for the norm $\|s\|=\langle | s|, P\rangle$. If $u \in \bar{T}$, let $E_{n} u \in S_{n}$ be a conditional expectation of $u$ given $S_{n}$. Note that $E_{n} u$ is uniquely defined. The martingale convergence theorem shows that $E_{n} u$ converges almost surely to a limit that belongs to the class $u$. Take an ultrafilter $\mathscr{U}$ finer than the filter of tails in the integers $\{1,2, \ldots\}$. Along $\mathscr{U}$ the $E_{n} u$ converge pointwise to a limit $\hat{u}$. From the martingale argument $\hat{u}$ belongs to $u$. The map $u \mapsto \hat{u}$ is a positive linear map of $T$ into the algebra $\mathscr{B}$ of bounded numerical functions on $\mathscr{X}$. (It is positive because $u^{+}$is a square, $u^{+}=v^{2}$ and $E_{n} v^{2} \geq\left(E_{n} v\right)^{2}$.) Also, the image $\hat{J}$ of the identity $J$ of $M$ is the function $I$, identically equal to unity. Finally if $u \in \bar{T}$ and $v \in \bar{S}$, then $(u v)^{\wedge}=\hat{u} \hat{v}$. This shows that the map $u \mapsto \hat{u}$ is very close to being a lifting of $\bar{T}$. However, it may not be a lifting. To obtain such a map consider the space $\mathscr{P}_{1}$ of all positive linear maps of $\bar{T}$ into $\mathscr{B}$ such that $\phi(J)=I$. It is easily seen that an element of $\mathscr{P}_{1}$ is extreme if and only if it is multiplicative.

Now for every idempotent $v \in \bar{T}$, let $v_{0}=\lim _{n}(\hat{v})^{n}$ and $v_{1}=\lim _{n}(\hat{v})^{1 / n}$. Then $v_{0}$ and $v_{1}$ are idempotent and $v_{0} \leq \hat{v} \leq v_{1}$. Let $\mathscr{P}_{2}$ be the subset of $\mathscr{P}_{1}$ formed by those maps $\phi$ such that if $v=v^{2} \in T$ then $v_{0} \leq \phi(v) \leq v_{1}$.

One can check that if $\phi \in \mathscr{P}_{2}$ then (i) $\phi(u)=\hat{u}$ for $u \in \bar{S}$ and (ii) if $\phi$ is extreme in $\mathscr{P}_{2}$ it is extreme in $\mathscr{P}_{1}$. To prove the first assertion suppose that $u \in \bar{S}$ is idempotent. Then $u_{0}=u_{1}=\hat{u}$. Thus $\phi(u)=\hat{u}$ and the equality extends to finite linear combinations of idempotents and, by uniform continuity, to all of $\bar{S}$. Also, if $\phi=\alpha \phi_{1}+(1-\alpha) \phi_{2}$ with $\phi_{i} \in \mathscr{P}_{1}$ and $\alpha \in[0,1]$, then for any idempotent $v \in \bar{T}$ one has

$$
0=v_{0}[I-\phi(v)]=\alpha v_{0}\left[I-\phi_{1}(v)\right]+(1-\alpha) v_{0}\left[I-\phi_{2}(v)\right] .
$$

Thus if $\alpha(1-\alpha) \neq 0$ one must have $v_{0}\left[I-\phi_{1}(v)\right]=v_{0}\left[I-\phi_{2}(v)\right]=0$, that is, $\phi_{i}(v) \geq v_{0}$. Similarly $\phi_{i}(v) \leq v_{1}$. Therefore $\phi$ could not be extreme in $\mathscr{P}_{2}$ if it was not in $\mathscr{P}_{1}$.

Now $\mathscr{P}_{2}$ is compact for the topology of pointwise convergence on $\bar{T} \times \mathscr{X}$. Thus $\mathscr{P}_{2}$ has at least one extreme point. Thus, there is a representation $\phi$ of the algebra $\bar{T}$ such that $v_{0} \leq \phi(v) \leq v_{1}$ for $v=v^{2} \in \bar{T}$. This gives a lifting of $\bar{T}$ and a selection set $\phi(\bar{T}) \supset S$.

The preceding argument shows that, according to Zorn's lemma, any selection set is contained in a maximal selection set. Now let $S$ be a maximal selection set. Let $w$ be an idempotent of $M$ that does not belong to $\bar{S}$. Since $\bar{S}$ has a complete unit ball, it is a complete lattice. Thus there is a largest idempotent $u \in \bar{S}$ such that $u \leq w$ and a smallest idempotent $v \in \bar{S}$ such that $w \leq v$. Let $s=v-u$. This is an idempotent. So is $s w$ and this $s w$ does not belong to $\bar{S}$. Let $y_{1}=u, y_{2}=J-v, y_{3}=s w$, and $y_{4}=v-s w$. Let $G$ be the subset of $M$ formed by combinations of the type $\sum s_{j} y_{j}$ with $s_{j} \in \bar{S}$. This is an algebra. In a sum $\sum s_{j} y_{j}$ one can assume that $s_{i} y_{i}=s_{i}$ for $i=1,2$ and $s_{i}\left(y_{3}+y_{4}\right)=s_{i}$ for $i=3$, 4. In such a case the coefficients $s_{i}$ are uniquely determined. It is easily verifiable that the unit ball of $G$ is complete for the $P$ norm.

Now let $\phi$ be the representation of $\bar{S}$ onto $S$ defined by $\phi(s) \in S$. Let $\hat{y}_{3}$ and $\hat{y}_{4}$ be two idempotent elements of $\mathscr{B}$ such that $\hat{y}_{3}+\hat{y}_{4}=I-\phi\left(y_{1}+y_{2}\right)$. If $x \in G$ has the form $x=\sum s_{i} y_{i}$, with the $s_{i}$ uniquely chosen as above, let $\hat{x}=\phi\left(s_{1} y_{1}\right)+\phi\left(s_{2} y_{2}\right)+\phi\left(s_{3}\right) \hat{y}_{3}+\phi\left(s_{4}\right) \hat{y}_{4}$. The map so defined is positive, linear, and multiplicative. It coincides with the map $\phi$ on $\bar{S}$. Furthermore if $\langle | x|, P\rangle=0$, then $\langle | s_{i} y_{i}|, P\rangle=0$ for $i=1, \ldots, 4$. Hence $\langle | s_{i}|, P\rangle=0$ and $\phi\left(s_{i}\right)=0$ so that $\hat{x}=0$.

This shows that one can extend $\phi$ to a lifting of $G$ and contradicts the maximality of $S$. One concludes that $S=M$, thus yielding the assertion of the theorem.

Theorem 5 admits extensions to certain other $M$-spaces. Consider for instance a set $\mathscr{X}$ with a $\sigma$-field $\mathscr{A}$ and an $L$-space $L$ of finite signed measures on $\mathscr{A}$. Such a system is called $\Sigma$-finite if there exists a partition $\left\{A_{\beta} ; \beta \in B\right\}$ (of arbitrary cardinality) by elements $A_{\beta} \in \mathscr{A}$ such that
(i) a bounded function $f$ is $\mathscr{A}$-measurable if and only if every $f I_{A_{\beta}}$ is measurable;
(ii) for such a bounded function $\langle f, \mu\rangle=\sum\left\langle f I_{A_{\beta}}, \mu\right\rangle$ for every $\mu \in L$;
(iii) the restrictions of the $\mu \in L$ to any fixed $A_{\beta}$ are dominated by a finite measure.

Let $\mathscr{M}$ be the space of bounded $\mathscr{A}$-measurable functions and let $M_{0}$ be the space of their equivalence classes considered as a subspace of the dual $M$ of $L$. It is easily seen that if $\{\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{A}, L\}$ is $\Sigma$-finite then $M_{0}=M$ and $M$ admits a lifting to $\mathscr{M}$.

Conversely if there is a lifting of $M$ to $\mathscr{M}$ then $\{\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{A}, L\}$ must be $\Sigma$-finite as
can be seen by representing $L$ as a sum of dominated bands. An intermediate condition is the equality $M_{0}=M$, which means that the unit ball $B$ of $M_{0}$ satisfies any one of the following equivalent conditions:
(a) $B$ is $w\left(M_{0}, L\right)$ compact,
(b) $B$ is complete for the structure of uniform convergence on the intervals of $L$,
(c) $B$ is a complete lattice.

It has been shown by Fremlin that the equality $M_{0}=M$ does not imply $\Sigma$ finiteness. However, there is a theorem of J. M. G. Fell which says the following: Assume that $L$ is a band generated by a family $\left\{\mu_{\alpha} ; \alpha \in[0,1]\right\}$. Assume also that $M_{0}=M$. Then the system is $\Sigma$-finite. To show this, one may assume that the $\mu_{\alpha}$ are probability measures and that they are disjoint. Then there are idempotents $J_{\alpha} \in M$ such that $\left\langle J_{\alpha}, \mu_{\alpha}\right\rangle=1$ and $\left\langle J_{\alpha}, \mu_{\beta}\right\rangle=0$ if $\alpha \neq \beta$. Let $\phi=\sum_{\alpha} \alpha J_{\alpha}$. This is an element of $M$.

Since $M_{0}=M$, there is some bounded measurable function $f$ such that the class of $f$ is $\phi$. One may assume $0 \leq f \leq 1$. Then take $A_{\alpha}=\{x: f(x)=\alpha\}$. By construction $I_{A_{\alpha}} \in J_{\alpha}$ for each $\alpha$ and the $\Sigma$-finiteness properties can easily be checked.

Remark. The lifting of Theorem 5 selects for each $s \in M$ an element $\hat{s} \in s$ that is bounded and integrable, but not necessarily measurable with respect to the original $\sigma$-field $\mathscr{A}$. If $\mathscr{X}$ is a Polish space with its $\sigma$-field of Borel sets, one can obtain a Borel lifting. However, it is very easy to obtain a lifting of $M$ to the space of Borel functions on a Borel subset $\mathscr{X}_{0} \subset \mathscr{X}$ such that $P\left(\mathscr{X} \backslash \mathscr{X}_{0}\right)=0$. One just "lifts" a countable rational linear subspace of $M$ dense in $M$ for the norm $\|u\|=\langle | u|, P\rangle$.

To terminate this section, let us mention a very important theorem of Dunford, Pettis, and Grothendieck. We have seen, above, that in the duality between $L$ and $M$ spaces the intervals are weakly compact. The Dunford-PettisGrothendieck theorem says that the $w(L, M)$-compact subsets of $L$ are "nearly" contained in intervals. The proof is a bit long. We shall start with some results that can be used for other purposes as well. The first is a lemma that can be used in the proof of the Vitali-Hahn-Saks theorem or in the proof of A.D. Alexandrov's theorem on limits of sequences of $\sigma$-smooth functionals.

Lemma 3. Let $\mathscr{X}$ be a set. Let $H$ be a vector lattice of bounded functions defined on $\mathscr{X}$. Assume that $I \in H$ and that $H$ is an algebra for the pointwise operations. Let $S$ be a set of positive linear functionals defined on $H$. Assume that $\mu \in S$ implies $\|\mu\| \leq 1$. Let $B$ be the unit ball $B=\{f: f \in H,|f| \leq I\}$. For each $\xi>0$, let $W(\xi)=\{f: f \in B,\langle\mu| f,| \rangle<\xi$ for all $\mu \in S\}$.

Let $C$ be a convex symmetric subset of $H$ and let $\delta$ be a number, $0<\delta<1$. If for some $f \in B$ the set $[f+W(4 \delta)] \cap B$ is contained in $C$, then $W\left(\delta^{2}\right) \subset 2 C$.

Proof. Let $\alpha$ be a number $\alpha \in(0,1)$ and let $h$ be an arbitrary element of $B$. Define a function $g$ by

$$
g=\frac{1}{\alpha}\{[2 \alpha \wedge(\alpha \vee|h|)]-\alpha\}
$$

This is an element of $B$ and $g(x)=0$ if $|h(x)| \leq \alpha$. Also, $g(x)=1$ if



$|h(x)| \geq 2 \alpha$. Define four functions $u_{i} ; i=1, \ldots, 4$ as follows:

$$
\begin{aligned}
& u_{1}=(1-g) f+g h \\
& u_{2}=(1-g) f-g h \\
& u_{3}=(1-2 \delta) f+(I-g) h \\
& u_{4}=(1-2 \delta) f-(I-g) h
\end{aligned}
$$

Then $\frac{1}{2}\left(u_{1}-u_{2}\right)=g h$ and $\frac{1}{2}\left(u_{3}-u_{4}\right)=(I-g) h$ so that $\frac{1}{2}\left(u_{1}-u_{2}\right)+ \frac{1}{2}\left(u_{3}-\right.$
$\left.u_{4}\right)=h$. Also,

$$
\left|u_{i}\right|=|(1-g) f \pm g h| \leq(1-g)|f|+g|h| \leq I \quad \text { for } i=1,2 .
$$

Similarly $\left|u_{i}\right| \leq(1-2 \delta)|f|+(I-g)|h| \leq I$ for $i=3$, 4 . Thus, if $u_{i} \in f+W(4 \delta) \subset C$ for all $i$, one will have $h \in 2 C$.

Now for $i=1,2$, one has $u_{i}-f=g(h \pm f)$. This implies $\langle | u_{i}-f|, \mu\rangle \leq \langle g|(h \pm f)|, \mu\rangle \leq 2\langle g, \mu\rangle$. However, $\langle\alpha g, \mu\rangle \leq\langle g| h|, \mu\rangle \leq\langle | h|, \mu\rangle$. Thus if $\alpha$ is taken equal to $\delta$, the inclusion $h \in W\left(\delta^{2}\right)$ implies $\langle | u_{i}-f|, \mu\rangle \leq 2 \delta$ for all $\mu \in S$. Therefore $u_{i} \in f+W(2 \delta)$ for $i=1,2$. For $i=3,4$, one can write $u_{i}-f=2 \delta f \pm(I-g) h$. Since $|(I-g) h| \leq 2 \delta$, this gives $\left|u_{i}-f\right| \leq 4 \delta$ and the conclusion follows.

To apply this, let $\mathscr{X}, H$ be as above. Let $L$ be a band in the Riesz dual of $H$ and let $\mathscr{S}$ be an increasingly directed family of norm bounded subsets of $L$. For each $S \in \mathscr{S}$, let $S^{*}=\{\lambda ; \lambda \in L,|\lambda| \leq|\mu|$ for some $\mu \in S\}$ be the convex symmetric solid hull of $S$. On $H$, let $\mathscr{T}(\mathscr{S})$ be the topology of uniform convergence on the sets $S^{*}, S \in \mathscr{S}$.

Proposition 3. Assume that for the topology $\mathscr{T}(\mathscr{S})$ the unit ball $B$ of $H$ is a Baire space.
