Let $\mathscr{F}$ be the space of linear functionals whose restrictions to $B$ are $\mathscr{T}(\mathscr{S})$-continuous. Let $\mathscr{V}$ be a filter on $\mathscr{F}$. Assume that $\mathscr{V}$ is Cauchy for $w(\mathscr{F}, H)$ and that it has a countable base. Then
(i) $\mathscr{V}$ converges for $w(\mathscr{F}, H)$ to a $\mu \in \mathscr{F}$,
(ii) for each $\varepsilon>0$, there is an $S \in \mathscr{S}$, a $\delta>0$, and a $V \in \mathscr{V}$ such that $\phi \in$ Vimplies $|\phi(f)|<\varepsilon$ for every $f \in B$ such that $\sup \left\{\langle f, \mu\rangle ; \mu \in S^{*}\right\}<\delta$.

Proof. For $\gamma \in B$ and $V \in \mathscr{V}$, let $\rho_{V}(\gamma)=\sup \{|\phi(\gamma)-\psi(\gamma)| ;(\phi, \psi) \in V \times V\}$. The function $\rho_{V}$ is lower semicontinuous for the $\mathscr{T}(\mathscr{S})$ topology. Let $C_{V}=\left\{\gamma ; \gamma \in B, \rho_{V}(\gamma) \leq(\varepsilon / 6)\right\}$. Each $C_{V}$ is a closed convex symmetric set. Since $\mathscr{V}$ is Cauchy, $\cup\left\{C_{V} ; V \in \mathscr{V}\right\}=B$. Thus, by the Baire property, one of the $C_{V}$ must have an interior point. That is, there is an $f \in B$, a $\delta>0$, a $V \in \mathscr{V}$, and an $S \in \mathscr{S}$ such that the relations $\gamma \in B$ and $|\langle\gamma-f, \mu\rangle|<\delta$ for all $\mu \in S^{*}$ imply $\rho_{V}(\gamma)<\varepsilon / 6$. If $b$ is a bound for the norms of the elements of $S$, an application of Lemma 3 above shows that $\gamma \in B$ and $|\langle\gamma, \mu\rangle|<\delta^{2} b^{-2}$ for all $\mu \in S^{*}$ will then imply $\rho_{\boldsymbol{V}}(\gamma) \leq(2 / 3) \varepsilon$. In addition, given $\psi \in V$ there is a $\delta_{1}<\delta^{2} b^{-2}$ and an $S_{1} \supset S$ such that if $\gamma \in B$ and $|\langle\gamma, \mu\rangle|<\delta_{1}$ for all $\mu \in S_{1}^{*}$ then $|\psi(\gamma)|<\varepsilon / 3$. This implies $|\phi(\gamma)|<\varepsilon$ for all $\gamma \in B$ such that $|\langle\gamma, \mu\rangle|<\delta_{1}$ for all $\mu \in S_{1}$. Statement (ii) follows. To prove (i) it is sufficient to note that, since $\mathscr{V}$ is Cauchy, the limit $\omega(f)=\lim _{\mathscr{V}} \phi(f)$ exists for all $f \in H$.

For a suitable $V \in \mathscr{V}$ and a suitable neighborhood $U$ of zero for $\mathscr{T}(\mathscr{S})$, one will have $|(\phi-\psi)(\gamma)| \leq 2 \varepsilon / 3$ for all $\gamma \in U$ and $(\phi, \psi) \in V \times V$. Therefore $|(\omega-\phi)(\gamma)| \leq 2 \varepsilon / 3$ for $\phi \in V$ and $\gamma \in U$. The continuity of $\omega$ follows.

For the next result, recall that a subset $S$ of a topological space $F$ is called countably relatively compact in $F$ if every countable infinite subset $D$ of $S$ has at least one accumulation point in $F$. That is, there is an $x \in F$ such that every neighborhood of $x$ contains an infinite subset of $D$. The set $S$ is called sequentially relatively compact if every sequence $\left\{x_{n}\right\}$ in $S$ contains a subsequence that converges to an element of $F$. The set $S$ is relatively compact in $F$ if its closure is compact.

Proposition 4 (Smulian). Let $F$ be a Banach space with dual $G$. Let $S \subset F$ be countably relatively compact for $w(F, G)$. Then $S$ is also sequentially relatively compact in $F$ for $w(F, G)$.

Proof. Let $D$ be an infinite countable subset of $S$ and let $D_{0}$ be the union of $D$ with one of the accumulation points $x_{0} \in F$. It is enough to work in the closed linear subspace spanned by $D_{0}$. This is a separable Banach space. Thus, we may as well assume that $F$ is separable. Now if $S$ is countably relatively compact and $y \in G$, the set $\{\langle y, x\rangle ; x \in S\}$ must be bounded. Therefore by Mackey's theorem the set $S$ is strongly bounded and so is $S \bigcup\left\{x_{0}\right\}$. Therefore, on $D_{0}$, the $w(F, G)$ topology coincides with the topology of pointwise convergence on a $w(G, F)$ dense subset of the unit ball of $G$. Since $F$ is separable, the unit ball contains a countable dense set inducing on $D_{0}$ a metric topology. The result follows.

There is a companion result due to F . Eberlein that says that the countably $w(F, G)$ relatively compact subsets of $F$ are also relatively compact. This can be shown either by using the theorem on interchange of limits (see Section 5 of this Appendix) or by showing that if $S$ is $w(F, G)$ countably relatively compact, each element of the closure of $S$ is already in the closure of a countable subset of $S$. We shall use the result later, but will not give its proof.

Consider now an arbitrary vector lattice $E$ with a unit $I$. Let $L$ be an arbitrary band in the Riesz dual of $E$. Let $M$ be the Riesz dual of $L$. If $L$ does not separate the points of $E$, one could replace it by the quotient space ( $E / L^{0} )$. Thus we shall assume that $L$ separates $E$. Let $\omega(M, L)$ be the topology of uniform convergence on the intervals of $L$.

Theorem 6 (Dunford, Pettis, Grothendieck). Let $S$ be a subset of $L$. The following conditions are all equivalent:
(1) $S$ is relatively $w(L, M)$ countably compact in $L$.
(2) $S$ is relatively sequentially compact in $L$.
(3) If $\left\{u_{n}\right\},\left|u_{n}\right| \leq I$ is a sequence in $M$ such that $u_{n} \rightarrow 0$ for $\omega(M, L)$ then

$$
\limsup _{n}\left\{\left|\left\langle u_{n}, \phi\right\rangle\right| ; \phi \in S\right\}=0
$$

(4) There is an element $\lambda \geq 0$ of $L$ such that $S$ is entirely contained in the band generated by $\lambda$ and such that

$$
\lim _{m \rightarrow \infty} \sup \{\||\phi|-|\phi| \wedge m \lambda\| ; \phi \in S\}=0
$$

(5) For each $\varepsilon>0$, there is a lattice interval $J$ of $L$ such that $\inf \{\|\phi-\mu\| ; \mu \in$
$J\}<\varepsilon$ for all $\phi \in S$.
(6) $S$ is $\omega$-equicontinuous on the unit ball $B$ of $E$.
(7) $S$ is $\omega$-equicontinuous on the unit ball $\bar{B}$ of $M$.
(8) $S$ is relatively $w(L, M)$-compact in $L$.

Proof. Let us assume that $S$ is relatively countably compact. Then it is norm bounded and, according to Smulian, relatively sequentially compact. If (3) does not hold, there is a sequence $\left\{\phi_{n}\right\}, \phi_{n} \in S$ and a number $\alpha>0$ and a sequence $\left\{u_{n}\right\},\left|u_{n}\right| \leq I$ such that $\left|\left\langle u_{n}, \phi_{n}\right\rangle\right| \geq \alpha$. Since $S$ is relatively sequentially compact, one can assume that $\phi_{n}$ tends for $w(L, M)$ to some $\phi_{0} \in L$. Let $\lambda=\left|\phi_{o}\right|+\Sigma 2^{-n}\left|\phi_{n}\right|$. Let $L(\lambda)$ be the band generated by $\lambda$. Taking a quotient, the $u_{n}$ may be replaced by elements of the unit ball $\bar{B}(\lambda)$ of the dual $M(\lambda)$ of $L(\lambda)$. On $\bar{B}(\lambda)$ the topology $\omega[M(\lambda), L(\lambda)]$ can be metrized by the norm $\|s\|=\langle | s|, \lambda\rangle$. For this metric, $\bar{B}(\lambda)$ is complete and therefore a Baire space. Since $M(\lambda)$ can be represented as a space of bounded functions, Proposition 3 became applicable, showing that the convergent sequence $\left\{\phi_{n}\right\}$ is $\omega[M(\lambda), L(\lambda)]$ equicontinuous if restricted to $\bar{B}(\lambda)$. Thus, if $\left|\left\langle u_{n}, \phi_{n}\right\rangle\right| \geq \alpha>0$, the sequence $\left\{u_{n}\right\}$ cannot converge to zero for $\omega(M, L)$.

Now take a first $\lambda_{0}$ arbitrarily in $S$ and construct a sequence $\lambda_{0}, \lambda_{1}, \ldots, \lambda_{n}$, … recursively as follows. Take an $\varepsilon \in(0,1 / 2)$. If $\lambda_{0}, \lambda_{1}, \ldots, \lambda_{n}$ have been selected, let $L_{n}$ be the band they generate and let $L_{n}^{\prime}$ be the complementary band. For $\lambda \in S$ let $\Pi_{n} \lambda$ be its projection in $L_{n}^{\prime}$. Select a $\lambda_{n+1} \in S$ so that

$$
\left\|\Pi_{n} \lambda_{n+1}\right\| \geq(1-\varepsilon) \sup \left\{\left\|\Pi_{n} \lambda\right\| ; \lambda \in S\right\}
$$

Let us show that if ( 3 ) holds the numbers $\left\|\Pi_{n} \lambda_{n+1}\right\|$ must tend to zero. If not, let $L_{\omega}$ be the band generated by the sequence $\left\{\lambda_{n}\right\}$ and let $L_{\omega}^{\prime}$ be its complementary band. There are elements $u_{n}$ such that $\left|u_{n}\right| \leq I,\left\langle u_{n}, \phi\right\rangle=0$ for $\phi \in L_{n}$ or $\phi \in L_{\omega}^{\prime}$, but $\left\langle u_{n}, \Pi_{n} \lambda_{n+1}\right\rangle=\left\|\Pi_{n} \lambda_{n+1}\right\|$. Clearly, the sequence $u_{n}$ tends to zero for $\omega(M, L)$. Thus, by (3), $\left\langle u_{n}, \lambda_{n+1}\right\rangle \rightarrow 0$. However, $\left\langle u_{n}, \lambda_{n+1}\right\rangle= \left\langle u_{n}, \Pi_{n} \lambda_{n+1}\right\rangle$.

It follows from this that $S$ must be contained in the band generated by $\lambda=\Sigma 2^{-n}\left|\lambda_{n}\right|$. Suppose now that, for this $\lambda$, condition (4) is violated. Then it must be violated either by the positive parts $\mu^{+}$or the negative parts $\mu^{-}$of the $\mu \in S$. Suppose, for instance, that the positive parts violate (4). For $\mu \in S$ let $\mu^{m}=\mu^{+}-\left(\mu^{+} \wedge m \lambda\right)$.

Take a number $\alpha>0$ and a sequence $\left\{\mu_{m}\right\}$ such that $\left\|\mu_{m}^{m}\right\| \geq \alpha$. Let $L\left(\mu_{m}^{m}\right)$ be the band generated by $\mu_{m}^{m}$ and let $L^{\prime}\left(\mu_{m}^{m}\right)$ be its complement. Take a $u_{m} \in M$ so that $0 \leq u_{m} \leq I$ and such that $\left\langle u_{m}, \phi\right\rangle=0$ for $\phi \in L^{\prime}\left(\mu_{m}^{m}\right)$ but $\left\langle u_{m}, \phi\right\rangle=\|\phi\|$ if $\phi \geq 0$ belong to $L\left(\mu_{m}^{m}\right)$. Then $u_{m} \rightarrow 0$ for $\omega(M, L)$. Indeed $\left\langle u_{m}, \mu_{m}^{+}\right\rangle \geq\left\langle u_{m}, m \lambda\right\rangle$ and $\left\langle u_{m}, \mu_{m}^{+}\right\rangle \leq\left\|\mu_{m}\right\|$. Therefore $m\left\langle u_{m}, \lambda\right\rangle \leq\left\|\mu_{m}\right\|$ which stays bounded. However, $\left\langle u_{m}, \mu_{m}^{m}\right\rangle=\left\|\mu_{m}^{m}\right\| \geq \alpha$. Thus condition (3) would be violated.

That (4) implies (5) is clear. It is also clear that (5) implies that $S$ is $\omega(M, L)$ equicontinuous on the unit ball $B$ of $E$. If so, it is uniformly equicontinuous on $B$, hence also on the completion $\bar{B}$ of $B$. This equicontinuity certainly implies
(3) and therefore (5). However, since the intervals of $L$ are $w(L, M)$ compact, it is easily seen that (5) implies (8). Since (8) implies (1), all the conditions are equivalent.

The following corollary was used in the proof of Theorem 1, Chapter 1. Corollary 1. Let $E$ be a uniform lattice with dual $E^{\prime}$ and second dual $E^{\prime \prime}$. Then every relatively $w\left(E^{\prime}, E^{\prime \prime}\right)$-compact subset of $E^{\prime}$ is also relatively compact for the Mackey topology $\tau\left(E^{\prime}, E\right)$.

Proof. According to condition (5) of Theorem 6, it is enough to prove that the intervals of $E^{\prime}$ are relatively compact for $\tau\left(E^{\prime}, E\right)$. Since they are $w\left(E^{\prime}, E\right)$ closed, it is enough to show that they are $\tau\left(E^{\prime}, E\right)$-precompact or equivalently that if $K$ is a $w\left(E, E^{\prime}\right)$-compact subset of $E$ and if $A$ is an interval of $E^{\prime}$, that $A$ is precompact for the uniform convergence on $K$.

However, this is equivalent to the precompactness of $K$ for uniform convergence on $A$. If that was not true one could find a sequence $\left\{u_{n}\right\}, u_{n} \in K$ and an $\varepsilon>0$ such that $\sup \left\{\left|\left\langle u_{n}, \lambda\right\rangle-\left\langle u_{k}, \lambda\right\rangle\right| ; \lambda \in A\right\}>\varepsilon$ for all $n \neq k$. However from $\left\{u_{n}\right\}$ one can extract a subsequence that converges for $w\left(E, E^{\prime}\right)$. Such a sequence is bounded. It converges pointwise. Hence by the Lebesgue theorem it converges for $\omega\left(E, E^{\prime}\right)$ and therefore cannot satisfy the assumed inequalities.

Remark. It is also true that the $\tau\left(E^{\prime}, E\right)$ relatively compact sets are $w\left(E^{\prime}, E^{\prime \prime}\right)$ relatively compact.

## 9. Lattices of Numerical Functions

In this section, $\mathscr{X}$ will denote an arbitrary set. If no indications to the contrary are given, operations such as addition, multiplication, or lattice operations on numerical functions defined on $\mathscr{X}$ will refer to the corresponding operations carried point by point.

Let $H$ be a vector lattice of bounded numerical functions on $\mathscr{X}$. Such a lattice will be called leveled if $u \in H$ implies that $u \wedge 1 \in H$. It will be called full if every interval $[u, v]=\{s \in H ; u \leqq s \leqq v\}$ is complete for uniform convergence on $\mathscr{X}$.

A (bounded) element $u$ of $H$ will be called a $K$-function if there is a $v \in H$ such that $v(x) \geqq 1$ for every $x$ such that $|u(x)|>0$. A leveled vector lattice $H$ of bounded functions will be called a $\mathscr{K}$-lattice if (1) it is full, (2) every element of $H$ is a $K$-function, and (3) there is no element $x$ of $\mathscr{X}$ such that $u(x)=0$ for every $u \in H$.

Let $S$ be an arbitrary set of bounded numerical functions on a set $\mathscr{X}$. Assume
for simplicity that $S$ separates the points of $\mathscr{X}$ (otherwise, one would first identify elements of $\mathscr{X}$ which are not separated). One can associate with the system $\{\mathscr{X}, S\}$ a compact space $\hat{\mathscr{X}}$ and a locally compact space $T$ as follows.

Let $U$ be the smallest uniform structure for which all the elements of $S$ are uniformly continuous on $\mathscr{X}$. Let $\hat{\mathscr{X}}$ be the completion of $\mathscr{X}$ for this structure, Since $S$ separates the points of $\mathscr{X}$, the structure $U$ is separated. Therefore, $\widehat{\mathscr{X}}$
is a compact Hausdorff space. The elements of $S$ extend by continuity to the whole of $\hat{\mathscr{X}}$ giving a subset $\hat{S}$ of the space of continuous functions on $\hat{\mathscr{X}}$.

The set of pairs $(x, y) \in \hat{\mathscr{X}} \times \hat{\mathscr{X}}$ such that $\hat{s}(x)=\hat{s}(y)$ for every $s \in S$ is a closed subset of $\widehat{\mathscr{X}} \times \widehat{\mathscr{X}}$. Therefore, the space $\mathscr{Z}$, obtained by identifying elements of $\hat{\mathscr{X}}$ which are not separated by $\hat{S}$, is also a compact Hausdorff space. The elements of $\hat{S}$ can be transferred to $\mathscr{Z}$ and $\mathscr{X}$ can be identified with a subset of $\mathscr{Z}$. Thus $\mathscr{Z}$ is also the completion of $\mathscr{X}$. Since this is $\hat{\mathscr{X}}$ by definition we must have $\hat{\mathscr{X}}=\mathscr{Z}$. Therefore $\hat{S}$ separates the points of $\hat{\mathscr{X}}$.

It follows from this that there is at most one element $\omega$ of $\hat{\mathscr{X}}$ such that $s(\omega)=0$ for all $s \in S$. This point will be called the point at infinity of $\hat{\mathscr{X}}$. The space $T=\hat{\mathscr{X}} \backslash \omega$ will be called the locally compact space associated with the system $\{\mathscr{X}, S\}$.

Consider now two distinct points $x$ and $y$ of $\hat{\mathscr{X}}$ and assume that $S$ is a linear space. Then the function $s \mapsto(s(x), s(y))$ maps $\widehat{S}$ into a linear subspace $V(x, y)$ of the plane. Such a subspace is either the origin, or a line, or the whole plane. Since $\hat{S}$ separates the points of $\hat{\mathscr{X}}$, the first possibility is not available. To say that $V(x, y)$ is a line amounts to saying that there are coefficients $\alpha \neq \beta$ such that $\alpha s(x)=\beta s(y)$ for every $s \in \hat{S}$. Under relatively weak assumptions such a situation cannot occur as shown by the following lemma.

Lemma 1. Assume that $S$ separates the points of $\mathscr{X}$ and that $S$ is generated by its positive elements. Furthermore, assume that if $s \in S$, then either $s \wedge 1$ or $s^{2}$ belong to $S$. Then there is at most one point $\omega \in \widehat{\mathscr{X}}$ such that $s(\omega)=0$ for every $s \in \hat{S}$ and if $x \neq \omega, y \neq \omega$, then $V$ defined above is the whole twodimensional plane.

Proof. Since $\hat{S}$ separates $\hat{\mathscr{X}}$, there is at most one point $\omega$ such that $s(\omega)=0$ for every $s \in \widehat{S}$. Suppose that $x \neq \omega$ and $y \neq \omega$ and $\alpha s(x)=\beta s(y)$ for every $s \in \hat{S}$. Then $\alpha \neq \beta$ and $\alpha \beta \neq 0$. Since $S$ is generated by its positive elements, the set $\hat{S}^{+}$of these positive elements also separates the points of $\hat{\mathscr{X}}$. Therefore, there is an $s \in \hat{S}^{+}$such that $s(x) \geqq 0, s(y) \geqq 0, s(x) \neq s(y)$, and $\alpha s(x)=\beta s(y)$. If $\alpha>0$ and $s(x)>0$, then $\beta s(y)>0$ implies $\beta>0$. Similarly, $s(y)>0$ implies $\alpha \beta>0$. Thus one can assume $0<\alpha<\beta$. This being the case, let $s$ be an element of $S$ such that $s(y)=\sqrt{\alpha / \beta}$. Let $u=s \wedge 1$ and $v=s^{2}$. Under the first kind of assumption we have $s(x)=(\beta / \alpha) s(y)=\sqrt{\beta / \alpha}$ and $u(x)=\min (\sqrt{\beta / \alpha}, 1)=1=(\beta / \alpha) u(y)=\sqrt{\beta / \alpha}$. Under the second type of assumption $v(x)=s^{2}(x)=(\beta / \alpha) s^{2}(y)=1$ and $v(x)=(\beta / \alpha)^{2} s^{2}(y)=\beta / \alpha$. Hence $\beta=\alpha$ in both cases, contrary to the assumption.

Consider again an arbitrary vector space $S$ of bounded numerical functions which separates the points of $\mathscr{X}$. Let $T$ be the locally compact space associated with $\{\mathscr{X}, S\}$; let $\mathscr{K}(T)$ be the space of continuous functions with compact support on $T$; and let $\overline{\mathscr{K}}(T)$ be the space of continuous functions which vanish at infinity on $T$.

Theorem 1. Let $\bar{S}$ be the uniform closure of $S$ in $\mathscr{B}(\mathscr{X})$. Assume that one of the following conditions is satisfied:
(a) if $s \in S$, then $s^{2} \in \bar{S}$;
(b) if $s \in S$, then $s \wedge 1$ and $|s|$ belong to $\bar{S}$.

Then $\bar{S}$ is isomorphic, latticewise, algebraically and as a Banach space to the space $\overline{\mathscr{K}}(T)$ of continuous numerical functions which vanish at infinity on $T$.

Proof. This is an immediate consequence of the Stone-Weierstrass theorem.
If $H$ is a leveled lattice of bounded functions on $\mathscr{X}$ and if $T$ is the locally compact space associated with $\{\mathscr{X}, H\}$, we can also obtain the following lemma.

Lemma 2. Let $H$ be a leveled vector lattice of bounded functions on $\mathscr{X}$. Let $f$ be an element of the positive cone $\mathscr{K}^{+}$of $\overline{\mathscr{K}}$. There is a sequence $\left\{f_{n}\right\}$ of elements of $\mathscr{K}^{+} \cap H$ such that $f_{n}$ increases to $f$ uniformly on $T$.

Proof. According to the Stone-Weierstrass theorem, for every $n$ there is a $g_{n} \in H^{+}$such that $\left|g_{n}-f\right|<1 / 2 n$ on $T$. Let $h_{n}=g_{n}-\left(g_{n} \wedge 1 / 2 n\right)$. Then $h_{n} \in \mathscr{K}^{+} \cap H$ and $0<h_{n} \leqq f$. Furthermore, $\left|f-h_{n}\right| \leqq 1 / n$ on $T$. The sequence $\left\{f_{n}\right\}$ defined by $f_{n}=\sup \left\{h_{j} ; j \leqq n\right\}$ has the desired property.

It follows from this that in a leveled vector lattice of bounded functions the $\mathscr{K}$-functions are precisely the restrictions of the elements of $\mathscr{K} \cap \hat{H}$ to $\mathscr{X}$. Indeed, for every $\mathscr{K}$-function $u$ its extension $\hat{u}$ to $T$ has compact support on $T$. Conversely, if $\hat{u} \in \mathscr{K} \cap \hat{H}$, there is an element $\hat{v}$ of $\mathscr{K}^{+}$such that $|\hat{u}(x)|>0$ implies $\hat{v}(x)>2$. According to Lemma 1, there is a $\hat{w} \in \mathscr{K}^{+} \cap H$ such that $0 \leqq \hat{w} \leqq \hat{v}$ and $|\hat{v}-\hat{w}|<1$. This implies $w(x) \geqq 1$ for every $x$ such that $|u(x)|>0$.

If in addition $H$ is full, then the $\mathscr{K}$-functions of $H$ are precisely the restrictions to $\mathscr{X}$ of the elements of $\mathscr{K}(T)$. In other words, a $\mathscr{K}$-lattice which separates the points of $\mathscr{X}$ is identical to the space of restrictions to $\mathscr{X}$ of the elements of $\mathscr{K}(T)$.

Let $H$ be a $\mathscr{K}$-lattice on a set $\mathscr{X}$. Let $U_{\sigma}$ (resp. $L_{\sigma}$ ) be the class of functions comprised of pointwise suprema (resp. infima) of nonempty countable subsets of $H$. Similarly, let $U_{\tau}$ and $L_{\tau}$ be the spaces obtained by taking pointwise suprema and infima of otherwise unrestricted nonempty subsets of $H$. The classes $U_{\sigma}$ and $U_{\tau}$ as well as $U_{\sigma} \cap L_{\sigma}$ and $U_{\tau} \cap L_{\tau}$ have some significance in the theory of integration. For the present we shall prove the following.

Theorem 2. Let $\mathscr{T}$ be the topology induced by $H$ on $\mathscr{X}$. The class $U_{\tau}$ is the class of all lower semicontinuous functions which are bounded below on $\{\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{T}\}$. Hence, $U_{\tau} \cap L_{\tau}$ is the class of all bounded continuous numerical functions on $\mathscr{X}$. If $\{\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{T}\}$ has the property that every closed subset of $\mathscr{X}$ is of the form $F=\{x: h(x)=0\}$ for some $h \in U_{\sigma} \cap L_{\sigma}$, then $U_{\sigma}=U_{\tau}$ and $U_{\sigma} \cap L_{\sigma}=U_{\tau} \cap L_{\tau}$.

Proof. Let $g$ be an element of $U_{\tau}$. One can assume without loss of generality that $g$ is non-negative. Let $\bar{g}$ be the pointwise supremum of the family of bounded continuous functions $\gamma$ defined on $\widehat{\mathscr{X}_{H}}=\widehat{\mathscr{X}}$ vanishing at $\omega$ on $\widehat{\mathscr{X}}$ and such that $\gamma \leqq g$ on $\mathscr{X}$. Clearly, $\bar{g}$ is lower semicontinuous on $\hat{\mathscr{X}}$. Let us show that $g=\bar{g}$ on $\mathscr{X}$. If $g(x)>a$, there is an open neighborhood $V$ of $x$ in $\mathscr{X}$ such that $g(y)>a$ for $y \in V$. However, since $\omega \notin \mathscr{X}$, there is an open neighborhood $W$ of $x$ in $\widehat{\mathscr{X}}$ such that $\omega \notin W$ and $\mathscr{X} \cap W \subset V$. There exists then a continuous function $f$ defined on $\widehat{\mathscr{X}}$ such that $f(x)=a$ and $f(z)=0$ for $z \in W^{c}$ while $0 \leqq f \leqq a$. It follows that $g \geqq f$ on $\mathscr{X}$ and that $\bar{g}(x) \geqq a$. Hence $g=\bar{g}$ on $\mathscr{X}$. However, $\bar{g}$ is on $T$ the supremum of elements of $\mathscr{K}^{+}$. Since each element of
$\mathscr{K}^{+}$is a supremum of elements of $\mathscr{K}^{+} \cap H$, the first result follows.
For the second statement, it is obviously sufficient to show that every bounded element of $U_{\tau}$ belongs to $U_{\sigma}$. Suppose then that $0 \leqq g \leqq 1$ and let $g^{\prime}=\bar{g} \wedge 1$ be a leveled off version of the lower semicontinuous extension constructed above. Divide the interval $[0,1]$ by the dyadic numbers $\left\{k / 2^{m}\right\}, k=0,1, \ldots, 2^{m}$. Let $G_{k, m}$ be the open set $G_{k, m}=\left\{x \in \widehat{\mathscr{X}} ; g^{\prime}(x)>k / 2^{m}\right\}$ and let

$$
f_{m}(x)=\left(\frac{1}{2^{m}}\right) \sum_{k=1}^{2^{m}} I_{G_{k, m}}
$$

Then $f_{m} \leqq g^{\prime} \leqq f_{m}+\left(1 / 2^{m}\right)$ and $f_{m}$ is lower semicontinuous. Furthermore, $f_{m}$ increases to $g^{\prime}$ as $m$ increases. To prove the second result it is thus sufficient to show that the indicators of open sets have the desired property. Let $G$ be open in $\mathscr{X}$ and let $F=\mathscr{X} \cap G^{c}$. Then there is by assumption an $h \in U_{\sigma} \cap L_{\sigma}$ such that $h(x)=0$ for $x \in F$ and $h(x) \neq 0$ for $x \in G$. One can assume $0 \leqq h \leqq 1$. Let $\gamma=\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} h^{1 / n}$. Then $\gamma \in U_{\sigma}$ and $\gamma(x)=1$ for $x \in G$ and $\gamma(x)=0$ for $x \in F$. Thus $I_{G} \in U_{\sigma}$.

This second result applies in particular to the case where $\{\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{T}\}$ happens to be metrizable and where $H$ is the space of all bounded functions which are uniformly continuous for a given metric compatible with the topology.

Finally we shall use the following results. Let $H$ be a leveled lattice of bounded functions on a set $\mathscr{X}$. Assume that $H$ separates the points of $\mathscr{X}$ and that no point $x \in \mathscr{X}$ is such that $u(x)=0$ for every $x \in \mathscr{X}$. Let $\mathscr{T}$ be the topology defined by $H$ on $\mathscr{X}$. It is easily seen that the class $U_{\tau} \cap L_{\tau}$ is precisely the class of all bounded numerical functions which are continuous for $\mathscr{T}$ on $\mathscr{X}$.

Therefore, in order that the inequality $f \leqq g\left(f \in L_{\tau}, g \in U_{\tau}\right)$ imply the existence of a $u \in U_{\tau} \cap L_{\tau}$ such that $f \leqq u \leqq g$, it is necessary and sufficient that $\mathscr{X}$ be a normal space for the topology $\mathscr{T}$. On the contrary, the existence of intermediate functions for the classes $L_{\sigma}$ and $U_{\sigma}$ does not place any restrictions on the topology $\mathscr{T}$, as can be seen from the following result.

Proposition 1. Let $H$ be a leveled lattice of bounded function on a set $\mathscr{X}$. If $f \in L_{\sigma}$ and $g \in U_{\sigma}$ are such that $f \leqq g$, there is an element $h$ of $L_{\sigma} \cap U_{\sigma}$ such that $f \leqq h \leqq g$.

Proof. Let $\left\{u_{m}\right\}$ be a sequence of elements of $H$ decreasing pointwise to $f$. Let $\left\{v_{m}\right\}$ be a sequence of elements of $H$ increasing pointwise to $g$.

Let $\mathscr{Z}$ be the set obtained by identifying the points of $\mathscr{X}$ which are not separated by the elements of the above sequences. To simplify, assume that $\mathscr{X}$ is already separated by $H$. Assume that the smallest uniform structures for which the elements of $H$ (resp. the $u_{m}$ 's and $v_{m}$ 's) are uniformly continuous are on $\mathscr{X}$ and (resp. $\mathscr{Z}$ ). Let $\hat{\mathscr{X}}$ and $\hat{\mathscr{Z}}$ be the completions of the spaces $\mathscr{X}$ and $\mathscr{Z}$, respectively. The canonical map $\phi$ of $\mathscr{X}$ onto $\mathscr{Z}$ extends to a canonical map (also denoted $\phi$ ) of $\hat{\mathscr{X}}$ onto $\hat{\mathscr{Z}}$. Furthermore, the space $\hat{\mathscr{Z}}$ is a compact metrizable space. The functions $f$ and $g$ can be considered as functions defined on $\mathscr{Z}$. On the metric space $\mathscr{Z}$ there is a continuous function $\gamma$ such that $f \leqq \gamma \leqq g$ on $\mathscr{Z}$. Let $\bar{\gamma}$ be the largest lower semicontinuous extension of
$\gamma$ to $\hat{\mathscr{Z}}$. If $H$ defines a point $\omega$ of $\hat{\mathscr{X}}$ at infinity, then $\hat{\mathscr{Z}}$ also has a point at infinity: $\omega^{\prime}$ and $\omega^{\prime}=\phi(\omega)$. The function $\bar{\gamma}$ is, on the separable compact space $\hat{\mathscr{Z}}$, the pointwise supremum of a sequence of continuous functions. On $\hat{\mathscr{Z}} \cap\{\omega\}^{c}$ it is the supremum of a sequence $\left\{\alpha_{n}\right\}$ of continuous functions with compact support. Let $\beta_{n}=\alpha_{n} \circ \phi$. Then $\beta_{n}$ is a continuous function with compact support on $T=\widehat{\mathscr{X}} \cap\{\omega\}^{c}$. Since $\beta_{n}$ is a uniform limit on $T$ of an increasing sequence of elements of $\mathscr{K}^{+} \cap H$, it follows that whether $\omega \in \mathscr{X}$ or $\omega \notin \mathscr{X}$, the function $h=\gamma \circ \phi$, is an element of $U_{\sigma}$ when restricted to $\mathscr{X}$. Similarly $h \in L_{\sigma}$. Hence the result.

To obtain a somewhat better result let us note the following. Let $U$ be the smallest uniform structure for which the elements of $H$ are uniformly continuous. Let $\widehat{\mathscr{X}}$ be the compact Hausdorff space completion of $\mathscr{X}$ for $U$ and let $\hat{H}= C(\hat{\mathscr{X}})$ be the space of extensions of elements of $H$ to $\hat{\mathscr{X}}$.

Let $f$ and $g$ be two extended numerical functions on $\mathscr{X}$. Assume that $f \leqq g$. One can define upper and lower semicontinuous functions $\bar{f}$ and $\underline{g}$ on the whole of $\widehat{\mathscr{X}}$ by the relations

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{f} & =\inf \{\hat{h} ; \hat{h} \in \hat{H}, h \geqq f\} \\
\underline{g} & =\sup \{\hat{h} ; \hat{h} \in \hat{H}, h \leqq g\}
\end{aligned}
$$

By construction $\bar{f}$ is upper semicontinuous and $f \leqq \bar{f}$ on $\mathscr{X}$. If there is an $h \in H$ such that $f \leqq h \leqq g$ on $\mathscr{X}$, then we must have $\bar{f} \leqq h \leqq \underline{g}$ on $\hat{\mathscr{X}}$. Conversely, if $\bar{f} \leqq \underline{g}$ on $\widehat{\mathscr{X}}$, there is such an $h \in H$ since $\widehat{\mathscr{X}}$ is compact.

Proposition 2. Let $H$ be a vector lattice of bounded functions on the set $\mathscr{X}$. Assume that $I \in H$, that $H$ separates the points of $\mathscr{X}$, and that $H= U_{\sigma}(H) \cap L_{\sigma}(H)$. Let $T$ be the topology defined on $\mathscr{X}$ by $H$. Let $U$ be the class of functions $g \in U_{\tau}$ which are such that there is a compact $K$ of $\{\mathscr{X}, T\}$ such that for each neighborhood $V$ of $K$ there is a function $g_{1} \in U_{\sigma}$ such that $g(x)=g_{1}(x)$ for $x \in V^{c}$. If $(-f) \in U$ and $g \in U$ and $f \leqq g$, then there is an $h \in H$ such that $f \leqq h \leqq g$.

Proof. The class $U_{\tau}$ is precisely the class of lower semicontinuous functions which are bounded below on $\mathscr{X}$. Let $\widehat{\mathscr{X}}$ be the completion of $\mathscr{X}$ and let $g$ be the extension $\underline{g}=\sup \{\hat{h} ; \hat{h} \in \hat{H}, h \leqq g\}$. For $g \in U_{\tau}$ this gives $\underline{g}(x)=g(x)$ for every $x \in \mathscr{X}$.

Assume that the pair $\{f, g\}, f \leqq g$ satisfies the requirements of the proposition. Let $K$ be a compact subset of $\mathscr{X}$. Then, there is a $\gamma \in C(K)$ such that $f \leqq \gamma \leqq g$ on $K$. This function $\gamma$ can be extended to the whole of $\widehat{\mathscr{X}}$, since $\widehat{\mathscr{X}}$ itself is compact. Thus we can assume $\gamma \in C(\widehat{\mathscr{X}})$ and $f \leqq \gamma \leqq g$ on $K$. Let $f^{\prime}=f-\gamma$ and $g^{\prime}=g-\gamma$. Then $g^{\prime} \in U$ and $f^{\prime} \in[-U]$. Also $f^{\prime} \leqq g^{\prime}$ on $\mathscr{X}$ and $f^{\prime} \leqq 0 \leqq g^{\prime}$ on $K$. It is therefore sufficient to consider the case where $K$ is a compact such that $f \leqq 0 \leqq g$ on $K$ and such that $g$ and ( $-f$ ) coincide with $U_{\sigma}$ functions outside $K$.

Assume first that $f<g$ on $K$. The set $S=\{x \in \mathscr{X}, f(x)<g(x)\}$ is then an open neighborhood of $K$. Hence, there is an open set $V$ of $\hat{\mathscr{X}}$ such that
$S=\widehat{\mathscr{X}} \cap V$. Let $\gamma$ be an element of $C(\widehat{\mathscr{X}})$ such that $0 \leqq \gamma \leqq 1$ and such that $\gamma(x)=0$ for $x \in K$ and $\gamma(x)=1$ for $x \in V^{c}$. Consider the functions $\gamma f$ and $\gamma g$ on $\mathscr{X}$. Then $\gamma f \leqq \gamma g$ on $\mathscr{X}$ and $\gamma g$ and $(-\gamma f)$ belong to $U_{\sigma}$. According to Proposition 1 there is an element $h$ of $H$ such that $\gamma f \leqq h \leqq \gamma g$ on $\mathscr{X}$. This implies $h=0$ on $K$ : hence, $f \leqq h \leqq g$ on $\mathscr{X}$.

If $f \leqq g$ on $\mathscr{X}$, but if the inequality is not strict on $K$, the preceding argument applies to the functions $f_{n}=f-1 / n$ and $g_{n}=g+1 / n$. Therefore, for each integer $n$, there exists an $h_{n} \in H$ such that $f-1 / n \leqq h_{n} \leqq g+1 / n$ on $\mathscr{X}$, with $h_{n}=0$ on $\mathscr{X}$. Let then $g^{\prime}=\sup _{n}\left(h_{n}-1 / n\right)$ and $f^{\prime}=\inf _{n}\left(h_{n}+1 / n\right)$. Clearly, $f-(2 / n) \leqq g^{\prime}$ for every $n$. Therefore $f^{\prime} \leqq g^{\prime}$. However, $g^{\prime}$ and $\left(-f^{\prime}\right)$ both belong to $U_{\sigma}$. This implies the existence of an element $h \in H$ such that $f^{\prime} \leqq h \leqq g^{\prime}$. Hence the result.

## 10. Extensions of Positive Linear Functions

Let $H$ be a vector space of numerical functions on a set $\mathscr{X}$. Let $\phi$ be a positive linear functional defined on $H$. This section describes a widely used procedure which provides an enlargement of the domain of the positive linear functional $\phi$. The procedure can be specialized to give the Darboux-Eudoxus extension, whose importance derives from the fact that such an extension is uniquely determined by the monotonicity properties of $\phi$. If suitable restrictions are imposed on the linear functional itself, the Daniell and MacShane extensions are also obtainable from the general procedure.

Since set functions are often used in the sequel we have also indicated briefly a fundamental result of L. H. Loomis, linking linear functionals to additive set functions.

Let $f$ be an arbitrary function from $\mathscr{X}$ to $[-\infty,+\infty]$. The symbol $\tilde{\phi}(f)$ will denote the element of $[-\infty,+\infty]$ defined by

$$
\tilde{\phi}(f)=\sup \{\phi(h) ; h \in H, h \leqq f\} .
$$

The function $\tilde{\phi}$ so defined has for domain the space of all functions from $\mathscr{X}$ to $[-\infty,+\infty]$. However, $\tilde{\phi}(f)>-\infty$ if and only if there is some $h \in H$ such that $h \leqq f$.

Lemma 1. On the domain where $\tilde{\phi}$ is not equal to $(-\infty)$, the function $\tilde{\phi}$ possesses the following properties:
(i) $\tilde{\phi}\left(f_{1}+f_{2}\right) \geq \tilde{\phi}\left(f_{1}\right)+\tilde{\phi}\left(f_{2}\right)$,
(ii) for $\alpha$ real $\alpha>0$ one has $\tilde{\phi}(\alpha f)=\alpha \tilde{\phi}(f)$,
(iii) $\tilde{\phi}(f+h)=\tilde{\phi}(f)+\tilde{\phi}(h)$ for $h \in H$,
(iv) $\tilde{\phi}\left(f_{1}\right) \leq \tilde{\phi}\left(f_{2}\right)$ if $f_{1} \leqq f_{2}$.

Proof. These relations are immediate consequences of the definitions. For instance, the equality asserted in (iii) results from the following string of relations:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tilde{\phi}(f+h) & =\sup _{u}\{\phi(u) ; u \in H, u \leqq g+h\} \\
& =\sup _{v}\{\phi(v+h) ; v \in H, v \leqq g\} \\
& =\sup _{v}\{\phi(h)+\phi(h) ; v \in H, v \leqq g\} \\
& =\phi(h)+\sup _{v}\{\phi(v) ; v \in H, v \leqq g\} .
\end{aligned}
$$

One can also introduce another function $\tilde{\tilde{\phi}}$ by the relation $\tilde{\tilde{\phi}}(f)=-\tilde{\phi}(-f)= \inf \{\phi(h) ; h \in H, f \leqq h\}$. By construction $\tilde{\phi}(f) \leqq \tilde{\tilde{\phi}}(f)$. Also $\tilde{\tilde{\phi}}(f)=\infty$ if and only if there is no $h \in H$ such that $f \leqq h$.

The linearity and positivity of $\phi$ imply that $\phi$ is increasing. That is, $h_{1} \leqq h_{2}$ implies $\phi\left(h_{1}\right) \leqq \phi\left(h_{2}\right)$. Any increasing extension of $\phi$ to a domain which includes the function $f$ must assign to $f$ a value belonging to the interval $[\tilde{\phi}(f), \tilde{\tilde{\phi}}(f)]$. The set of functions $f$ such that $\tilde{\phi}(f)=\tilde{\tilde{\phi}}(f)$ and such that $\tilde{\phi}(f)$ is finite will be called the Eudoxus domain of $\phi$ and denoted $\mathscr{E} u[H, \phi]$.

Proposition 1. The Eudoxus domain $\mathscr{E} u[H, \phi]$ is a vector space. On this space $\tilde{\phi}$ is a positive linear functional. Furthermore, $\mathscr{E} u(H, \phi)$ is the largest vector space containing $H$ to which $\phi$ possesses an extension which is uniquely defined by the property of being positive and linear.

Proof. The fact that $\mathscr{E} u(H, \phi)$ is a linear space on which $\tilde{\phi}$ is positive linear is
a particular case of a result proved in Proposition 2 below. For a function $f$ such that $\tilde{\phi}(f)=\tilde{\tilde{\phi}}(f)=-\infty$ or $\tilde{\phi}(f)=\tilde{\tilde{\phi}}(f)=+\infty$, no real increasing extension of $\phi$ is possible. If $\tilde{\phi}(f)<\tilde{\tilde{\phi}}(f)$ one can easily check that any real number $a$ included in the interval $[\tilde{\phi}(f), \tilde{\tilde{\phi}}(f)]$ can be taken as value of a positive linear extension of $\phi$ to the vector space spanned by $\mathscr{E} u(H, \phi)$ and $f$.

In spite of the fact that beyond $\mathscr{E} u(H, \phi)$ the extension is not unique, it is often convenient to have such extensions available. A theorem of M. Riesz, obtainable from the argument of Proposition 1 by application of Zorn's lemma shows that we can always extend $\phi$ to a positive linear functional defined on the space of functions which are bounded above and below by elements of $H$. It is preferable, however, to proceed quite differently. For this we shall use the following definitions.

Definition 1. A set $V$ of functions from $\mathscr{X}$ to $(-\infty,+\infty]$ will be called a convex cone over $H$ if it satisfies the following requirements:
(a) the relations $v_{i} \in V, i=1,2$ and $\alpha \in(0, \infty)$ imply $\alpha v_{i} \in V$ and $v_{1}+v_{2} \in V$;
(b) for each $v \in V$ there is an $h \in H$ such that $h \leqq v$;
(c) $H \subset V$.

Definition 2. Let $G$ be a convex cone over $H$. Such a set $G$ will be called a cone of distributivity of $\tilde{\phi}$ if $g_{i} \in G, i=1,2$ implies $\tilde{\phi}\left(g_{i}\right)<\infty$ and

$$
\tilde{\phi}\left(g_{1}+g_{2}\right)=\tilde{\phi}\left(g_{1}\right)+\tilde{\phi}\left(g_{2}\right)
$$



For now we shall assume that a particular cone of distributivity $G$ has been selected. Description of some possible selections will be found further on in this section.

Let $\mathscr{E}$ be the set of functions $f$ from $\mathscr{X}$ to $[-\infty,+\infty]$ which are such that there is a pair $(u, v)$ of elements of $G$ for which $(-u) \leqq f \leqq v$. Then $G \subset \mathscr{E}$ and the real elements of $\mathscr{E}$ form a vector space.

For each pair $(u, v)$ of elements of $G$ let $[-u, v]$ be the interval $[-u, v]=\{f: f \in \mathscr{E},(-u) \leqq f \leqq v\}$. This family of intervals is a base for a topology $\mathscr{T}_{G}$ on $\mathscr{E}$. For $\mathscr{T}_{G}$ the set $H$ is discrete; hence $\phi$ defined on $H$ is certainly $\mathscr{T}_{G}$ continuous on $H$. A first extension procedure, similar to the EudoxusDarboux method is the following.

If $f \in \mathscr{E}$ and $(-u) \leqq f \leqq v$ for $u \in G$ and $v \in G$, then $u+\underset{\sim}{v} \geqq 0$. Therefore $\tilde{\phi}(u+v)=\tilde{\phi}(u)+\tilde{\phi}(v) \geqq 0$. In other words the interval $[-\tilde{\phi}(u), \tilde{\phi}(v)]$ is not empty.

Definition 3. A function $f \in \mathscr{E}$ belongs to the domain $\mathscr{E}(G, \phi)$ of the interval extension of $\phi$ if the intersection of the intervals $[-\tilde{\phi}(u), \tilde{\phi}(v)], u \in G, v \in G$, $(-u) \leqq f \leqq v$ is reduced to a single point. This single point will be taken as value of the extension of $\phi$ at $f$ and denoted $\phi_{I}(f)$.

An immediate consequence of this definition can be stated as follows.
Proposition 2. The domain $\mathscr{E}(G, \phi)$ of the interval extension of $\phi$ contains $G$. For $g \in G$ one has $\phi_{I}(g)=\tilde{\phi}(g)$. Furthermore, $\phi_{I}$ is $\mathscr{T}_{G}$-continuous on $\mathscr{E}(G, \phi)$ and the real elements of $\mathscr{E}(G, \phi)$ form a vector space on which $\phi_{I}$ is a positive linear functional.

Proof. Let $g$ be an element of $G$. By definition $\tilde{\phi}(g)=\sup \{\phi(h) ; h \in H, h \leqq g\}$. Since $H \subset G$ the relation $h \in H$ implies $-h \in G$. For each $\varepsilon>0$ there is an $h \in H$ such that $h \leqq g$ and $\tilde{\phi}(g)-\phi(h)<\varepsilon$. Thus $G \subset \mathscr{E}(G, \phi)$ and $\phi_{I}(g)=\tilde{\phi}(g)$. The relation $(-u) \leqq f \leqq v$ is equivalent to $(-v) \leqq(-f) \leqq u$. Thus if $f \in \mathscr{E}(G, \phi)$ then $(-f) \in \mathscr{E}(G, \phi)$. It is clear that $\alpha>0, f \in \mathscr{E}(G, \phi)$ implies $\alpha f \in \mathscr{E}(G, \phi)$. Let $f_{1}$ and $f_{2}$ be two elements of $\mathscr{E}(G, \phi)$ which do not take infinite values of opposite signs. Let $\left(u_{i}, v_{i}\right), i=1,2$ be pairs of elements of $G$ such that $\left(-u_{i}\right) \leqq f_{i} \leqq v_{i}$ and $\tilde{\phi}\left(u_{i}+v_{i}\right)<\varepsilon / 2$. Then $-\left(u_{1}+u_{2}\right) \leqq f_{1}+f_{2} \leqq\left(v_{1}+v_{2}\right)$ and $\tilde{\phi}\left(u_{1}+u_{2}\right)+\tilde{\phi}\left(v_{1}+v_{2}\right)=\tilde{\phi}\left(u_{1}+v_{1}\right)+\tilde{\phi}\left(u_{2}+v_{2}\right)<\varepsilon$. Therefore, $f_{1}+f_{2} \in \mathscr{E}(G, \phi)$. Since $\left|\phi_{I}\left(f_{i}\right)-\tilde{\phi}\left(v_{i}\right)\right|<\varepsilon / 2$ and $\tilde{\phi}\left(v_{1}+v_{2}\right)=\tilde{\phi}\left(v_{1}\right)+\tilde{\phi}\left(v_{2}\right)$, one has also $\phi_{I}\left(f_{1}+f_{2}\right)=\phi_{I}\left(f_{1}\right)+\phi_{I}\left(f_{2}\right)$. The relation $\phi_{I}(\alpha f)=\alpha \phi_{I}(f)$ is clear. The assertion that $\phi_{I}$ is $\mathscr{T}_{G}$-continuous is essentially a restatement of the definition of $\mathscr{E}(G, \phi)$ and $\phi_{I}$. This completes the proof of the proposition.

Another standard method of extension utilizes a seminorm $\rho$ and a pseudometric defined by the following process. For each $f \in \mathscr{E}$ let $\rho(f)$ be the element of [ $0, \infty$ ] defined by

$$
\rho(f)=\inf \{\tilde{\phi}(g) ; g \in G,|f| \leqq g\} .
$$

The set $\mathscr{E}_{\rho}$ of functions $f$ such that $\rho(f)<\infty$ is precisely the set of elements of $\mathscr{E}$ which are bounded in absolute value by some $g \in G$. The set $\mathscr{E}_{\rho}$ is never empty but it may happen to consist of the single function identically equal to zero on $\mathscr{X}$.

Let $f_{1}$ and $f_{2}$ be two elements of $\mathscr{E}$. If $f_{1}(x)$ and $f_{2}(x)$ are infinite but equal let $f_{1}(x)-f_{2}(x)=0$. With this convention define $d\left(f_{1}, f_{2}\right)=\rho\left(f_{1}, f_{2}\right)$. Call a function $f$ negligible if $\rho(f)=0$. The following lemma shows that on $\mathscr{E}_{\rho}$ the function $\rho$ has the characteristic properties of a seminorm and therefore that $d$ has the essential properties of a pseudometric.

Lemma 2. Let $f$ be an arbitrary element of $\mathscr{E}_{\rho}$. Then every function $u$ from $\mathscr{X}$ to $[-\infty,+\infty]$ which is such that $|f(x)|<\infty$ implies $u(x)=0$ is a negligible element of $\mathscr{E}_{\rho}$. Furthermore let $f_{i}, i=1,2$ be two elements of $\mathscr{E}_{\rho}$ and let $s_{i}, i=1$, 2 be two functions such that $\left|f_{1}(x)\right|+\left|f_{2}(x)\right|<\infty$ implies $s_{i}(x)=f_{1}(x)+f_{2}(x)$. Then $\rho\left(s_{1}\right)=\rho\left(s_{2}\right)$ and $\rho\left(s_{i}\right) \leqq \rho\left(f_{1}\right)+\rho\left(f_{2}\right)$. Finally, if $f \in \mathscr{E}_{\rho}$ and if $\alpha \neq 0$ is real then $\rho(\alpha f)=|\alpha| \rho(f)$.

Proof. The last statement is an immediate consequence of the definition. To prove the first statement note that the condition imposed on $u$ implies $|u| \leqq \alpha|f|$ for every $\alpha>0$. This gives $\rho(u) \leqq \alpha \rho(f)$; hence, $\rho(u)=0$ since $\rho(f)<\infty$. For the second statement suppose that $g_{i} \in G$ and that $\left|f_{i}\right| \leqq g_{i}$. Then $\left|s_{i}\right| \leqq \left|f_{1}\right|+\left|f_{2}\right| \leqq g_{1}+g_{2}$. It follows that $\rho\left(s_{i}\right) \leqq \tilde{\phi}\left(g_{1}+g_{2}\right)=\tilde{\phi}\left(g_{1}\right)+\tilde{\phi}\left(g_{2}\right)$ and therefore $\rho\left(s_{i}\right) \leqq \rho\left(f_{1}\right)+\rho\left(f_{2}\right)$. Finally, let $u(x)=+\infty$ if $\left|f_{1}(x)\right|+\left|f_{2}(x)\right|=\infty$. Let $u(x)=0$ otherwise. Then $\left|s_{1}\right| \leqq\left|s_{2}\right|+u$. Since $u$ is negligible it follows that $\rho\left(s_{1}\right) \leqq \rho\left(s_{2}\right)+\rho(u)=\rho\left(s_{2}\right)$. This completes the proof of the proposition.

On the vector space $H$ itself we can write $\left|\phi\left(h_{1}\right)-\phi\left(h_{2}\right)\right| \leqq d\left(h_{1}, h_{2}\right)$. This suggests that $\phi$ can be extended by continuity to the closure $H_{\rho}$ of $H$ in $\mathscr{T}$ for the pseudometric $d$. One of the important facts of the theory is that the extension so obtained coincides with the extension obtained in Proposition 2.

Proposition 3. Let $H_{\rho}$ be the closure of $H$ in $\mathscr{E}$ for the pseudometric induced by $\rho$. Then $H_{\rho}$ coincides with the domain $\mathscr{E}(G, \phi)$ of the interval extension $\phi_{I}$ of $\phi$ and $\phi_{I}$ is continuous for the pseudometric induced by $\rho$.

Proof, Note first that $G$ and $(-G)$ are contained in $H_{\rho}$. Indeed, if $g \in G$ for each $\varepsilon>0$ there is an $h \in H, h \leqq g$ such that $\tilde{\phi}(g) \leqq \phi(h)+\varepsilon$. This gives $\tilde{\phi}(g-h)=\rho(g-h)=\tilde{\phi}(g)-\tilde{\phi}(h) \leqq \varepsilon$. By definition $f \in \mathscr{E}$ belongs to $\mathscr{E}(G, \phi)$ if and only if for every $\varepsilon>0$ there is a pair ( $u, v$ ) of elements of $G$ such that $0 \leqq v-f \leqq u+v, 0 \leqq f+u \leqq u+v$, and $\tilde{\phi}(u+v)<\varepsilon$. Therefore, if $f \in \mathscr{E}(G, \phi)$ then $f$ is in the intersection of the closures of $G$ and $(-G)$ and therefore in $H_{\rho}$ since $G \subset H_{\rho}$.

Conversely, suppose that $f \in \mathscr{E}$ is such that there are elements $u$ and $v$ of $G$ for which $d[f,(-u)]<\varepsilon$ and $d(f, v)<\varepsilon$. This implies the existence of $g_{i} \in G$ such that $|f+u| \leqq g_{1}$ and $|f-v| \leqq g_{2 \text { ~ }}$ with $\tilde{\phi}\left(g_{i}\right)<\varepsilon$. It follows that $-\left(u+g_{1}\right) \leqq f \leqq v+g_{2}$ and that $\tilde{\phi}\left(v+g_{2}\right)+\tilde{\phi}\left(u+g_{1}\right) \leqq 4 \varepsilon$. If this holds for every $\varepsilon>0$, one concludes that $f \in \mathscr{E}(G, \phi)$. Finally $\phi_{I}$ is continuous for the pseudometric induced by $\rho$. To show this, assume that $f_{i} \in \mathscr{E}(G, \phi), i=1,2$ are such that $\rho\left(f_{1}-f_{2}\right)<\varepsilon$. There is a $g \in G$ such that $\left|f_{1}-f_{2}\right| \leqq g$ and $\tilde{\phi}(g)<\varepsilon$. Let $\left(u_{i}, v_{i}\right)$ be elements of $G$ such that $\tilde{\phi}\left(u_{i}+v_{i}\right)<\varepsilon$ and $-u_{i} \leqq f_{i} \leqq v_{i}$. Then $-u_{1}-g \leqq f_{2} \leqq v_{1}+g$. Hence $\phi_{I}\left(f_{1}\right)$ and $\phi_{I}\left(f_{2}\right)$ both belong to an interval of length at most $4 \varepsilon$. The result follows.

Remark. The set $H_{\rho}=\mathscr{E}(G, \phi)$ is the closure of $H$ in $\mathscr{E}$. It is not necessarily the closure of $H$ in the set $\mathscr{E}_{\rho}$ of functions $f$ such that $\rho(f)<\infty$. In fact $\mathscr{E}_{\rho}$
may be reduced to the zero function and $H$ may be quite large.
However, if $H \subset \mathscr{E}_{\rho}$, that is if for every $h \in H$ there is a $g \in G$ such that $|h| \leqq g$, then the closure of $H$ in $\mathscr{E}$ and the closure of $H$ in $\mathscr{E}_{\rho}$ are identical. This follows from the fact that $H \subset \mathscr{E}_{\rho}$ implies $\rho(h)<\infty$ for each $h \in H$ and therefore $\rho(f)<\infty$ for each $f \in H_{\rho}$.

Another remark needed in the sequel is the following.
Lemma 3. For every positive element $f$ of $\mathscr{E}(G, \phi)$ the value $\phi_{I}(f)$ coincides with $\rho(f)$. Furthermore, if either one of the sets $H$ or $G$ is a lattice the same is true
of $\mathscr{E}(G, \phi)$. Also, if $u$ is an arbitrary real function such that $h \in H$ implies $h \wedge u \in G$ then $f \in \mathscr{E}(G, \phi)$ implies $f \wedge u \in \mathscr{E}(G, \phi)$.

Proof. The first statement follows from the definition of $\rho$. For the second statement note that if $u_{i}$ and $v_{i}$ are arbitrary real functions then

$$
\left|\left(u_{2} \wedge v_{2}\right)-\left(u_{1} \wedge v_{1}\right)\right| \leqq\left|u_{2}-u_{1}\right|+\left|v_{2}-v_{1}\right|
$$

This inequality remains valid for extended numerical functions if differences of equal infinities are defined to be zero. This implies

$$
\rho\left[\left(u_{2} \wedge v_{2}\right)-\left(u_{1} \wedge v_{1}\right)\right] \leqq \rho\left(u_{2}-u_{1}\right)+\rho\left(v_{2}-v_{1}\right)
$$

The desired result follows.
The finite elements of $\mathscr{E}(G, \phi)$ form a vector space which henceforth will be denoted $\mathscr{D}[H, \phi, G]$. The positive linear extension to $\mathscr{D}[H, \phi, G]$ obtained in Propositions 2 and 3 will be denoted by the same symbol as the original functional given on $H$.

The preceding arguments are usually carried through for the purpose of relating the linear function $\phi$ to an additive set function (a content) by means of an integration procedure. In this connection, the following fundamental lemma of L. H. Loomis will be sufficient for our purposes.

Proposition 4. Let $\mathscr{D}=\mathscr{D}[H, \phi ; G]$ be the domain of extension of $\varphi$. Let $f$ be a numerical function defined on $\mathscr{X}$ and such that for every $\lambda>0$ the function $f \wedge \lambda$ belongs to $\mathscr{D}$. Then, except perhaps for a countable set of values of $\lambda>0$ the indicators of the sets $A_{\lambda}=\{x: f(x)>\lambda\}$ belong to $\mathscr{D}$.

Proof. For every numerical function $v$ let $\Delta(v)$ be the number

$$
\Delta(v)=\inf \{\phi(g) ; g \in \mathscr{D}, g \geqq v\}-\sup \{\phi(f) ; f \in \mathscr{D}, f \leqq v\}
$$

Let $0<\lambda_{0}<\lambda_{1}<\cdots<\lambda_{n}<\infty$ be a finite sequence of numbers and let $u_{m}, A_{m}$, and $v_{m}$ be defined by the relations

$$
\begin{aligned}
u_{m} & =\left[\left(f \wedge \lambda_{m}\right)-\left(f \wedge \lambda_{m-1}\right)\right]\left[\lambda_{m}-\lambda_{m-1}\right]^{-1} \\
A_{m} & =\left\{x: f(x)>\lambda_{m}\right\} \\
v_{m} & =I_{A_{m}}
\end{aligned}
$$

By construction, $0 \leqq u_{m} \leqq 1$ and $u_{m}(x)=0$ for $x \in A_{m-1}^{c}$. Also $u_{m}(x)=1$ for $x \in A_{m}$. It follows that $u_{m+1} \leqq v_{m} \leqq u_{m} \leqq v_{m-1}$. Thus

$$
\Delta\left(v_{m}\right) \leqq \phi\left(u_{m}\right)-\phi\left(u_{m+1}\right)
$$

This implies

$$
\sum_{j=1}^{n-1} \Delta\left(v_{j}\right) \leqq \phi\left(u_{1}\right)-\phi\left(u_{n}\right) \leqq \phi\left(u_{1}\right)
$$

Fix $\lambda_{0}$ and $\lambda_{1}$ and an $\varepsilon>0$. Select, if possible, points $\lambda_{j}$ such that $\Delta\left(v_{j}\right)> \varepsilon$. The preceding inequality shows that there are at most $\varepsilon^{-1} \phi\left(u_{1}\right)$ such points. The result follows immediately.

Suppose now that $G$ is leveled. Then every $f \in \mathscr{D}$ has the property described in Proposition 4. Let $\mathscr{A}$ be the class of sets $A$ such that $I_{A} \in \mathscr{D}[H, \phi ; G]$. Let $\mathscr{R}_{0}$ be the set of finite linear combinations of the type $\sum \alpha_{j} I_{A_{j}}$ with $A_{j} \in \mathscr{A}$ and $\alpha_{j}$ real. Since $\mathscr{D}$ is a vector space $\mathscr{R}_{0} \subset \mathscr{D}$ and $\phi$ is positive linear on $\mathscr{R}_{0}$. Let $\mathscr{R}$ be the domain of the Eudoxus extension $\mathscr{E} u\left(\mathscr{R}_{0}, \phi\right)=\mathscr{D}\left[\mathscr{R}_{0}, \phi ; \mathscr{R}_{0}\right]$.

Proposition 5 (Loomis). If $H$ of $G$ or more generally if $\mathscr{D}$ is leveled then $\mathscr{A}$ is a ring and $\mathscr{R}$ is a leveled vector lattice. Let $f \in \mathscr{D}[H, \phi ; G]$ be a bounded function such that for some $v \in \mathscr{D}[H, \phi ; G]$ the inequality $f(x) \neq 0$ implies $v(x) \geqq 1$. Then $f \in \mathscr{R}$.

Proof. Let $A$ and $B$ denote two arbitrary elements of $\mathscr{A}$. Let $f=I_{A}+I_{B}$ and let $g=I_{A}-I_{B}$. Then $f \wedge 1=I_{A \cup B}$ and $f-f \wedge 1=I_{A \cup B}$. Also, $2 g-[(2 g) \wedge$ 1] $=I_{A \cup B^{c}}$. Thus $\mathscr{A}$ is a ring. It follows that $\mathscr{R}_{0}$ is a leveled lattice.

For the second statement suppose that $f$ and $v$ satisfy the conditions stated in the proposition. Let $b=\sup |f(x)|<\infty$. Let $\lambda_{j} ; j=0,1, \ldots, n+1$ be a finite sequence of numbers such that $0=\lambda_{0}<\lambda_{1}<\cdots<\lambda_{n}<\lambda_{n+1}=b$. Take these numbers in such a way that $A_{j}=\left\{x ; f(x)>\lambda_{j}\right\} \in \mathscr{A}$ for all $j>0$. Let $g$ and $h$ be the functions

$$
\begin{aligned}
g & =\sum_{j=1}^{n} \lambda_{j} I\left\{x ; \lambda_{j}<f(x) \leqq \lambda_{j+1}\right\} \\
h & =\sum_{j=1}^{n} \lambda_{j+1} I\left\{x ; \lambda_{j}<f(x) \leqq \lambda_{j+1}\right\}
\end{aligned}
$$

Then $g \leqq f^{+} \leqq h \leqq b v$. Also, $h-g \leqq\left[\max _{j}\left(\lambda_{j+1}-\lambda_{j}\right)\right] v$. It follows that $\phi(g-h) \leqq \phi(v) \max _{j}\left(\lambda_{j+1}-\lambda_{j}\right)$. According to Proposition 4 the differences $\lambda_{j+1}-\lambda_{j}$ can be made arbitrarily small. We conclude that $f^{+} \in \mathscr{D}\left[\mathscr{R}_{0}, \phi ; \mathscr{R}_{0}\right]$. The same argument applies to $f^{-}$; hence the proposition.

Remark 1. The preceding proposition shows that a bounded function $f$ satisfying the requirement that there is a $v$ such that $v(x) \geqq 1$ if $f(x) \neq 0$, belongs to $\mathscr{D}\left[\mathscr{R}_{0}, \phi ; \mathscr{R}_{0}\right]$ if and only if the sets $A_{\lambda}=\{x: f(x)>\lambda\}$ and $A_{\lambda}^{\prime}=\{x: f(x)<-\lambda\}$ belong to $\mathscr{A}$ for a dense set of values $\lambda>0$.

Remark 2. The function $\mu$ defined on the ring $\mathscr{A}$ by $\mu(a)=\phi\left(I_{A}\right)$ is a content, that is, a finitely additive positive set function. Proposition 5 shows that the values of $\phi$ on $\mathscr{R}$ can be recovered from $\mu$ by an integration procedure
using Lebesgue-type finite sums. However, the functions of $\mathscr{D}[H, \phi ; G]$ which are not $K$-functions do not necessarily belong to $\mathscr{R}$. For them the set function $\mu$ may be of very little relevance.

Remark 3. The functions $g$ and $h$ introduced in the proof of Proposition 5 can also be given another representation which is useful in many contexts. If $A_{j}=\{x: f(x)>\lambda\}$, then $g$ is also equal to

$$
g=\sum_{j=1}^{n} \alpha_{j} I_{A_{j}}
$$

with $\alpha_{j}=\lambda_{j}-\lambda_{j-1}>0$. Thus $\phi(g)$ is a sum of the type $\sum_{j=1}^{n} \alpha_{j} \phi\left(I_{A_{j}}\right)$ with $\alpha_{j}>0$. We shall use this for the case where the functions $f$ are continuous in which case the sets $A_{j}$ are open and $g$ is lower semicontinuous.

Remark 4. In the preceding arguments the vector space $H$ and the cone $G$ were left rather unrestricted. It is reasonable to expect, however, that extensions of positive linear functionals will give unrewarding results unless the set $H$ contains a large supply of positive functions. A very weak requirement of this type is the requirement that $H \subset \mathscr{E}_{\rho}$ or equivalently that for every $h \in H$ there is a $g \in G$ such that $|h| \leqq g$. Nevertheless, the reader will observe that such a requirement is not satisfied by the "improper Riemann integrals" of last century's calculus.

Remark 5. The preceding arguments use the fact that $H$ is a space of numerical functions on $\mathscr{X}$ only through the incidental allowance of infinite values for elements of $G$. This is both a concession to tradition and an allowance for simplicity in the choice of $G$. Otherwise the arguments can be carried for abstract ordered linear spaces. In such a setting it may be more appropriate to take instead of the closure of $H$ in $\mathscr{E}(G, \phi)$ the completion of $H$ for the pseudometric induced by $\rho$. Unless further restrictions are imposed on the triple $\{H, \phi, G\}$ the space $\mathscr{D}[H, \phi, G]$ cannot be expected to be complete, even if $H \subset \mathscr{E}_{\rho}$.

The procedure of extension described in Propositions 2 and 3 relies on the existence of "cones of distributivity" $G$ which are larger than $H$ itself. To show that the procedure does not automatically reduce to the Eudoxus method it remains to show that the cones $G$ can often be taken very substantially larger than $H$. The following proposition is a step in this direction.

Proposition 6. Let $G$ be an arbitrary cone of, distributivity and let $W$ be an arbitrary convex cone over $H$. Assume that $W$ is such that if $(-u) \in W, v \in W$ and $u \leqq v$ there is an $f \in \mathscr{D}[H, \phi ; G]$ such that $u \leqq f \leqq v$. Then the elements $u$ of $W$ such that $\tilde{\phi}(u)<\infty$ form a cone of distributivity.

Proof. It is sufficient to show that $\gamma_{i} \in W$ implies $\tilde{\phi}\left(\gamma_{1}+\gamma_{2}\right) \leqq \tilde{\phi}\left(\gamma_{1}\right)+ \tilde{\phi}\left(\gamma_{2}\right)$. Let $b$ be a number $b<\tilde{\phi}\left(\gamma_{1}+\gamma_{2}\right)$ and let $h \in H$ be such that $h \leqq \gamma_{1}+\gamma_{2}$ and $\phi(h) \geqq b$. The inequality $h \leqq \gamma_{1}+\gamma_{2}$ can also be written $h-\gamma_{1} \leqq \gamma_{2}$. Since $\gamma_{1}-h \in W$ and $\gamma_{2} \in W$, by assumption one can find an $\mathrm{f} \in \mathscr{D}[H, \phi, G]$ such that $h-\gamma_{1} \leqq f \leqq \gamma_{2}$ or equivalently $h \leqq \gamma_{1}+\underset{\sim}{f}$ and $f \leqq \gamma_{2}$.

This implies $\phi(f) \leqq \tilde{\phi}\left(\gamma_{2}\right)$ and $\phi(h-f) \leqq \tilde{\phi}\left(\gamma_{1}\right)$. By addition one obtains

$$
b \leqq \phi(h)=\phi(h-f)+\phi(f) \leqq \tilde{\phi}\left(\gamma_{1}\right)+\tilde{\phi}\left(\gamma_{2}\right)
$$

The result follows.
The preceding proposition, or even a weaker result, is already sufficient to provide extensions to sizable classes of functions. In particular, it covers the usual "representation by integrals with respect to Baire measures". Suppose that $H$ is a full vector lattice of functions on a set $\mathscr{X}$. Assume that $H$ separates the points of $\mathscr{X}$ and that the unit function $I$ belongs to $H$. Let $U_{\tau}$ (resp. $U_{\sigma}$ ) be the space of functions which are pointwise suprema of nonempty subsets of $H$ (resp. pointwise suprema of nonempty countable subsets of $H$ ). Let $L_{\tau}=-U_{\tau}$ and $L_{\sigma}=-U_{\sigma}$. Let $\mathscr{T}$ be the topology induced by $H$ on $\mathscr{X}$. Define a set $W, U_{\sigma} \subset W \subset U_{\tau}$ as follows. A function $g \in U_{\tau}$ belongs to $W$ if there is a compact $K$ of $\{\mathscr{X}, \mathscr{T}\}$ such that for each neighborhood $V$ of $K, g$ coincides on $V^{c}$ with an element of $U_{\sigma}$.

Lemma 4. Let $H$ be a full vector lattice of bounded functions such that $I \in H, H=U_{\sigma} \cap L_{\sigma}$ and such that $H$ separates the points of $\mathscr{X}$. Then the set of elements $g$ of $W$ such that $\tilde{\phi}(g)<\infty$ is a cone of distributivity. If the topology induced by $H$ is normal the set of elements $g$ of $U_{\tau}$ such that $\hat{\phi}(g)<\infty$ is also a cone of distributivity.

This follows immediately from the above Proposition 6 and Proposition 2 of Section 9. The lemma is applicable in particular to the case where $H$ is the set $C^{b}(\mathscr{X})$ of bounded continuous functions for a given completely regular topology on $\mathscr{X}$. In this case $H=U_{\sigma} \cap L_{\sigma}=U_{\tau} \cap L_{\tau}$. The family of sets $A$ such that $I_{A} \in \mathscr{D}[H, \phi: W]$ is a ring which contains at least the compact subsets of $\mathscr{X}$ and the zero sets $\mathscr{Z}$ of the type $\mathscr{Z}=\{x: f(x)=0\}$ for $f \in H$. When $\mathscr{X}$ is not completely regular and $H=C^{b}(\mathscr{X})$ the topology $\mathscr{T}$ induced by $H$ is strictly weaker than the original topology of $\mathscr{X}$. Identifying points which are not separated by $H$, the results still apply to this topology instead of the original one. If on the contrary $\mathscr{X}$ is a normal space and $H=C^{b}(\mathscr{X})$, one can take $U_{\tau}$ instead of $W$ and the ring obtainable fron $\mathscr{D}\left[H, \phi: U_{\tau}\right]$ contains all the open sets and all the closed sets.

Another remark which may occasionally be useful in ascertaining the existence of large domains of extensions is the following easy proposition.

Proposition 7. Let $V$ be a convex cone over $H$. If $G_{0} \subset V$ is a cone of distributivity of $\tilde{\phi}$ there exists a maximal subset of $V$, say $G$, which is a cone of distributivity and is such that $G_{0} \subset G$. For such a maximal cone $G$ :
(i) the relations $g_{i} \in V, g_{1}+g_{2} \in G$, and $\tilde{\phi}\left(g_{1}+g_{2}\right) \leqq \tilde{\phi}\left(g_{1}\right)+\tilde{\phi}\left(g_{2}\right)$ imply $g_{i} \in G$,
(ii) if $H$ and $V$ are lattices, so is $G$,
(iii) if $H$ and $V$ are leveled, so is $G$.

Proof. The existence of maximal cones of distributivity follows from an immediate application of Zorn's lemma. To prove the remainder suppose that $g_{1}+g_{2} \in G$ and that $h \in H, h \leqq g_{1}$. Then $\tilde{\phi}\left(g_{1}\right)+\tilde{\phi}(h)=\tilde{\phi}\left(g_{1}+h\right) \leqq \tilde{\phi}\left(g_{1}+g_{2}\right)<\infty$. Therefore $\tilde{\phi}\left(g_{i}\right)<\infty$. Furthermore if $U$ is an arbitrary element of $G$, one can write

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tilde{\phi}\left[\left(u+g_{1}\right)+g_{2}\right] & \geqq \tilde{\phi}\left(u+g_{1}\right)+\tilde{\phi}\left(g_{2}\right) \\
& \geqq \tilde{\phi}(u)+\tilde{\phi}\left(g_{1}\right)+\tilde{\phi}\left(g_{2}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

By assumption the left-hand side can also be written $\tilde{\phi}\left[u+\left(g_{1}+g_{2}\right)\right]= \tilde{\phi}(u)+\tilde{\phi}\left(g_{1}+g_{2}\right)$ and so can the right-hand side. Subtracting the finite quantity $\tilde{\phi}\left(g_{2}\right)$ one obtains $\tilde{\phi}\left(u+g_{1}\right)=\tilde{\phi}(u)+\tilde{\phi}\left(g_{1}\right)$, hence $g_{1} \in G$ since $G$ is maximal.

To prove (ii), let $h_{i}, i=1,2$ be elements of $H$ such that $h_{i} \leqq g_{i}$. Then $h_{1} \wedge h_{2} \leqq g_{1} \wedge g_{2}$ and $h_{1} \vee h_{2} \leqq g_{1} \vee g_{2}$. Since $\tilde{\phi}$ is monotone this gives

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tilde{\phi}\left(g_{1} \wedge g_{2}\right)+\tilde{\phi}\left(g_{1} \vee g_{2}\right) & \geqq \tilde{\phi}\left(h_{1} \wedge h_{2}\right)+\tilde{\phi}\left(h_{1} \vee h_{2}\right) \\
& =\phi\left[\left(h_{1} \wedge h_{2}\right)+\left(h_{1} \vee h_{2}\right)\right] \\
& =\phi\left(h_{1}+h_{2}\right)=\phi\left(h_{1}\right)+\phi\left(h_{2}\right) .
\end{aligned}
$$

Taking suprema over $h_{1}$ and $h_{2}$ separately yields

$$
\tilde{\phi}\left(g_{1} \wedge g_{2}\right)+\tilde{\phi}\left(g_{1} \vee g_{2}\right) \geqq \tilde{\phi}\left(g_{1}\right)+\tilde{\phi}\left(g_{2}\right)
$$

If $g_{i} \in G$, the right-hand side is equal to $\tilde{\phi}\left(g_{1}+g_{2}\right)$ and therefore to $\tilde{\phi}\left[\left(g_{1} \wedge g_{2}\right)+\left(g_{1} \vee g_{2}\right)\right]$. The result follows now from part (i).

Similarly, assume that $V$ is leveled and let $g$ be an element of $G$. Let $h \in H$ be such that $h \leqq g$. Then $g=(g \wedge 1)+[g-(g \wedge 1)]$ with $h \wedge 1 \leqq g \wedge 1$ and $h-(h \wedge 1) \leqq g-(g \wedge 1)$. Since $H$ is a leveled vector space we have $\underset{\sim}{h} \wedge 1 \in H$ and $h-(h \wedge 1) \in H$. Also $g-(g \wedge 1) \leqq g-(h \wedge 1)$; hence $\tilde{\phi}[g-(g \wedge 1)] \leqq \tilde{\phi}(g)-\phi(h \wedge 1)<\infty$.

These inequalities imply

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tilde{\phi}(g) & =\sup _{h}\{\phi(h \wedge 1)+\phi[h-(h \wedge 1)] ; h \in H, h \leqq g\} \\
& =\tilde{\phi}(g \wedge 1)+\tilde{\phi}[g-(g \wedge 1)]
\end{aligned}
$$

The result would then follow from (i) if $g-(g \wedge 1) \in V$. If not, let $u$ be an arbitrary element of $G$. Then

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tilde{\phi}(u+g) & \geqq \tilde{\phi}[u+(g \wedge 1)]+\tilde{\phi}[g-(g \wedge 1)] \\
& \geqq \tilde{\phi}(u)+\tilde{\phi}(g \wedge 1)+\tilde{\phi}[g-(g \wedge 1)] \\
& =\tilde{\phi}(u)+\tilde{\phi}(g)=\tilde{\phi}(u+g)
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore $\tilde{\phi}[u+(g \wedge 1)]=\tilde{\phi}(u)+\tilde{\phi}(g \wedge 1)$ for every $u \in G$. The result follows from the maximality of $G$.

According to this result, whenever $H$ is a lattice or whenever $H$ is leveled, one can impose the same condition on cones of distributivity without impairing their size.

All of this holds, of course, without any specific restriction on the functional $\phi$. It turns out, however, that many of the functionals of interest do satisfy continuity restrictions which allow derivation of properties stronger than the ones obtained so far in this section. A class of continuity restrictions will be
described and studied in the next section. For the present we shall only give a very brief account of some basic features of the Daniell theory of integration which are related to the subject of this section. As usual, we shall assume that $H$ is a vector space of functions on a set $\mathscr{X}$ and that $\phi$ is a positive linear functional on $H$.

Definition 4. Let $V$ be a convex cone over $H$. Let $G$ be a cone distributivity which is maximal in $V$. We shall say that the system $\{H, \phi, G, V\}$ is of Lebesgue type if $H$ and $V$ are lattices and if for every sequence $\left\{g_{n}\right\}, g_{n} \in G$ such that $g_{n}$ increases pointwise to $g$, the limit $g$ belongs to $V$ and $\tilde{\phi}\left(g_{n}\right)$ converges to $\tilde{\phi}(g)$.

The following result is fundamental. It is essentially a version of Fatou's lemma.

Theorem 1. Let $\{H, \phi, G, V\}$ be a system of Lebesgue type. Let $\left\{f_{n}\right\}$ be an arbitrary sequence of non-negative functions defined on $\mathscr{X}$. The following relations hold:
(a) If $\left\{f_{n}\right\}$ increases pointwise to $f$ then $\rho(f)=\sup \rho\left(f_{n}\right)$.
(b) If $\left\{f_{n}\right\}$ increases pointwise to $f$ and $f_{n} \in \mathscr{D}[H, \phi, G]$, then $f \in \mathscr{D}[H, \phi, G]$ if and only if $\sup \rho\left(f_{n}\right)<\infty$.
(c) Without any assumptions on $f_{n}$ (except that $f_{n} \geqq 0$ ) one has

$$
\rho\left[\liminf _{n} f_{n}\right] \leqq \liminf \rho\left(f_{n}\right)
$$

Proof. It follows from the definition of $\rho$ that $0 \leqq f_{n} \leqq f$ implies $\sup \rho\left(f_{n}\right) \leqq \rho(f)$. Thus it is sufficient to prove (a) under the assumption that $\sup _{n} \rho\left(f_{n}\right)< \infty$. For each integer $n$ let $g_{n} \in C$ be such that $f_{n} \leqq g_{n}$ and

$$
\rho\left(f_{n}\right) \leqq g_{n} \leqq \rho\left(f_{n}\right)+\varepsilon 2^{-n}
$$

Let $h_{n}=\sup \left\{g_{j} ; j \leqq n\right\}$. Then $h_{n} \in G$ and $f_{n} \leqq g_{n} \leqq h_{n}$. Furthermore $h_{n} \leqq h_{n+1}$ and $h_{n} \wedge g_{n+1} \geqq f_{n} \wedge f_{n+1}=f_{n}$. Let us show that

$$
\rho\left(h_{m}\right) \leqq \rho\left(g_{m}\right)+\varepsilon\left(1-2^{-m}\right)
$$

This is true by assumption for $m=1$. If it is true for $m=1,2, \ldots, n$ one can write $h_{n+1}=h_{n} \vee g_{n+1}$ and

$$
h_{n}+g_{n+1}=\left(h_{n} \vee g_{n+1}\right)+\left(h_{n} \wedge g_{n+1}\right)=h_{n+1}+\left(h_{n} \wedge g_{n+1}\right)
$$

Since $\tilde{\phi}(f)=\rho(f)$ for $f \geqq 0, f \in G$ and since $\tilde{\phi}$ is distributive on $G$, this gives

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho\left(h_{n+1}\right)+\rho\left(h_{n} \wedge g_{n+1}\right) & =\rho\left(h_{n}\right)+\rho\left(g_{n+1}\right) \\
& \leqq \rho\left(f_{n}\right)+\varepsilon\left(1-2^{-n}\right)+\rho\left(f_{n+1}\right)+\varepsilon 2^{-(n+1)}
\end{aligned}
$$

On the left-hand side of this inequality $h_{n} \wedge g_{n+1} \geqq f_{n}$. The desired inequality is therefore obtainable by subtraction of $\rho\left(f_{n}\right)$ from both sides.

One concludes from this inequality that

$$
\sup _{n} \rho\left(f_{n}\right) \leqq \sup _{n} \rho\left(h_{n}\right) \leqq \sup _{n} \rho\left(f_{n}\right)+\varepsilon .
$$

However, by assumption, $\sup \rho\left(h_{n}\right)=\rho\left(\sup h_{n}\right) \geqq \rho(f)$ and (a) follows. The fact that $g_{n} \in G, g_{n} \geqq 0, g_{n} \uparrow g$ and $\tilde{\phi}(g)<\infty$ implies $g \in G$ is now a consequence of the maximality of $G$. Indeed, for $u \in G$ if $u+g_{n} \uparrow u+g$ then $\tilde{\phi}\left(u+g_{n}\right)=\tilde{\phi}(u)+\tilde{\phi}\left(g_{n}\right)$ and, by passage to the limit, $\tilde{\phi}(u+g)=\tilde{\phi}(u)+\tilde{\phi}(g)$. Suppose now that $f_{n} \in \mathscr{D}[H, \phi, G]$ and that $f_{n} \uparrow f$ such that $\rho(f)<\infty$. For $\varepsilon>0$ let $g \in G$ be such that $f \leqq g$ and $\tilde{\phi}(g)<\rho(f)+\varepsilon$. Then

$$
\rho\left(f-f_{n}\right) \leqq \rho\left(g-f_{n}\right)=\phi(g)-\phi\left(f_{n}\right) \leqq \rho(f)-\rho\left(f_{n}\right)+\varepsilon .
$$

Since $\varepsilon$ is arbitrary this gives $\rho\left(f-f_{n}\right) \leqq \rho(f)-\rho\left(f_{n}\right)$. According to the preceding argument $\rho\left(f_{n}\right) \uparrow \rho(f)$ and therefore $\rho\left(f-f_{n}\right) \rightarrow 0$ so that $f \in \mathscr{D}[H, \phi, G]$.

To obtain statement (c) let $s_{n}=\inf _{m \geqq n} f_{m}$. Then $s_{n}$ increases to $s= \liminf _{n} f_{n}$. Therefore

$$
\liminf _{n} \rho\left(f_{n}\right) \geqq \liminf _{n} \rho\left(s_{n}\right)=\rho(s)
$$

This concludes the proof of the theorem.
Note. The results stated in the above theorem extend to the case where sequences are replaced by filters with countable bases. However, they do not extend to arbitrary filters. On the other hand, the topology of pointwise convergence used in Definition 4 and in Theorem 1 can be replaced by a variety of other topologies, some of which will be studied in Section 11. The only properties used are properties of compatibility with the linear and lattice structures of $H, G$, and $V$.

A similar remark applies to the next theorem, called the Lebesgue dominated convergence theorem. One of the implications of this theorem is that continuity for monotone sequences implies continuity for convergent lattically bounded sequences.

Theorem 2. Assume that $\{H, \phi, G, V\}$ is a system of Lebesgue type. Let $\left\{f_{n}\right\}$ be a sequence of elements of $\mathscr{D}[H, \phi, G]$ which is such that $f_{n}$ converges pointwise to $f$ and such that for each $\varepsilon>0$ there is a $g \in G, g \geqq 0$ for which $\rho\left[\left|f_{n}\right|-\left|f_{n}\right| \wedge g\right]<\varepsilon$. Then $f \in \mathscr{D}[H, \phi, G]$ and $\rho\left(f_{n}-f\right) \rightarrow 0$. In particular, $\phi\left(f_{n}\right) \rightarrow \phi(f)$.

Proof. Let $\varepsilon$ and $g$ satisfy the stated relations, Let $u_{n}=\left[g \wedge f_{n}\right] \vee[-g]$. Then $u_{n}$ converges pointwise to $u=[g \wedge f] \wedge[-g]$. By Fatou's lemma,

$$
\rho(u-f) \leqq \liminf _{n} \rho\left(u_{n}-f_{n}\right) \leqq \varepsilon .
$$

Thus it is sufficient to prove the result under the stronger assumption $\left|f_{n}\right| \leqq g$. Assume this and let $s_{n}=\inf _{k \geqq n} f_{k}$ and $t_{n}=\sup _{k \geqq n} f_{k}$. According to Theorem 1 both $s_{n}$ and $t_{n}$ belong to $\mathscr{D}[H, \phi, G]$. Since $s_{n}$ increases to $f$, Theorem 1 implies that $f \in \mathscr{D}[H, \phi, G]$ and that $\rho\left(f-s_{n}\right) \rightarrow 0$. Reversing signs,
it follows that $\rho\left(t_{n}-f\right) \rightarrow 0$ and therefore that $\rho\left(f_{n}-f\right) \leqq \rho\left(t_{n}-s_{n}\right) \leqq \rho\left(t_{n}-f\right)+\rho\left(f-s_{n}\right)$ tends to zero. This completes the proof of the theorem.

One of the notable consequences of Theorem 2 is the Riesz-Fischer theorem which states that for a system $\{H, \phi, G, V\}$ of Lebesgue type the space $\mathscr{D}[H, \phi, G]$ is complete for the seminorm $\rho$. This can be seen as follows. Let $\left\{f_{n}\right\}$ be a Cauchy sequence, One can assume $\sup _{m}\left\{\rho\left(f_{n}-f_{m}\right) ; m \geqq n\right\}< 2^{-n}$. Appropriate application of Theorem 1 shows that $f^{\prime}=\liminf _{n} f_{n}$ and $\limsup _{n} f_{n}=f^{\prime \prime}$ belong to $\mathscr{D}[H, \phi, G]$ and that $\rho\left(f^{\prime \prime}-f^{\prime}\right)=0$ so that $\rho\left(f_{n}-f^{\prime}\right) \rightarrow$ 0.

The completeness of $\mathscr{D}[H, \phi, G]$ is very closely related to the Lebesgue property but does not imply it. An example can be constructed as follows. Take for $\mathscr{X}$ the integers $\mathscr{X}=\{1,2,3, \ldots\}$. Let $H$ be the space of functions $h$ defined on $\mathscr{X}$ and such that as $n \rightarrow \infty$ the product $n h(n)$ converges to a finite limit. Let $\phi(h)=\lim n h(n)$. Take $V=H$ so that the space $\mathscr{D}[H, \phi, G]$ is the Eudoxus extension $\mathscr{E} u(H, \phi)$. This is complete but $\phi$ does not possess the Lebesgue property.

Definition 5. Let $H$ be a vector lattice of numerical functions on a set $\mathscr{X}$ and let $\phi$ be a linear functional defined on $H$. One says that $\phi$ is pointwise $\sigma$-smooth on $\{H, \mathscr{X}\}$ if for every sequence $\left\{h_{n}\right\}$ of elements of $H$ which decreases to zero pointwise on $\mathscr{X}$ the values $\phi\left(h_{n}\right)$ converge to zero.

One says that $\phi$ is pointwise $\tau$-smooth on $\{H, \mathscr{X}\}$ if the $\phi\left(h_{\nu}\right) \rightarrow 0$ for every decreasingly directed family $\left\{h_{\nu}\right\}$ of elements of $H$ tending to zero pointwise on $H$.

This definition is an important special case of the definitions to be found in Section 11. We shall also refer to positive pointwise $\sigma$-smooth functionals as Daniell functionals or even Daniell integrals. The positive pointwise $\tau$-smooth functionals will be called MacShane functionals or integrals.

Let $U_{\sigma}$ be the usual class of suprema of nonempty countable subsets of $H$ and let $U_{\tau}$ be the class of suprema of arbitrary nonempty subsets of $H$.

Lemma 5. Let $\phi$ be a Daniell functional on the vector lattice $H$. Then $\tilde{\phi}$ is distributive on $U_{\sigma}$ and $u_{n} \in U_{\sigma}, u_{n} \uparrow u$ implies $u \in U_{\sigma}$ and $\tilde{\phi}\left(u_{n}\right) \uparrow \tilde{\phi}(u)$.

Similarly, let $\phi$ be a MacShane functional on the lattice $H$. Then $\tilde{\phi}$ is distribu-
tive on $U_{\tau}$ and for every increasingly directed set $\left\{u_{\nu}\right\}, u_{\tau} \in U_{\tau}$ such that $u_{\nu} \uparrow u$ one has $u \in U_{\tau}$ and $\tilde{\phi}(u)=\sup _{v} \phi\left(u_{v}\right)$.

Proof. Since this can be found in most texts we shall only sketch the proof of the second statement.

Let $A_{v}=\left\{h ; h \in H, h \leqq u_{v}\right\}$. Let $A=\bigcup_{v} A_{v}$. Then $A$ is increasingly directed and $u=\sup \{h, h \in A\}$. Suppose that $b<\tilde{\phi}(u)$ and that $\gamma \in H$ is such that $\gamma \leqq u$ and $\phi(\gamma) \geqq b$. Consider $\gamma \wedge h$ for $h \in A$. Then $\sup \{\gamma \wedge h ; h \in A\}=\gamma$ and, by assumption $\sup _{h} \phi(\gamma \wedge h)=\phi(\gamma) \geqq b$. However, if $h \in A_{\nu}$ then $\gamma \wedge h \leqq u_{\nu}$. Thus $\sup [\phi(\gamma \wedge h) ; h \in A] \leqq \sup \tilde{\phi}\left(u_{v}\right)$. The result follows. The proof of the first statement is similar. The distributivity follows from the convergence property.

This lemma implies in particular that for a Daniell functional $\phi$ one can take



for cone of distributivity the set $G$ of elements $g \in U_{\sigma}$ such that $\tilde{\phi}(g)<\infty$. The domain $\mathscr{D}\left[H, \phi, G, U_{\sigma}\right]$ obtained in this fashion will be called the domain of the Daniell extension of $\{H, \phi\}$.

The importance of this particular extension derives from the fact that Theorems 1 and 2 apply to it. In addition the Daniell domain has the following remarkable property.

Lemma 6. Let $\phi$ be a Daniell functional on $H$. The Daniell extension of $\phi$ to the domain $\mathscr{D}=\mathscr{D}\left[H, \phi, G, U_{\sigma}\right]$ is uniquely determined by the properties of being linear positive and pointwise $\sigma$-smooth on $\{\mathscr{D}, \mathscr{X}\}$.

If $u \notin \mathscr{D}$ is a function such that $|u| \leqq f$ for some $f \in \mathscr{D}$ and $\mathscr{D}^{\prime}$ is the linear space spanned by $u$ and $\mathscr{D}$, there exist several distinct positive pointwise $\sigma$ smooth linear extensions of $\phi$ to $\mathscr{D}^{\prime}$.

The proof is straightforward and will be left to the reader. To simplify the notation let $\mathscr{L}[H, \phi]$ be the Daniell space $\mathscr{D}\left[H, \phi, G, U_{\sigma}\right]$ attached to the Daniell functional $\phi$ on $H$. The preceding lemma has the following easy consequence.

Corollary. Let $H_{i}, i=1,2$ be two vector lattices on a set $\mathscr{X}$. Let $\phi_{i}$ be a Daniell functional on $H_{i}$ and let $\mathscr{L}\left(H_{i}, \phi_{i}\right)$ be the corresponding Daniell spaces.

Assume that
(1) $H_{1} \subset \mathscr{L}\left(H_{2}, \phi_{2}\right)$ and $H_{2} \subset \mathscr{L}\left(H_{1}, \phi_{1}\right)$.
(2) On $H_{1}$ the functional $\phi_{1}$ coincides with the extension of $\phi_{2}$ and on $H_{2}$ the functional $\phi_{2}$ coincides with the extension of $\phi_{1}$.
Then $\mathscr{L}\left(H_{1}, \phi_{1}\right)=\mathscr{L}\left(H_{2}, \phi_{2}\right)$ and the extensions of the $\phi_{i}$ coincide.
Proof. Since $H_{1} \subset \mathscr{L}\left(H_{2}, \phi_{2}\right)=\mathscr{L}\left(\mathscr{L}\left(H_{2}, \phi_{2}\right), \phi_{2}\right)$ it follows that $\mathscr{L}\left(H_{1}, \phi_{2}\right) \subset \mathscr{L}\left(H_{2}, \phi_{2}\right)$. The agreement of $\phi_{1}$ and $\phi_{2}$ on $H_{1}$ implies $\mathscr{L}\left(H_{1}, \phi_{2}\right)=\mathscr{L}\left(H_{1}, \phi_{1}\right)$. The result follows.

A particular consequence of this corollary is that, on the real line, description of probability measures through probabilities attached to intervals and through expectations of bounded continuous functions are equivalent. The Eudoxus domain $\mathscr{E} u(H, \phi)$ is usually much smaller than $\mathscr{L}(H, \phi)$. We shall need the following characterization of $\mathscr{E} u(H, \phi)$.

Proposition 8. Let $\phi$ be a Daniell or MacShane functional on the lattice H . Define the seminorm $\rho$ from the cone $U_{\sigma}$ in the Daniell case and from the cone $U_{\tau}$ in the MacShane case. Let $f$ be a numerical function such that $|f| \leqq h_{0}$ for some $h_{0} \in H$. Then $f \in \mathscr{E} u(H, \phi)$ if and only if there are functions $u \in U_{\sigma}$ and $v \in-U_{\sigma}$ such that $u \leqq f \leqq v$ and $\rho(v-u)=0$.

If $\phi$ has MacShane's property, this condition is already implied by the existence of $u \in U_{\tau}$ and $v \in-U_{\tau}$ such that $u \leqq f \leqq v$ and $\rho(v-u)=0$.

Proof. If $f \in \mathscr{E} u(H, \phi)$ for each integer $n$ there is a pair $\left(u_{n}, v_{n}\right) u_{n} \in H, v_{n} \in H$ such that $u_{n} \leqq f \leqq v_{n}$ and $\rho\left(v_{n}-u_{n}\right)<2^{-n}$. One can assume that $u_{n}$ increases with $n$ and that $v_{n}$ decreases as $n$ increases. Let $u=\sup _{n} u_{n}$ and $v=\inf v_{n}$. Then $u \in U_{\sigma}, v \in\left(-U_{\sigma}\right)$, and $u \leqq f \leqq v$ and $\rho(v-u)=0$.

Conversely suppose that $u \leqq f \leqq v, u \in U_{\tau}, v \in-U_{\tau}$. Suppose that $\phi$ has MacShane's property and that $\rho(v-u)=0$. Let $\left\{u_{v}\right\}$ be an increasingly directed family $u_{v} \in H$ such that $\sup u_{v}=u$. Let $\left\{v_{v}\right\}$ be a decreasingly directed family $v_{v} \in H$ such that $\inf v_{v}=v$. Then $v_{v}-u_{v}$ decreases to $v-u$. Lemma 5 applied to $\tilde{\phi}\left(u_{v}-v_{v}\right)=-\rho\left(v_{v}-u_{v}\right)$ shows that $\rho\left(v_{v}-u_{v}\right)$ converges
to $\rho(v-u)=0$. This argument also applies to the Daniell case provided that $u \in U_{\sigma}$ and $-v \in U_{\sigma}$ and provided that one selects for the directed families $\left\{u_{v}\right\}$ and $\left\{v_{v}\right\}$ sequences or filters with countable bases. Hence the result.
