Remark 1. According to this proposition if $\phi$ has Daniell's property then $U_{\sigma} \cap\left(-U_{\sigma}\right) \subset \mathscr{E} u(H, \phi)$. If $\phi$ has MacShane's property then $U_{\tau} \cap\left(-U_{\tau}\right) \subset \mathscr{E} u(H, \phi)$.

Remark 2. Daniell's property carries over from $H$ to $U_{\sigma} \cap\left(-U_{\sigma}\right)$ and MacShane's property carries over from $H$ to $U_{\tau} \cap\left(-U_{\tau}\right)$. However, Daniell's property carries over to $\mathscr{E} u(H, \phi)$ but MacShane's property does not.

In spite of the fact that the domain of extensions $\mathscr{L}(H, \phi)=\mathscr{D}\left[H, \phi, G, U_{\sigma}\right]$ of a Daniell functional seems to be obtained through countable operations only, it is a very large domain indeed. One particular result needed in the sequel is that the class of measurable functions is closed under application of the operation " $A$ ".

To show this we shall assume, for the remainder of this section, that $H$ is a leveled lattice.

If $\phi$ is $\sigma$-smooth and positive on a leveled vector lattice $H$ then $\phi$ is already well defined by its restriction to the bounded elements of $H$. Further, $\phi$ is well defined by its restriction to the space $\widehat{\mathscr{K}}$ of $K$-functions of $H$ since every pointwise bounded positive element of $H$ is the limit of an increasing sequence of $K$-functions. The following results are almost obvious.

Lemma 7. If $H$ is a leveled lattice so is the Daniell space $\mathscr{L}=\mathscr{L}(H, \phi)$. For every $\lambda>0$ and every $f \in \mathscr{L}$ the indicator $I\{x: f(x)>\lambda\}$ belongs to $\mathscr{L}$. The indicator $I\{x:|f(x)|=\infty\}$ is negligible. Two extended numerical functions $f$ and $g$ are equivalent in the sense that $\rho(f-g)=0$ if and only if $I\{x: f(x) \neq g(x)\}$ is negligible.

Lemma 8. If $H$ is a leveled lattice the product th of $f \in \mathscr{L}$ by a bounded element $h$ of $H$ is again an element of $\mathscr{L}$. If $\gamma$ is a bounded numerical function such that $\gamma h \in \mathscr{L}$ for every $h \in H_{0}$ then $\gamma f \in \mathscr{L}$ for every $f \in \mathscr{L}$.

Proof. If $h$ is a positive $K$-function the uniform closure of the interval $[-h, h]$ belongs to $\mathscr{L}$. Hence the product of two elements of $H_{0}$ is an element of $\mathscr{L}$. The result follows from the inequality

$$
\rho[f-\gamma h] \leqq\|\gamma\| \rho(f-h)
$$

and the fact that $\mathscr{L}$ is the completion of $\widehat{\mathscr{K}}$ for the seminorm $\rho$.
For reasons which can easily be understood by reverting to the locally compact space $T$ defined by $\mathscr{X}$ and $\widehat{\mathscr{K}}$, we shall say that a function $f$ defined on $\mathscr{X}$ is locally integrable if for every $h \in \widehat{\mathscr{K}}$ the product $f h$ belongs to $\mathscr{L}$. Similarly $f$ will be called locally bounded (resp. locally negligible) if for every $h \in \hat{\mathscr{K}}$ the product $f h$ is bounded (resp. negligible).

Lemma 9. Let $\left\{f_{v}\right\}$ be a directed system of locally integrable functions. Assume that there is a function $f$ such that $\rho\left[\left(f_{v}-f\right) h\right] \rightarrow 0$ for every $h \in \widehat{\mathscr{K}}$. Then $f$ is locally integrable.

It follows from the definition that the locally bounded locally integrable functions form an algebra. Lemma 9 implies that these functions also form a
lattice. One concludes from this that a locally bounded positive function is locally integrable if and only if $f \wedge h \in \mathscr{L}$ for every $h$ in the positive cone of $\hat{\mathscr{K}}$.

For some purposes it is necessary to use a result which goes somewhat deeper than the usual countable passages to the limit. This result is connected with the theory of "analytic sets" and the interversion of operators sup and inf carried on countable sets.

Consider first a double sequence $\left\{a_{m, n} ; m=1,2, \ldots, n=1,2, \ldots\right\}$ of extended real numbers and suppose that one desires to find the extended real $a=\inf _{m} \sup _{n} a_{m, n}$. This can be accomplished by interversion of the operations inf and sup as follows. Let $Z$ be the space of sequences $z=\left\{z_{1}, z_{2}, \ldots\right\}$ of positive integers. It is easily seen that

$$
a=\inf _{m} \sup _{n} a_{m, n}=\sup _{z \in Z} \inf _{m} a_{m, z_{m}}
$$

This can also be stated in a slightly different form. For $z \in Z$ denote by $\bar{z}_{k}$ the $k$-tuple $\bar{z}_{k}=\left\{z_{1}, z_{2}, \ldots, z_{k}\right\}$ and let $f\left(\bar{z}_{k}\right)$ be equal to $\inf _{m \leqq k} a_{m, z_{m}}$. Then $a=\sup _{z} \inf _{m} f\left(\bar{z}_{m}\right)$.

By analogy, suppose given for each $k$-tuple $\bar{z}_{k}=\left\{z_{1}, z_{2}, \ldots, z_{k}\right\}$ an extended numerical function $f\left(\bar{z}_{k}\right)$ defined on the set $\mathscr{X}$. The function

$$
f=\sup _{z \in Z} \inf _{k} f\left(\bar{z}_{k}\right)
$$

is called the result of operation (A) applied to the system of functions $\left\{f\left(\bar{z}_{k}\right) ; k=1,2, \ldots ; z \in Z\right\}$.

Proposition 8. For each integer $k$ and each $k$-tuple $\bar{z}_{k}=\left\{z_{1}, z_{2}, \ldots, z_{k}\right\}$ of positive integers let $f\left(\bar{z}_{k}\right)$ be an extended numerical function defined on $\mathscr{X}$. Let

$$
f=\sup _{z \in Z} \inf _{k} f\left(\bar{z}_{k}\right)
$$

Assume that each $f\left(\bar{z}_{k}\right)$ is locally integrable and that $f$ is locally bounded. Then $f$ is locally integrable.

The proof will not be given here. It can be carried out by mimicking Saks, Theory of the Integral, page 47.

To conclude this section it remains to mention the very important theorem due to Lebesgue and Fubini on repeated integrals. Let $\mathscr{X}$ and $\mathscr{Y}$ be two sets carrying leveled vector lattices, $H$ and $K$, respectively. Let $\mu$ (resp. $v$ ) be a positive $\sigma$-smooth linear functional on $H$ (resp. K).

If $f$ is defined on $\mathscr{X} \times \mathscr{Y}$ and the function $f_{x}$ defined by $y \rightarrow f(x, y)$ is in $\mathscr{L}(K, v)$ denote $\int f(x, y) v(d y)$ the value $v\left(f_{x}\right)$ of $v$ at $f_{x}$. If in turn the function $v(f)$ defined by $x \rightarrow v\left(f_{x}\right)$ is an element of $\mathscr{L}(H, \mu)$ let $\int \mu(d x) \int f(x, y) v(d y)$ be the value of $\mu$ at $v(f)$.

Theorem. Let $J$ be a vector lattice of numerical functions on $\mathscr{X} \times \mathscr{Y}$. Assume that the following properties hold:
(a) Except for values of $x$ which belong to a $\mu$-negligible set, the function $y \rightarrow f(x, y)$ belongs to $\mathscr{L}(K, v)$. Except for values of $y$ which belong to a $v$-negligible
set, the function $x \rightarrow f(x, y)$ belongs to $\mathscr{L}(H, \mu)$.
(b) The two iterated integrals

$$
\int \mu(d x) \int f(x, y) v(d y)
$$

and

$$
\int v(d y) \int f(x, y) \mu(d x)
$$

exist and are equal. Then there exists a uniquely defined Daniell function $\lambda$ on $J$ such that (a) holds for every $f \in \mathscr{L}(J, \lambda)$ and such that
(c)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\langle\lambda, f\rangle & =\int \mu(d x) \int f(x, y) v(d y) \\
& =\int v(d y) \int f(x, y) \mu(d x) \quad \text { for all } f \in \mathscr{L}(J, \lambda)
\end{aligned}
$$

Proof. The equality (c) defines $\lambda$ on $J$. It is clear that $\lambda$ is positive linear and pointwise $\sigma$-smooth on $J$. If $g$ belongs to the $U_{\sigma}$ class on $J$, let $\left\{f_{n}\right\}$ be a sequence of elements of $J$ increasing pointwise to $g$. Then, for $x$ fixed, $f_{n}(x,.) \uparrow g(x,$.$) .Henceforalmostevery x \in \mathscr{X}$ the function $g(x,$.$) belongstothe U_{\sigma}$ class on $\mathscr{L}(K, v)$ and

$$
v[g(x, .)]=\lim _{n} v\left[f_{n}(x, .)\right]
$$

Since, by assumption (b), $v\left[f_{n}(x).,\right]$ belongs to $\mathscr{L}(H, \mu)$, it follows that

$$
\lambda^{*}(g)=\mu^{*}\left[v^{*}(g)\right]=\lim _{n} \mu\left[v\left(f_{n}\right)\right]=\lim v\left[\mu\left(f_{n}\right)\right]=v^{*}\left[\mu^{*}(g)\right]
$$

If $\mu^{*}\left[v^{*}(g)\right]$ is finite then $v^{*}(g)$ must be almost everywhere finite, so that $g(x,.) \in \mathscr{L}(K, v)$ for almost every $x$.

Suppose now that $h \in \mathscr{L}(J, \lambda)$. For an $\varepsilon>0$ let $f \in L_{\sigma}(J)$ and $g \in U_{\sigma}(J)$ be such that $f \leqq k \leqq g$ and $\lambda^{*}(g)<\lambda_{*}(f)+\varepsilon^{2}$. This implies $\lambda^{*}(g-f)<\varepsilon^{2}$ hence $\mu^{*}\left[v^{*}(g-f)\right]<\varepsilon^{2}$. It follows that

$$
\mu^{*}\left\{I\left\{x ; v^{*}[g(x, .)-f(x, .)]>\varepsilon\right\}\right\}<\varepsilon
$$

Letting $\varepsilon$ decrease to zero one concludes that the set of values of $x$ where $h(x, \cdot)$ does not belong to $\mathscr{L}(K, v)$ is $\mu$-negligible. The result follows by symmetry.

Let $H$ be a leveled vector lattice on the set $\mathscr{X}$ and let $\phi$ be a positive $\sigma$ smooth linear functional on $H$. Let $\mathscr{L}$ be the space of integrable functions on $\{H, \mathscr{X}, \phi\}$ and let $\mathscr{M}$ be the space of locally bounded locally integrable functions on $\{H, \mathscr{X}, \phi\}$. The function which to $u \in \mathscr{M}$ and $f \in \mathscr{L}$ associates the number $\langle u, f\rangle=\phi(u, f)$ is a bilinear function on $\mathscr{M} \times \mathscr{L}$. Further, if $N$ is
the seminorm defined on $\mathscr{L}$ as above and $\|u\|$ is the seminorm $\|u\|=$ infimum of numbers $\alpha$ such that $\{x: u(x)>\alpha\}$ is negligible, then

$$
|\langle u, f\rangle| \leqq\|u\| N(f) .
$$

Thus the bilinear form in question is continuous on the product $\mathscr{M} \times \mathscr{L}$. Instead of $\mathscr{M}$ and $\mathscr{L}$ it is more convenient to use the spaces $M$ and $L$ obtained by identifying to zero functions which have vanishing seminorms. For the equivalence classes so obtained we shall use the same notations $\langle\rangle,,\|\quad\|$, and $N$ as above.

The two spaces $M$ and $L$ are then placed in duality by the bilinear form, each of the two spaces being identified to a subspace of the topological dual of the other. The following result is essentially due to F. Riesz.

Theorem. Let $L^{*}$ be the dual space of the Banach space $L$. Then every interval of $L$ is $w\left(L, L^{*}\right)$-compact. The bilinear form $\langle$,$\rangle definedaboveidentifies M$ to a subspace of $L^{*}$ which coincides with $L^{*}$ when and only when $M$ is a complete sublattice of $L^{*}$.

Further, to each positive $g \in L$ associate the subspace $L_{g}$ of elements $f \in L$ such that $|f|=\sup _{n}[|f|, n g]$. For every $\lambda \in L^{*}$ and every $g \in L$ there is a $u \in M$ such that $\lambda(f)=\langle u, f\rangle$ for every $f \in L_{g}$.

Proof. The two spaces $M$ and $L$ are always in coreticulated duality as can easily be checked. Let $M_{g}$ be the space obtained by identifying two elements $U_{i}$ of $M$ if $g\left(u_{1}-u_{2}\right)=0$. The two spaces $L_{g}$ and $M_{g}$ are also in coreticulated duality.

Let $S$ be a bounded increasingly directed subset of $M_{g}$. There is an $s \in M_{g}$ such that $\langle s, f\rangle=\sup \{\langle u, f\rangle ; u \in S\}$ for every positive $f \in L_{g}$. To construct such an $s$ it is sufficient to take an increasing sequence $\left\{u_{n}\right\}, u_{n} \in S$ such that $\lim _{n}\left\langle u_{n}, g\right\rangle=\sup \{\langle u, g\rangle ; u \in S\}$ and let $s=\lim u_{n}$.

Similarly for every bounded increasingly directed set $F \subset L_{g}$ there is an $h \in L_{g}$ such that $\langle u, h\rangle=\sup \{\langle u, f\rangle ; f \in F\}$ for every positive $u \in M_{g}$. Therefore $L_{g}$ and $M_{g}$ are in complete coreticulated duality so that $M_{g}$ is the dual of the Banach space $L_{g}$ according to Theorem 5 Section 7.

It follows that the intervals of $L_{g}$ are $w\left(L_{g}, M_{g}\right)$ compact; hence $w(L, M)$ and $w\left(L, L^{*}\right)$-compact. Since $L^{*}$ is a complete lattice the equality $M=L^{*}$ is equivalent to the statement that the intervals of $M$ are $w(M, L)$-compact, hence the theorem.

The preceding theorem may be completed as follows. If $u$ is an element of $M$ its norm as linear functional on $L$ is precisely $\|u\|$, hence if $\omega$ is an element of $L^{*}$ which is positive and corresponds to a $u \in M$, the class $u$ contains a locally bounded function $\check{u}$ such that $0 \leqq \check{u} \leqq\|\omega\|$.

Consider now on the leveled lattice $H$ two positive $\sigma$-smooth functions $\psi$ and $\omega$ and let $\phi=\psi+\omega$. Let $L, L^{*}$, and $M$ be defined as above and assume that $L^{*}$ and $M$ can be identified. If $f$ is integrable on $\{H, \mathscr{X}, \phi\}$, it is also integrable on $\{H, \mathscr{X}, \phi\}$ and $\{H, \mathscr{X}, \omega\}$ and $\phi(f)=\psi(f)+\omega(f)$.

In particular, $|\omega(f)| \leqq \phi(|f|)=N(f)$. Therefore, the map $f \rightarrow \omega(f)$ defines a positive linear functional of norm at most unity on $L$. Since we have assumed
that $M$ is identifiable to $L^{*}$ there is a locally integrable bounded function $u$ such that $0 \leqq u \leqq 1$ and $\omega(f)=\phi(u, f)$ for every $f \in L$. This may also be written

$$
\omega(f)=\psi(u, f)+\omega(u, f)
$$

Applying the formula repeatedly this gives

$$
\omega(f)=\psi\left[\left(\sum_{k=1}^{n} u^{k}\right) f\right]+\omega\left(u^{n} f\right)
$$

Considering only positive functions $f$ and letting $q=\sum_{k=1}^{\infty} u^{k}$ and $s= \lim _{n} u^{n}$, this implies

$$
\omega(f)=\psi(q f)+\omega(s f)
$$

for every positive $f \in \mathscr{L}$.
In addition, $\psi(s f)=0$ for every positive $f \in \mathscr{L} . \omega(f) \geqq \psi(q f) \geqq n \psi(s f)$ for every $n$. To simplify the preceding expressions we can introduce the following definition.

Definition. Let $\psi$ and $\omega$ be two positive $\sigma$-smooth functionals on $\{H, \mathscr{X}\}$. One says that $\psi$ dominates $\omega$ if for every positive function $f$ the equality $\psi^{*}(f)=$ 0 entails $\omega(f)=0$.

The preceding argument can then be summarized in the following theorem.
Theorem (Radon-Nikodym). Let $\psi$ and $\omega$ be two positive $\sigma$-smooth linear functionals on $H$. Suppose that $\psi$ dominates $\omega$ and that the space of equivalence classes of bounded locally integrable functions on $\{H, \mathscr{X}, \psi\}$ is a complete lattice. Then there is a positive locally integrable function $q$ (called the density of $\omega$ with respect to $\psi)$ such that

$$
\omega(f)=\psi(q f)
$$

for every integrable function $f$ on $\{H, \mathscr{X}, \omega\}$.
Proof. Let $\phi=\psi+\omega$, and let the spaces $L, L^{*}$, and $M$ be defined as above. Note first that two locally bounded functions which are equivalent for $\psi$ are also equivalent for $\phi$.

Let $h$ be a fixed positive function which is $\phi$-integrable and suppose that for each integer $n$ there is an $f_{n}, 0 \leqq f_{n} \leqq k$ such that $\psi\left(f_{n}\right)<1 / 2^{n+1}$ and $\omega\left(f_{n}\right)>\varepsilon$. This implies that $g_{n}=\sup _{k \geqq n} f_{k} \leqq k$ and $\psi\left(g_{n}\right)<1 / 2^{n}$ while $\omega\left(g_{n}\right)>\varepsilon$. Therefore $\psi(g)=0$ and $\omega(g) \geqq \varepsilon$ for $g=\inf _{n} g_{n}$. In other words, if $\psi$ dominates $\omega$ then $\omega$ belongs to the Riesz space generated by $\psi$.

Let $S$ be an increasingly directed bounded subset of $M$. By assumption there is a $u \in M$ such that $\psi(u f)=\sup \{\psi(s f) ; s \in S\}$ for every positive $\phi$ integrable function $f$. Since this can be written $\lim _{s} \psi[(u-x) f]=0$ it follows that $\lim _{S} \omega[(u-s) f]=0$; hence that $\phi(u f)=\sup \{\phi(s f) ; s \in S\}$. Finally this implies that $L^{*}$ is identical to $M$.

By the previous argument, for $f \in \mathscr{L}$,

$$
\omega(f)=\psi(q f)+\omega(s f)
$$

but since $\psi(s f)=0$ we also have $\omega(s f)=0$, hence $\omega(f)=\psi(q f)$. Finally, since $\mathscr{L}$ contains $H$ the equality remains true for every $f$ which is $\omega$-integrable.

## 11. Smooth Linear Functionals

In this section we shall consider a set $\mathscr{X}$, a vector lattice $H$ of bounded numerical functions defined on $\mathscr{X}$, and a family $\mathscr{F}$ of subsets of $\mathscr{X}$. It will be assumed that the lattice $H$ satisfies the following requirement.
(A) The lattice $H$ is leveled and full. For each $x \in \mathscr{X}$ these exists an $f \in H$ such that $f(x)>0$.

The family $\mathscr{F}$ will be used to induce uniform structures and the corresponding topologies on the lattically bounded subsets of $H$. Specifically, if $B$ is a lattically bounded subset of $H$, the $\mathscr{F}$-uniformity on $B$ is the structure of uniform convergence on the sets $F \in \mathscr{F}$.

If $H$ does not separate the points of $\mathscr{X}$ two elements $x_{1}$ and $x_{2}$ of $\mathscr{X}$ will be called $H$-equivalent if $h\left(x_{1}\right)=h\left(x_{2}\right)$ for every $h \in H$.

Since the family $\mathscr{F}$ is used only to define uniform structures, two families $\mathscr{F}$ and $\mathscr{F}^{\prime}$ which define the same uniform structures can be considered equivalent. Thus, it is possible without loss of generality to assume that $\mathscr{F}$ is subjected to the following requirement.
(B) The family $\mathscr{F}$ is increasingly directed. Every $F \in \mathscr{F}$ is contained in an element $F^{\prime}$ of $\mathscr{F}$ which is saturated for the relation of $H$-equivalence.

It can be observed that two families $\mathscr{F}$ and $\mathscr{F}^{\prime}$ induce the same uniformities on the lattically bounded subsets of $H$ if and only if they induce the same systems of neighborhoods of zero.

When $H$ does not separate the points of $\mathscr{X}$, one can make use of condition (B) and take the quotient of $\mathscr{X}$ for the relation of $H$-equivalence. Thus if convenient, one can assume that $H$ separates the points of $\mathscr{X}$. We shall do so in particular to define by the method of Section 9 a locally compact space $T$ associated with the pair $\{\mathscr{X}, H\}$. The set $\hat{H}$ of extensions to $T$ of elements of $H$ need not coincide with the space $\overline{\mathscr{K}}(T)$ of continuous functions which vanish at the infinity of $T$. However, the requirement that $H$ is "full" implies, among other things, that for every $h \in \hat{H}$ and every bounded continuous function $\gamma$ defined on $T$ the product $\gamma h$ belongs to $\hat{H}$.

It will be seen in subsequent sections that the smoothness properties introduced in Section 10 under the names of Daniell and MacShane are too weak for certain purposes but too strong for some others. To allow flexibility, three variations are introduced below. The first is true continuity. The second is the property used by MacShane with pointwise convergence replaced by $\mathscr{F}$ convergence. The third is for $\mathscr{F}$-convergence the analogue of the pointwise sequential continuity used by Daniell. By analogy with the usual notation for integrals the value at $h$ of a linear functional $\phi$ will be denoted $h \phi$.

Definition 1. A linear functional $\phi$ defined on $H$ is called relatively continuous on $\{H, \mathscr{F}\}$ if its restriction to every lattically bounded subset $B$ of $H$ is continuous on $B$ for the $\mathscr{F}$-topology.

The space of such functionals will be denoted $M_{\kappa}[H, \mathscr{F}]$.
Definition 2. A linear functional $\phi$ defined on $H$ is $\tau$-smooth on $\{H, \mathscr{F}\}$ if $\gamma_{\nu} \phi \rightarrow 0$ for every decreasing directed set $\left\{\gamma_{\nu}\right\}$ such that $\gamma_{\nu}$ converges to zero uniformly on the sets $F \in \mathscr{F}$.

Definition 3. A linear functional $\phi$ defined on $H$ is called $\sigma$-smooth on $\{H, \mathscr{F}\}$ if $\gamma_{n} \phi \rightarrow 0$ for every decreasing sequence $\left\{\gamma_{n}\right\}$ which converges to zero uniformly on the sets $F \in \mathscr{F}$.

The space of $\tau$-smooth functionals will be denoted $M_{\tau}[H, \mathscr{F}]$. Similarly the space of $\sigma$-smooth functionals will be denoted $M_{\sigma}[H, \mathscr{F}]$. In the preceding definitions we have used the uniformities induced by $\mathscr{F}$ on each lattically bounded subset of $H$. One can also used instead a single uniformity defined by a locally convex topology on $H$ as follows.

Definition. Let $\Phi$ be a set of linear functionals on the vector lattice $H$. The set $\Phi$ will be called $\mathscr{F}$-tight if for every symmetric lattically bounded subset $B$ of $H$ the set $\Phi$ is equicontinuous for the $\mathscr{F}$-topology on $B$.

It is obvious that equicontinuity on $B$ is equivalent to equicontinuity at zero. For each $x \in \mathscr{X}$ let $\delta_{x}$ be the linear functional defined by $h \delta_{x}=h(x)$. If $F \in \mathscr{F}$ then $\left\{\delta_{x}: x \in F\right\}$ is $\mathscr{F}$-tight. The convex hull of an $\mathscr{F}$-tight set is $\mathscr{F}$-tight. If $\Phi$ is $\mathscr{F}$-tight and $h \in H$ then the range $\{h \phi ; \phi \in \Phi\}$ is bounded. Note also that an $\mathscr{F}$-tight set is, by definition, a subset of $M_{\kappa}[H, \mathscr{F}]$. This leads to the following easy lemma.

Lemma 1. The topology of uniform convergence on the $\mathscr{F}$-tight subsets of $M_{\kappa}[H, \mathscr{F}]$ is a locally convex topology compatible with the vector and lattice structure of $H$. It is the largest locally convex topology which coincides with the topology of $\mathscr{F}$-convergence on each lattically bounded subset of $H$. For this topology the dual of $H$ is $M_{\kappa}[H, \mathscr{F}]$.

Note that on $H$ the topology of $\mathscr{F}$-convergence is usually substantially weaker than the topology of uniform convergence on the $\mathscr{F}$-tight subsets of $M_{\kappa}[H, \mathscr{F}]$. It is not claimed here that $\mathscr{F}$-tight convergence is the largest topology compatible with the duality between $H$ and $M_{\kappa}[H, \mathscr{F}]$. We shall prove later on that this is the case under certain additional restrictions.

It is clear that for any given triple $\{H, \mathscr{F}, \mathscr{X}\}$ one has $M_{\kappa} \subset M_{\tau} \subset M_{\sigma}$. It is also clear that if $\mathscr{F}_{1} \subset \mathscr{F}_{2}$ or more generally if every $F_{1} \in \mathscr{F}_{1}$ is contained in some $F_{2} \in \mathscr{F}_{2}$, then $M_{i}\left[H, \mathscr{F}_{1}\right] \subset M_{i}\left[H, \mathscr{F}_{2}\right]$. Thus, the largest of all the classes
considered here is the class $M_{\sigma}[H,\{\mathscr{X}\}]$. This particular class will be denoted $M(H)$ or even $M$ if no confusion ensues. As will be shown below, the apparent generality available from the choice of the family $\mathscr{F}$ is partially illusory. To show this, and to describe the properties of the various classes, the following results are essential.

Proposition 1. Assume that $H$ and $\mathscr{F}$ satisfy the conditions (A) and (B). Let $i$ be one of the indices $\sigma, \tau$, or $\kappa$. If $\phi \in M_{i}[H, \mathscr{F}]$ then $\phi$ is order bounded and its positive and negative parts $\phi^{+}$and $\phi^{-}$belong to the space $M_{i}[H, \mathscr{F}]$.

Proof. Let $f$ be an element of $H$ such that $f>0$. The value of $\phi^{+}$at $f$ can be defined by the relation

$$
f \phi^{+}=\phi^{+}(f)=\sup \{\gamma \phi ; 0 \leqq \gamma \leqq f, \gamma \in H\} .
$$

The function so defined will be linear positive on $H$ if and only if $\phi^{+}(f)<\infty$ for each $f \geqq 0, f \in H$.

To show the existence of $\phi^{+}$it is already sufficient to show that $\phi^{+}(f)<\infty$ for $f \in H$ and $\phi \in M(H)$. Assuming the contrary, there would be an $f \in H$ and a sequence $\left\{\gamma_{n}\right\}, 0 \leqq \gamma_{n} \leqq f$ such that $\gamma_{n} \phi \geqq 4^{n}$. Since $H$ is full, the function $\gamma=\sum_{n \geqq 1} 2^{-n} \gamma_{n} \leqq \sum_{n \geqq 1} 2^{-n} f=f$ belongs to $H$. The condition $\phi \in M(H)$ implies

$$
\gamma \phi=\lim _{n} \sum_{k \leqq n}\left[2^{-k} \gamma_{k}\right] \phi=\lim \sum_{k \leqq n} 2^{-k}\left|\gamma_{k} \phi\right| \geqq \sum_{k} 2^{k}
$$

This contradicts the finiteness of $\phi$ on $H$. Hence $\phi^{+}$exists.
For the other statements suppose first that $i=\kappa$. Then for a fixed $f \in H$ and a fixed $\varepsilon>0$ there is an $F \in \mathscr{F}$ and a $\delta>0$ such that $|\gamma| \leqq f$ and $\left|\gamma I_{F}\right|<\delta$ implies $|\gamma \phi|<\varepsilon$. This implies $\left|\phi^{+}(\gamma)\right|<\varepsilon$ and therefore $\phi \in M_{\kappa}[H, \mathscr{F}]$ implies $\phi^{+} \in M_{\kappa}[H, \mathscr{F}]$.

If the index $i$ is equal to $\tau$, let $\left\{g_{v}\right\}$ be an increasingly directed family of elements of $H$ which converges to $f \in H$ uniformly on the sets $F \in \mathscr{F}$. If $i=\sigma$, let $\left\{g_{v}\right\}$ be a sequence having the same property.

The definition of $\phi^{+}$implies

$$
\phi^{+}(f) \geqq \lim _{v} \phi^{+}\left(g_{v}\right)=\sup _{v} \phi^{+}\left(g_{v}\right)
$$

Let $b$ denote a real number strictly smaller than $\phi^{+}(f)$.
By definition there is a $u \in H$ such that $0 \leqq u \leqq f$ and $u \phi \geqq b$. The functions $u \wedge g_{v}$ increase to $u$ uniformly on the sets $F \in \mathscr{F}$. Therefore there is a $v_{0}$ such that $\left(u \wedge g_{v}\right) \phi>b$ for every $v \geqq v_{0}$. This implies $\phi^{+}\left(g_{v}\right) \geqq\left(u \wedge g_{v}\right) \phi>b$ and completes the proof of the lemma.

Remark. The preceding argument proves more generally that $\phi^{+}(f)= \sup \phi^{+}\left(g_{v}\right)$ whether $f \in H$ or not provided that if $u \in H, u<f$ then $u \wedge g_{v}$ increases to $u$ uniformly on the elements of $\mathscr{F}$.

Let $\hat{\mathscr{K}}$ be the set of $K$-functions of $H$. That is, $u \in \hat{\mathscr{K}}$ if there is a $g \in H$ such
that $u(x) \neq 0$ implies $g(x) \geqq 1$. Let $\hat{\mathscr{C}}$ be the family of subsets $C$ of $\mathscr{X}$ which are such that there is a $u \in \widehat{\mathscr{K}}$, with $0 \leqq u \leqq 1$ and $u(x) \geqq 1$ for $x \in C$.

Proposition 2. For each $g \geqq 0, g \in H$, let $\mathscr{K}_{g}$ be the set of elements $u \in H$ such that $u(x) \neq 0$ implies $g(x) \geqq 1$. Let $\mathscr{F}^{*}$ be the family of sets of the type $F \cap C, F \in \mathscr{F}, C \in \mathscr{C}$. For $i=\sigma, \tau$, or $\kappa$ an element $\phi$ of $M(H)$ belongs to $M_{i}[H, \mathscr{F}]$ if and only if for each $g \in \hat{\mathscr{K}}$ the restriction of $\phi$ to $\mathscr{K}_{g}$ belongs to $M_{i}\left[\mathscr{K}_{g}, \mathscr{F}\right]$. Also, $\phi \in M(H)$ belongs to $M_{i}[H, \mathscr{F}]$ if and only if $\phi \in M_{i}\left[H, \mathscr{F}^{*}\right]$.

Proof. It is obvious that $\phi \in M_{i}[H, \mathscr{F}]$ implies $\phi \in M_{i}\left[\mathscr{K}_{g}, \mathscr{F}\right]$. To prove the converse let $\left\{\gamma_{\nu}\right\}$ be a filter of elements of $H$ which converges to zero for the $\mathscr{F}$-topology. If $i=\tau$ we shall assume that $\gamma_{\nu}$ decreases to zero. If $i=\sigma$ we shall assume that $\left\{\gamma_{v}\right\}$ is a sequence decreasing to zero. In all cases assume $\left|\gamma_{v}\right| \leqq f, f \in H$. According to Proposition 1 , one can suppose that $\phi \geqq 0$.

For a given $\varepsilon>0$ let $u \in \widehat{\mathscr{K}}$ be such that $0 \leqq u \leqq f$ and $(f-u) \phi<\varepsilon$. This is possible since $\phi \in M(H)$. Let $g$ be an element of $\hat{\mathscr{K}}$ such that $u(x)>0$ implies $g(x) \geqq 1$. Consider the functions $\gamma_{\nu}^{*}$ defined by $\gamma_{\nu}^{*}=u \wedge \gamma_{\nu}$ if $\gamma_{\nu} \geqq 0$ and by $\gamma_{\nu}^{*}=\left[u \wedge \gamma_{\nu}\right] \vee(-u)$ if $\gamma_{\nu}$ takes positive and negative values. The $\gamma_{\nu}^{*}$ tend to zero for the $\mathscr{F}$-topology. Furthermore the sequential or decreasing character of $\gamma_{\nu}$ is transferred to $\gamma_{\nu}^{*}$. Therefore if $\phi \in M_{i}\left[\mathscr{K}_{g}, \mathscr{F}\right]$ the values $\gamma_{\nu}^{*} \phi$ converge to zero. The difference $\left|\gamma_{\nu}-\gamma_{\nu}^{*}\right|$ is smaller than $f-u$. Therefore $\left|\gamma_{\nu} \phi-\gamma_{\nu}^{*} \phi\right| \leqq(f-u) \phi<\varepsilon$. This implies the first statement.

To prove the second statement note that $M_{i}\left[H, \mathscr{F}^{*}\right] \subset M_{i}[H, \mathscr{F}]$ but that for $g \in \widehat{\mathscr{K}}, g \geqq 0$ one has also $M_{i}\left[\mathscr{K}_{g}, \mathscr{F}^{*}\right]=M_{i}\left[\mathscr{K}_{g}, \mathscr{F}\right]$ since there is a $u \in \widehat{\mathscr{K}}$ such that $\{x: g(x) \neq 0\} \subset C=\{x: u(x) \geqq 1\}$.

A statement analogous to the statement of Proposition 2 can be used to reduce the study of elements of $M(H)$ to the study of linear functionals whose norm $\|\phi\|=\sup \{|\gamma \phi| ; \gamma \in H,|\gamma| \leqq I\}$ is finite. For this purpose, let $T$ be the locally compact space associated with $\{H, \mathscr{X}\}$. Let $\Gamma$ be the space of bounded continuous functions defined on $T$ and let $\widehat{\Gamma}$ be the space of their restrictions to $\mathscr{X}$.

For $g \in H$ let $g \cdot \phi$ be the linear functional defined on $\widehat{\Gamma}$ by the relation

$$
\gamma(g \cdot \phi)=(\gamma g) \phi
$$

This is well defined since $\gamma \in \widehat{\Gamma}$ and $g \in h$ implies $g \gamma \in H$.
Corollary. An element $\phi$ of $M(H)$ belongs to $M_{i}[H, \mathscr{F}, \mathscr{X}]$ if and only if for each $g \in \hat{\mathscr{K}}, g \geqq 0$ the functional $g \phi$ belongs to $M_{i}[\hat{\Gamma}, \mathscr{F}]$.

Proposition 3. For $i=\sigma, \tau$, or $\kappa$ the space $M_{i}[H, \mathscr{F}]$ are bands in the Riesz dual $H^{*}$ of $H$.

Proof. According to Proposition 1, it is sufficient to show that each positive cone $M_{i}^{+}$has the following properties:
(a) If $\psi \in H^{*}$ and $\phi \in M_{i}^{+}$and $0 \leqq \psi \leqq \phi$ then $\psi \in M_{i}^{+}$.
(b) If $\left\{\phi_{v}\right\}$ is an increasingly directed family of elements of $M_{i}^{+}$such that $\phi_{v} \leqq \psi$ for some $\psi \in H^{*}$, then $\phi=\sup \phi_{v}$ belongs to $M_{i}{ }^{+}$.

For property (a) and for $i=\sigma$ or $\tau$ note that if $g_{\alpha} \in H$ and $g_{\alpha} \downarrow 0$ then $g_{\alpha} \psi \leqq g_{\alpha} \phi \rightarrow 0$. If $i=\kappa$ let $F \in \mathscr{F}$ and $\delta>0$ be such that $0 \leqq g \leqq f$ and $\left|g I_{F}\right|<\delta$ implies $g \phi<\varepsilon$. Then $g \psi<g \phi<\varepsilon$.

For property (b) and for $i=\tau$, let $\left\{g_{\alpha}\right\}$ be an increasingly directed family of elements of $H$ which converges to $f$ uniformly on the sets $F \in \mathscr{F}$. For $i=\sigma$ suppose that $\left\{g_{\alpha}\right\}$ is a sequence. Then

$$
\sup _{\alpha} \sup _{v} g_{\alpha} \phi_{v}=\sup _{v} \sup _{\alpha} g_{\alpha} \phi_{v}
$$



By definition of the supremum $\phi=\sup \phi_{v}$, the left-hand side is equal to $\sup _{\alpha} g_{\alpha} \phi$. If each $\phi_{v}$ belongs to the appropriate $M_{i}^{+}$space, the right-hand side is equal to $\sup _{v} f \phi_{v}$. Hence the result for $i=\sigma$ or $\tau$.

For the case $i=\kappa$ let $h$ be an arbitrary $K$-function of $H$ and let $\|\phi\|_{h}= \sup \{|\gamma \phi| ; \gamma \in H,|\gamma| \leqq h\}$. If $\phi=\sup _{\nu} \phi_{\nu}$ then $\lim _{\nu}\left\|\phi-\phi_{\nu}\right\|_{h}=0$. For a given $\varepsilon>0$ let $v_{0}$ be such that $v \geqq v_{0}$ implies $\left\|\phi-\phi_{v}\right\|_{h}<\varepsilon$. Let $F \in \mathscr{F}$ and $\delta>0$ be such that $0 \leqq f \leqq h, f \in H$ and $\left|f I_{F}\right|<\delta$ imply $\left|\phi_{\nu_{0}}(f)\right|<\varepsilon$. Then $|f \phi| \leqq\left|f \phi_{\nu_{0}}\right|+\left\|\phi-\phi_{\nu_{0}}\right\|_{h}<2 \varepsilon$. This concludes the proof of the proposition.

A wealth of additional information on the structure of the spaces $M_{i}\{H, \mathscr{F}\}$ can be obtained by replacing the space $\mathscr{X}$ by the locally compact space $T$ associated with $\{\mathscr{X}, H\}$. This amounts to transferring the linear functionals $\phi$ from $H$ to the space $\hat{H}$ according to a particular case of the following scheme.

Definition. Let $\left\{H_{j}, \mathscr{F}_{j}, \mathscr{X}_{j}\right\} ; j=1,2$ be two triples satisfying the requirements (A) and (B). A map $S$ from $H_{2}$ to $H_{1}$ will be called a transfer from $\left\{H_{1}, \mathscr{F}_{1}, \mathscr{X}_{1}\right\}$ to $\left\{\boldsymbol{H}_{2}, \mathscr{F}_{2}, \mathscr{X}_{2}\right\}$ if it is positive, linear, and continuous for the $\mathscr{F}_{j}$-convergences. It will be called a proper transfer if images of $K$-functions are $K$-functions.

If $f$ is a function from $\mathscr{X}_{1}$ to $\mathscr{X}_{2}$ such that $h \circ f \in H_{1}$ for each $h \in H_{2}$, then the map $h \mapsto h \circ f$ is a transfer if and only if for every $F_{1} \in \mathscr{F}_{1}$ there is an $F_{2} \in \mathscr{F}_{2}$ such that $f\left(F_{1}\right) \subset F_{2}$.

Let $S$ be a transfer from $\left\{H_{1}, \mathscr{F}_{1}, \mathscr{X}_{1}\right\}$ to $\left\{H_{2}, \mathscr{F}_{2}, \mathscr{X}_{2}\right\}$. For each $\phi \in M\left(H_{1}\right)$ let $S_{\phi}$ be the linear functional defined on $H_{2}$ by $h\left(S_{\phi}\right) h=(h S) \phi$. Then the image by $S$ of $M_{i}\left[H_{1}, \mathscr{F}_{1}\right]$ is a linear subspace of $M_{i}\left(H_{2}, \mathscr{F}_{2}\right)$ and positive elements are mapped into positive elements. If $\Phi$ is an $\mathscr{F}_{1}$-tight subset of $M_{\kappa}\left[H_{1}, \mathscr{F}_{1}\right]$ then $S(\Phi)$ is an $\mathscr{F}_{2}$-tight subset of $M_{\kappa}\left[H_{2}, \mathscr{F}_{2}\right]$. Indeed the image by $S$ of a lattically bounded subset of $H_{2}$ is a lattically bounded subset of $H_{1}$ and $S$ is continuous on lattically bounded sets. It follows that a transfer $S$ is also continuous as function from $H_{2}$ to $H_{1}$ if for $j=1,2$ the spaces $H_{j}$ are given the topologies of $\mathscr{F}_{j}$-tight convergence.

For $i=\kappa, \tau$, or $\sigma$ the transfer map $S$ from $M_{i}\left[H_{1}, \mathscr{F}_{1}\right]$ to $M_{i}\left[H_{2}, \mathscr{F}_{2}\right]$ is actually the transpose of the corresponding map from $H_{2}$ to $H_{1}$. Therefore, $S$ is always continuous for the weak topologies $w\left[M_{i}\left[H_{j}, \mathscr{F}_{j}\right], H_{j}\right]$ induced on the $M_{i}$ spaces by the corresponding $H$ space. In particular, the image by $S$ of a weakly compact subset of $M_{i}\left[H_{1}, \mathscr{F}_{1}\right]$ is a weakly compact subset of $M_{i}\left[H_{2}, \mathscr{F}_{2}\right]$. Similarly, images of weakly relatively countably compact or weakly relatively sequentially compact are weakly relatively countably compact or weakly relatively sequentially compact.

Suppose now that, in addition to the conditions (A) and (B) the lattice $H$ is subjected to the further requirement that $H$ separates the points of $\mathscr{X}$. Let then $T$ be the locally compact space associated with $\{\mathscr{X}, H\}$. For each $\gamma \in H$ let $\hat{\gamma}$ be its extension by continuity to $T$.

The map $\gamma \mapsto \hat{\gamma}$ is an isomorphism of the vector lattice $H$ onto the lattice $\hat{H}$. The map $\gamma \mapsto \hat{\gamma}$ that associates with each $\gamma \in \hat{H}$ its restriction $\hat{\gamma}$ to $\mathscr{X}$ is the inverse isomorphism.

For every $F \in \mathscr{F}$ let $\bar{F}$ be its closure in $T$. Let $\widehat{\mathscr{F}}$ be the family $\widehat{\mathscr{F}}=$
$\{\bar{F} ; F \in \mathscr{F}\}$. One can transfer the linear functionals $\phi$ defined on $H$ to the space $\hat{H}$ by writing $\hat{h} \hat{\phi}=h \phi$.

Lemma 2. The map $\phi \mapsto \hat{\phi}$ is an isomorphism of the space $M_{i}[H, \mathscr{F}]$ onto the space $M_{i}[\hat{H}, \hat{\mathscr{F}}]$.

This is immediate. Taking advantage of the isomorphism, we shall use the same symbol $\phi$ to denote the linear functional on $H$ and its image on $\hat{H}$. We shall also need the following remarks.

Lemma 3. Let $\mathscr{C}$ be the family of compact subsets of $T$. The conditions $\phi \in M_{\sigma}[H,\{\mathscr{X}\}]$ and $\phi \in M_{\kappa}[\hat{H}, \mathscr{C}]$ are equivalent.

Proof. With an obvious abuse of notation, it is clear that $M_{\kappa}[\hat{H}, \mathscr{C}] \subset M_{\sigma}[H,\{\mathscr{X}\}]$. Proposition 2 shows that $M_{\kappa}[\hat{H}, \mathscr{C}]=M_{\kappa}[\hat{H},\{T\}]$. Thus it is sufficient to show that $\phi \geqq 0, \phi \in M_{\sigma}[H,\{\mathscr{X}\}]$ already implies $\phi \in M_{\kappa}[H,\{\mathscr{X}\}]$.

Let $f \geqq 0$ be an element of $H$ and let $\varepsilon>0$ be a given number. There is a sequence $\left\{u_{n}\right\}, u_{n} \in \mathscr{K}$ which increases to $f$ uniformly on $\mathscr{X}$. Thus, since $\phi \in M[H,\{\mathscr{X}\}]$ there is a $u \in \widehat{\mathscr{K}}, 0 \leqq u \leqq f$ such that $f \phi-u \phi<\varepsilon$. For this $u$ there is a $g \geqq 0, g \in \widehat{\mathscr{K}}$ such that $u(x)>0$ implies $g(x) \geqq 1$. Let $a=g \phi$. Let $\gamma$ be an arbitrary element of $H$ such that $0 \leqq \gamma \leqq f$ and such that $a \gamma(x)<\varepsilon$ whenever $g(x) \geqq 1$. Write $\gamma_{1}=\gamma_{1}+\gamma_{2}$ with $\gamma_{1}=\gamma u$ and $\gamma_{2}=\gamma-(\gamma u)$. These relations imply $a \gamma_{1}(x) \leqq \varepsilon g(x)$ for every $x \in \mathscr{X}$. Also $0 \leqq \gamma_{2} \leqq f-(f u)=f-u$. Thus

$$
\gamma \phi \leqq \frac{1}{a} \varepsilon(g \phi)+(f-u) \phi<2 \varepsilon
$$

The result follows.
A generalization of this lemma will be given below (Proposition 6). For the present, note that Proposition 2 and this lemma concur in showing that there is no effective loss of generality in assuming that $\mathscr{F}$ satisfies a supplementary condition (C) as follows:
(C) For every $F \in \mathscr{F}$ there is a $K$-function $u \in H$ such that $0 \leqq u \leqq 1$ and such that $u(x) \geqq 1$ for every $x \in F$.

It is equivalent to say that the family $\widehat{\mathscr{F}}$ is a subset of the class $\mathscr{C}$ of compact subsets of $T$. We shall assume this for the remainder of this section. Let $V$ be the space of functions from $T$ to $(-\infty,+\infty]$ which are suprema of nonempty subsets of $\hat{H}$. Let $\phi$ be a positive element of $M_{\sigma}[\hat{H}, \mathscr{C}]=M_{\kappa}[\hat{H}, \mathscr{C}]$. For every $v \in V$ let $\phi^{*}(v)$ be the upper bound

$$
\phi^{*}(v)=\sup \{\gamma \phi ; \gamma \in \hat{H}, \gamma \leqq v\} .
$$

Proposition 4. Assume $\phi \in M_{\sigma}(\hat{H}, \mathscr{C}), \phi \geqq 0$. If $\left\{g_{v}\right\}$ is an increasingly directed family of elements of $V$ then

$$
\phi^{*}\left(\sup _{v} g_{v}\right)=\sup _{v} \phi^{*}\left(g_{v}\right)
$$

Furthermore, if $g_{i} \in V, i=1,2$, then

$$
\phi^{*}\left(g_{1}+g_{2}\right)=\phi^{*}\left(g_{1}\right)+\phi^{*}\left(g_{2}\right) .
$$

Proof. Let $g=\sup _{v} g_{v}$. Then $g \in V$. To prove the result, it is sufficient to show that if $f \in \hat{H}, f \leqq g$, then $f \phi \leqq \sup _{v} \phi^{*}\left(g_{v}\right)$. For this purpose let $A_{v}= \left\{h ; h \in \hat{H}, h \leqq g_{v}\right\}$. Let $A=\bigcup_{v} A_{v}$. The set $A$ is increasingly directed and $\sup \{f \wedge \gamma ; \gamma \in A\}=f$. According to Dini's theorem the functions $f \wedge \gamma, \gamma \in A$ increase to $f$ uniformly on the compact subsets of $T$. Lemma 3 implies that under these conditions $(f \wedge \gamma) \phi$ increases to $f \phi$. The result follows as in Lemma 5, Section 10.

Let $V_{\phi}$ be the set $V_{\phi}=\left\{v: v \in V, \phi^{*}(v)<\infty\right\}$. One can extend the domain of the linear functional $\phi$ by the method of Section 10 to a domain $\mathscr{D}\left[\hat{H}, \phi ; V_{\phi}\right]$. We shall denote $\phi^{*}$ the extension so obtained. Furthermore we shall call $\rho^{*}$ the seminorm defined by $\phi^{*}$ according to the relation $\rho^{*}(f)=\inf \left\{\phi^{*}(v) ; v \in V\right.$, $v \geqq|f|\}$. Note that $\phi^{*}$ and $\rho^{*}$ are subject to the application of Theorems 1 and 2 in Section 10. Also if $\left\{f_{v}\right\}$ is a decreasingly directed family of upper semicontinuous functions tending pointwise to zero on $T$ and if $f_{v} \leqq h_{0}$ for some $h \in \hat{H}$ then $\rho^{*}\left(f_{v}\right) \rightarrow 0$.

Proposition 5. Assume that the conditions (A), (B), and (C) are satisfied. Let $S=\bigcup\{\bar{F}: F \in \mathscr{F}\}$. Let $\Sigma$ be the family of finite subsets of $S$. Let $\mathscr{C}(S)$ be the family of compact subsets of $S$.

Then, for $i=\sigma$ or for $i=\tau$ one has

$$
M_{i}[H, \mathscr{F}]=M_{i}[\hat{H}, \Sigma]=M_{i}[\hat{H}, \mathscr{C}(S)]
$$

In other words, the elements of $M_{i}[H, \mathscr{F}]$ coincide with the linear functionals which are pointwise $i$-smooth on $\{\hat{H}, S\}$.

Proof. The inclusions

$$
M_{i}[\hat{H}, \Sigma] \subset M_{i}[\hat{H}, \hat{\mathscr{F}}] \subset M_{i}[\hat{H}, \mathscr{C}(S)]
$$

are clear, since $\hat{\mathscr{F}} \subset \mathscr{C}(S)$. The inclusion $M_{i}[\hat{H}, \mathscr{C}(S)] \subset M_{i}[\hat{H}, \Sigma]$ is an immediate consequence of Dini's theorem.

According to this proposition the results of Sections 10 and in particular Theorems 1 and 2 apply to the restrictions of elements of $\hat{H}$ to the set $S$. This has the following useful consequence which generalizes Lemma 3.

Proposition 6. Assume that $\{H, \mathscr{F}, \mathscr{X}\}$ satisfies conditions (A) and (B), but not necessarily (C). For each compact $K \subset T$ let $\mathscr{F}_{K}=\{F \cap K ; F \in \hat{\mathscr{F}}\}$.

Assume that $\mathscr{F}$ has a countable base, or more generally that each $\mathscr{F}_{K}$ has a countable base. Then

$$
M_{\sigma}[H, \mathscr{F}]=M_{\tau}[H, \mathscr{F}]=M_{\kappa}[H, \mathscr{F}] .
$$

Proof. One can assume $\phi \geqq 0, \phi \in M_{\tau}[H, \mathscr{F}]$. According to Proposition 2 it is sufficient to show that for each $g \in \mathscr{K}$, the functional $\phi$ restricted to $\mathscr{K}_{g}$ belongs to $M_{\kappa}\left[\mathscr{K}_{g}, \mathscr{F}\right]$. Let $K$ be a compact neighborhood of the support of $g$ in $T$. Let $\left\{F_{n}\right\}$ be an increasing sequence of elements of $\widehat{\mathscr{F}}$ such that $\left\{F_{n} \cap \mathscr{K}\right\}$ is a base for $\mathscr{F}_{K}$. If $\phi \notin M_{\kappa}\left[\mathscr{K}_{g}, \mathscr{F}\right]$ there is an $\alpha>0$, an $f \in \kappa_{g}, f \geqq 0$ and for each integer $n$, a $\gamma_{n} \in \mathscr{K}_{g}$ such that $\left|\gamma_{n}\right| \leqq f,\left|\gamma_{n} I_{F_{n}}\right|<2^{-n}$, and $\left|\gamma_{n} \phi\right| \geqq \alpha$.

It is clear that one may assume $\gamma_{n}>0$. If $F$ is an arbitrary element of $\hat{\mathscr{F}}$ then $\gamma_{n} \rightarrow 0$ uniformly on $F$. Indeed $\gamma_{n}(x)=0$ for $x \in F \cap K^{C}$ and there is an $m$ such that $F \cap K \subset F_{m} \cap K$. This implies $\left|\gamma_{n} I_{F}\right| \leqq 2^{-n}$ for $n \geqq m$. It follows that $\left\{\gamma_{n}\right\}$ converges to zero pointwise on $S=\bigcup\{F ; F \in \widehat{\mathscr{F}}\}$. Under these conditions the Lebesgue theorem (Theorem 2, Section 10) implies that $\gamma_{n} \phi \rightarrow 0$. This contradicts the definition of the sequence $\left\{\gamma_{n}\right\}$ and proves the desired result.

The characterization of elements of $M_{i}[H, \mathscr{F}]$ by pointwise smoothness on the set $S=\bigcup[\bar{F} ; F \in \mathscr{F}]$ given in Proposition 5 for $i=\sigma$ or $\tau$ does not extend to $M_{\kappa}[H, \mathscr{F}]$. To obtain another characterization applicable to $M_{\kappa}[H, \mathscr{F}]$, additional terminology is necessary. Let $\rho^{*}$ be the pseudonorm $\rho^{*}(f)=\inf \left\{\phi^{*}(v) ; v \in V, v \geqq|f|\right\}$. Call a set $A \subset T$ negligible if $\rho^{*}\left(I_{A}\right)=0$. Call A locally negligible if every $x \in T$ possesses a neighborhood $W_{x}$ such that $A \cap W_{x}$ is negligible. It follows from Theorem 1, Section 10 that a countable union of (locally) negligible sets is (locally) negligible. One can also verify that a set $A$ is locally negligible if and only if for every compact $C \subset T$ the set $A \cap C$ is negligible.

In one direction this follows from the fact that each neighborhood $W_{x}$ contains a compact neighborhood of $x$. In the other direction, suppose $A$ locally negligible and let $C$ be a compact subset of $T$. For each $x \in C$ let $W_{x}$ be an open set such that $x \in W_{x}$ and such that $A \cap W_{x}$ is negligible. There is a finite set $\left\{x_{j}\right\}$ such that $C \subset \bigcup W_{x}$. Thus $(A \cap C) \subset \bigcup_{j}\left(A \cap W_{x_{j}}\right)$ is negligible.

Proposition 7. Assume that conditions (A), (B), and (C) are satisfied. Let $\phi$ be a positive linear functional $\phi \in M_{\sigma}[H,\{\mathscr{X}\}]$. The following statements are equivalent:
(a) $\phi$ belongs to $M_{\kappa}[H, \mathscr{F}]$.
(b) $A$ subset $A$ of $T$ is locally negligible if and only if for every $F \in \widehat{\mathscr{F}}$ the set $A \cap F$ is negligible.

Proof. Let $K$ be a compact subset of $T$. Let $u, 0 \leqq u \leqq 1$, be an element of $\mathscr{K}$ such that $u(x)=1$ for $x \in K$. Let $\phi$ be a positive element of $M_{\kappa}[H, \mathscr{F}]$, and let $A$ be a subset of $K$. If $A$ is not negligible then $\rho^{*}\left(I_{A}\right)=4 \alpha$ with $\alpha>0$.

By assumption there is an $F \in \widehat{\mathscr{F}}$ and a $\delta>0$ such that $0 \leqq \gamma \leqq u, \gamma \in \hat{H}$ and $\left|\gamma I_{F}\right|<\delta$ implies $\gamma \phi<\alpha$. Let $f \geqq 0$ be an arbitrary element of $V$ such that $I_{A \cap F} \leqq f$ and let $h=\sup \left\{\gamma ; \gamma \in \hat{H}, \gamma \leqq u, \gamma I_{F} \leqq \delta\right\}$. Then $f+h \in V$ and $I_{A} \leqq f+h$. However, $\phi^{*}(h)=\phi^{*}(f)+\phi^{*}(h) \leqq \alpha+\phi^{*}(f)$. Taking an infimum over $f \in V$, this leads to the inequality $\rho^{*}\left(I_{A}\right) \leqq \alpha+\rho^{*}\left(I_{A \cap F}\right)$. Since $\rho^{*}\left(I_{A}\right)=4 \alpha$ we conclude that $\rho^{*}\left(I_{A \cap F}\right) \geqq 3 \alpha$ and that $A \cap F$ is not negligible. Thus (a) implies (b).

Conversely, assume that if each $A \cap F$ set, $F \in \widehat{\mathscr{F}}$ is negligible then $A$ is locally negligible. Let $S=\bigcup\{F ; F \in \widehat{\mathscr{F}}\}$ and let $K$ be a compact subset of $T$. The set $K \cap S^{C}$ is negligible since $\left(K \cap S^{C}\right) \cap F$ is empty for $F \in \widehat{\mathscr{F}}$. Let $B=K \cap S$. Let $\mathscr{D}=\mathscr{D}\left[\hat{H}, \phi, V_{\phi}\right]$ be the domain of the extension $\phi^{*}$. Since $K \cap S^{C}$ is negligible its indicator belongs to $\mathscr{D}$. Furthermore, $\mathscr{D}$ includes $I_{K}$ and therefore also $I_{B}=I_{K}-I_{K \cap S^{c}}$. Let $\beta=\sup \left\{\left(I_{F \cap K}\right) \phi ; F \in \widehat{\mathscr{F}}\right\}$. There is an increasing sequence $\left\{F_{n}\right\}, F_{n} \in \widehat{\mathscr{F}}$ such that $\beta=\sup _{n}\left\{\left(I_{F_{n} \cap K}\right) \phi\right\}$. Let
$D=\bigcup_{n}\left(K \cap F_{n}\right)$ and let $A=B \cap D^{C}$. By Theorem 2, Section 10, the indicator of $D$ belongs to $D$. Thus, for any $F \in \widehat{\mathscr{F}}$ the indicator $I_{A \cap F}$ belongs to $\mathscr{D}$ and

$$
\rho^{*}\left[I_{A \cap F}+I_{F_{n} \cap K}\right]=\rho^{*}\left(I_{A \cap F}\right)+\rho^{*}\left(I_{F_{n} \cap K}\right)
$$

However, $(A \cap F) \cup\left(F_{n} \cap K\right)=K \cap\left(F \cup F_{n}\right)$. Since $\sup _{n} \rho^{*}\left(I_{F_{n} \cap K}\right)=\beta$, this gives $\rho^{*}\left(I_{A \cap F}\right)=0$. According to assumption (b) this implies that $A$ is locally negligible and therefore negligible since $A \subset K$. Thus, given $\varepsilon>0$ there is an $F \in \hat{\mathscr{F}}$ such that $F \cap K \subset B$ and $\rho^{*}\left(I_{B}-I_{F \cap K}\right)<\varepsilon$. Property (a) follows easily.

When the condition $\phi \in M_{\kappa}$ is replaced by $\phi \in M_{\sigma}$ or $\phi \in M_{\tau}$ one can also obtain a characterization in terms of local negligibility as follows.

Proposition 8. Assume that the system $\{H, \mathscr{F}, \mathscr{X}\}$ satisfies (A), (B), and (C). Let $S=\bigcup\{F ; F \in \hat{\mathscr{F}}\}$. Let $\phi$ be a positive element of $M_{\sigma}[H,\{\mathscr{X}\}]$. Then $\phi \in M_{\tau}[H, \mathscr{F}]$ if and only if every compact $K \subset T \cap S^{c}$ is negligible. Also, $\phi \in M_{\sigma}[H, \mathscr{F}]$ if and only if every compact $G_{\delta}$ set $K \subset T \cap S^{c}$ is negligible.

Proof. The indicator $I_{K}$ of a compact set $K$ is upper semicontinuous. Thus there is a decreasingly directed family $\left\{\gamma_{v}\right\}$ of elements of $\mathscr{K}$ such that $\gamma_{\nu} \geqq I_{K}$ and such that $\gamma_{\nu}$ decreases to $I_{K}$ pointwise. When $K$ is a $G_{\delta}$ one can take for $\left\{\gamma_{\nu}\right\}$ a sequence of elements of $\mathscr{K}$. Suppose that $K \subset T \cap S^{c}$. If $\phi \in M_{\tau}$ or if $\phi \in M_{\sigma}$ but $\left\{\gamma_{\nu}\right\}$ is a sequence, $\gamma_{\nu} \phi$ decreases to zero since $\gamma_{\nu}$ tends to zero pointwise on $S$. It follows that $\rho\left(I_{K}\right) \leqq \gamma_{\nu} \phi$ must be equal to zero.

Conversely, let $\left\{\gamma_{v}\right\}, \gamma_{v} \leqq \gamma_{0}$, be an arbitrary decreasingly directed family of positive elements of $\mathscr{K}$ such that $\gamma_{\nu}$ decreases to zero on $S$. Let $v= \lim \gamma_{\nu}$. Then $v$ is upper semicontinuous and equal to zero on $S$. The set $K_{\varepsilon}=\{x: v(x) \geqq \varepsilon\}$ is a compact subset of $T \cap S^{c}$ and $v \leqq \varepsilon \gamma_{0}+\left\|\gamma_{0}\right\| I_{K_{r}}$. Thus for the extension $\phi^{*}$ of $\phi$ one can write $\phi^{*}(v) \leqq\left\|\gamma_{0}\right\| \rho\left(I_{K_{f}}\right)+\varepsilon \gamma_{0} \phi$. If $K_{\varepsilon}$ is negligible this gives $\phi^{*}(v) \leqq \varepsilon \gamma_{0} \phi$, hence $\phi^{*}(v)=0$. However (Lemma 5, Section 10), $\phi^{*}(v)=\lim _{v} \gamma_{v} \phi$. Therefore $\gamma_{v} \phi \rightarrow 0$. In the case where negligibility holds only for compact $G_{\delta}$ 's, suppose that $\left\{\gamma_{\nu}\right\}$ is a decreasing sequence. Let $K_{\nu}=\left\{x: \gamma_{\nu}(x) \geqq \varepsilon\right\}$. Then $K_{\varepsilon}=\bigcap_{\nu} K_{\nu}$. However, $K_{\nu}=\bigcap_{n}\left\{x: \gamma_{\nu}(x)>\varepsilon-(1 / n)\right\}$. Therefore $K_{\varepsilon}$ is a $G_{\delta}$ and the desired result still follows.

It is clear from these characterizations that when a system $\{H, \mathscr{F}, \mathscr{X}\}$ satisfying the conditions (A), (B), and (C) is such that every positive element of $M[H,\{\mathscr{X}\}]$ is an element of $M_{\tau}[H, \mathscr{F}]$, then our set $S=\bigcup\{\bar{F} ; F \in \mathscr{F}\}$ must be the set $T$ itself. This condition is also sufficient as can be seen from Proposition 2.

The condition that every positive element of $M[H,\{\mathscr{X}\}]$ be an element of $M_{\sigma}[H, \mathscr{F}]$ is somewhat less restrictive. The following lemma of A. D. Alexandroff gives an equivalent restriction.

Proposition 9. Assume that $\{H, \mathscr{F}, \mathscr{X}\}$ satisfies the conditions (A), (B), and (C) and that $H$ separates the points of $\mathscr{X}$. The following conditions are equivalent.
(1) Every positive $K$-function of $H$ attains its maximum at some point of $S= \bigcup\{\bar{F} ; F \in \mathscr{F}\}$.
(2) If $\left\{u_{n}\right\}$ is a sequence of positive elements of $\mathscr{K}$ decreasing pointwise to zero on $S$ then $\sup \left\{u_{n}(x) ; x \in S\right\} \rightarrow 0$.
(3) Every positive element of $M[H,\{\mathscr{X}\}]$ belongs to $M_{\sigma}[H, \mathscr{F}]$.

Proof. Suppose that (1) is satisfied and that $\left\{u_{n}\right\}$ decreases pointwise to zero on $S$. If (2) is not satisfied, then taking a subsequence if necessary, there is an $\varepsilon>0$ and points $x_{n} \in S$ such that $u_{n}\left(x_{n}\right) \geqq \varepsilon$. Let $v_{n}=u_{n} \wedge \varepsilon$ and let $\gamma= \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} 2^{-n} v_{n}$. For each $x \in S$ one has $0 \leqq \gamma(x)<\varepsilon$. Also, $\sup \{\gamma(x) ; x \in S\}=\varepsilon$ and $0 \leqq \gamma \leqq v_{0}$. Therefore, $\gamma \in \mathscr{K}$ and $\gamma$ does not attain its maximum on $S$. Thus (1) implies (2). Furthermore if $u_{n}$ reaches its maximum on $S$ and $\left\{u_{n}\right\}$ decreases to zero uniformly on $S$ then $\sup \left\{u_{n}(x) ; x \in S\right\}=\sup \left\{u_{n}(x) ; x \in T\right\}$ tends to zero. Hence (1) implies (3). Finally if (1) is not satisfied, let $u \geqq 0$ be an element of $\mathscr{K}$ which does not reach its maximum on $S$. Assume, without loss of generality, $\sup \{u(x), x \in \mathscr{X}\}=1$.

Let $Q=\{x ; x \in T, u(x)=1\}$. Then $Q$ is a compact $G_{\delta}$ of $T$ and $Q \cap S=\varnothing$. Let $q$ be an arbitrary point of $Q$ and let $h \phi=h(q)$. Then $\phi \in M[H,\{\mathscr{X}\}]$ but $\phi \notin M_{\sigma}[H, \mathscr{F}]$ since $\left(u^{n}\right) \phi=[u(q)]^{n}=1$ in spite of the fact that $u^{n}$ decreases to zero pointwise on $S$.

Since the linear functionals which belong to $M_{\tau}[H, \mathscr{F}]$ are generally better behaved than the other elements of $M_{\sigma}[H, \mathscr{F}]$, it would be interesting to have a characterization of those systems $\{H, \mathscr{F}, \mathscr{X}\}$ for which $M_{\sigma}[H, \mathscr{F}]=M_{\tau}[H, \mathscr{F}]$.

## 12. Derivatives and Tangents

Definitions. Let $\mathscr{Y}$ be a topological vector space. Let $T$ be a subset of the real line. A function $f$ from $T$ to $\mathscr{Y}$ is called differentiable at $t \in T$ if there is an element $a \in \mathscr{Y}$ such that

$$
\lim _{\substack{\tau \rightarrow t \\ \tau \in T}} \frac{1}{\tau-t}[f(\tau)-f(t)-a(\tau-t)]=0
$$

The element $a$ is called the derivative of $f$ at $t$ relative to $T$.
Let $\mathscr{X}$ be a vector space, let $T$ be a subset of $\mathscr{X}$, and let $f$ be a function from $T$ to $\mathscr{Y}$. Let $x_{0}$ be a particular point of $T$. The function $f$ is called radially (resp. biradially) differentiable at $x_{0}$ if its restriction to rays (resp. lines) through $x_{0}$ is differentiable at $x_{0}$.

Let $\mathscr{B}$ be a family of symmetric subsets of $\mathscr{X}$ such that $B_{i} \in \mathscr{B}$ for $i=1,2$ implies $B_{1} \cup B_{2} \subset B_{3} \in \mathscr{B}$. Let $A$ be a linear map from $\mathscr{X}$ to $\mathscr{Y}$. The function $f$ is said to possess at $x_{0}$ the $\mathscr{B}$-derivative $A$ if as $t \rightarrow 0$ the ratio

$$
\frac{1}{|t|}\left[f\left[x_{0}+t\left(x-x_{0}\right)\right]-f\left(x_{0}\right)-t A\left(x-x_{0}\right)\right]
$$

tends to zero uniformly for $x$ in the elements of $\mathscr{B}$. If in addition $\mathscr{X}$ is locally convex and $A$ is a continuous map from $\mathscr{X}$ to $\mathscr{Y}$ then $A$ is called a Fréchet $\mathscr{B}$-derivative. The family $\mathscr{B}$ used in most applications is the family of bounded subsets of $\mathscr{X}$. Another very usable family is that of compact sets.

Note that with the present definition of derivative the existence at $x_{0}$ of a $\mathscr{B}$-derivative does not imply that $f$ is continuous at $x_{0}$. Let $x_{0}$ be a point in a topological vector space $\mathscr{X}$. Let $r=\left\{x: x=x_{0}+\tau\left(u_{0}-x_{0}\right), \tau>0\right\}$ with $u_{0} \in \mathscr{X}, u_{0} \neq x_{0}$ be a ray originating at $x_{0}$. For each $x \in r$ and each neighborhood $V$ of the origin of $\mathscr{X}$ let $V(x, r)$ be the cone

$$
V(x, r)=\left\{y: y=x_{0}+\tau\left(u-x_{0}\right), \tau>0, u \in x+V\right\}
$$

The system of neighborhoods so obtained can be used to topologize the set of rays originating at $x_{0}$.

Let $S$ be a subset of $\mathscr{X}$ and let $x_{0}$ be a point of the closure $\bar{S}$ of $S$. For each neighborhood $W$ of $x_{0}$ let $C\left(x_{0}, S, W\right)$ be the set of rays $\left\{x: x=x_{0}+t\left(u-x_{0}\right)\right.$, $t>0\}$ for $u \in S \cap\left(x_{0}+W\right)$ and $u \neq x_{0}$. The contingent of $S$ at $x_{0}$ is the set $C\left(x_{0}, S\right)=\bigcap_{W} \overline{C\left(x_{0}, S, W\right)}$.

While the preceding definitions rely heavily on linear structures, it will be convenient in many cases to use a definition of tangent sets which involve a metric structure but no linearity assumption.

Let $\mathscr{X}$ be a metric space with distance $\rho$. Let $T_{i}, i=1,2$ be two subsets of $\mathscr{X}$. Let $S(x, \varepsilon)$ be the sphere of center $x$ and radius $\varepsilon$. The two sets $T_{i}$ are called tangent at $x$ if as $\varepsilon \rightarrow 0$ the ratio

$$
\frac{1}{\varepsilon} \rho\left\{T_{i} \cap S(x, \varepsilon), T_{2} \cap S(x, \varepsilon)\right\}
$$

tends to zero.
In metric spaces we shall also use $p$-measures defined as follows. Let $T$ be a subset of the metric space $\mathscr{X}$. For every $\varepsilon>0$ let $\mathscr{C}=\left\{S\left(x_{n}, \varepsilon_{n}\right), n=1,2, \ldots\right\}$, $\varepsilon_{n} \leqq \varepsilon$ be a cover of $T$ by spheres of radius inferior to $\varepsilon$. If such a cover exists, let $\mu(T, \mathscr{C}, \varepsilon)=\sum_{n} \varepsilon_{n}^{p}$; if not, let $\mu=\infty$. Let $\mu_{p}(T)=\sup _{\varepsilon} \inf _{\mathscr{C}} \mu(T, \mathscr{C}, \varepsilon)$. The value $\mu_{p}(T)$, finite or infinite, will be called the $p$-measure of $T$.

A function $f$ from a metric space $\mathscr{X}$ to a metric space is called Lipschitzian if there is a number $K<\infty$ such that

$$
\rho\left[f\left(x_{1}\right), f\left(x_{2}\right)\right] \leqq K \rho\left(x_{1}, x_{2}\right)
$$

Proposition 1. If $f$ is Lipschitzian and $T$ has finite (resp. zero) $p$-measure, then $f(T)$ has finite (resp. zero) p-measure.

In fact, if $\mathscr{C}=\left\{S\left(x_{n}, \varepsilon_{n}\right) ; n=1,2, \ldots\right\}$ covers $T$, then $f\left[S\left(x_{n}, \varepsilon_{n}\right) \cap T\right]$ has a diameter smaller than $2 K \varepsilon_{n}$ so that $\mu_{p}[f(T)] \leqq(2 K)^{p} \mu_{p}(T)$.

## The Vitali Covering Theorem-Density Theorems

Let $\mathscr{X}$ be a metric space with distance $\rho$. Let $A$ be a precompact subset of $\mathscr{X}$. Let $\mathscr{F}$ be a family of closed subsets of $\mathscr{X}$. For every $F \in \mathscr{F}$ let $F^{\prime}$ be the set $F^{\prime}=\{x \in \mathscr{X}, \rho(x, F) \leqq 4 \operatorname{diam} F\}$.

Let us say that $\mathscr{F}$ blankets $A$ if for every $x \in A$ and every $\varepsilon>0$ there is an $F \in \mathscr{F}$ such that $x \in F$ and $0<\operatorname{diam} F<\varepsilon$.

Let $G$ be an open subset of $\mathscr{X}$ such that $A \subset G$. Let $\left\{F_{p}\right\}$ be a sequence of elements of $\mathscr{F}$ constructed as follows. $F_{1}$ is an arbitrary element of $\mathscr{F}$ such that $F_{1} \subset G$. If $F_{1}, F_{2}, \ldots, F_{p}$ have been chosen let $S_{p}=\bigcup_{j=1}^{p} F_{j}$ and let $\delta_{p}$ be the supremum of the diameters of sets $F \in \mathscr{F}$ such that $F \cap A \neq \varnothing, F \cap S_{p}=\varnothing$, and $F \subset G$. If $A \subset S_{p}$, stop here. If not, let $F_{p+1}$ be an element of $\mathscr{F}$ such that $F_{p+1} \subset G, F_{p+1} \cap A \neq \varnothing, F_{p+1} \cap S_{p}=\varnothing$, and $\operatorname{diam} F_{p+1}>\left(\delta_{p} / 2\right)$.

Theorem 1. Let $B=A \cap\left(\bigcup_{j=1}^{\infty} F_{j}\right)^{c}$ and let $R_{N}=\bigcup_{n>N} F_{j}^{\prime}$. If $\mathscr{F}$ blankets $A$ then, for every integer $N$ the set $B$ is contained in $R_{N}$.

Proof. If the sequence $\left\{F_{p}\right\}$ is finite the result is obvious. If not, $\delta_{p}$ must tend to zero as $p \rightarrow \infty$ since $A$ is precompact. Let $x$ be a point of $B \cap R_{N}^{c}$ if such a
point exists. Since $x \in B \subset A$ and since $\mathscr{F}$ contains arbitrarily small sets containing $x$, there exists an element $F \in \mathscr{F}$ such that $x \in F \subset G$ and $F \cap S_{N}= \varnothing$ while $\delta=\operatorname{diam} F>0$. Since $x \in B$ the set $F$ cannot be one of the $F_{p}$ 's. Since $\delta>0$ there is a $p_{0}$ such that $\delta>\delta_{p}$ for $p \geqq p_{0}$. It follows from the definition of $\delta_{p}$ that $F$ must have points in common with some $F_{j}$ for $N<j \leqq p_{0}$. Let $v+1$ be the smallest integer such that $F \cap F_{v+1} \neq \varnothing$. This implies $F \cap S_{v}=\varnothing$ hence $\operatorname{diam} F \leqq \delta_{v}$. Also by construction $v \geqq N$. Let $y$ be an element of $F \cap F_{\nu+1}$. Since $x \in R_{N}^{c}$ by assumption, we have $x \notin F_{\nu+1}^{\prime}$ hence $\rho\left(x, F_{\nu+1}\right)>4$ diam $F_{\nu+1}$. Thus

$$
\rho(x, y) \geqq \rho(x, F)>4 \operatorname{diam} F_{v+1}
$$

Since both $x$ and $y$ belong to $F$, this implies $\delta_{v} \geqq \operatorname{diam} F \geqq \rho(x, y) \geqq 4 \operatorname{diam} F_{v+1}>2 \delta_{v}$. This contradiction concludes the proof of the theorem.

Let $\mathscr{E}$ be a finite dimensional vector space, product of $k$ copies of the real line with a norm denoted $|\mid$. We shall denote $\lambda$ the Lebesgue measure which gives mass unity to the unit ball of $\mathscr{E}$. Measurability will be understood in the sense of Lusin.

Theorem 2 (Small Density Theorem). Let $E$ be a measurable subset of $\mathscr{E}$. For each $x \in \mathscr{E}$ and each $\varepsilon$ let $S(x, \varepsilon)$ be the ball of center $x$ and radius $\varepsilon$.

Let

$$
\phi(x, \varepsilon)=\frac{\lambda\{E \cap S(x, \varepsilon)\}}{\lambda\{S(x, \varepsilon)\}}
$$

For almost every $x \in E$ (resp. $E^{c}$ ), $\phi(x, \varepsilon)$ tends to unity (resp. zero) as $\varepsilon \rightarrow 0$.
Theorem 2 is a special case of a famous theorem of Lebesgue which implies in particular that a Lebesgue integrable function is almost everywhere equal to the derivative of its indefinite Lebesgue integral. To state the theorem, let us introduce the following notation. If $f$ defined on $\mathscr{E}$ is Lebesgue integrable, let

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left(R_{\varepsilon} f\right)(x) & =\frac{1}{\lambda[S(x, \varepsilon)]} \int_{S(x, \varepsilon)}|f(y)-f(x)| \lambda(d y) \\
& =\frac{1}{\lambda[S(0, \varepsilon)]} \int_{S(0, \varepsilon)}|f(x+y)-f(x)| \lambda(d y)
\end{aligned}
$$

Clearly $\left(R_{\varepsilon} f\right)(x) \geq 0$ and

$$
\int\left(R_{\varepsilon} f\right)(x) \lambda(d x) \leq 2 \int|f(x)| \lambda(d x)
$$

Also if $f$ and $g$ are two integrable functions

$$
\left(R_{\varepsilon} f\right)(x) \leq\left(R_{\varepsilon} g\right)(x)+\left[R_{\varepsilon}(f-g)\right](x) .
$$

Theorem 3. Let $f$ be Lebesgue integrable on $\mathscr{E}$. Then as $\varepsilon \rightarrow 0$, the transform ( $R_{\varepsilon} f$ ) tends to zero in $L_{1}$-norm and almost everywhere.

Proof. The result is trivial if $f$ is a continuous function. Since every integrable function can be approximated in $L_{1}$-norm by continuous functions with compact support, the $L_{1}$-norm convergence follows from the inequalities written just before the statement of the theorem.

To obtain the almost everywhere convergence suppose that the set $E$ where $\limsup _{\varepsilon \rightarrow 0}\left(R_{\varepsilon} f\right)(x)>0$ has positive Lebesgue measure. Then there is a compact $K \subset E$ and a number $a>0$ such that
(i) restricted to $K$ the function $f$ is continuous,
(ii) for every $x \in K$ one has

$$
\limsup _{\varepsilon \rightarrow 0}\left(R_{\varepsilon} f\right)(x) \geq 2 a
$$

(iii) the Lebesgue measure $\lambda(K)$ is strictly positive.

Let then $g$ be a continuous function with compact support such that $g(x)= f(x)$ for $x \in K$. Such a function exists by Tietze's theorem. Consider $h=(f-g)$ and $\phi=|h|$. For $x \in K$ we have $h(x)=0$ hence

$$
\left(R_{\varepsilon} h\right)(x)=\frac{1}{S(x, \varepsilon)} \int_{S(x, \varepsilon)}|h(y)| \lambda(d y)=\frac{1}{S(x, \varepsilon)} \int_{S(x, \varepsilon)} \phi(y) \lambda(d y)
$$

Now let $G$ be an open set such that $K \subset G$ and $\int_{G} \phi(x) \lambda(d x)<(a / 4) \lambda(K)$, and let $\mathscr{F}$ be the class of closed balls $S$ centered at points $x \in K$, contained in $G$ and such that $\int_{S} \phi(x) \lambda(d x) \geq a \lambda(S)$. By assumption this class blankets $K$. Thus by Theorem 1 there is a sequence $S_{j} \in \mathscr{F}$ such that if $C=\bigcup S_{j}$ then $B=K \backslash C$ is contained in every $\bigcup_{j \geq N} S_{j}^{\prime}$. Since $\bigcup_{j} S_{j} \subset G, \lambda\left(\bigcup_{j \geq N} S_{j}\right) \rightarrow 0$ as $N \rightarrow \infty$. By homogeneity this implies that $\lambda\left(\bigcup_{j \geq N} S_{j}^{\prime}\right) \rightarrow 0$ as $N \rightarrow \infty$ and therefore that $\lambda(B)=0$. Thus $\lambda(K)=\lambda(C)$. By definition of $C$ we have

$$
\int_{C} \phi(x) \lambda(d x) \geq a \lambda(C)=a \lambda(K)
$$

However,

$$
\int_{C} \phi(x) \lambda(d x) \leq \int_{G} \phi(x) \lambda(d x)<\frac{a}{4} \lambda(K)
$$

This is a contradiction and the result follows.
The points $x \in \mathscr{E}$ at which $\left(R_{\varepsilon} f\right)(x) \rightarrow 0$ are called Lebesgue points of $f$. Remark. Theorem 3 has been proved for real valued functions. It also applies to Bochner integrable functions $f$ with values in a Banach space.

Indeed, for such an $f$ and each $\alpha>0$, there is a continuous $g$ such that $\int|f-g| d \lambda<\alpha$ and the preceding argument shows that

$$
\limsup _{\varepsilon} \frac{1}{S(x, \varepsilon)} \int_{S(x, \varepsilon)}|f(y)-g(y)| \lambda(d y)
$$

exists and has an integral inferior to $\alpha$. Therefore $\int\left[\lim \sup _{\varepsilon \rightarrow 0} R_{\varepsilon}(f)\right] d \lambda< \alpha$ for any $\alpha>0$. The result follows.

## Existence of Derivatives and Tangent Planes

Theorem 1 (Lebesgue). Let $f$ be a continuous numerical nondecreasing function defined on an interval $[a, b]$ of the line. The set of points of $[a, b]$ where $f$ does not admit a finite derivative has Lebesgue measure zero.

Proof. Let $(\bar{D} f)(x)$ and $(\underline{D} f)(x)$ be the upper and lower limits of the ratio

$$
\frac{1}{\tau}[f(x+\tau)-f(x)]
$$

as $\tau \rightarrow 0$. Let $0<\alpha<\beta<\infty$ be two numbers and let $A$ be the set $A=\{x:(\underline{D} f)(x)<\alpha<\beta<(\bar{D} f)(x)\}$. Let $\mathscr{F}_{1}$ be the family of intervals [ $u, v$ ] such that $u<v$ and $f(v)-f(u)<\alpha(v-u)$. This family blankets A .

By application of Vitali's theorem, it is easily seen that there is a negligible set $B$ and a sequence of disjoint intervals $\left[u_{j} ; v_{j}\right] \in \mathscr{F}_{1}$ such that $A \subset \left\{\bigcup_{j}\left[u_{j}, v_{j}\right]\right\} \cup B$. Let $A_{j}=A \cap\left[u_{j}, v_{j}\right]$. Let $\mathscr{F}_{2 j}$ be the family of intervals [ $\left.s, t\right]$ such that $s<t,[s, t] \subset\left(u_{j}-\varepsilon, v_{j}+\varepsilon\right)$, and $f(t)-f(s)>\beta(t-s)$. The family $\mathscr{F}_{2 j}$ blankets $A_{j}$. Hence, there is a negligible set $B_{j}$ and a countable family of intervals $\left[s_{k}, t_{k}\right] \in \mathscr{F}_{2 j}$ such that $A_{j} \subset B_{j} \cup\left\{\bigcup_{k}\left[s_{k}, t_{k}\right]\right\}$. Since $f$ is increasing we have

$$
f\left(v_{j}+\varepsilon\right)-f\left(u_{j}-\varepsilon\right) \geqq \sum_{k}\left[f\left(t_{k}\right)-f\left(s_{k}\right)\right] \geqq \beta \sum\left(t_{k}-s_{k}\right) \geqq \beta \lambda^{*}\left(A_{j}\right)
$$

Letting $\varepsilon \rightarrow 0$ this gives $\alpha\left(v_{j}-u_{j}\right)>f\left(v_{j}\right)-f\left(u_{j}\right) \geqq \beta \lambda^{*}\left(A_{j}\right)$. Hence

$$
\alpha \sum_{j}\left(v_{j}-u_{j}\right) \geqq \beta^{*}(A)
$$

Finally

$$
\sum_{j}\left(v_{j}-u_{j}\right) \geqq \lambda^{*}(A)
$$

so that



$$
\alpha \lambda^{*}(A) \geqq \beta \lambda^{*}(A) .
$$

Since $\alpha<\beta$ this can happen only if $\lambda(A)=0$. Letting $\alpha, \beta$ vary through rational points and taking the union of the sets so obtained gives the desired result.

Let $f$ be a numerical function defined on a measurable subset $E$ of the $k$ dimensional Euclidean space $\mathscr{E}$. For $x \in \mathscr{E}$ and $\varepsilon>0$ let $S(x, \varepsilon)$ be the sphere of center $x$ and radius $\varepsilon$. The vector $A \in \mathscr{E}^{\prime}$ is called the approximate derivative of $f$ at $x$ if for every $\delta>0$ the ratio

$$
\frac{1}{\lambda\{S(x, \varepsilon)\}} \lambda\{y ; y \in E \cap S(x, \varepsilon),|f(y)-f(x)-A(y-x)|>\delta|y-x|\}
$$

tends to zero as $\varepsilon \rightarrow 0$.
Suppose that $K_{n}$ is a sequence of compact subsets of $E$ such that $\lambda\{E \cap \left.\left(\bigcup K_{n}\right)^{c}\right\}=0$. Assume that the restriction of $f$ to $K_{n}$ has an approximate derivative (with respect to $K_{n}$ ) at almost all points of $K_{n}$. Then $f$ has an approximate derivative at almost all points of $E$. This follows from the fact that almost all points of $K_{n}$ are points of density of $K_{n}$.

Lemma 1. Let $f$ be a Banach valued function defined on a measurable set $E$. Let $A$ be a measurable subset of $E$ such that for every $x \in A$ there is an $\varepsilon_{0}$ and a $K$ such that if $y$ and $z$ are elements of $S\left(x, \dot{\varepsilon}_{0}\right)$ with $y \in A$ then

$$
|f(y)-f(z)| \leqq K|y-z|
$$

If $f$ possesses an approximate derivative at almost all points of $A$ then $f$ possesses a derivative at almost all points of $A$.

Proof. Let $x$ be a point of density of $A$. Assume that at $x$ the function $f$ possesses the approximate derivative $a$. For every pair ( $u, v$ ) of elements of $\mathscr{E}$ such that $x+u$ and $x+v$ belong to $E$ we have

$$
\begin{aligned}
f(x+v)-f(x)-a v= & f(x+v)-f(x+u)+f(x+u) \\
& -f(x)-a u+a(u-v)
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence, if $|u|<\varepsilon_{0}$ and $|v|<\varepsilon_{0}$, and $x+u \in A$

$$
|f(x+v)-f(x)-a v| \leqq|a||u-v|+K|u-v|+|f(x+u)-f(x)-a u|
$$

For a given $\alpha>0$ let $B(\varepsilon)$ be the set of points $x+u \in S(x, \varepsilon) \cap A$ such that

$$
|f(x+u)-f(x)-a u|<\alpha|u|
$$

Let

$$
\delta(\varepsilon)=1-\frac{\lambda[B(\varepsilon) \cap S(x, \varepsilon)]}{\lambda[S(x, \varepsilon)]}
$$

There is some constant $C$ such that $S(x, \varepsilon) \cap B^{c}(\varepsilon)$ contains no sphere of radius $C \delta^{1 / k}(\varepsilon) \varepsilon$. Thus for each $x+v \in S(x, \varepsilon)$ there is an $x+u \in B(\varepsilon)$ such that $|u-v|<C \delta^{1 / k}(\varepsilon) \varepsilon$. Consequently, to each $x+v \in S\left(x, \varepsilon_{0}\right)$ we can associate a $u$ such that $x+u \in B[|v|],|u| \leqq|v|$, and $|u-v|<C \delta^{1 / k}(|v|)|v|$. For such a choice of $u$ the preceding inequality becomes

$$
|f(x+v)-f(x)-a v| \leqq[|a|+K] c \delta^{1 / k}[|v|]|v|+\alpha|v|
$$

Since $\delta(|v|) \rightarrow 0$ and since $\alpha$ is arbitrary, the result follows. Let $E$ be a measurable subset of $\mathscr{E}$. Let $v$ be a fixed vector of $\mathscr{E}$. For each $x \in \mathscr{E}$ let $S_{x}$ be the set of points $\{y: y \in E$ and $y=x+\alpha v\}$. This set will be called the section of $E$ in the direction $v$ through $x$. The set $S_{x}$ can be identified to a subset of a real line. A point $y \in S_{x}$ which is a point of linear density of $S_{x}$ will be called a point of linear density in $E$ in the direction $v$.

Lemma 2. If $E$ is a compact set the subset of $E$ formed by the point of linear density in the direction $v$ is an $F_{\sigma, \delta}$.

Proof. For $a<0<b$ let $S(x ; a, b)$ be the set $S[x:(a, b)]=\{\alpha: x+\alpha v \in E$ and $a \leqq \alpha \leqq b\}$. Let $Q_{n, k}$ be the set of points $x \in E$ such that $b-a<1 / k$ implies $\lambda\{S[x:(a, b)]\} \geqq(1-1 / n)(b-a)$. The set $D$ of points of density is $D=\bigcap_{n \cup k} Q_{n, k}$.

Let $\left\{x_{j}\right\}$ be a sequence of points of $E$ converging to $x \in E$. Let $\alpha$ be such that $\alpha \in \bigcap_{n} \bigcup_{j>n} S\left[x_{j} ; a, b\right]$. Then $\alpha \in S[x ; a, b]$ because, for every $n$ there is a $j_{n}>n$ such that $x_{j_{n}}+\alpha v \in E$, hence $\lim x_{j_{n}}+\alpha v=x+\alpha v \in E$. It follows that

$$
\lim _{j \rightarrow \infty} \lambda\left\{S\left[x_{j} ; a, b\right]\right\} \leqq \lambda\{S[x ; a, b]\}
$$

Hence each $Q_{n, k}$ is closed. This completes the proof.
Theorem 2. Let $f$ be a real valued Lipschitzian function defined on a measurable subset $E$ of the $k$-dimensional space $\mathscr{E}$. At almost every point of $E$ the function $f$ admits a derivative.

Proof. Suppose that $|f(x)-f(y)|<K|x-y|$. If $k=1$ the function $f(x)-f\left(x_{0}\right)+K\left(x-x_{0}\right)$ is nondecreasing continuous on $E$ for $x \geqq x_{0}$. It can be extended to a continuous nondecreasing function defined on the whole line. Hence, the result according to the Lebesgue theorem.

Suppose the theorem valid for $k=1,2, \ldots, n$. Then it also holds for $k= n+1$. To see this denote by $u$ vectors of the form $u=\left[u_{1}, u_{2}, \ldots, u_{n}, 0\right]$ and by $v$ vectors of the form $\left[0,0,0, \ldots, 0, v_{n+1}\right]$. According to the assumption made, for almost every $x \in E$, there is a vector $A(x)$ such that

$$
\lim \frac{1}{|u|}|f(x+u)-f(x)-A(x) u|=0
$$

and

$$
\lim \frac{1}{|v|}|f(x+v)-f(x)-A(x) v|=0
$$

Let

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta_{n}(x)= & \sup _{|u| \leqq 1 / n} \frac{1}{|u|}|f(x+u)-f(x)-A(x) u| \\
& +\sup _{|v| \leqq 1 / n} \frac{1}{|v|}|f(x+v)-f(x)-A(x) v|
\end{aligned}
$$

The function $\Delta_{n}$ is measurable and $\Delta_{n} \rightarrow 0$ almost surely on $E$. According to Egorov's theorem, $\Delta_{n} \rightarrow 0$ uniformly on the compacts of a $\lambda$-dense family. On such a compact $K$, one can assume that $A$ is continuous.

Suppose $x \in K$ and $x+u+v \in K$. Then

$$
\begin{aligned}
R(x, u, v)= & \frac{1}{|u+v|}|f(x+u+v)-f(x)-A(x)(u+v)| \\
\leqq & \frac{1}{|u+v|}|f(x+u+v)-f(x+v)-A(x+v) u| \\
& +\frac{1}{|u+v|}|f(x+v)-f(x)-A(x) v| \\
& +\frac{1}{|u+v|}|A(x+v)-A(x)||u|
\end{aligned}
$$

at least whenever $x+v \in K$. Let $x$ be a point of density $K$ such that $x$ be also a point of linear density in the direction $v_{0}=[0,0, \ldots, 1]$. Let $K(x, \varepsilon)$ be the set of points $y \in K$ such that $y \in S(x, \varepsilon)$ and $y=x+u+v$ with $x+v \in K$. Clearly $\lambda\{K(x, \varepsilon)\} / \lambda\{S(x, \varepsilon)\}$ tends to unity as $x \rightarrow 0$. Since $\Delta_{n} \rightarrow 0$ uniformly on $K$ this shows that at such a point $x$ the ratio $R(x, u, v)$ tends to zero "approximately" so that $f$ admits the approximate derivative $A(x)$.

However, according to Fubini's theorem and Lemma 2, almost all points of $K$ are points of linear density in the direction $v_{0}$. The statement of the theorem is then a consequence of Lemma 1.

Corollary. If $f$ defined on a measurable set $E$ is such that

$$
\limsup _{|y| \rightarrow 0} \frac{1}{|y|}|f(x+y)-f(x)|<\infty
$$

at all points of $E$ except possibly a countable set, then $f$ is differentiable at almost all points of $E$.

Let $E$ be an arbitrary subset of the $k$-dimensional space $\mathscr{E}$. A ray $\{x+\alpha v, \alpha \geqq 0\}$ (with $v$ a unit vector) will be called a weak tangent of $E$ at $x \in E$ if there is a sequence $\left\{x_{n}\right\}$ of elements of $E$ such that $x_{n} \neq x$ and $x_{n}=x+\alpha_{n} v_{n}$ where $\alpha_{n} \rightarrow 0$ and where the unit vector $v_{n}$ tends to the unit vector $v$. The set of weak tangent rays at $x$ is the contingent at $x$. It follows from the definition that the contingent at $x$ is closed.

Theorem 2. Assume that $F$ is a subset of $E$ such that for each $x \in F$ the contingent $C(x, E)$ of $E$ at $x$ does not include the ray of direction $v(x)$. Then,
except for a subset of zero $(k-1)$-measure for $x \in F$ the contingent $C(x, E)$ is either a plane or constitutes a half-space.

Proof. Assume first $v(x)=v$ fixed. One may assume that the elements of $\mathscr{E}$ are written $x=\left[x_{1}, x_{2}, \ldots, x_{k}\right]$ where $[0,0, \ldots, 0,1]$ is precisely the vector $v$. In other words, each $x$ can be written $x=[u, \alpha v]$ where $u$ is ( $k-1$ ) dimensional. For every integer $n$ let $F_{n}$ be the set of points $x=[u, \alpha v] \in F$ such that if $\left|u_{1}-u\right| \leqq 1 / n$ and $\left|\alpha_{1}-\alpha\right| \leqq 1 / n$ then for every $\left[u_{1}, \alpha_{1} v\right] \in E$

$$
\alpha_{1}-\alpha \leqq n\left|u_{1}-u\right|
$$

Clearly $F=\bigcup_{n} F_{n}$. Furthermore, each $F_{n}$ can be covered by a sequence $\left\{F_{n, k}\right\}$ of disjoint sets having diameters inferior to $1 / n$. For each $F_{n, k}$ let $G_{n, k}= \left\{u ;[u, \alpha v] \in F_{n, k}\right.$ for some $\left.\alpha\right\}$.

If $[u, \alpha v]$ and $\left[u_{1}, \alpha_{1} v\right]$ both belong to $F_{n, k}$, then the inequality (*) implies

$$
\left|\alpha_{1}-\alpha\right| \leqq n\left|u_{1}-u\right|
$$

Therefore, $F_{n, k}$ is the graph of a Lipschitzian function $f_{n, k}$ defined on $G_{n, k}$. Consequently, $f_{n, k}$ can be extended by continuity to a Lipschitzian function defined on the closure $\bar{G}_{n, k}$ of $G_{n, k}$. It follows that $f_{n, k}$ is differentiable at almost all points of $G_{n, k}$.

Let $A_{n, k}$ be the derivative of $f_{n, k}$ and let $u_{0} \in G_{n, k}$ be a point of density of $\bar{G}_{n, k}$. Given $\delta>0$ there is an $\varepsilon$ such that

$$
\left|f_{n, k}(u)-f_{n, k}\left(u_{0}\right)-A\left(u_{0}\right)\left(u-u_{0}\right)\right|<\varepsilon\left|u-u_{0}\right|
$$

for every $u \in S\left(u_{0}, \varepsilon\right) \cap \bar{G}_{n, k}$.
Let $x_{m}=\left[u_{m}, \alpha_{m} v\right]$ be a point of $E$. Assume that $x_{m} \neq x_{0}=\left[u_{0}, \alpha_{m} v\right]= \left[u_{0}, f_{n, k}\left(u_{0}\right) v\right]$ and that $x_{m} \rightarrow x_{0}$.

Since $u_{0}$ is a point of density of $\bar{G}_{n, k}$, there is a sequence $\eta_{m}$ tending to zero such that for each $u \in S\left(u_{0}\right)\left|u_{m}\right|$ one can find a $u^{\prime \prime} \in \bar{G}_{n, k} \cap S\left(u_{0},\left|u_{m}\right|\right)$ such that $\left|u^{\prime \prime}-u_{0}\right| \leqq\left|u-u_{0}\right|$ and $\left|u^{\prime \prime}-u\right|<\eta_{m}\left|u-u_{0}\right|$. Therefore, $u^{\prime \prime}$ being a limit point of $G_{n, k}$, for each $m$ there is a $u_{m}^{\prime} \in G_{n, k}$ such that

$$
\left|u_{m}^{\prime}-u_{0}\right| \leqq\left|u_{m}-u_{0}\right| \text { and }\left|u_{m}^{\prime}-u_{m}\right|<\eta_{m}\left|u_{m}-u_{0}\right|
$$

As soon as $\left|x_{m}-x_{0}\right|<1 / n$ we shall have

$$
\alpha_{m}-\alpha_{0} \leqq n\left|u_{m}-u_{0}\right|
$$

Also

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \alpha_{m}-\alpha_{0}-A\left(u_{0}\right)\left(u_{m}-u_{0}\right) \\
& \quad=\alpha_{m}-f_{n, k}\left(u_{m}^{\prime}\right)+f_{n, k}\left(u_{m}^{\prime}\right)-f_{n, k}\left(u_{0}\right)-A\left(u_{0}\right)\left(u_{m}^{\prime}-u_{0}\right)+A\left(u_{0}\right)\left(u_{m}^{\prime}-u_{m}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence

$$
\begin{aligned}
\alpha_{m} & -\alpha_{0}-A\left(u_{0}\right)\left(u_{m}-u_{0}\right) \\
& \leqq \alpha_{m}-f_{n, k}\left(u_{m}^{\prime}\right)+\left|A\left(u_{0}\right)\right| \eta_{m}\left|u_{m}-u_{0}\right|+\varepsilon_{m}\left|u_{m}-u_{0}\right| \\
& \leqq\left[\left|A\left(u_{0}\right)\right| \eta_{m}+\varepsilon_{m}\right]\left|u_{m}-u_{0}\right|+\left[\alpha_{m}-f_{n, k}\left(u_{m}^{\prime}\right)\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

Consider the point $x_{m}^{\prime}=\left[u_{m}^{\prime}, \alpha_{m}^{\prime} v\right]=\left[u_{m}^{\prime}, f_{n, k}\left(u_{m}^{\prime}\right) v\right]$ of $F_{n, k}$. Since $x_{m}^{\prime} \in F_{n, k}$ (for $m$ large enough) we have $\alpha_{m}-\alpha_{m}^{\prime} \leqq n\left|u_{m}-u_{m}^{\prime}\right| \leqq n \eta_{m}\left|u_{m}-u_{0}\right|$.
