Finally, $\alpha_{m}-$

$$
\begin{gathered}
\alpha_{0}-A\left(u_{0}\right)\left(u_{m}-u_{0}\right)=\left\{\left[\left|A\left(u_{0}\right)\right|+n\right] \eta_{m}+\varepsilon_{m}\right\}\left|u_{m}-u_{0}\right| \text { and } \\
\quad \limsup _{m \rightarrow \infty} \frac{1}{\left|u_{m}-u_{0}\right|}\left|\alpha_{m}-\alpha_{0}-A\left(u_{0}\right)\left(u_{m}-u_{0}\right)\right| \leqq 0
\end{gathered}
$$

This shows that at almost all points $u_{0}$ of $G_{n, k}$ the contingent of $E$ is situated "below" the plane $\alpha=\alpha_{0}+A\left(u_{0}\right)\left(u-u_{0}\right)$. Further, every ray of this plane is in $C(x, E)$.

Furthermore, the graph $F_{n, k}$ has finite ( $k-1$ )-measure as graph of a Lipschitzian function and, except for points forming a subset of $F_{n, k}$ of zero ( $k-1$ )measure the contingent of $E$ is on the one side of a plane which does not include the $[x+\alpha v], \alpha>0$ ray.

If $v(x)$ varies with $x$, let $\left\{v_{j}\right\}$ be a countable set of unit vectors dense in the unit sphere. Since $C(x, E)$ is closed, for each $x \in F$ there will be a $v_{j}$ to which the preceding argument applies. Taking a union over $j$ it follows that, except for a subset of $F$ of zero ( $k-1$ )-measure there is a plane $H(x)$ such that the contingent $C(x, E)$ is contained in the half-space $H_{1}(x)$ determined by $H(x)$ which does not contain the direction $v(x)$.

Let $F^{\prime}$ be the subset of $F$ formed by the point $x$ at which $C(x, E)$ is contained in a half-space $H_{1}(x)$ but is not the entire half-space. For each $v_{j}$ let $F_{j}^{\prime}$ be the subset of $F^{\prime}$ formed by those points $x$ whose contingent excludes the direction $v_{j}$ but where the ray of direction $v_{j}$ is in $H_{1}(x)$. Since the set of $v_{j}$ is dense in every half-space we have $F^{\prime}=\bigcup F_{j}^{\prime}$.

For $x \in F_{j}^{\prime}$ except those of a set of ( $k-1$ )-measure zero the contingent is contained in two distinct half-spaces $H_{1}(x)$ and $H_{1}^{\prime}(x)$ and includes the planes $H(x)$ and $H^{\prime}(x)$. This is impossible and shows that $F^{\prime}$ has $(k-1)$-measure zero.

Corollary. If at every point of $F$ the contingent $C(x, E)$ excludes a whole line, then at all points of $F$ except those of a subset of $(k-1)$-measure zero $C(x, E)$ is a plane.

The theorem can be stated as follows.
Theorem 3. Let $E$ be a subset of a $k$-dimensional Euclidean space $\mathscr{E}$. Let $F$ be the subset of $E$ where $C(x, E)$ is not the entire space. Then
(1) $F$ is a countable union of sets of finite $(k-1)$-measure.
(2) At all points of $F$ except those of a set of zero $(k-1)$-measure the set $E$ possesses either a weakly tangent plane or a weakly tangent half-space.
(3) Let $G$ be the subset of $E$ at which $C(x, E)$ excludes a whole line. Then at all points of $G$ except for a set of zero $(k-1)$-measure the set $E$ possesses a weakly tangent plane.

Finally note that the set of points $x \in E$ where $C(x, E)$ is contained in a convex acute cone is countable.

The foregoing theorems about contingents can be applied to the graphs of numerical functions defined on an interval of the line. They yield a variety of differentiability and integrability theorems. To state them it is convenient to introduce the Dini derivates of a function $f$ from $I$ to $(-\infty,+\infty)$. The upper derivate $\bar{D} f$ at $x$ is defined by

$$
(\bar{D} f)(x)=\underset{\tau \rightarrow 0}{\lim \sup } \frac{1}{\tau}[f(x+\tau)-f(x)]
$$

If in this expression $\tau$ is allowed to tend to zero by positive values only, one obtains the upper right derivate ( $D^{r} f$ ) ( $x$ ). If $\tau$ tends to zero by values $\tau<0$, one obtains the upper left derivate. The lower derivates $\underline{D}, D_{r}, D_{l}$ are defined similarly, using "lim inf" instead of "lim sup". The derivates $D_{l}$ and $D^{r}$ are called "opposite". So are $D^{l}$ and $D_{r}$.

For any set $S \subset I$, let $\Gamma(f, S)$ be the graph of $f$ restricted to $S$, that is

$$
\Gamma(f, S)=\{(x, y) ; x \in S, y=f(x)\}
$$

Theorem 4 (Denjoy). Assume that at each point of a set $S \subset I$ one of the Dini derivates is finite. Then it is equal to its opposite except on a set $S_{0} \subset S$ such that the graph $\Gamma\left(f, S_{0}\right)$ has length zero.

Proof. Dividing $S$ in four pieces, one sees that it is enough to consider the case where $D^{r} f$ is finite on $S$. At each point $x \in S$ the contingent of $\Gamma(f, I)$ is not the entire plane. Thus at all points of $(x, y)$ of $\Gamma(f, S)$ except those of a set of zero length the contingent of $\Gamma(f, I)$ is either a line or a half-space. If it is a line, the function $f$ is differentiable at that point. If it is a half-space, it must be the set $\{(u, v):(v-y) \leq a(u-x)\}$ for $a=\left(D^{r} f\right)(x)$. The result follows.

Corollary. Assume that for each $x \in S$ either $\left(D^{r} f\right)(x)$ and $\left(D_{r} f\right)(x)$ are both finite, or that $\left(D^{l} f\right)(x)$ and $\left(D_{l} f\right)(x)$ are both finite, or that one of $(\bar{D} f)(x)$ or $(\underline{D} f)(x)$ is finite. Then there is a set $S_{0} \subset S$ such that $\Gamma\left(f, S_{0}\right)$ has length zero and such that $f$ is differentiable at each $x \in S \backslash S_{0}$.

Proof. According to Theorem 3 except for a set $S_{0}$ such that $\Gamma\left(f, S_{0}\right)$ has finite length, the contingent of $\Gamma(f, I)$ is either a line or a half-space. However, if for instance $\left(D_{r} f\right)(x)$ and $\left(D^{r} f\right)(x)$ are both finite, the contingent cannot be a half-space. Similarly $(\bar{D} f)(x)<\infty$ implies that the contingent is not a halfspace. Hence the result.

From these results one can obtain differentiability theorems for functions subject to certain restrictions introduced by Banach and Lusin.

A function $f$ defined on a set $S$ is said to satisfy Banach's condition ( $T_{2}$ ) if the set of points $y$ such that $\{x: f(x)=y\}$ is not at most countable has Lebesgue measure zero.

A function $f$ satisfies Lusin's condition ( N ) on $S$ if it transforms every subset of Lebesgue measure zero into a set of Lebesgue measure zero. A first lemma is as follows.

Lemma 3. Let $I=[a, b], a<b$ be a finite interval and let $f$ be a continuous function satisfying ( $T_{2}$ ) on $I$. Assume that $f(a) \leq f(b)$. Let $Y$ be the set $Y \subset f(I)$ of points $y$ at which $E y=\{x: x \in I, f(x)=y\}$ is at most countable. Then there is a map $y \mapsto \phi(y)$ from $Y$ to I such that (i) $\phi(y)$ is an isolated point of $E y$, (ii) $(\bar{D} f)[\phi(y)] \geq 0$, and (iii) $f[\phi(Y)]=Y$.

Proof. If $y \in Y$ and if $E y$ is reduced to a point $x$, then $(\bar{D} f)(x) \geq 0$. Indeed if $(\bar{D} f)(x)<0$, there is an $\varepsilon>0$ such that $t \in I,(t-x)<\varepsilon$ implies $f(t)<f(x)$, and $t \in I,-\varepsilon<t-x<0$ implies $f(t)>f(x)$. The first inequality implies that $f(x) \geq f(b)$. The second implies $f(x) \leq f(a)$. Thus $f(x)=f(a)=f(b)$ contrary to assumption. Therefore if $E y$ is reduced to a point one can take $\phi(y)$ equal to that point.

If $E y$ is not reduced to a point, it is countable and closed. Hence $E y$ contains two distinct isolated points, say $u$ and $v, u<v$, such that $(u, v) \cap E y$ is empty. By the same kind of argument at one of the two points the upper derivate $\bar{D} f$ must be non-negative. We shall take for $\phi(y)$ the point in question. The result follows.

Lemma 4. Let $f$ be defined and continuous on an interval I. Let $S \subset I$ be such that at each $x \in S$ the function $f$ does not have a finite or infinite derivative. Assume also that each point $x \in S$ is an isolated point of $\{t: t \in I, f(t)=f(x)\}$, then the graph $\Gamma(f, S)$ has length zero and, in particular, $f(S)$ has Lebesgue measure zero.

Proof. Since $x$ is isolated in $E y, y=f(x)$, there is some $\varepsilon>0$ such that $f(t)-f(x)$ is different from zero and keeps a constant sign in each of the intervals $(x-\varepsilon, x)$ and $(x, x+\varepsilon)$. If the sign of the difference does not change as $t$ crosses $x$, then $x$ is either a strict local maximum or strict local minimum. Let $S_{M}(n)$ be the set of points $s \in S$ such that if $|t-s|<1 / n$ and $t \neq s$ then $f(t)<f(s)$. Two points of $S_{M}(n)$ are at distance at least $1 / n$. Thus $S_{M}=\bigcup_{n} S_{M}(n)$ must be countable. The same applies to the set $S_{m}$ of strict local minima. For a point $x \in S \backslash\left(S_{M} \cup S_{m}\right)$ the fact that $f(t)-f(x)$ changes sign as $t$ crosses $x$ implies that the four Dini derivates $D^{r}, D_{r}, D_{l}$, and $D^{l}$ must have the same sign. Suppose for instance that they are positive. Then one of them must be finite, otherwise $f$ would have an infinite derivative at $x$. The result follows then from Denjoy's theorem.

An important corollary is as follows. Let $f$ be defined on an interval $I_{0}$. Let $P$ be the set of $x \in I_{0}$ where $f$ admits a finite or infinite derivative $f^{\prime}(x) \geq 0$. Let $N$ be the set where the derivative $f^{\prime}(x)$ exists and is such that $-\infty \leq f^{\prime}(x) \leq 0$.

Theorem 4. Let $I_{0}$ be a nondegenerate interval on which $f$ is continuous and satisfies ( $T_{2}$ ). Let $\lambda$ denote the Lebesgue measure and let $N$ and $P$ be the sets just defined. Then
(i) for any interval $I=[a, b] \subset I_{0}, a<b$, one has $\lambda[f(I)] \leq \max \{\lambda[f(N \cap I)]$, $\lambda[f(P \cap I)]\}$ and $-\lambda[f(N \cap I)] \leq f(b)-f(a) \leq \lambda[f(P \cap I)]$.
(ii) The set $N \cup P$ is not countable.

Proof. Let $Y$ be the set $Y \subset f(I)$ at which $E y$ is countable. Suppose $f(a) \leq f(b)$. Let $S$ be the subset of $I$ of the form $\phi(Y)$ obtained in Lemma 3. Then by Lemma $4 f\left\{S \cap(P \cup N)^{c}\right\}$ has measure zero. Since each $x \in S$ has been selected so that $(\bar{D} f)(x) \geq 0$, this implies also that $S \cap N=\varnothing$,
hence $\lambda[f(S \cap P)]=\lambda[f(S)]=\lambda(Y)=\lambda[f(I)]$ by condition ( $T_{2}$ ). However $\lambda[f(S \cap P)] \leq \lambda[f(I \cap P)]$. This gives the statement (i) if $f(a) \leq f(b)$. If on the contrary $f(a)>f(b)$, the result can be obtained by looking at $-f$. Hence the first statement.

For the second statement note that either $f$ is constant, in which case $P= N=I_{0}$ or then, for some $I$, one of the two $\lambda[f(P \cap I)]$ or $\lambda[f(N \cap I)]$ must be different from zero. Hence one of the ( $P \cap I$ ) or ( $N \cap I$ ) must be uncountably infinite.

Corollary 1. Let $g$ and $h$ be two continuous functions whose difference $f$ satisfies $\left(T_{2}\right)$. If $f^{\prime}(x)=0$ wherever it exists, then $g$ and $h$ differ by a constant.

Corollary 2. Let $f$ be a continuous function which satisfies ( $T_{2}$ ). Let $P^{+}$be the set of points $x$ where a derivative $f^{\prime}(x)$ exists and satisfies $0<f^{\prime}(x) \leq \infty$. If $f\left(P^{+}\right)$has measure zero, then $f$ is decreasing.

Proof. Indeed, if $f$ is differentiable at every point of a measurable set $E$ (here $P \backslash P^{+}$), then $\lambda[f(E)] \leq \int I_{E}(x)\left|f^{\prime}(x)\right| d x$ as can be readily seen by passage to the limit from the sets $E(n)$ where $E(n)=\left\{x ;\left|f^{\prime}(x)\right| \leq n\right\}$. Here we have $\lambda\left[f\left(P \backslash P^{+}\right)\right]=\lambda[f(P)]$ but $\lambda\left[F\left(P \backslash P^{+}\right)\right]=0$. Hence the result.

A result similar to Theorem 4 can be obtained if instead of ( $T_{2}$ ) one uses Lusin's condition ( N ) according to the following assertion.

Theorem 5. Let $f$ be a continuous function defined on a finite closed interval $I$ and satisfying there Lusin's condition ( N ). Then
(i) $f$ satisfies ( $T_{2}$ ).
(ii) If $S_{\infty}$ is the set of points $x$ where $f^{\prime}(x)$ exists and $\left|f^{\prime}(x)\right|=\infty$, then $f\left(S_{\infty}\right)$ has Lebesgue measure zero.

Proof. We shall only sketch the proof. For details see the book Theory of the Integral by Saks, page 282. First note that for every closed set $S \subset I$ there is a $G_{\delta}$ set $A \subset S$ such that $f(S)=f(A)$ and such that $f$ is injective if restricted to $A$. To construct $A$, for each $y \in f(S)$ take $\phi(y)$ equal to $\inf \{x: x \in S, f(x)=y\}$. That $A$ is a $G_{\delta}$ follows from the fact that, if $x \in S \backslash A$, there is an integer $n$ such that $f(x)=f(t)$ for some $t$ such that $x-t \geq 1 / n$. The set $B_{n}$ of points $x$ having this property is closed. Hence $A=S \cap\left(\bigcup B_{n}\right)^{c}$ is a $G_{\delta}$.

Now if $E$ is any measurable subset of $I$ and if $n<\infty$, one can find a $G_{\delta}$ set, say $A_{n}$ such that $\lambda\left[f(E) \backslash f\left(A_{n}\right)\right]<1 / n$ and such that $f$ is injective if restricted to $A_{n}$. Indeed $E$ can be written in the form $E=M \cup\left(\bigcup_{n} F_{n}\right)$ where $\lambda(M)=0$ and where the $F_{n}$ are compact and such that $F_{n} \subset F_{n+1}$. This and condition (N) yields $\lambda[f(M)]=0$ and $\lambda[f(E)]=\lim _{n} \lambda\left[f\left(F_{n}\right)\right]$ because the compacts $f\left(F_{n}\right)$ increase to $f\left(\bigcup_{n} F_{n}\right)$. Now, by the previous argument, there is in $F_{n}$ a $G_{\delta}$ set $A_{n}$ such that $f\left(F_{n}\right)=f\left(A_{n}\right)$ and such that $f$ restricted to $A_{n}$ is injective.

It follows, by taking $B=\bigcup_{n} A_{n}$, that $E$ contains a $G_{\delta, \sigma}$ set $B$ such that $\lambda[f(E) \backslash f(B)]=0$ and such that for each $y \in f(B)$ the set $\{x: x \in B, f(x)=y\}$ is at most countable.

Now for each measurable $E \subset I$, let $\mathscr{F}(E)$ be the class of measurable sets $B \subset E$ such that $\lambda[f(E)-f(B)]=0$ and such that, restricted to $B$, the function $f$ assumes any value $y \in f(B)$ at most a countable number of times. Let $\beta$ be $\sup \{\lambda(B): B \in \mathscr{F}(I)\}$. Let $B_{n} \in \mathscr{F}(I)$ such that $\lambda\left(B_{n}\right) \geq \beta-(1 / n)$
and let $B_{\infty}=\bigcup B_{n}$. Then $B_{\infty} \in \mathscr{F}(I)$. Let $V$ be in the class $\mathscr{F}\left(I \backslash B_{\infty}\right)$. Then $\lambda(V)=0$ by definition of $B_{\infty}$. Thus, by condition ( N ) one can assert that $\lambda[f(V)]=0$. By the second part of the argument carried out above this implies $\lambda\left[f\left(I \backslash B_{\infty}\right)\right]=0$. Thus $f$ satisfies ( $T_{2}$ ) since if $y \notin f\left(I \backslash B_{\infty}\right)$, the set $E y=\{x: f(x)=y\}$ is contained in $B_{\infty}$ and countable.

For the second statement consider the graph $\Gamma(f, I)$. At each $x$ such that $\left|f^{\prime}(x)\right|=\infty$, this graph has a contingent contained in a line parallel to the $y$ axis. It follows from the beginning of the proof of Theorem 2 that the projection of that part of $\Gamma(f, I)$ on the $x$-axis has measure zero. The image by $f$ of this projection has measure zero by condition ( N ).

Now consider a function $f$ defined on a compact interval $I=[a, b]$. Let $P^{*}$ be the set of points $x$ where $f^{\prime}(x)$ exists and is such that $0 \leq f^{\prime}(x)<\infty$. Define $N^{*}$ similarly. Let $\dot{f}(x)=f^{\prime}(x)$ for $x \in P^{*} \cup N^{*}$ and let $\dot{f}(x)=0$ otherwise.

Theorem 6. Assume that $f$ is continuous and that it satisfies condition (N) on the compact interval $I=[a, b]$. Assume in addition that either $\int_{N^{*}}\left|f^{\prime}(x)\right| d x< \infty$ or that $\int_{P^{*}} f^{\prime}(x) d x<\infty$.

Then for each $t \in[a, b]$ the value $f(t)-f(a)$ is equal to the Lebesgue integral $f(t)-f(a)=\int_{a}^{t} \dot{f}(x) d x$.

Proof. Consider, for instance, the case where $\int_{P^{*}} f^{\prime}(x) d x<\infty$. Let $P$ be the set where $f^{\prime}(x)$ exists and $0 \leq f^{\prime}(x) \leq \infty$. Then, by Theorem 4 for any interval $J=[\alpha, \beta] \subset I$ one has

$$
f(\beta)-f(\alpha) \leq \lambda[f(P \cap J)]=\lambda\left[f\left(P^{*} \cap J\right)\right]
$$

However

$$
\lambda\left[f\left(P^{*} \cap J\right)\right] \leq \int_{J}[\dot{f}(x)]^{+} d x
$$

Therefore $g(t)=f(t)-\int_{a}^{t}[\dot{f}(x)]^{+} d x$ is a continuous decreasing function. It follows that $g$ and therefore $f$ are functions of bounded variation.

Thus, by Theorem 1, the derivative $f^{\prime}$ exists and is finite on a set $D$ such that $\lambda(I \backslash D)=0$. Now consider the signed measure $\mu$ induced by the function $f$ by writing $\mu\{(\alpha, \beta]\}=f(\beta)-f(\alpha)$. It has the form $\mu(A)=s(A)+\int_{A} \dot{f}(x) d x$ where $s$ is singular with respect to the Lebesgue measure. However, for any interval $I=[\alpha, \beta]$ we have

$$
\begin{aligned}
|f(\beta)-f(\alpha)| & \leq \max \{\lambda[f(I \cap D \cap N)], \lambda[f(I \cap D \cap P)]\} \\
& \leq \int_{J}|\dot{f}(x)| d x
\end{aligned}
$$

Thus $|\mu(A)| \leq \int_{A}|\dot{f}(x)| d x$ for any set in the field generated by the intervals. It follows that $s=0$. Hence the result.

## Primitives on the Line

Let $F$ and $f$ be two functions defined on an interval $I$ of the line and taking values in a normed space. One says that $F$ is the Newton primitive of $f$ if the derivative $F^{\prime}(x)$ exists and is equal to $f(x)$ for all $x$. This definition is a rather restrictive one, as can be seen by taking for $f$ a staircase kind of a function. There are many variations of it that are easier to work with. Many of them depend on added restrictions on the function $F$. Here are some definitions.

The function $F$ is called absolutely continuous (AC) on a subset $S$ of the line if for every $\varepsilon>0$ there is a $\delta>0$ such that for every finite sequence $\left\{\left(\alpha_{j}, \beta_{j}\right), \alpha_{j}<\beta_{j}\right\}$ of intervals whose extremities belong to $S$ the inequality $\sum_{j}\left[\beta_{j}-\alpha_{j}\right]<\delta$ implies $\sum_{j}\left|F\left(\beta_{j}\right)-F\left(\alpha_{j}\right)\right|<\varepsilon$. The function $F$ is called (ACG) on $S$ if it is continuous on $S$ and if $S$ is the countable union of a family of sets on each of which $F$ is (AC). There are stronger conditions, called $\mathrm{AC}_{*}$ and $\mathrm{ACG}_{*}$, obtainable as follows.

Let $F$ be defined on an interval $I$. For every interval $J \subset I$ let $\Omega(F, J)$ be the oscillation $\Omega(F, J)=\sup \{|F(x)-F(y)|:(x, y) \in J \times J\}$ of $F$ on $J$. The strong variation of $F$ on a set $E$ is the sup of the sums $\sum_{j} \Omega\left(F, J_{j}\right)$ where the $J_{j}$ are disjoint intervals with extremities in $E$.

A function is $\mathrm{VB}_{*}$ on a set $E$ if its strong variation there is finite. It is $\mathrm{VBG}_{*}$ if $E$ is a countable union of sets where $F$ is $\mathrm{VB}_{*}$. The function $F$ is $\mathrm{AC}_{*}$ on $E$ if it is bounded on an interval containing $E$ and such that for each $\varepsilon>0$ there is a $\delta>0$ such that if $J_{j} ; j=1,2, \ldots$ is any finite sequence of intervals with extremities in $E$ then $\sum_{j} \Omega\left(F, J_{j}\right)<\varepsilon$. A function is $\mathrm{ACG}_{*}$ on $E$ if it is continuous on $E$ and if $E$ is a countable union of sets where $F$ is $\mathrm{AC}_{*}$.

Some definitions of "primitives" are as follows, for $f$ defined on an interval I,
(a) Lebesgue. $F$ is (AC) and $F^{\prime}(x)=f(x)$ almost everywhere on $I$.
(b) Perron. $F$ is $\mathrm{ACG}_{*}$ and $F^{\prime}(x)=f(x)$ almost everywhere on $I$.
(c) Denjoy. $F$ is ACG and the approximate derivative $F_{a p}^{\prime}(x)=f(x)$ almost everywhere on $I$.

Actually, the definition (b) attributed here to Perron is due to Denjoy. Perron used major and minor functions. A major function $U$ of $f$ is one such that $(\underline{D U})(x) \geq f(x)$ for all $x$. A minor one $L$ is such that $(\overline{D L})(x) \leq f(x)$ for all $x$. The function $f$ is called Perron integrable on $(a, b)$ if for each $\varepsilon>0$ there are major functions $U$ and minor functions $L$ such that $|[U(b)-U(a)]-[L(b)-L(a)]|< \varepsilon$. The pointwise inf of the major functions with $U(a)=0$ is then the Perron primitive of $f$. It can be shown that it satisfies the requirement (b).

It can also be shown, by a fairly straightforward argument, that any ACG function on an interval $I$ satisfies Lusin's condition (N). Therefore, by Theorem 6 of the preceding section, Denjoy primitives agree with Lebesgue primitives if the function $f$ is Lebesgue integrable. In any event the sum of two ACG functions is ACG and it follows easily from Theorem 5, Section 2, that an ACG function $F$ is increasing if either $D^{r} F(x) \geq 0$ or if $F_{a p}^{\prime}(x) \geq 0$ except on a set whose image by $F$ has Lebesgue measure zero. In particular two ACG functions with the same approximate derivative almost everywhere differ only



by a constant.

## Continuity of the Differentiation Operation

Let $F_{n}$ be a sequence of continuous nondecreasing functions defined on an interval $[a, b]$ and let $f_{n}(x)$ be equal to the derivative $D F_{n}(x)$ if this exists and to zero otherwise.

Lemma 1. Assume that both $\sum_{n}\left|F_{n}(a)\right|$ and $\sum\left[F_{n}(b)-F_{n}(a)\right]$ exist and are finite. Let $S_{n}=\sum_{j=1}^{n} F_{n}$. Then
(1) $S_{n}$ converges uniformly to a limit $S$ as $n \rightarrow \infty$.
(2) In order that $S$ be absolutely continuous it is necessary and sufficient that each $F_{n}$ be absolutely continuous.
(3) The series of derivatives $\sum f_{n}$ converges almost everywhere.

Proof. One can assume $F_{n}(a) \equiv 0$. Then $S_{n+p}-S_{n}$ is a nondecreasing function. Hence $\left|S_{n+p}(x)-S_{n}(x)\right| \leqq\left|S_{n+p}(b)-S_{n}(b)\right|$, hence the first assertion. For the second assertion note that $G_{n}=S-S_{n}=\lim _{p \rightarrow \infty}\left(S_{n+p}-S_{n}\right)$ is nondecreasing. Hence, for any finite system $\left\{\left(\alpha_{j} ; \beta_{j}\right)\right\}$ of disjoint intervals $0 \leqq \sum_{j}\left[G_{n}\left(\beta_{j}\right)-G_{n}\left(\alpha_{j}\right)\right] \leqq G_{n}(b)$, therefore, if all the $F_{n}$ are absolutely continuous so is $S$. The converse follows from the inequality

$$
\sum_{j} F_{n}\left(\beta_{j}\right)-F_{n}\left(\alpha_{j}\right) \leqq \sum_{j}\left[S\left(\beta_{j}\right)-S\left(\alpha_{j}\right)\right]
$$

As for (3), by the Lebesgue theorem the derivative $[D S](x)$ exists almost everywhere. Since $S-S_{n}$ is nondecreasing we have $D S_{n} \leqq D S_{n+1} \leqq D S$, hence $D S_{n}$ increases almost everywhere to a finite limit.

Theorem 1 (Fubini). Let $F_{n}$ be a sequence of continuous nondecreasing functions such that $\sum_{n}\left\{F_{n}(b)-F_{n}(a)\right\}<\infty$. Let $S(x)=\sum_{n}\left[F_{n}(x)-F_{n}(a)\right]$. Then $D S=\sum_{n} D F_{n}$ almost everywhere.

Proof. Let $\left\{n_{k}\right\}$ be an increasing sequence of integers such that $S(b)- \sum_{j=1}^{n_{k}}\left[F_{n}(b)-F_{n}(a)\right]<2^{-k}$. Let $V_{k}(x)=S(\mathrm{x})-\sum_{j=1}^{n_{k}}\left[F_{n}(x)-F_{n}(a)\right]$. Then $\sum V_{k}$ is finite. Hence $\sum D V_{k}$ converges almost everywhere by Lemma 1. Thus $D S-D S_{n_{k}} \rightarrow 0$ almost everywhere. Since $D S_{n}$ increases as $n$ increases, the result follows.

Corollary. Let $\left\{f_{n}\right\}$ be a sequence of functions admitting Lebesgue primitives $F_{n}$. Assume that $f_{n} \leqq f_{n+1}$ a.e. and that $F_{n}(a)=0$ while $\left|F_{n}(b)\right| \leqq M<\infty$. Then $f=\lim f_{n}$ admits a Lebesgue primitive $F=\lim F_{n}$.

Proof. The inequality $f_{n} \leqq f_{n+1}$ a.e. implies that the primitive $F_{n+1}-F_{n}$ is nondecreasing. The result follows from Theorem 1 and Lemma 1, part 2, applied to these differences.

Finally note that
Theorem 2. If $F$ is continuous and $-M \leqq \underline{D} F \leqq \bar{D} F \leqq M$ at all points of $[a, b]$ except those of a countable set or more generally if F is ACG,

$$
\begin{aligned}
& D^{r} F \geqq-M \\
& D_{r} F \leqq M
\end{aligned}
$$

at almost all points of $[a, b]$, then $|F(b)-F(a)| \leqq M|b-a|$.

## Cauchy Theory

Let $G$ be an open subset of the complex plane. An arc in $G$ is a continuous function from $[0,1]$ to $G$. If $\gamma$ is an $\operatorname{arc}$ in $G$ then $\gamma(0)$ is called the initial point of the arc and $\gamma(1)$ the terminal point.

Two arcs $\beta$ and $\gamma$ having the same initial and terminal points are called homotopic if there is a continuous function $\alpha$ defined on the space $[0,1] \times[0,1]$ such that $\alpha(0, t)=\beta(t)$ and $\alpha(1, t)=\gamma(t)$. An arc $\gamma$ is called a path if $\gamma$ is the Lebesgue primitive of a bounded function $\gamma^{\prime}$.

If $f$ is a continuous function defined on $G$ and $\gamma$ is a path, the function $t \rightarrow f[\gamma(t)] \gamma^{\prime}(t)$ admits a Lebesgue primitive $F_{\gamma}$. The value $F_{\gamma}[\gamma(1)]-F_{\gamma}[\gamma(0)]$ will be denoted $\mathscr{P}[f, \gamma]$.

A continuous function $f$ defined on $G$ is called
(1) analytic on $G$ if for every $x \in G$ there is a neighborhood $V$ of $x$ such that $f$ admits in $V$ a power series expansion

$$
f(x+\xi)=\sum_{k=0}^{\infty} C_{k} \xi^{k}
$$

(2) differentiable, if for every $x \in G$ there exists a complex number $f^{\prime}(x)$ such that

$$
\lim _{|\xi| \rightarrow 0} \frac{1}{|\xi|}\left|f(x+\xi)-f(x)-\xi f^{\prime}(x)\right|=0
$$

Furthermore, we shall say that $f$ possess properties:
(A) If for every $x \in G$ there is a neighborhood $V$ and a function $g_{x}$ such that $f(y)=g_{x}^{\prime}(y)$ for every $y \in V$.
(B) If for every $x \in G$ and $\varepsilon>0$ there is a $V$ and a function $g_{x}$ such that

$$
\left|f(y)-g_{x}^{\prime}(y)\right| \leqq \varepsilon|y-x|
$$

for $y \in V$.
Let $a$ and $b$ be two elements of the complex plane $C$. By the segment $[a, b]$ is meant the set $[a, b]$ considered as an arc of initial point $a$ and terminal point $b$. A chain is an $n$-tuple (not ordered) $\left\{\left[a_{j} ; b_{j}\right], j=1,2, \ldots, n\right\}$ where each $\left[a_{j} ; b_{j}\right]$ is a segment.

Let $\left\{a_{1}, a_{2}, a_{3}\right\}$ be an ordered triple in $C$. Let $a_{1}^{\prime}=\frac{1}{2}\left(a_{2}+a_{3}\right), a_{2}^{\prime}= \frac{1}{2}\left[a_{3}+a_{1}\right], a_{3}^{\prime}=\frac{1}{2}\left[a_{1}+a_{2}\right]$. To the triple $\left\{a_{1}, a_{2}, a_{3}\right\}$ we shall associate the chain $\left[a_{2}, a_{3}\right],\left[a_{3}, a_{1}\right],\left[a_{1}, a_{2}\right]$ and call this the triangle $T=\left[a_{1}, a_{2}, a_{3}\right]$. Also we shall associate the chain

$$
T^{*}=\left\{\left[a_{1}^{\prime}, a_{2}^{\prime}, a_{3}^{\prime}\right],\left[a_{2}^{\prime}, a_{1}, a_{3}^{\prime}\right],\left[a_{3}^{\prime}, a_{2}, a_{1}^{\prime}\right],\left[a_{1}^{\prime}, a_{3}, a_{2}^{\prime}\right]\right\}
$$

called the subdivision of the triangle $\left[a_{1}, a_{2}, a_{3}\right]$.
If $K=\left\{\gamma_{j}\right\}$ is a chain, by $\mathscr{P}(f, K)$ will be meant the $\operatorname{sum} \sum_{j} \mathscr{P}\left(f, \gamma_{j}\right)$ on the
segments of $K$. Simple computation shows that $\mathscr{P}(f, T)=\mathscr{P}\left(f, T^{*}\right)$, therefore, $\mathscr{P}(f, T)$ is always the sum of the $\mathscr{P}\left(f, T_{j}\right)$ where $T_{0}, T_{1}, T_{2}, T_{3}$ are the triangles forming $T^{*}$.

Lemma 1 (Cauchy-Goursat). Let $f$ be a function having property (B). Then, for every triangle $T=\left[a_{1}, a_{2}, a_{3}\right]$ such that the convex hull $\tilde{T}$ of $\left(a_{1}, a_{2}, a_{3}\right)$ be entirely in $G$ we have $\mathscr{P}(f, T)=0$.

Proof. Suppose $|\mathscr{P}(f, T)|=\alpha \neq 0$. Let $T^{*}$ be the subdivision of $T$. Then since $\mathscr{P}(f, T)=\sum_{j=0}^{4} \mathscr{P}\left(f, T_{j}\right)$ there is at least a $j$ such that $\left|\mathscr{P}\left(f, T_{j}\right)\right| \geqq \alpha / 4$.

Repeat the subdivision operation on this particular $T_{j}$. One obtains in this a sequence of triangles $\left\{T_{n}\right\}$ such that $\widetilde{T}_{n} \supset \widetilde{T}_{n+1}$ and $\left|\mathscr{P}\left(f, T_{n}\right)\right| \geqq \alpha / 4^{n}$. The intersection of these $\tilde{T}_{n}$ is reduced to a single point $x$. Further $x \in \tilde{T} \subset G$. Therefore, for any $\varepsilon>0$ there is a neighborhood $V$ of $x$ and a function $g^{\prime}$ such that

$$
\left|f(y)-g^{\prime}(y)\right| \leqq \varepsilon|y-x|
$$

One of the $T_{n}$ is contained in $V$. Thus

$$
\left|\mathscr{P}\left(f, T_{n}\right)-\mathscr{P}\left(g^{\prime}, T_{n}\right)\right| \leqq \varepsilon\left(\operatorname{diam} T_{n}\right) \text { length } T_{n}
$$

Since for each subdivision the diameter and length of $T_{n+1}$ are $1 / 2$ of that of $T_{n}$ we have

$$
\left|\mathscr{P}\left(f, T_{n}\right)-\mathscr{P}\left(g^{\prime}, T_{n}\right)\right| \leqq \frac{\varepsilon A}{4^{n}}
$$

However, $\mathscr{P}\left(g^{\prime}, T_{n}\right)=0$ so that $\alpha \leqq \varepsilon A$ must be zero.
Lemma (Morera). If $f$ is continuous and for every triangle $T$ such that $\tilde{T} \subset G$, one has $\mathscr{P}(f, T)=0$ then $f$ has property (A).

Proof. Let $T$ be a triangle such that $\widetilde{T} \subset G$ and $x$ is interior to $\widetilde{T}$. For every $y \in \widetilde{T}$ let

$$
\begin{aligned}
F(y) & =\mathscr{P}\{f,[x, y]\} \\
F(y+\xi) & =\mathscr{P}\{f,[x, y+\xi]\}=\mathscr{P}[f,[x, y]]+\mathscr{P}[f,[y, y+\xi]]
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence

$$
\begin{aligned}
F(y+\xi)-F(y) & =\mathscr{P}[f,[y, y+\xi]] \\
& =\xi f(y)+|\xi| \varepsilon(\xi)
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore, $F^{\prime}(y)=f(y)$.
Let $f$ be a continuous function having property (A) and let $\gamma$ be an arc in $G$. For each point $\gamma(t)$ of the arc let $V(t)$ be an open neighborhood in which $f^{\prime}=g_{t}^{\prime}$. Since $\Gamma=\{\gamma(t) ; t \in[0,1)\}$ is compact there is an $\varepsilon>0$ such that if $\left|t_{1}-t_{2}\right|<\varepsilon$ then $\gamma(t) \in V\left(t_{1}\right) \cap V\left(t_{2}\right)$ for every $t$ such that $t_{1} \leqq t \leqq t_{2}$.

Divide the interval $[0,1]$ by $0=t_{0}<t_{1}<\cdots<t_{n}=1$ such that $t_{j+1}-t_{j}<\varepsilon$. For each $t_{j}$ let $F_{j}$ be a primitive of $f$ in $V\left(t_{j}\right)$. The value

$$
\mathscr{P}[f, \gamma]=\sum_{j=0}^{n-1}\left[F_{j}\left(t_{j+1}\right)-F_{j}\left(t_{j}\right)\right]
$$

does not depend on the subdivision or on the choice of the $F_{j}$. It will be called the primitive difference of $f$ along $\gamma$.

Lemma 3 (Cauchy). Let $f$ be a continuous function having property (A). Let $\alpha, \beta$ be two homotopic arcs in $G$ with endpoints $(a, b)$. Then $\mathscr{P}(f, \alpha)=\mathscr{P}(f, \beta)$.

Proof. Let $\gamma$ be a continuous function defined on the square $[0,1] \times[0,1]$ taking values in $G$ and such that $\gamma(0, t)=\alpha(t), \gamma(1, t)=\beta(t)$.

For each point ( $s, t$ ) let $V(s, t)$ be an open convex neighborhood of $\gamma(s, t)$ in which $f=F_{s, t}^{\prime}$.

There is an $\varepsilon>0$ such that if $\operatorname{dist}\left[\left(s_{1}, t_{1}\right),\left(s_{2}, t_{2}\right)\right] \leqq 2 \varepsilon,(s, t) \in V\left(s_{1}, t_{1}\right) \cap V\left(s_{2}, t_{2}\right)$ for $i=1,2, \ldots$ for every ( $s, t$ ) in the square defined by ( $s_{1}, t_{1}$ ) and $\left(s_{2}, t_{2}\right)$.

Divide [ 0,1] by

$$
\begin{aligned}
& 0=s_{0}<s_{1}<s_{2}<\cdots<s_{n}=1 \\
& 0=t_{0}<t_{1}<t_{2}<\cdots<t_{n}=1
\end{aligned}
$$

such that $\left(s_{j+1}-s_{j}\right)<\varepsilon,\left(t_{j+1}-t_{j}\right)<\varepsilon$.
It is enough to show that if $\gamma_{j}(t)=\gamma\left(s_{j}, t\right)$ then $\mathscr{P}\left(f, \gamma_{j+1}\right)=\mathscr{P}\left(f, \gamma_{j}\right)$. Dropping the indices $j$ for simplicity let $\alpha(t)=\gamma_{j}(t)$ and $\beta(t)=\gamma_{j+1}(t)$. Consider the chains $C_{k}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
& {\left[\alpha(0), \alpha\left(t_{1}\right)\right],\left[\alpha\left(t_{1}\right), \alpha\left(t_{2}\right)\right], \ldots,\left[\alpha\left(t_{k}\right), \alpha\left(t_{k+1}\right)\right]} \\
& {\left[\alpha\left(t_{k+1}\right), \beta\left(t_{k+1}\right)\right],\left[\beta\left(t_{k+1}\right), \beta\left(t_{k}\right)\right], \ldots,\left[\beta\left(t_{1}\right), \beta\left(t_{0}\right)\right]}
\end{aligned}
$$

If $\mathscr{P}(f, \alpha) \neq \mathscr{P}(f, \beta)$ there is a first $k$ such that $\mathscr{P}\left(f, C_{k}\right) \neq 0$. However, if $\mathscr{P}\left(f, C_{k}\right)=0$ then

$$
\mathscr{P}\left(f, C_{k+1}\right)=\mathscr{P}\left(f, C_{k}\right)+\mathscr{P} f,\left[\alpha\left(t_{k+1}\right), \alpha\left(t_{k+2}\right)\right]+\cdots+=0
$$

Hence the result.
Corollary 1 (Cauchy's Formula). Let $\gamma$ be an arc contained in $G$ and such that $\gamma(0)=\gamma(1)$. For $x \in G$, let $\alpha_{r}(t)=x+r e^{2 \pi i t}$. Assume that $f$ is continuous at $x$ and that it possesses property (B) on $G \backslash\{x\}$. Assume also that for $r$ sufficiently small the arcs $\gamma$ and $\alpha_{r}$ are homotopic in $G \backslash\{x\}$. Then

$$
2 \pi i f(x)=\mathscr{P}(g, \gamma)
$$

where $g(y)=(y-x)^{-1} f(y)$.
Indeed $g$ possesses property (B) on $G \backslash\{x\}$. Thus $\mathscr{P}(g, \gamma)=\mathscr{P}\left(g, \alpha_{r}\right)$ but $\mathscr{P}\left(g, \alpha_{r}\right)=\int_{0}^{1} f\left(x+r e^{2 \pi i t}\right) d t$ tends to $f(x)$ as $r \rightarrow 0$.

Corollary 2. Let $f$ be continuous on G. Assume that there is an isolated set $S$ such that $f$ satisfies (B) on $G \backslash S$. Then $f$ is analytic on $G$.

This follows from the above since Cauchy's formula yields an analytic function.
